admission_criteria: Inpatient admission for acute decompensated HF (ADHF) with any high-risk feature: respiratory distress/hypoxia (SpO2 <90%) needing supplemental O2 or NIPPV; hypoperfusion or cardiogenic shock (SBP <90 mmHg, cool extremities, rising lactate); need for IV vasoactives/inotropes; concomitant ACS or troponin elevation; new/worsening AKI or cardiorenal syndrome; symptomatic/unstable arrhythmia; significant hyponatremia (Na <130); refractory congestion requiring IV diuresis; or markedly elevated NT-proBNP with instability. Observation/short-stay for mild-moderate congestion with good early diuretic response, SBP >120, no hypoxia, normal troponin/renal function. ED discharge reserved for low-risk (e.g., MEESSI-AHF low-risk) with reliable follow-up.
risk_stratification: GWTG-HF in-hospital mortality risk score (age, SBP, BUN, HR, Na, COPD, race; c-index 0.75): predicted mortality ranges ~0.4% (low decile) to >9.7% (high decile) -> high scores drive telemetry/ICU escalation and advanced-HF referral. ADHERE classification tree: admission BUN >=43 mg/dL, then SBP <115 mmHg, then Cr >=2.75 mg/dL flag highest risk (in-hospital mortality ~2% low-risk to ~22% high-risk) -> higher level of care. MEESSI-AHF for ED disposition (low-risk -> obs/discharge). I-NEED-HELP markers (Inotropes, NYHA IV/NP peptides, End-organ dysfunction, EF <20%, Defibrillator shocks, Hospitalizations, Edema/diuretic escalation, Low BP, Prognostic med intolerance) flag need for advanced-HF/transplant/LVAD evaluation.
initial_workup_labs: BNP or NT-proBNP (diagnosis + prognosis; NT-proBNP rule-in age-stratified >450 [<50y], >900 [50-75y], >1800 [>75y] pg/mL, rule-out <300; BNP >100 rule-in, <100 rule-out) -> confirms ADHF/severity; serial trend gauges decongestion. High-sensitivity troponin -> exclude ACS/myocardial injury (prognostic). CMP/BMP (Na, K, BUN, Cr/eGFR, glucose) -> cardiorenal status, drives diuretic and GDMT dosing. CBC -> anemia/infection precipitant. LFTs -> congestive hepatopathy. TSH; iron studies (ferritin, TSAT) -> IV iron if deficient. Lactate + venous gas if hypoperfusion/shock. Urinalysis; spot urine sodium at 2h to gauge diuretic response. HbA1c, lipids; consider SPEP/free light chains if amyloid suspected in new HF.
initial_imaging_dx: 12-lead ECG on arrival (ischemia, arrhythmia, QRS duration for CRT candidacy). Chest X-ray within hours (pulmonary congestion, effusion, alternative dx). POCUS/lung ultrasound for B-lines and IVC to quantify congestion at bedside. Transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) during hospitalization to define LVEF phenotype (HFrEF <=40%, HFmrEF 41-49%, HFpEF >=50%), valvular disease, and filling pressures (Class 1) - especially for new HF or clinical change. Coronary angiography/CT coronary angiography if ischemic etiology suspected. Right heart catheterization for unclear hemodynamics or cardiogenic shock.
time_critical_interventions: Early IV loop diuretic ideally within ~1-2h of arrival for congestion (door-to-diuretic; earlier decongestion associated with better outcomes). Hypertensive acute pulmonary edema/SCAPE: immediate NIPPV + IV nitroglycerin. Cardiogenic shock (the 'shock' in the DRG): rapid recognition, activate shock team, start inotrope/vasopressor (norepinephrine first-line) and evaluate temporary mechanical circulatory support within the first hours. Concomitant STEMI/NSTEMI: cath-lab activation per ACS pathway (door-to-balloon <=90 min). Assess diuretic response early: spot urine Na at 2h (goal >50-70 mEq/L) and urine output goal >100-150 mL/h over first 6h; if inadequate, double the loop dose promptly.
initial_management: IV loop diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide, torsemide) at 2-2.5x total daily home oral loop dose IV; bolus and continuous infusion equivalent (DOSE trial) - titrate to euvolemia by urine output/weight. Diuretic resistance: add sequential nephron blockade with a thiazide (IV chlorothiazide, oral metolazone) +/- acetazolamide; consider ultrafiltration if truly refractory. Vasodilators (nitroglycerin, nitroprusside) for hypertensive ADHF. Low-output/shock: inotropes (dobutamine, milrinone), vasopressor norepinephrine first-line, MCS (IABP/Impella) as needed. Avoid IV fluids/volume (patients are congested); restrictive transfusion (Hgb <7-8 g/dL) only if needed; IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) for iron deficiency (ferritin <100, or 100-300 with TSAT <20%). Initiate/continue HFrEF GDMT once stable: ARNI sacubitril/valsartan (or ACEi/ARB), evidence-based beta-blocker (carvedilol, metoprolol succinate, bisoprolol), MRA (spironolactone, eplerenone), and SGLT2i (dapagliflozin, empagliflozin) started in-hospital regardless of EF/diabetes (EMPULSE); NIPPV/O2 for hypoxia; VTE prophylaxis.
day1_milestones: Confirm ADHF diagnosis and LVEF phenotype; identify and treat precipitant (ischemia, AF with RVR, medication/dietary nonadherence, NSAIDs, infection, uncontrolled HTN, arrhythmia). Start IV diuresis; set target dry weight, order daily weights and strict I/O, restrict Na/fluids. Telemetry monitoring; wean O2. Initiate SGLT2i early and continue/begin other GDMT pillars as BP and renal function permit. Baseline and repeat BMP for K+/renal trend; replete K+/Mg. Document baseline congestion exam (JVP, edema, lungs, weight) and hemodynamic profile (warm/cold, wet/dry).
day2_milestones: Confirm effective decongestion - net negative fluid balance, weight trending down, improving dyspnea/orthopnea; escalate diuretic (double dose or add thiazide) if response inadequate. Reassess volume exam daily; correct electrolytes (K+ >4.0, Mg repletion). Advance/add GDMT pillars toward all four for HFrEF; up-titrate as BP/renal allow. Reassess renal function and adjust for cardiorenal changes. Begin structured HF education (daily weights, Na/fluid restriction, symptom red flags) and mobilize patient; refer to PT/OT and cardiac rehab.
day3plus_milestones: Achieve euvolemia (JVP normalized, resolution of orthopnea/edema, at goal weight). Convert IV to oral loop diuretic and observe >=24h to confirm stable weight, renal function, and urine output on the oral regimen before discharge. Ensure all appropriate GDMT pillars initiated with a written up-titration plan; document rationale for any omitted pillar. Finalize device candidacy assessment (ICD/CRT if EF <=35% - implantation typically deferred ~3 months to reassess EF on GDMT outpatient). Complete teach-back education; confirm <=7-day follow-up and post-discharge labs; discharge when hemodynamically stable on oral regimen >=24h.
consults_multidisciplinary: Cardiology/HF specialist (advanced-HF consult for cardiogenic shock, EF <20%, recurrent admissions, or I-NEED-HELP markers -> transplant/LVAD eval). Interventional cardiology if ischemic etiology; electrophysiology for device evaluation. Clinical pharmacy for GDMT optimization, medication reconciliation, and anticoagulation. HF nurse navigator/educator for teach-back. Registered dietitian for Na (~2-3 g/day) and fluid education. Care management/social work early for transitions of care, home health, medication access, and social determinants. PT/OT for deconditioning and cardiac-rehab referral. Palliative care for advanced/end-stage disease or high symptom burden. Nephrology for refractory cardiorenal syndrome/need for ultrafiltration.
monitoring_level: Most patients: floor with continuous telemetry (arrhythmia surveillance during diuresis and GDMT titration). Step-down/CCU: NIPPV, high-dose/continuous IV diuretic requiring close monitoring, frequent IV vasodilator titration. ICU/CCU: cardiogenic shock, acute respiratory failure requiring intubation, IV inotropes/vasopressors, mechanical circulatory support, invasive hemodynamic (PA catheter) monitoring, or refractory ventricular arrhythmia. Escalation triggers: worsening hypoxia or rising O2 requirement, SBP <90 mmHg or hypoperfusion, rising lactate, oliguria/worsening AKI, unstable arrhythmia, or altered mental status.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: sodium restriction ~2-3 g/day and fluid restriction 1.5-2 L/day (especially if hyponatremic/volume-overloaded); heart-healthy diet with dietitian teaching. Mobility: early mobilization to prevent deconditioning; referral to cardiac rehabilitation (Class 1). VTE prophylaxis: pharmacologic (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily or UFH 5000 U SC q8-12h) for acutely ill medical inpatients unless contraindicated (Padua >=4 = high risk). Glucose control avoiding hyper/hypoglycemia (note SGLT2i). Lines/catheters: remove urinary catheter early once diuresis assessable, minimize central access, de-escalate telemetry when stable. Continue daily weights and strict intake/output throughout.
discharge_criteria: Achieved euvolemia/near-euvolemia (resolution of congestion, at/near dry weight, normalized JVP, minimal edema). Transitioned to oral diuretic for >=24h with stable weight, renal function, and electrolytes. Hemodynamically stable - off IV vasoactives >=24h, adequate SBP, and O2 at/near baseline (room air or home O2). GDMT initiated/optimized with a written titration plan; precipitant identified and addressed. Follow-up appointment scheduled within 7 days, education completed with teach-back, and a safe discharge disposition confirmed.
discharge_planning: Disposition home (with home health/telemonitoring as needed) or SNF if deconditioned. Full medication reconciliation (GDMT, diuretic dose, anticoagulation for AF) with attention to affordability/access. Follow-up visit within 7 days (Class 1) plus early phone contact at 48-72h and post-discharge BMP. Teach-back education: daily weights, Na/fluid restriction, medication adherence, and symptom red flags (weight gain >2-3 lb/day or >5 lb/week, worsening dyspnea/orthopnea/edema) with clear call-back instructions. Written discharge instructions covering the 6 HF elements (activity, diet, discharge meds, follow-up, weight monitoring, worsening symptoms). Enroll in HF transitions-of-care/disease-management program; consider implantable PA-pressure monitoring (CardioMEMS) in selected NYHA III with prior admission. Update vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal, COVID-19).
expected_los: Blended benchmark across the collapsed triplet: DRG 291 (w/MCC) GMLOS ~3.8 days (ALOS ~5.0, RW ~1.28); DRG 292 (w/CC) GMLOS ~2.9 days (ALOS ~3.7, RW ~0.85); DRG 293 (no CC/MCC) GMLOS ~2.1 days (ALOS ~2.5, RW ~0.57). Typical U.S. HF hospitalization ~4-5 days.
target_los: Optimized best-practice: DRG 293 ~2 days; DRG 292 ~2.5-3 days; DRG 291 ~3.5-4 days (longer with ICU/shock/MCS); blended target ~3 days. Caveat: never discharge before euvolemia and >=24h stable on oral diuretic - premature discharge with residual congestion is the dominant driver of 30-day readmission, so LOS reduction must not precede decongestion.
quality_metrics: CMS HRRP 30-day risk-standardized readmission rate (HF is a penalty-target condition) and 30-day risk-standardized mortality. GWTG-HF achievement measures: evidence-based beta-blocker at discharge; ACEi/ARB/ARNI for LVSD; MRA; SGLT2i at discharge; anticoagulation for AF; ICD/CRT counseling or placement; LVEF assessed/documented; complete discharge instructions; post-discharge follow-up appointment <=7 days; influenza/pneumococcal vaccination. AHRQ PQI-08 (HF admission rate - ambulatory-care-sensitive). Legacy Joint Commission/CMS HF core measures (HF-1 to HF-4, now retired) continued via GWTG registry and CMS eCQMs.
common_complications: Worsening renal function/cardiorenal syndrome from over-diuresis (mitigate: serial BMP, titrate diuretic, avoid nephrotoxins/NSAIDs). Electrolyte derangements - hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hyponatremia (mitigate: daily labs, repletion protocols, fluid restriction). Diuretic resistance (mitigate: IV dosing, sequential nephron blockade, ultrafiltration). Symptomatic hypotension from GDMT up-titration (mitigate: staged titration, BP monitoring). Arrhythmias - AF/RVR, VT (mitigate: telemetry, electrolyte optimization). Hospital-acquired AKI, delirium, VTE, infection, and falls (mitigate: VTE prophylaxis, early mobilization, line/catheter removal, delirium precautions). Contrast-associated nephropathy if catheterized.
readmission_drivers: Residual congestion at discharge - the #1 modifiable driver (lever: discharge only at euvolemia, oral-diuretic conversion >=24h). Medication nonadherence and failure to initiate/up-titrate GDMT (lever: in-hospital GDMT start + STRONG-HF rapid up-titration + affordability screening). Dietary Na/fluid nonadherence (lever: dietitian teaching, teach-back). Missed early follow-up or omitted post-discharge labs (lever: <=7-day visit, 48-72h phone call). Social determinants - transport, health literacy, low support, cost (lever: social work/care management, transitions-of-care program). Uncontrolled comorbidities (renal disease, COPD, AF, diabetes) and absent home weight monitoring (lever: remote monitoring, daily weights, comorbidity co-management).
guideline_source: Primary: 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure (Heidenreich et al., Circulation 2022;145(18):e895-e1032) - current Class I framework. Supported by the 2024 ACC Expert Consensus Decision Pathway for Treatment of Heart Failure With Reduced Ejection Fraction (JACC 2024; PMID 38466244 - the current update to the 2021 Maddox et al. ECDP) and the 2024 ACC ECDP on Clinical Assessment, Management, and Trajectory of Patients Hospitalized With Heart Failure (PMID 39127954), plus corroborating ESC 2021/2023 acute and chronic HF guidelines.

Felker GM, et al. Diuretic Strategies in Acute Decompensated HF (DOSE). N Engl J Med. 2011;364(9):797-805. PMID 21366472 (DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1005419). Velazquez EJ, et al. PMID 20123668 (DOI 10.1161/CIRCOUTCOMES.109.854877). Citations retrieved via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong - predominantly Class I recommendations from a current (2022) ACC/AHA/HFSA society guideline, reinforced by multiple RCTs (DOSE, PIONEER-HF, EMPULSE, STRONG-HF) and the externally validated GWTG-HF risk score. Decongestion, in-hospital GDMT initiation/up-titration, and early structured follow-up are high-certainty. Select operational targets (exact door-to-diuretic time, specific LOS targets, spot urine-sodium cutoffs extrapolated from ESC guidance) are consensus/expert-opinion and graded moderate.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed triplet spans a wide severity gradient. DRG 293 (no CC/MCC): uncomplicated 'warm-and-wet' congestion, good diuretic response, floor/telemetry, rapid oral conversion, LOS ~2 days. DRG 292 (w/CC): complicating conditions (e.g., AKI stage 1, hyponatremia, diabetes with complication, moderate COPD, AF) prolong diuresis and require closer monitoring, LOS ~3 days. DRG 291 (w/MCC): cardiogenic shock (the 'shock' in the DRG title), acute respiratory failure requiring NIPPV/intubation, AKI requiring dialysis, acute MI, or major arrhythmia -> ICU/CCU with inotropes/vasopressors/MCS and invasive monitoring, palliative/advanced-HF evaluation, highest mortality (GWTG-HF high decile ~10%, ADHERE high-risk ~22%) and LOS ~5+ days. Accurate documentation of MCC/CC drivers (cardiogenic shock, acute-on-chronic HF specificity, AKI, hyponatremia) determines correct DRG assignment and severity-of-illness capture.
pathway_pearls: 1) Decongest completely - discharge at euvolemia, not merely 'improved'; residual congestion is the top readmission driver, so convert to oral diuretic and observe >=24h before discharge. 2) Start all four GDMT pillars in-hospital before discharge (SGLT2i is safe regardless of EF/diabetes per EMPULSE; ARNI initiation is safe per PIONEER-HF) and up-titrate rapidly with structured early follow-up (STRONG-HF: full doses by ~2 weeks, visit <=7 days cuts death/readmission ~34%). 3) Identify and treat the precipitant every admission (ischemia, AF/RVR, nonadherence, NSAIDs, infection, uncontrolled HTN). 4) Lock in the transitions-of-care bundle: <=7-day follow-up, 48-72h phone call, teach-back education, daily weights, and post-discharge labs.
verification_notes: Verified against PubMed (via PubMed MCP) and web sources (ACC/JACC, AHA journals, JAMA/MDCalc). ALL SIX key_citations/PMIDs are REAL and correctly attributed - titles, journals, volumes, issues, pages, and DOIs all match PubMed exactly: Heidenreich 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA HF Guideline (PMID 35363499, DOI 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001063); Felker/DOSE (PMID 21366472, DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1005419); Velazquez/PIONEER-HF (PMID 30415601, DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1812851); Voors/EMPULSE (PMID 35228754, DOI 10.1038/s41591-021-01659-1); Mebazaa/STRONG-HF (PMID 36356631, DOI 10.1016/S0140-6736(22)02076-1); Peterson/GWTG-HF (PMID 20123668, DOI 10.1161/CIRCOUTCOMES.109.854877). No fabricated PMIDs. risk_stratification: GWTG-HF details confirmed from the source abstract - c-index 0.75, mortality range 0.4%-9.7%, and the 7 variables (age, SBP, BUN, HR, Na, COPD, race) are exact. ADHERE CART thresholds (BUN >=43, SBP <115, Cr >=2.75 mg/dL; mortality ~2% low-risk to ~22% high-risk) match Fonarow JAMA 2005;293(5):572-580. MEESSI-AHF and I-NEED-HELP are real, correctly described tools. time_critical_interventions: door-to-balloon <=90 min correct/current; norepinephrine first-line vasopressor for cardiogenic shock correct; SCAPE NIPPV+IV nitroglycerin correct; urine Na/urine-output targets are consensus/expert (row acknowledges this in evidence_grade). initial_management: drug classes correct (2-2.5x home loop dose per DOSE, four-pillar GDMT, restrictive transfusion Hgb <7-8 g/dL, IV iron criteria ferritin <100 or 100-300 with TSAT <20%) - all consistent with 2022 guideline. quality_metrics: HRRP, GWTG-HF achievement measures, AHRQ PQI-08, and retired HF-1 to HF-4 core measures all real and applicable. expected/target_los: DRG 291/292/293 are titled 'Heart Failure & Shock' (shock IS in the title, as the row states); GMLOS/ALOS/RW values are plausible and close to recent CMS MS-DRG figures. STRONG-HF ~34% risk reduction matches the reported RR 0.66. Only one defect found: guideline_source misdates the supporting ACC ECDP as '2023' (should be 2024). All genuinely high-stakes clinical numerics, thresholds, drug classes, and primary citations verified correct. Attribution: article metadata retrieved from PubMed."; ISSUES: guideline_source: cites a '2023 ACC Expert Consensus Decision Pathway on optimizing HF treatment' that does not exist - the current ACC optimization pathway is the 2024 ACC ECDP for Treatment of HFrEF (PMID 38466244), which updated the 2021 Maddox et al. version. Minor/moderate: a misdated SUPPORTING reference; the primary 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA guideline is cited correctly.; Minor (not corrected - defensible): PIONEER-HF (PMID 30415601) is cited as 'N Engl J Med. 2018;380(6):539-548.' Volume 380/issue 6 is the Feb 7, 2019 print issue; PubMed lists the epub date as 2018-11-11, so '2018' matches the PubMed publication_date field and the PMID/volume/issue/pages/DOI are all correct. Ambiguous epub-vs-print year, not a fabrication.

admission_criteria: Diagnosis requires compatible clinical syndrome PLUS radiographic infiltrate (or lung-ultrasound consolidation). Admit inpatient if PSI class IV-V or CURB-65 >=2, OR hypoxemia (SpO2 <92% RA or new/increased O2 requirement), OR inability to maintain PO intake/oral antibiotics, OR hemodynamic instability, OR decompensated major comorbidity (HF/COPD/CKD/DM), OR failed outpatient therapy, OR unsafe home/social situation. ED discharge/outpatient if PSI class I-II (or CURB-65 0-1) and SpO2 >=92% RA and tolerating PO and no unstable comorbidity. Observation/short-stay for PSI III or CURB-65 =2 with expected rapid resolution. Direct ICU admission if severe CAP (>=1 major or >=3 minor IDSA/ATS criteria), septic shock, or need for mechanical ventilation.
risk_stratification: PSI/PORT (2019 ATS/IDSA-preferred): class I-II (<=70 pts, mortality 0.1-0.7%) -> outpatient; class III (71-90, ~2.8%) -> obs/short stay; class IV-V (>90, 8-29%) -> inpatient/consider ICU. CURB-65 (Confusion; BUN >19 mg/dL; RR >=30; SBP<90 or DBP<=60; age >=65): 0-1 -> outpatient, 2 -> admit/short-stay, >=3 -> inpatient +/- ICU. IDSA/ATS severe-CAP definition for ICU: >=1 MAJOR (mechanical ventilation; septic shock on vasopressors) OR >=3 MINOR (RR>=30; PaO2/FiO2<=250; multilobar infiltrates; confusion; BUN>=20; WBC<4; platelets<100k; temp<36C; hypotension needing aggressive fluids). qSOFA/SOFA + lactate to trigger sepsis bundle; SMART-COP optional for vasopressor/ventilation prediction.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential; CMP (BUN/creatinine feed CURB-65 and PSI); glucose; lactate if sepsis/severe (repeat 3-6h for clearance); VBG/ABG if hypoxic; blood cultures x2 BEFORE antibiotics (severe CAP, or MRSA/Pseudomonas empiric coverage, or intubated); sputum Gram stain and culture (severe CAP or drug-resistant risk); pneumococcal and Legionella urinary antigens (severe CAP); respiratory viral PCR panel including influenza/SARS-CoV-2/RSV (2025 ATS - guides antibiotic de-escalation); MRSA nasal-swab PCR (high negative predictive value to stop empiric vancomycin); procalcitonin (do NOT use to withhold initial antibiotics; trend to support de-escalation/duration); HIV screen. Positive urinary antigen -> narrow therapy; negative MRSA PCR -> discontinue MRSA coverage.
initial_imaging_dx: Chest radiograph (PA + lateral) in ED to confirm infiltrate - required for CAP diagnosis; lung ultrasound is an acceptable alternative where expertise exists (2025 ATS). CT chest if diagnostic uncertainty, suspected complication (empyema, abscess, post-obstructive/endobronchial lesion), or non-resolving pneumonia. Thoracentesis with pleural fluid analysis (pH, LDH, protein, glucose, Gram stain/culture, cell count) within ~24h for any parapneumonic effusion >1 cm on decubitus/ultrasound to exclude complicated effusion/empyema. ECG on arrival (baseline QTc before macrolide/fluoroquinolone; screen pneumonia-associated cardiac events). Bedside echo only if cardiac complication suspected.
time_critical_interventions: Antibiotics as soon as possible - first dose in the ED; target within 4-6h of arrival for CAP, and within 1 hour if septic shock/severe sepsis (Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 / CMS SEP-1). SEP-1 3-hour bundle when sepsis present: measure lactate, draw blood cultures before antibiotics, give broad-spectrum antibiotics, and 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4 mmol/L; reassess lactate/perfusion at 3-6h. Titrate oxygen to SpO2 92-96%. Do NOT delay antibiotics to obtain diagnostics. Re-screen for severe-CAP criteria within the first hours; early (vs delayed) ICU transfer lowers mortality.
initial_management: Empiric antibiotics by severity (2019 ATS/IDSA): INPATIENT NON-SEVERE, no resistant-organism risk - beta-lactam (ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV q24h, or ampicillin-sulbactam 1.5-3 g IV q6h, or cefotaxime) PLUS macrolide (azithromycin 500 mg IV/PO q24h), OR respiratory fluoroquinolone monotherapy (levofloxacin 750 mg or moxifloxacin 400 mg q24h); doxycycline is an alternate atypical agent. SEVERE CAP - beta-lactam PLUS macrolide, OR beta-lactam PLUS respiratory fluoroquinolone (dual). Add MRSA (vancomycin dosed to AUC, or linezolid 600 mg q12h) or Pseudomonas (piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g q6h, cefepime 2 g q8h, or meropenem 1 g q8h) coverage ONLY with locally-validated risk factors (prior MRSA/Pseudomonas isolation; hospitalization + IV antibiotics within 90 days) - the HCAP category is abandoned; obtain cultures and de-escalate at 48h. Corticosteroids: hydrocortisone 200 mg/day IV for severe CAP (CAPE COD; 2025 ATS suggests systemic steroids in severe CAP) - NOT in non-severe CAP, avoid in influenza/aspergillosis. Oseltamivir if influenza positive/suspected in season. Supportive: oxygen, isotonic fluids (30 mL/kg for sepsis), norepinephrine first-line for refractory shock. Source control: drain complicated parapneumonic effusion/empyema (chest tube +/- intrapleural tPA-DNase per MIST2).
day1_milestones: Confirm radiographic infiltrate and assign severity (PSI/CURB-65 + severe-CAP criteria); draw cultures and urinary/viral/MRSA studies before or with first antibiotic; deliver first antibiotic dose in the ED; complete sepsis bundle if applicable; titrate O2 to 92-96%; triage ICU vs step-down vs floor; start hydrocortisone if severe CAP; initiate VTE prophylaxis; obtain viral PCR/influenza status; establish resuscitation and de-escalation plan; engage antimicrobial stewardship; document baseline functional/mental status.
day2_milestones: Reassess response (fever curve, WBC, oxygenation, hemodynamics, mental status, RR); review culture/urinary-antigen/viral-PCR and MRSA nasal-swab results and DE-ESCALATE - narrow beta-lactam, stop empiric MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage on negative studies, stop antibiotics if confirmed viral with low procalcitonin and clinically stable; assess Halm clinical-stability criteria; begin mobilization/PT; ensure adequate PO intake; wean oxygen; confirm any effusion is drained; identify candidates for early IV-to-PO switch.
day3plus_milestones: Most reach clinical stability by 48-72h (afebrile, HR<=100, RR<=24, SBP>=90, SpO2>=90%/PaO2>=60, normal/baseline mental status, tolerating PO); once stable and hemodynamically sound with functioning GI tract, SWITCH IV to oral - no need to observe on oral therapy before discharge (Halm); target total antibiotic duration 5 days (minimum 3) for non-severe patients reaching stability (2025 ATS), 5-7 days for severe; discharge the same day stability/oral switch is achieved. If NOT improving by day 3, investigate failure: wrong diagnosis, resistant/atypical organism, empyema/abscess, endobronchial obstruction - obtain CT chest and repeat cultures, escalate consults.
consults_multidisciplinary: Pharmacy/antimicrobial stewardship early (dosing, de-escalation, IV-to-PO conversion, duration); Infectious Disease for severe/immunocompromised/non-resolving or resistant organisms; Pulmonology/Interventional Pulmonology for complicated effusion/empyema, non-resolving pneumonia, or bronchoscopy; Critical Care for severe CAP/ICU needs; Physical/Occupational Therapy within 24-48h for deconditioning and mobility (especially elderly); Care Management and Social Work for disposition, home oxygen, home health, adherence/substance-use barriers; Nutrition if malnourished or poor intake; Respiratory Therapy for oxygen and airway clearance; Palliative Care for advanced illness or goals-of-care discussions.
monitoring_level: ICU for >=1 major criterion (mechanical ventilation or vasopressor-requiring septic shock), typically >=3 minor criteria, or lactate/perfusion failure. Step-down/intermediate for high or escalating O2 requirement or 2 minor criteria. Telemetry for cardiac comorbidity/arrhythmia risk or QTc concern on macrolide + fluoroquinolone. Floor for non-severe CAP. Escalation triggers: rising FiO2/O2 need, RR>=30, PaO2/FiO2<=250, rising lactate, hypotension, new confusion, or NEWS2/MEWS rise. Prefer early ICU admission over delayed transfer (mortality benefit).
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet as tolerated with aspiration precautions and swallow evaluation if aspiration/stroke risk; early mobilization within 24h (reduces LOS, deconditioning, delirium, VTE); pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily or heparin 5000 units SC q8h) unless contraindicated, with mechanical prophylaxis if bleeding risk; glucose target 140-180 mg/dL (avoid tight <110 control); remove unnecessary IV lines and urinary catheters early; oxygen-weaning protocol; smoking-cessation counseling initiated in-house.
discharge_criteria: Clinically stable (many dischargeable the same day stability is reached): trending afebrile (T<=37.8-38C), HR<=100, RR<=24, SBP>=90, SpO2>=90% or PaO2>=60 on room air or baseline O2, normal/baseline mental status, tolerating oral intake and oral antibiotics, no unstable comorbidity, and adequate support to continue therapy at home. Do NOT prolong stay solely to observe the first oral dose (Halm). Discharge with <=1 abnormal stability criterion is acceptable but carries higher readmission/mortality risk - document and mitigate.
discharge_planning: Prescribe oral antibiotics to complete a short total course (typically 5 days, individualized by stability); full medication reconciliation; schedule PCP follow-up within 7 days (48-72h for higher-risk); provide return precautions and symptom-monitoring education; administer/ensure pneumococcal (PCV20, or PCV15 + PPSV23), influenza, COVID-19, and RSV vaccination per ACIP before discharge or at follow-up; reinforce smoking cessation; arrange home oxygen and home health if needed; selective follow-up chest imaging in 6-8 weeks only for older/smoking patients or persistent symptoms to exclude malignancy (not routine); communicate transition-of-care summary to PCP.
expected_los: Benchmark GMLOS approximately 4.8 days for DRG 193 (with MCC), 3.8 days for DRG 194 (with CC), and 2.9 days for DRG 195 (without CC/MCC); blended group GMLOS roughly 3.8-4.0 days, with arithmetic mean (AMLOS) running about 1-1.5 days higher.
target_los: Optimized target 3-4 days for uncomplicated inpatient CAP that reaches clinical stability by 48-72h with early IV-to-PO switch; ~2.5-3 days for low-severity DRG 195; 5-6 days for severe/MCC cases. Most PSI III-IV patients are dischargeable on day 3.
quality_metrics: CMS outcome measures: 30-day risk-standardized mortality (MORT-30-PN), readmission (READM-30-PN), and payment (PAYM-30-PN); Hospital-Wide Readmission; SEP-1 severe sepsis/septic shock bundle when sepsis present; PSI-90 patient-safety composite; antimicrobial stewardship metrics (IV-to-PO conversion, de-escalation at 48h, total duration <=5-7 days); influenza and pneumococcal vaccination prior to discharge; blood cultures before antibiotics and timely first-dose antibiotic administration; C. difficile SIR; LOS index/observed-to-expected. (Legacy PN-3b/PN-5/PN-6 core measures retired; concepts persist within stewardship and sepsis measures.)
common_complications: Parapneumonic effusion/empyema - mitigate by early thoracentesis of effusions >1 cm and chest-tube +/- intrapleural tPA-DNase drainage; respiratory failure/ARDS - severity triage and early ICU; sepsis/septic shock - bundle + steroids for severe CAP; hospital-acquired C. difficile from broad antibiotics - stewardship and de-escalation; delirium (especially elderly) - minimize sedatives, early mobilization, reorientation; AKI - avoid nephrotoxins, judicious fluids; VTE - prophylaxis; pneumonia-associated cardiac events (MI, atrial fibrillation, HF decompensation) - telemetry and comorbidity management; QT prolongation from macrolide + fluoroquinolone - ECG monitoring; lung abscess and hyponatremia.
readmission_drivers: Decompensation of comorbidity (COPD, HF, CKD, diabetes) is the leading driver and is frequently the non-pneumonia cause of bounce-back; premature discharge before clinical stability (each unmet Halm criterion raises risk); inadequate or failed antibiotic therapy / resistant organism; missed complication (empyema, abscess, obstructing malignancy); functional decline and poor PO intake in the elderly; medication nonadherence, cost, or absent follow-up; aspiration. Prevention levers: confirm clinical stability before discharge, early follow-up within 7 days, medication reconciliation, vaccination, comorbidity optimization, and secured home support/home health.
guideline_source: American Thoracic Society / Infectious Diseases Society of America (ATS/IDSA) 2019 Community-Acquired Pneumonia guideline (Metlay et al.), updated by the ATS 2025 CAP clinical practice guideline (corticosteroids in severe CAP, shorter antibiotic duration, lung ultrasound, respiratory-virus testing); Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 and CMS SEP-1 apply when sepsis/septic shock is present.
key_citations: According to PubMed: Metlay JP et al. Diagnosis and Treatment of Adults with Community-acquired Pneumonia (ATS/IDSA). Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2019;200(7):e45-e67 (PMID 31573350; DOI 10.1164/rccm.201908-1581ST); Jones BE, Ramirez JA et al. Diagnosis and Management of Community-acquired Pneumonia: An Official American Thoracic Society Clinical Practice Guideline (2025 update). Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2026;212(1):24-44 (PMID 40679934; DOI 10.1164/rccm.202507-1692ST); Dequin PF et al. Hydrocortisone in Severe Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAPE COD). N Engl J Med 2023;388:1931-1941 (PMID 36942789; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2215145); Fine MJ et al. Pneumonia Severity Index/PORT. N Engl J Med 1997;336:243-250 (PMID 8995086); Lim WS et al. CURB-65. Thorax 2003;58:377-382 (PMID 12728155).
evidence_grade: Strong - anchored by a Class I multisociety clinical practice guideline (ATS/IDSA 2019, GRADE-based) plus the 2025 ATS update, validated prognostic rules (PSI, CURB-65), and RCT evidence (CAPE COD hydrocortisone; short-course non-inferiority trials). Selected elements are conditional/moderate: precise antibiotic duration, corticosteroid subgroup selection, empiric MRSA/Pseudomonas thresholds, and routine follow-up imaging.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 195 (no CC/MCC): lower-severity, generally PSI I-III, floor-level care, GMLOS ~2.9d, target 2-3d. DRG 194 (CC, e.g., moderate comorbidity, uncomplicated pleural effusion, COPD): GMLOS ~3.8d. DRG 193 (MCC, e.g., sepsis/septic shock, acute respiratory failure, empyema, ARDS, AKI): shifts patient onto the severe-CAP pathway (ICU/step-down, hydrocortisone, dual antibiotics, longer duration), GMLOS ~4.8d with materially higher mortality and readmission. Because MCC drivers (acute respiratory failure, sepsis, empyema) also define clinical severity, accurate documentation/capture of these diagnoses is essential to reflect true acuity and resource use.
pathway_pearls: 1) Stratify severity up front with PSI (guideline-preferred) or CURB-65 plus the IDSA/ATS severe-CAP criteria - it drives site of care, ICU triage, steroid use, and antibiotic breadth; avoid over-admitting low-risk PSI I-II. 2) Give the RIGHT antibiotic FAST (first dose in ED; <1h if septic shock) but abandon reflexive broad MRSA/Pseudomonas ('HCAP') coverage - use local risk factors plus MRSA nasal PCR and cultures, then de-escalate at 48h. 3) Drive the IV-to-PO switch and discharge the moment Halm clinical stability is met (~day 2-3); total antibiotics 5 days for most (2025 ATS) - the single biggest LOS lever. 4) Add hydrocortisone for genuinely severe CAP (CAPE COD/2025 ATS), always screen/drain parapneumonic effusions, and treat the decompensating comorbidity (HF/COPD) that actually drives readmission.
verification_notes: Verified against PubMed (get_article_metadata / search_articles) and the official ATS press release. Four of five PMIDs are correct and correctly attributed: 31573350 (Metlay 2019 ATS/IDSA, DOI 10.1164/rccm.201908-1581ST), 36942789 (Dequin CAPE COD, NEJM 2023, DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2215145), 8995086 (Fine PSI, NEJM 1997), 12728155 (Lim CURB-65, Thorax 2003). The fifth (42127879) is the only hard error: it points to a Long/Gottlieb EM review, not the primary ATS guideline (correct = PMID 40679934) — corrected. Clinical high-stakes content all checks out: PSI class cutoffs/mortality (I-II <=70/0.1-0.7%, III 71-90/~2.8%, IV-V >90/8-29%), CURB-65 components/thresholds (BUN>19 mg/dL = urea>7 mmol/L, RR>=30, SBP<90 or DBP<=60, age>=65), and IDSA/ATS severe-CAP definition (>=1 major / >=3 of 9 minor) are accurate and unchanged by the 2025 update. Empiric antibiotic classes and doses (ceftriaxone 1-2 g q24h, amp-sulbactam 1.5-3 g q6h, azithromycin 500 mg, levofloxacin 750 mg, moxifloxacin 400 mg, vancomycin AUC-dosed, linezolid 600 mg q12h, pip-tazo 4.5 g q6h, cefepime 2 g q8h, meropenem 1 g q8h), hydrocortisone 200 mg/day IV for severe CAP (CAPE COD), norepinephrine first-line, HCAP abandonment/48h de-escalation, and MIST2 intrapleural tPA-DNase are all correct. Time-critical items correct: septic-shock antibiotics <1h and SEP-1 3-hour bundle (lactate, cultures-before-antibiotics, broad-spectrum, 30 mL/kg for hypotension or lactate >=4), O2 92-96%. LOS benchmarks (DRG 193 GMLOS ~4.8d, 194 ~3.8d, 195 ~2.9d) are plausible vs CMS. Quality metrics (MORT-30-PN, READM-30-PN, PAYM-30-PN, SEP-1, PSI-90, C. diff SIR; retired PN-3b/PN-5/PN-6) are real and applicable. The 2025 ATS guideline is real and its four recommendation domains (systemic corticosteroids in severe CAP, <5-day min-3 duration, lung ultrasound as CXR alternative, withholding antibiotics in viral-positive healthy outpatients) are accurately reflected. Two minor framing caveats noted (4-6h antibiotic target; '5 days' vs guideline's <5-day) but not clinically dangerous, so not formally corrected.; ISSUES: CITATION MISATTRIBUTION (key_citations): The 2025 ATS CAP guideline is cited as 'PMID 42127879,' but that PMID is actually a secondary EM review by Long B & Gottlieb M in Am J Emerg Med 2026;107:16-20 (DOI 10.1016/j.ajem.2026.05.020), NOT the primary guideline. Correct primary source is PMID 40679934 (Jones BE, Ramirez JA et al., AJRCCM 2026;212(1):24-44; DOI 10.1164/rccm.202507-1692ST). Corrected.; MINOR (time_critical_interventions): The 'within 4-6h of arrival' antibiotic target for CAP is not a currently guideline-endorsed hard target; the old CMS PN-5 4-hour time-to-antibiotic measure was retired to avoid misdiagnosis/overtreatment. Current ATS/IDSA guidance is 'as soon as possible, first dose in the ED.' The <1h target for septic shock (SSC 2021/SEP-1) is correct. Framing is defensible but the 4-6h number should be read as an operational benchmark, not a mandated guideline metric.; MINOR (day3plus_milestones / pathway_pearls): Non-severe duration is framed as 'typically/5 days for most'; the 2025 ATS guideline actually recommends LESS THAN 5 days (minimum 3) for outpatients and non-severe admitted patients reaching clinical stability. The row does capture the 'minimum 3' correctly, so this is a slight over-statement of duration rather than an error.
scope_and_volume_notes: HCUP #4 principal dx (0.74M, 2018); somewhat depressed 2020-2021 as viral pneumonia shifted to COVID coding. Bacterial & non-COVID viral pneumonia.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 193 — Simple Pneumonia and Pleurisy with MCC; 194 — Simple Pneumonia and Pleurisy with CC; 195 — Simple Pneumonia and Pleurisy without CC/MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 5/5 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Original independent adversarial high-stakes review completed; current pass revalidated citation resolvability and FY2026 coding/taxonomy.
data_quality_notes: Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk.

admission_criteria: Elective OA hip/knee arthroplasty; excludes fracture. Since CMS removed TKA (2018) and THA (2020) from the inpatient-only list, site-of-service is a case-by-case 2-midnight/medical-necessity decision, NOT automatic inpatient. Default = outpatient/same-day for optimized ASA I-II patients going home. Inpatient (DRG 469/470) justified when a 2-midnight stay is expected due to: ASA III-IV cardiopulmonary disease needing monitoring; bilateral or complex/revision arthroplasty; therapeutic-anticoagulation bridging; high fall risk or no home support; anticipated post-acute needs; or peri-op complication. Observation for borderline/social delay. Document inpatient rationale explicitly.
risk_stratification: ASA Physical Status (III-IV -> monitored inpatient bed, medicine co-management); RAPT (Risk Assessment & Prediction Tool) <=6 predicts non-home discharge -> pre-plan post-acute; Charlson Comorbidity Index / frailty screen; Caprini VTE score (higher -> potent anticoagulant vs aspirin); HbA1c target <7.5-8.0% (>8% -> optimize/defer, PJI risk); BMI >40 and serum albumin <3.5 g/dL flag for optimization; preop Hgb work-up/iron if anemic (Hgb <13 men/<12 women); MSSA/MRSA nasal screen -> decolonization. High VTE risk = prior VTE, thrombophilia, active malignancy -> escalate prophylaxis.
initial_workup_labs: Preop: CBC (baseline Hgb, anemia optimization), BMP (renal function for anticoagulant/NSAID dosing, electrolytes), coagulation panel if anticoagulated, type & screen, HbA1c, albumin/prealbumin (nutrition), urinalysis only if symptomatic. POD0/POD1: CBC (postop Hgb -> transfuse if <7 g/dL, or <8 with CAD/symptoms), BMP (Na/K, renal, glucose). Trend Hgb if drains or large EBL. Group-and-save routinely; crossmatch only for anemia/revision.
initial_imaging_dx: Preop weight-bearing plain radiographs of the affected joint (AP pelvis + lateral hip / standing AP + lateral + skyline knee) for templating; CT/MRI only for complex deformity or revision planning. Preop ECG/CXR by age/comorbidity (ECG if cardiac disease or >65). Postop AP/lateral radiograph of the prosthesis before or shortly after discharge to confirm component position and exclude periprosthetic fracture. NO routine postop DVT ultrasound (only if symptomatic).
time_critical_interventions: Surgical antibiotic prophylaxis within 60 min before incision (cefazolin; vancomycin/clindamycin infused within 120 min) -> antibiotic-on-time. Tranexamic acid at/around incision (IV 1 g pre-incision + 1 g at closure, or 15-20 mg/kg; topical alternative) to cut blood loss/transfusion ~50%. Maintain intraop normothermia >=36C. Mobilize within 3-6 h of surgery (POD0, same-day PT). Discontinue prophylactic antibiotics within 24 h. Start VTE chemoprophylaxis 12-24 h postop once hemostasis secure.
initial_management: Neuraxial (spinal) anesthesia preferred over general (lower morbidity). Opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia: scheduled acetaminophen + NSAID/COX-2 (celecoxib/ibuprofen) + single-dose IV dexamethasone + local infiltration analgesia (ropivacaine/liposomal bupivacaine) +/- adductor canal block for TKA (motor-sparing; AVOID femoral nerve block/dense motor block -> fall risk); opioids rescue-only; limit gabapentinoids. Antibiotic: cefazolin 2 g IV (3 g if >120 kg), redose at 4 h or EBL >1.5 L. Tranexamic acid as above. Restrictive transfusion (Hgb <7 g/dL, or <8 with cardiac disease/symptoms). PONV prophylaxis; euvolemic fluids; normothermia; avoid/limit urinary catheter.
day1_milestones: POD0: spinal anesthesia + local infiltration analgesia; tranexamic acid given; ambulate within 3-6 h with PT (weight-bearing as tolerated); sit at edge of bed, stand, take steps same day; start scheduled multimodal analgesia; regular diet once alert; wean IV fluids early; avoid/remove Foley; assess for same-day discharge (outpatient candidates) vs overnight; initiate VTE prophylaxis + mechanical IPC; PONV control; confirm distal neurovascular status.
day2_milestones: POD1: check CBC/BMP; transition to oral analgesia; ambulate >=twice with PT, stairs assessment, transfers independent/supervised; OT for ADLs and joint precautions (esp. posterior-approach THA); confirm spontaneous voiding (screen for POUR); bowel regimen; postop prosthesis radiograph; confirm VTE prophylaxis; meet functional discharge milestones -> many DRG-470 patients discharge home this day; case management finalizes home health/DME.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ (mainly DRG-469/MCC, revision, bilateral, or complicated cases): progress to independent transfers, safe stairs, and ambulation-distance goal; wound check (no concerning drainage/erythema); pain controlled on orals; comorbidity stabilized (cardiac/renal/glycemic); off supplemental O2; treat complications (POUR, ileus, delirium, anemia); escalate to inpatient rehab/SNF only if failing home-discharge milestones. Target discharge by POD1-2 (470) once criteria met.
consults_multidisciplinary: Physical therapy (POD0, same-day) + Occupational therapy (ADLs, joint precautions); Case management/social work EARLY (ideally preop) for disposition, home health, DME (walker, raised toilet seat); Anesthesia/regional team; Pharmacy (multimodal analgesia, anticoagulant selection/renal dosing, med rec); Hospitalist co-management or cardiology/nephrology for ASA III-IV; Nutrition if albumin low/diabetic; Infectious Disease only for suspected PJI. Preoperative structured 'joint class' patient education.
monitoring_level: PACU then orthopedic surgical floor (no telemetry for uncomplicated cases). Telemetry/step-down for significant cardiac history, arrhythmia, or OSA on postop opioids/regional. ICU rarely required in elective optimized patients -> reserved for major intraop event, hemodynamic instability, or acute cardiopulmonary decompensation (an MCC -> DRG 469). Escalation triggers: SpO2 drop/respiratory depression, hypotension or ongoing bleeding (falling Hgb), chest pain/dyspnea (r/o PE/ACS), altered mental status, acute kidney injury, uncontrolled pain.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: preop carbohydrate loading, avoid prolonged fasting (ERAS); resume regular diet POD0 once alert; treat malnutrition. Mobility: early ambulation POD0 within hours, weight-bearing as tolerated, PT/OT daily, fall precautions (motor-sparing blocks). VTE: mechanical IPC in-hospital + early mobilization; risk-stratified chemoprophylaxis -> standard risk = aspirin 81 mg BID (THA ~30-35 d, TKA ~14-30 d); high risk (prior VTE/thrombophilia/cancer) = potent anticoagulant (rivaroxaban 10 mg daily, enoxaparin 40 mg SC, or warfarin). Glucose <180 mg/dL, avoid hypoglycemia. Lines/catheter: avoid/remove urinary catheter within 24 h, monitor for POUR; remove IV once tolerating orals.
discharge_criteria: Afebrile, hemodynamically stable, adequate/stable Hgb; pain controlled on oral analgesics; tolerating diet; independent spontaneous voiding (no retention); mobilizing safely with PT-cleared assistive device (transfers, ambulation, stairs if needed at home); dry/intact wound without concerning drainage; VTE prophylaxis prescribed and understood; safe discharge environment and support confirmed; no acute medical issue requiring inpatient care.
discharge_planning: Disposition: HOME with outpatient/home PT preferred (better outcomes and lower cost than SNF; avoid routine SNF). Medication reconciliation incl. VTE prophylaxis + bowel regimen + tapering opioids; schedule surgeon follow-up (2-4 wks; wound/staple check ~10-14 d) and outpatient PT; teach joint precautions (THA), wound care, and s/s of infection/DVT/PE to report; arrange DME; provide written pathway and safety-net phone contact; confirm anticoagulation plan and monitoring (INR if warfarin). Consider post-discharge phone/telehealth check.
expected_los: CMS GMLOS benchmark: DRG 470 (without MCC, >95% of inpatient cases) ~2.2-2.4 days; DRG 469 (with MCC) ~5.0-5.4 days. Note many optimized THA/TKA are now outpatient/same-day (0 nights) after CMS removed them from the inpatient-only list, so the remaining inpatient cohort skews comorbid/complex. Typical uncomplicated inpatient stay 1-2 days.
target_los: Optimized ERAS target: 0-1 day for DRG-470/standard-risk (same-day or POD1 discharge home); <=1 midnight for the majority. DRG-469/MCC or revision/bilateral: 2-3 days. Outpatient / 23-hour pathway is the best-practice benchmark for ASA I-II patients.
quality_metrics: CMS Hospital-level THA/TKA Risk-Standardized Complication Rate (COMP-HIP-KNEE: mechanical complication, PJI/wound infection, PE/DVT, AMI/pneumonia/sepsis, death within 90 d); THA/TKA 30-day Risk-Standardized Readmission (READM-30 Hip/Knee); mandatory THA/TKA Patient-Reported Outcome PRO-PM (HOOS,JR / KOOS,JR pre- vs ~1-yr post) in Hospital IQR; SSI surveillance (NHSN); perioperative antibiotic timing and VTE prophylaxis (legacy SCIP); ERAS protocol compliance; same-day/home-discharge rate; historical CJR bundled-payment episode spend/quality.
common_complications: Periprosthetic joint infection/SSI (~0.5-1%; mitigate: on-time weight-based antibiotics, MSSA/MRSA decolonization, glycemic control, normothermia); VTE/DVT-PE (mitigate: risk-stratified prophylaxis + early mobilization); acute blood-loss anemia/transfusion (mitigate: tranexamic acid, restrictive threshold); postop urinary retention (avoid Foley, monitor); prosthetic dislocation (THA ~1-2%; precautions); periprosthetic fracture; TKA stiffness/arthrofibrosis -> manipulation under anesthesia; delirium/POCD in elderly (limit opioids/benzos); ileus; falls (motor-sparing blocks); PONV; medical decompensation (cardiac, AKI).
readmission_drivers: Top drivers: wound complications/persistent drainage and PJI/SSI; VTE (DVT/PE); TKA stiffness requiring manipulation; THA dislocation; periprosthetic fracture; uncontrolled pain and urinary retention; medical (cardiac, pneumonia, UTI, GI/ileus, AKI). Prevention levers: outpatient/home-discharge pathways (SNF associated with more complications); preop optimization (HbA1c, anemia, nutrition, staph decolonization, smoking/BMI); patient education on warning signs; strict VTE prophylaxis adherence; wound-management protocols; structured PT; early post-discharge phone/telehealth check and timely follow-up.
guideline_source: ERAS Society Consensus for perioperative care in total hip/knee replacement 2020 (Wainwright et al., Acta Orthopaedica); AAOS Clinical Practice Guideline - Preventing VTE in Elective Hip & Knee Arthroplasty; ICM-VTE Hip & Knee 2022 (JBJS); AAHKS/AAOS osteoarthritis-management CPGs; ASA/ASRA perioperative and regional-anesthesia guidance; ASHP/IDSA/SIS/SHEA Surgical Antimicrobial Prophylaxis guideline 2013.
key_citations: Wainwright TW et al. ERAS Society consensus THR/TKR, Acta Orthop 2020;91(1):3-19 (PMID 31663402; doi:10.1080/17453674.2019.1683790); ICM-VTE Hip & Knee, JBJS 2022;104(Suppl 1):180-231 (PMID 35315610; doi:10.2106/JBJS.21.01529); CRISTAL RCT, JAMA 2022;328(8):719-727 (aspirin 3.45% vs enoxaparin 1.82% symptomatic 90-d VTE; PMID 35997730); PEPPER RCT (NCT02810704; aspirin 81 mg BID vs warfarin target INR ~2.0 vs rivaroxaban 10 mg daily -> comparable efficacy, shared decision-making); Anderson DR et al. Aspirin or Rivaroxaban (EPCAT II), NEJM 2018;378(8):699-707 (PMID 29466159; doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1712746). Source: PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong overall - multi-society consensus (ERAS Society 2020, AAOS, ICM-VTE 2022) backed by Level I RCTs/meta-analyses for core elements (spinal anesthesia, tranexamic acid, opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia, early mobilization, antibiotic prophylaxis). VTE-prophylaxis AGENT SELECTION is Moderate/individualized (aspirin vs potent anticoagulant debated across PEPPER and CRISTAL); optimal aspirin dose/duration = Moderate. Discharge disposition (home > SNF) = Moderate, observational.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed to a BINARY MCC split: DRG 469 (WITH MCC) vs 470 (WITHOUT MCC) - no separate CC tier. ~95%+ of elective inpatient cases fall in 470. An MCC (acute MI, PE, acute respiratory or renal failure, sepsis, stroke, acute blood loss requiring intervention) usually reflects a postoperative complication or comorbidity decompensation that derails the ERAS fast-track -> ICU/step-down, LOS rising from ~2 to 5+ days, higher transfusion/mortality, and SNF/rehab discharge. Bilateral and revision procedures raise complexity/blood loss even without an MCC. Fast-track assumes preoptimized ASA I-II; ASA III-IV -> plan inpatient, monitored bed, medicine co-management.
pathway_pearls: 1) Site-of-service is a documented medical-necessity decision, NOT automatic inpatient - default optimized patients to outpatient/same-day and reserve inpatient (DRG 469/470) for comorbid/complex cases. 2) The 'big three' fast-recovery levers: spinal anesthesia + tranexamic acid + opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia with motor-sparing blocks -> mobilize POD0. 3) VTE prophylaxis is risk-stratified: aspirin 81 mg BID for standard risk, potent anticoagulant for prior-VTE/thrombophilia/active-cancer. 4) Preop optimization (HbA1c <8%, anemia, nutrition, staph decolonization) plus HOME (not SNF) discharge are the biggest levers on PJI, transfusion, and 30-day readmission.
verification_notes: Verified against PubMed (article metadata/citation lookup) and web sources. CONFIRMED EXACT: Wainwright ERAS Society consensus (PMID 31663402, Acta Orthop 2020;91(1):3-19, doi 10.1080/17453674.2019.1683790); ICM-VTE Hip & Knee (PMID 35315610, JBJS 2022;104(Suppl 1):180-231, doi 10.2106/JBJS.21.01529); CRISTAL RCT (PMID 35997730, JAMA 2022;328(8):719-727) with the quoted aspirin 3.45% vs enoxaparin 1.82% 90-d symptomatic VTE rates matching the abstract verbatim; EPCAT II (PMID 29466159, NEJM 2018;378(8):699-707) exists and is correctly described except author initials (DR, not FA). PEPPER is a real 3-arm trial (registry NCT02810704: ASA 81 mg BID, warfarin, rivaroxaban 10 mg) now reported (2026); its exact N (18,883) and INR range (1.5-2) could not be substantiated and were flagged/softened. CMS IPO-list removal dates confirmed (TKA effective 2018, THA effective 2020). Clinical high-stakes content is sound: antibiotic prophylaxis timing (cefazolin <=60 min; vancomycin <=120 min), weight-based cefazolin 2 g/3 g with 4-h redose, TXA dosing, normothermia >=36C, restrictive transfusion (Hgb <7, or <8 with cardiac disease) all match ASHP/IDSA/SIS/SHEA 2013 SAP guidance and AABB restrictive strategy. Risk scores real with correct directionality/thresholds (ASA III-IV, RAPT <=6 -> non-home discharge, Charlson, Caprini, HbA1c <7.5-8%, WHO anemia cutoffs). DRG 470 GMLOS ~2.2-2.4 d / DRG 469 ~5.0-5.4 d plausible. Quality measures (COMP-HIP-KNEE RSCR, READM-30 Hip/Knee, HOOS,JR/KOOS,JR PRO-PM in Hospital IQR, NHSN SSI, legacy SCIP, CJR) all real and applicable. No door-to-balloon/tPA/thrombectomy targets apply to this elective surgical pathway. Only defects are citation-metadata level (one author misattribution + one unverifiable enrollment figure); no clinically actionable error found.; ISSUES: key_citations: EPCAT II attributed to 'Anderson FA' — correct first author is Anderson DR (David R. Anderson) per PubMed PMID 29466159 (NEJM 2018;378(8):699-707). Misattributed author initials.; key_citations: PEPPER 'n=18,883' could not be verified against the trial registry or reported results (registered/planned enrollment ~25,000); specific figure is unsubstantiated and was softened in the correction.; key_citations: PEPPER warfarin arm described as 'INR 1.5-2' but the registered protocol (NCT02810704) targets INR ~2.0 (range 1.7-2.2); minor imprecision.; Non-defect note: CRISTAL used aspirin 100 mg/d (not 81 mg), but the row does not state a CRISTAL dose, so no error — the row's 81 mg BID pertains to standard US prophylaxis/PEPPER/EPCAT II, which is correct.
scope_and_volume_notes: Underlying dx is osteoarthritis (HCUP #3, 1.13M, 2018). Inpatient volume fell sharply after CMS removed TKA (2018) and THA (2020) from inpatient-only list -> many now outpatient. Elective; excludes fracture cases.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 469 — Major Hip and Knee Joint Replacement or Reattachment of Lower Extremity with MCC or Total Ankle Replacement; 470 — Major Hip and Knee Joint Replacement or Reattachment of Lower Extremity without MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 4/4 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.

admission_criteria: Confirmed STEMI (persistent ST-elevation >=1mm in >=2 contiguous leads or new LBBB with ischemia) -> emergent admission + reperfusion; NSTEMI (rise/fall of hs-cTn above 99th-percentile URL with ischemic symptoms or ischemic ECG changes) -> admit; unstable angina with high-risk features (ongoing/refractory chest pain, dynamic ST-depression, hemodynamic or electrical instability) -> admit; objective admit triggers: positive troponin, ischemic ECG changes, HEART score >=4, GRACE-defined intermediate/high risk; ED discharge/observation acceptable only for HEART 0-3 with 2 negative serial hs-cTn (0/1h or 0/2h) and no dynamic ECG changes -> accelerated diagnostic protocol/observation, not inpatient
risk_stratification: HEART score (ED chest pain): 0-3 low (~1-2% 6-wk MACE, discharge/observe), 4-6 moderate (admit/observe), >=7 high (early invasive); GRACE 2.0 (NSTE-ACS): >140 = high risk -> early invasive <24h, <=140 with risk features -> invasive within 72h, also predicts in-hospital/6-mo mortality; TIMI risk score (NSTE-ACS 0-7; STEMI 0-14); Killip class I-IV (HF/shock at presentation, prognostic); CRUSADE or ARC-HBR bleeding score to individualize DAPT duration; ESC hs-cTn 0/1h algorithm for rule-in/rule-out
initial_workup_labs: 12-lead ECG <=10 min of arrival, repeat serial q15-30min if ongoing symptoms; high-sensitivity cardiac troponin at 0h and 1h (or 0/2h) - rise/fall confirms MI and triggers admission/invasive strategy; CBC (baseline anemia/bleeding); BMP/CMP + magnesium (K/Mg repletion to prevent arrhythmia, creatinine for contrast load and renal drug dosing); glucose + HbA1c; fasting-independent lipid panel within 24h; PT/INR + aPTT before anticoagulation; BNP/NT-proBNP if HF suspected; type & screen if CABG possible; MINOCA workup if non-obstructive coronaries
initial_imaging_dx: 12-lead ECG <=10 min (add posterior leads V7-V9 for suspected posterior MI, right-sided V4R for inferior/RV involvement); invasive coronary angiography = definitive diagnostic + therapeutic study (emergent in STEMI; <=24h in high-risk NSTEMI); transthoracic echocardiography for LVEF, wall-motion, and mechanical complications (urgent if unstable/new murmur, otherwise before discharge); portable CXR; cardiac MRI or coronary CT if MINOCA or unclear etiology
time_critical_interventions: STEMI is time-critical - prehospital 12-lead ECG with field cath-lab activation; primary PCI FMC-to-device <=90 min (direct arrival at PCI-capable hospital) / <=120 min (interhospital transfer); door-in-door-out at non-PCI hospital <=30 min; if PCI not achievable <=120 min -> fibrinolysis (tenecteplase) door-to-needle <=30 min then transfer for angiography 2-24h (immediate rescue PCI if failed reperfusion); aspirin ASAP; NSTEMI: immediate invasive <2h if unstable (shock, refractory ischemia, sustained VT/VF, mechanical complication, acute HF), early invasive <24h if GRACE >140
initial_management: Antiplatelet: aspirin 162-325 mg load chewed then 81 mg daily + P2Y12 inhibitor - ticagrelor (180 mg load/90 mg BID) or prasugrel (60 mg/10 mg, PCI only, avoid prior stroke/TIA) PREFERRED over clopidogrel (600 mg/75 mg); anticoagulation: UFH weight-based or bivalirudin (primary PCI), enoxaparin, or fondaparinux (medically managed NSTEMI); high-intensity statin in-hospital (atorvastatin 80 mg or rosuvastatin 40 mg), LDL-C goal <70 mg/dL, add ezetimibe/PCSK9i if unmet; oral beta-blocker <=24h if no HF/shock; ACEi/ARB <=24h if LVEF<40%/HTN/DM/anterior MI; MRA (eplerenone) if LVEF<=40% + HF/DM; nitrates/morphine for refractory ischemia (morphine sparingly - blunts P2Y12 absorption); O2 only if SpO2<90%; cardiogenic shock: revascularize culprit only (CULPRIT-SHOCK) + microaxial flow pump/Impella (DanGer Shock); transfusion: restrictive Hgb 7-8 g/dL traditional but MINT 2023 supports liberal (~10 g/dL) in acute MI + anemia - individualize
day1_milestones: Reperfusion achieved (STEMI primary PCI; NSTEMI invasive per risk); admit to CICU/step-down; radial access-site check; load and continue DAPT + anticoagulation + high-intensity statin; continuous telemetry with serial ECG/troponin; treat arrhythmias and replete K>4.0/Mg>2.0; start beta-blocker and ACEi/ARB once hemodynamically stable; assess/treat HF and shock; establish code status; capture door-to-device time metric
day2_milestones: Transthoracic echo for LVEF, wall-motion, mechanical complications; uptitrate GDMT (beta-blocker, ACEi/ARB) and add MRA if LVEF<=40% + HF/DM; start SGLT2 inhibitor if HF/DM; transition CICU -> telemetry/step-down if stable >24h; early mobilization with progressive ambulation; place cardiac-rehab referral; review glucose and lipid panel; begin structured patient education emphasizing DAPT adherence
day3plus_milestones: Optimize and confirm tolerance of GDMT doses; complete staged revascularization if planned; if LVEF<=35% arrange wearable cardioverter-defibrillator (LifeVest) with ICD reassessment ~40 days post-MI (90 days post-revascularization); reinforce full secondary-prevention bundle + smoking cessation; finalize cardiac-rehab enrollment; confirm no recurrent ischemia/arrhythmia >=24h; complete discharge planning and follow-up scheduling
consults_multidisciplinary: Interventional cardiology/cath-lab (emergent for STEMI); cardiology (all patients); cardiac surgery if left-main/complex multivessel/CABG candidate or mechanical complication; shock/MCS team (IABP, Impella, VA-ECMO) for cardiogenic shock; EP or HF service for low EF or malignant arrhythmia; cardiac rehabilitation; clinical pharmacy (DAPT/anticoagulation/GDMT reconciliation); dietitian + diabetes educator; smoking-cessation counseling; PT/OT for mobilization; case management/social work for medication access and rehab logistics; palliative care for refractory shock/expired-track (DRG 283-285)
monitoring_level: CICU/ICU: STEMI first ~24h post-PCI, cardiogenic shock, mechanical complication, hemodynamic instability, high-grade AV block, sustained VT/VF, vasopressors or MCS; step-down/telemetry: stable NSTEMI and uncomplicated post-PCI after 24h; continuous telemetry >=24-48h then floor-with-telemetry; escalation triggers: recurrent chest pain/ST changes, hypotension/shock, new murmur (mechanical complication), sustained arrhythmia, hypoxia, re-rising troponin
nutrition_mobility_vte: Heart-healthy/low-sodium, diabetes-appropriate diet (NPO pre-cath then advance); early mobilization - radial access ambulate early, femoral bedrest 2-6h then progressive ambulation; glucose target 140-180 mg/dL, avoid hypoglycemia and overly tight control; VTE - therapeutic ACS anticoagulation covers the acute phase, add pharmacologic/mechanical prophylaxis once anticoagulation stopped if not fully mobile; early arterial sheath/central line/Foley removal; continue statin; enforce smoking cessation
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable and chest-pain-free >=24h; successful revascularization or a defined revascularization plan; no significant/unstable arrhythmia; ambulating at/near baseline and tolerating oral GDMT; LVEF assessed; DAPT + high-intensity statin + beta-blocker + ACEi/ARB (+ MRA if indicated) initiated and tolerated; cardiology follow-up and cardiac-rehab referral arranged; patient education completed
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home; medication reconciliation - DAPT (aspirin 81 mg + ticagrelor/prasugrel/clopidogrel, >=12 months default), high-intensity statin +/- ezetimibe/PCSK9i (LDL<70), beta-blocker, ACEi/ARB, MRA if EF<=40%, SL nitroglycerin, PPI if bleeding risk, SGLT2i if HF/DM; follow-up cardiology 1-2 weeks + PCP, repeat lipid panel 4-8 weeks, EF reassessment ~40 days if reduced; Class I cardiac-rehab referral; education (never stop DAPT prematurely, symptom recognition/call-911, diet, smoking, activity); teach-back + transitions-of-care follow-up call; verify medication affordability/access
expected_los: Severity-tiered CMS GMLOS benchmarks: DRG 282 (AMI discharged alive, no CC/MCC) ~2.2 days; DRG 281 (w CC) ~3.4 days; DRG 280 (w MCC) ~4.8 days; national mean AMI stay ~4-5 days; expired tiers 283-285 variable ~1.5-3.5 days
target_los: Uncomplicated PCI-treated NSTEMI/STEMI 2-3 days (low-risk primary PCI dischargeable at 48-72h); complicated/MCC cases (shock, acute HF, mechanical complication) 5-10+ days
quality_metrics: NCDR Chest Pain-MI Registry (ACTION-GWTG); primary-PCI FMC-to-device <=90 min and median time-to-reperfusion; door-in-door-out <=30 min; CMS AMI measures - aspirin, P2Y12, and high-intensity statin at discharge, beta-blocker, ACEi/ARB for LVSD; 30-day risk-standardized mortality (MORT-30-AMI) and readmission (READM-30-AMI); cardiac-rehab referral rate; tobacco-cessation counseling; LDL/lipid management; defect-free composite care
common_complications: Arrhythmias (VT/VF, AF, bradyarrhythmia/high-grade AV block); acute HF/cardiogenic pulmonary edema; cardiogenic shock; mechanical complications (papillary-muscle rupture/acute MR, VSD, free-wall rupture, typically day 3-5); recurrent ischemia/stent thrombosis/reinfarction; access-site bleeding and contrast-induced nephropathy; LV thrombus, RV infarction, pericarditis (Dressler). Mitigation: prompt reperfusion, telemetry + electrolyte repletion, echo surveillance, radial access, contrast minimization + hydration, DAPT adherence, GDMT optimization
readmission_drivers: HF decompensation; recurrent ischemia/reinfarction/stent thrombosis (especially premature DAPT cessation); arrhythmia; bleeding on DAPT; incomplete revascularization; medication non-adherence and poor follow-up; missed cardiac rehab; SDOH/medication-affordability barriers. Prevention levers: robust medication reconciliation, DAPT-adherence education, early (<=7 day) follow-up, guaranteed cardiac-rehab enrollment, transitions-of-care phone call, address affordability
guideline_source: 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes (PRIMARY); 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Guideline for Coronary Artery Revascularization; Fourth Universal Definition of Myocardial Infarction (2018)
key_citations: Rao SV, O'Donoghue ML, Ruel M, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI ACS Guideline. Circulation 2025;151(13):e771-e862 (PMID 40014670; DOI 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001309; also JACC 2025;85(22):2135-2237, PMID 40013746); Mehta SR et al. COMPLETE - Complete Revascularization with Multivessel PCI for MI. N Engl J Med 2019 (PMID 31475795); Thiele H et al. CULPRIT-SHOCK. N Engl J Med 2017 (PMID 29083953); Thygesen K et al. Fourth Universal Definition of MI (2018) (PMID 30153967); Moller JE et al. DanGer Shock - microaxial flow pump in STEMI cardiogenic shock. N Engl J Med 2024 (PMID 38587239); Carson JL et al. MINT - transfusion strategy in MI. N Engl J Med 2023 (PMID 37952133). Citations retrieved via PubMed
evidence_grade: Strong - anchored to Class I recommendations of the current (2025) ACC/AHA multisociety ACS guideline supported by multiple large RCTs (COMPLETE, CULPRIT-SHOCK, DanGer Shock, ISAR-REACT 5); the optimal transfusion threshold is the main area of moderate/uncertain evidence (MINT 2023 favors a liberal ~10 g/dL target but was not definitively conclusive)
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MCC tier (DRG 280) driven by cardiogenic shock, acute HF, AKI, respiratory failure, or sustained VT/VF -> CICU + possible MCS, longer LOS (~5-10+ days), higher mortality and palliative needs; CC tier (DRG 281) e.g. AF, diabetes with complications, moderate HF -> step-down, moderate LOS (~3-4 days); no-CC/MCC (DRG 282) uncomplicated PCI-treated MI -> telemetry only, 2-3 days; expired tiers (283-285) reflect late presentation, refractory shock, mechanical complications, or out-of-hospital cardiac arrest and should trigger early goals-of-care/palliative involvement
pathway_pearls: 1) Time is muscle - activate the cath lab off the prehospital ECG; STEMI FMC-to-device <=90 min (direct) / <=120 min (transfer), door-in-door-out <=30 min. 2) Risk-stratify NSTEMI by GRACE - >140 drives invasive angiography <24h, instability drives immediate <2h. 3) The non-negotiable discharge bundle prevents readmission and death: DAPT (ticagrelor/prasugrel preferred, >=12 months) + high-intensity statin (LDL<70, add ezetimibe/PCSK9i) + beta-blocker + ACEi/ARB + MRA if EF<=40% + Class I cardiac-rehab referral + <=7-day follow-up. 4) Radial access + complete revascularization + relentless DAPT-adherence education to prevent stent thrombosis
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: HCUP #6 principal dx (0.66M, 2018). Includes STEMI/NSTEMI, discharged alive (280-282) and expired (283-285). Frequently paired with PCI/cath; ED/cath-lab entry.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 280 — Acute Myocardial Infarction, Discharged Alive with MCC; 281 — Acute Myocardial Infarction, Discharged Alive with CC; 282 — Acute Myocardial Infarction, Discharged Alive without CC/MCC; 283 — Acute Myocardial Infarction, Expired with MCC; 284 — Acute Myocardial Infarction, Expired with CC; 285 — Acute Myocardial Infarction, Expired without CC/MCC
