admission_criteria: Admit (inpatient) for: hemodynamic instability from AF/flutter (SBP <90, shock, acute pulmonary edema/decompensated HF, ongoing ischemia/ACS, AF-related syncope); RVR refractory to ED IV rate control (persistent HR >110-120 despite adequate therapy); new AF as manifestation of a serious precipitant needing inpatient care (ACS, acute HF, sepsis, PE, thyrotoxicosis, decompensated COPD); symptomatic bradyarrhythmia/high-grade AV block/conduction disease needing monitoring or pacemaker; ischemic stroke/TIA or systemic embolism with newly detected AF; in-hospital antiarrhythmic (dofetilide/sotalol) initiation which mandates telemetry/QT monitoring (Class I). Observation (<48h) for stable rate-controlled AF needing brief monitoring or rate titration; ED discharge for hemodynamically stable, rate-controlled patients with anticoagulation addressed and reliable follow-up (e.g., recent-onset AF cardioverted in ED per an Ottawa-type aggressive protocol)
risk_stratification: CHA2DS2-VASc (stroke/thromboembolism): >=2 men / >=3 women => oral anticoagulation Class I (annual TE risk >=2%); 1 man / 2 women => OAC reasonable (Class IIa); 0 man / 1 woman => omit OAC; drives the anticoagulation decision. HAS-BLED >=3 = high bleeding risk => correct modifiable factors and increase follow-up, NOT used to withhold OAC (ORBIT is an alternative bleeding score). EHRA symptom score I-IV guides rate- vs rhythm-control intensity. Resting HR target <110 bpm supported by RACE II (lenient non-inferior to strict). LVEF on TTE stratifies to HFrEF pathway (ablation per CASTLE-AF). When AF is secondary, stratify the precipitant (qSOFA/SOFA for sepsis, Wells/PESI for PE, HEART/GRACE for ACS)
initial_workup_labs: CBC (anemia/infection); BMP/CMP with Mg, Ca, Phos (electrolytes, renal/hepatic function for DOAC selection and dosing by CrCl); TSH/free T4 (thyrotoxicosis precipitant); troponin if ischemia suspected (mild rises common with RVR); BNP/NT-proBNP if HF suspected; baseline PT/INR + aPTT before anticoagulation; digoxin level if applicable; consider alcohol level/tox screen (holiday heart). Triggers: K+ and Mg2+ repletion to K >4.0 and Mg >2.0 to reduce arrhythmogenicity; reduced CrCl => renal DOAC dose adjustment or agent change; elevated troponin => ischemia evaluation; abnormal TSH => endocrine treatment of precipitant
initial_imaging_dx: 12-lead ECG immediately (confirm AF/flutter vs other SVT, rate, ischemia, pre-excitation/WPW, baseline QT) plus continuous telemetry; CXR (HF, pneumonia, cardiomegaly). TTE during admission to assess LVEF, valvular disease (esp moderate-severe mitral stenosis which mandates warfarin), LA size and structural disease to finalize rate vs rhythm strategy and AAD safety. TEE before early cardioversion when AF is >=48h or of unknown duration and 3 weeks of prior therapeutic anticoagulation has not been completed, to exclude LA/LAA thrombus (Class I). Ischemic evaluation if ACS suspected
time_critical_interventions: Hemodynamically unstable AF (hypotension/shock, pulmonary edema, ongoing ischemia) => emergent synchronized DC cardioversion now (Class I); do not delay for drugs. Pre-excited (WPW) AF => AVOID all AV-nodal blockers (adenosine, non-DHP CCB, digoxin, IV amiodarone, beta-blockers) => use procainamide or DCCV. Acute rate control target HR <110 bpm within first hours (RACE II). Cardioversion anticoagulation rule: if AF clearly <48h, cardiovert with anticoagulation; if >=48h/unknown, either >=3 weeks therapeutic OAC OR TEE-guided approach first, then >=4 weeks OAC after (Class I). Initiate anticoagulation promptly at diagnosis when CHA2DS2-VASc indicates. Repletion of K+/Mg2+ in first hours. If AF is driven by ACS/sepsis/PE, run that condition's time-critical bundle in parallel
initial_management: Rate control first-line: IV beta-blocker (metoprolol 2.5-5 mg boluses or esmolol infusion) OR IV non-dihydropyridine CCB (diltiazem 0.25 mg/kg then infusion, or verapamil); choose CCB for reactive airway, beta-blocker for ischemia; AVOID non-DHP CCB in HFrEF (LVEF <40%). Digoxin as add-on for hypotension/HF or refractory rate. IV amiodarone for rate control in critically ill/HF when others fail. Rhythm control: synchronized DCCV 150-200 J biphasic; pharmacologic cardioversion with IV/oral flecainide or propafenone (ONLY without structural/ischemic heart disease), ibutilide, amiodarone, or in-hospital dofetilide/sotalol with QT monitoring; pill-in-pocket flecainide/propafenone for select outpatients. Anticoagulation: DOAC preferred over warfarin (Class I) - apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban, renally dose-adjusted - EXCEPT mechanical valve or moderate-severe mitral stenosis => warfarin INR 2-3. Reversal: idarucizumab (dabigatran), andexanet alfa or 4F-PCC (Xa inhibitors), vitamin K + 4F-PCC (warfarin). Treat precipitant (thyroid, sepsis, HF diuresis, ACS, PE, hypoxia, alcohol); optimize K+ >4.0, Mg2+ >2.0
day1_milestones: Confirm AF/flutter on ECG and establish onset/duration; identify and begin treating any precipitant. Achieve resting HR <110 bpm or symptom control; begin transition from IV to oral rate agents. Make and document the anticoagulation decision per CHA2DS2-VASc and start DOAC unless contraindicated. Order TTE; replete electrolytes. If unstable, perform DCCV; if a rhythm-control candidate, plan TEE/cardioversion. Continuous telemetry; grade symptoms (EHRA). Begin risk-factor counseling (alcohol, weight, OSA screen, BP)
day2_milestones: Sustained rate or rhythm control confirmed on ORAL regimen while ambulating; verify HR at target off IV drips. Review TTE (LVEF, valves) to finalize long-term strategy and ablation candidacy. Anticoagulation established with dose confirmed for renal function. If dofetilide/sotalol started, continue mandated in-hospital QT/telemetry monitoring window. Confirm precipitant resolving. Obtain cardiology/EP input on rhythm strategy - early rhythm control (EAST-AFNET 4) and ablation for HFrEF (CASTLE-AF). Start discharge planning, medication reconciliation and patient education
day3plus_milestones: Stable rate/rhythm on oral meds >=24h, ambulating without symptomatic RVR. Anticoagulation plan finalized with adherence counseling. Complete in-hospital AAD loading window if applicable (e.g., dofetilide ~5 doses/3 days with QTc checks). Precipitant treated/resolving. Confirmed follow-up (cardiology/EP and anticoagulation) before discharge. Most uncomplicated AF discharges Day 1-2; Day 3+ is mainly for AAD loading, procedures (ablation/LAAO/pacemaker), or MCC-complicated cases
consults_multidisciplinary: Cardiology for rate/rhythm strategy (most cases). Electrophysiology for ablation candidacy, AAD initiation, LAAO (Watchman) when OAC contraindicated, pacemaker/conduction disease, and WPW. Pharmacy for anticoagulation selection/renal dosing, AAD/QT and interaction review, transitions. Anticoagulation/INR clinic for warfarin patients. Care management and social work for disposition, medication access and adherence. Nutrition/dietitian if HF or weight management. Endocrinology (thyrotoxicosis), Pulmonary/Sleep (OSA), Neurology/Stroke (embolic event). PT/OT for deconditioned/elderly or post-stroke patients
monitoring_level: ICU/step-down for hemodynamic instability, shock, decompensated HF, ongoing ischemia/ACS, post-arrest, refractory RVR requiring IV drips with instability, or high-grade AV block awaiting pacing. Telemetry floor is standard for AF-RVR undergoing rate control, mandatory during dofetilide/sotalol/ibutilide use, and post-cardioversion. Escalation triggers: SBP <90, sustained HR >150 refractory to therapy, new ischemia, hypoxia, altered mental status, or QTc >500 ms on AAD (hold drug/escalate). De-escalate to floor once rate-controlled and hemodynamically stable
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: heart-healthy/low-sodium if HF; NPO peri-cardioversion/TEE for sedation. Mobility: early ambulation once rate-controlled, no restriction; PT if deconditioned. VTE prophylaxis: therapeutic anticoagulation for AF (per CHA2DS2-VASc) generally covers VTE; if OAC withheld/contraindicated use mechanical SCDs +/- prophylactic-dose heparin/LMWH by VTE risk. Glucose: inpatient target 140-180 mg/dL, avoid hypoglycemia. Lines/catheters: minimize and remove promptly. Maintain K+ >4.0 and Mg2+ >2.0 throughout
discharge_criteria: Resting HR controlled <110 bpm on an oral regimen (or restored sinus rhythm) with acceptable symptom control; hemodynamically stable >=24h with no ongoing ischemia; anticoagulation initiated (or a documented decision/contraindication) at a dose appropriate to renal function; precipitant treated or clearly resolving; if an antiarrhythmic was started, the required in-hospital telemetry/QT monitoring window is completed; follow-up arranged and patient educated on medications and anticoagulation
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home (SNF if deconditioned or post-stroke). Medication reconciliation focused on the anticoagulant (agent, renal-adjusted dose) and rate/rhythm agents, removing QT-prolonging interactions. Follow-up: cardiology/EP within 1-2 weeks (sooner if AAD or ablation planned), primary care, and anticoagulation/INR clinic within 3-7 days for warfarin. Education: nature of AF, anticoagulation adherence and bleeding precautions, rate/rhythm medications, stroke warning signs (FAST/BE-FAST), and when to seek care. Transitions: communicate plan to PCP/cardiology, secure DOAC access/prior authorization, and hand off the risk-factor plan (alcohol cessation, weight loss, OSA testing, BP/diabetes control)
expected_los: CMS GMLOS benchmarks (FY2024-25 MS-DRG v41): DRG 308 (with MCC) ~3.6 days; DRG 309 (with CC) ~2.5 days; DRG 310 (without CC/MCC) ~1.8 days; blended typical stay ~2-3 days for the collapsed group
target_los: ~1.5-2 days for uncomplicated AF/RVR (DRG 310), ~2 days with CC, ~3 days for MCC/complicated; select recent-onset AF cardioverted and rate-controlled can be managed in ED/observation and discharged same day, avoiding inpatient admission entirely
quality_metrics: Anticoagulation prescribed at discharge for AF/flutter when indicated (AHA Get With The Guidelines-AFIB core measure; analogous to STK-3). GWTG-AFIB registry measures: CHA2DS2-VASc documented, LVEF/TTE assessment, rate/rhythm plan documented, appropriate DOAC dosing (avoid under/over-dosing - a key safety metric). Avoidance of class IC antiarrhythmics in structural/ischemic heart disease. 30-day all-cause readmission and ED revisit for AF; time to rate control; LOS; observation-vs-inpatient status appropriateness; tobacco-cessation and patient education documentation
common_complications: Anticoagulant-related bleeding (GI, intracranial) - mitigated by correct renal dosing, reversal agents, and fixing modifiable HAS-BLED factors. Thromboembolic stroke, especially peri-cardioversion - mitigated by the 48h rule/TEE and therapeutic anticoagulation. Drug-induced bradycardia/hypotension/AV block from rate or rhythm agents - mitigated by titration and telemetry. AAD proarrhythmia/QT prolongation and torsades (dofetilide, sotalol, ibutilide) - mitigated by in-hospital initiation, continuous ECG and Mg repletion. Decompensated HF from non-DHP CCB in HFrEF - avoided by agent selection. Recurrent RVR and digoxin toxicity
readmission_drivers: Recurrent symptomatic AF/RVR - lever: optimize rate/rhythm and refer early for rhythm control/ablation. Anticoagulation gaps from non-adherence, cost/access, or bleeding - lever: ensure DOAC access, education, and a reversal plan. HF decompensation - lever: guideline-directed HF therapy and comorbidity control. Untreated precipitants (thyrotoxicosis, OSA, alcohol, uncontrolled BP) - lever: structured risk-factor modification. Bleeding events and inappropriate DOAC dosing - lever: pharmacy dose verification. Missing/late follow-up - lever: schedule cardiology and anticoagulation follow-up before discharge
guideline_source: 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation (primary, United States; Joglar et al, J Am Coll Cardiol 2024;83:109-279 / Circulation 2024;149:e1-e156); 2024 ESC Guidelines for the Management of Atrial Fibrillation (AF-CARE framework, EACTS collaboration; Van Gelder et al, Eur Heart J 2024;45:3314-3414); supportive: 2023 AHA Scientific Statement 'Atrial Fibrillation Occurring During Acute Hospitalization' (Chyou et al, Circulation 2023;147:e676-e698) for inpatient/acute AF management, and the 2018 CHEST/ACCP guideline 'Antithrombotic Therapy for Atrial Fibrillation' (Lip et al, Chest 2018;154:1121-1201) for anticoagulation/stroke-risk stratification
key_citations: Joglar JA, et al. 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS AF Guideline. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2024;83(1):109-279 (PMID 38043043; also Circulation. 2024;149(1):e1-e156). Van Gelder IC, et al. 2024 ESC AF Guidelines. Eur Heart J. 2024;45(36):3314-3414 (PMID 39210723). AFFIRM - Wyse DG, et al. NEJM 2002;347(23):1825-33 (PMID 12466506; rate vs rhythm, no mortality difference). RACE II - Van Gelder IC, et al. NEJM 2010;362(15):1363-73 (PMID 20231232; lenient <110 bpm non-inferior). ARISTOTLE - Granger CB, et al. NEJM 2011;365(11):981-92 (PMID 21870978; apixaban > warfarin); other pivotal DOAC trials RE-LY, ROCKET-AF, ENGAGE AF-TIMI 48. EAST-AFNET 4 - Kirchhof P, et al. NEJM 2020;383(14):1305-16 (PMID 32865375; early rhythm control). CASTLE-AF - Marrouche NF, et al. NEJM 2018;378(5):417-27 (PMID 29385358; ablation in HFrEF). Citations verified via PubMed
evidence_grade: Strong - anchored by a current Class I society guideline (2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS) with multiple Class I recommendations supported by RCTs: anticoagulation by CHA2DS2-VASc and DOAC preference (ARISTOTLE and the DOAC trials), peri-cardioversion anticoagulation timing, lenient rate target (RACE II), early rhythm control (EAST-AFNET 4), and ablation in HFrEF (CASTLE-AF). Inpatient LOS targets and day-by-day sequencing are operational extrapolation/consensus rather than trial-derived
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 310 (no CC/MCC): stable AF/RVR or isolated conduction disorder, brief telemetry, rate control plus anticoagulation initiation, LOS ~1.8 days, discharge Day 1-2. DRG 309 (CC): a comorbidity/complication (moderate HF, CKD altering DOAC choice, minor bleeding, electrolyte derangement) modestly extends stay to ~2.5 days. DRG 308 (MCC): major complication or precipitant (acute HF, ACS, sepsis, ischemic stroke, major bleed, respiratory failure, need for ICU/step-down or pacemaker) drives higher-acuity monitoring and LOS ~3.6+ days with greater complication and readmission risk - here AF is frequently secondary to the MCC that dominates care and coding
pathway_pearls: 1) Unstable AF = immediate synchronized DCCV, don't chase drugs; pre-excited (WPW) AF = NO AV-nodal blockers, use procainamide or DCCV. 2) Anticoagulate by CHA2DS2-VASc (>=2 men/>=3 women = Class I), DOAC preferred over warfarin; never use HAS-BLED to withhold OAC - use it to fix modifiable bleeding risk; honor the 48h cardioversion rule (TEE if >=48h/unknown, then >=4 weeks OAC). 3) Lenient rate control <110 bpm (RACE II) suffices for most; avoid non-DHP CCB in HFrEF; always treat the precipitant (holiday heart, thyroid, sepsis, OSA). 4) Move early to rhythm control/ablation referral (EAST-AFNET 4; CASTLE-AF for HFrEF), lock in risk-factor modification (>=10% weight loss, alcohol cessation, OSA, BP), and guarantee cardiology + anticoagulation follow-up to prevent bounce-back; initiate dofetilide/sotalol ONLY in-hospital with QT/telemetry
verification_notes: Verified all 6 flagged high-stakes areas via PubMed (MCP) and web sources. CITATIONS: all 7 key_citations PMIDs verified exact against PubMed metadata (journal/volume/issue/pages/first author) — 2023 ACC/AHA (38043043, JACC 2024;83(1):109-279, DOI 10.1016/j.jacc.2023.08.017), 2024 ESC (39210723, Eur Heart J 2024;45(36):3314-3414), AFFIRM/Wyse (12466506), RACE II/Van Gelder (20231232), ARISTOTLE/Granger (21870978), EAST-AFNET 4/Kirchhof (32865375), CASTLE-AF/Marrouche (29385358); none fabricated or misattributed. THRESHOLDS/SCORES: CHA2DS2-VASc (>=2 men/>=3 women = Class I, ~>=2%/yr), HAS-BLED (>=3 = high, used to modify not withhold OAC), EHRA I-IV, RACE II lenient <110 bpm — all real and correct; CHA2DS2-VASc and HAS-BLED thresholds independently corroborated by the 2018 CHEST guideline abstract and the ACC 2023 guideline summary (DOAC preferred over warfarin except mitral stenosis/mechanical valve confirmed). DRUG CLASSES: IV beta-blocker/non-DHP CCB first-line rate control, avoid non-DHP CCB in HFrEF, digoxin/IV amiodarone as adjuncts, WPW pre-excited AF -> avoid all AV-nodal blockers incl IV amiodarone and use procainamide/DCCV, DOAC-preferred anticoagulation (Class I), and reversal agents (idarucizumab/andexanet/4F-PCC/vit K) all correct and current; no transfusion/fluid threshold applicable to AF. TIME-CRITICAL: emergent synchronized DCCV for instability and the <48h vs >=48h/unknown cardioversion-anticoagulation rule (>=3 wk OAC or TEE, then >=4 wk after) are correct/current. LOS: DRG 310 GMLOS 1.80 exact; 308/309 ~0.2 d high vs FY2025 v42 but plausible for cited FY2024 (minor note only). QUALITY METRICS: GWTG-AFIB core measure and the STK-3 (Joint Commission AF/flutter anticoagulation) analogy are real and applicable. Only substantive defect: guideline_source mislabels the 2018 CHEST antithrombotic guideline as inpatient/acute-AF guidance — corrected. Attribution: verification of citations and guideline scope performed using PubMed.; ISSUES: guideline_source: the 'supportive: 2018 CHEST/ACCP guidance on inpatient management of acute AF/flutter' is a scope misattribution — the 2018 CHEST/ACCP AF guideline (PMID 30144419) is 'Antithrombotic Therapy for Atrial Fibrillation' (Lip et al, Chest 2018;154:1121-1201), which addresses anticoagulation/stroke-risk stratification, not inpatient/acute AF management. Corrected (see corrections). The primary 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS and secondary 2024 ESC guidelines are correct and current.; expected_los / severity_cc_mcc_notes (minor, not corrected): DRG 308 (~3.6 d) and DRG 309 (~2.5 d) GMLOS run ~0.2 d above the current FY2025 MS-DRG v42 values (3.40 and 2.30 d per icdlist.com); DRG 310 (~1.8 d) matches exactly (1.80). Values are labeled approximate and are benchmark-plausible for the cited FY2024 v41, so left as-is, but note the row's 'FY2024-25 ... v41' is internally slightly inconsistent (v41 = FY2024; FY2025 = v42).
scope_and_volume_notes: HCUP #7 principal dx (0.62M, 2018). Dominated by atrial fibrillation/flutter and conduction disorders.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 308 — Cardiac Arrhythmia and Conduction Disorders with MCC; 309 — Cardiac Arrhythmia and Conduction Disorders with CC; 310 — Cardiac Arrhythmia and Conduction Disorders without CC/MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 7/7 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Original independent adversarial high-stakes review completed; current pass revalidated citation resolvability and FY2026 coding/taxonomy.
data_quality_notes: Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk.

admission_criteria: NICU/inpatient (not well-newborn couplet care or ED discharge) for: gestational age <35 wks or birth weight <2000 g; respiratory distress needing FiO2 >0.30-0.40, CPAP, or mechanical ventilation (persistent grunting/retractions/tachypnea, Silverman-Andersen elevated); need for resuscitation beyond routine (PPV, intubation, chest compressions, epinephrine) or recurrent apnea; 5-min Apgar <=5, cord pH <7.0 or base deficit >=12 with encephalopathy (HIE/cooling eval); persistent hypoglycemia <40-45 mg/dL needing IV dextrose; suspected/proven sepsis needing IV antibiotics (EOS-calculator risk >= threshold or clinically ill); duct-dependent CHD/major anomaly/surgical abdomen needing prostaglandin or intervention; hyperbilirubinemia at escalation-of-care or exchange threshold; neonatal abstinence needing pharmacotherapy. Stable term/late-preterm >=35 wks, feeding, thermoregulating, normal transition -> couplet care/observation
risk_stratification: Apgar 1 & 5 min (<=3 severe depression; 5-min <=5 triggers cord gas + HIE eval); gestational age by New Ballard + birth-weight percentile on Fenton curve (primary risk determinant; <28 wks/<1000 g ELBW = highest risk); Kaiser Permanente Neonatal EOS calculator for >=34-35 wks (risk/1000: >=3 empiric antibiotics+cultures, 1-3 enhanced observation/labs, <1 routine); SNAPPE-II and CRIB-II for illness-severity/mortality prediction driving ICU intensity and family counseling; Silverman-Andersen retraction score -> escalate respiratory support; modified Sarnat staging for HIE (moderate-severe/Stage II-III -> therapeutic hypothermia); AAP 2022 hour-specific bilirubin nomogram stratified by gestational age + neurotoxicity risk factors -> phototherapy/escalation/exchange; modified Bell staging for NEC (>=IIA = definite -> NPO, antibiotics, surgery consult); International Classification of ROP zone/stage/plus (Type 1 -> treat)
initial_workup_labs: POC heel-stick glucose at 30-60 min and serial -> IV dextrose if <40-45 mg/dL; umbilical cord/arterial blood gas + lactate -> pH <7.0 or base deficit >=12 with encephalopathy activates cooling pathway; CBC with differential + I:T ratio and blood culture when sepsis risk -> guides empiric antibiotics/duration; serial CRP/procalcitonin support antibiotic discontinuation if repeatedly normal; total/direct bilirubin or transcutaneous bili plotted on nomogram, blood type/Rh + DAT (Coombs) if mother O or Rh-negative -> phototherapy decision; electrolytes/BMP, ionized calcium, magnesium (ELBW fluid-electrolyte mgmt); state newborn metabolic screen and CCHD pulse-ox screen at >=24 h; ventilated/ELBW infants get serial gases and glucose for support titration
initial_imaging_dx: Chest radiograph within first hours for respiratory distress (RDS ground-glass/air bronchograms; r/o pneumothorax, pneumonia, CDH); echocardiography for suspected duct-dependent CHD (before/with prostaglandin), hemodynamically significant PDA, or PPHN; cranial ultrasound (IVH screen) for <32 wks/<1500 g at day 3-7 then repeat 4-6 wks and as indicated; abdominal radiograph AP + left-lateral-decubitus/cross-table for feeding intolerance/suspected NEC (pneumatosis, portal venous gas, free air); aEEG/EEG for seizure monitoring during HIE cooling and brain MRI day 4-10 post-cooling for prognostication; dilated retinal ROP exam first at 31 wks postmenstrual age if born <27 wks, or 4 wks chronological age if born >=27 wks
time_critical_interventions: Golden Hour delivery-room bundle: maintain normothermia 36.5-37.5 C (polyethylene wrap + hat + radiant warmer for <32 wks), defer cord clamping >=60 s, achieve SpO2 >=80% and HR >100 by 5 min; NRP Golden Minute PPV within 60 s if apneic/HR <100, escalate to intubation/chest compressions/epinephrine 0.02 mg/kg IV if HR <60 despite adequate ventilation; CPAP >=6 cmH2O for spontaneously breathing preterm with early LISA/thin-catheter surfactant when FiO2 >=0.30 at MAP >=6; therapeutic hypothermia initiated within 6 h of birth for moderate-severe HIE (33.5 C x 72 h) - begin passive cooling immediately and transfer to cooling center; antibiotics within 1 h for clinical sepsis/septic shock after cultures; emergent prostaglandin E1 for suspected duct-dependent CHD; admission normothermia + early IV dextrose/parenteral nutrition in ELBW
initial_management: Perinatal optimization: antenatal corticosteroids (betamethasone/dexamethasone) for threatened delivery <=34 wks + MgSO4 neuroprotection <32 wks. Surfactant: poractant alfa 200 mg/kg initial (superior to 100) or beractant via LISA/INSURE. Respiratory: CPAP 6-8 cmH2O, volume-targeted lung-protective ventilation if needed; caffeine citrate 20 mg/kg load then 5-10 mg/kg/day to prevent BPD/facilitate extubation. Empiric EOS antibiotics: ampicillin + gentamicin, narrow/stop at 36-48 h if cultures negative and infant well. Fluids/nutrition: D10W 60-80 mL/kg/day titrated to glucose/electrolytes; early parenteral amino acids 3-3.5 g/kg/day + lipids day 1; trophic feeds with mother's own/donor milk. Hypoglycemia: 2 mL/kg D10W bolus + infusion for glucose <40-45. Restrictive transfusion (per TOP/ETTNO): PRBC by postnatal-age/respiratory-support Hgb thresholds (~11-12 g/dL if ventilated early down to ~7 g/dL stable/older); platelets typically <25-50k. Hemodynamically significant PDA: acetaminophen or ibuprofen/indomethacin. Bilirubin: intensive phototherapy at threshold, exchange +/- IVIG (isoimmune) at exchange threshold. HIE: cooling + phenobarbital first-line for seizures. Shock/PPHN: dopamine/dobutamine/epinephrine, inhaled nitric oxide for term/near-term PPHN
day1_milestones: DOL 0-1: complete Golden Hour; secure respiratory support and vascular access (UAC/UVC in ELBW) with line-position confirmation on radiograph; establish euthermia and euglycemia; start IV dextrose + early parenteral amino acids/lipids; send and act on EOS labs, start empiric antibiotics if indicated, obtain CXR; plan first cranial US; begin trophic feeds with colostrum/mother's milk if stable; initiate developmental/neuroprotective care and lactation support; family consent, prognosis counseling, and involvement
day2_milestones: DOL 2: reassess antibiotics at 36-48 h and discontinue if cultures negative and clinically well (stewardship to reduce NEC/resistance); advance enteral feeds per standardized feeding protocol (~20-30 mL/kg/day) monitoring tolerance; continue caffeine; wean FiO2/CPAP toward SpO2 90-95% and extubate to noninvasive support if criteria met; recheck bilirubin and continue phototherapy as needed; HIE infants continue cooling (hour ~24-48) with aEEG/seizure surveillance and electrolyte/coagulation management; ongoing skin-to-skin and lactation
day3plus_milestones: DOL 3+ to discharge readiness: advance to full enteral feeds (~150-160 mL/kg/day), add human-milk fortifier, wean off parenteral nutrition, and remove central lines once feeds established (reduces CLABSI); complete IVH screening cranial US day 3-7; rewarm after 72 h cooling and obtain MRI day 4-10 for HIE; ROP screening at appropriate PMA, AABR hearing screen and repeat metabolic screen before discharge; wean respiratory support to room air (or define home-O2 plan); establish oral nippling with consistent weight gain 15-20 g/kg/day; immunizations at 2 months chronological age if still admitted; car-seat tolerance screen and RSV prophylaxis (nirsevimab) planning
consults_multidisciplinary: Neonatology-led with Maternal-Fetal Medicine at delivery and early lactation consultant; pediatric ophthalmology (ROP), pediatric cardiology (echo/PDA/CHD), pediatric surgery (NEC/anomalies), pediatric neurology (HIE/seizures); clinical genetics/dysmorphology for congenital anomalies and palliative care for periviable/lethal conditions; NICU registered dietitian (growth/fortification), NICU clinical pharmacist (weight-based dosing, parenteral nutrition), respiratory therapy, PT/OT + developmental specialist, and social work + case management engaged early; audiology for hearing screen
monitoring_level: Level III/IV NICU for <32 wks, <1500 g, mechanical ventilation, surgical/cardiac disease, HIE cooling, PPHN, or vasoactive support; Level II special-care nursery for stable 32-35 wks on CPAP/low support, feeding/growing, or phototherapy; continuous cardiorespiratory + pulse-oximetry monitoring throughout. Escalation triggers: recurrent apnea/bradycardia/desaturation, rising FiO2 or respiratory failure, hemodynamic instability, abdominal distension/bloody stools (NEC), or seizures. Stable late-preterm >=35 wks without these criteria may remain in mother-baby couplet care
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: mother's own milk first-line, pasteurized donor human milk if unavailable (reduces NEC vs formula), standardized feeding protocol from trophic feeds to full enteral with human-milk fortifier for growth; early parenteral nutrition in preterm; target weight gain 15-20 g/kg/day. Developmental care (mobility equivalent): skin-to-skin/kangaroo care, minimal handling with clustered cares, supportive positioning, non-nutritive sucking, noise/light control. VTE: routine pharmacologic prophylaxis NOT indicated in neonates; emphasis on umbilical/central-line care bundles, early line removal, and catheter-thrombosis vigilance. Glucose: maintain ~50-100 mg/dL, treat <40-45, avoid hyperglycemia in ELBW. Remove UAC/UVC/PICC as soon as enteral feeds are established to reduce CLABSI/thrombosis
discharge_criteria: Physiologic maturity: sustained thermoregulation in an open crib, apnea-free interval (typically 5-7 days off caffeine with no significant apnea/bradycardia), and all-oral feeding with consistent weight gain; stable in room air or an established home-oxygen plan; passing car-seat tolerance test if <37 wks; completed newborn metabolic screen, AABR hearing screen, CCHD pulse-ox, ROP exams to resolution or defined follow-up, and cranial imaging; immunizations current; caregivers demonstrate feeding and care competence. Readiness is usually reached around 36-37 wks postmenstrual age
discharge_planning: Disposition home with high-risk-infant follow-up (or transfer for ongoing surgical/cardiac/cooling care); medication reconciliation (vitamin D, preterm iron supplementation, caffeine wean) and RSV immunoprophylaxis (nirsevimab or palivizumab) planning; schedule pediatrician visit within 24-48 h (with bilirubin rebound check for jaundice), high-risk neurodevelopmental follow-up clinic, ophthalmology (ROP), audiology, subspecialty (cardiology/surgery/neurology) as needed, and early-intervention referral; caregiver education on safe sleep (back-to-sleep, no bed-sharing), feeding, infant CPR for high-risk infants, and home apnea/oxygen if applicable; warm handoff with discharge summary and chronic-condition care plan (e.g., BPD/home O2) to PCP
expected_los: Highly gestational-age-driven and bimodal across the collapsed group; approximate GMLOS by DRG: 794 (other significant problems) ~2.5-4 d; 793 (full-term with major problems) ~4-6 d; 792 (prematurity without major problems) ~7-9 d; 791 (prematurity with major problems) ~11-14 d; 790 (extreme immaturity/RDS) ~14-18 d benchmark but extremely preterm/ELBW commonly 60-100+ d (discharge near 36-37 wks PMA); 789 (died/transferred) ~1.5-3 d. Blended group GMLOS roughly 6-9 d (these MDC-15 DRGs are largely non-Medicare, so figures are approximate benchmarks)
target_los: Largely biology-limited, not compressible: target is discharge at the earliest safe physiologic maturity (~36-37 wks PMA) achieved by preventing excess days from NEC/CLABSI/BPD and by standardized feeding advancement and discharge-readiness bundling. Practical near-best-practice targets: 794 ~2-3 d; 793 ~3-5 d; 792 ~6-7 d; 791 ~10-12 d; extreme-preterm limited to PMA maturation minus avoidable complication days. The lever is reducing morbidity-driven excess LOS, not shortening physiologic maturation
quality_metrics: Joint Commission Perinatal Care measures (PC-05 exclusive breast-milk feeding; antenatal steroid administration); Vermont Oxford Network / CPQCC benchmarks: survival without major morbidity, BPD (O2 at 36 wks PMA), severe IVH (grade III-IV), NEC >=Stage II, CLABSI/healthcare-associated infection rate, ROP requiring treatment, and growth velocity; process metrics: Golden Hour bundle compliance, admission normothermia rate, deferred cord clamping rate, antenatal steroid exposure, human-milk feeding at discharge; unplanned extubation rate, mortality, 30-day readmission, and timely ROP/hearing/metabolic screening completion
common_complications: Respiratory - RDS, bronchopulmonary dysplasia, pneumothorax, apnea of prematurity (mitigate: antenatal steroids, CPAP-first/LISA, caffeine, lung-protective ventilation, SpO2 90-95% targeting); neurologic - IVH/PVL, HIE (mitigate: deferred cord clamping, minimal handling, avoid BP/CO2 swings, cooling); GI - necrotizing enterocolitis and feeding intolerance (mitigate: human milk, standardized feeding, antibiotic stewardship); infection - early/late-onset sepsis and CLABSI (mitigate: line bundles, early line removal, stop empiric antibiotics at 36-48 h); cardiac - hemodynamically significant PDA, PPHN; ophthalmologic - ROP (mitigate: judicious oxygen, SpO2 targeting, timely screening); metabolic/hematologic - hypoglycemia, hyperbilirubinemia/kernicterus, anemia of prematurity, electrolyte derangements
readmission_drivers: Top drivers: feeding difficulty/poor weight gain/dehydration and jaundice rebound needing readmission (late-preterm highest risk); respiratory - RSV/viral bronchiolitis, BPD exacerbation, apnea; infection/sepsis. Prevention levers: rigorous discharge-readiness (documented feeding competence and weight-gain trajectory), pediatrician follow-up within 24-48 h with bilirubin recheck, lactation support, RSV immunoprophylaxis (nirsevimab), caregiver education and infant CPR, home-oxygen optimization, smoke-free environment, and reliable linkage to high-risk follow-up clinic
guideline_source: AAP + AHA Neonatal Resuscitation Program 8th edition / 2020 AHA-AAP Neonatal Resuscitation Guidelines with 2023-2024 focused updates; European Consensus Guidelines on the Management of Respiratory Distress Syndrome: 2025 (7th version, Sweet et al, Neonatology); AAP 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline - Management of Hyperbilirubinemia in the Newborn >=35 weeks; AAP 2018 Clinical Report - Management of Neonates >=35 weeks with Suspected/Proven Early-Onset Sepsis; AAP/AAO/AAPOS 2018 ROP Screening policy statement; AAP 2025 Clinical Report - Therapeutic Hypothermia for Neonatal HIE; ACOG antenatal corticosteroids/periviability guidance
key_citations: Sweet DG et al. European Consensus Guidelines on the Management of Respiratory Distress Syndrome: 2025. Neonatology 2025 (PMID 41802129; DOI 10.1159/000551062); Kemper AR et al. AAP CPG Revision: Management of Hyperbilirubinemia >=35 wks. Pediatrics 2022;150(3) (PMID 35927462; DOI 10.1542/peds.2022-058859); Puopolo KM et al. Management of Neonates >=35 0/7 wks With Suspected/Proven EOS. Pediatrics 2018;142(6) (PMID 30455342; DOI 10.1542/peds.2018-2894); Fierson WM et al. Screening Examination of Premature Infants for ROP. Pediatrics 2018;142(6) (PMID 30478242; DOI 10.1542/peds.2018-3061); Shankaran S et al. Whole-body hypothermia for neonates with HIE. NEJM 2005;353:1574-84 (PMID 16221780; DOI 10.1056/NEJMcps050929); Schmidt B et al. Caffeine therapy for apnea of prematurity (CAP). NEJM 2006;354:2112-21 (PMID 16707748; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa054065); SUPPORT Study Group. Early CPAP versus surfactant in extremely preterm infants. NEJM 2010;362:1970-9 (PMID 20472939; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa0911783) and Target ranges of oxygen saturation (PMID 20472937). Citations retrieved via PubMed
evidence_grade: Strong for core condition-specific interventions - multiple Class I RCTs and current Class I society guidelines support antenatal corticosteroids, surfactant/LISA, caffeine, therapeutic hypothermia, SpO2 targeting, CPAP-first, and phototherapy/exchange thresholds (AAP, AHA/NRP, European RDS 2025). Moderate-to-Consensus for bundled supportive care, feeding-advancement protocols, and LOS/target-LOS figures, which are largely local-protocol and expert-opinion driven given the heterogeneous collapsed DRG group
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Unlike adult DRGs, MDC-15 neonatal DRGs are NOT split by CC/MCC; severity is encoded by the DRG hierarchy itself via gestational-age/birth-weight band plus presence of major vs other significant problems. 790 (extreme immaturity/RDS) and 791 (prematurity with major problems) are highest acuity -> Level III/IV NICU, ventilation, weeks-to-months LOS, and high complication burden (BPD, IVH, NEC, ROP, sepsis); 792 (prematurity without major problems) is moderate, feeding/growing, days-to-weeks; 793-794 (term/near-term with problems) are shorter with targeted therapy (sepsis rule-out, phototherapy, TTN, hypoglycemia, NAS); 789 (died/transferred) reflects mortality or need for higher-level surgical/cardiac/cooling transfer. Dominant LOS/cost drivers are gestational age and whether major-problem complications occur
pathway_pearls: Perinatal optimization is the single biggest lever: antenatal corticosteroids <=34 wks, MgSO4 neuroprotection <32 wks, delivery at/transfer to a level-appropriate center, and a disciplined Golden Hour (normothermia 36.5-37.5 C, deferred cord clamping >=60 s, CPAP-first with SpO2 targeting). Respiratory strategy: noninvasive-first (CPAP >=6) + LISA surfactant + caffeine to avoid intubation and BPD; target SpO2 90-95% (European 2025: 90-94% for <28 wks; alarm limits 89-95%) - higher oxygen worsens ROP. Gut/infection stewardship: mother's own/donor human milk + standardized feeding to prevent NEC, stop empiric antibiotics at 36-48 h if cultures negative, and central-line bundles with early removal to prevent CLABSI. Discharge is physiology-gated (feeds, thermoregulation, apnea-free, ~36-37 wks PMA): bundle ROP/hearing/metabolic/car-seat screens and 24-48 h follow-up with bilirubin recheck to prevent readmission
verification_notes: Verified against live sources (PubMed MCP + web); did not rely on memory for the checkable claims. Per PubMed, all 8 key_citations are authentic and correctly attributed: European RDS 2025/7th version (DOI https://doi.org/10.1159/000551062), Kemper AAP hyperbilirubinemia 2022 (https://doi.org/10.1542/peds.2022-058859), Puopolo EOS 2018 (https://doi.org/10.1542/peds.2018-2894), Fierson ROP 2018 (https://doi.org/10.1542/peds.2018-3061), Shankaran hypothermia 2005 (https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMcps050929), Schmidt caffeine 2006 (https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa054065), and the two SUPPORT 2010 papers (CPAP https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa0911783; oxygen https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa0911781). guideline_source cross-checks: NRP 8th ed IV epinephrine 0.02 mg/kg confirmed current (web/AAP); AAP therapeutic-hypothermia clinical report confirmed real (PubMed PMID 41581784, https://doi.org/10.1542/peds.2025-073627) — only nuance is the 2025-vs-2026 label. time_critical_interventions numerics current and correct (normothermia 36.5-37.5 C, deferred cord clamping >=60 s, SpO2 ~80% & HR>100 by 5 min, hypothermia 33.5 C x72h <6h, CPAP >=6, antibiotics <1h, PGE1 for duct-dependent CHD). risk_stratification score names/thresholds all real (Apgar, New Ballard, Fenton, Kaiser EOS calculator risk/1000 tiers, SNAPPE-II, CRIB-II, Silverman-Andersen, modified Sarnat, AAP 2022 hour-specific bilirubin nomogram, modified Bell >=IIA, ICROP Type 1). initial_management drug classes/doses correct for guidelines (poractant 200 mg/kg, caffeine 20 mg/kg load then 5-10/day, ampicillin+gentamicin, MgSO4 <32 wks, antenatal steroids <=34 wks, iNO for term/near-term PPHN, phenobarbital first-line HIE seizures); importantly transfusion thresholds are neonatal-appropriate (age/support-dependent Hgb ~11-12 down to ~7 g/dL; platelets ~25-50k post-PlaNeT-2) rather than the adult <7 g/dL, so no error. quality_metrics real and applicable (Joint Commission PC-05 exclusive breast-milk feeding, PC-03 antenatal steroids; VON/CPQCC BPD/severe IVH/NEC>=II/CLABSI/ROP benchmarks). expected_los/target_los are appropriately hedged DRG-band GMLOS approximations for non-Medicare MDC-15 DRGs and are plausible; not independently reconcilable to a single CMS table but not implausible. Overall: no material corrections warranted.; ISSUES: NON-MATERIAL (not correction-worthy): guideline_source lists the AAP therapeutic hypothermia clinical report as 'AAP 2025'. Per PubMed it is Zanelli SA et al, 'Therapeutic Hypothermia for Neonatal Hypoxic-Ischemic Encephalopathy: Clinical Report,' Pediatrics 2026;157(2) (PMID 41581784; DOI 10.1542/peds.2025-073627). The manuscript is 2025-numbered/online-ahead-of-print, so '2025' is defensible, but the citable print year is 2026. The report is REAL and the pathway's description is accurate (cooling 33.5-34.5 C initiated <6 h, continued 72 h, for >=36 0/7 wks moderate-severe HIE); the row's stated 33.5 C x 72 h sits at the low end of the endorsed range. No change required.; No fabricated or misattributed citations found — all 8 key_citations verified exact-match against PubMed (Sweet 2025 European RDS 7th version PMID 41802129 / DOI 10.1159/000551062; Kemper 2022 hyperbilirubinemia PMID 35927462; Puopolo 2018 EOS PMID 30455342; Fierson 2018 ROP PMID 30478242; Shankaran 2005 whole-body hypothermia PMID 16221780; Schmidt 2006 CAP caffeine PMID 16707748; SUPPORT 2010 CPAP-vs-surfactant PMID 20472939 and oxygen-saturation PMID 20472937). Titles, journals, volumes/pages, authors and DOIs all correct.; No high-stakes numeric or clinical errors identified in time_critical_interventions, risk_stratification, or initial_management (see verification_notes).

admission_criteria: Inpatient admission for KDIGO Stage 2-3 AKI, or Stage 1 with a life-threatening/functional complication: refractory hyperkalemia (K >6.0-6.5 mmol/L or ECG changes), severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.15-7.20 or HCO3 <15), volume overload/pulmonary edema unresponsive to diuretics, oligoanuria (UO <0.3 mL/kg/h), uremic symptoms (encephalopathy, pericarditis, uremic bleeding), any emergent-dialysis indication, obstruction needing decompression, or suspected rapidly progressive intrinsic disease (RPGN/vasculitis/TMA/myeloma cast nephropathy/AIN) needing urgent workup; also admit when AKI complicates sepsis, decompensated HF, or cirrhosis (hepatorenal). ED discharge/observation appropriate for isolated Stage 1 pre-renal AKI that corrects with limited fluids, normal K/acid-base, tolerating PO, and reliable 48-72h recheck
risk_stratification: KDIGO stage (SCr and UO) is the master stratifier - Stage 1: SCr 1.5-1.9x baseline or >=0.3 mg/dL rise in 48h, UO <0.5 mL/kg/h x6-12h; Stage 2: SCr 2.0-2.9x, UO <0.5 x>=12h; Stage 3: SCr >=3.0x or >=4.0 mg/dL or RRT or eGFR <35 (<18y), UO <0.3 x>=24h or anuria >=12h -> stage drives monitoring/nephrology/RRT. Furosemide Stress Test (1.0-1.5 mg/kg IV; 2h UO <200 mL predicts progression to Stage 3/RRT) triages ICU vs floor. Cell-cycle-arrest biomarker TIMP-2*IGFBP7 (NephroCheck) >0.3 elevated / >2.0 high risk triggers KDIGO care bundle. FENa <1% / FEUrea <35% (pre-renal) vs FENa >2% + muddy-brown casts (ATN). Mehran score for contrast-AKI risk; MELD/ICA-AKI criteria for hepatorenal; SOFA/APACHE II for ICU need; MAKE30 as outcome anchor
initial_workup_labs: BMP/CMP with SCr trended against baseline (K, HCO3, Ca, phos, Mg, albumin); VBG for pH/HCO3; CBC (anemia + thrombocytopenia -> TMA; eosinophilia -> AIN); urinalysis with MICROSCOPY (muddy-brown granular casts=ATN; dysmorphic RBC/RBC casts=GN; WBC casts/eosinophiluria=AIN; bland=pre/post-renal); urine Na/Cr for FENa (FEUrea if on diuretics); spot UPCR/UACR; CK + LDH/haptoglobin/smear if rhabdo or TMA suspected. If intrinsic/unexplained: ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM, C3/C4, SPEP/UPEP + serum free light chains, hepatitis/HIV serologies, cryoglobulins. Bladder scan/PVR for retention. Each abnormality triggers a branch: K>6 -> emergent Rx; casts -> intrinsic workup + biopsy pathway; positive serologies -> RPGN/immunosuppression pathway
initial_imaging_dx: Renal/bladder ultrasound within 24h (exclude obstruction; assess size/echogenicity/chronicity) - prioritize when cause unclear, no clear pre-renal trigger, single kidney, or suspected obstruction; immediate bladder scan/PVR + straight cath for suspected retention. Doppler US if renovascular compromise/renal-vein thrombosis suspected. Percutaneous kidney biopsy (typically within days) when intrinsic cause is unresolved and would change management (RPGN, suspected AIN, systemic disease, unexplained AKI). Minimize iodinated contrast; when unavoidable, pre-procedure isotonic volume expansion (contrast-associated AKI risk lower than historically believed per ACR-NKF 2020)
time_critical_interventions: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes or K >6.5: IV calcium gluconate/chloride for membrane stabilization NOW, then insulin+dextrose, nebulized albuterol, and K removal (loop diuretic if urine-making, GI binder, or dialysis). Emergent RRT for AEIOU indications: refractory Acidosis, Electrolyte (hyperkalemia), Intoxication (dialyzable toxin), fluid Overload refractory to diuretics, Uremia (pericarditis/encephalopathy/bleeding). Relieve urinary obstruction urgently (Foley/ureteral stent/percutaneous nephrostomy). Sepsis-associated AKI: antibiotics <=1h + hemodynamic resuscitation to MAP >=65 mmHg (target 80-85 in chronic HTN). STOP all nephrotoxins immediately (NSAIDs, RAASi, aminoglycosides, iodinated contrast)
initial_management: Restore renal perfusion with judicious BALANCED crystalloids (Lactated Ringer's / Plasma-Lyte preferred over 0.9% saline - SMART trial lower MAKE30); AVOID hydroxyethyl starch and AVOID over-resuscitation/fluid overload. Vasopressor norepinephrine first-line for MAP >=65. Treat the cause: sepsis (source control + antibiotics), cardiorenal (decongestion), hepatorenal (albumin 1 g/kg + terlipressin or norepinephrine, or midodrine+octreotide). Discontinue nephrotoxins, renally dose ALL drugs, hold RAASi/SGLT2i/metformin during acute illness. Loop diuretics ONLY for volume overload - NOT for renoprotection or to force diuresis; NO low-dose dopamine, fenoldopam, or natriuretic peptides. Hyperkalemia: Ca, insulin/glucose, beta-agonist, patiromer/sodium-zirconium-cyclosilicate, dialysis. Sodium bicarbonate if pH <7.1 (BICAR-ICU benefit in AKIN 2-3). Glucose target 110-180 mg/dL
day1_milestones: Classify AKI as pre-renal/intrinsic/post-renal and assign KDIGO stage vs documented baseline; complete labs + urine microscopy + FENa + renal US/bladder scan. Stop nephrotoxins, reconcile and renally dose every medication (pharmacy). Restore euvolemia and MAP >=65; strict I/O, daily weights, Foley only if obstruction/critical UO monitoring. Correct life-threatening K/acid-base. Nephrology consult same-day if Stage 2-3, unclear cause, or possible RRT. Furosemide stress test in oliguric Stage 1-2 to prognosticate. Initiate KDIGO supportive care bundle in high-risk patients
day2_milestones: Reassess volume status and de-resuscitate if overloaded; trend SCr/UO/electrolytes every 12-24h (twice-daily BMP if unstable). Confirm nephrotoxin-free medication list and correct renal dosing. If intrinsic cause unresolved, proceed to biopsy and start empiric immunosuppression (e.g., pulse steroids) for confirmed/high-probability RPGN or vasculitis. Remove Foley and unnecessary lines when no longer indicated. Reassess RRT need daily - absent an emergent AEIOU indication, adopt watchful waiting (no benefit to preemptive early RRT per STARRT-AKI/AKIKI)
day3plus_milestones: Demonstrate trajectory: down-trending SCr and improving UO (recovery) vs plateau/progression. If dialysis-dependent, secure durable access (tunneled catheter/planned AVF) and arrange outpatient HD. Cautiously resume held medications once hemodynamically stable and recovering (RAASi/SGLT2i often deferred to discharge with a monitoring plan). Finalize discharge readiness, AKD surveillance labs, and follow-up. Document AKI stage and ATN vs unspecified for accurate severity capture
consults_multidisciplinary: Nephrology early for Stage 2-3, intrinsic/unexplained AKI, or RRT decisions (early consult linked to better outcomes). Urology/Interventional Radiology for obstruction (stent/nephrostomy). Critical care for hemodynamic instability or emergent/continuous RRT. Clinical pharmacy Day 1 for renal dosing + med reconciliation. Renal dietitian for protein/electrolyte-tailored nutrition. Care management/social work for dialysis logistics and disposition. Vascular access surgery/IR if chronic dialysis anticipated. PT/OT for deconditioning. Rheumatology/Hematology-Oncology for vasculitis, TMA, or myeloma
monitoring_level: ICU: hemodynamic instability/vasopressors, continuous RRT, severe hyperkalemia or acidosis with instability, or respiratory compromise from overload. Step-down/telemetry: significant hyperkalemia (telemetry until K controlled), labile electrolytes, or intermittent HD. Floor: hemodynamically stable Stage 1-2 without life-threatening derangement. Escalation triggers: refractory/rising hyperkalemia, worsening acidosis, new oligoanuria with rising SCr, volume overload/hypoxemia, or uremic complications (pericarditis, encephalopathy)
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: adequate energy 20-30 kcal/kg/day; protein 0.8-1.0 g/kg (non-catabolic, no RRT), up to 1.0-1.5 on intermittent HD and ~1.7 on CRRT; sodium <2 g/day if overloaded; restrict potassium and phosphorus if elevated. Early mobilization with PT to counter deconditioning. VTE prophylaxis: prefer unfractionated heparin or anti-Xa-monitored/dose-reduced enoxaparin (LMWH accumulates in renal failure) or mechanical prophylaxis if uremic bleeding risk. Glucose 110-180 mg/dL (avoid hypoglycemia). Remove Foley early to prevent CAUTI and de-line to prevent CLABSI
discharge_criteria: Renal function recovering or stably plateaued without ongoing RRT need (or a definitive outpatient dialysis plan in place); euvolemic and off IV fluids/diuretic drips; electrolytes and acid-base corrected and stable (K <5.5, no significant acidosis); hemodynamically stable with adequate urine output; underlying precipitant treated/controlled; medications reconciled, renally dosed, nephrotoxins removed, and a plan documented for resuming RAASi/SGLT2i
discharge_planning: Disposition home vs SNF; dialysis-dependent patients discharged only with a confirmed outpatient HD chair and access. Medication reconciliation: remove/adjust nephrotoxins, no NSAIDs, restart RAASi/SGLT2i with a monitoring plan, sick-day rules. Schedule AKD surveillance BMP within 7-14 days and nephrology follow-up within 30 days to 3 months (AKI survivors carry elevated CKD/recurrent-AKI/CV/mortality risk). Patient education: hydration, nephrotoxin/contrast avoidance, recognizing recurrence, medication adherence. Transitions: communicate AKI stage, baseline, and med changes to PCP/dialysis unit; refer to AKI survivor clinic where available
expected_los: Approx 4-5 days blended; MS-DRG GMLOS roughly 2.7-3.0d for 684 (no CC/MCC), ~4.3-4.5d for 683 (CC), ~6.4-6.7d for 682 (MCC); dialysis-requiring/ATN cases run longer
target_los: 3-4 days for uncomplicated pre-renal/ATN with early recovery under the ideal pathway; longer when new dialysis dependence or intrinsic disease requiring biopsy/immunosuppression
quality_metrics: No standalone CMS core measure for AKI; track process + outcome: AKI e-alert recognition rate, nephrotoxin stewardship and renal-dosing compliance, KDIGO care-bundle adherence, avoidance of fluid overload, appropriate (non-premature) RRT timing, accurate AKI stage/ATN documentation for severity capture. Outcomes: MAKE30 (major adverse kidney events at 30d), in-hospital mortality, dialysis dependence at discharge, progression to CKD, 30-day readmission. Safety overlaps: AHRQ PSI dialysis-requiring AKI, CAUTI/CLABSI, hospital-acquired AKI rate; AKI is a component of SEP-1 sepsis outcomes and contrast-stewardship programs
common_complications: Hyperkalemia, metabolic acidosis, volume overload/pulmonary edema, and uremia; progression to dialysis-requiring AKI; drug toxicity from missed renal dosing; iatrogenic fluid overload from over-resuscitation; uremic-platelet bleeding; hypoglycemia from insulin therapy; CAUTI/CLABSI; and failure of renal recovery/CKD progression. Mitigated by the KDIGO bundle (euvolemia with balanced crystalloids, nephrotoxin avoidance, hemodynamic optimization, glucose control), pharmacy-driven renal dosing, strict I/O with daily weights, early nephrology involvement, and prompt electrolyte/acid-base management
readmission_drivers: Recurrent AKI, volume overload/HF decompensation, and hyperkalemia (often after RAASi restart); incomplete renal recovery; missed AKD surveillance labs and nephrology follow-up; medication errors (reintroduced nephrotoxins, non-renally-dosed drugs). Prevention levers: guaranteed BMP recheck within 7-14 days, nephrology follow-up within 30-90 days, rigorous discharge med reconciliation, cautious diuretic/RAASi titration with monitoring, and structured patient education on hydration and nephrotoxin/contrast avoidance
guideline_source: KDIGO (Kidney Disease: Improving Global Outcomes) - 2012 Clinical Practice Guideline for Acute Kidney Injury is the current PUBLISHED standard; KDIGO 2026 AKI/AKD Clinical Practice Guideline is in public-review draft (March 2026, first major update since 2012, adding biomarkers and formal AKD staging). Supported by ADQI consensus statements and the 2020 ACR-NKF consensus on iodinated contrast media and AKI
key_citations: Per PubMed: KDIGO 2012 AKI Clinical Practice Guideline (Kidney Int Suppl 2012;2:1-138; summary Khwaja A, Nephron Clin Pract 2012;120:c179-84, PMID 22890468); KDIGO 2026 AKI/AKD Guideline public-review draft (Mar 2026); STARRT-AKI Investigators, Timing of Initiation of RRT in AKI, NEJM 2020;383:240-51, PMID 32668114; Gaudry S et al (AKIKI), Initiation Strategies for RRT in the ICU, NEJM 2016;375:122-33, PMID 27181456; Zarbock A et al (ELAIN), Early vs Delayed RRT, JAMA 2016;315:2190-9, PMID 27209269; Semler MW et al (SMART), Balanced Crystalloids vs Saline, NEJM 2018;378:829-39, PMID 29485925; PrevAKI biomarker-guided KDIGO bundle, Kullmar/Meersch/Zarbock, BMJ Open 2020, PMID 32265240 (DOI 10.1136/bmjopen-2019-034201)
evidence_grade: Moderate-to-Strong. AKI definition/staging and core supportive care rest on a rigorous international guideline (KDIGO), but many individual AKI recommendations are Level 2 (2B/2C) or ungraded/expert consensus; notably strong RCT evidence underpins that preemptive early RRT offers no mortality benefit (STARRT-AKI, AKIKI, ELAIN) and that balanced crystalloids reduce major adverse kidney events (SMART). Etiology-specific and biomarker-guided elements are extrapolated/emerging - grade lowered accordingly
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed group spans a wide severity gradient. DRG 684 (no CC/MCC): mild Stage 1, floor-level, LOS ~2-3d. DRG 683 (CC): Stage 2 or an AKI-attributable complication (e.g., hyperkalemia) or comorbidity, LOS ~4-5d. DRG 682 (MCC): Stage 3/dialysis-requiring AKI, ATN (N17.0), or sepsis-associated AKI needing ICU/CRRT, LOS ~6-8d with markedly higher mortality. Documentation of ATN vs unspecified AKI and of dialysis status is the key severity/coding lever. Clinically, AKI is one of the most common CC/MCC secondary diagnoses elevating DRG weight across sepsis, HF, and other admissions
pathway_pearls: 1) Nail the cause in the first 24h - bladder scan + renal US + urine microscopy + FENa; immediately relieve obstruction and restore perfusion (euvolemia, MAP >=65). 2) Run the KDIGO bundle: aggressive nephrotoxin stewardship + pharmacy renal dosing + balanced crystalloids avoiding overload - diuretics, dopamine, and fenoldopam do NOT protect kidneys. 3) Do not rush dialysis - absent an emergent AEIOU indication, watchful waiting is non-inferior (STARRT-AKI/AKIKI) and spares line/access complications. 4) Treat AKI as a chronic-disease sentinel event: guarantee AKD follow-up labs within 7-14 days plus nephrology within weeks, restart RAASi/SGLT2i thoughtfully, and document stage/ATN for severity
