admission_criteria: Biochemical diagnosis drives admission (not glucose alone). DKA (2024 consensus): glucose >=200 mg/dL OR known diabetes, PLUS ketosis (beta-hydroxybutyrate >=3.0 mmol/L or ketonuria >=2+), PLUS metabolic acidosis (venous pH <7.3 or HCO3 <18 mmol/L). HHS: glucose >=600 mg/dL, effective osmolality >300 (or total >320) mOsm/kg, minimal ketones (BHB <3.0), pH >=7.3/HCO3 >=15, with altered mental status. Admit inpatient for: any HHS; moderate/severe DKA; hemodynamic instability; K <3.3 or >5.5; depressed sensorium; serious precipitant needing inpatient care (sepsis, ACS, stroke, pancreatitis, pregnancy); inability to self-manage; recurrent DKA. Mild DKA (pH >7.25, HCO3 >=15, alert, tolerating PO, reliable follow-up) may be managed in ED-observation/short-stay on a nurse-driven SC insulin protocol; ED discharge only after anion-gap closure and ketone/BHB resolution.
risk_stratification: 2024 consensus DKA severity tiers drive disposition and insulin route: MILD (pH >7.25, HCO3 >=15, BHB <=6, alert) -> floor/SC protocol; MODERATE (pH 7.0-7.25, HCO3 10-<15, alert/drowsy) -> floor/step-down; SEVERE (pH <7.0, HCO3 <10, BHB >6, stupor/coma) -> ICU/step-down + IV insulin. Effective osmolality [2xNa + glucose/18] >320 mOsm/kg and/or GCS <12 predict higher mortality -> ICU. qSOFA >=2 or NEWS2/MEWS escalation flags precipitant sepsis. APACHE II stratifies ICU HHS (mortality 5-20% vs DKA <1%). Serial anion gap and BHB track resolution; presenting K and corrected Na drive repletion urgency. Glucose level does NOT gauge severity (euglycemic DKA exists).
initial_workup_labs: STAT: POC glucose, venous blood gas (pH, HCO3), point-of-care or serum beta-hydroxybutyrate, BMP/CMP (anion gap, K, corrected Na [+1.6 per 100 mg/dL glucose >100], BUN/Cr, calculated effective osmolality). CBC (leukocytosis is common WITHOUT infection). Phosphate and magnesium. HbA1c if none within 90 days (distinguishes acute vs chronic, guides discharge regimen). Lactate. Urinalysis/urine ketones. Precipitant workup: ECG + troponin (silent MI), lipase (if abdominal pain), beta-hCG, blood/urine cultures, procalcitonin if infection suspected. REPEAT BMP + venous gas + BHB every 2-4 h to trend anion-gap/BHB closure and potassium; K guides insulin timing.
initial_imaging_dx: DKA/HHS are BIOCHEMICAL diagnoses - no imaging required to confirm. ECG immediately (hyper-/hypokalemia changes; occult ischemia as precipitant). CXR if infection, aspiration, or hypoxia suspected. CT head ONLY for focal neurologic deficit or unexplained/persistent altered mental status (evaluate stroke or cerebral edema - the latter rare in adults). Directed imaging for precipitant: RUQ ultrasound or CT abdomen/pelvis if pancreatitis/cholecystitis/intra-abdominal source suspected. Point-of-care ultrasound for volume/cardiac assessment in shock or HHS with cardiac comorbidity.
time_critical_interventions: Hour 0-1: isotonic IV crystalloid 500-1000 mL/h for first 2-4 h (15-20 mL/kg in first hour if hypovolemic). CHECK POTASSIUM before starting insulin. Begin weight-based insulin within ~1 h of fluids once K >=3.3-3.5: IV 0.1 U/kg/h for severe DKA; 0.05 U/kg/h for HHS. If K <3.5, HOLD insulin and replete first. Target glucose fall 50-75 mg/dL/h (~10%/h); add D5-D10 when glucose <250 mg/dL and reduce insulin to 0.05 U/kg/h. In HHS avoid effective-osmolality drop >3 mOsm/kg/h and glucose fall too rapid (cerebral edema risk). Recheck electrolytes/venous gas/BHB q2-4h. Antibiotics <=1 h if septic shock precipitant; treat the trigger in parallel.
initial_management: FLUIDS: 0.9% saline OR balanced crystalloid (LR/Plasma-Lyte - may reduce hyperchloremic acidosis and possibly speed resolution; both endorsed 2024) at 500-1000 mL/h initially, then switch to 0.45% NaCl if corrected Na normal/high. INSULIN: regular IV infusion 0.1 U/kg/h (severe DKA) or SC rapid-acting analog (lispro/aspart) 0.1 U/kg q1h or 0.2 U/kg q2h for mild-moderate DKA on the floor; HHS 0.05 U/kg/h; optional 0.1 U/kg IV bolus. Add 5-10% dextrose when glucose <250 mg/dL to keep insulin running until gap/BHB clears. POTASSIUM: KCl 10-20 mmol/h and hold insulin if K <3.5; 10-20 mmol/L in fluids if K 3.5-5.0 (target 4-5); none if >5.0 (recheck q2h). BICARBONATE only if pH <7.0 (isotonic, controversial, no proven benefit). Replete phosphate/Mg if severe/symptomatic. Continue home basal insulin if already on it. HOLD SGLT2 inhibitors. Source control of precipitant.
day1_milestones: Confirm diagnosis and 2024-consensus severity tier; identify and treat precipitant. Achieve volume resuscitation and hemodynamic stability. Potassium in target 4-5 mmol/L. Start IV insulin infusion (severe/HHS) or nurse-driven SC protocol (mild-moderate). Hourly-to-q2h glucose, q2-4h anion gap/BHB/electrolytes; demonstrate DOWNWARD anion-gap/BHB trend. Add dextrose once glucose <250. Initiate VTE prophylaxis (especially HHS). Target glucose 140-180 mg/dL (ICU) once stabilizing. Consult endocrine/inpatient glycemic team (new-onset, recurrent, pump/CGM, brittle). Hold nephrotoxins/SGLT2i; flag insulin-access/adherence barriers on day 1.
day2_milestones: Achieve resolution: DKA = BHB <0.6 mmol/L AND (venous pH >=7.3 OR HCO3 >=18) with anion gap closed; HHS = effective osmolality <300 mOsm/kg, glucose <250, urine output >0.5 mL/kg/h, cognition normalized. TRANSITION off IV insulin by overlapping SC basal insulin 1-2 h (ADA hospital chapter: 2-4 h) BEFORE stopping the drip - never stop naked (rebound DKA). Start basal-bolus regimen (weight-based ~0.5-0.6 U/kg/day, or resume/adjust home regimen; avoid sliding-scale-only). Advance diet to consistent-carbohydrate as nausea/vomiting resolve. Remove unnecessary IV lines/urinary catheter. Begin diabetes self-management education and dietitian consult. Reconcile outpatient medications.
day3plus_milestones: Sustained euglycemia on SC regimen without ketoacidosis recurrence; tolerating diet; precipitant treated/resolving. Complete structured diabetes self-management education with teach-back (insulin technique, glucose/ketone monitoring, hypoglycemia treatment, sick-day rules - 'never stop insulin'). Secure prescriptions AND physical supplies BEFORE discharge (insulin/pens, glucometer or CGM, ketone strips, needles). Arrange follow-up <=2-4 weeks (endocrine/PCP; sooner for new T1DM or recurrent DKA). Address psychosocial/financial barriers (insulin cost/access) especially in recurrent DKA. Finalize transitions-of-care handoff. Most uncomplicated DKA is discharge-ready by 48-72 h.
consults_multidisciplinary: Endocrinology / inpatient diabetes glycemic-management team EARLY (new-onset T1DM, recurrent DKA, insulin pump/CGM, brittle or steroid-induced control). Certified diabetes care & education specialist for DSME before discharge. Clinical pharmacy (insulin titration, med reconciliation, cost/access programs). Registered dietitian (carbohydrate counting, meal planning). Care management/social work (insulin affordability, follow-up logistics, housing, transportation). Behavioral health/psychiatry if intentional insulin omission, eating disorder, depression, or substance use. Critical care for severe DKA/HHS. Precipitant-specific: infectious disease, cardiology (ACS), OB (pregnancy), nephrology (AKI/ESRD), surgery (acute abdomen).
monitoring_level: ICU/step-down: severe DKA (pH <7.0, HCO3 <10), depressed sensorium/GCS <12, hemodynamic instability, K <3.3 or >5.5, effective osmolality >320 or HHS, comorbid critical illness (sepsis/ACS), or need for hourly IV insulin titration exceeding floor nurse-to-patient ratios. Telemetry for significant K derangement or cardiac precipitant. Floor/step-down: mild-moderate DKA on nurse-driven SC or low-rate IV protocol where q1-2h glucose checks are feasible. Escalation triggers: refractory or worsening acidosis / non-closing anion gap, persistent hypokalemia, new or worsening altered mental status (headache + GCS drop = cerebral edema), hypoxia/respiratory failure, hemodynamic decline, arrhythmia.
nutrition_mobility_vte: NUTRITION: NPO or clear liquids until nausea/vomiting resolve and anion gap closing, then advance to consistent-carbohydrate diet with prandial insulin timed to intake. MOBILITY: early mobilization/PT once hemodynamically stable (counters deconditioning and VTE, especially HHS). VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH e.g. enoxaparin, or UFH) for HHS (markedly prothrombotic/hyperosmolar) and most admitted DKA per risk assessment; mechanical prophylaxis if anticoagulation contraindicated. GLUCOSE CONTROL: 140-180 mg/dL critically ill, 100-180 non-critical; avoid hypoglycemia <70 (treat <54 aggressively). LINES/CATHETERS: remove central/arterial lines and Foley as soon as not required. Replete K, phosphate, magnesium.
discharge_criteria: Crisis resolved (DKA: anion gap closed, BHB <0.6 mmol/L, venous pH >=7.3 or HCO3 >=18, tolerating oral intake; HHS: osmolality and glucose normalized, mental status at baseline). Off IV insulin and stable >=12-24 h on subcutaneous regimen across >=1 meal cycle. Electrolytes (K, Mg, phosphate) normalized. Precipitant treated or on a stable outpatient course. Patient/caregiver can demonstrate insulin administration, glucose and ketone self-monitoring, hypoglycemia treatment, and sick-day rules (teach-back). Confirmed possession of insulin, supplies, and a scheduled follow-up appointment.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home (SNF/rehab if functional decline or HHS-related deconditioning). Medication reconciliation with an explicit insulin regimen (basal-bolus or resumed/adjusted home therapy) and a plan for SGLT2i (hold during acute-illness/DKA-risk window). GUARANTEE affordability and physical possession at discharge: insulin, pens/syringes, glucometer or CGM, ketone (BHB) strips - insulin cost is a leading recurrent-DKA driver. Structured DSME with written sick-day action plan ('never stop insulin; check ketones when ill/hyperglycemic'). Schedule endocrine/PCP follow-up within 2-4 weeks (sooner for new-onset T1DM or recurrent DKA). Warm handoff/communication to outpatient team; arrange CGM where eligible; social work for cost/access and transitional-care follow-up call.
expected_los: DKA mean LOS approximately 2.5-3.5 days. MS-DRG GMLOS (CMS, approximate): 637 Diabetes with MCC ~3.5-3.9 days; 638 with CC ~2.8-3.0 days; 639 without CC/MCC ~2.1-2.4 days. HHS runs longer (older, more comorbid, higher complication burden).
target_los: Uncomplicated mild-moderate DKA: 1.5-2 days under the ideal pathway (selected mild cases resolvable in ED-observation/short-stay <24 h on an SC insulin protocol, avoiding ICU). HHS or MCC-tier: 3-4 days. Overall optimized target ~2 days for DKA without a major precipitant or complication.
quality_metrics: CMS Hospital Harm eCQMs: Severe Hyperglycemia (proportion of hospital-days with glucose >300 mg/dL) and Severe Hypoglycemia (glucose <40 mg/dL with a proximate antihyperglycemic). Inpatient hypoglycemia rate (<54 and <40 mg/dL). Glucose time-in-range 70-180. Time to anion-gap/BHB resolution. Appropriate IV-to-SC transition with basal overlap (avoid correction-insulin-only/sliding-scale). A1C measured this admission. DSME completion. 30-day all-cause and DKA-specific readmission. Mortality (DKA <1%; HHS 5-20%). Follow-up scheduled within 30 days.
common_complications: Hypokalemia - the most dangerous, as insulin/fluids shift K intracellularly (check K before insulin, q2-4h thereafter, replete, hold insulin if <3.5). Hypoglycemia (add dextrose at glucose <250, titrate insulin to target). Hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis from large-volume normal saline (balanced crystalloids mitigate). Rebound ketoacidosis from premature IV-insulin discontinuation (overlap SC basal 1-2 h). Cerebral edema (rare in adults - avoid osmolality drop >3 mOsm/kg/h and overly rapid glucose fall; monitor for headache/GCS decline). Volume overload/pulmonary edema (elderly, CKD, HF). Venous thromboembolism (especially HHS - prophylax). Hypophosphatemia and hypomagnesemia. AKI.
readmission_drivers: Insulin nonadherence/omission and insulin cost or access - the dominant driver of recurrent DKA in young adults with type 1 diabetes. Absent or inadequate DSME and sick-day education. Poor or missed outpatient follow-up. Psychosocial factors: depression, eating disorders, substance use, housing instability. New/undiagnosed diabetes without established care. Missed SGLT2i-associated euglycemic DKA. LEVERS: guarantee insulin supply and affordability at discharge, provide CGM and ketone meter, deliver structured teach-back education with a written sick-day plan, book follow-up <=2-4 weeks, integrate behavioral health, place a transitional-care call, and address social determinants of health.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: ADA/EASD/JBDS/AACE/DTS 2024 'Hyperglycemic Crises in Adults With Diabetes: A Consensus Report' (Umpierrez et al.), the current multi-society standard superseding the 2009 ADA statement. SUPPORTING: American Diabetes Association, Standards of Care in Diabetes-2025 (and -2026), Section 16 'Diabetes Care in the Hospital' and Section 6 'Glycemic Goals, Hypoglycemia, and Hyperglycemic Crises.'
key_citations: Umpierrez GE, Davis GM, ElSayed NA, ... Dhatariya KK. Hyperglycemic Crises in Adults With Diabetes: A Consensus Report. Diabetes Care 2024;47(8):1257-1275 (PMID 39052901; DOI 10.2337/dci24-0032); parallel Diabetologia 2024;67(8):1455-1479 (PMID 38907161; DOI 10.1007/s00125-024-06183-8). ADA. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes-2025. Diabetes Care 2025;48(Suppl 1):S321. Rodriguez Alvarez P, et al. Hyperglycemic crises in adults: a look at the 2024 consensus report. Cleve Clin J Med 2025;92(3):152-158 (PMID 40032308; DOI 10.3949/ccjm.92a.24089). Defante MLR, et al. Subcutaneous rapid-acting insulin analogues in mild-to-moderate DKA: meta-analysis of 8 RCTs. J Diabetes Complications 2024;38(12):108882 (PMID 39437698; DOI 10.1016/j.jdiacomp.2024.108882). Ersoz HO, et al. SC lispro vs IV regular insulin in mild-moderate DKA. Int J Clin Pract 2006;60(4):429-433 (PMID 16620355). Yan JW, et al. BRISK-ED balanced crystalloids vs saline pilot RCT. Emerg Med J 2024;41(2):103-111 (PMID 38050056). Citations retrieved and verified via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong overall - anchored to a current multi-society consensus guideline (ADA/EASD/JBDS/AACE/DTS 2024) plus ADA Standards of Care (Grade A-C recommendations). Core fluid/insulin/potassium and glycemic-target principles are well established. MODERATE for several 2024 revisions: the standardized BHB-based diagnostic/resolution thresholds and severity tiers (consensus-driven), SC rapid-acting insulin for mild-moderate DKA (RCTs + meta-analysis, modest sample sizes), and balanced crystalloids over saline (biologically plausible, small/pilot RCTs, larger multicenter trials pending). WEAK/expert-opinion: bicarbonate therapy (reserved for pH <7.0, no outcome benefit). Prevention/readmission levers are largely observational.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The collapsed DRG group maps clinical severity onto coding tiers. 639 (no CC/MCC) approximates mild-moderate DKA managed on the floor with an SC protocol, LOS ~2 days. 638 (CC) adds complications such as moderate electrolyte disturbance, stage 1 AKI, or uncomplicated infection - longer titration and monitoring, LOS ~3 days. 637 (MCC) captures severe DKA and HHS with sepsis, acute respiratory/renal failure, ACS, or coma - ICU/step-down, higher mortality (HHS 5-20% vs DKA <1%), LOS 4-6+ days. CLINICAL severity (pH, HCO3, BHB, mental status, effective osmolality) and the precipitant - NOT the glucose value - drive ICU need, complication rate, and LOS; HHS skews older, more comorbid, and toward the MCC tier.
pathway_pearls: 1) Diagnose and dose by ketones/acidosis, not glucose - recognize euglycemic DKA (SGLT2 inhibitors, pregnancy, low intake, alcohol) and HOLD SGLT2 inhibitors; glucose can be near-normal in true DKA. 2) Potassium first: check K before every insulin start and hold insulin if K <3.5 while repleting - hypokalemia, not hyperglycemia, is the lethal error. 3) Never stop the drip naked - overlap subcutaneous basal insulin 1-2 h before ending the IV infusion to prevent rebound ketoacidosis, and add dextrose once glucose <250 so insulin keeps running until the anion gap/BHB clears. 4) Discharge is the highest-yield prevention step - guarantee insulin affordability/supply, a ketone meter, sick-day rules, and follow-up within 2-4 weeks to break the recurrent-DKA readmission cycle; use SC-insulin protocols for mild-moderate DKA to keep them off the ICU.
verification_notes: Verified via PubMed (attribution: data retrieved from PubMed) and web sources; no high-stakes errors found, so corrections is empty. CITATIONS: All 6 key_citations independently confirmed real and correctly attributed via PubMed metadata lookup — none fabricated or misattributed. Umpierrez et al. consensus, Diabetes Care 2024;47(8):1257-1275, PMID 39052901, DOI 10.2337/dci24-0032 (https://doi.org/10.2337/dci24-0032); parallel Diabetologia 2024;67(8):1455-1479, PMID 38907161, DOI 10.1007/s00125-024-06183-8; Rodriguez Alvarez, Cleve Clin J Med 2025;92(3):152-158, PMID 40032308, DOI 10.3949/ccjm.92a.24089; Defante meta-analysis (8 RCTs, 415 patients — matches the row) J Diabetes Complications 2024;38(12):108882, PMID 39437698; Ersoz Int J Clin Pract 2006;60(4):429-33, PMID 16620355; Yan BRISK-ED pilot RCT Emerg Med J 2024;41(2):103-111, PMID 38050056. Volumes, issues, pages, authors, and DOIs all match the row exactly. GUIDELINE SOURCE: ADA/EASD/JBDS/AACE/DTS 2024 consensus is the correct, current multi-society standard superseding the 2009 ADA statement; ADA Standards of Care 2025/2026 appropriately cited. CLINICAL NUMERICS: Confirmed against the primary consensus report (PMC11272983) and the CCJM 2024 summary — DKA diagnostic triad (glucose >=200/known DM; BHB >=3.0 or ketonuria 2+; pH <7.3/HCO3 <18), HHS criteria (glucose >=600; effective osm >300 or total >320; BHB <3.0; pH >=7.3/HCO3 >=15), the three severity tiers (mild pH>7.25/HCO3>=15/BHB<=6; moderate pH 7.0-7.25/HCO3 10-<15; severe pH<7.0/HCO3<10/BHB>6), DKA resolution (BHB <0.6 AND pH>=7.3 OR HCO3>=18), HHS resolution (osm<300, glucose<250, UOP>0.5 mL/kg/h, cognition improved), insulin dosing (0.1 U/kg/h severe DKA, 0.05 U/kg/h HHS, SC rapid-acting analog for mild-moderate), fluids 500-1000 mL/h x2-4h, potassium hold <3.5, dextrose at glucose <250 — ALL MATCH the 2024 consensus. QUALITY METRICS: CMS Hospital Harm eCQMs verified — Severe Hypoglycemia = glucose <40 mg/dL with a hypoglycemic medication administered within 24h before (p4qm.org #3503e; eCQI CMS816v4); Severe Hyperglycemia = hospital-days with glucose >300 mg/dL (eCQI CMS871). Both real and correctly described. NO fabricated scores/citations, no wrong drug classes, no incorrect thresholds. Only four minor guideline-nuance caveats (see issues_found), none rising to a high-stakes correction. Note: no door-to-balloon/tPA/door-to-needle targets apply to this metabolic condition — correctly absent from the row. Confidence High.; ISSUES: MINOR (HHS diagnostic definition, admission_criteria/risk_stratification): the row lists 'with altered mental status' as part of the HHS definition. The 2024 consensus (Umpierrez et al., Diabetes Care 2024) explicitly REMOVED altered mental status as a required diagnostic criterion for HHS (it is characteristic but no longer part of the biochemical definition). Carryover from the 2009 statement; does not change management, so not corrected.; MINOR (DKA resolution, day2_milestones/discharge_criteria): the row appends 'with anion gap closed' to the resolution definition. The 2024 consensus explicitly recommends AGAINST using the anion gap as a resolution criterion (hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis confounds it) and defines resolution as BHB <0.6 mmol/L AND (venous pH >=7.3 OR HCO3 >=18) — which the row states correctly. The AG add-on is a slight deviation from the cited standard but reflects common bedside practice.; MINOR (potassium threshold, time_critical_interventions): the row hedges 'K >=3.3-3.5' before starting insulin, blending the 2009 threshold (3.3) with the current 2024 consensus threshold (3.5). Elsewhere it correctly uses 'hold insulin if K <3.5,' matching the current standard. Not an error, just a blended range.; MINOR (HHS osmolality drop limit, time_critical_interventions/initial_management): the row states 'avoid effective-osmolality drop >3 mOsm/kg/h.' The 2024 consensus gives a range of 3.0-8.0 mOsm/kg/h; the row uses the conservative lower bound, which is acceptable and safe.; NOTE (expected_los): MS-DRG 637/638/639 GMLOS figures (~3.5-3.9 / ~2.8-3.0 / ~2.1-2.4 days) are labeled 'approximate' and are plausible against national benchmarks, but were sanity-checked for plausibility rather than line-verified against the current CMS FY GMLOS table.
scope_and_volume_notes: HCUP #5 principal dx (diabetes with complication, 0.68M, 2018). Includes diabetic ketoacidosis and hyperosmolar states; severe DKA -> Critical Care.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 637 — Diabetes with MCC; 638 — Diabetes with CC; 639 — Diabetes without CC/MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 6/6 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Original independent adversarial high-stakes review completed; current pass revalidated citation resolvability and FY2026 coding/taxonomy.
data_quality_notes: Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk. Current DRG overlap with pathway rank(s): 93.

admission_criteria: Admit per GOLD hospitalization indications: marked increase in dyspnea intensity (new/worsening resting breathlessness), tachypnea RR>=24-30, SpO2<90% or PaO2<60 mmHg on room air, new or worsening hypercapnia/acidosis (PaCO2>45 mmHg, pH<7.35), new cyanosis or peripheral edema, altered mental status (confusion/lethargy), failure to respond to initial ED bronchodilator+steroid therapy, hemodynamic instability, or serious comorbidity (pneumonia, HF, arrhythmia, PE); insufficient home support. ED-discharge/observation appropriate if rapid symptom response, SpO2>=90% at baseline, no acidosis, ambulatory, adequate support. ICU/HDU if acute respiratory failure with pH<7.25 and/or deterioration on NIV, need for intubation, refractory hypoxemia (PaO2<40 mmHg), reduced consciousness, or hemodynamic instability.
risk_stratification: DECAF score (Dyspnea eMRCD 5a/5b; Eosinopenia <0.05x10^9/L; Consolidation on CXR; Acidemia pH<7.30; Atrial Fibrillation; range 0-6): 0-1 low risk (in-hospital mortality ~1-2%) -> early supported discharge/hospital-at-home candidate; 2 intermediate; 3-6 high risk (mortality ~15-50%) -> senior review, escalation planning, and early palliative/ceiling-of-care discussion. Rome 2021 severity (mild/moderate/severe by dyspnea VAS, RR, HR, SpO2, CRP, and ABG pH/PaCO2). CURB-65 or PSI if coexisting pneumonia. Wells+PERC if PE suspected (PE found in ~16% of unexplained AECOPD). Assess blood eosinophils and prior exacerbation frequency to guide steroid and maintenance therapy.
initial_workup_labs: ABG at presentation and after every FiO2 change (pH, PaCO2, PaO2, HCO3/base excess) - defines acute-on-chronic respiratory failure and directs NIV; CBC with differential (eosinophil count for DECAF and steroid guidance; WBC/left shift); BMP (electrolytes, renal, bicarbonate); CRP and/or procalcitonin (bacterial infection/antibiotic stewardship); BNP/NT-proBNP (co-existing HF); high-sensitivity troponin (concomitant myocardial injury/type-2 MI - common and prognostic); venous/arterial lactate if unwell; blood cultures if febrile/septic; sputum Gram stain and culture if frequent exacerbations, bronchiectasis/prior Pseudomonas, mechanical ventilation, or antibiotic failure; respiratory viral PCR (influenza, SARS-CoV-2, RSV); glucose (steroid hyperglycemia); theophylline level if applicable.
initial_imaging_dx: Chest radiograph on admission (detects pneumonia, pneumothorax, effusion, pulmonary edema; alters management in ~16-21%); 12-lead ECG (arrhythmia esp. AF, RV strain, ischemia/type-2 MI); CT pulmonary angiography if PE suspected (elevated D-dimer, unexplained/refractory exacerbation - PE prevalence ~16%); bedside or formal echocardiography if cor pulmonale, RV dysfunction, or HF suspected. Do NOT perform diagnostic spirometry during the acute exacerbation (unreliable); confirm/stage COPD with post-bronchodilator spirometry 4-6 weeks after recovery if not already documented.
time_critical_interventions: Controlled/titrated O2 immediately to target SpO2 88-92% (venturi mask or low-flow nasal cannula) - avoid hyperoxia which worsens hypercapnia and increases mortality (Austin RCT, RR 0.42); recheck ABG within 30-60 min and after each FiO2 change. Short-acting bronchodilators within the first hour. Systemic corticosteroid and, if indicated, antibiotic within the first few hours. In acute hypercapnic respiratory failure (pH<=7.35 AND PaCO2>=45 mmHg) start NIV/BiPAP within 60 min - first-line therapy reducing mortality (RR 0.54) and intubation (RR 0.36); reassess ABG at 1-2h and, if no improvement or pH<7.25/deteriorating, escalate to invasive ventilation without delay.
initial_management: Bronchodilators: short-acting beta2-agonist (albuterol/salbutamol) +/- short-acting muscarinic antagonist (ipratropium) via MDI+spacer or nebulizer, scheduled plus PRN. Systemic corticosteroid: prednisone 40 mg PO daily x5 days (REDUCE - 5d noninferior to 14d); IV methylprednisolone 40-60 mg if unable to take PO/critically ill; consider blood-eosinophil-guided steroid limitation (CORTICO-COP) in selected patients. Antibiotics x5 days if >=2 Anthonisen cardinal symptoms with increased sputum purulence, or requiring NIV/invasive ventilation: amoxicillin-clavulanate, macrolide (azithromycin), or doxycycline; add antipseudomonal cover (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or antipseudomonal fluoroquinolone) if risk factors (severe airflow limitation, bronchiectasis, prior Pseudomonas, frequent courses). Controlled O2 to SpO2 88-92%; NIV for hypercapnic acidosis; treat trigger and comorbid HF/arrhythmia/PE. Avoid sedatives, routine methylxanthines, and aggressive IV fluids.
day1_milestones: Identify/confirm trigger (viral or bacterial infection, PE, HF decompensation, non-adherence, air pollution); start controlled O2 to 88-92%, scheduled short-acting bronchodilators, day-1 systemic corticosteroid, and antibiotics if indicated; initiate NIV if pH<=7.35/PaCO2>=45; calculate DECAF and set monitoring level and ceiling of care; screen for cardiac injury (troponin/ECG) and VTE; begin VTE pharmacoprophylaxis; document smoking status and offer cessation pharmacotherapy; respiratory-therapy-led inhaler assessment; dietitian screen if low BMI.
day2_milestones: Reassess ABG/oxygenation and wean FiO2 toward baseline; wean/discontinue NIV as acidosis resolves (pH normalizing, PaCO2 improving); transition nebulizers to MDI+spacer with observed inhaler-technique teaching; review sputum/blood cultures and procalcitonin to narrow or stop antibiotics; optimize maintenance inhaled therapy (step up to LABA+LAMA; add ICS if blood eosinophils>=300 cells/uL or >=2 exacerbations/yr); early mobilization with PT; reinforce smoking cessation; begin structured discharge planning and follow-up scheduling.
day3plus_milestones: Confirm clinical stability >=24h: off NIV, SpO2 88-92% on baseline O2 or room air, SABA needed no more often than q4h, able to eat/sleep/ambulate to baseline; complete 5-day steroid and antibiotic courses (may finish as outpatient); finalize maintenance inhaler regimen and assess home O2 need (resting PaO2<=55 mmHg or SpO2<=88%, retested when stable); verify inhaler-technique competency; refer to pulmonary rehabilitation within 4 weeks (reduces readmissions, pooled OR ~0.44 [95% CI 0.21-0.91], moderate-quality evidence per current Cochrane review); complete vaccinations; arrange 7-day (48-72h for high-risk) follow-up; provide written COPD action plan; discharge low-DECAF patients early via supported-discharge/hospital-at-home.
consults_multidisciplinary: Respiratory therapy day 0 (bronchodilator delivery, NIV setup, inhaler teaching); Pulmonology for severe/frequent exacerbations, NIV, diagnostic uncertainty, or home-O2/advanced-therapy decisions; Case management/social work early for disposition, DME/home O2, and transitions of care; Physical therapy/OT for deconditioning and functional assessment; Clinical pharmacy for inhaler technique, medication reconciliation, and steroid/antibiotic stewardship; Dietitian if malnutrition/low BMI; Smoking-cessation service; Palliative care for advanced COPD, high DECAF, recurrent admissions, or goals-of-care/ceiling decisions; Cardiology if concurrent arrhythmia, HF, or myocardial injury.
monitoring_level: Floor with continuous pulse oximetry for uncomplicated exacerbations responding to therapy; telemetry if arrhythmia (AF), cardiac comorbidity, or troponin elevation; step-down/HDU or ICU for NIV per institutional capability; ICU for invasive ventilation, hemodynamic instability, pH<7.25 or worsening acidosis/consciousness despite NIV, refractory hypoxemia (PaO2<40 mmHg), or respiratory/cardiac arrest. Escalation triggers: rising PaCO2 or falling pH on NIV at 1-2h, RR>30, declining GCS, SpO2<88% despite titrated O2, or hemodynamic compromise.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet as tolerated; screen and treat malnutrition (low BMI/weight loss is an adverse prognostic marker) with oral nutrition support; early mobilization and out-of-bed activity to prevent deconditioning and delirium; VTE pharmacoprophylaxis (LMWH e.g. enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily, or UFH) unless contraindicated - hospitalized AECOPD is high VTE risk; monitor and treat steroid-induced hyperglycemia (target glucose <180 mg/dL with basal/correction insulin); remove unnecessary IV lines and urinary catheters promptly; avoid sedative-hypnotics and uncontrolled high-flow oxygen.
discharge_criteria: GOLD readiness: inhaled short-acting bronchodilator required no more frequently than every 4h; clinically and ABG-stable for 12-24h; ambulating, eating, and sleeping without significant dyspnea; off NIV with acid-base normalized; able to use maintenance inhalers correctly (technique verified); home O2 need assessed if hypoxemic; follow-up, medications, and home care arranged; patient/caregiver understands the regimen and action plan and is confident to manage at home.
discharge_planning: Disposition home (early supported discharge/hospital-at-home for DECAF 0-1) vs. skilled facility for deconditioned/complex patients; full medication reconciliation with escalation of maintenance therapy (LABA+LAMA +/- ICS by eosinophils/exacerbation history; consider roflumilast or prophylactic azithromycin in frequent exacerbators; ensure short steroid/antibiotic course completion); verify and document inhaler technique; smoking-cessation pharmacotherapy plus counseling; vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal, COVID-19, RSV, Tdap); written personalized COPD action plan with rescue-pack guidance; schedule follow-up within 7 days (48-72h if high-risk) plus pulmonary-rehabilitation referral within 4 weeks; arrange home O2/DME and community respiratory nursing; send discharge summary to PCP.
expected_los: Typical ~3.5-4.5 days for hospitalized AECOPD; Medicare MS-DRG GMLOS approx: 190 (w/MCC) ~4.2-4.5 days, 191 (w/CC) ~3.2-3.4 days, 192 (w/o CC/MCC) ~2.7-2.9 days.
target_los: ~2.5-3 days under the optimized pathway (controlled O2, early NIV, 5-day steroid, prompt MDI transition, early mobilization, bundled discharge); DECAF 0-1 patients <=1-2 days via early supported discharge/hospital-at-home.
quality_metrics: CMS Hospital Readmissions Reduction Program COPD 30-day all-cause readmission (READM-30-COPD; penalized) and CMS COPD 30-day mortality (MORT-30-COPD); process measures: controlled O2 with target SpO2 88-92%, appropriate 5-day systemic corticosteroid and guideline-concordant antibiotic use, timely NIV in hypercapnic acidosis, smoking-cessation counseling, pulmonary-rehabilitation referral, vaccination status, inhaler-technique documentation, and spirometric confirmation of COPD; NIV/intubation rates; LOS and observed-to-expected mortality. Note: legacy Joint Commission COPD core measures retired - claims-based readmission/mortality measures now dominate.
common_complications: Progression to acute hypercapnic respiratory failure needing NIV/intubation (mitigated by controlled O2 88-92% and early NIV); hospital-acquired or aspiration pneumonia; VTE/PE (prophylaxis plus low threshold for CTPA); steroid-induced hyperglycemia and delirium (short 5-day course, glucose control); cardiac - AF, HF decompensation, type-2 MI (telemetry, troponin/ECG); pneumothorax (esp. with bullae or NIV); Clostridioides difficile from antibiotics (stewardship, 5-day courses); deconditioning and falls (early mobilization, PT).
readmission_drivers: Premature discharge/incomplete recovery; poor inhaler technique and medication non-adherence; failure to refer to pulmonary rehabilitation; continued smoking; no or late follow-up; undertreated comorbidities (HF, anxiety/depression, OSA); absent written action plan; unaddressed home O2 need; social determinants (housing, air quality, access). Prevention levers: standardized discharge bundle, verified inhaler technique, early (7-day/48-72h) follow-up, pulmonary-rehab referral, smoking cessation, optimized maintenance inhalers, vaccination, and palliative care for advanced disease.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: GOLD - Global Initiative for Chronic Obstructive Lung Disease, 2026 Global Strategy Report (updated annually; 2025 and 2026 editions current). Supporting: ATS/ERS guidance on management of COPD exacerbations; ERS/ATS 2017 Clinical Practice Guidelines on Noninvasive Ventilation for Acute Respiratory Failure; NICE NG115 (COPD); Rome proposal 2021 (updated exacerbation severity definition/classification).

REDUCE: short-term vs conventional glucocorticoids in AECOPD, JAMA 2013;309:2223-31 (PMID 23695200; doi:10.1001/jama.2013.5023). Osadnik CR et al. NIV for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure in AECOPD, Cochrane 2017;7:CD004104 (PMID 28702957; doi:10.1002/14651858.CD004104.pub4). Austin MA et al. Titrated vs high-flow O2 prehospital, BMJ 2010;341:c5462 (PMID 20959284; doi:10.1136/bmj.c5462). Steer J et al. DECAF score, Thorax 2012;67:970-6 (PMID 22895999; doi:10.1136/thoraxjnl-2012-202103); Echevarria C et al. DECAF validation, Thorax 2016;71:133-40 (PMID 26769015; doi:10.1136/thoraxjnl-2015-207775). evidence_grade: Strong overall - Class I/Grade A society guideline (GOLD) supported by multiple RCTs and Cochrane meta-analyses: NIV in hypercapnic acidosis (high-quality), controlled/titrated oxygen (RCT), and 5-day systemic corticosteroids (RCT, noninferiority); antibiotics for purulent or ventilated exacerbations (moderate). Risk stratification (DECAF) externally validated. Eosinophil-guided steroid limitation and specific optimized LOS targets are moderate/emerging.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG splits on complication tier: 190 (MCC) - typically acute respiratory failure (J96.0-/J96.2-, incl. acute-on-chronic hypercapnic failure), often NIV/ICU, longest LOS (~4.2-4.5 d) and highest mortality; 191 (CC) - significant comorbidity/complication (e.g., some pneumonias, arrhythmia), intermediate LOS (~3.3 d); 192 (no CC/MCC) - uncomplicated, shortest LOS (~2.7 d). Higher tiers shift care to telemetry/step-down/ICU, NIV, and longer stays; accurate documentation of respiratory-failure type, cor pulmonale, and acute comorbidities is essential for severity capture and correct DRG assignment.
pathway_pearls: 1) Titrate O2 to SpO2 88-92% - never routine high-flow/hyperoxia (raises PaCO2 and mortality). 2) Start NIV early for pH<=7.35 with PaCO2>=45 mmHg - the single highest-yield intervention (mortality RR 0.54, intubation RR 0.36); escalate promptly if no improvement at 1-2h. 3) Use short courses - prednisone 40 mg x5 days, and antibiotics x5 days only when sputum is purulent or patient is ventilated - to avoid overtreatment. 4) The discharge bundle drives the readmission metric: verified inhaler technique, optimized maintenance inhalers, smoking cessation, vaccination, written action plan, 7-day follow-up, and pulmonary-rehab referral within 4 weeks.
verification_notes: Verified against PubMed (article metadata) and web sources; did not rely on memory. CITATIONS — all 6 key_citations are REAL and correctly attributed (PMID, DOI, journal, year, volume, pages verified): Leuppi REDUCE JAMA 2013;309:2223-31 (PMID 23695200); Osadnik NIV Cochrane 2017;7:CD004104 (PMID 28702957); Austin BMJ 2010;341:c5462 (PMID 20959284); Steer DECAF Thorax 2012;67:970-6 (PMID 22895999); Echevarria DECAF validation Thorax 2016;71:133-40 (PMID 26769015); Sivapalan CORTICO-COP Lancet Respir Med 2019;7:699-709 (PMID 31122894). No fabricated/misattributed PMIDs. TIME-CRITICAL — numeric targets match sources exactly: controlled O2 SpO2 88-92%; Austin titrated-O2 mortality RR 0.42 (0.20-0.89) confirmed; NIV indication pH<=7.35 AND PaCO2>=45 mmHg matches GOLD/Cochrane; NIV mortality RR 0.54 (0.38-0.76) and intubation RR 0.36 (0.28-0.46) match Osadnik Cochrane verbatim. RISK STRATIFICATION — DECAF name/components/thresholds (eMRCD 5a/5b, eosinopenia <0.05x10^9/L, consolidation, acidaemia pH<7.30, AF; range 0-6) correct per Steer/Echevarria; Rome 2021 severity classification is real (Celli et al, AJRCCM 2021;204:1251); PE ~16% in UNEXPLAINED AECOPD confirmed (Aleva et al, Chest 2017;151:544-554, 16.1% [95% CI 8.3-25.8], PMID 27522956). INITIAL MANAGEMENT — SABA+/-SAMA, prednisone 40 mg PO x5 days (REDUCE noninferiority) and antibiotic classes all guideline-concordant. QUALITY METRICS — READM-30-COPD (HRRP, penalized) and MORT-30-COPD are real CMS claims-based measures; TJC COPD core-measure retirement accurate. GUIDELINE — GOLD 2026 report is real and current (released Dec 2025). ONE ERROR corrected: outdated pulmonary-rehab readmission OR (0.22 -> 0.44). One minor unflagged item: DRG 190 GMLOS slightly high. Overall the row is clinically sound and well-sourced.; ISSUES: day3plus_milestones: pulmonary-rehabilitation readmission benefit stated as 'OR ~0.22' is outdated — the current Cochrane review (Puhan 2016, CD005305.pub4, PMID 27930803) reports pooled readmission OR 0.44 (95% CI 0.21-0.91) and notes newer trials show smaller effects. 0.22 reflects the superseded 2011 review. CORRECTED.; expected_los / severity_cc_mcc_notes (minor, not corrected): DRG 190 GMLOS given as '~4.2-4.5 days' is at the high end — recent CMS FY GMLOS for DRG 190 runs closer to ~3.9-4.2 days (4.5 approaches the arithmetic mean, not geometric). DRG 191 (~3.2-3.4) and 192 (~2.7-2.9) are accurate. Values are hedged as 'approx' and shift year-to-year/by data source, so left as-is; exact current figure could not be pulled (source paywalled/rate-limited).

admission_criteria: Admit ALL confirmed acute ischemic stroke (AIS) to an organized inpatient stroke unit (Class I — reduces death/dependency); ED discharge only for fully resolved TIA with reliable, expedited outpatient workup and ABCD2 <4; admit to observation/short-stay for low-risk TIA (ABCD2 0-3) needing same-admission vessel imaging; escalate to neuro-ICU/ICU for: any IV thrombolysis (mandatory 24h monitoring), post-endovascular thrombectomy (EVT), NIHSS >=15 or large-territory/malignant-edema risk, hemorrhagic transformation, fluctuating/declining exam, airway compromise, or BP requiring continuous IV titration; posterior-circulation/basilar occlusion always inpatient
risk_stratification: NIHSS (0-42): severity + treatment gate — NIHSS >=6 with LVO drives EVT candidacy, NIHSS >=10 required for basilar-artery EVT, higher NIHSS predicts worse outcome/higher ICH risk; ASPECTS (0-10 on non-contrast CT): >=6 favorable, ASPECTS 3-5 = large core now EVT-eligible if age <80 (SELECT2); baseline modified Rankin Scale (mRS) documents pre-stroke function (0-1 ideal for reperfusion but not absolute); ABCD2 for TIA (>=4 = high 90-day recurrence, admit); CHA2DS2-VASc drives anticoagulation decision if AF found; bedside dysphagia screen (fail -> strict NPO); TOAST classification guides secondary prevention
initial_workup_labs: STAT fingerstick glucose is the ONLY lab that must precede thrombolysis (treat if <60 mg/dL, exclude hypoglycemic stroke mimic); CBC/platelets, PT/INR/aPTT, BMP (electrolytes/creatinine), troponin, type & screen — but do NOT delay tPA awaiting coagulation results unless recent anticoagulant use or coagulopathy suspected; ECG on arrival (detect AF/MI); hs-troponin (concurrent MI); pregnancy test if applicable; secondary-prevention panel = fasting lipids/LDL, HbA1c; selective young/cryptogenic-stroke workup (hypercoagulable panel, toxicology)
initial_imaging_dx: Non-contrast CT head IMMEDIATELY (door-to-CT <=25 min, door-to-CT-interpretation <=45 min) to exclude hemorrhage/mimics before thrombolysis; CT angiography head+neck to detect large-vessel occlusion (LVO); CT perfusion OR MRI DWI/PWI (automated RAPID-type mismatch) mandatory to select extended-window (6-24h) EVT and 4.5-9h/wake-up thrombolysis candidates; continuous cardiac telemetry >=24h and 12-lead ECG for AF; transthoracic +/- transesophageal echo for cardioembolic source; carotid duplex/CTA/MRA for anterior-circulation stenosis
time_critical_interventions: Door-to-needle (IV thrombolysis) <=60 min, stretch goal <=45 min (Target: Stroke Phase III: >=85% treated within 60 min); IV thrombolysis standard window <=4.5h from last-known-well, extended to 4.5-9h and wake-up stroke with advanced-imaging mismatch (Class 2a); EVT <=6h for anterior LVO, extended to 6-24h with perfusion/clinical mismatch selection (DAWN/DEFUSE-3), large core ASPECTS 3-5 age <80 within 24h (SELECT2), and basilar occlusion <=24h with NIHSS >=10; door-to-device (arrival-to-first-pass) <=90 min direct-arriving / <=60 min transfer patients in >=50%
initial_management: Thrombolytics (Class I, co-equal): tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg single IV bolus (max 25 mg) OR alteplase 0.9 mg/kg (max 90 mg, 10% bolus then 60-min infusion); BP: lower to <185/110 before lytic and maintain <180/105 x24h after (IV labetalol, nicardipine or clevidipine), but do NOT intensively lower to <140 systolic (Class III harm) — permissive hypertension up to 220/120 if no lytic/EVT; antiplatelet: aspirin 160-325 mg within 24-48h (delay 24h post-tPA until repeat CT); DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel 300-600 mg load then 75 mg) for minor stroke NIHSS <=3 or high-risk TIA, start <=24h x21 days then single agent (CHANCE/POINT); isotonic IV fluids (avoid hypotonic/dextrose); glucose target 140-180 mg/dL; post-tPA symptomatic ICH reversal = cryoprecipitate + tranexamic/aminocaproic acid
day1_milestones: Admit stroke unit/neuro-ICU; STRICT NPO until nurse-administered dysphagia screen passed (before any oral meds/food); serial NIHSS/neuro checks (q15min x2h then q30-60min post-reperfusion); post-tPA/EVT BP protocol x24h; 24h repeat non-contrast CT before starting any antithrombotic when lytic/EVT given; initiate aspirin (or DAPT for minor stroke) once 24h CT clears hemorrhage (or immediately if no lytic); normothermia and glucose 140-180; VTE prophylaxis with intermittent pneumatic compression (IPC) within 24h; begin etiologic workup (telemetry, ECG)
day2_milestones: Antithrombotic therapy confirmed by end of hospital day 2 (STK-5); start high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80 mg or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg); complete cardioembolic workup (echocardiogram, ongoing telemetry) and vessel imaging (carotid); PT/OT and speech-language pathology (SLP) formal evaluations; transition IV to oral antihypertensives; begin early (but not hyperacute <24h aggressive) mobilization; determine AF status and plan anticoagulation start timing by infarct size (1-3-6-12 day rule); glucose/BP optimization
day3plus_milestones: Finalize TOAST etiologic classification and full secondary-prevention bundle (antiplatelet vs anticoagulation for AF, high-intensity statin to LDL <70, BP control, diabetes management, smoking cessation); for symptomatic carotid stenosis 50-99% schedule revascularization (CEA/CAS) ideally within 2 weeks; complete rehab assessment and determine disposition (home vs inpatient rehab facility [IRF] vs SNF); deliver stroke education; confirm swallow safety or enteral-feeding plan; arrange follow-up; discharge when neurologically stable and prevention plan in place
consults_multidisciplinary: Vascular neurology / stroke team STAT (hyperacute); neuro-interventional/endovascular for LVO STAT; neurosurgery for hemorrhagic transformation or malignant edema (decompressive hemicraniectomy <48h if age <60); PT/OT + SLP within 24-48h (dysphagia, mobility, ADLs); cardiology for cardioembolic source/PFO; PM&R for rehab disposition; clinical pharmacy (thrombolytic dosing, anticoagulation transitions); registered dietitian (dysphagia diet, enteral nutrition); case management/social work early for disposition and transitions; vascular surgery for carotid revascularization
monitoring_level: Neuro-ICU or ICU x24h after any IV thrombolysis or EVT (BP + neuro checks q15min tapering to q1h); stroke-unit step-down/telemetry for stable non-reperfused patients; continuous cardiac telemetry >=24-48h for all (paroxysmal AF detection); escalate/re-image for neurologic decline (NIHSS increase >=4), depressed consciousness, pupillary change, signs of malignant cerebral edema or hemorrhagic transformation, refractory hypertension, or airway compromise
nutrition_mobility_vte: Strict NPO until a validated bedside dysphagia screen is passed BEFORE any oral intake or medications (single highest-yield aspiration-pneumonia lever); failed screen -> SLP evaluation, modified diet or NG tube; VTE prophylaxis with intermittent pneumatic compression within 24h for all immobile patients (CLOTS-3), add pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH/UFH) once hemorrhage excluded and stable; early mobilization out of bed as tolerated but AVOID very-early high-intensity mobilization <24h (AVERT harm signal); maintain glucose 140-180 mg/dL and normothermia; remove indwelling urinary catheters early (CAUTI + mobility)
discharge_criteria: Neurologically stable or improving with no active fluctuation; hemorrhagic transformation excluded/stable on imaging when thrombolysis/EVT given; safe swallow confirmed or enteral-feeding plan established; complete secondary-prevention regimen initiated (antithrombotic, high-intensity statin, BP control, AF anticoagulation plan); stroke etiology determined or definitive workup plan set; rehabilitation needs assessed and appropriate disposition arranged; patient/caregiver educated and able to participate in ongoing care
discharge_planning: Disposition matched to function (home +/- services vs IRF vs SNF vs LTACH) by mRS/FIM and rehab eval; medication reconciliation emphasizing antithrombotic + statin + antihypertensive + AF anticoagulant with adherence counseling; stroke education (STK-8: warning signs, call 911, personal risk factors, medications, follow-up); schedule neurology follow-up within 1-4 weeks + PCP; arrange carotid revascularization and AF-anticoagulation start timing; outpatient rehab/therapy referrals; ensure warm handoff and transition-of-care summary
expected_los: Typical average ~4-5 days for ischemic stroke; MS-DRG GMLOS spans ~2.1 days (066, w/o CC/MCC) to ~3.3 days (065, CC) to ~5.0-5.3 days (064/061, MCC tier); tPA-treated groups (061-063) run similar-to-slightly-higher given mandatory 24h monitoring
target_los: 3 days for uncomplicated moderate stroke on optimized pathway; 2-3 days for minor stroke/high-risk TIA; 6-10+ days for large-territory/hemorrhagic/MCC-tier requiring ICU, hemicraniectomy, or complex disposition
quality_metrics: GWTG-Stroke / Joint Commission / CMS STK core measures: STK-1 VTE prophylaxis; STK-2 antithrombotic at discharge; STK-3 anticoagulation for AF; STK-4 thrombolytic therapy in eligible patients; STK-5 antithrombotic by end of hospital day 2; STK-6 statin at discharge; STK-8 stroke education; STK-10 assessed for rehabilitation; plus dysphagia screen before PO, NIHSS documentation, door-to-needle <=60 min (>=85%), door-to-device <=90/60 min, door-to-CT <=25 min; outcome measures: 30-day risk-adjusted mortality and readmission
common_complications: Symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage/hemorrhagic transformation (post-tPA ~2-6%) — mitigated by strict BP control, weight-based dosing, 24h re-imaging; malignant cerebral edema (large MCA infarct) — early neurosurgery/hemicraniectomy; aspiration pneumonia — dysphagia screening + NPO; DVT/PE — IPC then LMWH; UTI — avoid/early-remove catheters; early recurrent stroke — prompt secondary prevention; post-stroke seizures, depression, falls, and reperfusion injury
readmission_drivers: Recurrent stroke/TIA from inadequate secondary prevention (uncontrolled AF/BP, missed statin/antithrombotic, untreated carotid stenosis); aspiration pneumonia; falls/injury; medication nonadherence; poor care transitions and missed follow-up; VTE — prevention levers: complete guideline secondary-prevention bundle, appropriate AF anticoagulation with correct start timing, high-intensity statin, BP control, dysphagia management, early (<=1-4 week) follow-up, med reconciliation, and structured stroke education
guideline_source: PRIMARY: 2026 AHA/ASA Guideline for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke (Prabhakaran et al., American Heart Association/American Stroke Association, Stroke, Jan 2026 — replaces 2018 guideline + 2019 Powers update); OPERATIONAL: AHA Target: Stroke Phase III and Get With The Guidelines-Stroke; SECONDARY PREVENTION: AHA/ASA 2021 Guideline for the Prevention of Stroke in Patients With Stroke and TIA (Kleindorfer)
key_citations: Citations verified via PubMed. 2026 AHA/ASA AIS Guideline, Stroke 2026, PMID 41582814, DOI https://doi.org/10.1161/STR.0000000000000513; NINDS rt-PA Study, NEJM 1995, PMID 7477192; ECASS III (alteplase 3-4.5h), NEJM 2008, PMID 18815396; HERMES thrombectomy meta-analysis, Lancet 2016, PMID 26898852; EXTEND-IA TNK (tenecteplase), NEJM 2018, PMID 29694815; AcT (tenecteplase non-inferiority), Lancet 2022, PMID 35779553; DAWN (EVT 6-24h), NEJM 2018, PMID 29129157; DEFUSE-3 (EVT 6-16h), NEJM 2018, PMID 29364767; SELECT2 (large-core EVT), NEJM 2023, PMID 36762865; POINT (DAPT minor stroke/TIA), NEJM 2018, PMID 29766750
evidence_grade: Strong — Class I society guideline. Thrombolysis, mechanical thrombectomy, and organized stroke-unit care are each Class I/Level A, backed by multiple high-quality RCTs and individual-patient-data meta-analyses; extended-window thrombolysis (4.5-9h/wake-up) and large-core EVT are newer Class 2a/2b recommendations with slightly lower certainty
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MCC tier (061 tPA+MCC, 064 hemorrhage/infarct+MCC) = larger infarcts, hemorrhagic transformation, malignant edema, aspiration pneumonia, sepsis; drive neuro-ICU stay, possible hemicraniectomy, and LOS ~5-10+ days with higher mortality; CC tier (062, 065) intermediate; without CC/MCC (063, 066) = minor stroke/short 2-3 day stay; the 061-063 split flags IV-thrombolysis use (mandatory 24h ICU-level monitoring); the collapsed 064-066 also captures hemorrhagic stroke, which carries higher mortality and LOS than pure infarction and shifts management toward BP/ICP control and neurosurgery
pathway_pearls: Speed is brain: hit door-to-CT <=25 min and door-to-needle <=60 (goal <=45) min — fingerstick glucose is the ONLY lab that must precede thrombolysis; never wait on coags/platelets unless anticoagulant use or coagulopathy is suspected. Tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg single bolus is now co-equal to alteplase (Class I) — it is faster and simplifies drip-and-ship transfers. Advanced imaging (CTP/MRI mismatch) unlocks late windows: EVT 6-24h (DAWN/DEFUSE-3), large-core ASPECTS 3-5 EVT (SELECT2), and thrombolysis 4.5-9h/wake-up. Do NOT over-lower BP: <185/110 before lytic and <180/105 after, but intensive lowering to <140 systolic is harmful (Class III); and screen swallow before ANY oral intake to prevent aspiration pneumonia
verification_notes: Independently verified via PubMed MCP (get_article_metadata on all 10 PMIDs) and WebSearch/WebFetch of primary sources — did not rely on memory. CITATIONS: All 10 key_citations PMIDs resolve to the correct articles with correct authors, journals, years, and DOIs — NINDS (7477192), ECASS III (18815396/Hacke), HERMES (26898852/Goyal), EXTEND-IA TNK (29694815/Campbell), AcT (35779553/Menon), DAWN (29129157/Nogueira), DEFUSE-3 (29364767/Albers), SELECT2 (36762865/Sarraj), POINT (29766750/Johnston). No fabrication or misattribution. GUIDELINE SOURCE: The 2026 AHA/ASA Guideline for the Early Management of AIS (Prabhakaran et al., Stroke, PMID 41582814, DOI 10.1161/STR.0000000000000513) is REAL and current — confirmed on ahajournals.org and AHA professional/newsroom pages; it does replace the 2018 guideline + 2019 Powers update. TIME-CRITICAL: door-to-needle <=60 (stretch <=45) min, door-to-CT <=25 min, tPA <=4.5h + extended 4.5-9h/wake-up (Class 2a), EVT <=6h + 6-24h (DAWN/DEFUSE-3), large-core ASPECTS 3-5 age <80 within 24h, door-to-device <=90 min — all confirmed against the 2026 guideline (emDocs verbatim quotes) and Target: Stroke Phase III one-pager. RISK SCORES: NIHSS (0-42), ASPECTS (0-10), ABCD2, CHA2DS2-VASc, mRS, TOAST are all real with correct thresholds; NIHSS>=6 EVT gate and ASPECTS 3-5 large-core age<80 match guideline. INITIAL MGMT: TNK 0.25 mg/kg max 25 mg co-equal Class I with alteplase 0.9 mg/kg max 90 mg (confirmed); BP <185/110 pre-lytic, <180/105 x24h post, permissive to 220/120, intensive <140 systolic = Class III harm (confirmed); DAPT for minor/non-disabling stroke; cryoprecipitate + antifibrinolytic for post-tPA ICH — all correct. No transfusion-threshold claim present (N/A for stroke). QUALITY METRICS: STK-1/2/3/4/5/6/8/10 all confirmed as active measures in the Joint Commission v2026A1 specifications manual (STK-7/STK-9 correctly omitted as retired/nonexistent); GWTG-Stroke and CMS STK real. LOS: ~4-5 day average and the MS-DRG 061-066 GMLOS gradient (~2.1d for 066 up to ~5.0-5.3d for 061/064 MCC tier) are plausible and consistent with national MS-DRG benchmarks. This row is exceptionally accurate and well-sourced; no clinical corrections warranted.; ISSUES: No corrections required. All high-stakes claims verified accurate and current. Two minor, non-blocking attribution nuances noted below (neither is a clinical error).; MINOR (attribution, not a threshold error): The parenthetical '(SELECT2)' is attached to the 'large core ASPECTS 3-5 EVT-eligible if age <80' claim. The SELECT2 trial itself enrolled ages 18-85, not <80. However, the stated clinical threshold 'age <80' is CORRECT and matches the 2026 AHA/ASA guideline's actual recommendation text for ASPECTS 3-5 EVT in the 6-24h window (which adopted a more conservative <80 cutoff than SELECT2's enrollment, harmonizing with ANGEL-ASPECT which capped at 80). Because the actionable threshold matches the current guideline, no correction is made; the researcher may optionally broaden the citation label to the large-core EVT evidence set (SELECT2/ANGEL-ASPECT/RESCUE-Japan LIMIT).; MINOR (source nuance): 'NIHSS >=10 required for basilar-artery EVT' reflects the ATTENTION trial (NIHSS >=10); the companion BAOCHE trial used NIHSS >=6 in the 6-24h window, so >=10 is not universally 'required.' Reasonable as a conservative representation; not independently contradicted by a guideline source, so left unchanged.
