admission_criteria: Most acute uncomplicated pyelonephritis in healthy nonpregnant women is treated OUTPATIENT (oral fluoroquinolone) per IDSA/ESCMID 2010; reserve inpatient admission for objective indications: hemodynamic instability / sepsis / septic shock (SBP<90 or MAP<65, lactate>=2); inability to tolerate oral intake or meds (intractable vomiting); suspected/proven urinary obstruction or complicating anatomy (obstructing stone, hydronephrosis, indwelling hardware); AKI (Cr rise >=0.3 mg/dL or >=1.5x baseline); pregnancy; known/suspected multidrug-resistant organism needing IV therapy; failure of >=48-72h outpatient therapy; significant immunocompromise or poorly controlled diabetes (DKA risk, emphysematous pyelo); uncontrolled pain; renal/perinephric abscess or emphysematous pyelonephritis on imaging; poor social support / nonadherence. ED discharge or observation is appropriate for stable, tolerating-PO patients without sepsis, obstruction, or AKI.
risk_stratification: No pyelonephritis-specific validated severity score exists (unlike CURB-65/PSI for pneumonia); use sepsis and deterioration tools. qSOFA >=2 of 3 (SBP<=100, RR>=22, GCS<15) flags higher risk but SSC-2021 recommends AGAINST it as a sole screen; use SIRS or NEWS2/MEWS for early-warning surveillance (NEWS2>=5 escalate, >=7 urgent/critical-care review). SOFA increase >=2 defines organ dysfunction (Sepsis-3). Serum lactate >=2 mmol/L drives fluid resuscitation and repeat measurement; >=4 mmol/L = high-risk, ICU-level. MDRO/ESBL risk stratification (IDSA 2025 4-step): prior resistant urinary isolate, fluoroquinolone or broad-spectrum antibiotic exposure within 90 days-12 months, healthcare/travel exposure, known ESBL colonization -> broadens empiric choice. Charlson comorbidity index and diabetes flag emphysematous pyelo / abscess risk.
initial_workup_labs: Urinalysis with microscopy (pyuria >=10 WBC/hpf, leukocyte esterase, nitrites, bacteria; absence of pyuria argues against UTI and prompts alternate dx); urine Gram stain AND urine culture with susceptibilities BEFORE first antibiotic dose (essential for de-escalation). Blood cultures x2 before antibiotics (positive in ~20-30% of pyelonephritis, higher if septic; informs 7-day bacteremia course). CBC with differential (leukocytosis/left shift or leukopenia = severity/MCC marker). CMP/BMP (creatinine for AKI, electrolytes, glucose). Serum lactate (sepsis screen; repeat in 2-4h if >=2). Beta-hCG in reproductive-age women. Procalcitonin/CRP adjunctive (supports sepsis, trends response, aids de-escalation). If diabetic: glucose, beta-hydroxybutyrate/ABG to exclude DKA. Coags/DIC panel if septic shock.
initial_imaging_dx: Routine imaging NOT required for uncomplicated pyelonephritis. Renal/bladder ultrasound (often bedside POCUS) first-line to exclude hydronephrosis/obstruction, preferred in pregnancy, AKI, or to avoid contrast. CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (non-contrast/stone protocol if AKI or to find calculus) is the definitive study for: suspected obstruction or stone, sepsis/septic shock, no clinical improvement by 48-72h, suspected abscess or emphysematous pyelonephritis, immunocompromise/diabetes, or recurrent disease. STAT/urgent imaging (within hours) if obstructing infected stone (pyonephrosis) suspected -> this is a decompression emergency. Consider MR urography if contrast contraindicated.
time_critical_interventions: If sepsis/septic shock: execute Surviving Sepsis Campaign Hour-1 bundle and CMS SEP-1: measure lactate (remeasure within 2-4h if >2 mmol/L); draw blood cultures before antibiotics; administer broad-spectrum IV antibiotics immediately (within 1h for septic shock/probable sepsis; SSC allows up to 3h if sepsis without shock and diagnosis uncertain); begin 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid within 3h for hypotension or lactate >=4; start norepinephrine to target MAP>=65 mmHg for fluid-refractory hypotension. SEP-1 3-hour and 6-hour bundle compliance is the tracked metric. SOURCE CONTROL is time-critical and the single highest-yield lever: emergent urinary decompression (retrograde ureteral stent or percutaneous nephrostomy) for an obstructed, infected system, ideally within ~6h of recognizing obstructive urosepsis; drain renal/perinephric abscess. Antibiotics alone fail an undecompressed obstructed kidney.
initial_management: Empiric IV antibiotics per severity + local antibiogram + MDRO risk (IDSA 2025 cUTI): non-septic complicated UTI/pyelonephritis -> 3rd-gen cephalosporin (ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV q24h), OR fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV q12h / levofloxacin 750 mg IV q24h), OR piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375-4.5 g IV q6-8h; AVOID carbapenems when not septic. Septic/high-MDRO-risk -> antipseudomonal beta-lactam (cefepime 2 g IV q8h or pip-tazo) or carbapenem (meropenem 1 g q8h; ertapenem 1 g q24h if ESBL and not critically ill); single-dose aminoglycoside (gentamicin/tobramycin) may be added empirically in the critically ill. Known ESBL -> carbapenem. Fluids: balanced crystalloid (lactated Ringer's) preferred over saline (SSC-2021). Vasopressors: norepinephrine first-line to MAP>=65, add vasopressin, hydrocortisone 200 mg/day for refractory shock. Antipyretics, antiemetics, analgesia; correct DKA if present. Source control as above. De-escalate/narrow to culture-directed therapy at 48-72h.
day1_milestones: Cultures (urine + blood) obtained before antibiotics; empiric IV antibiotics started within target window; complete SSC Hour-1 / CMS SEP-1 bundle if septic (lactate, cultures, antibiotics, fluids, vasopressors); achieve hemodynamic stabilization (MAP>=65) and begin lactate clearance; ultrasound/CT to identify or exclude obstruction; if obstruction present, arrange emergent decompression (urology/IR) same day; establish monitoring level (floor vs step-down vs ICU); antiemetics/analgesia; assess and trend AKI; document MDRO risk and antibiogram-based empiric choice; VTE prophylaxis ordered; assess urinary catheter necessity (remove or justify).
day2_milestones: Review preliminary culture and susceptibility results (typically available 24-48h) and DE-ESCALATE/narrow antibiotics; confirm clinical trajectory (fever curve, hemodynamics, WBC, lactate, renal function trending toward normal); begin IV-to-oral switch once afebrile ~24h, hemodynamically stable off pressors, tolerating PO, and an effective susceptibility-matched oral agent exists (IDSA 2025 endorses early oral step-down, including for bacteremic cUTI); wean/discontinue vasopressors and de-escalate from ICU/step-down if stable; remove indwelling urinary catheter if no longer indicated (CAUTI prevention); mobilize; confirm source control adequacy if decompressed.
day3plus_milestones: Most patients defervesce within 48-72h; if persistent fever or lack of improvement by 48-72h, obtain/repeat CT to exclude abscess, obstruction, or emphysematous pyelonephritis and reassess antibiotic adequacy (resistant organism, inadequate source control) with ID input. Finalize oral regimen matched to susceptibilities; plan total course: 5-7 days fluoroquinolone or 7 days non-fluoroquinolone for non-bacteremic complicated UTI/pyelonephritis, 7 days for gram-negative bacteremic UTI with clinical improvement, 10-14 days only for men with suspected bacterial prostatitis or undrained complications (IDSA 2025). Verify renal recovery, sustained afebrile status, and stable vitals; complete discharge planning and transitions of care.
consults_multidisciplinary: Urology: obstructing stone/hydronephrosis, pyonephrosis, abscess, emphysematous pyelonephritis, recurrent/complicated anatomy, need for stent (early/emergent for obstructive urosepsis). Interventional radiology: percutaneous nephrostomy or abscess drainage. Infectious diseases: MDRO/ESBL/CRE, bacteremia, treatment failure, complex regimen or OPAT planning. Nephrology: significant or non-resolving AKI, CKD. Critical care: septic shock / vasopressor need. Pharmacy: renal dose adjustment, aminoglycoside/vancomycin levels, antimicrobial stewardship and IV-to-PO conversion. Care management/social work: disposition, OPAT setup, adherence barriers (early, Day 1-2). Nutrition if DKA or poor intake.
monitoring_level: Floor/telemetry for stable pyelonephritis without organ dysfunction. Step-down/intermediate for sepsis with organ dysfunction requiring frequent monitoring or low-dose single pressor per unit policy. ICU for septic shock (vasopressor-dependent hypotension, lactate>=4, MAP<65 despite 30 mL/kg crystalloid), respiratory failure, or severe metabolic derangement. Escalation triggers: rising lactate or failure to clear, MAP<65 or escalating pressor requirement, worsening AKI/oliguria, new altered mentation, NEWS2>=7, or clinical deterioration despite source control -> reassess for undrained/obstructed source and broaden antibiotics.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: advance from clear liquids to regular as nausea/ileus resolves; no fluid restriction (support renal perfusion). Early mobilization / out of bed as hemodynamics allow. VTE prophylaxis: pharmacologic (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily or UFH 5000 units SC q8-12h) for acutely ill medical inpatients unless contraindicated (use Padua/IMPROVE risk); mechanical prophylaxis if bleeding risk. Glucose control target 140-180 mg/dL (SSC-2021; avoid <110); manage/exclude DKA in diabetics. Line/catheter stewardship: remove indwelling urinary catheter as early as possible and reassess necessity daily (CAUTI bundle); if catheter-associated UTI, exchange or remove the catheter as part of source control; discontinue IV access once transitioned to oral antibiotics.
discharge_criteria: Afebrile ~24-48h; hemodynamically stable off vasopressors; clinically improving (resolving flank pain, normalizing WBC, downtrending inflammatory markers); tolerating oral intake and an oral antibiotic to which the organism is susceptible; adequate pain control on oral analgesia; source control achieved (obstruction relieved / abscess drained); stable or improving renal function; reliable follow-up and ability to complete the oral course.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home; complete the total antibiotic course orally (typically 7 days pyelonephritis/complicated UTI, 5-7 days if fluoroquinolone), with the oral agent matched to final susceptibilities. Arrange OPAT only if no effective oral option (e.g., resistant organism). Medication reconciliation; explicit stop date to avoid excess duration (stewardship). PCP follow-up within 1-2 weeks; urology follow-up for definitive stone management after infection clears; repeat imaging for drained abscess. Patient education: hydration, adherence and full-course completion, recurrence prevention, and red-flag return precautions (recurrent fever, worsening flank pain, vomiting, decreased urine output). Address the underlying driver (stone, obstruction, catheter, uncontrolled diabetes) as part of transitions of care.
expected_los: CMS MS-DRG GMLOS benchmarks (approximate, IPPS): DRG 690 (kidney/UTI without MCC) ~2.9-3.1 days; DRG 689 (with MCC, e.g., severe sepsis, AKI, septic shock) ~4.2-4.5 days. Uncomplicated inpatient pyelonephritis commonly 2-3 days; HCUP mean stay for the group roughly 3-4 days; urosepsis with ICU/decompression extends materially.
target_los: 2-3 days for non-septic complicated UTI/pyelonephritis under an early IV-to-oral switch pathway (oral step-down once afebrile 24-48h and tolerating PO); 3-4 days for sepsis without shock; 4-6+ days for septic shock requiring ICU and urinary decompression. Short-course therapy and early oral conversion are the primary LOS levers.
quality_metrics: CMS SEP-1 Severe Sepsis/Septic Shock Management Bundle (3h and 6h bundle compliance) - highly relevant given urosepsis; now weighted in Hospital VBP Safety domain. NHSN CAUTI rate and catheter utilization ratio. 30-day all-cause and same-cause readmission. Antimicrobial stewardship measures: days of therapy (DOT), appropriate empiric selection, de-escalation rate, IV-to-PO conversion rate, and excess-duration (>7 day) avoidance. Blood cultures drawn before antibiotics; time-to-appropriate-antibiotic. Mortality for sepsis cohort. Hospital-onset C. difficile (SIR) as an antibiotic-related outcome.
common_complications: Progression to sepsis/septic shock; AKI; gram-negative bacteremia; renal or perinephric abscess (suspect if no defervescence by 48-72h); emphysematous pyelonephritis (diabetics - high mortality, needs urgent urology); obstructive pyonephrosis; antibiotic-associated C. difficile colitis; catheter-associated reinfection; rarely papillary necrosis. Mitigation via the pathway: early appropriate empiric antibiotics + de-escalation, prompt source control (decompression/drainage), CT reassessment when not improving by 48-72h, catheter removal/CAUTI bundle, short-course stewardship to limit C. difficile, and glucose control in diabetics.
readmission_drivers: Inadequate source control (retained obstructing stone or undrained abscess); discharge oral agent not matched to susceptibilities or resistant/ESBL organism; premature discharge before defervescence; recurrent or relapsing infection; catheter-associated reinfection; medication nonadherence or incomplete course; uncontrolled underlying diabetes. Prevention levers: susceptibility-matched oral step-down verified before discharge, definitive stone/obstruction management planned with urology, adequate observed clinical improvement, patient education with return precautions, timely PCP/urology follow-up, and removal of unnecessary catheters.
guideline_source: IDSA 2025 Clinical Practice Guideline on Management and Treatment of Complicated Urinary Tract Infections (duration/step-down), Trautner et al, Clin Infect Dis 2025; IDSA/ESCMID 2010 International Clinical Practice Guidelines for Treatment of Acute Uncomplicated Cystitis and Pyelonephritis in Women, Gupta et al, Clin Infect Dis 2011; Surviving Sepsis Campaign International Guidelines 2026, Prescott et al, Intensive Care Med 2026 (updates/replaces the 2021 SSC guideline by Evans et al; the Hour-1 resuscitation targets referenced in this pathway - antibiotics within 1h for shock / up to 3h for possible sepsis without shock, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, MAP>=65, norepinephrine first-line - are carried forward unchanged, and the recommendation to prefer NEWS/NEWS2/MEWS/SIRS over qSOFA as a sole screen was strengthened to a strong recommendation), plus CMS SEP-1 bundle (still keyed to the earlier severe sepsis/septic shock bundle definitions). Adjunct: AUA/CUA/SUFU recurrent uncomplicated UTI guideline (2019, amended 2022) for recurrence prevention.
key_citations: IDSA 2025 cUTI duration guideline: Trautner BW, Cortes-Penfield NW, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2025 (PMID 41419448; DOI 10.1093/cid/ciaf462). IDSA/ESCMID 2010 pyelonephritis/cystitis: Gupta K, Hooton TM, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2011;52(5):e103-20 (PMID 21292654; DOI 10.1093/cid/ciq257). Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2026 (CURRENT): Prescott HC, Antonelli M, et al. Intensive Care Med. 2026;52(5):863-936 (PMID 41870560; DOI 10.1007/s00134-026-08361-1) - supersedes the 2021 SSC guideline: Evans L, Rhodes A, et al. Intensive Care Med. 2021;47(11):1181-1247 (PMID 34599691; DOI 10.1007/s00134-021-06506-y). Landmark short-course RCT (pyelonephritis): Sandberg T, et al. Ciprofloxacin 7 vs 14 days. Lancet. 2012;380(9840):484-90 (PMID 22726802; DOI 10.1016/S0140-6736(12)60608-4). Gram-negative bacteremia (68% urinary source) 7 vs 14 days: Yahav D, et al. Clin Infect Dis. 2019;69(7):1091-8 (PMID 30535100; DOI 10.1093/cid/ciy1054). Citations retrieved and verified via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong overall - anchored by current society guidelines (IDSA 2025 complicated UTI; IDSA/ESCMID 2010; Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2026, which updates the 2021 guideline with unchanged core resuscitation targets, largely strong/best-practice statements) reinforced by randomized-trial evidence for short-course therapy (Sandberg Lancet 2012; Yahav CID 2019). Antibiotic selection and duration and the sepsis bundle are high-confidence; several inpatient-admission thresholds and the timing of urinary decompression are consensus/expert-opinion and partly extrapolated (moderate), as no pyelonephritis-specific severity score is validated.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The collapsed group splits on complications: DRG 690 (without CC/MCC) = uncomplicated pyelonephritis, floor-level, LOS ~3 days, oral step-down early, minimal imaging. DRG 689 (with MCC) is driven by severe sepsis, septic shock, acute kidney injury/failure, or bacteremia as the MCC -> step-down/ICU care, vasopressors, mandatory source-control imaging and often decompression, longer LOS (~4-5+ days), and higher mortality. Diabetes/immunocompromise shift risk toward emphysematous pyelonephritis and abscess (urgent urologic intervention). Obstruction converts a medical admission into a time-critical surgical-decompression case regardless of DRG label.
pathway_pearls: 1) Obstruction + infection = surgical emergency: an obstructed, infected kidney needs emergent decompression (ureteral stent or percutaneous nephrostomy, target ~within 6h) - antibiotics alone will not control the source and the patient will spiral into shock. 2) Cultures before antibiotics, then empiric per local antibiogram and MDRO/ESBL risk, then DE-ESCALATE at 48-72h - most is E. coli, but ESBL prevalence dictates whether ceftriaxone or a carbapenem is the right empiric. 3) Short course wins: 7 days total for pyelonephritis (Sandberg) and for gram-negative urinary-source bacteremia (Yahav), with early IV-to-oral switch once afebrile and tolerating PO (IDSA 2025) - this drives LOS down and cuts C. difficile and line days. 4) Pull the catheter and confirm a susceptibility-matched oral regimen before discharge - the two cheapest levers against CAUTI and 30-day bounce-back.
verification_notes: Checked against live sources (PubMed MCP + web); did not rely on memory. CITATIONS: All 5 key citations independently verified REAL and correctly attributed via PubMed metadata - PMID 41419448 (IDSA 2025 cUTI duration, Trautner, DOI 10.1093/cid/ciaf462), PMID 21292654 (IDSA/ESCMID 2010, Gupta, 52(5):e103-20, DOI 10.1093/cid/ciq257), PMID 34599691 (SSC 2021, Evans, 47(11):1181-1247, DOI 10.1007/s00134-021-06506-y), PMID 22726802 (Sandberg, Lancet 2012;380(9840):484-90, cipro 7 vs 14d), PMID 30535100 (Yahav, CID 2019;69(7):1091-8; 68% urinary source confirmed in abstract). No fabricated or misattributed PMIDs/DOIs. TIME-CRITICAL: SSC targets verified against both 2021 and the new 2026 guideline - antibiotics within 1h (shock/probable sepsis) and up to 3h (possible sepsis without shock), 30 mL/kg crystalloid within 3h, norepinephrine to MAP>=65 - all correct and current; no door-to-balloon/needle or tPA fields present (correctly N/A for UTI). RISK STRATIFICATION: qSOFA thresholds (SBP<=100, RR>=22, GCS<15) correct; SSC recommends against qSOFA as a sole screen (strengthened to STRONG in 2026, favoring NEWS/NEWS2/MEWS/SIRS) - row is accurate; SOFA >=2, lactate >=2/>=4 correct. INITIAL MANAGEMENT: drug classes/doses verified standard (ceftriaxone 1-2g q24h, cipro 400mg IV q12h, levo 750mg q24h, pip-tazo 3.375-4.5g q6-8h, cefepime 2g q8h, meropenem 1g q8h, ertapenem 1g q24h; hydrocortisone 200mg/day; balanced crystalloid; glucose 140-180). IDSA 2025 durations verified via IDSA/emDocs: 5-7d FQ, 7d non-FQ, 7d GN-bacteremic cUTI, 10-14d men/prostatitis, and IV-to-oral step-down incl. bacteremia - all match the row. QUALITY METRICS: SEP-1 (now in Hospital VBP Safety domain), NHSN CAUTI, C. diff SIR - all real/applicable. DRG defs correct. ONLY MATERIAL FINDING: SSC 2021 has been superseded by SSC 2026 (Prescott et al, Intensive Care Med 2026;52(5):863-936, PMID 41870560) - a citation-currency update, not a clinical error, since all cited numbers are unchanged. Three low-risk corrections issued to update the guideline year. According to PubMed."; ISSUES: CURRENCY (moderate): guideline_source, key_citations, and evidence_grade cite Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 (Evans et al, PMID 34599691) as the current sepsis guideline. A 2026 SSC guideline (Prescott HC, Antonelli M, et al; Intensive Care Med 2026;52(5):863-936; PMID 41870560; DOI 10.1007/s00134-026-08361-1) was published 23 Mar 2026 and replaces the 2021 version. NOT a clinical error - the 2026 update carries forward every numeric target this pathway relies on (antibiotics within 1h for shock / 3h for possible sepsis without shock, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, MAP>=65, norepinephrine first-line, glucose 140-180) unchanged, and it strengthened the 'prefer NEWS/NEWS2/MEWS/SIRS over qSOFA' recommendation to a strong recommendation - consistent with the row's risk_stratification claim.; MINOR (no correction needed): The inline '(SSC-2021)' tags in time_critical_interventions, initial_management, and nutrition_mobility_vte are now technically the superseded edition year, but each cited recommendation (balanced crystalloid preferred over saline, hydrocortisone 200 mg/day for refractory shock, glucose 140-180 avoid <110, 30 mL/kg, MAP>=65) remains valid under SSC 2026 - substance is correct, only the year label is stale. Left uncorrected to avoid noise; the guideline_source fix covers the pointer.; NOTE (not an error): expected_los GMLOS values (DRG 690 ~2.9-3.1d; DRG 689 ~4.2-4.5d) are explicitly labeled approximate and are consistent with FY2024-2025 IPPS ranges; exact CMS Table 5 GMLOS figures sit behind a paywall and could not be confirmed to the decimal, but the ballpark and the DRG definitions (689 = with MCC, 690 = without MCC) are correct.
scope_and_volume_notes: HCUP #14 principal dx (0.51M, 2018). Includes pyelonephritis; frequent source of sepsis.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 689 — Kidney and Urinary Tract Infections with MCC; 690 — Kidney and Urinary Tract Infections without MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 6/6 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Original independent adversarial high-stakes review completed; current pass revalidated citation resolvability and FY2026 coding/taxonomy.
data_quality_notes: Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk.

admission_criteria: Inpatient (IV) indicated for IDSA "severe" nonpurulent SSTI or systemic toxicity: SIRS >=2 (temp >38 or <36C, HR >90, RR >24, WBC >12k or <4k); sepsis/hypotension; failure of >=48-72h outpatient oral antibiotics with progression; suspected necrotizing infection (pain out of proportion, bullae, crepitus, skin necrosis, dishwater discharge) = surgical emergency admit; immunocompromise (neutropenia, transplant, cirrhosis, poorly controlled diabetes); inability to tolerate/adhere to orals; extensive or facial/periorbital/hand/perineal involvement; unstable comorbidity (HF, ESRD, PVD, severe lymphedema). Eron Class III-IV or Dundee Class 3-4 -> admit for IV. Eron I-II / Dundee 1-2 without comorbidity -> ED discharge on orals +/- OPAT/observation; isolated abscess without systemic signs -> I&D and discharge (not admission).
risk_stratification: Eron classification I-IV and Dundee classification (SIRS criteria + comorbidity: Class 1 none, Class 2 comorbidity, Class 3 SIRS features, Class 4 sepsis) -> Dundee >=3 drives IV/admit; Dundee validated against mortality/LOS/readmission (Cutfield 2019; Class 4 ~9% 30-day mortality). LRINEC score (Wong 2004): >=6 intermediate / >=8 high risk for necrotizing fasciitis -> urgent surgical consult (PPV ~92% at >=6) BUT low sensitivity, so a low score does NOT exclude NF - serial exams govern. qSOFA >=2 or SIRS >=2 -> sepsis workup/escalation; NEWS2 for ward deterioration; SOFA if organ dysfunction. Mark/measure erythema borders to quantify progression.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential (leukocytosis/left shift or neutropenia); BMP (creatinine/AKI, glucose, sodium - feed LRINEC); CRP (LRINEC + trend); lactate if sepsis suspected (>2 resuscitate, >=4 high-risk per SEP-1); HbA1c/glucose in diabetics; CK if necrotizing suspected; blood cultures ONLY if systemic toxicity, immunocompromise, neutropenia, animal bite/water immersion, or severe disease (routine yield low ~2-5%, not for uncomplicated cellulitis - Choosing Wisely); Gram stain + culture of purulent drainage/abscess after I&D to guide MRSA vs MSSA de-escalation; HIV/immune eval if recurrent or atypical.
initial_imaging_dx: Diagnosis is primarily clinical. Point-of-care ultrasound at presentation to detect occult abscess (directs I&D) and to screen for DVT mimic. Plain radiograph if crepitus/soft-tissue gas, retained foreign body, or osteomyelitis concern. Contrast CT or MRI urgently (fascial edema/gas/non-enhancement) if necrotizing fasciitis suspected - but do NOT delay surgical exploration for imaging in an unstable patient (operative exploration is the definitive diagnostic). Venous duplex to exclude DVT when unilateral leg swelling is ambiguous; echocardiogram only if bacteremia/endocarditis concern.
time_critical_interventions: If sepsis/septic shock: CMS SEP-1 bundle - draw lactate and blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4, reassess perfusion and repeat lactate. Necrotizing soft-tissue infection = surgical emergency: broad-spectrum antibiotics PLUS toxin-suppressing clindamycin/linezolid AND emergent operative debridement within hours (mortality rises sharply with delay >12-24h) - do not wait for imaging. Abscess: incision and drainage is the primary time-sensitive intervention. Non-septic cellulitis: first IV antibiotic dose given in the ED.
initial_management: Nonpurulent cellulitis/erysipelas (beta-hemolytic strep + MSSA): moderate -> IV cefazolin (or ceftriaxone; IV penicillin if strep confirmed); mild -> oral cephalexin, dicloxacillin, or penicillin VK. Severe/necrotizing -> emergent surgery + empiric IV vancomycin (or linezolid/daptomycin for MRSA) PLUS piperacillin-tazobactam (broad gram-neg/anaerobe) PLUS clindamycin for toxin suppression. Purulent (MRSA-directed): I&D + culture, add TMP-SMX or doxycycline (mild-moderate) or IV vancomycin/linezolid/daptomycin (severe). Adding empiric MRSA coverage to nonpurulent cellulitis does NOT improve cure (Moran 2017). Elevate limb; treat portal of entry (tinea pedis, fissures, edema/lymphedema); analgesia. Total course 5 days for uncomplicated cellulitis (extend to 7-14 if severe/immunocompromised/slow response) per IDSA.
day1_milestones: Classify purulent vs nonpurulent and severity tier (Eron/Dundee); start appropriate IV antibiotic (first dose in ED); achieve source control - I&D of any abscess with culture, surgical eval if necrotizing; mark/measure and photo-document erythema borders; elevate affected limb; analgesia; complete sepsis bundle if applicable; treat entry portal and comorbidities (glycemic control, diurese peripheral edema); VTE risk assessment + prophylaxis; set expectation for IV-to-PO conversion.
day2_milestones: Reassess erythema against marked borders (expect stabilization/regression by 24-48h - note early modest expansion can occur despite effective therapy); trend fever, WBC, CRP; review culture/susceptibility and de-escalate/narrow (stop empiric MRSA coverage if cultures negative and nonpurulent); assess IV-to-PO switch criteria (afebrile >=24h, hemodynamically stable, tolerating PO, erythema not advancing); if worsening -> re-image for occult abscess/necrotizing process, re-examine, broaden or obtain surgical re-eval; begin discharge planning and arrange follow-up.
day3plus_milestones: Convert to oral antibiotics once afebrile and improving; complete total 5-day course (7-14 days if severe/immunocompromised/slow responder); confirm no undrained collection; verify erythema, swelling, and pain regressing; optimize predisposing factors (lymphedema/edema compression once infection controlled, treat tinea pedis, venous insufficiency, glycemic control) to prevent recurrence; finalize disposition - most uncomplicated cellulitis is dischargeable by day 2-4.
consults_multidisciplinary: Surgery (general/ortho/plastics) urgently for suspected necrotizing infection or abscess needing OR drainage; Infectious Diseases for severe/immunocompromised/recurrent/atypical exposure, bacteremia, or treatment failure; Antimicrobial Stewardship/Pharmacy for vancomycin dosing, de-escalation, and IV-to-PO; Vascular/Wound care for chronic ulcers, PVD, venous disease; Endocrinology/diabetes team for glycemic control; PT/OT for mobility and lymphedema; Care management/Social work for OPAT setup, housing, and IV-drug-use needs; Nutrition; Dermatology when pseudocellulitis (stasis dermatitis) vs true cellulitis is uncertain.
monitoring_level: Med-surg floor for most (Eron III / Dundee 3). Telemetry if significant cardiac comorbidity or electrolyte derangement. Step-down/ICU for septic shock, post-op necrotizing infection, rapidly progressive infection, vasopressor requirement, or organ dysfunction (Eron IV / Dundee 4). Escalation triggers: qSOFA >=2, lactate >=4, hypotension refractory to 30 mL/kg fluids, expanding necrosis/crepitus, or new organ dysfunction -> ICU transfer + emergent surgical consult.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Regular diet tailored to comorbidity (diabetic/renal); elevate affected limb and mobilize early as tolerated to reduce dependent edema; pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (LMWH/UFH) per risk unless contraindicated (reduced mobility + inflammation raise risk); inpatient glucose target 140-180 mg/dL; avoid/remove unnecessary central lines and urinary catheters (infection-source stewardship); compression therapy for chronic lymphedema once acute infection is controlled.
discharge_criteria: Afebrile >=24h; hemodynamically stable; erythema, swelling, and pain regressing (not advancing) against marked borders; tolerating oral antibiotics and oral intake; adequate source control (abscess drained, no undrained collection); pain controlled; able to complete therapy at home or via OPAT; comorbidities stabilized; reliable 24-72h follow-up arranged.
discharge_planning: Complete oral antibiotic course (total 5 days uncomplicated); full medication reconciliation; arrange OPAT if continued IV needed; schedule 24-72h follow-up (PCP/clinic/ED) to confirm improvement; patient education on limb elevation, marking spread, and red flags (fever, expanding redness, blistering, severe pain); recurrence prevention (skin/foot hygiene, treat tinea pedis, manage edema/lymphedema, weight and glycemic control); prophylactic penicillin V 250 mg BID for recurrent (>=2 episodes) leg cellulitis (Thomas PATCH I, NNT 5); Social work for IV-drug-use/homelessness; ensure transportation and pharmacy access.
expected_los: MS-DRG 603 (Cellulitis without MCC) GMLOS ~3.2-3.4 days (AMLOS ~3.9-4.1); MS-DRG 602 (Cellulitis with MCC) GMLOS ~4.8-5.0 days (AMLOS ~5.9) per CMS IPPS Table 5 (exact figure varies by fiscal year). Typical observed inpatient LOS 3-4 days overall.
target_los: 2-3 days for uncomplicated cellulitis (DRG 603) with same-day I&D of any abscess and IV-to-PO switch by ~48h; 4-5 days for complicated/MCC cases (DRG 602). Many low-risk patients can be diverted to observation or direct OPAT to achieve <=2 inpatient days.
quality_metrics: CMS SEP-1 (if sepsis present); appropriate empiric antibiotic selection and antimicrobial stewardship - avoid unnecessary MRSA/broad-spectrum coverage for nonpurulent cellulitis and avoid routine blood cultures in uncomplicated SSTI (Choosing Wisely); IV-to-PO conversion rate; 30-day all-cause and same-site readmission; observed LOS vs GMLOS; hospital-onset C. difficile and bacteremia rates; VTE prophylaxis compliance; door-to-antibiotic and door-to-I&D times.
common_complications: Occult/undrained abscess requiring drainage (mitigate: POCUS + timely I&D); misdiagnosed pseudocellulitis - stasis dermatitis, DVT, gout, contact dermatitis (mitigate: reassess at 48h, dermatology/vascular input); bacteremia/sepsis; progression to necrotizing fasciitis (mitigate: LRINEC, serial marked-border exams, low surgical threshold); C. difficile and antibiotic adverse effects (mitigate: narrow-spectrum, short course, stewardship); vancomycin-associated AKI (mitigate: level-guided dosing); recurrence; DVT from immobility/inflammation.
readmission_drivers: Premature discharge/inadequate treatment duration; inadequate source control (undrained abscess); misdiagnosis (non-infectious pseudocellulitis re-presenting); unaddressed predisposing factors (chronic edema/lymphedema, tinea pedis, venous insufficiency, obesity, poorly controlled diabetes); nonadherence or poor access to oral antibiotics; IV drug use. Prevention levers: 24-72h follow-up, treat entry portals and edema, prophylactic penicillin for recurrent leg cellulitis (PATCH I, NNT 5), diabetes/lymphedema management, and monitored OPAT.
guideline_source: IDSA 2014 - Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections (Stevens DL et al., Infectious Diseases Society of America, Clin Infect Dis 2014) - PRIMARY; remains the current authoritative U.S. SSTI guideline as of 2026. Supplemented by CMS SEP-1 for concurrent sepsis and Choosing Wisely / antimicrobial-stewardship principles.
key_citations: IDSA 2014 SSTI guideline: Stevens DL et al., Clin Infect Dis 2014;59(2):e10-52 (PMID 24947530; DOI 10.1093/cid/ciu296). Moran GJ et al., cephalexin +/- TMP-SMX for uncomplicated cellulitis, JAMA 2017;317(20):2088-96 (PMID 28535235; DOI 10.1001/jama.2017.5653). Talan DA et al., TMP-SMX vs placebo for drained abscess, NEJM 2016;374(9):823-32 (PMID 26962903; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1507476). Daum RS et al., antibiotics for smaller abscesses, NEJM 2017;376(26):2545-55 (PMID 28657870; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1607033). Wong CH et al., LRINEC score, Crit Care Med 2004;32(7):1535-41 (PMID 15241098; DOI 10.1097/01.ccm.0000129486.35458.7d). Thomas KS et al., PATCH I penicillin prophylaxis, NEJM 2013;368(18):1695-703 (PMID 23635049; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1206300). Cutfield T et al., Dundee classification validation, J Antimicrob Chemother 2019;74(1):200-6 (PMID 30295790; DOI 10.1093/jac/dky400). Citations retrieved via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong for core management - IDSA Class I society guideline reinforced by multiple RCTs (I&D as primary abscess therapy; adjunctive TMP-SMX/clindamycin improves abscess cure - Talan 2016, Daum 2017; no benefit of MRSA coverage in nonpurulent cellulitis - Moran 2017; prophylactic penicillin for recurrence - Thomas 2013). Moderate for triage/severity tools (Eron/Dundee observational validation) and LRINEC (retrospective, limited sensitivity). Overall: Strong - Class I guideline + RCT evidence, with moderate-strength operational triage instruments.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 603 (no CC/MCC): uncomplicated cellulitis, floor-level, rapid IV-to-PO, LOS ~3 days. DRG 602 (with MCC): MCC drivers = sepsis/septic shock, bacteremia, AKI, DKA/uncontrolled diabetes, respiratory failure -> ICU/step-down, ID consult, prolonged IV, LOS ~5 days, higher mortality/readmission (Dundee Class 4 ~9% 30-day mortality). CC-tier factors (diabetes with complications, PVD, obesity, chronic ulcers) modestly extend LOS. Note: necrotizing infection requiring operative debridement maps to separate surgical DRGs (e.g., 463-465 skin debridement), NOT the medical 602/603 pair.
pathway_pearls: 1) Classify purulent vs nonpurulent AND severity (Eron/Dundee) up front - it dictates I&D, MRSA coverage, and admit-vs-discharge. 2) Source control beats antibiotics: drain every abscess (I&D + culture) and get emergent surgery for necrotizing infection - antibiotics alone will not cure a collection. 3) Do not over-cover: nonpurulent cellulitis is strep/MSSA (IV cefazolin), adding MRSA coverage does not improve cure (Moran 2017), and routine blood cultures are low-yield - practice stewardship. 4) Reassess at 48h against marked borders; if not improving think pseudocellulitis (stasis dermatitis, DVT) or undrained abscess rather than reflexively broadening antibiotics. 5) Prevent bounce-back: IV-to-PO early, 5-day course, treat tinea/edema/lymphedema, and give prophylactic penicillin for recurrent leg cellulitis.

admission_criteria: Acute inpatient psychiatric admission for imminent danger to self (active suicidal ideation with plan/intent or recent attempt; C-SSRS 4-5), danger to others (homicidal ideation, threatening/assaultive behavior), or grave disability/inability to meet basic self-care needs due to psychosis or mania; also acute psychosis/manic episode with impaired insight and judgment, catatonia, command hallucinations, first-episode psychosis needing stabilization, medically-supervised initiation of high-risk agents (clozapine, lithium, ECT), or failure of outpatient/crisis/PHP stabilization; involuntary hold per state civil-commitment statute (typ. 72h emergency hold for danger-to-self/others/gravely disabled); ED discharge or observation appropriate for passive ideation without plan, strong protective factors, engaged outpatient care, and a completed safety plan; use structured risk assessment, not clinical gestalt alone
risk_stratification: Columbia-Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS): active ideation with intent/plan (cat 4-5) or recent attempt -> admit + 1:1 continuous observation; ideation with method, no plan -> close observation; Joint Commission NPSG 15.01.01 mandates validated suicide screen on all behavioral-health patients; Brost Violence Checklist (BVC) >=2 or DASA elevated -> imminent-aggression precautions/de-escalation; Young Mania Rating Scale (YMRS) >=20 = moderate-severe mania (track >=50% reduction as response); PANSS/BPRS to grade and trend psychosis; CIWA-Ar >=8-10 (alcohol) or COWS (opioid) drives withdrawal treatment given high SUD comorbidity; AIMS for tardive dyskinesia; baseline BMI/waist for metabolic risk
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential incl baseline ANC (must be >=1500/uL before clozapine); CMP (electrolytes, renal, hepatic); TSH (thyroid-driven mood/psychosis); fasting glucose/HbA1c and fasting lipid panel (baseline metabolic monitoring per ADA/APA consensus before/with antipsychotic); urine drug screen + blood alcohol (substance-induced psychosis/mania common) -> stimulant/positive screen reframes dx; urine or serum hCG in people of childbearing potential BEFORE valproate/lithium/carbamazepine (teratogenicity); lithium and valproate levels if already prescribed; CK if agitated/restrained (rhabdo) and to help flag NMS; prolactin baseline if risperidone/paliperidone; B12/folate; RPR/HIV and ammonia (valproate encephalopathy) if indicated
initial_imaging_dx: Neuroimaging is NOT routine for typical psychiatric presentation; obtain CT head or preferably MRI brain within 24-48h ONLY for first-episode psychosis with atypical features, new focal neuro deficit, new psychosis age >40, delirium, catatonia, or exam red flags; baseline 12-lead ECG within 24h for QTc before/after QT-prolonging antipsychotics (goal QTc <500 ms; hold/switch agent if >500); EEG for seizure, catatonia (exclude nonconvulsive status), or suspected autoimmune/limbic encephalitis; anti-NMDA-receptor antibody and paraneoplastic workup for atypical first-episode psychosis with neuro/autonomic signs
time_critical_interventions: Agitation (Project BETA): verbal de-escalation FIRST, target calming within minutes without oversedation, oral meds preferred; undifferentiated agitation -> lorazepam 2 mg; psychotic agitation -> 2nd-gen antipsychotic (olanzapine 10 mg PO/IM, risperidone 2 mg PO, ziprasidone/droperidol IM) +/- benzodiazepine (do NOT combine IM olanzapine + IM benzodiazepine -> cardioresp depression); high suicide risk -> initiate 1:1 line-of-sight observation and ligature/sharps/contraband removal within minutes; catatonia -> lorazepam 1-2 mg IV/IM challenge (response confirms dx), ECT if malignant; NMS (hyperthermia, rigidity, autonomic instability, high CK) -> STOP antipsychotic immediately, supportive care, dantrolene/bromocriptine, consider ECT; acute dystonia -> benztropine/diphenhydramine IM; akathisia -> propranolol/dose reduction; restraint/seclusion only when imminent danger and less-restrictive measures fail, time-limited with continuous monitoring and post-event debrief (HBIPS-2/-3 hours minimized)
initial_management: Schizophrenia/psychosis: 2nd-gen antipsychotic MONOTHERAPY first-line (risperidone, olanzapine, aripiprazole, quetiapine, paliperidone) chosen by side-effect profile/prior response, titrated to effective dose, avoid routine polypharmacy; treatment-resistant (failed >=2 adequate antipsychotic trials) -> CLOZAPINE (superior efficacy, Kane 1988) under REMS ANC monitoring (weekly x6mo, then q2wk x6mo, then monthly; hold ANC <1000, stop <500); long-acting injectable antipsychotic for nonadherence. Acute mania (CANMAT/ISBD first-line): lithium, quetiapine, divalproex, asenapine, aripiprazole, paliperidone, risperidone, or cariprazine as monotherapy, OR lithium/divalproex + antipsychotic for severe mania; STOP antidepressants; lithium 0.8-1.2 mEq/L, valproate 50-125 ug/mL. Bipolar depression first-line: quetiapine, lurasidone (+ lithium/divalproex), lithium, lamotrigine, or cariprazine; AVOID antidepressant monotherapy. Adjunct short-course benzodiazepine for agitation/insomnia then taper; ECT for refractory mania, psychotic depression, catatonia, malignant NMS, or high suicide risk; treat comorbid withdrawal (CIWA-guided benzodiazepine, buprenorphine for OUD); reconcile and restart home psychotropics
day1_milestones: Complete psychiatric evaluation + collateral history; structured suicide and violence risk assessment (C-SSRS, BVC); establish provisional diagnosis and legal status (voluntary vs involuntary hold documented); set observation level (1:1 / close / q15-min) and environmental safety (unit/contraband/ligature search); start or adjust index pharmacotherapy; draw baseline labs, metabolic panel, ECG, UDS and pregnancy test; begin least-restrictive trauma-informed milieu and orient patient; notify family/collateral with consent; START discharge planning on Day 1 (insurance, housing, outpatient provider)
day2_milestones: Reassess with scales (YMRS/PANSS/BPRS) and re-screen suicide/violence risk; evaluate medication response and tolerability (EPS via AIMS, akathisia, sedation, orthostasis); titrate to therapeutic dose and recheck lithium/valproate level; de-escalate observation level as safety improves; engage patient in group therapy/OT; hold multidisciplinary treatment-team meeting and set estimated discharge date; social work active on disposition, benefits, and housing; begin arranging aftercare with warm-handoff plan
day3plus_milestones: Trend toward remission (target >=50% reduction YMRS/PANSS), behavioral control, no agitation/restraint; confirm therapeutic drug levels, tolerable side effects, and an adherence plan (consider LAI); finalize collaborative Stanley-Brown safety plan with lethal-means counseling; deliver relapse-prevention psychoeducation and family meeting; LOCK an aftercare appointment within 7 days with warm handoff; arrange housing/transport, filled or bridge medications, and community supports (ACT/case management, peer support); resolve legal-hold/capacity issues; complete discharge-readiness assessment
consults_multidisciplinary: Attending psychiatry daily; psychiatric social work Day 1 (disposition, benefits, housing, guardianship); case management/care coordination for transitions; clinical pharmacy for med reconciliation and clozapine/lithium/valproate/QTc and interaction monitoring; psychology for therapy/neuropsych testing; occupational therapy for functional assessment and groups; peer-support specialist; hospitalist/internal medicine for medical comorbidity, abnormal labs, or withdrawal; addiction medicine for co-occurring SUD; neurology if organic etiology suspected; nutrition for metabolic risk; chaplaincy; involve family/guardian and APS if vulnerable adult
monitoring_level: Most patients: locked psychiatric unit with tiered behavioral observation (1:1 continuous / line-of-sight / q15-min) driven by suicide and violence risk; medical monitoring on telemetry or med-surg for QTc >500 ms or arrhythmia, NMS, serotonin syndrome, severe withdrawal/DTs, lithium toxicity, or unstable vitals/overdose; escalation triggers to medical/ICU: hyperthermia with rigidity (NMS), hemodynamic instability, declining level of consciousness, seizure, refractory agitation, or deteriorating medical status; on clozapine initiation monitor for myocarditis, seizures, orthostasis, and ileus
nutrition_mobility_vte: Regular diet with nutrition consult for metabolic-syndrome risk; encourage activity/exercise groups and structured milieu; offer smoking cessation with NRT (TOB measure); metabolic monitoring on antipsychotics: weight/BMI, and fasting glucose and lipids at baseline, 12 weeks, then annually per ADA/APA 2004 consensus; VTE prophylaxis usually unnecessary in ambulatory psychiatric patients but REQUIRED (mechanical +/- pharmacologic) with prolonged restraint/seclusion, catatonia, or heavy sedation given real immobility risk; bowel regimen for clozapine (constipation/ileus); fall precautions with sedation/orthostasis; minimize lines/catheters (rarely indicated)
discharge_criteria: No longer an imminent danger to self or others (suicidal/homicidal ideation resolved or manageable with an active safety plan); psychosis or mania sufficiently stabilized with improved reality testing and behavioral control; adherent to and tolerating a stable medication regimen with therapeutic drug levels and manageable side effects; medically stable; collaborative safety plan completed; adequate outpatient follow-up and social supports arranged; safe discharge environment confirmed; capacity to participate in aftercare
discharge_planning: Disposition matched to acuity: home with outpatient mental health, PHP/IOP step-down, ACT team, crisis/residential stabilization, or supported housing; medication reconciliation with bridge/starter supply and clear written instructions, LAI administered before discharge if chosen; SCHEDULE follow-up appointment within 7 days (confirm an actual appointment, not just a referral; warm handoff) to satisfy FUH/FAPH; transmit transition record (TR) with diagnoses, medications, follow-up, and warning signs to patient and outpatient provider; lethal-means safety counseling, 988 crisis line, relapse warning signs, and patient/family psychoeducation; enroll case management/peer support; address SDOH (housing, transport, benefits, food)
expected_los: DRG 885 acute-care Medicare GMLOS ~5.4 days (AMLOS ~7 days); real-world dedicated inpatient psychiatric-unit stays for acute schizophrenia/manic episodes typically run 7-11 days, so ~6-8 days is a realistic typical benchmark
target_los: ~5-7 days under an optimized pathway (Day-1 discharge planning, rapid protocolized stabilization, warm handoff), while avoiding premature discharge that raises readmission and post-discharge suicide risk
quality_metrics: CMS IPFQR: HBIPS-2 (hours of physical restraint), HBIPS-3 (hours of seclusion), HBIPS-5 (multiple antipsychotics at discharge with appropriate justification), TR (transition record), SMD (screening for metabolic disorders), SUB-2/3 (alcohol/substance use), TOB-2/3 (tobacco), IMM-2 (influenza), Follow-Up After Psychiatric Hospitalization (FAPH), 30-day all-cause unplanned readmission following psychiatric hospitalization, Medication Continuation Following Inpatient Psychiatric Discharge, FCHE (health equity); Joint Commission NPSG 15.01.01 (suicide risk reduction); HEDIS FUH 7-day and 30-day follow-up after hospitalization for mental illness
common_complications: Agitation/aggression and restraint/seclusion-related injury (mitigate with BETA de-escalation and BVC screening); antipsychotic side effects: EPS/acute dystonia/akathisia (benztropine, propranolol, dose adjust), NMS (early recognition, stop agent), metabolic syndrome/weight gain, QTc prolongation/arrhythmia (ECG monitoring), sedation/falls, anticholinergic delirium; clozapine: agranulocytosis (ANC/REMS), myocarditis, ileus, seizures; lithium toxicity; valproate hyperammonemia/thrombocytopenia; lamotrigine SJS (slow titration); inpatient suicide/self-harm (environmental safety + observation); missed medical illness or delirium masquerading as psychiatric (thorough workup)
readmission_drivers: Medication nonadherence (top lever -> long-acting injectable antipsychotics, simplified regimens, adherence supports); inadequate or delayed outpatient follow-up (-> appointment within 7 days confirmed before discharge, warm handoff); untreated co-occurring substance use (-> integrated dual-diagnosis care and MAT); premature discharge/incomplete stabilization; social instability - homelessness, poverty, poor support (-> SDOH intervention, case management, ACT, supported housing); low insight and family disengagement; the immediate post-discharge period is the peak suicide-risk window (-> safety plan, caring contacts, 988)
guideline_source: APA Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Patients With Schizophrenia, 3rd edition (2020); CANMAT/ISBD 2018 Guidelines for the Management of Bipolar Disorder (with 2023 evidence update); AAEP Project BETA (Best Practices in Evaluation and Treatment of Agitation, 2012); CMS Inpatient Psychiatric Facility Quality Reporting (IPFQR)/Joint Commission HBIPS measures and NPSG 15.01.01; APA bipolar guideline (2002) is dated, so CANMAT/ISBD is the primary bipolar reference
key_citations: Keepers GA et al. The APA Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Patients With Schizophrenia. Am J Psychiatry 2020;177(9):868-872 (PMID 32867516; DOI 10.1176/appi.ajp.2020.177901); Yatham LN et al. CANMAT and ISBD 2018 guidelines for the management of patients with bipolar disorder. Bipolar Disord 2018;20(2):97-170 (PMID 29536616; DOI 10.1111/bdi.12609); Keramatian K et al. CANMAT/ISBD Guidelines: 2023 Update of Evidence. Focus 2023;21(4):344-353 (PMID 38695002; DOI 10.1176/appi.focus.20230009); Kane J et al. Clozapine for the treatment-resistant schizophrenic: double-blind comparison with chlorpromazine. Arch Gen Psychiatry 1988;45(9):789-796 (PMID 3046553; DOI 10.1001/archpsyc.1988.01800330013001); Lieberman JA et al. (CATIE) Effectiveness of antipsychotic drugs in chronic schizophrenia. N Engl J Med 2005;353:1209-1223 (PMID 16172203; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa051688); AAEP Project BETA psychopharmacology consensus, West J Emerg Med 2012
evidence_grade: Strong for core pharmacotherapy - Class I / Level 1 RCT evidence plus APA 2020 and CANMAT/ISBD 2018 society guidelines; clozapine for treatment-resistant schizophrenia is Level 1. Moderate for milieu, LOS, and transition-of-care elements (quality-measure and observational evidence). Consensus/expert opinion for acute-agitation management (Project BETA) and for operational suicide-risk observation thresholds (validated screens have modest positive predictive value)
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MS-DRG 885 is a single collapsed DRG with no CC/MCC subdivision (psychiatric DRGs are not severity-tiered like medical DRGs, and most true acute psychiatric stays are paid under IPF-PPS per diem rather than IPPS); nonetheless clinical acuity shifts the pathway markedly: catatonia, NMS, severe agitation requiring restraint, comorbid substance withdrawal/DTs, medical instability, treatment resistance needing clozapine initiation, or an ECT course all drive longer LOS, medical co-management or ICU transfer, and higher complication risk; co-occurring medical illness moves monitoring to telemetry/medical; and involuntary/forensic status or placement barriers (no housing/bed) create administrative days that extend LOS well beyond clinical readiness
pathway_pearls: 1) Screen suicide AND violence risk on arrival with validated tools (C-SSRS, BVC) to set observation level and environmental safety - the highest-stakes lever, as the inpatient stay and the days right after discharge are peak-risk; 2) Begin disposition planning Day 1 and LOCK a follow-up appointment within 7 days with a warm handoff (FUH) - the single biggest readmission-prevention and quality-measure lever; 3) Adherence is the crux - use long-acting injectable antipsychotics for nonadherent/relapsing patients, simplify the regimen, and treat co-occurring substance use with integrated care; 4) Right drug + right monitoring - SGA monotherapy first-line, do not delay clozapine in treatment resistance, track lithium/valproate levels, metabolic panel and QTc, and always attempt verbal de-escalation before chemical or physical restraint to minimize HBIPS restraint/seclusion hours

scope_and_volume_notes: HCUP #16 principal dx (schizophrenia/psychotic disorders, 0.40M, 2018) plus bipolar. Single high-volume DRG (885); a top-5 Medicare DRG. Includes acute inpatient psychiatric stays.

admission_criteria: Objective inpatient/ICU indications: acute respiratory failure on ABG - Type 1 hypoxemic (PaO2 <60 mmHg or SpO2 <90% on room air, or PaO2/FiO2 <300) or Type 2 hypercapnic (PaCO2 >45 mmHg with pH <7.35); new or escalating O2 requirement to hold SpO2 >=88-90%; respiratory distress with RR >30, accessory-muscle use, or paradoxical breathing; need for HFNC/NIV/invasive ventilation; impaired airway protection or GCS <=8. ED discharge/observation only for brief, fully reversible hypoxemia normalizing on <=2-3 L NC with a treated cause and no acidosis. ICU admit for intubation, NIV dependence, high-FiO2 HFNC, shock, or pH <7.30; step-down for stable NIV/weaning.
risk_stratification: PaO2/FiO2 (Berlin/ARDS: mild 200-300, moderate 100-200, severe <100) and SpO2/FiO2 <315 (2023 Global ARDS definition) grade hypoxemic severity and trigger prone/NMB/ECMO consideration; HACOR score >5 at 1 h predicts NIV failure -> escalate to intubation; ROX index [(SpO2/FiO2)/RR] <3.85 predicts HFNC failure while >=4.88 at 2/6/12 h is reassuring; qSOFA >=2 / SOFA for sepsis-associated ARF; CURB-65 >=2 or PSI class IV-V (pneumonia overlay); DECAF >=3 for AECOPD mortality; APACHE II for ICU mortality; GCS <=8 mandates intubation for airway protection.
initial_workup_labs: STAT arterial blood gas (defines type/severity, selects device) with serial ABG/VBG; CBC, CMP/BMP, lactate (sepsis/hypoperfusion); BNP or NT-proBNP (<100 pg/mL makes cardiogenic edema unlikely); high-sensitivity troponin and ECG; respiratory viral PCR panel (influenza, RSV, SARS-CoV-2); procalcitonin to support bacterial/antibiotic decisions; blood and sputum cultures pre-antibiotics; D-dimer if PE suspected; consider TSH and toxicology; type and screen. Triggers: acidosis/hypercapnia -> NIV; rising lactate -> sepsis bundle; elevated BNP/troponin -> cardiac workup and diuresis.
initial_imaging_dx: Portable chest X-ray STAT (bilateral infiltrates -> ARDS, cephalization/effusion -> cardiogenic edema, consolidation -> pneumonia, lucency -> pneumothorax); bedside point-of-care lung/cardiac ultrasound within minutes (B-lines, effusion, pneumothorax, gross LV/RV function); CT pulmonary angiography if PE suspected (Wells + D-dimer, or direct if high pretest); transthoracic echocardiography within 24-48 h to separate cardiogenic from non-cardiogenic edema and assess RV; bronchoscopy if diagnostic sampling or mucus-plug clearance needed.
time_critical_interventions: Secure airway immediately for apnea, GCS <=8, or refractory hypoxemia/distress. Start NIV within the first hour for AECOPD with pH <=7.35/PaCO2 >45 and CPAP/NIV for cardiogenic pulmonary edema (Class I); reassess at 1-2 h - if HACOR >5, worsening pH/PaCO2, or ROX <3.85, intubate without delay. From intubation, apply lung-protective ventilation (Vt 6 mL/kg predicted body weight, Pplat <30, driving pressure <15) immediately. If sepsis-associated, run SEP-1: lactate, cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1 h, 30 mL/kg crystalloid. Early prone positioning within 12-36 h for moderate-severe ARDS with P/F <150.
initial_management: Titrate oxygen to SpO2 88-92% if hypercapnic risk / 92-96% otherwise, avoiding hyperoxia (ICU-ROX); device by phenotype - HFNC for de novo hypoxemic ARF (FLORALI), BiPAP for hypercapnic AECOPD, CPAP for cardiogenic edema. Treat the cause: bronchodilators (SABA albuterol + SAMA ipratropium) plus systemic corticosteroid (prednisone 40 mg x5 d; dexamethasone for COVID) for COPD; guideline antibiotics for pneumonia (ATS/IDSA); IV loop diuretic (furosemide) +/- nitrates for cardiogenic edema; anticoagulation for PE. For ARDS: lung-protective ventilation with PEEP/FiO2 titration, conservative fluids after resuscitation (FACTT), neuromuscular blockade (cisatracurium) for severe P/F <150 with dyssynchrony, prone >=16 h/day; analgesia-first light sedation (RASS 0 to -2). VV-ECMO referral (EOLIA/CESAR criteria) for refractory severe ARDS.
day1_milestones: Stabilize oxygenation/ventilation and confirm/treat etiology; lock in lung-protective settings with PEEP titration; start definitive therapy (antibiotics/steroids/bronchodilators/diuretics); begin conservative fluid strategy once perfused; initiate VAP bundle (HOB 30-45 degrees, oral care, sedation targets) and daily SAT/SBT screening; VTE prophylaxis and stress-ulcer prophylaxis if indicated; set analgosedation goal RASS 0 to -2; de-escalate O2 device if improving; document goals of care.
day2_milestones: Reassess P/F and wean FiO2/PEEP; perform paired spontaneous awakening + spontaneous breathing trial; assess extubation readiness (passes SBT, RSBI <105, adequate cough/secretion clearance, cuff leak, hemodynamic stability, FiO2 <=0.4 and PEEP <=5); narrow antibiotics per cultures/procalcitonin; start early enteral nutrition (24-48 h) and early mobilization/PT; screen for and treat delirium while minimizing benzodiazepines; if on NIV, trial weaning windows.
day3plus_milestones: Liberate from ventilator after a successful SBT; apply prophylactic post-extubation HFNC/NIV in high-risk patients to prevent reintubation; continue ABCDEF/ICU-liberation bundle with progressive mobility and delirium management; transfer ICU-to-floor when off advanced support and stable; for failure to wean (>=10-14 days or repeated failed extubations - the DRG 207 trajectory) evaluate for tracheostomy and passy-muir valve; address post-ICU syndrome and begin disposition/discharge planning.
consults_multidisciplinary: Pulmonary/critical care intensivist (primary) and respiratory therapy (ventilator management, weaning protocols) from admission; infectious disease for complex/resistant infection; cardiology if cardiogenic; nephrology if AKI/RRT; PT/OT for early mobility (Day 1-2); speech-language pathology for post-extubation swallow and trach/PMV; dietitian for early enteral nutrition; pharmacy for antimicrobial stewardship and sedation; care management/social work early for disposition (home O2, SNF, LTACH); palliative care for goals-of-care in prolonged ventilation or poor prognosis.
monitoring_level: ICU for intubated patients, NIV dependence, high-FiO2 HFNC, shock, pH <7.30, or high HACOR/low ROX; intermediate/step-down for stable NIV or active weaning; telemetry floor once off advanced support with SpO2 stable on <=4 L NC and no progressive hypercapnia. Escalation triggers: rising FiO2 or PaCO2, falling pH, RR >30, SpO2 <88%, declining mental status, or hemodynamic instability - activate rapid response and reassess for intubation.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Early enteral nutrition within 24-48 h (trophic or full both acceptable - EDEN), avoiding overfeeding; early mobilization/PT even while intubated (reduces ICU-acquired weakness and delirium); VTE prophylaxis with LMWH (enoxaparin) or UFH, mechanical SCDs if pharmacologic prophylaxis contraindicated (high VTE risk); glucose 140-180 mg/dL (NICE-SUGAR); daily assessment for line/Foley/catheter removal (CLABSI/CAUTI prevention); stress-ulcer prophylaxis (PPI/H2) for mechanical ventilation >48 h or coagulopathy.
discharge_criteria: Liberated from mechanical ventilation with stable gas exchange; SpO2 >=88-90% on room air or an established home O2 regimen for >=24 h; pH/PaCO2 returned to patient baseline (no acidosis); underlying cause treated/improving with hemodynamic stability and afebrile; able to clear secretions and protect airway; tolerating oral intake, mobilizing at or near baseline, delirium resolved to baseline; home oxygen qualified and arranged if resting PaO2 <=55 mmHg or SpO2 <=88%.
discharge_planning: Disposition - home with/without home O2 and home health, SNF, inpatient rehab, or LTACH for ventilator-dependent/prolonged-weaning patients (DRG 207); medication reconciliation with inhaler-technique teach-back, steroid taper, antibiotic course, and diuretic/anticoagulation plans; arrange and qualify home oxygen; schedule PCP follow-up within 7 days and pulmonology within 1-2 weeks; refer to pulmonary rehabilitation; smoking-cessation counseling and vaccination (influenza, pneumococcal, COVID); disease-specific action plan (COPD/HF self-care) with written warning signs and teach-back.
expected_los: Varies by DRG in the collapsed group: DRG 189 (pulmonary edema and respiratory failure, no invasive vent) GMLOS ~3.8-4.2 days; DRG 208 (respiratory diagnosis with ventilator support <=96 h) GMLOS ~4.9-5.4 days; DRG 207 (ventilator support >96 h / prolonged) GMLOS ~12.7-13.7 days.
target_los: DRG 189 target 3-4 days; DRG 208 target 4-5 days; DRG 207 target 10-12 days - achieved via daily paired SAT/SBT, conservative fluids, early mobility, and early tracheostomy/LTACH planning to compress ventilator days.
quality_metrics: SEP-1 if sepsis-associated; low-tidal-volume/lung-protective ventilation compliance; spontaneous breathing trial and ABCDEF-bundle compliance; ventilator-free days; CDC Ventilator-Associated Event (VAE/VAP) rate; reintubation rate <48-96 h; risk-adjusted ICU/hospital mortality; VTE prophylaxis rate; CLABSI/CAUTI/C. diff rates; 30-day all-cause and respiratory readmission; IMM-2/influenza and pneumococcal immunization; tobacco-cessation (TOB) intervention; advance-care-planning documentation.
common_complications: Ventilator-associated pneumonia (mitigated by VAP bundle, early SBT/extubation); barotrauma/pneumothorax (lung-protective ventilation); ICU-acquired weakness/critical illness myopathy (early mobility, minimize NMB/sedation); delirium (ABCDEF bundle, avoid benzodiazepines); VTE/PE (prophylaxis); extubation failure/reintubation (SBT-based liberation, prophylactic post-extubation HFNC/NIV); fluid overload (conservative fluids); nosocomial infection (CLABSI/CAUTI/C. diff), pressure injury (prone-position care), and right-heart failure in severe ARDS.
readmission_drivers: Under-optimized underlying disease (COPD/HF) with inadequate inhaler/diuretic/O2 regimen; premature discharge with unresolved hypercapnia; poor inhaler technique and non-adherence; missed early follow-up and no pulmonary rehab; recurrent infection, continued smoking, inadequate home O2. Prevention levers: disease-specific bundles, medication reconciliation with teach-back, follow-up <=7 days, pulmonary-rehab referral, home health, vaccination, and smoking cessation.
guideline_source: ERS/ATS Clinical Practice Guidelines - Noninvasive Ventilation for Acute Respiratory Failure (2017); ESICM Guidelines on ARDS (2023); ATS/ESICM/SCCM Mechanical Ventilation in Adult ARDS (2017) with SCCM/ATS updates; ACCP/ATS Liberation from Mechanical Ventilation in Critically Ill Adults (2017); A New Global Definition of ARDS (2023/2024); overlays: GOLD 2025 (COPD), Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021, SCCM PADIS/ICU-Liberation 2018.
key_citations: ERS/ATS NIV guideline, Eur Respir J 2017 (PMID 28860265); ESICM ARDS guidelines, Intensive Care Med 2023 (PMID 37326646); A New Global Definition of ARDS, Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2024 (PMID 37487152); ARDSNet ARMA low tidal volume, NEJM 2000 (PMID 10793162); PROSEVA prone positioning, NEJM 2013 (PMID 23688302); FLORALI HFNC, NEJM 2015 (PMID 25981908); ACURASYS cisatracurium, NEJM 2010 (PMID 20843245); ROSE neuromuscular blockade, NEJM 2019 (PMID 31112383); ICU-ROX conservative oxygen, NEJM 2020 (PMID 31613432); ROX index, Roca et al, Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2019 (PMID 30576221).
evidence_grade: Strong - core oxygenation/ventilation levers (lung-protective ventilation, prone positioning, NIV for AECOPD/cardiogenic edema, HFNC for hypoxemic ARF, SBT-based liberation) rest on multiple Class I RCTs and current ATS/ERS/ESICM/ACCP guidelines. Moderate for etiology-specific management, because respiratory failure is a heterogeneous severity overlay whose treatment is extrapolated from condition-specific guidelines (pneumonia, COPD, HF, sepsis).
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The collapsed group spans a wide severity gradient. Acute respiratory failure (J96.0x) is itself an MCC that drives DRG assignment and weight. DRG 189 (no invasive vent) is managed with O2/HFNC/NIV at shorter LOS (~4 days); DRG 208 (vent <=96 h) and DRG 207 (vent >96 h) reflect invasive ventilation, with 207 (prolonged vent) carrying the longest LOS (~13 days), highest cost/mortality, and frequent tracheostomy + LTACH disposition. Added MCCs (ARDS, septic shock, AKI/RRT) mandate ICU, prolong ventilator days, and raise mortality; prolonged mechanical ventilation >=96 h and tracheostomy are the dominant LOS/cost drivers.
pathway_pearls: (1) Classify the failure early by ABG (Type 1 hypoxemic vs Type 2 hypercapnic) and match device to phenotype - CPAP/NIV for cardiogenic edema and hypercapnic AECOPD (Class I), HFNC for de novo hypoxemic ARF - with a hard 1-2 h reassessment (HACOR >5 / ROX <3.85) so a failing trial never delays intubation. (2) From intubation, lock in lung-protective ventilation (6 mL/kg PBW, Pplat <30, driving pressure <15) and prone >=16 h/day for P/F <150 - the highest mortality-impact levers. (3) Run the ABCDEF/ICU-liberation bundle daily (paired SAT+SBT, light analgosedation, delirium prevention, early mobility, conservative fluids) to convert ventilator days into ventilator-free days and avoid the DRG 207 trajectory. (4) Treat the cause, not just the gas exchange, and build the disease-specific discharge bundle (inhaler teaching, home O2 qualification, <=7-day follow-up, pulmonary rehab, vaccination, smoking cessation) to prevent bounce-back.
verification_notes: Verified via PubMed MCP (get_article_metadata on all 10 PMIDs) and WebSearch/abstract review; PubMed cited as source. CITATIONS: all 10 PMIDs are genuine and correctly attributed to the correct trial name, journal, and year (spot-checked authors: Brower/Guerin/Frat/Papazian/Moss/Mackle/Roca/Rochwerg/Grasselli/Matthay) — ICU-ROX correctly cited as NEJM 2020 (vol 382 print; online 2019). RISK SCORES: HACOR >5 at 1h (real, confirmed via MDCalc/ESICM/Duan) and ROX index formula (SpO2/FiO2)/RR with <3.85 failure and >=4.88 at 2/6/12h reassuring (real, confirmed via Roca 2019/MDCalc) both correct; Berlin P/F strata, SpO2/FiO2 <=315 (confirmed in Global Definition abstract), DECAF >=3, CURB-65 >=2, PSI IV-V, qSOFA >=2, GCS <=8 all correct. TIME-CRITICAL: 6 mL/kg PBW, Pplat <30, driving pressure <15, NIV within 1h, SEP-1 abx <=1h + 30 mL/kg, prone for P/F <150 all current/correct. MANAGEMENT: O2 88-92%/92-96%, prednisone 40mg x5d (GOLD), cisatracurium reserved for severe ARDS + dyssynchrony (appropriately nuanced post-ROSE), prone >=16h/day (PROSEVA), VV-ECMO EOLIA/CESAR all correct; no transfusion claim made (appropriate). LOS: DRG titles confirmed (189 pulmonary edema/resp failure, 207 vent >96h, 208 vent <=96h); ranges plausible vs benchmarks. GUIDELINES: ERS/ATS NIV 2017, ESICM ARDS 2023, ATS/ESICM/SCCM Mech Vent 2017, ACCP/ATS Liberation 2017, Global Definition 2023/24, GOLD 2025, SSC 2021, PADIS 2018 all real and current. QUALITY METRICS (SEP-1, VAE/VAP, ABCDEF, ventilator-free days, reintubation, IMM-2, TOB, CLABSI/CAUTI) all real/applicable. Row is accurate and well-sourced; no corrections warranted.; ISSUES: No material/high-stakes errors found. All 10 key_citations PMIDs verified against PubMed as real, correctly titled, and correctly attributed to the named trial/guideline (ARMA 10793162, PROSEVA 23688302, FLORALI 25981908, ACURASYS 20843245, ROSE 31112383, ICU-ROX 31613432, ROX index 30576221, ERS/ATS NIV 28860265, ESICM ARDS 37326646, Global Definition 37487152). No fabrication or misattribution.; Trivial (non-blocking): the 2023 Global ARDS Definition uses SpO2/FiO2 <=315 (with SpO2 <=97%); the row writes it as strict '<315'. Threshold value and source are otherwise correct (confirmed against the definition abstract: HFNC >=30 L/min, PaO2/FiO2 <=300 or SpO2/FiO2 <=315).; Minor (non-blocking): exact CMS GMLOS values for DRG 189/207/208 sit behind paywalls, so the decimal figures were not independently confirmed; however the DRG titles were confirmed and the hedged '~' ranges (207 ~13d, 208 ~5d, 189 ~4d) are consistent with established national benchmarks and are plausible.
