admission_criteria: Mostly ELECTIVE, procedure-driven admission (not a medical decision rule); status set by 2-Midnight Rule + case complexity since CMS removed most lumbar (22630/22633) & cervical fusions from the Inpatient-Only list (2021-2022). Inpatient warranted for: multilevel (>=2-3 levels) or combined anterior/posterior/deformity-scoliosis correction; anticipated blood loss/transfusion; prolonged prone/anterior op time; ASA III-IV, significant OSA/cardiopulmonary disease, frailty, advanced age; expected stay crossing 2 midnights. Outpatient/ASC or 23-h observation appropriate for selected low-risk single-level ACDF or single-level MIS-TLIF/lateral. URGENT/EMERGENT inpatient (non-elective subset of DRG family): traumatic instability, cauda equina syndrome, epidural abscess, progressive neuro deficit, pathologic/metastatic cord compression
risk_stratification: ASA Physical Status (III-IV -> optimize, step-down/ICU consideration); mFI-5 modified 5-item Frailty Index (CHF, diabetes, COPD, dependent functional status, HTN-on-meds) -> score >=2 flags high risk (NSQIP n=53,252: medical complications 14.9%, surgical 2.9%, 30-d readmission 6.3%, mortality 0.68%) -> prehab/optimization; Risk Analysis Index (RAI) frailty (validated, outperforms mFI in some spine cohorts); ACS-NSQIP Surgical Risk Calculator (procedure-specific % SSI/complication/readmission/discharge-to-facility); Apfel score 0-4 -> PONV prophylaxis intensity; Caprini score -> VTE prophylaxis tier; STOP-BANG >=3 -> OSA postop monitoring; RCRI for cardiac risk; anemia screen (Hgb <13 g/dL men / <12 women; ERAS target Hgb 13 g/dL for complex cases) -> preop iron/EPO
initial_workup_labs: CBC (baseline Hgb/Hct/platelets; screen & correct anemia, ERAS target Hgb >=13 g/dL complex cases); BMP (renal function/glucose); PT/INR-aPTT if anticoagulated or bleeding risk; Type & Screen for 1-2 level, Type & Crossmatch >=2 units for multilevel/combined/deformity; HbA1c (target <7-8%; hyperglycemia predicts SSI); albumin/prealbumin (hypoalbuminemia <3.5 g/dL predicts SSI/complications -> nutrition consult); urinalysis if symptomatic (treat UTI before instrumentation); pregnancy test; ECG per age/cardiac risk; MRSA screen where protocolized
initial_imaging_dx: Standing AP/lateral +/- flexion-extension radiographs (instability, alignment, sagittal balance); MRI = gold standard for neural compression/stenosis/disc/cord; CT for bony anatomy & pedicle/screw planning and fusion assessment; CT-myelogram if MRI contraindicated; DEXA if osteoporosis suspected (screw purchase, consider cement augmentation). Intraop: navigation/O-arm & neuromonitoring (SSEP/MEP/EMG) for deformity, cervical, or high cord-risk cases. Preop medical clearance; TTE/cardiology if RCRI elevated. Elective/pre-scheduled timing; EMERGENT MRI (hours) only for cauda equina, epidural abscess, or acute cord compression
time_critical_interventions: SSI-prevention bundle: cefazolin <=60 min before incision (ERAS High/Strong), redose q4h intraop or after >1500 mL blood loss; chlorhexidine-alcohol skin prep; TXA loading dose before incision; maintain normothermia >=36C (pre-warm + forced-air) and glucose <180-200 mg/dL; surgical safety time-out/checklist; mechanical VTE prophylaxis (SCDs) at induction. For EMERGENT subset: cauda equina syndrome decompression ideally <24-48h; epidural abscess with deficit -> urgent decompression; traumatic incomplete SCI/cord compression -> early decompression <24h improves neuro recovery
initial_management: Preop multimodal pre-emptive analgesia: acetaminophen 1000 mg PO + gabapentinoid (gabapentin 300-600 mg or pregabalin 75-150 mg) + NSAID/COX-2 (celecoxib 200-400 mg) (ERAS Moderate/Strong); avoid routine benzodiazepines. Antibiotic: cefazolin 2 g IV (3 g if >=120 kg) <60 min pre-incision (vancomycin if MRSA/beta-lactam allergy; +/- intrawound vancomycin powder). Intraop: balanced GA + neuromuscular blockade; TXA 15 mg/kg load + 2nd dose ~3h later or infusion (two-dose reduces total/hidden blood loss, Zhu 2020 RCT); near-euvolemic balanced crystalloid, goal-directed for comorbid/complex; intrathecal morphine or long-acting local wound infiltration (ropivacaine/liposomal bupivacaine) +/- ESP/regional block; PONV: dexamethasone 4-8 mg + ondansetron; opioid-sparing ketamine +/- lidocaine infusion. Restrictive transfusion trigger Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if cardiac disease/symptomatic). No routine wound drain for short-segment fusion. Source control (debridement + culture-directed antibiotics) for infection cases
day1_milestones: POD0: extubate in OR/PACU (watch airway for anterior cervical); document intact motor/sensory neuro exam as baseline; scheduled multimodal analgesia (acetaminophen + NSAID/COX-2 + gabapentinoid ATC, opioid PRN only); MOBILIZE same day - out of bed and ambulate >=10 m/30 ft (ERAS Strong); start clear liquids, advance diet as tolerated; PONV control; mechanical VTE prophylaxis (SCDs); glucose <180; avoid/early-remove urinary catheter; incentive spirometry; screen single-level ACDF/lumbar for same-day or next-morning discharge
day2_milestones: POD1: PT/OT evaluation, ambulate TID and stairs as able; transition IV to oral analgesia; advance to regular diet; remove urinary catheter and confirm spontaneous voiding (screen for retention); start bowel regimen (senna/PEG) to prevent opioid ileus/constipation; wound check; in high-VTE-risk patients consider starting chemoprophylaxis at ~24h once hemostasis assured; active discharge planning - many single/2-level cervical and lumbar patients are discharge-ready POD1
day3plus_milestones: POD2+: independent ambulation, stairs and ADLs; pain controlled on oral regimen; tolerating diet, voiding, bowel movement returning; wound dry/intact; Hgb/vitals stable without ongoing transfusion need; PT/care-management clears disposition (home vs rehab). Most non-cervical single/2-level fusions discharge POD2-3; combined anterior/posterior, deformity, or MCC-tier patients often POD3-5+. Escalate workup for fever, wound drainage, new neuro change, or uncontrolled pain
consults_multidisciplinary: Anesthesia/preop optimization clinic (preop); PT/OT POD0-1 (mobilization, bracing, disposition); care management/discharge planner from POD0 (home vs rehab, DME); pharmacy (multimodal analgesia, opioid stewardship, anticoagulation dosing); nutrition if albumin low/malnourished; hospitalist/medicine co-management for ASA III-IV & multimorbidity; endocrine for diabetes/glucose control; acute pain service for chronic-opioid/complex patients; social work (home support, opioid-use-disorder, placement); intraop neuromonitoring; Infectious Diseases for vertebral osteomyelitis/epidural abscess cases
monitoring_level: Most 1-2 level cervical/lumbar fusions -> surgical floor. Step-down/telemetry or ICU for: anterior cervical (airway-edema watch, dysphagia, expanding neck hematoma first 24h); multilevel/deformity/combined anterior-posterior with large blood loss or prolonged prone positioning (POVL/airway risk); ASA IV/severe cardiopulmonary disease or severe OSA; high transfusion requirement; intraop complication (durotomy, cord monitoring change). Escalation triggers: new/progressive neuro deficit, expanding neck swelling/stridor/airway compromise (cervical), hemodynamic instability, respiratory failure, uncontrolled pain, or suspected spinal epidural hematoma -> emergent MRI + return to OR
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: clear fluids allowed up to 2h preop, solids up to 6h preop (ERAS High/Strong); resume oral intake POD0 and advance as tolerated; limit opioids + bowel regimen to prevent ileus. Mobility: early mobilization POD0 (ambulate >=10 m), PT BID-TID, out of bed for meals. VTE: mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs/IPC) for ALL patients + early ambulation (ERAS Strong); pharmacologic chemoprophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily or UFH 5000 U) reserved for high-risk (prolonged immobility, malignancy, prior VTE, anterior approach, >=4 levels, SCI), typically started 24-48h postop once hemostasis assured, balancing epidural-hematoma risk (NASS 2nd ed.). Glucose <180-200 mg/dL. Remove urinary catheter POD1 or avoid; early central/arterial line removal
discharge_criteria: Pain controlled on oral analgesics; ambulating independently/at functional baseline (PT-cleared); tolerating oral diet; voiding spontaneously without retention; bowel function returning; afebrile with wound dry/intact and no infection signs; stable neurologic exam; Hgb and vitals stable without ongoing transfusion; safe disposition confirmed; patient/caregiver understands wound care, activity/bracing restrictions, and medication plan
discharge_planning: Disposition home for >80-90% of elective cases; home health or acute/subacute rehab for frail, deformity, or complex/combined patients. Medication reconciliation with opioid stewardship (limited quantity + taper plan + bowel regimen), restart home meds, define VTE-prophylaxis duration. Follow-up: surgeon 2-4 weeks (wound/staple removal ~10-14 days) with imaging; PCP notification; DME (walker, brace); outpatient PT referral. Patient education on red flags (fever, wound drainage, new weakness, bowel/bladder dysfunction, calf pain/dyspnea) and opioid disposal; structured transitions-of-care and early telephone follow-up to curb pain-driven readmission
expected_los: Benchmark GMLOS varies by tier across the collapsed family: cervical without CC/MCC (473) ~1.5-2 d; non-cervical single-level without MCC (460) ~2.5-3.5 d; with CC tiers ~4-5 d; with MCC (459/471) ~6-8 d; combined anterior/posterior (453-455) ~5-11 d (up to ~10-11 d for 453 w/MCC); deformity/curvature/malignancy/infection (456-458) ~4-10 d. Overall roughly 2-4 d uncomplicated vs 7-11 d for MCC/combined tiers (note: FY2025 IPPS further split non-cervical fusion into single- vs multi-level DRGs)
target_los: Under an ideal ERAS pathway: single-level ACDF/cervical outpatient-to-1 d; 1-2 level lumbar MIS/TLIF ~1.6-3 d (ERAS cohorts: MIS-TLIF 2.4->1.6 d, Band 2022; open TLIF 4.6->3.6 d, Porche 2021); multilevel/combined/deformity 3-5 d; MCC tier optimized 5-7 d. ERAS typically shaves ~1 day versus conventional care while cutting inpatient opioid use and maintaining readmission rates
quality_metrics: ERAS protocol-compliance %; NHSN/CMS deep & organ-space surgical site infection; PSI-90 composite incl. PSI-12 perioperative VTE; 30-day all-cause readmission; 30-day mortality; NSQIP risk-adjusted morbidity/mortality; legacy SCIP measures (antibiotic timing/selection/discontinuation, normothermia, VTE prophylaxis); opioid metrics (inpatient MME, discharge opioid quantity); transfusion/blood-utilization rate; return-to-OR & unplanned reintubation; discharge-to-home %; CAUTI/falls/pressure-injury HACs; patient-reported outcomes (ODI, NRS/VAS pain, PROMIS); registry fusion/reoperation & pseudarthrosis/adjacent-segment rates
common_complications: Surgical site infection (~1-4%, higher with posterior/instrumented/diabetes/obesity -> mitigate: cefazolin timing, intrawound vancomycin, glucose <180, chlorhexidine, minimize OR time); durotomy/CSF leak (careful technique, primary repair, flat rest); dysphagia/hoarseness/airway hematoma (anterior cervical -> 24h airway watch); acute blood-loss anemia/transfusion (TXA + restrictive threshold); VTE/DVT-PE (mechanical + selective chemoprophylaxis + early mobilization); urinary retention/UTI (early catheter removal, void trials); ileus/constipation (opioid-sparing + bowel regimen); postoperative delirium (elderly -> minimize benzos/opioids, sleep hygiene, mobilize); spinal epidural hematoma (rare, emergent -> serial neuro checks); implant malposition/failure; postoperative visual loss (prone, rare)
readmission_drivers: 30-day readmission ~2.6-6.3% (NSQIP/mFI-5 cohort 6.3%). Top drivers: uncontrolled/intractable pain (leading surgical cause, ~24% of readmissions); SSI/wound dehiscence/drainage (~9-15%); recurrent symptoms/adjacent-segment disease/hardware issues; medical (GI, cardiovascular, VTE, UTI). Prevention levers: robust multimodal analgesia + structured opioid taper + early telephone follow-up; wound surveillance and early clinic check; glucose/nutrition optimization; postop medical co-management of comorbid patients; VTE prophylaxis; explicit red-flag education and transitions-of-care; frailty-based prehabilitation
guideline_source: PRIMARY: ERAS Society - Consensus statement for perioperative care in lumbar spinal fusion (Debono et al., The Spine Journal, 2021). SUPPORTING: NASS Clinical Guideline - Antithrombotic Therapies for Adults Undergoing Spine Surgery, 2nd Edition (2025); ACS-NSQIP Surgical Risk Calculator & mFI-5 frailty; CDC/WHO and SHEA-IDSA surgical-site-infection prevention; ASA preoperative fasting guidance; NASS coverage/appropriateness guidelines for lumbar & cervical fusion

evidence_grade: Moderate-to-Strong. The ERAS lumbar fusion consensus is a GRADE-based multidisciplinary Society statement with many Strong recommendations, but underlying evidence quality is mixed - High/Strong for antibiotic prophylaxis, normothermia, TXA, and multimodal analgesia; Low/Moderate for goal-directed fluids, chemoprophylaxis timing, and early-mobilization LOS effect; prehabilitation excluded for poor/conflicting evidence. Several elements are extrapolated from other surgical fields and single-center ERAS cohorts, so overall grade is tempered to Moderate
severity_cc_mcc_notes: CC/MCC tier drives intensity across the collapsed group: without CC/MCC (455/458/460/473) = shortest LOS (~1.5-3.5 d), floor care, outpatient-eligible for single-level cervical/lumbar; with CC (454/457/472) intermediate; with MCC (453/456/459/471) = longest LOS (~6-11 d), more ICU/step-down, higher transfusion, and higher SSI/VTE/readmission/mortality. Combined anterior/posterior (453-455) and deformity/curvature/malignancy/infection (456-458) are inherently more complex - greater blood loss, longer/staged operations, higher complication rates than single-approach non-cervical (459-460) or cervical (471-473). MCC frequently reflects a perioperative complication (respiratory failure, acute blood-loss anemia requiring transfusion, AKI, sepsis) that both lengthens stay and represents the very events the pathway aims to prevent; frailty (mFI-5 >=2) compounds MCC risk
pathway_pearls: 1) Bundle the three intraop levers: cefazolin <=60 min pre-incision with q4h redosing, TXA (loading + second dose) to cut blood loss/transfusion, and strict normothermia + glucose <180 - these drive the SSI and transfusion metrics. 2) Opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia (scheduled acetaminophen + NSAID/COX-2 + gabapentinoid + regional/intrathecal/wound infiltration, ketamine/lidocaine adjuncts) is THE lever against the #1 readmission driver (uncontrolled pain) and enables POD0 mobilization and early discharge. 3) Mobilize POD0 (>=10 m) with SCDs for all; reserve chemoprophylaxis for high-risk patients and start it 24-48h postop only after hemostasis - balance VTE against epidural-hematoma risk. 4) Anterior cervical cases: guard the airway - monitor for expanding neck hematoma/dysphagia the first 24h; risk-stratify with mFI-5/RAI frailty and prehabilitate the frail before elective surgery

admission_criteria: STEMI: universal emergent inpatient/CCU admission for primary PCI (no observation pathway). NSTE-ACS: admit for invasive management, timing by risk — very-high-risk (hemodynamic/electrical instability, refractory/ongoing ischemia, mechanical complication, cardiogenic shock) to immediate cath; high-risk (GRACE >140, dynamic hs-troponin, dynamic ST changes) early invasive <24h; intermediate-risk <72h. Elective/chronic coronary syndrome (CCS): PCI is largely OUTPATIENT/observation — reserve true inpatient admission for complex/high-risk anatomy (unprotected left main, bifurcation, CTO, atherectomy/rotablation), peri-procedural complication, staged multivessel PCI, decompensated HF, advanced CKD needing monitored hydration, or failure to meet same-day-discharge (SDD) criteria. Decision rule: uncomplicated elective PCI meeting SDD criteria should NOT be admitted as inpatient (2-midnight rule); ACS, complication, or comorbidity requiring >1 midnight justifies inpatient status.
risk_stratification: GRACE 2.0 (ischemic risk): >140 = high risk -> early invasive <24h; also predicts in-hospital/6-mo mortality. TIMI risk score (NSTEMI 0-7): higher score -> greater benefit from invasive/intensive antithrombotic strategy. HEART score in ED: 0-3 low (candidate for discharge), >=4 admit/observe. Killip class (STEMI): III/IV -> ICU/CCU, mechanical support, markedly higher mortality. CRUSADE bleeding score: stratifies in-hospital major bleeding -> guides access site and antithrombotic intensity. ARC-HBR criteria: identifies high-bleeding-risk -> abbreviated DAPT (1-3 mo). Mehran contrast-associated AKI risk score: guides isotonic hydration and contrast-volume minimization. SYNTAX score: lesion complexity for PCI-vs-CABG Heart Team decision (>=33 with left main/3-vessel favors CABG). PRECISE-DAPT / DAPT score: individualizes DAPT duration.
initial_workup_labs: 12-lead ECG within 10 min of first medical contact (repeat/continuous) — triage STEMI vs NSTE. High-sensitivity cardiac troponin with 0/1h or 0/2h serial algorithm — rule-in/rule-out MI. CBC (baseline Hgb/platelets for bleeding/transfusion). BMP/renal panel with eGFR — CI-AKI risk and renal dosing of antithrombotics/contrast planning; K+/Mg optimization for arrhythmia. Coags (PT/INR, aPTT) especially if on oral anticoagulant. Fasting lipid panel and HbA1c — secondary-prevention targets. Type & screen if high bleeding risk. BNP/NT-proBNP if HF suspected. Trigger: rising troponin -> ACS pathway/cath activation; eGFR <30 or high Mehran -> nephroprotection.
initial_imaging_dx: 12-lead ECG <=10 min (gatekeeper). Invasive coronary angiography (diagnostic cath) — STEMI: immediate primary PCI; NSTE very-high-risk immediate, high-risk <24h, intermediate <72h. Transthoracic echocardiography (TTE) during stay to assess LVEF, wall-motion, and mechanical complications (guides GDMT, ICD planning, discharge). Chest X-ray for congestion/alternative dx. Coronary CT angiography reserved for select low-to-intermediate-risk chest pain to exclude obstructive CAD (NOT for STEMI/high-risk NSTE). Intravascular imaging (IVUS/OCT) intra-procedure for left main/ambiguous lesions and stent optimization.
time_critical_interventions: STEMI reperfusion clock: first-medical-contact-to-device <=90 min at PCI-capable center (direct presenter), <=120 min for transfers; classic door-to-balloon <=90 min. If PCI cannot be delivered <=120 min FMC: fibrinolysis with door-to-needle <=30 min, then transfer for angiography at 2-24h (pharmacoinvasive). Aspirin 162-325 mg (non-enteric, chewed) ASAP. Parenteral anticoagulation at time of PCI. Cardiogenic shock or resuscitated out-of-hospital arrest with STEMI -> immediate cath/PCI of culprit. NSTE-ACS has no reperfusion clock but very-high-risk features mandate cath within 2h; high-risk within 24h.
initial_management: Dual antiplatelet therapy: aspirin load 162-325 mg then 81 mg daily PLUS oral P2Y12 inhibitor — prasugrel 60 mg or ticagrelor 180 mg PREFERRED over clopidogrel in ACS-PCI (Class 1); clopidogrel 600 mg if prasugrel/ticagrelor contraindicated or patient on oral anticoagulant. Peri-PCI anticoagulation: weight-based ACT-guided unfractionated heparin or bivalirudin (enoxaparin alternative). GP IIb/IIIa inhibitor (eptifibatide/tirofiban) as bailout for large thrombus/no-reflow. High-intensity statin early: atorvastatin 80 mg or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg (add ezetimibe/PCSK9 to reach LDL). Beta-blocker within 24h if no HF/shock/contraindication. ACEi/ARB if LVEF <=40%, HTN, DM, or anterior MI; add MRA if LVEF <=40% with HF/DM. Radial-first arterial access (mortality + bleeding benefit). Avoid routine morphine (blunts P2Y12 absorption) and routine oxygen (only if SaO2 <90%). CI-AKI prophylaxis: isotonic saline hydration + contrast minimization. AF on OAC: short course triple therapy then drop aspirin -> OAC + clopidogrel.
day1_milestones: POD0 (procedure day): perform primary/urgent PCI or diagnostic-to-PCI; confirm procedural success (TIMI 3 flow, adequate stent expansion). Achieve radial-band or femoral hemostasis; assess access site q shift for hematoma/bleeding. Admit to CCU/step-down on continuous telemetry for arrhythmia/reocclusion surveillance. Confirm DAPT loaded + maintenance, high-intensity statin started, anticoagulation completed. Serial troponin/ECG; hemodynamic stabilization; treat reperfusion arrhythmia. Resume diet after sheath removal; early mobilization 2-6h post-radial hemostasis. Initiate secondary-prevention orders and cardiac-rehab referral.
day2_milestones: POD1: obtain TTE for LVEF/wall-motion if not yet done. Titrate guideline-directed medical therapy (beta-blocker, ACEi/ARB, high-intensity statin, DAPT, MRA if indicated). Screen for complications — recheck creatinine for CI-AKI, monitor Hgb/access site for bleeding, telemetry for arrhythmia. Ambulate to baseline; PT if deconditioned. Complete medication reconciliation and begin discharge planning; diabetes optimization; structured tobacco-cessation counseling. Uncomplicated elective and low-risk NSTEMI patients are typically discharge-ready POD1.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ (reserved for complicated/MCC-tier course): manage large-infarct STEMI with HF or cardiogenic shock, sustained arrhythmia, CI-AKI, major bleeding, or respiratory failure. Perform planned staged PCI of non-culprit vessels (COMPLETE strategy) during index stay or scheduled. Optimize/uptitrate GDMT and diuresis; resolve complications; wean mechanical support (IABP/Impella). Reassess LVEF for ICD/wearable-defibrillator planning if EF <=35% (with 40-day/90-day waiting periods post-MI/revascularization). Confirm objective discharge readiness. Most uncomplicated patients discharge by POD1-2; MCC-tier patients POD3-5.
consults_multidisciplinary: Interventional cardiology (primary operator). Heart Team including cardiac surgery for unprotected left main, complex multivessel/high SYNTAX, or CABG consideration (Class 1). Cardiac rehabilitation referral before discharge (Class 1 — mortality benefit). Clinical pharmacy for DAPT/anticoagulant selection, renal dosing, and drug-interaction/affordability review. Care management + social work for disposition, medication access/cost, and adherence barriers. Nutrition for cardioprotective/diabetic diet counseling. Endocrine/diabetes if uncontrolled hyperglycemia; nephrology for CI-AKI or advanced CKD; advanced HF/shock team if cardiogenic shock or low EF; electrophysiology for malignant arrhythmia/ICD evaluation; tobacco-cessation service.
monitoring_level: ICU/CCU: STEMI, cardiogenic shock (Killip III/IV), sustained VT/VF or high-grade AV block, mechanical complication, or mechanical circulatory support (IABP/Impella). Step-down/CCU 24h then telemetry floor: uncomplicated primary PCI. Telemetry floor: stable NSTEMI. Recovery/telemetry then floor or same-day discharge: uncomplicated elective PCI. Escalation triggers: recurrent chest pain or new ST changes (rule out acute stent thrombosis), hemodynamic instability/hypotension, malignant arrhythmia, expanding access-site or retroperitoneal hematoma/hemorrhage, or acute kidney injury.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: NPO peri-procedure, then resume cardioprotective low-sodium (diabetic/renal as indicated) diet. Mobility: early ambulation 2-6h after radial hemostasis, 4-6h after femoral; PT for deconditioned/elderly. VTE prophylaxis: risk-assess — therapeutic peri-PCI antithrombotics plus early mobilization often suffice for short stays; add pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin/heparin) or mechanical device if prolonged immobility and acceptable bleeding risk. Glycemic control: target 140-180 mg/dL, avoid hypoglycemia, insulin as needed. Lines/catheters: remove arterial sheath promptly post-procedure, minimize/early-remove Foley and central lines to reduce infection/bleeding.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable without ongoing ischemia/chest pain; no significant arrhythmia for >=24h (post-MI). Access site hemostatic with no expanding hematoma or pseudoaneurysm. Renal function stable or improving (CI-AKI resolving). Ambulating at baseline and tolerating diet/oral meds. Full secondary-prevention regimen prescribed, obtained, and affordable (especially DAPT). Patient/caregiver demonstrates understanding (teach-back) of DAPT adherence and warning symptoms; follow-up arranged. Elective same-day-discharge criteria: successful uncomplicated PCI, stable vitals/ECG, hemostatic access, ambulatory, no active bleeding, and adequate 4-6h post-procedure observation.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home; SNF/rehab only if deconditioned or complicated. Medication reconciliation: DAPT (aspirin 81 mg + prasugrel/ticagrelor/clopidogrel) with EXPLICIT duration and do-not-stop-early counseling; high-intensity statin (+/- ezetimibe/PCSK9); beta-blocker; ACEi/ARB (+/- MRA/SGLT2i); sublingual nitroglycerin PRN; PPI if GI bleeding risk on DAPT. Follow-up: cardiology within 7-14 days, PCP, cardiac-rehab enrollment, repeat lipid/renal labs. Patient education: stent-thrombosis risk of premature DAPT cessation, return-precautions, smoking cessation, and risk-factor targets (LDL-C >=50% reduction and <70 mg/dL, consider <55; BP <130/80; HbA1c <7%). Transitions of care: 48-72h post-discharge phone call, confirm medication access, reinforce adherence with teach-back.
expected_los: Collapsed DRG 246-251 spans roughly 2-4.5 days by tier. Without-MCC tiers (DRG 247 DES, 249 non-DES, 251 no-stent) GMLOS approximately 2 days (modal DRG 247 near 1.9-2.0 days). With-MCC/complex tiers (DRG 246, 248, 250) GMLOS approximately 3.5-4.5 days. Group-weighted typical LOS approximately 2.5-3 days.
target_los: Elective uncomplicated PCI: same-day discharge (0-1 day) per SCAI LOS consensus. Uncomplicated NSTEMI: 1-2 days. Uncomplicated STEMI: 2-3 days. Complicated/MCC-tier (shock, HF, CI-AKI, major bleeding, staged multivessel): 3-4 days.
quality_metrics: ACC NCDR CathPCI Registry participation and metrics. STEMI timeliness: FMC-to-device/door-to-balloon <=90 min and median time to primary PCI (legacy CMS AMI-8a/OP-3). Discharge secondary-prevention meds: aspirin, P2Y12 inhibitor, high-intensity statin, beta-blocker, and ACEi/ARB for LVEF <=40%. Cardiac-rehab referral rate (Class 1). Radial-access rate; contrast-associated AKI rate; access-site/bleeding and vascular complication rate. Appropriate Use Criteria (AUC) concordance for revascularization. NCDR risk-adjusted in-hospital PCI mortality. CMS risk-standardized 30-day AMI mortality and 30-day AMI readmission. Same-day-discharge rate among SDD-eligible elective PCI (efficiency/stewardship). Tobacco-cessation counseling.
common_complications: Access-site bleeding/hematoma, retroperitoneal hemorrhage (femoral), and pseudoaneurysm — mitigated by radial-first access, CRUSADE-guided antithrombotic intensity, and meticulous hemostasis. Contrast-associated AKI — mitigated by isotonic hydration, contrast minimization, Mehran risk-guided care. Peri-procedural MI, acute/subacute stent thrombosis, and no-reflow — mitigated by potent DAPT, adequate anticoagulation, and stent optimization (IVUS/OCT). Arrhythmia (VT/VF, reperfusion arrhythmia, inferior-MI AV block) — telemetry/CCU monitoring. Coronary perforation/tamponade and dissection (rare). Cardiogenic shock, HF, and mechanical complications (papillary muscle rupture, VSD, free-wall rupture) with large STEMI. Radial artery occlusion; peri-procedural stroke.
readmission_drivers: Recurrent ischemia/ACS and stent thrombosis from DAPT non-adherence or premature discontinuation — the leading preventable driver; levers: intensive education, affordability check before discharge, PPI for GI protection, avoid unnecessary interruptions. Heart-failure decompensation after MI — lever: GDMT optimization, daily-weight education, early follow-up. Bleeding on DAPT — lever: ARC-HBR-based duration tailoring. Access-site vascular complications. Incomplete revascularization/residual angina — lever: planned staged PCI, symptom review. Arrhythmia. Cross-cutting prevention: 7-14 day cardiology follow-up, cardiac-rehab enrollment, medication reconciliation, teach-back, and a transitional-care call.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes (Circulation, 2025) and 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Guideline for Coronary Artery Revascularization (JACC/Circulation, 2021) — together these retired the 2013 STEMI, 2014 NSTE-ACS, and 2016 DAPT-duration documents. SUPPORTING: SCAI Expert Consensus on Length of Stay Following PCI (same-day-discharge/LOS optimization); ESC 2023 ACS Guideline (concordant).
key_citations: Rao SV, O'Donoghue ML, Ruel M, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes. Circulation. 2025;151(13):e771-e862. PMID 40014670; DOI https://doi.org/10.1161/CIR.0000000000001309. Lawton JS, Tamis-Holland JE, Bangalore S, et al. 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Guideline for Coronary Artery Revascularization. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2021;79(2):e21-e129. PMID 34895950; DOI https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jacc.2021.09.006. Bikdeli B, et al. Antiplatelet and Anticoagulant Therapy in the 2025 ACC/AHA ACS Guideline: Key Recommendations. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2025;85(22):2074-2078. PMID 40467132; DOI https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jacc.2025.04.002. Landmark trials: COMPLETE (Mehta SR et al., NEJM 2019, complete vs culprit-only revascularization in STEMI; PMID 31475795); MATRIX and RIVAL (radial vs femoral access in ACS); ISAR-REACT 5 (prasugrel vs ticagrelor in ACS); ISCHEMIA (invasive vs optimal medical therapy in stable CAD). Citations retrieved and verified via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong — Class 1 / Level of Evidence A society recommendations underpin the core levers (primary PCI timing in STEMI, radial-first access, potent DAPT with prasugrel/ticagrelor, high-intensity statin, cardiac-rehab referral). LOS optimization and same-day-discharge components rest on Moderate evidence (SCAI expert consensus plus observational/feasibility data). Staged complete revascularization is RCT-supported (COMPLETE). Overall: Strong, current multi-society Class I guideline foundation.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The six DRGs collapse two axes: device type (DES 246/247; non-DES/BMS 248/249; no-stent/balloon-only or aborted 250/251) and severity (with-MCC or 4+ arteries/stents vs without). MCC tiers (246/248/250) capture STEMI with cardiogenic shock/HF, CI-AKI, major bleeding, malignant arrhythmia, or respiratory failure -> ICU/CCU care, mechanical support, LOS approximately 3.5-4.5 days, and higher mortality. Non-MCC tiers (247/249/251) are predominantly stable NSTEMI or elective PCI -> telemetry-level care, LOS approximately 2 days, many same-day-discharge eligible. Note the 4+ vessels/stents modifier escalates to the higher DRG on procedural complexity even absent an MCC. The pathway scales monitoring intensity, consult breadth, and LOS directly with severity tier.
pathway_pearls: 1) STEMI is a clock — FMC-to-device <=90 min (<=120 min transfer); default to radial access for its mortality and bleeding advantage. 2) DAPT is sacred — prefer prasugrel or ticagrelor over clopidogrel in ACS-PCI, and counsel patients to NEVER stop early (stent thrombosis); verify medication affordability BEFORE discharge. 3) Do not over-admit elective PCI — uncomplicated cases belong in same-day-discharge/observation, not inpatient (ISCHEMIA showed no mortality benefit of PCI over optimal medical therapy in stable CAD). 4) Engineer out the two biggest avoidable in-stay harms — contrast-associated AKI (isotonic hydration, contrast minimization, Mehran score) and bleeding (CRUSADE/ARC-HBR risk, radial-first) — and front-load the Class 1 cardiac-rehabilitation referral.
verification_notes: Verified against live sources (PubMed metadata via MCP + AHA/ACC/JACC web sources), not memory. CITATIONS/PMIDs — all four confirmed real and correctly attributed: (1) PMID 40014670, 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI ACS Guideline, Circulation 2025;151(13):e771-e862, DOI 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001309, Rao/O'Donoghue/Ruel — exact match; (2) PMID 34895950, 2021 ACC/AHA/SCAI Coronary Artery Revascularization, JACC 2021;79(2):e21-e129, DOI 10.1016/j.jacc.2021.09.006, Lawton/Tamis-Holland/Bangalore — exact match; (3) PMID 40467132, Bikdeli et al. JACC 2025;85(22):2074-2078, DOI 10.1016/j.jacc.2025.04.002 — match (title lightly paraphrased, see issues_found); (4) PMID 31475795, COMPLETE, Mehta et al. NEJM 2019;381(15):1411-1421, DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1907775 — exact match. No fabricated or misattributed citations. GUIDELINE_SOURCE — 2025 ACS guideline is current and confirmed to supersede the 2013 STEMI, 2014 NSTE-ACS, and 2016 DAPT documents; 2021 revascularization guideline current. TIME-CRITICAL — FMC-to-device <=90 min (direct)/<=120 min (transfer), classic D2B <=90 min, door-to-needle <=30 min for fibrinolysis, pharmacoinvasive angiography 2-24h: all correct and current per 2025 guideline. RISK_STRATIFICATION — every score name real with correct thresholds: GRACE >140 high-risk/early invasive <24h; TIMI NSTEMI 0-7; HEART 0-3 low / >=4 admit; Killip III/IV; CRUSADE; ARC-HBR (abbreviated DAPT 1-3 mo); Mehran CI-AKI; SYNTAX >=33 high-complexity tertile favoring CABG; PRECISE-DAPT/DAPT. INITIAL_MANAGEMENT — confirmed via AHA 'Top Things to Know' + HVT comparative editorial: ticagrelor or prasugrel PREFERRED over clopidogrel is Class I LOE A (row's '(Class 1)' is correct); radial-first preferred over femoral (mortality/bleeding benefit); high-intensity statin; UFH/bivalirudin; avoid routine morphine/oxygen (O2 only if SaO2<90%) — all correct. No transfusion-threshold claim is made in the row, so none to correct. LDL guidance in row (>=50% reduction, <70, consider <55) aligns with 2025 guideline (add non-statin if LDL >=70; intensify if 55-<70). LOS — SDD (0-1d) for uncomplicated elective PCI per SCAI consensus; NSTEMI 1-2d, STEMI 2-3d, MCC-tier 3-4d; DRG 246-251 GMLOS ~2d (non-MCC) to 3.5-4.5d (MCC): all plausible vs national benchmarks. QUALITY_METRICS — NCDR CathPCI Registry, D2B/FMC-to-device <=90, legacy CMS AMI-8a and OP-3, cardiac-rehab referral (Class I A confirmed), AUC concordance, radial rate, CI-AKI rate, CMS 30-day AMI mortality/readmission: all real and applicable. Overall: exceptionally accurate, internally consistent, and current row; no material corrections required."; ISSUES: NON-MATERIAL (no correction warranted): The key_citations Bikdeli reference paraphrases the article title as '...in the 2025 ACC/AHA ACS Guideline: Key Recommendations' whereas the actual PubMed title is '...in the 2025 ACC/AHA Guideline for Acute Coronary Syndromes: Key Recommendations.' PMID 40467132, DOI 10.1016/j.jacc.2025.04.002, journal (JACC), volume/issue (85(22)), pages (2074-2078), and first author (Bikdeli B) are all correct. This is a cosmetic abbreviation, not a misattribution.; OBSERVATION (not an error in the row): The verification checklist hint referenced a generic 'door-to-needle <=60min' target; for STEMI fibrinolysis the correct guideline target is door-to-needle <=30 min, which is exactly what the row states. The row is correct; the hint's number does not apply to STEMI.
scope_and_volume_notes: 5th most frequent OR procedure (HCUP: 0.48M, 2018). Drug-eluting/bare-metal stent and balloon angioplasty; cath-lab. Inpatient portion only (large outpatient volume excluded).
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 250 — Percutaneous Cardiovascular Procedures without Intraluminal Device with MCC; 251 — Percutaneous Cardiovascular Procedures without Intraluminal Device without MCC; 318 — Percutaneous Coronary Atherectomy without Intraluminal Device; 321 — Percutaneous Cardiovascular Procedures with Intraluminal Device with MCC or 4+ Arteries/Intraluminal Devices; 322 — Percutaneous Cardiovascular Procedures with Intraluminal Device without MCC; 323 — Coronary Intravascular Lithotripsy with Intraluminal Device with MCC; 324 — Coronary Intravascular Lithotripsy with Intraluminal Device without MCC; 325 — Coronary Intravascular Lithotripsy without Intraluminal Device; 359 — Percutaneous Coronary Atherectomy with Intraluminal Device with MCC; 360 — Percutaneous Coronary Atherectomy with Intraluminal Device without MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 4/4 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Original independent adversarial high-stakes review completed; current pass revalidated citation resolvability and FY2026 coding/taxonomy.
data_quality_notes: Replaced retired PCI stent DRGs 246-249 with current intraluminal-device, atherectomy, and coronary intravascular lithotripsy families; retained current no-device PCI DRGs 250-251. Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk.

admission_criteria: Admit (inpatient) for: hemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg, HR >100, orthostasis, shock index >1), ongoing overt bleeding (active hematemesis, fresh melena, brisk hematochezia), Hgb drop or transfusion requirement, high-risk score (Glasgow-Blatchford >=2 upper; Oakland >8 lower), suspected variceal/cirrhotic bleed, hemodynamically significant anticoagulation-associated bleed, or need for urgent therapeutic endoscopy; ED discharge/outpatient acceptable ONLY for very-low-risk upper GI bleed (GBS 0-1: no melena, normal vitals/Hgb, no syncope, no liver or heart failure — ACG 2021) or low-risk lower GI bleed (Oakland <=8, ~95% probability of safe discharge — ACG 2023); Observation for intermediate-risk pending Hgb/vitals trend; direct ICU for shock, massive hematemesis, active variceal hemorrhage, or airway compromise
risk_stratification: Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS, 0-23): 0-1 = very low risk -> outpatient management (ACG 2021), >=7 predicts endoscopic/hemostatic intervention, >=12 = high risk (Lau NEJM 2020 urgent-endoscopy population); AIMS65 (Age>65, INR>1.5, Albumin<3.0 g/dL, Altered mental status, SBP<=90): >=2 predicts higher in-hospital mortality; Rockall score (pre- and post-endoscopy) >=5 = high rebleeding/mortality; Forrest classification at endoscopy: Ia/Ib/IIa/IIb = high-risk stigmata -> endoscopic therapy + high-dose PPI, IIc/III = low risk -> early feed/discharge; Oakland score (lower GI, 0-35) <=8 -> safe discharge; Child-Pugh (C, or B with active bleeding) and MELD stratify variceal patients for early/pre-emptive TIPS and prognosis
initial_workup_labs: CBC with serial Hgb/Hct q4-6h (caution: initial Hgb underestimates acute loss before equilibration); type & crossmatch >=2 units; CMP with BUN/Cr (elevated BUN:Cr >30:1 suggests upper source); LFTs + albumin + total bilirubin (cirrhosis/Child-Pugh); coagulation panel PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen; lactate and venous blood gas (hypoperfusion); explicit anticoagulant/antiplatelet and NSAID history; H. pylori testing (endoscopic biopsy plus stool antigen — retest if negative given false-negatives in acute bleeding); troponin/ECG if CAD or demand-ischemia risk. Triggers: INR elevation -> reversal, platelets <50k with active bleeding -> platelet transfusion, fibrinogen <150 -> cryoprecipitate, rising lactate -> escalate resuscitation, low albumin/high INR -> feeds AIMS65/Child-Pugh scoring
initial_imaging_dx: Upper GI: EGD within 24h of presentation (ACG 2021); if variceal/cirrhotic suspected, EGD within 12h (Baveno VII); urgent EGD <6h is NOT superior to 6-24h even in high-risk GBS>=12 (Lau NEJM 2020) — do not rush at expense of resuscitation. Lower GI: if hemodynamically stable, non-urgent inpatient colonoscopy after adequate bowel prep (urgent <24h colonoscopy shows no outcome benefit — ACG 2023); if ongoing severe/unstable LGIB, CT angiography FIRST (detects >=0.3-0.5 mL/min) then IR transcatheter embolization or targeted colonoscopy. Obscure/massive with negative EGD+colonoscopy: CTA, tagged-RBC scintigraphy (detects 0.1-0.5 mL/min), push enteroscopy or video capsule. CXR if aspiration suspected
time_critical_interventions: First hour: two large-bore IVs (>=18G), balanced crystalloid resuscitation, activate massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1) if shock; suspected variceal bleed = immediate bundle -> vasoactive drug (octreotide/terlipressin/somatostatin) PLUS IV ceftriaxone 1 g STAT, both BEFORE endoscopy (antibiotics reduce mortality/rebleeding — Baveno VII); STAT anticoagulation reversal for life-threatening bleed; erythromycin 250 mg IV (prokinetic) 30-120 min pre-endoscopy to improve mucosal visualization (ACG 2021); airway protection/intubation before endoscopy if massive hematemesis or altered mental status; EGD within 24h (variceal within 12h); severe unstable LGIB -> CTA then IR embolization within hours
initial_management: Resuscitation with balanced crystalloid; RESTRICTIVE RBC transfusion threshold Hgb <7 g/dL (target 7-9), transfuse <8 if active ACS/CAD — restrictive strategy improved 6-week survival 95% vs 91% (Villanueva NEJM 2013); variceal target Hgb 7-8. Non-variceal: IV PPI (pantoprazole 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/h infusion or 40 mg IV BID); after endoscopic hemostasis of high-risk stigmata, high-dose PPI x72h then oral PPI BID x2 weeks (ACG 2021). Variceal: vasoactive agent (octreotide 50 mcg bolus + 50 mcg/h x2-5 days) + ceftriaxone 1 g/24h up to 7 days + lactulose for HE prophylaxis (Baveno VII). Reversal: warfarin -> 4-factor PCC + IV vitamin K 10 mg; dabigatran -> idarucizumab 5 g; apixaban/rivaroxaban -> andexanet alfa or 4F-PCC; do NOT delay endoscopy to fully normalize INR (safe at INR <2.5); platelets if <50k with active bleeding. Source control: endoscopic hemostasis (through-the-scope clips, thermal/bipolar coagulation, TC-325 hemostatic powder; EVL band ligation for esophageal varices, cyanoacrylate injection for gastric varices), IR embolization if endoscopy fails, pre-emptive/early TIPS for high-risk variceal; hold nitrates/antihypertensives and non-essential anticoagulants/NSAIDs
day1_milestones: Complete resuscitation and achieve hemodynamic stability; document validated risk score (GBS/AIMS65/Rockall) and set disposition level (ICU vs step-down vs floor); perform definitive endoscopy with hemostasis and document Forrest class (upper) or stigmata/source (lower); complete anticoagulation reversal if indicated and transfuse to restrictive threshold; start IV PPI (non-variceal) or full variceal bundle (vasoactive + ceftriaxone + lactulose); obtain H. pylori biopsy; keep NPO peri-endoscopy; serial Hgb q4-6h and continuous monitoring for rebleeding
day2_milestones: Confirm sustained hemostasis with Hgb stable across >=2 checks and no ongoing transfusion; transition IV to oral PPI if low-risk stigmata (continue high-dose PPI through 72h for high-risk stigmata); advance diet (clears -> regular) if no rebleeding and de-escalate monitoring (ICU -> step-down/floor); variceal: continue vasoactive drug and antibiotics, reassess for early/pre-emptive TIPS in high-risk (Child-Pugh C <14 or B with active bleeding), begin NSBB planning; reintroduce essential home medications; start mechanical VTE prophylaxis and early mobilization; manage alcohol withdrawal if applicable
day3plus_milestones: Sustained hemostasis with Hgb stable off transfusion >=24-48h and tolerating oral diet/meds; complete vasoactive course (2-5 days) and antibiotic course (variceal) and any planned EVL session; finalize antithrombotic plan with cardiology risk-benefit — resume ASA for secondary CV prevention within 1-3 days after hemostasis, restart therapeutic anticoagulation at 7-14 days weighing thrombotic risk; act on H. pylori result and confirm eradication plan; finalize PPI regimen; complete discharge-readiness assessment, medication reconciliation, and arrange outpatient endoscopy/EVL surveillance and repeat EGD for gastric ulcers at 8-12 weeks to confirm healing/exclude malignancy
consults_multidisciplinary: GI/therapeutic endoscopy — urgent, all patients; Hepatology — cirrhosis/variceal, Day 0-1; Interventional radiology — early for embolization or TIPS candidacy (high-risk variceal or failed endoscopy); Surgery — on standby as rescue for refractory bleeding; Critical care — unstable/ICU patients; Cardiology — antithrombotic management in ACS/CAD/atrial fibrillation; Pharmacy — anticoagulation reversal, PPI and vasoactive dosing; Nutrition — diet advancement and cirrhosis/malnutrition; Care management and social work — alcohol use disorder, transitions, follow-up coordination; Blood bank/transfusion medicine — massive transfusion support
monitoring_level: ICU: hemodynamic instability/shock, active variceal hemorrhage, ongoing transfusion requirement, massive hematemesis or airway-protection need, post-massive-transfusion; Step-down/telemetry: stabilized after hemostasis but high-risk stigmata (Forrest Ia-IIb), significant cardiopulmonary comorbidity, or cirrhosis; Floor: low-risk stigmata (Forrest IIc/III), stable Hgb, fully resuscitated; Escalation triggers: recurrent hematemesis or hematochezia, HR >100 or SBP <90, Hgb drop >2 g/dL, new/worsening tachycardia, rising lactate, or altered mental status -> rapid response and repeat endoscopy/IR
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: NPO peri-endoscopy, then advance clears -> regular within 24h after hemostasis (low-risk stigmata can feed early); cirrhotics need adequate protein and avoidance of prolonged fasting. Mobility: early ambulation once hemodynamically stable. VTE: mechanical prophylaxis (intermittent pneumatic compression) during active bleeding; HOLD pharmacologic prophylaxis until hemostasis confirmed, then resume LMWH/UFH. Glucose control: target 140-180 mg/dL. Lines/catheters: remove large-bore access and urinary catheter once stable; minimize central-line dwell time
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable >=24h; Hgb stable without transfusion >=24-48h with no recurrent overt bleeding; tolerating oral intake and oral medications; endoscopic hemostasis achieved with no high-risk stigmata mandating continued inpatient monitoring, and anticoagulation reversal complete; bleeding etiology identified with a definitive management plan; antithrombotic resumption plan defined; risk profile (GBS-informed) appropriate for outpatient follow-up
discharge_planning: Disposition home for most; SNF if deconditioned/frail. Medication reconciliation: PPI BID x2 weeks then daily for peptic ulcer disease; discontinue/replace NSAIDs and add gastroprotection; resume ASA early if needed for secondary CV prevention; NSBB (carvedilol preferred) titrated for variceal secondary prophylaxis; lactulose +/- rifaximin for cirrhotics. Follow-up: GI within 2-4 weeks, repeat EGD at 8-12 weeks for gastric ulcer healing/malignancy exclusion, serial EVL until varix eradication, H. pylori test-of-cure. Education: recognize rebleeding (melena/hematemesis/lightheadedness) and return precautions, alcohol cessation, NSAID avoidance, medication adherence. Transitions: anticoagulation clinic linkage, PCP appointment, and discharge summary to PCP and GI
expected_los: Approx GMLOS benchmarks by collapsed DRG: 379 (no CC/MCC) ~2.3-2.5 days; 378 (with CC) ~3.2-3.4 days; 377 (with MCC) ~4.3-4.5 days; blended group expected LOS ~3-4 days
target_los: Optimized target: very-low-risk upper GI (GBS 0-1) avoid admission entirely (ED discharge/observation, 0 inpatient days); uncomplicated non-variceal post-hemostasis 2-3 days; variceal/cirrhotic 4-5 days; blended best-practice target ~2-3 days with an early-discharge protocol for low-risk stigmata
quality_metrics: Documented pre-endoscopic risk stratification (GBS/AIMS65); time-to-endoscopy <24h (variceal <12h); adherence to restrictive transfusion threshold (Hgb <7); antibiotic prophylaxis administered in cirrhotic GI bleed (validated quality indicator); vasoactive drug given in suspected variceal bleed; H. pylori testing and treatment in ulcer disease; PPI protocol adherence for high-risk stigmata; rebleeding rate; in-hospital and 30-day mortality; 30-day all-cause readmission. Note: unlike sepsis (SEP-1), there is no dedicated CMS core measure for GI bleed — institutions track via internal dashboards and UGIB care-bundle indicators (e.g., NICE/BSG)
common_complications: Rebleeding (peptic ulcer 10-15%, higher with high-risk stigmata) -> high-dose PPI, repeat/second-look endoscopy, IR embolization; aspiration pneumonia from massive hematemesis/altered mentation -> pre-endoscopy airway protection, prokinetic, head-of-bed elevation; transfusion-associated TACO/TRALI -> restrictive strategy; in cirrhotics hepatic encephalopathy (lactulose prophylaxis), SBP/infection (prophylactic ceftriaxone), hepatorenal syndrome; demand ischemia/ACS from anemia -> transfuse at <8 g/dL if CAD; contrast-induced nephropathy from CTA; TIPS-related encephalopathy
readmission_drivers: Recurrent bleeding from incomplete hemostasis or high-risk lesion; failure to test/treat H. pylori; continued NSAID/antiplatelet use without gastroprotection; variceal rebleed due to NSBB nonadherence or incomplete band eradication; alcohol relapse and decompensated cirrhosis; premature or incorrect anticoagulation resumption; inadequate follow-up. Prevention levers: guideline-concordant PPI regimen, H. pylori eradication with test-of-cure, secondary prophylaxis (NSBB + serial EVL), structured GI follow-up, alcohol counseling, and rigorous medication reconciliation
guideline_source: ACG Clinical Guideline: Upper Gastrointestinal and Ulcer Bleeding (American College of Gastroenterology, 2021); Management of Patients With Acute Lower Gastrointestinal Bleeding: An Updated ACG Guideline (American College of Gastroenterology, 2023); Baveno VII — Renewing Consensus in Portal Hypertension (Baveno Cooperation / de Franchis et al., 2022) for variceal/portal-hypertensive bleeding; supported by AASLD portal hypertension guidance

evidence_grade: Strong — anchored by multiple current GRADE-based Class I society guidelines (ACG 2021, ACG 2023, Baveno VII 2022) supported by landmark RCTs (Villanueva restrictive transfusion; Lau endoscopy timing). Select elements are moderate/conditional: exact sub-24h endoscopy timing, non-urgent vs urgent colonoscopy in LGIB, DOAC reversal-agent choice, platelet transfusion thresholds, and pre-emptive TIPS patient selection rest on moderate- to low-quality evidence
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 379 (no CC/MCC): typically non-variceal, low-risk stigmata, brief LOS ~2-3 days, floor-level care, rare ICU; DRG 378 (with CC): moderate severity — comorbidity or symptomatic anemia requiring transfusion, telemetry/step-down, LOS ~3 days; DRG 377 (with MCC): high severity — variceal/cirrhotic bleed, hemodynamic shock, massive transfusion, acute kidney injury, hepatic encephalopathy, aspiration; requires ICU and often TIPS/IR, LOS ~4.5+ days with the highest rebleeding and mortality. The principal severity up-shifters across the collapsed group are variceal etiology, hemodynamic instability/shock, and need for ICU or TIPS
pathway_pearls: 1) Risk-stratify in the ED — GBS 0-1 (upper) or Oakland <=8 (lower) can be safely discharged, preserving inpatient beds and avoiding low-value admissions; 2) In any suspected variceal/cirrhotic bleed, give a vasoactive drug PLUS IV ceftriaxone immediately, before endoscopy — prophylactic antibiotics independently reduce mortality; 3) Use restrictive transfusion (Hgb <7, target 7-8 in variceal) — over-transfusion raises portal pressure and worsens survival; 4) Target endoscopy within 24h (variceal within 12h) but do not chase urgent <6h timing (no mortality benefit even in GBS>=12) and do not delay endoscopy to fully normalize INR
verification_notes: Verified against PubMed (primary source) and web. CITATIONS: all 5 key_citations are REAL, CORRECT, and CURRENT — PMID 33929377 (ACG Upper GI/Ulcer Bleeding, Laine, Am J Gastroenterol 2021;116(5):899-917, doi 10.14309/ajg.0000000000001245); PMID 36735555 (ACG Lower GI Bleeding, Sengupta, 2023;118(2):208-231, doi ...002130); PMID 35120736 (Baveno VII, de Franchis, J Hepatol 2022;76(4):959-974, doi 10.1016/j.jhep.2021.12.022); PMID 23281973 (Villanueva, NEJM 2013;368(1):11-21, doi ...NEJMoa1211801); PMID 32242355 (Lau, NEJM 2020;382(14):1299-1308, doi ...NEJMoa1912484). Every PMID/DOI/journal/volume/page/year matches — no fabrication or misattribution. Guideline_source (ACG 2021, ACG 2023, Baveno VII 2022, AASLD support) is accurate and current. Abstracts independently CONFIRM: GBS 0-1 -> outpatient, restrictive Hgb<7 transfusion threshold, erythromycin pre-endoscopy, EGD within 24h (ACG 2021); Villanueva 6-week survival 95% vs 91% with HR 0.55 (exact match); Lau GBS>=12 urgent <6h vs 6-24h endoscopy = no 30-day mortality benefit (exact match); high-dose PPI x3 days then oral BID x2 weeks (exact match). RISK SCORES: names/thresholds correct — GBS range 0-23, AIMS65 components (Age>65, INR>1.5, Albumin<3.0, AMS, SBP<=90), Rockall high-risk, Forrest stigmata, Oakland range 0-35 with <=8 safe-discharge cutoff, Child-Pugh/MELD for pre-emptive TIPS (Child-Pugh C<14 or B with active bleeding per Baveno VII). TIME-CRITICAL & MANAGEMENT: vasoactive+ceftriaxone-before-endoscopy bundle, octreotide 50 mcg bolus+50 mcg/h, ceftriaxone 1 g, erythromycin 250 mg IV, pantoprazole 80 mg+8 mg/h, reversal agents (4F-PCC+vit K, idarucizumab 5 g, andexanet), CTA-first for unstable LGIB — all correct and guideline-concordant (no cardiac/stroke time targets apply to this condition). LOS: DRG 377/378/379 GMLOS ranges (~4.3-4.5 / ~3.2-3.4 / ~2.3-2.5) are plausible vs national MS-DRG benchmarks. QUALITY METRICS: correct that there is no dedicated CMS core measure for GI bleed (unlike SEP-1); listed indicators are legitimate. ONLY substantive error: Oakland <=8 = 95% (not ~98%) probability of safe discharge — corrected in admission_criteria with the Oakland 2017 source. One minor defensible-simplification note on Forrest IIb left unchanged.; ISSUES: admission_criteria overstates the Oakland score discharge probability: it claims Oakland <=8 gives '~98% probability of safe discharge,' but the derivation/validation study (Oakland 2017, Lancet Gastroenterol Hepatol; the evidence ACG 2023 relies on) reports 95%. Corrected.; Minor, non-blocking nuance (left unchanged): risk_stratification groups Forrest IIb (adherent clot) with high-risk stigmata mandating endoscopic therapy + high-dose PPI. ACG 2021 treats adherent clots as a conditional/intermediate category (endoscopic therapy 'may be considered' after clot removal). Grouping IIb as high-risk is a defensible pathway simplification, not a clear error, so it was not corrected.
scope_and_volume_notes: Includes variceal and non-variceal, upper and lower GI bleeding. Severe/unstable -> Critical Care; endoscopy-driven.

admission_criteria: Uncomplicated symptomatic cholelithiasis/biliary colic is elective/ambulatory-not admitted. Inpatient admission for: TG18-diagnosed acute cholecystitis, especially Grade II (moderate) or Grade III (severe/organ dysfunction); acute cholangitis (Charcot triad fever+jaundice+RUQ pain); choledocholithiasis with biliary obstruction; acute gallstone pancreatitis; sepsis/SIRS or septic shock; intractable pain, persistent vomiting or dehydration failing ED/observation therapy; need for urgent ERCP or emergent/open/converted cholecystectomy; significant comorbidity (ASA III-IV) or anticoagulation requiring optimization. Observation status acceptable for mild biliary colic resolving with analgesia and tolerating PO with normal LFTs/lipase and no cholecystitis on imaging.
risk_stratification: TG18 acute-cholecystitis severity: Grade I mild (no organ dysfunction, mild inflammation); Grade II moderate (WBC >18,000/mm3, palpable tender RUQ mass, symptoms >72h, or marked local inflammation-gangrenous/emphysematous cholecystitis, pericholecystic or hepatic abscess, biliary peritonitis); Grade III severe (any organ dysfunction: hypotension needing vasopressors, altered mentation, PaO2/FiO2 <300, Cr >2.0 mg/dL or oliguria, INR >1.5, platelets <100,000). TG18 cholangitis grading I-III (Reynolds pentad=Grade III). ASA-PS and Charlson Comorbidity Index drive operative timing (TG18 flowchart: early lap chole if CCI <=5 and ASA-PS <=2 for Grade I-II; CCI <=3 and ASA-PS <=2 at an advanced center for selected Grade III). APACHE II >=7 = high-risk (CHOCOLATE-still favors cholecystectomy over drainage). ASGE choledocholithiasis probability high/intermediate/low. Gallstone pancreatitis severity by BISAP/Ranson/APACHE II and revised Atlanta.
initial_workup_labs: CBC (leukocytosis >18,000 = TG18 Grade II marker; left shift); CRP (>3 mg/dL supports TG18 systemic-inflammation criterion); complete LFTs-AST, ALT, ALP, GGT, total/direct bilirubin (elevation flags choledocholithiasis/cholangitis and triggers ASGE CBD-stone risk stratification, e.g., bilirubin >4 mg/dL = high-probability predictor); lipase/amylase (>3x ULN = gallstone pancreatitis); serum lactate (sepsis/hypoperfusion); blood cultures x2 BEFORE antibiotics in suspected cholangitis/sepsis; PT/INR and platelets (TG18 Grade III hepatic/hematologic dysfunction and pre-op coagulopathy); BMP/creatinine (renal dysfunction, hydration); type and screen; beta-hCG in women of childbearing age; HbA1c and glucose for perioperative optimization.
initial_imaging_dx: Transabdominal RUQ ultrasound FIRST-LINE within hours of presentation (gallstones, wall thickening >4-5 mm, pericholecystic fluid, sonographic Murphy sign, CBD diameter >6 mm suggests stone/obstruction). Cholescintigraphy (HIDA) most specific test when US equivocal (non-visualization of gallbladder = acute cholecystitis). CT abdomen/pelvis when complications (perforation, emphysematous cholecystitis, abscess) or alternative diagnosis suspected. For choledocholithiasis: ASGE pathway-high probability (CBD stone seen on imaging, clinical cholangitis, or bilirubin >4 mg/dL with dilated CBD) go directly to therapeutic ERCP within 24h; intermediate probability get MRCP or EUS (or intraoperative cholangiography) before ERCP to avoid unnecessary ERCP; low probability proceed to cholecystectomy with no further ductal imaging. Selective intraoperative cholangiography/laparoscopic ultrasound for uncertain anatomy or suspected injury.
time_critical_interventions: Septic shock from acute cholangitis/Grade III disease: Surviving Sepsis 1-hour bundle-measure lactate, draw blood cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1h, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension/lactate >=4, vasopressors to MAP >=65. Acute cholangitis: biliary decompression/ERCP-URGENT within 24-48h, EMERGENT <12h for Grade III/suppurative cholangitis not responding to resuscitation. Acute cholecystitis: EARLY laparoscopic cholecystectomy-ideally within 24h of admission (ACDC) and within 7-10 days of symptom onset; index-admission surgery is standard of care (WSES 2020). Gallstone pancreatitis with concurrent cholangitis or ongoing obstruction: ERCP within 24-72h; same-admission cholecystectomy before discharge for mild disease (PONCHO). Surgical antibiotic prophylaxis within 60 min of incision.
initial_management: NPO; IV balanced isotonic crystalloid resuscitation; multimodal analgesia (NSAID e.g. ketorolac and/or opioid, acetaminophen); antiemetics. Empiric antibiotics per TG18 (Gomi 2018): community-acquired Grade I-II-cefazolin/cefuroxime or ceftriaxone, add metronidazole for anaerobic/biliary-enteric coverage; Grade III or healthcare-associated-piperacillin-tazobactam, or cefepime/ceftazidime plus metronidazole, or a carbapenem (meropenem/ertapenem), add vancomycin/linezolid if enterococcal risk. Grade I after uncomplicated cholecystectomy: stop antibiotics within 24h (or prophylaxis only); Grade II-III/cholangitis: 4-7 days after source control. SOURCE CONTROL is the pathway core: cholecystectomy (definitive), ERCP + sphincterotomy/stone extraction for CBD stones/cholangitis, or percutaneous cholecystostomy reserved for the truly non-operable. Reverse anticoagulation pre-op (vitamin K/4-factor PCC for warfarin, hold DOACs, platelets if <50,000); correct electrolytes and glucose.
day1_milestones: POD0 (operative day): pre-op optimization, WHO surgical safety checklist, prophylactic antibiotic within 60 min of incision, VTE risk assessment. Early laparoscopic cholecystectomy achieving the Critical View of Safety (SAGES multi-society)-conclusive identification of the 2 tubular structures entering the gallbladder before clipping/dividing; use intraoperative cholangiography or laparoscopic US and adopt subtotal (fenestrating/reconstituting) cholecystectomy or convert to open as a bailout for the difficult gallbladder rather than risk bile duct injury (WSES). For CBD stones/cholangitis: ERCP with sphincterotomy and stone clearance +/- stent. ERAS: multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia, PONV prophylaxis, minimize drains/tubes, early oral intake and ambulation within hours; most Grade I lap chole eligible for same-day discharge.
day2_milestones: POD1: advance to regular diet; discontinue IV fluids once tolerating PO; transition to oral analgesia; ambulate independently; remove urinary catheter; assess any surgical drain output and character (bilious output flags leak); confirm downtrending LFTs and WBC; verify return of bowel function; screen for surgical-site infection. Uncomplicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy is discharge-ready. For open/converted or complicated cases: pulmonary toilet/incentive spirometry, wound assessment, continued monitoring for bile leak or ileus.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ (open, common-duct-exploration, or complicated cases): continue recovery to return of bowel function and adequate pain control on oral agents; drain management and removal when output low and non-bilious; if rising bilirubin, bilious drain output, or RUQ pain/fever, obtain HIDA/MRCP and treat bile leak with ERCP + sphincterotomy/stent +/- percutaneous drainage of biloma; evaluate for retained CBD stone (post-op ERCP if IOC positive or LFTs rising). Discharge when afebrile, hemodynamically stable, tolerating diet, ambulating at baseline, and no evidence of ongoing sepsis or leak. High-risk patients initially managed with cholecystostomy return for interval cholecystectomy in ~4-6 weeks.
consults_multidisciplinary: Acute-care/general or HPB surgery as primary team-early. Gastroenterology/advanced endoscopy for ERCP in choledocholithiasis/cholangitis. Anesthesia/pre-op medicine for optimization of ASA III-IV. Interventional radiology for percutaneous cholecystostomy or biloma/abscess drainage. Critical care/intensivist for Grade III cholecystitis or cholangitis with septic shock. Pharmacy for antibiotic stewardship and periprocedural anticoagulation. Case management/care coordination and social work early for disposition (elderly, non-operable, placement needs). Nutrition and PT/OT for deconditioned or elderly patients. Mandatory referral of any suspected or confirmed bile duct injury to an experienced surgeon/multidisciplinary hepatobiliary team (SAGES strong recommendation).
monitoring_level: Grade I-II acute cholecystitis, operable: surgical floor; telemetry if cardiac comorbidity. Grade III acute cholecystitis or acute cholangitis with organ dysfunction/septic shock: ICU or step-down for vasopressor support, close hemodynamic/respiratory monitoring, and urgent source control. Post-op uncomplicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy: PACU then floor or same-day discharge. Escalation triggers: rising lactate, hypotension/MAP <65 despite fluids, new vasopressor need, oliguria/rising creatinine, altered mental status, hypoxemia, or qSOFA >=2-escalate to ICU and expedite biliary drainage.
nutrition_mobility_vte: ERAS pathway: clear liquids advancing to regular diet as tolerated on POD0; no routine nasogastric tube. Early mobilization within hours of surgery. Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (acetaminophen, NSAIDs, local anesthetic port infiltration) with PONV prophylaxis. VTE prophylaxis by Caprini risk-mechanical SCDs plus pharmacologic LMWH (enoxaparin) or unfractionated heparin unless active bleeding/high bleeding risk; continue as indicated in higher-risk/open cases. Glucose control target <180 mg/dL. Early removal of urinary catheter and IV lines; minimize and promptly remove surgical drains.
discharge_criteria: Tolerating oral diet and fluids; pain controlled on oral analgesics; afebrile and hemodynamically stable; ambulating at or near baseline; no clinical or laboratory evidence of bile leak, retained stone, or ongoing sepsis (downtrending WBC/LFTs); spontaneous voiding; surgical wounds/incisions intact and acceptable; any drain either removed or with a clear outpatient management plan; adequate home support and understanding of return precautions.
discharge_planning: Disposition home for the majority of laparoscopic cases (often same-day or POD1); SNF/rehab only for deconditioned/elderly or complicated open cases. Medication reconciliation-discontinue antibiotics unless a defined course remains, resume home medications, and specify anticoagulation restart timing. Schedule surgery clinic follow-up in 2-4 weeks; GI/endoscopy follow-up for biliary stent removal (~4-6 weeks) or planned interval cholecystectomy. Education on wound/drain care, activity, diet, and RED-FLAG return precautions (jaundice, fever/rigors, worsening RUQ pain, bilious drain output, intractable vomiting). Communicate final surgical pathology (incidental gallbladder carcinoma requires oncology referral) and send a transitions-of-care summary to the PCP.
expected_los: Tiered by DRG: uncomplicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy (DRG 419) typically same-day to ~2 days (GMLOS ~1.8 days); with CC (418) ~3 days; with MCC (417) ~5-6 days. Open cholecystectomy without CDE (414-416) ~3-8 days rising with CC/MCC. Cholecystectomy with common duct exploration (411-413) ~3-9 days rising with CC/MCC. Overall admitted-cohort typical ~2-4 days. (CMS GMLOS values vary by fiscal-year MS-DRG version.)
target_los: Optimized best-practice: uncomplicated symptomatic stones/biliary colic and many Grade I acute cholecystitis-ambulatory/same-day or <=1 day; Grade I-II acute cholecystitis with early laparoscopic cholecystectomy ~1-2 days; mild gallstone pancreatitis with same-admission cholecystectomy ~2-3 days; complicated, open, converted, or CDE cases ~3-5 days. Early index-admission surgery shortens stay markedly (ACDC: 5.4 vs 10.0 days for immediate vs delayed).
quality_metrics: Perioperative process measures: prophylactic antibiotic within 60 min of incision and appropriate selection/discontinuation; VTE prophylaxis ordered; normothermia/glucose control. Condition-specific: index/same-admission cholecystectomy rate for acute cholecystitis and mild gallstone pancreatitis (PONCHO, ASGE); bile duct injury rate and documented Critical View of Safety (SAGES); laparoscopic-to-open conversion rate; surgical-site infection rate; unplanned return to OR; 30-day readmission; mortality. SEP-1 sepsis bundle compliance when septic cholangitis present. ERAS protocol compliance. Choosing Wisely-avoid routine intraoperative cholangiography and avoid unnecessary ERCP in low CBD-stone-probability patients.
common_complications: Bile duct injury (~0.2-0.5% laparoscopic-mitigated by Critical View of Safety, intraoperative cholangiography for unclear anatomy, and subtotal cholecystectomy/conversion bailout); bile leak/cystic-duct stump or duct-of-Luschka leak (manage with ERCP + sphincterotomy/stent +/- percutaneous drainage); retained CBD stone (intraoperative cholangiography and post-op ERCP); surgical-site/intra-abdominal infection or abscess; conversion to open; intra-operative or post-op bleeding; postoperative ileus; sepsis; VTE; in elderly/Grade III patients cardiopulmonary events and higher mortality; post-ERCP pancreatitis.
readmission_drivers: Retained or recurrent common bile duct stones; bile leak/biloma; surgical-site infection or intra-abdominal collection/abscess; recurrent gallstone-related events (pancreatitis, cholecystitis, cholangitis, colic) when cholecystectomy is deferred-index/same-admission cholecystectomy prevents these (PONCHO: 5% vs 17% recurrent events); post-ERCP pancreatitis; uncontrolled pain or diet intolerance; premature discharge. Prevention levers: same-admission definitive surgery, complete ductal clearance (IOC/ERCP), Critical View of Safety technique, judicious drain management, and structured early follow-up with clear return precautions.
guideline_source: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18; Japanese Society of Hepato-Biliary-Pancreatic Surgery / international consensus)-diagnosis, severity grading, management flowchart, and antimicrobial therapy for acute cholecystitis and cholangitis. World Society of Emergency Surgery (WSES) 2020 updated guidelines for acute calculous cholecystitis. SAGES/AHPBA/IHPBA/SSAT/EAES multi-society Safe Cholecystectomy practice guideline 2020 (bile duct injury prevention/Critical View of Safety). ASGE 2019 guideline on endoscopy in evaluation and management of choledocholithiasis. Reinforced by ACDC, CHOCOLATE, and PONCHO randomized trials.
key_citations: TG18 flowchart for management of acute cholecystitis-Okamoto et al., J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci 2018, PMID 29045062, doi 10.1002/jhbp.516. TG18 antimicrobial therapy for acute cholangitis and cholecystitis-Gomi et al. 2018, PMID 29090866, doi 10.1002/jhbp.518. 2020 WSES guidelines for acute calculus cholecystitis-Pisano et al., World J Emerg Surg 2020, PMID 33153472, doi 10.1186/s13017-020-00336-x. Safe cholecystectomy multi-society guideline (bile duct injury prevention)-Brunt et al., Surg Endosc 2020, PMID 32399938, doi 10.1007/s00464-020-07568-7. ASGE guideline on choledocholithiasis-Buxbaum et al., Gastrointest Endosc 2019, PMID 30979521, doi 10.1016/j.gie.2018.10.001. ACDC trial (early vs delayed)-Gutt et al., Ann Surg 2013, PMID 24022431, doi 10.1097/SLA.0b013e3182a1599b. CHOCOLATE trial (high-risk)-Loozen et al., BMJ 2018, PMID 30297544, doi 10.1136/bmj.k3965. PONCHO trial (gallstone pancreatitis)-da Costa et al., Lancet 2015, PMID 26460661, doi 10.1016/S0140-6736(15)00274-3. (Citations retrieved from PubMed.)
evidence_grade: Strong-multiple current Class I/II society guidelines (TG18 2018, WSES 2020, SAGES multi-society 2020, ASGE 2019) reinforced by high-quality RCTs. GRADE-strong evidence supports early index-admission laparoscopic cholecystectomy (ACDC), same-admission cholecystectomy for mild gallstone pancreatitis (PONCHO), cholecystectomy over percutaneous drainage even in high-risk patients (CHOCOLATE), and Critical View of Safety / intraoperative biliary imaging (SAGES strong recs). Caveat: TG18 diagnostic criteria have limited sensitivity (~53% vs pathology, Joseph 2018) and severity grading is imperfect, so clinical judgment supplements the algorithm.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The collapsed group splits three ways by approach/complexity-laparoscopic without CDE (417-419), open without CDE (414-416), and any cholecystectomy with common duct exploration (411-413)-each subdivided by MCC/CC/none. MCC drivers (sepsis/septic shock, acute respiratory/renal/hepatic failure, gangrenous or perforated/emphysematous cholecystitis, suppurative cholangitis) map to TG18 Grade III, drive ICU admission, markedly longer LOS, and higher mortality. CC (moderate comorbidity, choledocholithiasis, localized infection, controlled organ stress) yields intermediate stays. The open and CDE tiers reflect difficult anatomy, failed laparoscopy, or duct stones requiring exploration-longer LOS and more complications. Pathway levers to down-shift severity: early source control plus sepsis bundles to prevent organ failure, laparoscopic-first with CVS to avoid open conversion, and ERCP-first ductal clearance to avoid open CDE.
pathway_pearls: 1) EARLY index-admission laparoscopic cholecystectomy is the single highest-yield lever-ACDC showed morbidity 11.8% vs 34.4% and LOS 5.4 vs 10.0 days vs delayed surgery; PONCHO mandates same-admission surgery for mild gallstone pancreatitis; CHOCOLATE shows cholecystectomy beats drainage even in high-risk patients. 2) Prevent the catastrophic bile duct injury: achieve and document the Critical View of Safety, get intraoperative cholangiography for unclear anatomy, and default to subtotal cholecystectomy or open conversion for the difficult gallbladder (SAGES). 3) Risk-stratify CBD stones (ASGE)-high probability straight to ERCP, intermediate to MRCP/EUS, low straight to surgery-to avoid both missed stones and unnecessary ERCP. 4) Grade III cholecystitis/cholangitis = sepsis bundle within 1 hour plus biliary decompression within 12-24h; reserve percutaneous cholecystostomy for the truly non-operable.

scope_and_volume_notes: 7th most frequent OR procedure (HCUP: 0.34M, 2018). Laparoscopic (417-419) and open (414-416), with/without common duct exploration. Underlying dx: cholecystitis/cholelithiasis (HCUP #18 biliary tract disease).
