admission_criteria: Most acute gastroenteritis/GERD/functional GI is ED-discharge or <24h observation after oral rehydration + antiemetics; reserve INPATIENT admission for objective failure/instability. Admit for: failed ED oral-rehydration trial with intractable vomiting; moderate-severe dehydration with hemodynamic instability (SBP <90, HR >100, or orthostasis) not corrected by IV bolus; AKI (KDIGO >=1, creatinine >=1.5x baseline) or significant electrolyte/acid-base derangement (K <3.0 or >6.0, Na <125 or >155, HCO3 <15/severe anion-gap acidosis); dysentery with sepsis physiology, high fever, or immunocompromise; need for ongoing IV fluids/antiemetics >24h; esophageal food-bolus impaction needing urgent EGD; complicated/bleeding esophagitis or odynophagia preventing intake; decompensated comorbidity (DKA/uncontrolled diabetes, CKD, frailty, elderly without home support). Use observation (not inpatient) when expected resolution <24-48h
risk_stratification: WHO/clinical dehydration classification (mild <5%, moderate 5-9%, severe >=10% body-weight loss) -> severe drives IV resuscitation + admission; orthostatic vitals (SBP fall >=20 mmHg or HR rise >=30 bpm on standing) flag volume depletion. qSOFA >=2 or NEWS2 >=5 -> sepsis workup/escalation, do NOT mislabel as simple gastroenteritis. KDIGO AKI staging (stage 1 = Cr 1.5-1.9x baseline) -> fluids + hold nephrotoxins. Dysentery/severity criteria (grossly bloody/mucoid stool, T >38.5C, >=6 unformed stools/24h, or immunocompromise) -> send stool studies + consider targeted empiric antibiotics UNLESS STEC suspected. If esophagitis-related upper GI bleed: Glasgow-Blatchford Score <=1 = low risk (outpatient), higher -> admit + EGD; AIMS65 adjunct. Gastroparesis symptom burden tracked by GCSI (not an admission trigger)
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential (leukocytosis; markedly elevated WBC predicts STEC/HUS risk; eosinophilia suggests EoE); CMP/BMP (Na, K, Cl, HCO3, BUN/Cr for dehydration + AKI, glucose, anion gap); magnesium + phosphate with vomiting/refeeding risk; lipase if epigastric pain (exclude pancreatitis); venous lactate + blood cultures if sepsis screen positive; VBG for acid-base; urinalysis (specific gravity, ketones); beta-hCG in women of childbearing age; type & screen + hemoccult if bleeding. Targeted stool studies when dysentery/immunocompromised/severe/duration >7d: multiplex GI PCR panel or culture PLUS Shiga-toxin EIA (STEC), C. difficile toxin/PCR if antibiotic-associated or healthcare-onset, Giardia antigen/O&P if travel/waterborne or >14d. HbA1c if new hyperglycemia; TSH if gastroparesis suspected; troponin/ECG if chest pain to exclude cardiac mimic of GERD
initial_imaging_dx: Not routinely imaged. Upright CXR/acute abdominal series if perforation/obstruction suspected (free air); CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast during index visit for diagnostic uncertainty, severe/localized pain, or suspected complication. EGD: urgent within 24h for esophageal food-bolus impaction, EMERGENT <6h if complete obstruction or inability to manage secretions (aspiration risk); EGD for complicated/erosive esophagitis, odynophagia, or dysphagia with biopsies for eosinophilic/infectious esophagitis; REPEAT EGD at 8-12 weeks for LA grade C/D erosive esophagitis to confirm healing and exclude Barrett esophagus. Gastric-emptying scintigraphy (4-hour solid-phase; >10% retention at 4h = delayed) is the gold standard for gastroparesis, done outpatient after EGD excludes mechanical obstruction and after stopping emptying-delaying drugs (opioids, GLP-1 agonists, anticholinergics). ECG to assess electrolyte effects/QT and exclude cardiac cause
time_critical_interventions: No reperfusion-style bundle for benign GI, but time-anchored actions: antiemetic + start oral or IV rehydration within the first hour of ED triage for the dehydrated/vomiting patient. Esophageal food-bolus impaction -> endoscopic disimpaction within 24h (emergent <6h if complete obstruction or airway/secretion compromise). If sepsis identified (qSOFA >=2 / elevated lactate) -> Surviving Sepsis 1-hour bundle: blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid, measure + recheck lactate (SEP-1). Urgently correct life-threatening derangements (K <2.5, severe symptomatic hyponatremia, severe acidosis). Esophagitis-related upper GI bleed -> resuscitate, IV PPI, EGD within 24h
initial_management: Rehydration: FIRST-LINE reduced-osmolarity oral rehydration solution (ORS) when tolerated; IV balanced isotonic crystalloid (lactated Ringer or 0.9% NaCl) bolus then maintenance for moderate-severe dehydration/failed ORT, replacing K/Mg/Phos. Antiemetics: ondansetron (5-HT3 antagonist) first-line (monitor QTc); metoclopramide (also prokinetic), promethazine/prochlorperazine (phenothiazines) alternatives; cannabinoid hyperemesis/cyclic vomiting -> haloperidol or droperidol + topical capsaicin. Antidiarrheals: loperamide for non-dysenteric watery diarrhea ONLY (avoid with fever/bloody stool/suspected STEC or C. diff); bismuth subsalicylate alternative. Antibiotics targeted NOT routine: empiric azithromycin (preferred) or fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin) only for moderate-severe traveler's diarrhea or febrile dysentery WITHOUT STEC features; AVOID empiric antibiotics AND antimotility agents when E. coli O157:H7/STEC suspected (increases HUS risk); C. difficile -> oral fidaxomicin or vancomycin (metronidazole reserved); Giardia -> tinidazole/metronidazole. Acid suppression: PPI (omeprazole/pantoprazole/esomeprazole) standard once-daily x8 weeks for erosive esophagitis/GERD (twice-daily for LA C/D or partial response), IV PPI if bleeding/NPO; infectious esophagitis -> fluconazole (Candida)/acyclovir (HSV)/ganciclovir (CMV); EoE -> PPI trial, swallowed topical budesonide/fluticasone, elimination diet. Gastroparesis prokinetics: metoclopramide first-line (lowest dose, <12 weeks; tardive-dyskinesia black box), alternatives domperidone or IV erythromycin. Transfuse RBC at restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 with cardiovascular disease) if bleeding esophagitis; inpatient glucose target 140-180 mg/dL
day1_milestones: Confirm working diagnosis and actively exclude surgical abdomen, sepsis, DKA, and cardiac mimic; send targeted stool studies; correct volume deficit and electrolytes with documented response; start scheduled antiemetics and begin oral-rehydration/clear-liquid challenge; VTE risk assessment (Padua/IMPROVE) with prophylaxis decision; medication review to stop nephrotoxins and unnecessary/empiric antibiotics; if STEC possible, ensure NO empiric antibiotics or antimotility agents; if food impaction, complete EGD and protect airway; set observation-vs-inpatient status appropriately and begin discharge planning on day 1
day2_milestones: Reassess hydration, renal function, and electrolytes (trend Cr, K, Mg, HCO3); advance diet as tolerated (low-residue then regular); transition IV fluids/antiemetics to oral; confirm diarrhea/vomiting frequency decreasing; review culture/PCR/C. difficile/Shiga-toxin results and tailor or de-escalate antimicrobials with a defined stop date; discontinue IV fluids once oral intake adequate and remove peripheral IV/urinary catheter; reinforce patient education and early mobilization; identify and address the underlying driver (GERD lifestyle, gastroparesis, EoE, C. diff)
day3plus_milestones: Tolerating oral intake and oral medications without intractable vomiting; renal function and electrolytes stable or improving; hemodynamically stable and afebrile >=24h; diarrhea/vomiting resolving; antimicrobial plan finalized with defined duration; arrange indicated outpatient follow-up (repeat EGD at 8-12 weeks for LA C/D erosive esophagitis, gastric-emptying study/motility clinic for gastroparesis, EoE biopsy follow-up, GI clinic); confirm no unresolved red flags (ongoing bleeding, HUS features, obstruction); discharge when criteria met - most uncomplicated cases reach readiness by day 2, MCC cases by day 3
consults_multidisciplinary: Gastroenterology: food-bolus impaction/EGD, refractory or erosive/infectious esophagitis, suspected EoE (biopsies), gastroparesis workup, GI bleeding, refractory symptoms. Infectious Disease: complicated/recurrent C. difficile, immunocompromised host, invasive/resistant enteric pathogens. Surgery: peritonitis, obstruction, or perforation on imaging. Nutrition/dietitian: dehydration + refeeding risk, gastroparesis diet (small, frequent, low-fat/low-fiber), EoE elimination diet. Pharmacy: QTc review of antiemetics, antibiotic stewardship/de-escalation, PPI deprescribing, medication reconciliation. Care management/social work: hydration risk factors, frail/elderly living alone, substance use (cannabinoid hyperemesis). PT/OT for deconditioned or elderly patients to ensure safe discharge
monitoring_level: General medical/surgical floor for the large majority. Telemetry if significant electrolyte derangement (hypo/hyperkalemia), baseline QTc prolongation with QT-prolonging antiemetics, or cardiac comorbidity. Step-down/ICU only for hemodynamic instability/septic shock, severe metabolic derangement requiring aggressive correction, massive GI hemorrhage, or airway compromise/aspiration. Escalation triggers: persistent hypotension or tachycardia despite adequate resuscitation, rising lactate, worsening AKI, altered mental status, or NEWS2 >=7
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: early oral rehydration, avoid prolonged NPO; advance clear liquids -> low-residue -> regular as tolerated; gastroparesis = small frequent low-fat/low-fiber meals (liquid nutrition if severe); EoE = elimination diet; watch refeeding electrolytes. Mobility: early ambulation to prevent deconditioning, especially in the elderly. VTE: risk-assess (Padua/IMPROVE); pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin or unfractionated heparin) for moderate-high risk without active bleeding, mechanical prophylaxis if bleeding risk; many short-stay low-risk patients need only early mobilization. Glucose target 140-180 mg/dL; manage diabetic gastroparesis glycemia. Remove peripheral IVs and urinary catheters promptly once oral intake is adequate to reduce CLABSI/CAUTI
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable and afebrile; tolerating oral fluids and food without intractable vomiting; oral medications tolerated; electrolytes and renal function corrected or reliably improving; pain controlled; diarrhea/vomiting frequency clearly decreasing; safe hydration achievable at home; appropriate follow-up arranged; red-flag conditions (GI bleed, HUS, obstruction, sepsis) excluded or resolved
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home; SNF/rehab for the deconditioned elderly. Medication reconciliation: define antimicrobial duration, set 8-week PPI course for erosive esophagitis with planned reassessment/step-down, PRN antiemetic with QTc caution, stop inappropriate antimotility agents, deprescribe unnecessary PPIs. Follow-up: PCP within 7-14 days; GI for EGD/biopsy results, repeat EGD (LA C/D) at 8-12 weeks, gastric-emptying/motility clinic, EoE follow-up. Education: oral-rehydration technique, explicit return precautions (bloody stools, persistent vomiting, decreased urination, high fever, confusion), hand hygiene/food safety, GERD lifestyle measures (weight loss, head-of-bed elevation, avoid late meals), cannabis cessation for hyperemesis. Transitions: report reportable enteric pathogens to public health; provide food-handler/childcare/school work-exclusion guidance
expected_los: GMLOS 2.5 days for DRG 392 (without MCC; AMLOS 3.2 days, relative weight 0.78) and 3.8 days for DRG 391 (with MCC; AMLOS 5.1 days, relative weight 1.27); blended typical stay 2.5-3.8 days (CMS IPPS benchmarks)
target_los: Non-MCC (DRG 392): 1-2 days, with many cases appropriately diverted to observation status or ED discharge after an oral-rehydration + antiemetic pathway; with-MCC (DRG 391): ~3 days once AKI/electrolytes corrected and oral intake restored. Primary lever is minimizing avoidable inpatient conversions rather than shaving inpatient days
quality_metrics: Not a mandated CMS core-measure condition, but track: 30-day all-cause readmission rate; avoidable-admission / observation-appropriateness rate and inpatient-vs-observation status accuracy; LOS index (observed/expected); antibiotic stewardship (avoidance of inappropriate empiric antibiotics, especially in suspected STEC); appropriate C. difficile testing/treatment and CDI standardized infection ratio; SEP-1 compliance when sepsis present; PPI stewardship/deprescribing; hospital-acquired conditions (CAUTI, CLABSI); HCAHPS patient experience
common_complications: Hypovolemic AKI; electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia, hyponatremia, hypomagnesemia) with arrhythmia risk (QTc prolongation from ondansetron); antibiotic-associated C. difficile; HUS if antibiotics/antimotility given in STEC; aspiration from vomiting or food impaction; Mallory-Weiss tear from retching; refeeding electrolyte shifts; healthcare-associated infection (CAUTI/CLABSI). Pathway mitigates via targeted (not empiric) antibiotics, STEC antibiotic avoidance, QTc monitoring, aggressive early oral rehydration, electrolyte repletion, and prompt line/catheter removal
readmission_drivers: Recurrent dehydration/inability to maintain oral intake; premature discharge before tolerating diet; untreated or undertreated root cause (gastroparesis, EoE, refractory GERD, recurrent C. difficile); missed serious diagnosis (IBD, malignancy, mesenteric ischemia); medication nonadherence; social barriers (poor access, elderly living alone). Prevention levers: confirm reliable oral tolerance and corrected labs before discharge, explicit written return precautions, early PCP/GI follow-up, definitive treatment of the underlying driver, a concrete home hydration plan, and C. difficile recurrence counseling
guideline_source: ACG Clinical Guideline: Diagnosis, Treatment, and Prevention of Acute Diarrheal Infections in Adults (American College of Gastroenterology, 2016); IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Infectious Diarrhea (Infectious Diseases Society of America, 2017); ACG Clinical Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of GERD (2022); ACG Clinical Guideline: Gastroparesis (2022); ACG/CAG Clinical Guideline: Management of Dyspepsia (2017). All remain current as of 2026 with no superseding major revision

Am J Gastroenterol 2016;111(5):602-22 (PMID 27068718; doi.org/10.1038/ajg.2016.126). Shane AL et al. 2017 IDSA Guidelines for Infectious Diarrhea. Clin Infect Dis 2017;65(12):e45-e80 (PMID 29053792; doi.org/10.1093/cid/cix669). Katz PO et al. ACG Clinical Guideline for Diagnosis and Management of GERD. Am J Gastroenterol 2022;117(1):27-56 (PMID 34807007; doi.org/10.14309/ajg.0000000000001538). Camilleri M et al. ACG Clinical Guideline: Gastroparesis. evidence_grade: Moderate overall. Component society guidelines are STRONG for specific recommendations (ACG/IDSA GRADE-strong / Class I for PPI in erosive esophagitis, oral rehydration therapy, targeted rather than routine antimicrobials, and antibiotic avoidance in suspected STEC given ~14-17x HUS risk). However, NO single dedicated inpatient care-pathway guideline exists for this collapsed catch-all DRG - the admission thresholds, LOS targets, and day-by-day milestone sequencing are expert-consensus/extrapolated good-practice assembled across the individual condition guidelines
severity_cc_mcc_notes: This is a 2-tier group split by MCC only (DRG 391 with MCC vs DRG 392 without MCC; there is no separate CC-only DRG in this family). MCCs that drive the split, higher acuity, and longer stay include acute renal failure/AKI, severe electrolyte and acid-base disorders, sepsis, GI hemorrhage, aspiration pneumonia, and hypovolemic shock. With-MCC (391) roughly doubles complexity: GMLOS 3.8 vs 2.5 days, higher likelihood of telemetry/step-down, and ID/GI/nephrology involvement. Accurate documentation and coding of AKI stage, specific electrolyte disorders, sepsis, and dehydration severity drives correct tier assignment; many no-MCC (392) cases are genuinely observation-appropriate rather than inpatient
pathway_pearls: 1) Much of this high-volume DRG is avoidable inpatient volume - an aggressive early oral-rehydration + antiemetic pathway with an observation-status track prevents unnecessary admissions and shortens LOS. 2) Antibiotic discipline is the highest-stakes lever: do NOT give empiric antibiotics or antimotility agents for febrile bloody diarrhea when STEC/E. coli O157:H7 is possible (raises HUS risk ~14-17x, Wong 2000 NEJM); reserve empiric azithromycin/fluoroquinolone for dysentery/traveler's diarrhea without STEC features, and test-and-treat C. difficile appropriately. 3) Do not anchor - screen every presumed gastroenteritis for sepsis (qSOFA/NEWS2), AKI, DKA, and surgical abdomen/ACS masquerading as GERD before labeling it benign. 4) Confirm reliable oral tolerance and corrected electrolytes/renal function before discharge to prevent dehydration bounce-backs, and set a defined 8-week erosive-esophagitis PPI course with planned reassessment while deprescribing unnecessary PPIs
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: High-volume medical GI catch-all (a top-10 Medicare DRG). Includes gastroenteritis, GERD/esophagitis, functional GI disorders.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 391 — Esophagitis, Gastroenteritis and Miscellaneous Digestive Disorders with MCC; 392 — Esophagitis, Gastroenteritis and Miscellaneous Digestive Disorders without MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 6/6 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Current pass completed citation-resolvability, coding, taxonomy, schema, and consistency checks. The source workflow contains no completed adversarial high-stakes clinical verdict for this row; clinician revalidation remains required before protocol or order-set use.
data_quality_notes: Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk. Current DRG overlap with pathway rank(s): 112.

admission_criteria: Inpatient admission (not ED d/c or obs) for: symptomatic or profound hyponatremia — serum Na <120 mmol/L, or <125 with symptoms (nausea/vomiting, confusion, seizure, coma), or ACUTE (<48h) drop needing IV 3% saline and q2-4h Na monitoring; hypokalemia K <2.5 mEq/L, or <3.0 with ECG changes/arrhythmia/paralysis/digoxin use, or refractory to oral repletion; severe dehydration/hypovolemia with AKI, hemodynamic instability, or unable to tolerate PO after ED resuscitation; suspected adrenal crisis or symptomatic SIADH needing water restriction + monitoring. ED-discharge/observation instead: mild asymptomatic hyponatremia 130-134 with clear reversible cause; mild hypokalemia 3.0-3.4 replaceable orally; mild dehydration that corrects with ED fluids and tolerates PO.
risk_stratification: No single classic risk score; stratify on 3 axes. (1) SYMPTOM severity (European CPG 2014): SEVERE (vomiting, seizures, GCS <=8, cardiorespiratory distress) or MODERATELY-severe (nausea w/o vomiting, headache, confusion) -> immediate 3% hypertonic saline; mild -> cause-directed. (2) BIOCHEMICAL severity: mild Na 130-134, moderate 125-129, profound/severe <125 mmol/L; apply hyperglycemia correction (+~2.4 mmol/L Na per 100 mg/dL glucose >100). (3) ACUITY: acute <48h (cerebral-edema risk, correct promptly) vs chronic >=48h/unknown (osmotic demyelination risk, correct slowly). ODS high-risk flags -> tighter cap (<=8/24h): Na <=105, hypokalemia, alcoholism, malnutrition, advanced liver disease. Hypokalemia: severe <2.5 or QTc prolongation/U-waves/arrhythmia -> monitored bed. Volume status (hypovolemic vs euvolemic/SIADH vs hypervolemic) via exam + urine osm/urine Na drives etiology and therapy.
initial_workup_labs: STAT BMP (Na, K, Cl, HCO3, BUN, Cr, glucose) with corrected Na; serum osmolality (confirm hypotonic <275 mOsm/kg); the pivotal paired URINE osmolality + URINE sodium (Uosm >100 with UNa >30 in euvolemia = SIADH/impaired free-water excretion; UNa <20-30 = hypovolemia/low effective circulating volume); serum magnesium, calcium, phosphate (Mg mandatory before/with K repletion; PO4 for refeeding risk); TSH and 8am cortisol (exclude hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency); serum uric acid (low <4 mg/dL supports SIADH) and LFTs; VBG; lipids/total protein if pseudohyponatremia suspected; CK if weakness. Triggers: Uosm/UNa classify hypo-/eu-/hypervolemic and set therapy; low cortisol -> stress-dose steroids; low Mg -> replace (refractory hypokalemia otherwise); low PO4 -> refeeding precautions.
initial_imaging_dx: STAT 12-lead ECG for any significant K derangement (U-waves, flattened T, ST depression, QT prolongation -> torsades risk in hypokalemia; peaked T/widened QRS if hyperkalemia) — drives telemetry decision. Imaging is not diagnostic of the electrolyte disorder itself but targets etiology: chest X-ray +/- CT chest for SIADH source (malignancy, pneumonia); non-contrast CT head if focal deficit, seizure, or unexplained severe AMS to exclude alternative pathology and assess edema; POCUS (IVC/lung) to adjudicate volume status at bedside. MRI brain (pons/extrapontine) reserved for suspected osmotic demyelination — imaging lags clinical signs by 1-2 weeks, so a normal early MRI does not exclude ODS. No routine advanced imaging.
time_critical_interventions: Severe symptomatic hyponatremia (seizure/coma/cardioresp distress): 150 mL 3% (hypertonic) NaCl IV over 20 min (or 2 mL/kg), recheck Na at ~20-30 min, REPEAT bolus up to 2-3x until serum Na rises 5 mmol/L or symptoms resolve — target +5 mmol/L in the FIRST HOUR to relieve cerebral edema (SALSA: rapid intermittent bolus preferred over continuous infusion). Hard ceilings: <=10 mmol/L rise in first 24h, <=8 mmol/L/24h thereafter; stop hypertonic when Na up 10 or reaches 130. Recheck Na q2h during active hypertonic therapy. Overcorrection approaching/exceeding limit: RESCUE with D5W (3 mL/kg/h) +/- desmopressin 2-4 mcg IV/SC q6-8h to re-lower. Severe hypokalemia with ECG changes/arrhythmia/paralysis: IV KCl <=10 mEq/h peripheral (up to 20 mEq/h central) on telemetry + IV magnesium. Suspected adrenal crisis: hydrocortisone 100 mg IV immediately.
initial_management: Etiology-directed. HYPOVOLEMIC hyponatremia/dehydration: restore volume with isotonic crystalloid — balanced solution (LR/Plasma-Lyte) generally preferred over 0.9% saline for resuscitation (SMART: lower MAKE30, less hyperchloremic acidosis), but anticipate ADH shutoff -> brisk aquaresis/auto-overcorrection once euvolemic. EUVOLEMIC/SIADH: fluid restriction <800 mL-1 L/day first-line; if inadequate, add oral urea 15-30 g/day or NaCl tablets + loop diuretic (furosemide); tolvaptan (V2-antagonist) only specialist-initiated, boxed warning (in-hospital start, no fluid restriction initially, avoid in liver disease, monitor overcorrection). HYPERVOLEMIC (HF/cirrhosis): sodium + fluid restriction, loop diuretic, treat underlying. SEVERE SYMPTOMATIC (any volume): 3% saline per protocol above. HYPOKALEMIA: KCl oral if K >2.5 and tolerating (20 mEq raises serum ~0.2 mEq/L; each 0.3 below normal ~100 mEq deficit), IV if <2.5/NPO/symptomatic; ALWAYS co-repletion magnesium (MgSO4 1-2 g IV). Correct hypophosphatemia/hypocalcemia. Stop culprits (thiazides, SSRI/SNRI, carbamazepine, desmopressin, NSAIDs); stress-dose steroids if adrenal insufficiency.
day1_milestones: Establish volume status and etiology (Uosm/UNa, TSH, cortisol resulted); classify acute vs chronic and ODS risk. Start targeted therapy and achieve CONTROLLED Na rise within limits (aim +4-6, cap <=10 mmol/L/24h; <=8 if high-risk). Serial Na q2-4h if on hypertonic/high-risk, q6h otherwise; explicit overcorrection watch (if trending fast -> D5W +/- desmopressin). Begin K + Mg repletion; recheck K q4-6h until >3.5; continuous telemetry if K <2.5 or ECG changes. Deprescribe offending drugs. Strict I/O, daily weight, place fluid-restriction order if SIADH; monitor UOP hourly if aquaresis risk. Avoid hypotonic maintenance fluids. Foley only if genuinely indicated.
day2_milestones: Na on-target trajectory (cumulative <=8-10 per 24h; re-lower with D5W/desmopressin if overshoot detected); transition from hypertonic to oral/maintenance strategy. K and Mg normalized or normalizing; convert IV to oral potassium. Volume status optimized; culprit meds held/adjusted. Document definitive cause (SIADH subtype, drug-induced, GI losses, adrenal/thyroid). Begin patient education on fluid restriction/salt and supplement plan. De-escalate telemetry once K >3.5 and no arrhythmia. Early mobilization; assess fall risk (hyponatremia causes gait instability).
day3plus_milestones: Serum Na stable and safely in range (typically >=125-130 and rising <=8/24h, asymptomatic) on a sustainable oral regimen for >=24h without IV hypertonic; K and Mg stable on oral repletion; euvolemic and tolerating PO. Underlying cause treated or chronic plan set (e.g., water restriction +/- urea/tolvaptan for persistent SIADH, diuretic adjustment for HF/cirrhosis). No clinical signs of ODS. Finalize discharge meds, outpatient lab plan, and education. Non-MCC (DRG 641) cases typically discharge day 2-3; profound/MCC (DRG 640) or ICU-requiring cases extend to day 4+.
consults_multidisciplinary: Nephrology (complex/refractory dysnatremia, ODS-risk correction, tolvaptan/urea management, RRT questions); Endocrinology (SIADH, adrenal insufficiency, hypothyroidism); Critical care/intensivist if ICU-level (seizing, intubated, q1-2h Na); Pharmacy (hypertonic saline + K/Mg protocol dosing, culprit-drug review, tolvaptan stewardship, desmopressin clamp); Clinical nutrition/dietitian early (malnutrition is prevalent in this DRG, refeeding-syndrome monitoring, electrolyte-rich diet, fluid-restriction adherence); Care management + social work early (elderly, recurrent admissions, supplement/med access, alcohol use disorder); PT/OT for deconditioned/fall-risk patients; Palliative care for malignancy-driven SIADH; Psychiatry if primary polydipsia.
monitoring_level: ICU: severe symptomatic hyponatremia needing 3% saline with q1-2h Na, active seizures, GCS <=8/airway compromise, arrhythmia from severe hypokalemia, or hemodynamic instability. Step-down/telemetry: active 3% saline with q2-4h Na, K <2.5 or ECG changes, IV KCl >10 mEq/h, or high overcorrection risk on desmopressin clamp. Floor: hemodynamically stable, oral or low-rate IV repletion, Na monitoring q6-12h. Escalation triggers: new neurologic change or seizure, Na rise >8-10 mmol/L/24h (overcorrection), refractory K <2.5, new arrhythmia, or hemodynamic decline.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet cause-specific: fluid restriction <800 mL-1 L/day for SIADH/hypervolemic states, with liberal sodium/protein to aid solute-driven water excretion in SIADH; potassium- and magnesium-rich diet plus oral repletion for hypokalemia; cautious stepwise refeeding in malnourished/alcoholic patients with q6-12h K/PO4/Mg to prevent refeeding syndrome. Early mobilization with PT for deconditioned elderly; fall precautions (chronic hyponatremia impairs gait/attention). VTE prophylaxis by risk (Padua >=4): pharmacologic LMWH/UFH unless contraindicated, otherwise mechanical. Glucose control per protocol (correct hyperglycemia contributing to hyponatremia). Remove peripheral IV/central lines and Foley promptly once repletion complete and aquaresis risk passed to limit line-associated infection.
discharge_criteria: Serum Na corrected to a safe range (typically >=125-130 mmol/L, asymptomatic, corrected at a safe rate) and stable on an oral/sustainable regimen for >=24h with no IV hypertonic and no ongoing overcorrection risk; K >=3.5 mEq/L and Mg normal on oral repletion with no ECG changes/arrhythmia; euvolemic and tolerating oral intake; underlying cause identified with a documented management plan; culprit medications reconciled/deprescribed; and patient/caregiver able to follow the plan with a scheduled outpatient recheck.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home; SNF if deconditioned/frail. Medication reconciliation: stop or substitute culprits (thiazides, SSRI/SNRI, carbamazepine, desmopressin, NSAIDs, PPIs for hypomagnesemia); provide K/Mg supplements; give explicit SIADH plan (fluid-restriction target in mL/day, salt/urea, tolvaptan monitoring if applicable). CRITICAL follow-up: repeat BMP within 24-72h (catches re-drop or delayed over-rise in chronic hyponatremia) with PCP, plus nephrology/endocrinology within 1-2 weeks for persistent SIADH/adrenal/thyroid causes. Education: fluid-restriction adherence, recurrence symptoms, avoid OTC NSAIDs, alcohol counseling. Transitions: written action plan, arranged lab draw, and address social drivers (supplement access, dietary support, transportation).
expected_los: CMS IPPS benchmark (MDC 10): DRG 640 (with MCC) GMLOS ~3.7-3.9 days (AMLOS ~4.7-4.9); DRG 641 (without MCC) GMLOS ~2.7-2.9 days (AMLOS ~3.3-3.5); blended national mean ~3.2-3.5 days.
target_los: ~2.5-3.0 days blended under a protocolized pathway (DRG 641 non-MCC ~2 days; DRG 640 MCC ~3.5 days); mild orally-replaceable cases diverted to ED/observation <24h to avoid inpatient admission.
quality_metrics: No condition-specific CMS core measure (no SEP-1 analog). Track: sodium overcorrection rate (>10 mmol/L/24h, or >8 in high-ODS-risk) as a patient-safety metric; osmotic demyelination incidence (treat as preventable never-event); iatrogenic hyponatremia/hypervolemia from inappropriate hypotonic IV fluids (fluid stewardship); severe hypoglycemia/hyperkalemia adverse-drug-event rates (NQF); CMS Hospital-Wide 30-day all-cause readmission; observed-to-expected LOS and O/E mortality; medication-reconciliation completion; 24-72h post-discharge BMP completion; malnutrition screening/documentation and accurate CC/MCC capture (coding integrity for this DRG); PSI-90 safety composite.
common_complications: Osmotic demyelination syndrome (central pontine myelinolysis) from overcorrection — mitigate with strict <=10/<=8 mmol/L/24h limits, serial Na q2-4h, and proactive D5W/desmopressin. Cerebral edema/seizure from undertreated acute severe hyponatremia — mitigate with prompt 3% saline. Auto-overcorrection once hypovolemia is repleted (ADH suppression -> brisk aquaresis) — anticipate, monitor UOP, desmopressin clamp. Iatrogenic worsening from hypotonic maintenance fluids. Hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia-induced arrhythmia/torsades — telemetry + Mg co-repletion. Rebound/recurrent hyponatremia; falls/fractures from gait instability; refeeding syndrome in malnourished; phlebitis/line infection from peripheral hypertonic or concentrated KCl.
readmission_drivers: Top drivers: unresolved/uncontrolled SIADH (persistent free-water retention); restart or continuation of the culprit drug (thiazide, SSRI); poor fluid-restriction adherence; recurrent GI losses/dehydration in the frail elderly; missed outpatient follow-up labs (undetected re-drop); decompensated heart failure or cirrhosis; alcohol use disorder; malnutrition. Prevention levers: treat the definitive cause before discharge, deprescribe the culprit, provide a concrete fluid-restriction plan with education, ensure an EARLY 24-72h post-discharge BMP and clinic visit, establish nephrology/endocrinology linkage, and address social determinants (supplement access, nutrition, alcohol/nutrition referral).
guideline_source: PRIMARY: European hyponatraemia Clinical Practice Guideline 2014 — joint ESE (European Society of Endocrinology) + ESICM + ERA-EDTA/ERBP (Spasovski et al.); and US Expert Panel Recommendations 2013 (Verbalis et al., Am J Med). SUPPORTING RCT/evidence: SALSA RCT 2021 (hypertonic-saline delivery); SMART 2018 (balanced crystalloids vs saline); AAFP Potassium Disorders review 2023 (hypokalemia thresholds/replacement); ADA standards for glycemic contribution. No single dedicated US society guideline exists for hypokalemia or dehydration — those elements are expert-consensus/extrapolated.
key_citations: Spasovski G, et al. Clinical practice guideline on diagnosis and treatment of hyponatraemia. Nephrol Dial Transplant 2014;29(Suppl 2):i1-i39. PMID 24569496 (DOI 10.1093/ndt/gfu040). Verbalis JG, et al. Diagnosis, evaluation, and treatment of hyponatremia: expert panel recommendations. Am J Med 2013;126(10 Suppl 1):S1-S42. PMID 24074529 (DOI 10.1016/j.amjmed.2013.07.006). Baek SH, et al. Risk of overcorrection in rapid intermittent bolus vs slow continuous infusion of hypertonic saline (SALSA RCT). JAMA Intern Med 2021;181(1):81-92. PMID 33104189 (DOI 10.1001/jamainternmed.2020.5519). Semler MW, et al. Balanced crystalloids versus saline in critically ill adults (SMART). N Engl J Med 2018;378:829-839. PMID 29485925 (DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1711584). MacMillan/Sterns desmopressin-to-prevent-overcorrection evidence. Citations via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Moderate overall. Hyponatremia management rests on society consensus guidelines (European 2014 largely graded 1C/2C/expert-opinion; US 2013 expert consensus) reinforced by a few moderate-quality RCTs (SALSA for bolus vs infusion; SMART for balanced crystalloids). Correction-rate ceilings and ODS-prevention/desmopressin strategies are chiefly observational and consensus (low-to-moderate). Hypokalemia and dehydration thresholds are expert-consensus/extrapolated with no high-grade RCT defining exact cutoffs. Net grade: Moderate — society consensus guidelines plus selected RCTs; several operational thresholds are consensus/expert opinion.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed group splits into DRG 640 (with MCC) vs 641 (without MCC). MCC drivers here: profound hyponatremia with encephalopathy/seizure/coma, osmotic demyelination, acute respiratory failure, severe protein-calorie malnutrition, sepsis, AKI, or arrhythmia. MCC tier shifts the pathway to ICU/step-down for hypertonic saline with q1-2h Na, longer LOS (~3.8-4.9 vs ~2.8-3.4 days), more consults, and intensified overcorrection surveillance. Non-MCC (641): mild-to-moderate disturbance, oral repletion, floor-level care, short 2-3 day stay. Accurate documentation of malnutrition, metabolic encephalopathy, and comorbid AKI is pivotal to correct DRG assignment; acute-vs-chronic acuity and symptomatic-vs-asymptomatic status modulate monitoring intensity independent of the DRG split.
pathway_pearls: (1) Never correct Na faster than 10 mmol/L/24h (<=8 if high-ODS-risk: Na <=105, low K, alcohol, malnutrition, liver disease); check Na q2-4h and keep D5W + desmopressin ready to RE-LOWER — ODS is a preventable never-event. (2) For severe symptomatic hyponatremia (seizure/coma): 150 mL 3% saline over 20 min, repeat to +5 mmol/L in the first hour, then slow down — treat the brain first; rapid intermittent bolus >= continuous infusion (SALSA). (3) The paired URINE osmolality + URINE sodium (with volume exam, TSH, cortisol) is the single highest-yield step — it separates SIADH (restrict water) from hypovolemia (give isotonic fluid, then watch for auto-overcorrection). (4) Always give magnesium WITH potassium and get an ECG — hypokalemia is refractory until Mg is repleted. (5) Deprescribe the culprit (thiazide/SSRI/desmopressin) and book a 48-72h outpatient BMP — where readmissions are won or lost.

admission_criteria: Inpatient (ASAM Level 3.7 medically-monitored or 4 medically-managed) vs ED discharge/ambulatory withdrawal (ASAM Level 1-WM/2-WM). Admit if: PAWSS >=4; prior delirium tremens or withdrawal seizure; CIWA-Ar >=15-19 or persistently rising despite treatment; unstable vitals (HR>120, SBP>160 or DBP>100, temp>=38.3C); comorbid acute illness (CAD/arrhythmia, GI bleed, pancreatitis, sepsis, decompensated cirrhosis, DKA), significant electrolyte derangement, Wernicke signs, pregnancy, inability to tolerate PO, unsafe home/no support, active suicidality or psychosis, or polysubstance/opioid co-withdrawal needing MOUD. ED discharge/outpatient WM if mild (CIWA-Ar<10), PAWSS<4, no complicated-withdrawal history, reliable support and follow-up.
risk_stratification: PAWSS (10 items) score >=4 = high risk for complicated/severe withdrawal -> prophylaxis + closer monitoring (Maldonado 2014, 100% sens/spec at threshold 4). CIWA-Ar (10 items, max 67): <8-10 minimal (usually no meds), 8-15 mild-moderate, >=15 (esp >18-20) severe -> drives symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing (medicate when CIWA-Ar >=8-10). COWS (11 items) for opioid withdrawal: 5-12 mild, 13-24 moderate, 25-36 moderately severe, >36 severe -> start buprenorphine when COWS >=8-12 to avoid precipitated withdrawal. Screening: AUDIT-C (>=4 men/>=3 women) or CAGE (>=2); Wernicke Caine criteria (>=2 of 4 present).
initial_workup_labs: CBC (macrocytosis, thrombocytopenia), CMP with LFTs/glucose/renal, magnesium, phosphate, potassium (repletion targets Mg>2, K>4, normal PO4), serum ethanol level, urine drug screen, lipase, INR/coags & ammonia if cirrhosis/encephalopathy, lactate, CK + urinalysis (rhabdomyolysis), beta-hCG (all women of childbearing age), TSH; consider osmolar gap/methanol-ethylene glycol if altered mental status; hepatitis B/C and HIV screening; blood cultures/procalcitonin if febrile. Abnormalities trigger: aggressive electrolyte repletion, refeeding precautions, sepsis workup, hepatology involvement.
initial_imaging_dx: ECG on arrival (QTc before/with QT-prolonging agents, arrhythmia, alcoholic cardiomyopathy); non-contrast CT head for first seizure, focal deficit, head trauma, or persistent/atypical altered mental status; CXR if aspiration/infection suspected; EEG if atypical or recurrent seizures or nonconvulsive status epilepticus; LP if meningoencephalitis suspected; RUQ ultrasound if hepatic decompensation. No routine imaging for uncomplicated withdrawal.
time_critical_interventions: Give parenteral THIAMINE BEFORE any dextrose/glucose (prevent Wernicke); check and treat hypoglycemia. Front-load/rapidly escalate benzodiazepine (diazepam 10-20 mg or lorazepam 2-4 mg IV q1-2h) or single-dose phenobarbital 10 mg/kg IV for severe/high-risk withdrawal to abort progression to seizures/DTs; abort a withdrawal seizure with IV benzodiazepine and treat status epilepticus. Opioid overdose -> immediate naloxone. Airway protection in DTs. No formal door-to target, but treat CIWA-Ar >=8-10 without delay and reassess q1-2h.
initial_management: First-line = BENZODIAZEPINES via symptom-triggered (CIWA-Ar-guided) dosing, preferred over fixed-schedule (Saitz 1994): long-acting self-tapering chlordiazepoxide 50-100 mg or diazepam 5-20 mg; use lorazepam 2-4 mg (no active metabolites, glucuronidated) in cirrhosis/elderly/delirium. BARBITURATE phenobarbital 10 mg/kg load as first-line or benzodiazepine-resistant adjunct (reduces ICU admission; Rosenson 2013). Adjuncts: anticonvulsants gabapentin/carbamazepine/valproate for mild-moderate; alpha-2 agonist dexmedetomidine or antipsychotic haloperidol ONLY as adjunct to a benzodiazepine for autonomic hyperactivity/agitation (do NOT prevent seizures, never monotherapy). Repletion: thiamine 500 mg IV TID x3-5d if Wernicke suspected (else 100 mg IV daily prophylaxis), folate, multivitamin, IV magnesium/phosphate/potassium; isotonic fluids for volume/electrolyte losses. OPIOID: start MOUD - buprenorphine/naloxone induction at COWS>=8-12 or methadone; naltrexone only after full detox.
day1_milestones: Establish diagnosis and severity (CIWA-Ar q1-4h, PAWSS, COWS if opioids); start symptom-triggered benzodiazepine or phenobarbital; give thiamine/folate/multivitamin and correct Mg/K/PO4; IV fluids PRN; treat comorbid acute illness; initiate MOUD if OUD (buprenorphine when COWS>=8); telemetry if cardiac risk/QTc concern; VTE prophylaxis; screen for polysubstance and tobacco use; suicide/psychiatric risk assessment; consult addiction medicine/psychiatry and social work; deliver motivational interviewing/brief intervention (SUB-2).
day2_milestones: Reassess trajectory; taper benzodiazepine as CIWA-Ar falls (<8-10); continue thiamine and electrolyte repletion, monitor for refeeding syndrome; de-escalate from ICU/telemetry to floor if stable; continue/optimize MOUD; begin AUD relapse-prevention pharmacotherapy discussion (naltrexone/acamprosate); addiction medicine follow-up; engage peer recovery coach; mobilize; treat comorbidities; begin disposition/level-of-care planning along the ASAM continuum.
day3plus_milestones: Withdrawal resolved (CIWA-Ar consistently <8-10 off benzodiazepine >=24h, no DTs/seizures); initiate/stabilize maintenance MOUD (buprenorphine/methadone) or AUD meds (naltrexone PO 50 mg or XR 380 mg IM, acamprosate 666 mg TID); finalize ASAM level-of-care (residential 3.5/3.7, PHP, IOP, or outpatient MAT); provide naloxone kit + overdose education; relapse-prevention plan; warm handoff/bridge appointment; medication reconciliation; schedule follow-up <=7 days (ideally within 72h for buprenorphine bridge); treat/stabilize co-occurring psychiatric illness.
consults_multidisciplinary: Addiction medicine/psychiatry Day 1 (withdrawal management, MOUD, level-of-care); social work/case management Day 1 (placement, insurance, SDOH, transport); psychiatry for co-occurring disorder/suicidality; peer recovery coach/certified recovery specialist; pharmacy (MOUD dosing, drug interactions, QTc); nutrition (malnutrition, refeeding risk); PT/OT if deconditioned/neuropathy/falls; hepatology if decompensated cirrhosis/alcoholic hepatitis; neurology if seizures/Wernicke-Korsakoff; OB if pregnant; chaplaincy.
monitoring_level: ICU for severe/complicated DTs needing continuous or very-high-dose benzodiazepine/phenobarbital infusion, dexmedetomidine/propofol, airway compromise, refractory withdrawal (CIWA-Ar persistently >20-25 despite escalation = benzodiazepine resistance), status epilepticus, hyperthermia, or hemodynamic instability. Step-down/telemetry for moderate-severe withdrawal, high benzodiazepine requirement, cardiac comorbidity, or QTc/electrolyte concern. Floor for mild-moderate withdrawal with stable vitals. Escalate for seizures, CIWA-Ar >20-25 despite escalating doses, hypoxia, or need for continuous infusion.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Thiamine before carbohydrates; screen and monitor for refeeding syndrome (replete phosphate/Mg/K); diet as tolerated with aspiration precautions in delirium; nutrition consult for malnutrition; early mobilization with fall precautions (delirium, ataxia, peripheral neuropathy); pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin/heparin) unless coagulopathy/thrombocytopenia (reassess in cirrhosis/GI bleed); glucose monitoring; remove peripheral/central lines and urinary catheters as soon as no longer needed.
discharge_criteria: Withdrawal controlled/resolved - CIWA-Ar consistently <8-10 for >=24h without benzodiazepine (or off escalating doses), no active seizures/DTs; hemodynamically stable, afebrile, tolerating PO; electrolytes corrected; comorbid medical/psychiatric conditions stabilized and patient not actively suicidal; MOUD initiated/stable and naloxone dispensed if OUD; aftercare level-of-care arranged with a confirmed follow-up appointment.
discharge_planning: Disposition along ASAM continuum (residential 3.5/3.7, PHP, IOP, or outpatient with MAT) matched to severity/support; medication reconciliation including MOUD and AUD pharmacotherapy; dispense naloxone kit with overdose-prevention education (patient + family); buprenorphine bridge prescription/warm handoff to addiction clinic; follow-up appointment <=7 days (ideally within 72h for MOUD); mutual-help/peer recovery referral (AA/NA/SMART), harm-reduction and syringe services, hepatitis/HIV linkage; address SDOH (housing, transport, insurance); relapse-prevention and trigger plan; note X-waiver no longer required (2023 MAT Act) so any DEA-registered clinician can continue buprenorphine.
expected_los: DRG 897 (no rehab, no CC/MCC) GMLOS ~3.3 days (ALOS ~4.3, relative weight ~0.88); DRG 896 (with MCC, e.g., DTs/seizure/aspiration/sepsis) longer ~5-6 days; DRG 895 (with rehabilitation therapy) longer ~7+ days; DRG 894 (left AMA) short ~1-2 days. Uncomplicated alcohol withdrawal typically 3-5 days.
target_los: ~3 days for uncomplicated withdrawal under a symptom-triggered/phenobarbital front-loading pathway (can be 2-3 days); optimization targets compressing ICU time in DTs and preventing premature AMA departures, NOT rushing detox at the expense of MOUD initiation and safe linkage.
quality_metrics: Joint Commission/CMS behavioral-health SUB measures - SUB-1 (alcohol/drug use screening), SUB-2 (brief intervention provided/offered), SUB-3 (SUD treatment provided/offered at discharge), SUB-4 (status after discharge); HEDIS IET (Initiation within 14d & Engagement within 34d of AOD treatment); HEDIS/CMS FUA (follow-up after ED visit for AOD at 7 & 30 days); MOUD initiation rate and naloxone-at-discharge rate; 30-day all-cause readmission; leaving-AMA rate; IPFQR measures for psychiatric units; tobacco (TOB) measures.
common_complications: Withdrawal seizures and delirium tremens (mortality ~1-5% treated, up to ~15% untreated) - mitigated by PAWSS-based prophylaxis and prompt symptom-triggered/phenobarbital dosing; aspiration pneumonia; over-sedation/respiratory depression from benzodiazepines; Wernicke-Korsakoff (prevented by high-dose IV thiamine before glucose); hypoglycemia; hypomagnesemia/hypophosphatemia/hypokalemia and refeeding syndrome; arrhythmia; rhabdomyolysis; falls/trauma; precipitated opioid withdrawal from early buprenorphine (avoid via COWS>=8-12 induction); underlying alcoholic hepatitis/pancreatitis/infection.
readmission_drivers: Failure to initiate/continue MOUD or AUD pharmacotherapy; no warm handoff/linkage to outpatient treatment; leaving AMA (DRG 894); untreated co-occurring psychiatric illness; absent naloxone/overdose education; social determinants (homelessness, no insurance/transport); relapse; inadequate or late follow-up; undertreated withdrawal. Prevention levers: start MOUD in-hospital, bridge clinics with <72h follow-up, peer recovery coaches, universal naloxone distribution, contingency management, integrated behavioral health, and SDOH navigation.
guideline_source: ASAM Clinical Practice Guideline on Alcohol Withdrawal Management (2020) - primary for alcohol withdrawal; ASAM National Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Opioid Use Disorder, 2020 Focused Update - for OUD/MOUD; supported by APA Practice Guideline for the Pharmacological Treatment of Patients With AUD (2018), VA/DoD Clinical Practice Guideline for SUD (2021), and SAMHSA TIP 63 (Medications for OUD).
key_citations: ASAM Alcohol Withdrawal Management CPG, J Addict Med 2020;14(3S Suppl 1):1-72 (PMID 32511109, doi:10.1097/ADM.0000000000000668); ASAM National Practice Guideline for OUD 2020 Focused Update, J Addict Med 2020;14(2S Suppl 1):1-91 (PMID 32511106, doi:10.1097/ADM.0000000000000633); Maldonado JR et al, PAWSS, Alcohol 2014;48:375-90 (PMID 24657098, doi:10.1016/j.alcohol.2014.01.004); Saitz R et al, symptom-triggered vs fixed chlordiazepoxide, JAMA 1994;272:519-23 (PMID 8046805); Rosenson J et al, single-dose IV phenobarbital RCT, J Emerg Med 2013;44:592-8 (PMID 22999778, doi:10.1016/j.jemermed.2012.07.056); Wesson & Ling, COWS, J Psychoactive Drugs 2003;35:253-9 (PMID 12924748, doi:10.1080/02791072.2003.10400007). Source: PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong for core elements - symptom-triggered benzodiazepines (multiple RCTs; ASAM Class I recommendation) and MOUD (buprenorphine/methadone mortality benefit, strong RCT evidence); Moderate for phenobarbital and anticonvulsant/alpha-2 adjuncts (RCT + guideline-supported); Consensus/expert opinion for the high-dose thiamine regimen (observational, extrapolated). Overall: Strong - current ASAM 2020 society guidelines plus landmark RCTs.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 897 (no CC/MCC) = uncomplicated withdrawal, floor-level, LOS ~3 days. DRG 896 (MCC: DTs, withdrawal seizure/status, aspiration pneumonia, sepsis, GI bleed, decompensated cirrhosis, DKA) drives ICU/step-down need, continuous benzodiazepine/phenobarbital or dexmedetomidine, longer LOS (~5+ days) and higher mortality. DRG 895 (with rehabilitation therapy) carries longer stays for inpatient rehab services. DRG 894 (left AMA) = short, incomplete-treatment stays with high readmission - a key prevention target for warm handoff and MOUD before departure.
pathway_pearls: 1) Use CIWA-Ar-guided SYMPTOM-TRIGGERED benzodiazepine dosing over fixed-schedule to cut benzodiazepine exposure and LOS (Saitz 1994) - but front-load/fixed-dose or use phenobarbital for PAWSS>=4 or prior DTs/seizures. 2) Give parenteral THIAMINE BEFORE glucose, high-dose IV (500 mg TID) at any Wernicke suspicion - cheap, prevents irreversible Korsakoff. 3) The admission IS the intervention: START MOUD (buprenorphine/methadone) in-hospital, dispense NALOXONE, and arrange a warm handoff/<72h bridge - do not just detox-and-discharge. 4) Risk-stratify with PAWSS early to set monitoring level; single-dose phenobarbital front-loading reduces ICU admissions (Rosenson 2013).
