admission_criteria: Objective inpatient indications (vs ED-discharge/observation) for non-delivery antepartum admission: preeclampsia WITH severe features (sustained BP >=160 systolic or >=110 diastolic, or severe-feature labs/symptoms); new preeclampsia at/near term pending delivery; gestational HTN/preeclampsia without severe features needing serial surveillance not feasible outpatient; preterm labor 24-34 wk with regular contractions AND documented cervical change or advanced dilation (>=3 cm) for steroids/magnesium/tocolysis; PPROM <37 wk for expectant latency management; hyperemesis with dehydration + ketonuria >=1+, >=5% pre-pregnancy weight loss, electrolyte disturbance (hypokalemia/hyponatremia), or failed ED/outpatient antiemetics; eclampsia, HELLP, suspected abruption, significant bleeding, or non-reassuring fetal status. ED-discharge/observation appropriate for: false/prodromal labor with NO cervical change + negative fetal fibronectin + cervical length >=30 mm; mild NVP tolerating oral fluids on oral antiemetics; isolated gestational HTN <160/110 with reassuring labs and reliable follow-up.
risk_stratification: Preterm labor: transvaginal cervical length <25 mm (before 24 wk) and/or positive fetal fibronectin raise 7-14 day delivery risk; negative fFN + cervical length >=30 mm has high negative predictive value (delivery within 7-14 days <1-2%) -> supports discharge and avoiding tocolysis/steroids. Preeclampsia: severe-feature criteria (BP >=160/110, platelets <100,000, creatinine >1.1 mg/dL or doubling, AST/ALT >2x ULN, pulmonary edema, refractory headache/visual symptoms) drive magnesium + delivery timing; fullPIERS model predicts adverse maternal outcome within 48 h; sFlt-1/PlGF ratio <=38 has high NPV to rule out preeclampsia within 1 week. Hyperemesis: PUQE score grades severity (mild <=6, moderate 7-12, severe >=13) and drives escalation; ketonuria + >=5% weight loss define hyperemesis gravidarum. Maternal Early Warning Criteria (MEWC/MEOWS): SBP <90/>160, DBP >100, HR <50/>120, RR <10/>30, SpO2 <95%, oliguria, altered mentation -> urgent bedside evaluation/escalation.
initial_workup_labs: Preeclampsia panel: CBC with platelet count, serum creatinine, AST/ALT, LDH, uric acid, +/- haptoglobin, bilirubin and peripheral smear if HELLP suspected; urine protein:creatinine ratio (>=0.3) or 24-h urine protein (>=300 mg) or dipstick >=2+; type and screen. Hyperemesis: BMP (K+, Na+, bicarbonate, BUN/creatinine), urinalysis for ketones/specific gravity, TSH/free T4 (transient gestational hyperthyroidism), LFTs and lipase if severe, magnesium/phosphate, +/- thiamine level; serial weights. Preterm labor/PPROM: CBC, GBS culture, urinalysis and culture, sterile speculum with nitrazine/ferning or PAMG-1 (AmniSure)/IGFBP-1 for rupture, wet mount and GC/chlamydia NAAT; fetal fibronectin only if membranes intact and no recent exam/intercourse. Universal: confirm gestational age, ABO/Rh type (Rho[D] immune globulin if Rh-negative with bleeding); glucose monitoring baseline before steroids.
initial_imaging_dx: Obstetric ultrasound on admission: fetal biometry/estimated fetal weight, presentation, amniotic fluid index or single deepest pocket, placental location (exclude previa/abruption). Transvaginal cervical length for suspected preterm labor. Antenatal fetal surveillance: NST or continuous electronic fetal monitoring on admission; biophysical profile and umbilical artery Doppler for FGR/preeclampsia/PPROM. Chest imaging/echocardiography if pulmonary edema suspected; RUQ ultrasound if HELLP/hepatic subcapsular concern. Serial growth ultrasound every 3-4 weeks during expectant management; amniotic fluid and BPP 1-2x/week for PPROM/preeclampsia.
time_critical_interventions: Acute sustained severe-range BP (>=160/110 confirmed persistent >=15 min): administer first-line IV/oral antihypertensive within 30-60 minutes (AIM/ACOG target; do not delay) -> this is the marquee time-critical maternal-safety target. Magnesium sulfate seizure prophylaxis started promptly for preeclampsia with severe features/eclampsia (4-6 g IV load over 20-30 min, then 1-2 g/h). Antenatal corticosteroids: give FIRST dose ASAP when preterm birth anticipated within 7 days (benefit onset ~24 h, maximal 48 h-7 days). Magnesium for fetal neuroprotection when delivery anticipated <32 wk, ideally started >=4 h before birth. Eclamptic seizure: magnesium 4-6 g bolus + airway protection. PPROM/preterm labor: latency antibiotics and intrapartum GBS prophylaxis initiated on admission.
initial_management: Acute severe HTN antihypertensives: IV labetalol 10-20 mg then 20-80 mg q10-30 min (max 300 mg cumulative), IV hydralazine 5 mg then 5-10 mg q20-40 min (max 20 mg), or oral immediate-release nifedipine 10-20 mg q20-30 min; maintenance oral labetalol or extended-release nifedipine; methyldopa second-line; AVOID ACE inhibitors/ARBs (teratogenic). Chronic HTN: treat to <140/90 per CHAP trial. Magnesium sulfate for seizure prophylaxis (severe features/eclampsia) and separately for neuroprotection <32 wk; calcium gluconate 1 g IV is toxicity antidote. Antenatal corticosteroids: betamethasone 12 mg IM q24h x2 OR dexamethasone 6 mg IM q12h x4 (24 0/7-33 6/7 wk; single late-preterm course 34 0/7-36 6/7 wk per ALPS; single rescue course if prior >7-14 days and birth anticipated <=7 days). Tocolysis ONLY to gain 48 h for steroids/transfer (24-34 wk): first-line nifedipine (CCB) or indomethacin (COX inhibitor, preferred <32 wk); terbutaline (beta-agonist) short-term only (FDA black-box for prolonged use); NO maintenance tocolysis. PPROM latency antibiotics: IV ampicillin 2 g q6h + erythromycin 250 mg q6h x48 h, then oral amoxicillin 250 mg q8h + erythromycin base 333 mg q8h to complete 7 days (azithromycin 1 g PO once substitutes for erythromycin). Hyperemesis: IV crystalloid (NS/LR) with potassium repletion; thiamine 100 mg IV BEFORE any dextrose (prevent Wernicke); stepwise antiemetics - pyridoxine (B6) + doxylamine first-line, then antihistamine (dimenhydrinate)/dopamine antagonists (promethazine, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine), ondansetron (5-HT3), methylprednisolone for refractory (avoid <10 wk); enteral then parenteral nutrition if persistent. Rho(D) immune globulin if Rh-negative with bleeding.
day1_milestones: Admission Day 1: confirm diagnosis and gestational age; establish maternal-fetal stability; initiate condition-specific therapy (first antenatal steroid dose, magnesium, antihypertensives, antiemetics + thiamine/fluids, latency antibiotics). Draw baseline preeclampsia severity labs; continuous or scheduled fetal monitoring; strict intake/output. Treat any sustained severe-range BP within 60 minutes and start magnesium if severe features/eclampsia. MFM consult; determine expectant-management-vs-delivery plan and document a gestational-age-based delivery-timing plan. Start VTE prophylaxis; resuscitate hyperemesis with electrolyte correction.
day2_milestones: Day 2: complete/continue antenatal steroid course (second betamethasone dose at 24 h or ongoing dexamethasone); reassess and DISCONTINUE tocolysis at 48 h (no maintenance). Repeat preeclampsia labs (CBC, LFTs, creatinine) and symptom review; titrate oral antihypertensives to goal and trend BP. Hyperemesis: transition IV to oral antiemetics/fluids once tolerating, verify electrolyte correction, document weight trend and ketone clearance. Continue fetal surveillance (NST/BPP); reassess membranes/labor progression and infection markers (PPROM). Reaffirm delivery-timing decision; begin discharge planning if stabilized.
day3plus_milestones: Day 3+ to discharge readiness: stabilized preterm labor - contractions quiescent, no further cervical change, steroid course complete, tocolysis stopped, reassuring fetal status -> discharge candidate. Preeclampsia without severe features - BP controlled on oral agents, stable/improving labs, no severe-feature symptoms, reassuring fetal testing -> discharge with intensified surveillance vs planned delivery at 37 0/7 wk (or 34 0/7 wk if severe features). Hyperemesis - tolerating oral intake/antiemetics, stable electrolytes, weight stabilizing -> discharge. PPROM and severe preeclampsia <34 wk on expectant management - transition to prolonged inpatient antenatal monitoring (serial labs, fetal testing, infection surveillance) until a delivery indication or gestational-age threshold (PPROM/severe features 34 0/7 wk).
consults_multidisciplinary: Maternal-Fetal Medicine early for all high-acuity antepartum admissions; Neonatology/NICU antenatal counseling for anticipated preterm birth; Obstetric Anesthesia for severe preeclampsia/possible delivery. Dietitian/Nutrition for hyperemesis (enteral/parenteral planning and refeeding monitoring); Pharmacy for magnesium/antihypertensive/antiemetic dosing and medication reconciliation. High-acuity/1:1 nursing during magnesium infusions; Social work/Case management for prolonged antepartum stay, transportation, and psychosocial support. Subspecialty as indicated: Nephrology (renal dysfunction), Hematology (HELLP/thrombocytopenia), GI and Psychiatry (refractory hyperemesis), Critical care/ICU for eclampsia or pulmonary edema; Lactation consult as delivery approaches.
monitoring_level: Most patients managed on labor-and-delivery or antepartum unit with intermittent or continuous fetal monitoring. Magnesium infusions require high-acuity nursing: hourly deep-tendon reflexes, respiratory rate >=12, urine output >=30 mL/h (>=100 mL/4h), continuous pulse oximetry, and readily available calcium gluconate. Step-down/ICU for: eclampsia, HELLP with organ dysfunction, pulmonary edema, refractory severe hypertension, magnesium toxicity requiring ventilatory support, hemodynamic instability or hemorrhage. Escalation triggers per Maternal Early Warning Criteria (persistent severe HTN, SpO2 <95%, oliguria, altered mentation, non-reassuring fetal status).
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: advance as tolerated; NPO or clears if delivery imminent or during acute hyperemesis; thiamine before any dextrose; enteral tube feeding, then parenteral nutrition, for refractory hyperemesis with ongoing weight loss (monitor for refeeding syndrome). Mobility: encourage ambulation; avoid routine strict bed rest (VTE and deconditioning risk) except where obstetrically indicated. VTE: pregnancy is hypercoagulable - mechanical prophylaxis (intermittent pneumatic compression) for antepartum inpatients, add pharmacologic LMWH/UFH prophylaxis for prolonged immobility or additional risk factors (per ACOG PB 196). Glucose: monitor and treat steroid-induced hyperglycemia with fingersticks after betamethasone, especially in GDM/pregestational diabetes. Lines/catheters: remove Foley and peripheral IVs promptly once magnesium stopped and patient mobilizing.
discharge_criteria: Stabilized preterm labor: contractions resolved, no ongoing cervical change, antenatal steroid course complete, tocolysis stopped, reassuring fetal status. Preeclampsia without severe features: BP consistently <160/110 (controlled toward <140/90 on oral agents), stable or improving labs, no severe-feature symptoms, reassuring fetal surveillance, and reliable home BP monitoring/follow-up in place. Hyperemesis: tolerating oral intake and oral antiemetics for >=24 h, corrected electrolytes, resolved ketosis, stable or rising weight. General: maternal-fetal stability achieved, patient educated on warning signs with teach-back, and follow-up arranged.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home with intensified outpatient surveillance; expectant-management subgroups (severe preeclampsia <34 wk, PPROM) remain inpatient until a delivery indication. Medication reconciliation: oral antihypertensives, STANDING (not PRN) multi-agent antiemetics, pyridoxine, thiamine/prenatal vitamins, low-dose aspirin 81 mg if indicated for preeclampsia prophylaxis, and 17-OHP/vaginal progesterone counseling if prior spontaneous preterm birth; provide home BP cuff with thresholds. Follow-up: MFM/OB within 3-7 days (48-72 h for hypertensive disorders); antenatal testing schedule (NST/BPP 1-2x/week) and serial growth ultrasound. Education: strict return precautions (severe/persistent headache, visual changes, RUQ/epigastric pain, decreased fetal movement, regular contractions, leaking fluid, vaginal bleeding, intractable vomiting); postpartum preeclampsia counseling; warm handoff/transitions-of-care to outpatient team; enroll in telehealth BP monitoring program where available.
expected_los: Heterogeneous group. GMLOS roughly 2-3 days for the medical antepartum DRGs (781/782/831-833), approximately 1-1.5 days for false labor (780), and 3-5 days when an O.R. procedure is involved (817-819, e.g., cerclage). Expectant management of severe preeclampsia <34 wk or PPROM can extend antenatal inpatient stay to days-to-weeks until a delivery indication or gestational-age threshold is reached.
target_los: Approximately 1.5-2 days for stabilize-and-discharge presentations (hyperemesis rehydration, threatened/false preterm labor discharged after the 48-h steroid window, mild preeclampsia stabilization). Expectant-management subgroups (PPROM, severe preeclampsia <34 wk) are intentionally longer and are delivery-indication/gestational-age driven, not LOS-reduction targets.
quality_metrics: Timely treatment of acute severe-range hypertension within 60 minutes (AIM Severe Hypertension in Pregnancy safety bundle / SMFM). Antenatal corticosteroid administration for anticipated preterm birth 24-34 wk (perinatal quality collaborative metric; formerly Joint Commission PC-03). Magnesium sulfate for neuroprotection <32 wk when indicated. AIM Maternal VTE Prevention bundle compliance. Appropriate intrapartum GBS prophylaxis; avoidance of non-indicated or maintenance tocolysis. Severe maternal morbidity (SMM) rate, ICU transfer rate, eclampsia rate; 7- and 30-day readmission (especially hypertensive). Appropriate risk-based triage/transfer per ACOG-SMFM Levels of Maternal Care.
common_complications: Progression to eclampsia, HELLP, or pulmonary edema - iatrogenic fluid overload with magnesium is a key driver, so fluid-restrict (~80 mL/h total) with strict I/O; magnesium toxicity (monitor reflexes/respiratory rate/urine output; antidote calcium gluconate 1 g IV). Preterm labor progressing to preterm birth; PPROM complicated by intra-amniotic infection/chorioamnionitis, cord prolapse, or abruption (latency antibiotics, infection surveillance, avoid digital exams). Hyperemesis: Wernicke encephalopathy (prevent with thiamine before dextrose), hypokalemia/electrolyte derangement, Mallory-Weiss tear, refeeding syndrome, VTE. Steroid-induced hyperglycemia; medication effects (labetalol bradycardia, nifedipine/terbutaline tachycardia, indomethacin ductal constriction/oligohydramnios especially >32 wk).
readmission_drivers: Uncontrolled or new postpartum-onset hypertension/preeclampsia (a leading cause of postpartum readmission) - mitigate with home BP monitoring, 48-72 h follow-up, patient education, and timely antihypertensive titration. Recurrent hyperemesis from inadequate or PRN-only outpatient antiemetics - mitigate with a standing multi-agent regimen and early follow-up. Recurrent preterm contractions/threatened labor; missed appointments; medication nonadherence; poor understanding of return precautions. Prevention levers: standardized discharge bundles, telehealth monitoring programs, progesterone for prior spontaneous preterm birth, guaranteed MFM follow-up, and transportation/social support.
guideline_source: ACOG (American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists), primary - condition-specific bulletins: Practice Bulletin 222 Gestational Hypertension and Preeclampsia (2020); Practice Bulletin 171 Management of Preterm Labor (2016, reaffirmed); Practice Bulletin 234 Prediction and Prevention of Spontaneous Preterm Birth (2021); Practice Bulletin 217 Prelabor Rupture of Membranes (2020); Practice Bulletin 189 Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy (2018); Committee Opinion 713 Antenatal Corticosteroid Therapy for Fetal Maturation (2017); Committee Opinion 652 Magnesium Sulfate Use in Obstetrics. Supplemented by SMFM Consult Series and the Alliance for Innovation on Maternal Health (AIM) patient-safety bundles.
key_citations: ACOG Practice Bulletin 222, Gestational Hypertension and Preeclampsia. Obstet Gynecol 2020;135:e237-e260 (PMID 32443079). ACOG PB 217, Prelabor Rupture of Membranes, 2020 (PMID 32080050). ACOG PB 189, Nausea and Vomiting of Pregnancy, 2018 (PMID 29266076). ACOG PB 171, Management of Preterm Labor, 2016. Gyamfi-Bannerman C, et al. Antenatal Betamethasone for Women at Risk for Late Preterm Delivery (ALPS). N Engl J Med 2016;374:1311-20 (PMID 26842679; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1516783). Tita AT, et al. Treatment for Mild Chronic Hypertension during Pregnancy (CHAP). N Engl J Med 2022;386:1781-92 (PMID 35363951; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2201295). Rouse DJ, et al. Magnesium sulfate for prevention of cerebral palsy (BEAM). N Engl J Med 2008;359:895-905 (PMID 18753646). Magpie Trial Collaborative Group. Magnesium sulphate for pre-eclampsia (Magpie). Lancet 2002;359:1877-90 (PMID 12057549). Citations verified via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong overall for the highest-stakes interventions - anchored to current ACOG Level A-B recommendations and multiple landmark RCTs: ALPS (late-preterm steroids), CHAP (BP treatment to <140/90), BEAM (magnesium neuroprotection), and Magpie (magnesium for preeclampsia/eclampsia). Moderate-to-consensus for tocolytic agent selection, expectant-management specifics, and hyperemesis pharmacology sequencing (largely Level B-C with limited RCT data). LOS figures are operational extrapolations given marked DRG heterogeneity, not trial-derived.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Without CC/MCC (782, 833, 819): typically brief stabilize-and-discharge (false labor, mild NVP, gestational HTN without severe features); floor-level care, LOS ~1-2 d. With CC (781, 831-CC, 818): moderate acuity - preeclampsia without severe features requiring surveillance, hyperemesis with electrolyte derangement, PPROM latency; LOS ~2-4 d with higher monitoring intensity. With MCC (831-MCC, 817): severe features/eclampsia/HELLP, pulmonary edema, AKI, sepsis/chorioamnionitis, or magnesium toxicity -> step-down/ICU, longer LOS, higher severe maternal morbidity and delivery likelihood. The O.R.-procedure DRGs (817-819) add operative/anesthesia risk (e.g., cerclage). Rising severity shifts the group from home-discharge antepartum toward delivery or prolonged high-acuity expectant management.
pathway_pearls: 1) Treat sustained severe-range BP (>=160/110) within 60 minutes - every antepartum unit needs a stocked hypertension-emergency order set (IV labetalol/hydralazine, PO immediate-release nifedipine); this is the single highest-yield maternal-safety lever. 2) Use the 48-hour window well - give the FIRST antenatal steroid dose immediately and reserve tocolysis solely to buy 48 h (or enable transfer); no maintenance tocolysis; add magnesium neuroprotection if <32 wk. 3) In hyperemesis, give thiamine BEFORE any dextrose-containing fluids and discharge on STANDING (not PRN) multi-agent antiemetics to prevent Wernicke and bounce-back. 4) Right-size disposition by gestational-age delivery thresholds (37 wk for gestational HTN/preeclampsia without severe features; 34 wk for severe features and PPROM) and mind iatrogenic pulmonary edema (fluid-restrict on magnesium with strict I/O).
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Non-delivery obstetric admissions: preterm/false labor, hyperemesis, preeclampsia without delivery, threatened preterm labor, other antepartum diagnoses (with/without OR procedure). DRG numbering approximate post-FY2020.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 817 — Other Antepartum Diagnoses with O.R. Procedures with MCC; 818 — Other Antepartum Diagnoses with O.R. Procedures with CC; 819 — Other Antepartum Diagnoses with O.R. Procedures without CC/MCC; 831 — Other Antepartum Diagnoses without O.R. Procedures with MCC; 832 — Other Antepartum Diagnoses without O.R. Procedures with CC; 833 — Other Antepartum Diagnoses without O.R. Procedures without CC/MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 7/7 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Current pass completed citation-resolvability, coding, taxonomy, schema, and consistency checks. The source workflow contains no completed adversarial high-stakes clinical verdict for this row; clinician revalidation remains required before protocol or order-set use.
data_quality_notes: Removed retired antepartum DRGs 780-782; retained the current operative and non-operative antepartum families. Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk.

mdc: 21 — Injuries, Poisonings & Toxic Effects of Drugs
admission_criteria: Heterogeneous MDC-21 bucket (device/graft/implant complication, post-procedural condition, adverse drug effect/poisoning) — admit vs ED-discharge/observation by systemic burden and need for inpatient-only therapy. Inpatient indications: sepsis/septic shock or SIRS with suspected device/wound source (qSOFA >=2, NEWS2 >=5); deep or organ-space surgical-site infection or prosthetic/device/graft infection requiring IV antibiotics +/- source control (I&D, explant, drainage); post-procedural hemorrhage with Hgb drop >=2 g/dL, transfusion need, hemodynamic instability, or expanding/compressive hematoma; mechanical device failure needing intervention (dislocation, migration, obstruction, leak, malfunction of critical implant); anastomotic leak; wound dehiscence with fascial involvement/evisceration; symptomatic poisoning/toxic drug effect needing antidote or monitored support; failure of outpatient/OPAT therapy; inability to maintain hydration/oral intake or control pain. ED-discharge/observation: superficial SSI without systemic signs (oral antibiotics + outpatient wound care), stable seroma, minor hematoma without Hgb drop, asymptomatic device finding. Decision support: NEWS2 >=5 or qSOFA >=2 -> admit + rapid-response; observation for <24h single-intervention or monitoring needs.
risk_stratification: qSOFA >=2 (RR>=22, SBP<=100, GCS<15) or NEWS2 >=5 -> sepsis pathway, rapid-response, ICU eval; SOFA rise >=2 defines organ dysfunction/sepsis. 2018 ICM/MSIS periprosthetic-joint-infection score (Parvizi): serum CRP >1 mg/dL=2, D-dimer >860 ng/mL=2, ESR >30 mm/h=1; synovial WBC >3000/uL=3, alpha-defensin +=3, leukocyte-esterase ++=3, PMN% >80%=2, synovial CRP >6.9 mg/L=1 -> aggregate >=6 = infected (OR for source control), 2-5 inconclusive (use intraoperative frozen-section/purulence/culture). Vascular graft/endograft infection -> MAGIC criteria (clinical/radiologic/laboratory major+minor). Suspected CIED/valve infection with bacteremia -> modified Duke criteria + TEE. Lactate >2 mmol/L -> repeat/resuscitate, >4 -> shock protocol. Shock index >0.9 or ongoing transfusion -> escalate hemorrhage response. Comorbidity/frailty: Charlson index, 5-item modified Frailty Index (mFI-5), ACS-NSQIP risk calculator for reoperation/mortality; Caprini/Padua for VTE risk.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential, CMP, lactate, coagulation panel (PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen), CRP, ESR, procalcitonin; TWO sets blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics (mandatory for any device/prosthesis/graft or bacteremia concern); type-and-screen/crossmatch if bleeding. Source-directed micro: image-guided aspirate or operative tissue cultures + Gram stain (aspirate prosthetic joints, sample >=3-5 periprosthetic tissues, NEVER swab a sinus tract); synovial fluid cell count/PMN%/crystals/culture for PJI; urine culture if catheterized; drain/wound fluid culture. Triggers: lactate >=2 -> resuscitation bundle; falling Hgb -> transfuse + hunt source; rising CRP/ESR/procalcitonin -> cross-sectional imaging for occult collection; positive cultures -> narrow/tailor therapy; vancomycin/aminoglycoside levels once dosed; troponin/BNP if cardiac device. For poisoning/toxic-effect subset: acetaminophen and salicylate levels, drug-specific levels, osmolar/anion gap, ABG, ECG (QRS/QTc).
initial_imaging_dx: Site-directed and time-anchored. Plain radiographs of prosthesis/hardware first (loosening, lucency, periosteal reaction, fracture, device position). Contrast CT of surgical bed/abdomen-pelvis within 6-24h for deep/organ-space collection, abscess, anastomotic leak, or hematoma; CT angiography for graft infection, pseudoaneurysm, or active extravasation/bleeding. Ultrasound for superficial collection and duplex for DVT; IR image-guided aspiration/drainage same day when a drainable collection is found. Echocardiography for suspected CIED/valve infection or S. aureus bacteremia — TTE then TEE within 24-48h (higher sensitivity for lead/valve vegetations). Water-soluble contrast study or CT-with-enteric-contrast for suspected GI anastomotic leak. Nuclear WBC-tagged scan or FDG-PET-CT for occult prosthetic/graft infection when CT is equivocal. Goal: identify a controllable source within the first 24h.
time_critical_interventions: Septic shock -> Surviving Sepsis 2021 hour-1 bundle: measure lactate (repeat if >2), blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1h, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4, norepinephrine to MAP >=65 if fluid-refractory; re-measure lactate at 2-4h for clearance. Suspected sepsis without shock -> antibiotics within 3h after rapid assessment. SOURCE CONTROL is time-critical: drainage/debridement/device or graft explant/hematoma evacuation as soon as feasible, ideally <6-12h and no later than 24h (SSC/SIS-IDSA). Necrotizing soft-tissue infection -> emergent surgical debridement within hours. Major hemorrhage -> activate massive-transfusion protocol and reverse anticoagulation immediately. CIED pocket infection or device-related bacteremia -> complete system (generator + ALL leads) removal is Class I (AHA 2024), performed promptly once stabilized. Poisoning -> antidote within the agent-specific window (e.g., N-acetylcysteine for acetaminophen).
initial_management: Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics AFTER cultures: vancomycin (MRSA) + antipseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam or cefepime; carbapenem such as meropenem if ESBL risk) + metronidazole for intra-abdominal/anaerobic source; guided by local antibiogram and prior isolates, then de-escalate on culture data. Biofilm-active therapy for retained hardware: add rifampin for staphylococcal PJI/device infection managed with retention (always with a companion agent); fluoroquinolone for susceptible gram-negatives. Fluids: balanced crystalloid 30 mL/kg for hypoperfusion then dynamic reassessment (passive-leg-raise, stroke-volume variation); norepinephrine first-line vasopressor, MAP >=65. Transfusion: restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL (7-8 for cardiac disease); platelets/FFP/cryoprecipitate per active bleeding and coags. Reversal: warfarin -> 4-factor PCC + IV vitamin K; dabigatran -> idarucizumab; factor-Xa inhibitors -> andexanet alfa or 4F-PCC; heparin -> protamine; tranexamic acid for surgical bleeding. SOURCE CONTROL: I&D, percutaneous/operative drainage, debridement, device/graft explant or exchange, hematoma evacuation, revision; DAIR (debridement + implant retention) for ACUTE PJI (symptoms <3-4 wk, stable implant, healthy soft tissue, susceptible organism) vs 1- or 2-stage exchange for chronic/unstable. Glucose <180 mg/dL; multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia; hold anticoagulation/antiplatelet per bleeding. Poisoning subset: agent-specific antidotes (naloxone, N-acetylcysteine, digoxin-Fab, hydroxocobalamin) + supportive care.
day1_milestones: Confirm the source and lock a definitive source-control plan; book OR/IR if drainage/debridement/explant indicated. Empiric antibiotics started within target after cultures; complete sepsis bundle and document lactate clearance if septic. Achieve hemodynamic stabilization; meet transfusion goals and complete anticoagulant reversal if bleeding. Engage index proceduralist + Infectious Diseases + IR consults Day 0-1. Baseline functional/frailty assessment; make VTE-prophylaxis decision balancing bleeding risk (start mechanical prophylaxis now). Control pain on a multimodal regimen; begin mobilization if hemodynamically stable. Establish code status/goals of care in severe/high-mortality presentations. Review all indwelling lines/catheters for necessity.
day2_milestones: Culture-directed de-escalation of antibiotics at ~48h with ID input on regimen, route, and total duration; begin OPAT planning. Reassess response to source control: drain output, wound exam, repeat imaging if not improving. Expect downtrending CRP/procalcitonin, afebrile trend, normalized lactate, stable hemoglobin. Advance diet, escalate mobility with PT/OT, and remove unnecessary central lines/urinary catheters. Resume pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin or UFH) once hemostasis is secure. Transition IV-to-PO antimicrobials/analgesia where feasible; initiate discharge-planning and case management for disposition/OPAT.
day3plus_milestones: Demonstrate sustained improvement: afebrile >=24-48h, hemodynamically stable off vasopressors, inflammatory markers trending down, hemoglobin stable with no active bleeding. Confirm definitive source control achieved and manage/remove drains. Finalize antimicrobial plan and duration (e.g., pathogen-directed IV or high-bioavailability therapy ~4-6 wk after source control for organ-space/staged disease; rifampin-based combination for staphylococcal PJI treated by DAIR) and secure PICC/OPAT. Off supplemental O2, tolerating diet with adequate PO intake, pain controlled on oral meds, ambulating at safe baseline. Confirm disposition, DME, home-health/SNF, complete education, and schedule follow-up. Discharge once objective criteria met.
consults_multidisciplinary: Infectious Diseases — mandatory for device/prosthetic/graft infection, bacteremia (especially S. aureus), complex/prolonged antimicrobials and OPAT (Day 0-1). Index proceduralist/surgical service for source control: orthopedics (PJI), vascular surgery (graft), cardiology/electrophysiology (CIED extraction), general/colorectal (anastomotic leak) — Day 0-1. Interventional Radiology for image-guided drainage. Pharmacy/antimicrobial-stewardship + anticoagulation dosing (Day 0-1). PT/OT early for functional assessment and mobility. Care/case management + social work for OPAT, SNF/rehab, transitions (Day 1-2). Nutrition for malnutrition/wound healing. Medical Toxicology/Poison Control for the poisoning/toxic-effect subset. Palliative care for goals-of-care in high-mortality cases. Hospitalist is pathway owner (Nursing/Med-Surg medical service).
monitoring_level: ICU: septic shock on vasopressors, respiratory failure, active hemorrhage on massive-transfusion protocol, refractory hemodynamic instability, or lactate not clearing. Step-down/telemetry: sepsis without shock needing frequent monitoring, high-risk post-source-control patients, CIED infection with bacteremia/arrhythmia risk, or significant-but-stabilized bleeding. Floor/med-surg: hemodynamically stable, source controlled, on an appropriate antimicrobial regimen. Escalation triggers: NEWS2 >=7 (or any single parameter =3), new MAP <65, rising lactate, new/increasing oxygen requirement, brisk drain output or falling Hgb -> rapid-response/MET activation.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: advance as tolerated; NPO if awaiting OR or with active anastomotic leak; nutrition consult and protein optimization for malnutrition (low albumin/prealbumin) to support wound healing. Mobility: early mobilization from Day 1 unless contraindicated, with PT/OT. VTE prophylaxis: mechanical (sequential compression) universally; pharmacologic (enoxaparin or UFH) once bleeding risk acceptable — individualized in the hemorrhage subset, guided by Caprini/Padua. Glucose control target <180 mg/dL, avoid hypoglycemia. Lines/catheters: remove central venous catheters and Foley as early as possible with daily necessity review (CLABSI/CAUTI prevention). Pressure-injury prevention bundle.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable and afebrile >=24-48h; off vasopressors and off supplemental O2 (or at baseline). Source control achieved with a definitive plan in place; any drains stable and manageable in the discharge setting. Tolerating oral intake with pain controlled on an oral regimen. Antimicrobial plan finalized with OPAT or oral therapy arranged and vascular access secured. Stable/improving inflammatory markers and stable hemoglobin with no active bleeding. Ambulating at a safe baseline; safe disposition confirmed. Patient/caregiver education completed and follow-up arranged.
discharge_planning: Disposition: home +/- home health, home OPAT, or SNF/rehab (LTAC if prolonged IV therapy/complex wounds). Medication reconciliation with explicit antibiotic stop date, anticoagulation plan, and analgesia taper. OPAT coordination: PICC placement, weekly safety labs (CBC, CMP, drug levels), and ID follow-up. Follow-up: index surgeon at 1-2 wk, ID at 1-2 wk, PCP, wound care; device clinic for CIED reimplantation timing. Patient education: wound/drain care, anticoagulation teaching, and red flags (fever, increasing drainage/erythema, bleeding, device-site pain) with return precautions. Transitions of care: warm handoff, discharge summary to PCP and surgeon within 48h, teach-back confirmation.
expected_los: Approximate CMS GMLOS by severity tier: ~2.7 days (DRG 921/923, no CC/MCC), ~3.8-4.0 days (DRG 920, with CC), ~5.5-6 days (DRG 919 and 922, with MCC); blended benchmark ~4 days. (Figures are approximate GMLOS ranges — a broad, heterogeneous DRG family, so single-value precision is limited.)
target_los: Blended best-practice target ~3 days under the ideal pathway (early source control + Day-0 ID engagement + 48h stewardship de-escalation + OPAT hardwiring); ~2 days for simple no-CC/MCC device/wound complications up to ~5 days for MCC-tier septic/hemorrhagic presentations requiring explant and prolonged IV therapy.
quality_metrics: CMS SEP-1 (severe sepsis/septic-shock bundle) when the complication is post-procedural sepsis. AHRQ/CMS PSI-90 composite and its component indicators: PSI-09 (perioperative hemorrhage/hematoma), PSI-10 (postoperative AKI), PSI-11 (postoperative respiratory failure), PSI-12 (perioperative PE/DVT), PSI-13 (postoperative sepsis), PSI-15 (unrecognized abdominopelvic accidental puncture/laceration). CMS HAC Reduction Program; NHSN SSI, CLABSI, CAUTI, and C. difficile standardized infection ratios. 30-day readmission and mortality; return-to-OR rate. Antimicrobial-stewardship metrics: blood cultures drawn before antibiotics, days-of-therapy, IV-to-PO conversion, ID consultation for S. aureus bacteremia. Restrictive-transfusion adherence and VTE-prophylaxis rate.
common_complications: Progression to sepsis/septic shock -> mitigate with early hour-1 bundle and prompt source control. Persistent/recurrent infection or treatment failure with retained hardware (biofilm) -> ID-directed duration + biofilm-active agents, escalate DAIR failure to staged exchange. Bleeding/hematoma from procedure or anticoagulation -> reversal agents, restrictive transfusion, surgical hemostasis. Acute kidney injury (hypoperfusion, contrast, nephrotoxic vancomycin/aminoglycosides) -> renal dosing, level monitoring, avoid nephrotoxins. C. difficile from broad antibiotics -> stewardship and early narrowing. Device-related bloodstream infection/endocarditis. CLABSI/CAUTI -> early line/catheter removal. VTE -> risk-stratified prophylaxis. Delirium in older adults -> non-pharmacologic prevention bundle. Wound dehiscence or reaccumulated collection -> drain management and re-imaging.
readmission_drivers: Recurrent/relapsed infection from inadequate source control or premature antibiotic discontinuation -> complete source control, ID-directed duration, structured OPAT monitoring. OPAT/PICC complications (line infection, thrombosis, drug toxicity) -> weekly labs and scheduled ID follow-up. Antimicrobial adverse effects (AKI, cytopenias, C. difficile) -> monitoring and stewardship. Wound/drain problems and uncontrolled pain -> wound-care teaching, clear instructions, early surgeon follow-up. Anticoagulation-related rebleeding or thrombosis -> explicit resumption/bridging plan and teaching. Missed follow-up and poor transitions -> pre-scheduled surgeon + ID visits, 48h discharge summary, teach-back. Medication nonadherence and social barriers -> case management and social work.
guideline_source: Entity-specific society guidelines (heterogeneous DRG — no single unifying guideline): IDSA Prosthetic Joint Infection CPG (2013) + 2018 ICM/MSIS PJI Definition; CDC/HICPAC Surgical Site Infection Prevention Guideline (2017); Surgical Infection Society-IDSA Complicated Intra-abdominal Infection Guidelines (2010; IDSA 2024 diagnostic update); AHA Scientific Statement on CIED Infections (2024) + HRS Expert Consensus on Lead Management/Extraction (2017); ESVS Vascular Graft/Endograft Infection CPG (2020) + AHA Vascular Graft Infection Statement (2016, MAGIC criteria); Surviving Sepsis Campaign (2021) with CMS SEP-1; ERAS Society perioperative recovery guidelines; AHRQ/CMS Patient Safety Indicators (PSI-90).

SIS/IDSA Complicated Intra-abdominal Infection Guidelines, Clin Infect Dis 2010;50(2):133-164; Chakfe N et al. ESVS 2020 Vascular Graft/Endograft Infection CPG, Eur J Vasc Endovasc Surg 2020; Kusumoto FM et al. 2017 HRS Lead Management and Extraction Consensus. Attribution: article metadata retrieved from PubMed.
evidence_grade: Moderate overall (composite/consensus framework) — this collapses a heterogeneous MDC-21 family, so there is no single unifying RCT or guideline. Individual pathway components rest on strong, current society guidelines: source control + culture-directed therapy for device/prosthetic infection (IDSA PJI, Strong/Class I for source control); Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 (strong for early antibiotics/source control and norepinephrine, conditional/weak for several fluid and resuscitation targets); CDC SSI 2017 and AHA 2024 CIED (Class I complete device removal). Where the pathway generalizes across the whole DRG bucket (blended LOS, unified antimicrobial durations, poisoning/toxic-effect subset) the evidence is extrapolated/expert-opinion and graded lower. Net: Strong for the anchor entities, Moderate-to-consensus for the collapsed group.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 921/923 (no CC/MCC): localized complication (uncomplicated superficial SSI, minor mechanical device issue, small hemorrhage, mild adverse drug effect) — floor care, often a single intervention, rapid IV-to-PO, LOS ~2-3 days. DRG 920 (with CC): deeper infection, moderate bleeding, or stage 1-2 AKI — longer antimicrobial course, possible telemetry/step-down, LOS ~4 days. DRG 919 and 922 (with MCC): septic shock, respiratory failure, major hemorrhage on MTP, AKI needing renal replacement, or need for device/graft explant with prolonged OPAT — ICU-level care, LOS ~5.5-6+ days, higher mortality and readmission; principal MCC drivers are sepsis and acute organ failure. The 922/923 injury/poisoning/toxic-effect line shifts management toward toxicology, antidotes, and monitored medical stabilization rather than surgical source control. Accurate CC/MCC and PSI/HAC documentation materially affects the LOS benchmark and reimbursement across the group.
pathway_pearls: 1) Source control is the single highest-yield lever: draw cultures first, then achieve early drainage/debridement/device or graft explant (target <24h) — antibiotics alone rarely cure hardware, graft, or organ-space infection because of biofilm. 2) Engage Infectious Diseases AND the index proceduralist on Day 0; decide DAIR (acute, stable implant, susceptible organism) versus staged exchange early, and hardwire 48h stewardship de-escalation plus OPAT/PICC to compress LOS. 3) Two systems in tension — the infection subset needs procedures/anticoagulation held for source control while the hemorrhage subset needs immediate reversal; protocolize reversal agents (idarucizumab, andexanet alfa/4F-PCC, protamine, vitamin K) and a restrictive transfusion trigger (Hgb <7 g/dL). 4) This is a coding- and quality-sensitive DRG: rigorous CC/MCC and PSI-90/HAC capture, SEP-1 bundle compliance, and blood-cultures-before-antibiotics drive both outcomes and benchmark performance.

admission_criteria: Admit for confirmed mechanical small-bowel obstruction (SBO) or ileus with any of: complete/high-grade obstruction or clear transition point on CT; intractable vomiting or inability to tolerate oral intake; dehydration, AKI, or electrolyte derangement; marked distension/pain requiring NG decompression; or ANY strangulation red flag (peritonitis, fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis, metabolic acidosis, continuous pain, CT ischemia signs) — these mandate urgent surgical evaluation, not observation. ED discharge/short observation is reserved only for low-grade partial SBO that resolves with tolerating PO and has no high-risk features. Bypass floor admission for direct OR when peritonitis, closed-loop obstruction, or bowel ischemia is present.
risk_stratification: Zielinski multivariate model (World J Surg 2010): 4 predictors of need for operation — vomiting, absent small-bowel feces sign, mesenteric edema, free intraperitoneal fluid; all 4 present = sensitivity 96%, PPV 90% (drives early surgical decision). MDCT ~90% accuracy for strangulation; ischemia signs = reduced/absent bowel-wall enhancement, closed-loop, pneumatosis intestinalis, portal-venous gas, mesenteric fluid → urgent OR. Physical exam only 48% sensitive for strangulation — do NOT rely on exam alone. Labs: WBC >10,000/mm3, CRP >75 mg/L, lactate >2 mmol/L flag ischemia/peritonitis and escalate. Water-soluble contrast challenge: contrast reaching colon on radiograph within 24h predicts non-operative resolution (pooled sens/spec ~96%).
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential (leukocytosis/left shift → ischemia); BMP/CMP (K+, Cl, HCO3 — expect hypokalemic hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis from vomiting/NG loss; BUN/Cr for dehydration/AKI); serum lactate (>2 mmol/L suggests ischemia); CRP (>75 mg/L flags peritonitis); magnesium/phosphate; venous blood gas for acidosis; type & screen (possible OR); lipase to exclude pancreatitis mimic; urinalysis; beta-hCG in women of childbearing age; coags/INR if anticoagulated. Trend CBC, BMP, and lactate q12-24h; a rising lactate or worsening leukocytosis triggers repeat imaging and surgical reassessment.
initial_imaging_dx: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (multidetector CT with multiplanar reconstruction) is first-line and Level I evidence (EAST) — obtain early, within hours of admission, to confirm SBO, define the transition point, exclude non-adhesive etiology (hernia, malignancy, closed-loop, gallstone ileus) and detect strangulation/ischemia (~90% accuracy). Supine/upright plain abdominal films as adjunct and for serial monitoring of gas pattern. Water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin/diatrizoate) challenge: 50-100 mL via NGT with follow-up abdominal radiograph at 8-24h — contrast reaching the colon predicts successful non-operative resolution. Upright CXR if perforation suspected (free air).
time_critical_interventions: Strangulation, closed-loop, peritonitis, or CT ischemia → OR without delay (target <6h from recognition; delay increases bowel resection and mortality). Start IV crystalloid resuscitation and NG decompression at triage. The non-operative trial has a hard ceiling: structured reassessment at 48h, water-soluble contrast challenge if unresolved by 48-72h, and failure to resolve by 72h → surgery (trials beyond 3 days increase morbidity in those ultimately operated). Acute colonic pseudo-obstruction (Ogilvie's) with cecal diameter >10-12 cm refractory to 24-48h conservative care → IV neostigmine 2 mg under continuous cardiac monitoring (atropine at bedside) or colonoscopic decompression.
initial_management: "Drip and suck": NPO; nasogastric tube decompression for vomiting/distension; isotonic crystalloid (LR or NS) resuscitation with aggressive electrolyte repletion (K+, correct hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis, Mg/phosphate); Foley for strict I/O. Analgesia and antiemetics while MINIMIZING opioids (opioids worsen ileus/dysmotility). No routine antibiotics for uncomplicated SBO; give broad-spectrum coverage (e.g., piperacillin-tazobactam covering gram-negatives + anaerobes) only for suspected strangulation, perforation, or perioperative prophylaxis. Transfusion not a core element (restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL if indicated). Reverse anticoagulation if urgent OR needed (vitamin K/4-factor PCC for warfarin; idarucizumab for dabigatran; andexanet/PCC for factor Xa inhibitors). Definitive source control = surgery (adhesiolysis; laparoscopic preferred for simple single-band ASBO) for failed non-op trial or ischemia.
day1_milestones: Day 1: Early contrast CT confirms SBO, transition point, and excludes ischemia/closed-loop/non-adhesive cause; NGT placed with output quantified; volume resuscitation with electrolyte correction under way; risk-stratify (Zielinski 4 signs, lactate, CRP, WBC); surgical consult and co-management established for every admitted SBO; VTE prophylaxis started; operative vs non-operative track assigned. Any ischemia/peritonitis/closed-loop → same-day OR.
day2_milestones: Day 2 (~48h): Reassess with serial exam and trended labs; if not clinically resolving, administer water-soluble contrast challenge and obtain abdominal radiograph at 8-24h — contrast in colon → continue non-operative management and plan feeding; contrast failing to reach colon or any clinical deterioration/rising lactate → proceed to surgery. Monitor for return of bowel function (flatus, decreasing NG output and distension). Reassess opioid use and advance mobility.
day3plus_milestones: Day 3+: Non-operative success (70-90% of ASBO) — return of bowel function → clamp/remove NGT, advance diet, transition to oral intake, ambulate, control pain → discharge. Non-resolution by 72h → operative management (adhesiolysis +/- resection). Post-operative or persistent functional ileus: supportive care, strict opioid minimization, correct electrolytes, early mobilization, consider gum-chewing/sham feeding; manage ACPO as above. Discharge readiness when tolerating diet and passing flatus/stool without recurrent distension.
consults_multidisciplinary: Acute-care/general surgery on admission for ALL SBO (co-management and ownership of operative-timing decision — non-negotiable, even for non-operative cases); radiology for CT interpretation and transition-point localization; nutrition/dietitian if NPO anticipated >5-7 days (consider TPN); pharmacy for electrolyte repletion protocols and opioid stewardship; case management/discharge planning from Day 1; gastroenterology for colonoscopic decompression in ACPO; oncology/palliative care for malignant obstruction; PT/OT for deconditioned or elderly patients.
monitoring_level: Floor/med-surg for uncomplicated partial SBO on a non-operative trial. Telemetry/step-down for significant electrolyte derangement, large fluid shifts, elderly with comorbidity, or during neostigmine administration (bradycardia risk — continuous cardiac monitoring, atropine available). ICU for strangulation/ischemia with sepsis, hemodynamic instability, severe metabolic acidosis, or complicated post-operative course. Escalate for rising lactate, worsening pain or peritoneal signs, hemodynamic instability, or high NG output with hypovolemia.
nutrition_mobility_vte: NPO with NG decompression initially; advance diet only after return of bowel function (flatus/BM, decreased distension, NG output <500 mL/day): clear liquids → regular as tolerated; TPN if NPO expected >7 days or malnourished. Early ambulation to reduce ileus, VTE, and pneumonia. Pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (LMWH, e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily, or UFH 5000 U SC q8h) unless active bleeding or imminent surgery, plus mechanical SCDs. Glucose target 140-180 mg/dL. Remove NGT and Foley early once resolving to prevent aspiration and CAUTI.
discharge_criteria: Return of bowel function (passing flatus/stool); tolerating oral diet without vomiting; NGT removed >=24h without recurrent distension; adequate pain control on oral analgesics; electrolytes and renal function corrected; no signs of ongoing obstruction or ischemia; ambulating at baseline. Post-operative patients additionally: stable wound, no signs of anastomotic leak or SSI, and independent oral intake.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home; SNF/rehab if deconditioned or elderly. Medication reconciliation (minimize constipating opioids/anticholinergics; add bowel regimen); patient education on recurrent-SBO red flags (vomiting, distension, obstipation, escalating pain → return to ED); surgical follow-up in 2-4 weeks (wound check, adhesion-barrier/hernia-repair discussion); PCP follow-up; oncology linkage for malignant obstruction. Address the modifiable cause (hernia, tumor, internal hernia) definitively. Warm handoff and clear return precautions to the outpatient team.
expected_los: DRG-benchmarked GMLOS: DRG 388 (w/MCC) ~4.5 days (AMLOS 6.0); DRG 389 (w/CC) ~2.9 days (AMLOS 3.6); DRG 390 (w/o CC/MCC) ~2.2 days (AMLOS 2.6). Non-operative resolution typically 4-5 days; operatively managed cases run longer (~10-16 days) but migrate out to bowel-procedure DRGs.
target_los: Optimized best-practice: uncomplicated non-operative ASBO 2-3 days, accelerated by early contrast CT plus a water-soluble contrast challenge at 48h to expedite the non-op-vs-op decision; DRG 390 ~2 days; DRG 389 ~2.5-3 days; DRG 388/operative 5-7 days with laparoscopic adhesiolysis and ERAS elements.
quality_metrics: WSES Bologna guideline compliance (BJS 2025 data link compliance to fewer bowel resections and complications); time-to-CT; water-soluble contrast challenge utilization and protocol adherence; rate of nontherapeutic laparotomy (minimize); rate of delayed surgery >72h requiring bowel resection (marker of missed strangulation); VTE prophylaxis rate; laparoscopic adhesiolysis rate in eligible cases; LOS index vs GMLOS; 30-day readmission for recurrent SBO; in-hospital mortality. No SBO-specific CMS core measure (e.g., no SEP-1 analog) exists.
common_complications: Strangulation → bowel ischemia/necrosis/perforation (mitigate: early CT, Zielinski risk stratification, serial exam/lactate, 72h non-op ceiling, low threshold for OR); aspiration pneumonia (NG decompression, head-of-bed elevation); hypokalemic hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis and AKI (aggressive fluid/electrolyte repletion); VTE (prophylaxis + mobility); prolonged post-operative ileus (opioid minimization, early mobilization); surgical-site infection/anastomotic leak if resected; recurrent obstruction.
readmission_drivers: Recurrent adhesive SBO (high lifetime recurrence — ~20% at 5 years in younger patients, rising with each episode); premature discharge before durable bowel-function return; unaddressed underlying cause (hernia, malignancy, Crohn's); post-discharge dehydration/electrolyte issues; malignant obstruction. Prevention levers: confirm sustained bowel function before discharge, apply adhesion barriers and definitively repair causal hernia/tumor at surgery, red-flag education, early surgical follow-up, and disciplined non-op-vs-op decision-making to avoid incomplete resolution.
guideline_source: WSES Bologna Guidelines for diagnosis and management of adhesive small bowel obstruction (ASBO) — 2017 update (World Society of Emergency Surgery ASBO working group; Ten Broek et al., 2018); EAST Practice Management Guideline: Evaluation and Management of Small-Bowel Obstruction (Eastern Association for the Surgery of Trauma; Maung et al., 2012). Adjuncts: ACR Appropriateness Criteria (imaging) and ACG/ASCRS guidance for ileus/acute colonic pseudo-obstruction.
key_citations: Citations verified via PubMed with DOI links: Ten Broek RPG et al. Bologna guidelines for ASBO: 2017 update. World J Emerg Surg 2018;13:24. PMID 29946347, https://doi.org/10.1186/s13017-018-0185-2; Maung AA et al. EAST PMG: Evaluation and management of SBO. J Trauma Acute Care Surg 2012;73:S362-9. PMID 23114494, https://doi.org/10.1097/TA.0b013e31827019de; Zielinski MD et al. SBO—who needs an operation? A multivariate prediction model. World J Surg 2010;34:910-9. PMID 20217412, https://doi.org/10.1007/s00268-010-0479-3; Abbas SM et al. Meta-analysis of oral water-soluble contrast in ASBO. Br J Surg 2007;94:404-11. PMID 17380561, https://doi.org/10.1002/bjs.5775; Ceresoli M et al. Water-soluble contrast agent in ASBO: systematic review/meta-analysis. Am J Surg 2016;211:1114-25. PMID 26329902, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.amjsurg.2015.06.012; Koh A, Adiamah A et al. Therapeutic role of WSCM in ASBO: meta-analysis. J Gastrointest Surg 2019;24:473-83. PMID 31485900, https://doi.org/10.1007/s11605-019-04341-7; Abbas S et al. Cochrane: Oral water-soluble contrast for ASBO. Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2005;(1):CD004651. PMID 15674958.
evidence_grade: Moderate to Strong. Level I evidence for MDCT in evaluation (EAST); water-soluble contrast challenge has strong diagnostic accuracy (pooled sensitivity/specificity ~96%, Cochrane/BJS meta-analyses) but modest and debated therapeutic benefit (Ceresoli positive: fewer operations, LOS -2.18d; Koh/Adiamah negative: LOS -0.15d, no reduction in surgery). The 72-hour non-operative ceiling and operative-timing thresholds are consensus/expert opinion (WSES). Overall a blend of Class I imaging evidence, meta-analytic contrast data, and expert-consensus thresholds. (Evidence retrieved via PubMed.)
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 390 (no CC/MCC): simple partial ASBO that resolves non-operatively; GMLOS ~2.2d; floor care. DRG 389 (CC): dehydration/AKI, electrolyte derangement, or moderate comorbidity; GMLOS ~2.9d; may need step-down. DRG 388 (MCC): strangulation/ischemia, sepsis, perforation, severe metabolic acidosis, or major comorbidity → ICU-level care, frequently operative, GMLOS ~4.5d (AMLOS 6.0); mortality concentrates here, so escalate monitoring, expedite OR, and expect resection/complications. Note: surgically managed obstruction migrates OUT of DRG 388-390 into bowel-procedure DRGs (rank ~35), so this medical group over-represents non-operative and early-course cases.
pathway_pearls: 1) Get an early IV-contrast MDCT on every admitted SBO — it fixes the transition point, excludes non-adhesive/closed-loop causes, and detects ischemia (~90%); never rely on physical exam (only 48% sensitive) or plain films alone. 2) Respect the 72-hour non-operative ceiling and use the water-soluble contrast challenge at 48h to decide — contrast-to-colon by 24h predicts non-op success (sens/spec ~96%), while trials beyond 3 days increase morbidity. 3) Never miss strangulation — Zielinski's 4 signs (vomiting, absent small-bowel feces sign, mesenteric edema, free fluid) plus rising lactate/CRP/WBC or CT closed-loop mandate urgent OR; surgeon co-management from Day 1. 4) Win on the "medical" complications — aggressive K+/volume repletion, NG decompression to prevent aspiration, opioid minimization, early mobility and VTE prophylaxis; cut recurrence with adhesion barriers and definitive repair of the causal hernia/tumor.
verification_notes: Verified against current sources via the PubMed MCP and web search. CITATIONS: All 7 PMIDs are real and correctly attributed (author, journal, year, volume, pages, DOI all match) — Ten Broek 2017 Bologna update, World J Emerg Surg 2018;13:24, PMID 29946347 (https://doi.org/10.1186/s13017-018-0185-2); Maung EAST PMG, PMID 23114494 (https://doi.org/10.1097/TA.0b013e31827019de); Zielinski, PMID 20217412 (https://doi.org/10.1007/s00268-010-0479-3); Abbas BJS 2007, PMID 17380561 (https://doi.org/10.1002/bjs.5775); Ceresoli 2016, PMID 26329902 (https://doi.org/10.1016/j.amjsurg.2015.06.012); Koh/Adiamah, PMID 31485900 (https://doi.org/10.1007/s11605-019-04341-7); Abbas Cochrane 2005 CD004651, PMID 15674958. No fabricated or misattributed references. RISK STRATIFICATION: Zielinski multivariate model verified verbatim — 4 predictors (vomiting, absent small-bowel feces sign, mesenteric edema, free intraperitoneal fluid), all-4 sensitivity 96% and PPV 90% match the PubMed abstract. Water-soluble contrast pooled sens/spec ~96% is accurate (Abbas BJS 97%/96%; Cochrane 96%/96%). Ceresoli (positive: fewer ops, LOS -2.18d) and Koh/Adiamah (negative: LOS -0.15d, no reduction in surgery) are both characterized accurately, and evidence_grade's balanced framing of the therapeutic debate is correct. TIME-CRITICAL: neostigmine 2 mg IV with cardiac monitoring/atropine, cecal diameter >10-12 cm, 24-48h ACPO conservative trial, 48-72h contrast challenge, and 72h non-op ceiling all align with WSES/EAST. INITIAL MANAGEMENT: drug classes correct (drip-and-suck, opioid minimization, pip-tazo reserved for strangulation/perforation, restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL, LR/NS, anticoagulation reversal agents by class). VTE (enoxaparin 40 mg/UFH 5000 U q8h) and glucose 140-180 standard. QUALITY METRICS: named measures real/applicable; correctly states no SBO-specific CMS core measure / no SEP-1 analog. GUIDELINE CURRENCY: Bologna 2017 update and EAST 2012 PMG remain the current guidelines (EAST website still lists the 2012 Maung PMG; no newer EAST/WSES ASBO guideline published). The 'BJS 2025' item is the compliance-outcomes study (BJS 2025;112(4):znaf080), correctly described, not a new guideline. Only soft, non-decision-critical observations remain (see issues_found); none warrant a correction to the cell values.; ISSUES: No factual errors, fabrications, or misattributions found. All six focus areas verified correct.; SOFT (not an error): time_critical_interventions gives an OR target of '<6h from recognition' for strangulation/closed-loop/ischemia. This is reasonable expert-consensus framing (delay increases resection/mortality) but is NOT a hard, validated guideline-mandated numeric standard analogous to door-to-balloon; it should be read as an operational goal, not a measured quality metric. Not incorrect.; SOFT (not an error): risk_stratification's 'physical exam only 48% sensitive for strangulation' could not be pinned to a specific current primary source on independent search. The figure is plausible and commonly cited, and the operative conclusion (do not rely on exam alone) is well-supported by the literature. Non-decision-critical supporting statistic.; SOFT (not an error): expected_los/target_los DRG 388/389/390 GMLOS values (~4.5 / ~2.9 / ~2.2 days; AMLOS 6.0 / 3.6 / 2.6) are plausible, internally consistent, and match the correct MS-DRG grouping (G.I. obstruction w MCC / w CC / w/o CC-MCC), but were not cross-checked line-by-line against a specific CMS FY2025 MS-DRG table.
scope_and_volume_notes: Medical G.I. obstruction DRGs (388-390); surgically-managed cases fall under bowel-procedure DRGs (rank 35). Includes adhesive SBO, ileus.

admission_criteria: Inpatient (vs ED discharge/observation) when danger-to-self/others or grave disability: active suicidal ideation with plan and/or intent, recent/aborted/interrupted suicide attempt or preparatory acts (C-SSRS ideation 4-5 or any recent "behavior"); homicidal ideation; inability to maintain basic self-care/safety; psychotic features, catatonia, or severe psychomotor retardation; need for inpatient-only treatment (ECT initiation, complex med change/monitoring, medically-complicated withdrawal) or failure of PHP/IOP; severe MDD (PHQ-9 >=20 / MADRS >34) with functional collapse and inadequate outpatient safety/support. ED discharge or observation acceptable when passive ideation without plan/intent/means access, protective factors + reliable supervision, feasible safety plan, means restriction achievable, and rapid (<=7-day) follow-up secured (SPI+ pathway). Use involuntary hold per state statute if criteria met and patient declines voluntary admission.
risk_stratification: C-SSRS (Columbia-Suicide Severity Rating Scale) screen ALL patients (TJC NPSG 15.01.01): ideation 1-2 (wish to be dead/nonspecific active)->outpatient safety planning; ideation 3 (active with method, no plan/intent)->enhanced monitoring; ideation 4-5 (active with intent, or plan+intent) or any past-month suicidal "behavior"->admit + 1:1/close observation (Posner 2011). PHQ-9 severity bands: 5-9 mild, 10-14 moderate, 15-19 moderately severe, 20-27 severe; positive item-9 triggers full C-SSRS/safety assessment. MADRS >34 or HAM-D >=24 = severe (drives ECT consideration + measurement-based care). Screen bipolarity with MDQ + YMRS (Young Mania Rating Scale) before antidepressant monotherapy. AUDIT-C/CIWA-COWS for co-occurring substance use/withdrawal. Post-discharge suicide risk is highest in first week-to-3-months (Chung 2017: 1132/100k person-years <3mo; 2078/100k if admitted for suicidality) -> mandates guaranteed 7-day follow-up.
initial_workup_labs: Medical-clearance panel: CBC, CMP (electrolytes/renal/hepatic/glucose), TSH (hypothyroid mimic), B12/folate, vitamin D. Urine toxicology + blood alcohol (co-occurring use/withdrawal). Urine hCG in reproductive-age women (valproate teratogenicity, med safety). If antipsychotic/mood stabilizer planned: fasting glucose/HbA1c, lipid panel, weight/BMI/waist (SMD baseline metabolic screen). Lithium candidate: BUN/Cr/eGFR, TSH, calcium, ECG, pregnancy; valproate: LFTs, CBC/platelets. Intentional-overdose presentation: acetaminophen + salicylate levels, extended tox, ECG (QRS/QTc for TCA). Baseline ECG (QTc) before QT-prolonging agents (citalopram >40 mg, antipsychotics, methadone). RPR/HIV, ammonia, ceruloplasmin, or heavy-metal only if clinically indicated.
initial_imaging_dx: Depression/mood disorder is a clinical DSM-5 diagnosis - routine neuroimaging NOT indicated for typical presentations. Neuroimaging (CT head, preferably MRI brain) during admission if: first-episode psychosis, atypical/new-onset especially age >50, focal neuro findings, catatonia, or delirium. Baseline ECG on admission (QTc) before/with QT-prolonging psychotropics; repeat after dose changes. EEG if catatonia vs nonconvulsive status, atypical episodic features. Pre-ECT workup when indicated: anesthesia evaluation, ECG, basic labs, +/- cardiac/spine assessment per risk (no routine structural imaging unless red flags).
time_critical_interventions: Universal validated suicide-risk screen (C-SSRS) at presentation + environmental/ligature risk assessment (TJC NPSG 15.01.01). Screen-positive -> immediate (minutes) safety: 1:1 continuous or line-of-sight observation, remove means (belts/cords/sharps/meds), search belongings, ligature-resistant environment. Psychiatric evaluation by qualified clinician within hours; initiate/optimize antidepressant same day. Stanley-Brown Safety Planning Intervention + lethal-means counseling completed before any disposition/discharge decision (SPI+ ~45% fewer suicidal behaviors and 2x follow-up engagement over 6 months - Stanley 2018). For acute suicidal ideation-with-intent needing rapid response: esketamine nasal spray 84 mg (or IV ketamine) added to hospitalization + oral antidepressant -> rapid MADRS reduction at 24h (ASPIRE I/II); expedite ECT for psychotic/catatonic/severe-suicidal cases. If overdose/medical instability: stabilize medically first (ABCs; N-acetylcysteine for acetaminophen, IV sodium bicarbonate for TCA wide-QRS) before psychiatric admission.
initial_management: First-line antidepressants (VA/DoD 2022, pick one): SSRI (sertraline, escitalopram, fluoxetine), SNRI (venlafaxine XR, duloxetine), bupropion, mirtazapine, vortioxetine, vilazodone, or trazodone; start low/titrate, counsel 2-4 wk latency. Avoid as initial agents: MAOIs, TCAs, nefazodone, and esketamine/ketamine (reserve for TRD/acute suicidality). Psychotic depression: antidepressant + antipsychotic (e.g., SSRI + olanzapine or quetiapine) OR ECT (first-line for psychotic/catatonic/severe). If reclassified bipolar depression: quetiapine, lurasidone, lumateperone, cariprazine, lithium, or lamotrigine - AVOID antidepressant monotherapy. Partial-response augmentation: atypical antipsychotic (aripiprazole, brexpiprazole, quetiapine XR), lithium, or T3; rTMS or esketamine for treatment-resistant (>=2 failed adequate trials). Catatonia: lorazepam challenge 1-2 mg then scheduled benzodiazepine, ECT if refractory. Sleep/agitation: trazodone/mirtazapine hs; avoid standing benzodiazepines. Start evidence-based psychotherapy inpatient (CBT, behavioral activation, IPT; DBT for impulsivity/self-harm) + milieu; brief intervention for substance use. Continue effective antidepressant >=6 months after remission.
day1_milestones: Complete psychiatric diagnostic evaluation + DSM-5 diagnosis; document C-SSRS and risk formulation; set observation level (1:1 vs Q15). Result/review medical-clearance labs; medication reconciliation; initiate/optimize first-line antidepressant (+ antipsychotic/mood stabilizer if indicated). Begin Stanley-Brown safety plan + lethal-means counseling; secure milieu. Obtain collateral (family, outpatient providers, prior records); confirm legal status (voluntary vs hold). Baseline measurement (PHQ-9/MADRS); orient to unit; start groups/psychotherapy; assess sleep/nutrition. If ECT candidate: consult ECT service, begin pre-ECT workup/consent.
day2_milestones: Reassess suicide risk (C-SSRS) and mental status daily; step down observation (1:1 -> line-of-sight/Q15) as risk improves. Confirm medication tolerability/adherence, manage side effects, titrate dose; watch for activation/akathisia and early SI increase (FDA young-adult warning, first weeks). Continue psychotherapy/groups + behavioral activation; family engaged in treatment planning. Begin active discharge planning: identify outpatient provider, verify benefits/insurance, address housing/social needs, care management engaged. If ECT: deliver index treatments (2-3x/week), monitor response and cognitive effects.
day3plus_milestones: Demonstrable reduction in suicidal ideation/intent with no active plan/means access; C-SSRS improved; patient future-oriented and able to use safety plan. Measurement-based improvement (PHQ-9/MADRS trending down); medication regimen stabilized and tolerated. Finalize aftercare: outpatient appointment scheduled <=7 days (warm handoff), prescriptions/med supply arranged, means-restriction plan confirmed with family. Review completed safety plan with patient + supports; psychoeducation on relapse warning signs and crisis resources (988). Consider caring-contacts and step-down to PHP/IOP; confirm transportation and follow-up logistics.
consults_multidisciplinary: Psychiatry (attending) leads; consultation-liaison psychiatry if boarding on med-surg (scatter bed). Psychology/therapist (CBT, behavioral activation, DBT for personality/impulse-control/self-harm - DRG 883); social work day 1 (disposition, housing, benefits, collateral, guardianship/legal). Care/case management (utilization + transitions of care); clinical pharmacy (regimen, interactions, QTc, metabolic monitoring, pharmacogenomics if indicated); registered dietitian (nutrition/metabolic). ECT service + anesthesia when indicated; addiction medicine for co-occurring SUD; hospitalist/internal medicine for medical comorbidity or post-overdose; OT/recreational therapy; peer-support specialist; chaplaincy; family meeting; child psychiatry + school/family systems for adolescent/childhood presentations.
monitoring_level: Psychiatric inpatient unit (locked, ligature-resistant); observation tiered to risk: 1:1 continuous for active intent/behavior/recent attempt, line-of-sight for high risk, Q15-minute checks for moderate, routine for low. Medical telemetry/ICU reserved for medical instability: overdose, serotonin syndrome or NMS, severe withdrawal, catatonia with autonomic instability, refeeding, or QTc prolongation/arrhythmia. Escalation triggers: escalating SI or suicidal behavior, agitation/aggression, self-harm, medical decompensation, worsening psychosis, or refusal of vitals/intake -> raise observation level, medical transfer, or least-restrictive restraint/seclusion per policy (minimize; tracked as HBIPS-2/3).
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: regular; monitor PO intake (depression-related anorexia); dietitian if malnourished/refeeding risk; metabolic-conscious diet with antipsychotics. Mobility: encourage milieu participation and behavioral activation/structured exercise (adjunctive antidepressant benefit); patients generally ambulatory so routine pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis usually unnecessary - reassess and add mechanical +/- pharmacologic prophylaxis if immobile, catatonic, or restrained. Glucose/metabolic: baseline + ongoing weight, glucose, and lipids on antipsychotics (SMD measure); lithium/valproate levels with renal/hepatic monitoring. Lines/catheters: usually none - remove promptly any placed for overdose/medical care; minimize restraint/seclusion (least-restrictive) with continuous monitoring when used.
discharge_criteria: Suicidal ideation resolved or returned to chronic baseline without plan, intent, or means access; no imminent danger to self/others; acceptable C-SSRS. Psychiatrically stable: mood/psychosis improved, medication regimen established and tolerated, able to participate in aftercare. Completed/updated Stanley-Brown safety plan + lethal-means counseling with support persons; means restriction in place. Confirmed outpatient follow-up within 7 days (appointment actually made, not just referred); medication access and reconciliation done; adequate housing/support/supervision; patient agrees to and can enact the plan.
discharge_planning: Disposition: home with outpatient psychiatry/therapy; step-down to PHP/IOP; residential/crisis stabilization; or transfer to state/longer-term facility. Medication reconciliation + prescriptions with quantity-limited/means-restricted dispensing when overdose risk; educate on 2-4 wk latency, side effects, adherence, and black-box SI monitoring. Schedule follow-up <=7 days (FUH 7-day measure; targets peak post-discharge risk window - Chung 2017); provide 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline, written crisis plan, and caring-contacts/follow-up calls. Send transition record to outpatient providers/PCP (TR measure); engage family/supports; link to SUD treatment; communicate continuing-care plan to next provider (HBIPS continuing-care/med-continuation).
expected_los: Typical ~5-7 days for behavioral-health depression admissions (HCUP mood-disorder mean LOS ~6-7 days). IPPS grouper GMLOS benchmarks are roughly ~4 days for DRG 881 (depressive neuroses) and ~5.5-6 days for DRG 885 (psychoses); note most psychiatric stays are actually reimbursed under IPF PPS per-diem rather than IPPS GMLOS weights. Use ~6 days as the working benchmark.
target_los: ~4-5 days under an optimized pathway (same-day psychiatric evaluation, early measurement-based pharmacotherapy, discharge planning from Day 1, step-down to PHP/IOP) - explicitly without shortening below the point of stabilization or sacrificing the guaranteed <=7-day follow-up linkage that prevents readmission and post-discharge suicide.
quality_metrics: TJC NPSG 15.01.01 (suicide-risk screening + environmental/ligature risk reduction). CMS IPFQR / TJC HBIPS set: HBIPS-1 admission screening; HBIPS-2/3 hours of physical restraint & seclusion; HBIPS-5 justification for multiple antipsychotics at discharge; SUB-2/3 alcohol & drug use screening/treatment; TOB-2/3 tobacco treatment; TR transition record with specified elements; SMD screening for metabolic disorders; IMM-2 influenza immunization. Claims-based: Follow-Up After Hospitalization for Mental Illness (FUH 7-day and 30-day, HEDIS); Medication Continuation Following Inpatient Psychiatric Discharge; 30-day all-cause unplanned readmission and 30-day ED-visit following psychiatric hospitalization. Process/outcome: documented measurement-based care (PHQ-9), safety-plan completion, time-to-follow-up.
common_complications: In-hospital suicide attempt/self-harm -> ligature-resistant environment, 1:1 observation, means removal, environmental audit (NPSG). Medication adverse effects: antidepressant activation/akathisia and early SI increase (young adults), serotonin syndrome, SSRI-induced hyponatremia/SIADH, QTc prolongation, antipsychotic metabolic effects/EPS/NMS, lithium or valproate toxicity -> measurement-based monitoring, ECG, electrolytes, metabolic labs. Manic/mixed switch on antidepressant from unrecognized bipolar -> pre-screen MDQ/YMRS, avoid antidepressant monotherapy. Agitation/aggression requiring restraint/seclusion (minimize; HBIPS), falls, and substance withdrawal. Balance over-restriction/alarm-fatigue against under-monitoring by tiering observation to risk.
readmission_drivers: No or failed outpatient follow-up -> guarantee a <=7-day appointment with warm handoff/caring contacts (FUH). Medication nonadherence or access/cost barriers -> med reconciliation, affordability, education, long-acting options where appropriate. Untreated co-occurring substance use -> integrated SUD treatment/linkage. Inadequate social support, housing instability, or continued means access -> social work, means restriction, family engagement. Premature discharge before stabilization or residual suicidal ideation; personality/impulse-control comorbidity (DRG 883) driving recurrent short stays -> DBT + PHP/IOP step-down. Post-discharge first week-to-3-months is peak suicide-risk window (Chung 2017; Tai 2025) -> intensive transitional care.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: VA/DoD Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Major Depressive Disorder, Version 4.0 (2022, GRADE-based). VA/DoD Clinical Practice Guideline for Assessment and Management of Patients at Risk for Suicide (2019). The Joint Commission National Patient Safety Goal 15.01.01 (Suicide Prevention; R3 Report Issue 18) + Zero Suicide framework. APA (American Psychiatric Association) Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Patients with Major Depressive Disorder, 3rd ed (2010, older) and APA guideline on assessment/management of suicidal behaviors. CMS IPFQR + TJC HBIPS measure sets. For reclassified bipolar depression: CANMAT/ISBD 2018.
key_citations: According to PubMed: Posner K et al. The Columbia-Suicide Severity Rating Scale. Am J Psychiatry 2011;168(12):1266-77, PMID 22193671, doi 10.1176/appi.ajp.2011.10111704. McQuaid JR et al. Synopsis of the 2022 VA/DoD MDD Clinical Practice Guideline. Ann Intern Med 2022;175(10):1440-51, PMID 36122380, doi 10.7326/M22-1603. Stanley B, Brown GK et al. Safety Planning Intervention with follow-up vs usual care (SPI+). JAMA Psychiatry 2018;75(9):894-900, PMID 29998307, doi 10.1001/jamapsychiatry.2018.1776. Chung DT et al. Suicide Rates After Discharge From Psychiatric Facilities: systematic review/meta-analysis. JAMA Psychiatry 2017;74(7):694-702, PMID 28564699, doi 10.1001/jamapsychiatry.2017.1044. Fu DJ et al. Esketamine nasal spray for MDD with active suicidal ideation (ASPIRE I). J Clin Psychiatry 2020;81(3):19m13191, PMID 32412700, doi 10.4088/JCP.19m13191; Ionescu DF et al. ASPIRE II. Int J Neuropsychopharmacol 2021;24(1):22-31, PMID 32861217, doi 10.1093/ijnp/pyaa068. Tai A et al. Meta-analysis of risk factors for suicide after psychiatric discharge. Aust N Z J Psychiatry 2025;59(8):679-91, PMID 40576057, doi 10.1177/00048674251348372.
evidence_grade: Moderate-to-Strong overall. Pharmacotherapy, ECT, and measurement-based care rest on Strong evidence (VA/DoD 2022 GRADE recommendations, society-endorsed). Validated suicide screening (C-SSRS) and Stanley-Brown safety planning are Strong for tool validity and are regulatory-mandated (TJC), though inpatient-specific process-outcome evidence is largely observational. Inpatient LOS targets, milieu, and observation-tiering specifics are consensus/expert-opinion with limited RCT support. Caveat: esketamine produces rapid depressive-symptom reduction but did NOT significantly beat placebo on the suicidality-severity endpoints in ASPIRE I/II - so it is adjunctive for rapid symptom control, not a proven anti-suicide monotherapy.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Key coding nuance: MDC-19 DRGs 881, 882, 883 (and 885) are SINGLE-TIER - they are NOT split by CC/MCC, so comorbidity does not create a higher-weighted sibling DRG as in medical MS-DRGs, and most cases are reimbursed under IPF PPS per-diem (with facility/patient-level adjustments) rather than IPPS GMLOS weights. Clinically, acuity still governs the pathway: simple/moderate depression (881) -> shortest LOS, floor observation; psychotic or catatonic depression and bipolar presentations (often grouped to 885) -> antipsychotic/ECT, closer monitoring, longer LOS; active suicidal intent/recent attempt -> 1:1 and longer stabilization; medical complications (overdose, NMS/serotonin syndrome, withdrawal, QTc/arrhythmia) -> telemetry/ICU and med-surg boarding. Personality/impulse-control disorders (883) tend toward recurrent brief admissions and respond better to DBT + step-down than to prolonged inpatient care.
pathway_pearls: 1) Screen everyone with C-SSRS and treat the environment, not just the patient: means removal + ligature-resistant milieu + observation tiered to risk (NPSG 15.01.01) is what prevents the sentinel event. 2) The discharge IS the intervention - complete a Stanley-Brown safety plan with lethal-means counseling and lock in a follow-up appointment <=7 days with warm handoff/caring contacts, because the first post-discharge week is the peak suicide-risk window (Chung 2017; SPI+ Stanley 2018). 3) Practice measurement-based care (PHQ-9/MADRS every contact) and set the 2-4 week antidepressant-latency expectation - discharge on a tolerated regimen with a plan rather than waiting for full inpatient remission; reserve ECT for psychotic/catatonic/severe-suicidal/rapid-response cases. 4) Always rule out bipolarity (MDQ/YMRS) before antidepressant monotherapy and integrate substance-use treatment - missed bipolar disorder and untreated SUD are the top preventable failure modes and readmission drivers.
