condition: Hip / Femur Fracture Repair (except major joint)
admission_criteria: Radiographically/MRI-confirmed proximal femur fragility fracture (femoral neck, intertrochanteric, or subtrochanteric) in an older adult = inpatient SURGICAL admission — nearly universal, not observation (expected LOS greater than or equal to 2 midnights satisfies CMS Two-Midnight rule; note hip fixation is NOT on CMS inpatient-only list so document medical necessity). Operative repair is the default; nonoperative only for truly nondisplaced/stable patterns in nonambulatory patients or when comfort-focused goals/prohibitive risk. X-ray-negative but clinical suspicion (groin pain, unable to bear weight) -> MRI for occult fracture, still admit. Isolated high-energy femoral shaft in younger patients = same surgical admission but trauma-protocol driven
risk_stratification: Nottingham Hip Fracture Score (NHFS 0-10): greater than or equal to 5 = high 30-day mortality (~10%+) -> intensify orthogeriatric co-management, monitored bed, goals-of-care discussion; ASA Physical Status III-IV -> anesthesia planning/monitored recovery; Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI) greater than or equal to 2 -> perioperative cardiac risk (but do NOT delay for routine stress testing); Clinical Frailty Scale greater than or equal to 5 and Charlson Comorbidity Index -> disposition/prognosis; 4AT greater than or equal to 4 on admission -> delirium pathway; malnutrition via MNA or albumin less than 3.5 g/dL -> nutrition support; ACS-NSQIP/SORT calculator for individualized risk/consent
initial_workup_labs: CBC (baseline Hgb for transfusion planning, anemia); BMP/CMP (electrolytes, renal function, glucose — correct actively harmful derangements e.g. K+ before OR); PT/INR/aPTT (reverse anticoagulation, target INR less than 1.5 for surgery); type-and-screen/crossmatch; albumin/prealbumin + 25-OH vitamin D, calcium, TSH (nutrition + Fracture Liaison Service work-up); HbA1c; troponin ONLY if ischemic symptoms/ECG change (not routine); lactate/VBG if hemodynamically unstable; urinalysis but do NOT treat asymptomatic bacteriuria; ECG on all patients
initial_imaging_dx: AP pelvis + cross-table lateral hip X-ray on ED arrival (target within ~1h); if X-ray negative with clinical suspicion -> MRI hip within 24h (gold standard for occult fracture; CT if MRI contraindicated); preoperative 12-lead ECG all patients; focused TTE ONLY for new murmur/suspected severe aortic stenosis, decompensated HF, or unexplained dyspnea — do NOT routinely delay surgery for echo; CXR per anesthesia; avoid routine preoperative stress testing (delays surgery without changing management)
time_critical_interventions: Surgery within 24-48h of admission (AAOS 2021; Klestil meta-analysis: surgery within 48h = 20% lower 12-month mortality and fewer complications; NHFD international target under 36h) — medically-fit patients to OR without ritual delay; preoperative regional analgesia (fascia iliaca compartment or PENG block) placed in ED within hours of arrival; first analgesia within ~30 min of ED arrival; expedited anticoagulation reversal (warfarin -> IV vitamin K plus/minus 4-factor PCC to INR less than 1.5; DOAC per agent-specific timing/andexanet/idarucizumab); note HIP ATTACK (Lancet 2020) showed goal-under-6h ultra-acceleration did NOT further reduce mortality — the target is AVOID DELAY, not sub-6h heroics
initial_management: Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia: regional nerve block (fascia iliaca/PENG) + scheduled acetaminophen plus/minus low-dose opioid; AVOID meperidine and minimize NSAIDs/gabapentinoids in elderly/renal; tranexamic acid IV/topical to cut blood loss and transfusion (AAOS strong); RESTRICTIVE transfusion — trigger Hgb less than 8 g/dL or symptomatic anemia (FOCUS trial NEJM 2011; AAOS threshold no higher than 8 g/dL); euvolemic IV fluids, avoid over-resuscitation; anesthesia spinal OR general — equivalent (REGAIN NEJM 2021); definitive fixation by pattern: displaced femoral neck -> arthroplasty (hemi; total THA if active/cognitively intact), nondisplaced neck -> internal fixation (cannulated screws), intertrochanteric -> sliding hip screw (stable) or cephalomedullary nail (unstable), subtrochanteric -> long cephalomedullary nail; delirium-prevention bundle from arrival; Foley only if essential
day1_milestones: ADMISSION/PRE-OP DAY: confirm fracture pattern and surgical plan; place fascia iliaca/PENG block + start multimodal analgesia; orthogeriatric/hospitalist co-management consult activated (Day 1); expedited optimization — reverse anticoagulation, correct only actively harmful electrolyte/volume/anemia issues; NPO, consent, OR booked within 24-48h window; screen 4AT (delirium) and Braden (pressure injury) + pressure-relieving mattress; mechanical VTE prophylaxis on; document baseline ambulation/cognition/residence; Fracture Liaison Service pathway initiated; avoid Foley/lines/tethers; goals-of-care for NHFS greater than or equal to 5
day2_milestones: POD0 (day of surgery): definitive fixation completed; tranexamic acid given intraop; restrictive transfusion only if Hgb less than 8/symptomatic; multimodal analgesia continued; MOBILIZE same day or POD1 — out of bed, weight-bear as tolerated (immediate WBAT standard for modern implants/arthroplasty); start/resume pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis per surgeon timing; reintroduce oral diet and home meds; remove Foley POD0-1; incentive spirometry; active surveillance for postoperative delirium, hypotension, and post-op anemia
day3plus_milestones: POD1-POD2+ to discharge: PT twice daily, progress weight-bearing/distance/stairs; OT for ADLs and equipment; continue delirium prevention and treat if present; transition to oral analgesia + bowel regimen; wound check; confirm VTE prophylaxis agent + duration (extended 28-35 days); protein/nutrition supplementation; START secondary fracture prevention — calcium/vitamin D and initiate or plan anti-resorptive (e.g., zoledronic acid) + DEXA/FLS referral; finalize disposition; medication reconciliation; falls-prevention education; most patients medically ready by POD2-3
consults_multidisciplinary: Orthopedic surgery (primary); orthogeriatric/geriatric medicine OR hospitalist co-management from Day 1 (proven mortality + LOS benefit); anesthesia/pre-op clinic; PT and OT starting POD0-1; care management/discharge planner Day 1 (disposition is often the LOS rate-limiter); pharmacy (med rec, VTE, Beers-criteria review); dietitian (screen all, supplements); social work; Fracture Liaison Service/endocrinology for bone health; cardiology/anticoagulation ONLY if active issue; palliative care for very high NHFS or comfort-focused cases
monitoring_level: Most patients -> surgical floor, telemetry if cardiac history; step-down/ICU reserved for hemodynamic instability, recent MI/decompensated HF/severe aortic stenosis, respiratory failure, ongoing resuscitation or active bleeding, or high NHFS with instability. Escalation triggers: rising NEWS2/MEWS, hypotension, hypoxia/SpO2 drop, chest pain or troponin rise, falling serial Hgb/active bleeding, delirium with hemodynamic instability. Avoid over-monitoring stable patients — tethers impede the early mobilization that drives outcomes
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: screen ALL (MNA/albumin); oral protein/energy supplements reduce complications; correct vitamin D deficiency; early oral intake post-op. Mobility: mobilize within 24h of surgery (POD0-1), weight-bear as tolerated — the single strongest modifiable outcome lever; PT twice daily. VTE: mechanical IPC immediately PLUS pharmacologic — LMWH (enoxaparin) first-line, or aspirin (PREVENT CLOT/CRISTAL non-inferior in fracture), fondaparinux, or a DOAC; ASH 2019 favors pharmacologic over none; extend 28-35 days post-op. Glucose target under 180 mg/dL, avoid hypoglycemia. Lines/catheters: remove Foley by POD1, minimize IV tethers to enable mobility
discharge_criteria: Pain controlled on oral analgesia; medically stable (vitals, serial Hgb stable, no active untreated issue); mobilizing safely with PT-cleared assistive device at a level matching the discharge destination; tolerating diet; wound intact without infection; delirium resolved or managed with a plan; VTE prophylaxis established and taught; osteoporosis/secondary-prevention plan documented; safe disposition confirmed with services in place
discharge_planning: Disposition driven by prior function/support: home with home-health + outpatient PT vs skilled nursing facility/inpatient rehab (early PT assessment sets this). Medication reconciliation — VTE duration, analgesia taper, resume/adjust anticoagulation, and START osteoporosis therapy. Follow-up: orthopedics 2-6 weeks (wound + X-ray), primary care 1-2 weeks, Fracture Liaison Service/DEXA, geriatrics. Patient/caregiver education: weight-bearing status, falls prevention, VTE and infection warning signs, wound care. Structured warm handoff (transitions-of-care) to SNF/PCP with explicit follow-up appointments booked before discharge
expected_los: Benchmark CMS GMLOS by tier: DRG 482 (no CC/MCC) ~4.0 days; DRG 481 (CC) ~4.9 days; DRG 480 (MCC) ~6.3 days; blended acute-stay typical ~4.5-5 days (arithmetic mean AMLOS runs higher, ~5-8 days, skewed by complicated MCC cases and disposition delays)
target_los: ~3-4 days to medical readiness under an optimized orthogeriatric/ERAS pathway (surgery within 24-48h, POD0-1 mobilization, co-management) for uncomplicated 481/482; ~5-6 days for MCC-tier 480. Caveat: the true endpoint is frequently disposition-limited (awaiting SNF/rehab bed), not medical readiness — so post-acute placement is the key LOS lever
quality_metrics: Time-to-surgery under 24-48h (NHFD under 36h) — headline process metric; 30-day mortality; 30-day all-cause readmission; VTE prophylaxis rate (legacy SCIP-VTE) and VTE incidence; postoperative delirium incidence (4AT-screened); early-mobilization rate (POD0-1); orthogeriatric co-management adoption; osteoporosis-treatment/FLS initiation before discharge (secondary-fracture prevention); restrictive-transfusion appropriateness; return-to-prefracture-residence and patient-reported mobility; PSI-90 patient-safety composite; registries: AAOS/American Joint Replacement Registry + ACS-NSQIP (US analog to NHFD Best Practice Tariff)
common_complications: Postoperative delirium — most common (up to 30-50%): mitigated by regional analgesia, delirium bundle, avoiding Beers meds, early mobilization; anemia/blood loss: TXA + restrictive transfusion; pressure injury: early mobilization + pressure mattress + Braden; VTE/DVT-PE: mechanical + extended pharmacologic prophylaxis; pneumonia/aspiration: mobilization, incentive spirometry, oral care; CAUTI/UTI: avoid and early-remove Foley; cardiac (MI, HF, arrhythmia): co-management, avoid surgical delay; surgical — infection, implant failure, arthroplasty dislocation, femoral-neck nonunion/AVN; functional decline and recurrent falls
readmission_drivers: 30-day readmission ~10-15%. Top drivers: medical decompensation (pneumonia, CHF, MI, AKI, delirium), VTE, surgical-site infection, implant complication/dislocation, recurrent falls/second fragility fracture, inadequate pain control, and poor care transitions. Prevention levers: orthogeriatric co-management, VTE-prophylaxis adherence, early mobilization, delirium prevention, thorough med reconciliation, Fracture Liaison Service + osteoporosis pharmacotherapy (cuts secondary fractures ~half), structured transitions with booked follow-up, and caregiver education
guideline_source: PRIMARY: AAOS "Management of Hip Fractures in Older Adults" Evidence-Based Clinical Practice Guideline (adopted December 2021; summary published J Am Acad Orthop Surg 2022) — 16 recommendations + 3 options. SUPPORTING: ASH 2019 guidelines for VTE prevention in surgical patients; ACC/AHA perioperative cardiovascular evaluation; orthogeriatric co-management models (American Geriatrics Society; British Orthopaedic Association/British Geriatrics Society "Blue Book"; NICE CG124); ERAS/AAHKS perioperative principles
key_citations: AAOS CPG Summary — O'Connor MI, Switzer JA. J Am Acad Orthop Surg 2022;30(20):e1291-e1296 (PMID 36200817; DOI 10.5435/JAAOS-D-22-00125). HIP ATTACK Investigators. Lancet 2020;395(10225):698-708 — accelerated surgery (PMID 32050090; DOI 10.1016/S0140-6736(20)30058-1). Klestil T et al. Sci Rep 2018;8:13933 — timing meta-analysis, surgery within 48h lowers mortality (PMID 30224765; DOI 10.1038/s41598-018-32098-7). Carson JL et al (FOCUS). N Engl J Med 2011;365(26):2453-2462 — restrictive transfusion (PMID 22168590; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1012452). Neuman MD et al (REGAIN). N Engl J Med 2021;385(22):2025-2035 — spinal vs general anesthesia equivalent (PMID 34623788; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2113514). ASH 2019 surgical VTE prevention — Anderson DR et al. Blood Adv 2019;3(23):3898-3944 (PMID 31794602). [Source: PubMed]
evidence_grade: Strong overall — Class I/II society guideline (AAOS 2021) with strong recommendations for tranexamic acid, restrictive transfusion (Hgb under 8), and interdisciplinary/orthogeriatric care, reinforced by high-quality RCTs (FOCUS transfusion; REGAIN anesthesia; HIP ATTACK timing) and large meta-analyses (Klestil timing). Moderate/evolving for exact optimal timing (under 24 vs under 48h), aspirin vs LMWH for VTE, and immediate WBAT protocols. Secondary-fracture prevention (osteoporosis Rx/FLS) is strongly evidence-based
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 480 (with MCC — e.g., sepsis, acute MI, respiratory or acute renal failure, major delirium sequelae): higher step-down/ICU need, GMLOS ~6+ days, higher NHFS-driven mortality, complex disposition, intensified co-management and palliative input for very high risk. DRG 481 (with CC): intermediate, GMLOS ~5 days. DRG 482 (no CC/MCC): healthier, GMLOS ~4 days, floor-level, fast-track to rehab. Across this collapsed group, LOS/mortality are dominated by medical comorbidity burden rather than the fixation itself — so the MCC tier is where co-management, monitored beds, and goals-of-care discussions concentrate
pathway_pearls: 1) Do NOT delay surgery for ritual "medical clearance" — operate within 24-48h and only correct actively harmful, reversible problems (severe anemia, coagulopathy, decompensated HF, sepsis, dangerous electrolytes); skip routine stress tests/echo. 2) Orthogeriatric co-management from Day 1 is the single biggest mortality and LOS lever. 3) Preoperative fascia iliaca/PENG block + multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia + delirium bundle prevents the #1 complication (delirium) and cuts opioids. 4) Bundle the quiet wins — TXA, restrictive transfusion (Hgb under 8), POD0-1 mobilization, extended VTE prophylaxis, and START osteoporosis therapy/FLS before discharge to prevent the next fracture (and the next admission)

scope_and_volume_notes: Femur fixation is the 8th most frequent OR procedure (HCUP: 0.31M, 2018). Predominantly geriatric fragility hip fractures; ED entry, often ICU/medical co-management.

admission_criteria: Reserve INPATIENT status for objective high-risk features; most chest pain belongs in ED discharge or OBSERVATION, not inpatient. ED discharge if HEART 0-3 with two negative serial hs-troponins and non-ischemic ECG (30-day MACE <1-2%). Observation (not full inpatient) for intermediate-risk HEART 4-6 needing serial troponin + anatomic/functional testing. Admit as inpatient only for: rising/positive hs-troponin (->ACS/NSTEMI pathway, different DRG); ongoing or refractory rest angina; dynamic ischemic ECG changes; hemodynamic instability; new HF or significant arrhythmia; high-risk noninvasive test; left main/severe multivessel or high-risk anatomy on CTA/angiography awaiting revascularization; or HEART >=7. Known CAD/angina: admit for crescendo/refractory angina despite intensified GDMT or planned inpatient revascularization.
risk_stratification: HEART score (History, ECG, Age, Risk factors, Troponin): 0-3 = low (30-day MACE ~0.9-1.7%) -> early discharge; 4-6 = moderate (~12-17%) -> observation + testing; 7-10 = high (~50-65%) -> admit/invasive. HEART Pathway = HEART <=3 PLUS negative hs-troponin at 0 and 3h -> safe early discharge (RCT 30-day MACE <1%). EDACS <16 with negative 0/2h troponin -> low risk. ESC 0/1h or 0/2h hs-cTn algorithms for rapid rule-out (below assay limit) and rule-in (delta). TIMI and GRACE for confirmed-ACS prognosis (GRACE >140 -> early invasive if troponin+). Known CAD: CCS angina class; pre-test probability of obstructive CAD; FFR/iFR <=0.80 defines a lesion warranting revascularization.
initial_workup_labs: High-sensitivity cardiac troponin (hs-cTnT/I) at 0h and 1-3h serial -- central rule-out/rule-in; rising/falling delta triggers ACS pathway. 12-lead ECG interpreted within 10 min of arrival, repeated with recurrent symptoms or serially. BMP (renal function for contrast/GDMT dosing, K/Mg), CBC (anemia as an ischemia trigger), glucose + HbA1c, lipid panel for GDMT. NT-proBNP/BNP if HF suspected. D-dimer only if PE genuinely considered (with Wells/PERC) -- not reflexive. TSH if tachyarrhythmia. CK-MB and myoglobin NOT recommended once hs-troponin is available.
initial_imaging_dx: 12-lead ECG <10 min (exclude STEMI -> if STEMI, off-pathway to primary PCI). Portable CXR to screen alternative causes (pneumothorax, effusion, widened mediastinum). Coronary CT angiography (CCTA) = first-line for intermediate-risk without known CAD (2021 guideline Class 1), ideally within the observation stay <24h; add CAC score/CT-FFR. Stress testing (exercise ECG, stress echo, SPECT/PET MPI, or stress CMR) for intermediate/high-risk or known CAD. TTE if instability, new murmur, or suspected wall-motion/structural cause. CT aorta or CTPA if dissection (ADD-RS + D-dimer) or PE suspected. Invasive coronary angiography for high-risk features or a positive noninvasive test.
time_critical_interventions: ECG within 10 min of arrival to exclude STEMI (if STEMI, leave pathway: door-to-balloon <=90 min). Accelerated rule-out with serial hs-troponin at 0/1h or 0/2h (ESC algorithm) or 0/3h (HEART Pathway); a single hs-cTn below the assay limit with symptoms >3h may rule out. Continuous telemetry until ACS excluded. If troponin rises/positive -> ACS off-ramp: early invasive within 24h if high-risk (GRACE >140, refractory ischemia). By definition this DRG bucket excludes AMI, so the time-critical priority is the RAPID, protocolized rule-out that averts unnecessary admission -- not reperfusion.
initial_management: If ACS not yet excluded: aspirin 162-325 mg chewed (then 75-100 mg daily). Anti-ischemic: sublingual nitroglycerin 0.4 mg q5min x3 PRN; beta-blocker (metoprolol) or CCB (amlodipine/diltiazem) for angina; add long-acting nitrate (isosorbide) or ranolazine. High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40-80 mg or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg), LDL goal <70 (consider <55) mg/dL; add ezetimibe/PCSK9i if not at goal. ACE-I/ARB if HTN, diabetes, CKD, or LVEF <=40%. SGLT2i and/or GLP-1 RA for CCD with diabetes or high CV risk. Colchicine 0.5 mg daily optional (Class 2b) to lower MACE. Parenteral anticoagulation (heparin/enoxaparin) ONLY if ACS confirmed -- not for non-cardiac chest pain. Non-cardiac: PPI trial (GERD), treat MSK/anxiety. No specific transfusion trigger; if anemia is contributory use a restrictive Hgb 7-8 g/dL strategy.
day1_milestones: Day 1 (ED/Observation): ECG <10 min and first hs-troponin resulted; HEART/EDACS computed and risk tier assigned. Complete serial troponins (0/1h, 0/2h, or 0/3h). Telemetry; reassess and quantify pain. Start ASA + high-intensity statin + antianginal as indicated. Choose anatomic vs functional test and order CCTA or stress. Document shared decision-making. Low-risk (HEART Pathway negative) -> discharge same day with secured follow-up.
day2_milestones: Day 2: Complete provocative/anatomic testing (CCTA or stress). If negative/low-risk -> discharge with outpatient follow-up (majority out by now). If positive -> cardiology consult, plan invasive coronary angiography, intensify GDMT (statin, antianginals, risk-factor meds). Begin medication reconciliation and discharge teaching; confirm follow-up appointments.
day3plus_milestones: Day 3+ (minority only): reserved for angiography/PCI, refractory angina requiring titration, or complications. Post-cath: monitor access site and renal function post-contrast, resume/optimize GDMT. Revascularization vs continued optimal medical therapy decided per ISCHEMIA (stable CAD does not mandate PCI). Cardiac rehab referral; secondary-prevention regimen finalized. Discharge once chest-pain-free, hemodynamically stable, and with a definitive plan.
consults_multidisciplinary: Cardiology for positive testing, high-risk features, or revascularization decisions; interventional cardiology if angiography/PCI. Clinical pharmacy for GDMT optimization and medication reconciliation. Care management/social work for follow-up scheduling, transportation, and medication access. Cardiac rehabilitation referral for confirmed CAD (Class 1). Nutrition counseling and tobacco-cessation services. Behavioral health if panic/anxiety drives non-cardiac chest pain. PT/OT only if deconditioned -- rarely needed in this population.
monitoring_level: Telemetry/observation unit for rule-out ACS (most patients). General cardiac floor if admitted and stable. Step-down/ICU reserved for hemodynamic instability, ongoing/refractory ischemia, significant arrhythmia, or a post-PCI complication -- uncommon in this DRG group. Escalation triggers: rising hs-troponin, recurrent or refractory angina, new ischemic ECG changes, sustained arrhythmia, hypotension, or acute HF.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Heart-healthy diet (Mediterranean/DASH, sodium-restricted); no NPO unless a procedure is pending. Early ambulation, minimal activity restriction. VTE prophylaxis by risk (Padua/Caprini) -- pharmacologic (enoxaparin 40 mg or UFH) if admitted with low bleed risk; many short-stay observation patients need only ambulation. Inpatient glucose target 140-180 mg/dL. Remove IV access and discontinue telemetry promptly once ACS is excluded to enable discharge.
discharge_criteria: ACS excluded (serial hs-troponin negative, no dynamic ECG change). Chest pain resolved or attributed to a defined non-cardiac cause. Negative/low-risk anatomic or functional test, OR low-risk with outpatient testing arranged and a safety-net in place. Hemodynamically stable, no active arrhythmia. GDMT initiated and follow-up secured.
discharge_planning: Disposition home for the majority. Full medication reconciliation ensuring aspirin, high-intensity statin, and antianginals as indicated. Follow-up: PCP within 1-2 weeks; cardiology within 1-2 weeks for confirmed/likely CAD or for deferred outpatient stress testing. Patient education: return precautions, sublingual nitroglycerin use, risk-factor modification, symptom diary. Cardiac rehab referral if CAD confirmed. Explicitly communicate pending results and the outpatient testing plan (closed-loop) to prevent bounce-back.
expected_los: Approx GMLOS by DRG: chest pain 313 ~1.4 d; angina 311 ~1.7 d; atherosclerosis 303 ~1.6 d / 302 (w MCC) ~2.6 d; circulatory disorders w cardiac cath 287 ~2.2 d / 286 (w MCC) ~4.4 d. Blended typical 1.5-2.5 days, up to ~4-4.5 days with cath plus MCC.
target_los: ~1 day (often <24h observation) for chest-pain/angina/atherosclerosis rule-out via the accelerated HEART Pathway; ~1-2 days when cardiac catheterization is performed. Best-practice target ~1 day, with a large share converted to observation status rather than inpatient.
quality_metrics: Median time to ECG <=10 min (legacy CMS OP-5); aspirin at arrival when ACS suspected (legacy OP-4). Low-value-care measures: inpatient admission rate and advanced cardiac imaging/stress rate for LOW-risk chest pain (Choosing Wisely -- keep low). Observation-status appropriateness. 30-day all-cause readmission and ED revisit rate. Confirmed CAD: statin prescribed at discharge, LDL <70 mg/dL attainment, BP control, tobacco-cessation counseling, cardiac rehab referral (performance measure). Cardiac cath appropriate-use criteria. NCDR Chest Pain-MI Registry / ACC Chest Pain Center accreditation metrics.
common_complications: Missed ACS/MI -- the feared miss; mitigated by serial hs-troponin, a validated accelerated pathway, and closed-loop outpatient follow-up. Contrast-associated nephropathy from CCTA/angiography -- mitigate with hydration, minimized contrast volume, renal-function check. Access-site bleeding/hematoma post-cath -- radial-first access. Overtesting harms: false-positive stress -> downstream invasive testing and complications -- mitigate with proper risk stratification (test only intermediate/high-risk). Arrhythmia. Anchoring that misses PE or aortic dissection -- maintain the differential.
readmission_drivers: Recurrent chest pain (frequently non-cardiac) driving repeat ED visits -- mitigate with a definitive diagnosis, PPI trial for GERD, behavioral-health referral for panic, and clear return precautions. Inadequate GDMT / nonadherence -- reconciliation, affordability check, teach-back. Missed obstructive CAD -- ensure outpatient testing is actually completed (safety-net). Poor follow-up access -- schedule PCP/cardiology before discharge. Uncontrolled risk factors (HTN, diabetes, tobacco, LDL).
guideline_source: PRIMARY: 2021 AHA/ACC/ASE/CHEST/SAEM/SCCT/SCMR Guideline for the Evaluation and Diagnosis of Chest Pain (Gulati et al., Circulation 2021) + 2023 AHA/ACC/ACCP/ASPC/NLA/PCNA Guideline for the Management of Patients With Chronic Coronary Disease (Virani et al., Circulation 2023). COMPANION for the rule-in/ACS off-ramp: 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes (Rao et al., Circulation 2025). Sourced via PubMed.

evidence_grade: Strong -- anchored by current Class I ACC/AHA guideline recommendations (2021 chest pain, 2023 CCD) and supported by RCT evidence (HEART Pathway RCT; ISCHEMIA) with well-validated risk scores (HEART, EDACS, GRACE). Operational LOS and observation-conversion targets are Moderate (consensus/quality-improvement extrapolation).
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed group spans very-low-acuity non-specific chest pain (313, GMLOS ~1.4 d, floor/observation, complications rare) to circulatory disorders with cardiac cath + MCC (286, GMLOS ~4.4 d). An MCC (e.g., acute kidney injury, acute HF, major arrhythmia, diabetes with complication) drives step-down/ICU need, contrast-nephropathy risk with cath, and longer LOS, and raises DRG weight. With-cath DRGs (286/287) imply an invasive procedure and post-procedure monitoring -> longer stay. No-CC non-specific chest pain should ideally be observation, not inpatient -- status integrity is the main severity/cost lever here.
pathway_pearls: 1) ECG <10 min + serial hs-troponin under a validated accelerated pathway (HEART Pathway/EDACS with 0/1h or 0/3h troponin) to safely discharge low-risk from ED/observation -- the single biggest LOS and cost lever; avoid reflexive inpatient admission. 2) Right-size testing: CCTA first-line for intermediate-risk without known CAD; do NOT order stress/advanced imaging in low-risk. 3) ISCHEMIA-informed restraint: stable CAD does not mandate revascularization -- optimize GDMT first; resist the oculostenotic reflex. 4) Status integrity + a closed-loop outpatient safety-net (follow-up scheduled, pending results communicated) to prevent bounce-backs.
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: HCUP #17 principal dx (coronary atherosclerosis, 0.36M, 2018). Includes angina, non-specific chest pain (313), and circulatory disorders with cardiac cath without AMI (286-287). Chest-pain subset is ED-observation heavy.

data_quality_notes: Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk.

admission_criteria: Diagnosis requires >=2 of 3 (Revised Atlanta 2012): (1) epigastric pain radiating to back consistent with AP; (2) lipase (preferred) or amylase >3x ULN; (3) characteristic cross-sectional imaging. Admit inpatient if: cannot maintain oral hydration/nutrition; pain needing parenteral opioids; SIRS >=2 or any organ dysfunction; predicted severe (BISAP >=3, APACHE II >=8); rising/elevated BUN (>20 mg/dL) or hematocrit (>44%); significant comorbidity (age >60, CHF, cirrhosis, immunosuppression); hypertriglyceridemic (TG >1000) or biliary AP with obstruction/cholangitis. Route predicted-severe/organ-failure cases to ICU or step-down. ED discharge/observation reasonable only for HAPS=0 (no rebound/guarding, normal hematocrit, normal creatinine) mild interstitial AP tolerating PO with reliable follow-up.
risk_stratification: Revised Atlanta 2012 severity: mild (no organ failure, no local/systemic complication), moderately severe (transient organ failure <48h and/or local complication), severe (persistent organ failure >48h) - drives disposition/LOS. Modified Marshall score: persistent (>48h) score >=2 in respiratory/renal/cardiovascular = severe -> ICU. BISAP (BUN >25, Impaired mentation, SIRS >=2, Age >60, Pleural effusion): >=3 -> higher mortality, ICU/step-down. APACHE II >=8 = severe. Ranson >=3 at 48h and Glasgow-Imrie >=3 = severe. Persistent SIRS >=2 at 24-48h and CRP >150 mg/L at 48h predict severe course. Rising BUN and hemoconcentration (Hct >44%) drive fluid escalation. CT Severity Index / modified CTSI grades necrosis burden. HAPS=0 identifies benign course suitable for early feeding/discharge.
initial_workup_labs: Lipase (>3x ULN diagnostic; more sensitive/specific than amylase - do not order both routinely); CBC with differential (WBC; hematocrit as hemoconcentration/prognostic marker); CMP (BUN and creatinine prognostic and drive resuscitation; glucose; calcium - hypocalcemia = severity); LFTs (ALT >150 U/L or >3x ULN strongly suggests gallstone etiology -> RUQ ultrasound); fasting triglycerides (>1000 mg/dL = hypertriglyceridemic AP -> insulin +/- apheresis pathway); CRP at 48h (>150 mg/L = severe); lactate; magnesium/phosphate/ionized calcium; LDH; venous blood gas if unstable. If idiopathic/recurrent: IgG4 (autoimmune), calcium/PTH, genetic testing consideration. Serial BUN/Hct every 6-12h during resuscitation to gauge response.
initial_imaging_dx: Transabdominal ultrasound within 24h in ALL patients to assess gallstones/biliary sludge/CBD dilation (identifies most common etiology). Contrast-enhanced CT (CECT) NOT routine at presentation; reserve for diagnostic uncertainty or clinical deterioration/failure to improve at 48-72h (pancreatic necrosis best demonstrated at 72-96h). MRI/MRCP for suspected retained choledocholithiasis, idiopathic AP, or when CT contrast contraindicated. EUS after recovery for occult microlithiasis/idiopathic recurrent AP. CXR to detect pleural effusion (severity marker). Avoid early/repeat CT that does not change management.
time_critical_interventions: First 6-12h is the critical window: initiate goal-directed moderate lactated Ringer's resuscitation immediately and REASSESS volume status/BUN/urine output at 6h (moderately aggressive hydration matters most in first 6-12h; benefit wanes after 24-48h). Urgent ERCP within 24h ONLY for AP complicated by acute cholangitis or persistent biliary obstruction/cholestasis (NOT routine in predicted-severe biliary AP without cholangitis - APEC trial negative). Start oral/enteral feeding within 24-48h (avoid prolonged NPO). Hypertriglyceridemic AP (TG >1000): start insulin infusion +/- plasmapheresis early. No time-critical antibiotic bundle - antibiotics only for proven/strongly suspected infection.
initial_management: FLUIDS: lactated Ringer's preferred over normal saline (less SIRS/hyperchloremic acidosis); MODERATE goal-directed resuscitation per WATERFALL - 10 mL/kg bolus only if hypovolemic (none if euvolemic) then 1.5 mL/kg/h, titrated to HR<120, MAP 65-85, UOP >0.5-1 mL/kg/h, falling BUN/Hct; AVOID aggressive 20 mL/kg bolus + 3 mL/kg/h (more fluid overload, no benefit). ANALGESIA: multimodal - IV opioids (hydromorphone, morphine, or fentanyl), scheduled acetaminophen, PCA for severe pain; antiemetics (ondansetron). NO prophylactic antibiotics for sterile necrosis/predicted-severe disease. ETIOLOGY-SPECIFIC: gallstone -> same-admission cholecystectomy (mild) or ERCP if cholangitis; hypertriglyceridemia -> insulin infusion, apheresis if severe/organ failure, later fibrate/omega-3; alcohol -> CIWA-guided withdrawal management, thiamine before glucose, folate/multivitamin; hypercalcemia -> treat cause. Repuce electrolytes (Ca, Mg, K, phosphate).
day1_milestones: Confirm diagnosis and etiology (US result, TG, ALT/LFTs); risk-stratify (BISAP, SIRS count, Marshall, Atlanta class); start moderate LR with mandatory 6h reassessment of BUN/UOP/vitals; achieve pain and nausea control; determine severity trajectory and set correct level of care (floor vs step-down/ICU); begin early low-fat oral feeding if mild and pain improving; initiate VTE pharmacoprophylaxis; start alcohol-withdrawal prophylaxis + thiamine if alcoholic etiology; dietitian and (for biliary AP) surgery notified.
day2_milestones: Reassess severity at 48h (CRP >150, persistent SIRS or organ failure -> reclassify moderately severe/severe); advance to solid low-fat diet as tolerated; de-escalate/taper IV fluids after 24-48h to avoid overload; general/HPB surgery consult and cholecystectomy planning for gallstone AP; MRCP/EUS if suspected retained CBD stone; if clinically deteriorating or not improving, obtain CECT to evaluate necrosis/local complications; transition opioids toward oral analgesia; verify VTE prophylaxis and glucose control.
day3plus_milestones: Discharge-readiness track for mild AP: tolerating oral diet, pain controlled on oral meds, resolving SIRS -> same-admission laparoscopic cholecystectomy BEFORE discharge for mild biliary AP (PONCHO). For moderately severe/severe/necrotizing: continue enteral nutrition, treat complications, DELAY any necrosis intervention 4-6 weeks until walled-off (PANTER/POINTER), reserve step-up drainage (percutaneous/endoscopic then minimally invasive necrosectomy) for infected/symptomatic necrosis - avoid early open necrosectomy. Address etiology definitively (lipid clinic, alcohol counseling/rehab referral); monitor for new exocrine/endocrine insufficiency; finalize discharge plan.
consults_multidisciplinary: Gastroenterology (all; ERCP/EUS and etiology workup); General/HPB surgery (biliary AP for same-admission cholecystectomy; necrosis for step-up); advanced endoscopy + interventional radiology (necrosis drainage); Critical care (predicted-severe/organ failure) early; Dietitian/nutrition (early, feeding plan - within 24-48h); Clinical pharmacy (fluids, analgesia, insulin, stewardship); Addiction medicine + social work (alcohol etiology); Endocrinology (hypertriglyceridemia, new diabetes); Case management/discharge planning; PT/OT for deconditioned or ICU/prolonged-stay patients.
monitoring_level: ICU or step-down for: persistent organ failure (Marshall >=2 any system >48h), BISAP >=3 or APACHE II >=8, hemodynamic instability, hypoxia/respiratory compromise, large-volume resuscitation needs, or hypertriglyceridemic AP on insulin infusion/apheresis. Telemetry/medical floor for mild-to-moderately severe hemodynamically stable AP. Escalation triggers: rising BUN/Hct despite adequate fluids, new/worsening organ dysfunction, hypoxia (SpO2 <92% / rising O2 need), oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/h), persistent/worsening SIRS, intra-abdominal hypertension or abdominal compartment syndrome (bladder pressure >20 mmHg with new organ failure), lactate elevation.
nutrition_mobility_vte: NUTRITION: early oral low-fat solid diet within 24-48h in mild AP (no need for clear-liquid-first ladder or normalized lipase/pain-free prerequisite); if unable to eat or severe, start enteral nutrition within 48-72h - nasogastric equals nasojejunal (PYTHON), continuous preferred over bolus, AVOID parenteral nutrition unless enteral fails/contraindicated. MOBILITY: early ambulation. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin or UFH) - AP is prothrombotic with splanchnic (splenic/portal/mesenteric) vein thrombosis risk; mechanical prophylaxis if active bleeding. GLUCOSE: target <180 mg/dL, insulin as needed (watch necrosis-related diabetes). LINES: remove central lines/Foley/unnecessary catheters as soon as feasible to cut infection risk.
discharge_criteria: Tolerating oral diet without significant pain or nausea; pain controlled on oral analgesics; resolved/resolving SIRS with normalized or clearly improving organ function; hemodynamically stable off IV fluid resuscitation; etiology addressed or definitive plan in place (cholecystectomy performed or ERCP with sphincterotomy done for those unfit for surgery; TG-lowering plan; alcohol-cessation plan); no unaddressed local complication (e.g., infected necrosis, symptomatic pseudocyst) requiring continued inpatient care.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home (SNF/rehab if deconditioned post-severe course). Medication reconciliation: stop unnecessary meds, start fibrate/omega-3 for hypertriglyceridemia, ensure abstinence support for alcohol; if cholecystectomy deferred, schedule within 2-4 weeks and counsel on recurrence risk (or document ERCP+sphincterotomy as bridge). Follow-up: GI in 2-4 weeks, surgery post-op, PCP; arrange outpatient MRCP/EUS for idiopathic AP. Patient education: alcohol and tobacco cessation, low-fat diet, warning signs of recurrence/cholangitis. Transitions: communicate pending results, monitor for exocrine (steatorrhea -> enzyme replacement) and endocrine (diabetes) insufficiency after necrotizing AP.
expected_los: CMS GMLOS benchmarks (Disorders of pancreas except malignancy): MS-DRG 440 no CC/MCC ~2.7-2.9 d; MS-DRG 439 w/CC ~3.5-3.8 d; MS-DRG 438 w/MCC ~5.0-5.4 d. Mild interstitial AP typically 3-4 days; moderately severe 5-7 days; severe/necrotizing frequently 2-4+ weeks.
target_los: Uncomplicated mild AP: 2-3 days under early-feeding + same-admission cholecystectomy pathway (target ~3 d). Moderately severe AP: 4-6 days. Severe/necrotizing: individualized, often weeks - not compressible without compromising safety.
quality_metrics: No CMS condition-specific core measure exists; track institutional/registry process + outcome measures: early oral/enteral feeding within 48h; lactated Ringer's use; avoidance of prophylactic antibiotics (antibiotic stewardship); same-admission cholecystectomy rate for mild biliary AP; appropriate (guideline-concordant) ERCP utilization; VTE prophylaxis compliance; time-to-fluid and 6h reassessment documentation; in-hospital mortality; ICU utilization/transfer rate; 30-day all-cause readmission; LOS index vs GMLOS; cholecystectomy outcomes via ACS NSQIP.
common_complications: LOCAL: acute peripancreatic/pancreatic fluid collections, acute necrotic collection, walled-off necrosis, infected necrosis, pseudocyst, splanchnic (splenic/portal/mesenteric) vein thrombosis, pseudoaneurysm/GI bleeding. SYSTEMIC: persistent organ failure (ARDS/respiratory, AKI, cardiovascular shock), refractory SIRS, intra-abdominal hypertension/abdominal compartment syndrome, hyperglycemia, hypocalcemia. MITIGATION: goal-directed moderate fluids (avoid both under- and over-resuscitation), early enteral nutrition to preserve gut-barrier integrity and reduce infected necrosis, no prophylactic antibiotics, VTE prophylaxis for splanchnic thrombosis, and delayed step-up (not early open) intervention for necrosis.
readmission_drivers: Recurrent biliary AP or gallstone complications when cholecystectomy is deferred (17% vs 5% with same-admission surgery - PONCHO); ongoing alcohol use; enlarging/symptomatic pseudocyst or evolving/infected necrosis; inadequate pain control or premature discharge with diet intolerance; uncontrolled hypertriglyceridemia; new pancreatic exocrine/endocrine insufficiency. PREVENTION LEVERS: same-admission cholecystectomy (or ERCP+sphincterotomy if unfit), structured alcohol-cessation referral, aggressive lipid control, confirmed diet tolerance before discharge, and early (2-4 week) GI follow-up with clear return precautions.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: American College of Gastroenterology (ACG) Guidelines: Management of Acute Pancreatitis, 2024 (Tenner et al., Am J Gastroenterol). Supporting: AGA Institute Guideline on Initial Management of Acute Pancreatitis, 2018; Revised Atlanta Classification, 2012 (severity definitions); IAP/APA evidence-based guidelines, 2013; WSES guidelines for severe acute pancreatitis, 2019.

evidence_grade: Strong - anchored to a current Class I ACG 2024 society guideline reinforced by multiple high-quality RCTs (WATERFALL fluids, PONCHO cholecystectomy timing, APEC ERCP, PANTER/POINTER necrosis intervention, PYTHON feeding route). Select recommendations (exact feeding timing, NG vs NJ, LR vs NS) are conditional/moderate-quality; overall body of evidence is strong and internally consistent.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed DRG group spans the full Atlanta severity spectrum. MS-DRG 440 (no CC/MCC) = mild interstitial AP: floor-level, early feeding, same-admission cholecystectomy, LOS ~2-3 d, mortality <1-3%. MS-DRG 439 (w/CC) = moderately severe (transient organ failure, local collections, or complicating condition such as AKI/dehydration/electrolyte derangement): may need step-down, LOS ~3.5-4 d. MS-DRG 438 (w/MCC) = severe/necrotizing with persistent organ failure, sepsis, or ARDS: ICU-driven, enteral nutrition, step-up interventions, LOS ~5-10+ d (necrosis weeks), mortality up to 20-30% with infected necrosis. Persistent SIRS/organ failure and (peri)pancreatic necrosis are the principal escalators of ICU need, LOS, and complications.
pathway_pearls: 1) Right-size the fluids: moderate goal-directed lactated Ringer's (10 mL/kg bolus only if hypovolemic then 1.5 mL/kg/h) with a mandatory 6h BUN/UOP reassessment - WATERFALL showed aggressive fluids caused more fluid overload (20.5% vs 6.3%) with no benefit. 2) Feed early: low-fat oral diet within 24-48h in mild AP; enteral over parenteral; NG equals NJ - preserves gut barrier and lowers infected necrosis. 3) Do NOT give prophylactic antibiotics for sterile/predicted-severe necrosis; reserve for cholangitis or proven infected necrosis. 4) Same-admission laparoscopic cholecystectomy for mild gallstone AP (PONCHO: recurrent gallstone complications cut from 17% to 5%); urgent ERCP only for cholangitis/persistent obstruction, not routine. 5) For necrosis, delay and step up: wait 4-6 weeks for walled-off necrosis and use drainage-first step-up rather than early open necrosectomy (PANTER/POINTER).

admission_criteria: Confirmed acute appendicitis (clinical + imaging) is an indication for urgent appendectomy = surgical admission; uncomplicated appendicitis treated by laparoscopic appendectomy is typically ambulatory/23h short-stay (observation), NOT a true inpatient stay. Reserve inpatient (>=2 midnights) admission for: complicated/perforated appendicitis (abscess, phlegmon, generalized peritonitis, free perforation on CT); sepsis/septic shock (qSOFA>=2, lactate>=2); ASA>=3 / significant comorbidity, immunocompromise, or age>65; pregnancy; failed non-operative management; persistent ileus or inability to tolerate PO; need for percutaneous drainage. Antibiotics-first ED discharge is an option only for select uncomplicated cases WITHOUT appendicolith with reliable follow-up.
risk_stratification: Adult diagnostic scores (WSES-endorsed): Alvarado <5 sufficiently sensitive to rule OUT appendicitis (discharge/observe, not for positive dx); AIR score 0-4 low (outpatient follow-up), 5-8 intermediate (active observation + imaging), 9-12 high (surgical consult; sens 92%/spec 63%, best performer); Adult Appendicitis Score (AAS) <11 low-probability, 11-15 intermediate, >=16 high (93% specificity). Pediatric Appendicitis Score (PAS) <=3 low (discharge), 4-6 equivocal (observe/US), >=7 high (surgery). Complicated-vs-uncomplicated predictors: symptom duration >48h, age>65, elevated CRP, WBC>15, appendicolith, appendiceal diameter >10mm, extraluminal gas/fluid on CT. Sepsis overlay: SIRS or qSOFA>=2 / lactate>=2 triggers sepsis bundle.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential (leukocytosis/left shift supports dx; normal WBC lowers probability); CRP (rising CRP + WBC raises complicated-disease risk); BMP/electrolytes (dehydration, AKI); urinalysis (exclude UTI/nephrolithiasis; sterile pyuria can occur); beta-hCG in ALL women of childbearing age (mandatory before imaging/OR); lactate + blood cultures if septic; type & screen and coagulation (INR/PTT) pre-op; lipase to exclude pancreatitis when atypical. Abnormal results trigger IV fluid resuscitation, sepsis bundle, or broadened differential.
initial_imaging_dx: POCUS/graded-compression ultrasound = first-line in adults and children (sens 0.78-0.88, spec 0.81-0.94; WSES strong rec); conditional strategy: contrast-enhanced LOW-DOSE CT abdomen/pelvis only if US negative/equivocal (sens 0.95, spec 0.94) - cuts CT use ~50%. Pregnancy: graded-compression US first, MRI (no gadolinium) if inconclusive - avoid CT. Children: US first, MRI/CT only if inconclusive to limit radiation. Obtain imaging expeditiously in ED (<4-6h) to enable source control within 24h; report appendicolith, diameter, perforation, and abscess.
time_critical_interventions: Source control is URGENT, not emergent: appendectomy safe within 24h of admission for uncomplicated disease (in-hospital delay 7-24h does NOT increase perforation/SSI); avoid >24-48h (LOS 2.1d if <24h vs 3.4d at 24-48h vs 5.4d if >48h). Preoperative antibiotics within 60 min of incision. If septic shock/generalized peritonitis: Surviving Sepsis 1-hour bundle - broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1h, lactate, blood cultures, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate>=4, then expedited OR or percutaneous drainage for source control (ideally <6h).
initial_management: IV balanced crystalloid (LR) resuscitation; NPO; multimodal analgesia (IV acetaminophen + opioid PRN); antiemetics. Single-dose preoperative broad-spectrum prophylaxis covering gram-negatives + anaerobes: cefoxitin 2g OR cefazolin 2g + metronidazole 500mg OR piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375g (ertapenem if beta-lactam allergy). SOURCE CONTROL = laparoscopic appendectomy (preferred over open: SSI OR 0.30, faster diet, shorter LOS). Antibiotics-first (non-operative) is an option for uncomplicated WITHOUT appendicolith via shared decision. Complicated with abscess/phlegmon: IV antibiotics + image-guided percutaneous drainage of abscess >3-4cm. Transfuse only for symptomatic anemia (restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL).
day1_milestones: POD0: laparoscopic appendectomy with intra-operative grading (AAST/WSES severity); single-dose prophylaxis ONLY, no continuation if uncomplicated; start ERAS - clear liquids advancing to regular diet as tolerated same day; ambulate within hours of PACU recovery; opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia (scheduled acetaminophen + NSAID/ketorolac, opioids PRN); remove urinary catheter; PONV prophylaxis and antiemetics. Uncomplicated cases: target same-day or 23h discharge.
day2_milestones: POD1 (chiefly complicated cases; most uncomplicated already discharged): afebrile trend, pain controlled on oral analgesia, tolerating diet, passing flatus, ambulating independently, voiding; continue IV antibiotics ONLY if complicated/perforated (day 1 of a 3-5 day course); evaluate for organ-space infection if persistent fever/leukocytosis; convert IV-to-oral antibiotics once tolerating PO; wound check and line/catheter removal.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ (complicated/perforated): complete 3-5 day antibiotic course (STOP-IT: fixed 4-day course sufficient with adequate source control), finishing orally (amoxicillin-clavulanate, or metronidazole + fluoroquinolone/cephalosporin); resolution of ileus, tolerating regular diet, afebrile >=24h, downtrending WBC; if persistent sepsis/collection -> CT + percutaneous drainage; confirm no undrained organ-space abscess before discharge; plan interval appendectomy for abscess/phlegmon managed non-operatively (especially >40y, given ~20% neoplasm yield) with colonic evaluation.
consults_multidisciplinary: General/acute-care/pediatric surgery = primary (immediate). Interventional radiology for percutaneous abscess drainage. Anesthesia/pre-op evaluation. Pharmacy for antimicrobial stewardship (selection, de-escalation, 3-5d duration). Care/case management early for complicated cases and disposition. Nutrition if prolonged ileus/complicated course. OB for pregnant patients; Infectious Disease for resistant organisms/immunocompromise. Gastroenterology/colonoscopy referral after interval management in patients >40y (rule out malignancy). PT/OT rarely required (typically young, ambulatory patients).
monitoring_level: Floor/med-surg or ambulatory short-stay for the vast majority (uncomplicated laparoscopic appendectomy). Telemetry only for cardiac comorbidity. Step-down/ICU reserved for perforated appendicitis with sepsis/septic shock, hemodynamic instability, respiratory compromise, or major comorbidity. Escalation triggers: qSOFA>=2, rising lactate>=2, hypotension, oliguria/AKI, tachypnea, worsening peritonitis, or vasopressor need -> upgrade to ICU with sepsis bundle.
nutrition_mobility_vte: ERAS: no routine NG tube; clear liquids POD0 advancing to regular diet as tolerated (early feeding safe even in complicated disease). Early mobilization POD0. VTE: low-moderate risk - Caprini/Padua-based; mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs) + early ambulation for uncomplicated; add pharmacologic LMWH (enoxaparin 40mg daily) or heparin for complicated/perforated, prolonged immobility, malignancy, or high Caprini score. Glucose control target <180 mg/dL. Remove urinary catheter POD0-1; early IV-to-PO conversion and saline-lock/remove lines promptly. Opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia throughout.
discharge_criteria: Tolerating oral diet and oral analgesia; pain controlled; afebrile (or clear downtrend; afebrile >=24h if complicated); return of bowel function (flatus); ambulating and voiding independently; stable vitals with no SIRS; no evidence of undrained collection; for complicated cases an oral antibiotic plan established to complete 3-5 days; wound intact. Uncomplicated laparoscopic patients frequently meet criteria same day / POD0-1.
discharge_planning: Disposition home in nearly all cases. Medication reconciliation (short opioid script + bowel regimen; oral antibiotics ONLY for complicated to complete 3-5 days). Surgical follow-up at 1-2 weeks; wound-care and warning-sign education (fever, worsening/persistent pain, redness/discharge, intractable nausea) with explicit return precautions. Antibiotics-first patients: counsel ~27-39% recurrence within 1-5 years and provide safety-net follow-up. Phlegmon/abscess: schedule interval evaluation and colonoscopy/imaging if >40y. Provide a 24-48h nurse call-back for complicated discharges to reduce bounce-backs.
expected_los: Benchmark GMLOS by legacy DRG (MS-DRG 338-343, valid through FY2023): 343 uncomplicated dx w/o CC/MCC ~1.6-1.8 days; 342 uncomplicated w/CC ~2.3-2.5 days; 341 uncomplicated w/MCC ~4 days; 340 complicated dx w/o CC/MCC ~2.5 days; 339 complicated dx w/CC ~3.7-3.9 days; 338 complicated dx w/MCC ~5.5-5.8 days. Overall blended ~2-3 days.
target_los: Uncomplicated laparoscopic appendectomy: same-day or <=23h (0-1 day) under an ERAS/ambulatory pathway. Complicated/perforated without abscess: 2-4 days. Perforated with abscess requiring drainage: 4-6 days. Best practice beats GMLOS by ~0.5-1 day via early feeding, opioid-sparing analgesia, and eliminating unnecessary postoperative antibiotics in uncomplicated disease.
quality_metrics: Antibiotic prophylaxis timing within 60 min and appropriate selection/discontinuation <24h in uncomplicated (legacy SCIP-Inf); NHSN/NSQIP surgical-site and organ-space infection rate; negative appendectomy rate (target <5-10%, lowered by scores + conditional imaging); unplanned reoperation and 30-day readmission; laparoscopic completion rate; time-to-OR/source control within 24h; ERAS protocol compliance; perioperative normothermia; VTE prophylaxis (VTE-6); appropriate 3-5 day antibiotic duration in complicated disease (stewardship); NSQIP-Peds metrics in children; opioid-stewardship / patient-reported pain.
common_complications: Surgical-site infection and organ-space/intra-abdominal abscess (highest after perforation; mitigate via source control, laparoscopy [SSI OR 0.30], and adequate 3-5d antibiotics); postoperative ileus (mitigate with early feeding and opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia); appendiceal stump leak/stump appendicitis; retained appendicolith; wound infection; dehydration from prolonged ileus; rare VTE; negative appendectomy (mitigate with validated scores + conditional US/CT); missed appendiceal/colonic neoplasm in older adults (colonic evaluation after interval management).
readmission_drivers: Intra-abdominal/organ-space abscess after perforated appendicitis (top driver); SSI; postoperative ileus, nausea, dehydration, and inadequate pain control; non-operative management failure/recurrence (~27-39% at 1-5 years, higher with appendicolith - CODA appendectomy rate 41% with vs 25% without appendicolith). Prevention levers: complete 3-5d antibiotics for complicated disease with adequate source control; low threshold for CT + percutaneous drainage of collections; multimodal analgesia with bowel regimen; structured wound care and warning-sign education; early surgical follow-up plus 24-48h nurse call-back; appropriate selection for antibiotics-first (avoid if appendicolith).
guideline_source: PRIMARY: WSES (World Society of Emergency Surgery) Jerusalem Guidelines - Diagnosis and treatment of acute appendicitis: 2020 update (Di Saverio et al., World Journal of Emergency Surgery, 2020). Supporting: EAES consensus; ACS/Surgical Infection Society surgical-infection and antimicrobial-prophylaxis standards; ERAS principles; APSA (pediatric). Evidence anchored by CODA (NEJM 2020), APPAC 5-year (JAMA 2018), and STOP-IT (NEJM 2015) randomized trials.
key_citations: Di Saverio S, et al. Diagnosis and treatment of acute appendicitis: 2020 update of the WSES Jerusalem guidelines. World J Emerg Surg. 2020;15:27 (PMID 32295644; DOI 10.1186/s13017-020-00306-3). CODA Collaborative (Flum DR, et al). A Randomized Trial Comparing Antibiotics with Appendectomy for Appendicitis. N Engl J Med. 2020;383:1907-1919 (PMID 33017106; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2014320). Salminen P, et al. Five-Year Follow-up of Antibiotic Therapy for Uncomplicated Acute Appendicitis (APPAC RCT). JAMA. 2018;320:1259-1265 (PMID 30264120; DOI 10.1001/jama.2018.13201). Podda M, et al. Diagnosis of acute appendicitis based on clinical scores. Acta Biomed. 2021;92:e2021231 (PMID 34487066). Sourced via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong - anchored by a GRADE-based international society guideline (WSES 2020) plus multiple high-quality RCTs (CODA NEJM 2020, APPAC/APPAC 5-yr JAMA, STOP-IT NEJM 2015). Diagnostic scoring and conditional imaging = strong; laparoscopic approach and prophylaxis timing = strong; antibiotics-first for uncomplicated = moderate (non-inferior for complications but ~27-39% recurrence, worse with appendicolith); ERAS/same-day discharge = moderate.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Severity is driven by complicated (perforated/abscess/peritonitis) vs uncomplicated principal diagnosis AND the CC/MCC overlay. Uncomplicated w/o CC/MCC (343): ambulatory/23h, ~1.6-1.8d, minimal antibiotics, floor care. Adding CC (342) or MCC (341; e.g., sepsis, AKI, aspiration) escalates monitoring, extends LOS (~2.5 to ~4d), and may require ICU. Complicated principal dx (338-340) adds organ-space infection risk, percutaneous drainage, 3-5d antibiotics, and interval-appendectomy planning; with MCC (338) LOS ~5.5-5.8d with ICU/sepsis-bundle care. NOTE: legacy DRGs 338-343 were restructured under FY2024 IPPS (effective 10/1/2023), but the severity logic (complicated dx +/- CC/MCC) persists.
pathway_pearls: 1) Diagnose with a validated score (AIR/AAS/PAS) + conditional US-then-low-dose-CT to slash negative appendectomies and radiation (US first, MRI in pregnancy). 2) Source control is URGENT not emergent - laparoscopic appendectomy within 24h is safe and shortens LOS; don't over-image or over-delay. 3) Antibiotic discipline: single preop dose within 60 min; NONE postoperatively for uncomplicated; only 3-5 days for complicated with adequate source control (STOP-IT). 4) Run an ERAS ambulatory pathway (early feeding, opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia, same-day/23h discharge) for uncomplicated disease; reserve inpatient beds and percutaneous drainage for perforated/abscess, and counsel appendicolith patients against antibiotics-first.

scope_and_volume_notes: 19th most frequent OR procedure (HCUP: 0.19M, 2018). With (338-340) and without (341-343) complicated principal diagnosis; mostly acute appendicitis, ED entry.

admission_criteria: All major small/large bowel resections are inpatient status (inherently invasive, >2-midnight expectation) — elective for colorectal cancer, complicated/recurrent diverticulitis, IBD, refractory obstruction, or symptomatic mass. Emergent inpatient indications: complete/high-grade mechanical obstruction (SBO/LBO) failing 48-72h nonoperative trial or with closed-loop/strangulation; perforation with free air or peritonitis; complicated diverticulitis Hinchey III (purulent) / IV (feculent); acute mesenteric ischemia; obstructing or perforating colorectal cancer; toxic megacolon; lower-GI bleed requiring resection. Observation/ED-discharge instead: uncomplicated diverticulitis Hinchey 0/Ia on oral antibiotics; low-grade partial adhesive SBO on a monitored trial without ischemia.
risk_stratification: ACS-NSQIP Surgical Risk Calculator (predicted 30d morbidity/mortality) drives ICU vs floor + prehab; ASA class I-IV; RCRI for cardiac risk. Colorectal-specific: CR-POSSUM / P-POSSUM morbidity-mortality. Emergency laparotomy: NELA risk score — predicted mortality >5% mandates consultant-led care, >10% triggers ICU. Frailty: Clinical Frailty Scale >=5 (frail) -> geriatric co-management/prehab. Nutrition: NRS-2002 >=3 at-risk (>=5 severe), weight loss >10-15%/6mo, BMI <18.5, or albumin <3.0-3.5 g/dL -> preop optimization / delay elective surgery. Sepsis screen for perforation: qSOFA >=2 / SOFA. POD3-4 CRP >150 mg/L flags anastomotic leak (high NPV). Anastomotic-leak risk (male, low pelvic anastomosis, malnutrition, steroids, smoking) drives diverting-stoma decision.
initial_workup_labs: CBC (anemia from cancer/bleed, leukocytosis in ischemia/sepsis); CMP/BMP (electrolytes, AKI, obstruction dehydration); LFTs; coags INR/aPTT; type & screen/crossmatch; lactate (>2 mmol/L -> ischemia/sepsis, serial clearance); CRP baseline + POD3-4 trend (leak surveillance); albumin/prealbumin (nutrition); HbA1c (>8% higher SSI -> optimize); CEA baseline (colorectal cancer, surveillance); blood cultures if septic; VBG; pregnancy test. Preop patient-blood-management: iron studies, treat iron-deficiency anemia with IV iron before elective surgery.
initial_imaging_dx: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV (+/- oral) contrast is first-line for obstruction, perforation, ischemia, diverticular Hinchey staging, and cancer staging — emergent within 1-2h if peritonitis/ischemia suspected; CT angiography if mesenteric ischemia suspected. Upright CXR/CT for free air. Water-soluble (Gastrografin) contrast challenge for adhesive SBO (predicts/promotes resolution). Elective cancer: colonoscopy with biopsy for tissue dx; staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis + CEA; rectal cancer adds pelvic MRI +/- endorectal US for T/N staging. Avoid colonoscopy in acute obstruction/perforation.
time_critical_interventions: Septic shock from perforation: Surviving Sepsis bundle — broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1h, balanced-crystalloid resuscitation, lactate clearance; source control (OR) ideally <6h (per WSES). Acute mesenteric ischemia: emergent revascularization/resection ASAP within the bowel-viability window (mortality rises hourly). Closed-loop/strangulated obstruction and feculent/purulent peritonitis: emergent laparotomy. NELA emergency-laparotomy timing by urgency (septic shock/ischemia <2h, peritonitis <6h) with senior review. Surgical antibiotic prophylaxis IV within 60 min of incision (cefazolin+metronidazole or cefoxitin), redose if case >3-4h or EBL >1.5L. Maintain intraop normothermia and glucose <180 mg/dL.
initial_management: Resuscitation with balanced crystalloid (LR/Plasmalyte) to euvolemia — avoid over-resuscitation (RELIEF: excessive restriction raised AKI). NG decompression for obstruction. Empiric antibiotics for perforation/peritonitis: piperacillin-tazobactam, or ceftriaxone+metronidazole, or cefoxitin (per SIS/IDSA complicated intra-abdominal infection). Restrictive transfusion threshold Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 with cardiac disease); correct preop anemia with IV iron. Source control = resection +/- primary anastomosis vs diversion (Hartmann); percutaneous drainage for Hinchey II abscess. Elective prep: mechanical bowel prep PLUS oral antibiotics (halves SSI), carbohydrate loading, avoid prolonged fasting (clears to 2h, solids to 6h), smoking/alcohol cessation >=4 wk, prehabilitation. Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia + VTE prophylaxis from the outset.
day1_milestones: POD0: minimally invasive (laparoscopic/robotic) approach preferred; multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia with regional (TAP block or spinal for MIS; thoracic epidural for open) + acetaminophen/NSAID; NO routine NG tube (remove at case end); early oral intake same day (clear then regular diet as tolerated); out of bed and ambulate POD0; euvolemic goal-directed fluids, stop maintenance IV early once drinking; multimodal PONV prophylaxis; normoglycemia; sham feeding (chewing gum); plan early urinary-catheter removal; document ERAS bundle elements.
day2_milestones: POD1: remove urinary catheter (delay only for low pelvic/rectal dissection); discontinue IV fluids on adequate PO; regular diet; transition to oral analgesia, minimize opioids; ambulate >=3x and out of bed >6-8h; continue VTE prophylaxis + IPC; monitor for return of bowel function and early ileus/leak signs (tachycardia, distension); check CRP/WBC trend; reinforce ostomy self-care teaching if applicable; begin discharge-readiness assessment.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+: confirm GI-2 recovery (tolerating diet + flatus/BM, typically POD2-3); remove remaining lines/drains; vigilant leak surveillance POD3-7 (fever, tachycardia, prolonged ileus, rising CRP >150 mg/L / WBC) -> CT with rectal contrast and reoperation/drainage if positive; treat postoperative ileus (rule out mechanical cause, correct electrolytes, prokinetic/alvimopan). Discharge readiness POD2-4 for MIS, POD4-6 for open. Finalize education, stoma competency, and transitions before discharge.
consults_multidisciplinary: Colorectal/surgical oncology (primary); anesthesia/preoperative optimization clinic; medical oncology for cancer adjuvant planning; WOC/enterostomal therapy nurse for PREOP stoma siting + education (essential when ostomy planned) and postop teaching; registered dietitian for nutrition risk; PT/OT for prehab and early mobility; pharmacy for med reconciliation, multimodal analgesia and antibiotic stewardship; case management/social work early for disposition and home health; geriatrics/perioperative medicine for frail elderly; palliative care for advanced malignancy; interventional radiology for abscess drainage.
monitoring_level: Uncomplicated elective MIS colectomy -> surgical ward with routine vitals (telemetry only if cardiac indication). ICU/step-down for: septic shock, high NELA-risk emergency laparotomy, vasopressor need, hemodynamic instability, extensive resection/ischemia, significant comorbidity, or frail elderly. Escalation triggers: NEWS/MEWS >=5-7, HR >90-100, hypotension/oliguria, rising lactate, new organ dysfunction, or clinical suspicion of anastomotic leak/sepsis -> rapid response, imaging, surgical re-evaluation.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: early feeding POD0 (regular diet as tolerated), no prolonged NPO; oral nutrition supplements for at-risk patients. Mobility: out of bed POD0, structured ambulation >=3x/day with hours-out-of-bed targets. VTE: mechanical IPC + pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin/LMWH or UFH) within 24h balancing bleeding risk; EXTENDED 28-day post-discharge LMWH for abdominopelvic cancer resections. Glucose: target <180 mg/dL, avoid hypoglycemia. Lines/catheters: remove urinary catheter POD1 (avoid CAUTI), heplock/remove IV when PO adequate, avoid routine intra-abdominal drains and NG tubes.
discharge_criteria: Tolerating oral diet with adequate intake; pain controlled on oral analgesics; return of bowel function (flatus or bowel movement / functioning stoma); ambulating at/near baseline and independent with ADLs; no signs of sepsis or leak (afebrile, stable vitals, down-trending WBC/CRP); competent in ostomy self-care and high-output management if applicable; safe home support and follow-up arranged.
discharge_planning: Disposition: home (majority) +/- home health nursing; SNF/rehab only for deconditioned/frail. Medication reconciliation: opioid taper + bowel regimen, resume home meds, dispense extended VTE prophylaxis for cancer. Follow-up: surgeon 1-2 wk (wound/ostomy check), pathology/staging review, medical oncology for adjuvant therapy in cancer, stoma clinic. Education: red-flag warning signs (fever, worsening pain, obstruction, dehydration/high-output stoma), ostomy and hydration teaching, ERAS recovery expectations. Transitions: post-discharge phone call within 24-72h, ostomy/nurse hotline, readmission-prevention review.
expected_los: Approximate CMS benchmark GMLOS by tier: DRG 331 (no CC/MCC) ~4 days; DRG 330 (with CC) ~7 days; DRG 329 (with MCC) ~11-12 days. Traditional open colectomy historically ~6-8 days.
target_los: Under a full ERAS pathway with minimally invasive resection: 2-4 days for uncomplicated laparoscopic/robotic colectomy; 4-6 days for open or complex/rectal resection. Emergent MCC cases individualized (often 10-14+ days).
quality_metrics: Colon-surgery SSI (NHSN/CMS HAC-reportable) and organ-space SSI; ACS-NSQIP colectomy-targeted 30-day morbidity/mortality; ERAS-bundle compliance (target >70-80%, dose-response to outcomes); anastomotic leak rate; postoperative ileus rate; 30-day readmission; return to OR; CAUTI/CLABSI; VTE prophylaxis (VTE-1/VTE-2) and VTE event rate; normothermia, perioperative glucose, prophylactic-antibiotic timing; SEP-1 if septic presentation; opioid-free/PONV metrics.
common_complications: Postoperative ileus (most common, ~10-20%) — mitigated by MIS, opioid-sparing analgesia, gum, early feeding/mobility, euvolemia, alvimopan. Anastomotic leak (~3-8%, higher for low rectal) — mitigated by MBP+oral antibiotics, well-perfused tension-free anastomosis (ICG perfusion assessment), CRP surveillance, selective diverting stoma. SSI (~5-15% colon) — MBP+OA, normothermia, glucose control, wound protectors. Also: CAUTI (early catheter removal), VTE, pneumonia/atelectasis, AKI, ileus/SBO, C. difficile, anastomotic bleed, delirium in elderly.
readmission_drivers: Leading driver after ileostomy: dehydration/AKI from high-output stoma -> prevented by stoma education, oral rehydration solutions, loperamide/antidiarrheals, and early hydration follow-up. Others: postoperative ileus/SBO, SSI/organ-space abscess, anastomotic leak presenting after discharge, uncontrolled pain, failure to thrive. Prevention levers: structured stoma pathway, 24-72h post-discharge call, early clinic visit, wound-care coordination, and clear return-precaution education.
guideline_source: ERAS Society 2025 (and 2018) Guidelines for Perioperative Care in Elective Colorectal Surgery; ASCRS + SAGES 2023 Clinical Practice Guidelines for Enhanced Recovery After Colon and Rectal Surgery; ERAS Society 2023 Emergency Laparotomy recommendations (for emergent presentations); supported by ACS-NSQIP. Attribution: guideline/trial details retrieved via PubMed.
key_citations: Gustafsson UO et al. ERAS Society Recommendations 2025, Surgery 2025;184:109397 (PMID 40783294; doi:10.1016/j.surg.2025.109397); Gustafsson UO et al. ERAS Society 2018, World J Surg 2019;43:659-695 (PMID 30426190; doi:10.1007/s00268-018-4844-y); Irani JL, Hedrick TL et al. ASCRS/SAGES Enhanced Recovery CPG, Surg Endosc 2023;37:5-30 (PMID 36515747; doi:10.1007/s00464-022-09758-x); Scott MJ et al. ERAS Emergency Laparotomy Part 2, World J Surg 2023;47:1850-1880 (PMID 37277507; doi:10.1007/s00268-023-07020-6); Myles PS et al. RELIEF trial (restrictive vs liberal fluids), N Engl J Med 2018;378:2263-2274 (PMID 29742967; doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1801601). Retrieved via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong — multiple current GRADE-based Class I society guidelines (ERAS Society 2025/2018; ASCRS/SAGES 2023) underpinned by RCTs and meta-analyses; several elements high-quality (MBP+oral antibiotics for SSI, restrictive/zero-balance fluids, extended VTE prophylaxis in cancer, MIS approach). Emergency-presentation pathway is moderate (partly extrapolated from elective/emergency-general-surgery data per ERAS Emergency Laparotomy 2023).
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 331 (no CC/MCC): elective, uncomplicated, MIS + full ERAS, ward-level, LOS ~3-4d, low complication/mortality. DRG 330 (with CC): comorbidity or minor complication (ileus, transfusion, mild AKI, UTI), LOS ~7d, occasional step-down. DRG 329 (with MCC): sepsis, anastomotic leak, respiratory failure, AKI needing dialysis, reoperation, or emergent perforation/ischemia -> ICU, LOS 11-14+d, markedly higher mortality; emergent presentations cluster in this tier and drive the group's cost/mortality. Frailty and malnutrition shift patients upward across all tiers.
pathway_pearls: 1) ERAS-bundle compliance >70-80% is the single biggest lever on LOS and complications — measure and audit adherence, not just individual elements. 2) Mechanical bowel prep PLUS oral antibiotics for elective colon resection roughly halves SSI (do not use MBP alone). 3) Prevent ileus (the #1 LOS driver) with MIS + multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia + early feeding/mobility + euvolemia (zero-balance, not dry — RELIEF showed over-restriction raises AKI). 4) The #1 readmission after ileostomy is high-output-stoma dehydration/AKI — preop stoma marking/education plus a postop hydration/antidiarrheal protocol and early follow-up call prevents it.
