mdc: 21 — Injuries, Poisonings & Toxic Effects of Drugs
admission_criteria: Inpatient/ICU admission for: persistent altered mental status or GCS not returning to baseline; airway compromise or need for intubation; opioid respiratory depression requiring naloxone infusion; hemodynamic instability/shock or vasopressors; seizures; QRS >100 ms, QTc >500 ms, or any dysrhythmia; severe metabolic acidosis (pH <7.2 or wide anion gap); need for antidote infusion (NAC, high-dose insulin, fomepizole) or hemodialysis; ongoing absorption from sustained/extended-release or high-risk (massive) ingestion; acetaminophen level on/above the 150 mcg/mL (4h) treatment line; rising or symptomatic salicylate level; ANY intentional self-harm ingestion requiring monitoring plus psychiatric evaluation. ED discharge/observation acceptable only if asymptomatic with non-toxic agent or sub-toxic dose, normal vitals/mental status/ECG after an adequate observation window (>=4-6h; >=6h opioid, longer for sustained-release/methadone), acetaminophen below treatment line at >=4h, negative salicylate, Poison Control concurrence, and no active suicidal risk.
risk_stratification: Rumack-Matthew nomogram (acetaminophen): treatment line 150 mcg/mL at 4h triggers NAC; high-risk line 300 mcg/mL at 4h triggers augmented NAC plus toxicology consult. King's College Criteria for acetaminophen-induced acute liver failure (O'Grady 1989): arterial pH <7.30 after resuscitation OR all three of INR >6.5 (PT >100s) + creatinine >3.4 mg/dL (300 micromol/L) + grade III-IV encephalopathy; arterial lactate >3.5 mmol/L early or >3.0 post-resuscitation drives transplant referral. ECG risk: QRS >100 ms drives sodium bicarbonate and flags seizure/arrhythmia risk; QRS >160 ms high ventricular-tachycardia risk; QTc >500 ms torsades risk. Salicylate (EXTRIP): level >100 mg/dL (7.2 mmol/L) mandates hemodialysis. Osmolar gap >10 plus anion-gap acidosis suggests toxic alcohol. GCS <=8 drives intubation. Poisoning Severity Score (PSS grade 0-4: none/minor/moderate/severe/fatal) for severity grading. Columbia-Suicide Severity Rating Scale (C-SSRS) for suicide risk. APACHE II for ICU prognostication.
initial_workup_labs: STAT serum acetaminophen (draw at >=4h post-ingestion) AND salicylate level on EVERY intentional or unknown overdose regardless of history; serum ethanol; fingerstick glucose immediately. VBG/ABG with lactate (acid-base, respiratory status); calculated anion gap; measured serum osmolality plus calculated osmolality for osmolar gap (toxic alcohol screen). CMP including AST/ALT and total bilirubin (hepatotoxicity), creatinine/BUN, electrolytes with K/Mg/Ca/phosphate. CBC; coagulation INR/PT. Creatine kinase (rhabdomyolysis). Serum/urine beta-hCG in women of childbearing age. Co-oximetry (methemoglobin, carboxyhemoglobin) if suspected. Targeted drug concentrations as indicated: digoxin, lithium, valproate, carbamazepine, phenytoin, iron, theophylline. Urine drug screen has limited utility and rarely alters acute management. Triggers: acetaminophen on/above line -> NAC; high salicylate -> alkalinization/HD; wide osmolar gap -> fomepizole; rising AST/ALT or INR -> hepatology and King's College monitoring.
initial_imaging_dx: 12-lead ECG immediately on arrival (assess QRS width, QTc, dysrhythmia) and repeat serially. Continuous cardiac telemetry plus pulse oximetry, with capnography for sedated/obtunded patients. Chest radiograph (aspiration pneumonitis, non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema/ARDS). Non-contrast CT head if altered mental status without clear toxicologic cause, head trauma, or focal neurologic deficit. Abdominal radiograph (KUB) for radiopaque agents (iron, heavy metals, sustained-release/enteric-coated tablets) or suspected body packing/stuffing. EEG if persistent unexplained altered mental status or suspected nonconvulsive seizure.
time_critical_interventions: Immediate ABCs; intubate if GCS <=8 or airway compromise. Naloxone 0.04-0.4 mg IV (RCEM: 100-200 mcg every 60s) titrated to respiratory rate >10 and SpO2 >92% for opioid respiratory depression. Call Poison Control 1-800-222-1222 / medical toxicology immediately at presentation. Single-dose activated charcoal 1 g/kg (50-100 g adult) within 1-2h of ingestion if airway protected and toxin adsorbable. Empiric NAC within 8h of ingestion for high-risk acetaminophen (>200 mg/kg or >10 g) when level is delayed. Sodium bicarbonate 1-2 mEq/kg IV bolus immediately for QRS >100 ms or sodium-channel-blocker dysrhythmia. Early high-dose insulin euglycemia therapy for life-threatening beta-blocker/calcium-channel-blocker toxicity. Whole-bowel irrigation (polyethylene glycol) for sustained-release/enteric-coated agents, iron/lithium, or body packers. Continuous 1:1 observation and suicide precautions from arrival for any intentional ingestion.
initial_management: Supportive core: supplemental oxygen, IV crystalloid, norepinephrine for refractory shock. Agent-specific antidotes: Opioids -> naloxone bolus then infusion at ~60% (two-thirds) of the total reversal dose per hour. Acetaminophen -> N-acetylcysteine >=300 mg/kg IV or PO over first 20-24h (load 150 mg/kg over >=1h; empiric start for massive ingestion). Salicylate -> sodium bicarbonate infusion for serum and urine alkalinization (target urine pH 7.5-8) with aggressive potassium repletion, plus hemodialysis at threshold. Beta-blocker/CCB -> high-dose insulin (1 U/kg bolus then 1-10 U/kg/h) + IV calcium + glucagon + vasopressors; VA-ECMO for refractory cardiogenic shock. Sodium-channel blockers/TCA -> sodium bicarbonate. Toxic alcohols (methanol/ethylene glycol) -> fomepizole 15 mg/kg load + hemodialysis. Digoxin -> digoxin-specific immune Fab (DigiFab). Cyanide -> hydroxocobalamin. Local anesthetic -> 20% IV lipid emulsion. Sulfonylurea -> dextrose + octreotide. Organophosphate/carbamate -> atropine + pralidoxime. Methemoglobinemia -> methylene blue. Sympathomimetic/cocaine -> benzodiazepines. Enhanced elimination (hemodialysis) for salicylate, toxic alcohols, lithium, valproate, metformin-associated lactic acidosis. Benzodiazepine overdose -> supportive care (avoid flumazenil, seizure risk).
day1_milestones: Stabilize ABCs, secure airway if needed, deliver antidote(s), establish continuous cardiac/respiratory monitoring. Confirm the agent(s); obtain >=4h acetaminophen and salicylate levels, ECG, fingerstick glucose, pregnancy test. Contact Poison Control / medical toxicology and start antidotes; begin serial toxin levels (salicylate every 2h until falling; acetaminophen with AST/ALT and INR to guide NAC). Institute suicide precautions with 1:1 sitter and environmental/ligature-means removal; place behavioral health and social work consults on Day 1. Start VTE prophylaxis and aspiration precautions.
day2_milestones: Reassess toxin clearance and trend (downtrending levels), resolving acidosis, and neurologic/hemodynamic recovery; wean antidote infusions (hold naloxone taper overnight per protocol) and vasopressors. Continue NAC to defined stopping criteria (acetaminophen undetectable + AST/ALT improving + INR <2 + clinically well) rather than a fixed duration. Complete formal psychiatric evaluation with C-SSRS and determine disposition (inpatient psychiatry vs discharge with safety plan). De-escalate monitoring level if stable; remove unnecessary lines/Foley; advance diet and mobilize.
day3plus_milestones: Medical readiness: toxin cleared, off antidote infusions, stable vitals/ECG/mental status, resolving organ injury (normalizing AST/ALT, renal function); escalate to hepatology/transplant pathway if King's College Criteria met. Complete OUD interventions: initiate buprenorphine (MOUD), dispense take-home naloxone, place addiction-medicine referral. Finalize psychiatric disposition (transfer to inpatient psych bed vs discharge with safety plan and follow-up) with documented medical clearance. Discharge once medically stable AND psychiatric disposition is secured.
consults_multidisciplinary: Medical toxicology / Poison Control Center (1-800-222-1222) immediately at presentation. Psychiatry/behavioral health for all intentional self-harm after medical stabilization (per Joint Commission NPSG 15.01.01; C-SSRS). Critical care/intensivist for ICU-level care. Nephrology for hemodialysis/CRRT (salicylate, toxic alcohols, lithium). Hepatology/transplant service for acetaminophen hepatotoxicity meeting King's College Criteria. Addiction medicine plus social work/case management for substance use disorder, MOUD initiation, take-home naloxone, and housing/insurance barriers. Pharmacy for antidote dosing and compatibility. Cardiology/ECMO team for refractory cardiotoxicity. PT/OT if deconditioned after prolonged obtundation.
monitoring_level: ICU for: intubation/airway compromise; shock or vasopressors; high-dose insulin or other antidote infusions requiring titration; hemodialysis for poisoning; seizures; QRS >100 ms or dysrhythmia; QTc >500 ms; severe acid-base disturbance; persistent altered mental status; salicylate or toxic alcohol requiring dialysis. Step-down/telemetry for: hemodynamically stable patients needing cardiac surveillance (QRS/QTc monitoring), naloxone infusion, or moderate ingestion with resolving symptoms. Floor for: stable, resolving, low-risk agent with no cardiac/CNS concern, often awaiting psychiatric disposition. Escalation triggers: falling GCS, widening QRS or lengthening QTc, new dysrhythmia, hypotension, worsening acidosis, or recurrent respiratory depression.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: NPO until alert with protected airway, then advance as tolerated; maintain aspiration precautions with head-of-bed elevated. Mobility: early mobilization once hemodynamically and neurologically stable. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH/unfractionated heparin) unless coagulopathy from hepatotoxicity or active bleeding, in which case mechanical prophylaxis. Glucose: monitor closely, especially during high-dose insulin therapy (watch hypoglycemia and hypokalemia) and sulfonylurea overdose (dextrose plus octreotide). Lines/catheters: remove central lines and Foley as soon as no longer required to limit infection.
discharge_criteria: Medical: return to baseline mental status; stable vitals through the required observation window; normal or normalizing ECG (no QRS/QTc prolongation, no dysrhythmia); resolved acid-base disturbance; downtrending or cleared toxin level; off antidote infusions with adequate post-infusion observation; NAC stopping criteria met; resolving organ injury. Behavioral: completed psychiatric safety assessment with disposition secured (either admission to psychiatry or discharge with an active safety plan) and no active suicidal intent for any medical-floor discharge.
discharge_planning: Disposition: transfer to inpatient psychiatry with documented medical clearance, or discharge home with outpatient behavioral health. Medication reconciliation with lethal-means restriction counseling (secure/limit medications, lock boxes, limit acetaminophen quantity). For OUD: prescribe take-home naloxone, initiate or continue buprenorphine (MOUD), and arrange a warm handoff to addiction treatment. Follow-up: behavioral health/psychiatry within 7 days (HEDIS FUH), primary care, hepatology if hepatotoxicity, and addiction medicine. Education: Poison Control number, Safety Planning Intervention, 988 crisis line, and family/caregiver engagement in the transition of care.
expected_los: Approximately 2.5-3.5 days blended: MS-DRG 918 (without MCC) GMLOS roughly 2.0-2.5 days; MS-DRG 917 (with MCC) GMLOS roughly 3.5-4.0 days (exact CMS IPPS figures paywalled at time of build; values are typical benchmark estimates). Stays are frequently extended beyond medical readiness by psychiatric bed availability (boarding).
target_los: Under the ideal pathway with parallel psychiatric processing: MS-DRG 918 ~1.5-2 days and MS-DRG 917 ~3 days. Medical clearance is often achievable within 24-48h; the primary optimization lever is eliminating excess psychiatric-boarding days after medical stability.
quality_metrics: Joint Commission NPSG 15.01.01 (validated suicide risk screening, environmental risk assessment, and safety monitoring) for self-harm presentations. Follow-Up After Hospitalization for Mental Illness (HEDIS FUH 7-day and 30-day). Substance-use screening and treatment measures (Joint Commission SUB-2/SUB-3) with take-home naloxone dispensed and MOUD initiation for OUD. Time-to-antidote (naloxone, NAC) and Poison Control Center consultation rate. Restraint/seclusion (HBIPS) measures where applicable. 30-day all-cause readmission and ED revisit rate. Hospital-acquired complication rates (aspiration pneumonia, VTE).
common_complications: Aspiration pneumonia/pneumonitis (mitigated by airway protection, head-of-bed elevation, early intubation when GCS <=8). Non-cardiogenic pulmonary edema from opioid or salicylate (supportive oxygenation/PEEP). Rhabdomyolysis and AKI from prolonged down-time (IV fluids, CK monitoring). Hepatotoxicity/acute liver failure from acetaminophen (NAC, King's College monitoring, transplant referral). Cardiac dysrhythmias/arrest from cardiotoxic agents (telemetry, sodium bicarbonate, antidotes, VA-ECMO). Seizures from benzodiazepine overdose (avoid flumazenil). Hypoglycemia/hypokalemia from high-dose insulin therapy and sulfonylurea (glucose/potassium monitoring). Withdrawal syndromes (opioid by COWS, alcohol/benzodiazepine by CIWA-Ar). Anaphylactoid reaction to IV NAC (slow the infusion, antihistamine).
readmission_drivers: Untreated substance use disorder and recurrent overdose -> initiate MOUD (buprenorphine), dispense take-home naloxone, harm reduction, and addiction referral. Untreated or undertreated psychiatric illness and repeat self-harm -> psychiatric follow-up within 7 days (FUH), safety planning, and lethal-means restriction. Poor outpatient linkage and social determinants (housing, insurance, transportation) -> case management and warm handoff. Premature discharge before a delayed- or sustained-release toxin fully clears -> enforce adequate observation windows and serial levels.
guideline_source: 2023 American Heart Association Focused Update on the Management of Patients With Cardiac Arrest or Life-Threatening Toxicity Due to Poisoning (AHA, 2023). Management of Acetaminophen Poisoning in the US and Canada: A Consensus Statement (America's Poison Centers / American Academy of Clinical Toxicology / American College of Medical Toxicology / Canadian Association of Poison Control Centres, 2023). EXTRIP (Extracorporeal Treatments in Poisoning) Workgroup recommendations (2015-2023). Royal College of Emergency Medicine Acute Opioid Toxicity Best Practice Guideline (2024). Joint Commission National Patient Safety Goal 15.01.01 (suicide risk reduction).
key_citations: Citations verified via PubMed. Lavonas EJ, et al. 2023 AHA Focused Update on Management of Patients With Cardiac Arrest or Life-Threatening Toxicity Due to Poisoning. Circulation. 2023;148(16):e149-e184. PMID 37721023; DOI 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001161. Dart RC, et al. Management of Acetaminophen Poisoning in the US and Canada: A Consensus Statement. JAMA Netw Open. 2023;6(8):e2327739. PMID 37552484; DOI 10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2023.27739. Juurlink DN, et al. Extracorporeal Treatment for Salicylate Poisoning: EXTRIP Workgroup. Ann Emerg Med. 2015;66(2):165-181. PMID 25986310; DOI 10.1016/j.annemergmed.2015.03.031. O'Grady JG, et al. Early Indicators of Prognosis in Fulminant Hepatic Failure (King's College Criteria). Gastroenterology. 1989;97(2):439-445. PMID 2490426; DOI 10.1016/0016-5085(89)90081-4.
evidence_grade: Moderate-to-Strong (consensus-weighted). Anchored by AHA 2023 Class I/IIa guideline recommendations for critical antidotes and by a rigorous 4-society modified-Delphi consensus for acetaminophen; established antidotes (naloxone, N-acetylcysteine, high-dose insulin, sodium bicarbonate, fomepizole, digoxin-Fab, hydroxocobalamin) are standard of care. However, much underlying toxicology evidence is low quality (case reports/series rather than RCTs); EXTRIP salicylate recommendations are graded 1D (strong recommendation, very-low-quality evidence). Suicide screening/behavioral pathway rests on regulatory standards (NPSG 15.01.01) and validated tools (C-SSRS).
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The collapsed group splits into MS-DRG 917 (with MCC) vs 918 (without MCC). MCC-defining events (acute respiratory failure requiring intubation, acute liver failure/hepatic encephalopathy, AKI requiring dialysis, cardiac arrest, shock, aspiration pneumonia, severe metabolic acidosis, coma) drive ICU admission, add roughly 1-1.5 days of LOS, raise mortality, and correlate with antidote-infusion, dialysis, or VA-ECMO use. Without MCC, care is typically brief observation-level medical management, and LOS/disposition are frequently governed by psychiatric bed availability rather than medical acuity.
pathway_pearls: 1) Call Poison Control (1-800-222-1222) or medical toxicology early: it is a free 24/7 resource that changes management and antidote dosing. 2) Check acetaminophen AND salicylate on EVERY intentional or unknown overdose regardless of stated history, plus fingerstick glucose, ECG (QRS/QTc), and pregnancy test: occult co-ingestion is common and both agents are lethal yet treatable. 3) Medically clear does not equal ready for discharge: the rate-limiting step is usually the psychiatric bed, so parallel-process behavioral health, social work, and safety planning from Day 1 to prevent boarding days. 4) Treat OUD at this touchpoint: initiate buprenorphine, dispense take-home naloxone, and arrange a warm handoff to MOUD, since harm-reduction measurably reduces bounce-back and mortality.

scope_and_volume_notes: Includes opioid and polysubstance overdose, intentional self-harm poisonings. ED/ICU entry; behavioral health secondary owner.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 917 — Poisoning and Toxic Effects of Drugs with MCC; 918 — Poisoning and Toxic Effects of Drugs without MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.

admission_criteria: Confirmed acute PE requiring inpatient admission when any present: hemodynamic instability = high-risk/massive (SBP <90 mmHg or drop >=40 mmHg >15 min, vasopressor need, or PE-related cardiac arrest) -> ICU; RV dysfunction PLUS elevated troponin = intermediate-high -> monitored bed/ICU; sPESI >=1 or PESI Class III-V; hypoxemia needing supplemental O2 to keep SaO2 >=90%; any positive Hestia criterion (IV analgesia >24h, severe renal/hepatic impairment, pregnancy, active/high bleeding risk, poor social support/adherence); contraindication to anticoagulation needing IVC filter; concurrent proximal DVT with phlegmasia. ED discharge or <24h observation appropriate only for validated LOW-risk PE: sPESI 0 / PESI Class I-II, no RV dysfunction, normal troponin, Hestia-negative, anticoagulation accessible
risk_stratification: Pretest probability: Wells PE (>4 'likely' -> CTPA; <=4 'unlikely' -> D-dimer) or revised Geneva; PERC to exclude if low probability; YEARS algorithm; age-adjusted D-dimer (age x 10 ug/L if >50y). ESC 2019 severity tiers drive disposition: HIGH-risk = hemodynamic instability (cardiac arrest / obstructive shock / persistent hypotension) -> emergent reperfusion + ICU; INTERMEDIATE-HIGH = RV dysfunction (echo or CT RV/LV ratio >=1.0) AND positive troponin with PESI III-V/sPESI>=1 -> monitored bed + rescue-reperfusion trigger; INTERMEDIATE-LOW = only one (or neither) of RV dysfunction/troponin positive; LOW = PESI I-II / sPESI 0, no RV dysfunction, normal troponin -> home candidate. PESI: Class I <=65 / II 66-85 (30-day mortality <=3.5%) vs III 86-105 / IV 106-125 / V >125 (up to ~24%). sPESI: 0 = low (~1.0% 30-day mortality) vs >=1 = high (~10.9%); variables age >80, cancer, chronic cardiopulmonary disease, HR >=110, SBP <100, SaO2 <90%. Venous lactate (>2, optimized >=3.8 mmol/L) refines obstructive shock. Assess bleeding risk before any thrombolysis
initial_workup_labs: D-dimer (age-adjusted, high NPV) if low/intermediate pretest -> rules out if negative; high-sensitivity cardiac troponin -> RV myocardial injury, drives intermediate-high tier; BNP/NT-proBNP -> RV strain/prognosis; CBC (baseline Hgb + platelets before anticoagulation, HIT surveillance); CMP/creatinine -> DOAC vs LMWH/UFH selection and contrast safety; PT/INR + aPTT baseline (UFH monitoring); fibrinogen before lysis; type and screen if thrombolysis/high bleeding risk; venous/arterial lactate for perfusion; ABG if hypoxemic; pregnancy test in women of childbearing age; lipase/LFTs as indicated. Defer thrombophilia panel (alters acute management rarely); age-appropriate cancer screening and history for unprovoked PE
initial_imaging_dx: CT pulmonary angiography (CTPA) = first-line, obtain emergently (<1-2h) in unstable/high suspicion; report RV/LV diameter ratio >=1.0 as RV dysfunction. Bedside TTE/POCUS immediately when too unstable for CT: RV dilation, McConnell sign, D-sign (septal flattening), TAPSE <16 mm, 60/60 sign -> presumptive Dx justifying reperfusion. V/Q scan alternative when CTPA contraindicated (contrast allergy, CrCl <30, pregnancy). Bilateral lower-extremity compression ultrasound to confirm DVT / as alternative when CT deferred. Formal TTE within 24h for intermediate/high-risk (RV function, PASP). ECG on arrival (sinus tachycardia, S1Q3T3, RV strain; exclude ACS). CXR to exclude alternative diagnoses
time_critical_interventions: Start therapeutic anticoagulation immediately/empirically on high or intermediate clinical suspicion with low bleeding risk WITHOUT waiting for imaging (ESC) -> target <1h. HIGH-risk PE: systemic thrombolysis without delay (alteplase 100 mg IV over 2h, or 0.6 mg/kg over 15 min max 50 mg) unless contraindicated; if contraindicated/failed -> emergent catheter-directed therapy or surgical embolectomy. Activate PERT (Pulmonary Embolism Response Team) for intermediate-high and high-risk within the first hour. PE-related cardiac arrest: consider thrombolysis during CPR; VA-ECMO as bridge. First-hour hemodynamic bundle: cautious fluid <=500 mL, norepinephrine first-line vasopressor, MAP >=65, O2 to SaO2 >=90%. Reassess intermediate-high q1-2h -> rescue reperfusion if decompensation
initial_management: Anticoagulation is the cornerstone. Parenteral: LMWH (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC q12h) or fondaparinux preferred; UFH IV (80 U/kg bolus + weight-based infusion, anti-Xa/aPTT titrated) reserved for high-risk, hemodynamic instability, CrCl <30, extreme body weight, or anticipated procedures/lysis. Oral DOAC preferred for most (CHEST 2021): apixaban 10 mg BID x7d then 5 mg BID, or rivaroxaban 15 mg BID x21d then 20 mg daily (no lead-in); edoxaban/dabigatran after >=5d LMWH; VKA (warfarin, INR 2-3) with LMWH bridge for antiphospholipid syndrome or severe renal failure. Reperfusion: systemic alteplase (reduced 50 mg dose in elderly/bleeding risk per MOPETT), catheter-directed thrombolysis (EKOS), or large-bore mechanical thrombectomy (FlowTriever; PEERLESS favored thrombectomy over CDT on ICU/complication endpoints). Hemodynamics: <=500 mL fluid, norepinephrine, dobutamine for low CO, VA-ECMO for refractory shock/arrest. Major bleeding reversal: hold drug; UFH -> protamine; VKA -> 4F-PCC + IV vitamin K; dabigatran -> idarucizumab; apixaban/rivaroxaban -> andexanet alfa or 4F-PCC. IVC filter only if absolute anticoagulation contraindication (retrievable). Cancer-associated: LMWH or apixaban/edoxaban (caution luminal GI/GU bleeding)
day1_milestones: Confirm CTPA diagnosis and assign ESC risk tier; document PERT decision. Initiate and dose-verify therapeutic anticoagulation; complete bleeding-risk assessment. Result troponin + BNP and assess RV function (TTE/CT RV/LV) -> separate intermediate-high from intermediate-low. Determine reperfusion need; if high-risk, complete reperfusion and ICU stabilization (wean vasopressors, SaO2 >=90%). Identify provoking factors (recent surgery, immobilization, estrogen, cancer, prior VTE, thrombophilia). Place in tier-appropriate monitored setting; begin pharmacy/VTE-nurse anticoagulation education
day2_milestones: Confirm 24h hemodynamic stability with no decompensation -> de-escalate ICU/step-down to floor. Transition parenteral to definitive oral anticoagulation (DOAC) once stable; ensure >=5d LMWH lead-in if edoxaban/dabigatran selected. Wean/discontinue supplemental O2 and ambulate. Reassess troponin trend/RV recovery in intermediate-high. Recheck CBC (Hgb stability, platelet trend for HIT). Establish anticoagulation duration plan (provoked vs unprovoked vs cancer); start discharge planning and DOAC prior-authorization/affordability check
day3plus_milestones: Stable on oral anticoagulation, ambulating at baseline, SaO2 >=90% on room air, pain controlled on oral agents, no recurrent symptoms or bleeding. Finalize and document regimen + duration (minimum 3 months; extended/indefinite if unprovoked, recurrent, or active cancer). Complete anticoagulation education (adherence, bleeding precautions, return precautions). Confirm DOAC prescription filled and affordable BEFORE discharge. Schedule follow-up (anticoagulation clinic/PCP 1-2 weeks; hematology if unprovoked/thrombophilia; pulmonology/PH clinic 3-6 months for CTEPH surveillance). Order age-appropriate cancer screening and estrogen cessation if unprovoked
consults_multidisciplinary: PERT activation (pulmonary/critical care + interventional cardiology or radiology + cardiac surgery + hematology) Day 0 for intermediate-high and high-risk. Pulmonology/critical care for intermediate-high/high. Interventional cardiology/radiology for catheter-directed therapy or mechanical thrombectomy. Cardiac surgery for surgical embolectomy / ECMO. Hematology for unprovoked, recurrent, thrombophilia, antiphospholipid, cancer-associated, or pregnancy-associated VTE. Cardiology for significant RV dysfunction/pulmonary hypertension. Pharmacy/anticoagulation service for dosing, interactions, reconciliation, and clinic referral. Care management/social work for DOAC access, follow-up, and home services. PT for mobility; nutrition and dietitian as needed
monitoring_level: High-risk/massive -> ICU with continuous hemodynamic monitoring, arterial line, vasopressors, reperfusion capability. Intermediate-high -> ICU or step-down/telemetry with continuous monitoring for 24-72h (highest decompensation window). Intermediate-low -> telemetry/monitored floor. Low-risk -> general floor or home. Escalation triggers to PERT/ICU: SBP <90 mmHg, rising/persistent HR >110, falling SaO2 or escalating O2 requirement, syncope, rising troponin or lactate, worsening RV on echo, altered mental status
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet as tolerated; hold intake if catheter intervention/lysis anticipated. Early mobilization once hemodynamically stable and anticoagulated -- routine bed rest is NOT indicated for stable PE. VTE prophylaxis: therapeutic anticoagulation IS the treatment; if anticoagulation is temporarily held (procedure/bleeding), apply mechanical prophylaxis (intermittent pneumatic compression). Inpatient glucose target 140-180 mg/dL. Avoid IM injections during thrombolysis/anticoagulation. Remove unnecessary lines and catheters early; retain central access only while vasopressors/ECMO required
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable >=24h off vasopressors; SaO2 >=90% on room air or stable home O2; established and tolerating definitive anticoagulation with confirmed access and affordability; no active bleeding with stable Hgb and platelet count; pain controlled on oral analgesia; ambulating at or near baseline; anticoagulation education completed with teach-back; follow-up appointments arranged and DOAC prescription filled/verified
discharge_planning: Disposition home for the majority; SNF if deconditioned/comorbid. Pharmacy-led medication reconciliation: correct DOAC agent/dose/duration, renal dosing, CYP3A4/P-gp interactions, avoid concomitant antiplatelet unless separately indicated. Follow-up: anticoagulation clinic or PCP within 1-2 weeks; hematology for unprovoked/thrombophilia; pulmonology/PH clinic at 3-6 months for CTEPH surveillance (persistent dyspnea -> TTE +/- V/Q). Patient education: adherence is critical (DOACs have short half-life), bleeding precautions, recognition of recurrent PE/DVT, do not stop abruptly. Transitions: written anticoagulation plan stating provoked vs unprovoked and stop date vs indefinite; teach-back; cancer screening and estrogen cessation if unprovoked
expected_los: Blended ~4 days: MS-DRG 176 (PE without MCC) GMLOS ~3.3 days; MS-DRG 175 (PE with MCC or acute cor pulmonale) GMLOS ~5.0 days
target_los: ~2.5-3 days blended under ideal pathway: low-risk (sPESI 0/Hestia-negative) 0-1 day via ED discharge or observation-to-home; intermediate-low 2-3 days; intermediate-high 3-4 days (front-loaded ICU/step-down); high-risk/massive 5-7+ days ICU
quality_metrics: Legacy TJC/CMS VTE measures: VTE-1 (VTE prophylaxis), VTE-2 (ICU prophylaxis), VTE-3 (anticoagulation overlap/bridging), VTE-4 (UFH by protocol/nomogram), VTE-5 (warfarin/anticoagulation discharge instructions), VTE-6 (potentially preventable hospital-acquired VTE). Process: time-to-therapeutic-anticoagulation, documented risk stratification, PERT activation rate, appropriate DOAC dosing, discharge on anticoagulation with documented duration and follow-up. Outcome: 30-day all-cause readmission, 30-day and in-hospital mortality, in-hospital major bleeding/intracranial hemorrhage rate, recurrent VTE rate; observation-vs-inpatient status appropriateness; HCAHPS
common_complications: Hemodynamic decompensation/RV failure -> mitigate with monitored bed, PERT, and predefined rescue-reperfusion protocol. Major bleeding including intracranial hemorrhage from anticoagulation/thrombolysis -> mitigate with bleeding-risk scoring, reduced-dose lysis in elderly, reversal agents on standby. Recurrent/progressive VTE -> mitigate with prompt therapeutic anticoagulation and no subtherapeutic gaps. Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia -> platelet monitoring, 4T score, switch to non-heparin agent. Contrast-induced nephropathy -> hydration, V/Q if CKD. CTEPH / post-PE syndrome (~2-4%) -> structured 3-6 month follow-up surveillance
readmission_drivers: Recurrent VTE from subtherapeutic dosing, nonadherence, or DOAC cost/access -> ensure filled prescription, education, anticoagulation clinic. Bleeding on anticoagulation -> dose/renal verification, interaction screening, early follow-up labs. Missed underlying malignancy or provoking factor -> cancer screening, hematology referral. Inadequate transition/follow-up -> early 1-2 week visit with written plan. Persistent dyspnea/CTEPH -> surveillance pathway. Suboptimal risk stratification causing premature discharge or unnecessary stay -> standardized PESI/sPESI + Hestia application
guideline_source: 2019 ESC Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of acute pulmonary embolism (ESC/ERS, 2019) -- primary; ACCP/CHEST Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease: Second Update of the CHEST Guideline (2021) -- antithrombotic management; supportive: ASH 2020 VTE guidelines and AHA/PERT Consortium statements on advanced therapy
key_citations: Konstantinides SV et al. 2019 ESC Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of acute pulmonary embolism (with ERS). Eur Heart J 2020;41(4):543-603 -- PMID 31504429, DOI 10.1093/eurheartj/ehz405; Stevens SM, Woller SC et al. Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease: Second Update of the CHEST Guideline and Expert Panel Report. Chest 2021;160(6):2247-2259 -- PMID 34352279, DOI 10.1016/j.chest.2021.07.056; Meyer G et al. (PEITHO) Fibrinolysis for Patients with Intermediate-Risk Pulmonary Embolism. N Engl J Med 2014;370:1402-1411 -- PMID 24716681, DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1302097; PEERLESS: Large-Bore Mechanical Thrombectomy vs Catheter-Directed Thrombolysis in Intermediate-Risk PE. Circulation 2024 -- DOI 10.1161/CIRCULATIONAHA.124.072364; PESI (Aujesky D et al., Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2005) and sPESI (Jimenez D et al., Arch Intern Med 2010) risk scores. Sourced via PubMed
evidence_grade: Strong for the core pathway -- anchored by Class I ESC 2019 society guideline plus GRADE-based CHEST 2021 strong recommendations for prompt anticoagulation, DOAC-preferred therapy, structured risk stratification, and home treatment of low-risk PE. Moderate for advanced reperfusion selection: systemic thrombolysis in intermediate-risk PE is reserved (PEITHO RCT showed decompensation benefit offset by major bleeding/stroke -> net harm), and catheter-directed/mechanical thrombectomy evidence rests on surrogate/composite endpoints (PEERLESS) without proven mortality benefit; PERT mortality data are observational
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MS-DRG 175 (PE with MCC or acute cor pulmonale) captures high-risk/massive and complicated intermediate-high presentations -- acute cor pulmonale, acute respiratory failure, cardiogenic/obstructive shock, thrombolysis-related intracranial or major hemorrhage, acute kidney injury -> ICU care, LOS ~5 days, higher mortality. MS-DRG 176 (PE without MCC) reflects hemodynamically stable low/intermediate-low disease managed on the floor with short LOS ~3.3 days. Acute cor pulmonale coded with the PE itself elevates to 175. Saddle-location alone does NOT change the tier -- hemodynamics, RV dysfunction, and biomarker positivity drive monitoring level, reperfusion need, complication rate, and LOS across the collapsed group
pathway_pearls: Start empiric therapeutic anticoagulation on clinical suspicion BEFORE imaging when bleeding risk is low (ESC) -- do not delay for the CTPA. Risk-stratify every PE with troponin PLUS RV imaging: intermediate-high needs a monitored bed and a predefined rescue-reperfusion trigger, NOT upfront systemic lysis (PEITHO net harm). Activate PERT early for intermediate-high/high-risk to standardize advanced-therapy decisions. Home-treat validated low-risk PE (sPESI 0 / Hestia-negative) and front-load DOAC access + adherence education to compress LOS and prevent recurrent-VTE readmissions

scope_and_volume_notes: Massive/submassive PE -> Critical Care. 175 = PE with MCC, 176 = without.

admission_criteria: Admit (inpatient) for: status epilepticus/SE (single seizure >=5 min or >=2 seizures without interictal recovery per ILAE t1); refractory or super-refractory SE; failure to return to neurologic baseline / persistent postictal encephalopathy >30-60 min; new focal deficit or depressed consciousness; first seizure with a structural/acute lesion on CT (hemorrhage, mass, stroke, edema); acute symptomatic seizure needing inpatient treatment (CNS infection, symptomatic hyponatremia <120, hypo/hyperglycemia, eclampsia, hypertensive emergency, alcohol/benzo withdrawal at risk of DTs, drug toxicity); seizure-related injury (aspiration, fracture, posterior shoulder dislocation, rhabdomyolysis); pregnancy, immunocompromise, or anticoagulation with seizure. ED discharge appropriate for a single self-limited seizure in known epilepsy back to baseline with normal exam, identified/correctable trigger, and reliable follow-up. Observation (<24h) for uncomplicated unprovoked first seizure that returned to baseline with normal CT/labs, pending outpatient EEG/MRI
risk_stratification: STESS (Status Epilepticus Severity Score, 0-6: consciousness, worst seizure type, age >=65, prior seizure history): STESS >=3 predicts higher mortality/poor outcome -> favor aggressive ICU-level treatment and cEEG; STESS 0-2 -> favorable, floor/step-down reasonable. EMSE and mSTESS are validated alternatives. First-seizure recurrence (AAN/AES 2015): 2-year recurrence risk 21-45%, higher with prior brain insult, epileptiform EEG, abnormal neuroimaging, or nocturnal seizure -> drives immediate vs deferred ASM. Screen for provoked cause: point-of-care glucose, sodium, and ASM level dictate immediate correction/reload. ESETT reminder: ~10% of presumed SE are psychogenic nonepileptic seizures -> stratify with exam, lactate, prolactin, cEEG
initial_workup_labs: Immediate point-of-care glucose (exclude hypoglycemia); CMP/BMP (Na, Ca, Mg, phosphate, glucose, BUN/Cr, LFTs); CBC; serum ASM levels if on therapy (phenytoin, valproate, levetiracetam, carbamazepine) -> reload if subtherapeutic; CK (rhabdomyolysis) and consider troponin; venous lactate and serum prolactin at 10-20 min (support true convulsion vs PNES); urine + serum toxicology, serum ethanol, ammonia; beta-hCG in women of childbearing age (eclampsia); ABG if prolonged/airway compromise. LP if fever, meningismus, immunocompromise, or persistent AMS (after imaging) -> CSF cell count, glucose/protein, HSV PCR, cultures, and start empiric acyclovir + ceftriaxone/vancomycin pending results. Autoimmune/paraneoplastic encephalitis antibody panel (anti-NMDA-R, LGI1, CASPR2, GABA-B) if new-onset refractory SE (NORSE)/cryptogenic
initial_imaging_dx: Noncontrast CT head emergently (target <30-60 min) for first seizure, trauma, focal deficit, persistent AMS, anticoagulation, or immunocompromise. MRI brain with/without contrast (higher yield for epileptogenic/structural lesion) for admitted first seizure within 24-48h or expedited outpatient. EEG: routine EEG within 24h for first-seizure workup; STAT/urgent continuous EEG (cEEG >=24-48h) if patient does not return to baseline after convulsions cease, is comatose, or has refractory SE -- nonconvulsive SE persists in up to ~20-48% of convulsive SE patients and is missed without cEEG. CTA/CTV if vascular etiology suspected; LP as above for suspected CNS infection/autoimmune
time_critical_interventions: SE is time-critical (ILAE t1=5 min, t2=30 min for convulsive SE). Stabilization 0-5 min (ABCs, O2, IV access, POC glucose, thiamine 100 mg IV before dextrose if malnourished/alcohol). Emergent initial therapy 5-20 min: full-dose benzodiazepine -- IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg (max 4 mg/dose, may repeat x1) OR IM midazolam 10 mg if no IV (RAMPART: IM midazolam 73.4% vs IV lorazepam 63.4% seizure cessation). Urgent-control/second-line ASM 20-40 min if seizing continues. Refractory SE (fails benzo + 1 ASM, ~40-60 min): continuous anesthetic infusion + intubation + cEEG. Overarching target: terminate seizures within 60 min; avoid benzodiazepine underdosing (a documented common error in ESETT)
initial_management: First-line benzodiazepine (AES Level A): IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg (max 4 mg), may repeat once; IM midazolam 10 mg (no IV); IV diazepam 0.15-0.2 mg/kg (max 10 mg). Second-line/urgent-control ASM -- ESETT-proven equivalent (~45-47% each), choose by comorbidity/availability and give FULL weight-based dose: IV levetiracetam 60 mg/kg (max 4500 mg), IV fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg (max 1500 PE, cardiac monitoring for hypotension/arrhythmia), or IV valproate 40 mg/kg (max 3000 mg; avoid in hepatic disease/pregnancy). Refractory SE: continuous IV anesthetics -- midazolam 0.2 mg/kg load then 0.05-2 mg/kg/h, propofol, or pentobarbital, titrate to seizure/burst-suppression on cEEG; ketamine adjunct. Treat provokers: D50 (after thiamine), hypertonic 3% saline for symptomatic hyponatremia, magnesium sulfate 4-6 g IV for eclampsia, pyridoxine for INH toxicity. Empiric acyclovir + antibiotics if CNS infection suspected; reload home ASM if known epilepsy with subtherapeutic level
day1_milestones: Day 1: terminate seizures and confirm cessation (cEEG if not waking to exclude nonconvulsive SE); secure airway/hemodynamics if intubated. Establish etiology (CT/MRI, labs, LP as indicated). Load and initiate maintenance ASM (levetiracetam is a common first maintenance agent given tolerability/interactions). Neurology/epileptology consult; neurocritical care if RSE. Treat the underlying cause (infection, metabolic, withdrawal, eclampsia). Institute seizure/fall/aspiration precautions, VTE prophylaxis, and glucose/electrolyte correction; screen/treat rhabdomyolysis with IV fluids
day2_milestones: Day 2: if refractory SE was controlled, begin weaning anesthetic infusion after >=24-48h seizure-free on cEEG. Transition IV to enteral/oral ASM and confirm therapeutic level/adequate dose. Complete MRI brain and any pending metabolic/infectious/autoimmune workup. Extubate when appropriate; SLP bedside swallow screen before oral intake; PT/OT evaluation. Reassess mental status trajectory. Pharmacy-led medication reconciliation with adherence counseling; de-escalate empiric antimicrobials if infection excluded
day3plus_milestones: Day 3+: seizure-free on a stable oral/enteral regimen and returned to (or near) neurologic baseline; off continuous infusions and off ICU/telemetry. Finalize etiologic diagnosis and long-term ASM plan; review EEG/MRI. Arrange epilepsy/neurology follow-up. Deliver driving-restriction counseling with mandatory state reporting where required, SUDEP counseling, seizure first-aid and rescue-medication (intranasal midazolam) education. Refractory/super-refractory or autoimmune (NORSE) cases: continued ICU care, immunotherapy (steroids/IVIG/rituximab), and prolonged stay
consults_multidisciplinary: Neurology/epileptology early (Day 0-1) for EEG interpretation and ASM strategy; neurocritical care/intensivist for SE/RSE; neurosurgery for resectable/structural lesion or tumor; infectious disease for CNS infection; clinical pharmacy for ASM loading, levels, and interactions; PT/OT and speech-language pathology (swallow) before oral intake; case management/social work for disposition, ASM affordability, adherence, driving, and substance-use referral; nutrition for prolonged/enteral feeding; psychiatry/behavioral health if psychogenic nonepileptic seizures (~10% of presumed SE) or substance use; obstetrics for eclampsia
monitoring_level: ICU/neuro-ICU: status epilepticus, refractory/super-refractory SE, continuous anesthetic infusions, intubation/airway compromise, cEEG monitoring for nonconvulsive SE, hemodynamic instability. Step-down/telemetry: post-SE stabilization, cardiac monitoring during fosphenytoin loading (hypotension/arrhythmia), high aspiration risk, frequent neuro checks. Floor: single or breakthrough seizure back to baseline on stable ASM. Escalation triggers: recurrent or ongoing seizure activity, declining GCS, respiratory depression, hypotension during ASM loading, new focal deficit, or nonconvulsive SE on cEEG
nutrition_mobility_vte: NPO with aspiration precautions until alert with a passed swallow screen; SLP evaluation before PO; advance diet as mentation clears; enteral nutrition if intubated/prolonged. Early mobilization once seizure-free and hemodynamically stable. VTE prophylaxis: mechanical (SCDs) initially, add pharmacologic (enoxaparin or heparin) once intracranial hemorrhage excluded and no active bleeding. Tight electrolyte correction (Na, Mg, phosphate, Ca) and glucose control; aggressive IV fluids for rhabdomyolysis/CK elevation. Remove Foley and unnecessary central lines early to reduce CAUTI/CLABSI
discharge_criteria: Seizure-free interval appropriate to severity (typically >=24h and off IV/anesthetic infusions); returned to neurologic baseline or a stable new baseline; on a stable oral/enteral ASM regimen and tolerating PO, with therapeutic level where applicable; underlying provoking cause identified and treated/corrected; hemodynamically stable with secure airway and no active complication (aspiration pneumonia, ongoing rhabdomyolysis); safety plan, rescue medication, and follow-up in place
discharge_planning: Disposition: home for most; acute rehab/SNF for deconditioning or persistent deficits; LTAC for prolonged refractory SE. Medication reconciliation emphasizing ASM adherence (nonadherence/subtherapeutic levels is the leading readmission driver) and prescribe a rescue benzodiazepine (intranasal/rectal) with a written seizure action plan. Follow-up: neurology/epilepsy within 1-2 weeks; complete outpatient EEG/MRI if not done inpatient. Education: seizure first aid, triggers (sleep deprivation, alcohol, missed doses, infection), and SUDEP counseling. Driving-restriction counseling plus mandatory state reporting where required; safety precautions (heights, swimming, bathing). Transitions: notify PCP, confirm pharmacy access/affordability, and close the loop on pending results
expected_los: Blended typical 3-4 days. MS-DRG 101 (seizures without MCC) CMS GMLOS ~2.6-2.9 days; MS-DRG 100 (seizures with MCC) CMS GMLOS ~4.0-4.5 days. Uncomplicated first-seizure or breakthrough-seizure workup 1-2 days; provoked seizure with correctable cause 2-3 days; refractory/super-refractory SE 7-21+ days (ICU-driven)
target_los: Optimized ~2-3 days blended. Uncomplicated breakthrough/first seizure back to baseline: <=1-2 days via expedited CT + inpatient or fast-track outpatient EEG/MRI and teleneurology. Provoked seizure with treatable trigger: 2-3 days. Convulsive SE responding to first/second-line therapy: 3-4 days. Refractory SE remains ICU-length-limited regardless of pathway
quality_metrics: AAN Epilepsy Quality Measurement Set (documented seizure type and frequency, etiology, ASM therapy, counseling on ASM side effects, SUDEP counseling, safety counseling, and for women of childbearing potential counseling on teratogenic ASM risk and folic acid). Process metrics: door-to-benzodiazepine time and time-to-second-line ASM; adequate weight-based benzodiazepine dosing (anti-underdosing); cEEG utilization for persistent AMS after convulsion. Outcome metrics: 30-day readmission and ED-revisit rate, ICU utilization, LOS, in-hospital mortality. Note: no seizure-specific CMS core measure exists (unlike SEP-1 for sepsis or GWTG for stroke), so internal registry/process metrics govern
common_complications: Aspiration pneumonia/pneumonitis (mitigate: NPO, aspiration precautions, swallow screen); benzodiazepine/anesthetic-induced respiratory depression and hypotension (mitigate: appropriate dosing, monitored setting, airway readiness); persistent nonconvulsive SE after convulsions stop (mitigate: cEEG); rhabdomyolysis and AKI (mitigate: IV fluids, CK monitoring); fosphenytoin-related hypotension/arrhythmia (mitigate: cardiac monitoring, controlled infusion rate); seizure-related trauma; hyperthermia; prolonged intubation with VAP and ICU delirium in RSE; recurrent seizures from ASM underdosing/subtherapeutic levels
readmission_drivers: ASM nonadherence/subtherapeutic levels (top driver) -> adherence counseling, regimen simplification, affordability check, rescue plan; inadequate or delayed outpatient follow-up and pending EEG/MRI -> schedule neurology visit and testing before discharge, teleneurology; ongoing alcohol/substance use and withdrawal -> social work and addiction referral; undiagnosed structural or autoimmune etiology -> complete inpatient workup; modifiable triggers (sleep deprivation, infection, missed doses); breakthrough seizures during ASM titration; psychogenic nonepileptic seizures misclassified as epilepsy -> psychiatry referral and avoid unnecessary ASM escalation
guideline_source: American Epilepsy Society (AES) 2016 -- Evidence-Based Guideline: Treatment of Convulsive Status Epilepticus (Glauser et al); Neurocritical Care Society (NCS) 2012 -- Guidelines for the Evaluation and Management of Status Epilepticus (Brophy et al); American Academy of Neurology / American Epilepsy Society (AAN/AES) 2015 -- Management of an Unprovoked First Seizure in Adults (Krumholz et al); ILAE 2015 Task Force -- Definition and Classification of Status Epilepticus (Trinka et al). All remain the current authoritative US references as of 2026
key_citations: Per PubMed: Glauser T, et al. Evidence-Based Guideline: Treatment of Convulsive Status Epilepticus in Children and Adults. Epilepsy Curr. 2016;16(1):48-61. PMID 26900382, https://doi.org/10.5698/1535-7597-16.1.48. Brophy GM, et al. Guidelines for the Evaluation and Management of Status Epilepticus. Neurocrit Care. 2012;17(1):3-23. PMID 22528274, https://doi.org/10.1007/s12028-012-9695-z. Krumholz A, et al. Management of an unprovoked first seizure in adults (AAN/AES). Neurology. 2015;84(16):1705-13. PMID 25901057, https://doi.org/10.1212/WNL.0000000000001487. Kapur J, et al. Randomized Trial of Three Anticonvulsant Medications for Status Epilepticus (ESETT). N Engl J Med. 2019;381(22):2103-13. PMID 31774955, https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1905795. Silbergleit R, et al. Intramuscular versus intravenous therapy for prehospital status epilepticus (RAMPART). N Engl J Med. 2012;366(7):591-600. PMID 22335736, https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1107494. Trinka E, et al. A definition and classification of status epilepticus (ILAE). Epilepsia. 2015;56(10):1515-23. PMID 26336950, https://doi.org/10.1111/epi.13121
evidence_grade: Strong for the acute SE algorithm: first-line benzodiazepines are AES Level A backed by RAMPART Class I RCT, and second-line ASM equivalence (levetiracetam=fosphenytoin=valproate, ~45-47%) is ESETT Class I RCT. Moderate-to-strong for the overall society guideline framework (AES 2016, NCS 2012, AAN/AES 2015). Consensus/expert opinion (limited RCT evidence) for third-line/refractory and super-refractory SE management and for inpatient LOS/disposition specifics. Overall: Strong for time-critical emergent management; Moderate/consensus for refractory-phase and pathway logistics
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed group splits into MS-DRG 100 (seizures with MCC) and 101 (without MCC). MCC/CC drivers that shift the pathway: status epilepticus, acute respiratory failure/intubation, aspiration pneumonia, metabolic/toxic encephalopathy or coma, sepsis, acute kidney injury, and rhabdomyolysis. MCC cases require ICU, cEEG, and often anesthetic infusions, lengthening LOS (~4.3 vs ~2.7-day GMLOS) and raising mortality (SE ~9-20%, higher in refractory SE). Without-MCC cases are typically single/breakthrough seizures or first-seizure workups managed on the floor with short stays. Coding accuracy matters: capture SE, encephalopathy, aspiration pneumonia, and respiratory failure to reflect true acuity and correct DRG assignment
pathway_pearls: 1) Treat SE as time-critical -- give a FULL weight-based benzodiazepine at 5 min (lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg or IM midazolam 10 mg when no IV per RAMPART); benzodiazepine underdosing is the most common and most consequential error (ESETT). 2) If the patient stops convulsing but does not wake up, get continuous EEG -- nonconvulsive SE is common and frequently missed. 3) Second-line ASM choice does not change efficacy (ESETT: levetiracetam=fosphenytoin=valproate); select by comorbidity/availability and dose fully rather than debating the agent. 4) ASM nonadherence/subtherapeutic levels is the top readmission lever -- reconcile medications, secure fast neurology follow-up, provide a rescue plan, and deliver driving/SUDEP counseling before discharge

scope_and_volume_notes: Includes status epilepticus (-> Critical Care) and first-time seizure workups; ED entry.

condition: Back & Neck Procedures except Spinal Fusion (Discectomy/Laminectomy)
admission_criteria: Most single/2-level lumbar microdiscectomy or laminectomy/decompression is now ambulatory/23h — inpatient admission is NOT automatic. Objective INPATIENT indications: emergent/urgent pathology — cauda equina syndrome (saddle anesthesia, post-void residual >100-200 mL, bowel/bladder dysfunction) or acute progressive/severe motor deficit (e.g., foot drop) needing decompression within 24-48h; multilevel (>=3 levels) or prolonged/high-blood-loss decompression; cervical laminectomy/laminoplasty with myelopathy (airway/respiratory watch); significant comorbidity (ASA III-IV, decompensated cardiac/pulmonary disease, moderate-severe OSA on CPAP, poorly controlled DM, CKD/dialysis); intraoperative complication (durotomy/CSF leak with repair, new deficit, hemodynamic instability); failure of PACU discharge milestones (uncontrolled pain, urinary retention, orthostasis, cannot ambulate); frailty/advanced age with poor home support or stairs. ED discharge/observation is appropriate for isolated axial back pain, stable radiculopathy without red flags, or uncomplicated same-day surgical candidates.
risk_stratification: ASA Physical Status (I-IV) drives monitoring/site of care; mFI-5 modified frailty index (>=2 = elevated complication/LOS risk) and Risk Analysis Index (RAI) for frailty; Charlson Comorbidity Index for comorbidity burden; Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI >=2, or NSQIP-MICA) for perioperative cardiac risk; STOP-BANG for OSA (>=5 high risk -> continuous pulse ox/CPAP, opioid caution); Caprini VTE score (>=5 high risk -> weigh chemoprophylaxis against epidural-hematoma risk); cauda equina screen: post-void residual >100-200 mL + saddle anesthesia -> STAT MRI + urgent decompression; mJOA for cervical myelopathy (mild >=15, moderate 12-14, severe <12 -> decompression for moderate-severe); Oswestry Disability Index (ODI) and NRS/VAS leg pain for surgical candidacy/PROs (not for acute triage).
initial_workup_labs: CBC (baseline Hgb, WBC); BMP (renal function, glucose); PT/INR + aPTT if anticoagulated or hepatic disease; type & screen for multilevel/higher-blood-loss cases; HbA1c for glycemic optimization (target <7-8%; >8-9% prompts optimization or deferral of elective surgery); ESR/CRP if infection/discitis suspected; urinalysis to exclude active UTI before elective decompression/implant; beta-hCG in women of childbearing age; MRSA nares screen with chlorhexidine/mupirocin decolonization per SSI bundle; post-void residual bladder scan if cauda equina suspected; albumin/prealbumin if malnutrition suspected (optimize preop).
initial_imaging_dx: MRI of the symptomatic spine WITHOUT contrast is the gold standard, confirming disc herniation or central/lateral-recess/foraminal stenosis concordant with the exam (STAT/within hours for cauda equina or acute progressive deficit; elective otherwise); CT myelogram if MRI contraindicated (pacemaker, non-MRI hardware, severe claustrophobia); standing AP/lateral plus flexion-extension radiographs to exclude instability/spondylolisthesis (dynamic instability redirects to fusion, outside this DRG); EMG/NCS optional to clarify level or differentiate peripheral neuropathy; intraoperative neuromonitoring (SSEP/EMG/MEP) for cervical/myelopathy and multilevel cases; intraoperative fluoroscopy/localization radiograph to confirm the correct level (wrong-level prevention).
time_critical_interventions: Cauda equina syndrome or acute/progressive severe motor deficit -> surgical decompression within 24-48h of onset (earlier decompression favors bladder/motor recovery); prophylactic antibiotic (cefazolin 2g, 3g if >120kg; vancomycin or clindamycin for beta-lactam allergy/MRSA) within 60 min before incision, redosed for duration/blood loss; maintain intraoperative normothermia (>=36C) and glucose <180-200 mg/dL (SSI bundle); postoperative spinal epidural hematoma (new/worsening deficit plus severe incisional pain within hours) -> STAT MRI and emergent surgical evacuation (target <6-8h to preserve neurologic function); postoperative cauda equina from re-herniation/hematoma -> emergent re-exploration; deep SSI/epidural abscess -> urgent debridement plus culture-directed antibiotics. Elective decompression is otherwise not time-critical.
initial_management: Antibiotic prophylaxis cefazolin (vancomycin/clindamycin for allergy/MRSA) within 60 min of incision. ERAS multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia: scheduled acetaminophen + NSAID (ketorolac/ibuprofen — permissible without fusion), preop gabapentinoid (gabapentin/pregabalin; dose-reduce or avoid in elderly/OSA for sedation-respiratory risk), local wound infiltration/liposomal bupivacaine +/- erector spinae plane block, IV/epidural steroid for nerve-root inflammation, short-acting oral opioid (oxycodone) PRN as rescue only, muscle relaxant PRN. PONV prophylaxis dexamethasone + ondansetron. Intraoperative tranexamic acid for multilevel to limit blood loss; goal-directed euvolemia (avoid fluid overload); maintain MAP for cord/root perfusion in myelopathy. Restrictive transfusion threshold Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 with active cardiac disease) — rarely needed for single-level. Perioperative anticoagulation per ASRA: hold warfarin ~5d (INR <1.4), DOACs 48-72h, therapeutic LMWH 24h; resume based on hemostasis/epidural-hematoma risk. Source control: watertight primary dural repair +/- sealant/lumbar drain for incidental durotomy; debridement for infection.
day1_milestones: POD0: extubate in OR and recover in PACU; verify intact/improved motor-sensory exam with scheduled neuro checks (q1-4h); EARLY mobilization the same day (ambulate within 3-6h of recovery — the ERAS cornerstone); advance to regular diet as tolerated; transition to oral multimodal analgesia; assess and document spontaneous voiding (screen for urinary retention with bladder scan); remove urinary catheter POD0 if one was placed; confirm dry wound/dressing with no CSF leak or expanding hematoma; begin bowel regimen; for uncomplicated single/2-level decompression in fit patients, meet same-day discharge criteria and discharge POD0 (outpatient pathway).
day2_milestones: POD1: independent or baseline ambulation including stairs if needed (PT evaluation/clearance if deconditioned); all analgesia oral on an opioid-sparing regimen; normal spontaneous voiding without retention; wound inspected with no CSF leak/hematoma/infection signs; bowel regimen effective (prevent opioid-induced constipation); neurologic exam stable; comorbidities stabilized; most single/2-level and many multilevel decompressions meet discharge criteria and go home POD1.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ (reserved for multilevel, comorbid, or complicated cases): manage incidental durotomy/CSF leak (once watertight repair is achieved, early mobilization is now supported over prolonged bed rest; monitor for positional headache/pseudomeningocele), refractory pain, or medical comorbidity; serial neuro exams; wound surveillance for SSI; confirm independent mobility, adequate oral analgesia, spontaneous voiding, and stable exam before discharge; escalate to STAT MRI +/- return-to-OR for any new deficit (hematoma/re-herniation); target discharge by POD2-3 for CC-tier and POD3-5 for MCC/device-tier cases.
consults_multidisciplinary: Physical therapy (POD0-1: mobility, gait, stairs, home safety, activity precautions) +/- occupational therapy for ADLs; care management/social work early (POD0) for disposition, home health, DME (walker), and transport; pharmacy for multimodal analgesia/opioid stewardship and anticoagulation management; hospitalist/internal medicine co-management for ASA III-IV or comorbidity (cardiac, DM, CKD, OSA); acute pain service/anesthesiology for opioid-tolerant or refractory pain; sleep medicine/pulmonary for significant OSA; endocrinology/diabetes for glucose optimization; nutrition if malnourished; neurology if a new/unexplained deficit; PM&R/physiatry for inpatient rehab planning when deconditioned.
monitoring_level: Most patients -> general surgical floor/med-surg with routine vitals plus scheduled neurovascular checks. Telemetry for significant cardiac disease/arrhythmia. Continuous pulse oximetry or step-down for moderate-severe OSA (STOP-BANG >=5) on opioids/gabapentinoids, high opioid requirement, or cervical laminectomy/laminoplasty (airway edema, respiratory watch). ICU/step-down reserved for hemodynamic instability, major intraoperative blood loss, respiratory failure, cervical myelopathy with respiratory compromise, or postoperative hematoma surveillance. Escalation triggers: new/progressive motor-sensory deficit, severe unrelenting incisional pain (hematoma), respiratory depression/hypoxia (especially OSA), urinary retention, hemodynamic instability, fever/wound drainage.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: regular diet POD0 as tolerated (no routine ileus); resume home medications. Mobility: early mobilization POD0 within hours is the ERAS cornerstone and the primary VTE prevention — avoid prolonged bed rest (early ambulation is now supported even after watertight durotomy repair). VTE: mechanical prophylaxis (intermittent pneumatic compression) for all plus early ambulation; pharmacologic prophylaxis is individualized and balanced against catastrophic spinal epidural hematoma — reserve LMWH/UFH for high VTE risk (Caprini >=5, prior VTE, malignancy, prolonged immobility, multilevel), typically starting 24-48h postop once hemostasis is assured and per surgeon. Glucose: target 140-180 mg/dL with insulin correction; avoid >180-200 (SSI risk). Lines/catheters: remove urinary catheter POD0-1 (CAUTI prevention), saline-lock IV early; a surgical drain (if placed for large decompression/durotomy) is removed when output is low.
discharge_criteria: Pain controlled on oral analgesics; ambulating safely and independently (or at pre-op baseline with an assistive device), including stairs if required for home; tolerating oral intake; spontaneous voiding without significant post-void residual/retention; neurologic exam stable or improved with no new deficit; wound dry/intact with no CSF leak, hematoma, or infection signs; afebrile with stable vital signs; safe disposition and support arranged; patient/caregiver understands wound care, activity restrictions, and red-flag return precautions.
discharge_planning: Disposition home in >90% (home health for wound checks/therapy when needed; SNF/acute rehab only for deconditioned or unsupported patients); medication reconciliation with an opioid-sparing regimen plus scheduled acetaminophen/NSAID, bowel regimen, opioid-taper plan, and timed resumption of home anticoagulation; schedule surgeon follow-up/wound check at ~2 weeks (suture/staple removal) and an outpatient PT referral; patient education on activity precautions (avoid bending/lifting/twisting, lifting limit ~5-10 lb, walking encouraged, no driving while on opioids), incision care, and red flags (fever, wound drainage/dehiscence, positional headache or clear drainage = CSF leak, new/worsening weakness or numbness, new bowel/bladder dysfunction -> return immediately); opioid-safety counseling plus PDMP review; return-to-work/activity timeline.
expected_los: Inpatient GMLOS by current 3-tier grouping: DRG 520 (no CC/MCC) ~1.5-2 days; DRG 519 (CC) ~2.5-3 days; DRG 518 (MCC or disc device/neurostimulator) ~4-5 days; blended inpatient GMLOS ~2-3 days. Note that a large and growing share are performed outpatient/same-day (0-day stays not captured in inpatient GMLOS).
target_los: Same-day discharge (outpatient/23h) for uncomplicated single- or two-level lumbar microdiscectomy/laminectomy in appropriately selected fit patients; <=1 day for uncomplicated admitted cases (DRG 520); 1-2 days with CC (DRG 519); 2-3 days for MCC/complicated/device cases (DRG 518). Overall optimized target ~1 day with maximal outpatient shift.
quality_metrics: Surgical site infection rate (SCIP-derived: antibiotic timing/selection/discontinuation, normothermia, perioperative glucose); 30-day readmission and 30-day unplanned reoperation/return-to-OR (hematoma, recurrent herniation, CSF leak); iatrogenic durotomy rate; wrong-level surgery (never event); perioperative VTE/PE (AHRQ PSI-12); PSI-90 composite and HACs (CAUTI, falls); patient-reported outcomes (ODI, NRS/VAS leg pain, PROMIS) and MCID achievement; opioid stewardship (discharge morphine-milligram-equivalents, new persistent opioid use); ERAS protocol compliance and same-day-discharge/ambulatory conversion rate; LOS index; HCAHPS; 30-day mortality (very low).
common_complications: Incidental durotomy/CSF leak (most common intraoperative event; ~3-9%, higher in stenosis/revision) -> intraoperative watertight primary repair +/- dural sealant/lumbar drain, with early mobilization supported once the repair is watertight; recurrent/residual disc herniation -> possible reoperation; new nerve-root injury/neurologic deficit; surgical site infection/discitis -> SSI-bundle prevention, culture-directed antibiotics +/- debridement; postoperative spinal epidural hematoma (rare, emergent) -> STAT MRI + evacuation; urinary retention (elderly, opioids, anesthesia) -> bladder scan/straight cath, early Foley removal; PONV and opioid-related respiratory depression (mitigated by multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia and OSA precautions); pseudomeningocele/positional headache from dural tear; DVT/PE (low; mitigated by early mobilization + mechanical prophylaxis).
readmission_drivers: Surgical site infection/wound complications (drainage, dehiscence, discitis) — the leading driver; CSF leak/pseudomeningocele with positional headache; uncontrolled pain; recurrent disc herniation or new/worsening neurologic deficit; urinary retention; VTE; comorbidity decompensation. Prevention levers: SSI-prevention bundle (antibiotic timing, decolonization, glucose, normothermia), meticulous watertight dural closure, multimodal analgesia with a clear opioid-taper plan, early (~2-week) surgeon wound check plus a nurse/telehealth follow-up call, structured patient education on red flags, and careful patient selection/readiness screening for same-day discharge.
guideline_source: North American Spine Society (NASS) Evidence-Based Clinical Guidelines — Lumbar Disc Herniation with Radiculopathy (2012; Spine J 2014) and Degenerative Lumbar Spinal Stenosis update (2011; Spine J 2013); ERAS Society Consensus Statement for Perioperative Care in Lumbar Spinal Fusion (2021) — extrapolated to non-fusion decompression; ASRA Anticoagulation in Regional Anesthesia/Pain guideline (4th ed, 2018) for perioperative anticoagulation timing; supported by the SPORT trials (surgical efficacy) and CDC/SCIP SSI-prevention standards.
key_citations: NASS: Kreiner DS et al. Evidence-based guideline for diagnosis/treatment of lumbar disc herniation with radiculopathy. Spine J 2014;14(1):180-91 (PMID 24239490; DOI 10.1016/j.spinee.2013.08.003). NASS: Kreiner DS et al. Evidence-based guideline for degenerative lumbar spinal stenosis (update). Spine J 2013;13(7):734-43 (PMID 23830297; DOI 10.1016/j.spinee.2012.11.059). ERAS: Debono B et al. Consensus statement for perioperative care in lumbar spinal fusion: ERAS Society recommendations. Spine J 2021;21(5):729-752 (PMID 33444664; DOI 10.1016/j.spinee.2021.01.001). SPORT (disc herniation RCT): Weinstein JN et al. JAMA 2006;296(20):2441-50 (PMID 17119140; DOI 10.1001/jama.296.20.2441). SPORT (spinal stenosis): Weinstein JN et al. N Engl J Med 2008;358(8):794-810 (PMID 18287602; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa0707136). Same-day discharge lumbar decompression systematic review: Goacher E et al. Brain Spine 2022;2:100888 (PMID 36248095; DOI 10.1016/j.bas.2022.100888). Citations retrieved via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Moderate overall. Surgical efficacy is Level I (SPORT RCTs) with NASS Class I/II society guidelines for diagnosis/indication; however the INPATIENT perioperative pathway (ERAS elements, VTE strategy, same-day discharge) is largely extrapolated from the ERAS lumbar-FUSION consensus (GRADE strong-to-moderate) and observational/systematic-review data, as no dedicated ERAS guideline exists for decompression without fusion — perioperative specifics are therefore moderate/consensus-level.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Current 3-tier (FY2015+): DRG 520 no CC/MCC = fit patient, uncomplicated single/2-level decompression, outpatient-to-1-day, floor-level care; DRG 519 with CC = comorbidity (DM, COPD, CKD) or minor complication (durotomy, urinary retention, postop anemia) -> +1-2 days, telemetry/co-management; DRG 518 with MCC OR disc device OR neurostimulator = major complication (epidural hematoma, PE, sepsis, respiratory/renal failure) OR device implant (artificial disc replacement, spinal cord stimulator) -> longest LOS (4-5+ days), possible step-down/ICU, highest cost — note device implantation elevates to 518 by grouper logic independent of clinical acuity. Predecessor 2-tier DRG 490 (w CC/MCC)/491 (w/o CC/MCC) were used through FY2014. Cauda equina and cervical myelopathy cases skew to higher-acuity tiers.
pathway_pearls: 1) Patient selection/shared decision-making: SPORT shows surgery speeds relief of radiculopathy/stenosis (2-yr outcomes converge) — operate for concordant MRI plus persistent radiculopathy (>6-12 wk) or urgently for cauda equina/progressive deficit, not for isolated axial back pain. 2) Default to an outpatient/same-day ERAS pathway for single-level uncomplicated decompression; reserve inpatient admission for multilevel, significant comorbidity (OSA/cardiac), cauda equina, intraoperative complication, or failed PACU milestones — the single biggest LOS/cost lever. 3) Opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia + early POD0 mobilization + early urinary-catheter removal drive faster, safer discharge. 4) Do not over-anticoagulate — balance VTE prophylaxis against catastrophic spinal epidural hematoma (mechanical + early ambulation for most; chemoprophylaxis only for high Caprini risk, timed with the surgeon); and treat cauda equina as the time-critical emergency (elevated PVR/saddle anesthesia -> STAT MRI -> decompress within 24-48h).
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Vertebral discectomy is the 10th most frequent OR procedure (HCUP: 0.29M, 2018). Laminectomy/discectomy/decompression without fusion; large share now outpatient.
