admission_criteria: Admit/observe based on identified serious cause or high short-term risk, NOT on the symptom alone (2017 ACC/AHA/HRS Class I: hospitalize when a serious medical condition potentially relevant to syncope is found). INPATIENT (>=2-midnight expectation): documented significant arrhythmia (sustained VT, high-grade/complete AV block, symptomatic bradycardia, sinus pause >3s), acute ischemia/positive troponin, structural disease (severe AS, HCM, LVEF <=35%, prior MI/HFrEF), exertional or supine syncope, syncope with palpitations/family history of SCD or channelopathy, suspected PE, significant injury/GI bleed, or new focal neuro deficit. OBSERVATION/short-stay telemetry (<24-48h): intermediate risk needing expedited monitoring/echo (CSRS medium risk, unexplained syncope with cardiac risk factors). ED DISCHARGE: CSRS very-low/low (score <=0), clear reflex/vasovagal or orthostatic mechanism, normal ECG, normal troponin, no structural heart disease
risk_stratification: Canadian Syncope Risk Score (CSRS) - primary validated tool (AUC 0.91): 9 predictors - vasovagal predisposition -1, heart disease +1, any ED SBP <90 or >180 +2, troponin >99th percentile +2, abnormal QRS axis (<-30 or >100 deg) +1, QRS >130 ms +1, QTc >480 ms +2, ED diagnosis vasovagal -2, ED diagnosis cardiac +2 (range -3 to +11). Categories drive disposition: very-low (-3 to -2, 0.4-0.7% 30-day serious outcome) and low (-1 to 0, 1.2-1.9%) = discharge; medium (1-3, 3.1-8.1%) = observation + consider 15-day outpatient monitor; high (4-5, 12.9-19.7%) and very-high (6-11, up to ~51%) = admit/telemetry; threshold <=0 = 97.8% sensitivity for 30-day serious outcome. Adjuncts: San Francisco Syncope Rule (CHESS: CHF, Hct <30%, abnormal ECG, SOB, SBP <90 - any positive = high risk); OESIL (age >65, CV history, no prodrome, abnormal ECG; >=2 = higher mortality)
initial_workup_labs: 12-lead ECG immediately on arrival (Class I, single highest-yield test - screen for arrhythmia, ischemia, long/short QT, pre-excitation/WPW, Brugada, epsilon wave, conduction disease); high-sensitivity troponin (exclude ACS; CSRS +2 if >99th percentile); CBC (Hct <30% flags anemia/occult GI bleed = SFSR high-risk); BMP (electrolytes, renal, glucose); point-of-care glucose (hypoglycemia as reversible cause); orthostatic vital signs (supine then standing at 1 and 3 min - SBP drop >=20 or DBP drop >=10 mmHg = orthostatic hypotension); targeted hCG in women of childbearing age; consider BNP/D-dimer only if clinically indicated. Routine broad lab panels are NOT recommended (Class III No Benefit) - target testing to history/exam/ECG
initial_imaging_dx: Continuous cardiac (telemetry) monitoring started immediately in ED - most actionable arrhythmias declared within 2h in low-risk and within 6h in medium/high-risk patients. TTE only when structural heart disease suspected by history/exam/ECG (murmur, HF, abnormal ECG) - Class IIa, NOT routine. Exercise/stress testing for exertional syncope (Class IIa). Ambulatory monitoring escalated by risk: external loop/patch monitor 2-4 weeks for medium risk, implantable loop recorder (ILR) for recurrent, infrequent, unexplained syncope with suspected arrhythmia (Class IIa). Tilt-table testing for suspected reflex/orthostatic syncope when diagnosis unclear (Class IIa). EP study for select structural/conduction disease. AVOID routine head CT, carotid ultrasound, EEG, and brain MRI in atraumatic syncope without focal neuro signs (Class III No Benefit - top low-value overuse target)
time_critical_interventions: ECG within 10 min of arrival to triage ACS/malignant arrhythmia (activate STEMI pathway if ischemia). Continuous monitoring initiated at once to capture rhythm during any recurrence. Unstable arrhythmia = immediate ACLS: atropine/transcutaneous pacing for symptomatic bradycardia or high-grade AV block; synchronized cardioversion for unstable tachyarrhythmia. Expedite echo/CTPA when critical aortic stenosis or PE suspected. There is no thrombolytic/door-to-balloon-style clock for uncomplicated syncope itself - time-criticality is rapid detection of the dangerous cause; use CSRS-directed monitored windows (arrhythmia essentially excluded by ~2h in low-risk, ~6h in medium/high-risk) to drive prompt disposition rather than open-ended admission
initial_management: Treat the identified CAUSE, not the symptom. Reflex/vasovagal: patient education and reassurance (Class I), physical counterpressure maneuvers (Class IIa), increased salt/fluid; for recurrent - midodrine (alpha-1 agonist, Class IIb), fludrocortisone, beta-blocker only if age >=42 (POST trial), consider SSRI; dual-chamber pacing only for select patients >=40y with recurrent reflex syncope and documented asystolic pauses (Class IIb). Orthostatic hypotension: isotonic IV/oral volume repletion (Class I acute), deprescribe offending agents (Class IIa), midodrine/droxidopa/fludrocortisone and compression garments for chronic neurogenic OH. Cardiac/arrhythmic: rhythm-specific - permanent pacemaker for symptomatic bradyarrhythmia/high-grade AVB, ICD if SCD criteria (LVEF <=35%, inherited arrhythmia), antiarrhythmics/ablation for tachyarrhythmia, ACS pathway for ischemia. Hold/adjust antihypertensives, diuretics, and QT-prolonging drugs. If GI-bleed cause: restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL
day1_milestones: Complete history/physical, orthostatic vitals, 12-lead ECG, hs-troponin, and targeted labs; calculate and DOCUMENT CSRS. Start continuous telemetry and risk-stratify to discharge vs observation vs inpatient. Obtain TTE within 24h only if structural disease suspected. Perform medication reconciliation to identify culprit hypotensive/bradycardic/QT-prolonging drugs. Fall/injury assessment and screen. Correct reversible triggers (volume, electrolytes, glucose, drug adjustment). For very-low/low CSRS with normal ECG and troponin: discharge same day with structured follow-up rather than reflex admission
day2_milestones: Review telemetry for captured arrhythmia (6h of negative monitoring excludes most actionable arrhythmia in medium/high-risk). Complete pending echo/stress testing. Cardiology/EP input on need for ambulatory monitor or ILR. Reassess orthostatics after volume/drug intervention; confirm safe ambulation with nursing/PT. If no serious cause identified and monitoring is negative: arrange 15-day outpatient cardiac monitoring for selected medium-risk and all high-risk patients and discharge - do not prolong the stay for continued inpatient telemetry
day3plus_milestones: Day 3+ inpatient stay is reserved for a CONFIRMED serious cause requiring definitive in-hospital therapy (pacemaker/ICD implant, catheter ablation, coronary revascularization, PE anticoagulation, structural intervention) - at which point the encounter re-groups OUT of DRG 312 into a cause-specific DRG. Device-implant patients: post-procedure telemetry, wound check, device interrogation, then next-day discharge. For genuinely uncomplicated syncope, LOS beyond 48h reflects pathway failure or social/disposition barriers - target discharge readiness by 48h with outpatient workup arranged
consults_multidisciplinary: Cardiology/Electrophysiology: suspected cardiac or arrhythmic syncope, device candidacy, ILR placement, ablation, channelopathy - early (Day 1) when high-risk. Neurology: ONLY if seizure vs syncope is unclear or a focal deficit is present (not routine). PT/OT: gait, balance, and fall-risk assessment, especially in elderly/frail. Pharmacy: deprescribe culprit hypotensive/bradycardic/QT-prolonging agents and review polypharmacy (high-yield in geriatrics). Care management/social work: falls prevention, home-safety, and driving-restriction counseling. Geriatrics: elderly with recurrent falls or frailty. Nutrition: reinforce salt/fluid liberalization for reflex/orthostatic syncope
monitoring_level: Telemetry/monitored bed is the DEFAULT for admitted or observed syncope (arrhythmia surveillance is the core reason to keep the patient). ICU/step-down reserved for hemodynamic instability, sustained VT, high-grade AV block awaiting pacing, ACS, hemodynamically significant PE, or critical aortic stenosis. Non-telemetry floor only for a clearly established non-cardiac mechanism. Escalation triggers: recurrent syncope, sustained/symptomatic arrhythmia, hypotension, chest pain, or rising troponin. Most uncomplicated syncope belongs in observation-status telemetry, not inpatient ICU
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet as tolerated; liberalize salt and fluid intake for reflex and orthostatic syncope. Early mobilization with fall precautions and assisted first ambulation (recheck orthostatics before walking). VTE prophylaxis: formally risk-assess (Padua/Caprini) - most short observation stays do NOT warrant pharmacologic prophylaxis, and prolonged bed rest should be actively avoided; use mechanical/pharmacologic prophylaxis only for admitted, immobile patients. Glucose: identify and treat hypoglycemia as a reversible cause. Lines/catheters: keep IV access minimal and remove promptly; avoid unnecessary urinary catheters (fall, delirium, and UTI risk in elderly)
discharge_criteria: No serious cause identified after an appropriate, guideline-concordant workup OR the identified cause has been treated/stabilized; CSRS very-low/low (or medium risk with negative monitoring and outpatient monitor arranged); telemetry negative for actionable arrhythmia over the monitored window; hemodynamically stable with orthostatic hypotension managed and patient ambulating safely; reversible triggers corrected (volume, offending drugs, electrolytes, glucose); follow-up and any indicated ambulatory cardiac monitoring arranged; driving/occupational counseling delivered
discharge_planning: Disposition: home for the majority; SNF/rehab only for fall-related deconditioning or functional decline. Medication reconciliation with active deprescribing of hypotensive, bradycardic, and QT-prolonging drugs. Follow-up: PCP within 1-2 weeks; cardiology/EP for medium-high risk; arrange 15-day ambulatory ECG (patch/loop) for selected medium-risk and all high-risk discharged patients, with an ILR pathway for recurrent unexplained cases. Education: reflex-syncope counterpressure maneuvers, trigger avoidance, hydration/salt, and clear return precautions. Provide cause-specific driving and occupational restrictions per guideline/state law. Transitions: explicit handoff to PCP/cardiology regarding pending monitor results and device follow-up
expected_los: GMLOS benchmark ~2.2 days for MS-DRG 312 (arithmetic mean LOS ~2.5-2.6 days); a large and increasing share of these encounters are appropriately managed as <2-midnight observation stays rather than as true multi-day inpatient admissions
target_los: ~1 day under an ideal CSRS-driven pathway (0-1 inpatient day): very-low/low-risk discharged from the ED same day (0 inpatient days); medium-risk managed in observation/telemetry <24-48h with residual surveillance shifted to outpatient monitoring; reserve multi-day inpatient LOS for a confirmed serious cause, which re-groups out of DRG 312
quality_metrics: Avoidance of low-value neuroimaging in atraumatic syncope without focal signs (Choosing Wisely / ACEP; historical CMS OP-15 measured brain CT use in ED syncope); documented use of a validated risk score (CSRS); observation-status vs inpatient utilization and 2-midnight-rule compliance / avoidable-admission rate; 30-day serious outcomes, ED return visits, and all-cause readmission; ED length of stay and throughput; telemetry-appropriateness / bed-utilization; guideline-concordant testing rate (proportion avoiding routine echo/carotid US/EEG)
common_complications: Recurrent syncope with falls and injury, especially in the elderly - mitigated by fall precautions, cause-directed therapy, and PT/home-safety review. Missed serious arrhythmia or cardiac cause leading to sudden cardiac death - mitigated by mandatory ECG, CSRS risk-stratification, in-hospital telemetry, and risk-appropriate ambulatory monitoring/ILR. Iatrogenic harm from over-testing (incidentalomas, contrast nephropathy) and hospital-acquired delirium/deconditioning from unnecessary admission of low-risk elderly - mitigated by the observation pathway and early discharge. Iatrogenic orthostatic hypotension from over-diuresis or polypharmacy - mitigated by medication reconciliation
readmission_drivers: Undiagnosed arrhythmia missed at index visit (leading to return or SCD) - lever: risk-appropriate 15-day ambulatory monitoring and ILR for high-risk/recurrent patients. Recurrent reflex or orthostatic syncope from inadequate counseling or medication management - lever: counterpressure-maneuver training, deprescribing of culprit drugs, hydration/salt. Falls and injury in frail elderly - lever: PT, home-safety evaluation, geriatric medication review. Uncontrolled comorbidity (heart failure, arrhythmia, structural disease) - lever: expedited cardiology follow-up and medication optimization at transition
guideline_source: PRIMARY: 2017 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Patients With Syncope (American College of Cardiology / American Heart Association / Heart Rhythm Society, 2017). COMPLEMENTARY: 2018 ESC Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of syncope (European Society of Cardiology, 2018). RISK TOOL: Canadian Syncope Risk Score (derived CMAJ 2016; prospectively multicenter-validated JAMA Internal Medicine 2020)
key_citations: Shen WK, et al. 2017 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Patients With Syncope. Circulation. 2017;136(5):e60-e122 (PMID 28280231; DOI 10.1161/CIR.0000000000000499); Brignole M, et al. 2018 ESC Guidelines for the diagnosis and management of syncope. Eur Heart J. 2018;39(21):1883-1948 (PMID 29562304); Thiruganasambandamoorthy V, et al. Development of the Canadian Syncope Risk Score. CMAJ. 2016;188(12):E289-E298 (PMID 27378464; DOI 10.1503/cmaj.151469); Thiruganasambandamoorthy V, et al. Multicenter Emergency Department Validation of the Canadian Syncope Risk Score. JAMA Intern Med. 2020;180(5):737-744 (PMID 32202605; DOI 10.1001/jamainternmed.2020.0288); Goldberger ZD, et al. ACC/AHA/HRS Versus ESC Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Syncope. J Am Coll Cardiol. 2019;74(19):2410-2423 (PMID 31699282; DOI 10.1016/j.jacc.2019.09.012); Shen WK, et al. Syncope Evaluation in the Emergency Department Study (SEEDS). Circulation. 2004;110(24):3636-3645 (PMID 15536093). Citations retrieved and verified via PubMed
evidence_grade: Strong overall - anchored by a Class I society guideline (2017 ACC/AHA/HRS: Class I recommendations for history/physical, resting 12-lead ECG, and formal short-/long-term risk stratification) plus a prospectively derived and multicenter-validated decision tool (CSRS, AUC 0.91, sensitivity 97.8% at threshold <=0). Evidence for specific reflex-syncope pharmacotherapies is weaker (many Class IIb with limited/variable efficacy) and diagnostic-yield thresholds for advanced testing are moderate; the low-value-imaging avoidance recommendations are Class III (No Benefit) and well supported
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MS-DRG 312 (Syncope and Collapse) is a SINGLE, non-severity-split DRG - there is no 3-tier CC/MCC subdivision within this base group (unlike, e.g., arrhythmia 308-310). Critical operational nuance: when a serious cause IS confirmed, the encounter re-groups OUT of 312 into a higher-weighted, cause-specific DRG with longer LOS and possible ICU need - e.g., cardiac arrhythmia/conduction disorder (308-310), acute MI (280-282), heart failure (291-293), pulmonary embolism (175-176), GI hemorrhage (377-379), or device-implant DRGs (pacemaker 242-244, ICD 222-227/319-320). DRG 312 therefore captures the uncomplicated/no-serious-cause-found cohort - short LOS, telemetry level. Coexisting CC/MCC comorbidities raise documented severity in the RECEIVING DRG, not within 312 itself
pathway_pearls: 1) ECG + CSRS drive disposition: a normal ECG, normal hs-troponin, and CSRS <=0 support safe same-day/ED discharge - resist reflexive admission of low-risk patients. 2) Ruthlessly eliminate low-value neuroimaging: routine head CT, carotid ultrasound, EEG, and brain MRI have near-zero yield in atraumatic syncope without focal signs (Class III) - a top cost, throughput, and radiation-safety lever. 3) Right-size the setting to observation/telemetry <24-48h using short monitored windows (arrhythmia declared within ~2h low-risk, ~6h medium/high-risk) and shift residual surveillance to 15-day ambulatory monitoring or an ILR rather than prolonging inpatient stay. 4) Treat the cause and deprescribe the culprit: reconcile hypotensive/bradycardic/QT-prolonging drugs and teach reflex-syncope counterpressure maneuvers to prevent injurious bounce-back

scope_and_volume_notes: Classic ED-observation/short-stay condition; single DRG 312. Telemetry/medical bed demand.

condition: Anemia & Red Blood Cell Disorders (incl. Sickle Cell)
admission_criteria: Admit (inpatient) when ED/observation fails or a complication is present. SICKLE VOC: pain uncontrolled after >=2-3 parenteral opioid doses over ~6h, inability to maintain PO hydration/intake, OR any acute complication — acute chest syndrome (new infiltrate + resp symptoms), fever >=38.5C/101.3F in functionally asplenic patient, Hgb fall >=2 g/dL below steady-state baseline or retic <1% (aplastic crisis), priapism >4h, splenic/hepatic sequestration, acute stroke/focal deficit, AKI, multiorgan failure, SpO2 <95%. ANEMIA: symptomatic anemia (rest dyspnea, angina, syncope, tachycardia, hemodynamic instability), Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 with active bleeding or cardiac disease), acute blood loss with instability, brisk hemolysis, or transfusion need with unstable source. OBSERVATION (<24h): VOC expected to resolve with short parenteral analgesia + hydration, or simple transfusion of stable chronic anemia without active bleed. ED DISCHARGE: pain controlled on oral regimen, tolerating PO, no complication, reliable hematology follow-up.
risk_stratification: No single validated composite score exists for VOC severity — pain is patient-reported (0-10) titrated to an individualized plan; risk is driven by complication triggers. ANEMIA/BLOOD LOSS: WHO severity (Hgb moderate 8-9.9, severe <8 g/dL); Shock Index (HR/SBP) >=1.0 flags significant blood loss; Glasgow-Blatchford Score (if GI source) 0-1 low-risk/outpatient-eligible, higher drives admission + endoscopy; reticulocyte index <2% = inappropriate marrow response (hypoproliferative). SCD PHENOTYPE/MORTALITY MARKERS: hemolytic phenotype (low Hgb, high LDH, high retic, low haptoglobin), TR jet velocity >2.5 m/s on TTE predicts pulmonary hypertension/mortality, prior ACS and high steady-state WBC predict ACS. Action: Hgb <7 (or <8 if cardiac) triggers restrictive transfusion (AABB 2023); hypoxia/organ dysfunction triggers exchange transfusion; febrile SCD triggers empiric antibiotics within 1h.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential + reticulocyte count (core: separates hypoproliferative vs hemolytic/blood-loss; compare to SCD steady-state baseline); peripheral smear; TYPE & SCREEN with antibody screen + crossmatch (alloimmunization history critical in SCD). Hemolysis panel: LDH, haptoglobin, indirect bilirubin, DAT/Coombs if transfusion reaction suspected. New anemia workup: iron studies (ferritin, TSAT, TIBC — ferritin <30 ng/mL = iron deficiency, triggers source hunt/GI workup), B12, folate. BMP (AKI, baseline renal), LFTs. Hemoglobin electrophoresis/HPLC + quantitative HbS if SCD unconfirmed or exchange planned. Febrile SCD: blood cultures, CRP, reticulocyte (retic <1% + parvovirus B19 = aplastic crisis). G6PD if hemolytic/drug-triggered; pregnancy test in childbearing women (affects hydroxyurea/opioids); lactate + troponin if hemodynamic compromise.
initial_imaging_dx: Chest X-ray STAT in any SCD patient with chest pain, fever, hypoxia, or respiratory symptoms — new pulmonary infiltrate = acute chest syndrome (ACS). ECG + TTE if cardiac/anemia-related chest pain or pulmonary-hypertension screening (TR jet velocity). Non-contrast CT head <25 min for focal deficit/stroke; MRI/MRA brain for confirmation. Abdominal US/CT if splenic or hepatic sequestration suspected. GI blood-loss source: EGD within 24h for suspected upper GI bleed; colonoscopy/CT-angiography or tagged-RBC scan for lower/occult bleed. Transcranial Doppler is a pediatric outpatient stroke-screen, not an acute inpatient study.
time_critical_interventions: SCD VOC: first parenteral opioid within 30-60 min of arrival (time-to-first-analgesic <60 min is the key ASH quality target); reassess pain q15-30 min and re-dose. Febrile SCD (functional asplenia): empiric IV antibiotics (e.g., ceftriaxone) within 1h — encapsulated-organism sepsis risk. ACS: STAT CXR + oxygen + antibiotics (cephalosporin + macrolide for atypicals) + urgent simple transfusion, escalating to red-cell EXCHANGE if hypoxia progresses. Acute ischemic stroke in SCD: emergent exchange transfusion to lower HbS <30% (primary therapy, alongside standard stroke pathway). Hemorrhagic shock/massive bleed: activate Massive Transfusion Protocol, balanced 1:1:1 product ratio, transfuse to hemostasis regardless of threshold. Hemodynamically unstable severe anemia: transfuse immediately, do not wait for a numeric trigger.
initial_management: SCD VOC analgesia: individualized (patient-specific) parenteral opioid dosing per ASH 2020 — IV morphine or hydromorphone, escalate to PCA for moderate-severe pain; adjunct short-course ketorolac/NSAID (if renal function normal) + scheduled acetaminophen; AVOID meperidine. Fluids: euvolemic maintenance, oral preferred — do NOT over-hydrate (precipitates ACS/pulmonary edema). Oxygen only if SpO2 <95%. Incentive spirometry q1-2h awake (ACS prevention). Continue hydroxyurea — do not hold during admission. TRANSFUSION: SCD simple transfusion for symptomatic anemia or Hgb drop >2 below baseline, target Hgb NOT >10 g/dL (hyperviscosity); exchange transfusion for ACS with hypoxia, stroke, or multiorgan failure (target HbS <30%); use leukoreduced, extended phenotype-matched (Rh C/E, K) units to limit alloimmunization. NON-SCD anemia: restrictive threshold Hgb <7 g/dL (AABB 2023; <8 if preexisting cardiovascular disease, cardiac/orthopedic surgery, or active MI per MINT), single-unit-then-reassess; IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose or ferric derisomaltose) for iron deficiency; B12/folate repletion; treat the cause + source control. Anticoagulant-related bleed reversal: vitamin K + 4F-PCC (warfarin), idarucizumab (dabigatran), andexanet alfa or 4F-PCC (factor Xa inhibitors). Folic acid for chronic hemolysis.
day1_milestones: Establish diagnosis and steady-state baseline Hgb; achieve functional pain control with titrated parenteral opioid, convert to PCA if repeated boluses needed. Send type & screen; transfuse per restrictive threshold if indicated. Identify and begin treating the precipitant (infection, dehydration, hypoxia). Start incentive spirometry q1-2h, VTE pharmacoprophylaxis, and bowel regimen with opioids. Hematology consult day 0-1 (mandatory for SCD and unexplained anemia). Baseline respiratory exam + continuous pulse oximetry if any respiratory symptom; screen actively for evolving ACS.
day2_milestones: Begin parenteral-to-oral opioid transition once pain reduced >=30-50% and functional; taper by patient-specific plan. Reassess Hgb response to transfusion and ongoing hemolysis/bleeding; continue iron/B12/folate repletion. Confirm no ACS evolution; maintain incentive spirometry. Mobilize; PT if deconditioned. Act on anemia-source results (e.g., schedule/complete endoscopy for GI bleed, IV iron for deficiency). Re-evaluate hydroxyurea dose/adherence.
day3plus_milestones: Pain controlled on oral regimen at or near home baseline, tolerating PO. Transfusion complete with stable Hgb and no active hemolysis or bleeding. Precipitant treated; disease-modifying therapy optimized (hydroxyurea dose maximized; plan post-discharge options given voxelotor withdrawal and limited crizanlizumab availability). Complete medication reconciliation, follow-up scheduling, and patient education. Discharge process should begin by Day 2 for uncomplicated cases to hit target LOS.
consults_multidisciplinary: Hematology — early (Day 0-1), essential for SCD and refractory/unexplained anemia. Transfusion medicine/blood bank — for red-cell exchange, complex crossmatch, alloimmunization/delayed hemolytic reaction. Pain management/palliative — refractory pain or chronic high-dose opioids. GI — bleeding source; Cardiology — cardiac anemia/ACS-type presentations; Nephrology — AKI/CKD. Pharmacy — opioid stewardship, IV iron, anticoagulation reversal. Care management + social work — early for high-utilizer SCD (psychosocial needs, transportation, insurance, opioid stigma, SDOH). Nutrition — iron/B12/folate deficiency. PT/OT — deconditioning.
monitoring_level: Floor with telemetry for most VOC and stable anemia; add continuous pulse oximetry for any SCD respiratory symptom. STEP-DOWN/ICU: acute chest syndrome with hypoxia or rising O2 requirement, need for red-cell exchange, acute stroke, multiorgan failure, sepsis/septic shock, hemodynamic instability, or massive transfusion. ESCALATION TRIGGERS: SpO2 <92% or falling, increasing oxygen need, new/expanding infiltrate, altered mental status, hypotension, Hgb continuing to fall despite transfusion, new AKI, or uncontrolled pain requiring escalating parenteral opioids.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: regular, iron-rich; correct iron/B12/folate deficiency; in SCD maintain euvolemia and AVOID over-hydration. Mobility: early ambulation + incentive spirometry q1-2h while awake (primary ACS-prevention lever). VTE prophylaxis: pharmacologic (LMWH/UFH) unless active bleeding or thrombocytopenia — SCD is prothrombotic with elevated VTE risk. Glucose control if on steroids or diabetic. Lines/catheters: remove ASAP; minimize central venous catheters in SCD (high infection and thrombosis risk, difficult future access). Bowel regimen mandatory with opioids.
discharge_criteria: Pain controlled on ORAL analgesics at the patient's home baseline for >=12-24h and tolerating PO. Hemodynamically stable; Hgb stable at/near baseline with no active bleeding or ongoing hemolysis. SpO2 at baseline with no evolving ACS. Precipitant identified and treated; able to self-hydrate and maintain nutrition. Safe disposition with confirmed follow-up and prescriptions in hand.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home. Medication reconciliation with an explicit, limited-quantity opioid taper plan and clear instructions; continue hydroxyurea, folic acid, and iron/B12 as indicated. Follow-up: hematology/SCD comprehensive clinic within 7 days, PCP; enroll in a day-hospital/infusion-center pathway so future VOCs bypass the ED. Embed an individualized VOC analgesia care plan in the EHR to standardize future ED dosing and counter opioid stigma. Patient education: hydration, trigger avoidance, incentive spirometry, and return precautions (fever, chest pain, priapism, focal neuro symptoms). For iron-deficiency anemia, confirm the outpatient GI-source workup is booked. Warm handoff + connect high-utilizers to case management/community health worker.
expected_los: DRG 812 (without MCC): CMS GMLOS ~2.5 days, arithmetic mean ~2.8 days. DRG 811 (with MCC): CMS GMLOS 3.7 days, arithmetic mean 4.9 days. Uncomplicated VOC typically 3-5 days; acute chest syndrome or organ-complicated stays run longer.
target_los: Uncomplicated VOC or transfusion-responsive anemia: 2-3 days. With MCC (ACS, AKI, sepsis): 3-4 days. Achieved by first-analgesic <60 min, early parenteral-to-oral opioid transition by Day 2, single-unit restrictive transfusion, and infusion-center diversion for mild crises.
quality_metrics: SCD: time-to-first-analgesic <60 min, use of individualized pain plan, incentive-spirometry compliance, 30-day all-cause readmission (SCD is among the highest, ~30-41% in adults), ED-visit and admit rates. Patient Blood Management: restrictive-threshold adherence, single-unit transfusion rate, pre-transfusion Hgb audit, appropriate-use rate, transfusion-reaction reporting (Joint Commission PBM measures). General: 30-day readmission, VTE prophylaxis rate, HCAHPS pain-communication domain, hospital-acquired infection rate.
common_complications: Acute chest syndrome — leading cause of SCD death; mitigate with incentive spirometry, avoid over-hydration/over-sedation, early recognition, and transfusion. Alloimmunization and delayed hemolytic transfusion reaction — mitigate with extended phenotype-matched units, minimizing transfusions, and honoring antibody history. Opioid-related respiratory depression, over-sedation, ileus — mitigate with monitoring, multimodal analgesia, and bowel regimen. TACO/TRALI — single-unit slow transfusion, diuresis. Transfusional iron overload (chronic) — monitor ferritin, chelation. VTE — prophylaxis. Also AKI, hospital-acquired infection, splenic sequestration, and parvovirus aplastic crisis.
readmission_drivers: SCD has one of the highest 30-day readmission rates (~30-41% in adults). Drivers: inadequate outpatient pain regimen, premature/abrupt opioid taper, missed hematology follow-up, unaddressed precipitant, and psychosocial/SDOH gaps (transportation, insurance, mental health, opioid stigma). Levers: hematology follow-up <=7 days, day-hospital/infusion-center diversion for VOC, EHR individualized care plan, hydroxyurea adherence, and care-management/community-health-worker linkage. For anemia: under-repletion and an unaddressed bleeding source — prevent with IV iron and a confirmed GI-source workup before discharge.
guideline_source: ASH 2020 Guidelines for Sickle Cell Disease — Management of Acute and Chronic Pain (Brandow et al) and Transfusion Support (Chou et al); AABB 2023 International Red Blood Cell Transfusion Guidelines (Carson et al); NHLBI Evidence-Based Management of Sickle Cell Disease, Expert Panel Report 2014. Supporting RCT: MINT trial (NEJM 2023) for the cardiac-anemia transfusion threshold.
key_citations: Brandow AM et al. ASH 2020 guidelines for SCD: management of acute and chronic pain. Blood Adv 2020;4(12):2656-2701 (PMID 32559294; DOI 10.1182/bloodadvances.2020001851). Chou ST et al. ASH 2020 guidelines for SCD: transfusion support. Blood Adv 2020;4(2):327-355 (PMID 31985807; DOI 10.1182/bloodadvances.2019001143). Carson JL et al. Red Blood Cell Transfusion: 2023 AABB International Guidelines. JAMA 2023;330(19):1892-1902 (PMID 37824153; DOI 10.1001/jama.2023.12914). Carson JL et al. (MINT) Restrictive or Liberal Transfusion Strategy in Myocardial Infarction and Anemia. N Engl J Med 2023;389:2446-2456 (PMID 37952133; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2307983). NHLBI Evidence-Based Management of SCD Expert Panel Report 2014. Landmark trials cited by name: TRICC (Hebert 1999), MSH hydroxyurea trial (Charache 1995) — PMIDs not independently verified in this build. Citations retrieved via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Moderate overall (mixed). Transfusion thresholds are STRONG — AABB 2023 strong recommendation, moderate-certainty evidence from 45 RCTs (restrictive Hgb <7; <8 in cardiovascular disease). SCD-specific pain and transfusion management is largely CONDITIONAL/low-certainty — most ASH 2020 recommendations are conditional (GRADE) because of sparse high-quality SCD trial data; several operational elements (incentive spirometry, exchange for ACS/stroke) rest on NHLBI 2014 consensus/expert opinion. Time-to-analgesia and individualized dosing are best-practice/quality-consensus rather than RCT-proven.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed group splits into DRG 811 (with MCC) vs 812 (without MCC). MCC tier (acute chest syndrome, sepsis/severe sepsis, acute respiratory failure, acute stroke, AKI, multiorgan failure) drives step-down/ICU care, red-cell exchange, higher mortality, and LOS from ~2.5-3 days up to 4-7+ days — this is where the high-utilizer sickle-crisis subset concentrates. Without MCC (uncomplicated VOC, simple transfusion, mild-moderate anemia): floor-level care, 2-3 days. CC-level modifiers (moderate anemia, uncomplicated infection) sit between. Accurate capture of ACS/AKI/respiratory failure is essential for correct DRG assignment and reflecting true resource use.
pathway_pearls: 1) Treat pain fast and individually — first parenteral opioid <60 min with a patient-specific dosing protocol and early PCA; under-treatment fuels distrust and ED bounce-back. 2) Prevent ACS (the top SCD killer): incentive spirometry q1-2h plus deliberate avoidance of over-hydration and over-sedation. 3) Transfuse restrictively (Hgb <7; <8 if cardiac), single-unit-then-reassess, and in SCD use extended phenotype-matched, leukoreduced units to prevent alloimmunization; escalate to EXCHANGE (target HbS <30%) for ACS/stroke/organ failure. 4) Own the transition: hematology follow-up <=7 days + infusion-center diversion + an EHR individualized care plan + continued hydroxyurea is the highest-yield lever against the ~33% readmission rate.

admission_criteria: Admit for acute decompensation of cirrhosis: overt hepatic encephalopathy West Haven grade >=2; acute variceal/GI bleed (hematemesis/melena/hemodynamic change); suspected/confirmed SBP (ascitic PMN >=250/mm3); AKI or hepatorenal syndrome (creatinine rise >=0.3 mg/dL in 48h or >=50% from baseline); tense/refractory ascites needing large-volume paracentesis; symptomatic hyponatremia (Na <125); new jaundice with coagulopathy (INR >1.5); ACLF with organ failure. Severe alcohol-associated hepatitis (Maddrey Discriminant Function >=32 OR MELD >20) = admit. ICU if shock, GI bleed with instability, grade 3-4 HE with airway risk, or ACLF with >=2 organ failures. ED discharge/observation acceptable only if compensated, diuretic-responsive ascites, mild HE reversible with lactulose plus reliable caregiver, hemodynamically stable, no active bleeding.
risk_stratification: MELD 3.0/MELD-Na (OPTN transplant priority + 90-day mortality; MELD-Na >=21 = high mortality, refer transplant); Child-Turcotte-Pugh A(5-6)/B(7-9)/C(10-15) stratifies mortality & TIPS candidacy; Maddrey Discriminant Function >=32 OR MELD >20 defines SEVERE alcohol-associated hepatitis -> corticosteroid candidacy; Lille score at day 7 >0.45 = steroid non-responder -> STOP steroids for futility, <0.45 = responder -> complete 28 days; Glasgow Alcoholic Hepatitis Score >=9 adverse; CLIF-C ACLF score grades acute-on-chronic organ failures (dominant mortality driver); West Haven grades HE I-IV; HVPG >=10 mmHg = clinically significant portal hypertension, >=12 variceal-bleed risk; for variceal bleed use AIMS65/Glasgow-Blatchford + Child-Pugh C(<14) or B-with-active-bleeding flags early-TIPS candidacy.
initial_workup_labs: CBC (thrombocytopenia/anemia signal portal HTN; WBC for infection), CMP (Na <125 flags hyponatremia; creatinine for AKI/HRS staging; bilirubin/AST/ALT with AST:ALT >2:1 alcohol pattern; albumin), PT/INR (synthetic function -> MELD); ammonia optional (supports but NOT required for HE diagnosis per AASLD); lactate + blood/urine cultures + CXR to find infection precipitant; DIAGNOSTIC PARACENTESIS in ALL patients with ascites on admission -> cell count+differential (PMN >=250/mm3 = SBP -> antibiotics), SAAG >=1.1 g/dL confirms portal HTN, total protein <1.5 g/dL flags need for SBP prophylaxis, culture inoculated into blood-culture bottles at bedside, glucose/LDH to exclude secondary peritonitis; etiology panel (HBsAg/anti-HCV/HIV, iron studies, ceruloplasmin, ANA/ASMA, A1AT, PEth/urine EtG alcohol biomarker); AFP for HCC surveillance; type & crossmatch if bleeding; Mg/phosphate/glucose for refeeding.
initial_imaging_dx: Abdominal ultrasound with Doppler within 24h (portal/hepatic vein patency & thrombosis, ascites volume, nodularity, HCC screen); diagnostic +/- large-volume therapeutic paracentesis (US-guided) at admission; EGD within 12h of presentation for suspected variceal bleeding once resuscitated (Baveno VII) with band ligation at index endoscopy; multiphasic CT/MRI liver if AFP elevated, mass seen, or portal vein thrombosis; TTE pre-TIPS or if cardiac dysfunction/cirrhotic cardiomyopathy suspected; non-contrast CT head ONLY if HE atypical, focal deficit, trauma, or failure to improve (exclude intracranial hemorrhage); EEG and transient elastography are not part of the acute workup.
time_critical_interventions: Acute variceal bleed bundle (Baveno VII): start IV vasoactive agent IMMEDIATELY at presentation/before endoscopy -- octreotide 50 mcg bolus then 50 mcg/h x3-5d, OR terlipressin, OR somatostatin; give prophylactic IV ceftriaxone 1 g/24h at admission (within hours; cuts mortality, rebleeding, infection); EGD with variceal band ligation within 12h once resuscitated; RESTRICTIVE transfusion trigger Hgb <7 g/dL (target 7-8). SBP: antibiotics immediately after diagnostic paracentesis + IV albumin 1.5 g/kg day 1 and 1 g/kg day 3. Septic shock/SBP-sepsis: antibiotics <=1h + lactate-guided resuscitation (CMS SEP-1). HRS-AKI: start vasoconstrictor + albumin early. Severe AH: begin corticosteroids only AFTER excluding/treating active infection & GI bleed; reassess with day-7 Lille. Grade 3-4 HE: protect airway.
initial_management: Ascites: Na restriction <2 g/day + spironolactone/furosemide (aldosterone antagonist + loop, 100:40 mg ratio); large-volume paracentesis for tense/refractory ascites WITH albumin 6-8 g per liter removed when >5 L. Hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose (nonabsorbable disaccharide) 30 mL q1-2h then titrate to 2-3 soft stools/day + rifaximin 550 mg BID add-on; correct precipitants. SBP: IV ceftriaxone/cefotaxime (piperacillin-tazobactam if nosocomial) + albumin 1.5 g/kg day1 & 1 g/kg day3. Variceal bleed: octreotide/terlipressin + ceftriaxone + endoscopic band ligation; RESTRICTIVE transfusion Hgb<7 (target 7-8); IV PPI; PTFE-covered early-TIPS <72h if Child-Pugh C<14 or B with active bleeding; balloon tamponade/covered esophageal stent as bridge. HRS-AKI: terlipressin+albumin (or norepinephrine, or midodrine+octreotide) + albumin ~1 g/kg (max 100 g) then 20-40 g/day. Severe AH: prednisolone 40 mg/day x28d (NOT pentoxifylline; STOPAH negative); +/- N-acetylcysteine adjunct. Alcohol withdrawal: symptom-triggered lorazepam (CIWA-Ar; no active metabolites) + thiamine 200-500 mg IV BEFORE dextrose, folate, Mg. Do NOT routinely correct INR or transfuse FFP absent active bleeding.
day1_milestones: Diagnostic paracentesis completed and precipitant identified (infection/bleed/constipation/electrolytes/meds/dehydration); risk-stratify (MELD 3.0, Child-Pugh, Maddrey DF and baseline Lille, West Haven grade, CLIF-C ACLF); initiate targeted therapy -- lactulose titrated, spironolactone/furosemide, empiric or culture-directed antibiotics, vasoactive + banding if bleeding, prednisolone if severe AH and infection excluded; start thiamine + symptom-triggered CIWA-Ar withdrawal protocol; resuscitate and correct Na/K/Mg/phosphate; assign ICU vs step-down vs floor; hold nephrotoxins/NSAIDs/sedatives; dietitian consult with 35-40 kcal/kg goal; early goals-of-care/code-status discussion given high short-term mortality.
day2_milestones: Reassess mental status and lactulose response (target 2-3 soft BMs; de-escalate if over-catharsis to avoid dehydration/hypernatremia); track weight/urine output/renal function -- diuretic goal net -0.5 kg/day without peripheral edema, up to -1 kg/day with edema; watch for evolving HRS; narrow antibiotics to culture data; repeat paracentesis at 48h if SBP to confirm >25% PMN decline; ensure enteral nutrition (NO protein restriction); addiction-medicine/behavioral-health consult and start AUD pharmacotherapy planning (baclofen or acamprosate; avoid disulfiram and naltrexone in decompensated disease); monitor steroid tolerance (glucose, infection surveillance); flag for transplant evaluation if MELD-Na high or not improving.
day3plus_milestones: Day-7 Lille score in AH steroid patients -- >0.45 = non-responder, STOP prednisolone; achieve targets: ascites controlled on oral diuretics, HE cleared on oral lactulose+rifaximin, creatinine stable and off vasoconstrictors, hemodynamically stable off vasoactives, tolerating oral diet; convert IV->PO regimens and set maintenance doses; confirm SBP cure and START secondary SBP prophylaxis (daily norfloxacin/ciprofloxacin or TMP-SMX); initiate liver-transplant evaluation for candidates (or palliative care if not a candidate); vaccinate (HAV/HBV, pneumococcal, influenza); plan serial LVP vs TIPS for refractory ascites; finalize disposition and AUD relapse-prevention plan.
consults_multidisciplinary: Hepatology/GI on admission (endoscopy, TIPS decision, transplant triage); interventional radiology (TIPS, refractory-ascites paracentesis, portal-vein thrombosis); transplant hepatology/surgery early if MELD-Na high or severe AH; addiction medicine + behavioral health/psychiatry for AUD (day 1-2); nephrology for HRS/AKI needing renal-replacement; registered dietitian for ALL (protein-calorie malnutrition/sarcopenia is near-universal); clinical pharmacy for hepatic dosing & reconciliation; palliative care for non-transplant candidates or refractory decompensation; care management + social work early for disposition, substance-use resources, insurance/housing; PT/OT for deconditioning/frailty; critical care if organ failures.
monitoring_level: ICU for active variceal hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability, grade 3-4 HE with airway compromise, septic shock, ACLF with >=2 organ failures, HRS requiring vasopressors/renal-replacement, or unstable post-TIPS; step-down/telemetry for recently controlled GI bleed, grade 2 HE, high transfusion requirement, or borderline hemodynamics; floor for stable decompensated cirrhosis, uncomplicated SBP, diuretic titration, mild HE. ESCALATE for rebleeding, MAP <65, rising lactate, deteriorating mental status, urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h, rising creatinine, or SpO2 decline.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: 35-40 kcal/kg/day + protein 1.2-1.5 g/kg/day -- DO NOT protein-restrict (worsens sarcopenia and does not improve HE); late-evening snack to limit overnight catabolism; enteral/NG feeding if oral intake inadequate in severe AH; thiamine/folate/multivitamin/zinc; monitor refeeding (phosphate/Mg/K). Mobility: early PT/OT and ambulation to reverse frailty/sarcopenia; fall precautions in HE. VTE: elevated INR is NOT auto-protective and does not contraindicate prophylaxis -- assess individually; mechanical prophylaxis if active bleed or platelets <50k; pharmacologic LMWH/heparin prophylaxis when bleeding risk acceptable (VTE and portal-vein thrombosis risk is real). Glucose control (tighter on steroids; guard against hypoglycemia from impaired gluconeogenesis). Remove Foley/central lines ASAP -- catheter infections precipitate HE/decompensation.
discharge_criteria: Mental status at or near baseline on oral lactulose +/- rifaximin with a reliable caregiver; ascites controlled on stable oral diuretic doses without tense ascites, with stable weight and renal function; no active bleeding and hemodynamically stable >=24-48h with stable hemoglobin; infection treated or on an appropriate regimen, afebrile; tolerating oral diet with adequate intake; Na >125 and electrolytes stable; AUD treatment plan initiated with abstinence counseling; outpatient follow-up and labs arranged; AH corticosteroid continuation/discontinuation decided by day-7 Lille.
discharge_planning: Disposition home vs SNF/rehab based on frailty. MED RECONCILIATION: diuretics with home daily-weight and BMP monitoring, lactulose + rifaximin, secondary SBP prophylaxis, non-selective beta-blocker (carvedilol 6.25 mg BID or nadolol) for variceal secondary prophylaxis, AUD pharmacotherapy, STOP NSAIDs/nephrotoxins. Follow-up: hepatology within 7-14 days + BMP within 1 week + transplant/addiction clinics; scheduled surveillance EGD for variceal eradication. PATIENT EDUCATION: 2 g Na diet, daily weights, lactulose titration to 2-3 stools/day, ALCOHOL ABSTINENCE, red-flag symptoms (confusion, melena/hematemesis, fever, rapid weight gain). Transitions: teach-back, caregiver engagement, HAV/HBV/pneumococcal/influenza vaccination, nurse-navigator/transitional-care enrollment.
expected_los: GMLOS approx: DRG 432/441 (with MCC) ~6-6.5 days; 433/442 (with CC) ~4.5-5 days; 434/443 (without CC/MCC) ~3.5 days. Observed national mean for decompensated-cirrhosis admissions ~5-7 days; severe alcohol-associated hepatitis often 7-10 days.
target_los: 4-5 days for uncomplicated decompensation (ascites/HE/uncomplicated SBP) under an expedited pathway; 6-8 days for severe alcohol-associated hepatitis or variceal hemorrhage requiring TIPS. Key levers: diagnostic paracentesis within 24h, prompt precipitant control, and early IV-to-oral regimen conversion.
quality_metrics: AASLD cirrhosis quality indicators (Kanwal measures): diagnostic paracentesis within 24h of admission for ascites; antibiotics + albumin for SBP; prophylactic antibiotics in variceal bleed; secondary SBP prophylaxis and non-selective beta-blocker at discharge; restrictive-transfusion adherence (Hgb<7 trigger); CMS SEP-1 bundle when septic shock/SBP-sepsis; alcohol screening/SBIRT + AUD treatment initiation; HAV/HBV and pneumococcal vaccination; 30-day all-cause readmission (cirrhosis is a national top-tier readmission diagnosis, ~20-30%); documented HCC surveillance plan; lactulose + rifaximin for overt HE; documented transplant-evaluation referral where indicated.
common_complications: Spontaneous bacterial peritonitis and other infections (mitigate with admission paracentesis + prophylaxis); hepatorenal syndrome/AKI (albumin, avoid nephrotoxins and over-diuresis, early terlipressin); HE progression/aspiration (lactulose, airway protection); variceal rebleeding (band-ligation eradication program, NSBB, early TIPS); hyponatremia (careful fluid management; avoid rapid correction -> osmotic demyelination); alcohol withdrawal/DTs (CIWA-Ar, thiamine); steroid-associated sepsis in AH (infection surveillance, Lille-guided discontinuation); refeeding syndrome (phosphate/Mg repletion); post-TIPS encephalopathy; ACLF with multiorgan failure (ICU escalation).
readmission_drivers: Recurrent overt HE (lactulose nonadherence or over-catharsis -> add rifaximin, caregiver education); refractory/recurrent ascites and diuretic mismanagement (Na-diet adherence, early clinic labs, TIPS evaluation); variceal rebleeding (complete band eradication + NSBB); recurrent SBP (secondary prophylaxis); ALCOHOL RELAPSE (addiction treatment is the single highest-yield lever); medication nonadherence and missed follow-up; hyponatremia/AKI. Prevention: rifaximin add-on (reduces HE hospitalization), structured hepatology follow-up <=7-14 days, AUD pharmacotherapy + counseling, medication reconciliation, scheduled paracentesis, teach-back education, transitional-care/nurse-navigator programs.
guideline_source: AASLD 2021 Practice Guidance -- Ascites, Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis & Hepatorenal Syndrome (Biggins et al); AASLD 2019/2020 Practice Guidance -- Alcohol-Associated Liver Diseases (Crabb et al); AASLD/EASL 2014 Practice Guideline -- Hepatic Encephalopathy (Vilstrup et al); Baveno VII 2022 -- Portal Hypertension/Variceal Hemorrhage (de Franchis et al); ACG 2024 Clinical Guideline -- Alcohol-Associated Liver Disease (supportive).
key_citations: AASLD Ascites/SBP/HRS 2021 Practice Guidance, Hepatology 74(2):1014-1048, PMID 33942342 (doi:10.1002/hep.31884); AASLD Alcohol-Associated Liver Diseases 2019 Guidance, Hepatology 71(1):306-333, PMID 31314133 (doi:10.1002/hep.30866); AASLD/EASL Hepatic Encephalopathy 2014, Hepatology 60(2):715-735, PMID 25042402 (doi:10.1002/hep.27210); Baveno VII (de Franchis), J Hepatol 2022;76(4):959-974, PMID 35120736 (doi:10.1016/j.jhep.2021.12.022); STOPAH (Thursz), prednisolone/pentoxifylline in alcoholic hepatitis, NEJM 2015;372:1619-1628, PMID 25901427 (doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1412278); CONFIRM (Wong), terlipressin+albumin for HRS-1, NEJM 2021;384:818-828, PMID 33657294 (doi:10.1056/NEJMoa2008290); Villanueva, restrictive vs liberal transfusion in acute UGIB, NEJM 2013;368:11-21, PMID 23281973 (doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1211801); Garcia-Pagan, early TIPS in variceal bleeding, NEJM 2010;362:2370-2379, PMID 20573925 (doi:10.1056/NEJMoa0910102); Sort, albumin in SBP, NEJM 1999;341:403-409, PMID 10432325 (doi:10.1056/NEJM199908053410603). Sourced via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong overall -- anchored by AASLD Class I practice guidance + Baveno VII consensus and multiple landmark RCTs (restrictive transfusion, albumin-for-SBP, early-TIPS, terlipressin-for-HRS all high-quality). Corticosteroids in severe alcohol-associated hepatitis are MODERATE/limited (STOPAH showed only borderline 28-day survival benefit, no 90-day or 1-year benefit, and increased infection); Maddrey DF and Lille thresholds are validated but observational.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 432 (MCC)/433 (CC)/434 (no CC/MCC) for cirrhosis & alcoholic hepatitis; 441-443 mirror this for other liver disorders. MCC drivers -- overt hepatic encephalopathy/coma, hepatorenal syndrome/acute renal failure, variceal hemorrhage, SBP/sepsis, ACLF -- push ICU/step-down care, LOS 7-10+ days, transplant evaluation, and markedly higher in-hospital mortality (ACLF grade 3 >50%). CC tier (ascites, hyponatremia, uncomplicated SBP) is typically floor-managed at 4-5 days. No-CC/MCC (compensated flare, diuretic titration) approaches ~3 days. Number and grade of organ failures (CLIF-C ACLF) is the dominant severity and resource lever across the collapsed group.
pathway_pearls: 1) DIAGNOSTIC PARACENTESIS within 24h on EVERY admission with ascites -- diagnoses SBP early and independently lowers mortality; do not defer for coagulopathy (routine FFP/platelets not required). 2) In variceal bleeding the two mortality-movers are EARLY prophylactic ceftriaxone + a vasoactive agent BEFORE endoscopy and RESTRICTIVE transfusion (Hgb<7) -- over-transfusion raises portal pressure and rebleeding. 3) Always find and treat the PRECIPITANT of decompensation (infection, GI bleed, constipation, electrolytes, nonadherence, meds) -- HE and ascites rarely worsen without one. 4) Alcohol Use Disorder treatment started IN-HOUSE (not deferred to follow-up) plus rifaximin add-on are the highest-yield readmission-prevention levers; sustained abstinence is the single strongest determinant of long-term survival.

scope_and_volume_notes: 432-434 = cirrhosis & alcoholic hepatitis; 441-443 = other liver disorders. Includes hepatic encephalopathy, ascites, variceal-associated decompensation.

admission_criteria: Mostly scheduled/elective structural cases, not ED admits. Intervention indicated for symptomatic severe AS (Stage D: Vmax >=4 m/s, mean gradient >=40 mmHg, AVA <=1.0 cm2) or severe AR/MR/MS meeting Class I/IIa criteria per Heart Team. Inpatient (SAVR, DRG 216-221) required for CPB/general anesthesia. TAVR (266-267) removed from CMS inpatient-only list 2020 -> observation/outpatient acceptable for uncomplicated transfemoral; inpatient for MCC, non-femoral access, hemodynamic instability, or anticipated conduction/vascular complications. Urgent/emergent admit for decompensated severe valve disease (HF, syncope, angina), acute severe AR, prosthetic valve dysfunction, or endocarditis with valve destruction.
risk_stratification: STS-PROM: <4% low, 4-8% intermediate, >8% high surgical risk; prohibitive = predicted >50% 1-yr mortality/morbidity or Heart Team judgment. 2020 ACC/AHA TAVR-vs-SAVR: age <65 or life expectancy >20 yr -> SAVR; 65-80 -> shared decision (transfemoral TAVR or SAVR); >80 or life expectancy <10 yr -> transfemoral TAVR (Class I). EuroSCORE II as adjunct. Frailty: 5-m gait speed >6 s, Katz ADL, grip strength, Essential Frailty Toolset, Fried criteria -> futility/disposition. Pre-existing RBBB = strongest predictor of post-TAVR high-grade AV block/PPM. CHA2DS2-VASc + HAS-BLED guide concomitant-AF antithrombotic choice.
initial_workup_labs: CBC (screen/treat preop anemia per patient blood management), CMP with eGFR (contrast/CPB AKI risk), PT/INR + aPTT, type & crossmatch, BNP/NT-proBNP, high-sensitivity troponin, HbA1c (glycemic optimization, target <8.5% preop), TSH, iron studies + ferritin, group & save. Infection screen: MRSA nares swab, urinalysis, dental evaluation (SAVR/endocarditis prophylaxis). Abnormal renal function triggers contrast-minimization/nephroprotection; anemia triggers IV iron +/- ESA and PBM before elective surgery.
initial_imaging_dx: TTE confirms severity/gradients/LVEF (Stage C/D). TEE for MR mechanism/valve morphology/LAA thrombus. ECG-gated cardiac CT angiography for TAVR annular sizing, coronary ostial height, leaflet calcification, and aortoiliofemoral access. Invasive coronary angiography pre-op to define concomitant CAD (SAVR+CABG vs TAVR +/- PCI). Baseline 12-lead ECG (flag RBBB/LBBB/PR prolongation). Carotid duplex/PFTs/frailty testing (5-m walk, 6-min walk distance) as indicated. Diagnostics completed in pre-procedure workup, not day-of.
time_critical_interventions: Elective surgical - not door-to-intervention timed. Perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis within 60 min of incision (cefazolin; vancomycin within 120 min if MRSA+/beta-lactam allergy); maintain normothermia and glucose <180 mg/dL. Continuous telemetry >=24 h post-TAVR to capture delayed high-grade AV block; temporary transvenous/valve pacing available intra-procedure. New persistent LBBB or high-grade AVB triggers ACC 2020 conduction-disturbance pathway (extended monitoring vs EP study vs permanent pacemaker). Emergent surgery for acute severe AR, prosthetic dysfunction, or endocarditis with hemodynamic collapse.
initial_management: SAVR: CPB valve replacement; bioprosthetic if age >65 or anticoagulation contraindicated, mechanical if <50-60 yr (shared decision). TAVR: minimalist transfemoral balloon-expandable (SAPIEN) or self-expanding (Evolut) under conscious sedation. Antithrombotics - post-TAVR aspirin 75-100 mg monotherapy (POPular TAVI: less bleeding vs DAPT) unless other indication; concomitant AF -> oral anticoagulation alone (DOAC or warfarin), avoid adding antiplatelet. Bioprosthetic SAVR: aspirin +/- VKA (INR 2-3) x3-6 mo (IIb). Mechanical valve: warfarin (INR 2.5 aortic / 3.0 mitral) + low-dose aspirin. PBM: intraop tranexamic acid, restrictive transfusion Hgb <7.5-8 g/dL. AF prophylaxis beta-blocker/amiodarone; statin + GDMT for LV dysfunction.
day1_milestones: TAVR POD0: transfer to monitored step-down/CCU on continuous telemetry; remove temporary pacing wire if no conduction disturbance; confirm femoral-access hemostasis; ambulate 4-6 h post-hemostasis; resume oral intake and home meds. SAVR POD0: ICU post-CPB, early extubation <6 h (ERAS), hemodynamic stabilization, chest-tube output monitoring, insulin infusion for glucose <180 mg/dL, multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia, maintain normothermia.
day2_milestones: TAVR POD1: repeat ECG + post-procedure TTE (valve function, paravalvular leak, effusion); if telemetry shows no new/progressive conduction disturbance and access site stable -> discharge home same day (3M/Vancouver next-day pathway). SAVR POD1: transfer ICU -> step-down, remove chest tubes when drainage <100-150 mL/8 h, early removal of invasive lines/Foley (ERAS), out-of-bed and ambulate, continue AF prophylaxis, advance diet, begin cardiac-rehab education.
day3plus_milestones: TAVR: majority discharged POD1-2. SAVR POD2-5: progressive mobilization, telemetry for post-op AF, diuresis toward preoperative weight, daily wound/sternal checks + sternal-precaution teaching, warfarin bridging/dose-titration to target INR if mechanical valve, electrolyte repletion, cardiac-rehab referral, disposition finalized. Discharge-readiness reassessed daily.
consults_multidisciplinary: Multidisciplinary Heart Team (interventional cardiology + cardiac surgery) pre-procedure = Class I; plus cardiac anesthesia and structural imaging. PT/OT for early mobilization + cardiac-rehab referral. Pharmacy (anticoagulation dosing, med reconciliation). Nutrition (preop malnutrition/anemia optimization). Case management/social work (disposition, home health) engaged from admission. Electrophysiology if conduction disturbance/PPM needed. Palliative care for prohibitive-risk/futility cases. Dental for SAVR/endocarditis clearance.
monitoring_level: SAVR: ICU POD0 (high ICU utilization), step-down POD1-2, telemetry floor thereafter. TAVR (minimalist): monitored step-down/telemetry unit with >=24 h continuous telemetry for delayed AV block; ICU reserved for general anesthesia, non-femoral access, or complications. Escalation triggers: high-grade/complete AV block, new persistent LBBB with PR/QRS prolongation, hemodynamic instability, pericardial effusion/tamponade, major vascular or access bleeding, stroke, new AF with instability.
nutrition_mobility_vte: ERAS: avoid prolonged fasting, preop carbohydrate loading, early oral nutrition, correct preop anemia/malnutrition. Early mobilization - ambulate within hours of TAVR, out of bed POD1 for SAVR. VTE prophylaxis: mechanical (SCDs) universally + pharmacologic (LMWH/UFH) once surgical bleeding risk acceptable (chest tubes out); TAVR relies on early ambulation +/- short pharmacologic course. Glycemic target <180 mg/dL (insulin; avoid tight <110). Early removal of lines, Foley, chest tubes, and pacing wires to reduce infection and immobility.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable; sinus or rate-controlled rhythm with NO high-grade conduction disturbance (or PPM implanted and interrogated); ambulating at/near baseline; tolerating oral intake; pain controlled on oral analgesia; stable Hgb and renal function; therapeutic INR if mechanical valve; no active bleeding or evolving vascular/access complication; access and sternal wounds intact; pre-discharge TTE confirming valve function without >= moderate paravalvular leak.
discharge_planning: Disposition home (majority TAVR) vs SNF/rehab (frail/complicated SAVR). Med reconciliation with explicit antithrombotic plan (ASA monotherapy post-TAVR; warfarin + INR target for mechanical valve; DOAC alone if AF), diuretic/GDMT titration. Endocarditis-prophylaxis education (antibiotic prophylaxis before dental procedures - prosthetic valve high-risk, Class IIa). Follow-up: structural/cardiology clinic at 2-4 wk with 30-day TTE, surgeon visit for SAVR, INR check within 3-5 days if on warfarin. Cardiac-rehab referral. Teach access-site/sternal care and HF/stroke/valve-dysfunction red flags; transitions-of-care phone call.
expected_los: TAVR (266-267) ~2-3 days (GMLOS ~2-4 days); SAVR (216-221) ~5-7 days; higher-severity tiers with MCC or concomitant CABG/cardiac cath 7-10+ days.
target_los: TAVR: next-day discharge POD1 (target <=1-2 days) via 3M/Vancouver minimalist pathway, with same-day discharge emerging in selected transfemoral patients; SAVR: 4-5 days with ERAS cardiac bundle.
quality_metrics: STS Adult Cardiac Surgery Database risk-adjusted operative mortality + morbidity composite; STS/ACC TVT Registry (mandatory for TAVR reimbursement): 30-day & 1-yr mortality, disabling stroke, new PPM, >= moderate paravalvular leak, device success, readmission. 30-day risk-standardized readmission + mortality (CMS). New permanent-pacemaker rate. Perioperative antibiotic timing/glucose/normothermia (SCIP-derived), PBM/blood-product utilization, ERAS compliance. AHRQ PSI-90.
common_complications: Conduction disturbance/high-grade AV block needing permanent pacemaker (TAVR ~6-25%, higher with self-expanding vs balloon-expandable; SAVR ~3-7%); vascular/access complications + bleeding (TAVR); disabling stroke ~1-3%; paravalvular leak; AKI (contrast/CPB); new-onset post-op AF (~20-40% SAVR); pericardial effusion/tamponade/annular rupture; sternal wound infection/mediastinitis (SAVR); delirium. Mitigation: minimalist transfemoral technique, preop RBBB flag + telemetry protocol, PBM, contrast minimization, beta-blocker/amiodarone AF prophylaxis, rigid sternal fixation, glucose control.
readmission_drivers: Heart failure/volume overload; new arrhythmia (AF, complete heart block/syncope requiring PPM); bleeding on antithrombotics; vascular access complications; AKI; infection (access site, sternal, endocarditis); diuretic-related electrolyte disturbance; pacemaker-related issues. Prevention: early (1-2 wk) follow-up + 30-day TTE, clear diuretic/anticoagulation plans, meet telemetry/PPM threshold before discharge, cardiac rehab, medication reconciliation, transitions-of-care phone call.
guideline_source: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Patients With Valvular Heart Disease (Otto & Nishimura); ERAS Cardiac Surgery Society Recommendations 2019 (Engelman et al.); 2020 ACC Expert Consensus Decision Pathway on Management of Conduction Disturbances after TAVR; STS/ACC TVT Registry standards; ESC/EACTS 2021 VHD guideline (adjunct).
key_citations: Per PubMed: 2020 ACC/AHA VHD Guideline - Otto CM, Nishimura RA, et al. Circulation 2021;143:e72-e227 (PMID 33972115). ERAS Cardiac - Engelman DT, et al. JAMA Surg 2019;154:755-766 (PMID 31054241). Conduction-disturbance consensus - Lilly SM, et al. J Am Coll Cardiol 2020;76:2391-2411 (PMID 33190683; DOI 10.1016/j.jacc.2020.08.050). PARTNER 3 - Mack MJ, et al. N Engl J Med 2019;380:1695-1705 (PMID 30883058). Evolut Low Risk - Popma JJ, et al. N Engl J Med 2019;380:1706-1715 (PMID 30883053). 3M TAVR - Wood DA, et al. JACC Cardiovasc Interv 2019;12:459-469 (PMID 30846085). POPular TAVI - Nijenhuis VJ, et al. N Engl J Med 2020;383:1447-1457 (DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2017815).
evidence_grade: Strong - multiple Class I ACC/AHA recommendations backed by RCTs (PARTNER 3, Evolut Low Risk) for TAVR-vs-SAVR selection and antithrombotic strategy (POPular TAVI). LOS-compression/next-day-discharge and ERAS-cardiac elements are Moderate (observational + consensus, e.g., 3M TAVR); post-TAVR conduction-disturbance management is expert-consensus/Level C.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Group collapses across severity: 216 (surgical valve + cardiac cath, MCC) / 217 (CC) / 218 (no CC/MCC); 219 (valve w/o cath, MCC) / 220 (CC) / 221 (no CC/MCC); 266 (TAVR w/ MCC) / 267 (TAVR w/o MCC). MCC drivers (acute HF, AKI, respiratory failure, stroke, cardiogenic shock, complete heart block) escalate ICU need, roughly double SAVR LOS, and raise mortality. Cath-inclusive DRGs (216-218) index concomitant CAD workup/intervention. SAVR tier carries high ICU utilization; TAVR w/o MCC (267) is the low-LOS, next-day-discharge-eligible cohort.
pathway_pearls: 1) Get the Heart Team + STS-PROM/age/frailty shared decision right - it determines TAVR vs SAVR and screens futility. 2) Pre-procedure RBBB is the key pacemaker-risk flag; a standardized telemetry/EP conduction pathway (ACC 2020) safely enables next-day discharge. 3) Aspirin monotherapy post-TAVR (POPular TAVI) - do NOT reflexively use DAPT, and add no antiplatelet to OAC when AF present. 4) Minimalist transfemoral TAVR + ERAS-cardiac bundle (early extubation, PBM, glucose <180, early mobilization) compresses LOS without increasing readmission.

scope_and_volume_notes: 266-267 = transcatheter (TAVR), fastest-growing; 216-221 = surgical valve +/- cardiac cath. High ICU utilization.

admission_criteria: Most elective CIED implants are same-day/23-h observation, not inpatient. Inpatient admission indicated for: hemodynamically significant or symptomatic high-grade AV block (Mobitz II, 2:1, third-degree/complete heart block) or symptomatic sinus node dysfunction needing urgent pacing or a temporary-pacing bridge; pacemaker-dependent patients; new secondary-prevention ICD after VF/hemodynamically unstable VT arrest or sustained VT without reversible cause; device implanted during an admission for AMI/decompensated HF/cardiogenic shock (DRG 222-226); CIED infection requiring lead extraction; peri-procedural complication (pneumothorax, tamponade, large pocket hematoma). CMS 2-midnight rule: bill inpatient when a >=2-midnight stay is expected and documented, otherwise observation.
risk_stratification: Primary-prevention ICD selection: LVEF <=35% + NYHA II-III on >=3 mo guideline-directed medical therapy (SCD-HeFT) or LVEF <=30% post-MI NYHA I (MADIT-II), with expected meaningful survival >1 yr; CRT/CRT-D when LBBB with QRS >=150 ms (Class I) or 120-149 ms (Class IIa); CHA2DS2-VASc (thromboembolic risk) with HAS-BLED (bleeding) to set periprocedural anticoagulation; PADIT infection-risk score (Prior procedures, Age, Depressed renal function [eGFR<30], Immunocompromised, procedure Type) - higher scores (approx >=7) flag elevated 1-yr infection risk and favor an antibacterial envelope; AV-block type (Mobitz II / third-degree) drives urgency of pacing; frailty/geriatric assessment gates device aggressiveness and survival benefit.
initial_workup_labs: CBC (baseline Hgb/platelets for bleeding risk, cytopenias); CMP with eGFR (contrast for concurrent cath, drug dosing, PADIT input, correct K+/Mg2+ for arrhythmia); coagulation panel PT/INR + aPTT (time and manage periprocedural anticoagulation, target INR generally <=3.0 for continued-warfarin implants); type & screen; troponin if concurrent ACS; BNP/NT-proBNP if HF; TSH and medication review for reversible bradycardia; blood cultures x2 with WBC/CRP if device infection suspected (drives TEE and extraction).
initial_imaging_dx: 12-lead ECG (document rhythm, AV-block grade, QRS duration/morphology for CRT eligibility); continuous telemetry to capture bradyarrhythmia/pauses/VT; TTE for LVEF (ICD/CRT indication), structural/valvular assessment and to exclude effusion (obtain before elective ICD if not recent); pre-procedure PA/lateral CXR (baseline); post-implant CXR within a few hours and/or at ~24 h to confirm lead position and exclude pneumothorax/hemothorax; device interrogation immediately post-implant and again before discharge (sensing, capture thresholds, impedances); TEE if CIED infection/endocarditis suspected.
time_critical_interventions: Pre-incision antibiotic prophylaxis: cefazolin 1-2 g IV within 60 min before skin incision (vancomycin 15 mg/kg within 120 min if MRSA-colonized or beta-lactam allergy) - single dose. Unstable bradycardia/complete heart block: apply transcutaneous pacing pads immediately, escalate to temporary transvenous pacing as a bridge to permanent implant. Post-arrest secondary-prevention ICD after reversible causes excluded. No classic door-to-device time metric (unlike STEMI door-to-balloon); for suspected CIED pocket/lead infection the time-sensitive intervention is prompt complete device + lead extraction (delays increase mortality). Avoid heparin bridging.
initial_management: Antibiotic prophylaxis (cefazolin; vancomycin for MRSA/allergy) - single pre-op dose, NO routine post-op oral antibiotics (PADIT negative). Periprocedural anticoagulation: CONTINUE warfarin (INR <=3.0) rather than heparin-bridge (BRUISE CONTROL); for DOACs, brief interruption (hold 1-2 doses) or continuation both acceptable (BRUISE CONTROL-2); continue ASA, note DAPT raises pocket-hematoma risk. Venous access via axillary/cephalic vein under ultrasound (lowers pneumothorax vs subclavian). Device selection: single/dual-chamber PPM, conduction-system pacing (His-bundle / left-bundle-branch-area) or CRT for high anticipated RV-pacing burden or HFrEF+LBBB; transvenous or subcutaneous ICD for primary/secondary prevention. Absorbable minocycline/rifampin antibacterial envelope in high-risk cases (replacements, upgrades, CRT-D, high PADIT) - WRAP-IT. Meticulous pocket hemostasis; hold/adjust AV-nodal blockers for bradycardia; correct electrolytes.
day1_milestones: POD0 (implant day): pre-procedure time-out, antibiotic delivered <=60 min pre-incision, implant in EP/cath lab under conscious sedation/MAC; immediate post-implant device interrogation (confirm capture, sensing, impedance); post-procedure CXR to confirm lead position and exclude pneumothorax; ipsilateral arm sling/immobilization; continuous telemetry; serial pocket checks for hematoma; resume anticoagulation per plan; ambulate same day if hemodynamically stable; pain control with acetaminophen +/- short-course opioid.
day2_milestones: POD1: repeat/delayed CXR to exclude delayed pneumothorax and confirm lead position; formal device interrogation and programming (rate response, tachytherapy zones, physiologic-pacing thresholds); wound inspection for hematoma/bleeding; reinforce arm-restriction education; confirm stable rhythm with capture on telemetry; enroll in remote monitoring and issue device ID card; MOST uncomplicated implants meet discharge criteria and go home on POD1.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ only if complications or the underlying admitting condition is unresolved: manage pneumothorax (small - observe; large - chest tube), pocket hematoma (compression, reverse over-anticoagulation, avoid evacuation unless threatening since evacuation raises infection risk), tamponade/perforation (echo, pericardiocentesis, surgery), lead dislodgement (revision), or device infection (complete extraction + IV antibiotics + ID). Otherwise optimize HF GDMT / ischemic care driving the stay and finalize disposition.
consults_multidisciplinary: Cardiac electrophysiology (implanter/programming); general cardiology/HF team (GDMT optimization, CRT candidacy); cardiothoracic surgery (backup for tamponade/perforation and for transvenous lead extraction); infectious disease (CIED infection/endocarditis); anticoagulation service/pharmacy (periprocedural bridging decisions, medication reconciliation); CIED device clinic + remote-monitoring enrollment; care management/social work (disposition, device education, driving/return-to-work counseling); PT if deconditioned or elderly; nutrition if HF/comorbid.
monitoring_level: Continuous telemetry standard post-implant to verify capture/sensing and detect arrhythmia. Floor/telemetry for uncomplicated implants. Step-down/ICU for: pacemaker-dependent patients on temporary pacing awaiting permanent implant, post-cardiac-arrest, cardiogenic shock/decompensated HF, or peri-procedural complication (tamponade, large pneumothorax, hemodynamic instability, electrical storm). Escalation triggers: loss of capture, hypotension, expanding hematoma, respiratory distress, recurrent VT/VF.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: resume regular/cardiac (low-sodium if HF) diet promptly after sedation wears off. Mobility: same-day ambulation; ipsilateral arm restriction - no shoulder abduction >90 degrees, no lifting >5 lb, no overhead reaching for 4-6 weeks to prevent lead dislodgement. VTE: early ambulation + mechanical prophylaxis (sequential compression devices); pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis individualized and often deferred given pocket-hematoma risk and short stay. Glucose control (target <180 mg/dL). Remove peripheral IV/Foley early; no indwelling lines at discharge.
discharge_criteria: Satisfactory device interrogation (appropriate sensing, capture thresholds, lead impedances, tachytherapy programmed); stable pneumothorax-free post-implant CXR; hemostatic pocket without expanding hematoma; pain controlled on oral analgesia; stable rhythm with confirmed capture on telemetry; ambulating at baseline; anticoagulation plan finalized; wound-care/arm-restriction/driving education completed and follow-up arranged; underlying admitting condition (AMI/HF/arrhythmia) stabilized.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home (SNF/rehab only if deconditioned or complications). Device ID card + manufacturer registration; enroll remote monitoring (Class I - earlier detection of arrhythmia/lead issues, fewer readmissions); document MRI-conditional status. Medication reconciliation: resume/adjust anticoagulation, do NOT prescribe routine post-op oral antibiotics, continue/optimize HF GDMT and antiarrhythmics. Education: wound care, arm restriction, signs of infection/hematoma, magnet/EMI precautions, driving restrictions (private driving approx 1 week after primary-prevention ICD or generator change; approx 3 months after secondary-prevention device or appropriate shock; commercial driving generally prohibited with an ICD). Follow-up: wound check 7-14 days; device clinic 2-6 weeks; cardiology/HF follow-up.
expected_los: Highly tier-dependent (CMS GMLOS approx): w/o CC/MCC tiers (DRG 227 defib, 244 PPM, 259 replacement) ~1.5-2 days; CC tier (243 PPM) ~2.5-3 days; MCC tiers (226 defib, 242 PPM, 258 replacement) ~5-6 days; defibrillator-with-cardiac-cath tiers (222-225) ~4-8 days depending on AMI/HF/shock and MCC burden. Elective uncomplicated implants are functionally same-day/overnight; inpatient LOS is driven by the underlying admitting diagnosis and CC/MCC burden, not the procedure itself.
target_los: Uncomplicated elective PPM/ICD/generator replacement: same-day discharge or <=1 midnight (target <=1 day). Secondary-prevention ICD or device implanted during an AMI/HF admission: bounded by the underlying condition - target 2-4 days once arrhythmia/HF stabilized. Avoid procedure-related boarding: implant early in the day, confirm interrogation + pneumothorax-free CXR, discharge next morning.
quality_metrics: Registry participation: ACC NCDR EP Device Implant Registry (formerly ICD Registry; CMS National Coverage Determination data-submission mandate for primary-prevention ICDs retired 2018, registry continues). Process/outcome measures: pre-op antibiotic prophylaxis timing/selection; procedural complication rates (pneumothorax, pocket hematoma, cardiac tamponade/perforation, lead dislodgement); CIED infection rate (30-day and 1-year); 30-day all-cause and device-related readmission; in-hospital mortality; evidence-based/appropriate-use documentation (LVEF, GDMT duration, post-MI/post-revascularization waiting-period adherence, expected survival >1 yr); remote-monitoring enrollment rate. No CIED-specific CMS core measure analogous to SEP-1.
common_complications: Pneumothorax ~1-2% (mitigate with axillary/cephalic ultrasound-guided access instead of subclavian); pocket hematoma ~2-10% (mitigate by continuing warfarin rather than bridging, meticulous hemostasis, avoiding unnecessary evacuation which raises infection risk); cardiac perforation/tamponade <1% (echo, pericardiocentesis, surgical backup); lead dislodgement ~1-3% (arm restriction, secure active fixation); CIED infection ~1-2%, higher for replacements/upgrades/CRT (single-dose prophylaxis, antibacterial envelope in high-risk, no bridging); upper-extremity/subclavian venous thrombosis; phrenic-nerve/diaphragmatic stimulation from LV lead (reprogram); inappropriate ICD shocks (guideline-directed programming with high-rate cutoffs and prolonged detection, MADIT-RIT principle); Twiddler syndrome.
readmission_drivers: Top drivers: pocket hematoma/bleeding; CIED infection (early and delayed); lead dislodgement/malfunction requiring revision; HF decompensation; inappropriate ICD shocks/electrical storm; recurrent arrhythmia. Prevention levers: no heparin bridging (BRUISE CONTROL); meticulous hemostasis + axillary/cephalic access; single-dose prophylaxis + antibacterial envelope in high-risk (WRAP-IT); remote monitoring for early lead/arrhythmia detection; guideline-directed ICD programming to cut inappropriate shocks; HF GDMT optimization; structured device-clinic and wound-check follow-up.
guideline_source: 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Guideline on the Evaluation and Management of Patients With Bradycardia and Cardiac Conduction Delay; 2017 AHA/ACC/HRS Guideline for Management of Patients With Ventricular Arrhythmias and the Prevention of Sudden Cardiac Death; 2023 HRS/APHRS/LAHRS Guideline on Cardiac Physiologic Pacing for the Avoidance and Mitigation of Heart Failure. Supporting: HRS/EHRA consensus documents on CIED infection prevention/management and transvenous lead extraction, and HRS consensus on CIED remote monitoring.
key_citations: 2018 ACC/AHA/HRS Bradycardia guideline (Kusumoto FM et al, Circulation/JACC 2019; PMID 30412709). 2017 AHA/ACC/HRS Ventricular Arrhythmia/SCD guideline (Al-Khatib SM et al; PMID 29084733). 2023 HRS/APHRS/LAHRS Cardiac Physiologic Pacing guideline (Chung MK et al, Heart Rhythm 2023; PMID 37283271). Landmark trials: MADIT-II (Moss AJ, NEJM 2002; PMID 11907286); SCD-HeFT (Bardy GH, NEJM 2005; PMID 15659722); DANISH (Kober L, NEJM 2016; PMID 27571011); BRUISE CONTROL continued-warfarin vs heparin bridge (Birnie DH, NEJM 2013; PMID 23659733); BRUISE CONTROL-2 DOAC (Birnie DH, Eur Heart J 2018; PMID 30462279); WRAP-IT antibacterial envelope (Tarakji KG, NEJM 2019; PMID 30883056); PADIT incremental antibiotics (Krahn AD, JACC 2018; PMID 30545448). Verified via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong - device indications rest on Class I ACC/AHA/HRS guideline recommendations backed by RCTs (MADIT-II, SCD-HeFT for primary-prevention ICD; multiple trials for CRT and physiologic pacing). Periprocedural anticoagulation management (BRUISE CONTROL / BRUISE CONTROL-2) and antibacterial envelope in high-risk patients (WRAP-IT) are RCT-supported. Some operational details (exact LOS targets, arm-restriction duration, VTE-prophylaxis timing) are consensus/expert-opinion and carry lower evidence grade.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The collapsed group spans defibrillator-with-cath (222-225), defibrillator-without-cath (226-227), permanent pacemaker (242-244), AICD generator procedures (245), and pacemaker device replacement (258-259). Without-CC/MCC tiers (227, 244, 259) behave like overnight-observation admissions (~1-2 d), often inpatient only by 2-midnight expectation or the admitting diagnosis. MCC (acute MI, cardiogenic shock, acute systolic HF, acute respiratory failure, AKI/dialysis, sepsis) shifts care to ICU/step-down, LOS ~5-8 d, with higher complication and mortality rates. 'With cardiac cath' tiers (222-225) add concurrent ischemic workup/revascularization, contributing contrast-associated AKI and DAPT that complicates pocket bleeding and lengthens stay. Replacements (258-259) are shorter but carry higher per-procedure infection risk (reoperation), making them antibacterial-envelope candidates.
pathway_pearls: 1) Do NOT bridge anticoagulation - continue warfarin through implant (BRUISE CONTROL cut clinically significant pocket hematoma from 16.0% to 3.5%, RR 0.19); brief hold or continuation of DOACs both reasonable (BRUISE CONTROL-2); heparin bridging is the single biggest modifiable driver of hematoma -> infection -> readmission. 2) One pre-incision antibiotic dose (cefazolin <=60 min; vancomycin <=120 min) + axillary/cephalic ultrasound-guided access; add an absorbable antibacterial envelope for high-risk cases (replacements, upgrades, CRT-D, high PADIT) per WRAP-IT; no routine post-op oral antibiotics (PADIT negative). 3) Right patient/right device: primary-prevention ICD only after LVEF <=35% confirmed on optimized GDMT for the mandated waiting period (>=40 d post-MI, >=90 d post-revascularization) with expected meaningful survival >1 yr; CRT when LBBB with QRS >=150 ms; physiologic/conduction-system pacing when high RV-pacing burden is anticipated. 4) Treat this as a <24-48h episode: the procedure is same-day and LOS is driven by the underlying condition - confirm satisfactory interrogation + pneumothorax-free CXR, enroll remote monitoring, and pre-book the wound check (7-14 d) and device clinic (2-6 wks) before discharge.

scope_and_volume_notes: AICD implant (222-227), permanent pacemaker (242-245), device replacement (258-259). EP/cath-lab.
