admission_criteria: Most benign hysterectomies are now scheduled and increasingly ambulatory/same-day (23h) — inpatient admission is the exception, not the rule. Objective indications for inpatient (vs same-day discharge): open/abdominal approach or conversion to laparotomy; prolonged/complex case (large uterus, extensive adhesions/endometriosis, concurrent prolapse or adnexal surgery); intraoperative complication (EBL >500-1000 mL, bladder/ureteral/bowel injury, transfusion); significant comorbidity requiring monitoring (ASA III-IV, decompensated cardiopulmonary disease, OSA on continuous oximetry); uncontrolled pain or intractable PONV; failed void trial with retention; late-day surgical finish; inadequate home support/distance. Emergent inpatient admission: symptomatic anemia or hemorrhage from AUB/fibroids with hemodynamic instability or transfusion need. Uncomplicated minimally invasive (MIS) cases meeting recovery milestones are discharged same day; decision rule = ambulatory unless a specific criterion mandates overnight stay.
risk_stratification: VTE — Caprini Risk Assessment Model: 0-2 low (mechanical prophylaxis only), 3-4 moderate (add pharmacologic), >=5 high (pharmacologic + mechanical, consider extended); gyn-specific data support a modified high-risk cutoff Caprini >=8 (Wagner 2022) to target extended prophylaxis and avoid overtreatment of low-risk MIS. Cardiac — Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI) and ASA Physical Status (I-IV) drive preop optimization/monitoring level. OSA — STOP-BANG >=3 triggers continuous pulse oximetry and opioid limitation. PONV — Apfel score (0-4); >=2 risk factors -> >=2 prophylactic antiemetics. Anemia — preoperative Hgb with iron studies/ferritin; treat iron-deficiency anemia preop (patient blood management). Malignancy exclusion — endometrial sampling for AUB must be benign before entering this pathway; frailty screen for older patients.
initial_workup_labs: Preoperative: CBC (baseline Hgb/Hct, detect anemia); type & screen, with type & crossmatch when significant hemorrhage anticipated (large fibroid uterus, abdominal approach); basic metabolic panel; urine or serum beta-hCG in all reproductive-age patients (positive -> cancel); coagulation studies only if anticoagulated or liver disease. AUB workup before benign designation: ferritin/iron studies, TSH, and endometrial biopsy/hysteroscopy to exclude hyperplasia/carcinoma. Triggers: Hgb <7-8 g/dL -> transfusion decision and preop optimization; positive hCG -> cancel; abnormal endometrial pathology -> reroute to gyn-oncology (DRG 736-741). Postoperative labs only if clinically indicated (symptomatic anemia, significant EBL -> CBC; fluid/renal concern -> BMP) — routine postop labs are not required after uncomplicated MIS.
initial_imaging_dx: Transvaginal +/- transabdominal pelvic ultrasound is first-line to characterize uterine size, fibroids, adenomyosis, and adnexa. Pelvic MRI for complex fibroids/adenomyosis or when US is inconclusive for surgical planning. Office endometrial biopsy or diagnostic hysteroscopy for AUB to exclude malignancy prior to benign classification. Intraoperative cystoscopy after hysterectomy to confirm bilateral ureteral patency and bladder integrity (strongly advised with concurrent prolapse repair or difficult dissection). No routine postoperative imaging; obtain CT abdomen/pelvis or ultrasound only when a complication is suspected (hematoma, pelvic abscess, ureteral injury, ileus/obstruction).
time_critical_interventions: Elective surgery, so time-critical targets are the perioperative bundle: antibiotic prophylaxis administered within 60 minutes before skin incision (SCIP-Inf-1) — cefazolin 2 g IV (3 g if >=120 kg); intraoperative redose if procedure >3-4 h (>2 antibiotic half-lives) or EBL >1500 mL; discontinue prophylaxis within 24 h. Modern fasting: clear liquids/carbohydrate drink up to 2 h preop, solids up to 6 h. Chlorhexidine skin antisepsis plus vaginal preparation (chlorhexidine-gluconate aqueous or povidone-iodine). Maintain active intraoperative normothermia (core temp >=36 C). Sequential compression devices applied before induction. Foley catheter removed within 6 h postoperatively or on POD0/POD1. Glucose maintained <180 mg/dL. For emergent hemorrhage: activate transfusion, tranexamic acid, and surgical/IR hemostatic control within minutes to hours.
initial_management: Source control = the hysterectomy itself, performed by the least invasive feasible route (ACOG 701 hierarchy: vaginal > laparoscopic/robotic > abdominal). Antibiotic prophylaxis: cefazolin 2 g (3 g if >=120 kg); beta-lactam/penicillin allergy -> clindamycin + (gentamicin OR aztreonam OR fluoroquinolone) or metronidazole + (gentamicin OR fluoroquinolone); cefoxitin/cefotetan or ampicillin-sulbactam acceptable alternatives. Opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia: scheduled acetaminophen 1 g q6h + NSAID (ketorolac/ibuprofen/celecoxib) + regional technique (TAP block, local wound infiltration, or spinal for vaginal cases), opioids as rescue only; routine gabapentinoids de-emphasized (sedation/no benefit). PONV prophylaxis with >=2 agents (dexamethasone + ondansetron +/- scopolamine) per Apfel. Fluids: goal-directed euvolemia with balanced crystalloid, avoid overload. Transfusion: restrictive threshold Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 with cardiac disease/symptoms); tranexamic acid for hemorrhage.
day1_milestones: POD0: For MIS (majority) target same-day discharge — full PACU recovery, multimodal oral analgesia established, early regular diet as tolerated, ambulation within hours of surgery, Foley removed with successful void trial and acceptable post-void residual, PONV controlled, hemostasis confirmed. For inpatient/abdominal cases: out of bed and ambulating POD0 evening, early feeding (clears advancing to regular diet — do NOT keep NPO), Foley out within 6 h or kept overnight, incentive spirometry, transition from IV to oral analgesia, VTE prophylaxis (mechanical +/- pharmacologic per Caprini) active, sham feeding/chewing gum to promote bowel recovery.
day2_milestones: POD1: Tolerating regular diet; converted to oral analgesia only; ambulating independently at baseline; Foley removed with documented successful void trial (rule out urinary retention); bowel-recovery measures continued (ambulation, chewing gum, stool softener/osmotic laxative, opioid minimization); afebrile and hemodynamically stable with no active bleeding; wound intact. Most MIS patients not discharged POD0 and many abdominal patients meet discharge criteria on POD1. Screen for postoperative urinary retention before discharge.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ (reserved for abdominal/open or complicated cases — a POD2+ stay after MIS signals a complication): confirm return of bowel function (flatus/bowel movement), tolerance of regular diet, independent mobility, adequate pain control on oral analgesics, and absence of infection, ileus, retention, or VTE. Actively manage any complication (postoperative ileus, urinary retention, SSI/cuff cellulitis, transfusion recovery, bladder/ureteral injury). Discharge when objective criteria met — typically POD1-2 for abdominal hysterectomy; extension beyond POD2 warrants complication workup.
consults_multidisciplinary: Preoperative anesthesia/perioperative optimization clinic for ASA III-IV, OSA, or cardiac disease. Pharmacy for medication reconciliation, multimodal analgesia and anticoagulation management. Care management/social work early for disposition, home support, and transitions. Urogynecology (concurrent prolapse) or gyn-oncology (upgraded pathology) as indicated. Hematology for complex anemia or anticoagulation. Nutrition for malnutrition/preoperative anemia optimization. Physical therapy for deconditioned/older patients. Urology or general surgery intraoperatively for bladder/ureteral or bowel injury; interventional radiology for hemorrhage. Cardiology/pulmonary consultation driven by comorbidity. Acute pain service for complex/opioid-tolerant patients.
monitoring_level: Routine pathway: PACU then surgical floor, or ambulatory recovery unit with same-day discharge for uncomplicated MIS. Telemetry/step-down for significant cardiac disease, large intraoperative fluid shifts/hemorrhage, or postoperative instability. ICU is rarely required — reserved for major hemorrhage with hemodynamic instability, massive transfusion, or decompensated comorbidity. OSA (STOP-BANG >=3) warrants continuous pulse oximetry with opioid limitation. Escalation triggers: rising early-warning score (NEWS/MEWS), tachycardia/hypotension or falling Hgb (occult hemorrhage), oliguria, hypoxia, uncontrolled pain, or intractable vomiting.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: early feeding — regular diet advanced within 24 h (often POD0); no routine nasogastric tube; chewing gum/sham feeding and osmotic laxative + stool softener to hasten bowel recovery. Mobility: early/same-day ambulation on POD0, out of bed multiple times daily. VTE: mechanical prophylaxis (sequential compression devices) universally intra- and postoperatively, plus pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily, or unfractionated heparin 5000 U SC q8-12h) stratified by Caprini — low-risk MIS same-day cases may use mechanical only; extended 28-day prophylaxis is standard for malignancy but NOT routine in benign disease (reserve for high Caprini/open cases). Glucose maintained <180 mg/dL. Lines/catheters: Foley removed within 6 h or POD1 with void trial; discontinue IV once tolerating PO; avoid abdominal drains.
discharge_criteria: Tolerating oral intake without intractable nausea/vomiting; pain controlled on oral analgesics; ambulating at or near baseline; spontaneous voiding after catheter removal (passed void trial with acceptable post-void residual, typically <150-200 mL); afebrile and hemodynamically stable; no active vaginal or intra-abdominal bleeding; adequate hemoglobin/hemodynamic status; return of bowel function (flatus) for abdominal cases (not mandatory for MIS same-day); patient/caregiver understands wound care, activity restrictions, and return precautions.
discharge_planning: Disposition home (routine). Medication reconciliation: discontinue unneeded preop meds; opioid stewardship (short 3-5 day non-opioid-first course with counseling); scheduled acetaminophen + NSAID; stool softener/laxative with any opioid; resume/bridge anticoagulation; continue oral iron for anemia. Education: pelvic rest, lifting/activity restrictions, no intercourse ~6 weeks and vaginal-cuff precautions after total hysterectomy, wound care, and explicit warning signs (fever, heavy bleeding, worsening pain, inability to void, unilateral leg swelling or dyspnea for VTE). Follow-up: nurse phone check within days, clinic visit at 2-6 weeks, and pathology review communicated. Transitions of care: discharge summary to PCP/referring clinician.
expected_los: Approximate CMS benchmarks (FY-dependent): MS-DRG 743 (non-malignancy WITHOUT CC/MCC) GMLOS ~1.4-1.6 days (AMLOS ~1.8, relative weight ~1.0-1.1); MS-DRG 742 (WITH CC/MCC) GMLOS ~2.2-2.5 days (AMLOS ~3+, relative weight ~1.6-1.7). Caveat: the large outpatient/23-hour shift means most uncomplicated MIS cases are now same-day (0 inpatient days) and fall outside these inpatient DRGs entirely; the DRGs increasingly capture open/abdominal and complicated cases.
target_los: Under an ideal ERAS pathway: minimally invasive (vaginal, laparoscopic, robotic) hysterectomy = same-day discharge (0 inpatient days) or <=23-hour observation; abdominal/open hysterectomy = 1 day (target <=1-2 days). Best practice concentrates cases in the ambulatory/POD1 range through route optimization and ERAS bundle compliance.
quality_metrics: SCIP/perioperative: prophylactic antibiotic within 1 h before incision (SCIP-Inf-1), appropriate agent selection (SCIP-Inf-2), discontinuation within 24 h (SCIP-Inf-3), VTE prophylaxis ordered and received (SCIP-VTE-1/2), perioperative normothermia and glucose control. ERAS protocol compliance rate (dose-dependent — higher compliance correlates with shorter LOS and fewer complications). Route-of-hysterectomy metric (% minimally invasive) as a surgical quality indicator; same-day discharge rate. Surgical site infection rate; ureteral/bladder injury rate; transfusion rate; PONV rate; cuff dehiscence rate. 30-day unplanned readmission and 30-day ED-return rates; opioid prescribing quantity metrics; patient-reported outcome measures.
common_complications: Surgical site infection / vaginal cuff cellulitis / pelvic abscess (mitigated by weight-based timely antibiotic prophylaxis, vaginal prep, MIS route). Urinary retention and UTI (early Foley removal, mandatory void trial). VTE (Caprini-based prophylaxis, early mobilization). Hemorrhage/hematoma requiring transfusion (meticulous hemostasis, MIS, restrictive transfusion, tranexamic acid). Bladder/ureteral/bowel injury (careful dissection, intraoperative cystoscopy). Vaginal cuff dehiscence (higher after laparoscopic closure; secure suture technique and activity restriction). Postoperative ileus (opioid-sparing analgesia, early feeding/mobility, chewing gum, laxatives). PONV (multimodal prophylaxis). Atelectasis (incentive spirometry, early ambulation).
readmission_drivers: Top drivers: surgical site/pelvic infection and abscess; vaginal cuff dehiscence; urinary retention and UTI; postoperative bleeding/hematoma; uncontrolled pain; VTE; bowel obstruction/ileus. 30-day readmission after benign hysterectomy is low (~2-5%). Prevention levers: MIS route + timely antibiotic prophylaxis (infection); documented successful void trial before discharge (retention); secure cuff closure + activity restriction and vaginal-cuff counseling (dehiscence); ERAS bowel regimen and opioid minimization (ileus); explicit written return precautions plus a scheduled nurse phone check within days; adequate but stewarded analgesia; timely 2-6 week follow-up.
guideline_source: ERAS Society Guidelines for Gynecologic (Oncology) Surgery — Nelson et al., 2019 update and 2026 update (the standard ERAS perioperative pathway applied to benign hysterectomy; derived from the gyn-oncology population and extrapolated to benign disease). ACOG Committee Opinion No. 701 (2017, reaffirmed) — Choosing the Route of Hysterectomy for Benign Disease. ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 195 (2018) — Prevention of Infection After Gynecologic Procedures. VTE prophylaxis per Caprini risk model with ACCP/ASH principles.

evidence_grade: Strong — Class I society guidelines. Core elements (minimally invasive route preference, weight-based timed antibiotic prophylaxis, opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia, early feeding/mobilization, same-day discharge) are backed by RCTs/meta-analyses and graded within the ERAS GRADE framework and ACOG Level A/B recommendations. One caveat lowering certainty slightly: the ERAS gynecologic guideline evidence base is derived from the gyn-oncology population and extrapolated to benign hysterectomy (still the accepted standard of care); benign-specific same-day-discharge and VTE-duration data are largely observational.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The collapsed group spans same-day MIS to complicated open surgery. MS-DRG 743 (WITHOUT CC/MCC) is the uncomplicated majority — MIS route, same-day/POD1, floor-level care. MS-DRG 742 (WITH CC/MCC) is triggered by acute blood-loss/symptomatic anemia or transfusion, pelvic infection/sepsis, AKI, bladder/ureteral/bowel injury, VTE, postoperative ileus/obstruction, or cardiac decompensation; these shift patients from ambulatory to multi-day stays, raise monitoring level (telemetry/step-down, rarely ICU), and account for most readmissions. Open/abdominal approach concentrates complications and severity in 742. The ideal pathway keeps the majority in 743/outpatient by MIS route selection and ERAS adherence, reserving 742 resource intensity for genuine complications.
pathway_pearls: 1) Route selection is the master lever — vaginal > laparoscopic/robotic > abdominal (ACOG 701); minimally invasive route independently drives same-day discharge, fewer SSIs/VTE, and lower cost, so maximize the MIS rate. 2) ERAS compliance is dose-dependent — bundle carbohydrate loading, opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia, >=2-agent PONV prophylaxis, early feeding/mobilization, and early Foley removal; higher compliance = shorter LOS and fewer complications. 3) Nail the SSI-prevention trio: weight-based cefazolin (2 g; 3 g if >=120 kg) within 60 min of incision + vaginal prep + intraoperative normothermia and glucose control. 4) Prevent the top bounce-backs: mandatory void trial before discharge, explicit written return precautions, and a proactive nurse phone check within 48-72 h to catch retention, infection, and cuff dehiscence early.

admission_criteria: Admit when incomplete response to ~1h of ED bronchodilator therapy: post-treatment FEV1 or PEF <40% predicted/personal best (GINA), persistent SpO2 <90-92% on room air, ongoing accessory-muscle use/dyspnea, or SABA need more often than every 3-4h; post-treatment FEV1/PEF 40-60% -> individualize (short-stay/observation vs admit) by risk and follow-up access; discharge home if post-treatment PEF/FEV1 >60-80% with sustained symptom improvement. Lower the admission threshold for high-risk features: prior near-fatal asthma (ICU/intubation), hospitalization or ED visit within the past year, SABA overuse or no ICS controller, poor adherence/health literacy, food allergy, psychosocial barriers, or unreliable follow-up. Life-threatening/status asthmaticus features -> direct ICU admission.
risk_stratification: Adults/adolescents -- objective airflow (PEF/FEV1 % predicted or personal best): mild >=70%, moderate 40-69%, severe <40%, life-threatening <25%; BTS/3Mg acute-severe criteria = PEF <50% + RR >25 + HR >110 or unable to complete a sentence in one breath -> escalate/admit. SpO2 <92% (or <90%) after 1h treatment predicts need for admission/ABG. Life-threatening: SpO2 <90-92% on O2, silent chest, poor effort/exhaustion, altered consciousness, bradycardia/hypotension, cyanosis, or normal-to-rising PaCO2 >=42 mmHg -> ICU. Children 2-17y -- PRAM (0-12: O2 sat, suprasternal + scalene retractions, air entry, wheeze): 0-3 mild, 4-7 moderate, 8-12 severe -> drives neb frequency, steroid, MgSO4, admission and PICU triage.
initial_workup_labs: Continuous pulse oximetry on all. VBG/ABG if severe or SpO2 <92% or PEF <50% -- watch for a normalizing/rising PaCO2 (impending failure), acidosis, and beta-agonist lactic acidosis that can mimic clinical worsening. BMP for potassium (SABA-induced hypokalemia -> replete) and glucose (steroid hyperglycemia). CBC (steroid/stress leukocytosis common; eosinophilia flags T2 phenotype/biologic candidacy). Respiratory viral panel (influenza, RSV, rhinovirus, SARS-CoV-2), especially pediatric. Serum theophylline level only if the patient is on it. Blood/sputum cultures and procalcitonin ONLY when bacterial infection is suspected -- not routine. BNP/troponin only if a cardiac mimic (HF) is in the differential.
initial_imaging_dx: Chest x-ray is NOT routine -- obtain only for suspected complication or diagnostic uncertainty (pneumonia, pneumothorax/pneumomediastinum, atelectasis/mucus plug, suspected foreign body in children, or failure to respond). The key 'diagnostic' is objective airflow: bedside PEF (and spirometry/FEV1 when able) at presentation and after each treatment cycle (~hourly) to grade severity and track response; PRAM scoring in children pre/post treatment. ECG if age >50, cardiac comorbidity, or significant tachyarrhythmia. No routine advanced imaging.
time_critical_interventions: The first hour is the bundle. Inhaled SABA within minutes of triage: albuterol/salbutamol 4-10 puffs pMDI+spacer or 2.5-5 mg nebulized every 20 min x3, or continuous 10-15 mg/h for severe. Systemic corticosteroid within 1h of arrival (single largest relapse-reduction lever): prednisone/prednisolone 40-60 mg PO or methylprednisolone 40-125 mg IV; pediatric dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg (max 16 mg) or prednisolone 1-2 mg/kg. Add ipratropium 0.5 mg (peds 0.25-0.5 mg) nebulized x3 in the first hour for moderate-severe (reduces admissions). Controlled/titrated O2 to SpO2 93-95% (children 94-98%). IV magnesium sulfate 2 g over 20 min (peds 40-50 mg/kg, max 2 g) for severe/life-threatening not responding within the first hour. Formal reassessment at 1h (PEF/FEV1, SpO2, work of breathing) drives disposition.
initial_management: First-line: inhaled SABA (albuterol/salbutamol) scheduled then weaned by response; inhaled SAMA (ipratropium) added for moderate-severe; systemic corticosteroids (prednisone/prednisolone 40-50 mg/day x5-7d adults; peds dexamethasone 0.6 mg/kg x1-2 doses or prednisolone 1-2 mg/kg/day x3-5d -- no taper for short courses). Controlled oxygen to SpO2 93-95%. Escalation for refractory/severe: IV magnesium sulfate 2 g; IV/SC beta-agonist (terbutaline 0.25 mg SC or infusion; epinephrine IM 0.3-0.5 mg or infusion 5-15 mcg/min); ketamine and/or heliox; NIV trial; aminophylline only rarely. Antibiotics are NOT routine -- reserve for documented bacterial infection (consolidation, purulent sputum + fever). Start/continue an ICS-containing controller (ICS-formoterol) during admission, not just at discharge.
day1_milestones: Confirm asthma and exclude mimics (HF, PE, pneumonia, COPD, vocal-cord dysfunction, anaphylaxis, aspirated foreign body). Grade severity objectively (PEF/FEV1; PRAM in children) and set scheduled SABA (weaning from q20min toward q1-2h as improving) + systemic steroid + controlled O2. Continuous SpO2 +/- cardiorespiratory monitoring; check and replete potassium; monitor glucose. Identify and remove triggers; begin inhaler-technique teaching with a spacer. Smoking-cessation counseling. VTE risk assessment (Padua/Caprini) for admitted adults. Set the target discharge date on day 1.
day2_milestones: Wean SABA to every 4-6h and consolidate to pMDI+spacer; continue systemic steroids. Optimize/step-up the controller: start or intensify ICS or ICS-LABA (ICS-formoterol) -- the discharge regimen begins now. Demonstrate SpO2 >=92-94% on room air including overnight; wean supplemental O2. Deliver structured asthma education and begin a written asthma action plan; re-check inhaler technique by teach-back. Reassess against discharge criteria.
day3plus_milestones: Confirm discharge readiness: off supplemental O2 >=12-24h (including overnight) with SpO2 >=92-94% RA, SABA spacing >=every 4h, PEF/FEV1 >=70% predicted/personal best, comfortable ambulation. Finalize the written action plan and verify inhaler technique by teach-back; controller prescribed/reconciled and confirmed affordable/available; follow-up booked; vaccinations addressed. Prolonged stays (Day 3+) usually reflect CC/MCC (respiratory failure, pneumonia) or slow responders -- escalate the controller, reconsider a mimic/complication, and consider pulmonary/allergy plus biologic evaluation.
consults_multidisciplinary: Respiratory therapy (nebulization, PEF/spirometry, inhaler and spacer teaching) -- day 0. Pharmacy (medication reconciliation, inhaler technique, cost/access, steroid/insulin) -- early. Asthma educator/RN and Social work/Care management (triggers, housing/environmental exposures, medication access, follow-up, SDOH) -- day 1. Pulmonology and/or Allergy-Immunology for severe or frequent exacerbations, diagnostic uncertainty, or biologic candidacy (omalizumab, mepolizumab/benralizumab, dupilumab, tezepelumab). Critical care/ICU (adult) or PICU + Pediatric Pulmonology (children) for status asthmaticus. Nutrition if comorbidity present; PT/OT only if deconditioned.
monitoring_level: Most patients: general floor with continuous pulse oximetry. Step-down/telemetry or continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring for continuous or frequent (more often than q2h) SABA, significant hypokalemia/arrhythmia risk, or cardiac comorbidity. ICU/PICU for status asthmaticus: continuous nebs beyond floor capacity, NIV, IV beta-agonist/magnesium infusions, exhaustion, altered mental status, SpO2 <90% despite O2, PEF <40% despite therapy, rising/normalizing PaCO2 (>=42 mmHg) with acidosis, hemodynamic instability, or need for intubation. Escalate immediately on any life-threatening feature.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet as tolerated with adequate hydration; avoid known food triggers. Early mobilization/ambulation as breathing allows. VTE prophylaxis by risk (Padua/Caprini) -- mechanical +/- pharmacologic; note systemic corticosteroids modestly raise VTE risk, though most short-stay asthmatics remain ambulatory. Glucose control with monitoring +/- correctional insulin for steroid hyperglycemia (especially diabetics). Monitor and replace potassium/magnesium (SABA-induced hypokalemia). Remove peripheral IV/lines promptly once IV therapy is no longer required.
discharge_criteria: Clinically stable >=12-24h on the ward regimen with SABA needed no more often than every 4h; off supplemental oxygen with SpO2 >=92-94% on room air sustained overnight; PEF or FEV1 >=70% of predicted or personal best; comfortable ambulation without significant dyspnea; correct inhaler technique demonstrated by teach-back; patient/caregiver understands medications and the written action plan; controller prescription secured.
discharge_planning: Discharge on an ICS-containing controller -- NEVER SABA-only (GINA); prefer ICS-formoterol as reliever/MART where appropriate. Complete the oral corticosteroid course (5-7d adults; 3-5d or dexamethasone in children, no taper). Full medication reconciliation; ensure inhaler + spacer supplied and affordable with a teach-back technique check. Provide a written asthma action plan. Schedule early follow-up (ideally 2-7 days; within 1 week for children) with PCP/pulmonology and arrange outpatient spirometry. Address trigger/environmental control, smoking cessation, and vaccinations (influenza, COVID-19, pneumococcal). Refer eligible severe/frequent-exacerbator patients for biologic evaluation.
expected_los: MS-DRG 203 (without CC/MCC): GMLOS ~2.2 days, AMLOS ~2.6 days (relative weight ~0.67). MS-DRG 202 (with CC/MCC): GMLOS ~2.9 days, AMLOS ~3.6 days (relative weight ~0.97). Uncomplicated pediatric asthma admissions commonly run 1-2 days; status asthmaticus with ICU care runs longer.
target_los: Optimized best-practice: MS-DRG 203 ~1.5-2.0 days; MS-DRG 202 ~2.5-3.0 days; uncomplicated pediatric asthma ~1-1.5 days. Achieved via first-hour steroid + 1h reassessment, structured SABA weaning, early controller start, and same-day discharge planning. Status asthmaticus (ICU ~2-4 days plus floor time) is the main upward outlier.
quality_metrics: HEDIS Asthma Medication Ratio (AMR) controller-to-total ratio >=0.50 (ages 5-64) and Medication Management for People with Asthma (MMA; >=50%/>=75% of treatment period covered). AHRQ preventable-admission indicators: PDI #14 (pediatric asthma admission rate) and PQI #5 (COPD/asthma in older adults) -- asthma is an ambulatory-care-sensitive condition. Process/outcome measures: systemic steroid within 1h, discharge on an ICS controller, written action plan provided, inhaler-technique teach-back, and 7-/30-day post-discharge follow-up plus ED-revisit/readmission tracking. Note: Joint Commission Children's Asthma Care (CAC-1/2/3) measures were retired (2016) and asthma is not in the CMS HRRP.
common_complications: Progression to status asthmaticus/respiratory failure needing NIV or intubation; barotrauma (pneumothorax/pneumomediastinum); beta-agonist effects (hypokalemia, tachyarrhythmia, tremor, lactic acidosis that can mimic worsening dyspnea); steroid hyperglycemia/mood/BP effects; mucus plugging with atelectasis; dehydration; hospital-acquired pneumonia; rarely hypoxic-ischemic injury from arrest. Mitigation: controlled O2, objective 1h reassessment, electrolyte/glucose monitoring, judicious sedation, and lung-protective ventilation with permissive hypercapnia if intubated.
readmission_drivers: SABA over-reliance with under-use of an ICS controller; poor inhaler technique; nonadherence; absent or delayed follow-up; unaddressed triggers (tobacco/second-hand smoke, allergens, mold, pets, occupational/environmental exposures); intercurrent viral infection; socioeconomic barriers (housing, insurance, drug cost); failure to prescribe/fill a controller at discharge; and no written action plan. Prevention levers: guaranteed ICS-containing controller at discharge, teach-back inhaler technique, action plan, early follow-up (48-72h to 1 week), trigger/SDOH mitigation, and biologics for eligible severe patients.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: GINA 2025 -- Global Initiative for Asthma, Global Strategy for Asthma Management and Prevention (2025 update; revised annually, 2026 update in circulation). US companion: NHLBI/NAEPP 2020 Focused Updates to the Asthma Management Guidelines (EPR-4 Working Group; builds on EPR-3, 2007). Supportive: pediatric PRAM severity scoring, ERS/ATS severe-asthma guidance, and NICE/BTS/SIGN acute-asthma guidance.
key_citations: GINA 2025 report -- Venkatesan P, Lancet Respir Med 2025 (PMID 40582369; DOI 10.1016/S2213-2600(25)00242-5). NHLBI 2020 Focused Updates -- Cloutier MM et al., J Allergy Clin Immunol 2020;146(6):1217-1270 (PMID 33280709; DOI 10.1016/j.jaci.2020.10.003). 3Mg trial (IV/nebulized magnesium in acute severe asthma) -- Goodacre S et al., Lancet Respir Med 2013;1(4):293-300 (PMID 24429154; DOI 10.1016/S2213-2600(13)70070-5) with HTA report 2014;18(22) (PMID 24731521). Early systemic corticosteroids / relapse prevention -- Rowe BH et al., Cochrane (CD002178; CD000195). PRAM validation -- Chalut/Ducharme, J Pediatr. Citations verified via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong -- Class I / Grade A society-guideline backing (GINA, NHLBI/NAEPP) for early systemic corticosteroids, inhaled SABA, controlled oxygen, and discharge on an ICS-containing controller. Moderate for ipratropium add-on in moderate-severe disease. Weak/uncertain for IV magnesium (the 3Mg RCT was negative for admission/dyspnea; still guideline-suggested for severe/life-threatening non-responders). Consensus/expert opinion for ICU ventilation strategy (permissive hypercapnia), ketamine, heliox, and IV beta-agonists.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 203 (no CC/MCC): mild-moderate exacerbation, floor care, GMLOS ~2.2d, target ~1.5-2d, low complication rate. DRG 202 (with CC/MCC): severity driven by acute respiratory failure (MCC), status asthmaticus, pneumonia, sepsis, or comorbid COPD/CHF -> more step-down/ICU time, GMLOS ~2.9d, higher relative weight (0.97 vs 0.67). Status asthmaticus with acute respiratory failure -> ICU +/- mechanical ventilation; intubation can shift the case out of 202/203 into respiratory-failure DRG 189 or ventilator DRGs 207 (MV >96h)/208, with markedly longer LOS. The substantial pediatric share generally carries shorter LOS but the same escalation logic (PRAM -> PICU).
pathway_pearls: 1) Give a systemic corticosteroid within the first hour AND never discharge on SABA-only -- send every patient out on an ICS-containing controller (ICS-formoterol MART) with a written action plan; this is the biggest lever against relapse/readmission. 2) Drive disposition with objective severity and 1-hour response (PEF/FEV1 in adults, PRAM in children), not subjective wheeze. 3) Use controlled/titrated oxygen to SpO2 93-95% (94-98% peds), not blanket high-flow; beware the tiring asthmatic whose PaCO2 'normalizes' (>=42 mmHg) -- that is impending failure -> ICU. 4) Skip routine chest x-ray and antibiotics (low-value unless a specific complication/infection is suspected); invest instead in inhaler teach-back, trigger/SDOH mitigation, and follow-up within 48-72h to 1 week.
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Bronchitis & asthma DRGs; substantial pediatric share. Status asthmaticus -> Critical Care.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 202 — Bronchitis and Asthma with CC/MCC; 203 — Bronchitis and Asthma without CC/MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 4/4 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Current pass completed citation-resolvability, coding, taxonomy, schema, and consistency checks. The source workflow contains no completed adversarial high-stakes clinical verdict for this row; clinician revalidation remains required before protocol or order-set use.
data_quality_notes: Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk. Current DRG overlap with pathway rank(s): 95.

mdc: 23 — Factors Influencing Health Status & Other Contacts
admission_criteria: IRF-level admission (vs SNF/home health/outpatient/LTACH) requires ALL Medicare IRF coverage criteria (42 CFR 412.622): patient can tolerate AND benefit from INTENSIVE multidisciplinary therapy = >=3 hrs/day, >=5 days/wk OR 15 hrs within any 7 consecutive days; needs >=2 therapy disciplines (PT, OT, SLP, prosthetics/orthotics) with at least one PT or OT; requires close rehab-physician supervision (face-to-face >=3 days/wk); requires coordinated interdisciplinary team (weekly conference); reasonable expectation of MEASURABLE, practical improvement in a predictable period; medically stable enough to participate (no active acute workup); documented in preadmission screening within 48h prior + post-admission physician evaluation within 24h + individualized overall plan of care (IPOC) within 4 days. Route to SNF if can only tolerate ~1-2 hrs/day (low-intensity), to LTACH if ventilator/complex-medical, to home health/outpatient if higher-functioning. 60% Rule: unit must keep >=60% of cases in the 13 qualifying diagnoses (stroke, SCI, brain injury, amputation, major multiple trauma, hip/femur fracture, neuro disorders, burns, active RA/vasculitis, severe bilateral weight-bearing OA, and joint replacement ONLY if bilateral, BMI>=50, or age>=85).
risk_stratification: Section GG self-care (GG0130) + mobility (GG0170) admission performance -> sets baseline, discharge goals, drives Case-Mix Group (CMG); CMG = Rehab Impairment Category + motor score + cognitive score + age -> expected LOS/payment tier; FIM (Functional Independence Measure, 18 items 18-126) + FIM efficiency (FIM gain/LOS) for benchmarking; AM-PAC '6-Clicks' mobility/activity -> disposition decision; NIHSS for stroke severity (>16 predicts poor functional prognosis, guides intensity/expectations); modified Rankin Scale (mRS 0-6); Morse Fall Scale >45 (or Hendrich II >=5) -> fall-prevention bundle; Braden Scale <=18 -> pressure-injury surface + q2h turning; Charlson Comorbidity Index / comorbidity tier -> medical-instability & transfer-back risk; Berg Balance <45/56 and 10-m walk/6-min walk test -> gait safety; MoCA / cognitive-FIM -> cognitive rehab & supervision level; GLIM/MUST malnutrition screen + albumin/prealbumin -> nutrition consult.
initial_workup_labs: CBC (post-op/occult anemia -> transfuse if symptomatic or Hgb<7 g/dL restrictive, <8 if cardiac); CMP (renal function for drug dosing, electrolytes, glucose, LFTs); glucose/HbA1c (target inpatient 140-180 mg/dL, secondary prevention); lipid panel (stroke -> high-intensity statin); INR if on warfarin (dose titration) / anti-Xa if DOAC concern; type & screen if recent surgery/anemia; UA + culture if catheter/symptoms (rule out CAUTI); wound culture if surgical-site concern; TSH/B12/folate if cognitive or neuro decline; nutrition labs (albumin, prealbumin, vitamin D for hip fracture) -> dietitian + bone-health rx; medication reconciliation with renal dosing. Abnormals trigger transfusion, anticoagulation adjustment, glucose protocol, nutrition consult, or hold/modify therapy.
initial_imaging_dx: Most cross-sectional imaging completed in acute care; in rehab targeted only: bedside dysphagia screen for ALL stroke patients BEFORE any oral intake, with videofluoroscopic swallow study (VFSS/modified barium swallow) or FEES within ~24-48h if screen failed (aspiration prevention); post-void residual/bladder scan for retention or neurogenic bladder; DVT duplex ultrasound if calf/leg symptoms; post-op joint radiographs only if pain/deformity/suspected dislocation; repeat CT/MRI brain only for new/worsening neuro deficit (not routine); ECG + telemetry only if arrhythmia/cardiac indication; confirm surgeon's weight-bearing status and hip precautions from operative note before mobilizing.
time_critical_interventions: Post-admission rehab-physician evaluation <24h of admission; therapy must begin within 36h of midnight of admission day; IPOC finalized by rehab physician + interdisciplinary team within 4 days (72-96h); dysphagia screen BEFORE first oral intake in stroke (NPO until passed) to prevent aspiration pneumonia; VTE prophylaxis ordered on admission; early but DOSE-TITRATED mobilization -- start out-of-bed activity ~24-48h, and AVOID ultra-early high-dose mobilization <24h post-stroke (AVERT trial showed worse 3-month outcomes); weekly interdisciplinary team conference to track Section GG/FIM progress vs goals; immediate recognition/transfer-back protocol for medical deterioration (early-warning score e.g. NEWS2).
initial_management: Core 'treatment' is intensive scheduled therapy (3 hrs/day): PT (gait, transfers, strength, balance, body-weight-support treadmill), OT (ADLs, upper-extremity task-specific repetitive practice, constraint-induced movement therapy for hemiparesis), SLP (dysphagia therapy, aphasia, cognition). Spasticity: stretching/orthotics, oral baclofen or tizanidine, focal botulinum toxin (onabotulinumtoxinA). Secondary stroke prevention: antiplatelet (aspirin/clopidogrel) or anticoagulation (DOAC/warfarin for AFib), high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80 mg), BP target <130/80, glucose control. Post-arthroplasty/fracture: multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (scheduled acetaminophen + NSAID + gabapentinoid, opioids PRN), weight-bearing/hip precautions per surgeon, osteoporosis rx after fragility fracture (calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonate, DEXA). Bowel/bladder program; skin care; PHQ-9 depression screen -> SSRI if indicated; multimodal pain control to enable participation; structured fall-prevention program.
day1_milestones: Rehab-physician post-admission H&P and exam <24h establishing medical stability and rehab prognosis; nursing admission assessment (Section GG baseline, Braden, Morse fall, skin, pain); medication reconciliation (confirm antiplatelet/anticoagulant, statin, analgesia, bowel regimen, VTE prophylaxis started); dysphagia screen before oral intake and diet order (stroke); PT/OT/SLP evaluations initiated with therapy start within 36h; confirm weight-bearing status and precautions from operative note; remove unnecessary Foley/IV lines (CAUTI/infection prevention); establish patient/family goals and preliminary estimated discharge date + disposition hypothesis; case management/social work engaged.
day2_milestones: Full 3 hrs/day therapy underway with tolerance assessed and dose titrated; complete all discipline evaluations and set measurable Section GG discharge goals; establish bowel/bladder continence plan; optimize analgesia and minimize sedating/anticholinergic meds to maximize participation; screen mood (PHQ-9) and cognition (MoCA); nutrition assessment and treat anemia if limiting; confirm home environment, caregiver availability, and durable-medical-equipment (DME) needs; verify secondary-prevention regimen in place.
day3plus_milestones: IPOC finalized by rehab physician + team within 4 days; weekly interdisciplinary team conference tracks FIM/Section GG gain vs goals and projects LOS/discharge date; progressive functional gains (independent transfers, increasing gait distance, stair training, ADL independence); caregiver training sessions and, when appropriate, a home therapeutic pass/trial; optimize spasticity, bone health, secondary prevention; order home evaluation/DME and arrange home-health or outpatient PT/OT; discharge when discharge goals met OR functional plateau (no measurable gain) reached -- typically day 8-16 by diagnosis; escalate/transfer back to acute promptly if medical deterioration.
consults_multidisciplinary: Core interdisciplinary team led by rehab physician (physiatrist): rehab nurses, PT, OT, SLP, rehab/neuro-psychology, therapeutic recreation, social work + case management (day 1 for disposition), registered dietitian, rehab pharmacist, prosthetist/orthotist. Specialty consults as needed: neurology/stroke (recurrent deficit, AFib), orthopedics (dislocation, wound, hardware), cardiology, wound care, urology (neurogenic bladder), psychiatry (refractory depression), physiatry spasticity clinic (botulinum toxin). Pharmacy for anticoagulation, polypharmacy, and Drug Regimen Review. Weekly team conference is mandatory (coverage requirement).
monitoring_level: Floor-level rehabilitation unit with capacity for close physician oversight (rehab-physician face-to-face >=3 days/wk); telemetry ONLY if arrhythmia/cardiac indication; patients must be medically stable -- NOT ICU/step-down candidates. Early-warning monitoring (e.g., NEWS2) with rapid-response access. Escalation / transfer-back-to-acute triggers: hemodynamic instability, chest pain, new/worsening neuro deficit, respiratory distress or hypoxia, suspected sepsis/pneumonia, GI bleed, acute abdomen, DVT/PE, uncontrolled hyper/hypoglycemia, surgical complication (dislocation, dehiscence, deep infection). Roughly 9-13% of IRF stays end in acute-care transfer -- a key monitored metric.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: diet advanced per dysphagia evaluation using IDDSI texture/thickened-liquid levels; high-protein intake for wound healing and sarcopenia; treat malnutrition (dietitian, oral supplements, enteral feeding if unsafe swallow); inpatient glucose target 140-180 mg/dL. Mobility: early, progressive, DOSE-TITRATED mobilization within the 3 hrs/day therapy (avoid ultra-early high-dose per AVERT); out-of-bed daily; fall-prevention bundle. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH enoxaparin, or aspirin/DOAC acceptable for low-risk arthroplasty per AAOS/ACCP) PLUS mechanical intermittent pneumatic compression; extended duration up to ~35 days for hip/knee arthroplasty and immobile stroke. Lines/catheters: remove Foley and unnecessary IV access early (CAUTI/infection prevention); bladder scan for retention.
discharge_criteria: Achieved individualized functional/Section GG discharge goals OR reached measurable functional plateau (no further gain from IRF-intensity therapy); safe for mobility, transfers, and ADLs at target level either independently or with trained caregiver support; medically stable with secondary-prevention regimen established; safe swallow and adequate nutrition/hydration; continence plan in place; home environment, DME, and caregiver ready; either graduated UP to lower level of care (home/outpatient) or, if unable to sustain intensity, transitioned to appropriate lower setting (SNF/home health).
discharge_planning: Disposition: ~75-80% discharge to community/home (Discharge-to-Community measure), remainder to SNF, home with home-health, or acute transfer. Medication reconciliation (anticoagulant/antiplatelet, statin, BP agents, analgesia, bowel regimen, osteoporosis rx). Follow-up: PCP within 7-14 days, specialty (stroke/neurology, orthopedics) as indicated, outpatient PT/OT or home-health therapy scheduled before discharge. Caregiver training completed and documented; home-safety evaluation; DME delivered (walker, wheelchair, commode, grab bars, hip-precaution equipment). Patient/caregiver education: stroke warning signs (BE-FAST), fall prevention, medication adherence, weight-bearing precautions. Transitions of care: Transfer of Health Information to provider AND patient (IRF QRP measures); warm handoff/summary to next provider.
expected_los: IRF-PPS clinical benchmark: overall mean ~12.8 days (MedPAC 2024). Condition-specific means: stroke ~15-16 days; brain injury ~16-18 days; hip/femur fracture ~13 days; debility/deconditioning ~13 days; other neurological ~13-14 days; lower-extremity joint replacement ~8-9 days. Acute-IPPS MS-DRG 945 (Rehabilitation w/ CC/MCC) GMLOS approximately 8-9 days and MS-DRG 946 (w/o CC/MCC) approximately 6-8 days (these acute-hospital DRGs run shorter than IRF-PPS stays; exact CMS Table-5 GMLOS not confirmed here, figures approximate).
target_los: Optimized best-practice targets with discharge-date benchmarking and FIM/Section-GG-efficiency tracking (functional gain per day), shown to cut LOS without harming outcomes (Durand 2020): overall ~11-12 days; stroke 12-14 days; lower-extremity joint replacement 5-7 days; debility ~10-11 days; hip fracture ~11-12 days. Lever is maximizing therapy dose/tolerance and early disposition planning, not premature discharge -- guard against plateau misjudgment and readmission.
quality_metrics: IRF Quality Reporting Program (IRF QRP): Change in Self-Care Score, Change in Mobility Score, Discharge Self-Care Score, Discharge Mobility Score, Discharge Function Score; Discharge to Community; Potentially Preventable 30-Day Post-Discharge Readmission + Potentially Preventable Within-Stay Readmission; Falls with Major Injury + Application of Falls; Changes in Skin Integrity (pressure ulcer/injury); Drug Regimen Review; Transfer of Health Information to Provider and to Patient; NHSN CAUTI, hospital-onset C. difficile, HCP influenza + COVID vaccination; Medicare Spending Per Beneficiary. Compliance/process: 60% Rule presumptive compliance, 3-hour/15-hour intensity documentation, CMG/coding accuracy, FIM gain and FIM efficiency (UDSMR/eRehabData registry).
common_complications: Falls (mitigate: Morse/Hendrich screen + fall-prevention bundle + supervised mobility); medical deterioration requiring acute transfer ~9-13% (recurrent stroke, MI, PE, sepsis, arrhythmia); aspiration pneumonia (dysphagia screen before oral intake, IDDSI diet modification, upright feeding); VTE (pharmacologic + mechanical prophylaxis); pressure injury (Braden, surfaces, q2h turning); CAUTI (early Foley removal); UTI and C. difficile (antibiotic stewardship); delirium (minimize sedatives/anticholinergics, orientation, sleep hygiene); post-stroke depression (PHQ-9 screen, SSRI); pain limiting participation (multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia); orthostasis/deconditioning; prosthetic hip dislocation (hip precautions, equipment).
readmission_drivers: Top bounce-back drivers: infection (pneumonia/aspiration, UTI/CAUTI, C. diff, surgical-site/wound), recurrent stroke or cardiac event, VTE/PE, falls with injury, dehydration/AKI, medication errors or nonadherence (esp. anticoagulation), inadequate caregiver support/unsafe discharge, and premature discharge or functional-plateau misjudgment. Prevention levers: optimize secondary prevention, medication reconciliation + Drug Regimen Review, dysphagia/nutrition management, structured fall prevention, caregiver training, warm handoff with Transfer-of-Health-Information, early PCP/specialty follow-up (7-14 days), and home-health/outpatient therapy continuity.
guideline_source: Regulatory: CMS IRF Coverage Requirements (42 CFR 412.622; Medicare Benefit Policy Manual Ch.1 Sec.110) and IRF-PPS classification / '60% Rule' (42 CFR 412.29), current. Clinical: AHA/ASA 2016 Guidelines for Adult Stroke Rehabilitation and Recovery (Winstein et al.); VA/DoD 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Stroke Rehabilitation; AAOS/AAHKS clinical practice guidelines for hip/knee OA and arthroplasty rehabilitation and VTE prophylaxis; CARF accreditation standards for inpatient rehabilitation.
key_citations: Winstein CJ, et al. Guidelines for Adult Stroke Rehabilitation and Recovery (AHA/ASA). Stroke. 2016;47(6):e98-e169. PMID 27145936, DOI 10.1161/STR.0000000000000098. AVERT Trial Collaboration. Efficacy and safety of very early mobilisation within 24 h of stroke onset (AVERT). Lancet. 2015;386(9988):46-55. PMID 25892679, DOI 10.1016/S0140-6736(15)60690-0. Eapen BC, et al. Stroke Rehabilitation: Synopsis of the 2024 VA/DoD Clinical Practice Guidelines. Ann Intern Med. 2025;178(2):249-268. PMID 39832369, DOI 10.7326/ANNALS-24-02205. Durand A, et al. Benchmarking length of stay for inpatient stroke rehabilitation without adversely affecting functional outcomes. J Rehabil Med. 2020;52(10):jrm00113. PMID 33000174, DOI 10.2340/16501977-2746. Regulatory: 42 CFR 412.29 (60% Rule) and 42 CFR 412.622 (IRF coverage); MedPAC Report to Congress Mar 2024 Ch.8 (IRF mean LOS 12.8 days). Citations via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Mixed, overall Moderate-to-Strong. Structure/coverage requirements = CMS regulatory mandate (high certainty for process, not efficacy). Organized inpatient multidisciplinary/stroke-unit rehabilitation reduces death and dependency = Strong (Class I, Level A in AHA/ASA 2016). Timing/dose of mobilization refined by AVERT (RCT-level: ultra-early high-dose harmful). Specific therapy techniques (task-specific practice, CIMT, gait training) = Moderate. Deconditioning/debility rehabilitation evidence is thinner and largely extrapolated from stroke/ortho populations (lower certainty). Joint-replacement rehab supported by AAOS/AAHKS CPGs (Moderate).
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MS-DRG 945 (Rehabilitation w/ CC/MCC) vs 946 (w/o) collapses a heterogeneous group. CC/MCC drivers (aspiration/pneumonia, sepsis, CHF, AKI, stage 3-4 pressure ulcer, malnutrition, delirium) raise medical acuity: longer LOS (+2-4 days), more physician oversight, occasional telemetry, higher acute-transfer-back risk (~9-13%), slower Section GG/FIM gain, and lower discharge-to-community rate. In IRF-PPS the analog is the CMG comorbidity tier plus admission motor/cognitive score and higher Charlson index. Without CC/MCC: shorter, more predictable stays, higher home-discharge rate, standard floor monitoring. Higher-severity cases warrant earlier medical-specialist co-management, closer nutrition/skin surveillance, and conservative dose titration.
pathway_pearls: 1) Get admission appropriateness and documentation right up front -- the >=3-hr/15-hr intensity, >=2 disciplines, and reasonable-improvement expectation must be in the preadmission screen + IPOC; wrong-setting placement (belongs in SNF or LTACH) destroys efficiency and fails coverage audit. 2) Front-load the enablers of therapy participation on Day 1: dysphagia screen before any oral intake, multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia, early Foley/line removal, VTE prophylaxis, and treat depression -- every missed therapy hour erodes functional gain. 3) Dose and time mobilization sensibly -- early but NOT ultra-early high-dose (AVERT) -- and manage to FIM/Section-GG efficiency (functional gain per day), not raw LOS. 4) Own the transition: secondary prevention + medication reconciliation + caregiver training + warm handoff + 7-14 day follow-up are the levers that cut the ~9-13% acute transfer-back and 30-day readmissions.

scope_and_volume_notes: Acute inpatient rehabilitation (post-stroke, post-joint, deconditioning). Included per instruction because volume ranks high; excludes freestanding-facility-only coding where not in acute NIS.

admission_criteria: Planned/urgent inpatient surgical admission for coronary revascularization after Heart Team decision (Class I for left main and complex multivessel disease); objective CABG indications: left main stenosis >=50%; 3-vessel CAD (esp. with diabetes per FREEDOM, or LVEF <=35%); significant (>=70%) proximal LAD plus multivessel disease; high anatomic complexity (SYNTAX >=33) not amenable to complete PCI; NSTE-ACS/refractory angina with high-risk surgical anatomy (urgent); STEMI with failed PCI or mechanical complication (emergent); all sternotomy CABG require inpatient status + ICU (never observation); stable single-vessel non-LAD disease is NOT a CABG admission (medical therapy/PCI instead)
risk_stratification: STS-PROM (STS ACSD risk calculator): <4% low, 4-8% intermediate, >=8% high -> drives Heart Team review, informed consent, ICU/resource planning and palliative input; EuroSCORE II >5% elevated operative risk; SYNTAX score <=22 low / 23-32 intermediate / >=33 high -> >=33 or left main favors CABG over PCI; Heart Team review mandatory (Class I) for left main/complex multivessel; frailty (Clinical Frailty Scale, 5-m gait speed >6s) predicts prolonged LOS and SNF disposition; post-op CHA2DS2-VASc guides anticoagulation if AF develops; KDIGO stage for post-op AKI surveillance
initial_workup_labs: CBC (anemia -> iron repletion/transfusion planning) with type & crossmatch; CMP/BMP (baseline creatinine/eGFR -> AKI risk); coagulation (PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen) + last P2Y12 dose/platelet function; HbA1c (>7-8% -> glycemic optimization, mediastinitis risk); lipid panel; troponin/BNP if ACS/HF; TSH; MRSA/S. aureus nasal screen -> mupirocin + chlorhexidine decolonization; group & screen for cardiopulmonary bypass
initial_imaging_dx: Diagnostic coronary angiography defines target vessels (done pre-admit for DRG 235-236, same-stay for 233-234, with PTCA for 231-232); TTE for LVEF, valve function, wall-motion; 12-lead ECG; CXR; carotid duplex if high-risk (age >65, PAD, prior TIA/stroke, bruit); PFTs/CT chest selectively; conduit assessment (radial Allen test/duplex, saphenous vein mapping); dental evaluation if indicated pre-op
time_critical_interventions: Elective CABG is not door-to-balloon time-critical, but perioperative bundle targets: antibiotic prophylaxis (cefazolin or cefuroxime) within 60 min of incision (vancomycin within 120 min), redose q3-4h or >1500 mL blood loss, discontinue <=48h; LIMA-to-LAD graft (Class I); maintain normothermia; continuous glucose <180 mg/dL; early extubation <=6h (fast-track ERAS); aspirin restart within 6h post-op (Class I, mortality benefit); NSTE-ACS -> urgent CABG per risk; STEMI with failed PCI/mechanical complication -> emergent OR
initial_management: Pre-op: hold P2Y12 (clopidogrel/ticagrelor 3-5d, prasugrel 7d), continue aspirin + high-intensity statin + beta-blocker, correct anemia (IV iron), carbohydrate loading (clears to 2h), CHG bath + mupirocin. Intra-op: antifibrinolytic (tranexamic acid or aminocaproic acid), goal-directed perfusion, multiarterial grafting when feasible, protamine heparin reversal. Post-op: restrictive transfusion Hgb <7.5 g/dL intra-op/ICU (TRICS III noninferior) and <7 on floor; vasoactives (norepinephrine, vasopressin) + inotropes (epinephrine, milrinone/dobutamine) for low cardiac output +/- IABP; insulin infusion target <180; multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (acetaminophen, dexmedetomidine, fascial-plane blocks, gabapentinoid); K+ >4.0, Mg >2.0
day1_milestones: POD0: ICU admission with arterial line + central line +/- PA catheter, mediastinal/pleural chest tubes, temporary epicardial pacing wires; early extubation goal <=6h; hemodynamic stabilization and vasoactive weaning; maintain normothermia and glucose <180 (insulin gtt); aspirin within 6h (Class I); multimodal analgesia; monitor mediastinal drainage and re-explore if >200 mL/h x2-3h or tamponade physiology; K/Mg repletion
day2_milestones: POD1: extubate if not already; transfer ICU -> step-down/telemetry; restart beta-blocker (AF prophylaxis) + statin; remove chest tubes when drainage <100-150 mL/8h and no air leak; remove PA catheter/CVC once off vasoactives; out of bed to chair and begin PT/mobilization; resume oral diet; incentive spirometry; Foley removal; start VTE prophylaxis (mechanical + pharmacologic once hemostasis secure); begin diuresis toward pre-op weight
day3plus_milestones: POD2+: remove epicardial pacing wires ~POD3-4 (stable rhythm, INR <1.5); telemetry surveillance for POAF -> amiodarone/rate control + anticoagulation if AF persists >48h; ambulate hallway independently and climb stairs; transition to oral analgesia; taper diuretics/inotropes; serial wound checks; cardiac rehab referral (Class I); finalize secondary-prevention regimen; discharge readiness typically POD4-6 (ERAS target POD4-5)
consults_multidisciplinary: Cardiac surgery + cardiac anesthesia + perfusion (intra-op); cardiology (pre & post, secondary prevention); Heart Team (pre-op, Class I for left main/complex); intensivist (ICU); PT/OT for early mobilization + cardiac rehab enrollment; pharmacy (medication reconciliation, anticoagulation, antibiotic stewardship); nutrition (pre-op screening, diabetics); endocrine/diabetes for glucose management; case management/social work (disposition, rehab, home health) from POD1; palliative care for high STS-PROM or frailty
monitoring_level: All sternotomy CABG -> ICU immediately post-op (ventilated, invasive hemodynamic monitoring); step-down/telemetry POD1 once extubated and off vasoactives; continuous telemetry until discharge (POAF surveillance). Escalation triggers: hemodynamic instability/rising pressor requirement, tamponade (rising CVP + falling cardiac output + high-then-abruptly-low chest-tube output + widened mediastinum), chest-tube output >200 mL/h -> re-exploration, new arrhythmia, respiratory failure, oliguria/rising creatinine, acute neurologic change
nutrition_mobility_vte: Resume oral diet POD0-1 (avoid prolonged fasting per ERAS); early mobilization out of bed POD1 with progressive ambulation; VTE prophylaxis = mechanical (SCDs) immediately + pharmacologic LMWH/UFH once surgical hemostasis secure (POD1-2), balanced against pericardial/pleural bleeding risk; glucose <180 mg/dL (insulin; avoid tight <110 control per NICE-SUGAR harm signal); line/tube removal cadence -> chest tubes POD1-2, Foley POD1-2, central lines when off pressors, pacing wires POD3-4
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable off IV inotropes/vasoactives >=24h; stable rhythm or rate-controlled AF with anticoagulation plan; adequate pain control on oral analgesia; ambulating at/near baseline with independent transfers; sternal wound intact without infection; tolerating diet, euvolemic near pre-op weight with adequate urine output; stable Hgb and renal function, appropriate INR if on warfarin; afebrile; sternal precautions and self-care education completed
discharge_planning: Disposition mostly home +/- home health; SNF/inpatient rehab for frail/deconditioned/complicated patients; med rec -> lifelong aspirin, high-intensity statin, beta-blocker, ACE/ARB (if LVEF reduced/DM/HTN), P2Y12 if SVG-only or recent ACS, amiodarone taper + anticoagulation if POAF, diuretic taper; follow-up -> surgeon 1-2 wk (wound), cardiology 2-4 wk, PCP; cardiac rehab referral (Class I, reduces mortality and readmission); education -> sternal precautions (no lifting >5-10 lb, no driving ~4 wk), wound care, daily weights, symptom red flags; transitions-of-care call within 48-72h
expected_los: GMLOS ~6 days uncomplicated (DRG 236, no cath/no MCC), rising to ~8 days (DRG 234/232 without MCC) and ~10-13 days for MCC tiers (DRG 231/233/235); national isolated-CABG median total LOS ~6-7 days (post-op ~5-6 days); figures are CMS IPPS/STS benchmarks, approximate
target_los: 4-6 days total for uncomplicated isolated CABG under a full ERAS pathway (post-op 4-5 days); early extubation <=6h and POD4-5 discharge achievable in low-risk (STS-PROM <4%) patients
quality_metrics: STS ACSD risk-adjusted operative mortality & major morbidity composite (star rating) -> stroke, renal failure, prolonged ventilation >24h, reoperation, deep sternal wound infection/mediastinitis; IMA/LIMA use rate; CMS 30-day risk-standardized CABG mortality & readmission measures; discharge on aspirin + statin (secondary prevention); perioperative glucose <180 and antibiotic timing/selection/duration (legacy SCIP-Card/Inf); cardiac rehab referral rate; ERAS protocol compliance; blood conservation/transfusion rate; PSI-90
common_complications: Post-op atrial fibrillation 20-40% (beta-blocker +/- amiodarone prophylaxis, K>4/Mg>2) - leading complication; bleeding/tamponade/reoperation 2-5% (tranexamic acid, point-of-care coagulation, transfusion protocol); AKI up to 20-30% (optimize hemodynamics, avoid nephrotoxins, restrictive transfusion); deep sternal wound infection/mediastinitis 1-3% (glucose <180, prophylaxis, mupirocin/CHG, skeletonized/single IMA in diabetics); stroke 1-2% (epiaortic scan, off-pump for porcelain aorta); prolonged ventilation/pneumonia (early extubation, incentive spirometry, mobilization); low cardiac output syndrome (inotropes/IABP); delirium (dexmedetomidine, sleep hygiene); pleural/pericardial effusion
readmission_drivers: Post-op AF/arrhythmia; heart failure/volume overload; pleural or pericardial effusion/tamponade; sternal or conduit-harvest-site wound infection; pneumonia; anticoagulation-related bleeding; recurrent angina/graft issues. Prevention levers: cardiac rehab enrollment, early (7-day) follow-up + transitions-of-care call, diuretic/volume management, POAF anticoagulation stewardship, wound surveillance, glucose control, medication reconciliation and adherence
guideline_source: ACC/AHA/SCAI 2021 Guideline for Coronary Artery Revascularization (Lawton et al.); ERAS Cardiac Society 2019 Perioperative Care in Cardiac Surgery (Engelman et al., JAMA Surg) updated by ERAS/STS 2024 Joint Consensus Statement (Grant et al., Ann Thorac Surg); supporting STS clinical practice guidelines (antibiotic prophylaxis; blood conservation)

JAMA Surg 2019;154:755-766, PMID 31054241, DOI 10.1001/jamasurg.2019.1153; ERAS/STS Joint Consensus Grant et al. Ann Thorac Surg 2024;117:669-689, PMID 38284956, DOI 10.1016/j.athoracsur.2023.12.006; FREEDOM trial Farkouh et al. NEJM 2012;367:2375-2384, PMID 23121323, DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1211585; TRICS III Mazer et al. NEJM 2017;377:2133-2144, PMID 29130845, DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1711818 (citations via PubMed)
evidence_grade: Strong - anchored by Class I ACC/AHA/SCAI 2021 recommendations (LIMA-to-LAD, aspirin <=6h post-op, beta-blocker/statin secondary prevention, Heart Team, cardiac rehab) plus RCT evidence (FREEDOM, TRICS III); many ERAS perioperative bundle elements are moderate-quality or expert-consensus (ERAS Cardiac 2019, ERAS/STS 2024). Overall strong for core revascularization/secondary prevention, moderate for ERAS specifics
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG family splits by cath-lab component AND severity: 231/232 = CABG with PTCA (often hybrid/ACS), 233/234 = CABG with same-stay diagnostic cardiac cath, 235/236 = CABG without cath (cath done pre-admit; typically elective isolated CABG); within each pair the odd DRG (231/233/235) carries MCC and the even (232/234/236) is without MCC. MCC-tier drivers: acute respiratory failure/prolonged ventilation, acute renal failure/dialysis, cardiogenic shock/IABP, sepsis/mediastinitis, acute MI, stroke - each adds ~3-7+ days LOS with higher ICU/step-down time and cost. Emergent/urgent status, LVEF <=35%, reoperation, and CKD/diabetes escalate severity and complication rates across the collapsed group
pathway_pearls: (1) Mortality-defining core: LIMA-to-LAD graft + aspirin within 6h post-op + high-intensity statin + cardiac rehab referral (all Class I). (2) Sternal-infection bundle: glucose <180 (insulin gtt), antibiotic prophylaxis within 60 min and <=48h, CHG bath + mupirocin decolonization, single/skeletonized IMA in diabetics. (3) Own the #1 complication (POAF): continue/restart beta-blocker, keep K>4/Mg>2, amiodarone in high-risk, anticoagulate if AF persists >48h. (4) ERAS engine for LOS: early extubation <=6h, opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia, POD1 mobilization, protocolized line/tube removal -> POD4-5 discharge
