admission_criteria: Craniotomy/craniectomy is inherently inpatient (CMS inpatient-only; not observation/ED-dischargeable) — admit ALL. Indication-specific triggers: ELECTIVE TUMOR — symptomatic/enlarging intracranial mass with mass effect, focal deficit, seizure, or need for tissue diagnosis/decompression (planned admission). TBI — GCS <=8 (severe) or deteriorating exam; CT surgical lesion (epidural hematoma >30 cm3, acute subdural >10 mm thickness or midline shift >5 mm, contusion/hematoma with mass effect or herniation, open/depressed skull fracture). SPONTANEOUS ICH — any acute intraparenchymal hemorrhage; cerebellar hemorrhage >3 cm or with brainstem compression/hydrocephalus = emergent. aSAH/VASCULAR — any subarachnoid hemorrhage or ruptured aneurysm/AVM. ED discharge reserved only for isolated minor concussion without surgical lesion (not this DRG group)
risk_stratification: Spontaneous ICH: ICH Score (Hemphill, 0-6: GCS 3-4=2 / 5-12=1; hematoma volume >=30 mL by ABC/2 =1; intraventricular extension =1; infratentorial origin =1; age >=80 =1) -> 30-day mortality 0pt=0%, 1=13%, 2=26%, 3=72%, 4=97%, 5-6 ~100%; drives ICU intensity + goals-of-care. TBI: GCS 3-8 = severe (ICP monitor/OR); Marshall or Rotterdam CT score; IMPACT/CRASH prognostic models. aSAH: Hunt-Hess (1-5) and WFNS grade for clinical severity; modified Fisher (0-4) for delayed cerebral ischemia/vasospasm risk (higher grade -> intensified monitoring). Tumor: Karnofsky Performance Status (KPS >=70) and ASA class for operative candidacy. All: VTE risk (Caprini/Padua), frailty, falls
initial_workup_labs: CBC (baseline Hgb/platelets), CMP with emphasis on sodium (cerebral edema/hyponatremia) and glucose, coagulation panel PT/INR + aPTT + fibrinogen (triggers reversal if elevated/on anticoagulant), type-and-screen/crossmatch for OR; in anticoagulated patients specific assays (anti-Xa activity for apixaban/rivaroxaban, thrombin time/dilute-TT for dabigatran). Point-of-care glucose; toxicology and lactate in trauma/polytrauma; beta-hCG in women of childbearing age; ECG + troponin (neurogenic stress cardiomyopathy, esp aSAH); serum osmolality and serial Na to guide/monitor hyperosmolar therapy; baseline for chemo-eligibility in tumor
initial_imaging_dx: Non-contrast CT head STAT (target door-to-CT <25 min for acute neuro emergency, mirroring stroke pathway) — diagnosis + hematoma volume + midline shift. CT angiography head/neck for aSAH/vascular and ICH spot-sign; catheter digital subtraction angiography (DSA) = gold standard for aneurysm/AVM, within hours. MRI brain with/without contrast for tumor characterization and surgical planning (functional MRI/DTI tractography if eloquent cortex; awake-mapping candidates). Serial/repeat non-contrast CT at ~6 h for ICH/contusion to detect expansion. POST-OP: CT within 24 h (or immediately for any neuro decline) to exclude hemorrhage; MRI within 24-48 h for tumor extent-of-resection assessment
time_critical_interventions: ICH — acute BP lowering to SBP target 140 (range 130-150) started within the first hour AND STAT anticoagulation reversal (goal INR <1.5) to limit hematoma expansion; emergent cerebellar decompression if >3 cm or deteriorating. aSAH — secure ruptured aneurysm ASAP (ideally <24 h) by coiling or clipping, start nimodipine immediately, keep SBP <160 pre-securing, EVD for acute hydrocephalus. TBI — immediately correct hypoxia (keep PaO2 >=60, SpO2 >=90) and hypotension (SBP >=110, or >=100 if 50-69 y) as even one episode worsens outcome; emergent evacuation of expanding EDH/SDH; treat herniation (hyperosmolar + brief hyperventilation). Reversal agents (PCC/idarucizumab/andexanet) given emergently in ED/ICU
initial_management: BP control: titratable IV nicardipine or clevidipine (labetalol adjunct) to target. Anticoagulation reversal: 4-factor PCC (e.g., 25-50 U/kg) + IV vitamin K 10 mg for VKA; idarucizumab 5 g for dabigatran; andexanet alfa or 4F-PCC for apixaban/rivaroxaban (ANNEXA-I); DDAVP if on antiplatelet undergoing surgery; routine platelet transfusion NOT advised for antiplatelet-associated ICH (PATCH harm) except pre-neurosurgery/severe thrombocytopenia. Edema/ICP: TUMOR -> dexamethasone 10 mg IV load then 4 mg q6h for vasogenic edema; TBI -> hyperosmolar therapy (mannitol 0.25-1 g/kg OR hypertonic saline 3%/23.4% bolus), steroids CONTRAINDICATED (CRASH = increased mortality); HOB 30 deg, normocapnia, sedation/analgesia. Seizures: levetiracetam preferred (or fos/phenytoin) — 7-day early post-traumatic prophylaxis in severe TBI, treat clinical seizures in ICH/SAH, no routine prophylaxis for tumors. Transfusion: restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL general threshold (higher ~7-9 debated for injured brain in SAH/TBI); correct coagulopathy to INR <1.5, platelets/fibrinogen for surgery. Fluids: isotonic 0.9% saline, avoid hypotonic; euvolemia in SAH; glucose 140-180. SOURCE CONTROL = the operation (evacuation/resection/aneurysm securing) plus EVD for hydrocephalus/ICP
day1_milestones: POD0: neuro-ICU admission post-op with arterial line and ICP monitor/EVD as indicated; hourly neuro checks (GCS, pupils, focal exam); maintain CPP 60-70 mmHg and ICP <22 mmHg; BP at indication-specific target; post-op CT within 24 h (or immediately if decline); extubate early when neuro exam and airway permit; analgesia via scalp/regional block + multimodal (acetaminophen, minimize sedating opioids to preserve exam); PONV prophylaxis (dexamethasone + ondansetron); maintain normothermia and glucose 140-180; mechanical VTE prophylaxis (IPC/SCDs) on; HOB 30 deg; start nimodipine 60 mg q4h in aSAH; establish dexamethasone taper plan in tumor
day2_milestones: POD1: wean sedation and reassess neuro exam; if stable low-risk elective supratentorial tumor, transfer out of ICU to step-down/floor (avoid reflex prolonged ICU); wean/remove arterial line and begin EVD weaning if ICP controlled; START pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (LMWH/UFH) at 24-48 h once post-op CT confirms hemostasis and neurosurgery clears; advance diet after swallow screen; PT/OT evaluation and early mobilization out of bed; neuro checks q2-4h; MRI for tumor extent-of-resection; monitor sodium q6-12h (SIADH/cerebral salt wasting/diabetes insipidus) and glucose; wound inspection
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ to discharge readiness: escalate mobilization/activity, transition IV to oral analgesia, continue steroid taper (tumor); delayed cerebral ischemia vigilance days 3-14 in aSAH (serial exam + transcranial Dopplers, treat with induced hypertension, IA vasodilator/angioplasty if refractory); remove catheters/central lines; surveil for hydrocephalus (may need VP shunt); assess rehab need and begin disposition planning; oncology/radiation/chemo planning per pathology and tumor board (tumor); stop 7-day AED prophylaxis if no seizure (TBI); confirm stable/improving neuro exam and stable imaging; screen for delirium and mood; nutrition optimization
consults_multidisciplinary: Neurosurgery (primary); neurocritical care/neurology (co-manage ICU); neuro-oncology + radiation oncology + medical oncology via tumor board (tumor); neuro-interventional/endovascular (aneurysm, AVM, vascular); anesthesiology/acute pain; PT/OT/speech-language pathology early (swallow evaluation before any PO); physiatry/PM&R for rehab disposition; clinical pharmacy (AEDs, hyperosmolar, reversal agents, anticoagulation restart, stewardship); nutrition/dietitian early (dysphagia, enteral plan); case management + social work from day 1 (most craniotomy patients need rehab placement); palliative care for high ICH-score/poor-prognosis patients; endocrinology for DI/pituitary/panhypopituitarism; ophthalmology/neuro-ophthalmology as indicated
monitoring_level: Neuro-ICU (q1h neuro checks) for: all TBI/ICH/aSAH, any ICP monitor or EVD, GCS <=8, need for hyperosmolar or vasoactive infusions, airway/hemodynamic instability, delayed-cerebral-ischemia risk window. Step-down/telemetry: extubated, off infusions, ICP controlled, stable exam. Floor: neurologically stable elective supratentorial tumor by POD1-2. ESCALATION TRIGGERS (STAT CT + neurosurgery): GCS drop >=2 points, new pupillary asymmetry or fixed/dilated pupil, new focal deficit, sustained ICP >22 mmHg, seizure, Cushing response (hypertension + bradycardia), acute sodium derangement, or hematoma expansion on imaging
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: SLP swallow screen before PO; initiate enteral feeding early and reach full basal caloric replacement by day 5-7 (BTF Level IIA — early feeding lowers mortality); enteral preferred over parenteral. Mobility: early mobilization POD1 out of bed with PT/OT (ERAS element). VTE: mechanical IPC from admission for ALL; add pharmacologic LMWH or low-dose UFH at 24-48 h post-craniotomy once CT confirms no hemorrhage and neurosurgery clears (balances rebleed vs thromboembolism). Glucose: target 140-180 mg/dL, avoid <100 and >180. Lines/catheters: remove Foley, arterial and central lines, and wean EVD as early as safe to reduce CAUTI/CLABSI/ventriculitis
discharge_criteria: Neurologically stable or improving exam sustained >=24-48 h; pain controlled on oral analgesics; tolerating diet with adequate nutrition/hydration; afebrile with wound healing and no CSF leak or infection; ambulating at functional baseline OR accepted to rehab; sodium and glucose stable; off IV vasoactive/hyperosmolar therapy; AED therapeutic if indicated; stable follow-up imaging; VTE prophylaxis and anticoagulation-restart plan defined; steroid taper prescribed; caregiver/rehab plan and safe disposition confirmed
discharge_planning: Disposition: acute inpatient rehab (IRF) or SNF for TBI/ICH/aSAH with deficits; home with services for uncomplicated tumor. Medication reconciliation: AEDs, steroid taper schedule, analgesics, and an explicit anticoagulation/antiplatelet RESTART decision and timing (high-stakes). Follow-up: neurosurgery ~2 weeks with staple/suture removal at 7-10 days and wound check; oncology/radiation within 2-4 weeks (tumor); vascular imaging/DSA surveillance (aneurysm/AVM). Patient education: seizure precautions and driving restrictions, wound care, and red-flag return criteria (worsening headache, new deficit, fever, wound drainage/CSF leak, seizure). Transitions: warm handoff to rehab, PCP notification, scheduled post-discharge sodium check (esp aSAH days 7-10)
expected_los: Highly variable across this collapsed 5-DRG group. CMS FY22 GMLOS benchmarks: DRG 027 (endovascular/craniotomy w/o CC/MCC, elective/uncomplicated) ~1.9 d; DRG 026 (w CC) ~3.8 d; DRG 024 (major device/acute complex CNS PDX w/o MCC) ~4.1 d; DRG 025 (craniotomy + endovascular w MCC) ~6.6 d; DRG 023 (major device implant or acute complex CNS PDX w MCC) ~7.1 d. Clinically: elective tumor typically 3-5 d; severe TBI/ICH/aSAH commonly 10-21+ d including neuro-ICU
target_los: Under an ideal ERAS-enabled pathway: elective supratentorial tumor 2-3 d (selected centers <48 h via early non-ICU protocol); uncomplicated endovascular/vascular (DRG 027) ~2 d; complex/MCC cases target at or modestly below GMLOS (DRG 023 ~6-7 d, DRG 025 ~6 d) by reducing avoidable ICU days and preventing complications; aSAH with delayed-cerebral-ischemia surveillance realistically ~14 d. Overall aim: meet GMLOS while achieving zero preventable reoperations/readmissions
quality_metrics: Risk-adjusted 30-day readmission and mortality; unplanned return to OR/reoperation; post-op intracranial hemorrhage rate; surgical site infection / meningitis / ventriculitis; CSF leak rate; VTE/DVT-PE (AHRQ PSI-12 perioperative PE/DVT) and AHRQ PSI-90 composite; CAUTI/CLABSI; dysphagia screening compliance; SEP-1 if concurrent sepsis; ERAS protocol compliance; ICU and total LOS; VTE-prophylaxis timing; normothermia/normoglycemia; falls; NSQIP and NeuroPoint (NPA/N2QOD) registry participation; Get With The Guidelines-Stroke measures for the hemorrhagic-stroke subset; 90-day functional outcome (modified Rankin Scale / Glasgow Outcome Scale); observed vs ICH-score-predicted mortality
common_complications: Post-op intracranial hematoma/rebleed (mitigate: serial CT, strict BP control, coagulopathy reversal); cerebral edema/raised ICP (hyperosmolar, HOB 30, monitoring); seizures (AED, cEEG if depressed consciousness); hyponatremia from SIADH or cerebral salt wasting and diabetes insipidus (q6-12h sodium, targeted therapy); delayed cerebral ischemia/vasospasm in aSAH days 3-14 (nimodipine, induced hypertension); hydrocephalus (EVD/VP shunt); CSF leak/pseudomeningocele; SSI/meningitis/ventriculitis (sterile technique, EVD care, timed antibiotics); VTE (IPC + timed LMWH); aspiration pneumonia (swallow screen, HOB); delirium; steroid hyperglycemia/GI ulcer; venous infarct. Pathway mitigates via protocolized neuro monitoring, sodium protocols, VTE-timing rules, and infection bundles
readmission_drivers: Seizures/breakthrough; wound infection/SSI/meningitis; CSF leak; symptomatic hydrocephalus requiring shunt; VTE (DVT/PE); delayed hyponatremia (esp aSAH days 7-14); steroid complications (hyperglycemia, GI bleed, myopathy); recurrent or expanding hemorrhage; medication mismanagement (subtherapeutic AED, mistimed anticoagulation restart); falls; dehydration/failure to thrive. Prevention levers: structured follow-up <=2 weeks with wound check, scheduled post-discharge sodium monitoring, explicit AED and steroid taper instructions, a written anticoagulation-restart protocol, caregiver education with red-flag criteria, early rehab placement, and PT/OT continuity
guideline_source: Indication-specific, all current: TRAUMA — Brain Trauma Foundation Guidelines for the Management of Severe TBI, 4th Edition (2016/2017) + BTF Surgical Management of TBI (2006) + Seattle International Severe TBI Consensus Conference (SIBICC 2019) + Neurocritical Care Society seizure-prophylaxis guideline (2024). SPONTANEOUS ICH — AHA/ASA 2022 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Spontaneous Intracerebral Hemorrhage. ANEURYSMAL SAH/VASCULAR — AHA/ASA 2023 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Hemorrhage. TUMOR — NCCN Central Nervous System Cancers + SNO/EANO 2021 anticonvulsant-prophylaxis guideline + Congress of Neurological Surgeons (CNS/AANS) tumor guidelines. PERIOPERATIVE — ERAS for elective craniotomy (systematic-review/RCT-based; no formal ERAS Society consensus statement yet)
key_citations: Carney et al, Guidelines for the Management of Severe Traumatic Brain Injury, 4th Edition, Neurosurgery 2017 (PMID 27654000; DOI 10.1227/NEU.0000000000001432); Greenberg et al, 2022 AHA/ASA Spontaneous ICH Guideline, Stroke 2022 (PMID 35579034; DOI 10.1161/STR.0000000000000407); Hoh et al, 2023 AHA/ASA Aneurysmal SAH Guideline, Stroke 2023 (PMID 37212182; DOI 10.1161/STR.0000000000000436); Hemphill et al, The ICH Score, Stroke 2001;32:891 (PMID 11283388; DOI 10.1161/01.STR.32.4.891); Pradilla et al, ENRICH — Early Minimally Invasive Removal of ICH, NEJM 2024;390:1277 (PMID 38598795; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2308440); Hutchinson et al, RESCUEicp decompressive craniectomy, NEJM 2016;375:1119 (PMID 27602507; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1605215); Cooper et al, DECRA, NEJM 2011;364:1493 (PMID 21434843; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1102077); Mendelow et al, STICH II, Lancet 2013;382:397 (PMID 23726393); Hanley et al, MISTIE III, Lancet 2019;393:1021 (PMID 30739747); Bullock et al, Surgical Management of Acute Subdural/Epidural Hematomas, Neurosurgery 2006 (PMID 16710968 / 16710967). Citations retrieved via PubMed
evidence_grade: Mixed, anchored Strong. Core neurocritical-care spine rests on Class I society guidelines: AHA/ASA ICH 2022 and aSAH 2023, plus BTF 4th Edition — nimodipine for aSAH is Class I/Level A, and BP control, anticoagulation reversal, and ICP thresholds (>22 mmHg, CPP 60-70) are Level II. Surgical selection is heterogeneous: emergent evacuation of expanding EDH/SDH and cerebellar ICH is strong/consensus; routine supratentorial ICH surgery was historically neutral (STICH, STICH II) but ENRICH (2024 RCT) now supports early minimally invasive evacuation of lobar ICH 30-80 mL (Moderate, single trial); decompressive craniectomy improves survival at the cost of disability (RESCUEicp) while early bifrontal DHC for diffuse TBI is discouraged (DECRA). ERAS-for-craniotomy is Moderate/emerging (systematic reviews + small RCTs, no formal consensus). OVERALL: Strong for medical/ICU management; Moderate for surgical selection and ERAS elements
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The 5-DRG collapse spans elective/low-acuity (027, 026: uncomplicated tumor/vascular, GMLOS 1.9-3.8 d, minimal or no ICU) to catastrophic (023, 025 with MCC: acute complex CNS diagnosis, GMLOS 6.6-7.1 d, prolonged neuro-ICU). MCC drivers that shift the pathway: coma/GCS <=8, cerebral edema/herniation, aspiration or nosocomial pneumonia, acute respiratory failure/mechanical ventilation, sepsis, acute kidney injury, and delayed cerebral ischemia. CC drivers: seizures, hyponatremia, UTI, focal neurologic deficit. Higher tiers mandate ICP monitoring, ventilator +/- tracheostomy and PEG, longer ICU stay, IRF/SNF rather than home disposition, more reoperations, and markedly higher mortality (ICH-score-driven). MCC roughly doubles LOS and cost. Trauma, hemorrhage, and aSAH skew high-severity; elective tumor and vascular skew low
pathway_pearls: 1) Time is brain twice: (a) reverse anticoagulation and lower SBP toward 140 within the first hour to halt hematoma expansion; (b) secure a ruptured aneurysm <24 h and start nimodipine 60 mg q4h x21 days (Class I — the single highest-yield aSAH intervention, improves outcome by reducing delayed cerebral ischemia, not radiographic vasospasm). 2) Steroids are indication-specific: dexamethasone treats TUMOR vasogenic edema but is CONTRAINDICATED in TBI (CRASH — increased mortality) — never reflex-steroid a traumatic head bleed. 3) VTE prophylaxis is the signature tension: IPC on day 0 for everyone, add LMWH/UFH at 24-48 h only after post-op CT confirms no hemorrhage — protocolize the timing to avoid both PE and rebleed. 4) Sodium is the silent killer: monitor Na q6-12h for SIADH/cerebral salt wasting/DI; and do NOT reflexively ICU-admit or over-sedate low-risk elective supratentorial tumors — an early non-ICU ERAS pathway with a preserved neuro exam is the biggest LOS lever

admission_criteria: Admit/observe (rather than ED-discharge) if any high-risk feature: ABCD2 >=4; crescendo or recurrent TIA (>=2 events in 7 days); DWI-positive acute infarct on MRI; symptomatic extracranial or intracranial large-vessel stenosis >=50%; cardioembolic source requiring anticoagulation (new or known AF, intracardiac thrombus); fluctuating/ongoing deficit; hypercoagulable state; or inability to complete brain+vessel imaging and start secondary prevention within 24-48h as an outpatient (no reliable rapid TIA-clinic access/follow-up). ED-discharge acceptable for low-risk patients (ABCD2 0-3, negative DWI and negative vessel imaging, no cardioembolic source, reliable follow-up) enrolled in an expedited (<48h) TIA-clinic pathway. AHA 2023: the aim of ED evaluation is to determine who can be safely discharged vs admitted; most true TIA is observation status (<24-48h), not full inpatient
risk_stratification: ABCD2 score (Age>=60=1; BP>=140/90=1; unilateral weakness=2 OR speech disturbance without weakness=1; duration>=60min=2 OR 10-59min=1; diabetes=1; range 0-7): 0-3 low (2-day stroke risk ~1.0%), 4-5 moderate (~4.1%), 6-7 high (~8.1%) -> score >=4 drives admit/observe. AHA 2023 caution: ABCD2 alone is INSUFFICIENT and should NOT be the sole triage determinant. Reclassify to high-risk regardless of ABCD2 when DWI-positive lesion, symptomatic >=50% large-vessel stenosis, or AF is present -> mandates admission and mechanism-directed therapy. NIHSS defines minor stroke (<=3, or <=5) for DAPT eligibility. Canadian TIA Score and ABCD2-I are adjuncts
initial_workup_labs: Immediate point-of-care glucose (hypoglycemia is a leading mimic). CBC; CMP; coagulation panel (PT/INR, aPTT) before/with antithrombotics; fasting lipid panel (LDL for statin target-setting); HbA1c (occult diabetes); troponin (concurrent ACS/demand ischemia). Selective: hypercoagulable panel and ESR/CRP (young or cryptogenic, suspected vasculitis/giant-cell arteritis), TSH, toxicology, pregnancy test. Triggers: low glucose->treat mimic; INR->anticoagulation decisions; LDL>=70->high-intensity statin; A1c>=6.5%->diabetes management; positive troponin->cardiac workup
initial_imaging_dx: Non-contrast CT head immediately to exclude hemorrhage/mimic. MRI brain with DWI preferred, obtained within 24h (~30-50% of clinically defined TIA are DWI-positive = true infarction, which reclassifies risk). Neurovascular imaging of BOTH intracranial and extracranial vessels at initial evaluation-CTA or MRA head/neck (or carotid duplex)-to detect >=50% stenosis or dissection. 12-lead ECG on arrival for AF; TTE (agitated-saline bubble study if PFO/cryptogenic suspected). Prolonged cardiac monitoring for occult paroxysmal AF: inpatient telemetry, then ambulatory (Holter, 30-day event monitor, or implantable loop recorder) in cryptogenic cases
time_critical_interventions: TIA is a medical emergency (90-day stroke risk up to 17.8%, ~half within 48h). Complete brain + neurovascular imaging and start an antithrombotic the SAME DAY (<24h). If a deficit recurs or persists -> activate stroke code: IV thrombolysis (tenecteplase or alteplase) door-to-needle <=60min within 4.5h; endovascular thrombectomy for large-vessel occlusion within window (up to 24h if perfusion-eligible). Symptomatic carotid 70-99% -> carotid endarterectomy/stenting within 2 weeks (ideally 48h-7d). A rapid ED/observation TIA protocol or TIA clinic completing workup within 24-48h reduces 90-day recurrent stroke ~80% (EXPRESS/SOS-TIA)
initial_management: Antiplatelet: for high-risk TIA (ABCD2>=4) or minor stroke (NIHSS<=3) start DAPT within 12-24h-aspirin (162-325mg load, then 81mg daily) + clopidogrel (300-600mg load, then 75mg daily) for 21 days, then single agent (POINT/CHANCE); use ticagrelor (180mg load, then 90mg BID) + aspirin if CYP2C19 loss-of-function carrier (CHANCE-2); low-risk patients get single antiplatelet (aspirin 81mg or clopidogrel 75mg). High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 40-80mg or rosuvastatin 20-40mg), LDL goal <70 mg/dL. AF/cardioembolic -> anticoagulate: DOAC (apixaban, rivaroxaban, dabigatran, edoxaban) preferred over warfarin unless mechanical valve or moderate-severe mitral stenosis; because true TIA has no infarct, anticoagulation can start promptly. BP: avoid aggressive acute lowering; long-term goal <130/80. Correct hypoxia/glucose. NO thrombolysis for a resolved TIA (no acute deficit)
day1_milestones: Confirm TIA vs mimic vs completed stroke; POC glucose; non-contrast CT then MRI-DWI; neurovascular imaging (CTA/MRA); 12-lead ECG and place on telemetry; calculate ABCD2 and integrate with imaging; start antithrombotic (DAPT if high-risk) plus high-intensity statin same day; document baseline NIHSS; nurse swallow screen before any oral intake/meds; Neurology/Stroke consult; classify mechanism (cardioembolic vs large-artery atherosclerosis vs lacunar/small-vessel vs cryptogenic)
day2_milestones: Complete mechanism workup-TTE, continue cardiac monitoring for AF, review carotid/vessel imaging results; if symptomatic carotid >=70% engage vascular surgery for CEA within 2 weeks; finalize secondary prevention (confirm antiplatelet/anticoagulant, statin, BP and glucose plan); confirm DWI-negative low-risk patients are discharge-ready; smoking-cessation counseling; begin discharge planning and medication reconciliation
day3plus_milestones: Reserved for complicated cases-large-vessel stenosis awaiting revascularization, newly diagnosed AF needing anticoagulation initiation/education, uncontrolled BP or glucose, or an evolving infarct. Finalize revascularization timing; confirm anticoagulation started and tolerated; complete the prolonged-monitoring plan (implant loop recorder if cryptogenic); ensure every secondary-prevention medication is initiated and adherence barriers addressed; discharge once workup complete and no recurrent events
consults_multidisciplinary: Neurology/Stroke (day 0, primary). Vascular surgery or neuro-interventional if symptomatic carotid or intracranial stenosis. Cardiology/electrophysiology for AF or structural cardioembolic source. Pharmacy for antithrombotic selection (CYP2C19), anticoagulation and drug-interaction review. PT/OT/SLP if any residual deficit or DWI-positive lesion (functional and swallow assessment). Care management/social work for follow-up logistics, transportation, medication access and insurance. Registered dietitian for stroke-prevention/diabetic diet. Rapid TIA-clinic referral for patients discharged from the ED
monitoring_level: Continuous cardiac telemetry (floor or step-down) is the core monitoring level-its primary purpose is detecting paroxysmal AF and recurrent ischemia. ICU is generally NOT required for uncomplicated TIA. Escalate to stroke code/higher acuity if: recurrent or crescendo symptoms, a new or persistent deficit (rising NIHSS), conversion to acute stroke requiring thrombolysis/thrombectomy, malignant hypertension, or airway compromise. Most patients are observation status; downgrade/discharge once workup is complete and the patient is stable
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: NPO until a bedside swallow screen is passed (aspiration risk with any deficit), then heart-healthy low-sodium DASH-style diet, diabetic as needed. Mobility: early mobilization/ambulation-no bed rest for a resolved TIA. VTE prophylaxis: early ambulation usually suffices; mechanical (intermittent pneumatic compression) or pharmacologic prophylaxis only if immobile/admitted with a deficit-note that antiplatelet/anticoagulant therapy often already provides coverage, so avoid stacking. Glucose: treat hypo- and hyperglycemia, target ~140-180 mg/dL. Minimize and early-remove IV lines; a urinary catheter is typically unnecessary
discharge_criteria: Neurologically at baseline (no recurrent or progressive deficit); TIA mimic and hemorrhage excluded; brain (DWI) and neurovascular imaging completed and reviewed; stroke mechanism identified or a definitive outpatient monitoring plan in place; secondary-prevention regimen started and tolerated (antiplatelet or anticoagulant + high-intensity statin + BP/glucose plan); carotid revascularization arranged if indicated; swallow safe; patient educated on BE-FAST warning signs and to call 911 for recurrence; reliable early follow-up scheduled
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home (rehab only if residual deficit). Medication reconciliation with explicit antithrombotic duration (e.g., DAPT x21 days then single agent), statin, anticoagulant if AF, and antihypertensives. Schedule Neurology/stroke-clinic follow-up within 1-2 weeks (rapid TIA clinic where available), PCP follow-up, and vascular surgery if carotid intervention is pending. Arrange completion of prolonged cardiac monitoring if cryptogenic. Patient education: BE-FAST warning signs, call 911 for recurrence, medication adherence, and risk-factor modification (smoking cessation, BP/lipid/glucose control, diet, exercise). Confirm medication access/affordability; structured transitions-of-care handoff to PCP
expected_los: MS-DRG 069 (Transient ischemia without thrombolytic): GMLOS ~2.0 days (AMLOS ~2.4-2.5 days; relative weight ~0.72-0.77, FY2024/25 IPPS). A large share of TIA care is observation status (<24-48h) that never reaches inpatient; typical inpatient stay 1-2 days
target_los: ~1 day (<24h) under an optimized rapid ED/observation TIA protocol-complete DWI + neurovascular imaging, ECG/telemetry and start secondary prevention within 24h, then discharge low/intermediate-risk patients to a rapid TIA clinic; reserve multi-day inpatient stays for carotid revascularization, new-AF anticoagulation, or an evolving stroke
quality_metrics: Get With The Guidelines-Stroke (GWTG-Stroke) TIA measures: antithrombotic prescribed at discharge; high/moderate-intensity statin at discharge with LDL-C measured; anticoagulation for AF; smoking-cessation counseling; stroke education; dysphagia/swallow screen; timely brain and neurovascular imaging; DAPT for high-risk TIA/minor stroke. Process: door-to-imaging time, proportion receiving MRI-DWI, proportion with neurovascular imaging, time-to-antithrombotic. Outcome: 90-day and 30-day recurrent stroke, 30-day readmission, ED-discharge safety. Joint Commission Primary/Comprehensive Stroke Center certification metrics
common_complications: Progression to completed ischemic stroke-the dominant risk (up to 17.8% at 90 days, ~half within 48h)-mitigated by same-day antithrombotic, high-intensity statin, early carotid revascularization, and rapid protocolized workup. Antithrombotic bleeding (GI, intracranial)-limit DAPT to ~21 days and add a PPI in high GI-risk patients. Missed paroxysmal AF-prolonged cardiac monitoring. Misdiagnosis of a TIA mimic (seizure, migraine aura, hypoglycemia, syncope, functional)-DWI and structured evaluation reduce it. Contrast-associated nephropathy from CTA-hydration and renal-function check
readmission_drivers: Recurrent TIA or stroke from inadequate secondary prevention: medication nonadherence or cost, missed occult AF (undertreated cardioembolic source), un-revascularized symptomatic carotid stenosis, uncontrolled BP/LDL/glucose, continued smoking, and subtherapeutic or delayed antithrombotic/anticoagulant therapy. Prevention levers: DAPT and statin adherence, prolonged AF monitoring with prompt DOAC, timely CEA/CAS (<2 weeks), early (1-2 week) stroke-clinic follow-up, structured BE-FAST education, and transitions-of-care with medication reconciliation
guideline_source: AHA Scientific Statement 2023: Diagnosis, Workup, Risk Reduction of Transient Ischemic Attack in the Emergency Department Setting (Stroke) - PRIMARY. Plus 2021 AHA/ASA Guideline for the Prevention of Stroke in Patients With Stroke and TIA (Stroke) for secondary prevention. Registry framework: AHA/ASA Get With The Guidelines-Stroke
key_citations: AHA 2023 TIA-in-ED Scientific Statement: Amin HP et al., Stroke 2023;54(3):e109-e121 (PMID 36655570; DOI 10.1161/STR.0000000000000418). 2021 AHA/ASA Secondary Prevention Guideline: Kleindorfer DO et al., Stroke 2021;52(7):e364-e467 (PMID 34024117; DOI 10.1161/STR.0000000000000375). POINT: Johnston SC et al., NEJM 2018;379:215-225 (PMID 29766750). CHANCE: Wang Y et al., NEJM 2013;369:11-19 (PMID 23803136). CHANCE-2 (ticagrelor, CYP2C19 LOF): Wang Y et al., NEJM 2021;385:2520-2530 (PMID 34708996). EXPRESS urgent-treatment: Rothwell PM et al., Lancet 2007;370:1432-1442 (PMID 17928046). Source: PubMed
evidence_grade: Strong - Class I society-guideline recommendations (AHA/ASA) supported by multiple high-quality RCTs (POINT, CHANCE, CHANCE-2 for DAPT; NASCET/ECST for carotid endarterectomy; SPARCL/Treat-Stroke-to-Target for statin and LDL<70) and rigorous population studies (EXPRESS/SOS-TIA). ED-triage and ABCD2 elements and the optimal admit-vs-observe threshold are lower certainty (expert consensus/observational)-AHA 2023 explicitly cautions against using ABCD2 as a sole triage tool
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MS-DRG 069 is a SINGLE, non-subdivided DRG (no CC/MCC split; CMS collapsed 'Transient ischemia without thrombolytic'), so comorbidity coding does not create a higher-weight sibling within 069. Higher-acuity/comorbid patients still drive longer LOS, more telemetry, and more consults. Key pathway divergence: if MRI shows acute infarction (DWI-positive), the case is a stroke, not a TIA-recode to cerebral-infarction DRGs (064-066, which carry MCC/CC tiers) or, if a thrombolytic was given, ischemic-stroke-with-thrombolytic DRGs (061-063). New AF requiring anticoagulation, symptomatic carotid needing CEA, or conversion to acute stroke all escalate acuity and LOS
pathway_pearls: 1) Tissue is the test-obtain MRI-DWI plus neurovascular imaging (CTA/MRA head+neck) early; a DWI-positive lesion or symptomatic >=50% large-vessel stenosis reclassifies to high risk and mandates admission REGARDLESS of ABCD2 (do not triage on ABCD2 alone-AHA 2023). 2) Antithrombotics same-day-DAPT (aspirin+clopidogrel, load then 21 days) for high-risk TIA/minor stroke within 24h cuts 90-day stroke ~25-30% (POINT/CHANCE); use ticagrelor+aspirin for CYP2C19 loss-of-function carriers (CHANCE-2); add a high-intensity statin to LDL<70. 3) Find the AF, fix the carotid-telemetry plus prolonged monitoring for occult AF (->DOAC); symptomatic carotid 70-99% -> CEA within 2 weeks. 4) Systematize speed-a rapid ED/observation TIA protocol or TIA clinic completing workup within 24-48h avoids inpatient admission and reduces recurrent stroke ~80% (EXPRESS/SOS-TIA); this is the LOS and cost lever

admission_criteria: Principal reason is delivery of antineoplastic therapy (ICD-10 Z51.11 chemo / Z51.0 radiotherapy encounter) that CANNOT be given safely in an outpatient infusion center. Objective inpatient indications: regimen requires multi-day continuous IV infusion or intensive monitoring (DA-EPOCH/infusional etoposide-vincristine-doxorubicin 96h + cyclophosphamide; high-dose methotrexate >=1-3.5 g/m2 with leucovorin rescue + urinary alkalinization; high-dose cytarabine; cisplatin regimens needing prolonged forced hydration; DHAP/ESHAP, hyperCVAD, BEP; high-dose IL-2; blinatumomab step-up/CRS watch; CAR-T bridging/lymphodepletion); high tumor-lysis-syndrome (TLS) risk needing inpatient rasburicase/monitoring (bulky high-grade disease, LDH >2x ULN); first cycle of a high-toxicity regimen; inability to self-manage (poor ECOG PS, comorbidity, distance/social); inpatient radiotherapy = total body irradiation (TBI) for HSCT conditioning or emergent/palliative RT (cord compression, SVC syndrome) in an already-admitted unstable patient. ED-discharge/observation or outpatient infusion center is appropriate for routine outpatient-deliverable single-agent or standard combination regimens.
risk_stratification: Cairo-Bishop TLS model -> low/intermediate/high by tumor type, bulk/WBC, LDH >2x ULN, baseline uric acid, renal function/involvement; HIGH -> pre-emptive rasburicase, INTERMEDIATE -> allopurinol/febuxostat + hydration (laboratory TLS = >=2 of: uric acid >=8 mg/dL, K >=6, phosphate >=4.5, Ca <=7 or >=25% change 3d pre to 7d post). Emetogenic-risk class (ASCO/NCCN/MASCC-ESMO): HIGH >90% (cisplatin, AC, carmustine, carboplatin AUC>=4), MODERATE 30-90%, LOW 10-30%, MINIMAL <10% -> selects antiemetic regimen. Febrile-neutropenia (FN) risk: regimen FN risk >20% -> primary G-CSF; 10-20% + patient factors -> consider G-CSF; when febrile, MASCC index >=21 = low risk (oral/outpatient candidate), <21 = high risk; CISNE score for apparently-stable patients. HBV reactivation risk (screen HBsAg/anti-HBc/anti-HBs; anti-CD20 or HSCT = high). Khorana score >=2 flags thrombosis risk. ECOG PS and CARG/CRASH geriatric assessment in older adults.
initial_workup_labs: CBC + differential (ANC for neutropenia, platelets for transfusion threshold); CMP (renal function for dosing/TLS, hepatic panel); baseline TLS panel: uric acid, LDH, phosphate, potassium, calcium, magnesium; beta-hCG in people of childbearing potential; HBV serology (HBsAg, anti-HBc total, anti-HBs) + HCV Ab + HIV before immunosuppressive therapy; eGFR/creatinine clearance for renally-cleared agents (carboplatin Calvert AUC dosing, methotrexate, cisplatin); pharmacogenomics where indicated (DPYD before fluoropyrimidines, TPMT/NUDT15 before thiopurines, G6PD before rasburicase — contraindicated if deficient); type & screen if transfusion anticipated; troponin/BNP baseline if cardiotoxic regimen. Triggers: ANC low or platelets low -> hold/dose-reduce; abnormal TLS labs -> intensify prophylaxis/monitoring; HBsAg+ or anti-HBc+ -> antiviral prophylaxis; abnormal renal/hepatic -> dose-adjust.
initial_imaging_dx: CXR to confirm central venous access tip position (PICC/port/tunneled line) same-day BEFORE any vesicant/infusional chemo; baseline LVEF by echocardiography (TTE) or MUGA before anthracyclines (doxorubicin) or trastuzumab; 12-lead ECG for QTc baseline before QT-prolonging regimens (e.g., arsenic, some TKIs, 5-HT3 antagonists at high dose); disease baseline/restaging CT or PET-CT is typically completed pre-admission and not repeated for treatment delivery; for inpatient radiotherapy, CT simulation for treatment planning within 24-48h (+ MRI for target delineation as indicated).
time_critical_interventions: Pre-administration chemotherapy-safety verification (ASCO/ONS Standards): independent two-clinician double-check of regimen/dose (BSA or Calvert AUC), cumulative anthracycline dose, signed informed consent, verified height/weight. TLS prophylaxis STARTED before cytotoxic therapy: IV hydration 2.5-3 L/m2/day + allopurinol (intermediate) or rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg (high risk), ideally 24-48h ahead. Antiemetic prophylaxis given BEFORE chemo, not PRN. High-dose methotrexate: leucovorin rescue begins 24h after MTX start, MTX levels at 24/48/72h, urine pH >=7 and output >=100 mL/m2/h, glucarpidase if MTX markedly elevated with AKI. Extravasation recognized within minutes -> agent-specific antidote (dexrazoxane for anthracycline, hyaluronidase for vinca). Febrile neutropenia: empiric anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam (cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam, or meropenem) within 1 hour of fever onset.
initial_management: Antiemetics by tier: HIGH = 4-drug NK1 RA (aprepitant/fosaprepitant/netupitant) + 5-HT3 RA (palonosetron/ondansetron/granisetron) + dexamethasone + olanzapine 5-10 mg; MODERATE = 5-HT3 + dexamethasone +/- NK1; LOW = single 5-HT3 or dexamethasone; breakthrough = olanzapine, metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, lorazepam. TLS: hydration + rasburicase (high) or allopurinol/febuxostat (intermediate), treat hyperkalemia/hyperphosphatemia, AVOID urinary alkalinization (Coiffier 2008). Nephroprotection for cisplatin: pre/post hydration + magnesium repletion. Growth factor: G-CSF (filgrastim/pegfilgrastim) primary prophylaxis when FN risk >20% (pegfilgrastim >=24h after cytotoxic completion). Restrictive transfusion: RBC for Hgb <7-8 g/dL; prophylactic platelets <10,000/uL (<20,000 if fever, <50,000 pre-procedure/bleeding). HBV antiviral prophylaxis entecavir/tenofovir for HBsAg+ or high-risk anti-HBc+. Mesna with high-dose cyclophosphamide/ifosfamide (hemorrhagic cystitis). Leucovorin rescue for HD-MTX. Neutropenia prophylaxis when prolonged nadir expected: fluoroquinolone, antifungal (fluconazole/posaconazole), acyclovir, TMP-SMX for PJP per NCCN.
day1_milestones: Confirm signed consent, baseline/TLS labs, pregnancy test, HBV status, verified access; complete two-clinician regimen verification and BSA/AUC dose check; administer pre-medications (antiemetics, TLS prophylaxis, hydration); begin chemotherapy first infusion and monitor for infusion/hypersensitivity reactions (rituximab, platinum, taxanes) with graded rate escalation; establish standing antiemetic + bowel regimen; strict intake/output; TLS labs q6-12h for high-risk; initiate patient education on regimen, neutropenic precautions, and side-effect expectations.
day2_milestones: Continue multi-day infusion (e.g., DA-EPOCH day 2 of 4-5); trend TLS labs and correct electrolytes, adjust hydration, repeat rasburicase if uric acid rebounds; reassess nausea control and escalate to olanzapine/breakthrough agents if CINV; HD-MTX -> check 24h level, START leucovorin rescue, verify urine pH/output; manage emergent toxicities (mucositis, diarrhea, delayed infusion reaction); reinforce mobility, VTE prophylaxis, and glucose monitoring (dexamethasone-induced hyperglycemia).
day3plus_milestones: Complete final day of infusion regimen; HD-MTX -> continue leucovorin until level <0.05-0.1 umol/L with recovering renal function, then discharge; confirm TLS resolved and electrolytes/renal function stable; administer pegfilgrastim >=24h after cytotoxic completion (not within 24h); nadir counseling with written FN precautions; ensure post-discharge antiemetic/prophylaxis prescriptions and next-cycle scheduling; discharge once infusion complete + acute toxicities controlled + oral intake/antiemetics tolerated.
consults_multidisciplinary: Medical oncology/hematology (primary) with oncology-certified nursing for administration; oncology pharmacy (mandatory — regimen verification, dosing, interactions, supportive-care orders); nutrition (anorexia, mucositis, TPN needs); social work/care management (financial toxicity, transportation, home infusion setup) early; palliative care for symptom burden/goals of care; nephrology (TLS with AKI, HD-MTX toxicity); infectious diseases (febrile neutropenia, prophylaxis); cardiology/cardio-oncology (anthracycline or trastuzumab cardiotoxicity); radiation oncology (RT component/DRG 849); reproductive endocrinology for fertility preservation BEFORE gonadotoxic therapy; hepatology for HBV+; psycho-oncology as needed.
monitoring_level: Most patients on oncology/med-surg floor with telemetry as indicated. Step-down/ICU for: high-risk TLS with AKI, hyperkalemia (K >=6) or arrhythmia; bulky tumor with airway/vascular compromise; high-dose IL-2; cytokine release syndrome (blinatumomab/CAR-T) grade >=2; neutropenic sepsis with hypotension; severe infusion/anaphylactic reaction. Escalation triggers: SBP <90 or shock, rising creatinine/potassium, respiratory compromise, CRS grade >=2, MASCC <21 with clinical instability, or refractory metabolic derangement -> transfer to higher level of care with vasopressor/organ support.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet as tolerated with antiemetic support; nutrition consult for mucositis/anorexia; food-safety counseling (formal "neutropenic diet" not evidence-supported). Early ambulation/mobilization. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH, e.g., enoxaparin) for hospitalized cancer patients unless contraindicated (platelets <50,000/uL, active bleeding) — ASCO VTE 2023; mechanical prophylaxis if pharmacologic contraindicated; consider ambulatory extension if Khorana >=2. Glucose control for dexamethasone-induced hyperglycemia. Central line/PICC/port stewardship: maintain patency, monitor for CLABSI, remove when no longer needed. Standing bowel regimen (constipation from 5-HT3 antagonists, vinca alkaloids, opioids).
discharge_criteria: Planned infusion regimen completed; acute chemo toxicities controlled (nausea managed on oral regimen, no active infusion reaction); TLS resolved with stable electrolytes and renal function; HD-MTX cleared to <0.05-0.1 umol/L with recovering creatinine; afebrile or febrile neutropenia resolving on an appropriate antibiotic plan; tolerating oral intake/hydration with adequate oral antiemetics; intact vascular access and adequate home support; G-CSF and prophylaxis plan in place.
discharge_planning: Disposition home for most (home health/home infusion if continued therapy; SNF rare). Medication reconciliation: oral antiemetics (ondansetron, prochlorperazine, olanzapine), dexamethasone taper, prophylactic antimicrobials, G-CSF plan (on-body injector or return visit >=24h post-chemo), HBV antiviral, allopurinol, bowel regimen. Follow-up: oncology clinic for next cycle + nadir CBC at day 7-14 and interval labs; 48-72h post-discharge check for high-risk. Education: neutropenic-fever precautions (call for temp >=38.0-38.3C / 100.4F), warning signs prompting ED, oral hydration, mucositis/oral care, contraception. Transitions: written chemo calendar, 24/7 oncology triage line, emergency/wallet card, symptom self-monitoring (ePRO).
expected_los: CMS MS-DRG GMLOS benchmarks (approximate, FY2024-25 tables): DRG 848 (no CC/MCC) ~2.5-3 days; DRG 847 (with CC) ~3.5-4 days; DRG 846 (with MCC) ~5-6 days; DRG 849 (radiotherapy) ~5-6 days; collapsed-group typical stay 3-5 days (exact GMLOS cells are behind CMS/vendor paywalls — figures are benchmark estimates).
target_los: Regimen-indexed target: uncomplicated single-agent/short course ~2 days; high-dose methotrexate 3-4 days (until MTX <0.05-0.1 umol/L); DA-EPOCH/infusional regimens 5 days (fixed by infusion duration); roughly ~1 day below GMLOS for uncomplicated cases when TLS, CINV, and febrile neutropenia are prevented; discharge the day infusion completes and acute toxicities are controlled rather than holding for the pegfilgrastim visit (give on-body injector).
quality_metrics: ASCO/ONS Chemotherapy Administration Safety Standards compliance (independent double-check, consent documentation, BSA/AUC dosing); ASCO QOPI measures (antiemetic guideline concordance, chemo consent, HBV screening pre-immunosuppression, fertility-preservation discussion); guideline-concordant CINV prophylaxis rate; febrile-neutropenia incidence and door-to-antibiotic <1h; TLS incidence; VTE prophylaxis rate; CLABSI rate; medication-error/HAC rate; CMS OP-35 (Admissions and ED Visits for Patients Receiving Outpatient Chemotherapy) as the linked outpatient measure; unplanned 30-day inpatient readmission.
common_complications: Chemo-induced nausea/vomiting (most frequent, controllable) -> prospective tier-matched prophylaxis; tumor lysis syndrome -> Cairo-Bishop risk stratification + hydration/rasburicase/allopurinol, monitoring, no alkalinization; febrile neutropenia/neutropenic sepsis -> primary G-CSF + antibiotics <1h; mucositis and diarrhea -> supportive/oral care; infusion/hypersensitivity reactions -> premedication + graded infusion; vesicant extravasation -> central access + antidotes; AKI and electrolyte disturbance (cisplatin, MTX) -> hydration/dose-adjust; cytopenias/anemia/thrombocytopenia -> restrictive transfusion thresholds; hemorrhagic cystitis (cyclophosphamide/ifosfamide) -> mesna + hydration; HBV reactivation -> screen + antiviral; steroid hyperglycemia and VTE -> monitoring + prophylaxis.
readmission_drivers: Febrile neutropenia/infection (leading driver) -> G-CSF, risk-based prophylactic antimicrobials, standardized FN education, prompt triage; dehydration from uncontrolled nausea/vomiting or diarrhea -> optimized oral antiemetics + hydration plan + early clinic contact; uncontrolled pain/mucositis; electrolyte abnormalities and AKI; VTE; failure to thrive/disease progression. Prevention levers: proactive symptom monitoring (telephone/ePRO triage), adequate oral antiemetic supply at discharge, G-CSF, 48-72h post-discharge follow-up, and OP-35-focused pathway design to shift acute events to clinic rather than readmission.
guideline_source: NCCN Guidelines: Antiemesis (v2.2025), Hematopoietic Growth Factors, Prevention & Treatment of Cancer-Related Infections. ASCO: Antiemetics Guideline Update (Hesketh, 2020); Outpatient Management of Fever & Neutropenia ASCO/IDSA (Taplitz, 2018); VTE Prophylaxis & Treatment in Cancer ASCO Update (Key, 2023); HBV Screening & Management Provisional Clinical Opinion (Hwang, 2020). ASCO/ONS Chemotherapy Administration Safety Standards (Neuss, 2016). TLS: Coiffier et al. evidence-based guidelines (JCO 2008) + Cairo et al. expert consensus risk model (Br J Haematol 2010). MASCC/ESMO emetogenicity classification (Jordan, 2023).

J Clin Oncol 2000;18(16):3038-51. PMID 10944139, doi:10.1200/JCO.2000.18.16.3038. Key NS et al. VTE Prophylaxis and Treatment in Cancer: ASCO Update. J Clin Oncol 2023;41(16):3063-71. PMID 37075273, doi:10.1200/JCO.23.00294. Hwang JP et al. HBV Screening and Management ASCO PCO Update. J Clin Oncol 2020;38(31):3698-3715. PMID 32716741, doi:10.1200/JCO.20.01757. Jordan K et al. MASCC/ESMO emetic risk classification update. Support Care Cancer 2023;32(1):53. PMID 38129530, doi:10.1007/s00520-023-08220-5.
evidence_grade: Mixed. STRONG (Class I / high-quality society guidelines, RCT-backed) for the supportive-care backbone: antiemesis (ASCO/NCCN/MASCC-ESMO), febrile-neutropenia management and MASCC stratification (ASCO/IDSA), G-CSF prophylaxis, VTE prophylaxis (ASCO 2023), HBV screening (ASCO PCO), and TLS prophylaxis (Coiffier/Cairo consensus). MODERATE / CONSENSUS-OPERATIONAL for the inpatient-delivery pathway structure itself — which regimens require inpatient administration, LOS targets, and site-of-care thresholds are institutional/consensus-driven rather than derived from a single unifying RCT. Overall: Strong for component interventions; Moderate/expert-consensus for pathway architecture and LOS.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 848 (no CC/MCC): straightforward planned short-course delivery, floor-level, LOS ~2.5-3 d. DRG 847 (with CC): a comorbidity/complication (anemia, dehydration, electrolyte disturbance, moderate infection) extends stay to ~3.5-4 d. DRG 846 (with MCC): major complication — neutropenic sepsis, TLS with AKI, respiratory failure, severe mucositis, or cytokine release syndrome — drives step-down/ICU use, LOS ~5-6 d+, and markedly higher cost; this is where the pathway's prophylaxis (TLS stratification, tier-matched antiemesis, G-CSF, <1h FN antibiotics) earns its keep by holding patients in the lower-severity tiers. DRG 849 (radiotherapy) is a distinct workflow — usually TBI conditioning for HSCT or inpatient palliative/emergent RT — with LOS driven by the underlying reason for inpatient status rather than the RT itself.
pathway_pearls: 1) Prospectively match emetogenic risk to the antiemetic regimen (4-drug NK1 + 5-HT3 + dexamethasone + olanzapine for high emetic risk) — CINV is the single most controllable complication and the top preventable driver of dehydration readmission. 2) Stratify TLS risk (Cairo-Bishop) BEFORE cycle 1 and pre-empt with hydration + rasburicase (high) or allopurinol (intermediate); do NOT alkalinize the urine. 3) Enforce the ASCO/ONS safety bundle every time — independent two-person verification, BSA/AUC dosing, cumulative anthracycline check, plus baseline HBV screen and LVEF — to prevent sentinel events. 4) Right-site the care: most modern regimens are outpatient-deliverable, so reserve inpatient beds for genuinely infusion-/monitoring-dependent regimens (HD-MTX, DA-EPOCH, high TLS risk) and discharge the moment the infusion completes and toxicities are controlled — give pegfilgrastim by on-body injector rather than holding a bed for it.

scope_and_volume_notes: Inpatient chemotherapy administration and radiotherapy; distinct from leukemia/lymphoma disease DRGs (rank 58).
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 846 — Chemotherapy without Acute Leukemia as Secondary Diagnosis with MCC; 847 — Chemotherapy without Acute Leukemia as Secondary Diagnosis with CC; 848 — Chemotherapy without Acute Leukemia as Secondary Diagnosis without CC/MCC; 849 — Radiotherapy
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 8/8 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Current pass completed citation-resolvability, coding, taxonomy, schema, and consistency checks. The source workflow contains no completed adversarial high-stakes clinical verdict for this row; clinician revalidation remains required before protocol or order-set use.
data_quality_notes: Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk.

admission_criteria: SURGICAL arm (DRG 163-166): planned same-day admission for anatomic resection (lobectomy/segmentectomy/sleeve/pneumonectomy/chest-wall resection) after complete multidisciplinary staging and physiologic clearance. MEDICAL arm (DRG 180-182): admit for symptomatic malignant pleural effusion needing drainage; post-obstructive pneumonia with hypoxia (SpO2 <90%) or sepsis; hemoptysis >100-200 mL/24h or any airway compromise; malignant central airway obstruction with stridor/respiratory distress; SVC syndrome with airway/cerebral edema; hypercalcemia of malignancy (Ca >14 mg/dL or symptomatic); symptomatic brain metastasis or malignant cord compression; intractable cancer dyspnea/pain; febrile neutropenia. ED-discharge/observation appropriate: small asymptomatic effusion, minor stable hemoptysis with normal gas exchange, workup completable as outpatient
risk_stratification: Surgical candidacy — ACCP/Brunelli algorithm on ppoFEV1 and ppoDLCO: both >60% predicted = low risk, no further test; either 30-60% = low-tech exercise screen (stair-climb >22 m or shuttle-walk >400 m = low risk); either <30% or failed screen = CPET, where VO2peak >20 mL/kg/min (or >75% pred) = low risk, 10-20 = moderate, <10 (or <35% pred) = prohibitive/high mortality risk (favor sublobar/SBRT). Cardiac: Thoracic RCRI (ThRCRI) >1.5 triggers cardiology/stress testing. Staging: IASLC/AJCC TNM 9th ed (2024). MPE prognosis: LENT and PROMISE scores stratify survival and guide IPC vs pleurodesis. Sepsis/post-obstructive pneumonia: qSOFA >=2, CURB-65. ECOG performance status and frailty screen. Cancer-associated VTE: Khorana score
initial_workup_labs: CBC-diff (anemia from hemoptysis, neutropenia), CMP (corrected calcium for hypercalcemia, LFTs, renal), coags INR/aPTT, type-and-screen/crossmatch (hemoptysis or pre-op), LDH, lactate and blood cultures if septic, ABG if hypoxic/hypercarbic. Pleural fluid on thoracentesis: cytology, pH, LDH, protein, glucose, ADA. Tissue must be sent for full molecular panel — EGFR, ALK, ROS1, BRAF V600E, KRAS G12C, MET exon14, RET, NTRK, HER2 — plus PD-L1 TPS (drives systemic therapy). Pre-op: spirometry+DLCO, HbA1c, albumin/prealbumin nutrition screen, pregnancy test where applicable
initial_imaging_dx: Contrast CT chest/upper abdomen; whole-body FDG-PET/CT for staging; brain MRI for stage >=IB or neuro symptoms. Tissue diagnosis and invasive mediastinal staging: EBUS-TBNA first-line (+/- EUS) for mediastinal nodes, navigational/flexible bronchoscopy, CT-guided core biopsy for peripheral lesions, mediastinoscopy only if EBUS non-diagnostic with high suspicion. Ultrasound-guided pleural drainage (ATS/STS/STR strong preference). Hemoptysis: CT-angiography then bronchoscopy; massive hemoptysis -> IR bronchial-artery angiography <1-2h. Pre-op TTE for cardiac risk. Malignant airway obstruction -> urgent rigid bronchoscopy
time_critical_interventions: Massive hemoptysis: protect airway, bleeding-side-down decubitus, urgent rigid bronchoscopy + bronchial-artery embolization (controls ~90%), reverse coagulopathy, IV/nebulized tranexamic acid. Malignant central airway obstruction with respiratory failure: emergent rigid bronchoscopy with debulking (laser/APC/cryo/mechanical) +/- airway stent. SVC syndrome with airway/cerebral edema: emergent endovascular SVC stent + steroids. Febrile neutropenia / septic post-obstructive pneumonia: antibiotics <=1h, lactate, cultures, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid if hypoperfusion (Surviving Sepsis/SEP-1). Hypercalcemic crisis: isotonic saline + calcitonin + IV zoledronic acid or denosumab. Malignant cord compression: dexamethasone + neurosurgery/RT <24h. Symptomatic effusion: US-guided large-volume thoracentesis (stop for symptoms). Surgical: antibiotic prophylaxis within 60 min of incision, VTE prophylaxis on schedule
initial_management: Post-obstructive pneumonia: broad-spectrum + anaerobic cover (ampicillin-sulbactam, or ceftriaxone+metronidazole; piperacillin-tazobactam if HCAP/aspiration risk). Hemoptysis: tranexamic acid, reverse anticoagulation (vitamin K, 4-factor PCC), transfuse per threshold. Symptomatic MPE (ATS/STS/STR): therapeutic drainage then indwelling pleural catheter (IPC) or talc pleurodesis (poudrage or slurry) matched to lung expandability; IPC preferred for non-expandable/failed pleurodesis. Hypercalcemia: isotonic saline + calcitonin 4 U/kg + zoledronic acid 4 mg IV or denosumab. Dyspnea/pain: titrated opioids, oxygen; corticosteroids for lymphangitic spread/airway edema. Transfusion restrictive threshold Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if active cardiac/perioperative). SURGICAL: anatomic resection — lobectomy standard, segmentectomy for peripheral cT1a-b (<=2 cm) N0 per JCOG0802/CALGB140503 — via VATS/robotic minimally invasive, with systematic mediastinal nodal dissection; multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (paravertebral block + acetaminophen + NSAID +/- gabapentinoid); euvolemic balanced crystalloid; stage- and biomarker-directed neoadjuvant/adjuvant therapy (chemo-immunotherapy; targeted agents for EGFR/ALK)
day1_milestones: MEDICAL Day 1: control the presenting emergency (effusion drained, hemoptysis stopped, airway secured, calcium trending down, sepsis bundle underway); confirm tissue histology and send molecular/PD-L1; initiate staging workup; document goals of care and prognosis; early palliative-care involvement if high symptom burden. SURGICAL POD0: extubate in OR; single chest tube to water seal (no routine external suction); sit out of bed / mobilize within 24h; multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia; incentive spirometry q1h; euvolemia; advance from clear liquids; pharmacologic + mechanical VTE prophylaxis; remove urinary catheter if placed and not required
day2_milestones: MEDICAL Day 2: diagnostic results returning; present at thoracic multidisciplinary tumor board for definitive plan; convert IV to oral therapy; IPC self-drainage teaching; mobilize; determine discharge vs continued inpatient workup. SURGICAL POD1: ambulate >=3x with chest physiotherapy; assess chest tube for air leak and output — remove if no air leak and serous drainage <=450 mL/24h (ERAS); discontinue epidural and transition to oral analgesia; regular diet; remove urinary catheter; continue VTE prophylaxis; screen for post-op atrial fibrillation
day3plus_milestones: MEDICAL Day 3+: definitive oncology plan finalized — initiate or schedule systemic therapy/RT, or discharge to complete as outpatient with rapid oncology follow-up; optimize palliative symptom control. SURGICAL POD2+: chest tube removed in most by POD1-3; pain controlled on oral analgesics; ambulating independently at baseline; post-pull CXR acceptable with no residual pneumothorax/leak; discharge POD2-4 for VATS/robotic lobectomy or segmentectomy, POD1-2 for wedge, POD5-7 for pneumonectomy; prolonged air leak managed with portable/Heimlich device to enable discharge
consults_multidisciplinary: Thoracic surgery; medical oncology; radiation oncology; interventional pulmonology (EBUS, airway, IPC); pulmonary medicine; interventional radiology (bronchial-artery embolization, SVC stent); pathology/molecular diagnostics; palliative care (early, high symptom burden); cardiology and anesthesia/acute pain (pre-op); PT/OT and respiratory therapy (early mobilization/prehab); registered dietitian (malnutrition common); care management and social work; smoking-cessation service; and mandatory central thoracic multidisciplinary tumor board for treatment sequencing
monitoring_level: Floor with telemetry for most medical admissions and uncomplicated post-lobectomy/segmentectomy (post-op AF common) after PACU/step-down. ICU or step-down for: massive hemoptysis, respiratory failure, post-obstructive pneumonia with septic shock, SVC syndrome with airway compromise, pneumonectomy (arrhythmia, post-pneumonectomy pulmonary edema, cardiac herniation risk), and physiologically high-risk resections (low ppoFEV1/ppoDLCO). Escalation triggers: SpO2 <90% despite supplemental O2, RR >30, rising vasopressor requirement, hemodynamically unstable new arrhythmia, expanding subcutaneous emphysema with large air leak
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: pre-op carbohydrate loading and avoidance of prolonged fasting (clears to 2h, solids to 6h); early oral/enteral intake advanced POD0; screen and treat cancer-related malnutrition. Mobility: out of bed and mobilizing within 24h, structured ambulation with chest physiotherapy and incentive spirometry. VTE: combined mechanical + pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH/heparin), with extended prophylaxis up to 4 weeks for high-risk cancer surgery; therapeutic LMWH for established cancer-associated thrombosis. Glucose target <180 mg/dL. Early removal of chest tube, urinary catheter, and central lines to limit infection and immobility
discharge_criteria: SURGICAL: chest tube removed (or stable ambulatory drainage device), pain controlled on oral analgesia, ambulating at baseline, afebrile, adequate oxygenation on room air or stable home O2, tolerating diet, voiding spontaneously, acceptable post-pull CXR. MEDICAL: presenting problem controlled (effusion drained/IPC functioning, hemoptysis resolved, infection improving on oral antibiotics, calcium normalized, symptoms managed), definitive oncology treatment plan and follow-up arranged, and a safe disposition confirmed
discharge_planning: Disposition home vs SNF/rehab (frail patients, pneumonectomy). Medication reconciliation including anticoagulation, analgesia taper with bowel regimen, and antiemetics. Teach IPC drainage, incision/chest-tube-site and portable-drain care, and return precautions. Smoking-cessation referral and pulmonary rehab. Schedule surgery follow-up (~1-2 weeks) and oncology/radiation-oncology visit; ensure molecular/PD-L1 results are routed so systemic/adjuvant therapy starts on time; arrange surveillance imaging. Palliative-care or hospice referral where prognosis warrants. Nurse follow-up phone call within 48-72h of discharge
expected_los: Combined DRG group. Medical respiratory neoplasm (180-182) GMLOS approximately 3.0-6.0 days (182 w/o CC/MCC ~3.0; 181 w/CC ~4.4; 180 w/MCC ~5.7). Major chest procedures (163-166) GMLOS approximately 3.7-9.0 days (165 w/o CC/MCC ~3.7; 164 w/CC ~5.5; 163 w/MCC ~9.0); contemporary real-world VATS/robotic lobectomy median actual LOS ~4 days
target_los: Under the ideal ERAS/minimally invasive pathway: VATS/robotic lobectomy or segmentectomy 2-4 days; wedge resection 1-2 days; pneumonectomy 5-7 days. Medical arm: uncomplicated symptomatic effusion with IPC/pleurodesis 1-3 days; hemoptysis post-embolization 2-4 days; post-obstructive pneumonia 4-5 days
quality_metrics: STS General Thoracic Surgery Database risk-adjusted mortality and major morbidity; ERAS protocol-element compliance; prolonged air-leak rate; R0 (complete) resection rate and adequacy of nodal staging (>=3 N2 stations / systematic dissection); 30- and 90-day mortality; 30-day readmission; VTE prophylaxis and event rate; SEP-1 when septic; tobacco-cessation screening/intervention (TOB-); catheter-associated UTI/central-line infection; time-to-adjuvant-therapy; multidisciplinary tumor-board and goals-of-care documentation; opioid-stewardship metrics
common_complications: Prolonged air leak (most common, ~10-15%); post-operative atrial fibrillation (~10-15%); atelectasis/pneumonia and respiratory failure; empyema and bronchopleural fistula; chylothorax; VTE; delirium. Medical arm: re-accumulating/loculated effusion, IPC-associated infection, recurrent hemoptysis, hospital-acquired pneumonia. Mitigation: minimally invasive resection, early ambulation, chest physiotherapy/incentive spirometry, opioid-sparing analgesia, early chest-tube and line removal, and treating IPC infection with antibiotics WITHOUT catheter removal (ATS/STS/STR)
readmission_drivers: Recurrent or loculated pleural effusion; pneumothorax/prolonged air leak after tube removal; empyema; pneumonia; post-operative atrial fibrillation; uncontrolled pain; VTE; dehydration/failure to thrive; IPC complications. Prevention levers: 48-72h nurse follow-up call, early surgical clinic visit, structured IPC/home-drain and return-precaution education, pulmonary rehabilitation, and proactive palliative symptom control
guideline_source: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines — Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer and Small Cell Lung Cancer (2025); ACCP/CHEST Diagnosis and Management of Lung Cancer 3rd ed, incl. Physiologic Evaluation (Brunelli 2013); ERAS Society / European Society of Thoracic Surgeons (ESTS) — Enhanced Recovery After Lung Surgery (Batchelor 2019); ATS/STS/STR — Management of Malignant Pleural Effusions (Feller-Kopman 2018); IASLC/AJCC TNM 9th edition staging (2024). Per PubMed-sourced attribution

Citations retrieved via PubMed
evidence_grade: Strong overall for the surgical-oncologic and perioperative pathway — anchored by Level-1 phase-3 RCT evidence (JCOG0802, CALGB/Alliance 140503) and predominantly strong-graded ERAS/ESTS recommendations plus Grade 1/2 ACCP physiologic-evaluation guidance. Moderate for several medical-palliative elements: the ATS/STS/STR malignant-pleural-effusion recommendations are GRADE conditional/weak (IPC vs pleurodesis equivalence), and hemoptysis/airway-emergency management rests on observational data and expert consensus rather than RCTs
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Severity tier drives resource use and pathway: WITHOUT CC/MCC (165/182) = minimally invasive resection or simple pleural drainage, floor care, LOS 2-4 days. WITH CC (164/181) = comorbidity burden (COPD, anemia, recurrent effusion) escalating monitoring and adding ~1-3 days. WITH MCC (163/180) = respiratory failure, sepsis/post-obstructive pneumonia, massive hemoptysis, empyema/bronchopleural fistula, unstable arrhythmia, or pneumonectomy driving ICU/step-down care, LOS 6-10 days, and markedly higher mortality; pneumonectomy and airway/hemoptysis emergencies concentrate in the MCC tier and dominate cost weight
pathway_pearls: 1) Secure tissue with full molecular/PD-L1 testing AND complete invasive mediastinal staging (EBUS + PET/CT + brain MRI) BEFORE committing to resection — the right operation on the right patient prevents futile thoracotomy. 2) Rigorously apply the ACCP ppoFEV1/ppoDLCO/VO2peak algorithm; do not resect prohibitive-risk lungs (VO2peak <10 mL/kg/min) — offer sublobar resection or SBRT instead. 3) Execute the ERAS bundle hard — minimally invasive resection, single chest tube with no external suction, pull the tube at <=450 mL/24h with no air leak, paravertebral opioid-sparing analgesia, and mobilization within 24h — this is what compresses LOS. 4) On the medical arm, match effusion therapy to lung expandability and prognosis (expandable lung/good LENT -> pleurodesis or IPC; non-expandable -> IPC), control airway obstruction and hemoptysis with IP/IR before they escalate to the ICU, and embed palliative care early
