condition: Chronic Kidney Disease / Other Kidney & Urinary Diagnoses
admission_criteria: Reserve inpatient admission for objective decompensation not correctable in ED/observation: severe/refractory hyperkalemia (K+ >6.0-6.5 mmol/L or ANY hyperkalemic ECG change); symptomatic uremia (encephalopathy, pericarditis/rub, uremic bleeding) = urgent-dialysis indication; volume overload with pulmonary edema/hypoxia refractory to oral diuresis or needing ultrafiltration; severe metabolic acidosis (HCO3 <15 mmol/L or pH <7.2); symptomatic/severe dysnatremia (Na <120 or >155); missed/inadequate dialysis with any of the above; need for new dialysis initiation or urgent biopsy/immunosuppression (RPGN, new nephrotic syndrome with declining function); obstructive uropathy needing drainage. ED-discharge/observation instead when: stable CKD, asymptomatic mild electrolyte shifts correctable in ED, single missed dialysis without hyperkalemia/overload (send to outpatient chair), uncomplicated stone passable medically. Decision drivers: K+ level + ECG, volume/oxygenation, uremic symptoms, acid-base severity, and access to timely outpatient dialysis
risk_stratification: CKD-EPI 2021 creatinine (race-free) eGFR to assign G-stage (G1 >=90 to G5 <15); UACR to assign A-stage (A1 <30, A2 30-300, A3 >300 mg/g); combine on KDIGO risk heat-map (green/yellow/orange/red) to set monitoring frequency and referral. KFRE (Kidney Failure Risk Equation, Tangri 4-/8-variable): 5-yr risk 3-5% -> nephrology referral; 2-yr >10% -> initiate multidisciplinary CKD care; 2-yr >40% -> modality education + dialysis-access/transplant preparation. Acute overlays: K+ >6.0-6.5 or peaked-T/widened-QRS/loss of P waves -> emergency K+ protocol; Na <120 or neuro symptoms -> hypertonic-saline pathway; if infection/sepsis overlap use qSOFA/SOFA. Small echogenic kidneys on US confirm chronicity vs reversible AKI
initial_workup_labs: CMP/BMP (creatinine vs baseline, BUN, eGFR, K+, HCO3, Ca, glucose) + serum phosphate and Mg; venous/arterial blood gas if HCO3 low; CBC (anemia of CKD, infection); iron studies TSAT + ferritin and reticulocyte count; intact PTH + 25-OH vitamin D (CKD-MBD); urinalysis with microscopy (dysmorphic RBCs, casts) + spot UACR/UPCR to quantify proteinuria; if nephritic/nephrotic pattern send ANA, ANCA, anti-GBM, C3/C4, hepatitis B/C, HIV, SPEP/UPEP + serum free light chains, cryoglobulins; troponin/BNP if cardiorenal/overload; type-and-screen if bleeding or biopsy planned. Triggers: high K+ -> STAT ECG + treatment; low HCO3 -> bicarbonate therapy; Hgb <10 -> iron/ESA workup; active urinary sediment or positive serologies -> biopsy pathway
initial_imaging_dx: STAT 12-lead ECG for any hyperkalemia (peaked T, PR prolongation, QRS widening, sine wave); renal/bladder ultrasound within 24h to assess size/echogenicity, hydronephrosis/obstruction and post-void residual (small echogenic kidneys = chronicity); CXR for pulmonary edema/effusions; bladder scan +/- Foley if outlet obstruction; non-contrast CT abdomen/pelvis if stone/obstruction suspected; TTE if uremic pericarditis, tamponade concern, or volume/cardiac assessment; percutaneous kidney biopsy for RPGN, unexplained nephrotic syndrome, or undifferentiated disease, typically within 24-72h once BP and coagulation optimized (hold antiplatelets/anticoagulants, correct uremic platelet dysfunction with DDAVP)
time_critical_interventions: Hyperkalemia with ECG changes = minutes-level emergency: IV calcium gluconate/chloride first for membrane stabilization (repeat if ECG persists), then intracellular shift with regular insulin 10 U + D50 (monitor glucose) plus nebulized albuterol; begin removal in parallel (loop diuretic if urine-making, GI cation binder, or emergent hemodialysis) and recheck K+ within 1h. Emergent dialysis for the AEIOU indications (severe Acidosis, refractory Electrolyte/hyperkalemia, Intoxication, refractory volume Overload/pulmonary edema, symptomatic Uremia: pericarditis/encephalopathy). Acute pulmonary edema: high-dose IV loop diuretic, nitrates, O2/NIV; if anuric -> urgent ultrafiltration. Severe symptomatic hyponatremia (seizure/coma): 3% saline bolus, but cap correction at <8-10 mEq/L/24h to prevent osmotic demyelination
initial_management: Hyperkalemia bundle: calcium (stabilize) + insulin/glucose + albuterol (shift) + removal via loop diuretic if urine output, GI binder (patiromer or sodium zirconium cyclosilicate; avoid SPS/kayexalate for acute use), sodium bicarbonate if acidotic, dialysis if refractory. Volume overload: IV loop diuretics (furosemide/bumetanide/torsemide), Na and fluid restriction, ultrafiltration if diuretic-resistant/anuric. Metabolic acidosis: oral/IV sodium bicarbonate targeting serum HCO3 >=22. Foundational CKD backbone (start/continue as stability allows): RAAS inhibitor (ACEi/ARB) for albuminuria (hold if hyperkalemic/hypovolemic AKI); SGLT2 inhibitor (dapagliflozin/empagliflozin) if eGFR >=20; finerenone (nonsteroidal MRA) for diabetic CKD with albuminuria if K+ <=4.8-5.0; moderate/high-intensity statin. Anemia: IV iron if TSAT <30% and ferritin <500; ESA if Hgb persistently <10 g/dL, target 10-11.5 (never chase >11.5-13). CKD-MBD: phosphate binders, manage PTH. Transfusion restrictive (Hgb <7, or <8 with active cardiac disease). Renally dose all drugs, avoid NSAIDs/nephrotoxins/contrast. Active GN: nephrology-directed immunosuppression (glucocorticoids +/- cyclophosphamide or rituximab) per biopsy
day1_milestones: Correct life-threatening derangements (K+, acidosis) and document ECG normalization; identify the precipitant (missed/inadequate dialysis, med nonadherence, obstruction, progression, new GN, nephrotoxin); nephrology consulted and renal US reviewed; assess dialysis access (fistula/graft/tunneled catheter) or plan for it; complete volume assessment and start diuresis or schedule dialysis/UF; reconcile and renally dose all meds, hold nephrotoxins and RAASi if indicated; establish baseline eGFR and quantify proteinuria; send GN serologies if sediment active; daily weights and strict I/O begun
day2_milestones: Reassess volume and electrolytes and confirm K+ trending down and HCO3 improving; deliver dialysis session if ESRD/indicated and gauge response; if new dialysis required, begin modality education and access planning (avoid subclavian lines, protect non-dominant arm for future fistula); (re)initiate foundational therapy (SGLT2i, RAASi, finerenone) as stability permits; perform biopsy if planned and start empiric immunosuppression if RPGN confirmed; dietitian (renal diet) and PT/OT evaluation; care-management engaged on outpatient dialysis placement/transport
day3plus_milestones: Electrolytes and volume stable on oral regimen off IV therapy; euvolemic or at dry weight with stable respiratory status; definitive outpatient plan locked in - named outpatient dialysis unit/chair confirmed for ESRD, or nephrology follow-up for CKD; dialysis-access plan finalized (AV fistula referral / PD catheter / tunneled catheter as bridge); immunosuppression/steroid-taper plan set if GN; anemia and CKD-MBD regimens established; medication reconciliation completed with hold-parameters and lab-recheck timing; patient/caregiver teaching done with teach-back; discharge-readiness confirmed
consults_multidisciplinary: Nephrology early (Day 0-1) as primary driver; vascular surgery / interventional access or IR for AV fistula-graft or tunneled catheter; transplant nephrology referral if candidate; renal dietitian Day 1-2 (low-K+/low-phosphate/sodium and fluid limits); clinical pharmacy for renal dosing, potassium/phosphate binders, ESA/iron, and RAASi/SGLT2i/finerenone reconciliation; care management + social work early for dialysis-unit placement, transportation, insurance, home health; PT/OT for deconditioning; palliative care if conservative/non-dialysis pathway or high symptom burden; cardiology for cardiorenal syndrome or uremic pericarditis; rheumatology for vasculitis/lupus nephritis
monitoring_level: ICU: severe hyperkalemia with arrhythmia/hemodynamic instability, emergent dialysis in an unstable patient, severe acidosis, symptomatic hyponatremia needing hypertonic saline with frequent Na checks, pulmonary edema needing NIV/intubation, or uremic pericarditis with tamponade. Telemetry/step-down: K+ >6.0 or any dynamic ECG change until corrected, arrhythmia risk, or hemodynamic monitoring during dialysis. Floor: hemodynamically stable, controlled electrolytes, routine dialysis. Escalation triggers: rising K+ despite therapy, new/evolving ECG changes, worsening acidosis, new hypoxia, hemodynamic instability, or altered mental status
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: renal, low-potassium, low-phosphate, sodium <=2 g/day with fluid restriction (ESRD ~1-1.5 L/day); protein individualized (0.6-0.8 g/kg/day in non-dialysis CKD to slow progression; ~1.0-1.2 g/kg/day on dialysis), dietitian-directed. Mobility: early ambulation with fall precautions (uremia/deconditioning). VTE prophylaxis: mechanical + pharmacologic - unfractionated heparin generally preferred in advanced CKD/ESRD (renal LMWH clearance/accumulation); if LMWH used, renally dose enoxaparin and consider anti-Xa monitoring; weigh uremic platelet-dysfunction bleeding risk and hold before biopsy. Glucose: individualized (A1c ~7-8%), cautious insulin dosing given reduced renal clearance/hypoglycemia risk. Lines/catheters: preserve vasculature - avoid PICC/subclavian in CKD stage 4-5 (protect future fistula sites), remove Foley and unnecessary lines early, and run a dialysis-catheter care bundle to prevent CRBSI
discharge_criteria: Potassium and acid-base normalized and stable on the oral regimen (K+ <5.5, HCO3 >=22); euvolemic or at dry weight with respiratory status at baseline on room air; uremic symptoms resolved and, for ESRD, dialysis regimen re-established with the next session scheduled; functional dialysis access or a concrete access plan in place; mental status stable and tolerating oral intake and medications; nephrology follow-up and outpatient dialysis chair confirmed
discharge_planning: Disposition home with a confirmed named outpatient dialysis chair/unit (or SNF if deconditioned), plus home health if needed. Medication reconciliation: renally dosed meds, potassium and phosphate binders, ESA/iron plan, and RAASi/SGLT2i/finerenone with explicit hold-parameters and a scheduled BMP recheck (within 3-7 days after starting/changing RAASi, diuretic, or binder). Follow-up: nephrology within 7-14 days (sooner for new dialysis or major med changes) and a dialysis-access surgery appointment. Education: renal diet, fluid/potassium restriction, medication and dialysis-schedule adherence, sick-day rules, and red-flag symptoms (rapid weight gain, dyspnea, missed-dialysis consequences). Transitions: warm handoff to the dialysis unit, confirm transportation, and teach-back before discharge
expected_los: CMS IPPS geometric-mean LOS by severity tier: DRG 700 (no CC/MCC) ~2.5-2.7 days; DRG 699 (with CC) ~3.2-3.5 days; DRG 698 (with MCC) ~4.4-4.7 days; blended typical ~3-4 days for the collapsed group
target_los: 2-3 days for uncomplicated electrolyte/volume decompensation with early nephrology involvement and coordinated dialysis; 4-5 days is legitimately justified when new dialysis initiation, vascular-access creation, or kidney biopsy with immunosuppression is required
quality_metrics: 30-day all-cause readmission and CMS standardized readmission ratio (SRR) - high in CKD/ESRD; ESRD QIP / EQRS measures for dialysis patients (dialysis adequacy Kt/V, long-term catheter rate and vascular-access/fistula rate, hypercalcemia, anemia/Hgb management); transitions-of-care measures (medication reconciliation completed, nephrology follow-up within 14 days); HAC avoidance - dialysis-catheter CRBSI and CAUTI; immunizations (influenza, pneumococcal, hepatitis B for dialysis) and depression screening. This DRG group is not tied to a single CMS core bundle (unlike SEP-1); performance is driven mainly by readmission, ESRD QIP, and safe-transition metrics
common_complications: Recurrent/rebound hyperkalemia (mitigate with serial K+, binders, dietary counseling, cautious RAASi); volume overload/pulmonary edema (daily weights, diuresis/UF, fluid restriction); dialysis-catheter bloodstream infection/CRBSI (access bundle, minimize catheter dwell, prioritize fistula); uremic platelet dysfunction/bleeding (DDAVP, dialysis, hold anticoagulation pre-procedure); hypoglycemia from reduced insulin clearance (cautious dosing, glucose monitoring); nephrotoxin/contrast exposure worsening residual function (avoid/minimize); dialysis-disequilibrium syndrome at first sessions (short, gradual initial runs); falls/delirium in uremic or elderly patients (precautions)
readmission_drivers: Missed/inadequate dialysis and nonadherence to fluid/diet/medications driving recurrent overload and hyperkalemia; poor transitions with no timely nephrology follow-up; dialysis-access complications (thrombosis, infection); medication issues (RAASi-induced hyperkalemia, over-diuresis and volume depletion). Prevention levers: confirm an outpatient dialysis chair before discharge, guarantee nephrology follow-up <=14 days, dietitian teaching with teach-back, optimize potassium binders, access surveillance, and social-work support for transportation and insurance
guideline_source: PRIMARY: KDIGO 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Evaluation and Management of Chronic Kidney Disease (Kidney International, 2024). Supporting society guidance: KDIGO 2025/2026 Anemia in CKD; KDIGO 2022 Diabetes Management in CKD; KDIGO 2017 CKD-MBD update; UK Kidney Association 2023 Management of Hyperkalaemia in Adults; KDIGO Controversies Conference on acute hyperkalemia
key_citations: KDIGO 2024 CKD Guideline, Kidney Int 2024;105(4S):S117-S314 (PMID 38490803; DOI 10.1016/j.kint.2023.10.018); EMPA-KIDNEY - Empagliflozin in Patients with CKD, N Engl J Med 2023;388:117-127 (PMID 36331190); DAPA-CKD - Dapagliflozin in Patients with CKD, N Engl J Med 2020;383:1436-1446 (PMID 32970396); CREDENCE - Canagliflozin and Renal Outcomes in T2DM and Nephropathy, N Engl J Med 2019;380:2295-2306 (PMID 30990260); FIDELIO-DKD - Effect of Finerenone on CKD Outcomes in T2DM, N Engl J Med 2020;383:2219-2229 (PMID 33264825). Dialysis-initiation timing is symptom-guided per the IDEAL trial (Cooper et al, NEJM 2010) - cited from memory, not tool-verified here
evidence_grade: Strong for the foundational CKD backbone - KDIGO 2024 is a Class I / Grade 1A society guideline underpinned by multiple large RCTs (SGLT2 inhibitors, finerenone, RAAS inhibition). Moderate-to-strong for acute decompensation management (hyperkalemia, volume overload, acidosis), where several steps rest on physiologic rationale and consensus rather than RCTs, and exact dialysis-initiation timing is symptom-guided (moderate). Overall grade lowered slightly because this is a heterogeneous collapsed DRG spanning ESRD, glomerular disease, electrolyte, and stone diagnoses, so the pathway is a composite rather than a single-disease protocol
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 700 (no CC/MCC): stable single-issue admissions (manageable electrolyte disturbance, nephrolithiasis, stable CKD workup) - floor care, LOS ~2.5d, low readmission. DRG 699 (with CC): moderate complications such as hyperkalemia, moderate anemia, or CKD stage 4-5 decompensation - LOS ~3.3d. DRG 698 (with MCC): major complications - severe hyperkalemia with ECG changes, acute pulmonary edema, uremic pericarditis/encephalopathy, sepsis overlap, or need for new dialysis initiation, often ICU - LOS ~4.6d with higher readmission and mortality. Tier is frequently set by accurate capture of the complication (severe hyperkalemia, metabolic encephalopathy, acute-on-chronic volume overload); precise documentation/coding of these MCC/CC diagnoses drives correct DRG assignment and expected LOS
pathway_pearls: 1) Treat hyperkalemia as two problems: calcium stabilizes and insulin/albuterol shift only buy time - always pair with definitive removal (binder, diuresis, or dialysis) and re-check K+ within the hour. 2) Engage nephrology and dialysis-access planning on Day 0-1 and CONFIRM an outpatient dialysis chair before discharge - the single highest-yield readmission lever in this DRG. 3) Protect the vasculature: no subclavian or PICC lines in advanced CKD and preserve the non-dominant arm for a future fistula. 4) Do not abandon the foundational backbone - start/continue SGLT2i (eGFR >=20), RAASi for albuminuria, and finerenone in diabetic CKD, but time-out on potassium and volume, renally dose everything, and avoid nephrotoxins/contrast

admission_criteria: Inpatient admission for all MAJOR lower-limb amputation (transtibial/BKA, through-knee, transfemoral/AKA) done for CLTI or diabetic foot; emergent same-day admission for wet gangrene, spreading/necrotizing foot infection, or systemic sepsis needing urgent operative source control; osteomyelitis with systemic toxicity; WIfI stage 3-4 tissue loss/rest pain not amenable to or failing revascularization; unreconstructable disease. Admit (not ED discharge/observation) when SIRS/qSOFA>=2, fever + leukocytosis, uncontrolled hyperglycemia/DKA-HHS, or hemodynamic instability; minor toe/ray amputation in a stable, afebrile, well-controlled patient may be 23-hr/outpatient but is admitted if IV antibiotics or revascularization are required. Major amputation is never an outpatient procedure
risk_stratification: SVS WIfI (Wound-Ischemia-foot Infection) threatened-limb stages 1-4 (stage 4 = high ~>25% 1-yr amputation risk) drives urgency; GVG PLAN framework (Patient risk-Limb-ANatomy). Perfusion/healing thresholds: toe pressure (preferred in diabetes/calcified vessels) >=30 mmHg, ankle pressure >50-70 mmHg, TcPO2 >40 mmHg predict amputation-level healing (TcPO2 20-30 poor, <15 mmHg fails-Poredos; BKA with TcPO2 <40 = 2x AKA-conversion/death-Columbo). VASQIP 30-day amputation-mortality score (8 factors: DNR, CHF, age>80, renal insufficiency, AKA level, dependent functional status, CAD, COPD): low 0-3=2.3%, moderate 4-7=4.3%, high 8-10=7.5%, very-high >10=17.5% (Jolissaint). RCRI for perioperative MACE; mFI-5 frailty and ASA class guide prehab/goals-of-care; albumin <3.0 g/dL and elevated HbA1c predict poor healing/SSI
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential (leukocytosis/left shift = infection); CMP (renal function for contrast/drug dosing, electrolytes); glucose + HbA1c (control + healing prognosis); lactate + 2 sets blood cultures if septic (SEP-1); CRP/ESR (infection/osteomyelitis trend), procalcitonin optional; albumin/prealbumin (nutrition/healing); coagulation panel PT-INR/aPTT; type & screen or crossmatch; lipid panel (statin optimization); deep-tissue and bone cultures (NOT superficial swab) to target antibiotics and grade DFI severity per IWGDF/IDSA. Preop ECG and troponin if cardiac symptoms; review/adjust anticoagulants and metformin/SGLT2 before OR
initial_imaging_dx: Plain foot/limb radiograph on presentation (subcutaneous gas, osteomyelitis, foreign body). Arterial assessment: ABI/TBI, segmental pressures with Doppler waveforms, toe pressures, and TcPO2 or skin-perfusion-pressure to select a healing level. CT angiography or MR angiography (or catheter DSA) of runoff within 24-48h when revascularization is being considered (GVG/2024 ACC-AHA PAD). MRI if deep abscess or extent of osteomyelitis is unclear. Preop CXR/ECG as indicated; TTE or stress testing only if it will change management and will not delay urgent source control
time_critical_interventions: Wet gangrene/necrotizing or septic foot = surgical emergency: broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1h of sepsis/septic-shock recognition + lactate + cultures (Surviving Sepsis Campaign / CMS SEP-1 bundle) and urgent/emergent operative source control (guillotine/open ankle amputation or wide debridement) same-day (ideally <24h) - do NOT delay source control for a full revascularization workup. Resuscitate and stabilize DKA/HHS concurrently. For CLTI without sepsis: urgent vascular referral and prompt revascularization assessment. De-escalate antibiotics on deep-tissue/bone culture results
initial_management: Empiric IV antibiotics by IWGDF/IDSA DFI severity - moderate: gram-positive incl. Staph +/- gram-negative (ampicillin-sulbactam, or ceftriaxone + metronidazole); severe/limb- or life-threatening: broad gram-positive/negative/anaerobe + MRSA coverage (piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem + vancomycin or linezolid), then narrow to cultures; ~5-7 days if infected bone fully resected at amputation, longer (up to weeks) if residual osteomyelitis. Source control = the amputation/debridement; staged guillotine open amputation first for wet gangrene/sepsis then delayed definitive closure at a healthy perfused level. Best medical therapy (GVG/2024 PAD): high-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80 / rosuvastatin), antiplatelet (aspirin 81 mg or clopidogrel), add low-dose rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + ASA if revascularized (VOYAGER-PAD), BP control, smoking cessation. Glucose target 140-180 mg/dL with insulin; correct DKA. Restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if symptomatic CAD). Multimodal analgesia from admission: regional/perineural block (sciatic-femoral catheter), acetaminophen, gabapentinoid, ketamine, minimal opioids to reduce phantom-limb pain
day1_milestones: POD0/day of surgery: pre-op optimization complete (glucose, volume, cardiac risk stratified, deep cultures sent, consent, amputation level marked jointly by vascular + rehab); antibiotic and VTE plans set. Intra-op: choose the most-distal level with objective healing potential (perfusion/TcPO2, nutrition, WIfI), tension-free myodesis/myoplasty; staged guillotine if infected/septic. POD0 recovery: regional block + multimodal analgesia active, rigid removable dressing or limb protector applied for BKA (controls edema, prevents knee-flexion contracture), stump elevated without knee flexion, resume statin/antiplatelet/cardiac meds, restart VTE prophylaxis, tight glucose control, early nutrition, and triage to step-down vs floor by hemodynamics
day2_milestones: POD1: out of bed to chair; start PT/OT (bed mobility, transfers, wheelchair training, contracture prevention, upper-body strengthening); inspect wound for hematoma/bleeding/ischemia; keep rigid dressing; transition to oral multimodal analgesia and wean opioids; de-escalate antibiotics on culture results; continue LMWH VTE prophylaxis and glycemic control; remove urinary catheter; dietitian assessment for protein-calorie/albumin optimization; begin prosthetic/rehab counseling and set a K-level ambulation goal; engage social work/case management for disposition
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ to discharge: advance mobility (standing, transfers, gait training with assistive device as tolerated); confirm healing trajectory and absence of infection; pain controlled on oral regimen; finalize antibiotic duration by residual-osteomyelitis status; staged patients proceed to definitive closure/revision once infection controlled and perfusion adequate; confirm disposition (acute inpatient rehab preferred over SNF when ambulation potential - linked to prosthesis receipt and survival); complete prosthetics referral and stump-shrinker/limb-protector education; medication reconciliation; schedule surgery (~2 wk), vascular, wound, and PM&R/prosthetics follow-up. ERAS/LEAP target: discharge ~POD3-5 after definitive amputation
consults_multidisciplinary: Vascular surgery (primary: level selection, revascularization, source control); infectious disease (complex/MDR DFI, osteomyelitis antibiotic duration); endocrinology/diabetes for glycemic control; PM&R/physiatry plus physical & occupational therapy early (POD0-1) for mobilization, contracture prevention, and prosthetic candidacy/K-level; prosthetist-orthotist counseling; wound care; nutrition/dietitian; pharmacy (stewardship, anticoagulation, analgesia); cardiology/anesthesia preop if high RCRI; psychology/psychiatry (adjustment, depression, body image); social work + case management at admission (disposition, home health, DME); palliative care/goals-of-care for frail, nonambulatory, or very-high-risk candidates; podiatry for contralateral-limb preservation
monitoring_level: Floor/ward for stable elective single-stage amputation (DRG 241, uncomplicated). Telemetry/step-down for significant CAD/arrhythmia risk or moderate comorbidity (DRG 240). ICU for septic shock, hemodynamic instability, DKA/HHS, respiratory failure, or major intra-op blood loss (typical MCC tier, DRG 239). Escalation triggers: hypotension or rising lactate, sepsis progression, uncontrolled glucose/DKA, stump hemorrhage or ischemia, new chest pain/arrhythmia, acute kidney injury, altered mental status. De-escalate to floor once hemodynamically stable, source-controlled, and glucose controlled
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: early oral/enteral high-protein diet for wound healing, treat malnutrition (albumin <3.0-3.5 g/dL), glycemic target 140-180 mg/dL avoiding hypoglycemia. Mobility: early mobilization POD0-1, out-of-bed POD1, daily PT/OT; prevent knee-flexion contracture in BKA (no pillow under knee, rigid removable dressing, periodic prone lying); wheelchair and transfer training plus upper-body conditioning for future prosthetic use. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg daily or weight-adjusted, or UFH) plus mechanical SCD on the contralateral limb - high VTE risk from immobility; continue through stay. Lines/catheters: remove Foley POD1, minimize central lines, discontinue regional catheter per protocol
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable and afebrile; infection source-controlled with appropriate oral antibiotics or OPAT arranged; stump wound healthy without active bleeding, dehiscence, or spreading infection; pain controlled on oral multimodal regimen; glucose controlled on discharge regimen; tolerating diet; safe mobility established (independent or with confirmed assistance - transfers/wheelchair or early gait) with required equipment (wheelchair, walker, rigid dressing/shrinker) in place; disposition confirmed; patient/caregiver education completed; follow-up scheduled
discharge_planning: Disposition: acute inpatient rehabilitation preferred for candidates with ambulation/prosthetic potential (associated with prosthesis receipt and survival - UAB ERP); SNF for subacute needs; home with home-health PT and DME (wheelchair, hospital bed, walker) for well-supported patients. Medication reconciliation: statin, antiplatelet +/- dual-pathway rivaroxaban, glycemic meds, tapering multimodal + neuropathic analgesia, VTE prophylaxis duration, antibiotic course/OPAT. Follow-up: vascular surgery ~1-2 wk (wound/staple check), PM&R/prosthetics, wound care, PCP/endocrine for diabetes; contralateral-foot protection (offloading, daily inspection). Education: stump care/desensitization, contracture prevention, phantom-limb pain, warning signs, smoking cessation. Warm handoff to rehab/SNF and OPAT coordination
expected_los: Collapsed DRG group benchmark (MDC 05, FY reference): DRG 241 (no CC/MCC) AMLOS ~4.0 days (GMLOS ~3-3.5); DRG 240 (CC) AMLOS ~7.3 days (GMLOS ~5.5-6); DRG 239 (MCC) AMLOS ~11.1 days (GMLOS ~8-9). Real-world total LOS ~5-14 days depending on sepsis, staged procedures, and rehab-bed placement; post-op LOS commonly 4-7 days
target_los: Optimized ERAS/LEAP target: post-amputation LOS ~3-5 days (LEAP median post-op 3 vs 5 days; UAB ERP AKA 6 vs 7 days); overall target ~4 days (no CC/MCC), ~6-7 days (CC), ~9-10 days (MCC), driven by prehab, multimodal analgesia + regional block, rigid dressing, early PT, and early disposition planning - note residual LOS is often limited by rehab-bed availability and staged closure rather than medical readiness
quality_metrics: SVS VQI lower-extremity amputation registry outcomes; 30-day mortality (benchmark ~4-7%, higher for AKA/MCC); 30-day readmission (high, ~20-30%); surgical site infection/SSI; stump revision or ipsilateral BKA-to-AKA conversion rate; postoperative ambulation/prosthesis-fitting rate and K-level; time-to-prosthesis; SEP-1 sepsis-bundle compliance for septic presentations; perioperative severe hyperglycemia/hypoglycemia eCQMs; VTE prophylaxis compliance; ERAS/pathway compliance; multidisciplinary amputation-prevention/limb-preservation metrics; goals-of-care documentation
common_complications: Stump: wound infection/SSI, dehiscence, hematoma, ischemic flap necrosis requiring revision or proximal re-amputation (BKA-to-AKA conversion ~24%, higher if TcPO2 <40); phantom-limb and residual-limb pain; knee-flexion contracture (BKA). Systemic: sepsis, MI/MACE (high CAD burden), AKI, delirium, pneumonia, VTE/PE, poor glycemic control, pressure injury, deconditioning/failure to ambulate. Mitigation: perfusion-guided distal-level selection, staged guillotine for infection, rigid removable dressing, multimodal analgesia + nerve block (halves phantom-limb pain), early PT and contracture prevention, VTE and glucose protocols, cardiac optimization, and nutrition support
readmission_drivers: Stump wound infection/dehiscence/necrosis needing revision or re-amputation; uncontrolled infection or residual osteomyelitis; poor glycemic control; contralateral-limb ulceration/CLTI progression; inadequate pain control; VTE; cardiac or renal decompensation; falls; care-transition gaps (DME, home health, OPAT). Prevention: perfusion-guided level selection, staged closure, nutrition and glucose optimization, structured wound follow-up at ~1-2 weeks, OPAT coordination, prosthetic/PT pathway, contralateral-foot protection, acute-rehab placement, medication reconciliation, and early post-discharge contact
guideline_source: Global Vascular Guidelines (GVG) on the Management of Chronic Limb-Threatening Ischemia - SVS/ESVS/WFVS 2019 (Conte MS et al); 2024 ACC/AHA/Multisociety Guideline for the Management of Lower Extremity Peripheral Artery Disease (Gornik HL et al); IWGDF/IDSA 2023 Guidelines on Diagnosis and Treatment of Diabetes-Related Foot Infections (Senneville E et al). Supported by SVS/VQI amputation practice and emerging ERAS-for-amputation pathways (LEAP)
key_citations: Conte MS et al. Global vascular guidelines on the management of chronic limb-threatening ischemia. J Vasc Surg 2019;69(6S):3S-125S (PMID 31159978; EJVES PMID 31182334). Gornik HL et al. 2024 ACC/AHA Lower Extremity PAD Guideline. Circulation 2024;149(24):e1313-e1410 (PMID 38743805). Senneville E et al. IWGDF/IDSA 2023 diabetes-related foot infections. Diabetes Metab Res Rev 2023;40(3):e3687 (PMID 37779323). Jolissaint JS et al. Risk prediction of 30-day mortality after lower extremity major amputation. J Vasc Surg 2019;70(6):1868-76 (PMID 31147118). Matheny H/O'Banion LA et al. LEAP ERAS pathway for vascular amputees. J Vasc Surg 2023;78(4):1057-63 (PMID 37315909). Sickels AD/Spangler EL et al. Enhanced Recovery Pathway for lower extremity amputation. Ann Vasc Surg 2025;119:99-110 (PMID 40320210). Columbo JA et al. BKA failure and TcPO2. Vasc Endovascular Surg 2016;50(8):554-8 (PMID 27909207)
evidence_grade: Strong for indications and best medical therapy - GVG 2019 and 2024 ACC/AHA PAD are Class I multisociety guidelines and IWGDF/IDSA 2023 is GRADE-based. Moderate for the amputation-specific perioperative/ERAS pathway - supported by cohort/observational studies (LEAP, UAB ERP) and extrapolated ERAS Society principles rather than large RCTs; level-selection perfusion thresholds are moderate-quality prognostic evidence. Overall: Strong for the vascular/infection foundation, Moderate for the surgical-recovery specifics
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 241 (no CC/MCC): usually planned single-stage amputation in a more stable patient; floor care; AMLOS ~4 days; lowest mortality. DRG 240 (with CC): a comorbidity/complication (diabetes with complication, CKD, cellulitis, moderate anemia); telemetry/step-down; AMLOS ~7 days. DRG 239 (with MCC): sepsis/septic shock, wet gangrene with SIRS, acute renal or respiratory failure, acute MI/CHF, or major hemorrhage; frequently needs ICU/step-down and staged guillotine + delayed completion; AMLOS ~11 days; highest mortality (VASQIP very-high tier ~17.5% at 30 days). Severity tier tracks with emergent source-control need, ICU use, higher AKA rate, longer antibiotics, and worse ambulation/survival
pathway_pearls: 1) Pick the most-distal level that will actually heal using objective perfusion (toe pressure/TcPO2 >40 mmHg, nutrition, WIfI) - knee salvage aids ambulation, but do not force a BKA that will fail (TcPO2 <40 = 2x conversion/death). 2) For wet gangrene/sepsis do source control FIRST (staged guillotine within hours, antibiotics within 1h) - do not delay for a full revascularization workup. 3) Deploy the ERAS/LEAP bundle from POD0 - regional + multimodal analgesia with a gabapentinoid (halves phantom-limb pain), rigid removable dressing, early PT, and prosthetic counseling - shortening LOS and speeding prosthesis. 4) These are high-mortality vasculopaths: optimize cardiac/glycemic/nutritional status, engage rehab and social work early (acute rehab beats SNF for ambulation and survival), and hold honest goals-of-care conversations in frail, nonambulatory, or very-high-risk patients

scope_and_volume_notes: Amputation for circulatory disorders (PAD, diabetic foot). Frequently follows osteomyelitis/diabetic ulcer admissions.

admission_criteria: Inpatient admission for chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI = PAD + ischemic rest pain, ulcer >2 weeks, or gangrene) or acute limb ischemia (ALI); specific triggers: ischemic rest pain requiring parenteral analgesia; new/progressive tissue loss or wet gangrene; diabetic/limb foot infection with systemic signs (fever, leukocytosis, sepsis) or deep/limb-threatening infection needing IV antibiotics +/- urgent debridement; ALI of any Rutherford class (I-IIb) needing anticoagulation and time-sensitive revascularization; severe hemodynamic compromise (ABI <0.4, ankle systolic <50 mmHg, toe pressure <30 mmHg, TcPO2 <30 mmHg) with tissue loss; need for expedited inpatient revascularization work-up. ED discharge/observation appropriate for stable claudication (Rutherford 1-3), healing wounds without infection, ABI >0.4 without rest pain — manage as outpatient with urgent vascular referral
risk_stratification: SVS WIfI (Wound 0-3, Ischemia 0-3, foot Infection 0-3) -> clinical Stage 1-4 estimating 1-yr amputation risk and revascularization benefit: Stage 4 (very high) drives urgent revascularization/limb-salvage; Stage 1 low. Ischemia grade 3 = ankle SP <50 mmHg, toe pressure <30 mmHg, TcPO2 <30 mmHg. Rutherford ALI: I viable; IIa marginally threatened (salvageable if promptly treated); IIb immediately threatened (needs revascularization <6h); III irreversible (sensory loss + paralysis + inaudible Doppler) -> primary amputation. GLASS (Global Limb Anatomic Staging System) I-III grades anatomic complexity/target-artery patency for endo vs open. PLAN framework (Patient risk, Limb severity, ANatomy) per GVG: patient axis stratified by estimated 2-yr all-cause mortality (average vs high risk) to guide bypass vs endovascular. Fontaine/Rutherford chronic stage (4 rest pain, 5 minor, 6 major tissue loss)
initial_workup_labs: CBC (leukocytosis -> infection; anemia limits tissue O2 delivery); CMP/creatinine-eGFR (contrast-nephropathy risk, guides angiography/hydration; K+ baseline for reperfusion); HbA1c (glycemic control target, prognostic for healing); fasting lipid panel (statin intensity/LDL); coagulation panel + type & screen (pre-procedure); lactate (elevated -> advanced ischemia/reperfusion/sepsis); CK + myoglobin + urinalysis (rhabdomyolysis/pigment nephropathy in ALI/reperfusion); CRP/ESR + wound and blood cultures before antibiotics if infection; hs-troponin + BNP given polyvascular/cardiac comorbidity; albumin/prealbumin (wound-healing/nutrition). Triggers: rising lactate/K+/CK -> ICU + fasciotomy readiness; positive cultures -> targeted antibiotics; low eGFR -> minimize contrast/CO2 angiography
initial_imaging_dx: Resting ABI with Doppler waveforms at presentation (Class 1); toe pressures/toe-brachial index preferred in diabetes/ESRD with incompressible vessels; TcPO2 or skin perfusion pressure for wound-healing potential (Class 2a). Arterial duplex ultrasound for segmental disease. CT angiography (CTA runoff) or MR angiography for revascularization planning — expedite within 24h for CLTI/ALI. Digital subtraction angiography (DSA) as combined diagnostic-therapeutic gold standard at time of intervention. Foot radiographs (+/- MRI) if osteomyelitis suspected. Echocardiogram/CTA and ECG to seek embolic/thrombotic source in ALI. For Rutherford IIb ALI, imaging must NOT delay revascularization — proceed on clinical grounds/on-table angiography
time_critical_interventions: ALI: immediate systemic anticoagulation on diagnosis — unfractionated heparin bolus 80 U/kg (or 5000 U) then infusion ~18 U/kg/h titrated to aPTT 1.5-2.5x; revascularize Rutherford IIb within the ~6h golden window before irreversible muscle/nerve injury; catheter-directed thrombolysis an option for Rutherford I-IIa; Rutherford III -> primary amputation, do NOT attempt revascularization. CLTI: urgent (same-admission) vascular-specialist referral and time-to-revascularization to minimize tissue loss (Class 1). Limb-threatening foot infection/sepsis: empiric IV antibiotics within 1h + emergent surgical source control (I&D, debridement, minor amputation). Post-reperfusion: monitor for compartment syndrome and perform/prepare four-compartment fasciotomy; monitor for hyperkalemia/rhabdomyolysis
initial_management: Antiplatelet: single-agent aspirin 81 mg (or clopidogrel 75 mg) for symptomatic PAD (Class 1A). Dual-pathway (preferred post-revascularization/high-risk): low-dose rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + aspirin 81 mg — reduces MACE and MALE (VOYAGER PAD/COMPASS, Class 1A); avoid full-dose anticoagulation without separate indication. High-intensity statin (atorvastatin 80 mg or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg) targeting >=50% LDL reduction and LDL <70 mg/dL; add ezetimibe/PCSK9 inhibitor if not at goal. ALI: therapeutic UFH infusion +/- catheter-directed thrombolysis. Analgesia: multimodal + opioids for ischemic rest pain. Foot infection: empiric broad-spectrum per IDSA/IWGDF (e.g., vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam for severe/limb-threatening), de-escalate by culture. BP <130/80 with ACE-I/ARB (Class 1). Diabetes: SGLT2i (empagliflozin/dapagliflozin/canagliflozin) + GLP-1 RA (semaglutide/liraglutide) reduce MACE (Class 1A); inpatient insulin to glucose 140-180. Smoking cessation: varenicline/NRT + counseling (Class 1). Source control: debridement/drainage/toe or minor amputation for uncontrolled infection/wet gangrene. Transfusion individualized/restrictive (Hgb <7-8 g/dL). Cilostazol ONLY for claudication (not CLTI/rest pain; contraindicated in HF)
day1_milestones: Confirm diagnosis and stage limb: WIfI + Rutherford ALI class + hemodynamic testing (ABI/toe pressure/TcPO2) done; urgent vascular-surgery/endovascular consult with documented revascularization plan (endo vs open vs medical) via PLAN/GLASS; start best medical therapy day 1 (antiplatelet, high-intensity statin, ACE-I/ARB, glucose control); therapeutic anticoagulation if ALI; empiric IV antibiotics + source control if infection; ischemic pain controlled; CTA/MRA or angiography ordered/performed with renal-protective contrast strategy; wound care + pressure offloading initiated, podiatry engaged for foot wounds; smoking-cessation counseling and pharmacotherapy started; baseline labs/HbA1c/eGFR reviewed
day2_milestones: Revascularization performed or scheduled (endovascular PVI or surgical bypass) when indicated, or continued medical optimization for non-candidates; post-procedure monitoring of distal pulses/Doppler signals, access site, and post-contrast renal function; reassess perfusion (ABI/toe pressure/TcPO2) and wound; initiate/continue dual-pathway antithrombotic (rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + aspirin) after revascularization; de-escalate antibiotics per culture/sensitivity; PT/OT mobilization and offloading reinforced; optimize glycemia and blood pressure; reassess and taper analgesia; begin disposition/discharge planning
day3plus_milestones: Confirm adequate/improved limb perfusion and favorable wound-healing trajectory with no progressive tissue loss or uncontrolled infection; finalize wound-care plan (staged debridement, negative-pressure wound therapy, appropriate offloading footwear); transition IV to oral/OPAT antibiotics once infection controlled; complete medication reconciliation to guideline-directed therapy (antiplatelet +/- rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID, high-intensity statin, ACE-I/ARB, SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA); ambulating with PT; arrange outpatient vascular surveillance (duplex), wound clinic, podiatry, and primary-care follow-up; discharge when pain controlled on orals, infection controlled, perfusion stable, and home wound-care/services in place
consults_multidisciplinary: Vascular surgery/endovascular specialist — Day 0, urgent, all CLTI/ALI (GVG: all suspected CLTI referred urgently); podiatry/foot-and-wound care — Day 0-1 for foot wounds/infection; infectious disease — severe/complex foot infection or osteomyelitis; endocrinology/diabetes team — glycemic optimization; cardiology — polyvascular disease/perioperative cardiac risk; PT/OT — mobility and offloading; wound-care nursing (+/- hyperbaric where indicated); nutrition — diabetic/wound-healing diet and hypoalbuminemia; pharmacy — antithrombotic dosing and antibiotic stewardship; care management/social work — home health, DME, transitions, SDOH; nephrology if CKD/contrast concerns; smoking-cessation service. Organize as a multidisciplinary "toe-and-flow" limb-salvage team / center of excellence (GVG) to maximize amputation-free survival
monitoring_level: Most medically managed CLTI admissions: floor with telemetry (high burden of concomitant CAD/arrhythmia). ICU/step-down for: ALI with reperfusion syndrome (hyperkalemia, rhabdomyolysis, compartment-syndrome watch/post-fasciotomy), sepsis/septic shock from foot infection, hemodynamic instability, major bleeding on antithrombotics, or complex post-revascularization course. Escalation triggers: new or worsening motor/sensory deficit (advancing ischemia), pain out of proportion or tense compartment (compartment syndrome), rising lactate or K+ or CK, hypotension/sepsis physiology, uncontrolled ischemic pain, or significant bleeding
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: diabetic/cardiac diet optimized for wound healing (adequate protein, correct hypoalbuminemia); NPO appropriately before procedures. Mobility: early mobilization/PT with strict foot-wound offloading (total-contact cast/offloading device), heel protection, avoid pressure injury. VTE prophylaxis: pharmacologic (LMWH or UFH) unless already on therapeutic anticoagulation (therapeutic heparin for ALI provides coverage) or high bleeding risk; add mechanical if pharmacologic contraindicated. Glucose control target 140-180 mg/dL with insulin. Lines/catheters: remove arterial access sheath per protocol with hemostasis/closure device, early Foley removal, minimize central lines to reduce infection
discharge_criteria: Limb perfusion stabilized or improved (successfully revascularized or medically stable) with no progressive ischemia; ischemic rest pain controlled on oral analgesics; infection controlled (afebrile, WBC trending down, on appropriate oral or OPAT antibiotics); wound-care plan established, demonstrated/teachable, with offloading in place; tolerating diet and ambulating at baseline or with a defined PT plan; guideline-directed best medical therapy initiated and tolerated; follow-up (vascular, wound clinic, podiatry, primary care) scheduled and home services arranged
discharge_planning: Disposition: home with home-health wound care, or SNF/acute rehab if deconditioned or post-amputation. Medication reconciliation to guideline-directed therapy: antiplatelet +/- low-dose rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID, high-intensity statin, ACE-I/ARB, SGLT2i/GLP-1 RA, glycemic agents, and OPAT antibiotics if needed. Follow-up: vascular surgery within 1-2 weeks (post-revascularization surveillance duplex), wound clinic, podiatry, primary care; scheduled foot surveillance. Education: smoking cessation, daily foot inspection/foot care, wound care, medication adherence, and red-flag symptoms (recurrent rest pain, new ulcer, cold/pale/painful limb). Structured transitions-of-care handoff, ensure DME/offloading footwear, address transportation/SDOH; refer to supervised exercise therapy for residual claudication once wounds healed
expected_los: GMLOS by DRG: 301 (no CC/MCC) 2.0 d, 300 (CC) 3.1 d, 299 (MCC) 3.9 d (AMLOS 2.5 / 3.9 / 5.5 d respectively); typical medically managed PAD/CLTI admission 2-4 days, extending to 5-7+ days with complex/deep foot infection, staged debridement, ALI reperfusion, or amputation
target_los: 2-3 days for ischemic rest pain / medical optimization or single-session endovascular revascularization (DRG 301); 3-4 days with CC-level infection/comorbidity (DRG 300). Expedited same-admission hemodynamic staging + revascularization decision, day-1 best medical therapy, and coordinated wound/discharge planning keep LOS at or below GMLOS
quality_metrics: SVS Vascular Quality Initiative (VQI) registry (PVI and infrainguinal-bypass modules); outcome measures: amputation-free survival, major (above-ankle) amputation rate, limb salvage and wound-healing rates, major adverse limb events (MALE), 30-day mortality, 30-day all-cause readmission; process/guideline-concordance: antiplatelet + high-intensity statin prescribed at discharge, smoking-cessation counseling documented, LDL and HbA1c at target, time-to-revascularization for ALI/CLTI; SEP-1 sepsis bundle compliance when foot infection meets sepsis criteria; hospital-acquired condition avoidance (CAUTI/pressure injury)
common_complications: Reperfusion injury / compartment syndrome after ALI revascularization (mitigate: serial neurovascular checks, prompt/prophylactic four-compartment fasciotomy, monitor K+/CK); contrast-induced AKI (mitigate: hydration, minimize contrast, CO2 angiography in CKD); arterial access-site complications — hematoma, pseudoaneurysm, retroperitoneal bleed (closure devices, ultrasound-guided access, monitoring); progressive foot infection/osteomyelitis/sepsis (source control, ID, culture-directed antibiotics); bleeding on antithrombotic therapy (dose-appropriate regimens, monitor); failed revascularization / restenosis / graft or stent thrombosis (surveillance duplex, dual-pathway antithrombotics); major amputation (limb-salvage team); cardiac events/MI from polyvascular disease (guideline-directed secondary prevention)
readmission_drivers: Wound non-healing or recurrent foot ulcer/infection (levers: wound clinic, offloading, nutrition, tight glycemic control, podiatry/diabetic-foot program); restenosis, graft failure, or recurrent ischemia (surveillance duplex, dual-pathway antithrombotics, statin adherence); medication non-adherence with antiplatelet/statin discontinuation (education, med reconciliation, affordability); uncontrolled diabetes (endocrine follow-up, foot care); continued smoking (cessation pharmacotherapy + counseling); inadequate follow-up/transitions (scheduled early vascular + wound follow-up, home health); amputation-related and repeat-revascularization complications
guideline_source: 2024 ACC/AHA/AACVPR/APMA/ABC/SCAI/SVM/SVN/SVS/SIR/VESS Guideline for the Management of Lower Extremity Peripheral Artery Disease (2024); Global Vascular Guidelines on the Management of Chronic Limb-Threatening Ischemia (SVS/ESVS/WFVS; Conte & Bradbury, 2019); supportive: ESC/ESVS 2024 Guidelines for peripheral arterial and aortic diseases; IDSA/IWGDF diabetic foot infection guidance

Conte MS, Bradbury AW et al. Global Vascular Guidelines on CLTI. J Vasc Surg 2019;69(6S):3S-125S (PMID 31159978; DOI 10.1016/j.jvs.2019.02.016). VOYAGER PAD — Bonaca MP et al. Rivaroxaban in PAD after Revascularization. N Engl J Med 2020;382(21):1994-2004 (PMID 32222135). BEST-CLI — Farber A et al. Surgery or Endovascular Therapy for CLTI. N Engl J Med 2022;387(25):2305-2316 (PMID 36342173). BASIL-2 — Bradbury AW et al. Vein bypass vs best endovascular treatment for CLTI. Lancet 2023;401(10390):1798-1809 (PMID 37116524). (Citations retrieved/verified via PubMed)
evidence_grade: Strong — Class I, Level A/B recommendations from the 2024 ACC/AHA multisociety guideline (single antiplatelet, low-dose rivaroxaban + aspirin, high-intensity statin, revascularization for CLTI) reinforced by RCTs (VOYAGER PAD, COMPASS). MODERATE for open-vs-endovascular strategy selection given conflicting pivotal RCTs (BEST-CLI favored vein bypass with adequate saphenous vein; BASIL-2 favored best endovascular first) — decision is patient-, conduit-, and anatomy-dependent. Diabetic-foot-infection specifics extrapolated from IDSA/IWGDF consensus (lower certainty)
severity_cc_mcc_notes: This is a MEDICAL DRG triad (no qualifying OR procedure) — when a patient undergoes major open bypass, peripheral endovascular intervention, or amputation the case typically regroups to a surgical/vascular-procedure or amputation DRG (the scope's rank-74 vascular procedures), so this pathway governs the diagnostic, medical-optimization, wound, and infection phase. DRG 301 (no CC/MCC, GMLOS 2.0 d): rest pain / minor tissue loss, medical optimization, floor, short stay. DRG 300 (CC, GMLOS 3.1 d): foot cellulitis/infection, CKD, diabetes complications, localized gangrene — ID/wound involvement, longer stay. DRG 299 (MCC, GMLOS 3.9 d): sepsis, ALI with reperfusion, acute kidney injury, respiratory failure, gangrene with systemic infection, or concurrent MI — higher acuity, ICU/step-down, more complications, higher mortality, longest stay; MCC capture (sepsis, acute renal failure, gangrene) also drives appropriate reimbursement
pathway_pearls: Stage every threatened limb on arrival with SVS WIfI + Rutherford ALI class: Rutherford IIb ALI = revascularize within the ~6h window; Rutherford III (paralysis + sensory loss + inaudible Doppler) = primary amputation — do not delay by chasing imaging. Start dual-pathway antithrombotic (aspirin 81 mg + rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID) plus high-intensity statin on day 1 — proven MACE/MALE reduction (VOYAGER PAD); best medical therapy is as outcome-defining as the procedure. Run a multidisciplinary "toe-and-flow" limb-salvage team (vascular + podiatry + ID + wound + endocrine) with urgent same-admission vascular referral for all CLTI to maximize amputation-free survival. Aggressive glycemic and foot care, smoking cessation, early offloading/wound care, and scheduled early vascular + wound follow-up are the highest-yield levers against recurrent tissue loss and 30-day readmission

admission_criteria: Admit when an acute complication of the neurodegenerative disease threatens airway, breathing, hydration/nutrition, or safety: aspiration pneumonia with hypoxia (SpO2 <92% RA), tachypnea, or sepsis; acute/hypercapnic respiratory failure (esp ALS: pCO2 >45 mmHg with symptoms, orthopnea, inability to clear secretions); severe dysphagia unable to maintain safe PO intake/hydration and needing enteral-access decision; parkinsonism-hyperpyrexia syndrome or disabling akinetic "off" from abrupt dopaminergic withdrawal (fever, rigidity, autonomic instability, CK rise); delirium/behavioral crisis superimposed on dementia with danger not manageable outpatient; fall with injury (fracture, ICH) or recurrent falls with functional collapse; dehydration/AKI/failure-to-thrive; autonomic crisis (urinary retention, ileus, severe orthostasis). ED-discharge/observation appropriate for isolated med refill, mild ED-correctable dehydration, single uncomplicated fall without injury, or stable chronic baseline symptoms.
risk_stratification: Pneumonia: CURB-65 (>=2 admit, >=3 consider ICU) and PSI/PORT (class IV-V admit). ALS respiratory: FVC <50% predicted (drives NIV, flags impending failure), SNIP <40 cmH2O, MIP weaker than -60 cmH2O, daytime pCO2 >45, nocturnal desaturation; ALSFRS-R for trajectory. Delirium: 4AT >=4 (probable delirium) or CAM/CAM-ICU screened q shift. Dysphagia/aspiration: failed bedside swallow screen (Yale/GUSS) -> NPO + SLP; MASA for severity. Frailty: Rockwood Clinical Frailty Scale >=5. Malnutrition: MUST or GLIM. Falls: Morse/Hester Davis. Deterioration/sepsis: NEWS2, qSOFA >=2 / SOFA. Pressure injury: Braden <=18.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with diff (leukocytosis->infection); CMP/BMP (dehydration, AKI, Na/electrolytes); CK (rhabdomyolysis / parkinsonism-hyperpyrexia); CRP +/- procalcitonin and 2 sets blood cultures if febrile/septic; VBG/ABG (ALS: hypercapnia, compensatory HCO3 rise = chronic CO2 retention); UA + culture (UTI is a leading delirium/decompensation trigger); lactate if sepsis suspected (SEP-1); TSH, B12, folate (reversible cognitive/functional contributors); albumin/prealbumin for nutrition; troponin/ECG if syncope/fall; QTc before any antipsychotic. Abnormal results trigger targeted therapy (antibiotics, fluids, electrolyte repletion, dopaminergic reinstatement).
initial_imaging_dx: CXR on arrival (aspiration infiltrate favors dependent/RLL segments). Non-contrast CT head within ~6h if head strike, focal deficit, anticoagulation, or acute unexplained mental-status change (rule out ICH/subdural). SLP bedside swallow evaluation within 24h; instrumental study - videofluoroscopic modified barium swallow (VFSS) or FEES - if aspiration/silent aspiration suspected. ALS: upright and supine spirometry/FVC, overnight pulse oximetry +/- transcutaneous/end-tidal capnography, ABG. ECG (autonomic dysfunction, QTc). Bladder scan for retention. EEG if nonconvulsive seizure suspected in unexplained altered mentation.
time_critical_interventions: PARKINSON - resume/continue home levodopa and dopaminergic drugs ON TIME, every time (within 15-30 min of the patient's home schedule, NOT standard med-pass times); reconcile the exact regimen in the first hours - missed/delayed doses precipitate rigidity, aspiration, falls, and parkinsonism-hyperpyrexia. Hard-stop EMR alert to block contraindicated dopamine-blocking agents (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, promethazine, haloperidol, droperidol). ASPIRATION PNEUMONIA/SEPSIS - empiric antibiotics within guideline window (<=1h in septic shock); SEP-1 3-hour bundle: lactate, cultures before abx, broad-spectrum antibiotic, 30 mL/kg crystalloid if hypotensive or lactate >=4. ALS RESPIRATORY FAILURE - initiate NIV/BiPAP promptly for symptomatic/hypercapnic insufficiency; mechanical insufflation-exsufflation (cough assist) for secretions. Parkinsonism-hyperpyrexia - reinstate dopaminergics immediately, cool, consider dantrolene/bromocriptine.
initial_management: Aspiration pneumonia (ATS/IDSA 2019): non-severe inpatient = beta-lactam (ampicillin-sulbactam 1.5-3 g IV q6h or ceftriaxone) PLUS macrolide (azithromycin), OR respiratory fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin/moxifloxacin) monotherapy; severe = beta-lactam + macrolide (or + fluoroquinolone). Do NOT add routine anaerobic coverage (clindamycin/metronidazole) unless lung abscess or empyema; add MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage only with validated risk factors/prior isolation; duration min 5 days and until afebrile/stable 48-72h. PARKINSON: continue carbidopa/levodopa + agonists/MAO-B/COMT at home dose and timing; if NPO use rotigotine transdermal patch (agonist) titrated by levodopa-equivalent dose, or crushed IR carbidopa/levodopa or enteral levodopa via NG - never abruptly stop; safe antiemetic = ondansetron/trimethobenzamide; PD psychosis = quetiapine/clozapine/pimavanserin. ALS: riluzole 50 mg BID (Level A), edaravone per protocol, secretion control (glycopyrrolate, atropine drops, amitriptyline, suction, cough assist), NIV; opioid/benzodiazepine titration for dyspnea in palliative context. DELIRIUM/DEMENTIA: treat the cause; nonpharmacologic first-line; reserve low-dose antipsychotic only for severe danger (avoid typicals in PD/DLB); avoid benzodiazepines (except withdrawal) and anticholinergics per Beers. Isotonic crystalloid for dehydration; aspiration precautions with HOB >=30 degrees.
day1_milestones: Medication reconciliation capturing the EXACT home PD regimen and clock times, entered as time-critical "do not delay" orders; flag/remove contraindicated dopamine blockers. NPO until swallow screen; SLP consult placed; aspiration precautions and HOB elevation. Start therapy for the acute driver (antibiotics/NIV/fluids). Baseline delirium screen (4AT/CAM) and launch HELP prevention bundle; remove tethers (Foley, unneeded telemetry). Establish code status/surrogate and open advance-care-planning/goals-of-care discussion (esp ALS, advanced dementia). Order VTE prophylaxis; fall precautions; PT/OT and pharmacy consults; identify caregiver and document baseline function.
day2_milestones: Instrumental swallow (VFSS/FEES) if indicated; advance to safest IDDSI texture or decide enteral-nutrition pathway. Reassess respiratory status (ALS: FVC trend, NIV tolerance/titration; wean O2 in pneumonia). De-escalate/streamline antibiotics to culture data; confirm clinical stability. Reassess delirium; taper sedating meds; sustain nonpharmacologic bundle; mobilize out of bed. Audit that every PD dose was delivered on time; maintain NPO route (patch/enteral). Implement dietitian recommendations and a bowel regimen (PD constipation). Engage case management/social work for disposition.
day3plus_milestones: Confirm stability: afebrile, stable or weaning O2 / established NIV settings, optimized motor state, resolving delirium. Finalize nutrition: safe oral diet vs gastrostomy decision (ALS PEG ideally placed before FVC <50% for safety; align with goals of care) - GI/IR consult if elected. Lock in swallow-safe diet and caregiver education on aspiration precautions. Complete palliative/goals-of-care pathway; hospice referral if appropriate (advanced ALS, end-stage dementia). PT/OT determine discharge destination (home + services vs SNF/rehab). Reconcile discharge PD regimen with caregiver trained on timing. Arrange follow-up (neurology/movement or ALS multidisciplinary clinic, PCP), home health, and DME (BiPAP, suction, cough assist, hospital bed).
consults_multidisciplinary: Neurology (movement-disorders for PD; neuromuscular/ALS specialist) early. Speech-language pathology for dysphagia Day 1 (highest yield). PT/OT Day 1 (mobility, falls, function, DME). Dietitian/nutrition Day 1-2. Respiratory therapy Day 1 (ALS NIV, cough assist). Pharmacy Day 1 (levodopa-equivalent dosing, contraindicated-med screen, Beers review). Palliative care / goals-of-care early for ALS and advanced dementia (high yield). Pulmonology (ALS respiratory) and GI/IR (PEG) as needed. Geriatrics or geriatric psychiatry for delirium/behavioral dementia. Case management + social work Day 1-2 (disposition, caregiver support, hospice). Ethics if goals-of-care conflict.
monitoring_level: Floor/med-surg for most (stable pneumonia, medication adjustment, behavioral decompensation, falls without instability). Telemetry for autonomic instability, syncope, QTc concern (antipsychotic use), or parkinsonism-hyperpyrexia. Step-down/ICU for acute hypercapnic respiratory failure needing NIV initiation/titration or failing NIV, aspiration with septic shock/ARDS, parkinsonism-hyperpyrexia syndrome, or airway compromise. Escalation triggers: rising pCO2 or falling FVC, SpO2 <90% despite O2, RR >30, worsening secretions/aspiration, hemodynamic instability, rising NEWS2, declining GCS.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: NPO until swallow screen, then SLP-guided texture-modified diet (IDDSI framework) with aspiration precautions and upright feeding; enteral route (NG then PEG) if PO unsafe/inadequate and consistent with goals. Mobility: early out-of-bed Day 1, PT/OT, structured fall-prevention bundle. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily or UFH 5000 units SC q8-12h) for these high-risk immobile patients; mechanical IPC if bleeding risk/contraindication. Glucose target 140-180 mg/dL. Lines/catheters: avoid/remove Foley early (delirium + CAUTI risk), minimize tethers. Bowel regimen for PD constipation; pressure-injury prevention (reposition, Braden-driven).
discharge_criteria: Acute driver resolved/stabilized: afebrile, stable O2/respiratory status (or established home NIV settings), hemodynamically stable. Safe sustainable nutrition/hydration plan in place (oral diet or functioning enteral access). Delirium resolved or back to cognitive baseline and behavior manageable. PD motor state optimized with a reconciled on-time medication schedule. Adequate symptom/pain control; bowel/bladder addressed. Confirmed disposition with services, a caregiver trained and able, and DME arranged. Follow-up scheduled and goals of care documented.
discharge_planning: Disposition matched to trajectory: home with home health/PT/OT, SNF, LTAC, inpatient rehab, or hospice. Medication reconciliation emphasizing EXACT PD dose times (not just frequency); provide an "Aware in Care"-style medication list; deprescribe Beers/anticholinergic agents; verify no contraindicated dopamine blockers. Follow-up: neurology/movement or ALS multidisciplinary clinic within 1-2 weeks, PCP within 7 days, SLP/nutrition as needed. Education: aspiration precautions, medication timing, fall prevention, NIV/cough-assist use, red flags; caregiver training and respite resources. Transitions: warm handoff to facility/home health; DME (BiPAP, suction, cough assist, hospital bed, commode); hospice enrollment if elected.
expected_los: DRG 057 (without MCC) GMLOS ~3.5-3.8 days; DRG 056 (with MCC) GMLOS ~5-7 days (CMS national ~5.0-5.5); blended benchmark ~4-5 days.
target_los: ~3 days for uncomplicated DRG 057 (early SLP + on-time dopaminergics + prompt disposition), with select behavioral/medication-adjustment admissions dischargeable in 2-3 days; 4-5 days for DRG 056 (MCC: aspiration/respiratory failure) under the optimized pathway.
quality_metrics: PSI-90 composite; falls-with-injury rate; HAC measures (CAUTI, CLABSI, pressure injury). Delirium screening rate; antipsychotic and physical-restraint avoidance. PD on-time medication administration rate and avoidance of contraindicated dopamine antagonists (Parkinson's Foundation quality targets). CAP/pneumonia process measures if aspiration pneumonia is primary; SEP-1 if sepsis. IMM-2 influenza/pneumococcal immunization. Advance-care-planning documentation rate. 30-day all-cause readmission and 30-day mortality; hospital-acquired aspiration/HAP rate.
common_complications: Aspiration pneumonia (often recurrent) - mitigate with early SLP, aspiration precautions, HOB elevation, oral care. Delirium - HELP bundle, avoid Beers meds/tethers. Falls/injury - fall bundle, PT, on-time meds. Parkinsonism-hyperpyrexia / disabling "off" - on-time dopaminergics, never abruptly withdraw. Respiratory failure (ALS) - proactive NIV and cough assist. VTE (prophylaxis), pressure injury (repositioning), CAUTI (Foley avoidance). Malnutrition/dehydration, constipation/ileus, orthostatic hypotension/syncope, and adverse drug events from contraindicated agents.
readmission_drivers: Recurrent aspiration pneumonia and progressive dysphagia; medication mismanagement (PD timing/wrong agents); falls; inadequate home support or caregiver burnout; failure to establish goals of care / late hospice referral driving revolving-door crisis admissions; intrinsic disease progression (ALS respiratory decline, dementia behavioral escalation). Prevention levers: SLP-directed diet + outpatient swallow follow-up, timely PEG decision, early palliative care/hospice, caregiver training, meticulous medication reconciliation, follow-up within 7 days, and home health/DME.
guideline_source: ALS: AAN Practice Parameter update - care of the patient with ALS: drug, nutritional, and respiratory therapies (Miller et al., Neurology 2009), plus FDA-approved riluzole and edaravone. Parkinson (inpatient): Parkinson's Foundation Hospital Care Recommendations (2023-2024) and AAN dopaminergic-therapy guideline (2021). Dementia/delirium: 2023 AGS Beers Criteria; Hospital Elder Life Program (HELP, Inouye); NICE delirium guidance (CG103). Aspiration/CAP: ATS/IDSA Community-Acquired Pneumonia guideline (Metlay et al., 2019).
key_citations: Miller RG et al. Practice parameter update: the care of the patient with ALS - drug, nutritional, and respiratory therapies. Neurology 2009;73(15):1218-26. PMID 19822872 (DOI 10.1212/WNL.0b013e3181bc0141). Metlay JP et al. Diagnosis and Treatment of Adults with Community-acquired Pneumonia (ATS/IDSA). Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2019;200(7):e45-e67. PMID 31573350 (10.1164/rccm.201908-1581ST). Edaravone (MCI-186) ALS 19 Study Group. Safety and efficacy of edaravone in well-defined ALS. Lancet Neurol 2017;16(7):505-12. PMID 28522181 (10.1016/S1474-4422(17)30115-1). Hshieh TT, ... Inouye SK. Hospital Elder Life Program: systematic review and meta-analysis. Am J Geriatr Psychiatry 2018;26(10):1015-33. PMID 30076080 (10.1016/j.jagp.2018.06.007). 2023 AGS Beers Criteria Update Expert Panel. J Am Geriatr Soc 2023. PMID 37139824. Chunga N et al. Effect of a best-practice advisory on contraindicated medications in hospitalized PD. Front Aging Neurosci 2023;15:1276761. PMID 38173555 (10.3389/fnagi.2023.1276761). evidence_grade: Moderate overall. Condition-specific anchors are strong: ALS AAN parameter (riluzole Level A; PEG and NIV Level B), ATS/IDSA CAP guideline (GRADE strong/moderate), and the HELP meta-analysis for delirium prevention. However, the UNIFIED inpatient pathway across this collapsed, heterogeneous DRG is largely consensus/expert-opinion (Parkinson's Foundation hospital-care recommendations, AGS Beers) and is extrapolated across three distinct diseases; no single RCT governs the DRG group, so overall strength is Moderate - mixed Class I/Level A-B society guideline plus consensus.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 056 (with MCC) vs 057 (without MCC). MCCs that drive 056 include aspiration pneumonia with sepsis or respiratory failure, acute respiratory failure (esp ALS), and acute metabolic encephalopathy -> step-down/ICU, NIV, LOS ~5-7 days, more complications and higher mortality. DRG 057 (no MCC) covers medication adjustment, behavioral decompensation, uncomplicated falls, and simple dysphagia -> floor-level, LOS ~3.5 days. The collapsed group spans very different trajectories - ALS respiratory failure (ICU/NIV, palliative), dementia behavioral crisis (delirium bundle, geriatric psych), and PD medication-related decompensation (movement neurology, on-time dosing) - so the pathway must branch by phenotype. Accurate CC/MCC capture (aspiration pneumonia, respiratory failure, dysphagia, malnutrition, encephalopathy) also governs correct DRG assignment.
pathway_pearls: 1) Parkinson meds "on time, every time" plus an EMR hard-stop on contraindicated dopamine blockers (metoclopramide, prochlorperazine, promethazine, haloperidol) - the single highest-yield lever; abrupt withdrawal causes parkinsonism-hyperpyrexia, and a best-practice advisory demonstrably cuts contraindicated administrations. 2) Early SLP dysphagia evaluation with aspiration precautions BEFORE first PO - aspiration pneumonia is the dominant in-stay complication and readmission driver. 3) Proactive delirium prevention with the HELP bundle; avoid Beers meds, restraints, and Foley tethers. 4) Early palliative care / goals-of-care and timely hospice referral - this DRG is progressive and terminal; aligning intensity of care prevents revolving-door admissions.

scope_and_volume_notes: A top-10 Medicare DRG (057). Includes Parkinson disease, dementia decompensation, ALS.

admission_criteria: ADMIT (vs ED discharge/observation) for: complicated diverticulitis on CT (abscess, free perforation/pneumoperitoneum, obstruction, fistula; modified Hinchey Ib-IV); sepsis/SIRS, peritonitis, or hemodynamic instability; inability to tolerate oral intake/hydration; uncontrolled pain; immunocompromise (chronic steroids, biologics, transplant, chemo), frailty, or significant comorbidity; failed outpatient therapy. IBD: acute severe UC by Truelove-Witts (>=6 bloody stools/day PLUS >=1 of temp >37.8C, HR >90, Hgb <10.5 g/dL, ESR >30 mm/h); Crohn flare with obstruction, intra-abdominal abscess, high fever, dehydration, malnutrition/failure to thrive, or refractoriness to outpatient treatment (AGA 2026 inpatient BPA-1). Uncomplicated diverticulitis in a reliable immunocompetent patient tolerating PO -> outpatient/observation.
risk_stratification: Diverticulitis: modified Hinchey/CT stage (Ia phlegmon; Ib pericolic abscess; II pelvic/distant abscess; III purulent, IV feculent peritonitis) drives drainage vs surgery -- abscess >=3-4 cm -> IR percutaneous drainage, <3-4 cm -> IV antibiotics alone. UC: Truelove-Witts defines severe UC and triggers admission + IV steroids; Day-3 Oxford/Travis criteria (>8 stools/day, OR 3-8 stools/day + CRP >45 mg/L) predict ~85% steroid failure -> escalate to rescue therapy; endoscopic Mayo/UCEIS grades mucosal severity. Malnutrition: NRS-2002/MUST. Sepsis in perforated disease: qSOFA/SOFA + lactate.
initial_workup_labs: CBC (leukocytosis, anemia -> transfusion/severity), CMP (electrolytes, renal, glucose), CRP + ESR (severity and the Day-3 UC decision), albumin (low = severe/poor steroid response), lactate + blood cultures if septic, type & screen, coagulation. IBD flare: STOOL C. difficile PCR/toxin (mandatory), stool culture/enteric pathogen panel, fecal calprotectin; pre-biologic screen -> latent TB (IGRA), HBV serologies, and TPMT/NUDT15 before thiopurines. Rising CRP with low albumin and anemia flags severe disease predicting steroid non-response.
initial_imaging_dx: Diverticulitis: contrast-enhanced CT abdomen/pelvis is the standard to confirm diagnosis and stage (Hinchey), especially at first presentation and severe cases (ACG 2026). IBD: upright/supine abdominal X-ray on admission to exclude toxic megacolon (transverse colon >5.5-6 cm) and free air; flexible sigmoidoscopy (unprepped, minimal insufflation) within 24-72h to grade severity, confirm dx, and biopsy for CMV; CT abdomen/pelvis if suspected abscess, perforation, or obstruction. Full colonoscopy deferred in the acute flare (perforation risk); interval colonoscopy 6-8 weeks after complicated diverticulitis to exclude malignancy.
time_critical_interventions: Septic/perforated diverticulitis: Surviving Sepsis 1-hour bundle -> lactate, blood cultures, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics <=1h, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4 mmol/L; urgent surgery for Hinchey III/IV or free perforation. ASUC: IV corticosteroids started on admission (Day 0); structured response assessment at 72h (Oxford/Travis) -> non-responders get rescue therapy within ~Day 3-5 with same-admission surgical consult; do not exceed 5-7 days of failing steroids before a colectomy decision. VTE pharmacologic prophylaxis ordered within 24h of admission.
initial_management: Complicated/septic diverticulitis: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics covering gram-negatives + anaerobes (ceftriaxone + metronidazole, OR piperacillin-tazobactam, OR ciprofloxacin + metronidazole), IV crystalloid, bowel rest/clear liquids, analgesia (avoid NSAIDs, minimize opioids); source control = IR percutaneous drainage for abscess >=3-4 cm. Uncomplicated immunocompetent diverticulitis: supportive care; antibiotics may be OMITTED (DIABOLO/AVOD RCTs). ASUC: IV methylprednisolone 60 mg/day (or hydrocortisone 100 mg q6h) per AGA BPA-7; steroid-refractory at 72h -> rescue infliximab 5 mg/kg (consider accelerated dosing) OR IV cyclosporine 2 mg/kg/day (equivalent per CONSTRUCT); treat C. difficile (oral vancomycin) and CMV (ganciclovir) if present; transfuse RBC only for Hgb <7 g/dL (restrictive). AVOID antimotility/anticholinergic/opioid agents (toxic megacolon risk).
day1_milestones: Confirm diagnosis/stage (CT or sigmoidoscopy); risk-stratify (Hinchey or Truelove-Witts); start IV steroids (ASUC) or IV antibiotics +/- drainage (complicated diverticulitis); send C. difficile + infection screen; order VTE prophylaxis; secure IV access, fluids and electrolyte repletion; baseline CRP/albumin/CBC; GI consult Day 0, plus surgical consult for ASUC or complicated/refractory diverticulitis; set diet (bowel rest vs clear liquids vs continue nutrition); document baseline stool frequency, bleeding, and abdominal exam.
day2_milestones: Reassess trajectory (stool frequency/blood, fever curve, HR, abdominal exam, urine output); trend CBC/CMP/CRP; continue IV steroids/antibiotics; repeat abdominal X-ray if any toxic-megacolon concern; ensure C. diff/CMV results are actioned; nutrition assessment; advance diet if diverticulitis improving; verify pre-biologic screen (TB/HBV) has resulted so rescue therapy is not delayed; reinforce surgical co-management readiness for ASUC.
day3plus_milestones: ASUC Day 3: apply Oxford/Travis criteria -> responders continue steroids and plan oral taper + maintenance/biologic bridge; non-responders start rescue therapy (infliximab/cyclosporine) with a defined 3-7 day endpoint -> colectomy if no response. Diverticulitis: transition IV->oral antibiotics once afebrile and tolerating diet, confirm abscess drained/downtrending, de-escalate monitoring. Achieve stable treatment response, oral intake, and pain control; build the discharge transition plan (AGA BPA-13); schedule interval colonoscopy for complicated diverticulitis.
consults_multidisciplinary: Gastroenterology (Day 0, essential); colorectal surgery EARLY -- at admission for ASUC (AGA BPA-6) and for complicated/refractory diverticulitis; interventional radiology for percutaneous abscess drainage; clinical pharmacy (biologic dosing, TB/HBV/TPMT-NUDT15 screening, steroid and insulin management); registered dietitian/nutrition (malnutrition screen); enterostomal/WOC nurse if colectomy/ostomy likely; care management + social work for transitions, adherence, and infusion-center access; ID if CMV/C. difficile or immunocompromise; PT/OT for deconditioned or elderly patients.
monitoring_level: Most patients: medical floor with vital-sign trending, +/- telemetry. Step-down/ICU for: septic shock or vasopressor need, toxic megacolon, perforation/peritonitis, significant GI bleeding requiring transfusion, or hemodynamic/respiratory instability. Escalation triggers: rising HR/fever with worsening abdominal distension, rising lactate, colonic dilation >6 cm on abdominal X-ray, falling hemoglobin, or new peritoneal signs -> urgent imaging/surgery and higher level of care.
nutrition_mobility_vte: VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH enoxaparin, or UFH) for ALL hospitalized IBD and diverticulitis patients -- rectal bleeding is NOT a contraindication (AGA BPA-5); add mechanical prophylaxis if active bleeding truly precludes drugs. Nutrition: continue oral/enteral nutrition when tolerated; bowel rest/clear liquids for complicated diverticulitis or impending surgery, advance as tolerated; dietitian for malnutrition, avoid routine TPN unless obstruction/prolonged NPO. Mobility: early ambulation. Glucose: target <180 mg/dL, treat steroid-induced hyperglycemia with insulin. Remove IV lines/urinary catheters early. AVOID opioids/antimotility agents.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable and afebrile >=24h; pain controlled on oral analgesia; tolerating oral diet and hydration; stable/downtrending stool frequency and bleeding (IBD) or resolving abdominal signs (diverticulitis); transitioned to oral antibiotics (diverticulitis) or an oral steroid taper +/- initiated maintenance therapy (IBD); no untreated complication (abscess drained, no toxic megacolon); stable/improving labs (CRP downtrending, Hgb stable); a clear treatment response established before discharge (AGA BPA-13).
discharge_planning: Medication reconciliation with an explicit steroid taper schedule and initiated/scheduled maintenance or biologic bridge (IBD); defined oral antibiotic course (if indicated) for diverticulitis; GI follow-up within 1-2 weeks; interval colonoscopy 6-8 weeks after complicated diverticulitis (or uncomplicated with alarm features / overdue CRC screening); surgical follow-up and elective-resection discussion for recurrent diverticulitis or ASUC; patient education (flare/red-flag warning signs, when to return, diet -- no need to avoid nuts/seeds/popcorn per ACG 2026, smoking cessation, limit NSAIDs); infusion-center coordination; primary-care handoff and adherence support.
expected_los: Approx 4-5 days typical. MS-DRG GMLOS benchmarks: 385 IBD w/ MCC ~5.3-5.8 d; 386 IBD w/ CC ~3.9-4.0 d; 387 IBD w/o CC/MCC ~2.9-3.0 d (note: 385-387 are formally the IBD triplet; medically managed diverticulitis usually groups to MS-DRG 371-373/391-392 but is bundled here operationally). Uncomplicated admitted diverticulitis ~3-4 d; complicated (abscess + drainage) ~5-7 d; ASUC ~5-7 d, longer with rescue therapy or colectomy.
target_los: 3-4 days for an uncomplicated IBD flare or medically managed uncomplicated diverticulitis under an optimized pathway; ~5 days for ASUC with a disciplined Day-3 decision point (steroid response -> taper, or prompt rescue/colectomy) that avoids drift; ~5-6 days for complicated diverticulitis with early IR drainage.
quality_metrics: VTE prophylaxis administration rate in hospitalized IBD (AGA BPA-5); C. difficile testing on every IBD-flare admission; documented 72h steroid-response assessment and time-to-rescue / time-to-surgical-consult in ASUC; antibiotic stewardship / de-implementation in uncomplicated diverticulitis (DIABOLO/AVOD); SEP-1 sepsis bundle compliance for perforated/septic cases; interval colonoscopy completion after complicated diverticulitis; 30-day all-cause readmission (IBD ~15-25%); steroid-free remission / steroid-sparing rate; nutrition screening; HCAHPS.
common_complications: Diverticulitis: abscess, free perforation/peritonitis, obstruction, colovesical/enteric fistula, sepsis, recurrence. IBD: toxic megacolon, perforation, VTE, C. difficile or CMV superinfection, GI hemorrhage/anemia, malnutrition, steroid hyperglycemia and opportunistic infection. Mitigation: mandatory VTE prophylaxis; universal C. diff (and CMV if refractory) testing; avoid opioids/antimotility agents; serial abdominal exam + abdominal X-ray for megacolon; hard 72h response checkpoint with early rescue/surgery; source control (IR drainage) for abscess.
readmission_drivers: IBD: discharge before a stable response, failed or absent steroid taper plan, no maintenance/biologic bridge, missed early GI follow-up, medication nonadherence, ongoing narcotic use, undertreated C. difficile. Diverticulitis: recurrent episodes, inadequately drained abscess / incomplete source control, missed underlying malignancy. Prevention levers: ensure stable response pre-discharge (BPA-13), clear transition plan, GI follow-up in 1-2 weeks, interval colonoscopy after complicated diverticulitis, maintenance/biologic initiated before discharge, patient education, opioid avoidance.
guideline_source: AGA Clinical Practice Update on Inpatient Management of Adults with IBD: Expert Review (2026); ACG Clinical Guideline Update: Ulcerative Colitis in Adults (2025); ACG Clinical Guideline: Colonic Diverticulitis (2026); ACG Management of Crohn's Disease in Adults (2018) and AGA Management of Moderate-to-Severe Ulcerative Colitis (2020); ACP Diagnosis & Management of Acute Left-Sided Colonic Diverticulitis (2022); ASCRS Treatment of Left-Sided Colonic Diverticulitis (2020).
