evidence_grade: Strong for core levers -- ACG UC (2025) and ACG Colonic Diverticulitis (2026) are GRADE-based Class I society guidelines, with RCT support for IV steroids, infliximab/cyclosporine rescue (Jarnerot 2005, CONSTRUCT 2016), and antibiotic omission in uncomplicated diverticulitis (DIABOLO/AVOD). Moderate / Best-Practice-Advice for inpatient specifics (the AGA 2026 inpatient IBD update is expert consensus, explicitly unrated for evidence quality). Day-3 Oxford/Travis thresholds are validated but predictive performance is attenuated in the biologic era.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MS-DRG 387 (no CC/MCC): uncomplicated flare or diverticulitis, floor care, LOS ~3 d. 386 (CC): anemia, dehydration, hypokalemia, or hypoalbuminemia -> ~4 d with closer monitoring. 385 (MCC): toxic megacolon, perforation, sepsis, acute blood-loss anemia requiring transfusion, or need for rescue therapy/colectomy -> ICU/step-down, LOS ~5.5-6+ d, higher readmission and mortality. The MCC tier drives early surgical involvement, a higher monitoring level, and the rescue-therapy/operative pathways; surgical bowel resection cases divert to the resection pathway (rank 35).
pathway_pearls: 1) ASUC discipline: IV steroids Day 0, a hard 72h Oxford/Travis checkpoint (>8 stools/day, or 3-8 + CRP >45 mg/L), with rescue (infliximab/cyclosporine) AND surgery teed up early -- never let steroid non-responders drift past Day 5-7. 2) VTE pharmacologic prophylaxis for EVERY hospitalized IBD patient despite bloody stool -- the highest-yield, most-missed safety lever (AGA BPA-5). 3) Diverticulitis stewardship: skip antibiotics in uncomplicated immunocompetent disease; admit + IV antibiotics + IR drainage (abscess >=3-4 cm) for complicated disease; interval colonoscopy after complicated episodes. 4) Always test C. difficile (and CMV if steroid-refractory) and avoid opioids/antimotility agents that precipitate toxic megacolon.

admission_criteria: HIGH-RISK febrile neutropenia is the dominant admit trigger: fever (single oral temp >=38.3 C / 101 F, OR >=38.0 C / 100.4 F sustained >=1h) WITH ANC <500/uL or expected to fall <500 within 48h - mandatory inpatient IV antibiotics; New/suspected acute leukemia (circulating blasts, unexplained pancytopenia, hyperleukocytosis) for diagnosis/induction; Oncologic emergencies - tumor lysis syndrome (TLS), hyperleukocytosis/leukostasis (WBC >100,000 or symptomatic), suspected acute promyelocytic leukemia (APL)/DIC, cord compression, SVC syndrome, hypercalcemia; Severe symptomatic cytopenias/bleeding needing transfusion support; Sepsis/hemodynamic instability or organ dysfunction; Inability to tolerate PO/dehydration; Inpatient-mandatory regimens (Burkitt DA-EPOCH-R or CODOX-M/IVAC, high-dose methotrexate, CAR-T monitoring); ED discharge/observation reserved for LOW-risk FN per ASCO/IDSA 2018 (MASCC >=21, CISNE 0, clinically stable, reliable, tolerating oral)
risk_stratification: MASCC Risk Index (febrile neutropenia): >=21 = low risk (candidate for oral/outpatient), <21 = high risk -> inpatient IV empiric therapy; CISNE score (for clinically STABLE FN): 0 = low, 1-2 = intermediate, >=3 = high risk (drives level of care/oral vs IV); Cairo-Bishop / Cairo-Coiffier TLS risk tier (low/intermediate/high per histology, bulk, WBC, baseline uric acid/renal function) -> intensity of prophylaxis; qSOFA >=2 and SOFA trend + lactate for sepsis escalation; ELN 2022 genetic risk (favorable/intermediate/adverse by NPM1, FLT3-ITD, TP53, karyotype) drives consolidation/transplant; IPI / R-IPI and Ann Arbor stage for lymphoma prognosis; ECOG performance status for treatment intensity
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential + manual smear review for blasts/schistocytes, reticulocyte count; CMP with K, phosphorus, calcium, uric acid, creatinine, LDH = TLS panel (repeat q4-6h if intermediate/high TLS risk); Coags PT/aPTT/INR + fibrinogen + D-dimer -> screen DIC/APL; 2 sets blood cultures (peripheral + each central lumen) BEFORE antibiotics, UA/urine culture, lactate; Type & screen/crossmatch; G6PD level before rasburicase; Peripheral flow cytometry + bone marrow aspirate/biopsy with cytogenetics/FISH/molecular (NPM1, FLT3-ITD, IDH1/2, CEBPA for AML; BCR-ABL1 for ALL; MYC/BCL2/BCL6 for aggressive lymphoma); HBV/HCV/HIV/CMV serologies before chemo; beta-2 microglobulin (lymphoma)
initial_imaging_dx: CXR on admission (infiltrate/leukostasis); CT chest/sinus/abdomen-pelvis for infection source or persistent fever >=4-7 days -> invasive fungal workup (serum galactomannan, 1,3-beta-D-glucan); Baseline transthoracic echocardiogram for LVEF before anthracycline; Diagnostic bone marrow biopsy/aspirate; Lumbar puncture with CSF cytology/flow for CNS staging (ALL, Burkitt, aggressive/CNS-risk lymphoma) once platelets/coags corrected; PET-CT for lymphoma staging (often outpatient); Doppler US for line-associated thrombosis if suspected
time_critical_interventions: Empiric broad-spectrum antipseudomonal antibiotic within 60 MINUTES of triage/presentation for febrile neutropenia - do NOT await ANC or culture results (door-to-antibiotic <=60 min); Septic shock -> Surviving Sepsis 1-hour bundle (lactate, cultures, antibiotics, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid, vasopressors to MAP >=65) and CMS SEP-1; Suspected APL -> start ATRA (all-trans retinoic acid) IMMEDIATELY on morphologic/coagulopathic suspicion, before genetic confirmation, and aggressively correct coagulopathy; High-risk TLS -> rasburicase + aggressive IV hydration begun at admission with q4-6h labs; Symptomatic leukostasis -> same-day cytoreduction (hydroxyurea/induction) +/- leukapheresis
initial_management: Empiric antipseudomonal beta-lactam MONOTHERAPY: cefepime 2g IV q8h, OR piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5g q6-8h, OR carbapenem (meropenem 1g q8h); ADD vancomycin only for hemodynamic instability, pneumonia, catheter/skin-soft-tissue infection, severe mucositis, or MRSA colonization; adjust for local resistance/prior cultures; Empiric antifungal (echinocandin - caspofungin/micafungin - or lipid amphotericin B / voriconazole) for persistent fever >=4-7 days in prolonged neutropenia; Antivirals (acyclovir) if HSV/VZV seropositive; TMP-SMX PJP prophylaxis (ALL/steroid-heavy); TLS: isotonic IV fluids 2.5-3 L/m2/day, rasburicase 0.2 mg/kg (avoid concurrent allopurinol), no K/Ca/phosphate in fluids, treat hyperkalemia; Restrictive transfusion (AABB 2023): RBC for Hgb <7 g/dL, prophylactic platelets for <10,000/uL (<20,000 if fever/sepsis, <50,000 for procedures/active bleeding), cryoprecipitate/FFP to keep fibrinogen >100-150 mg/dL in DIC/APL; Hydroxyurea 50-100 mg/kg/day for hyperleukocytosis; disease-specific therapy per NCCN/ELN (7+3, venetoclax+azacitidine, R-CHOP/Pola-R-CHP, ATRA+arsenic trioxide for APL)
day1_milestones: Confirm risk stratification (MASCC/CISNE) and TLS tier; empiric antibiotic delivered <=60 min with 2 culture sets drawn first; Establish/secure central venous access; start IV hydration + TLS prophylaxis; begin q4-6h TLS labs if high-risk; Hematology/Oncology consult; initiate diagnostic bone marrow; Rule in/out leukostasis, APL/DIC, and organ dysfunction; correct severe coagulopathy/cytopenias to thresholds; Baseline echocardiogram before anthracycline; establish goals of care/code status and reproductive/fertility counseling before gonadotoxic therapy
day2_milestones: Reassess cultures/sensitivities at 48h -> de-escalate or broaden antibiotics per stewardship; continue trending TLS labs (target downtrending uric acid, normalizing K/phosphate, stable creatinine); Finalize diagnosis pending flow/cytogenetics; lock treatment plan (induction 7+3, ven-aza, R-CHOP/Pola-R-CHP, ATRA/ATO for APL); Start definitive therapy when metabolically stable; continue transfusion support to thresholds; Antifungal reassessment if fever persists; monitor mucositis, glucose (steroid-induced), differentiation syndrome (APL)
day3plus_milestones: Daily source-control and antimicrobial review; add empiric antifungal + CT chest/sinus if fever persists >=4-7 days; Monitor count nadir/recovery and manage complications (typhlitis/neutropenic enterocolitis, differentiation syndrome, sepsis, VOD); For induction admissions - full supportive care through the 3-4 week nadir to count recovery; For FN/supportive admissions - transition to oral antibiotics and plan discharge once afebrile 24-48h and ANC >500/uL and rising; Integrate molecular/MRD results to finalize risk-adapted therapy and HSCT/CAR-T referral
consults_multidisciplinary: Hematology/Oncology - immediate, primary; Infectious Disease for complicated/persistent/fungal infection; Critical Care for shock or leukostasis with respiratory failure; Nephrology for TLS/AKI/renal replacement; Apheresis/Transfusion Medicine for leukapheresis and product support; Palliative Care early for symptom control and goals of care; Clinical Pharmacy for chemotherapy verification, renal dosing, antimicrobial stewardship; Nutrition for mucositis/weight loss (+/- TPN); PT/OT for deconditioning on prolonged stays; Social Work/Case Management for long LOS, financial toxicity, transplant coordination; Fertility/Reproductive counseling and Dental before cytotoxic therapy; Psycho-oncology
monitoring_level: Floor/med-surg heme-onc unit with neutropenic precautions and protective isolation for stable patients; Telemetry for TLS/hyperkalemia/arrhythmia risk and anthracycline monitoring; Step-down/ICU for septic shock requiring vasopressors, symptomatic leukostasis with hypoxemic respiratory failure, severe TLS with AKI/RRT, DIC with active bleeding, or CAR-T grade >=3 CRS/ICANS (ASTCT grading); Escalate on qSOFA >=2, MAP <65, rising lactate, new hypoxia, altered mental status, or any new organ dysfunction
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet - safe food-handling guidance (strict "neutropenic diet" not evidence-supported); aggressive oral care/mucositis protocol; nutrition consult; consider TPN for severe mucositis/enterocolitis; Mobility - early ambulation and PT to counter deconditioning on prolonged stays; VTE - pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH) when platelets >50,000/uL and no active bleeding, mechanical SCDs when thrombocytopenic/contraindicated (malignancy is prothrombotic); Glucose target 140-180 mg/dL for steroid-induced hyperglycemia; Lines - strict CLABSI insertion/maintenance bundle, daily line-necessity review, remove for tunnel infection or persistent candidemia
discharge_criteria: Afebrile >=24-48h with negative or source-controlled cultures and a defined antibiotic course/plan; ANC recovering (>500/uL and rising) OR robust outpatient support for continued neutropenia; Hemodynamically stable with organ dysfunction resolved/stable; TLS resolved (normalized uric acid/K/phosphate, stable creatinine); Adequate oral intake and controlled pain/nausea/mucositis; Cytopenias stable or with an outpatient transfusion plan; Reliable follow-up, urgent-care access, and caregiver/education in place
discharge_planning: Disposition home with outpatient oncology/infusion or home health (SNF rarely, mainly deconditioned elderly); Medication reconciliation including oral chemo/TKIs and prophylactic antimicrobials (levofloxacin, posaconazole, acyclovir, TMP-SMX), antiemetics, and growth factors (pegfilgrastim if indicated); Schedule oncology follow-up and labs within 48-72h with next-cycle chemo and transfusion plan; Neutropenic and FEVER return-precaution education (call/return for temp >=38.0-38.3 C) and central-line care teaching; Warm handoff/transitions-of-care communication to outpatient team; transplant/CAR-T referral coordination; pharmacy and financial-assistance enrollment
expected_los: Heterogeneous collapsed group. Lymphoma & non-acute leukemia and neutropenic-fever admissions (DRG 840-842): CMS GMLOS approx 3-7 days (with MCC ~6-7, with CC ~4-5, without CC/MCC ~3). De novo ACUTE-LEUKEMIA induction admissions (DRG 834-837): 25-40 days (biologically fixed by count recovery). Major-OR-procedure DRGs 820-825 add procedural days. Practical blended benchmark ~5-7 days for the FN/lymphoma-predominant cohort that drives volume
target_los: Febrile-neutropenia/supportive admissions: 4-5 days with pathway (antibiotics <=60 min, 48h de-escalation, early oral switch and outpatient transition for low-risk MASCC >=21/CISNE 0); Acute-leukemia induction: ~25-30 days (target is eliminating avoidable excess days via infection prevention and discharge promptly at count recovery, not compressing the biological nadir)
quality_metrics: CMS SEP-1 sepsis bundle when septic/septic shock; time-to-first-antibiotic <=60 min in febrile neutropenia (institutional/QOPI registry); CLABSI rate and C. difficile rate; antimicrobial de-escalation/stewardship compliance; 30-day all-cause readmission and ED revisit; VTE prophylaxis adherence; IMM-2 influenza vaccination; ASCO QOPI chemotherapy-safety and documentation-of-risk-stratification measures; palliative care/goals-of-care documentation and avoidance of systemic chemo in last 14 days of life (end-of-life measures)
common_complications: Neutropenic sepsis/septic shock (mitigated by <=60-min empiric antibiotics + source control); tumor lysis syndrome/AKI (risk-based rasburicase + hydration + q4-6h labs); invasive fungal infection (posaconazole prophylaxis; empiric antifungal + CT/galactomannan for persistent fever); mucositis and typhlitis/neutropenic enterocolitis (oral care, bowel rest + broad-spectrum antibiotics); DIC/hemorrhage especially APL (early ATRA + aggressive product support to fibrinogen/platelet targets); differentiation syndrome from APL/IDH inhibitors (dexamethasone); anthracycline cardiotoxicity (baseline echo); transfusion reactions, delirium, VTE; CAR-T CRS/ICANS (tocilizumab and corticosteroids per ASTCT)
readmission_drivers: Recurrent febrile neutropenia/infection and central-line infections; treatment-related cytopenias requiring transfusion; dehydration, nausea, and mucositis limiting oral intake; disease progression; inadequate outpatient support or delayed follow-up. Prevention levers: antimicrobial + growth-factor prophylaxis, explicit fever return precautions, oncology follow-up within 48-72h, home infusion/outpatient antibiotics, standing antiemetic and oral-care regimens, medication reconciliation, and caregiver education
guideline_source: IDSA 2010 (Freifeld) and ASCO/IDSA 2018 (Taplitz) febrile neutropenia guidelines; NCCN Prevention and Treatment of Cancer-Related Infections plus disease-specific NCCN Guidelines (AML, ALL, B-cell/T-cell NHL, Hodgkin) v2024-2025; ELN 2022 (Dohner) AML diagnosis/management; Cairo-Coiffier 2010 TLS risk/prophylaxis consensus; AABB 2023 (Carson) Red Blood Cell Transfusion guidelines; Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 / CMS SEP-1; ASTCT 2019 (Lee) CRS/ICANS consensus grading for CAR-T

evidence_grade: Strong for the supportive-care backbone - Class I / Category 1 society guidelines with RCT support (empiric antibiotics <=60 min and risk-based therapy per IDSA/ASCO; restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 and platelets <10,000 per AABB 2023; risk-based TLS prophylaxis/rasburicase; ELN 2022 genetic risk-adapted AML therapy). Moderate for MASCC/CISNE risk tools and for leukapheresis in leukostasis (observational/consensus, no survival benefit shown). LOS targets and disease-specific sequencing across this collapsed multi-disease DRG group are partly extrapolated/consensus - graded lower accordingly
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MCC-tier drivers (sepsis/septic shock, acute respiratory failure, AKI needing dialysis, DIC, TLS with AKI) escalate to ICU, lengthen LOS, and raise mortality - these populate the with-MCC DRGs (834, 840). CC-tier (electrolyte disturbance, dehydration, symptomatic anemia) sits intermediate (835, 841). Without CC/MCC (836, 842) are typically short, uncomplicated FN or staging admissions at ~3-day GMLOS. De novo acute-leukemia induction is inherently long and complex regardless of coded tier; the with-major-OR-procedure DRGs (820-825, e.g., HSCT-associated or diagnostic/port procedures) add procedural LOS, cost, and complication risk on top of the medical pathway
pathway_pearls: 1) Febrile neutropenia is a true emergency - deliver an antipseudomonal beta-lactam within 60 minutes of arrival, before ANC or cultures result. 2) Suspect APL and give ATRA IMMEDIATELY on morphologic/coagulopathic suspicion (before genetic confirmation) while aggressively supporting the coagulopathy - the single highest-yield early-mortality lever. 3) Stratify TLS up front (Cairo-Coiffier); for high-risk histology/high WBC (Burkitt, ALL, AML) give rasburicase (check G6PD first) with aggressive hydration and q4-6h metabolic labs. 4) Right-size care and resource use to validated risk (MASCC >=21 / CISNE 0 = low risk eligible for step-down, oral switch, early discharge) but treat profound/prolonged neutropenia as high-risk inpatient by default, and hold to restrictive transfusion thresholds (Hgb <7, platelets <10,000)

admission_criteria: INPATIENT/ICU admission = hypertensive EMERGENCY: severe HTN (usually >180/120 mmHg) WITH acute/progressive target-organ damage (TOD) — hypertensive encephalopathy, acute ischemic stroke/ICH/SAH, ACS/acute MI, acute HF/flash pulmonary edema, acute aortic dissection, AKI, malignant retinopathy (grade III-IV/papilledema), thrombotic microangiopathy/MAHA, eclampsia/severe preeclampsia, or sympathetic/catecholamine crisis; floor/step-down if TOD is mild or resolving. ED DISCHARGE/OBSERVATION for hypertensive URGENCY = BP >180/120 WITHOUT acute TOD: treat with oral agents, confirm a downward trend, arrange <=7-day follow-up — do NOT admit asymptomatic severe HTN (low-value care).
risk_stratification: Primary triage is TOD present (emergency -> ICU) vs absent (urgency -> outpatient); no single unifying score. TOD-specific tools: Keith-Wagener-Barker retinopathy grade III-IV = emergency; NIHSS for ischemic-stroke severity/thrombolysis decision; ICH Score for hemorrhage prognosis; Aortic Dissection Detection Risk Score (ADD-RS >=1 -> CTA) for suspected dissection; HEART score for concurrent chest pain/ACS; peripheral-smear schistocytes + LDH/haptoglobin for TMA. eGFR and troponin quantify renal/cardiac injury and drive escalation.
initial_workup_labs: CMP (creatinine/eGFR, K, Na -> AKI, secondary causes); CBC with peripheral smear (schistocytes -> TMA/MAHA); troponin (myocardial injury); BNP/NT-proBNP (HF); LDH, haptoglobin, bilirubin (hemolysis); UA + urine protein:creatinine ratio (proteinuria, hematuria, RBC casts); 12-lead ECG (LVH, ischemia); beta-hCG in reproductive-age women; urine toxicology (cocaine/amphetamine). Secondary-HTN screen (plasma metanephrines, aldosterone:renin, TSH) usually deferred to outpatient. Abnormal Cr/troponin/smear -> upgrade to emergency + ICU.
initial_imaging_dx: 12-lead ECG immediately (<10 min if chest pain); CXR (widened mediastinum, pulmonary edema); STAT non-contrast CT head (door-to-CT <25 min) for any neuro symptom to exclude ICH/stroke; STAT CT angiography chest/abdomen/pelvis if dissection suspected (tearing pain, pulse/BP differential, wide mediastinum); TTE for suspected HF, ischemia, or aortic-root involvement; dilated fundoscopy for hemorrhages/exudates/papilledema.
time_critical_interventions: General emergency: lower MAP by no more than 10-20% in the first hour, then a further 5-15% over the next 23 h (target ~160/100-110 by 2-6 h) via IV titratable infusion + intra-arterial line; avoid overcorrection (watershed ischemia/AKI). EXCEPTIONS: aortic dissection -> SBP <120 mmHg AND HR <60 within 20 min (IV beta-blocker FIRST, then vasodilator); acute ischemic stroke -> <185/110 before IV thrombolysis and <180/105 for 24 h after, otherwise permissive HTN treating only if >220/120; ICH -> acute SBP to ~140 (target 130-150, avoid <130); SAH -> SBP <160 until aneurysm secured; eclampsia -> <160/110 plus magnesium sulfate.
initial_management: IV titratable first-line: nicardipine (5-15 mg/h) or clevidipine (1-2 mg/h, titrate) — dihydropyridine CCBs for most emergencies; labetalol (alpha/beta; 20 mg bolus then infusion) incl stroke/pregnancy; esmolol (beta) for dissection/perioperative; IV nitroglycerin for ACS/pulmonary edema; sodium nitroprusside RESERVE (cyanide risk; dissection-with-beta-blocker or refractory); hydralazine for eclampsia; fenoldopam (D1 agonist) with renal impairment; phentolamine or benzodiazepines for catecholamine/stimulant crisis (NEVER unopposed beta-blockade); enalaprilat for acute LV failure. URGENCY: restart/intensify long-acting ORAL agents (amlodipine, ACEi/ARB, chlorthalidone, labetalol) — AVOID short-acting sublingual nifedipine. Transition IV->oral once stable ~24 h.
day1_milestones: Classify emergency vs urgency; ICU admit + arterial line for emergencies; start the appropriate IV titratable agent and hit the organ-specific staged BP target; identify and treat the specific end-organ injury; medication reconciliation to uncover nonadherence (the leading precipitant); screen precipitants (missed meds, stimulants, NSAIDs, decongestants, OSA, clonidine/beta-blocker withdrawal); trend baseline TOD labs.
day2_milestones: Confirm end-organ recovery (down-trending creatinine/troponin, stable/improving neuro exam); wean the IV infusion and transition to an oral regimen; de-escalate ICU -> telemetry/floor once off the titratable drip; begin structured adherence and home-BP-monitoring education; dietitian for DASH/sodium counseling.
day3plus_milestones: BP stable on the oral regimen for >=24 h; end-organ dysfunction stable or resolving; ambulating without symptoms; secondary-HTN workup arranged as outpatient; finalize a simplified regimen (single-pill combination); confirm medication access/affordability; schedule <=7-day follow-up; complete education and discharge.
consults_multidisciplinary: Nephrology (AKI, TMA, refractory/secondary HTN); Cardiology (ACS, HF, dissection medical management); Neurology/Stroke (ischemic stroke, ICH); Cardiothoracic/Vascular surgery (aortic dissection, emergent); OB (eclampsia/severe preeclampsia); Ophthalmology (malignant retinopathy); Endocrinology if pheochromocytoma/primary aldosteronism suspected. Pharmacy Day 1 (regimen + adherence); Dietitian (DASH, sodium <1500 mg/day); Care management/Social work early (medication affordability/access — key readmission lever).
monitoring_level: ICU with continuous intra-arterial BP monitoring for all true emergencies on IV titratable infusions; step-down/telemetry as the infusion is weaned; floor once stable on oral therapy. Escalation triggers: recurrent SBP >180 or symptomatic rebound, new/worsening end-organ signs (neuro deterioration, chest pain, rising creatinine, falling urine output, hypoxia).
nutrition_mobility_vte: DASH dietary pattern, sodium <1500 mg/day (or >=1 g/day reduction), alcohol moderation; early mobilization once hemodynamically stable; VTE prophylaxis per risk — pharmacologic unless hemorrhagic contraindication (in ICH use mechanical first, add pharmacologic after 24-48 h if stable); glucose control; remove arterial and central lines promptly once off IV infusions to limit line infection.
discharge_criteria: BP controlled and stable on an oral regimen for >=24 h; no active/progressing end-organ injury (creatinine, troponin, neuro exam stable or improving); precipitant identified and addressed; patient able to obtain and self-administer medications; documented follow-up within 7 days.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home; medication reconciliation favoring single-pill combinations to cut complexity; verify affordability/access (dominant readmission lever); provide a validated home BP monitor with teach-back + BP log; schedule PCP/hypertension-clinic follow-up within 7 days; teach red-flag symptoms; address the root cause of nonadherence; arrange outpatient secondary-HTN evaluation when indicated; warn against abrupt discontinuation (rebound).
expected_los: CMS MS-DRG benchmark (approx, IPPS Table 5): DRG 305 Hypertension without MCC GMLOS ~2.2-2.4 days; DRG 304 Hypertension with MCC GMLOS ~3.5-3.9 days.
target_los: Urgency: <24 h / observation or ED discharge (avoid inpatient admission). Emergency without MCC: 2-3 days with early ICU->floor de-escalation. Emergency with MCC: 3-4 days, driven by the qualifying end-organ complication.
quality_metrics: Controlling High Blood Pressure (HEDIS/CMS CBP <140/90; NQF 0018); 30-day all-cause readmission and 72-h ED revisit; appropriate-ICU-utilization and avoidance of admitting asymptomatic urgency (low-value-care metric); door-to-CT <25 min when a stroke component is present; medication-reconciliation completion; documented <=7-day follow-up; avoidance of iatrogenic overly rapid BP reduction; GWTG-Stroke registry measures if stroke/ICH present.
common_complications: Iatrogenic hypotension causing watershed ischemic stroke or AKI from over-rapid lowering (mitigate: staged MAP reduction, arterial line, no sublingual short-acting nifedipine); rebound HTN from abrupt med cessation, esp clonidine/beta-blockers (taper/continue); progression of the index end-organ injury; cyanide/thiocyanate toxicity with prolonged nitroprusside (limit duration/dose, monitor, prefer nicardipine/clevidipine); reflex tachycardia from pure vasodilators (pair with a beta-blocker).
readmission_drivers: Medication nonadherence (dominant — cost, access, complexity, side effects); inadequate/absent outpatient follow-up; undiagnosed secondary hypertension; ongoing substance use (cocaine/methamphetamine); low health literacy; no home BP monitoring. Prevention levers: single-pill combinations, medication-affordability assistance, home BP monitoring, structured <=7-day follow-up, adherence counseling, and definitive workup/treatment of secondary causes.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: 2025 AHA/ACC (multisociety) Guideline for the Prevention, Detection, Evaluation and Management of High Blood Pressure in Adults (retires/replaces the 2017 ACC/AHA guideline). Condition-specific adjuncts: 2022 ACC/AHA Aortic Disease Guideline (dissection BP targets); AHA/ASA 2019 Acute Ischemic Stroke and 2022 Spontaneous ICH guidelines (neuro BP targets); ACOG guidance for eclampsia/severe preeclampsia.

INTERACT2, Anderson et al., NEJM 2013;368:2355-65 (DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1214609; PMID 23713578). 2022 ACC/AHA Aortic Disease Guideline (PMID 36322642). Hypertensive-crisis systematic review, Cureus 2024;16(8):e66694 (PMID 39262522). Citations retrieved via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Moderate overall. The core framework — BP >=180/120 definition, emergency-vs-urgency triage, staged MAP reduction, and evidence AGAINST aggressively treating asymptomatic urgency — is strong Class I society-guideline consensus. Most organ-specific numeric BP targets are consensus/expert opinion (Level C) with limited RCT support; the ICH target is RCT-informed (INTERACT2 benefit signal; ATACH-2 no benefit and more renal injury at target <140). Condition-specific targets are extrapolated where trial data are thin.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 304 (with MCC): the MCC is typically the qualifying emergency itself — acute renal failure, acute MI, acute HF/pulmonary edema, ICH/ischemic stroke, hypertensive encephalopathy, acute respiratory failure, or aortic dissection — driving ICU admission, IV titratable therapy, specialist consults, higher complication risk, and LOS ~3.5-4+ days. DRG 305 (no MCC/CC): urgency or emergency with mild/resolved end-organ involvement, usually floor-level, LOS ~2-2.4 days; a substantial share represents potentially avoidable admission of asymptomatic severe HTN — a capacity/throughput (RTDC) opportunity.
pathway_pearls: 1) Emergency vs urgency is THE decision: acute end-organ damage -> ICU + IV titratable agent; no TOD -> oral meds + <=7-day outpatient follow-up, NOT admission (avoid low-value bed use). 2) Controlled, staged reduction — MAP down <=10-20% in hour one (target ~160/100 by 2-6 h); arterial line + nicardipine/clevidipine/labetalol; never sublingual short-acting nifedipine or overcorrection (iatrogenic stroke/AKI). 3) Match the target to the organ: dissection SBP <120 & HR <60 with beta-blocker first; pre-tPA stroke <185/110 else permissive to 220/120; ICH ~140; eclampsia <160/110 + magnesium; catecholamine crisis phentolamine/benzos, never unopposed beta-blockade. 4) Discharge is an adherence intervention: single-pill combos + medication affordability + home BP monitor + 7-day follow-up prevents the bounce-back.

admission_criteria: INPATIENT admit indications: obstructing stone WITH infection/suspected urosepsis (fever >38C, WBC >12k or <4k, pyuria/positive nitrite-leukocyte esterase, +/- hypotension) = urgent-decompression emergency and the dominant inpatient driver; intractable pain or vomiting despite adequate ED opioids/NSAIDs/antiemetics; AKI or creatinine rise from obstruction; obstruction of a solitary, transplanted, or only-functioning kidney; bilateral simultaneous obstruction; high-grade hydronephrosis with a stone unlikely to pass (>10 mm). OBSERVATION: borderline pain control or <24 h post-decompression monitoring. ED DISCHARGE (not admit): uncomplicated ureteral stone <=10 mm, pain controlled, afebrile, no pyuria, normal renal function, tolerating PO, reliable follow-up.
risk_stratification: qSOFA >=2 (RR>=22, SBP<=100, GCS<15) or SIRS >=2 with obstruction -> sepsis pathway + urgent decompression; serum lactate >=2 mmol/L (>=4 = shock) and elevated procalcitonin -> Surviving Sepsis 1-h bundle. CT stone size drives disposition: <5 mm ~70-90% spontaneous passage, 5-10 mm ~50%, >10 mm low likelihood -> intervention. Hydronephrosis grade (mild/moderate/severe) and forniceal rupture flag high-grade obstruction; STONE score predicts ureteral-stone presence in the ED. Post-decompression urosepsis risk: pyonephrosis + high procalcitonin (Lu 2023). Guy's Stone Score / S.T.O.N.E. nephrolithometry estimate PCNL complexity and stone-free probability.
initial_workup_labs: CBC (leukocytosis/bandemia or leukopenia = infection); CMP/BMP (creatinine/eGFR for AKI, K+, HCO3); urinalysis with microscopy (hematuria in ~85%; pyuria/nitrite/LE -> infected obstruction; pH >7.0 suggests struvite/urease organisms, pH <5.5 uric-acid stone; crystals); urine culture BEFORE antibiotics; if febrile/septic: 2 sets blood cultures, serum lactate, procalcitonin, coags/INR; serum calcium and uric acid; urine or serum hCG in reproductive-age women (guides imaging + management); send any retrieved stone for composition analysis. Triggers: pyuria + obstruction -> urgent decompression + cultures; rising creatinine -> nephrology-aware, minimize nephrotoxins.
initial_imaging_dx: Non-contrast low-dose CT abdomen/pelvis (NCCT) = gold standard, obtained during ED work-up (target <2-4 h in suspected complicated/obstructing stone; sensitivity ~97%, specificity ~95%) - reports stone size, location, density (Hounsfield units; >1000 HU less SWL-responsive), hydronephrosis grade, perinephric stranding/forniceal rupture, and anatomy for intervention. Renal/bladder ultrasound (including POCUS) first-line in pregnancy and children and for radiation-sparing follow-up; MR urography or low-dose CT if ultrasound non-diagnostic in pregnancy. KUB to track a radiopaque stone (not uric-acid). ECG/CXR for pre-procedure/anesthesia clearance before decompression.
time_critical_interventions: URGENT DECOMPRESSION of an infected, obstructed kidney is the source-control emergency: drain ASAP, ideally <6-12 h from diagnosis and NOT delayed >24 h - delay >=2 days independently raises in-hospital mortality ~29% (Haas, J Urol 2020). Surviving Sepsis 1-h bundle if septic: blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics <=1 h, measure and repeat lactate, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4, norepinephrine for MAP <65 after fluids. Definitive stone removal (URS/PCNL/SWL) is DEFERRED until sepsis clears - never lithotripse or fully instrument an actively infected system.
initial_management: Analgesia: NSAIDs first-line (ketorolac 15-30 mg IV q6h) unless AKI/bleeding/CKD - superior to opioids for renal colic; add IV opioid (morphine/hydromorphone) as rescue; antiemetics (ondansetron, metoclopramide); IV balanced crystalloid to euvolemia (no evidence forced diuresis aids passage). MET: tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO daily x ~30 days for distal ureteral stones 5-10 mm (AUA Strong; benefit concentrated in larger distal stones per SUSPEND). Infected obstruction: empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics per local antibiogram - ceftriaxone or piperacillin-tazobactam; carbapenem if ESBL/prior-resistant-GNR risk; cover E. coli/Klebsiella/Proteus +/- enterococcus; de-escalate on culture. DECOMPRESSION: retrograde ureteral stent OR percutaneous nephrostomy - equivalent mortality (Wong 2024; Lu 2023 RCT); PCN favored if hemodynamically unstable, pyonephrosis, or large stone burden; stent if prompt definitive treatment is feasible. Uric-acid stones: urinary alkalinization with potassium citrate to pH 6.5-7.0.
day1_milestones: Confirm diagnosis on NCCT and risk-stratify; draw urine + blood cultures and start empiric antibiotics <=1 h if infected; PERFORM urgent decompression (stent or PCN) the same day for infected obstruction and send collecting-system aspirate for culture; complete the sepsis bundle and document lactate clearance; achieve pain/nausea control on scheduled NSAID +/- opioid; establish urine-output monitoring and track post-drainage output and creatinine; start MET for a retained passable ureteral stone; consent and plan interval definitive stone therapy once stabilized.
day2_milestones: Confirm clinical improvement - afebrile >24 h, hemodynamically stable, vasopressors off, lactate normalized; de-escalate/narrow antibiotics to a culture-directed agent and set total course (typically ~7-14 days for complicated UTI/urosepsis from time of source control); verify decompression efficacy (declining creatinine, improving urine output, resolving hydronephrosis); transition IV to oral analgesia; advance diet as tolerated and ambulate; begin discharge planning and schedule interval definitive stone treatment (URS/PCNL/SWL) after sepsis resolution.
day3plus_milestones: Sustained afebrile and hemodynamically stable on oral antibiotics + oral analgesia, tolerating diet; renal function stable/improving; nephrostomy/stent care and red-flag education completed; interval definitive stone treatment (URS +/- laser, mini-PCNL/PCNL, or SWL) scheduled outpatient typically 1-2 weeks post-sepsis; for high-risk/recurrent stone formers arrange outpatient 24-h urine metabolic evaluation (deferred >=4-6 weeks after the acute event/stent removal); discharge when criteria met (often HD2-3 uncomplicated, HD4-5 with MCC/urosepsis).
consults_multidisciplinary: Urology - EMERGENT (same-day decompression + definitive stone plan), the primary consult. Interventional radiology - if percutaneous nephrostomy is chosen. Nephrology - AKI, solitary/transplant kidney, or metabolic stone evaluation. Infectious disease - ESBL/MDR organisms or complicated urosepsis. Critical care - septic shock/vasopressor need. Pharmacy - antimicrobial stewardship + renal dose adjustment. Care management/social work - stent/nephrostomy home care, transport, and follow-up. Registered dietitian - recurrent stone-former dietary counseling. Anesthesia - pre-procedure evaluation.
monitoring_level: ICU: septic shock requiring vasopressors, lactate >=4 not clearing, respiratory failure, or hemodynamic instability during/after decompression. Step-down/telemetry: sepsis without shock, significant comorbidity/MCC, or ongoing lactate clearance post-drainage. Floor: obstruction with controlled pain/AKI without infection, or stabilized post-decompression. Escalation triggers: MAP <65 after 30 mL/kg fluid, rising/non-clearing lactate, new organ dysfunction, urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h, worsening mentation, or recurrent fever spikes suggesting inadequate drainage.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: NPO pre-procedure/anesthesia, then advance as tolerated with generous oral hydration (prevention target urine output ~2-2.5 L/day); dietitian counseling on higher fluid intake, normal-calcium/low-sodium/reduced-animal-protein and reduced-oxalate diet for recurrence prevention (post-acute). Early ambulation. VTE prophylaxis: risk-stratify (Caprini/Padua), mechanical + pharmacologic (enoxaparin or UFH) unless active bleeding/imminent PCNL, held per procedure timing. Glycemic control per inpatient protocol. Catheters: remove Foley early once stable; ureteral stent/nephrostomy remains until interval definitive treatment; discontinue IV access when tolerating oral meds.
discharge_criteria: Afebrile >=24 h and hemodynamically stable off vasopressors; pain controlled on oral analgesics; tolerating oral fluids, diet, and medications; renal function stable or improving; decompression device (stent/nephrostomy) functioning with adequate urine output; transitioned to an appropriate oral antibiotic with a defined remaining course; interval definitive stone procedure and urology follow-up scheduled; patient/caregiver educated on stent/nephrostomy care and return-precaution red flags.
discharge_planning: Disposition home for most; home-health for nephrostomy care as needed. Medication reconciliation: complete oral antibiotic course, oral analgesia taper, tamsulosin 0.4 mg for stent discomfort or a retained stone, potassium citrate for uric-acid/hypocitraturic stones. Schedule urology follow-up within 1-2 weeks for definitive stone treatment and stent removal - enroll in a STENT-TRACKING/registry system to prevent a retained/forgotten ureteral stent (encrustation risk). Patient education: aggressive hydration, expected stent symptoms (dysuria, urgency, flank discomfort with voiding), and red flags (fever/chills, uncontrolled pain, anuria, heavy hematuria/clots). Provide stone-composition results + prevention plan; PCP follow-up and outpatient 24-h urine for recurrent formers.
expected_los: CMS GMLOS benchmarks: DRG 694 (stones w/o MCC) 2.1 d (arithmetic 2.7 d, RW 0.78); DRG 693 (stones w MCC) 3.7 d (arithmetic 4.9 d, RW 1.34); DRG 691/692 (w ESW lithotripsy, w/ and w/o CC-MCC) generally ~1-3 d (much ESWL is outpatient). Blended typical inpatient LOS ~2-4 days.
target_los: Under the ideal pathway: uncomplicated obstruction/pain-driven admit <=1-2 days; infected obstruction with prompt (<12 h) decompression ~3 days (w/o MCC) and ~4 days (w MCC/urosepsis, limited by IV antibiotic and clinical clearance). Protocolized rapid-decompression pathways cut time-to-decompression and LOS (Haas 2021); aim to meet or beat GMLOS via same-day decompression and early IV-to-PO antibiotic/analgesia switch.
quality_metrics: CMS SEP-1 severe sepsis/septic shock bundle compliance (urosepsis cohort); institutional time-to-decompression (<24 h; stretch <6-12 h); blood cultures drawn before antibiotics; antimicrobial stewardship/de-escalation rate; 30-day all-cause and stone-related readmission; CAUTI and Foley-day metrics; retained/forgotten-stent rate (stent-tracking compliance); low-dose CT utilization (radiation stewardship); definitive-procedure stone-free and retreatment rates; 7-30 day ED return for recurrent colic.
common_complications: Urosepsis/progression to septic shock - notably transient bacteremia after manipulating a pyonephrotic system (mitigate: decompress before definitive treatment, low-pressure drainage, culture-directed antibiotics; pyonephrosis + high procalcitonin flag risk - Lu 2023); obstructive AKI (mitigate: prompt decompression); stent-related LUTS/hematuria, migration, encrustation, and forgotten stent (mitigate: alpha-blocker, stent registry/tracking); nephrostomy dislodgement/bleeding; inadequate drainage with persistent fever (re-image, reposition); procedural - steinstrasse post-SWL, ureteral perforation/avulsion post-URS, hemorrhage/urinoma/sepsis post-PCNL; opioid-related ileus/constipation (mitigate: NSAID-first analgesia + bowel regimen).
readmission_drivers: Recurrent colic or new obstruction from residual/passing fragments; stent intolerance/complications (encrustation, migration, forgotten stent); recurrent or persistent UTI/urosepsis from inadequate source control or a resistant organism; failure of spontaneous passage needing urgent intervention; dehydration and inadequate pain control. Prevention levers: a guaranteed interval definitive-treatment appointment + stent-removal tracking, tamsulosin for stent symptoms, a culture-directed full antibiotic course, hydration and red-flag education, and metabolic prevention for recurrent formers to cut lifetime recurrence (~50% at 5-10 yr).
guideline_source: PRIMARY: American Urological Association (AUA) - Surgical Management of Kidney and Ureteral Stones Guideline, 2025 (published J Urol 2026; updates the 2016 AUA/Endourological Society guideline) + AUA Medical Management of Kidney Stones Guideline 2014 (amended 2019). European Association of Urology (EAU) Guidelines on Urolithiasis 2025. For infected obstruction: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 + CMS SEP-1; IDSA complicated-UTI principles for antimicrobial selection.
key_citations: AUA Surgical Management of Kidney and Ureteral Stones Guideline 2025, Part III (Pearle et al, J Urol 2026;215(2):132-141; PMID 41263325; DOI 10.1097/JU.0000000000004844) and Part I 2016 (Assimos et al, J Urol 2016;196:1153-60; PMID 27238616); AUA Medical Management of Kidney Stones (Pearle et al, J Urol 2014;192:316-24; PMID 24857648; DOI 10.1016/j.juro.2014.05.006); SUSPEND MET RCT (Pickard et al, HTA/Lancet 2015; PMID 26244520; DOI 10.3310/hta19630); delayed decompression + mortality (Haas et al, J Urol 2020;204:1256-62; PMID 32501124; DOI 10.1097/JU.0000000000001182); emergency decompression PCN vs stent RCT (Lu et al, Ann Med 2023;55:965-72; PMID 36883206; DOI 10.1080/07853890.2023.2169343); nationwide PCN vs stent analysis (Wong et al, JAMMI 2024;9:73-81; PMID 40641810; DOI 10.3138/jammi-2023-0030). Citations retrieved via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong overall - anchored in AUA Class/Strong society-guideline recommendations (urgent decompression of an infected obstruction = Strong Recommendation) and Surviving Sepsis Campaign. MET carries MODERATE/nuanced evidence (SUSPEND RCT negative overall; benefit concentrated in distal ureteral stones 5-10 mm). Decompression-modality choice (stent vs nephrostomy) is Moderate - equivalent mortality by RCT and large propensity-matched cohort, so choose by local expertise and patient factors.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The collapsed group splits by severity: 691/692 = stones treated with ESW lithotripsy during the stay (with/without CC-MCC), 693 = stones WITH MCC, 694 = WITHOUT MCC. Dominant MCC/CC drivers are urosepsis/severe sepsis, septic shock, and acute kidney failure/ATN from obstruction - these move the patient to step-down/ICU, roughly double relative weight (1.34 vs 0.78) and LOS (GMLOS 3.7 vs 2.1 d), and add procedural risk. Without-MCC (694) cases are pain/obstruction-driven short stays. Accurate capture of sepsis severity, AKI stage, and organ dysfunction is essential for correct DRG assignment and expected-LOS benchmarking.
pathway_pearls: 1) Infected + obstructed kidney = emergency source control: decompress ASAP (<6-12 h), never delay >24 h (delay >=2 d raises death odds ~29%), and never lithotripse/URS an actively infected system. 2) Draw blood + collecting-system cultures BEFORE antibiotics, then broad-spectrum IV abx <=1 h in sepsis, and de-escalate on sensitivities. 3) Stent vs nephrostomy has equivalent mortality - choose by speed/availability and patient stability (PCN if unstable/pyonephrosis), not dogma. 4) Guarantee the exit: mandatory stent-tracking registry + a scheduled interval definitive stone treatment to prevent forgotten/encrusted stents and bounce-back obstruction.

admission_criteria: Admit (usually ICU) for acute endocrine emergency: THYROID STORM — Burch-Wartofsky (BWPS) >=45, or impending storm BWPS 25-44 with end-organ dysfunction, or Japanese Thyroid Association TS1/TS2 criteria met; ADRENAL CRISIS — hypotension/shock unresponsive to fluids with suspected adrenal insufficiency (AI), or AI patient unable to tolerate oral stress dosing, or severe hyponatremia/hyperkalemia/hypoglycemia; MYXEDEMA COMA — hypothermia + altered mental status + hypothyroidism; SEVERE MALNUTRITION — GLIM stage 2 with electrolyte derangement or high refeeding risk unable to feed safely as outpatient; INBORN/OTHER METABOLIC (642) — metabolic decompensation (hyperammonemia, severe anion-gap acidosis). ED discharge/observation acceptable for: mild uncomplicated thyrotoxicosis (BWPS <25) without end-organ dysfunction; known-AI patient responding to oral stress dose; moderate malnutrition without electrolyte derangement
risk_stratification: Burch-Wartofsky Point Scale (BWPS): >=45 highly suggestive of thyroid storm -> aggressive multimodal blockade + ICU; 25-44 impending storm -> thionamide + monitored bed; <25 storm unlikely -> seek alternate cause. Japanese Thyroid Association (JTA/Akamizu) criteria: thyrotoxicosis + combinations of CNS dysfunction/fever/tachycardia/CHF/GI-hepatic -> TS1 (definite) vs TS2 (suspected); mortality ~10-11%. Adrenal: no formal score — random cortisol <5 ug/dL (140 nmol/L) during stress supports AI; 250 ug cosyntropin peak cortisol <18 ug/dL confirms. ASPEN refeeding risk: fall in phosphate/K/Mg by 10-20% (mild), 20-30% (moderate), >30% or organ dysfunction/thiamine deficiency (severe) within 5 days of feeding; NICE high-risk = BMI<16, weight loss>15%, minimal intake>10d, low baseline K/Phos/Mg. GLIM: >=1 phenotypic (weight loss, low BMI, reduced muscle mass) + >=1 etiologic (reduced intake/assimilation, inflammation) -> stage 1 moderate vs stage 2 severe. Overlay qSOFA/SOFA if septic precipitant
initial_workup_labs: Thyroid: TSH, free T4, free T3/total T3, TSH-receptor antibody (TRAb) for Graves; CBC (leukocytosis), CMP with LFTs and glucose, calcium. Adrenal: paired morning serum cortisol + plasma ACTH drawn BEFORE steroids only if it does not delay treatment, CMP (hyponatremia/hyperkalemia/hypoglycemia), 250 ug cosyntropin stimulation test, plasma renin + aldosterone, 21-hydroxylase antibodies. Malnutrition/refeeding: phosphate, magnesium, potassium at baseline then q6-12h during refeeding, thiamine, CBC, CMP, 25-OH vitamin D (prealbumin only as a trend, not diagnostic). Precipitant screen (all): blood/urine cultures, CXR, ECG, troponin, beta-hCG, tox screen, cross-check cortisol/TSH. IEM (642): ammonia, lactate, ABG with anion gap, plasma amino acids, urine organic acids, acylcarnitine profile
initial_imaging_dx: ECG on arrival (rate, atrial fibrillation, QTc) — immediate in storm and any K derangement; CXR for CHF/precipitant/aspiration; TTE if CHF or tachyarrhythmia in storm. Thyroid ultrasound with Doppler for etiology (non-urgent); radioactive iodine uptake DEFERRED until stable and off iodine load. Adrenal CT (unenhanced) if primary AI of unclear cause (hemorrhage, TB, infiltration, metastasis); pituitary MRI if secondary AI suspected. CT head if altered mental status unexplained (rule out alternate cause in storm/myxedema). Body-composition/muscle assessment (non-acute) for malnutrition staging
time_critical_interventions: ADRENAL CRISIS: hydrocortisone 100 mg IV/IM bolus IMMEDIATELY (do NOT await cortisol result) then 200 mg/24h continuous infusion or 50 mg IV q6h; 1000 mL isotonic saline in the first hour then 4-6 L/24h; IV dextrose for hypoglycemia — steroid given within minutes of suspicion. THYROID STORM: begin multimodal blockade within the first hour — beta-blocker + thionamide immediately, iodine given >=1 hour AFTER thionamide, glucocorticoid, active cooling; ICU. REFEEDING: give thiamine 100-300 mg IV before/with first calories and correct K/Phos/Mg before or as feeding begins; start 10-20 kcal/kg/day (~50% of needs) and advance over 3-5 days. MYXEDEMA COMA: IV levothyroxine load +/- liothyronine + stress-dose steroids + passive rewarming urgently
initial_management: THYROID STORM ('5 blocks'): beta-blocker (block adrenergic + T4->T3 conversion) propranolol 60-80 mg PO q4-6h or 0.5-1 mg IV, esmolol infusion if hemodynamically tenuous; thionamide (block synthesis) PROPYLTHIOURACIL 500-1000 mg load then 250 mg q4h (preferred in true storm — also blocks peripheral conversion) or methimazole 60-80 mg/day; iodine (block release) SSKI 5 drops PO q6h or Lugol's, given >=1h after thionamide; glucocorticoid (block conversion + treat relative AI) hydrocortisone 100 mg IV q8h or dexamethasone 2 mg IV q6h; bile-acid sequestrant (block enterohepatic recirculation) cholestyramine 4 g PO QID; supportive acetaminophen + cooling (NEVER aspirin — displaces T4); refractory -> therapeutic plasma exchange or emergent thyroidectomy. ADRENAL CRISIS: hydrocortisone bolus/infusion as above + isotonic saline + dextrose; treat precipitant; mineralocorticoid not needed while HC >50 mg/day. SEVERE MALNUTRITION/REFEEDING: hypocaloric start 10-20 kcal/kg/day, aggressive phos/Mg/K repletion, thiamine 100-300 mg IV, multivitamin/trace elements, enteral preferred over parenteral. MYXEDEMA COMA: levothyroxine 200-400 ug IV load then 50-100 ug/day +/- liothyronine 5-20 ug + hydrocortisone 100 mg q8h until AI excluded
day1_milestones: ICU or monitored admission; confirm diagnosis and identify precipitant (infection, non-adherence, surgery, iodine load, MI); START definitive therapy same hour (multimodal blockade for storm, IV steroids for crisis, controlled refeeding for malnutrition); continuous telemetry; electrolytes q6-12h if refeeding, hemodynamic/neuro checks if crisis/storm; Endocrinology consult day 1; fluid resuscitation and correction of glucose/electrolytes; thiamine before calories; VTE prophylaxis; place central line/Foley only if truly needed; for storm demonstrate early downtrend in HR, temperature and agitation
day2_milestones: STORM: clinical improvement (BWPS trending down, afebrile, rate controlled, mentation clearing); convert IV to oral thionamide and beta-blocker; begin weaning iodine per plan; step down from ICU. CRISIS: hemodynamically stable, electrolytes/glucose normalizing; begin tapering stress-dose hydrocortisone toward oral maintenance; add fludrocortisone once HC <50 mg/day (primary AI). REFEEDING: advance calories toward goal if phos/K/Mg stable, continue repletion, dietitian-directed plan. De-escalate monitoring level; reassess and remove lines/catheters
day3plus_milestones: STORM: on stable oral antithyroid regimen, euthyroid trajectory, definitive therapy (RAI or thyroidectomy) planned as outpatient. CRISIS: transitioned to oral hydrocortisone maintenance 15-25 mg/day divided + fludrocortisone 0.05-0.2 mg/day; sick-day/stress-dosing education delivered with teach-back; emergency IM hydrocortisone kit and steroid emergency card issued. REFEEDING/MALNUTRITION: at goal calories with electrolytes stable for >=48h, transitioned to oral/enteral maintenance nutrition plan. Confirm discharge-readiness criteria met and complete patient education
consults_multidisciplinary: Endocrinology for every case on day 1; Critical care/intensivist for thyroid storm, myxedema coma, refractory adrenal crisis, or severe refeeding with cardiorespiratory compromise; Registered dietitian/nutrition support team day 1 for malnutrition and all refeeding-risk patients; Clinical pharmacy for complex dosing and drug interactions; Endocrine surgery (+/- ENT, anesthesia) for definitive thyroid therapy/thyroidectomy candidacy; Cardiology for atrial fibrillation or high-output CHF in storm; Psychiatry/eating-disorder team if anorexia-driven malnutrition; Genetics/metabolic specialist for inborn errors (642); Infectious disease if infectious precipitant; Social work/case management and PT/OT for deconditioning and transitions of care
monitoring_level: ICU: thyroid storm (BWPS >=45), myxedema coma, adrenal crisis with refractory shock, severe refeeding with cardiac/respiratory compromise, hyperammonemic metabolic encephalopathy. Step-down/telemetry: impending storm (BWPS 25-44), resolving adrenal crisis, high-risk refeeding with arrhythmia potential. Floor: stable adrenal insufficiency on oral stress dosing, moderate malnutrition, controlled thyrotoxicosis. Escalation triggers: rising BWPS, new/worsening tachyarrhythmia, hemodynamic instability or hypotension unresponsive to fluids+steroid, deteriorating mental status, refractory hypoglycemia, or phosphate/potassium falling despite repletion
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: refeeding protocol starting 10-20 kcal/kg/day advancing to goal over 3-5 days, enteral route preferred, thiamine 100-300 mg IV before the first calorie; NPO in unstable storm/crisis then advance as tolerated. Glucose: inpatient target 140-180 mg/dL — IV dextrose for AI hypoglycemia, insulin sliding scale for steroid-induced hyperglycemia. Mobility: early mobilization and PT once hemodynamically stable. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH or heparin) unless active bleeding/DIC — storm is hypermetabolic and AF-prone, raising thromboembolic risk. Lines/catheters: remove central lines and urinary catheters as early as possible to prevent CLABSI/CAUTI
discharge_criteria: STORM: afebrile, heart rate <100 and controlled, resolved altered mental status, tolerating oral antithyroid drug + beta-blocker, downtrending thyrotoxic signs, definitive-therapy plan established. CRISIS: hemodynamically stable off IV steroids >=24h on oral hydrocortisone (+ fludrocortisone if primary AI), normal electrolytes and glucose, sick-day education complete, emergency injection kit and steroid card issued. REFEEDING/MALNUTRITION: electrolytes stable for >=48h at goal nutrition, tolerating oral/enteral intake, no active arrhythmia. All: precipitant identified/treated and follow-up arranged
discharge_planning: Medication reconciliation: antithyroid drug + beta-blocker (storm); hydrocortisone + fludrocortisone + emergency IM hydrocortisone kit + steroid emergency card + MedicAlert (crisis); oral nutrition supplements + electrolyte prescriptions (malnutrition). Education with teach-back: sick-day rules and stress dosing (AI — highest-yield); thionamide agranulocytosis warning (stop drug and get CBC for fever/sore throat) and PTU hepatotoxicity; refeeding safety. Follow-up: Endocrinology within 1-2 weeks; repeat thyroid function tests at 2-4 weeks after storm; dietitian follow-up for malnutrition; PCP within 7 days. Transitions: written care plan, confirm prescriptions filled, arrange home health/home nutrition where indicated
expected_los: Collapsed group GMLOS varies by severity tier (approximate CMS IPPS benchmarks): DRG 645 endocrine disorders without CC/MCC ~2.5-2.8 days; DRG 644 with CC ~3.3-3.6 days; DRG 643 with MCC ~4.5-4.9 days; DRG 642 inborn/other metabolic disorders ~3.0-3.5 days. Blended group typical LOS ~3.5-4 days; thyroid storm/myxedema/adrenal crisis (MCC tier) skew toward 5+ days with 1-2 ICU days
target_los: Uncomplicated thyrotoxicosis or stable AI (DRG 645): 2-3 days; with CC (644): ~3 days; thyroid storm, adrenal crisis, or severe malnutrition/refeeding (MCC, 643): 4-5 days including 1-2 ICU days. Optimized target ~0.5-1 day below GMLOS achieved via first-hour endocrine consult, protocolized multimodal blockade / immediate empiric steroid, and dietitian-led refeeding with pre-emptive electrolyte and thiamine repletion
quality_metrics: No endocrine-emergency-specific CMS core measure exists; applicable measures: 30-day all-cause readmission; inpatient glycemic safety eCQMs (severe hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia); Joint Commission nutrition screening within 24h of admission with GLIM diagnosis capture. Pathway process metrics: time-to-empiric-steroid in suspected adrenal crisis; time-to-multimodal-blockade in thyroid storm; thiamine-before-feed compliance and electrolyte-monitoring adherence during refeeding. Safety/outcome: CLABSI/CAUTI, hospital-acquired VTE, in-hospital mortality (thyroid storm ~10%), ICU length of stay
common_complications: Thyroid storm: high-output CHF, atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, hyperthermia, thromboembolism, thionamide agranulocytosis, PTU hepatotoxicity — mitigated by telemetry, avoiding aspirin, and CBC/LFT surveillance. Adrenal crisis: refractory shock, hyponatremia, hypoglycemia, hyperkalemic arrhythmia — mitigated by immediate steroid + saline + dextrose. Refeeding syndrome: hypophosphatemia, hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, Wernicke encephalopathy, fluid overload/CHF, arrhythmia — mitigated by slow calorie advance, thiamine, and scheduled electrolyte repletion. General: delirium, VTE, healthcare-associated infection from lines/catheters
readmission_drivers: Adrenal: recurrent crisis from inadequate sick-day/stress dosing, missed doses, or no emergency kit — lever: structured education, emergency IM hydrocortisone kit, steroid card, MedicAlert. Thyroid: antithyroid-drug non-adherence, delayed definitive therapy, relapse of thyrotoxicosis — lever: expedite RAI/surgery and close thyroid-function follow-up. Malnutrition: recurrent electrolyte derangement and untreated root cause (anorexia, malignancy, alcohol use disorder, malabsorption) plus inadequate outpatient nutrition — lever: dietitian follow-up, home nutrition support, treat underlying disease. Cross-cutting: missed or under-treated precipitant (occult infection) and poor care transitions
guideline_source: American Thyroid Association (ATA) 2016 Guidelines for Diagnosis and Management of Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis (Ross et al.) — thyrotoxicosis/thyroid storm; Japanese Thyroid Association thyroid storm diagnostic criteria (Akamizu et al. 2012); Endocrine Society 2016 Clinical Practice Guideline on Diagnosis and Treatment of Primary Adrenal Insufficiency (Bornstein et al.), ESE/Endocrine Society 2024 Joint Guideline on Glucocorticoid-induced Adrenal Insufficiency, and Society for Endocrinology 2016 Emergency Management of Adrenal Crisis; ASPEN 2020 Consensus Recommendations for Refeeding Syndrome (da Silva et al.) and GLIM 2018/2019 criteria for malnutrition (Cederholm/Jensen et al.)
key_citations: Ross DS et al. 2016 ATA Guidelines for Hyperthyroidism and Other Causes of Thyrotoxicosis. Thyroid 2016;26(10):1343-1421 (PMID 27521067; DOI 10.1089/thy.2016.0229). Akamizu T et al. Diagnostic criteria, clinical features, and incidence of thyroid storm based on nationwide surveys. Thyroid 2012;22(7):661-79 (PMID 22690898; DOI 10.1089/thy.2011.0334). Bornstein SR et al. Diagnosis and Treatment of Primary Adrenal Insufficiency: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2016;101(2):364-89 (PMID 26760044; DOI 10.1210/jc.2015-1710). ESE/Endocrine Society Joint Guideline: Diagnosis and Therapy of Glucocorticoid-induced Adrenal Insufficiency. J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2024;109(7):1657. da Silva JSV et al. ASPEN Consensus Recommendations for Refeeding Syndrome. Nutr Clin Pract 2020;35(2):178-195 (PMID 32115791; DOI 10.1002/ncp.10474). Cederholm T, Jensen GL et al. GLIM criteria for the diagnosis of malnutrition. Clin Nutr 2019;38(1):1-9 (PMID 30181091; DOI 10.1016/j.clnu.2018.08.002). Burch HB, Wartofsky L. Point scale for thyroid storm, Endocrinol Metab Clin North Am 1993
evidence_grade: Moderate overall — a strong current society-guideline backbone (ATA 2016, Endocrine Society 2016 + ESE/ES 2024, ASPEN 2020, GLIM) but the core acute-crisis drug regimens for thyroid storm, adrenal crisis, and myxedema coma rest on consensus/expert opinion and observational data (randomized trials are lacking and largely infeasible/unethical). BWPS and JTA criteria are validated observational tools; refeeding and GLIM thresholds are consensus-derived. Immediate empiric steroid in suspected adrenal crisis and thionamide-before-iodine sequencing in storm are high-consensus, low-direct-RCT-evidence practices
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The collapsed group spans floor-level 2-day stays to ICU 5+ day crises, and the presence of a crisis/coma/severe-malnutrition diagnosis (the MCC) is the single biggest driver of resource use. DRG 643 (MCC): thyroid storm/thyrotoxic crisis (E05.x with storm), myxedema coma (E03.5), Addisonian crisis (E27.2), and severe protein-calorie malnutrition (E40-E43) all classify as MCC -> ICU, LOS ~4.5-4.9d, mortality up to ~10%. DRG 644 (CC): thyrotoxicosis without storm, moderate malnutrition (E44), adrenal insufficiency without crisis -> telemetry/floor, ~3.4d. DRG 645 (no CC/MCC): stable/uncomplicated endocrine disorder -> floor, ~2.6d. DRG 642 (inborn/other metabolic): decompensation variable — hyperammonemia or severe acidosis drives ICU admission and longer stay
pathway_pearls: 1) In suspected adrenal crisis give hydrocortisone 100 mg IV NOW — never delay for cortisol/ACTH; draw them first only if it does not delay the steroid. 2) In thyroid storm sequence is critical: thionamide FIRST, iodine >=1 hour later (iodine before blocking synthesis fuels hormone production), use PTU rather than methimazole in true storm for peripheral-conversion blockade, and never give aspirin (raises free T4). 3) Refeeding kills through hypophosphatemia — give thiamine before the first calorie, start at 10-20 kcal/kg/day, and check phosphate/potassium/magnesium q6-12h, correcting before advancing calories. 4) The highest-yield readmission lever for adrenal insufficiency is the discharge safety net: sick-day/stress-dose teach-back plus an emergency IM hydrocortisone kit and steroid card before the patient leaves

scope_and_volume_notes: Inborn/other metabolic (642), endocrine disorders (643-645): thyroid storm, adrenal crisis, malnutrition.

condition: Skin Ulcer / Wound Debridement & Skin Graft
admission_criteria: Inpatient admission for: systemic infection/sepsis accompanying an ulcer (SIRS/qSOFA >=2, temp >38 or <36C, HR >90, WBC >12k or <4k, hypotension, lactate >2); moderate-to-severe SSTI or diabetes-related foot infection (IWGDF/IDSA moderate DFI with complicating features or severe DFI) requiring IV antibiotics + urgent source control; suspected necrotizing soft-tissue infection, wet/gas gangrene, deep abscess, or osteomyelitis needing operative debridement; wound requiring excisional debridement or split-thickness skin graft (STSG)/flap under anesthesia; threatened limb (SVS WIfI stage 3-4) needing revascularization; uncontrolled hyperglycemia/DKA or comorbidity precluding outpatient care; failure of outpatient/oral therapy or inability to offload/self-care. ED discharge/observation appropriate for mild SSTI or superficial ulcer without systemic signs manageable with oral antibiotics + outpatient wound care; observation for a short IV-antibiotic trial when cellulitis response is uncertain.
risk_stratification: Pressure injury: NPIAP/EPUAP staging (Stage 1-4, Unstageable, Deep Tissue Pressure Injury) + Braden Scale (<=18 at-risk, <=12 high-risk -> intensify prevention bundle/support surface). Diabetes-related foot: SVS WIfI (Wound/Ischemia/foot-Infection each 0-3; clinical stage 4 = high amputation risk -> urgent revascularization/debridement) and IWGDF/IDSA infection grade (mild/moderate/severe). SSTI severity: IDSA 2014 mild/moderate/severe; if crepitus/pain out of proportion/bullae, LRINEC >=6 raises necrotizing suspicion (does NOT rule out -> surgical exploration governs). Sepsis: qSOFA/SOFA, NEWS2 -> escalate. Venous ulcer: CEAP (active ulcer = C6) + ABI to gate compression (ABI <0.8 caution, <0.5 severe PAD). Perfusion: ABI, toe pressure, TcPO2. Nutrition: malnutrition screen, albumin/prealbumin.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential (leukocytosis/left shift), CMP (glucose, renal function for antibiotic/contrast dosing), HbA1c (diabetes control/prognosis), CRP + ESR baseline (ESR >70 mm/hr supports osteomyelitis), lactate if septic, blood cultures x2 before antibiotics when systemic signs; procalcitonin optional. Deep tissue/operative cultures — NOT superficial swabs (IDSA/IWGDF) — and bone biopsy (histopathology + culture) as gold standard when osteomyelitis suspected. Nutrition markers (albumin, prealbumin), coags + type & screen pre-op. Serial point-of-care glucose. Abnormal results trigger: cultures -> antibiotic tailoring; ESR/CRP -> imaging for osteo; lactate/WBC -> sepsis bundle; albumin low -> nutrition consult.
initial_imaging_dx: Plain radiographs of the affected part within hours (cortical erosion/periosteal reaction = osteomyelitis; soft-tissue gas = necrotizing infection). Bedside probe-to-bone test (positive in an infected DFU raises osteomyelitis probability). MRI (most sensitive for osteomyelitis, deep abscess, extent) within 24-48h when deep infection/osteo suspected. Arterial assessment: ABI/toe pressures + arterial duplex; if ischemia -> CTA/angiography to plan revascularization. Venous duplex ultrasound (reflux/obstruction) for venous ulcers. CT for necrotizing infection/deep collection/gas. Bone biopsy for definitive osteomyelitis diagnosis and organism-directed therapy. Do NOT delay emergent debridement for imaging when necrotizing infection is clinically suspected.
time_critical_interventions: Necrotizing soft-tissue infection = surgical emergency: emergent OR debridement within hours (clinical diagnosis; do not wait for imaging) + broad-spectrum antibiotics with clindamycin/linezolid for toxin suppression. Septic shock (Surviving Sepsis/CMS SEP-1): broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1h, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, repeat lactate, and source control (drainage/debridement) as soon as feasible. Wet/gas gangrene: urgent operative debridement or amputation. Threatened limb with critical ischemia (WIfI stage 4): urgent revascularization to enable healing. General principle: prompt drainage/debridement of any closed-space or deep infection is the decisive time-critical lever. Elective STSG is NOT time-critical — optimize perfusion, glucose, and a clean wound bed first.
initial_management: Empiric antibiotics by severity/MRSA-risk, then de-escalate to deep-culture results: mild (strep/MSSA) cephalexin/dicloxacillin or IV cefazolin; MRSA-risk/purulent add vancomycin, linezolid, or daptomycin (TMP-SMX/doxycycline if mild); moderate-severe DFI broad gram-pos/gram-neg +/- anaerobe with ampicillin-sulbactam, piperacillin-tazobactam, or ceftriaxone+metronidazole (ertapenem/meropenem if prior resistant/severe); necrotizing = piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem + vancomycin + clindamycin. Source control: sharp/excisional surgical debridement of all nonviable tissue (serial as needed), abscess drainage, and negative-pressure wound therapy (NPWT) after debridement (Armstrong/Lavery Lancet 2005). Offloading (DFU: total contact cast/removable device) and graded compression ~40 mmHg for venous ulcers once significant PAD excluded. Revascularize ischemic limbs. Inpatient glucose target 140-180 mg/dL with insulin (ADA). Multimodal analgesia (acetaminophen, limited opioids, gabapentinoid for neuropathic pain). Optimize nutrition (protein, vitamin C, zinc, arginine). Restrictive transfusion threshold Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if cardiac disease) to optimize graft perfusion. Tetanus prophylaxis for traumatic wounds.
day1_milestones: POD0: definitive source control in OR (excisional debridement +/- staged/immediate STSG or flap); obtain deep-tissue and bone cultures intraoperatively; document wound bed via TIME framework (Tissue, Infection/inflammation, Moisture, Edge). Start/continue empiric IV antibiotics; apply NPWT or appropriate moisture-balanced dressing; immobilize/bolster graft site if grafted. Initiate glycemic control (140-180), multimodal analgesia, and correct offloading device/support surface. Stabilize hemodynamics and complete sepsis bundle if septic. Place early wound/ostomy nursing, nutrition, and PT consults. Confirm perfusion adequacy for planned closure.
day2_milestones: POD1: reassess wound/flap/graft viability and perfusion (STSG bolster typically undisturbed 3-5 days unless infection/hematoma suspected); review preliminary culture/Gram stain and narrow/de-escalate antibiotics; confirm defervescence and downtrending WBC/CRP. Advance diet, continue insulin to target, and mobilize with strict offloading (PT/OT). Assess need for repeat/serial debridement. Verify vascular plan (revascularization if ischemic bed). Continue pressure-injury prevention (repositioning q2h, pressure-redistributing surface).
day3plus_milestones: POD2+: serial debridement until a clean, granulating bed, then definitive closure/graft if staged; STSG bolster takedown POD3-5 with graft-take assessment (target >90% take), continued immobilization/NPWT-over-graft. Transition IV to oral antibiotics once clinically stable and source-controlled (IWGDF/IDSA support early oral step-down) and set total duration: soft-tissue infection ~1-2 weeks; osteomyelitis ~3 weeks if resected to clean margins vs ~6 weeks if residual infected bone. Finalize offloading (TCC/boot) and compression plan (venous), arrange home NPWT/DME, and confirm outpatient wound-care pathway. Assess discharge readiness.
consults_multidisciplinary: Operating service — plastic, podiatric, vascular, or general surgery (debridement/graft/flap). Vascular surgery for perfusion assessment/revascularization when ischemic. Infectious diseases for osteomyelitis, resistant organisms, complex/prolonged therapy, and stewardship. Wound/ostomy/continence (WOC) nursing for staging, dressings, and NPWT — early. Endocrinology/diabetes for glycemic control. Physical/occupational therapy for mobility, offloading, and ADLs. Registered dietitian for early malnutrition correction. Care management/social work for disposition, home health, and DME (NPWT pump, offloading device, compression). Pharmacy for antibiotic dosing and OPAT setup. Podiatry and a multidisciplinary wound clinic for follow-up.
monitoring_level: Most patients: general medical-surgical/floor. Telemetry or step-down for sepsis, hemodynamic lability, or significant cardiopulmonary comorbidity. ICU for septic shock, post-debridement necrotizing infection, vasopressor need, respiratory failure, or DKA. Escalation triggers: rising NEWS2/MEWS, qSOFA >=2, rising lactate, hypotension, expanding necrosis/erythema despite therapy, uncontrolled hyperglycemia/DKA, or graft/flap compromise.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: high-protein diet 1.25-1.5 g/kg/day, dietitian-directed; supplement vitamin C, zinc, and arginine to support wound healing; correct hypoalbuminemia. Glucose: basal-bolus/correction insulin to 140-180 mg/dL, avoid hypoglycemia. Mobility: early mobilization with strict offloading of the operative/ulcer site; repositioning q2h and pressure-redistributing surface to prevent new/worsening pressure injury; protect graft/flap per immobilization protocol. VTE prophylaxis: pharmacologic (enoxaparin/LMWH or heparin) plus mechanical per Caprini risk unless active bleeding/high bleed risk — important in these often immobilized surgical patients. Lines/catheters: remove Foley early and minimize central-line days.
discharge_criteria: Source controlled with a clean/granulating bed or graft taking (>90%) and no undrained infection; afebrile >=24h and hemodynamically stable with downtrending WBC/CRP; pain controlled on oral analgesics; tolerating oral antibiotics (or OPAT arranged) with a defined stop date; glucose adequately controlled; offloading/compression device in place with patient/caregiver able to manage it; a concrete wound-care plan established (home health/NPWT arranged, DME delivered); and a safe disposition confirmed.
discharge_planning: Disposition: home with skilled home-health wound care vs SNF/rehab (common when non-weightbearing/complex wound care needed); arrange OPAT if prolonged IV antibiotics (though oral step-down is preferred). Medication reconciliation with antibiotic stop date, glycemic regimen, analgesics, and VTE-prophylaxis duration. Schedule follow-up: operating surgeon/wound clinic in 1-2 weeks (graft check), plus podiatry, vascular, ID, and PCP/endocrinology as indicated. Patient education with teach-back: offloading/footwear adherence (the dominant DFU-recurrence lever), compression adherence (venous), glucose control, smoking cessation, nutrition, pressure-injury repositioning, wound signs of infection, and return precautions. Warm handoff to home-health/next facility with DME (NPWT pump, TCC/offloading boot, compression) confirmed.
expected_los: Highly tier-dependent (blended ~5-8 days). Non-CC/MCC procedural or medical ulcer (575/578/594): GMLOS ~3-4 days. With CC (574/577/593): ~5-7 days. With MCC (573/576/579/592, e.g., sepsis/osteomyelitis/gangrene): ~9-13 days.
target_los: Optimized ~4-5 days blended: simple single debridement/STSG without CC/MCC 2-3 days; with CC 4-5 days; MCC/osteomyelitis/sepsis 7-9 days. Key shortening levers: early IV-to-oral antibiotic step-down (IWGDF/IDSA), OPAT/home-NPWT setup, and multidisciplinary wound-clinic handoff.
quality_metrics: Hospital-acquired pressure injury (HAPI) rate and Stage 3/4 pressure ulcer as CMS HAC — with rigorous present-on-admission (POA) staging documentation (major reimbursement/DRG driver); CMS SEP-1 when sepsis present; perioperative antibiotic prophylaxis timing and antimicrobial-stewardship metrics (days of therapy, de-escalation rate); surgical site infection/wound-complication and graft-loss rates; VTE prophylaxis (VTE-1/2, PSI-12); inpatient glycemic control eCQMs (severe hyperglycemia/hypoglycemia); limb-salvage/amputation rate; 30-day all-cause readmission; and LOS/GMLOS index.
common_complications: Graft/flap failure or partial loss (mitigate with meticulous bed preparation, hemostasis, immobilization/NPWT bolster, infection control, and optimized perfusion/glucose/nutrition); surgical-site or persistent/recurrent infection (deep cultures, targeted antibiotics, serial debridement); progression of osteomyelitis to amputation; sepsis/bacteremia; hematoma under graft (hemostasis); hyperglycemia/hypoglycemia; new or worsening pressure injury from immobility (repositioning, support surfaces); VTE (prophylaxis); AKI from nephrotoxic antibiotics/contrast (vancomycin/aminoglycoside monitoring); and delirium/deconditioning/malnutrition in older adults.
readmission_drivers: Top drivers: nonhealing wound or graft loss; recurrent/undertreated infection (inadequate antibiotic duration, resistant organisms, missed osteomyelitis); poor offloading adherence (DFU recurrence ~40% at 1 year); poor glycemic control; inadequate perfusion/progressive PAD; nonadherence to compression (venous-ulcer recurrence); malnutrition; and social barriers (inability to perform wound care, DME gaps, no home health, transportation). Prevention levers: multidisciplinary wound-clinic follow-up, home-health wound care, enforced offloading/compression with proper footwear/DME, glucose optimization, timely revascularization, nutrition support, and teach-back patient education.
guideline_source: EPUAP/NPIAP/PPPIA International Clinical Practice Guideline: Prevention and Treatment of Pressure Ulcers/Injuries (2019); IWGDF/IDSA Guidelines on Diagnosis and Treatment of Diabetes-related Foot Infections (2023) plus the IWGDF 2023 guideline suite (offloading, PAD, wound healing, classification); IDSA Practice Guidelines for Diagnosis and Management of Skin and Soft Tissue Infections (2014); SVS/AVF Management of Venous Leg Ulcers (2014); SVS Lower Extremity Threatened Limb (WIfI) Classification (2014). Supportive: WOCN Society wound guidelines and ADA Standards of Care (inpatient glycemia 140-180 mg/dL).
key_citations: EPUAP/NPIAP/PPPIA. Prevention and Treatment of Pressure Ulcers/Injuries: Clinical Practice Guideline, 3rd ed., 2019 (development protocol: Kottner J et al. J Tissue Viability 2019; PMID 30658878; DOI 10.1016/j.jtv.2019.01.001). Senneville E et al. IWGDF/IDSA 2023 diabetes-related foot infections. Diabetes Metab Res Rev 2023;40(3):e3687 (also Clin Infect Dis); PMID 37779323; DOI 10.1002/dmrr.3687. Stevens DL et al. IDSA SSTI 2014. Clin Infect Dis 2014;59(2):e10-52; PMID 24973422; DOI 10.1093/cid/ciu444. Mills JL et al. SVS WIfI. J Vasc Surg 2014;59(1):220-34; PMID 24126108; DOI 10.1016/j.jvs.2013.08.003. O'Donnell TF et al. SVS/AVF venous leg ulcers. J Vasc Surg 2014;60(2 Suppl):3S-59S; PMID 24974070. Armstrong DG, Lavery LA. NPWT after partial diabetic foot amputation (RCT). Lancet 2005;366:1704-10; PMID 16291063; DOI 10.1016/S0140-6736(05)67695-7. Lazaro-Martinez JL et al. Antibiotics vs conservative surgery for diabetic foot osteomyelitis (RCT). Diabetes Care 2014;37(3):789-95; PMID 24130347; DOI 10.2337/dc13-1526. (Citations retrieved from PubMed.)
evidence_grade: Mixed. Strong (Class I / GRADE strong, RCT-supported) for infection management and antibiotic selection/duration (IDSA 2014, IWGDF/IDSA 2023), for NPWT after debridement (Armstrong/Lavery RCT), and for antibiotic-vs-surgery equivalence in select non-ischemic forefoot osteomyelitis (Lazaro-Martinez RCT). Moderate for compression in venous ulcers and revascularization in threatened limbs. Consensus/expert (good-practice statements, limited RCT) for wound-bed preparation, debridement technique/timing, and much of pressure-injury prevention/treatment — evidence-graded lower accordingly.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Without CC/MCC (575/578/594): localized ulcer, single clean debridement or simple STSG, no systemic infection; floor care; LOS ~2-4 days; home with home health. With CC (574/577/593): accompanying cellulitis, controlled comorbidity (diabetes, CKD), moderate infection, need for serial debridement; LOS ~5-7 days. With MCC (573/576/579/592): sepsis/septic shock, osteomyelitis, necrotizing infection, gangrene, critical limb ischemia, or acute organ dysfunction (AKI, respiratory failure) driving ICU/step-down, revascularization or major amputation, and prolonged IV antibiotics/OPAT; LOS ~9-14+ days with higher mortality, readmission, and amputation risk. Frequent CC/MCC drivers: osteomyelitis, sepsis, gangrene, DKA, AKI, and protein-calorie malnutrition. Accurate POA staging of pressure injuries and coding of complications is essential for correct DRG assignment and HAC avoidance.
pathway_pearls: 1) Source control first — adequate and serial surgical debridement with DEEP tissue/bone cultures (never superficial swabs), then NPWT over the debrided bed, drives outcomes (Armstrong/Lavery RCT). 2) Perfusion before you graft — assess and correct ischemia (ABI/toe pressures, WIfI staging, revascularize); grafts and flaps fail on ischemic beds. 3) Offloading/pressure redistribution is non-negotiable — total contact cast for DFU and repositioning/support surfaces for pressure injuries are the biggest levers for healing and preventing recurrence/readmission. 4) Right-size antibiotics — early IV-to-oral step-down with a defined duration (soft tissue ~1-2 weeks; osteomyelitis 3 weeks if resected to clean margin vs 6 weeks if residual bone) shortens LOS/OPAT; pair with tight glucose (140-180) and accurate pressure-injury POA documentation.
