admission_criteria: Inpatient admission for persistent altered mental status/coma NOT reversed by bedside correction (glucose, naloxone) or with an identified cause requiring inpatient treatment/monitoring; GCS <=8 or inability to protect airway; new focal neuro deficit or structural lesion on CT; acute encephalopathy with hemodynamic/respiratory instability, seizure, or MCC-level metabolic derangement (severe dysnatremia, hyperammonemia, uremia, DKA/HHS, myxedema); suspected CNS infection or ingestion needing observation/antidote; failure to return to baseline after ED workup. ED discharge/OBSERVATION appropriate for rapidly and fully reversed causes (corrected hypoglycemia back to baseline, simple alcohol/benzodiazepine intoxication clearing, uncomplicated syncope with full recovery) with safe disposition
risk_stratification: Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): <=8 = coma -> secure airway/intubate; FOUR Score (Full Outline of UnResponsiveness, 0-16; adds brainstem reflexes + respiration, scores intubated pts, detects locked-in/herniation) -> lower total predicts higher in-hospital mortality; RASS -5 to +4 to titrate sedation/agitation; delirium screens CAM or CAM-ICU (positive = acute onset + inattention + [disorganized thinking OR altered LOC]) and 4AT (>=4 = probable delirium); NIHSS if acute stroke suspected; qSOFA >=2 / SOFA for sepsis-associated encephalopathy; serum osmolar gap >10 and anion gap flag toxic alcohol/toxidrome; calculated corrected Na and rate-of-change guide dysnatremia risk (osmotic demyelination if overcorrected)
initial_workup_labs: STAT point-of-care glucose (immediate, all patients); CBC, CMP (Na/K/BUN/Cr/glucose/Ca), LFTs + serum ammonia; VBG/ABG (CO2 narcosis, acid-base) + lactate; Mg, phosphate; TSH/free T4 and AM cortisol if endocrine cause; serum osmolality with calculated osmolar & anion gaps; toxicology panel = ethanol, acetaminophen, salicylate, urine drug screen + targeted levels (digoxin, lithium, valproate/AED, carboxyhemoglobin if CO); troponin + 12-lead ECG; coags/INR (hepatic, reversal planning); blood cultures + UA/urine culture if febrile/septic; beta-hCG in women of childbearing age. Abnormals trigger: glucose<70->dextrose; ammonia high->lactulose; Na derangement->controlled correction; positive tox->antidote; positive cultures/pyuria->antibiotics
initial_imaging_dx: Non-contrast CT head STAT (target <30-60 min from arrival; <25 min door-to-CT if stroke code) to identify hemorrhage/mass/herniation/hydrocephalus; add CT angiography head+neck if basilar/large-vessel occlusion suspected (posterior-circulation stroke is a classic coma cause CT may miss early); 12-lead ECG immediately; lumbar puncture (CSF cell count, glucose, protein, Gram stain/culture, HSV-1/2 PCR, meningoencephalitis panel, opening pressure) after CT when infection suspected but DO NOT delay antibiotics for it; EEG / continuous EEG early for unexplained or persistent coma or suspected nonconvulsive seizures (NCSE found in ~8% of comatose patients without clinical seizure activity); MRI brain with DWI within 24-48h for stroke/encephalitis/PRES/osmotic demyelination/anoxic injury; TTE if cardioembolism or endocarditis suspected; CXR for aspiration/infection
time_critical_interventions: Immediate reversible-cause bundle ("coma cocktail"/DONT): bedside glucose then Dextrose if <70 mg/dL; Oxygen/airway; Naloxone if opioid toxidrome; Thiamine (give before/with dextrose in malnourished/alcohol-use). Airway protection/intubation for GCS <=8 or lost protective reflexes. Suspected acute bacterial meningitis -> empiric IV antibiotics + dexamethasone within 1 hour of arrival, never delayed for CT/LP. Suspected HSV encephalitis -> empiric IV acyclovir without waiting for PCR. Suspected status epilepticus/NCSE -> benzodiazepine immediately then loading antiseizure med. Acute ischemic stroke -> IV thrombolytic (door-to-needle <=60 min) within window and/or thrombectomy for LVO/basilar (up to 24h in selected). Symptomatic severe hyponatremia or herniation -> 3% hypertonic saline bolus. Hypertensive emergency, CO poisoning (100% O2), and other antidote-responsive toxidromes addressed emergently
initial_management: Dextrose (D50W 25-50 g) for hypoglycemia; thiamine 100-500 mg IV (500 mg IV TID if Wernicke suspected) before/with glucose; naloxone 0.04-0.4 mg IV titrated up to ~4 mg for opioid reversal (flumazenil generally AVOIDED-seizure risk). Empiric meningitis regimen: ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h + vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg + ampicillin 2 g q4h if age>50/immunocompromised/pregnant (Listeria) + dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg q6h with/before first dose; add acyclovir 10 mg/kg IV q8h for suspected HSV. Seizure: lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg IV then levetiracetam/fosphenytoin/valproate load. Hepatic encephalopathy: lactulose +/- rifaximin. Dysnatremia corrected at controlled rate (Na change <=8 mEq/L/24h to avoid osmotic demyelination); 3% saline for symptomatic hyponatremia/edema. Antidotes as indicated: fomepizole (toxic alcohols), sodium bicarbonate (TCA/salicylate), digoxin-Fab, hydroxocobalamin (cyanide), physostigmine (anticholinergic). Treat agitation with dexmedetomidine and nonpharmacologic measures; AVOID benzodiazepines for delirium (PADIS); antipsychotics only for distressing agitation, not routine
day1_milestones: Secure airway and hemodynamics; complete reversible-cause bundle (glucose/thiamine/naloxone); obtain CT head + core labs + ECG within first hour. Establish a working etiologic category (toxic-metabolic vs structural/vascular vs infectious vs seizure vs endocrine). Start targeted therapy (antibiotics/antivirals, antiseizure, lactulose, sodium correction, antidote). Perform LP and/or EEG if indicated. Correct glucose and electrolytes; identify and treat infection. Baseline CAM-ICU/CAM delirium screen and RASS-targeted light sedation. Review and stop deliriogenic/culprit medications. Frequent neuro checks (q1-2h) and assign correct monitoring level; begin VTE prophylaxis assessment; NPO pending swallow safety
day2_milestones: Reassess and trend mental status (GCS/FOUR); if improving, de-escalate care and daily sedation interruption/spontaneous awakening. Integrate results: EEG (confirm/exclude NCSE), culture/CSF/HSV PCR data to narrow or stop empiric antibiotics/antivirals; obtain MRI brain if cause still unexplained. Continue controlled electrolyte correction with monitoring of rate. Treat reversible delirium contributors (pain, hypoxia, constipation, urinary retention, sleep disruption). Mobilize per ABCDEF bundle, reorientation, restore sensory aids (glasses/hearing). SLP dysphagia screen; advance diet if airway/swallow safe. Remove unnecessary lines/Foley catheter
day3plus_milestones: Confirm definitive diagnosis and complete/continue targeted treatment course. Document cognitive trajectory to baseline or plateau; if prolonged disorder of consciousness, counsel on prognosis (nontraumatic etiology less favorable) and pursue serial standardized assessment. PT/OT/SLP functional evaluation to determine disposition (home vs rehab/SNF). Finalize deprescribing of deliriogenic/culprit agents and medication reconciliation. For substance-related coma, initiate addiction treatment + naloxone kit; for recurrent metabolic causes, optimize chronic regimen (e.g., lactulose titration, diabetes plan). Arrange follow-up and confirm discharge readiness
consults_multidisciplinary: Neurology (coma, seizure/NCSE, stroke) and Neurocritical care/ICU early; Infectious Disease for meningitis/encephalitis; Medical Toxicology/Poison Control for ingestions; Nephrology (uremia, severe dysnatremia, dialyzable toxins), Hepatology (hepatic encephalopathy), Endocrinology (myxedema coma, adrenal crisis) as etiology dictates; Psychiatry (primary psychiatric/substance) and Geriatrics (elderly delirium). Multidisciplinary: PT/OT and Speech-Language Pathology (swallow + cognition) within 24-48h; Pharmacy for medication reconciliation, antidote dosing, and deliriogenic-drug review; Nutrition for early enteral feeding planning; Care Management/Social Work early for disposition, substance-use resources, and caregiver support
monitoring_level: ICU: GCS <=8 or intubation/airway compromise, status epilepticus/NCSE, herniation or elevated ICP needing hypertonic saline, hemodynamic instability, refractory or high-risk toxidrome (e.g., TCA), need for vasoactive/ventilator support. Step-down/telemetry: improving but requiring frequent neuro checks or cardiac monitoring (TCA/dysrhythmia risk), active sedation weaning, controlled sodium correction. Floor: protecting airway, hemodynamically stable, etiology identified and improving. Escalation triggers: GCS drop >=2 points, new focal deficit, clinical or electrographic seizure, hypoxia/hypoventilation, hemodynamic instability, or agitation/delirium unsafe for current setting
nutrition_mobility_vte: NPO until airway secured and SLP dysphagia screen passed; start enteral nutrition within 24-48h if intake will be delayed. Early mobilization and PT via ABCDEF bundle to prevent delirium and deconditioning. VTE prophylaxis: pharmacologic LMWH/UFH unless active hemorrhage/coagulopathy, then mechanical (intermittent pneumatic compression). Glucose target 140-180 mg/dL, avoiding hypoglycemia. Remove Foley catheter and central/peripheral lines as early as possible (reduce CAUTI/CLABSI and delirium). Bowel and bladder regimen, sleep-wake preservation, minimize restraints, and restore glasses/hearing aids
discharge_criteria: Return to baseline mental status OR a stable, safe new baseline with an appropriate care plan; underlying etiology identified and treated or definitive treatment underway; airway protected with stable vitals and adequate oxygenation; no ongoing seizures and corrected/controlling metabolic or toxic derangements; tolerating oral intake or enteral nutrition plan established; delirium resolving with reversible contributors addressed; safe disposition and follow-up secured (capacity/safety assessed if residual cognitive deficit)
discharge_planning: Disposition matched to function/cognition: home with services, or acute rehab/SNF/LTAC for residual deficits. Medication reconciliation with deprescribing of culprit/deliriogenic agents (benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, sedating opioids, polypharmacy in elderly). Follow-up scheduling: PCP within 7 days, Neurology for seizure/stroke, plus etiology-specific specialist; for opioid overdose provide naloxone kit and warm handoff to addiction services. Patient/caregiver education on delirium recurrence, red-flag symptoms, medication safety, and cause-specific prevention (e.g., lactulose adherence, glucose management). Transitions-of-care summary documenting cognitive status, residual deficits, and pending results (final cultures/EEG/MRI)
expected_los: GMLOS benchmark approximately 3.4 days for MS-DRG 080 (nontraumatic stupor & coma WITH MCC) and approximately 2.6 days for MS-DRG 081 (WITHOUT MCC) [CMS IPPS-style figures, approximate-verify against current fiscal-year table]; real-world undifferentiated AMS/coma admissions typically run 3-5 days given diagnostic workup plus treatment of the reversible cause
target_los: 2-3 days under the ideal pathway when a reversible cause (hypoglycemia, simple intoxication, dysnatremia, infection-associated delirium) is rapidly identified and corrected; rapidly reversible toxidromes/hypoglycemia may resolve in <24h (observation/<1 day); persistent unexplained coma, meningoencephalitis, or NCSE will extend beyond 5 days
quality_metrics: Delirium screening compliance (CAM/CAM-ICU) and full ABCDEF-bundle adherence; SEP-1 bundle if sepsis-associated encephalopathy; GWTG-Stroke door-to-CT/door-to-needle metrics if stroke identified; restraint use/hours and inpatient fall rate; medication reconciliation completion; hospital-acquired conditions monitored (CAUTI, CLABSI, aspiration pneumonia, VTE, pressure injury); IMM-2 immunization; 30-day all-cause readmission and inpatient mortality; for overdose cohorts, naloxone-distribution/OUD treatment-initiation (SUB) measures
common_complications: Aspiration pneumonia (mitigate: airway protection, dysphagia screen before PO, head-of-bed elevation); worsening/prolonged delirium (mitigate: ABCDEF bundle, avoid benzodiazepines, reorientation, sleep hygiene); falls and pressure injury (early mobility, prophylaxis); VTE (prophylaxis); iatrogenic harm-over-sedation, osmotic demyelination from too-rapid sodium correction, and missed nonconvulsive status epilepticus (mitigate: RASS-targeted sedation, capped Na correction <=8 mEq/L/24h, early EEG); CAUTI/CLABSI (early catheter/line removal)
readmission_drivers: Unresolved or recurrent underlying cause (recurrent hypoglycemia, decompensated cirrhosis/recurrent hepatic encephalopathy, substance-use relapse, uncontrolled seizures, poorly controlled dysnatremia); missed or evolving diagnosis (subtle NCSE, endocrine/toxic causes); deliriogenic medications and polypharmacy; inadequate follow-up and social/cognitive barriers. Prevention levers: deprescribe culprit meds, addiction treatment + take-home naloxone, lactulose/rifaximin adherence for HE, tight glucose management, structured follow-up, and caregiver education with clear red-flag instructions
guideline_source: Neurocritical Care Society (NCS) Emergency Neurological Life Support (ENLS): Approach to the Patient with Coma, v6.0 (2023-2024); SCCM PADIS Clinical Practice Guidelines 2018 (Devlin et al.) for delirium/sedation; IDSA Practice Guidelines for the Management of Bacterial Meningitis 2004 (Tunkel et al.) for empiric antibiotics + dexamethasone timing; ACNS Consensus Statement on Continuous EEG in Critically Ill Adults and Children 2015 (Herman et al.) for NCSE detection; AAN/ACRM/NIDILRR Disorders of Consciousness Practice Guideline Update 2018 (Giacino et al.) for prolonged DoC prognosis. NOTE: AMS/coma is a symptom-based admission with no single unifying society guideline-this pathway integrates the above plus etiology-specific guidelines (AHA/ASA stroke, ADA hyperglycemic crises, AASLD hepatic encephalopathy, NCS status epilepticus)
key_citations: Wijdicks EF et al. Validation of a new coma scale: The FOUR score. Ann Neurol 2005;58(4):585-93. PMID 16178024, DOI 10.1002/ana.20611. Towne AR et al. Prevalence of nonconvulsive status epilepticus in comatose patients. Neurology 2000;54(2):340-5 (NCSE in ~8% of comatose patients without clinical seizure activity). PMID 10668693, DOI 10.1212/wnl.54.2.340. Devlin JW et al. Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Prevention and Management of Pain, Agitation/Sedation, Delirium, Immobility, and Sleep Disruption in Adult Patients in the ICU (PADIS). Crit Care Med 2018;46(9):e825-e873. PMID 30113379, DOI 10.1097/CCM.0000000000003299. Herman ST et al. ACNS Consensus Statement on Continuous EEG in Critically Ill Adults and Children. J Clin Neurophysiol 2015. PMID 25626778. Giacino JT et al. Practice Guideline Update: Disorders of Consciousness (AAN/ACRM/NIDILRR). Arch Phys Med Rehabil 2018;99(9):1699-1709 (also Neurology 2018). PMID 30098791, DOI 10.1016/j.apmr.2018.07.001. Tunkel AR et al. IDSA Practice Guidelines for the Management of Bacterial Meningitis. Clin Infect Dis 2004;39(9):1267-84. (Citations retrieved via PubMed.)
evidence_grade: Moderate overall. No single Class I society guideline governs undifferentiated AMS/coma-it is a symptom-based syndrome, so the overall diagnostic algorithm rests on expert consensus (NCS ENLS). However, individual component pathways carry strong evidence: Class I/strong for empiric meningitis antibiotic+dexamethasone timing (IDSA), acute stroke reperfusion (AHA/ASA), and status epilepticus treatment (NCS); strong/conditional GRADE recommendations for delirium/sedation (SCCM PADIS 2018); validated scores (FOUR, CAM-ICU). Some elements (early EEG for occult NCSE, disposition timing) are Moderate/consensus and partly extrapolated
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed pair: MS-DRG 080 (WITH MCC) vs 081 (WITHOUT MCC). MCC-tier drivers-coma itself, acute respiratory failure/mechanical ventilation, aspiration pneumonia, sepsis, acute kidney injury, status epilepticus-shift care toward ICU utilization, longer LOS (roughly +0.8-1 day) and higher mortality. Without MCC, cases skew to rapidly reversible toxic-metabolic causes managed on telemetry/floor with short stays. Because the DRG is symptom-based, once a definitive structural/infectious/toxic diagnosis is established the case is frequently REASSIGNED to a more specific DRG (stroke, meningitis, poisoning, seizure, hyperglycemic crisis)-so 080/081 disproportionately capture encephalopathies without a reclassifying structural diagnosis
pathway_pearls: 1) Do the reversible-cause bundle FIRST and protect the airway before extensive workup: bedside glucose + dextrose, thiamine BEFORE glucose in at-risk patients, naloxone for opioid toxidrome, and intubate at GCS <=8-cheap, fast, life-saving. 2) Do not miss nonconvulsive status epilepticus-it causes ~8% of unexplained coma (Towne 2000); get EEG/continuous EEG early in any persistent or unexplained coma. 3) Suspected bacterial meningitis = empiric antibiotics + dexamethasone within 1 hour; never delay for CT or LP. 4) Prevent iatrogenic harm: avoid benzodiazepines for delirium (PADIS), cap sodium correction at <=8 mEq/L/24h to prevent osmotic demyelination, remove lines/catheters early, and run the ABCDEF delirium bundle
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Nontraumatic stupor & coma, encephalopathy workups; ED entry. Frequently a symptom-based admission preceding definitive dx.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 080 — Nontraumatic Stupor and Coma with MCC; 081 — Nontraumatic Stupor and Coma without MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 5/5 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Current pass completed citation-resolvability, coding, taxonomy, schema, and consistency checks. The source workflow contains no completed adversarial high-stakes clinical verdict for this row; clinician revalidation remains required before protocol or order-set use.
data_quality_notes: Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk.

admission_criteria: Pre-MDC procedural group (DRG 003/004): assigned to any inpatient with tracheostomy PLUS invasive mechanical ventilation (MV) >96 consecutive hours (or a qualifying major OR procedure), regardless of underlying diagnosis; there is NO ED-discharge/observation pathway. Upstream ICU-admission trigger: acute respiratory failure needing invasive MV — PaO2/FiO2 <200-300, refractory hypoxemia (SpO2 <90% despite high-flow/NIV), hypercapnic respiratory acidosis (pH <7.30 with rising PaCO2), inability to protect airway (GCS <=8), or excessive work of breathing/impending arrest. Group entry occurs when a ventilated ICU patient FAILS liberation and enters prolonged MV. Tracheostomy indications (drive the DRG): failed extubation x>=2, anticipated/confirmed prolonged ventilation, need for pulmonary toilet/secretion management, unweanable neuromuscular/airway pathology, or upper-airway obstruction.
risk_stratification: Rapid Shallow Breathing Index (RSBI = f/Vt) <105 breaths/min/L predicts SBT/extubation success (>105 predicts failure); SBT tolerance = RR <35, SpO2 >=90%, HR <140, stable BP, no distress over 30-120 min. WIND weaning classification (short/difficult/prolonged) segments trajectory. ProVent / ProVent-14 score at ventilator day 21 (points for age >=50 and >=65, platelets <=150x10^9/L, vasopressor requirement, hemodialysis) stratifies 1-year mortality — >=3-4 risk factors approaches >80-90% 1-yr mortality and should trigger goals-of-care/palliative referral. Cuff-leak test (leak <110 mL or <10-15% of tidal volume) flags post-extubation stridor risk -> pre-extubation corticosteroids. MRC-sum-score <48/60 = ICU-acquired weakness; diaphragm thickening fraction <20% on ultrasound predicts weaning failure. APACHE II/SOFA for global severity; CAM-ICU or ICDSC each shift for delirium; NUTRIC >=5 = high nutrition risk.
initial_workup_labs: ABG (P/F ratio, PaCO2, pH — defines failure severity and weaning readiness); CBC (platelets feed ProVent and pre-procedure bleeding risk; WBC for infection); CMP (renal function for HD scoring/dosing; Na/K); aggressive electrolyte panel with phosphate and magnesium (hypophosphatemia/hypomagnesemia/hypokalemia impair diaphragm and stall weaning -> replete); lactate; coags (INR/PTT/fibrinogen) and type-and-screen before tracheostomy (target platelets >50x10^9/L, INR <1.5, hold/reverse anticoagulation); respiratory + blood cultures and procalcitonin when VAP/VAE suspected; cortisol/ACTH stim if pressor-dependent; TSH; glucose/HbA1c. Chronic phase: weekly CBC/CMP, drug levels, trend nutrition markers (prealbumin — inflammatory, interpret with CRP).
initial_imaging_dx: Daily/PRN CXR (ETT/trach position, new VAP infiltrate, effusion, post-procedure pneumothorax); flexible bronchoscopy for mucus plugging/lobar collapse and to guide bedside percutaneous tracheostomy. Point-of-care ultrasound: diaphragm thickening fraction/excursion (predicts weaning outcome) and lung US for consolidation/effusion; bedside echo to identify cardiac (weaning-induced pulmonary edema) contribution to SBT failure. CT chest for complex/unresolving disease. Procedure: percutaneous dilational tracheostomy at bedside (bronchoscopic/US-guided) vs open surgical tracheostomy in OR — deferred until prolonged ventilation is confirmed (typically day 7-14, NOT <4 days per TracMan). Instrumental swallow evaluation (FEES or videofluoroscopy) by SLP before oral intake and prior to decannulation.
time_critical_interventions: Every-morning coordinated Spontaneous Awakening Trial + Spontaneous Breathing Trial (SAT+SBT, the A-B of the ABCDEF bundle): pass SAT safety screen, lighten sedation, then SBT for 30-120 min on low-level pressure-support (PSV 5-8 cmH2O over PEEP 5) — preferred over T-piece/CPAP (ATS/ACCP 2017). Continuous VAP/VAE bundle from hour 0: head-of-bed 30-45 degrees, sedation minimization, oral care, subglottic secretion drainage ETT when >48-72h ventilation anticipated. Diaphragm- and lung-protective ventilation to avoid ventilator-induced diaphragm dysfunction. Tracheostomy timing: do NOT place routinely <4 days (TracMan — no mortality/LOS benefit; clinicians poorly predict who needs prolonged MV); place once prolonged MV confirmed. Post-percutaneous trach: confirm placement; treat the FIRST tube change (deferred to day 7-10 until tract matures) and any accidental decannulation as an airway emergency. Early mobilization once hemodynamically stable (within 24-72h).
initial_management: Lung/diaphragm-protective ventilation: Vt 6-8 mL/kg predicted body weight, plateau <30 cmH2O, titrate PEEP/FiO2 to SpO2 88-95%, avoid over-assist/over-sedation. Analgesia-first, light sedation (PADIS): opioid (fentanyl/hydromorphone) for pain, then dexmedetomidine or propofol PREFERRED over benzodiazepines; target RASS -2 to 0. Delirium: nonpharmacologic bundle first (reorientation, sleep hygiene, mobility, remove deliriogenics); antipsychotics only for distressing agitation, not routine. VAP/HAP: empiric anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam/cefepime/meropenem) +/- MRSA coverage (vancomycin/linezolid), then de-escalate to a 7-day course. Restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL (TRICC); platelets >50k before trach. Aggressive phosphate/Mg/K repletion to support weaning. Early enteral nutrition. Glucose 140-180 mg/dL. VTE and stress-ulcer prophylaxis while ventilated. Secretion management: heated humidification, scheduled suctioning, mucolytics, chest physiotherapy, mechanical cough-assist. Source control of underlying sepsis. Tracheostomy (percutaneous or open) with initially cuffed tube.
day1_milestones: ICU Day 1 (intubation/stabilization): lock in lung- and diaphragm-protective ventilator settings; establish analgesia-first LIGHT sedation (dexmedetomidine/propofol, RASS -2 to 0), avoid benzodiazepines; initiate VAP/VAE bundle (HOB 30-45 degrees, oral care, subglottic-drainage ETT); start early enteral nutrition within 24-48h; VTE + stress-ulcer prophylaxis; identify and treat the underlying cause with source control; screen SAT/SBT eligibility; central line/arterial line as needed with daily necessity review; first structured family meeting to set expectations and establish surrogate/goals-of-care baseline.
day2_milestones: ICU Day 2-3: perform daily coordinated SAT then SBT (PSV 5-8/PEEP 5, 30-120 min); calculate RSBI (<105 favorable) and assess extubation readiness (adequate oxygenation on FiO2 <=0.4, PEEP <=5-8, hemodynamic stability, intact cough/airway protection, cuff-leak test in high-risk); screen delirium (CAM-ICU/ICDSC) each shift; de-escalate/narrow antibiotics per cultures and stop at day 7 if VAP; begin progressive mobility (passive ROM -> sitting -> standing) once stable; replete phosphate/Mg/K; if extubation fails, classify difficult vs prolonged weaning (WIND) and begin planning trajectory.
day3plus_milestones: Day 4+ through liberation/discharge: continue daily SBT; if extubation fails >=2x or prolonged MV is confirmed, plan tracheostomy at ~day 7-14 (avoid routine <4d — TracMan). Peri-trach POD0: confirm tube position/security, cuffed tube, post-procedure CXR to exclude pneumothorax. POD1+: stoma care, analgesia, defer first tube change to day 7-10 (tract maturation). Weaning: transition ventilated prolonged-MV patients to unassisted tracheostomy-collar sprinting (Jubran — faster weaning than PSV in PMV) with progressive duration; then cuff deflation -> one-way speaking valve -> capping trials; SLP swallow assessment. Decannulation readiness: off ventilator/positive-pressure >=24-48h, tolerates capping/cuff-deflation, effective spontaneous cough, manageable secretions, patent upper airway, safe swallow. If still ventilator-dependent at day ~14-21, transfer to LTACH/regional weaning center.
consults_multidisciplinary: Pulmonary/critical care (primary driver of weaning); ENT or surgery/interventional pulmonology/IR for tracheostomy and airway complications; respiratory therapy (protocolized SBT/weaning, secretion clearance); PT/OT for early progressive mobility; speech-language pathology (swallow evaluation, speaking valve, AAC communication); registered dietitian (enteral regimen, indirect calorimetry, PEG planning); clinical pharmacy (analgosedation, antimicrobial stewardship, delirium meds, renal dosing); palliative care EARLY — especially high ProVent/multiorgan failure — for symptom control and goals-of-care; case management/social work for LTACH/SNF placement and family logistics; psychiatry/psychology for ICU delirium and post-intensive-care syndrome; wound/ostomy for device-related pressure injury; ethics if goals-of-care conflict; physiatry for rehab trajectory.
monitoring_level: ICU (1:1-1:2 nursing, continuous SpO2 +/- EtCO2, telemetry, arterial line) for any actively ventilated or unstable patient. Ventilator step-down/specialized weaning unit once stable on low settings and tolerating SBTs/trach-collar trials. LTACH or regional weaning center for prolonged ventilator dependence (>21 days) that is otherwise medically stable. Escalation triggers back to ICU: acute desaturation, rising ventilator requirements or failed SBT with distress, hemodynamic instability/new pressor need, new sepsis, tracheostomy tube obstruction or accidental decannulation (airway emergency), and any tracheal/stomal sentinel bleed (evaluate urgently for tracheo-innominate fistula).
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: early enteral feeding within 24-48h, 25-30 kcal/kg/day and protein 1.2-2.0 g/kg/day (ASPEN/SCCM), guided by indirect calorimetry where available; AVOID overfeeding (excess CO2 load impairs weaning); place PEG when prolonged enteral access is anticipated; advance to oral intake only after a passed instrumental swallow. Mobility: early progressive mobilization/rehabilitation (PADIS 2025 conditional recommendation for enhanced rehab) but dose appropriately — TEAM trial showed no ventilator-free-day benefit and more adverse events with high-intensity aggressive mobilization. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH/UFH) plus mechanical devices. Glucose 140-180 mg/dL. Daily line/Foley necessity review with prompt removal; daily sedation lightening; bowel regimen; meticulous device-related pressure-injury prevention (trach flange/ties, ETT).
discharge_criteria: Discharge from acute hospital (usually to LTACH/SNF, sometimes home): hemodynamically stable off vasopressors; source of sepsis/infection controlled and afebrile trend; EITHER liberated from the ventilator OR stable on chronic ventilator settings appropriate for the receiving setting; tracheostomy stoma mature and stable with secretions that are manageable by the destination's capability; nutrition established (enteral/PEG tolerated, or safe oral intake); for decannulated patients — >=24-48h of capping tolerance, effective cough, passed swallow, and a patent airway; durable medical equipment (ventilator, suction, oxygen, trach supplies) and trained caregivers/facility secured; goals of care and code status documented.
discharge_planning: Disposition tiered to ventilator dependence: LTACH/ventilator-weaning unit (most patients still vent-dependent — Medicare LTACH site-neutral criteria generally require prior ICU stay or >=96h MV), ventilator-capable SNF, inpatient rehab, or home with home-health plus DME (ventilator, suction, oxygen concentrator, trach/consumables). Complete medication reconciliation with sedation/antibiotic taper plans. Schedule follow-up: pulmonary/critical care clinic, ENT for trach management/decannulation, PCP, and rehab. Structured caregiver/facility training and return-demonstration on suctioning, trach care, speaking-valve use, and the accidental-decannulation emergency plan, with written instructions. Warm handoff to LTACH including an explicit weaning plan. Revisit advance directives. Enroll in post-ICU/PICS follow-up addressing physical, cognitive, and psychological recovery.
expected_los: Among the highest-LOS DRGs in the IPPS. Acute-hospital benchmarks (approximate FY2024-25 IPPS): DRG 003 (with major OR procedure) GMLOS ~28 days, AMLOS ~34 days, relative weight ~17-18; DRG 004 (without major OR procedure) GMLOS ~14 days, AMLOS ~19 days, relative weight ~10-11. Total ventilator-dependent illness course frequently extends 30-60+ days when the subsequent LTACH/weaning phase is included.
target_los: Optimized acute-hospital target ~20-24 days for DRG 003 and ~11-13 days for DRG 004, achieved by protocolized daily SAT+SBT/ABCDEF bundle to minimize ventilator days, aggressive VAE and ICU-acquired-weakness prevention, and TIMELY transfer to an LTACH/weaning center once the patient is acutely stable but still ventilator-dependent (~day 14-21). Further acute-LOS reduction is constrained by chronic-critical-illness biology, not by hospital process; the highest-yield lever is avoiding preventable ventilator days rather than premature discharge.
quality_metrics: NHSN Ventilator-Associated Event (VAE)/VAP rate and ventilator-utilization ratio; ventilator-free days; ABCDEF bundle and daily SAT+SBT compliance; sedation-in-target (% time at goal RASS) and delirium-free/coma-free days (CAM-ICU); reintubation within 48-72h and unplanned-extubation rate; tracheostomy complication and decannulation rates; time-to-LTACH-transfer; ICU/hospital and 1-year mortality; 30-day readmission; CLABSI/CAUTI; hospital-acquired pressure injury (incl. device-related); early-mobilization compliance; palliative-care consult rate for high-ProVent patients. CMS: reported via PSI-90 composite and NHSN device-associated infection reporting (no single condition-specific CMS core measure).
common_complications: VAP/VAE (mitigate: HOB 30-45 degrees, oral care, sedation minimization, subglottic drainage); ventilator-induced diaphragm dysfunction and ICU-acquired weakness (minimize sedation, diaphragm-protective vent, early mobility); delirium (ABCDEF bundle); tracheostomy-specific — early stomal hemorrhage, LATE tracheo-innominate artery fistula (sentinel bleed = emergency), tube obstruction/mucus plugging (humidification/suction), accidental decannulation (secure tube, defer first change to day 7-10), stomal infection, and late tracheal stenosis/granulation/tracheomalacia and tracheoesophageal fistula; pneumothorax (percutaneous procedure); VTE/PE; GI stress-ulcer bleeding; malnutrition/catabolism; CLABSI/CAUTI; AKI; dysphagia and aspiration.
readmission_drivers: Recurrent pneumonia/respiratory failure; tracheostomy tube obstruction or mucus plugging; aspiration events; trach complications (bleeding, stenosis, granulation, tracheomalacia); inadequate caregiver training or insufficient home support; premature or unsafe decannulation; recurrent sepsis/line infection; progressive deconditioning and failure to thrive. Prevention levers: rigorous caregiver/facility competency training with a written accidental-decannulation emergency plan, reliable heated humidification and suctioning, scheduled ENT/pulmonary follow-up, swallow-safety verification before oral intake, LTACH continuity-of-care handoff, and early goals-of-care alignment/palliative involvement to avoid non-beneficial rehospitalization.
guideline_source: No single society 'tracheostomy/prolonged-MV' master guideline exists; pathway synthesizes: ATS/ACCP (CHEST) 2017 Clinical Practice Guideline on Liberation from Mechanical Ventilation; SCCM PADIS 2018 Guidelines and 2025 Focused Update (pain/anxiety/agitation-sedation/delirium/immobility/sleep, ABCDEF bundle); SHEA/IDSA 2022 Update on Strategies to Prevent VAP/VAE/NV-HAP; ASPEN/SCCM 2016 critical-care nutrition guidelines; plus landmark RCTs on tracheostomy timing (TracMan 2013) and PMV weaning method (Jubran 2013), and Global Tracheostomy Collaborative consensus for trach care/decannulation.
key_citations: Fan E et al. Liberation from Mechanical Ventilation in Critically Ill Adults: An Official ATS/ACCP Clinical Practice Guideline. Ann Am Thorac Soc 2017;14(3):441-443 (PMID 28029806, DOI 10.1513/AnnalsATS.201612-993CME). Young D et al. Effect of Early vs Late Tracheostomy on Survival (TracMan RCT). JAMA 2013;309(20):2121-2129 (PMID 23695482, DOI 10.1001/jama.2013.5154). Jubran A et al. Tracheostomy collar vs pressure-support weaning in prolonged MV: RCT. JAMA 2013;309(7):671-677 (PMID 23340588). Lewis K et al. Focused Update to PADIS Guidelines. Crit Care Med 2025;53(3):e711-e727 (PMID 39982143, DOI 10.1097/CCM.0000000000006574). Hodgson CL et al. Early Active Mobilization during Mechanical Ventilation (TEAM). N Engl J Med 2022 (PMID 36286256). Klompas M et al. Strategies to Prevent VAP/VAE/NV-HAP: 2022 Update. Infect Control Hosp Epidemiol 2022 (PMID 35589091). Risk model: ProVent/ProVent-14 score (Carson; multicenter validation). Citations retrieved via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Moderate overall (mixed). STRONG / Class I (high-quality RCTs + society guidelines) for the liberation core — daily coordinated SAT+SBT with low-level PSV, light analgesia-first sedation favoring dexmedetomidine/propofol over benzodiazepines, restrictive transfusion, VAP/VAE-prevention bundle, and appropriately-dosed early mobility. WEAKER / consensus-expert for tracheostomy timing (TracMan NEGATIVE — no mortality/LOS benefit to early trach), decannulation criteria, PMV weaning method (single-center RCT evidence), and LTACH-transition practices. The pathway is a synthesis rather than a single validated care bundle, so overall grade is capped at Moderate.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: This is a Pre-MDC procedural group defined by resource use, not by an underlying diagnosis: by definition every patient already meets an MCC-level acuity (tracheostomy + MV >96h). The collapsed pair splits on procedure burden — DRG 003 (tracheostomy WITH a qualifying major OR procedure or highest complexity) carries the longer stay (GMLOS ~28d, RW ~17-18) and heavier ICU footprint; DRG 004 (tracheostomy + MV >96h WITHOUT a major OR procedure) is shorter (GMLOS ~14d, RW ~10-11). Accumulating organ failures that populate the ProVent score (vasopressor dependence, hemodialysis, thrombocytopenia) plus ARDS, neuromuscular injury, and nosocomial infection push toward prolonged ICU course, LTACH dependence, and markedly higher 1-year mortality — the practical severity axis within the group.
pathway_pearls: 1) Do NOT rush the tracheostomy — TracMan shows no mortality or LOS benefit to placement <4 days and clinicians poorly predict who needs prolonged ventilation; trach when prolonged MV is confirmed (~day 7-14) after failed liberation. 2) Liberate relentlessly — every-morning coordinated SAT+SBT (ABCDEF bundle) with low-level PSV SBTs, light analgosedation (dexmedetomidine/propofol, avoid benzodiazepines); in already-tracheostomized PMV patients, unassisted trach-collar sprinting weans faster than pressure support (Jubran). 3) Prevent VAE and ICU-acquired weakness — HOB 30-45 degrees, oral care, minimize sedation, diaphragm-protective ventilation, and appropriately-dosed early mobility are the biggest levers on ventilator days and LOS. 4) Right-site and right-goal early — once acutely stable but vent-dependent, transfer promptly to an LTACH/weaning unit, and align goals of care with palliative care early (high ProVent score = very high 1-year mortality).

admission_criteria: Severe TBI (GCS 3-8): intubate + neuro-ICU admission mandatory. Moderate (GCS 9-12): admit ICU/step-down. ANY positive head CT (contusion, SDH, EDH, tSAH, IVH, DAI, depressed/basilar skull fx) admits regardless of GCS. Mild TBI (GCS 13-15) admits if: abnormal CT, focal deficit, seizure, anticoagulation/coagulopathy, persistent vomiting, GCS not returning to 15, or unreliable social situation; use Canadian CT Head Rule / New Orleans Criteria to select who needs CT. Isolated concussion with GCS 15 + normal CT + reliable caregiver = ED discharge with return precautions or brief (<24h) observation, NOT inpatient. Neuro-worsening at any point mandates admission/escalation.
risk_stratification: Glasgow Coma Scale: 3-8 severe, 9-12 moderate, 13-15 mild (drives airway/disposition); GCS-Pupils (GCS-P) score adds pupil reactivity. CT severity: Marshall classification (I-VI) and Rotterdam CT score (2-6; higher predicts higher ICP and 6-mo mortality). Prognostic calculators: IMPACT and CRASH models (age, GCS/motor, pupil reactivity, CT findings, hypoxia/hypotension, labs) estimate 6-mo mortality/unfavorable outcome and inform goals-of-care. Actions: salvageable severe TBI with abnormal CT -> place ICP monitor; normal CT with >=2 of (age>40, uni/bilateral motor posturing, SBP<90) -> also monitor ICP (BTF). Anticoagulated: check INR/anti-Xa to trigger reversal.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, CMP, coagulation panel (PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen), type & screen/crossmatch, ABG + lactate, POC glucose, ethanol level, urine toxicology, beta-hCG. Anticoagulant assays when relevant: anti-Xa (DOAC/LMWH), dilute thrombin time/thrombin time (dabigatran). Viscoelastic testing (TEG/ROTEM) for trauma-induced coagulopathy. Baseline serum sodium/osmolality (guides hyperosmolar therapy) and platelet count. Triggers: elevated INR or DOAC-positive -> immediate reversal; plt <75-100k or antiplatelet use + planned neurosurgery -> platelets +/- DDAVP; fibrinogen <150-200 -> cryo/fibrinogen; establish Na baseline for later CSW/SIADH/DI surveillance.
initial_imaging_dx: Non-contrast CT head STAT (target <30-60 min from arrival for moderate/severe or CT-decision-rule-positive mild) - the diagnostic cornerstone. CT cervical spine for associated injury; CTA head/neck if blunt cerebrovascular injury suspected (expanded Denver/Memphis criteria, basilar/cervical-spine fx). Routine repeat/interval NCCT head at ~6h (or immediately if any neuro-worsening) to detect hematoma progression/delayed traumatic ICH; further repeat within 24h. MRI brain for DAI or when CT-negative but exam discordant/prognostication (non-emergent). Invasive neuromonitoring in severe TBI: ICP monitor (EVD preferred if hydrocephalus/CSF diversion, else parenchymal bolt) +/- brain tissue oxygen (PbtO2) probe; cEEG if unexplained depressed consciousness.
time_critical_interventions: Prevent secondary injury (biggest mortality lever): avoid hypoxia (keep SpO2>=90/PaO2>=60) and hypotension - maintain SBP >=110 mmHg (age 15-49 or >70) or >=100 (age 50-69) per BTF; secure airway if GCS<=8. Herniation rescue: hyperosmolar bolus (23.4% hypertonic saline or mannitol 0.5-1 g/kg) + transient hyperventilation to PaCO2 30-35 as a bridge to OR/CT only. Tranexamic acid within 3h of injury for GCS 9-15 with intracranial bleeding (CRASH-3): 1g IV over 10 min then 1g over 8h. Anticoagulation reversal ASAP (door-to-reversal <=60-90 min). Emergent neurosurgical evacuation for EDH/SDH with mass effect/herniation (grouped under craniotomy DRG). Early transfer to trauma/neurosurgical center if not on site.
initial_management: ABCs; RSI/intubation if GCS<=8. Targets: ICP <22 mmHg, CPP 60-70 mmHg (BTF 4th ed). HOB 30 deg, midline neck, normothermia, normocapnia (PaCO2 35-45), analgosedation (fentanyl + propofol; avoid hypotension). Osmotherapy: hypertonic saline (3% infusion; 23.4% bolus for herniation) or mannitol 0.25-1 g/kg. Hemodynamics: isotonic crystalloid + norepinephrine to CPP goal; avoid hypotonic/glucose-containing fluids. Transfusion: restrictive Hgb ~7 g/dL threshold generally (a higher ~9 g/dL target in acute severe TBI is debated - HEMOTION 2024 showed no clear benefit). Reversal (NCS/SCCM): warfarin -> 4F-PCC + IV vitamin K 10 mg; dabigatran -> idarucizumab; apixaban/rivaroxaban -> andexanet alfa or 4F-PCC; UFH -> protamine; antiplatelet + neurosurgery -> platelets +/- DDAVP. Antiseizure prophylaxis x7 days (levetiracetam preferred, or phenytoin). Glucose 140-180 mg/dL; mechanical VTE prophylaxis immediately.
day1_milestones: Secure airway/hemodynamics; achieve ICP<22 / CPP 60-70; serial GCS + pupils q1h. Obtain repeat NCCT at ~6h; place EVD/ICP monitor and/or neurosurgical decompression as indicated. Complete anticoagulation reversal; finish TXA infusion. Maintain normoxia/normotension/normocapnia/normothermia/normoglycemia bundle. Start mechanical VTE prophylaxis; begin early enteral nutrition planning (initiate within 24-48h). Initiate goals-of-care/family communication for devastating injury (IMPACT/CRASH-informed). Mild/concussion tier: observation, antiemetics + acetaminophen, confirm GCS 15 and stable exam, plan discharge with return precautions.
day2_milestones: Stabilize/consolidate ICP control; if safe, perform sedation holiday for neurologic exam (SIBICC sedation-hold heatmap) and wean hyperosmolar therapy as ICP allows. Start pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (LMWH/UFH) at 24-72h post-injury if hemorrhage stable on repeat CT (ACS TQIP). Advance enteral feeds toward caloric goal; SLP swallow evaluation before oral intake. Begin PT/OT mobilization once ICP tolerant; assess extubation readiness/spontaneous breathing trial. Repeat CT if any change. Moderate/mild tiers: reassess symptom trajectory; discharge if improving with stable CT.
day3plus_milestones: Remove ICP monitor/EVD per resolution criteria; extubate; de-escalate neuro-ICU -> step-down -> floor. Confirm imaging stability; wean sedation and assess arousal/command-following. Complete the 7-day antiseizure prophylaxis course then STOP (continue only if clinical/electrographic seizure occurred - deprescribe). Early PM&R/rehabilitation consult and disposition planning (home vs acute inpatient rehab vs SNF/LTAC). Decide tracheostomy/PEG (~day 5-7) if persistently low arousal or failure to protect airway. Establish return-to-activity/sport graduated plan and discharge readiness for milder injuries.
consults_multidisciplinary: Neurosurgery immediately for all intracranial injury; Trauma surgery for polytrauma; Neurocritical care/intensivist for severe TBI. PM&R/rehabilitation early (drives disposition); PT/OT/SLP. Neurology for seizures/cEEG interpretation. Pharmacy (reversal agents, osmotherapy, ASM dosing, anticoagulation stewardship); Registered dietitian (early enteral nutrition); Case management + social work early for disposition/transitions; Palliative care for devastating/non-survivable injury. Mild TBI: concussion clinic/PCP referral, psychiatry/neuropsychology as needed; Ophthalmology/OMFS/ENT for associated orbital/facial injury.
monitoring_level: Neuro-ICU: GCS<=8, ICP monitor in place, post-craniotomy/craniectomy, intubated, or refractory ICP - with continuous ICP/CPP, ETCO2, arterial line, often PbtO2, and cEEG for unexplained coma. ICU or step-down: moderate TBI (GCS 9-12) with q1-2h neuro checks. Step-down/telemetry or monitored floor: mild TBI with abnormal CT or anticoagulation, neuro checks q2-4h + surveillance repeat CT. Escalation triggers (STAT CT + escalate care): GCS drop >=2 points, new pupillary asymmetry/fixed pupil, sustained ICP>22, CPP<60, new seizure, or Cushing response (HTN + bradycardia + irregular respirations).
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: initiate enteral feeding within 24-48h, reach full caloric goal by day 5-7 (BTF); SLP-guided diet advancement; glycemic target 140-180 mg/dL (avoid hypo/hyperglycemia). Mobility: early PT/OT once ICP tolerant, HOB 30 deg, progressive mobilization. VTE: mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs) on admission; add pharmacologic LMWH/UFH at 24-72h once hemorrhage stable on repeat CT (TQIP); IVC filter only if anticoagulation contraindicated. Line/catheter stewardship: remove EVD, arterial/central lines, and Foley as soon as clinically unnecessary to reduce ventriculitis/CLABSI/CAUTI.
discharge_criteria: Neurologically stable or improving with ICP-directed therapy discontinued and stable/improving repeat CT (no ongoing hematoma expansion). Liberated from ventilator or stable tracheostomy. Tolerating nutrition (safe PO or established enteral access). Pain and seizures controlled; hemodynamically stable off vasopressors; VTE prophylaxis established. Safe disposition with appropriate ongoing level of care arranged. Mild/concussion tier: return to GCS 15, symptoms controlled/improving, reliable caregiver present, and documented return precautions.
discharge_planning: Disposition matched to function: home, acute inpatient rehabilitation (moderate-severe with rehab potential), or SNF/LTAC (prolonged low-arousal/trach-PEG). Medication reconciliation including antiseizure stop/taper and an explicit, multidisciplinary anticoagulation-resumption plan (timing balances rebleed vs thrombosis). Follow-up: neurosurgery clinic with interval imaging (and cranioplasty planning post-craniectomy), PM&R/rehab, concussion clinic/PCP for mild TBI. Education: post-concussive symptoms, graduated return-to-work/school/sport protocol, driving restrictions, red-flag return precautions; caregiver support. Transitions: warm handoff and screening for post-TBI depression/PTSD.
expected_los: Wide collapsed group; CMS FY22 GMLOS by tier: concussion DRG 088/089/090 = 3.6/2.7/1.9d; coma <1h DRG 085/086/087 = 4.6/3.0/2.0d; coma >1h DRG 082/083/084 = 4.2/3.3/2.1d. Blended ~2-4 days; MCC tiers have arithmetic mean LOS up to 6.4-6.5 days. Refractory ICP crisis, decompressive craniectomy, or tracheostomy/PEG extends stay to 2-4+ weeks.
target_los: Concussion/mild (090/089): <=1-2 days or ED-observation pathway; moderate or coma without CC/MCC (084/087): 2-3 days; uncomplicated nonoperative severe TBI: ~4-5 days to step-down then transfer. Key lever = zero avoidable ICU days plus early acute-rehab transfer rather than prolonged acute-care boarding once neurologically stable.
quality_metrics: ACS TQIP TBI risk-adjusted mortality (registry benchmark); adherence to CPP/ICP targets and documented avoidance of hypoxia/hypotension (secondary-insult metrics); time-to-CT and time-to-neurosurgical intervention. TXA-within-3h rate; anticoagulation door-to-reversal time. VTE prophylaxis initiation timeliness and VTE/PE event rate; hospital-acquired infection rates (CLABSI/CAUTI/VAP/ventriculitis). Early rehabilitation consult rate; goals-of-care documentation; 30-day readmission and ED-return-after-mild-TBI rate; appropriate 7-day (not indefinite) antiseizure prophylaxis.
common_complications: Hematoma progression / delayed traumatic ICH (mitigate: 6h + 24h repeat CT, coagulopathy correction). Intracranial hypertension/herniation (tiered SIBICC therapy, osmotherapy). Post-traumatic seizures (7-day prophylaxis, cEEG). Sodium disorders - cerebral salt wasting, SIADH, diabetes insipidus (serial Na, targeted therapy). Paroxysmal sympathetic hyperactivity/dysautonomia. Ventilator-associated pneumonia, VTE/PE, pressure injury, ICU delirium (bundled prevention). Post-craniectomy hydrocephalus and syndrome of the trephined.
readmission_drivers: Persistent post-concussive syndrome / uncontrolled symptoms; new/recurrent seizures. Delayed or chronic subdural hematoma, especially anticoagulated elderly (levers: careful anticoagulation stewardship, reversal, interval imaging, structured follow-up). Recurrent falls in elderly (fall-prevention program, medication/anticoagulation review). Post-surgical complications - shunt-dependent hydrocephalus, cranioplasty/wound infection, VTE. Inadequate rehab access or follow-up, and untreated substance use.
guideline_source: Brain Trauma Foundation - Guidelines for the Management of Severe Traumatic Brain Injury, 4th Edition (2016/2017; still current in 2026); ACS TQIP Best Practices in the Management of Traumatic Brain Injury (revised 2024); Seattle International Severe TBI Consensus Conference (SIBICC) tiered management algorithm (2019); Neurocritical Care Society/SCCM Guideline for Reversal of Antithrombotics in Intracranial Hemorrhage (2016); Neurocritical Care Society Guidelines for Seizure Prophylaxis in Adults Hospitalized with Moderate-Severe TBI (2024).

RESCUEicp decompressive craniectomy. NEJM 2016;375(12):1119-30 (PMID 27602507). Frontera JA, et al. Reversal of Antithrombotics in ICH (NCS/SCCM). Neurocrit Care 2016;24(1):6-46 (PMID 26714677). NCS Seizure Prophylaxis in Moderate-Severe TBI. Neurocrit Care 2024 (PMID 38316735). Papa L, Stiell IG, et al. Canadian CT Head Rule vs New Orleans Criteria. Acad Emerg Med 2012;19(1):2-10 (PMID 22251188).
evidence_grade: Strong overall - anchored by a Class I society guideline (BTF 4th ed) plus landmark RCTs. Strong for secondary-insult prevention and physiologic targets (ICP>22, CPP 60-70, avoid hypoxia/hypotension; BTF Level IIA/IIB) and for early TXA in mild-moderate TBI (CRASH-3, high-quality RCT). Moderate for decompressive craniectomy (RESCUEicp/DECRA RCTs - mortality reduction traded against higher severe disability/vegetative state). Consensus/expert (Class III) for the SIBICC tiered ICP algorithm and much of ICU micro-management where RCT evidence is lacking.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: This collapsed group spans mild concussion to prolonged coma, so CC/MCC status is the dominant driver of intensity. Concussion tier (088-090): mild, frequently observation/short-stay (GMLOS 1.9-3.6d); the MCC on 088 usually reflects comorbid/associated injury rather than brain severity. Coma tiers (082-087): MCC (aspiration/VAP, respiratory failure, sepsis, major hemorrhage, cerebral edema) drives neuro-ICU admission, mechanical ventilation, ICP monitoring, tracheostomy/PEG, longer LOS (GMLOS up to 4.6d, mean up to 6.5d), and higher mortality; without CC/MCC these run ~2 days. Note operative evacuation (craniotomy) groups to separate surgical DRGs, so 082-090 skew toward nonoperatively-managed injuries.
pathway_pearls: 1) Obsessively prevent secondary insults - no hypoxia, no hypotension (SBP>=110), maintain CPP 60-70/ICP<22 - this is the single biggest mortality lever. 2) Time-critical trio for the bleeding brain: TXA within 3h (CRASH-3), rapid anticoagulation reversal (door-to-reversal <=60-90 min), and mandatory repeat CT at ~6h to catch hematoma progression. 3) Give antiseizure prophylaxis for ONLY 7 days (levetiracetam) then deprescribe - indefinite continuation is a common, avoidable error. 4) Start pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis at 24-72h once the hemorrhage is stable, and engage PM&R/rehabilitation and goals-of-care early - both shorten avoidable acute-care LOS.

admission_criteria: Most acute LBP/sciatica is self-limited and should be discharged or managed outpatient (ACP/NICE) — do NOT admit uncomplicated mechanical back pain. Reserve inpatient admission for: intractable pain despite maximized ED multimodal analgesia (cannot mobilize/perform ADLs or needs parenteral opioids); new or progressive motor deficit or suspected cauda equina (surgical emergency); red flags needing inpatient workup/treatment — suspected spinal infection (fever, IVDU, immunosuppression, recent bacteremia, high ESR/CRP), suspected malignancy/pathologic fracture (known cancer, unexplained weight loss, night/rest pain), suspected cord compression; acute vertebral compression fracture with uncontrolled pain or inability to mobilize; failed safe discharge from functional decline (cannot ambulate/toilet), esp. frail elderly living alone. Observation (<48h) for pain expected to resolve quickly without red flags or while awaiting PT clearance; ED discharge for ambulatory, orally-controlled pain without red flags.
risk_stratification: No validated 'admission' score exists for back pain — disposition is red-flag- and function-driven. STarT Back Screening Tool (9 items, 0-9): total <=3 = low risk (self-management/education); total >=4 with psychosocial subscale [items 5-9] <=3 = medium (structured physiotherapy); psychosocial subscale >=4 = high (psychologically-informed PT) — matched pathways cut disability/cost (Hill, Lancet 2011). Red-flag screen is the critical stratifier: cauda equina (saddle anesthesia, bilateral sciatica, urinary retention with post-void residual >100-200 mL, fecal incontinence, progressive weakness) -> EMERGENT MRI + spine surgery; infection flags (fever, IVDU, immunosuppression, indwelling lines) -> ESR/CRP + blood cultures + MRI; malignancy flags (prior cancer = highest yield, weight loss, age >50, pain >6 wk, night pain) -> MRI + labs; fracture flags (age >70, osteoporosis, chronic glucocorticoids, trauma) -> radiographs +/- MRI. qSOFA >=2 or SIRS if infection -> sepsis pathway. FRAX/BMD to quantify osteoporosis after fragility VCF.
initial_workup_labs: Uncomplicated mechanical LBP needs NO routine labs. When red flags present: CBC with differential; ESR AND CRP (both — high sensitivity for vertebral osteomyelitis/inflammation per IDSA; elevation -> pursue infection/malignancy/inflammatory cause); CMP (renal function to dose NSAIDs/gadolinium). Infection suspected: 2 sets blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics (positive in ~58% of vertebral osteomyelitis, IDSA), +/- procalcitonin; urinalysis. Malignancy suspected: SPEP/UPEP + serum free light chains (myeloma), calcium, alkaline phosphatase, LDH, PSA (men). Inflammatory back pain (young, morning stiffness, alternating buttock pain): HLA-B27, ESR/CRP. VCF: calcium, phosphate, 25-OH vitamin D, PTH, TSH, alk phos, SPEP (secondary-osteoporosis and myeloma screen).
initial_imaging_dx: Key stewardship lever: NO imaging for uncomplicated LBP within the first 6 weeks absent red flags (ACP/ACR AC 2021/Choosing Wisely; OP-8 measure). Red flags drive imaging: suspected cauda equina/cord compression/progressive deficit -> EMERGENT MRI lumbar (+/- whole spine) without and with contrast, within hours; suspected spinal infection (osteomyelitis/discitis/epidural abscess) -> MRI with and without gadolinium (most sensitive/specific, IDSA), target <24h, emergent if deficit; suspected malignancy -> MRI with contrast (CT/PET/bone scan per oncology); suspected fracture -> plain radiographs first, then MRI with STIR to date acute vs old edema and assess augmentation candidacy. If MRI contraindicated -> CT myelogram or CT. Image-guided (CT) biopsy of disc/endplate if osteomyelitis suspected and blood cultures negative (IDSA).
time_critical_interventions: Cauda equina syndrome = surgical emergency: emergent MRI within hours + immediate spine/neurosurgery consult; decompression ideally <48h from onset (earlier better for bladder/bowel recovery). Spinal epidural abscess with neuro deficit: emergent MRI, blood cultures then empiric IV antibiotics, urgent neurosurgical decompression — deficits progress rapidly. Malignant spinal cord compression: immediate dexamethasone (e.g., 10 mg IV then 4-6 mg q6h) + urgent MRI + radiation oncology/neurosurgery within 24h. Sepsis from spinal infection: Surviving Sepsis bundle — lactate, cultures, antibiotics <=1h, fluids. For the common intractable-pain admission there is no minute-level target; goal is rapid multimodal analgesia and out-of-bed mobilization within hours plus a Day-1 PT consult.
initial_management: Multimodal, opioid-sparing analgesia (ACP/NICE): scheduled NSAID first-line (ibuprofen, naproxen, or short-course ketorolac — monitor renal/GI) + scheduled acetaminophen (adjunct; ineffective as monotherapy for acute LBP); short-course skeletal muscle relaxant for acute pain (cyclobenzaprine, methocarbamol, tizanidine — avoid/limit in elderly per Beers). Radicular pain: gabapentinoids of limited benefit (NICE NG59 advises against for sciatica); duloxetine an option for chronic. Short-acting opioids only for severe refractory pain at lowest effective dose/shortest duration (ACP last-line) with a mandatory bowel regimen. Core non-pharmacologic: superficial heat, reassurance/education, stay active, early PT — NOT bed rest. VCF: analgesia +/- short-term calcitonin, TLSO bracing, early mobilization, START osteoporosis therapy (calcium/vit D + bisphosphonate/denosumab/anabolic); vertebral augmentation (kypho/vertebroplasty) only for refractory pain — evidence conflicting (sham-controlled negative, open-label positive), shared decision. Infection: empiric IV vancomycin + cefepime/ceftriaxone after cultures, narrow to culture, ~6-week course (IDSA). Cord compression: dexamethasone + RT/surgery. IV fluids only if volume-depleted; transfusion not applicable (non-hemorrhagic condition).
day1_milestones: Document full neuro exam + structured red-flag screen and check post-void residual if retention suspected; risk-stratify with STarT Back. Establish multimodal analgesia and titrate to a functional pain level (able to mobilize); track NRS. Order targeted labs/imaging ONLY if red flags — resist reflex MRI. Place PT and OT consults Day 1; get patient out of bed / begin early mobilization. If infection suspected: 2 blood cultures drawn, empiric antibiotics started, ID consult. If cauda equina/cord compression: MRI + surgical consult completed emergently. VTE risk assessment (Padua/Caprini) and prophylaxis; start bowel regimen with any opioid; set expected LOS and begin discharge planning.
day2_milestones: Reassess pain and transition IV->PO analgesia; begin opioid taper. PT advances mobility — assess ambulation, transfers, stairs; OT evaluates ADLs and home safety. Review pending imaging/culture results and refine the diagnosis (narrow antibiotics, confirm or exclude serious pathology). For VCF: complete secondary-osteoporosis workup and initiate bone therapy; order DXA if not done. Care management/social work engaged for disposition (home vs home-health vs SNF/acute rehab). Confirm no evolving red flags; de-escalate monitoring frequency.
day3plus_milestones: Pain controlled on an oral regimen; mobility independent or safe-with-assist per PT. Definitive diagnosis and targeted plan finalized — antibiotic course/OPAT for osteomyelitis, augmentation decision for VCF, surgical plan if applicable, osteoporosis meds started. Disposition confirmed and equipment/services arranged; medication reconciliation with opioid stewardship. Most uncomplicated admissions reach discharge readiness by Day 2-3; complicated cases (infection, neuro deficit, frailty) extend to Day 4+.
consults_multidisciplinary: PT (mobility/function) + OT (ADLs/home safety) — Day 1, the cornerstone. Care management/social work — early for disposition and transitions. Pharmacy — multimodal analgesia, opioid stewardship, renal dosing, osteoporosis meds. Spine surgery/neurosurgery/orthopedic spine — neuro deficit, cauda equina, instability, augmentation candidacy. Infectious diseases — vertebral osteomyelitis/discitis/epidural abscess (co-manage per IDSA). Interventional radiology/pain — image-guided biopsy, vertebral augmentation, epidural steroid injection for radiculopathy. PM&R — multidisciplinary rehab and subacute-rehab planning. Oncology/radiation oncology — malignant cord compression/pathologic fracture. Rheumatology — inflammatory spondyloarthropathy. Endocrinology — complex/secondary osteoporosis. Dietitian — frail/malnourished (impacts LOS).
monitoring_level: General medical floor is standard — this is a low-acuity DRG; telemetry only for a comorbid cardiac indication. Step-down/ICU reserved for sepsis/septic shock from spinal infection, high parenteral-opioid needs with respiratory-depression risk, evolving cord compression requiring frequent neuro checks, or unstable comorbidity. Neuro checks q2-4h if any deficit or post-decompression. Escalation triggers: new/progressive weakness or a sensory level -> emergent MRI + surgery; new saddle anesthesia or urinary retention (rising PVR) -> cauda equina workup; qSOFA >=2 or lactate elevation -> sepsis pathway; opioid-induced respiratory depression -> naloxone, capnography for high-risk patients.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: regular, no restriction; ensure protein, calcium and vitamin D (esp. VCF); dietitian for frail/malnourished. Mobility: EARLY mobilization is the central lever — out of bed Day 1, PT-directed progressive ambulation; AVOID bed rest (prolongs recovery, ACP/NICE). VTE: risk-assess (Padua/Caprini); immobile inpatients get pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin or UFH) unless bleeding risk; mechanical SCDs if anticoagulation contraindicated; fully ambulatory patients may not need pharmacologic prophylaxis. Glucose <180 mg/dL; watch steroid-induced hyperglycemia. Scheduled bowel regimen (senna/docusate +/- PEG) with every opioid. Minimize lines/Foley — remove early to prevent CAUTI/falls; if retention/cauda equina, catheterize transiently and trend PVR.
discharge_criteria: Pain controlled on an oral regimen at a functional level; safe mobility per PT (ambulate/transfer independently or with confirmed help/equipment); voiding without retention (cauda equina excluded); serious pathology excluded OR a definitive treatment plan established; neurologically stable with no progressive deficit; disposition, follow-up and medications (including bowel regimen and, for VCF, osteoporosis therapy) reconciled and arranged.
discharge_planning: Disposition: home for most, home-health PT, or SNF/acute rehab for functional decline. Medication reconciliation: limit opioids to a short course (<7 days) with taper, NSAID with GI/renal precautions, bowel regimen, and osteoporosis therapy for VCF (bisphosphonate/denosumab + calcium/vitamin D). Follow-up: PCP within 1-2 weeks; spine/PM&R/neurosurgery as indicated; ID with OPAT and weekly labs (CBC, ESR/CRP, renal) for osteomyelitis; DXA/endocrine referral for VCF. Education with teach-back: stay active, self-management/exercise, STarT Back-matched pathway, and explicit red-flag return precautions (new weakness, bowel/bladder dysfunction, fever). Arrange outpatient PT, equipment, transportation, and medication access.
expected_los: MS-DRG 552 (Medical Back Problems w/o MCC): GMLOS 2.9 d, ALOS 3.6 d, relative weight 0.9613; MS-DRG 551 (w/ MCC): GMLOS 4.5 d, ALOS 5.9 d, relative weight 1.6761 (CMS MS-DRG V43, FY2026). Blended typical stay ~3-4 days.
target_los: w/o MCC: 2-3 days (many are candidates for a <2-day observation pathway); w/ MCC: ~4 days. Discharge is gated by pain-to-oral-analgesia conversion, PT-cleared safe mobility, and disposition — not by the diagnosis itself — so proactive Day-1 PT/OT and disposition planning are the levers to hit target.
quality_metrics: Imaging stewardship: OP-8 (lumbar MRI for LBP without prior conservative therapy; lower is better) and Choosing Wisely 'no imaging for LBP within 6 weeks without red flags'. Opioid stewardship: MME limits, PDMP check, co-prescribed naloxone, avoid opioid+benzodiazepine. VTE prophylaxis rate. Early-mobilization/functional-status documentation. Osteoporosis Management after fracture (HEDIS OMW) — a major care gap for VCF. 30-day all-cause readmission; observation-status appropriateness; LOS vs GMLOS. Hospital-acquired conditions (CAUTI, VTE, falls, pressure injury). Patient-reported outcomes (RMDQ/Oswestry, pain NRS). For osteomyelitis: appropriate antibiotic duration and ID involvement.
common_complications: Opioid-related: constipation/ileus, oversedation/respiratory depression, delirium (esp. elderly) — mitigate with opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia + scheduled bowel regimen. NSAID-related: AKI, GI bleed, fluid retention — dose-limit, monitor renal function. Immobility: VTE/PE, deconditioning, pressure injury, functional decline — early mobilization + prophylaxis. Delirium and falls in the elderly — delirium precautions, PT, minimize tethers. Urinary retention/CAUTI — limit Foley. Highest-stakes: missed serious diagnosis (cauda equina, epidural abscess, occult malignancy) — mitigate with structured red-flag screening, PVR, and serial neuro exams. Osteomyelitis: treatment failure/relapse, recurrent bacteremia; VCF: adjacent-level fracture.
readmission_drivers: Inadequate pain control or premature discharge; under-treated or missed serious pathology (progressive deficit, occult infection); functional decline/unsafe home/inadequate support -> falls; opioid complications (constipation, sedation); osteoporosis not addressed after a fragility VCF -> recurrent adjacent-level fracture; antibiotic failure or OPAT line complications in osteomyelitis. Prevention levers: multimodal analgesia with a clear taper, Day-1 PT/OT with safe-disposition planning, red-flag return-precaution education, initiate osteoporosis therapy before discharge, timely PCP/specialty follow-up, and structured OPAT coordination.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: American College of Physicians (ACP) 2017 — Noninvasive Treatments for Acute/Subacute/Chronic Low Back Pain (treatment); NICE Guideline NG59 (2016, updated 2020) — Low Back Pain and Sciatica in Over 16s (assessment, STarT Back risk-stratified pathways); ACR Appropriateness Criteria Low Back Pain (2021 Update) — imaging/red flags; IDSA 2015 — Diagnosis and Treatment of Native Vertebral Osteomyelitis (infection subset). Supportive: North American Spine Society (NASS) and osteoporosis guidance (AACE 2020; Endocrine Society; ASBMR secondary-fracture-prevention) for VCF.
key_citations: ACP 2017: Qaseem A et al, Noninvasive Treatments for Acute/Subacute/Chronic Low Back Pain, Ann Intern Med 2017;166:514-530 (PMID 28192789, https://doi.org/10.7326/M16-2367); ACR Appropriateness Criteria Low Back Pain 2021 Update: Hutchins TA et al, J Am Coll Radiol 2021;18(11S):S361-S379 (PMID 34794594, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jacr.2021.08.002); NICE NG59 Low Back Pain and Sciatica in Over 16s (2016, updated 2020); IDSA Native Vertebral Osteomyelitis: Berbari EF et al, Clin Infect Dis 2015;61(6):e26-46 (PMID 26229122, https://doi.org/10.1093/cid/civ482); STarT Back RCT: Hill JC et al, Lancet 2011;378:1560-71 (PMID 21963002, https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(11)60937-9); Vertebroplasty sham-controlled (negative): Buchbinder R et al, N Engl J Med 2009;361:557-68 (PMID 19657121, https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa0900429); VERTOS II (positive vs conservative): Klazen CA et al, Lancet 2010;376:1085-92 (PMID 20701962, https://doi.org/10.1016/S0140-6736(10)60954-3). Citations retrieved and verified via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Moderate overall. Treatment and imaging recommendations rest on strong Class I society guidelines (ACP 2017, NICE NG59, ACR AC 2021, IDSA 2015). However, the specific INPATIENT pathway — admission thresholds, day-by-day milestones, LOS targets — is largely expert consensus/extrapolated, because no society guideline addresses medical (non-surgical) back-pain hospitalization directly (guidelines are outpatient-oriented). Red-flag and infection elements are guideline-backed; vertebral-augmentation evidence is conflicting (sham-controlled trials negative vs open-label VERTOS II positive), so that recommendation is weak/shared-decision.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The collapsed group splits sharply by severity. MS-DRG 552 (no MCC): uncomplicated intractable mechanical pain or stable VCF; floor care; GMLOS 2.9 d; target 2-3 d; analgesia + PT + disposition drive the stay. MS-DRG 551 (with MCC): the MCC usually means the 'back problem' is really something serious — vertebral osteomyelitis/discitis, spinal epidural abscess, sepsis, acute cord compression/neuro deficit, pathologic fracture from malignancy, or AKI — prompting ID/surgery/oncology involvement, possible step-down/ICU, prolonged IV antibiotics, GMLOS 4.5 d (ALOS 5.9 d), and higher complication/readmission risk. Such cases frequently re-route to infection (DRG 548/549) or oncology/surgical pathways once the true driver is identified.
pathway_pearls: 1) Red-flag relentlessly, image sparingly: a structured red-flag screen + post-void residual + serial neuro exams catch cauda equina/epidural abscess/malignancy, while withholding imaging for uncomplicated LBP (ACP/ACR/Choosing Wisely, OP-8) is the biggest combined safety + stewardship lever. 2) Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (scheduled NSAID + acetaminophen +/- short muscle relaxant) with a mandatory bowel regimen and rapid IV->PO conversion — pain is both the admission reason and the discharge gate. 3) Mobilize Day 1 with PT/OT and plan disposition immediately — mobility and a safe home, not pain, most often gate discharge; never prescribe bed rest. 4) Close the care gaps: blood cultures before antibiotics + 6-week course + ID for spinal infection (IDSA); and START osteoporosis therapy before discharge after any fragility VCF — the most-missed driver of recurrent fracture and readmission.
