admission_criteria: Inpatient admission driven by validated severity plus objective instability, not clinical gestalt alone. Admit if PSI/PORT class IV-V or CURB-65 >=2; hypoxemia SpO2 <92% or new supplemental-O2 requirement (PaO2 <60 mmHg); hemodynamic instability (SBP <90 needing fluids); inability to maintain oral intake/oral antibiotics or unreliable outpatient support; failure of outpatient therapy; decompensated comorbidity (HF, COPD, CKD, diabetes, immunosuppression); multilobar infiltrate or complicated parapneumonic effusion/empyema/lung abscess on imaging. ICU admission if >=1 IDSA/ATS major criterion or >=3 minor criteria. ED discharge appropriate only if PSI I-II / CURB-65 0-1, SpO2 >=92%, tolerating PO. Aspiration pneumonia: admit for hypoxemia, witnessed large-volume aspiration with systemic signs, or high recurrent-aspiration risk. Empyema, lung abscess, and complicated parapneumonic effusion always warrant admission.
risk_stratification: PSI/PORT (ATS/IDSA-preferred for site-of-care): class IV-V -> admit, class V -> consider ICU. CURB-65 (Confusion; Urea/BUN >19 mg/dL i.e. >7 mmol/L; RR >=30; BP SBP<90 or DBP<=60; age >=65): 0-1 outpatient, 2 inpatient/observation, >=3 inpatient with ICU assessment. IDSA/ATS 2007 severe-CAP criteria: >=1 major (invasive mechanical ventilation; septic shock on vasopressors) or >=3 of 9 minor (RR>=30; PaO2/FiO2<=250; multilobar infiltrates; confusion; BUN>=20; WBC<4000; platelets<100k; temp<36C; hypotension needing aggressive fluids) -> ICU. qSOFA >=2 / SOFA rise >=2 / lactate -> Surviving Sepsis / SEP-1 pathway. RAPID score for pleural infection (Renal/urea, Age, Purulence, Infection source, Dietary/albumin): 0-2 low, 3-4 medium, 5-7 high 3-month mortality -> flags early surgical referral.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential; CMP (BUN/creatinine drive CURB-65 and AKI detection, glucose, electrolytes, LFTs); lactate (sepsis screen, repeat if >=2); ABG/VBG if hypoxemic or hypercapnic. Blood cultures x2 BEFORE antibiotics in severe CAP or when empiric MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage started. Sputum Gram stain and culture (or endotracheal aspirate if intubated) in severe CAP / empiric broad coverage. Respiratory viral PCR panel (influenza, RSV, SARS-CoV-2) -> de-escalate/stop antibiotics if positive and non-severe. Legionella and pneumococcal urinary antigens in severe CAP. Procalcitonin to SUPPORT stopping antibiotics (never to withhold initial empiric therapy). CRP trend; HIV if risk factors. Pleural fluid analysis for any effusion >1 cm (pH, glucose, LDH, protein, Gram stain/culture, cell count, cytology) -> pH <7.2 mandates drainage.
initial_imaging_dx: Chest radiograph PA/lateral at presentation to confirm a demonstrable infiltrate (guideline-required for CAP diagnosis). Lung/thoracic ultrasound (POCUS) acceptable diagnostic alternative where expertise exists (2025 ATS) and to characterize/guide thoracentesis. CT chest (contrast) if diagnostic uncertainty, suspected complication (empyema, lung abscess, necrotizing pneumonia, post-obstructive/mass), or non-response at 48-72h. Diagnostic thoracentesis for any effusion >1 cm on decubitus/ultrasound. Bronchoscopy for suspected endobronchial obstruction, non-resolving pneumonia, or immunocompromised host. Routine follow-up CXR at discharge NOT required; outpatient chest imaging at 6-8 weeks for age >=50 or smokers to exclude malignancy.
time_critical_interventions: Guideline-concordant antibiotics as soon as possible after diagnosis; within 1 hour if septic shock (SEP-1 / Surviving Sepsis bundle); ASAP in ED for admitted CAP (do not delay for cultures beyond drawing them). Draw blood cultures BEFORE first antibiotic dose in severe CAP. Sepsis bundle: lactate within 3h and repeat if elevated, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4, vasopressors for MAP <65. Hypoxemia: immediate O2 to SpO2 92-96%, escalate HFNC/NIV/intubation for failure. Complicated effusion/empyema with pH <7.2 or frank pus -> prompt chest-tube drainage. Severe CAP/septic shock -> start corticosteroids early. Oseltamivir within 48h if influenza suspected/confirmed.
initial_management: Empiric antibiotics by severity. Inpatient non-severe: beta-lactam (ceftriaxone 1-2 g IV daily, cefotaxime, ampicillin-sulbactam, or ceftaroline) PLUS macrolide (azithromycin 500 mg) OR respiratory fluoroquinolone monotherapy (levofloxacin 750 mg or moxifloxacin 400 mg). Severe CAP: beta-lactam PLUS macrolide OR beta-lactam PLUS respiratory fluoroquinolone (beta-lactam+macrolide preferred). Add MRSA coverage (vancomycin dosed to AUC/trough, or linezolid 600 mg q12h) and/or antipseudomonal beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g q6h, cefepime, or meropenem) ONLY with prior isolation or locally validated risk, and obtain cultures to allow de-escalation. Aspiration pneumonia: standard CAP regimen; do NOT add routine anaerobic coverage (clindamycin/metronidazole) unless lung abscess, empyema, or necrotizing pneumonia (2019 ATS/IDSA; Bai 2024 Chest). Corticosteroids: hydrocortisone 200 mg/day IV x4-7 days for severe CAP/septic shock (CAPE COD), excluded in influenza/likely fungal. Source control: chest-tube drainage +/- intrapleural tPA 10 mg + DNase 5 mg BID x3 days for loculated empyema (MIST2); lung abscess = prolonged antibiotics + postural drainage. IV crystalloid for sepsis; supplemental O2/respiratory support; VTE prophylaxis.
day1_milestones: Confirm diagnosis and assign severity tier (PSI + IDSA/ATS criteria); blood cultures and urinary antigens drawn before/with first dose; start guideline-concordant empiric antibiotics; titrate O2 to SpO2 >=92%; run sepsis bundle if qSOFA>=2/shock (lactate, fluids, cultures); correct site of care (floor vs step-down vs ICU); dysphagia/aspiration screen and SLP referral if aspiration mechanism; thoracentesis for significant effusion; order VTE prophylaxis; establish code status/goals of care; assess corticosteroid indication in severe CAP; start oseltamivir if influenza suspected.
day2_milestones: Reassess clinical stability using Halm criteria (temp <=37.8C, HR <=100, RR <=24, SBP >=90, SpO2 >=90% or PaO2 >=60, normal mentation, tolerating PO). Review culture/PCR/antigen results and DE-ESCALATE: stop empiric MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage if cultures negative at 48h, narrow to targeted agent, discontinue antibiotics if respiratory virus positive and non-severe. Trend procalcitonin/CRP to support stopping. Begin IV-to-PO switch once hemodynamically stable and tolerating oral intake. Early mobilization and PT. Verify adequacy of any effusion drainage; assess chest-tube output.
day3plus_milestones: Continue de-escalation; complete minimum 5-day course, stopping once afebrile 48h and clinically stable (7 days if MRSA/Pseudomonas; prolonged 2-3+ weeks for lung abscess/empyema/necrotizing pneumonia). Ensure IV-to-PO conversion done; confirm oxygenation at/near baseline on room air or arrange home O2. Repeat imaging only if not improving (routine discharge CXR not needed); schedule outpatient chest imaging at 6-8 weeks for age >=50/smokers to exclude malignancy. Empyema: reassess for surgical referral (VATS decortication) if failing tube drainage/fibrinolytics or RAPID high-risk. Finalize disposition and confirm discharge readiness.
consults_multidisciplinary: Infectious Diseases (severe/resistant/non-resolving disease, bacteremia, immunocompromised). Pulmonology (parapneumonic effusion/empyema, non-resolving pneumonia, bronchoscopy). Interventional Pulmonology / Thoracic Surgery (empyema needing drainage or decortication, lung abscess). Critical Care for ICU-criteria patients. Speech-Language Pathology with formal dysphagia evaluation (aspiration pneumonia, stroke, frail elderly). PT/OT for deconditioning and functional/discharge assessment. Pharmacy for antimicrobial stewardship and vancomycin/AUC dosing. Registered dietitian/Nutrition (malnutrition, low albumin, texture-modified diet). Case management/Social work early for disposition, home O2, and OPAT coordination.
monitoring_level: ICU if >=1 IDSA/ATS major criterion (invasive mechanical ventilation or vasopressor-dependent septic shock) or >=3 minor criteria. Step-down/telemetry for high O2 needs (HFNC/NIV), borderline hemodynamics, or 1-2 minor criteria. General floor for non-severe CAP with continuous pulse oximetry. Escalation triggers: rising FiO2/O2 requirement, RR >=30, SpO2 <90% despite O2, rising lactate, hypotension, new confusion, or worsening early-warning score (NEWS2/MEWS) -> rapid-response activation and ICU evaluation.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: aspiration precautions with head-of-bed >=30 degrees; SLP-guided texture-modified diet if dysphagia, advance as tolerated; oral care/chlorhexidine to lower aspiration burden; nutrition support if malnourished or low albumin. Mobility: out-of-bed and early mobilization from Day 1; PT for deconditioning. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily or heparin 5000 units SC q8h) unless contraindicated, otherwise mechanical prophylaxis. Glucose: target 140-180 mg/dL, avoid hypoglycemia (<70) especially with corticosteroids. Lines/catheters: remove peripheral IV and urinary catheters early once PO tolerated and patient mobile; IV-to-PO antibiotic switch enables line removal.
discharge_criteria: Clinically stable >=24h with Halm criteria met: afebrile, HR <=100, RR <=24, SBP >=90, SpO2 >=90% on room air or stable home-O2 plan, normal mental status, tolerating oral intake and oral antibiotics. No unstable comorbidity requiring inpatient care; adequate effusion/empyema drainage if applicable; care sustainable at home or SNF. Discharge before clinical stability is associated with excess readmission and mortality and should be avoided. Routine repeat CXR before discharge not required if clinically improving.
discharge_planning: Disposition: home vs SNF vs home health (home O2, OPAT for empyema/resistant organisms). Medication reconciliation with a defined antibiotic STOP date and total course length (avoid excess duration). Smoking-cessation counseling. Pneumococcal (PCV20 or PCV15+PPSV23) and influenza vaccination per ACIP before discharge (IMM immunization measures). Dysphagia diet and aspiration-precaution education for aspiration pneumonia. PCP follow-up within 7 days (sooner if high-risk); pulmonology and 6-8 week interval chest imaging if age >=50 or smoker to exclude malignancy. Teach-back on warning signs and return precautions; structured transitions-of-care handoff to outpatient providers.
expected_los: Approximate CMS geometric-mean-LOS benchmarks by severity tier: DRG 177 (with MCC) ~6.5-7 days; DRG 178 (with CC) ~5 days; DRG 179 (without CC/MCC) ~3.7-4 days. Blended typical stay ~4-6 days.
target_los: Optimized best-practice targets under the pathway: DRG 179 non-severe 2-3 days (early IV-to-PO, short-course <=5-day antibiotics, day-2 stability check); DRG 178 ~4 days; DRG 177 / severe CAP or empyema 5-6 days (longer if ICU, necrotizing pneumonia, or decortication). Driven by early stability assessment, prompt de-escalation, and short-course therapy.
quality_metrics: CMS: 30-day risk-standardized pneumonia readmission (HRRP) and 30-day pneumonia mortality (Hospital Compare); SEP-1 sepsis bundle compliance when septic; blood cultures before antibiotics; timely and appropriate empiric antibiotic selection (stewardship); IMM-2 influenza and pneumococcal immunization prior to discharge; antibiotic duration/de-escalation stewardship metrics; IV-to-PO conversion rate; LOS observed-to-expected index. Aspiration-specific: documented dysphagia screening and SLP evaluation.
common_complications: Parapneumonic effusion/empyema (sample effusion >1 cm, drain if pH<7.2 or pus, add tPA/DNase or VATS). Acute respiratory failure/ARDS (O2, HFNC/NIV, lung-protective ventilation). Sepsis/septic shock (bundle plus source control). AKI (avoid nephrotoxins, maintain euvolemia). Clostridioides difficile colitis (narrow and shorten antibiotics; avoid unnecessary anaerobic coverage). Delirium (minimize sedatives, mobilize, orient). Lung abscess/necrotizing pneumonia (prolonged antibiotics +/- drainage). Hospital deconditioning and recurrent aspiration. Mitigation levers: stewardship/de-escalation, early mobility, SLP dysphagia management, VTE prophylaxis.
readmission_drivers: Premature discharge before clinical (Halm) stability; unaddressed dysphagia driving recurrent aspiration; decompensated comorbidities (COPD, HF, CKD, diabetes); inadequate or absent early follow-up; incomplete or excessively long antibiotic course; failure to fully drain empyema; missed post-obstructive/malignant etiology; frailty/poor functional status. Prevention levers: verify stability before discharge, SLP eval plus aspiration precautions, comorbidity optimization, follow-up within 7 days, pneumococcal/influenza vaccination, OPAT coordination, and teach-back education.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: ATS/IDSA 2019 Community-Acquired Pneumonia guideline (Metlay et al., Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2019) plus ATS 2025 CAP clinical practice guideline update (AJRCCM, 2026) for corticosteroids and antibiotic duration. Aspiration pneumonia: ATS/IDSA 2019 plus Bai et al. 2024 (Chest). Pleural infection/empyema: ERS/ESTS 2023 statement on management of pleural infection in adults, plus BTS Pleural Disease guideline. Sepsis overlap: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 and CMS SEP-1.
key_citations: Metlay JP et al. Diagnosis and Treatment of Adults with Community-acquired Pneumonia: Official ATS/IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline. Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2019;200(7):e45-e67 (PMID 31573350). ATS 2025 CAP Clinical Practice Guideline, AJRCCM 2026. Lim WS et al. Defining CAP severity (CURB-65). Thorax 2003;58:377-382 (PMID 12728155). Dequin PF et al. Hydrocortisone in Severe CAP (CAPE COD). N Engl J Med 2023;388:1931-1941 (PMID 36942789). Bai AD et al. Anaerobic Antibiotic Coverage in Aspiration Pneumonia. Chest 2024 (PMID 38387648). Rahman NM et al. Intrapleural tPA and DNase in Pleural Infection (MIST2). N Engl J Med 2011;365:518-526 (PMID 21830966). Rahman NM et al. RAPID clinical score for pleural infection. Chest 2014;145:848-855 (PMID 24264558). ERS/ESTS statement on management of pleural infection in adults, Eur Respir J 2023;61:2201062. Attribution: citations retrieved via PubMed; verify DOIs.
evidence_grade: Strong for core CAP management - high-quality Class I society guideline (ATS/IDSA CAP 2019, ATS 2025 update; ERS/ESTS pleural infection) supported by RCTs (CAPE COD for steroids, MIST2 for intrapleural tPA/DNase). Corticosteroids in severe CAP = moderate/conditional and evolving, with a STRONG recommendation AGAINST steroids in non-severe hospitalized CAP. Anaerobic coverage in aspiration pneumonia = moderate observational evidence supporting NOT routinely covering. Lung abscess management is largely consensus/expert opinion and extrapolated (lower grade).
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 179 (no CC/MCC): non-severe CAP, floor care, early IV-to-PO switch, short-course antibiotics, LOS 3-4d, low mortality. DRG 178 (CC): moderate disease - hypoxemia, uncomplicated parapneumonic effusion, or a controlled comorbidity; LOS ~5d. DRG 177 (MCC): severe CAP or aspiration with acute respiratory failure, septic shock, empyema, lung abscess, necrotizing pneumonia, or AKI; frequently ICU, requires corticosteroids, source control, and prolonged antibiotics; LOS 6.5-7+d with highest mortality and readmission. MCC drivers (acute respiratory failure, sepsis, empyema) should be accurately documented and trigger pathway escalation and higher monitoring level.
pathway_pearls: 1) Grade severity objectively (PSI + IDSA/ATS major/minor criteria) and place correctly - ICU for >=1 major or >=3 minor; under-triage of severe CAP is the biggest mortality lever. 2) Cultures before antibiotics, then immediate guideline-concordant empiric therapy; reserve MRSA/Pseudomonas coverage for prior isolation or validated local risk and mandatorily DE-ESCALATE at 48h. 3) Aspiration pneumonia: skip routine anaerobic coverage (prevents C. difficile) unless abscess/empyema/necrotizing, and get an SLP dysphagia evaluation to prevent recurrence. 4) Chase the effusion - sample any effusion >1 cm, drain early if pH<7.2 or pus, add intrapleural tPA/DNase for loculation and surgery for failures; pair short-course antibiotics with early IV-to-PO switch to compress LOS.

admission_criteria: Admit for: pleural infection (empyema/frank pus or complicated parapneumonic effusion, pleural pH <7.2) requiring chest-drain + IV antibiotics; large or symptomatic effusion with hypoxemia (SpO2 <90-92%) or respiratory distress; hemodynamic instability/sepsis (qSOFA >=2); effusion needing urgent therapeutic drainage with compromise; new large undiagnosed exudate requiring inpatient diagnostic + therapeutic thoracentesis; symptomatic recurrent malignant effusion needing IPC or pleurodesis; failed outpatient management. ED discharge/observation appropriate for: small asymptomatic effusion; known uncomplicated transudate manageable outpatient; simple free-flowing parapneumonic effusion (pH >7.2, no organisms) on oral antibiotics with reliable follow-up; stable post-thoracentesis without complication
risk_stratification: Light's criteria (exudate if pleural/serum protein >0.5, pleural/serum LDH >0.6, or pleural LDH >2/3 ULN serum LDH) -> separates exudate vs transudate and directs workup; Pleural fluid pH <7.2 (measured on blood-gas analyzer) in parapneumonic effusion -> mandates chest-tube drainage (supported by glucose <60 mg/dL [<3.4 mmol/L], LDH >1000); frank pus or positive Gram stain/culture = empyema -> drain; RAPID score (Renal/urea, Age, Purulence, Infection source [community vs hospital], Dietary/albumin), range 0-7: low 0-2 (~2% 3-mo mortality), medium 3-4 (~9%), high 5-7 (~29%) -> stratifies pleural infection, flags early surgical review; LENT score (pleural LDH, ECOG PS, serum Neutrophil-Lymphocyte ratio, Tumor type) -> prognosis in malignant effusion, guides IPC vs pleurodesis vs conservative; CURB-65/qSOFA/SOFA if coexistent pneumonia or sepsis
initial_workup_labs: Diagnostic thoracentesis with pleural fluid: pH (blood-gas analyzer), protein, LDH, glucose, cell count + differential, Gram stain, aerobic + anaerobic cultures, cytology (malignancy), ADA (>40 U/L suggests TB pleuritis); paired serum protein, LDH, albumin (Light's criteria + RAPID) and glucose; CBC, CMP, CRP (trend infection), coagulation/INR + platelets (pre-procedure), blood cultures if febrile, lactate if septic; NT-proBNP if transudate/heart-failure suspected; pleural fluid triglycerides (chylothorax), hematocrit (hemothorax) when indicated; serum urea and albumin feed RAPID risk score
initial_imaging_dx: Bedside thoracic ultrasound to characterize (volume, loculation, septation) and US-GUIDED thoracentesis is standard (ATS conditional rec; cuts pneumothorax 8.9%->1.0%) - perform within hours of admission for symptomatic/undiagnosed effusion; CXR (PA/lateral) on presentation and post-procedure to confirm drain position and exclude pneumothorax; contrast-enhanced CT chest within ~24h if empyema/malignancy suspected or complex/loculated (split-pleura sign) and to plan drainage/biopsy and pre-surgical referral; if malignancy suspected with negative cytology -> image-guided or thoracoscopic (medical/VATS) pleural biopsy for histology
time_critical_interventions: Empyema/complicated parapneumonic effusion: start IV antibiotics promptly and place chest drain SAME DAY - do not delay drainage; septic shock: antibiotics <=1h, serum lactate, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid, source control via drainage (Surviving Sepsis 2021/SEP-1); large symptomatic effusion with respiratory compromise: therapeutic drainage same day, limit to <=1.5 L per session to prevent re-expansion pulmonary edema; frank pus or pH <7.2 -> chest tube same day; loculated/failing empyema -> intrapleural t-PA 10 mg + DNase 5 mg twice daily for 3 days (6 doses) per MIST2
initial_management: Pleural infection empiric IV antibiotics - community-acquired: co-amoxiclav OR 2nd/3rd-gen cephalosporin (e.g., ceftriaxone) + metronidazole for anaerobic cover; penicillin-allergic: clindamycin + fluoroquinolone/cephalosporin; hospital-acquired: broaden to MRSA + Pseudomonas cover (vancomycin + piperacillin-tazobactam or meropenem); AVOID aminoglycosides (poor pleural penetration). Small-bore (10-14F) chest drain with scheduled saline flushes; intrapleural t-PA/DNase (MIST2) for loculation. Malignant effusion: therapeutic large-volume thoracentesis first to assess lung re-expansion/symptom attribution, then if expandable -> IPC or talc pleurodesis (slurry or poudrage) first-line, if non-expandable/trapped lung or failed pleurodesis -> IPC preferred (ATS 2018). Transudate: treat cause (loop diuretics for HF, manage cirrhosis/nephrotic/renal), therapeutic thoracentesis if symptomatic. Supplemental O2 to SpO2 >=92%, analgesia, VTE prophylaxis
day1_milestones: US-guided diagnostic +/- therapeutic thoracentesis completed and full pleural fluid panel sent; Light's criteria applied (exudate vs transudate); empiric IV antibiotics started if infection; chest drain placed for empyema/pH <7.2/pus with post-procedure CXR; RAPID and/or LENT calculated; oxygenation and hemodynamics stabilized; analgesia optimized; working diagnosis established (infective vs malignant vs transudative); respiratory/pulmonology and interventional consults initiated; VTE prophylaxis and glucose control started
day2_milestones: Review pleural fluid microbiology and cytology; de-escalate/narrow antibiotics to culture; assess drain output and CXR for lung re-expansion and drainage adequacy; if empyema not draining/loculated -> begin intrapleural t-PA/DNase or escalate; recalculate RAPID and trend CRP; optimize nutrition/albumin; early mobilization; if malignancy confirmed, plan definitive management (IPC vs talc pleurodesis) and oncology/palliative referral; confirm VTE prophylaxis continued
day3plus_milestones: Reassess response - clinical improvement, afebrile, falling CRP, radiographic resolution; complete intrapleural fibrinolytic course (up to 3 days) if used; thoracic surgery referral for VATS/decortication if failure to improve, persistent sepsis, or non-resolving collection by ~day 5-7 (BTS/MIST); assess pleurodesis success and remove drain when output <150-250 mL/day with re-expanded lung; transition IV to oral antibiotics once clinically stable and afebrile; IPC home-drainage teaching; finalize discharge plan and follow-up; total antibiotic course typically 2-6 weeks for pleural infection
consults_multidisciplinary: Respiratory/pulmonology (pleural specialist) day 0-1; interventional pulmonology/radiology for image-guided drainage and pleural biopsy; thoracic surgery for empyema failing medical therapy (VATS/decortication) or trapped lung; oncology + palliative care for malignant effusion; infectious diseases for complex/resistant pleural infection; pharmacy (antibiotic stewardship, anticoagulation, vancomycin dosing); dietitian/nutrition (hypoalbuminemia, catabolic state); PT/OT for deconditioning; care management/social work for IPC home care and community drainage services and disposition
monitoring_level: Floor/ward with continuous pulse oximetry for most; telemetry/step-down for hemodynamic instability, large-volume drainage, significant hypoxemia, or sepsis; ICU for septic shock, respiratory failure needing high-flow/NIV/ventilation, or re-expansion pulmonary edema. Escalation triggers: SpO2 <90% despite O2, rising vasopressor requirement, worsening sepsis, hemothorax or large air leak, or drain-related complication; post-thoracentesis monitoring for re-expansion pulmonary edema and pneumothorax
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet as tolerated with nutritional repletion (low albumin/prealbumin is an adverse RAPID marker); early mobilization to prevent deconditioning; pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (LMWH, e.g., enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) unless active bleeding/procedural contraindication, mechanical prophylaxis if contraindicated; inpatient glucose target 140-180 mg/dL; chest drain care with scheduled flushes, remove when output <150-250 mL/day and lung re-expanded; remove urinary catheters and central lines early; incentive spirometry and pulmonary hygiene
discharge_criteria: Afebrile >=24h with clinical improvement; adequate oxygenation on room air or stable home O2 (SpO2 >=92%); chest drain removed with acceptable post-removal CXR, OR IPC established with a functioning home-drainage plan; inflammatory markers (CRP) trending down; pain controlled on oral analgesia; tolerating oral intake; oral antibiotic regimen and duration defined; definitive effusion management decided (drained/pleurodesis/IPC); safe disposition with follow-up arranged
discharge_planning: Disposition home with community IPC drainage service or district nursing if IPC in situ; medication reconciliation including full antibiotic course (2-6 weeks for pleural infection) and analgesia; respiratory clinic follow-up at 2-4 weeks with repeat CXR to confirm resolution; oncology/palliative follow-up for malignancy; IPC/drain-care education and red-flag counseling (fever, increasing dyspnea, drain-site infection/leak); close the histology/cytology results loop; transition-of-care summary to PCP; ensure pending microbiology and biopsy results have an owner
expected_los: CMS MS-DRG GMLOS benchmarks approximately: DRG 188 (no CC/MCC) ~3.2 days; DRG 187 (with CC) ~4.2 days; DRG 186 (with MCC) ~5.4 days; blended typical ~4-5 days. Empyema/complicated pleural infection real-world LOS is often 7-14 days due to prolonged drainage plus extended IV antibiotics
target_los: Simple symptomatic or transudative effusion (thoracentesis + treat cause) 2-3 days; malignant effusion managed with ambulatory IPC (avoids inpatient pleurodesis dwell) 2-4 days; empyema/complicated parapneumonic effusion 5-7 days with early small-bore drainage, intrapleural t-PA/DNase for loculation, and early (day 5-7) surgical referral to prevent prolongation
quality_metrics: Ultrasound-guided pleural procedure rate; iatrogenic pneumothorax rate post-thoracentesis; time-to-drainage for empyema; SEP-1 bundle compliance when septic; appropriate empiric antibiotic selection and de-escalation (stewardship); 30-day readmission; 30-day and 90-day mortality (RAPID-linked); VTE prophylaxis rate; door-to-antibiotics in sepsis; unplanned return to OR or re-drainage; IPC-related infection rate; LOS vs GMLOS index; patient-reported dyspnea/QoL improvement
common_complications: Re-expansion pulmonary edema (mitigate by limiting drainage to <=1.5 L/session); iatrogenic pneumothorax (US guidance reduces); procedural bleeding/hemothorax (correct coagulopathy, US guidance); empyema loculation or failed drainage (intrapleural t-PA/DNase, timely surgery); chest-drain blockage/dislodgement (small-bore flushing protocol); IPC-associated infection/cellulitis (treat with antibiotics without catheter removal first per ATS); trapped/non-expandable lung; hospital-acquired infection; sepsis progression; antibiotic adverse effects (e.g., vancomycin, C. difficile)
readmission_drivers: Recurrent malignant effusion (definitive IPC/pleurodesis reduces recurrence); incompletely treated or relapsing pleural infection (ensure adequate drainage + full antibiotic course); premature drain removal; undertreated underlying transudative disease (HF, cirrhosis, renal/nephrotic - optimize diuresis and disease control); IPC complications (blockage, infection, symptomatic loculation); missed or delayed malignancy diagnosis; inadequate outpatient drainage support. Prevention levers: definitive source control, complete antibiotic course, robust respiratory follow-up with community drainage services, and treatment of the underlying cause
guideline_source: British Thoracic Society (BTS) Guideline for Pleural Disease, 2023; ATS/STS/STR Clinical Practice Guideline - Management of Malignant Pleural Effusions, 2018. Supporting: ACCP parapneumonic effusion consensus and Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 / CMS SEP-1 when septic
key_citations: Roberts ME, Rahman NM, Maskell NA, et al. British Thoracic Society Guideline for pleural disease. Thorax 2023;78(11):1143-1156 (PMID 37553157; DOI 10.1136/thorax-2023-220304). Feller-Kopman DJ, et al. Management of Malignant Pleural Effusions: An Official ATS/STS/STR Clinical Practice Guideline. Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2018;198(7):839-849 (PMID 30272503; DOI 10.1164/rccm.201807-1415ST). Rahman NM, et al. Intrapleural use of tissue plasminogen activator and DNase in pleural infection (MIST2). N Engl J Med 2011;365(6):518-526 (PMID 21830966; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1012740). Davies HE, et al. Effect of an indwelling pleural catheter vs chest tube and talc pleurodesis (TIME2). JAMA 2012 (PMID 22610520). Rahman NM, et al. A clinical score (RAPID) for pleural infection. CHEST 2014 (PMID 24264558); Corcoran JP, et al. Prospective validation of RAPID (PILOT). Eur Respir J 2020 (PMID 32675200). Clive AO, et al. LENT prognostic score for malignant pleural effusion. Thorax 2014 (PMID 25100651). Attribution: article metadata retrieved from PubMed
evidence_grade: Moderate overall - a mix of Class I RCT evidence and conditional/consensus guidance. Strong/RCT-backed: chest-drain source control and intrapleural t-PA 10mg + DNase 5mg (MIST2) for empyema. Conditional/low-to-very-low certainty (ATS 2018 GRADE): US guidance, large-volume thoracentesis, and IPC vs talc pleurodesis for malignant effusion (preference-sensitive). BTS 2023 is a comprehensive society guideline but several recommendations rest on consensus/expert opinion. Risk scores RAPID and LENT are prospectively validated
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 188 (no CC/MCC): simple symptomatic or transudative effusion, thoracentesis, floor care, LOS ~3 days. DRG 187 (CC): complicated parapneumonic effusion or moderate comorbidity, chest-tube drainage, LOS ~4 days. DRG 186 (MCC): empyema with sepsis or acute respiratory failure, or malignant effusion with cachexia/complications - higher likelihood of ICU, intrapleural fibrinolytics or surgery, LOS ~5.4+ days (empyema often 7-14 days). MCC drivers: sepsis, acute respiratory failure, empyema, metastatic malignancy with complications. Higher tiers correlate with longer antibiotic courses, surgical referral, and higher mortality (high RAPID)
pathway_pearls: 1) Always sample under ultrasound guidance (ATS), apply Light's criteria, and measure pleural pH on a blood-gas analyzer - pH <7.2 mandates a chest drain in a parapneumonic effusion. 2) In empyema, drain early with a small-bore (10-14F) tube and add intrapleural t-PA 10mg + DNase 5mg BID x3 days (MIST2) for loculation; escalate to thoracic surgery by day 5-7 if not improving. 3) For malignant effusion, do a therapeutic large-volume thoracentesis FIRST to confirm lung re-expansion and symptom relief, then choose IPC (ambulatory, shortens stay) vs talc pleurodesis by expandability and patient preference. 4) Cap each drainage at <=1.5 L to prevent re-expansion pulmonary edema, risk-stratify infection with RAPID, and never use aminoglycosides (poor pleural penetration)

scope_and_volume_notes: Often malignant or para-pneumonic; thoracentesis/chest-tube driven.

admission_criteria: Nearly all confirmed/suspected IE requires inpatient admission — this is not an ED-discharge or observation diagnosis; admit for 2023 Duke-ISCVID definite or possible IE, positive blood cultures with typical organisms (S. aureus, viridans strep, enterococci) plus fever/new regurgitant murmur, echo vegetation, or embolic phenomena; direct-to-ICU for acute severe aortic/mitral regurgitation with pulmonary edema/cardiogenic shock, septic shock, high-grade AV block, or large mobile vegetation needing urgent surgery; PWID with S. aureus bacteremia + fever = admit; observation/ED discharge is inappropriate given need for serial blood cultures, echocardiography, and prolonged (4-6 week) IV antibiotics
risk_stratification: Diagnose by 2023 Duke-ISCVID criteria: Definite = 2 major, OR 1 major+3 minor, OR 5 minor; Possible = 1 major+1 minor OR 3 minor (drives admission/empiric treatment); major = typical organism in >=2 cultures, echo/cardiac-CT vegetation-abscess or 18F-FDG-PET/CT uptake (prosthetic material), or intraoperative inspection; minor = predisposition (IVDU/prosthetic valve/prior IE), fever >=38C, vascular/immunologic phenomena, microbiologic evidence not meeting major; embolic risk: vegetation >10mm (esp >15mm) and mobile drives early-surgery consideration; surgical mortality risk by EuroSCORE II, STS score, or IE-specific PALSUSE/RISK-E/De Feo-Cormier scores; qSOFA/SOFA and lactate if septic
initial_workup_labs: >=3 sets of blood cultures from separate venipunctures BEFORE antibiotics (single highest-yield step; the 2-major-criterion) with hold-for-organism only if hemodynamically stable; CBC (anemia of chronic disease, leukocytosis), CMP (baseline renal function for antibiotic dosing), CRP + ESR (trend response), procalcitonin/lactate if septic; urinalysis (hematuria/proteinuria = immune-complex GN); coags and LFTs; HIV plus HBV/HCV serologies and urine drug screen (especially PWID); rheumatoid factor; if culture-negative at 48-72h send serologies/PCR for Coxiella burnetii (Q fever), Bartonella, Brucella, Legionella, Tropheryma whipplei
initial_imaging_dx: TTE first-line promptly (<12-24h) in all suspected IE; TEE (sensitivity ~90-95% vs TTE ~70%) when TTE nondiagnostic, when a prosthetic valve/CIED is present, or to define complications (perivalvular abscess, perforation, leaflet destruction); repeat TEE in 3-7 days if initial study negative but suspicion persists; admission and serial ECG (new PR prolongation/AV block signals perivalvular abscess); cardiac CT for perivalvular extension (ESC 2023 Class I); 18F-FDG-PET/CT for prosthetic-valve (>3 months post-op) and CIED IE (now a Duke-ISCVID major criterion); brain MRI for neuro symptoms/silent emboli; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for embolic screen (splenic/renal infarct, mycotic aneurysm)
time_critical_interventions: Draw blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics, but do NOT delay empiric therapy in sepsis/septic shock — antibiotics within 1h (Surviving Sepsis/SEP-1); EMERGENCY surgery <24h for acute severe aortic/mitral regurgitation with refractory pulmonary edema or cardiogenic shock; URGENT surgery within 3-5 days for heart failure, locally uncontrolled infection (abscess, fistula, persistent bacteremia >5-7 days despite appropriate antibiotics), or high embolic risk (vegetation >10mm with a prior embolic event, or >15mm); new complete heart block triggers urgent surgical evaluation
initial_management: Empiric IV antibiotics after cultures, tailored to acuity/valve type: native community-acquired — ampicillin + (flu)cloxacillin/oxacillin + gentamicin (ESC 2023) or US-style vancomycin + ceftriaxone; penicillin allergy — vancomycin + gentamicin; prosthetic/healthcare-associated — vancomycin + gentamicin + rifampin (+/- cefepime); always cover MRSA (vancomycin or daptomycin), especially PWID; pathogen-directed: penicillin-susceptible viridans strep — penicillin G/ceftriaxone x4wk (2wk if + gentamicin, uncomplicated native); MSSA — nafcillin/oxacillin or cefazolin x6wk; MRSA — vancomycin/daptomycin x6wk; enterococcus — ampicillin + ceftriaxone (or + gentamicin) x6wk; total 4-6wk IV from first negative culture; source control = valve surgery for indications, remove infected CIED/lines, drain abscesses; anticoagulation: continue only an existing mechanical-valve indication and avoid initiating new anticoagulation/antiplatelet (hold in S. aureus PVE with CNS embolism due to hemorrhage risk)
day1_milestones: Confirm >=3 blood-culture sets drawn pre-antibiotic; start acuity-appropriate empiric IV therapy; obtain TTE and ECG; activate the multidisciplinary Endocarditis Team (ID + cardiology + cardiac surgery) — Class I; risk-stratify with Duke-ISCVID; place on continuous telemetry; screen for emergent surgical indications (heart failure, shock, heart block); begin embolic/end-organ imaging if symptomatic; in PWID, initiate MOUD (buprenorphine/methadone) and consult addiction medicine and social work
day2_milestones: Review culture Gram stain/preliminary identification and narrow/target antibiotics with ID and microbiology; obtain TEE if not yet done or if complication suspected; repeat surveillance blood cultures q24-48h to document clearance; re-image/echo for any new murmur or conduction change; confirm surgical plan (emergency/urgent vs elective) with the Endocarditis Team; start therapeutic drug monitoring for vancomycin/aminoglycoside and follow renal function; define the antibiotic-delivery endgame (OPAT vs POET partial-oral vs inpatient completion)
day3plus_milestones: Document blood-culture clearance (the first negative culture starts the treatment clock); de-escalate to definitive pathogen-directed regimen with a fixed duration; trend CRP and temperature; complete embolic/CNS imaging; proceed with valve surgery if indicated; in stable left-sided IE (streptococci, E. faecalis, S. aureus, CoNS) without a surgical indication, consider POET partial-oral switch after >=10 days IV; establish OPAT with PICC/midline access, a weekly-lab plan, and ID follow-up; finalize disposition and transitions of care
consults_multidisciplinary: Class I multidisciplinary Endocarditis/Heart-Valve Team — infectious disease, cardiology, and cardiac surgery + clinical microbiology (Day 1); neurology (embolic stroke/mycotic aneurysm); nephrology (AKI/immune GN); addiction medicine + psychiatry for MOUD and dental for oral source in PWID/at-risk patients; pharmacy (OPAT, therapeutic drug monitoring); nutrition (malnutrition common); care management/social work early (OPAT logistics, PWID housing/insurance); PT/OT; palliative care for non-surgical/poor-prognosis candidates
monitoring_level: Continuous telemetry for ALL patients (surveil for new AV block/arrhythmia signaling perivalvular abscess) with serial ECG; ICU/step-down for acute heart failure, septic shock, hemodynamic instability, high-grade AV block, post-cardiac-surgery care, or large mobile vegetation with active embolization; floor for stable, afebrile, culture-cleared patients completing therapy; escalation triggers: new/worsening heart failure, new conduction delay, embolic event, persistent fever/bacteremia >5-7 days, or hemodynamic decline
nutrition_mobility_vte: Regular/heart-healthy diet with nutrition consult (malnutrition frequent, especially PWID); early mobilization as hemodynamics allow; VTE prophylaxis individualized — mechanical prophylaxis as default, pharmacologic held/deferred when an intracranial mycotic aneurysm or hemorrhagic embolic transformation is present (bleeding risk); glucose control; secure long-term IV access (PICC/midline) with an explicit line-safety plan in PWID (inpatient completion or POET oral alternative if line-misuse risk); remove unnecessary catheters/central lines promptly
discharge_criteria: Afebrile and hemodynamically stable; documented negative surveillance blood cultures (clearance achieved); no unmet indication for urgent surgery, or post-operative course stable; a defined, reliably deliverable antibiotic plan (OPAT, POET partial-oral, or inpatient completion) with monitoring arranged; in PWID, addiction treatment engaged and a safe antibiotic-delivery plan established; end-organ complications addressed and stabilized
discharge_planning: Disposition per delivery plan: OPAT (home infusion or SNF) with weekly CBC/CMP + antibiotic drug levels and ID oversight; POET partial-oral in eligible stable left-sided IE (a key LOS-reducing lever); or inpatient completion when OPAT is unsafe; medication reconciliation; schedule ID, cardiology, and cardiac-surgery follow-up plus an end-of-therapy baseline TTE; patient education on relapse red-flags (recurrent fever), dental hygiene, and an antibiotic-prophylaxis card for future procedures; PWID — MOUD continuation, harm reduction, addiction follow-up; structured transitions-of-care handoff to the OPAT team
expected_los: Collapsed-DRG GMLOS roughly 4.5-7 days (DRG 290 no-CC/MCC ~4.5d; 289 CC ~5.5d; 288 MCC ~7d), but this reflects only the acute inpatient portion; the true biological course is driven by the 4-6 week IV-antibiotic requirement, so real-world index-admission LOS is commonly 14-21+ days for complicated/surgical IE when therapy is completed inpatient before OPAT/SNF transfer
target_los: 7-14 days inpatient under an optimized pathway: stabilize, achieve source control, document blood-culture clearance, complete ~10 days IV, then transition to OPAT or POET partial-oral; uncomplicated right-sided PWID IE may be shorter, while no-OPAT-candidate PWID and post-surgical/complicated cases run longer
quality_metrics: Blood cultures obtained before antibiotics; door-to-antibiotic <1h and SEP-1 bundle compliance if septic; multidisciplinary Endocarditis Team utilization (process measure linked to lower mortality); timely echo (TTE/TEE) completion and appropriate/timely surgical referral; documented culture clearance; in-hospital, 30-day, and 1-year mortality; 30-day readmission; relapse/recurrence rate; OPAT-related complications, CLABSI, and C. difficile rates; in PWID — MOUD-initiation rate and patient-directed/AMA-discharge rate
common_complications: Heart failure from valve destruction (most common; leading surgical indication) — mitigated by serial echo and early-surgery referral; perivalvular abscess causing heart block (serial ECG/telemetry, cardiac CT); systemic embolization/stroke (~20-40%), splenic/renal infarct, mycotic aneurysm (embolic imaging, early surgery for large vegetations); persistent/uncontrolled infection and metastatic abscess (surveillance cultures, source control); AKI (immune GN plus antibiotic nephrotoxicity — therapeutic drug monitoring); septic shock; antibiotic/line complications — C. difficile, CLABSI, line thrombosis
readmission_drivers: Relapse/recurrence (inadequate duration, ongoing injection drug use, re-injection into the line); OPAT complications (line infection/thrombosis, drug ADRs, nephrotoxicity); progressive heart failure; new embolic events; incomplete antibiotic course or AMA/self-directed discharge (especially PWID); prevention levers — multidisciplinary Endocarditis Team, addiction treatment/MOUD, structured OPAT with weekly monitoring, a POET oral option for line-averse/PWID patients, and close ID/cardiology follow-up
guideline_source: AHA 2015 Scientific Statement on Infective Endocarditis in Adults (Baddour et al., Circulation) — primary US statement; 2023 ESC Guidelines for the Management of Endocarditis (endorsed by EACTS/EANM); 2023 Duke-ISCVID Diagnostic Criteria (ISCVID); 2016 AATS Consensus Guidelines on Surgical Treatment of IE
key_citations: Per PubMed: Baddour LM et al. AHA Scientific Statement, Circulation 2015;132(15):1435-1486 (PMID 26373316, https://doi.org/10.1161/CIR.0000000000000296); Delgado V et al. 2023 ESC Guidelines for the management of endocarditis, Eur Heart J 2023;44(39):3948-4042 (PMID 37622656, https://doi.org/10.1093/eurheartj/ehad193); Fowler VG et al. 2023 Duke-ISCVID Criteria, Clin Infect Dis 2023;77(4):518-526 (PMID 37138445, https://doi.org/10.1093/cid/ciad271); Iversen K et al. POET partial-oral trial, N Engl J Med 2019;380(5):415-424 (PMID 30152252, https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1808312); Kang DH et al. EASE early-surgery RCT, N Engl J Med 2012;366(26):2466-2473 (PMID 22738096, https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1112843); Pettersson GB et al. 2016 AATS Consensus, J Thorac Cardiovasc Surg 2017;153(6):1241-1258 (PMID 28365016, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jtcvs.2016.09.093)
evidence_grade: Strong — anchored in Class I AHA/ESC guideline statements and the 2023 Duke-ISCVID diagnostic criteria; antibiotic regimens and multidisciplinary-team recommendations are Class I; surgical-timing evidence is moderate (single RCT [EASE/Kang] plus large observational data); the partial-oral switch is moderate (single noninferiority RCT [POET] in selected stable left-sided IE); overall Strong with select Moderate domains
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 288 (MCC): heart failure, septic shock, ischemic/hemorrhagic stroke, perivalvular abscess with heart block, dialysis-requiring AKI, or valve surgery drive ICU care, the longest LOS, and highest in-hospital mortality (~15-20%+); DRG 289 (CC): moderate complications (uncomplicated AKI, anemia, embolic infarct) — intermediate LOS; DRG 290 (no CC/MCC): uncomplicated, frequently young PWID right-sided tricuspid IE responding to antibiotics — shortest acute stay but still requires 4-6 weeks of therapy; the MCC tier is what drives ICU utilization, valve surgery, neuro/embolic complications, and dialysis
pathway_pearls: 1) Three sets of blood cultures from separate sites BEFORE antibiotics is the highest-yield diagnostic step — do not reflexively start antibiotics and lose the organism (the one exception: do not delay in septic shock). 2) Activate the multidisciplinary Endocarditis Team (ID + cardiology + cardiac surgery) early — Class I and mortality-reducing; do not delay surgical evaluation for heart failure, uncontrolled infection, or large vegetations. 3) New AV block on serial ECG/telemetry means perivalvular abscess until proven otherwise, prompting urgent surgical/cardiac-CT evaluation. 4) Plan the antibiotic-delivery endgame by Day 1-2 — OPAT vs POET partial-oral vs inpatient completion is the primary LOS lever; in PWID, integrate MOUD/addiction care and reassess line safety.

scope_and_volume_notes: Acute & subacute endocarditis; rising with IV drug use. Long IV-antibiotic stays; some -> valve surgery/Critical Care.

admission_criteria: Most isolated lower-extremity DVT is now managed OUTPATIENT (ASH 2020 conditional rec: home over hospital treatment for uncomplicated proximal DVT at low complication risk). Objective INPATIENT indications: limb-threatening DVT — phlegmasia cerulea/alba dolens or massive iliofemoral thrombus needing catheter-directed thrombolysis/thrombectomy; high or active bleeding risk requiring monitored/reversible parenteral anticoagulation; concurrent symptomatic intermediate-high-risk PE (RV strain, hypoxia, hemodynamic compromise); severe renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min) or extreme body weight complicating DOAC use; active bleeding, severe thrombocytopenia (<50k), or need for IVC filter (AC contraindicated); significant comorbidity/decompensation; recurrent VTE despite therapeutic AC; social/access barriers (no home support, cannot obtain/afford/self-administer anticoagulant, unreliable follow-up). ED discharge/observation appropriate for uncomplicated proximal or distal DVT, stable, low bleeding risk, able to start a DOAC with reliable follow-up.
risk_stratification: Wells DVT score, two-level (>=2 = DVT "likely" -> compression US; <2 = "unlikely" -> high-sensitivity D-dimer, negative excludes DVT); provoked vs unprovoked classification drives duration. Bleeding risk: HAS-BLED, VTE-BLEED, or CHEST bleeding-risk factor count -> guides duration and thrombolysis eligibility. HERDOO2 rule (women, first unprovoked VTE: <=1 point may safely stop AC after ~5-12 months; >=2 continue). Inpatient prophylaxis context: Padua Prediction Score >=4 (or IMPROVE) = high risk warranting pharmacologic prophylaxis; Caprini score for surgical patients. If concurrent PE: sPESI/PESI and Hestia criteria for outpatient eligibility, plus troponin/BNP and RV assessment for risk tier.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with platelets (baseline before AC; screen thrombocytopenia; HIT surveillance with 4Ts if platelets fall on heparin); CMP with calculated CrCl (selects/doses DOAC — avoid dabigatran/rivaroxaban if CrCl <30, apixaban usable to CrCl 15-25); LFTs (avoid DOACs in Child-Pugh B/C); baseline PT/INR and aPTT before starting AC; high-sensitivity D-dimer ONLY when pretest probability low/unlikely (rules out, not in); type & screen if thrombolysis/procedure planned; pregnancy test in women of childbearing age; selective thrombophilia testing (antiphospholipid antibodies) only if unprovoked/recurrent/young and drawn before AC — NOT routine acutely; age-appropriate cancer screening for unprovoked VTE (not exhaustive occult-malignancy workup — SOME trial negative).
initial_imaging_dx: Whole-leg or proximal compression ultrasonography with Doppler is first-line, obtained within hours of presentation (same-day/ED) — the diagnostic test of choice; if proximal US negative but pretest probability high, either serial US in 5-7 days or proceed to CT/MR venography; CT venography or MR venography for suspected iliac/IVC/pelvic thrombus and to define proximal extent / May-Thurner anatomy before any intervention; if concurrent PE suspected (dyspnea, hypoxia, chest pain) CT pulmonary angiography; bedside or formal echocardiography if PE with possible RV strain or hemodynamic compromise.
time_critical_interventions: Start therapeutic anticoagulation promptly once DVT confirmed — and empirically BEFORE imaging if pretest probability high and bleeding risk low (do not wait for US if delay >4h). Phlegmasia cerulea dolens = limb/life-threatening emergency -> emergent vascular surgery/IR for catheter-directed thrombolysis or thrombectomy within hours plus systemic AC; monitor for compartment syndrome (fasciotomy if needed). For selected acute iliofemoral DVT considered for CDT, intervene within ~14 days of symptom onset (acute thrombus). No door-to-balloon-type mandate for uncomplicated DVT — the time target is simply prompt anticoagulation initiation.
initial_management: First-line = DOACs preferred over VKA (ASH 2020, CHEST 2021). Single-drug oral regimens: apixaban 10 mg PO BID x7 days then 5 mg BID (AMPLIFY), or rivaroxaban 15 mg PO BID x21 days then 20 mg daily with food (EINSTEIN). Parenteral lead-in (LMWH >=5 days) then dabigatran 150 mg BID or edoxaban 60 mg daily (Hokusai). LMWH (enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC q12h or 1.5 mg/kg daily; or fondaparinux) for pregnancy, some cancer, or DOAC-ineligible; IV unfractionated heparin (aPTT/anti-Xa-titrated) for severe renal failure, periprocedural, or high/uncertain bleeding risk (short half-life, reversible). VKA (warfarin, INR 2.0-3.0) with >=5-day LMWH bridge until INR >=2 x24h — reserved for antiphospholipid syndrome, severe renal impairment, mechanical valves. Cancer-associated VTE: DOAC (apixaban [Caravaggio], edoxaban, rivaroxaban) or LMWH. Retrievable IVC filter ONLY if AC absolutely contraindicated (acute proximal DVT + active bleeding) — remove once AC can start; no routine filter. CDT/pharmacomechanical thrombolysis NOT routine (ATTRACT: no reduction in overall PTS) — reserve for limb-threatening iliofemoral disease. Reversal on standby: idarucizumab (dabigatran); andexanet alfa or 4F-PCC (apixaban/rivaroxaban/edoxaban); IV vitamin K + 4F-PCC (warfarin); protamine (heparin).
day1_milestones: Confirm DVT on compression US and document proximal vs distal extent; initiate therapeutic anticoagulation (DOAC preferred) with correct loading dose; formally assess bleeding risk and reversibility; classify provoked vs unprovoked and identify precipitant; obtain baseline CBC/renal/hepatic/coags; assess limb viability (rule out phlegmasia); begin patient education on the anticoagulant and adherence; encourage ambulation (no bed rest); screen early for outpatient-conversion eligibility (stable, low bleeding risk, reliable follow-up).
day2_milestones: Reassess limb swelling/pain and perfusion; confirm anticoagulant tolerance and adherence and absence of bleeding; verify renal/hepatic-appropriate agent and dose; finalize provoked/unprovoked determination and planned AC duration (3-6 months vs indefinite); begin/advance discharge planning and medication-access verification (prior authorization for DOAC); consult hematology/vascular if unprovoked, recurrent, cancer-associated, or iliofemoral.
day3plus_milestones: Ensure clinically stable on the oral agent with improving limb symptoms and controlled pain; complete the AC duration/education plan and periodic-reassessment scheduling; arrange anticoagulation-clinic or PCP follow-up in 1-2 weeks; for unprovoked VTE ensure age-appropriate cancer screening ordered/completed; finalize transitions-of-care handoff; discharge when criteria met (most reach readiness by Day 1-2; Day 3+ typically reflects CC/MCC, phlegmasia post-thrombolysis, or bleeding).
consults_multidisciplinary: Hematology (unprovoked, recurrent, thrombophilia/antiphospholipid, cancer-associated, or complex duration decisions); Vascular surgery / Interventional radiology (iliofemoral or IVC thrombus, phlegmasia, CDT/thrombectomy candidacy, IVC filter placement/retrieval); Pharmacy/anticoagulation service (agent selection, renal/hepatic dosing, drug-interaction and adherence counseling, DOAC lead-in education); Care management/social work EARLY (home support, medication affordability/access, follow-up logistics); Oncology if active malignancy; Maternal-fetal medicine/OB if pregnant (LMWH, not DOAC/warfarin); PT for mobility as needed.
monitoring_level: General medical floor (telemetry not required) for isolated uncomplicated DVT; telemetry/step-down for concurrent intermediate-risk PE (RV strain, elevated troponin/BNP) or significant cardiopulmonary comorbidity; ICU for phlegmasia cerulea dolens with hemodynamic compromise, post-thrombolysis monitoring (serial bleeding/neuro checks), or high-risk PE. Escalation triggers: new hypoxia/dyspnea/hemodynamic instability (suspect PE), active or major bleeding, worsening limb ischemia or compartment syndrome, or hemodynamic deterioration.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Regular diet (consistent vitamin K intake if on warfarin); EARLY ambulation encouraged — bed rest does NOT reduce PE risk and prolongs recovery; graduated compression stockings offered for acute symptom relief but not mandated to prevent PTS (SOX trial negative for routine elastic stockings); therapeutic anticoagulation IS the treatment (prophylactic dosing not applicable once therapeutic); glycemic control per inpatient targets; in catheter-associated upper-extremity DVT remove the offending/nonfunctional catheter if no longer needed; discontinue unnecessary central lines.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable without hypoxia; no active bleeding and acceptable bleeding risk; therapeutic anticoagulation established, correctly dosed, and tolerated; pain controlled and limb viable/improving; patient or caregiver able to obtain, afford, and correctly self-administer the anticoagulant and understands lead-in/step-down dosing; documented follow-up arranged; return precautions understood.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home (self-care); complete medication reconciliation with explicit WRITTEN DOAC instructions emphasizing the loading/step-down schedule (apixaban 7-day, rivaroxaban 21-day) to prevent dosing errors; schedule anticoagulation clinic or PCP follow-up within 1-2 weeks and hematology if indicated; educate on bleeding warning signs, PE symptoms, adherence, and drug/food interactions; define total AC duration (3-6 months for provoked/transient; indefinite with periodic reassessment for unprovoked or persistent risk); provide compression stockings if symptomatic; confirm prescription coverage/prior authorization BEFORE discharge; formal transitions-of-care handoff to the outpatient anticoagulation manager.
expected_los: Approximately 2.5-3.5 days GMLOS (CMS MS-DRG 295 without CC/MCC ~2.5 days; MS-DRG 294 with CC/MCC ~3.4 days); arithmetic mean runs ~3.5-5 days in complicated/CC-MCC cases. The collapsed group is increasingly diverted to outpatient, so admitted cases skew toward complicated disease.
target_los: 0-2 days under the ideal pathway — many uncomplicated proximal DVTs bypass admission entirely (ED/observation start on a single-drug DOAC with outpatient follow-up); admitted patients targeted to <=1-2 days once therapeutic AC is established and the limb is stable; longer only for phlegmasia/thrombolysis or major CC/MCC.
quality_metrics: Time-to-therapeutic-anticoagulation after diagnosis; appropriate agent selection and renal/hepatic dosing; anticoagulation-related adverse-event / bleeding rate (patient-safety measure); documented AC duration plan and follow-up at discharge; 30-day all-cause and VTE-related readmission; hospital-acquired / potentially-preventable VTE (HAC) and appropriate inpatient VTE prophylaxis (legacy CMS VTE-1/VTE-2, Padua/Caprini-based); avoidance of inappropriate IVC filter and non-indicated CDT; documented patient anticoagulation education; observed-to-expected LOS and appropriate outpatient-diversion rate; HCAHPS.
common_complications: Anticoagulation-associated bleeding (GI, intracranial) — mitigate with bleeding-risk assessment, correct renal/hepatic dosing, and reversal-agent availability; symptomatic PE from clot propagation/embolization — prompt therapeutic AC and mobilization; post-thrombotic syndrome (long-term, up to ~40% of proximal DVT) — adequate AC, early ambulation, compression for symptoms; heparin-induced thrombocytopenia if heparin used — platelet monitoring and 4Ts scoring; recurrent VTE — ensure therapeutic levels and adherence; rarely limb loss from phlegmasia — emergent revascularization.
readmission_drivers: Recurrent VTE (subtherapeutic dosing, nonadherence, or unrecognized malignancy); anticoagulant-related bleeding; missed or progressing PE; DOAC transition/lead-in dosing errors (patient stops the twice-daily loading dose early or skips the step-down) and prescription access/cost failures; inadequate or absent follow-up. Prevention levers: crystal-clear written dosing instructions, pharmacist/anticoagulation-clinic transition, follow-up within 1-2 weeks, adherence and affordability support (co-pay assistance / prior auth resolved before discharge), and cancer screening for unprovoked events.
guideline_source: American Society of Hematology (ASH) 2020 Guidelines for Management of VTE: Treatment of DVT and PE (Ortel et al., Blood Advances 2020) and American College of Chest Physicians (CHEST) Antithrombotic Therapy for VTE Disease, Second Update 2021 (Stevens/Woller et al., Chest 2021); supported by ESC/ESVS 2021 venous guidance and the SIR 2022-2023 position statement on endovascular management of acute iliofemoral DVT.
key_citations: Per PubMed: ASH 2020 — Ortel TL et al., Blood Adv 2020;4(19):4693-4738, PMID 33007077 (DOI 10.1182/bloodadvances.2020001830); CHEST 2021 2nd update — Stevens SM, Woller SC et al., Chest 2021;160(6):2247-2259, PMID 34352279 (DOI 10.1016/j.chest.2021.07.056); AMPLIFY (apixaban) — Agnelli G et al., N Engl J Med 2013;369(9):799-808, PMID 23808982 (DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1302507); ATTRACT (pharmacomechanical CDT, negative for PTS) — Vedantham S et al., N Engl J Med 2017;377(23):2240-2252, PMID 29211671 (DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1615066); Caravaggio (apixaban in cancer-associated VTE) — Agnelli G et al., N Engl J Med 2020;382(17):1599-1607, PMID 32223112 (DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1915103).
evidence_grade: Strong — anchored by two current Class I / GRADE-based society guidelines (ASH 2020, CHEST 2021) plus multiple high-quality RCTs (AMPLIFY, EINSTEIN, Hokusai, Caravaggio) supporting DOAC first-line therapy and home treatment. Select elements are weaker/conditional: catheter-directed thrombolysis (ATTRACT negative for routine PTS prevention -> reserve for limb-threatening disease), routine compression stockings (SOX negative), and thrombophilia testing (expert-opinion/consensus).
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The collapsed pair splits on CC/MCC (MS-DRG 294 with, 295 without). MCC/CC drivers — active cancer, concurrent PE, acute bleed/anemia, AKI/CKD, sepsis, or phlegmasia — raise GMLOS (~2.5 -> ~3.4+ days), increase parenteral/procedural and ICU need (thrombolysis, filter), and drive complications and readmission. Uncomplicated DVT (295) increasingly bypasses admission entirely, so the admitted population skews toward the CC/MCC tier; cancer-associated and iliofemoral/phlegmasia cases dominate true inpatient stays and warrant hematology/vascular co-management and longer LOS.
pathway_pearls: (1) Don't admit reflexively — default uncomplicated proximal DVT to ED/observation start on a single-drug DOAC with outpatient follow-up (ASH 2020); reserve beds for phlegmasia, high bleeding risk, significant concurrent PE, or access/social barriers. (2) Get DOAC lead-in dosing exactly right — apixaban 10 mg BID x7 days then 5 mg BID; rivaroxaban 15 mg BID x21 days then 20 mg daily — and hand off with written instructions; dosing/transition errors are a top bounce-back driver. (3) CDT is not routine (ATTRACT negative) — reserve for limb-threatening iliofemoral DVT; encourage early ambulation over bed rest, and don't rely on compression stockings to prevent PTS (SOX negative). (4) Limit acute thrombophilia testing; do age-appropriate (not exhaustive) cancer screening for unprovoked VTE, and predefine duration (3-6 months provoked vs indefinite unprovoked, using HERDOO2 to de-escalate in women).
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Deep vein thrombophlebitis; increasingly managed outpatient, so inpatient volume modest.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 299 — Peripheral Vascular Disorders with MCC; 300 — Peripheral Vascular Disorders with CC; 301 — Peripheral Vascular Disorders without CC/MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 5/5 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Current pass completed citation-resolvability, coding, taxonomy, schema, and consistency checks. The source workflow contains no completed adversarial high-stakes clinical verdict for this row; clinician revalidation remains required before protocol or order-set use.
data_quality_notes: Replaced retired deep-vein-thrombophlebitis DRGs 294-295 with the current Peripheral Vascular Disorders severity triplet 299-301; this creates an intentional pathway-level overlap with the broader peripheral-vascular row. Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk. Current DRG overlap with pathway rank(s): 55.

admission_criteria: Inpatient admission for MEDICAL complications of a digestive malignancy (colorectal/gastric/esophageal/pancreatic/hepatobiliary/small-bowel; NOT a resection stay). Objective indications: CT-confirmed malignant bowel obstruction (MBO) with vomiting/obstipation/distension; overt GI bleed (hematemesis/melena/hematochezia) with hemodynamic instability, orthostasis, Hgb drop, or Glasgow-Blatchford >=2; intractable nausea/vomiting or cancer pain crisis failing outpatient regimen; malignant biliary obstruction with cholangitis (fever+jaundice+RUQ pain, Tokyo Grade II/III) or sepsis; symptomatic/tense malignant ascites needing large-volume paracentesis with respiratory compromise; hypercalcemia of malignancy (corrected Ca >12-14 mg/dL or symptomatic); febrile neutropenia; new symptomatic VTE; failure-to-thrive/cachexia with dehydration, AKI, or severe electrolyte derangement precluding oral intake. ED discharge/observation appropriate for: GI bleed Glasgow-Blatchford 0-1 (ACG very-low-risk -> outpatient EGD), controllable symptoms with tolerated oral intake, non-tense ascites for scheduled outpatient paracentesis.
risk_stratification: Glasgow-Blatchford Score (GBS) 0-1 = very-low-risk UGIB -> ED discharge + outpatient endoscopy; GBS >=7 predicts need for endoscopic therapy/transfusion -> admit + EGD <=24h (ACG 2021). AIMS65 / post-endoscopy Rockall >=3 -> higher rebleed/mortality, closer monitoring. ECOG performance status: 0-2 -> candidate for colonic stent/palliative surgery/home parenteral nutrition/next-line systemic therapy; 3-4 -> comfort-focused (venting gastrostomy, symptom regimen). Tokyo Guidelines 2018 cholangitis severity: Grade I (mild) antibiotics alone; Grade II (moderate) early biliary drainage; Grade III (organ dysfunction) urgent drainage + ICU. qSOFA >=2 / SOFA rise -> sepsis pathway. Corrected calcium >14 mg/dL or symptomatic -> hypercalcemic-crisis protocol. NRS-2002 >=3 or GLIM criteria (weight loss >5%, low BMI/muscle) -> malnutrition dx + nutrition consult; albumin <3.5 g/dL and CRP elevation = adverse prognosis. Khorana score >=2 -> high chemo-VTE risk. Palliative Prognostic Index (PPI) / PaP for prognostication and hospice timing.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential (anemia from bleed/marrow, neutropenia); CMP for electrolytes, renal function (AKI from vomiting/dehydration), LFTs (biliary obstruction, liver mets); Mg, phosphate; corrected calcium/ionized Ca (hypercalcemia of malignancy) -> triggers saline+calcitonin+bisphosphonate; albumin/prealbumin (nutrition + prognosis); lactate if sepsis/ischemia concern -> SEP-1 pathway; PT/INR/aPTT + fibrinogen (bleeding, pre-procedure, coagulopathy reversal); type & screen/crossmatch for active bleed; lipase (pancreatic); blood cultures x2 if febrile/septic; CRP and LDH (tumor burden/prognosis); tumor markers CEA (colorectal), CA 19-9 (pancreatic/biliary), AFP (hepatocellular) as baseline trend (not diagnostic); iron studies/reticulocyte for chronic anemia; urinalysis; beta-hCG in women of childbearing age before imaging/chemo.
initial_imaging_dx: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV (+/- oral/rectal) contrast is first-line for suspected obstruction/perforation/bleeding source within hours of admission -> defines transition point, cause, ischemia, carcinomatosis, ascites. Acute abdominal series/upright CXR for free air and obstructive pattern at presentation. Upper endoscopy (EGD) within 24h for UGIB (ACG); colonoscopy/CT for lower GI source. Water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) study can be diagnostic and therapeutic in partial small-bowel obstruction and predicts resolution. RUQ ultrasound then MRCP for biliary obstruction; ERCP for therapeutic biliary decompression (urgent <=24h in cholangitis). Diagnostic/therapeutic paracentesis for new ascites (cell count, culture, cytology, SAAG albumin). CT chest and image-guided/EUS biopsy for new/unstaged malignancy. Flexible sigmoidoscopy/CT to assess colonic stent candidacy for left-sided obstruction.
time_critical_interventions: Septic cholangitis/septic shock: SEP-1 bundle -> lactate + blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1h, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4, repeat lactate; biliary drainage (ERCP/PTC) <=24h for Tokyo Grade III, early (24-48h) for Grade II (Tokyo Guidelines 2018). Acute GI hemorrhage: resuscitate, restrictive RBC transfusion at Hgb <7 g/dL (target 7-9), erythromycin 250 mg IV 30-120 min pre-endoscopy as prokinetic, EGD <=24h with endoscopic hemostasis (ACG 2021). Suspected bowel ischemia, closed-loop obstruction, or perforation -> emergent surgical consult (surgical emergency, diverts off medical pathway). Hypercalcemic crisis: IV isotonic saline + calcitonin 4 IU/kg q12h (rapid) + IV zoledronic acid/denosumab (24-72h onset). Prompt NG decompression for high-output vomiting MBO.
initial_management: MBO (MASCC 2022): bowel rest/NPO, NG decompression if refractory vomiting; judicious IV fluids (avoid overload, ~1-1.5 L/day); antisecretory octreotide 100-300 mcg/day SC/IV or infusion (Grade A for reducing vomiting); antiemetic haloperidol 0.5-2 mg (butyrophenone, preferred in complete MBO); metoclopramide only in partial/no-colic obstruction (avoid in complete - perforation risk); corticosteroid dexamethasone 4-16 mg/day IV x3-5 days for anti-edema, taper if no benefit by 72h; anticholinergic hyoscine butylbromide/glycopyrrolate for colic/secretions; parenteral opioid (morphine/hydromorphone) for pain. GI bleed: IV PPI (pantoprazole 80 mg bolus then 8 mg/h or high-dose intermittent), restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL, reverse coagulopathy/hold anticoagulation, endoscopic hemostasis; TXA NOT recommended (HALT-IT negative); palliative RT or embolization for refractory tumor bleeding. Cholangitis: empiric piperacillin-tazobactam (or ceftriaxone+metronidazole; carbapenem if ESBL/healthcare-associated) + biliary drainage. Cachexia (ASCO 2020/2023): dietitian, oral nutrition supplements, short-course dexamethasone or progestin (megestrol, note VTE risk), olanzapine 2.5 mg daily to improve appetite/weight (ASCO 2023 rapid update); treat reversible nausea/constipation/thrush. Malignant ascites: therapeutic/large-volume paracentesis, tunneled catheter (PleurX) if recurrent. VTE prophylaxis LMWH unless active bleed/platelets <50k.
day1_milestones: Establish the principal complication (obstruction/bleed/cholangitis/cachexia/hypercalcemia) and confirm goals of care + code status. Complete CT abd/pelvis and identify source. Start disease-specific therapy (octreotide/PPI infusion/antibiotics/saline+calcitonin). Resuscitate: correct volume, K/Mg/Ca/phosphate, reverse coagulopathy. Achieve numeric pain and nausea control targets. Place NG if indicated; strict I/O; Foley only if needed for volume/paracentesis monitoring. Initiate consults day 1: medical oncology, palliative/supportive care (early - Temel/ASCO), GI/therapeutic endoscopy or IR, surgery if obstruction, registered dietitian, case management. Make VTE prophylaxis decision. Screen malnutrition (NRS-2002/GLIM). Define tissue-diagnosis/staging plan if new cancer.
day2_milestones: Reassess obstruction: return of flatus/stool, declining NG output, decreased distension -> clamp trial then clears. Perform indicated procedure: EGD/hemostasis, ERCP + biliary stent, or colonic self-expanding metal stent (ESGE 2020 - palliation or bridge to surgery in left-sided curable disease). De-escalate antibiotics per cultures (cholangitis). Taper/stop dexamethasone if no MBO benefit by 72h. Finalize nutrition plan (oral vs enteral vs parenteral by ECOG/prognosis). Document goals-of-care conversation and prognosis with palliative care engaged. Mobilize; continue VTE prophylaxis; transition IV to oral/transdermal analgesia if tolerating. Confirm Hgb stability trend after any bleed.
day3plus_milestones: MBO: tolerating diet with returned bowel function and NG removed, OR definitive palliation chosen (stent/venting gastrostomy) vs comfort-focused track if irreversible/poor performance status. Bleed: Hgb stable >=48-72h without transfusion, diet resumed, plan for oncologic treatment of tumor source. Cholangitis: afebrile, LFTs improving, stent patent, transition to oral antibiotics to complete course. Cachexia: sustainable intake or home nutrition arranged, appetite stimulant continued, follow-up dietitian. Finalize disposition (home + home health, home with hospice, SNF, inpatient hospice, or HPN setup). Oncology plan for next-line systemic/RT therapy or transition to best supportive care. Complete medication reconciliation and schedule follow-up. Escalate to definitive surgery only for select ECOG 0-2 curable/resectable disease.
consults_multidisciplinary: Medical oncology (staging, systemic therapy) - day 1. Palliative/supportive care - EARLY day 1 for concurrent symptom control + goals of care (Temel 2010; ASCO integration 2017). GI/therapeutic endoscopy (EGD hemostasis, colonic stent, ERCP). Interventional radiology (venting gastrostomy, percutaneous biliary drain, tunneled peritoneal catheter, tumor embolization). Surgery (resectability/palliative surgery for MBO; emergent if perforation/ischemia). Registered dietitian/nutrition support (all - malnutrition screen). Radiation oncology (palliative RT for bleeding/pain/obstruction). Clinical pharmacy (opioid rotation, anticoagulation, antiemetics, chemo). Case management/social work (disposition, hospice, HPN, financial). PT/OT (deconditioning, safe-discharge assessment). Chaplaincy/psycho-oncology for patient and caregiver support.
monitoring_level: Most medical digestive-malignancy admissions -> general medical/oncology floor. Telemetry for significant bleed, marked electrolyte derangement (hypercalcemia, hypokalemia), or cardiac comorbidity. Step-down/ICU for: hemodynamic instability or massive GI hemorrhage requiring ongoing transfusion, septic shock from cholangitis (Tokyo Grade III with organ dysfunction), respiratory compromise from tense ascites/aspiration, hypercalcemic crisis with altered mental status, or vasopressor need. Escalation triggers: MAP <65 after fluids, ongoing transfusion >2 U/6h or recurrent hematemesis, rising lactate, new organ dysfunction/qSOFA >=2, peritonitis or free air (surgical emergency), or GCS decline -> rapid response + ICU/surgery.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: NPO in active MBO or bleeding; graded reintroduction clears -> low-residue/low-fiber (<=10 g/day) once obstruction resolves (MASCC); malnutrition screen (NRS-2002/GLIM) within 24h with dietitian; enteral preferred over parenteral; parenteral/home parenteral nutrition reserved for selected ECOG 0-2 patients with slow-growing tumor and no refractory effusions/anemia/hypoalbuminemia; watch refeeding syndrome. Mobility: early ambulation, PT for deconditioning, aspiration precautions with head-of-bed elevation. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) for hospitalized active-cancer patients unless active bleeding or platelets <50k; mechanical if pharmacologic contraindicated (ASCO 2023). Glucose target 140-180 mg/dL (watch steroid-induced hyperglycemia). Lines/catheters: remove NG when output low and diet tolerated; early Foley removal; opioid-induced-constipation bowel regimen once obstruction excluded; assess central-line necessity if parenteral nutrition.
discharge_criteria: Symptoms (pain, nausea, vomiting) controlled on an oral/transdermal/subcutaneous regimen sustainable at home. Tolerating adequate oral intake OR a durable nutrition plan (enteral/home parenteral) established OR comfort-focused plan in place. Obstruction resolved (bowel function + diet tolerance) or definitively palliated (stent/venting tube). Hemodynamically stable with Hgb stable >=24-48h without transfusion. Afebrile with source controlled and biliary drainage patent; on appropriate oral antibiotics if applicable. Electrolytes/corrected calcium normalized and AKI resolving. Goals of care documented, disposition secured, follow-up and equipment/services arranged, caregiver educated on red flags.
discharge_planning: Disposition options: home with home health, home with hospice, SNF, inpatient hospice, or home parenteral nutrition setup for selected patients. Medication reconciliation: opioid + prophylactic bowel regimen, antiemetics, octreotide/steroid taper, PPI, anticoagulation, appetite stimulant, oral antibiotics. Schedule follow-up: medical oncology within 1-2 weeks, palliative care, PCP, and procedure care (stent/drain surveillance). Patient/caregiver education: ostomy/venting-tube/biliary-drain/peritoneal-catheter care, recurrent-obstruction and bleeding warning signs, fever, and clear return precautions. Transitions of care: warm handoff to hospice or home-health agency, complete MOLST/POLST and advance directives, confirm prescriptions and durable medical equipment, and provide a written after-visit summary with 24/7 contact for symptom escalation.
expected_los: Blended CMS GMLOS approximately 3.5-4.5 days across the collapsed group: DRG 374 (digestive malignancy w/MCC) GMLOS ~5.5 days (ALOS ~7 days); DRG 375 (w/CC) ~3.7-4 days; DRG 376 (w/o CC/MCC) ~2.7-3 days.
target_los: 3-4 days blended under the ideal pathway (DRG 376 ~2 days; DRG 375 ~3 days; DRG 374 ~4-5 days), achieved via day-1 palliative care integration, expedited endoscopy/colonic stent/ERCP within 24h, and disposition planning from admission; an irreversible-MBO or end-of-life comfort track may appropriately shorten to hospice transfer in 2-3 days.
quality_metrics: 30-day all-cause readmission rate; timeliness of palliative-care consultation and hospice enrollment for appropriate patients; SEP-1 bundle compliance when septic cholangitis/shock present; VTE prophylaxis rate (VTE-1/VTE-2); restrictive-transfusion (Hgb <7) adherence in GI bleed; ASCO QOPI end-of-life measures (systemic therapy within 14 days of death, ICU death, hospice enrollment <3 days before death); documented malnutrition screening; advance-care-planning/goals-of-care documentation rate; length-of-stay index (observed/expected); hospital-acquired conditions (CLABSI, CAUTI, C. difficile); pain reassessment compliance; 30-day mortality.
common_complications: Recurrent/refractory bowel obstruction (mitigate with octreotide, dexamethasone, venting gastrostomy); aspiration pneumonia from vomiting/NG (aspiration precautions, decompression, HOB elevation); rebleeding (endoscopic hemostasis, PPI infusion, restrictive transfusion); line/catheter and drain infections and sepsis, especially with parenteral nutrition (strict line care, minimize catheter days); AKI from dehydration/contrast/nephrotoxins (judicious fluids, renal dosing); electrolyte derangements and refeeding syndrome (slow refeeding, replace phosphate/Mg/K); VTE in highly thrombogenic tumors - pancreatic, gastric (LMWH prophylaxis); delirium from opioids/metabolic disturbance/hospitalization (delirium bundle, med review); opioid-induced constipation compounding obstruction (bowel regimen once obstruction excluded); deconditioning and pressure injury (early PT, repositioning).
readmission_drivers: Recurrent malignant bowel obstruction; uncontrolled pain/nausea from an inadequate discharge regimen; dehydration and failure-to-thrive with inability to maintain intake; recurrent GI bleeding; biliary or colonic stent occlusion with recurrent cholangitis/obstruction; disease progression; goals-of-care misalignment driving repeat aggressive admissions. Prevention levers: early palliative care and timely hospice referral; robust oral/transdermal symptom regimen with rescue dosing; dietitian follow-up and nutrition plan; scheduled stent/drain surveillance; home-health support and clear written return precautions; oncology follow-up within 1-2 weeks; caregiver education and 24/7 symptom-escalation contact.
guideline_source: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology (Colon Cancer v2.2025; plus Rectal, Gastric, Esophageal/Esophagogastric, Pancreatic, Hepatobiliary) - oncologic framework; MASCC 2022 malignant bowel obstruction recommendations; ASCO Management of Cancer Cachexia 2020 + Rapid Recommendation Update 2023 (olanzapine); ASCO VTE Prophylaxis and Treatment in Patients With Cancer 2023; ACG Upper GI and Ulcer Bleeding 2021 (tumor bleeding); ASCO Integration of Palliative Care into Standard Oncology Care 2017; ESGE Colonic Self-Expandable Metal Stents Update 2020; Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (malignant biliary obstruction/cholangitis); ESPEN/ASPEN nutrition-in-cancer guidance.
