evidence_grade: Moderate overall. Component evidence is strong for the GI-bleed transfusion threshold (Villanueva RCT; ACG GRADE, restrictive Hgb <7), VTE prophylaxis/treatment (ASCO 2023, strong), colonic self-expanding metal stents for palliation and bridge-to-surgery in left-sided obstruction (ESGE 2020, high-quality), and early palliative-care integration (Temel RCT; ASCO). Evidence is weaker/consensus-based for malignant bowel obstruction pharmacotherapy (MASCC 2022 grades range A to D, mostly low-quality) and for cachexia agents (olanzapine and progestins - limited/informal recommendation). No single unifying RCT covers the heterogeneous medical digestive-malignancy admission; the pathway integrates disease-specific society guidelines.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 376 (no CC/MCC): typically single, controllable issue (new-diagnosis workup, partial obstruction resolving with bowel rest, controlled symptoms) -> floor care, LOS ~2-3 days, low complication risk. DRG 375 (with CC): moderate acuity - anemia requiring transfusion, AKI stage 1, dehydration, electrolyte disorder, hypoalbuminemia, partial obstruction; LOS ~4 days, more consults/procedures. DRG 374 (with MCC): high acuity - septic cholangitis, major/ongoing hemorrhage, complete MBO with perforation risk, hypercalcemic crisis, severe protein-calorie malnutrition, sepsis or acute respiratory failure/aspiration, AKI requiring dialysis; step-down/ICU more likely, LOS ~5.5-7+ days, higher complication and in-hospital mortality, and a disproportionate share transitioning to hospice or comfort-focused care. Severity tier should trigger earlier palliative-care and goals-of-care engagement, not merely more intervention.
pathway_pearls: Engage palliative/supportive care on DAY 1 - many of these admissions are advanced/incurable disease, and early concurrent integration improves symptom control and quality of life, reduces aggressive end-of-life care, and can prolong survival (Temel 2010; ASCO 2017). Match intervention intensity to ECOG performance status and prognosis: ECOG 0-2 -> stent/surgery/home parenteral nutrition/next-line therapy candidates; ECOG 3-4 or refractory disease -> comfort-focused (venting gastrostomy + symptom regimen). For MBO use octreotide + dexamethasone + haloperidol and avoid prokinetics in complete obstruction; NG is for temporary decompression, not chronic management (MASCC). For tumor bleeding use restrictive transfusion (Hgb <7), PPI infusion, and EGD within 24h, and never omit VTE prophylaxis (cancer is prothrombotic) unless actively bleeding. Nail goals of care and disposition from admission to break the revolving-door readmission cycle.

admission_criteria: Most breast procedures are outpatient/23h-observation-eligible (partial mastectomy/lumpectomy, simple/total mastectomy +/- sentinel node biopsy, and increasingly mastectomy with immediate implant/tissue-expander reconstruction); true INPATIENT admission (the DRG) is reserved for: autologous free-flap reconstruction (DIEP/TRAM) requiring q1h flap monitoring; select bilateral mastectomy with immediate reconstruction; significant comorbidity (ASA III-IV, poorly controlled diabetes, CAD/CHF, OSA on home O2); large intraoperative blood loss or need for transfusion; intractable postoperative pain or PONV; anticoagulation bridging; inadequate home support precluding safe same-day discharge; and any postoperative complication (expanding hematoma, flap compromise, SSI/sepsis). ED/observation-vs-admit rule: uncomplicated mastectomy with controlled pain/PONV and completed drain teaching discharges same day; hematoma needing evacuation, flap ischemia, or systemic infection mandates inpatient care
risk_stratification: Caprini VTE Risk Assessment Model (2005): 0-2 low (mechanical prophylaxis/ambulation only), 3-4 moderate, 5-8 high, >=8 highest -> add pharmacologic prophylaxis and consider extended post-discharge chemoprophylaxis for high/highest; most mastectomy +/- reconstruction patients score 5-7 (high). ASA Physical Status I-IV for perioperative risk/level-of-care. STOP-BANG >=3 flags OSA -> enhanced postop respiratory monitoring, opioid caution. Breast Reconstruction Risk Assessment (BRA) score stratifies reconstruction complication risk. Modified 5-item Frailty Index and RCRI/Surgical Apgar for older or major-reconstruction patients. Apfel score (0-4) drives number of PONV prophylaxis agents. Nottingham Prognostic Index / AJCC stage guides oncologic (not admission) decisions
initial_workup_labs: Preoperative CBC (baseline Hgb/Hct, platelets; anemia triggers type & screen and optimization), BMP (renal function for drug dosing), coagulation (PT/INR, aPTT) if anticoagulated or undergoing reconstruction; type & screen for bilateral or autologous free-flap cases (higher EBL); beta-hCG in reproductive-age women; HbA1c in diabetics with glycemic optimization target <8% to reduce SSI. Tumor pathology with ER/PR/HER2 receptor status and final margin status drive adjuvant planning. If postoperative SSI suspected: CBC with differential, CRP, wound and blood cultures to guide antibiotics/source control
initial_imaging_dx: Diagnostic (outpatient, days-to-weeks preop): bilateral mammography plus targeted breast/axillary ultrasound; breast MRI in selected cases (dense breasts, occult primary, BRCA/high-risk); image-guided core-needle biopsy for tissue diagnosis before surgery; axillary US with FNA/core of suspicious nodes. Day of surgery: wire or wireless (radioactive seed/magnetic/RFID) localization of nonpalpable lesions; sentinel-node mapping with technetium-99m +/- isosulfan blue or indocyanine green. Systemic staging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis + bone scan or PET/CT) only for stage III+ or symptomatic disease. Postop: autologous flap monitoring with clinical checks +/- implantable Doppler; ultrasound/CT if hematoma or collection suspected
time_critical_interventions: Antimicrobial prophylaxis: cefazolin (clindamycin or vancomycin if beta-lactam allergy) within 60 min before incision; intraoperative redose cefazolin q4h or after EBL >1500 mL; DISCONTINUE prophylaxis by 24h postop even with drains or implants (no evidence of benefit for prolonged courses). Maintain normothermia (core temp >=36C, forced-air warming). Give >=2 PONV-prophylaxis agents intraoperatively. Autologous free-flap: minimize ischemia time and monitor flap q1h x24h; detected venous congestion/arterial compromise requires emergent return to OR within 4-6h to maximize flap salvage. Expanding postop hematoma threatening skin flap/airway -> emergent evacuation
initial_management: ERAS opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia: preop acetaminophen 1 g PO + NSAID/COX-2 (celecoxib 200-400 mg or ketorolac) + gabapentinoid (gabapentin 600 mg or pregabalin) + dexamethasone 4-8 mg (dual analgesic/antiemetic); regional block (pectoralis PECS I/II, paravertebral, or serratus anterior plane) +/- liposomal/plain bupivacaine wound infiltration; reserve opioids (oxycodone 5 mg PRN) as RESCUE only. PONV: dexamethasone + ondansetron, adding scopolamine/aprepitant by Apfel score. Transfusion: restrictive strategy, RBC for Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 with cardiac disease/symptoms). Closed-suction drains removed when output <30 mL/24h. SSI source control: targeted antibiotics + drainage; implant salvage vs explant by severity. Scheduled bowel regimen with any opioid use
day1_milestones: POD0 (day of surgery): emerge from anesthesia with multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia established; early oral intake advanced clears-to-regular (no routine NGT); ambulate within hours of surgery; PONV controlled; begin drain-output measurement and patient/caregiver drain teaching; assess flap perfusion q1h for autologous reconstruction; document spontaneous void; remove Foley if placed; for uncomplicated simple/total mastectomy +/- implant, screen against same-day discharge criteria and discharge home when met
day2_milestones: POD1: pain controlled on oral multimodal regimen with minimal/no IV opioid; tolerating regular diet; ambulating independently; patient/caregiver return-demonstrates independent drain care (stripping, measuring, logging, recognizing infection); flap stable with spacing of checks; discontinue IV fluids and saline-lock/remove IV; remove Foley if still present; complete discharge medication reconciliation and schedule follow-up; discharge admitted simple-mastectomy patients POD1
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ (largely limited to autologous free-flap reconstruction or complicated cases): serial flap checks with progressive mobilization, typical flap discharge POD2-4; confirm afebrile, hemodynamically stable, adequate pain control on oral analgesics, drains manageable at home, and wound intact without hematoma, skin-flap necrosis, or infection; complete drain, arm-ROM, and lymphedema-precaution teaching; arrange outpatient drain removal and medical/radiation oncology follow-up; discharge once all ERAS discharge criteria satisfied
consults_multidisciplinary: Preoperative multidisciplinary breast tumor board for oncologic plan; plastic surgery for reconstruction planning/shared OR; anesthesia/regional for ERAS blocks; medical and radiation oncology for adjuvant sequencing; genetics for BRCA/high-risk evaluation. Perioperative: PT/OT for shoulder ROM and lymphedema risk reduction; nursing for drain-care and wound education; pharmacy for opioid stewardship and medication reconciliation; nutrition for diabetics and wound healing; social work/care management for home support, transportation, and drain/DME supplies; psycho-oncology and support services for body image; certified lymphedema therapist; palliative care if metastatic
monitoring_level: Floor or 23h-observation for simple/total mastectomy +/- implant reconstruction. Higher-acuity monitored/step-down bed for autologous free-flap reconstruction with q1h flap checks (color, temperature, capillary refill, Doppler signal) x24h then spaced. Telemetry/ICU reserved for significant cardiopulmonary comorbidity or hemodynamic instability. Escalation triggers: flap venous congestion or arterial compromise (emergent OR); expanding hematoma with skin-flap/airway threat; postoperative hemorrhage with Hgb drop or hypotension; sepsis from SSI (qSOFA >=2 / SIRS); refractory pain or PONV
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: avoid prolonged fasting - clear liquids permitted up to 2h preop with complex-carbohydrate loading drink; resume regular diet POD0. Mobility: ambulate day of surgery; early shoulder/arm ROM to prevent frozen shoulder, with ipsilateral-arm lymphedema precautions. VTE: Caprini-directed - mechanical SCDs plus early ambulation for all; add pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily or UFH 5000 U) for Caprini >=5; consider extended 7-28 day post-discharge chemoprophylaxis for highest-risk cancer/major-reconstruction patients. Glucose target <180 mg/dL perioperatively to reduce SSI. Lines/catheters: remove Foley POD0-1, remove IV once tolerating orals, remove surgical drains when output <30 mL/24h
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable and afebrile; pain controlled on oral multimodal analgesics; tolerating oral intake; spontaneously voiding; ambulating at baseline; no active bleeding or expanding hematoma; flap viable and stable (reconstruction); patient/caregiver independently demonstrates drain care (strip, measure, record, recognize infection); antiemesis adequate; adequate home support confirmed; patient verbalizes warning signs and follow-up plan
discharge_planning: Disposition home for the vast majority, with home health for drain/wound care if a support gap exists. Medication reconciliation with an opioid-sparing script - standardized minimal opioid quantity (roughly <=10-20 tablets, often none) alongside scheduled acetaminophen + NSAID and a bowel regimen; resume home medications; document any extended VTE prophylaxis plan. Follow-up: surgeon at 1-2 weeks for wound, drains, and pathology/margin review; dedicated drain-removal visit; medical and radiation oncology referral for adjuvant therapy; reconstruction follow-up. Education: drain log, arm exercises, lymphedema precautions, SSI warning signs, activity restrictions. Transitions: written teach-back instructions, 24-48h nurse follow-up call, and a clear escalation pathway
expected_los: Very large outpatient/same-day share (many mastectomies never reach an inpatient DRG). Among admitted cases the CMS IPPS geometric-mean LOS (approximate) is: DRG 583 mastectomy for malignancy without CC/MCC ~1.4 days; DRG 582 with CC/MCC ~2.7 days; DRG 585 other breast procedures without CC/MCC ~2.1 days; DRG 584 with CC/MCC ~4.5 days. Simple mastectomy +/- implant trends to 0-1 day; autologous free-flap reconstruction ~2-4 days (exact FY GMLOS values behind CMS paywall, so figures are benchmark approximations)
target_los: Simple/total mastectomy +/- sentinel node biopsy +/- implant/expander: same-day (0 day) or <=23h observation; mastectomy with immediate implant-based reconstruction: 0-1 day; bilateral or autologous free-flap reconstruction: 1-2 days (reduced from 3-4). Overall optimized target <=1 day for the malignancy DRGs under an ideal ERAS pathway
quality_metrics: SSI rate (NHSN/NSQIP breast BRST module); same-day discharge rate; ERAS protocol compliance; opioid-sparing/opioid-free discharge rate and total MME prescribed (new-persistent-opioid-use surrogate); 30-day readmission, reoperation (hematoma/SSI), and unplanned return to OR; VTE rate; appropriate antibiotic prophylaxis selection/timing and discontinuation <=24h (SCIP-Inf equivalents); perioperative normothermia; margin re-excision rate (SSO-ASTRO benchmark); SLNB-appropriateness/axillary-management concordance; patient-reported outcomes (BREAST-Q); PONV rate; NAPBC accreditation standards; general surgical safety (PSI-90, HAC)
common_complications: Seroma (most common) - closed-suction drains with staged removal and outpatient aspiration; hematoma (~2-3%) - meticulous hemostasis, appropriate anticoagulation management, drain surveillance with OR evacuation if expanding; SSI/cellulitis (higher with implants) - cefazolin prophylaxis, normothermia, glucose control, no prolonged postop antibiotics; mastectomy skin-flap necrosis (esp. nipple-sparing) - ICG perfusion assessment, tension-free closure; autologous flap failure (~1-5%) - q1h monitoring with early return to OR; PONV/acute pain/opioid effects - ERAS multimodal analgesia; lymphedema, axillary cording, shoulder dysfunction - SLNB over ALND per Z0011 plus early PT; VTE - Caprini-directed prophylaxis
readmission_drivers: Surgical site infection/wound infection - leading readmission driver, especially with implant reconstruction (mitigate with prophylaxis, glucose control, early 1-week wound check, home health); hematoma/bleeding (hemostasis, anticoagulation management); seroma requiring drainage (drain teaching, outpatient aspiration clinic); flap complications/necrosis (monitoring, early salvage); uncontrolled pain/PONV/dehydration (ERAS multimodal, antiemetics, 24-48h nurse call); inadequate home drain management (structured teach-back, home health, clear instructions). Core prevention levers: structured drain education, 24-48h follow-up call, early wound check, opioid stewardship, and pre-discharge home-support assessment
guideline_source: ERAS Society - Consensus Review of Optimal Perioperative Care in Breast Reconstruction (2017); American Society of Breast Surgeons (ASBrS) - Pain Management in Breast Surgery multidisciplinary expert panel (2020) and Opioid/Pain Control Workgroup (2020); PROSPECT (ESRA) oncological breast surgery pain guideline (2020); NCCN Breast Cancer Guidelines (current v.2025) for oncologic surgical management; SSO-ASTRO margins for invasive BCS ("no ink on tumor," 2014) and SSO-ASTRO-ASCO DCIS margins (2 mm, 2016); ASCO Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy guideline (2014, updated 2017/2025) with ACOSOG Z0011 for axillary management; ASHP/IDSA/SIS/SHEA Surgical Antimicrobial Prophylaxis guidelines (2013, cefazolin); ACCP/ASH VTE prophylaxis using the Caprini model

evidence_grade: Mixed, overall Moderate-to-Strong. Strong (Class I, level-1 RCT + society guideline) for oncologic surgical management: margins (SSO-ASTRO), axillary management/SLNB (ACOSOG Z0011, ASCO). Strong for ERAS perioperative care and multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (ERAS Society, ASBrS, PROSPECT - RCT-backed consensus). Moderate for VTE prophylaxis in breast surgery (Caprini model extrapolated from cohort/QI data, no dedicated breast RCT) and for same-day-discharge LOS targets (large NSQIP cohorts and QI series rather than RCTs)
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The DRG pairs split strictly on CC/MCC (582 vs 583 for mastectomy-for-malignancy; 584 vs 585 for other breast procedures). Without CC/MCC (583/585): predominantly same-day/23h, floor-level care, GMLOS ~1-2 days, ERAS discharge POD0-1. With CC/MCC (582/584): a comorbidity (diabetes, CKD, CHF, COPD) or an in-stay complication (SSI/sepsis, postoperative hemorrhage/hematoma needing transfusion, flap failure, VTE/PE, acute renal failure) lengthens LOS to ~2-4.5 days with higher monitoring, more consults, and higher readmission risk; DRG 584 (other breast procedures with CC/MCC) captures debridement for necrotizing/deep SSI and reconstruction salvage and carries the longest stays. MCC drivers include sepsis, PE, MI, acute respiratory failure, and acute blood-loss anemia; pathway response is to escalate monitoring, achieve source control, and defer discharge until the complication resolves
pathway_pearls: 1) Default to same-day/23h discharge with structured drain teaching plus a 24-48h nurse follow-up call - NSQIP-safe and the single biggest LOS/cost lever. 2) ERAS opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia anchored by a regional block (PECS/paravertebral/serratus) with a standardized minimal opioid script - prevents new persistent opioid use (up to ~10% at 1 year) and pain/PONV-driven readmissions. 3) Caprini-directed VTE prophylaxis - most mastectomy +/- reconstruction patients score 5-7 (high), warranting pharmacologic +/- extended prophylaxis. 4) Antibiotic stewardship (cefazolin within 60 min, STOP at 24h even with drains/implants) combined with normothermia and glucose <180 mg/dL to cut SSI, the leading readmission driver; and choose SLNB over ALND (Z0011) to reduce lymphedema
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Mastectomy for malignancy (582-583) and other breast procedures (584-585); large outpatient share.

admission_criteria: Inpatient admission for IWGDF/IDSA severe infection (grade 4: systemic/SIRS, sepsis) or moderate infection (grade 3) with key morbidities—critical limb ischemia/severe PAD, immunosuppression, rapid progression, or inability to self-care/adhere; also need for IV antibiotics, urgent surgical debridement/drainage, substantial deep soft-tissue involvement, uncontrolled hyperglycemia/DKA/HHS, AKI, or documented bacteremia. Sepsis screen: SIRS >=2, qSOFA >=2, or NEWS2 elevation. Vertebral osteomyelitis: admit for new/progressive neuro deficit, suspected spinal epidural abscess, sepsis, uncontrolled pain, or need for image-guided biopsy. ED discharge/observation appropriate for mild (grade 2) and select moderate infections without systemic signs, reliable oral intake and follow-up, and no critical ischemia.
risk_stratification: IWGDF/IDSA infection grade (=PEDIS infection): grade 2 mild (erythema 0.5-2 cm, skin/subcutaneous only), grade 3 moderate (erythema >2 cm or deep structures—abscess/osteomyelitis/septic arthritis/fasciitis), grade 4 severe (SIRS/systemic); append '(O)' when bone involved—drives antibiotic breadth and admission. SVS WIfI (Wound-Ischemia-foot Infection) stage 1-4 stratifies 1-year amputation risk and revascularization benefit. qSOFA >=2 / SIRS >=2 / NEWS2 flags sepsis → cultures + antibiotics <1h and serum lactate. SINBAD score for ulcer severity/benchmarking. eGFR/Cockcroft-Gault for renal dosing of vancomycin and beta-lactams.
initial_workup_labs: CBC w/diff, CRP, ESR, procalcitonin, CMP (renal/hepatic for dosing), glucose + HbA1c, lactate if sepsis, coagulation panel, type-and-screen if surgery likely; blood cultures x2 BEFORE antibiotics (essential in vertebral osteomyelitis and severe DFI/suspected bacteremia). Osteomyelitis-supportive thresholds: ESR >=70 mm/h (sens 0.81, spec 0.80), CRP >=80 mg/L with ESR >60 mm/h, or procalcitonin >0.33 ng/mL. Obtain DEEP tissue/bone specimen (percutaneous or intraoperative bone biopsy = microbiologic gold standard; avoid superficial swabs) for culture + histopathology, ideally before antibiotics; if S. aureus bacteremia, repeat cultures to document clearance.
initial_imaging_dx: Plain radiographs first-line in all suspected osteomyelitis (cortical erosion/periosteal reaction; low early sensitivity—repeat in 2-4 wk). Bedside probe-to-bone test (sens 0.87, spec 0.83). MRI with/without gadolinium is imaging of choice (sens ~90%, spec ~80%)—within 24-48h for suspected vertebral osteomyelitis/spinal epidural abscess or deep foot infection. If MRI contraindicated: CT, tagged-WBC scan, or FDG-PET/CT. Vertebral osteomyelitis: whole-spine MRI if neuro signs; CT- or fluoroscopy-guided needle biopsy for microbiology when blood cultures are negative. ABI/toe pressures + arterial duplex/angiography if PAD suspected. TTE/TEE if S. aureus bacteremia to exclude endocarditis.
time_critical_interventions: If septic shock: Surviving Sepsis/CMS SEP-1 bundle—blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1h, lactate, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for MAP<65 or lactate >=4 mmol/L. Necrotizing infection, gas gangrene, compartment syndrome, or deep abscess → EMERGENT surgical debridement/source control within hours. Moderate-severe DFI: early surgery within 24-48h combined with antibiotics (IWGDF Rec 19). Spinal epidural abscess with neurologic deficit → emergent decompression (goal <24h from deficit onset) + neurosurgery. Critical limb ischemia → urgent vascular consult/revascularization. When hemodynamically stable (especially vertebral osteomyelitis), secure bone/blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics to preserve microbiologic yield.
initial_management: After cultures, empiric IV antibiotics: MRSA coverage—vancomycin (AUC/MIC target 400-600) or daptomycin 6-10 mg/kg or linezolid 600 mg BID—PLUS anti-pseudomonal/gram-negative beta-lactam (piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime, or a carbapenem) for severe/chronic/PAD/prior-antibiotic cases, with anaerobic coverage (metronidazole, or inherent in piperacillin-tazobactam) for ischemic/necrotic/foul wounds. Mild-moderate without MRSA risk: narrower amoxicillin-clavulanate, cephalexin, or cefazolin. De-escalate to culture-directed therapy at 48-72h. Source control: debride necrotic bone/tissue, drain abscess, minor/major amputation or bone resection as needed. Insulin for glycemic control (target 140-180 mg/dL), foot-wound offloading, wound care. IV-to-oral: OVIVA showed oral non-inferior to IV over the first 6 weeks when bioavailable susceptible agents exist (fluoroquinolone +/- rifampin, linezolid, clindamycin, TMP-SMX).
day1_milestones: Blood cultures x2 + deep tissue/bone cultures obtained; empiric IV antibiotics started (within 1h if septic); plain films ordered and MRI arranged; ID and surgical (ortho/podiatry/vascular/neurosurgery) consults placed; sepsis bundle completed if applicable; glucose stabilized with insulin; vascular assessment (ABI/toe pressures) if PAD; offloading initiated; pain controlled; probe-to-bone result documented.
day2_milestones: Review MRI and biopsy results; perform surgical debridement/source control if indicated (within 24-48h); adjust/narrow antibiotics per Gram stain and preliminary cultures; obtain ID recommendations on definitive regimen; continue glycemic optimization; PT/OT and wound-care evaluation; begin planning total antibiotic duration and disposition.
day3plus_milestones: Culture-directed definitive antibiotics; plan IV-to-oral transition (OVIVA) once stable with a susceptible oral option; set total duration—~3 wk if all infected bone resected with clean margins, ~6 wk for residual infected bone or non-surgical DFO (Gariani), 6 wk for native vertebral osteomyelitis (IDSA 2015); confirm adequate source control, afebrile status, and downtrending CRP/ESR; arrange OPAT/PICC if needed; finalize disposition and follow-up.
consults_multidisciplinary: Infectious Diseases (regimen/duration/OPAT) early; orthopedic/podiatric surgery (debridement/amputation), vascular surgery (PAD/CLI/revascularization), neurosurgery/spine (vertebral/epidural abscess); Endocrinology/diabetes for glycemic control; wound care/plastics; PT/OT for mobility and offloading; Pharmacy (vancomycin AUC dosing, OPAT); Nutrition (wound-healing protein/albumin optimization); Care management/social work (OPAT logistics, SNF/rehab placement); podiatry for foot preservation.
monitoring_level: Medical/surgical floor for most; telemetry or step-down for sepsis, borderline hemodynamics, or significant cardiopulmonary comorbidity; ICU for septic shock, necrotizing infection requiring aggressive resuscitation, post major-amputation instability, or spinal cord compromise. Escalation triggers: qSOFA >=2, hypotension/MAP<65, rising lactate, new/progressive neurologic deficit, respiratory failure, or vancomycin-associated AKI.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: consistent-carbohydrate/diabetic; optimize nutrition for wound healing (protein, correct hypoalbuminemia and micronutrient/glycemic status). VTE prophylaxis with LMWH or unfractionated heparin (especially post-op/reduced mobility) unless contraindicated. Early mobilization/PT with strict offloading (total-contact cast or removable boot) of foot wounds. Inpatient glucose target 140-180 mg/dL. Remove unnecessary vascular lines and urinary catheters promptly; PICC-line care/education if OPAT; smoking-cessation counseling.
discharge_criteria: Afebrile >=24-48h and hemodynamically stable; adequate source control achieved; tolerating the definitive antibiotic plan (oral regimen, or OPAT arranged with functioning access); downtrending CRP/ESR and WBC; glucose controlled; pain controlled on oral analgesia; wound stable with a defined care plan; safe mobility with offloading; follow-up secured.
discharge_planning: Disposition: home with oral antibiotics or OPAT vs SNF/rehab (common post-amputation or for complex OPAT). Medication reconciliation including full antibiotic course + explicit stop date, insulin, and analgesia. Schedule ID, surgery/podiatry, vascular, and wound-care follow-up; PICC teaching and weekly OPAT labs (CBC, CMP, CRP, vancomycin levels). Provide offloading device, diabetic foot self-exam education, glycemic and smoking-cessation counseling; arrange home health; explicit transition-of-care handoff to the OPAT/ID team.
expected_los: GMLOS 4.3 days for DRG 540 (with CC) and 6.2 days for DRG 539 (with MCC); arithmetic mean LOS 5.6 and 8.1 days respectively (CMS MS-DRG benchmarks).
target_los: Optimized target ~3-4 days without MCC (540) and ~5-6 days with MCC (539), achieved via early source control (surgery within 24-48h) and early IV-to-oral switch (OVIVA) with OPAT, so the 3-6 week antibiotic course completes outpatient rather than inpatient.
quality_metrics: CMS SEP-1 severe sepsis/septic shock bundle when applicable; blood cultures drawn before antibiotics; guideline-concordant empiric therapy with 48-72h de-escalation (antimicrobial stewardship); time-to-source-control; inpatient glycemic control (eCQM); 30-day all-cause readmission; major amputation/limb-salvage rate; OPAT/CLABSI and vancomycin-AKI rates; accurate CC/MCC and organism documentation.
common_complications: Bacteremia/sepsis (especially S. aureus), progression requiring amputation, treatment failure/recurrence, Clostridioides difficile and other adverse events from prolonged antibiotics, vancomycin-associated AKI, PICC-line CLABSI/DVT, hyperglycemic crises, and—for spinal disease—epidural abscess extension/cord compression. Mitigated by stewardship/de-escalation, adequate source control, offloading, structured OPAT monitoring, and glycemic control.
readmission_drivers: Persistent/recurrent osteomyelitis from inadequate debridement or premature antibiotic discontinuation, non-adherence to the prolonged course, OPAT/PICC-line complications, uncontrolled diabetes, PAD progression or new ulcer, and missed follow-up. Prevention: complete surgical debridement, ID-directed duration, strict offloading, glycemic optimization, weekly OPAT labs, early podiatry/wound follow-up, and diabetic-foot education.
guideline_source: IWGDF/IDSA 2023 Guidelines on the Diagnosis and Treatment of Diabetes-related Foot Infections; IDSA 2015 Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Treatment of Native Vertebral Osteomyelitis in Adults; reinforced by the OVIVA RCT (NEJM 2019) and the Gariani DFO duration RCT (CID 2021).
key_citations: Senneville E, et al. IWGDF/IDSA 2023, Diabetes Metab Res Rev 2023;40:e3687 (PMID 37779323; DOI 10.1002/dmrr.3687); parallel publication Clin Infect Dis 2023 (PMID 37779457; DOI 10.1093/cid/ciad527). Berbari EF, et al. IDSA 2015 Native Vertebral Osteomyelitis, Clin Infect Dis 2015;61:e26-46 (PMID 26229122; DOI 10.1093/cid/civ482). Li HK, et al. OVIVA—Oral vs Intravenous Antibiotics for Bone and Joint Infection, N Engl J Med 2019;380:425-436 (PMID 30699315; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1710926). Gariani K, et al. Three vs Six Weeks of Antibiotics for Diabetic Foot Osteomyelitis, Clin Infect Dis 2021;73:e1539-e1545 (PMID 33242083; DOI 10.1093/cid/ciaa1758). (Citations retrieved via PubMed.)
evidence_grade: Moderate overall—GRADE-based society guidelines (IWGDF/IDSA 2023, IDSA 2015) carry several conditional/low-certainty recommendations, but are reinforced by strong RCT evidence for antibiotic route (OVIVA: oral non-inferior to IV over first 6 weeks) and duration (Gariani: 3 wk non-inferior to 6 wk after surgical debridement), plus the 6-week duration standard for native vertebral osteomyelitis per IDSA 2015. Surgical timing/extent and much diabetic-foot osteomyelitis decision-making remain expert consensus, so net grade is Moderate.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed group splits by severity: DRG 539 Osteomyelitis with MCC (GMLOS 6.2 d, ALOS 8.1 d, RW ~1.97) vs DRG 540 with CC (GMLOS 4.3 d, ALOS 5.6 d, RW ~1.30); the base tier without CC/MCC sits lower still. MCC drivers—severe sepsis/septic shock, MRSA/S. aureus bacteremia, acute respiratory failure, AKI, gangrene, DKA—push toward step-down/ICU, longer LOS, and more debridement/amputation and OPAT. Accurate capture of these comorbidities and of the causative organism materially shifts DRG assignment, expected LOS, and reimbursement.
pathway_pearls: 1) Obtain a DEEP tissue/bone culture (not a superficial swab), ideally before antibiotics—targets therapy and avoids unnecessarily broad, prolonged coverage. 2) Early source control: debride/resect within 24-48h for moderate-severe infection and pair with prompt vascular assessment/revascularization in PAD to maximize limb salvage. 3) Switch IV-to-oral early (OVIVA)—non-inferior, avoids line complications, and lets the course finish outpatient, shortening LOS. 4) Duration discipline: ~3 weeks when all infected bone is resected with clean margins vs ~6 weeks for residual/non-surgical bone infection—don't default to prolonged IV.

scope_and_volume_notes: Frequently diabetic-foot related; long IV-antibiotic courses, often with amputation/debridement.

admission_criteria: Mostly scheduled/urgent surgical admits, not ED-discharge candidates. CAROTID: symptomatic stenosis 50-99% (NASCET method) with recent TIA/non-disabling stroke -> urgent inpatient CEA (or CAS/TCAR) during index admission, ideally within 14 days; asymptomatic stenosis >=70% -> elective same-day-admit surgery if life expectancy >3-5 yr. PERIPHERAL: chronic limb-threatening ischemia (CLTI = ischemic rest pain, non-healing ulcer, or gangrene; Rutherford 4-6) -> admit for revascularization; acute limb ischemia (Rutherford I-III) -> emergent admit + systemic heparin. Isolated intermittent claudication (Rutherford 1-3) is NOT an inpatient indication -> outpatient GDMT + supervised exercise first. Also admit for post-procedure neuro/hemodynamic monitoring or infected-wound source control.
risk_stratification: CAROTID: symptomatic threshold >=50% and asymptomatic >=70% stenosis by duplex/CTA drive operative indication; NIHSS + modified Rankin (mRS) grade symptomatic severity; acceptable perioperative stroke/death cap <6% symptomatic, <3% asymptomatic (higher = medical mgmt). PERIPHERAL/CLTI: WIfI (Wound-Ischemia-foot Infection) stage 1-4 -> 1-yr amputation risk + revascularization benefit; Rutherford/Fontaine class; GLASS (Global Limb Anatomic Staging System) grades infrainguinal anatomy/conduit strategy; ABI <0.90 diagnostic of PAD, ankle pressure <50 mmHg or toe pressure <30 mmHg or TcPO2 <30 = CLTI. PERIOP CARDIAC: RCRI or VSG-CRI (Vascular Study Group Cardiac Risk Index) estimates MACE and triggers cardiology/optimization.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, CMP (baseline creatinine/eGFR before iodinated contrast), coagulation panel (PT/INR, aPTT), type & screen or crossmatch, fasting lipid panel + HbA1c (guides statin intensity and glycemic target), high-sensitivity troponin/ECG if cardiac symptoms. CLTI/infected wound: wound and blood cultures, ESR/CRP, procalcitonin if systemic; acute limb ischemia: lactate and creatine kinase (rhabdo/reperfusion). Group & save mandatory for bypass. Abnormal renal function -> hydration/contrast-minimization protocol; anemia Hgb triggers restrictive transfusion planning.
initial_imaging_dx: CAROTID: carotid duplex ultrasound first-line; CTA neck (or MRA) to confirm stenosis grade + aortic-arch/anatomy for CEA-vs-stent decision; non-contrast CT or MRI brain in ALL symptomatic patients (exclude hemorrhage, size infarct) with door-to-brain-imaging <=25 min in acute stroke presentation; DSA only if noninvasive studies discordant. PERIPHERAL: ABI/TBI with segmental pressures + pulse-volume recordings, arterial duplex; CTA or MRA runoff for planning; catheter angiography is diagnostic and therapeutic. PRE-OP: ECG in all; TTE/functional stress testing selectively per RCRI, not routine.
time_critical_interventions: Symptomatic carotid stenosis: perform CEA within 14 days of index TIA/minor stroke (benefit is time-dependent, greatest in first 2 weeks) but avoid very early operation with large/hemorrhagic-transformation-risk infarct. ACUTE LIMB ISCHEMIA: immediate systemic heparin bolus + drip; revascularize a viable/threatened limb within ~6 h to prevent irreversible tissue loss and reperfusion injury. Acute stroke presentation: door-to-brain-imaging <=25 min; tPA/thrombectomy per stroke pathway (contraindicates immediate CEA). CLTI: expedited (days, not weeks) revascularization to heal wounds/preserve limb; urgent debridement + antibiotics if wet gangrene/spreading infection.
initial_management: ANTIPLATELET: aspirin 81-325 mg for all; add clopidogrel 75 mg (DAPT) periprocedurally for CAS/TCAR stenting. HIGH-INTENSITY STATIN: atorvastatin 40-80 mg or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg (>=50% LDL-C reduction, Class 1A). PAD antithrombotic: low-dose rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID + aspirin (VOYAGER-PAD/COMPASS) post-revascularization to cut MALE/MACE (Class 1A); therapeutic heparin for acute limb ischemia; intraoperative heparin to ACT target. BP control (avoid extremes periop for carotid). Restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 with active cardiac ischemia). Reversal: protamine for heparin; 4-factor PCC/vitamin K for warfarin if urgent. Source control: debridement + culture-directed antibiotics for infected CLTI. Smoking cessation, glucose <180 mg/dL.
day1_milestones: POD0. CEA/CAS: emerge/extubate; PACU then step-down neuro checks q15min tapering to q1h; STRICT blood-pressure control (individualized SBP ceiling to prevent cerebral hyperperfusion; treat hypotension from baroreceptor effect); continue aspirin + statin; vigilant neck exam for expanding hematoma; resume diet and ambulate same day. BYPASS: hemodynamic stabilization; document graft pulse/Doppler and pedal signals + serial foot exam; multimodal analgesia; monitor drains/wound for bleeding and calf compartments for reperfusion syndrome; euvolemia and renal protection after contrast.
day2_milestones: POD1. CEA/CAS/TCAR: majority discharge if neurologically intact/at baseline, BP controlled on oral agents, tolerating diet, ambulating independently; remove drain if placed. BYPASS: advance ambulation with PT; wound inspection; transition to oral analgesia; confirm sustained graft signal/pedal Doppler; remove urinary catheter; continue antiplatelet +/- rivaroxaban and VTE prophylaxis; begin wound-care and warning-sign teaching; glycemic and BP optimization.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ (bypass and complicated cases). Progressive mobilization to functional baseline; daily graft-signal and wound checks (watch for SSI, dehiscence, lymphocele); de-escalate IV to oral meds and antibiotics; optimize glucose/BP; complete diabetic foot-care and wound-care education; arrange home health/wound service; confirm discharge antiplatelet + high-intensity statin. Uncomplicated CEA is already home; MCC-tier patients (stroke, MI, sepsis, respiratory/renal failure) remain for organ-support and stabilization before discharge readiness.
consults_multidisciplinary: Vascular surgery (primary); neurology/stroke service for symptomatic carotid; cardiology/anesthesia for perioperative cardiac risk optimization; PT/OT early for mobility + functional/discharge assessment; wound care and podiatry for CLTI ulcers/diabetic foot; endocrinology or diabetes team for glycemic control; nutrition (wound healing, malnutrition); certified smoking-cessation counseling; pharmacy for medication reconciliation (antiplatelet, statin, anticoagulant interactions); care management + social work early for disposition, home health, and transportation to surveillance visits.
monitoring_level: CEA/CAS: PACU -> step-down/telemetry overnight for hourly neuro checks and tight BP management; ICU if new neuro deficit, hemodynamic lability, or cerebral-hyperperfusion concern. BYPASS: floor/telemetry is standard for infrainguinal; ICU/step-down for suprainguinal/aortic reconstruction, major MCC, hemodynamic instability, or ongoing resuscitation/transfusion. ESCALATION TRIGGERS: any new focal neuro deficit or seizure (hyperperfusion/stroke), expanding neck hematoma or stridor (airway emergency -> open at bedside), loss of graft Doppler signal/cold pulseless foot (graft thrombosis), hemodynamic instability, rising CK/compartment pressures.
nutrition_mobility_vte: DIET: resume regular diet POD0 for CEA; advance as tolerated after bypass (ERAS early feeding). MOBILITY: out of bed/ambulate POD0-1, progressive with PT (early mobility reduces VTE, deconditioning, pulmonary complications). VTE PROPHYLAXIS: mechanical (SCDs) universally + pharmacologic UFH/LMWH once surgical bleeding/graft risk acceptable; balance against neck-hematoma and graft-bleeding risk. GLUCOSE: target <180 mg/dL, avoid hypoglycemia. LINES/CATHETERS: remove urinary catheter POD1, arterial/central lines when hemodynamically stable to lower CAUTI/CLABSI.
discharge_criteria: CEA/CAS: neurologically at baseline, BP controlled on oral regimen, no expanding neck hematoma, tolerating diet, ambulating. BYPASS: palpable pulse or clearly dopplerable graft/pedal signal, perfused foot, wound intact without active bleeding or uncontrolled infection, pain controlled on oral analgesia, ambulating at functional baseline, tolerating diet, voiding. ALL: discharged on antiplatelet + high-intensity statin, glucose/BP acceptable, follow-up and any home wound care arranged.
discharge_planning: DISPOSITION: home for most; home + home-health wound care for CLTI/open wounds; SNF/rehab for functional decline or major amputation. MED REC: aspirin (+/- clopidogrel if stented), high-intensity statin, +/- rivaroxaban 2.5 mg BID, antihypertensives, glycemic agents; reconcile anticoagulant interactions. FOLLOW-UP: vascular surgery 2-4 weeks with duplex surveillance (carotid restenosis; graft patency at 1/3/6/12 mo); wound clinic. EDUCATION: stroke/TIA warning signs, graft-occlusion signs (cold/painful/pale foot), wound care, foot protection, and smoking cessation. Structured transition-of-care handoff to PCP.
expected_los: Uncomplicated CEA or CAS/TCAR: 1-2 days (often POD1 discharge); infrainguinal bypass: 3-5 days. Approx CMS geometric-mean LOS by DRG (benchmarks, vary by fiscal year): extracranial 039 (no CC/MCC) ~1.4 d, 038 (CC) ~2.3 d, 037 (MCC) ~5-6 d; other-vascular 254 (no CC/MCC) ~2.1 d, 253 (CC) ~3.3-3.6 d, 252 (MCC) ~5.5-6 d.
target_los: CEA and CAS/TCAR: overnight monitoring, discharge POD1 = 1 day (asymptomatic carotid feasible as 23-hour stay). Elective infrainguinal bypass under a vascular-ERAS pathway: 2-4 days. CLTI with wound/infection or MCC-tier physiology will legitimately run longer.
quality_metrics: SVS Vascular Quality Initiative (VQI) registry CEA and infrainguinal-bypass modules; perioperative stroke/death rate (target <3% asymptomatic, <6% symptomatic CEA); cranial-nerve-injury and restenosis rates; graft primary/secondary patency, limb salvage, amputation-free survival; CMS 30-day risk-standardized readmission; surgical-site infection (SSI/NHSN); PSI-90 including PSI-12 postop VTE; discharge on antiplatelet AND high-intensity statin; documented smoking-cessation counseling; vascular-ERAS compliance.
common_complications: CEA: perioperative stroke/TIA, cerebral hyperperfusion syndrome (headache, seizure, intracerebral hemorrhage), neck hematoma (airway emergency), cranial-nerve injury (hypoglossal, vagus/recurrent laryngeal, marginal mandibular), MI, BP lability, restenosis. BYPASS: graft thrombosis/occlusion, hemorrhage, SSI/wound dehiscence/lymphocele, reperfusion and compartment syndrome, MI, contrast-associated AKI, distal embolization/limb loss. MITIGATION: obsessive BP control + hourly neuro checks, meticulous hemostasis, DAPT for stents, serial graft-signal and wound surveillance, statin/antiplatelet continuation, early mobilization, glycemic control.
readmission_drivers: Leading (bypass): surgical-site infection and wound complications, graft occlusion/thrombosis, bleeding/hematoma, and progression to amputation. Carotid: recurrent neuro event, hypertensive crisis/hyperperfusion, neck hematoma. Cross-cutting: antiplatelet/statin discontinuation, uncontrolled diabetes and poor foot care, missed surveillance visits, inadequate home wound care. PREVENTION LEVERS: strict glycemic control + wound-care teaching and home-health, medication-adherence counseling, early (2-4 wk) follow-up with duplex surveillance, and structured transitions of care.
guideline_source: SVS 2022 Clinical Practice Guidelines for Management of Extracranial Cerebrovascular Disease (Society for Vascular Surgery, 2021/2022); 2024 ACC/AHA/Multisociety Guideline for the Management of Lower Extremity Peripheral Artery Disease (2024); SVS/ESVS/WFVS Global Vascular Guidelines on the Management of Chronic Limb-Threatening Ischemia (2019); ESVS 2023 Clinical Practice Guidelines on Atherosclerotic Carotid and Vertebral Artery Disease (secondary/European corroboration).
key_citations: AbuRahma AF et al. SVS clinical practice guidelines for management of extracranial cerebrovascular disease. J Vasc Surg 2022;75(1S):4S-22S (PMID 34153348; implementation document PMID 34153349, DOI 10.1016/j.jvs.2021.04.074). Gornik HL et al. 2024 ACC/AHA Lower Extremity PAD Guideline. Circulation 2024 (PMID 38743805, DOI 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001251). NASCET: Beneficial effect of carotid endarterectomy in symptomatic patients with high-grade stenosis. N Engl J Med 1991 (PMID 1852179). CREST: Brott TG et al. Stenting versus endarterectomy for carotid stenosis. N Engl J Med 2010;363:11-23 (PMID 20505173). BEST-CLI: Farber A et al. Surgery or endovascular therapy for CLTI. N Engl J Med 2022 (PMID 36342173). BASIL-2: Bradbury AW et al. Lancet 2023 (PMID 37116524). Source: PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong - multiple Class I / Level A society-guideline recommendations underpinned by landmark RCTs: SVS 2022 gives Grade 1 support for CEA in symptomatic 50-99% stenosis (NASCET/ECST) and CEA over transfemoral stenting in standard-risk symptomatic patients (CREST); ACC/AHA 2024 gives Class 1A for single-antiplatelet, dual-pathway rivaroxaban+aspirin, and high-intensity statin. Choice of open bypass vs endovascular in CLTI is Moderate/context-dependent given divergent trials (BEST-CLI favored vein bypass with adequate single-segment great saphenous vein; BASIL-2 favored best-endovascular-first for infrapopliteal disease) - individualize by conduit and anatomy.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed group spans wide acuity. WITHOUT CC/MCC (039/254): elective uncomplicated CEA or infrainguinal bypass, step-down/floor, LOS ~1-3 d. WITH CC (038/253): significant comorbidity or minor complication (e.g., controlled infection, minor cardiac event), LOS ~2-4 d. WITH MCC (037/252): perioperative stroke, MI, respiratory failure, sepsis/wet gangrene, acute kidney injury, or major bleeding -> ICU, LOS 5-7+ d, higher mortality and cost. Drivers of MCC tier: completed stroke in symptomatic carotid disease, and CLTI complicated by systemic infection/sepsis or major amputation.
pathway_pearls: 1) Symptomatic carotid = a clock: operate within 14 days of the index TIA/minor stroke - do not defer the CEA, benefit is front-loaded. 2) After CEA obsess over blood pressure + hourly neuro checks; an expanding neck hematoma with stridor is an airway emergency - open the wound at the bedside, do not wait for the OR. 3) For CLTI, stage with WIfI and specifically assess for a good single-segment great saphenous vein: BEST-CLI shows vein bypass is superior when a suitable conduit exists, while BASIL-2 supports endovascular-first for infrapopliteal disease or poor surgical candidates - match strategy to conduit and anatomy. 4) Never discharge without antiplatelet + high-intensity statin (plus smoking cessation) - the single biggest lever on limb and cardiovascular events.

admission_criteria: All ROSC after cardiac arrest -> mandatory ICU admission (no ED-discharge/observation pathway exists for true arrest or shock); Cardiogenic shock = SBP <90 mmHg >30 min OR need for vasopressors/inotropes/MCS to keep SBP >=90, PLUS end-organ hypoperfusion (lactate >2 mmol/L, cool/mottled extremities, altered mentation, urine <30 mL/h) and, when measured, CI <2.2 L/min/m2 with PCWP >15 mmHg -> ICU; SCAI SHOCK stage C or higher -> ICU; refractory VT/VF, comatose post-ROSC, need for mechanical ventilation or vasoactive support -> ICU; only non-admit scenario is a resuscitated vasovagal/reflex syncope later proven not to be arrest with no organ dysfunction
risk_stratification: SCAI SHOCK stages A-E drive escalation: A at-risk (~3% mortality), B beginning/hypotension without hypoperfusion (~7%), C classic hypoperfusion needing intervention + lactate >=2 (~12-18%), D deteriorating/escalating support (~40%), E extremis/refractory often on CPR-ECMO (~55-67%); cardiac-arrest modifier raises mortality at every stage; serial lactate >2 mmol/L with clearance target >=10%/h or normalization by 24h; CardShock and IABP-SHOCK II scores (age, prior CVA, glucose, creatinine, lactate, TIMI flow) triage 30-day mortality low ~20-30% (0-2) vs high ~70-80% (5-9); Cardiac Power Output (CPO) <0.6 W and PAPi <0.9 predict death/RV failure and trigger MCS/shock-team discussion; post-arrest use multimodal CAHP/OHCA scores but defer neuroprognosis to >=72h
initial_workup_labs: Serial lactate (perfusion + clearance); ABG/VBG; high-sensitivity troponin serial (+ECG -> activate cath lab); CBC; CMP for AKI and shock-liver transaminitis; NT-proBNP/BNP; coagulation PT/INR-aPTT-fibrinogen with DIC screen; type and screen; central venous ScvO2 <60% or mixed SvO2 <60% flags low output; electrolytes K/Mg/Ca/phosphate and glucose (correct to prevent arrhythmia); TSH; toxicology and beta-hCG if arrest unexplained; blood cultures/procalcitonin only if sepsis on differential; intra-arrest ETCO2 <10 mmHg predicts poor ROSC; rising lactate/creatinine or ScvO2 fall -> escalate support/MCS
initial_imaging_dx: 12-lead ECG immediately (<10 min) to detect STEMI/OMI -> emergent cath-lab activation; emergent coronary angiography for STEMI/STEMI-equivalent post-ROSC (for stable comatose non-STEMI without ongoing ischemia, routine immediate angiography has no benefit per COACT/TOMAHAWK -> defer); bedside POCUS/TTE within minutes-hours for LV/RV function, tamponade, valve/mechanical complications, volume status; formal TTE within 24h; portable CXR; CT head for comatose post-arrest without clear cardiac cause (exclude ICH/SAH) and CT pulmonary angiography if PE suspected; pulmonary artery (Swan-Ganz) catheter for phenotyping refractory or ambiguous shock (CI, PCWP, CPO, PAPi)
time_critical_interventions: High-quality CPR + defibrillation for VF/pVT within 2 min; epinephrine 1 mg IV q3-5min for non-shockable rhythms; for AMI-cardiogenic shock emergent revascularization is the ONLY intervention proven to cut mortality (SHOCK trial) -> door-to-balloon <=90 min; culprit-lesion-only PCI in multivessel disease (CULPRIT-SHOCK), NOT routine immediate multivessel; MAP target >=65 mmHg with norepinephrine first-line; serial lactate clearance; post-ROSC bundle within first hour: 12-lead <10 min, initiate temperature control, lung-protective ventilation, treat Hs and Ts; consider extracorporeal CPR (eCPR) for refractory shockable arrest at capable centers (2023 ACLS update - reasonable); antibiotics <=1h only if concurrent septic picture
initial_management: Vasopressor: norepinephrine first-line (SOAP II - fewer arrhythmias and lower mortality vs dopamine in cardiogenic shock), add vasopressin/epinephrine if refractory, avoid dopamine; Inotrope: dobutamine first-line, milrinone alternative if on beta-blocker or pulmonary HTN (DOREMI - equivalent); emergent PCI of culprit vessel, CABG if unsuitable anatomy/mechanical complication; Mechanical circulatory support matched to phenotype - microaxial flow pump (Impella CP) has RCT support in STEMI-CS (DanGer Shock: 180-day mortality 45.8% vs 58.5%, NNT ~8, at cost of bleeding/limb ischemia/RRT), VA-ECMO for refractory biventricular failure (but routine ECMO no benefit per ECLS-SHOCK), routine IABP NOT recommended (IABP-SHOCK II); fluids cautious/small boluses only if hypovolemic phenotype - most are congested and need decongestion; transfusion restrictive Hgb <7-8 g/dL (~8 if active ischemia); post-arrest temperature control 32-37.5C with sedation; reverse anticoagulation/bleeding source if causal (PCC/vitamin K, idarucizumab, andexanet)
day1_milestones: Stabilize post-ROSC; secure airway + lung-protective ventilation, arterial line and central venous access; complete emergent revascularization if AMI-CS; titrate vasoactives to MAP >=65 and rising lactate clearance; initiate controlled temperature 32-37.5C and hold constant; identify and treat reversible causes (Hs/Ts); ACTIVATE MULTIDISCIPLINARY SHOCK TEAM; hemodynamic phenotyping by echo +/- PAC and assign SCAI stage; continuous EEG if comatose; early family update and goals-of-care given very high mortality
day2_milestones: Reassess perfusion and drive lactate to normal; wean vasopressors/inotropes as tolerated; assess MCS weaning readiness (native pulsatility, improving CPO/CI); complete temperature-control period then transition to strict normothermia with active fever prevention through 72h; daily sedation interruption/SAT-SBT if not MCS-dependent; trend renal function and urine output, start CRRT for refractory AKI or diuretic-resistant overload; begin GATHERING neuroprognostic data (no decisions yet); initiate early enteral nutrition; begin decongestion/diuresis in congested phenotype
day3plus_milestones: Multimodal neuroprognostication >=72h after ROSC and off sedation for comatose survivors (pupillary/corneal reflexes, SSEP N20, NSE trend, EEG, MRI/CT - no single test in isolation); wean and explant MCS, decannulate; extubate when hemodynamically stable and neuro status permits; convert IV support to oral guideline-directed medical therapy (ARNI/ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker once euvolemic and off inotropes, MRA, SGLT2i, statin); ICD vs wearable defibrillator planning for EF <=35% (defer implant to 40 days post-MI / 90 days post-revascularization, bridge with LifeVest); advanced-therapy evaluation (durable LVAD/transplant) if myocardium does not recover; cardiac rehab referral; step-down once off all vasoactives and MCS 24-48h
consults_multidisciplinary: Interventional cardiology/cath lab (emergent, first hour); MULTIDISCIPLINARY SHOCK TEAM activation early (highest-yield systems lever); cardiac surgery (MCS cannulation, CABG, mechanical complications); advanced heart failure/transplant (durable support decisions); intensivist/critical care co-management; electrophysiology (arrhythmic arrest, ICD/WCD); neurology (post-arrest prognostication, status epilepticus); palliative care early given mortality; pharmacy (vasoactive/anticoagulation/GDMT); nutrition; PT/OT for early ICU rehab; case management and social work; perfusion team for ECMO
monitoring_level: ICU for essentially all - arterial line, central access, continuous telemetry, frequently mechanical ventilation, PAC for refractory/ambiguous shock; escalation triggers: rising lactate, CPO falling <0.6 W, escalating vasoactive-inotropic score, SCAI progression C->D->E -> MCS/ECMO and transfer to a tertiary shock hub (hub-and-spoke) if level-1 support unavailable; de-escalate to step-down/telemetry only after off all vasoactives/inotropes and MCS explanted, stable rhythm, and adequate end-organ perfusion sustained 24-48h
nutrition_mobility_vte: Early enteral nutrition within 24-48h once resuscitated (trophic feeds, avoid overfeeding; hold or use caution on high/escalating pressors or if bowel-ischemia concern); progressive early mobilization/ICU rehab once hemodynamically stable and off high support; VTE prophylaxis - mechanical SCDs immediately, add pharmacologic LMWH/UFH once bleeding risk acceptable (balance MCS access-site and post-arrest bleeding); glucose target 144-180 mg/dL with insulin infusion, avoid hypoglycemia; line/catheter stewardship - remove arterial/central lines and Foley as soon as unnecessary, with serial MCS access-limb perfusion checks
discharge_criteria: Off IV vasopressors/inotropes and MCS explanted >=24-48h; hemodynamically stable with adequate end-organ perfusion (normalized lactate, stable or improving renal function); euvolemic on oral diuretics; stable rhythm with arrhythmia addressed and ICD/WCD plan in place; tolerating oral guideline-directed medical therapy; neurologically recovered or stable disposition established for comatose survivors; underlying etiology treated or a definitive plan documented
discharge_planning: Disposition by neuro/functional status (home +/- services, SNF/LTAC, acute inpatient rehab, or hospice for devastating anoxic injury); medication reconciliation with full GDMT (ARNI/ACEi/ARB, beta-blocker, MRA, SGLT2i, statin, DAPT if stented) and anticoagulation as indicated; wearable cardioverter-defibrillator (LifeVest) at discharge if EF <=35% and not yet ICD-eligible; early follow-up cardiology/HF clinic within 7-14 days, EP for device, PCP; cardiac rehab enrollment; transitions-of-care phone call within 48-72h; patient/family education (HF self-care, daily weights, symptom triggers, family CPR/AED training); advance-care-planning documented
expected_los: Bimodal and skewed by high early mortality: per CMS, MS-DRG 296 (w MCC) GMLOS ~4-5 days, 297 (w CC) ~3-4 days, 298 (w/o CC/MCC) ~2-3 days; clinically, shock/arrest survivors typically run 7-14 days, with AMI-cardiogenic shock bridged on microaxial MCS often 10-14 days
target_los: ~7-10 days for shock/arrest survivors under an optimized shock-team pathway (early culprit revascularization, protocolized MCS weaning, timely decongestion and GDMT conversion); LOS compression is NOT the primary goal here given complexity - safe de-escalation dominates; devastating anoxic-injury cases transition to comfort care sooner
quality_metrics: GWTG-Resuscitation (in-hospital arrest) and CARES / Cardiac Arrest Registry to Enhance Survival (OHCA); survival to discharge with favorable neurologic outcome (CPC 1-2 / mRS 0-3); temperature-control protocol compliance and time-to-target-temperature; for AMI-CS door-to-balloon <=90 min and NCDR CathPCI/Chest Pain-MI Registry; shock-team activation rate and time-to-MCS; restrictive-transfusion adherence; AHRQ PSIs; 30-day risk-standardized mortality and readmission (AMI/HF measures); documentation of reversible-cause workup
common_complications: Hypoxic-ischemic (anoxic) brain injury - the leading cause of post-arrest death/disability, mitigated by controlled temperature, MAP >=65, and avoidance of hypoxia/hyperoxia/hypocapnia; recurrent/refractory ventricular arrhythmias (antiarrhythmics, EP, ICD); multi-organ failure - shock liver, AKI requiring CRRT; MCS complications (access-site bleeding, limb ischemia, hemolysis, stroke) countered by distal perfusion cannula and limb checks; ventilator-associated pneumonia and aspiration; DIC; bowel ischemia; CPR-related rib fractures; post-arrest myocardial stunning
readmission_drivers: Heart-failure decompensation/volume overload (GDMT titration, diuretic self-management education, HF clinic follow-up <=7-14d, remote monitoring); recurrent ischemia or incomplete revascularization (staged-PCI plan, DAPT adherence); arrhythmia recurrence (ICD/WCD bridge, EP follow-up); MCS access-site/device complications; medication non-adherence and poor care transitions (medication reconciliation, TOC call, cardiac rehab enrollment)
guideline_source: AHA 2025 Guidelines for CPR and Emergency Cardiovascular Care - Part 9 Adult Advanced Life Support and Part 11 Post-Cardiac Arrest Care; 2025 ACC Concise Clinical Guidance / Expert Consensus on the Evaluation and Management of Cardiogenic Shock (JACC 2025); 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Patients With Acute Coronary Syndromes; SCAI SHOCK Stage Classification Expert Consensus Update 2022; 2023 AHA Focused Update on Adult Advanced Cardiovascular Life Support (ESC 2023 ACS guideline supportive)
key_citations: 2025 AHA Part 11 Post-Cardiac Arrest Care, Circulation 2025 (PMID 41122894); 2023 AHA Focused Update Adult ACLS, Circulation 2023 (PMID 38108133); SCAI SHOCK Stage Classification Update, Naidu et al, JACC 2022 (DOI 10.1016/j.jacc.2022.01.018); 2025 ACC/AHA/ACEP/NAEMSP/SCAI ACS Guideline, Circulation 2025 (DOI 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001309); DanGer Shock (microaxial pump in STEMI-CS), Moller et al, NEJM 2024 (PMID 38587239); ECLS-SHOCK (routine ECMO no benefit), Thiele et al, NEJM 2023 (PMID 37634145); CULPRIT-SHOCK (culprit-only PCI), Thiele et al, NEJM 2017 (PMID 29083953); IABP-SHOCK II (IABP no benefit), Thiele et al, NEJM 2012 (PMID 22920912); TTM2 (hypothermia vs normothermia), Dankiewicz et al, NEJM 2021 (PMID 34133859); SOAP II (norepinephrine vs dopamine), De Backer et al, NEJM 2010 (PMID 20200382); SHOCK trial early revascularization, Hochman et al, NEJM 1999 (PMID not independently reverified here) - PubMed cited per MCP
evidence_grade: Moderate overall with pockets of strong evidence: STRONG / Class I for emergent culprit revascularization in AMI-cardiogenic shock, high-quality CPR + early defibrillation, and post-arrest controlled temperature/fever prevention; MODERATE for norepinephrine first-line vasopressor and phenotype-matched MCS (only DanGer Shock is a positive MCS RCT, in selected STEMI-CS; routine IABP and routine ECMO are negative); much of cardiogenic-shock pharmacotherapy and MCS timing remains expert-consensus/extrapolated given very high mortality and sparse RCTs - graded down accordingly
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MS-DRG 296 (w MCC) / 297 (w CC) / 298 (w/o CC/MCC); cardiogenic shock (R57.0) and cardiac arrest (I46.x) are themselves MCC-level, so most survivors land in 296 via shock plus ventilator >=96h, acute organ failures (AKI, hepatic, respiratory), or anoxic encephalopathy; higher tiers drive ICU care, MCS, CRRT, prolonged ventilation, and markedly higher LOS and mortality (SCAI D/E >50-60%); 298 essentially captures only brief resuscitated arrest without organ dysfunction and is short-stay; capture every organ-failure diagnosis plus SCAI stage, lactate, and vasoactive use for accurate severity and mortality-index reflection
pathway_pearls: 1) Emergent culprit-lesion-only revascularization is the single highest-yield mortality lever in AMI-cardiogenic shock - protect door-to-balloon <=90 min and do NOT do routine multivessel PCI (CULPRIT-SHOCK); 2) Activate a multidisciplinary Shock Team early and stratify by SCAI stage + hemodynamics (CPO, PAPi, lactate) - match the device to the phenotype rather than reflexively escalating (microaxial pump has RCT support in STEMI-CS; routine IABP and routine ECMO do not); 3) Norepinephrine is first-line vasopressor, dobutamine first-line inotrope - avoid dopamine (SOAP II); 4) Post-arrest the brain is the outcome-limiting organ - controlled temperature 32-37.5C, MAP >=65, avoid hyperoxia/hypoxia and hypocapnia, and defer neuroprognostication to a multimodal assessment at >=72h off sedation
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Cardiac arrest & shock DRGs; very high mortality. ICU/ED entry.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 296 — Cardiac Arrest, Unexplained with MCC; 297 — Cardiac Arrest, Unexplained with CC; 298 — Cardiac Arrest, Unexplained without CC/MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 8/8 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Current pass completed citation-resolvability, coding, taxonomy, schema, and consistency checks. The source workflow contains no completed adversarial high-stakes clinical verdict for this row; clinician revalidation remains required before protocol or order-set use.
data_quality_notes: Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk.

admission_criteria: Objective inpatient indications: acute decompensated HF with hypoxia (SpO2 <90%) or respiratory distress needing supplemental O2/NIV; hemodynamic instability (SBP <90 mmHg or hypoperfusion/cardiogenic shock -> ICU); new-onset cardiomyopathy with NYHA III-IV symptoms or newly reduced LVEF requiring monitored GDMT initiation; refractory volume overload needing IV diuresis; sustained/symptomatic ventricular arrhythmia (VT/VF), high-grade AV block, or unexplained syncope; troponin-positive suspected myocarditis; markedly elevated NT-proBNP (>1000 pg/mL) with congestion. ED discharge/observation acceptable for low-risk ADHF (good diuretic response, SBP >120, normal troponin/renal function, no hypoxia) risk-stratified by EHMRG/Ottawa HF Risk Scale. Suspected fulminant myocarditis or cardiogenic shock -> direct ICU + advanced-HF center transfer
risk_stratification: GWTG-HF risk score (in-hospital mortality; higher deciles -> closer monitoring); EHMRG 7-day mortality (high -> admit/ICU, low -> obs/discharge); Ottawa Heart Failure Risk Scale (serious adverse events); MAGGIC and Seattle Heart Failure Model (1-yr mortality -> advanced-therapy/transplant referral if high); NYHA class III-IV -> escalate; LVEF category HFrEF <=40% / HFmrEF 41-49% / HFpEF >=50% guides GDMT; NT-proBNP/BNP (prognostic; failure to fall >=30% during stay = worse outcome); HCM Risk-SCD 5-yr >=6% high (ICD indicated), 4-<6% intermediate, <4% low; INTERMACS profile 1-2 (cardiogenic shock -> MCS/transfer); CHA2DS2-VASc for AF anticoagulation; hyponatremia (Na <135) and elevated troponin flag higher risk
initial_workup_labs: BNP or NT-proBNP (diagnosis/prognosis, age-adjusted cutoffs); high-sensitivity troponin (myocardial injury/myocarditis -> if elevated pursue ischemia/myocarditis workup); CBC; CMP (Na, K, renal function -> cardiorenal, hyponatremia); Mg/Ca/phosphate; LFTs (congestive hepatopathy); TSH (thyroid cardiomyopathy); iron studies/ferritin/TSAT (treatable iron deficiency, hemochromatosis screen); HbA1c and lipids; serum/urine protein electrophoresis + serum free light chains (amyloid screen if restrictive/infiltrative or low-voltage-with-LVH discordance); HIV; urine/serum toxicology (cocaine/methamphetamine, alcohol); lactate if hypoperfusion; consider ANA/ESR/CRP/ACE if myocarditis/sarcoid. Triggers: Na<135 -> fluid restriction/prognostic flag; abnormal light-chain ratio -> amyloid pathway; elevated troponin -> CMR/coronary evaluation
initial_imaging_dx: 12-lead ECG immediately (arrhythmia, LVH/HCM pattern, low-voltage + pseudoinfarct -> amyloid, conduction disease); CXR (congestion, cardiomegaly, effusion); transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE) within 24-48h (immediately if unstable/new dx) — LVEF, chamber size, wall thickness, diastology, valves, LVOT gradient at rest + Valsalva for HCM, RV, pericardium, LV thrombus; cardiac MRI with late gadolinium enhancement for tissue characterization/etiology (myocarditis, infiltration, fibrosis, sarcoid, amyloid) during admission or expedited outpatient; coronary evaluation (invasive angiography or coronary CTA) to exclude ischemic etiology in new cardiomyopathy; continuous telemetry; 99mTc-pyrophosphate scintigraphy if ATTR amyloid suspected; ambulatory Holter/rhythm monitor for NSVT burden (HCM SCD risk); endomyocardial biopsy only in selected cases (suspected giant-cell/eosinophilic/sarcoid or fulminant myocarditis)
time_critical_interventions: ADHF with congestion: door-to-diuretic <60 min (early IV loop diuretic associated with lower mortality, REALITY-AHF); early NIPPV for cardiogenic pulmonary edema/respiratory distress; cardiogenic shock (INTERMACS 1-2): rapid recognition, inotrope/vasopressor + temporary mechanical circulatory support (IABP/Impella/VA-ECMO), early transfer to shock/advanced-HF center; suspected fulminant myocarditis: emergent MCS/ECMO + tertiary transfer; sustained VT/VF or electrical storm: immediate ACLS/synchronized cardioversion/defibrillation + antiarrhythmics; symptomatic high-grade AV block: temporary pacing; if ACS is the trigger -> cath lab per ACS pathway (door-to-balloon <=90 min); peripartum cardiomyopathy decompensation: expedite HF therapy + consider bromocriptine
initial_management: HFrEF (LVEF <=40%) GDMT four pillars, initiate in-hospital and sequence rapidly: (1) ARNI sacubitril/valsartan preferred (or ACEi lisinopril/enalapril, or ARB losartan/valsartan if ARNI not feasible); (2) evidence-based beta-blocker carvedilol/metoprolol succinate/bisoprolol; (3) MRA spironolactone/eplerenone; (4) SGLT2 inhibitor dapagliflozin/empagliflozin. Decongestion: IV loop diuretic (furosemide/bumetanide/torsemide) at >=2.5x home oral dose, bolus or infusion (DOSE trial); add thiazide/metolazone for diuretic resistance; strict I/O + daily weights. Hydralazine + isosorbide dinitrate for self-identified Black patients on GDMT or ACEi/ARB intolerance (A-HeFT). IV iron (ferric carboxymaltose) if ferritin <100 or 100-300 with TSAT <20%. Anticoagulate for AF (CHA2DS2-VASc) or LV thrombus. Cardiogenic shock: norepinephrine first-line + inotrope (dobutamine/milrinone) + temporary MCS. Obstructive HCM DIVERGES: negative inotropes (beta-blocker first-line, verapamil/diltiazem or disopyramide second-line), cardiac myosin inhibitor mavacamten for symptomatic obstruction — AVOID vasodilators, high-dose diuretics, nitrates, digoxin. ATTR cardiac amyloid: tafamidis + gentle diuresis
day1_milestones: Establish morphofunctional phenotype (dilated/hypertrophic/restrictive/arrhythmogenic/non-dilated LV) and probable etiology; obtain and review TTE + ECG; start continuous telemetry; begin decongestion with documented dry-weight target, strict I/O, daily weights; initiate >=1-2 GDMT pillars as BP/renal function allow (hold/adjust if SBP<90 or K+ elevated); send etiology labs (light chains, iron, TSH, tox); cardiology consult and screen for advanced-HF/shock features (INTERMACS); risk-stratify (NT-proBNP, SCD markers if HCM); VTE prophylaxis, supplemental O2 as needed; open goals-of-care/code-status discussion if advanced disease
day2_milestones: Assess diuretic response (net-negative balance, weight trend, congestion/symptom improvement) and up-titrate diuretic if inadequate; continue rapid GDMT sequencing to add remaining pillars (STRONG-HF strategy); replete K+/Mg and monitor renal function/potassium; review CMR/coronary results and plan remaining etiology workup; begin transition from IV to oral diuretic as euvolemia approaches; review telemetry for arrhythmia; start HF self-care education (sodium/fluid, weights, meds); refer genetic counseling if inherited cardiomyopathy; note primary-prevention ICD decision for non-ischemic HFrEF is deferred until >=3 months of optimized GDMT with LVEF reassessment
day3plus_milestones: Achieve euvolemia at/near target dry weight; stable on oral diuretic regimen >=24h; consolidate GDMT at maximally tolerated doses with a written up-titration plan; complete etiology workup or arrange expedited outpatient studies (CMR, PYP, genetics, coronary); finalize device/advanced-therapy plan (ICD/CRT candidacy, advanced-HF/transplant/LVAD referral if refractory or inotrope-dependent); confirm no rest dyspnea, stable renal function/K+, acceptable BP/HR; complete medication reconciliation with teach-back; schedule 7-day follow-up and enroll in HF disease-management/remote-monitoring program; cardiac rehab referral
consults_multidisciplinary: Cardiology/advanced-HF cardiology (primary, Day 0-1); electrophysiology for arrhythmia/device candidacy; interventional cardiology if ischemic workup; advanced-HF/transplant-LVAD referral if refractory/INTERMACS 1-3; HF clinical pharmacist for GDMT titration and affordability; HF nurse educator/navigator; care management + social work (transitions, medication access/prior-auth, home health) early; PT/OT for functional assessment + cardiac-rehab referral; registered dietitian (sodium/fluid, cachexia); genetics/genetic counseling for inherited cardiomyopathy (Class I); palliative care for NYHA IV/high symptom burden; OB/maternal-fetal medicine if peripartum; amyloid or cardiac-sarcoid multidisciplinary team when indicated
monitoring_level: Most stable cardiomyopathy/ADHF -> floor with continuous telemetry (arrhythmia + SCD surveillance); step-down/ICU for cardiogenic shock, inotrope/vasopressor or temporary MCS need, fulminant myocarditis, refractory ventricular arrhythmia/electrical storm, high-grade AV block requiring temporary pacing, respiratory failure needing NIV/intubation, or hemodynamic instability (SBP<90, hypoperfusion, rising lactate). Escalation triggers: worsening hypoxia, rising lactate, hypotension, oliguria (<0.5 mL/kg/h), sustained VT, or altered mentation -> upgrade level of care
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: sodium ~2-3 g/day (avoid extreme <1.5 g — SODIUM-HF showed no benefit), fluid restriction ~1.5-2 L/day if hyponatremic/congested, heart-healthy pattern, address cardiac cachexia, diabetic diet if DM. Mobility: early PT/mobilization to prevent deconditioning; structured cardiac-rehab referral at discharge (Class I). VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH/UFH) for hospitalized HF unless contraindicated, mechanical if bleeding risk. Glucose: moderate control, leverage SGLT2i, avoid hypoglycemia. Lines/catheters: remove central lines and Foley as early as possible to prevent CLABSI/CAUTI; daily weights and strict I/O throughout
discharge_criteria: Euvolemic or at/near target dry weight with resolved congestion (no rest dyspnea, minimal edema); transitioned to oral diuretic >=24h with stable weight; stable renal function and potassium on GDMT; off IV inotropes/vasoactives >=24h (or stable documented palliative plan); controlled rhythm; GDMT initiated with documented up-titration plan; at/near baseline functional status and PT-cleared for disposition; HF self-care education completed with teach-back; follow-up arranged and discharge medications obtained/affordable
discharge_planning: Disposition home with HF navigator/home health for most; SNF/acute rehab if deconditioned; transfer to LVAD/transplant center if advanced. Medication reconciliation ensuring all indicated GDMT prescribed with affordability/prior-auth addressed (ARNI, SGLT2i) plus explicit diuretic dosing plan. Early follow-up: cardiology/HF clinic visit within 7 days (Class I), BMP within ~7 days for renal/K+ after GDMT, 48-72h phone check. Patient education: daily weights, sodium/fluid limits, symptom-action plan, adherence, when to call. Enroll in HF disease-management/remote-monitoring; cardiac-rehab referral; advance-care planning when appropriate; arrange genetic counseling and first-degree-relative screening for inherited cardiomyopathy
expected_los: DRG-specific GMLOS benchmarks: 314 (Other circulatory dx w MCC) ~4.5 days; 315 (w CC) ~3.3-3.5 days; 316 (w/o CC/MCC) ~2.3-2.5 days; national ADHF median ~4 days. Blended typical stay ~3-4 days
target_los: ~3 days for uncomplicated stabilized decongestion with in-hospital GDMT initiation and STRONG-HF-style rapid outpatient up-titration + 7-day follow-up; ~4-5 days for MCC tier requiring etiology workup, device evaluation, or advanced-therapy assessment. Avoid premature discharge with residual congestion (drives readmission)
quality_metrics: CMS HRRP: 30-day risk-standardized HF readmission and 30-day mortality (publicly reported/penalized); GWTG-HF registry measures — ACEi/ARB/ARNI for LVEF<40%, evidence-based beta-blocker, MRA, and SGLT2i at discharge; anticoagulation for AF; ICD/CRT counseling/placement; documented LVEF/EF assessment; HF self-care education; influenza/pneumococcal vaccination; smoking-cessation counseling; scheduled follow-up; cardiac-rehab referral; defect-free composite care. Process/outcome: door-to-diuretic time, 7-day follow-up rate, LOS, 30-day all-cause readmission
common_complications: Cardiorenal syndrome/worsening renal function during diuresis (mitigate: measured diuresis, serial BMP, avoid over-diuresis); electrolyte derangement — hypokalemia, MRA-related hyperkalemia, hyponatremia (scheduled labs/repletion); GDMT-related hypotension/dizziness (careful sequencing/titration); diuretic resistance (sequential nephron blockade); arrhythmias including AF, VT, SCD (telemetry, GDMT, device evaluation); progression to cardiogenic shock (early recognition/MCS); hospital-acquired VTE, delirium, deconditioning, CLABSI/CAUTI, contrast-associated AKI (prophylaxis, early mobility, prompt line/catheter removal); digoxin toxicity if used
readmission_drivers: Residual congestion/incomplete decongestion at discharge (top driver — ensure euvolemia + daily-weight monitoring); failure to initiate/up-titrate GDMT (in-hospital initiation + STRONG-HF up-titration); poor transitions/no early follow-up (7-day visit, phone check, remote monitoring); medication nonadherence or cost barriers for ARNI/SGLT2i (affordability, pharmacist, education); dietary/fluid nonadherence (dietitian, teach-back); missed or undertreated etiology (amyloid, ischemia, arrhythmia, iron deficiency); decompensated comorbidities (renal, DM, COPD); absent disease-management enrollment
guideline_source: Primary: 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Guideline for the Management of Heart Failure (Circulation, 2022) — GDMT backbone for cardiomyopathy with HF; 2023 ESC Guidelines for the Management of Cardiomyopathies (European Heart Journal, 2023) — phenotype-based diagnosis, multimodality imaging, genetics, SCD risk; 2024 AHA/ACC/AMSSM/HRS/PACES/SCMR Guideline for the Management of Hypertrophic Cardiomyopathy (Circulation, 2024) — HCM-specific therapy and SCD stratification
key_citations: Heidenreich PA et al. 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA HF Guideline. Circulation 2022;145(18):e895-e1032 (PMID 35363499). Arbelo E et al. 2023 ESC Cardiomyopathies Guideline. Eur Heart J 2023;44(37):3503-3626 (PMID 37622657). Ommen SR et al. 2024 AHA/ACC HCM Guideline. Circulation 2024;149(23):e1239-e1311 (PMID 38718139). Landmark RCTs: PARADIGM-HF (sacubitril/valsartan) NEJM 2014;371:993-1004 (PMID 25176015); DAPA-HF NEJM 2019;381:1995-2008 (PMID 31535829); EMPEROR-Reduced NEJM 2020;383:1413-1424 (PMID 32865377); STRONG-HF (rapid up-titration) Lancet 2022;400:1938-1952 (PMID 36356631). Source: PubMed
evidence_grade: Strong — multiple current Class I society guidelines (ACC/AHA/HFSA 2022, ESC 2023, AHA/ACC HCM 2024) with RCT-backed Class I GDMT (PARADIGM-HF, DAPA-HF, EMPEROR-Reduced) and Class I in-hospital initiation/rapid up-titration (STRONG-HF). Etiology- and phenotype-specific arms (amyloid, sarcoid, peripartum, genetic) rest on lower-grade/observational evidence; LOS targets are operational benchmarks (moderate/expert consensus)
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 314 (w MCC) vs 315 (w CC) vs 316 (w/o CC/MCC): MCC-tier drivers (acute cardiogenic shock, acute respiratory failure, AKI, sustained ventricular arrhythmia, acute systolic/diastolic HF exacerbation, sepsis) shift patients toward ICU/step-down, temporary MCS, longer LOS (~4.5+ vs ~2.4 days), and higher device/advanced-therapy and complication rates. Coding note: when acute HF is the principal diagnosis, the case groups to DRG 291-293 (Heart Failure & Shock), not 314-316 — this triplet captures cardiomyopathy/other-circulatory PRINCIPAL diagnoses; accurate capture of MCC/CC comorbidities and the acute driver is essential for correct DRG assignment, LOS benchmarking, and severity-adjusted outcomes
pathway_pearls: (1) Phenotype first — cardiomyopathy is not one disease: separate HFrEF (four-pillar GDMT), obstructive HCM (negative inotropes + mavacamten, AVOID vasodilators/high-dose diuretics/digoxin), and infiltrative/amyloid (tafamidis, gentle diuresis); wrong bucket = harm. (2) Start GDMT in-hospital and up-titrate rapidly with close early follow-up (STRONG-HF) — do not defer to outpatient. (3) Discharge only at true euvolemia — residual congestion is the #1 readmission driver; pair with 7-day follow-up + daily weights. (4) Chase the etiology (CMR/LGE, free light chains, iron studies, coronary eval, genetics) — a treatable cause (ischemia, iron deficiency, amyloid, sarcoid, tachycardia-mediated, peripartum) changes management and prognosis
