admission_criteria: Most hernias are elective/outpatient (Fitzgibbons JAMA 2006: watchful waiting safe in minimally symptomatic men, acute incarceration only ~1.8/1000 patient-years) — so this inpatient DRG group is dominated by the EMERGENT complicated subset. Admit for: irreducible/incarcerated hernia with bowel obstruction or peritonism; suspected/confirmed strangulation (SIRS, focal tenderness/erythema/skin change over sac, lactate elevation); small-bowel obstruction from incarceration; failed gentle manual reduction (taxis); femoral hernia (high strangulation risk — admit and repair promptly); sepsis or hemodynamic instability; or major comorbidity/frailty precluding same-day discharge. ED discharge/expedited elective repair appropriate for a simple reducible hernia or a successfully reduced incarceration without ischemia in a reliable patient.
risk_stratification: No hernia-specific score; use EGS/perioperative tools. SIRS >=2 or qSOFA >=2 -> suspect strangulation/sepsis, expedite OR. Serum lactate >=2.0 mmol/L -> predictive of non-viable bowel (WSES) -> urgent exploration. CT reduced/absent bowel-wall enhancement (~56% sensitivity, 94% specificity for strangulation) -> immediate OR; elevated CPK and D-dimer are supportive. ASA class and Modified Frailty Index (mFI >=2 = frail -> higher 30-day morbidity/mortality; favors local anesthesia + prehab). P-POSSUM / NELA risk model / Emergency Surgery Score for mortality estimate and ICU triage. EHS groin classification and defect size (<3 cm vs larger) direct repair type/mesh choice.
initial_workup_labs: CBC (leukocytosis/left shift), CMP/BMP (electrolytes, dehydration/AKI from vomiting), serum lactate (strangulation/hypoperfusion -> escalate to OR if elevated), CRP, coagulation panel/INR, type & screen/crossmatch, VBG. CPK and D-dimer if strangulation suspected. Preop: glucose/HbA1c for glycemic optimization, pregnancy test where relevant. Rising WBC + lactate + CPK triggers move toward urgent exploration and broad-spectrum antibiotics.
initial_imaging_dx: Clinical diagnosis usually sufficient (HerniaSurge: physical exam confirms most inguinal hernias). CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast is imaging of choice for suspected incarceration/strangulation/SBO — obtain promptly (within a few hours), assessing bowel-wall enhancement/viability, transition point, free air/fluid; do NOT delay OR for imaging when strangulation is clinically obvious. Ultrasound for equivocal groin swelling and to distinguish femoral from inguinal (especially women). Upright CXR for free air; ECG/preop cardiac risk assessment as indicated.
time_critical_interventions: Strangulation is a surgical emergency: operate immediately when strangulation suspected (WSES Grade 1C) — time from symptom onset to surgery is the single most important prognostic factor; delay >24h raises bowel-resection rate, morbidity, and mortality. If septic: Surviving Sepsis bundle — broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1h, 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4, remeasure lactate. Gentle taxis (manual reduction) may be attempted ONLY for incarceration WITHOUT strangulation/peritonitis; avoid forceful reduction (reduction en masse risk); if reduced, observe for ischemia and consider diagnostic laparoscopy to confirm viability. Preop antibiotic prophylaxis within 60 min of incision; NG decompression for obstruction.
initial_management: Resuscitation: isotonic crystalloid, correct electrolytes, NG tube for obstruction/emesis, Foley for UOP. Antibiotics: cefazolin (1st/2nd-gen cephalosporin) prophylaxis <60 min; if strangulation/resection/contamination -> therapeutic GN+anaerobe cover (piperacillin-tazobactam, or cephalosporin+metronidazole; carbapenem if septic/resistant risk). Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (acetaminophen, NSAID if tolerated, regional/TAP block). VTE prophylaxis per risk. Source control = definitive repair. WSES mesh principles: viable bowel / clean or clean-contaminated -> synthetic mesh (lower recurrence, no rise in SSI; Grade 1A); gross contamination/dirty (spillage, established peritonitis) -> primary suture if defect <3 cm, else biologic or absorbable (polyglactin) mesh or staged/bridged delayed repair (Grade 2C) — avoid permanent synthetic in grossly contaminated fields. Resect non-viable bowel; assess viability by color, peristalsis, mesenteric pulsation (fluorescence if available); diagnostic laparoscopy option after spontaneous reduction (Grade 2B). Local anesthesia is a safe option for open groin repair in frail/high-comorbidity patients (shorter LOS, fewer complications).
day1_milestones: POD0: Pre-op — resuscitate to euvolemia, correct electrolytes/coagulopathy, glycemic control, antibiotic prophylaxis <60 min, mechanical VTE prophylaxis, site marking, informed consent. Intra-op — hernia repair/source control with ERAS elements (maintain normothermia, goal-directed fluids, opioid-sparing anesthesia, regional/TAP block, minimize/avoid drains), resect non-viable bowel, wound-class-appropriate mesh decision. Post-op — extubate, PACU, start multimodal analgesia and PONV prophylaxis, sips/clear liquids if no bowel resection, mobilize the same evening, continue VTE prophylaxis.
day2_milestones: POD1: Advance diet (regular if no resection; slower with resection/ileus) and assess return of bowel function; ambulate >=3-4x/day with incentive spirometry; transition IV to oral analgesia and stop IV fluids once tolerating PO; remove Foley within 24h to limit CAUTI/retention; assess wound for SSI/seroma and screen for urinary retention. Simple reduced/repaired incarceration without resection often meets discharge criteria and goes home POD1.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+: Confirm return of bowel function (flatus/BM), critical after bowel resection; continue diet advancement; escalate mobility and wean opioids; monitor for ileus/SBO, anastomotic leak (if resection), SSI/mesh infection; continue VTE prophylaxis and wound checks; reach discharge readiness. Prolonged stays reflect resection, ileus, sepsis, or comorbidity (MCC tier).
consults_multidisciplinary: Acute care/general surgery primary; anesthesia/preop clinic. Medicine or geriatrics co-management for frail/comorbid; cardiology for cardiac risk; critical care for septic/unstable. PT/OT for early mobility and function; nutrition (post-resection, malnourished, or open abdomen); pharmacy (antibiotic stewardship, anticoagulation, analgesia); care management/social work early for disposition and transitions; wound/ostomy nurse if stoma or open abdomen; palliative care for frail/poor-prognosis patients.
monitoring_level: Surgical floor for uncomplicated incarceration. Telemetry/step-down for significant comorbidity, resolving sepsis, or post-resection physiologic derangement. ICU for septic shock, hemodynamic instability, damage-control/open abdomen, respiratory failure, or vasopressor/high fluid needs. Escalation triggers: rising lactate, hypotension, increasing pressor requirement, new organ dysfunction, or signs of peritonitis/anastomotic leak.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: early oral intake within 24h (ERAS) — clears POD0 if no resection, advance to regular as tolerated; cautious advancement with resection/ileus. Mobility: out of bed the evening of surgery, then >=3-4x/day. VTE: mechanical (SCDs) plus pharmacologic (LMWH/UFH) per Caprini risk once hemostasis secure; extended prophylaxis for cancer/major reconstruction. Glucose target <180 mg/dL, avoid hypoglycemia. Lines/catheters: remove Foley <24h, limit and early-remove surgical drains, minimize central lines.
discharge_criteria: Tolerating adequate oral intake; pain controlled on oral analgesics; return of bowel function (flatus/BM — especially after resection); afebrile with stable vitals; ambulating at or near baseline; voiding spontaneously; wound without signs of infection; and safe disposition arranged.
discharge_planning: Disposition: home for most; SNF/rehab for deconditioned frail patients. Medication reconciliation including any extended VTE prophylaxis, analgesia taper with opioid stewardship, and restart of home meds. Follow-up: surgical clinic 2-4 weeks with wound/mesh surveillance; enroll in a hernia registry (ACHQC/EuraHS) for outcome tracking. Education: resume normal activity as comfortable (HerniaSurge — no arbitrary lifting restrictions), wound care, and warning signs (fever, redness, drainage, recurrent bulge, obstructive symptoms); counsel on recurrence and chronic postoperative inguinal pain. Transitions: written instructions, PCP notification, teach-back.
expected_los: Blended GMLOS varies by severity tier: without CC/MCC (DRG 352, 355) ~1-2 days (many now same-day/outpatient); with CC (351, 354) ~3-4 days; with MCC (350, 353) ~5-7 days. The emergent incarcerated/strangulated inpatient subset typically runs ~3-5 days, and ~6-8+ days when bowel resection or sepsis is involved.
target_los: Uncomplicated incarceration reduced and repaired: 1-2 days. Strangulation with segmental bowel resection under an ERAS pathway: 3-4 days. Reducible/elective disease: same-day/outpatient (0 inpatient days).
quality_metrics: Perioperative (SCIP-derived): antibiotic prophylaxis within 60 min and discontinuation <24h, maintained normothermia, VTE prophylaxis ordered/received (VTE-1/2). SSI surveillance including mesh infection (NHSN/NSQIP). 30-day readmission, reoperation, recurrence, and mortality; ERAS protocol compliance. Registry participation: ACHQC (Abdominal Core Health QC) / EuraHS. SEP-1 bundle compliance if septic. Same-day/outpatient conversion rate for eligible elective repairs; CAUTI rate (early Foley removal); opioid-free discharge rate.
common_complications: SSI/mesh infection (mitigate: prophylaxis timing, wound-class-appropriate mesh, avoid synthetic mesh in dirty fields); seroma/hematoma (hemostasis, selective drains); urinary retention (early Foley removal, limit opioids/IVF); ileus/SBO (opioid-sparing, early feeding + mobility); bowel injury/anastomotic leak after resection (viability assessment, monitoring); recurrence (mesh reinforcement when field permits); chronic postoperative inguinal pain (CPIP ~10-12%; nerve identification/handling, sound mesh technique); cardiopulmonary events/VTE in frail (prehab, prophylaxis, local-anesthesia option); missed femoral hernia in women (laparo-endoscopic approach to inspect femoral canal).
readmission_drivers: Top drivers: SSI/mesh infection, seroma, uncontrolled pain, ileus/obstruction, urinary retention, early recurrence, and dehydration. Prevention levers: wound-class-appropriate technique and mesh selection, antibiotic stewardship, multimodal analgesia with opioid stewardship, early follow-up (2-4 weeks), explicit warning-sign education with teach-back, hernia-registry tracking, and geriatric co-management of frail patients.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: WSES Guidelines for Emergency Repair of Complicated Abdominal Wall Hernias, 2017 update (World Society of Emergency Surgery; Birindelli et al., World J Emerg Surg 2017). PRIMARY: HerniaSurge Group International Guidelines for Groin Hernia Management 2018, updated 2023 (European Hernia Society/HerniaSurge Collaboration; Stabilini et al., BJS Open 2023). SUPPORTING: SAGES Guidelines for Laparoscopic Ventral Hernia Repair (2016); ACHQC ERAS-for-ventral-hernia consensus (2024); ERAS Society perioperative principles.
key_citations: According to PubMed: WSES 2017 update, PMID 28804507, DOI 10.1186/s13017-017-0149-y; HerniaSurge International Guidelines for Groin Hernia Management 2018, PMID 29330835, DOI 10.1007/s10029-017-1668-x; HerniaSurge Update 2023 (BJS Open), PMID 37862616, DOI 10.1093/bjsopen/zrad080; SAGES Laparoscopic Ventral Hernia Repair Guidelines 2016, PMID 27405477, DOI 10.1007/s00464-016-5072-x; ACHQC ERAS Ventral Hernia Consensus 2024, PMID 39542932, DOI 10.1007/s10029-024-03203-9; Fitzgibbons Watchful Waiting vs Repair RCT, JAMA 2006, PMID 16418463, DOI 10.1001/jama.295.3.285; Li et al. local anesthesia in frail open inguinal repair, J Visc Surg 2025, PMID 39794165, DOI 10.1016/j.jviscsurg.2024.12.004.
evidence_grade: Moderate overall. Elective groin/ventral repair is backed by strong RCT evidence and Class I society guidelines (HerniaSurge 2018/2023), but the emergent incarcerated/strangulated INPATIENT pathway rests largely on WSES GRADE 2B-2C (weak, low-to-moderate quality) recommendations plus observational data, with a few strong anchors (immediate surgery for suspected strangulation 1C; synthetic mesh for clean/clean-contaminated repair 1A). Net: Moderate — Class I guideline for the elective baseline combined with GRADE 1A/1C strong and 2B/2C weak emergency-specific recommendations.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 352/355 (no CC/MCC): reducible/simple disease, mostly outpatient/same-day, floor only, LOS ~1-2 days, no ICU. DRG 351/354 (CC): incarceration +/- ileus or moderate comorbidity, telemetry possible, LOS ~3-4 days. DRG 350/353 (MCC): strangulation with bowel resection, sepsis/peritonitis, or major comorbidity -> step-down/ICU, possible damage-control/open abdomen, biologic or staged repair, higher SSI/mortality, LOS ~6-8+ days. The 'other/ventral' DRGs (353-355) skew toward larger defects and more complex abdominal-wall reconstruction, driving longer LOS and higher complication rates than the groin DRGs (350-352).
pathway_pearls: 1) Time-to-OR is the dominant prognostic lever — do not delay exploration when strangulation is suspected; lactate >=2.0 mmol/L and reduced CT bowel-wall enhancement (94% specific) drive urgent operation. 2) Match the mesh to the wound — synthetic for viable/clean-contaminated fields (Grade 1A), and avoid permanent synthetic in contaminated/dirty fields (biologic, absorbable, primary <3 cm, or staged repair). 3) Femoral hernias and women carry high strangulation and missed-femoral risk — low threshold to repair, prefer a laparo-endoscopic approach to inspect the femoral canal. 4) Frailty pathway — local anesthesia + ERAS + geriatric co-management shortens LOS and cuts complications in the elderly emergent cohort; most simple reduced incarcerations are 1-2 day stays.

admission_criteria: Admit (not ED discharge/obs) for: newly diagnosed ITP with platelets <20x10^9/L (ASH 2019 suggests admission) or any thrombocytopenia with wet purpura/mucosal or WHO grade >=2 bleeding; suspected TTP (microangiopathic hemolytic anemia + thrombocytopenia, PLASMIC >=5) as an emergency for PLEX; suspected/confirmed HIT with new thrombosis (HITT) or needing parenteral non-heparin anticoagulation; anticoagulant-associated major/life-threatening bleed needing reversal (ICH, unstable GI/retroperitoneal); overt DIC (ISTH >=5) with bleeding/organ dysfunction; congenital factor deficiency (hemophilia/VWD) with major bleed needing factor; coagulopathy requiring correction for an urgent procedure. ED discharge/obs acceptable for: stable platelets >30-50x10^9/L without bleeding; supratherapeutic INR without bleeding (hold VKA +/- oral vitamin K, INR>10 give oral vitamin K); minor self-limited bleeding.
risk_stratification: 4Ts score (HIT): 0-3 low (<5% probability, do not test/treat), 4-5 intermediate, 6-8 high -> stop heparin + start non-heparin anticoagulant + send PF4 ELISA/SRA (HEP score is an alternative). PLASMIC score (TTP): 6-7 high (>70% probability ADAMTS13 <10%) -> urgent PLEX; 5 intermediate; 0-4 low. ISTH overt-DIC score >=5 = overt DIC -> treat cause + goal-directed transfusion. HAS-BLED >=3 = high bleeding risk (informs anticoagulation resumption). ISTH/WHO bleeding-severity grade stratifies urgency. ITP: platelet thresholds drive care (<20 admit, <30 treat, >=30 observe).
initial_workup_labs: CBC + platelet count with peripheral smear reviewed by heme (schistocytes -> TMA/TTP; platelet clumps -> pseudothrombocytopenia, recheck in citrate tube; blasts -> leukemia); PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen, D-dimer/FDP (DIC panel); hemolysis panel LDH, haptoglobin, reticulocyte, indirect bilirubin, DAT/Coombs (MAHA/TTP); type & screen/crossmatch; CMP (renal/hepatic); pregnancy test (HELLP); mixing study if isolated prolonged aPTT (inhibitor vs factor deficiency); ADAMTS13 activity + inhibitor drawn BEFORE PLEX (do not await result to treat); HIT PF4-heparin ELISA (report OD) + confirmatory serotonin-release assay (SRA); DOAC quantification (calibrated anti-Xa level; dilute thrombin time/TT for dabigatran); secondary-ITP workup HIV/HCV/H. pylori; detailed drug/heparin exposure history (quinine, sulfa, vancomycin, linezolid, GPIIb/IIIa inhibitors).
initial_imaging_dx: STAT non-contrast CT head (door-to-CT <25 min) for any neuro symptom or suspected anticoagulant-associated ICH; CT/US for occult bleed source (retroperitoneal, GI); bilateral lower-extremity Doppler US if HIT suspected to detect HITT; immediate peripheral blood smear microscopy; bone marrow biopsy ONLY if atypical features (older age, additional cytopenias, no steroid response) - not routine for typical ITP; endoscopy for GI source control per GI; echocardiography only if clinically indicated.
time_critical_interventions: Suspected TTP -> initiate therapeutic plasma exchange (PLEX/TPE) within 4-8 h of presentation (ideally <6 h); if PLEX delayed, infuse plasma as a bridge and start corticosteroid + caplacizumab immediately. Anticoagulant-associated ICH/life-threatening bleed -> reverse within 60 min (door-to-reversal <=60-90 min): 4F-PCC + IV vitamin K (warfarin), idarucizumab (dabigatran), andexanet alfa or 4F-PCC 50 U/kg (apixaban/rivaroxaban); concurrent BP control + neurosurgery. HIT -> stop ALL heparin (flushes, LMWH, heparin-coated lines) the moment 4Ts >=4 and start a non-heparin anticoagulant, before lab confirmation. Activate massive transfusion protocol for exsanguinating hemorrhage; TXA for selected mucosal bleeding.
initial_management: ITP: corticosteroid first-line - dexamethasone 40 mg/day x4 days OR prednisone 0.5-2 mg/kg/day short course (<=6 wk); add IVIG 1 g/kg/day x1-2 (or 0.4 g/kg/day x5) if bleeding/need rapid rise; platelet transfusion ONLY for life-threatening bleed (give WITH IVIG). HIT: stop heparin; start argatroban (hepatic clearance), bivalirudin, fondaparinux, or a DOAC; avoid platelet transfusion and warfarin until platelets >150k. TTP: daily PLEX (1-1.5 plasma volumes) + methylprednisolone/prednisone + caplacizumab 10 mg + rituximab; avoid platelet transfusion. DIC: treat cause; goal-directed - platelets, FFP if PT/aPTT >1.5x + bleeding, cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen concentrate if fibrinogen <100-150 mg/dL. VKA major bleed: 4F-PCC 25-50 U/kg (INR-based) + IV vitamin K 10 mg. DOAC: idarucizumab 5 g (dabigatran); andexanet or 4F-PCC 50 U/kg (Xa inhibitors). Heparin: protamine sulfate. Congenital: factor VIII/IX concentrate, VWF/DDAVP. Transfusion thresholds (AABB): prophylactic platelets <10x10^9/L (<20 for central line, <50 for major surgery/active bleed/LP, <100 for CNS/neurosurgery); restrictive RBC Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 cardiac).
day1_milestones: Establish subtype (smear reviewed, hematology consulted by day 0); start targeted therapy (steroid/PLEX/non-heparin anticoagulant/reversal agent); STOP offending drug (heparin, quinine) and document; achieve hemostasis / control active bleeding; if TTP, first PLEX completed + caplacizumab started; if reversal, confirm INR corrected/hemostasis and hold anticoagulant; baseline labs (platelets, coags, hemolysis markers) trended q6-24h; institute bleeding + fall precautions; hold antiplatelets/NSAIDs; match VTE prophylaxis to platelet count (mechanical SCDs if <30-50k).
day2_milestones: Assess response - platelet trend, bleeding cessation, hemolysis resolving; ITP expect steroid response day 2-5 (IVIG effect 1-3 days); TTP continue daily PLEX with platelets trending toward normal; HIT confirm therapeutic non-heparin anticoagulation (aPTT/anti-IIa) and platelet-recovery trend; review returning PF4/SRA and ADAMTS13 results and adjust; de-escalate monitoring if stable; reassess nutrition, mobility, and VTE prophylaxis; pharmacy review of culprit drugs and anticoagulant dosing.
day3plus_milestones: Sustained hemostasis/platelet recovery. TTP: continue PLEX until platelets >150x10^9/L for 2 consecutive days then stop; continue caplacizumab ~30 days post-PLEX + rituximab course; monitor ADAMTS13. HIT: transition to oral anticoagulant (warfarin overlap ONLY once platelets >150k, or a DOAC); plan duration 4 wk (isolated HIT) to 3 mo (HITT). ITP: begin steroid taper, arrange outpatient follow-up, plan second-line (TPO-RA romiplostim/eltrombopag, rituximab, or fostamatinib) if refractory. Anticoagulant bleed: define resumption timing (e.g., ~1-2 wk after major GIB; 4-8 wk after ICH if high thrombotic risk). Finalize discharge planning, education, follow-up labs.
consults_multidisciplinary: Hematology (core, day 0); transfusion medicine/apheresis (TTP PLEX, platelet-transfusion stewardship); critical care (TTP, unstable hemorrhage, HITT); neurosurgery/neurology (ICH); GI or interventional radiology (bleeding source control); nephrology (renal TMA); pharmacy (anticoagulation/reversal dosing, drug-induced thrombocytopenia review, day 0); care management/social work (anticoagulation-clinic linkage, cost/access for caplacizumab/TPO-RA, transitions); PT/OT (deconditioning, fall risk); nutrition.
monitoring_level: ICU: TTP on PLEX with instability, active life-threatening hemorrhage/ICH, hemodynamic instability, massive transfusion, airway compromise. Step-down/telemetry: stabilized significant bleeding, severe thrombocytopenia with wet purpura, HITT on anticoagulation. Floor: stable severe thrombocytopenia without active bleeding, uncomplicated anticoagulant reversal. Escalation triggers: new/expanding bleeding, neurologic change, hemodynamic instability, falling platelets or rising LDH (TTP relapse/exacerbation), new thrombosis, Hgb drop.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet as tolerated (soft, avoid trauma if severe thrombocytopenia); early mobilization with fall precautions; VTE-prophylaxis paradox - HOLD pharmacologic prophylaxis if platelets <30-50x10^9/L or active bleeding (use mechanical SCDs), and in HIT use ONLY non-heparin agents (never heparin/LMWH); resume pharmacologic prophylaxis once platelets recover and bleeding controlled; steroid-induced hyperglycemia monitoring (target 140-180 mg/dL, insulin PRN); PPI for GI bleed/steroid gastroprotection; remove unnecessary central lines/catheters (bleeding and HIT-thrombosis risk).
discharge_criteria: No active bleeding for >=24 h and hemostasis secured; platelet count in a safe, stable/rising range for subtype (ITP not falling and typically >20-30x10^9/L; TTP >150x10^9/L for 2 days off PLEX; HIT recovering >150x10^9/L); INR corrected or anticoagulation plan defined and therapeutic if required; offending drug stopped and allergy documented; hemodynamically stable with stable Hgb; tolerating oral therapy; follow-up labs and clinic arranged; patient educated on bleeding precautions and red flags.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home (SNF if deconditioned). Med reconciliation: explicit steroid taper schedule; anticoagulation plan (agent, duration, bridging, resumption date); STOP culprit drugs; for HIT document heparin allergy + issue wallet/MedicAlert card (lifelong heparin avoidance); PPI. Follow-up: hematology 1-2 wk with repeat CBC; anticoagulation clinic; PCP; TTP -> ADAMTS13 surveillance + relapse plan; HIT -> continue non-heparin anticoagulant. Education: bleeding precautions, avoid NSAIDs/antiplatelets, return precautions (mucosal bleeding, petechiae, neuro symptoms). Transitions: warm handoff, timed platelet recheck, written care plan.
expected_los: GMLOS ~3.6 days (AMLOS ~4.9 days) for MS-DRG 813 (single standalone DRG, no CC/MCC split); wide dispersion - uncomplicated INR reversal or ITP 1-3 days vs TTP or anticoagulant-associated ICH 7-14+ days.
target_los: ~3 days for uncomplicated subsets (isolated severe thrombocytopenia, VKA reversal without ICH) under an optimized pathway; TTP typically 7-10 days (PLEX-dependent) and anticoagulant-ICH 7-14+ days regardless of optimization - do not force these outliers to the mean.
quality_metrics: 30-day all-cause readmission; appropriate VTE prophylaxis (VTE-1/VTE-2) with heparin avoidance in HIT; hospital-acquired/potentially-preventable VTE (VTE-6); patient blood management - avoidance of inappropriate platelet transfusion in ITP/TTP/HIT and restrictive RBC use; anticoagulant/ADE safety (PSI-09 perioperative hemorrhage, bleeding adverse drug events); time-to-PLEX for TTP and door-to-reversal for anticoagulant-associated ICH; medication reconciliation completeness; subtype-specific mortality (TTP, DIC); HCAHPS patient experience.
common_complications: Major/recurrent bleeding incl. ICH and GI (mitigate: reversal, threshold-based transfusion, bleeding precautions); thrombosis - HIT/HITT, TTP microthrombi, DIC (non-heparin anticoagulation, treat cause); TACO/transfusion reaction (restrictive strategy); steroid toxicity - hyperglycemia, psychosis, infection (monitoring, PPI, taper); PLEX central-line complications (pneumothorax, infection, line thrombosis); refractory/relapsed disease incl. TTP exacerbation (rituximab, continue caplacizumab); infection from immunosuppression; hospital-acquired VTE from withheld prophylaxis.
readmission_drivers: ITP relapse after steroid taper (top driver -> early heme follow-up + second-line planning); TTP exacerbation/relapse with unrecovered ADAMTS13 (-> continue caplacizumab, rituximab, ADAMTS13 monitoring); recurrent bleeding on resumed or under-reversed anticoagulation (-> structured resumption plan, anticoagulation clinic); new thrombosis after premature anticoagulant discontinuation in HIT (-> ensure adequate treatment duration); medication errors incl. inadvertent heparin re-exposure in HIT (-> allergy documentation, education); untreated underlying driver (malignancy, liver disease, sepsis for DIC).
guideline_source: ASH 2019 ITP; ASH 2018 HIT (VTE guideline series); ISTH 2020 TTP diagnosis & treatment guidelines (+2025 focused update); ACC 2020 Expert Consensus Decision Pathway on Management of Bleeding in Patients on Oral Anticoagulants; Neurocritical Care Society/SCCM 2016 reversal of antithrombotics in ICH; AABB 2015 platelet transfusion guideline + AABB/ICTMG 2023 RBC transfusion thresholds; ISTH SSC overt-DIC scoring.
key_citations: Per PubMed: ASH 2019 ITP - Neunert et al., Blood Adv 2019 (PMID 31794604); ASH 2018 HIT - Cuker et al., Blood Adv 2018 (PMID 30482768); ISTH TTP treatment - Zheng et al., J Thromb Haemost 2020 (PMID 32914526) and diagnosis (PMID 32914582), 2025 focused update (PMID 40533296); PLASMIC score - Bendapudi et al., Lancet Haematol 2017 (PMID 28259520); ACC 2020 bleeding pathway - Tomaselli et al., JACC 2020 (PMID 32680646); RE-VERSE AD idarucizumab - Pollack et al., NEJM 2017 (PMID 28693366, doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1707278); ANNEXA-4 andexanet alfa - Connolly et al., NEJM 2019 (PMID 30730782); 4F-PCC vs plasma for VKA reversal - Sarode et al., Circulation 2013 (PMID 23935011, doi:10.1161/CIRCULATIONAHA.113.002283); AABB platelet transfusion - Kaufman et al., Ann Intern Med 2015 (PMID 25383671).
evidence_grade: Strong within defined subtypes (Class I society guidelines: ASH ITP and HIT, ISTH TTP, ACC anticoagulant-bleeding pathway, AABB transfusion thresholds, RCT-backed reversal agents idarucizumab/andexanet/4F-PCC). Overall MODERATE-to-STRONG for the collapsed DRG because no single guideline governs the whole heterogeneous group; cross-subtype pathway sequencing and some LOS targets are consensus/expert opinion.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MS-DRG 813 is a SINGLE standalone DRG - NOT split by CC/MCC (unlike RBC-disorder DRGs 811/812) - so the DRG does not reflect the enormous internal acuity range: from uncomplicated supratherapeutic INR (floor, 1-2 days) to TTP/overt DIC/anticoagulant-ICH (ICU, 7-14+ days, meaningful mortality). Command center must risk-stratify by clinical subtype + scores (4Ts, PLASMIC, ISTH-DIC), NOT by a DRG severity tier. MCC-level events (ICH, sepsis-DIC, acute kidney injury in TTP, major hemorrhage) drive ICU utilization, LOS, and mortality even though they do not change this base DRG; note some hemorrhage-predominant encounters group to other DRGs.
pathway_pearls: (1) Do NOT reflexively transfuse platelets - contraindicated/harmful in TTP and HIT (fuels thrombosis) and largely ineffective in ITP; reserve for life-threatening bleeding - this is the #1 error. (2) Time-to-therapy is the outcome lever: TTP -> PLEX within 4-8 h (do not wait for ADAMTS13); anticoagulant-ICH -> reverse within 60 min. (3) Suspected HIT: stop ALL heparin (flushes, LMWH, coated lines) and start a non-heparin anticoagulant BEFORE lab confirmation, never give warfarin until platelets >150k, and document lifelong heparin avoidance. (4) Drive action off the score - 4Ts >=4 workup/treat HIT, PLASMIC >=6 urgent PLEX, ISTH >=5 overt DIC - and match VTE prophylaxis to platelet count (mechanical if <30-50k).

scope_and_volume_notes: Thrombocytopenia (incl. ITP, drug-induced), coagulopathy, anticoagulant complications.

admission_criteria: Most procedures in this DRG family (744-750) are ambulatory/same-day or 23-hr observation; true INPATIENT admission is reserved for: hemodynamic instability or hemoperitoneum requiring emergent OR (ruptured ectopic, ruptured hemorrhagic cyst); sepsis/systemic infection (tubo-ovarian abscess >7 cm, septic/incomplete abortion, complicated PID failing outpatient therapy); acute surgical abdomen needing emergent laparoscopy/laparotomy (ovarian/adnexal torsion); intraoperative conversion to laparotomy or major complication (bowel/bladder/ureteral/vascular injury, EBL >500-1000 mL, transfusion); significant comorbidity requiring monitoring (CC/MCC — cardiopulmonary disease, uncontrolled DM, therapeutic anticoagulation); failure of same-day discharge criteria (intractable pain/N-V, inability to void or tolerate PO); apply CMS 2-midnight rule to distinguish inpatient from observation
risk_stratification: ASA Physical Status I-IV for perioperative risk; Caprini VTE score (0-2 low, 3-4 moderate, >=5 high) drives prophylaxis intensity per ACOG PB 232; adnexal-mass malignancy triage via ACR O-RADS US/MRI, ROMA (CA-125 + HE4), RMI, and IOTA ADNEX/Simple Rules — O-RADS 4-5 or elevated ROMA prompts gyn-onc referral (PB 174); ectopic triage by hemodynamic status + quantitative hCG + mass size + fetal cardiac activity (hCG >5,000 mIU/mL predicts single-dose MTX failure per PB 193); qSOFA/SIRS and lactate for septic abortion/TOA sepsis screen; RCRI for cardiac risk if comorbid; Apfel score (0-4) for PONV prophylaxis
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential (hemorrhage-related anemia, infection leukocytosis); quantitative serum beta-hCG — mandatory in all reproductive-age women to establish/exclude pregnancy and trend ectopic; type & screen/crossmatch for transfusion readiness in hemoperitoneum; comprehensive metabolic panel (renal/hepatic function pre-methotrexate and anesthesia); PT/INR and aPTT if bleeding or anticoagulated; blood type & Rh (Rho[D] immune globulin if Rh-negative); lactate + blood cultures if septic; urinalysis + urine hCG; GC/chlamydia NAAT plus vaginal wet-mount microscopy for suspected PID/TOA; CA-125 +/- HE4 (ROMA) and germ-cell markers (LDH, AFP, hCG, inhibin) for adnexal mass by age; pre-op pregnancy test before any OR case
initial_imaging_dx: Transvaginal + transabdominal pelvic ultrasound with color Doppler is first-line and obtained urgently (<1-2 h) in acute pelvic pain — diagnoses ectopic, characterizes adnexal mass (O-RADS), TOA, and torsion (absent/reduced ovarian arterial-venous flow, enlarged ovary, whirlpool sign; NOTE normal Doppler does NOT exclude torsion); correlate TVUS against the hCG discriminatory zone (~3,500 mIU/mL) for ectopic; CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast if diagnosis unclear or abscess/appendicitis suspected; MRI pelvis (O-RADS MRI) for indeterminate adnexal masses; CXR/ECG per age and comorbidity for pre-op assessment; diagnostic laparoscopy is the gold standard when torsion/ectopic is strongly suspected but imaging is equivocal — do NOT delay surgery for imaging in the unstable patient
time_critical_interventions: Ruptured ectopic/hemoperitoneum with instability: emergent OR without delay, activate massive transfusion protocol for hemorrhagic shock, expedited door-to-OR; ovarian/adnexal torsion: emergent surgical detorsion ideally within 6-8 h of diagnosis to salvage the ovary — time-sensitive; septic abortion/TOA with sepsis (Surviving Sepsis principles): IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 h, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4, and emergent source control (uterine evacuation or abscess drainage); Rho(D) immune globulin within 72 h in Rh-negative unsensitized patients; surgical antibiotic prophylaxis (cefazolin) within 60 min before incision for vagina/bowel-entering cases — most clean laparoscopy, adnexal cases, and D&C for missed/incomplete abortion do NOT require prophylaxis
initial_management: Ectopic: single-dose methotrexate 50 mg/m2 IM (hCG day 1/4/7, expect >=15% fall day 4-7, repeat dose if not) for stable, unruptured, hCG <5,000, mass <3.5-4 cm, no fetal cardiac activity, reliable follow-up; otherwise laparoscopic salpingectomy (preferred if tube damaged or contralateral tube healthy) or salpingostomy. TOA/PID inpatient parenteral regimen (CDC 2021): ceftriaxone 1 g IV q24h + doxycycline 100 mg IV/PO q12h + metronidazole 500 mg q12h (or cefotetan/cefoxitin + doxycycline), transition to oral doxycycline + metronidazole to complete 14 days; image-guided drainage or surgery for TOA >7 cm or no response by 48-72 h. Hemorrhage: restrictive transfusion — RBC for Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 with cardiac disease/symptoms); reverse anticoagulation (vitamin K/4F-PCC for warfarin, idarucizumab for dabigatran, andexanet-alfa/PCC for Xa inhibitors). Adnexal mass: minimally invasive intact removal in an endobag (avoid spill), frozen section if malignancy possible. Opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia (acetaminophen + NSAID/ketorolac +/- local/regional); source control is central
day1_milestones: POD0: minimally invasive route (laparoscopic/vaginal) whenever feasible per AAGL/ACOG for lower morbidity and shorter stay; ERAS intra-op bundle — maintain normothermia, goal-directed euvolemia, PONV prophylaxis with >=2 agents (dexamethasone + ondansetron), multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia, avoid routine drains/NGT; early oral intake within hours; ambulate the same day; remove Foley within 6 h/POD0; verify hemostasis, urine output, and pain control; for outpatient-intent cases meet same-day discharge criteria (spontaneous voiding, PO tolerance, controlled pain, independent ambulation)
day2_milestones: POD1: most non-emergent cases are already discharged POD0; for admitted patients — transition IV to oral analgesia and antibiotics, confirm return of bowel function/flatus and tolerance of regular diet, independent ambulation, afebrile with downtrending WBC (infection cases), spontaneous voiding after Foley removal, pain controlled on an oral regimen; reinforce discharge teaching and warning-sign education; for TOA, confirm clinical response (defervescence, decreasing pain and WBC) before oral switch
day3plus_milestones: POD2+: reserved for complicated/emergent cases (TOA, septic abortion, laparotomy, complications) — progress toward discharge: afebrile 24-48 h, hemodynamically stable, tolerating diet, adequate oral analgesia, ambulating, no ileus, wound healing without infection, labs stabilized/improving (Hgb, WBC); for TOA ensure clinical and imaging improvement with an outpatient oral-antibiotic plan completing the 14-day course; if not improving by 48-72 h escalate — repeat imaging, re-drainage, or surgery
consults_multidisciplinary: Gynecology / minimally invasive gynecologic surgery (primary); gynecologic oncology when adnexal mass is O-RADS 4-5, ROMA/CA-125 elevated, or frozen section suggests malignancy; anesthesia/pre-op optimization clinic; interventional radiology for image-guided TOA drainage; infectious disease for complicated or resistant infection; reproductive endocrinology/fertility counseling when future fertility is affected (salpingectomy, oophorectomy); pharmacy for methotrexate dosing and anticoagulation management; care management and social work engaged early for disposition, contraception/sterilization consent, and safety screening; acute pain service when indicated
monitoring_level: Routine surgical floor for the vast majority; PACU then discharge/observation for uncomplicated MIS; telemetry/step-down for post-hemorrhage instability, significant transfusion, or cardiopulmonary comorbidity; ICU reserved for hemorrhagic or septic shock, massive transfusion, respiratory failure, or major organ injury. Escalation triggers: rising MEWS/NEWS, SBP <90 or MAP <65, HR >120, SpO2 <92%, lactate >=4 or rising, urine output <0.5 mL/kg/h, uncontrolled bleeding, or signs of peritonitis
nutrition_mobility_vte: ERAS nutrition — clear liquids advancing to regular diet on POD0, no routine prolonged NPO; mobilize within hours of surgery. VTE prophylaxis per ACOG PB 232/Caprini: intermittent pneumatic compression intra-op and for all patients; add pharmacologic LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) or UFH 5,000 U SC q8-12h for moderate-high Caprini or malignancy; extended 28-day prophylaxis is for open oncologic surgery (rarely applicable in benign MIS). Early Foley removal (POD0/<6 h) to prevent CAUTI; glucose target <180 mg/dL; remove IV lines and avoid unnecessary drains promptly
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable; afebrile or defervescing on appropriate antibiotics (infection cases); pain controlled on oral analgesics; tolerating oral intake without intractable N/V; voiding spontaneously; ambulating at baseline; return of bowel function without ileus/obstruction; intact hemostasis and healing wound; stable/acceptable hemoglobin; for methotrexate-managed ectopic — appropriate hCG trend and assured reliable outpatient surveillance; for TOA — clinical improvement with an oral-antibiotic plan to complete the course
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home (same-day for uncomplicated MIS); medication reconciliation including oral analgesia, antibiotics to complete the course (PID/TOA 14 days), and methotrexate precautions; schedule follow-up — post-op visit 2-6 weeks, weekly serial hCG until non-pregnant (<5 mIU/mL) for ectopic, colposcopy/cytology surveillance after conization, and pathology review; patient education on warning signs (fever, heavy bleeding, worsening pain) and MTX precautions (avoid folic acid, NSAIDs, alcohol, sun exposure); contraception/sterilization counseling and future-fertility discussion; partner treatment and STI counseling for PID; confirm transportation and home support; structured transition-of-care handoff to outpatient gyn
expected_los: Predominantly ambulatory/23-hr observation; for true inpatient admissions CMS geometric-mean benchmarks run approximately 1.7-2.2 days for the without-CC/MCC DRGs (745/747/750), ~3-4.5 days for the with-CC/MCC DRGs (744/746/749), and ~1.5 days for cervical dilation (748); blended inpatient benchmark ~2-3 days (LOS figures are knowledge-based estimates — CMS Table 5 was inaccessible)
target_los: Under an ideal ERAS/MIS pathway: same-day discharge (0-1 day, 23-hr observation) for elective/uncomplicated cases; 1-2 days for uncomplicated emergent laparoscopy (ectopic, torsion); 2-3 days for TOA/septic abortion after source control and clinical response — roughly one full day below GMLOS for the CC/MCC tiers
quality_metrics: SCIP-derived perioperative measures — prophylactic antibiotic selection/timing (cefazolin <=60 min) and timely discontinuation; VTE prophylaxis ordered and received (PC-VTE); perioperative normothermia; NHSN surgical-site-infection rate; ERAS protocol compliance %; unplanned conversion-to-laparotomy rate; same-day-discharge rate for MIS; 30-day readmission and reoperation; unplanned ED return; PONV rate; opioid-prescribing/stewardship measures; catheter removal/CAUTI; methotrexate success rate (avoidance of unnecessary surgery); blood-transfusion rate; patient-reported outcome measures
common_complications: Surgical-site/pelvic infection and abscess (mitigate with appropriate prophylaxis, MIS, tissue handling); hemorrhage/hematoma requiring transfusion or reoperation (meticulous hemostasis, type & screen); visceral injury — bowel, bladder, ureter, or vessels — during laparoscopic entry/dissection (careful entry technique, ureteral identification, low threshold for cystoscopy); VTE (risk-based prophylaxis, early ambulation); PONV and ileus (multimodal antiemetics, opioid-sparing, early feeding); methotrexate failure with tubal rupture (strict hCG surveillance); conversion to laparotomy; anesthesia/cardiopulmonary events; retained products of conception after D&C
readmission_drivers: Post-op infection/TOA (ensure adequate source control plus full antibiotic course and follow-up); uncontrolled pain and N/V (robust multimodal plan and clear instructions); bleeding/hematoma; failed medical management of ectopic with subsequent rupture (surveillance hCG and explicit return precautions); missed visceral or ureteral injury presenting late (maintain suspicion, early imaging); VTE; dehydration after inadequate discharge teaching. Prevention levers: structured 48-72 h follow-up call/visit, reliable hCG tracking, teach-back on warning signs, ensured medication access, and a documented contraception plan
guideline_source: ERAS Society — Guidelines for Perioperative Care in Gynecologic/Oncology Surgery (2019; 2023 implementation update); ACOG Practice Bulletins — No. 193 Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy (2018), No. 174 Evaluation and Management of Adnexal Masses (2016), No. 195 Prevention of Infection After Gynecologic Procedures (2018), No. 232 Prevention of Venous Thromboembolism in Gynecologic Surgery (2021); CDC STI Treatment Guidelines 2021 (PID/tubo-ovarian abscess); AAGL minimally invasive gynecologic surgery guidance
key_citations: Via PubMed (attribution required): Nelson G et al. ERAS gynecologic/oncology guidelines-2019 update, Int J Gynecol Cancer 2019;29(4):651-668, PMID 30877144, https://doi.org/10.1136/ijgc-2019-000356; Nelson G et al. ERAS gynecologic oncology-2023 update, Gynecol Oncol 2023;173:58-67, PMID 37086524, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.ygyno.2023.04.009; ACOG PB 193 Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy, Obstet Gynecol 2018;131(3):e91-e103, PMID 29470343, https://doi.org/10.1097/AOG.0000000000002560; ACOG PB 174 Evaluation and Management of Adnexal Masses, Obstet Gynecol 2016;128(5):e210-e226, PMID 27776072, https://doi.org/10.1097/AOG.0000000000001768; ACOG PB 195 Prevention of Infection After Gynecologic Procedures, Obstet Gynecol 2018;131(6):e172-e189, PMID 29794678, https://doi.org/10.1097/AOG.0000000000002670; ACOG PB 232 Prevention of VTE in Gynecologic Surgery, Obstet Gynecol 2021;138(1):e1-e15, PMID 34259490, https://doi.org/10.1097/AOG.0000000000004445; Workowski KA et al. CDC STI Treatment Guidelines 2021, MMWR Recomm Rep 2021;70(4):1-187, PMID 34292926, https://doi.org/10.15585/mmwr.rr7004a1
evidence_grade: Moderate overall — Strong for individual components: antibiotic prophylaxis, VTE prophylaxis, tubal ectopic, and PID/TOA management rest on Class I / GRADE-strong society guidelines (ACOG, ERAS, CDC) supported by RCTs and meta-analyses. Weaker/consensus for the procedure-agnostic elements because this DRG group heterogeneously collapses many distinct benign procedures with no single unifying disease guideline — LOS targets, ovarian-torsion timing, and TOA size thresholds are extrapolated from cohort/observational data and expert consensus and are graded accordingly
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The odd/even DRG split is entirely CC/MCC-driven (744/746/749 with vs 745/747/750 without). Without CC/MCC = predominantly elective/ambulatory MIS, LOS ~1-2 days, floor-level care, low complication rate. With CC/MCC = comorbidity burden or acute complication (sepsis/TOA, hemorrhage/transfusion, visceral injury, cardiopulmonary disease, AKI, VTE) driving LOS ~3-4.5 days, higher acuity monitoring (step-down/ICU), and greater transfusion and readmission risk. An MCC (septic shock, PE, major hemorrhage, respiratory failure) shifts care toward the ICU and roughly doubles LOS. Emergent presentations (ruptured ectopic, torsion, TOA) more often carry CC/MCC and account for most inpatient bed-days in this otherwise outpatient-heavy family
pathway_pearls: 1) Right-site the encounter: the majority of these procedures belong in ambulatory/23-hr observation, not inpatient — reserve true inpatient status for hemodynamic instability, sepsis/TOA, or CC/MCC and apply the 2-midnight rule to prevent denials. 2) Minimally invasive route + ERAS is the LOS lever: laparoscopic/vaginal approach, opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia, PONV prophylaxis, early feeding/ambulation, and Foley out POD0 enable same-day discharge. 3) In reproductive-age acute pelvic pain always obtain a beta-hCG and TVUS first, do NOT delay OR for imaging in the unstable patient, expedite ectopic/torsion to surgery, and detorse-and-conserve a torsed ovary rather than reflexively removing a blue-black ovary. 4) Antibiotic and VTE stewardship: give cefazolin <=60 min only for vagina/bowel-entering cases (not clean laparoscopy or spontaneous-abortion D&C), risk-stratify VTE by Caprini, and for MTX-managed ectopic ensure strict weekly hCG surveillance with reliable follow-up

scope_and_volume_notes: D&C, conization, oophorectomy/adnexal, and other female reproductive OR procedures for non-malignant disease.

admission_criteria: Elective, scheduled admission for planned laparoscopic/robotic metabolic-bariatric surgery (MBS) meeting ASMBS/IFSO 2022 indications: BMI >=40, or BMI >=35 regardless of comorbidity, or BMI 30-34.9 with metabolic disease (esp. T2DM); Asian-population thresholds BMI >=27.5. Most primary sleeve/RYGB are 23-hour observation or same-day/short-stay. True inpatient (>=2-midnight) stay reserved for: revisional/complex or duodenal-switch/BPD procedures, super-obesity BMI >=60, severe OSA/OHS requiring monitored bed, significant cardiopulmonary disease, insulin-dependent diabetes needing titration, anticoagulation bridging, or an intra/early-postop complication (leak, bleed, arrhythmia). Post-discharge ED return with HR >120, fever, sustained pain, or inability to tolerate PO -> admit to rule out leak/VTE.
risk_stratification: OS-MRS (DeMaria) 0-5 pts: BMI>=50, male sex, HTN, age>=45, PE/VTE risk (prior thrombosis/PE, IVC filter, right-heart failure, obesity-hypoventilation) -> Class A 0-1 (~0.3% mortality), B 2-3 (~1.9%), C 4-5 (~7.6%); Class C -> consider tertiary center/ICU. Caprini VTE score drives chemoprophylaxis intensity and extended-duration decision. STOP-BANG >=3 screens OSA -> polysomnography/CPAP. Edmonton Obesity Staging System for comorbidity burden; ASA class; RCRI/METs for cardiac clearance; HbA1c for glycemic optimization; MBSAQIP risk calculator for morbidity/mortality/readmission estimate.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, CMP (glucose/renal/LFTs), HbA1c (optimize <7-8%), lipid panel; PT/INR + aPTT; type & screen; nutritional baseline (ferritin/iron studies, vitamin B12, folate, 25-OH vitamin D, thiamine, PTH/calcium, albumin/prealbumin); TSH; pregnancy test in women of childbearing age; H. pylori test-and-treat pre-op (lowers marginal-ulcer risk). Post-op: CBC to detect staple-line bleed; CMP + lactate if tachycardic/febrile to detect leak/sepsis. Abnormal baseline nutrition triggers repletion; anemia triggers iron/transfusion planning.
initial_imaging_dx: Pre-op: EGD or UGI contrast SELECTIVELY per symptoms/surgeon (not universally mandated by ERAS); RUQ ultrasound if biliary symptoms; 12-lead ECG all; TTE/stress testing if cardiac risk; polysomnography if STOP-BANG positive; CXR selective. Intra-op provocative leak test (intraoperative endoscopy, methylene-blue or air-leak test) at surgeon discretion. Post-op: routine contrast UGI swallow NOT recommended (selective only); CT abdomen/pelvis with oral+IV contrast is the study of choice when leak suspected (tachycardia/fever/pain); CT-angiography or lower-extremity duplex if PE/DVT suspected.
time_critical_interventions: Weight-based antibiotic prophylaxis (cefazolin 2-3 g, +metronidazole or alternative) within 60 min of incision; mechanical VTE prophylaxis (sequential compression devices) applied before induction plus pharmacologic dose per protocol; maintain normothermia; deliver the full ERAS bariatric bundle. Post-op RED-FLAG response: sustained tachycardia HR >120 is the earliest, most sensitive sign of anastomotic/staple-line leak -> immediate labs + CT or return to OR; never attribute persistent tachycardia to pain alone. Suspected leak -> emergent source control (re-operation/drainage); suspected PE -> anticoagulation + CTA without delay.
initial_management: Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia: scheduled acetaminophen, NSAID/ketorolac if no contraindication, local-anesthetic wound infiltration/TAP block, IV lidocaine and/or dexmedetomidine/ketamine adjuncts, gabapentinoid selective. Aggressive multi-agent PONV prophylaxis (dexamethasone + ondansetron + droperidol or aprepitant +/- scopolamine). Goal-directed near-zero-balance IV fluids (avoid overload). Early PPI (marginal-ulcer/reflux prophylaxis). VTE prophylaxis with LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg q12-24h or weight-adjusted/anti-Xa guided) or UFH plus SCDs. Glycemic control with insulin, target 140-180 mg/dL. NO routine nasogastric tube, NO routine abdominal drain, NO routine urinary catheter. Source control (re-op/drain/transfusion) for complications.
day1_milestones: POD0: complete laparoscopic/robotic sleeve or RYGB; extubate awake with head-of-bed elevated; regional/multimodal analgesia and PONV prophylaxis running; ambulate out of bed within 2-4 h of reaching floor/PACU; begin bariatric clear liquids/sips within hours; SCDs on + first LMWH dose given; incentive spirometry; resume home CPAP/BiPAP for OSA; remove Foley if one was placed. Screen low-risk sleeve patients for same-day discharge.
day2_milestones: POD1: advance to bariatric full liquids; confirm adequate PO hydration (goal ~48-64 oz/day sipping) and PO tolerance; pain controlled on oral/liquid analgesics; ambulating independently multiple times; afebrile with HR <100 and stable hemodynamics (no leak signs); voiding spontaneously; second LMWH dose; dietitian/nurse teaching completed. Most uncomplicated sleeve and RYGB patients DISCHARGE on POD1.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+: applies to CC/MCC tier or complicated course. Continue leak surveillance, advance staged bariatric diet, convert all medications to liquid/crushable/chewable forms, manage glycemia, treat ileus/nausea, ensure hydration. A stay extending to POD2+ usually signals a complication (leak, staple-line bleed, tachyarrhythmia, dehydration, respiratory issue) -> targeted workup (CT, CBC, lactate) and source control. Discharge once hemodynamically stable, tolerating PO, and hydrated.
consults_multidisciplinary: Pre-op MBSAQIP multidisciplinary pathway: registered dietitian (mandatory pre and post, lifelong supplementation plan), behavioral-health/psychology evaluation, bariatric medicine/endocrinology (diabetes, thyroid), anesthesiology (difficult-airway/OSA planning), cardiology and pulmonology/sleep medicine as indicated. Perioperative: pharmacy (VTE dosing, medication reconciliation to liquid/crushed forms), social work/case management (transport, home support, insurance/coverage), bariatric-trained nursing. PT/OT for super-obese mobility and safe-handling needs.
monitoring_level: Most primary cases: surgical floor or 23-hour observation unit with continuous pulse oximetry for OSA patients. Step-down/monitored bed for severe OSA/OHS on CPAP-BiPAP, significant cardiopulmonary disease, or super-super-obesity. ICU reserved for OS-MRS Class C, intra-op instability, respiratory failure, or leak/sepsis. Escalation triggers: sustained HR >120, SpO2 decline/hypoventilation, hypotension, oliguria, pain out of proportion, or rising lactate -> rapid-response activation plus leak/PE workup.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Staged bariatric diet: clears/sips POD0 -> full liquids POD1 -> pureed for 2-4 weeks; protein goal 60-80 g/day, hydration >48-64 oz/day; lifelong bariatric multivitamin + B12 + iron + calcium citrate + vitamin D (add thiamine if vomiting). Early mobilization within 2-4 h of surgery and frequently thereafter. VTE: SCDs + LMWH/UFH; extended post-discharge chemoprophylaxis (e.g., enoxaparin ~7-14 days, up to 2-4 weeks) for high Caprini/OS-MRS risk, prior VTE, or revisional surgery. Glucose target 140-180 mg/dL. Remove Foley/lines early. PPI prophylaxis (especially RYGB marginal ulcer).
discharge_criteria: Tolerating bariatric liquids with adequate PO hydration; pain controlled on oral analgesics; ambulating independently; afebrile and hemodynamically stable (HR <100, no sustained tachycardia); no signs of leak or bleeding; voiding; respiratory status stable at baseline O2/CPAP; patient able to manage supplementation and can teach-back red-flag symptoms. Same-day or POD1 discharge is appropriate for uncomplicated, selected low-risk patients.
discharge_planning: Disposition home for the vast majority. Medication reconciliation converting drugs to liquid/crushable/chewable forms, hold NSAIDs (especially after RYGB), start PPI, provide extended VTE-prophylaxis prescription if indicated. Detailed hydration/protein and staged-diet instructions; teach red flags (tachycardia, fever, worsening or sustained pain, inability to keep fluids down, calf pain/dyspnea) with 24/7 program contact. Structured nurse follow-up phone call at 24-72 h; surgeon visit at 1-2 weeks; dietitian and program follow-up; lifelong micronutrient-monitoring schedule; arrange transportation and home support.
expected_los: Approximately 1.5-2 days for uncomplicated cases (DRG 621 no CC/MCC GMLOS ~1.5-1.7 d; DRG 620 with CC ~2-2.5 d); DRG 619 with MCC often 5+ days. National MBSAQIP mean LOS for primary sleeve/RYGB is roughly 1.5-1.8 days.
target_los: Sleeve gastrectomy: same-day to POD1 (<=1 day) in selected low-risk patients under ERAS; Roux-en-Y gastric bypass: POD1 (1 day). Optimized best-practice target <=1 day for uncomplicated primary MBS.
quality_metrics: MBSAQIP accreditation standards and risk-adjusted registry benchmarking; 30-day readmission, reoperation, reintervention, and ED-visit rates; 30/90-day mortality; anastomotic/staple-line leak rate; VTE rate; surgical-site infection; ERAS protocol-compliance rate; OP-35/OP-36 (hospital admissions/ED visits after outpatient surgery); legacy SCIP process elements (timely prophylactic antibiotics, VTE prophylaxis, normothermia, perioperative glycemic control); patient-reported percent excess weight loss and comorbidity (T2DM/HTN/OSA) remission.
common_complications: Staple-line/anastomotic leak (~1-2%; sleeve ~1-3%) heralded by tachycardia -> early CT/re-operation; postoperative hemorrhage/staple-line bleed (~1-4%) -> restrictive transfusion, possible re-op; VTE/PE (leading cause of postop death, ~0.3-1%) -> chemoprophylaxis + early ambulation; PONV with dehydration -> multi-agent antiemetics + hydration coaching; marginal ulcer after RYGB (~1-5%) -> PPI, avoid NSAIDs, smoking cessation; strictures/stenosis; SSI; atelectasis/OSA-related hypoventilation -> CPAP + incentive spirometry; rhabdomyolysis in super-obese -> positioning, hydration, CK monitoring.
readmission_drivers: Dehydration with nausea/vomiting is the #1 (and largely preventable) driver -> hydration coaching, standing antiemetics, 24-72 h nurse call, outpatient IV-fluid pathway; abdominal-pain evaluations; anastomotic leak/intra-abdominal abscess; staple-line bleeding/anemia; VTE; marginal ulcer/GI bleed. Prevention levers: structured post-op education with teach-back, early scheduled clinic follow-up, low threshold for outpatient rehydration to avert admission, and clear 24/7 escalation contact.
guideline_source: ERAS Society, "Guidelines for Perioperative Care in Bariatric Surgery: Enhanced Recovery After Surgery (ERAS) Society Recommendations: A 2021 Update" (Stenberg et al., World J Surg 2022); ASMBS/IFSO 2022 Indications for Metabolic and Bariatric Surgery (Eisenberg et al., 2022); ASMBS Updated Position Statement on Perioperative Venous Thromboembolism Prophylaxis in Bariatric Surgery (Aminian et al., 2022); MBSAQIP (ACS/ASMBS) Accreditation Standards.
key_citations: Stenberg E et al. ERAS bariatric 2021 update. World J Surg 2022;46(4):729-751; PMID 34984504; DOI 10.1007/s00268-021-06394-9. Eisenberg D et al. 2022 ASMBS/IFSO indications for MBS. Obes Surg 2023;33(1):3-14 (also Surg Obes Relat Dis 2022); PMID 36336720; DOI 10.1007/s11695-022-06332-1. Aminian A et al. ASMBS updated position statement on perioperative VTE prophylaxis. Surg Obes Relat Dis 2022;18(2):165-174; PMID 34896011; DOI 10.1016/j.soard.2021.10.023. DeMaria EJ et al. Obesity Surgery Mortality Risk Score (OS-MRS). Surg Obes Relat Dis 2007;3(2):134-140; PMID 17386394; DOI 10.1016/j.soard.2007.01.005. (Attribution: PubMed.)
evidence_grade: Strong for patient selection/indications (ASMBS/IFSO 2022 society guideline) and for the ERAS multimodal framework overall; MODERATE for many individual bariatric-specific ERAS items, which the ERAS 2021 update explicitly grades as low-to-moderate quality (GRADE), often extrapolated from colorectal surgery. Overall: strong society-guideline backbone with moderate component-level evidence.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 621 (no CC/MCC): the same-day/POD1 ERAS target population; floor care; LOS ~1-1.7 d. DRG 620 (CC, e.g., insulin-dependent diabetes, controlled comorbidity, or minor complication): LOS ~2-2.5 d with more monitoring and glycemic/comorbidity management. DRG 619 (MCC, e.g., leak/sepsis, PE, respiratory failure, transfusion-requiring bleed, AKI): drives ICU/step-down care, reoperation/source control, LOS 5+ days, and materially higher mortality; OS-MRS Class C and super-obesity concentrate in this tier.
pathway_pearls: 1) Treat sustained tachycardia HR >120 as a leak or PE until proven otherwise -- it is the earliest, most sensitive sign; mandate a CT/return-to-OR pathway and never blame pain alone. 2) The enabling engine of same-day/POD1 discharge is opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia + aggressive multi-agent PONV prophylaxis + early ambulation + early oral hydration. 3) Dehydration is the top preventable readmission driver -- hard-wire hydration coaching, standing antiemetics, a 24-72 h nurse call, and an outpatient IV-fluid safety valve. 4) De-medicalize routine care: skip routine NGT, drains, Foley, and routine contrast leak studies, but give mechanical + pharmacologic (+/- extended) VTE prophylaxis and weight-based pre-incision antibiotics every single case.
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: OR procedures for obesity (sleeve gastrectomy, gastric bypass). Increasingly short-stay/outpatient.

mdc: 11/12 — Kidney & Urinary Tract / Male Reproductive System
admission_criteria: Mostly ELECTIVE surgical admissions: planned inpatient stay for radical prostatectomy/simple prostatectomy (DRG 707-708) or TURP/laser enucleation (DRG 668-670) requiring post-op continuous bladder irrigation (CBI) or comorbidity monitoring; modern robotic radical prostatectomy (RARP) and small-gland TURP increasingly same-day/23h observation. Objective INPATIENT indications for URGENT/EMERGENT BPH admission: acute urinary retention (AUR) refractory to >=2 trials-without-catheter (TWOC) on alpha-blocker; gross hematuria with clot retention needing CBI/clot evacuation; obstructive uropathy with post-renal AKI (rising creatinine, hydronephrosis on US); febrile UTI/urosepsis with obstruction (needs decompression + inpatient IV antibiotics); bladder calculi; obstruction-related renal insufficiency. OBSERVATION/DISCHARGE instead: uncomplicated AUR decompressed with catheter + alpha-blocker for outpatient TWOC; stable non-obstructing microscopic/light hematuria; ASA I-II uncomplicated TURP tolerating void
risk_stratification: IPSS (International Prostate Symptom Score) 0-7 mild / 8-19 moderate / 20-35 severe; IPSS >=8 with bother + QoL index (0-6) drives escalation to procedure. Qmax (peak uroflow) <10 mL/s = obstruction (10-15 equivocal); PVR >150-300 mL flags retention risk. Prostate volume (TRUS/MRI): <30, 30-80, >80 mL selects the procedure. AUA/NCCN prostate-cancer risk groups drive surgery vs surveillance: Low (PSA<10, Grade Group1/Gleason6, cT1-2a) favors active surveillance; Intermediate (PSA10-20 or GG2-3 or cT2b-c; favorable vs unfavorable) and High (PSA>20 or GG4-5 or >=cT3) favor RP or radiation, with PLND if nomogram LN risk >2-7%. Perioperative risk: ASA class, RCRI (Revised Cardiac Risk Index), STOP-BANG (OSA); Caprini score for VTE prophylaxis; qSOFA>=2/SOFA + lactate if urosepsis suspected
initial_workup_labs: Urinalysis + urine culture FIRST (must sterilize urine before any instrumentation - triggers targeted antibiotics, delays elective surgery if positive); CBC (baseline Hgb for hematuria/transfusion planning; leukocytosis in urosepsis); BMP/creatinine + eGFR (post-renal AKI, obstructive uropathy, drug dosing); PT/INR-aPTT (especially if anticoagulated - triggers hold/bridge plan); type & screen (TURP/prostatectomy bleeding risk); PSA (baseline; do NOT draw during acute retention/UTI - falsely elevated); glucose/HbA1c (perioperative control); for cancer confirm biopsy Grade Group/Gleason and pre-op PSA. Lactate + blood cultures if febrile/septic
initial_imaging_dx: Pre-op: uroflowmetry + PVR; cystoscopy (define anatomy, median lobe, exclude bladder tumor/stone); TRUS or prostate MRI for volume (drives procedure choice). Renal/bladder ultrasound if obstructive picture or elevated creatinine (hydronephrosis); CT urogram for gross hematuria (exclude upper-tract source). Prostate-cancer staging: multiparametric prostate MRI (PI-RADS) for local staging; for unfavorable-intermediate/high-risk, PSMA PET/CT (has largely supplanted bone scan + CT abd/pelvis for detecting nodal/metastatic disease) per current guidance. Urodynamics reserved for neurogenic/equivocal or prior failed surgery
time_critical_interventions: Obstructed + infected upper/lower tract (urosepsis): EMERGENT decompression (urethral or suprapubic catheter, or ureteral stent/percutaneous nephrostomy for upper tract) PLUS broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour (Surviving Sepsis) - source control is life-saving. Acute urinary retention: immediate bladder decompression. Gross hematuria with clot retention: urgent 3-way catheter, manual clot evacuation, start CBI. Surgical antibiotic prophylaxis: single weight-based dose within 60 min before incision (SCIP/ASHP - e.g., cephalosporin, or culture-directed), discontinue <=24h. Intra-op TUR-syndrome vigilance: limit resection time (<60-90 min), monitor for hyponatremia (bipolar/saline resection largely eliminates risk)
initial_management: Alpha-blockers (tamsulosin, silodosin, alfuzosin) for LUTS and to facilitate TWOC after AUR; 5-alpha-reductase inhibitors (dutasteride, finasteride) for glands >30-40 mL to shrink volume/reduce hematuria and progression, and pre-op to reduce prostate vascularity/bleeding; combination alpha-blocker + 5ARI for large symptomatic glands; storage-symptom agents (antimuscarinic or mirabegron/beta-3 agonist) if no high PVR. AUR: catheter decompression + alpha-blocker, TWOC in 3-7 days. Infection: culture-directed antibiotics; empiric broad-spectrum (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone) within 1h for urosepsis. RESTRICTIVE transfusion threshold Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if cardiac disease). Normal-saline CBI for post-op/clot hematuria. Anticoagulation: hold DOAC/clopidogrel per bleeding risk, low-dose ASA often continued; reversal for active bleed - vitamin K + 4F-PCC (warfarin), idarucizumab (dabigatran), andexanet/4F-PCC (factor Xa inhibitors). Source control = definitive endoscopic resection/enucleation
day1_milestones: POD0 (surgery day): confirm sterile urine culture, VTE risk assessment + mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs), antibiotic prophylaxis <60 min pre-incision, no bowel prep, ERAS carbohydrate loading. Intra-op: bipolar/saline TURP or anatomic enucleation (HoLEP/ThuLEP) for BPH, or nerve-sparing RARP +/- risk-appropriate pelvic lymph node dissection for cancer; minimize blood loss. POD0 recovery: Foley to gravity, CBI running if TURP/enucleation; early same-day ambulation; multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (acetaminophen/NSAID, regional/TAP block); early oral intake per ERAS; antiemetics; monitor for TUR syndrome (confusion, hyponatremia, bradycardia) after monopolar resection
day2_milestones: POD1 (frequently discharge day): TURP/HoLEP - wean CBI as effluent clears to rose/clear, remove catheter and perform trial of void, or discharge with catheter + leg bag; confirm spontaneous voiding without clot, ambulating, tolerating regular diet, pain controlled on oral analgesia; recheck Hgb/Na if clinically indicated. RARP - discharge POD1 is the benchmark (same-day increasingly standard); Foley remains 5-10 days (routine cystogram not required), confirm ambulation, return of flatus/bowel function, oral pain control, and teach catheter/leg-bag care. Discontinue IV fluids and transition all meds to oral
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ reserved for complicated (CC/MCC) courses: persistent hematuria/clot retention needing ongoing CBI or transfusion, TUR syndrome/symptomatic hyponatremia correction, postoperative ileus, UTI/urosepsis, cardiac event, or transfusion. RARP rarely exceeds POD1-2 unless ileus, anastomotic urine leak, or symptomatic lymphocele. Interventions: continued CBI with staged catheter removal, treat infection/electrolytes, PT for deconditioning, manage complications, then meet discharge-readiness criteria. Most uncomplicated patients are already home by this point
consults_multidisciplinary: Urology (primary surgeon). Anesthesia/pre-op optimization clinic (ASA, OSA, airway). For cancer: multidisciplinary GU tumor board (medical + radiation oncology), genetic counseling if high-risk/family history. Cardiology for cardiac-risk and antiplatelet/anticoagulation management; Nephrology if obstructive AKI; Infectious Disease for resistant/complex urosepsis. Pharmacy (anticoagulation bridging, antimicrobial stewardship, med reconciliation). Care management + social work early for disposition, catheter supplies, home health. PT/OT for elderly/deconditioned; pelvic-floor physical therapy + continence nurse for post-prostatectomy continence; Nutrition if malnourished
monitoring_level: Floor/med-surg for the majority; telemetry if significant cardiac disease or therapeutic anticoagulation. Step-down/ICU for: TUR syndrome with severe/symptomatic hyponatremia (Na <125 or seizure), hemorrhagic shock/massive transfusion, urosepsis with septic shock, or major cardiac/respiratory event. Escalation triggers: hemodynamic instability, Na <125 mmol/L or acute mental-status change, ongoing transfusion requirement (>2 units), rising lactate/qSOFA>=2, respiratory compromise (OSA/oversedation), or brisk unrelenting hematuria despite CBI
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: ERAS early oral intake POD0, advance to regular diet as tolerated, no routine NGT. Mobility: ambulate the evening of surgery (POD0), progressive same-day mobilization. VTE prophylaxis: mechanical (SCDs) universal + early ambulation; pharmacologic (LMWH/UFH) risk-based by Caprini - radical prostatectomy is high VTE-risk pelvic oncologic surgery so consider extended prophylaxis up to ~28 days for high-risk (Caprini>=5, PLND, prior VTE); for TURP/enucleation pharmacologic prophylaxis is usually withheld for bleeding risk (mechanical + mobilization). Glucose target <180 mg/dL. Catheter: Foley to gravity, CBI as needed; remove early per protocol (TURP catheter ~1-3 days, RARP ~5-10 days); remove IV lines promptly to reduce CAUTI/CLABSI
discharge_criteria: Voiding adequately after catheter removal OR discharged with catheter and demonstrating competent catheter/leg-bag care; urine clear to light-pink without obstructing clots and no active hematuria requiring CBI; afebrile and hemodynamically stable; stable Hgb without ongoing transfusion; normal/stabilized serum sodium (post-TURP); pain controlled on oral analgesics; tolerating diet with returning bowel function and ambulating at baseline; patient/caregiver understands red-flag return precautions (clot retention, inability to void, fever, heavy bleeding)
discharge_planning: Disposition home for the vast majority; arrange home health for catheter care when discharged with Foley. Medication reconciliation: resume/adjust anticoagulation with clear restart date, continue alpha-blocker as indicated, add stool softener/laxative, antibiotics only if culture-driven, deprescribe as appropriate. Provide catheter supplies + leg bag; schedule catheter removal/TWOC (RARP ~POD 5-10) and urology follow-up in 1-2 weeks for pathology review; PSA follow-up ~6-12 weeks post-radical prostatectomy then per surveillance schedule. Education: activity restriction (no heavy lifting/driving on narcotics), expect transient dysuria/urgency/hematuria after TURP with SECONDARY-HEMORRHAGE risk POD 7-21, pelvic-floor exercises for continence and erectile-rehab counseling after RP. Explicit transitions-of-care return precautions and clinic/nurse call-back number
expected_los: Blended GMLOS across the collapsed group approx 1.5-3 days (CMS FY2025 benchmarks, approximate): DRG 670 TURP w/o CC/MCC ~1.6 d; 669 TURP w/ CC ~2.6 d; 668 TURP w/ MCC ~4.5 d; 708 major male pelvic w/o CC/MCC ~1.5 d; 707 major male pelvic w/ CC/MCC ~2.7 d. Uncomplicated TURP/HoLEP and robotic radical prostatectomy typically 1-2 days
target_los: ~1 day for uncomplicated cases under the ideal pathway: RARP same-day or POD1 discharge is the modern best-practice benchmark; small/moderate-gland TURP and many enucleations discharge at 0-1 day (outpatient/23h observation); target <=2 days overall for non-complicated admissions
quality_metrics: Surgical antimicrobial prophylaxis timing <60 min, appropriate selection, and discontinuation <=24h (SCIP/ASHP); VTE prophylaxis compliance (VTE-1/-2); CAUTI rate (NHSN); surgical site infection; 30-day all-cause and procedure-specific (hematuria/retention/UTI) readmission; unplanned return to OR; blood-transfusion rate (TURP); ERAS protocol compliance; same-day-discharge rate for RARP (efficiency). Cancer registry/PRO outcomes: positive surgical margin rate, 12-month urinary continence and erectile-function recovery (patient-reported), and biochemical (PSA) recurrence. In-hospital mortality (low)
common_complications: Bleeding/hematuria with clot retention requiring CBI or transfusion (TURP ~2-5%, higher for large glands) - mitigated by bipolar/saline resection, anatomic enucleation, pre-op 5ARI, meticulous hemostasis. TUR syndrome/dilutional hyponatremia (<1-2% monopolar, near-zero with bipolar/saline). UTI/urosepsis - mitigated by pre-op urine sterilization + prophylaxis. Post-removal urinary retention. Retrograde ejaculation (~65-75% after TURP). Delayed urethral stricture/bladder-neck contracture. RARP-specific: anastomotic urine leak, lymphocele (with PLND), ileus, VTE/PE, rare rectal injury, positive margins, and functional urinary incontinence/erectile dysfunction - mitigated by nerve-sparing, limited risk-based PLND, ERAS, early pelvic-floor PT
readmission_drivers: Secondary/delayed hemorrhage from prostatic-fossa eschar sloughing (POD 7-21 after TURP) and clot retention; urinary retention after catheter removal; UTI/urosepsis; catheter blockage or dislodgement; after RARP - anastomotic leak, symptomatic lymphocele, VTE/PE, ileus. Prevention levers: thorough intra-op hemostasis and consideration of enucleation for large glands, treat bacteriuria before surgery + prophylaxis, alpha-blocker and stool softeners, structured catheter teaching, early (1-2 week) urology follow-up with TWOC, risk-based extended VTE prophylaxis, explicit written return precautions, and nurse/telehealth check-in call
guideline_source: PRIMARY: American Urological Association (AUA) Guideline 2026 - Management of Lower Urinary Tract Symptoms Attributed to Benign Prostatic Hyperplasia, Parts I-III (supersedes 2021 guideline / 2023 amendment). AUA/ASTRO Clinically Localized Prostate Cancer Guideline 2022 (amended 2026). SUPPORTING: ERAS Society perioperative-care guidance for urologic/major surgery; NCCN Prostate Cancer Guidelines; AUA/SUNA antimicrobial-prophylaxis and catheter best-practice; EAU (European Association of Urology) BPH/LUTS and Prostate Cancer guidelines
key_citations: AUA BPH Guideline 2026 Part III Procedural/Surgical Management, J Urol 2026;216(2):161-170, PMID 42095468, https://doi.org/10.1097/JU.0000000000005099; Part I Presentation/Evaluation PMID 42095481 (https://doi.org/10.1097/JU.0000000000005097) and Part II Medical Management PMID 42095477 (https://doi.org/10.1097/JU.0000000000005098); AUA BPH Guideline Amendment 2023, J Urol 2024;211(1):11-19, PMID 37706750, https://doi.org/10.1097/JU.0000000000003698; AUA/ASTRO Clinically Localized Prostate Cancer Guideline Part I, J Urol 2022;208(1):10-18, PMID 35536144, https://doi.org/10.1097/JU.0000000000002757; ProtecT 15-yr outcomes Hamdy et al., NEJM 2023;388:1547-1558, PMID 36912538, https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa2214122; ProtecT 10-yr Hamdy et al., NEJM 2016;375:1415-1424, PMID 27626136, https://doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1606220; LAPPRO robotic vs open RP Haglind et al., Eur Urol 2015;68:216-225, PMID 25770484, https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eururo.2015.02.029; HoLEP vs TURP meta-analysis Tan et al., Br J Surg 2007;94:1201-8, PMID 17729384, https://doi.org/10.1002/bjs.5916. Citations verified via PubMed
evidence_grade: Strong - anchored by Class I/high-grade society guidelines (AUA 2026 BPH, AUA/ASTRO 2022 localized prostate cancer) with individual BPH surgical statements rated Strong/Moderate (Grade A/B) and reinforced by RCT/meta-analytic evidence (ProtecT RCT for cancer management, LAPPRO for robotic vs open RP, multiple HoLEP-vs-TURP meta-analyses). Perioperative ERAS and same-day-discharge elements are Moderate; extended-VTE-prophylaxis duration in prostatectomy is Moderate/extrapolated from pelvic-oncology data
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 670/708 (no CC/MCC): uncomplicated endoscopic or robotic surgery, floor care, LOS ~1-1.5 d, high same-day/23h-observation eligibility. DRG 669/707 (CC): e.g., post-op hematuria needing transfusion, UTI, AKI, or moderate comorbidity; telemetry, LOS ~2.5-3 d. DRG 668 (TURP with MCC): TUR syndrome/severe hyponatremia, urosepsis/septic shock, hemorrhagic shock/massive transfusion, acute MI, AKI needing dialysis, or PE -> step-down/ICU, LOS 4.5+ d. An MCC roughly doubles-to-triples LOS and cost and drives ICU utilization; radical-prostatectomy CC/MCC courses are most often ileus, VTE/PE, transfusion, or anastomotic/lymphocele complications
pathway_pearls: 1) Sterilize the urine BEFORE instrumentation - obtain/act on urine culture and never operate through untreated infection (prevents urosepsis, the top MCC driver). 2) Default to bipolar/saline TURP or anatomic endoscopic enucleation (HoLEP/ThuLEP) - eliminates TUR syndrome, cuts transfusion, and enucleation is size-independent and the durable choice for glands >80 mL (lower retreatment); pre-op 5ARI reduces vascularity/bleeding. 3) RARP benchmark = same-day/POD1 discharge with catheter out ~5-10 days; limit PLND to higher-risk patients to reduce lymphocele/VTE, and drive functional recovery with early pelvic-floor PT. 4) Counsel every TURP patient on delayed secondary hemorrhage (POD 7-21) and retrograde ejaculation, and give explicit return precautions to prevent bounce-back
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Transurethral (668-670) and major pelvic/radical prostatectomy (707-708). BPH and prostate cancer.
