admission_criteria: All open aortic and endovascular (EVAR/TEVAR) repairs are inpatient surgical admissions (select uncomplicated infrarenal EVAR trending to 23-hr observation). Elective repair indicated for: infrarenal AAA >=5.5 cm men or >=5.0 cm women; ascending TAA >=5.5 cm (>=5.0 cm at experienced multidisciplinary aortic center or Marfan >=5.0/Loeys-Dietz >=4.5 cm); descending TAA >=5.5 cm; rapid expansion >=0.5 cm/yr (or >=0.3 cm/yr x2 yr); saccular/symptomatic morphology. Emergent: ruptured AAA (hypotension + back/abdominal pain + pulsatile mass) -> direct to OR; symptomatic/tender non-ruptured AAA -> urgent admission; acute aortic syndrome (dissection, intramural hematoma, penetrating ulcer). NOT admission: asymptomatic AAA below threshold -> outpatient surveillance
risk_stratification: SVS-endorsed VQI mortality risk score for shared decision-making before elective repair; Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI/Lee) >=2 -> cardiology eval + perioperative optimization; functional capacity <4 METs -> stress testing per ACC/AHA perioperative; ACS-NSQIP surgical risk calculator; frailty screen (mFI-5, Clinical Frailty Scale >=5) predicts morbidity/non-home discharge; ruptured AAA -> Hardman Index >=3 of 5 (age >76, Cr >2.15 mg/dL [>190 umol/L], Hgb <9 g/dL, loss of consciousness, ECG ischemia) predicts near-100% mortality; Glasgow Aneurysm Score for rupture prognosis; CTA-defined anatomic suitability (infrarenal neck >=10-15 mm length, angulation <60 deg, adequate iliac access) drives EVAR vs open
initial_workup_labs: CBC (baseline Hgb/plt); CMP (creatinine/eGFR for contrast-nephropathy risk + baseline renal); coagulation (PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen); type & crossmatch (>=4-6 units PRBC open, >=2 EVAR; activate massive transfusion protocol for rupture); lactate (elevation -> rupture/malperfusion/shock); baseline high-sensitivity troponin + ECG (perioperative MINS surveillance); ABG if unstable; BNP for cardiac risk; HbA1c/glucose; group & save; urinalysis. Triggers: Cr elevation -> nephroprotection/iso-osmolar contrast minimization; anemia -> optimize/transfuse; lactate >2-4 -> resuscitation and urgent OR
initial_imaging_dx: CT angiography (CTA) chest/abdomen/pelvis is gold standard for diagnosis, sizing, and operative planning -> STAT in suspected rupture (non-contrast + arterial phase; do NOT delay OR in unstable patient with known AAA); duplex ultrasound for screening/surveillance (one-time USPSTF screen men 65-75 ever-smokers); TTE + coronary/functional evaluation pre-op for elective per cardiac risk; post-EVAR surveillance CTA at 1 month then color duplex ultrasound (SVS: favor duplex if no endoleak/expansion); bedside ED ultrasound (aorta/FAST) in the unstable patient
time_critical_interventions: Ruptured AAA: door-to-intervention <90 min via 30-30-30 framework (<=30 min diagnosis/CTA, <=30 min transfer + anesthesia prep, <=30 min to proximal aortic control); EVAR preferred if anatomically feasible (IMPROVE); permissive/controlled hypotension target SBP ~70-90 mmHg (maintain consciousness/mentation, avoid over-resuscitation); activate balanced 1:1:1 massive transfusion protocol, consider TXA; aortic occlusion balloon for peri-arrest proximal control. Elective: antibiotic prophylaxis (cefazolin) within 60 min of incision; systemic heparin before aortic cross-clamp (protamine reversal); maintain normothermia >36 C. Postop troponin surveillance POD0-2 for MINS
initial_management: Elective pre-op optimization: moderate-high intensity statin (atorvastatin 40-80 mg), continue chronic beta-blocker (metoprolol; do NOT initiate high-dose acutely - POISE harm), continue aspirin 81 mg, BP control <130/80, smoking cessation >=4 wk, euvolemia, carbohydrate loading/avoid prolonged fasting. Acute aortic syndrome/dissection: IV beta-blocker first (esmolol/labetalol) targeting HR <60 + SBP 100-120, then vasodilator (nicardipine) after rate control, analgesia. Intraop: cefazolin, heparin/protamine, cell salvage, goal-directed fluid therapy, lung-protective ventilation. Transfusion: restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 with active cardiac disease) when stable; 1:1:1 in active hemorrhage; reverse anticoagulation (protamine for heparin; PCC/idarucizumab/andexanet for DOACs) before emergent surgery. Source control = definitive aortic repair (EVAR or open graft)
day1_milestones: POD0. Open repair: ICU admission with arterial line + central access, goal early extubation, epidural/regional analgesia + multimodal (acetaminophen, opioid-sparing), normothermia, maintain MAP/end-organ perfusion, urine output >0.5 mL/kg/h, serial Hgb/lactate, distal pulse/limb-perfusion checks (detect embolization/limb ischemia), troponin, NG only if ileus/distension, glucose <180. EVAR: PACU then telemetry/floor, groin access-site checks for hematoma/retroperitoneal bleed, distal pulse checks, early ambulation same evening, resume diet, monitor renal function post-contrast
day2_milestones: POD1. Open: confirm extubated, wean vasopressors, out of bed/mobilize with PT, continue epidural analgesia, assess return of bowel function, remove Foley early when appropriate, initiate VTE prophylaxis once hemostasis secure, transfer ICU -> step-down if stable, trend renal function/troponin, monitor for colonic ischemia (bloody diarrhea -> flexible sigmoidoscopy) and spinal cord ischemia (thoracoabdominal -> CSF drain + MAP augmentation). EVAR: discharge if hemodynamically stable, ambulating, tolerating diet, groin stable, pain controlled (many discharge POD1); arrange baseline/30-day surveillance CTA
day3plus_milestones: POD2+. Open: transfer to surgical floor, advance diet as ileus resolves (typically POD3-5), escalate mobilization, transition epidural to oral multimodal analgesia, sequentially remove lines/drains, monitor wound, target discharge readiness POD4-6. Ongoing vigilance for colonic ischemia, spinal cord ischemia, surgical-site infection, AKI, and cardiopulmonary events. Reassess disposition (home vs rehab) driven by frailty/functional status. EVAR patients typically already discharged; complex fenestrated/branched or TEVAR-with-CSF-drain cases follow the open-repair trajectory
consults_multidisciplinary: Vascular surgery (primary); cardiology/pre-op medicine for RCRI-driven perioperative optimization; anesthesiology/pre-anesthesia clinic; critical care for ICU management; nephrology if AKI/CKD/contrast nephropathy; PT/OT early (mobility + discharge disposition); nutrition (pre-op optimization, post-op ileus/feeding); pharmacy (antiplatelet/anticoagulation, statin, beta-blocker reconciliation); care management/social work (disposition, transitions); palliative care/goals-of-care for high-risk ruptured or futility discussions; formal multidisciplinary aortic team for complex thoracoabdominal/branched-fenestrated cases (ACC/AHA 2022 emphasis)
monitoring_level: Open aortic and complex/thoracoabdominal repairs -> ICU postop (arterial line, CVP, ~1-2 days) then step-down/telemetry; suprarenal/supraceliac clamp, large blood loss, or instability -> mandatory ICU. Uncomplicated infrarenal EVAR -> PACU then telemetry/surgical floor (ICU not routinely required); complex fenestrated/branched EVAR or TEVAR with spinal drain -> step-down/ICU. Escalation triggers: hemodynamic instability/ongoing bleeding, oliguria/AKI, new limb ischemia or pulse deficit, lower-extremity weakness (spinal cord ischemia), abdominal distension/bladder pressure >20 mmHg (abdominal compartment syndrome), rising lactate, troponin rise/ischemic ECG, respiratory failure
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: EVAR resume diet POD0; open advance as ileus resolves (clears -> regular POD3-5), NG only for distension, early oral nutrition per ERAS, avoid prolonged fasting, pre-op carbohydrate loading. Mobility: early mobilization POD0 (EVAR)/POD1 (open) with PT, out of bed to chair, progressive ambulation. VTE: combined mechanical (SCDs) + pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin or UFH) once surgical hemostasis assured, Caprini-stratified. Glucose: target <180 mg/dL on insulin protocol. Lines/catheters: remove arterial/central lines and Foley early (POD1-2) to reduce CLABSI/CAUTI; ongoing groin access-site vigilance for EVAR
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable off vasopressors; pain controlled on oral analgesics; afebrile with no active infection; tolerating oral diet with return of bowel function; ambulating at/near baseline; renal function stable or improving with adequate urine output; incision (laparotomy or groin access) healing without infection, hematoma, or pseudoaneurysm; distal perfusion intact; surveillance imaging and follow-up arranged; patient/caregiver education completed
discharge_planning: Disposition home (majority) vs skilled rehab (frail/deconditioned/open repair); medication reconciliation (antiplatelet - aspirin +/- clopidogrel per device/graft, moderate-high intensity statin, BP control, resume/optimize beta-blocker, glycemic agents); structured surveillance - post-EVAR CTA at 1 month then duplex ultrasound (annual if stable), open repair less intensive; vascular clinic follow-up 2-4 weeks with wound check; education on activity restriction (no heavy lifting 4-6 wk), incision care, smoking cessation, and RED-FLAG symptoms (new/severe back-abdominal pain, groin swelling/bleeding, fever, cold/painful leg = endoleak/rupture/limb-ischemia warnings); transitions-of-care handoff to PCP; enroll in risk-factor modification
expected_los: Highly procedure- and severity-dependent across the collapsed DRGs. Elective infrarenal EVAR without MCC (DRG 269-like): ~1-3 days (GMLOS ~2-3). Elective open AAA repair: ~5-8 days with 1-2 ICU days (DRG 237/268 with MCC GMLOS ~7 days). Complex thoracoabdominal/branched or MCC-level cases: 8-14+ days. Ruptured AAA: markedly longer with prolonged ICU. Blended group GMLOS roughly 4-7 days
target_los: Under ideal ERAS pathway: uncomplicated infrarenal EVAR <=1-2 days (select next-day/23-hr discharge); elective open AAA repair 4-5 days; complex open/thoracoabdominal 6-8 days. Reductions driven by goal-directed fluid therapy, early extubation, epidural analgesia, early mobilization/feeding, and early line/catheter removal
quality_metrics: SVS Vascular Quality Initiative (VQI) registry participation + risk-adjusted outcomes (30-day mortality, reintervention, post-EVAR surveillance completion); 30-day mortality for elective repair (benchmark EVAR <=2%, open <5%); institutional/operator volume thresholds (>=10 EVAR and >=10 open aortic cases/yr); ruptured AAA door-to-intervention <90 min; SSI/SCIP measures (antibiotic timing/selection, normothermia, glucose); VTE prophylaxis (VTE-1/VTE-2); ERAS protocol compliance; 30-day readmission rate; AHRQ PSI-90 (postop AKI, respiratory failure, hemorrhage); CMS outcome/mortality measures
common_complications: Cardiac (perioperative MI/MINS -> statin, beta-blocker continuation, troponin surveillance); respiratory failure/pneumonia (early extubation, pulmonary toilet, mobilization); AKI/contrast nephropathy (hydration, minimize/iso-osmolar contrast, avoid nephrotoxins); bleeding/anemia; ileus (ERAS, early feeding, mobility); colonic/mesenteric ischemia after IMA ligation (monitor bloody diarrhea -> sigmoidoscopy); spinal cord ischemia/paraplegia in thoracoabdominal repair (CSF drainage, MAP augmentation, staged repair); limb ischemia/distal embolization (trash foot); abdominal compartment syndrome (bladder-pressure monitoring, decompression); EVAR-specific - endoleak (treat type I/III and expanding type II, surveil stable type II), access-site hematoma/pseudoaneurysm, post-implantation syndrome, graft-limb occlusion; late graft infection
readmission_drivers: Wound/access-site complications + surgical-site infection (groin for EVAR, laparotomy for open) -> wound surveillance, sterile technique, early clinic wound check; endoleak/sac expansion/graft issues -> structured CTA/duplex surveillance + reintervention; cardiac/pulmonary events -> risk-factor optimization + cardiac rehab; AKI/renal failure; ileus/failure-to-thrive/dehydration -> nutrition + follow-up; uncontrolled pain. Prevention levers: 30-day surveillance imaging, early vascular clinic follow-up (2-4 wk), medication adherence (antiplatelet/statin), smoking cessation, and explicit red-flag education
guideline_source: Society for Vascular Surgery (SVS) 2018 - Practice Guidelines on the Care of Patients with an Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (Chaikof et al.); 2022 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Diagnosis and Management of Aortic Disease (Isselbacher et al.); ERAS Society + SVS 2022 Consensus - Perioperative Care in Open Aortic Vascular Surgery (McGinigle et al.). Supported by ESVS 2019/2024 AAA guidelines
key_citations: Chaikof EL et al. SVS practice guidelines on the care of patients with AAA. J Vasc Surg 2018;67:2-77 (PMID 29268916, DOI 10.1016/j.jvs.2017.10.044). Isselbacher EM et al. 2022 ACC/AHA aortic disease guideline. Circulation 2022;146:e334-e482 (PMID 36322642, DOI 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001106). McGinigle KL et al. Perioperative care in open aortic vascular surgery: ERAS/SVS consensus. J Vasc Surg 2022;75:1796-1820 (PMID 35181517, DOI 10.1016/j.jvs.2022.01.131). Landmark RCTs: Patel R et al. EVAR trial 1 15-yr, Lancet 2016;388:2366-74 (PMID 27743617) - early EVAR survival benefit lost with late sac-rupture risk mandating lifelong surveillance; IMPROVE trial 3-yr, BMJ 2017;359:j4859 (PMID 29138135) - endovascular-first strategy for ruptured AAA improves 3-yr survival and is cost-effective; DREAM and OVER trials show long-term EVAR-vs-open equivalence (per PubMed)
evidence_grade: Strong - anchored by multiple current Class I society guidelines (SVS 2018, ACC/AHA 2022) plus high-quality RCT evidence (EVAR-1, DREAM, OVER, IMPROVE) for diameter thresholds, EVAR-vs-open selection, and ruptured management. Perioperative ERAS elements are Moderate/GRADE-based consensus (ERAS-SVS 2022). Ruptured EVAR-first is a strong recommendation resting on lower-quality evidence per SVS
severity_cc_mcc_notes: CC/MCC tier is the dominant driver of LOS, cost, and mortality across the collapsed group. Without MCC (DRG 269/238/272) - typically elective EVAR or uncomplicated procedures, GMLOS ~2-4 days, low ICU need. With CC/MCC (DRG 268/270/271/237) - acute renal failure, respiratory failure, MI, sepsis, or rupture drive ICU utilization, GMLOS ~7 days, and materially higher mortality. Open repair exceeds EVAR for complications/ICU/LOS; suprarenal/supraceliac clamp and thoracoabdominal extent add spinal cord and visceral ischemia risk. Ruptured AAA is uniformly high-severity/high-mortality (open ~35-45%, EVAR ~25-30% 30-day mortality)
pathway_pearls: 1) Right procedure, right venue - EVAR-first when anatomy is suitable (especially ruptured, per IMPROVE) but explicitly counsel lifelong surveillance and late sac-rupture risk (EVAR-1 15-yr); concentrate cases in high-volume centers (>=10 EVAR and >=10 open/yr; mortality <=2% EVAR, <5% open). 2) Ruptured AAA is a time-critical emergency - door-to-intervention <90 min (30-30-30), permissive hypotension (SBP 70-90, keep the patient talking), balanced massive transfusion, do not crystalloid-flood. 3) Pre-op medical optimization changes outcomes - moderate-high statin, continue (do not acutely start high-dose) beta-blocker and aspirin, RCRI-guided cardiac workup, smoking cessation. 4) Run open aorta on ERAS - goal-directed fluids, epidural, normothermia, early extubation/mobilization/feeding, early Foley/line removal - and watch specifically for colonic ischemia (IMA) and spinal cord ischemia (CSF drain for thoracoabdominal)

scope_and_volume_notes: Major cardiovascular procedures (237-238) and aortic/heart-assist/endovascular repair (268-272), incl. AAA (open & EVAR). High ICU utilization.

admission_criteria: Elective surgical admission driven by planned procedure extent, not ED triage. Outpatient/23h-observation appropriate for uncomplicated minimally invasive (MIS) simple hysterectomy/BSO +/- sentinel node for clinical stage I endometrial cancer in fit patients (ERAS same-day pathway). Inpatient admission (>=2-midnight expectation) for: exploratory laparotomy/open comprehensive staging; ovarian cytoreduction/debulking; radical hysterectomy or pelvic exenteration; bowel resection/ostomy or diaphragm/upper-abdominal surgery; significant comorbidity (ASA III-IV); anticipated large blood loss/transfusion; or postoperative complication (uncontrolled pain, ileus, hemodynamic instability, hypoxia). Document inpatient status rationale under the 2-midnight rule.
risk_stratification: ASA Physical Status (III-IV = higher risk); ACS-NSQIP Surgical Risk Calculator (predicted morbidity/mortality, LOS, discharge destination); Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI >=2 -> cardiology optimization); Caprini VTE score (>=5 = highest risk, >=8 -> mandate extended prophylaxis); Apfel PONV score (>=2 of 4 -> >=2 antiemetic agents); ECOG/Karnofsky performance status; mFI-5 frailty index (elderly -> prehab/geriatrics co-management); nutrition risk (albumin <3.0-3.5 g/dL or >10% weight loss -> prehab/nutrition); for ovarian, Aletti Surgical Complexity Score and Fagotti laparoscopic score to predict R0 resectability and triage primary debulking vs neoadjuvant chemotherapy.
initial_workup_labs: CBC (baseline Hgb, transfusion planning); CMP (renal/hepatic, glucose); coagulation PT/INR + aPTT; type & screen/crossmatch (cross for debulking/radical); HbA1c and glucose (perioperative target <180 mg/dL); albumin/prealbumin (nutrition risk); pregnancy test/beta-hCG if reproductive age; urinalysis; site-specific tumor markers (CA-125 +/- HE4/ROMA for ovarian; CEA if mucinous); ascites cytology if effusion; confirm histologic diagnosis (endometrial biopsy/D&C, cervical biopsy) before definitive surgery. Pre-op anemia optimization with IV iron if Hgb low.
initial_imaging_dx: Endometrial: endometrial biopsy/D&C (diagnosis); TVUS; pelvic MRI for myometrial/cervical stromal invasion; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for high-grade histology staging. Ovarian: TVUS then contrast CT abdomen/pelvis to assess carcinomatosis/resectability; consider diagnostic laparoscopy (Fagotti score) before laparotomy. Cervical: exam under anesthesia, pelvic MRI (local extent), FDG-PET/CT for nodal/distant disease (FIGO 2018 permits imaging staging), cystoscopy/proctoscopy if advanced. Intraoperative frozen section for staging; sentinel-lymph-node mapping with indocyanine green (ICG).
time_critical_interventions: Elective case = perioperative safety bundle rather than emergent time targets. Antimicrobial prophylaxis within 60 min before incision (cefazolin +/- metronidazole; re-dose for EBL >1500 mL or case >3-4 h/>2 drug half-lives); WHO surgical safety checklist/time-out; maintain normothermia (>=36 C) and euglycemia; VTE prophylaxis initiated perioperatively (mechanical at induction, pharmacologic per bleeding risk). Rescue protocols if triggered: massive transfusion protocol for hemorrhage; sepsis 1-hour bundle (lactate, cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1 h, 30 mL/kg crystalloid) for postoperative septic shock.
initial_management: ERAS Society protocol. Pre-op: avoid prolonged fasting (clears to 2 h + carbohydrate loading), no routine mechanical bowel prep, multimodal pre-emptive analgesia (acetaminophen +/- celecoxib +/- selective single-dose gabapentinoid). Intra-op: MIS approach when oncologically appropriate (NOT early cervical cancer -> open radical hysterectomy per LACC); goal-directed euvolemic fluid therapy; regional/opioid-sparing analgesia (TAP or quadratus lumborum block; thoracic epidural for open laparotomy); PONV prophylaxis with >=2 agents (dexamethasone + ondansetron); avoid routine nasogastric tubes and intra-abdominal drains. Restrictive transfusion (Hgb <7 g/dL, or <8 with cardiac disease/symptoms). Source control = complete surgical resection (R0 cytoreduction for ovarian).
day1_milestones: POD0: extubate and recover; multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (scheduled acetaminophen/NSAID + regional block, opioid PRN only); early oral intake with same-day advancement toward regular diet (ERAS); out of bed/ambulate the evening of surgery; active PONV prophylaxis/treatment; euvolemia with early discontinuation of IV fluids; resume mechanical VTE prophylaxis and give first pharmacologic dose once hemostasis secure; glucose <180 mg/dL; for MIS simple hysterectomy, consider same-day discharge if ERAS criteria met.
day2_milestones: POD1: remove urinary catheter within 24 h with trial of void (radical hysterectomy may need longer/bladder-function check); transition fully to oral analgesia; advance to regular diet; ambulate independently >=3-4 times; assess return of bowel function (flatus) but do not require a bowel movement before discharge; discontinue IV fluids; review pathology/frozen-section results; most MIS and uncomplicated open-staging patients meet discharge criteria; begin discharge teaching including extended VTE prophylaxis.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+: for open cytoreduction/radical/exenteration or complications. Continue progressive mobilization and opioid-sparing analgesia; prevent/treat ileus (ambulation, gum chewing, alvimopan if bowel resection, minimize opioids); manage wound/drains if placed; confirm bowel function and adequate oral intake; step down from ICU/telemetry; escalate work-up for fever, tachycardia, oliguria, or rising WBC (rule out anastomotic leak/SSI/VTE); finalize adjuvant-therapy plan; discharge when objective criteria met (typically POD2-4 for open debulking).
consults_multidisciplinary: Gynecologic oncology (primary surgeon); anesthesiology/pre-op optimization clinic; pathology (intra-op frozen section, SLN ultrastaging); medical oncology (adjuvant chemotherapy, esp. ovarian/high-grade); radiation oncology (cervical, high-intermediate/high-risk endometrial); genetics (Lynch syndrome screening in endometrial, BRCA/HRD in ovarian); nutrition (malnourished/ovarian); PT/OT (early mobility, frailty/prehab); enterostomal/WOC nurse if ostomy; palliative care for advanced/symptomatic disease; pharmacy (medication reconciliation, anticoagulation teaching); social work/care management and nurse navigator (transitions, transport, home health) engaged from admission.
monitoring_level: Surgical floor for the majority (MIS staging, simple/uncomplicated open hysterectomy). Step-down/telemetry or ICU for: extensive cytoreduction (bowel resection, diaphragm stripping, splenectomy/upper-abdominal work), major blood loss/large-volume resuscitation, hemodynamic or respiratory instability, or significant cardiopulmonary comorbidity (typical of DRG 736 with MCC). Escalation triggers: sustained tachycardia/hypotension, falling SpO2/rising O2 requirement, oliguria/AKI, rising lactate, uncontrolled pain, or suspicion of hemorrhage, PE, sepsis, or anastomotic leak -> rapid response/ICU transfer.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: early oral intake POD0 with rapid advancement to regular diet (no routine NG tube). Mobility: out of bed POD0, progressive independent ambulation, PT for frailty. VTE: combined mechanical (intermittent pneumatic compression) + pharmacologic (LMWH enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily or UFH 5000 U SC q8-12h; pre-op start when bleeding risk acceptable); EXTENDED prophylaxis 28 days after major open abdominopelvic cancer surgery (ASCO 2023: LMWH preferred; apixaban or rivaroxaban acceptable alternatives). Glucose target <180 mg/dL. Lines/catheters: remove urinary catheter <=24 h and IV/central lines early to reduce CAUTI/CLABSI and aid mobility.
discharge_criteria: Adequate pain control on oral analgesics; tolerating oral intake/hydration; independent mobility at/near baseline; spontaneous voiding after catheter removal (or stable catheter plan for radical hysterectomy bladder dysfunction); return of bowel activity (flatus) without obstruction (bowel movement not mandatory); afebrile and hemodynamically stable with reassuring labs; no active complication; patient understands and has supplies/teaching for extended VTE prophylaxis, wound care, and warning signs; adequate social support/home services arranged.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home (home health for ostomy/complex wounds/frail elderly). Medication reconciliation with opioid stewardship (limited quantity + scheduled non-opioids + prophylactic bowel regimen) and extended VTE-prophylaxis prescription/teaching (28-day LMWH or DOAC). Follow-up: surgeon/wound check and pathology review at 1-2 weeks, plus medical/radiation oncology for adjuvant therapy; genetics referral if indicated. Patient education: wound care, vaginal-cuff precautions/pelvic rest ~6 weeks, VTE/infection/leak warning signs, activity limits; ERAS post-discharge phone call within 24-72 h and nurse-navigator transition support.
expected_los: By MS-DRG GMLOS (CMS FY2022): non-ovarian/non-adnexal malignancy (endometrial/cervical) DRG 741 w/o CC/MCC 1.6 d, 740 w/CC 2.8 d, 739 w/MCC 6.6 d; ovarian/adnexal malignancy DRG 738 w/o CC/MCC 2.6 d, 737 w/CC 4.2 d, 736 w/MCC 8.4 d; pelvic evisceration/radical hysterectomy/radical vulvectomy DRG 735 w/o CC/MCC 1.7 d, 734 w/CC-MCC 3.5 d. Overall typical 2-4 days for open staging/debulking; MIS often <=1 day.
target_los: MIS simple hysterectomy/staging for endometrial cancer: same-day to 1 day (SGO/ERAS same-day-discharge pathway). Open comprehensive staging: 1-2 days. Open ovarian cytoreduction: 2-4 days. Radical hysterectomy (cervical): 1-3 days. Pelvic exenteration remains longer (5-10+ days given urinary diversion/ostomy). Targets contingent on >=70-80% ERAS bundle compliance.
quality_metrics: ERAS bundle/element compliance (RECOvER checklist reporting); NHSN surgical-site infection after abdominal hysterectomy (CDC/CMS); legacy SCIP process measures (prophylactic antibiotic timing/selection, normothermia, VTE prophylaxis ordered/received); AHRQ PSI-12 perioperative PE/DVT and PSI-90 composite; CAUTI rate; 30-day unplanned readmission; unplanned return to OR/reoperation; 30-day mortality; blood-transfusion rate; opioid-prescribing metrics; MIS and same-day-discharge rates; SLN mapping rate; R0 (complete) cytoreduction rate for ovarian; SGO quality indicators.
common_complications: Postoperative ileus and PONV (mitigate: multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia, early feeding/ambulation, gum chewing, alvimopan for bowel resection, no routine NG); surgical-site/wound infection and vaginal-cuff cellulitis (timely antibiotics, normothermia, glucose control, chlorhexidine prep); VTE (mechanical + pharmacologic + 28-day extended prophylaxis); CAUTI (early catheter removal); acute blood-loss anemia/transfusion (restrictive threshold, cell salvage); ureteral/bladder/bowel injury (meticulous technique, intra-op cystoscopy); lymphocele/lower-limb lymphedema (favor SLN over full lymphadenectomy); anastomotic leak (if bowel resection); AKI (euvolemia); vaginal-cuff dehiscence (late).
readmission_drivers: Ileus/small-bowel obstruction; wound infection/dehiscence and cuff cellulitis; VTE (PE/DVT); dehydration/poor oral intake/failure to thrive; uncontrolled pain or opioid-related constipation; UTI; anastomotic leak or intra-abdominal abscess; symptomatic lymphocele. Prevention levers: 28-day extended VTE prophylaxis with teaching; opioid-sparing analgesia + prophylactic bowel regimen; early post-discharge phone call, nurse-navigator follow-up and rapid clinic access; wound-care and warning-sign education; ensure adequate oral intake and mobility before discharge.
guideline_source: ERAS Society Guidelines for Gynecologic/Oncology Surgery (Nelson et al. 2019 update; Nelson/Fotopoulou et al. 2023 implementation update) as the perioperative backbone; NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology (Uterine Neoplasms, Ovarian Cancer, Cervical Cancer; current versions) for oncologic surgical management/staging; ASCO 2023 VTE Prophylaxis and Treatment in Patients With Cancer guideline update; SGO practice statements; ESGO/ESTRO/ESP endometrial cancer guideline (2021) as concordant international reference; anchored by landmark RCTs LACC and FIRES.
key_citations: Per PubMed: Nelson G, et al. Guidelines for perioperative care in gynecologic/oncology: ERAS Society recommendations-2019 update. Int J Gynecol Cancer 2019;29(4):651-668, PMID 30877144 (doi.org/10.1136/ijgc-2019-000356). Nelson G, Fotopoulou C, et al. ERAS Society guidelines for gynecologic oncology-2023 update. Gynecol Oncol 2023;173:58-67, PMID 37086524 (doi.org/10.1016/j.ygyno.2023.04.009). Ramirez PT, et al. Minimally Invasive vs Abdominal Radical Hysterectomy for Cervical Cancer (LACC). N Engl J Med 2018;379:1895-1904, PMID 30380365 (doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1806395). Rossi EC, et al. SLN biopsy vs lymphadenectomy for endometrial cancer (FIRES). Lancet Oncol 2017;18:384-392, PMID 28159465 (doi.org/10.1016/S1470-2045(17)30068-2). Key NS, et al. VTE Prophylaxis and Treatment in Patients With Cancer: ASCO Update. J Clin Oncol 2023;41:3063-3071, PMID 37075273 (doi.org/10.1200/JCO.23.00294). Elias KM, et al. RECOvER Checklist. World J Surg 2019;43:1-8, PMID 30116862 (doi.org/10.1007/s00268-018-4753-0).
evidence_grade: Strong - anchored by ERAS Society Class I society guideline (GRADE-graded) plus multiple Level I RCTs: LACC (open radical hysterectomy superior to MIS in early cervical cancer), FIRES (ICG sentinel-node sensitivity 97.2%, NPV 99.6% for endometrial staging), and RCT-based ASCO extended VTE prophylaxis. Oncologic-approach and core ERAS elements = strong/high certainty; specific LOS targets and some implementation items = moderate (observational/consensus).
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed group spans three procedure families and severity tiers. Non-ovarian/non-adnexal malignancy (739-741; endometrial/cervical, often MIS): GMLOS 1.6 d without CC/MCC rising to 6.6 d with MCC. Ovarian/adnexal (736-738; staging/debulking, often laparotomy): GMLOS 2.6 -> 4.2 (CC) -> 8.4 d (MCC), with more ICU use, bowel resection and transfusion. Pelvic evisceration/radical hysterectomy/radical vulvectomy (734-735): GMLOS 1.7-3.5 d (exenteration longer in practice). CC drivers: acute blood-loss anemia/transfusion, ileus, UTI, AKI stage 1, atrial fibrillation. MCC drivers: sepsis, PE, respiratory failure, hemorrhagic shock, anastomotic leak, reoperation - each escalating ICU need, LOS and cost weight.
pathway_pearls: 1) Match approach to tumor: MIS for endometrial (equivalent oncologic outcome, less morbidity) but OPEN radical hysterectomy for early cervical cancer - MIS reduced disease-free and overall survival in LACC (highest-yield safety lever). 2) ICG sentinel-lymph-node mapping replaces routine full lymphadenectomy in clinically uterine-confined endometrial cancer (FIRES) - less lymphedema, shorter OR. 3) Drive full ERAS bundle compliance (>=70-80%): euvolemia, regional/opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia, PONV prophylaxis, early feeding/mobilization, catheter removal <=24 h, no routine NG/drain/bowel prep. 4) Prescribe AND teach 28-day extended VTE prophylaxis after open pelvic cancer surgery. 5) For ovarian, aim for complete (R0) cytoreduction and use diagnostic laparoscopy (Fagotti) to triage primary surgery vs neoadjuvant chemotherapy.

admission_criteria: Primary headache/intractable migraine is overwhelmingly an ED-discharge or ED-observation (<24h) condition; true INPATIENT admission (DRG 102/103) is the exception. Objective inpatient indications: status migrainosus (debilitating migraine >72h per ICHD-3) refractory to >=2 rounds of ED parenteral therapy over 4-6h requiring a scheduled IV bridge (repetitive dihydroergotamine q8h, IV lidocaine, IV valproate/magnesium series); intractable nausea/vomiting with dehydration, AKI, or electrolyte derangement not corrected in obs; suspected/confirmed dangerous SECONDARY headache needing inpatient care (SAH, meningitis/encephalitis, cerebral venous sinus thrombosis, mass, IIH with vision threat, RCVS, pituitary apoplexy, GCA)-often reassigns DRG; medication-overuse/opioid/butalbital-withdrawal requiring monitored detox; a major comorbidity/complication driving the with-MCC tier. Default to observation status; convert to inpatient only after documented failure of ED treatment or anticipated stay >24-48h
risk_stratification: Pathway hinges on excluding dangerous secondary headache before committing to the benign-migraine track. SNNOOP10 red/orange flags (systemic sx/fever; neoplasm hx; neuro deficit/reduced consciousness; sudden/thunderclap onset; onset age >50-65; pattern change/new headache; positional; precipitated by Valsalva/cough/exertion; papilledema; progressive/atypical; pregnancy/puerperium; painful eye w/ autonomic features; post-traumatic; immune pathology/HIV; painkiller overuse): ANY flag -> neuroimaging +/- LP/labs. Ottawa SAH Rule (alert, age >=15, new severe atraumatic headache peaking <=1h, no deficit): any of age >=40, neck pain/stiffness, witnessed LOC, onset during exertion, thunderclap/instantly-peaking, limited neck flexion -> 100% sensitive/15% specific -> CT head +/- LP/CTA; if none, SAH excluded. Non-contrast CT <6h of onset ~100% sensitive for SAH. ICHD-3 criteria to positively diagnose migraine; POUND (Pulsatile, 4-72 hOurs, Unilateral, Nausea, Disabling) >=4/5 = high likelihood migraine; HINTS exam if accompanying isolated vertigo
initial_workup_labs: Typical migraine needs minimal labs-keep targeted. POC glucose; BMP (electrolytes/renal-guides fluids, corrects vomiting-related hyponatremia/dehydration, safe DHE/NSAID/valproate dosing); CBC (leukocytosis->infection). beta-hCG in all of childbearing potential-mandatory before ergots/DHE, valproate, triptans, and imaging. If age >50 with jaw claudication/scalp tenderness/visual sx: STAT ESR + CRP -> if elevated, high-dose steroids + temporal artery biopsy for GCA. If fever/meningismus: blood cultures + lactate then LP (cell count, glucose, protein, Gram stain/culture, opening pressure)-do not delay antibiotics. If thunderclap with negative CT >6h: LP for xanthochromia/RBC. LFTs before valproate; ECG/QTc before QT-prolonging antiemetics (droperidol/prochlorperazine/chlorpromazine) and before DHE. Carboxyhemoglobin if CO-exposure history
initial_imaging_dx: Neuroimaging is NOT indicated for typical migraine with a normal neuro exam (ACEP 2019; Choosing Wisely)-reserve for SNNOOP10 red flags. Non-contrast CT head STAT (door-to-CT target <25-45 min for thunderclap/possible stroke): first-line for SAH/hemorrhage/mass; <6h from onset ~100% sensitive for SAH. LP after negative CT for suspected SAH (xanthochromia) or meningitis; measure opening pressure (IIH if >25 cmH2O). CTA/MRA for aneurysm/dissection/RCVS (string-of-beads); MRV/CTV for cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (peripartum, thrombophilia). MRI brain +/- contrast preferred for non-emergent structural evaluation (pituitary, posterior fossa, leptomeningeal enhancement, IIH signs). Fundoscopy/ocular ultrasound for papilledema; ECG before DHE/QT-prolonging antiemetics
time_critical_interventions: No door-to-balloon analog; the time-critical work is rescuing dangerous mimics and rapid symptomatic control to avoid admission. Thunderclap headache: door-to-CT <45 min; if SAH suspected, emergent neurosurgery/neuro-IR and BP control (target SBP <160). Suspected bacterial meningitis: empiric antibiotics + dexamethasone within <60 min of arrival, do NOT delay for CT/LP. Acute angle-closure glaucoma, GCA with visual loss, or pituitary apoplexy: same-hour ophthalmology/neurosurgery. Migraine itself: give first-line IV dopamine antagonist within ~30-60 min of rooming, stack agents, goal pain-freedom or >=50% relief and ED/obs discharge within 4-6h. Do not use opioids first-line (hydromorphone = Level A must-NOT-offer, AHS 2025)
initial_management: Per AHS 2025 ED guideline. First-line antidopaminergic antiemetics: prochlorperazine 10 mg IV (Level A-must offer, preferred) or metoclopramide 10-20 mg IV (Level B) if prochlorperazine unavailable; co-administer diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV or benztropine to prevent akathisia/dystonia (IV diphenhydramine as monotherapy = Level C may-not-offer). NSAIDs (Level B): ketorolac 15-30 mg IV or dexketoprofen IV. Triptan (Level B): sumatriptan 6 mg SC (avoid <24h of ergots; contraindicated in CAD/uncontrolled HTN/hemiplegic or basilar migraine). Nerve blocks: greater occipital nerve block with bupivacaine 0.5%/lidocaine (Level A), supraorbital block (Level B). Second-line (Level C may offer): IV chlorpromazine, IV valproate (avoid in pregnancy), IV magnesium 1-2 g (esp. aura), droperidol/haloperidol (ECG/QTc first). Recurrence prevention: dexamethasone 10-16 mg IV/IM single dose before discharge (RR 0.87). IV isotonic fluids for dehydration/vomiting (supportive; saline itself Level U). Refractory/status migrainosus (inpatient): repetitive IV DHE 0.5-1 mg q8h with antiemetic pretreatment (Raskin protocol) x2-3 days-contraindicated in CAD/PVD/uncontrolled HTN/pregnancy/recent triptan/CYP3A4 inhibitors; alternatives IV lidocaine (telemetry), IV valproate, IV magnesium, methylprednisolone bridge. AVOID opioids and butalbital
day1_milestones: Confirm ICHD-3 primary-headache diagnosis and that dangerous secondary causes are excluded (imaging/LP results resulted). Start scheduled inpatient bridge (DHE q8h, or IV valproate/magnesium series) plus scheduled antiemetic + NSAID with standing non-opioid PRN rescue. IV hydration, correct electrolytes, control nausea/vomiting toward PO tolerance. Baseline ECG/QTc; telemetry if on QT-prolonging agents or DHE. Neurology consult; obtain full medication history to identify overuse (triptan >10 d/mo, analgesic >15 d/mo, opioid/butalbital) and stop offending agents. Track pain score; goal >=30-50% reduction by end of Day 1; low-stimulation dark quiet room, early mobilization
day2_milestones: Continue/complete DHE or bridge series; reassess response, target headache freedom or >=50% sustained relief. Transition IV -> oral abortives and initiate/optimize preventive therapy (oral steroid bridge/taper plus long-term prevention: propranolol/metoprolol, topiramate, amitriptyline, candesartan, or CGRP mAb/gepant). Advance diet as nausea controlled; discontinue IV fluids and remove peripheral IV once off IV meds. Begin discharge planning: headache education, trigger/lifestyle counseling, initiate headache diary. Monitor for medication-overuse withdrawal symptoms and treat supportively
day3plus_milestones: Most intractable-migraine admissions discharge by Day 2-3 once the attack is broken and PO is tolerated. Complete DHE course (max ~2-3 days); confirm sustained relief off IV medications for >=12-24h. Finalize preventive regimen plus a non-opioid/non-butalbital acute rescue plan; complete medication-overuse counseling and taper plan. Schedule neurology follow-up; ensure no red-flag evolution. If not improving by Day 3, re-image and re-evaluate for a missed secondary cause (CVST, RCVS, IIH, dissection, GCA) rather than continuing symptomatic-only therapy
consults_multidisciplinary: Neurology/headache medicine-primary driver, consult Day 0-1 for refractory cases and preventive planning; Emergency Medicine for index management. Pharmacy (DHE/valproate dosing, QT review, drug interactions, MOH taper). Neurosurgery/neuro-IR (SAH/aneurysm/mass), Infectious Disease (meningitis/encephalitis), Ophthalmology (papilledema/IIH/GCA/angle-closure), OB (pregnancy-associated headache). Care management/social work (MOH, opioid-use disorder, frequent ED utilizers, psychosocial drivers, care plan). Behavioral health/psychology for comorbid depression/anxiety and CBT/biofeedback referral; PT for cervicogenic component. Nutrition if prolonged NPO/vomiting
monitoring_level: Floor or observation level for the vast majority. Telemetry with baseline + follow-up ECG for QT-prolonging antiemetics (droperidol, chlorpromazine, high-dose prochlorperazine), IV lidocaine infusion, or DHE (coronary/peripheral vasoconstriction risk). Step-down/ICU only for secondary emergencies: SAH (serial neuro checks, Hunt-Hess), status epilepticus, airway compromise, hemodynamic instability, or IV lidocaine per institutional cardiac-monitoring policy. Escalation triggers: new focal neuro deficit, declining GCS, seizure, refractory vomiting with hemodynamic change, or chest pain/arrhythmia on DHE
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: clears/NPO while actively vomiting, advance to regular as antiemetics control nausea-enable early PO. Mobility: early ambulation in a quiet, dark, low-stimulation room; no strict bed rest. VTE prophylaxis: short ambulatory stay generally warrants mechanical (SCDs); add pharmacologic (enoxaparin/UFH) if immobile, obese, prior VTE, or elevated Padua/Caprini score. Glucose monitoring if corticosteroids used. Line/catheter minimization: peripheral IV only, remove when off IV meds; avoid Foley
discharge_criteria: Headache freedom or >=50% sustained, tolerable pain reduction (goal NRS <=3-4). Tolerating oral intake and hydrated; nausea controlled; ambulating safely. No red-flag features; dangerous secondary causes excluded. Stable vitals with no active adverse drug effects (akathisia/dystonia resolved, no QT prolongation/arrhythmia). Effective non-opioid acute plan in place and single-dose dexamethasone given for recurrence prevention; preventive plan and follow-up arranged
discharge_planning: Disposition home in the great majority. Medication reconciliation stopping overused acute agents (limit triptans <=10 d/mo, simple analgesics <=15 d/mo; no opioids/butalbital); give single-dose dexamethasone pre-discharge and provide a non-opioid rescue kit (oral triptan +/- NSAID + antiemetic). Start/refer preventive therapy (topiramate, propranolol/metoprolol, amitriptyline, candesartan, or CGRP mAb/gepant) when attacks are frequent. Follow-up: neurology/headache clinic within 1-2 weeks (sooner if refractory), PCP within 1 week; headache diary, trigger counseling, MOH education, explicit red-flag return precautions. Transitions: communicate plan to PCP/neurology; establish a care plan for ED over-utilizers
expected_los: DRG 103 (Headaches without MCC) GMLOS ~2.1-2.3 days (ALOS ~2.6); DRG 102 (Headaches with MCC) GMLOS ~3.3-3.6 days (ALOS ~4.4). True intractable primary headache is largely an ED-observation problem (<24-48h) and should not become a prolonged inpatient stay-so real-world expected LOS ranges ~2.2 days (no MCC) to ~3.5 days (with MCC/DRG 102)
target_los: Optimized pathway: manage in ED-observation and discharge <24-48h (0-2 midnights) for uncomplicated cases; for genuine inpatient status migrainosus requiring a DHE course, target 2-3 days. Overall best-practice target ~1.5-2.5 days, with strong emphasis on keeping appropriate cases in observation status and avoiding unnecessary inpatient conversion
quality_metrics: Imaging stewardship: avoid neuroimaging for uncomplicated headache with normal exam (Choosing Wisely; legacy CMS OP-15 'Use of Brain CT in the ED for Atraumatic Headache'). Opioid stewardship: avoidance of opioids/butalbital for migraine (guideline Level A must-not-offer hydromorphone). ED throughput: OP-18 median arrival-to-discharge time, OP-22 left-without-being-seen, door-to-analgesia time. Recurrence prevention: dexamethasone-at-discharge rate. Outcomes: 30-day ED revisit/readmission for headache, patient-reported pain reduction, observation-status appropriateness (avoid unwarranted inpatient conversion). Process: documented ICHD-3 diagnosis and SNNOOP10/Ottawa red-flag screen
common_complications: Akathisia/acute dystonia/EPS from dopamine antagonists (prochlorperazine, metoclopramide, droperidol)-prevent/treat with diphenhydramine/benztropine. QT prolongation from antiemetics/droperidol-baseline ECG, avoid stacking QT drugs. DHE effects: nausea, chest/leg tightness, coronary/peripheral vasospasm-screen for CAD/PVD, antiemetic pretreatment. Medication-overuse/rebound headache and post-discharge recurrence-mitigate with dexamethasone, taper overused meds, start prevention. Vomiting-related hyponatremia/dehydration/AKI; antiemetic-induced sedation/hypotension; iatrogenic opioid exposure/dependence (avoid opioids). Missed secondary headache (SAH, CVST, meningitis, GCA)-mitigated by SNNOOP10/Ottawa adherence
readmission_drivers: Medication-overuse headache and continued opioid/butalbital use-mitigate with taper and stewardship. Inadequate or absent preventive therapy and no acute rescue plan-start prevention, provide rescue kit + dexamethasone. No or delayed neurology follow-up-schedule within 1-2 weeks. Missed secondary cause-re-image if atypical/progressive. Untreated psychiatric comorbidity, poor health literacy, and social/frequent-ED-utilizer drivers-address with behavioral health and care-management care plans
guideline_source: PRIMARY: American Headache Society (AHS) 2025 guideline update-acute treatment of migraine in adults in the ED, parenteral pharmacotherapies (Robblee et al., Headache 2025), updating the AHS 2016 evidence assessment (Orr et al.). ACEP 2019 Clinical Policy-critical issues in evaluation/management of adult ED patients with acute headache. ICHD-3 (International Classification of Headache Disorders, 3rd edition, 2018) for diagnosis. Supporting: AAN/AHS preventive-therapy guidance and American Migraine Foundation consensus

evidence_grade: Strong for the ED acute-treatment pathway-multiple Class I RCTs underpin AHS 2025 Level A/B recommendations (prochlorperazine IV, greater occipital nerve block, ketorolac, metoclopramide, sumatriptan SC) and the Ottawa SAH Rule is a Class I decision instrument. Moderate for recurrence prevention with dexamethasone (meta-analytic, modest ARR ~10%). Weak/consensus for the inpatient refractory bridge (repetitive IV DHE/Raskin protocol, IV lidocaine, IV valproate/magnesium for status migrainosus)-largely observational/expert opinion and extrapolated. Overall: Strong for ED symptomatic management; Moderate-to-consensus for the inpatient/refractory bridge
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 102 (with MCC) vs 103 (without MCC): the MCC is usually NOT the headache itself but a genuine major complication or comorbidity (aspiration pneumonia from vomiting, seizure, acute metabolic derangement/AKI, or a serious secondary headache) that mandates true inpatient status, telemetry/step-down, more consults and imaging, and a longer LOS (GMLOS ~3.5 vs ~2.2 days). A pure intractable migraine with no CC/MCC belongs in observation/DRG 103-adjacent care, not a prolonged inpatient stay; if it maps to 102, scrutinize whether a real secondary/serious cause is present-which may re-assign the DRG entirely. CC-level items (dehydration with AKI, hyponatremia, intractable vomiting) modestly extend obs/LOS; higher tiers lower the threshold for neurology consult, ICU/step-down, and neuroimaging
pathway_pearls: Right level of care: this is fundamentally an observation/ED-discharge condition-treat aggressively with stacked non-opioid parenterals and discharge <24h; reserve true inpatient admission for status migrainosus needing a scheduled DHE/bridge course or a real secondary cause. Lead with prochlorperazine IV + greater occipital nerve block (AHS 2025 Level A), add ketorolac or sumatriptan SC, co-give diphenhydramine to prevent akathisia, and give single-dose dexamethasone before discharge to cut 24-72h recurrence. No opioids/butalbital (Level A must-not-offer hydromorphone)-they worsen outcomes and drive medication-overuse headache and bounce-backs. Screen every patient with SNNOOP10 + Ottawa SAH Rule before committing to the benign pathway, image only red-flag patients (imaging stewardship), and always arrange neurology follow-up plus start/optimize prevention

admission_criteria: Inpatient admission for gross hematuria with clot retention/failure to void despite ED bladder irrigation; symptomatic or hemodynamically significant acute blood-loss anemia (Hgb <7-8 g/dL, orthostasis, tachycardia, ongoing transfusion need); malignant urinary obstruction with obstructive AKI (rising creatinine, hydronephrosis) especially bilateral or solitary kidney; obstructed + infected system (pyonephrosis/urosepsis) = emergency; hyperkalemia/uremia/fluid overload from obstruction; need for urgent urologic procedure (cystoscopic clot evacuation, ureteral stent, percutaneous nephrostomy); intractable pain. ED discharge/observation appropriate if hemodynamically stable, Hgb stable, voiding without clot, no obstruction/AKI/infection -> outpatient cystoscopy + CT urography workup.
risk_stratification: AUA/SUFU Microhematuria risk tiers: gross hematuria = automatically HIGH-risk -> cystoscopy + multiphasic CT urography (microscopic strata: low = men <40/women <50, 3-10 RBC/hpf, <10 pack-yr -> repeat UA 6mo; intermediate = age 40-59, 11-25 RBC/hpf, 10-30 pack-yr -> cystoscopy + renal US; high = age >=60, >25 RBC/hpf, >30 pack-yr -> cystoscopy + CTU); KDIGO AKI stage for obstructive AKI (Cr x1.5-1.9 = 1; x2-2.9 = 2; x3 or >=4.0 mg/dL or RRT = 3) drives decompression urgency + nephrology; qSOFA >=2 / SIRS / NEWS2 for infected obstruction -> emergent drainage + sepsis bundle; transfusion trigger restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if CAD/ACS); smoking pack-years quantify malignancy risk.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with serial Hgb/Hct to gauge blood loss; type & screen/crossmatch; CMP/BMP (creatinine/eGFR for obstructive AKI, K+ for hyperkalemia, HCO3); coagulation PT/INR + aPTT (identify/hold/reverse anticoagulants); urinalysis with microscopy confirming RBCs + urine culture (exclude infection); voided urine cytology for high-grade urothelial carcinoma; LFTs/LDH/calcium if known malignancy (mets/paraneoplastic); blood cultures x2 + lactate if febrile/septic; consider adjunct urine tumor markers (UroVysion FISH, NMP22) per 2025 amendment as adjunct, not a replacement for cystoscopy.
initial_imaging_dx: Multiphasic CT urography (CTU) is imaging of choice for upper-tract evaluation of gross hematuria — obtain same admission, ideally within 24-48h; MR urography if iodinated contrast contraindicated or low eGFR; renal/bladder ultrasound as rapid bedside test for hydronephrosis and bladder clot/retention (first-line if AKI or contrast concern); cystoscopy is definitive lower-tract evaluation — inpatient if actively bleeding/clot-retaining, otherwise outpatient within 1-2 weeks; non-contrast CT if concurrent stone/colic; CT chest/abdomen/pelvis for staging if muscle-invasive disease suspected.
time_critical_interventions: Obstructed + infected kidney (pyonephrosis/urosepsis) is a urologic emergency -> emergent decompression (percutaneous nephrostomy or retrograde stent) ASAP, goal within ~6h of recognition, PLUS Surviving Sepsis bundle: broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1h, lactate, blood cultures, 30 mL/kg crystalloid if hypotensive or lactate >=4; clot retention -> immediate large-bore 22-24 Fr 3-way catheter + manual clot evacuation + continuous bladder irrigation; severe/symptomatic anemia -> transfuse to restrictive Hgb >=7 (>=8 if CV disease), activate MTP only if exsanguinating; hyperkalemia K+ >6 or ECG changes -> emergent medical Rx + urgent decompression/RRT; life-threatening bleed on anticoagulant -> reverse (4F-PCC/vitamin K for warfarin, idarucizumab for dabigatran, andexanet or 4F-PCC for Xa inhibitors).
initial_management: Continuous bladder irrigation with normal saline via 3-way catheter + manual clot evacuation (Toomey/piston syringe); adjunct antifibrinolytic — intravesical or oral tranexamic acid may reduce irrigation volume/hematuria (weak/pilot evidence; avoid systemic TXA when upper-tract clot obstruction is a risk); IV isotonic crystalloid, correct coagulopathy, hold antiplatelets/anticoagulants and reverse if severe; RBC transfusion restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 with cardiac disease); decompression — retrograde ureteral stent preferred when feasible, percutaneous nephrostomy preferred if septic/unstable or stent not feasible (meta-analyses show no absolute superiority; stent shorter LOS, PCN lower failure rate); empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for infected obstruction (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone; carbapenem if ESBL risk) then narrow to culture; analgesia (acetaminophen, opioids sparingly, avoid NSAIDs in AKI/bleeding), antiemetics; refractory hemorrhage -> cystoscopic clot evacuation + fulguration, intravesical alum or aminocaproic acid, and rarely formalin/angioembolization/hyperbaric O2 for radiation or cyclophosphamide hemorrhagic cystitis.
day1_milestones: Achieve hemostasis with clear-to-pink irrigation and confirm catheter patency without clot retention; hemodynamic stabilization with serial CBC and transfusion per threshold; decompress any obstruction (stent/PCN) and document urine output + creatinine response; start/adjust antibiotics and source cultures if infected; complete labs, type & screen, reconcile and hold anticoagulation; initiate upper-tract imaging (CTU/US) and send urine cytology; obtain Urology consult and establish diagnostic plan (cystoscopy timing).
day2_milestones: Bleeding controlled — transition CBI to intermittent irrigation, trial of void or plan catheter removal once effluent clear; confirm Hgb stable off transfusion x24h; post-decompression creatinine trending down, obstruction relieved, patient afebrile; complete cystoscopy/CTU if not yet done and set tissue-diagnosis plan (schedule TURBT if indicated, generally outpatient/elective to avoid DRG shift); de-escalate antibiotics per cultures; mobilize, resume diet, and reassess adding pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis once bleeding has stopped.
day3plus_milestones: Sustained hemostasis with spontaneous voiding and no clot (catheter removed or stable with a plan); Hgb stable and AKI resolved/improving (creatinine plateau or downtrend); definitive diagnostic and oncologic plan established (TURBT date, urology/oncology follow-up, staging if muscle-invasive); nephrostomy/stent care taught and antibiotics completed or transitioned to oral; address goals of care and palliative involvement if advanced/metastatic malignancy.
consults_multidisciplinary: Urology primary (day 0-1) for hematuria/obstruction management and procedures; Interventional Radiology for percutaneous nephrostomy when stent not feasible or patient septic; Nephrology for obstructive AKI, hyperkalemia, or RRT; Medical and Radiation Oncology once malignancy tissue-confirmed for staging/systemic therapy; Hematology for complex coagulopathy or anticoagulation reversal; Pharmacy for anticoagulation and antibiotic stewardship; Case management/Social work early for disposition and home catheter/nephrostomy care and transportation; Nutrition; Palliative care if advanced disease; PT/OT if deconditioned.
monitoring_level: Floor/med-surg for stable gross hematuria on CBI with stable Hgb; telemetry/step-down for hemodynamic instability, ongoing transfusion requirement, significant hyperkalemia, or sepsis needing closer monitoring; ICU for septic shock (urosepsis/pyonephrosis), massive or ongoing hemorrhage requiring vasopressors or massive transfusion, or life-threatening hyperkalemia/emergent RRT. Escalation triggers: hemodynamic instability, falling Hgb despite transfusion, refractory clot retention, rising lactate, oliguria/anuria, new fever/rigors.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet regular/as tolerated with attention to hydration; renal- or cardiac-appropriate modification if AKI/CHF. Early ambulation with fall precautions around catheter/irrigation tubing. VTE prophylaxis: mechanical (SCDs) while actively bleeding, add pharmacologic LMWH/UFH once hemostasis achieved and hematuria clearing (hospitalized cancer patients are high VTE risk per ASCO 2023) — do NOT give pharmacologic prophylaxis during active gross hematuria. Maintain glucose control; remove/convert 3-way catheter once irrigation is clear and patient voids; stent/nephrostomy managed and scheduled for exchange per urology/IR.
discharge_criteria: Hematuria cleared or clear-to-pink with spontaneous voiding and no clot retention (or catheter in place with defined plan), off continuous bladder irrigation >=24h; Hgb stable without transfusion >=24h; obstructive AKI resolved/improving and obstruction relieved with functioning stent/PCN and adequate urine output; afebrile, hemodynamically stable, infection controlled on appropriate oral or completing IV antibiotics; pain controlled on oral analgesia and tolerating diet; definitive diagnostic plan and follow-up arranged; patient/caregiver competent in catheter or nephrostomy care.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home, often with home health for catheter/nephrostomy/stent care; SNF if deconditioned. Medication reconciliation with a deliberate anticoagulation restart decision (urology input) and defined antibiotic course. Follow-up: Urology for cystoscopy/TURBT within 1-2 weeks, Oncology if malignancy confirmed, scheduled stent exchange or nephrostomy check, PCP for anemia/creatinine recheck. Patient education: hematuria/clot-retention warning signs and return precautions, hydration, tube care, and smoking-cessation counseling (key modifiable urothelial-cancer risk factor). Close the loop on pending pathology/imaging results before discharge.
expected_los: Tiered by DRG: 688 (no CC/MCC) GMLOS 2.0 days (ALOS 2.3); 687 (CC) GMLOS 3.3 days (ALOS 4.2); 686 (MCC) GMLOS 5.1 days (ALOS 6.9); blended typical ~2.5-3.5 days.
target_los: 2-3 days under optimized pathway (rapid hemostasis, same-admission expedited imaging + decompression, early conversion off CBI, TURBT deferred to outpatient when safe); MCC/septic tier ~4 days.
quality_metrics: Guideline-concordant hematuria evaluation rate (cystoscopy + CT urography completed/arranged for gross/high-risk hematuria); time-to-decompression for obstructed infected kidney and SEP-1 bundle compliance if urosepsis; blood-utilization/restrictive-transfusion adherence (patient blood management); CAUTI standardized infection ratio; hospital VTE rate; 30-day readmission and 30-day mortality; documented smoking-cessation counseling; documented follow-up scheduling (transitions-of-care).
common_complications: Recurrent clot retention/catheter obstruction (mitigate with large-bore 3-way catheter, brisk CBI, manual evacuation); symptomatic anemia needing transfusion (serial Hgb, restrictive threshold); CAUTI/urosepsis from indwelling catheter or instrumentation (aseptic technique, timely catheter removal, prompt antibiotics); persistent/worsening obstructive AKI and hyperkalemia (decompression, nephrology); stent/nephrostomy migration, blockage, or dislodgement; VTE from cancer plus immobility (balanced against bleeding); contrast-associated AKI from CTU (hydration, weigh MR urography).
readmission_drivers: Recurrent gross hematuria/clot retention when definitive control not achieved (ensure timely TURBT/fulguration and correct coagulopathy); stent or nephrostomy dysfunction, dislodgement, or tube-associated infection (structured tube care and scheduled exchanges); CAUTI/urosepsis (catheter stewardship); rebleeding after premature or uncontrolled anticoagulant resumption (deliberate restart plan); incomplete cancer workup/lost follow-up (close the loop on cystoscopy/TURBT/oncology referral); prevention levers = early follow-up, patient education, home health, and thorough medication reconciliation.

evidence_grade: Moderate overall. Hematuria evaluation pathway is Strong (AUA/SUFU society guideline, though several statements are conditional/expert-opinion, Grade C). Obstruction decompression modality choice is Moderate (observational meta-analyses; no absolute stent-vs-nephrostomy superiority). Hemostatic measures (CBI, intravesical/oral tranexamic acid, alum) are Low/Consensus (small or pilot trials plus expert opinion). Restrictive transfusion thresholds and sepsis bundles are Strong but extrapolated from other domains. No single society guideline governs the entire inpatient medical admission — pathway is a synthesis.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 688 (no CC/MCC): stable gross hematuria or uncomplicated obstruction, floor care, ~2-day stay, frequently expedited to outpatient workup. DRG 687 (CC): moderate acute blood-loss anemia requiring transfusion, obstructive AKI, or UTI, ~3.3-day stay. DRG 686 (MCC): urosepsis/septic shock, severe AKI needing RRT, massive hemorrhage, or hyperkalemia -> ICU/step-down, ~5-day stay with higher complication and mortality risk; MCC drivers (sepsis, acute renal failure, acute blood-loss anemia) also raise the relative weight (0.79 -> 1.05 -> 1.80). Key coding note: definitive TURBT or cystectomy moves the case OUT of these medical DRGs into surgical DRGs (transurethral/kidney-ureter procedures 656-663 or cystectomy 653-655).
pathway_pearls: 1) Gross hematuria = automatic HIGH-risk: guarantee same-episode (or firmly scheduled outpatient) cystoscopy + CT urography — never discharge without a closed-loop definitive diagnostic plan. 2) Obstructed + infected kidney (pyonephrosis) is a true emergency — decompress ASAP and give antibiotics within 1h; treat it like sepsis source control, do not wait for perfect imaging. 3) Get the catheter right — large-bore 22-24 Fr 3-way + brisk continuous bladder irrigation + manual clot evacuation prevents the #1 bounce-back (clot retention); transfuse restrictively (Hgb <7). 4) Sequence VTE prophylaxis (mechanical while bleeding, pharmacologic once hematuria clears) and make smoking-cessation counseling a required discharge deliverable.

scope_and_volume_notes: Medical kidney/urinary neoplasm admissions (hematuria, obstruction); cystectomy counted separately (rank 119).

admission_criteria: Confirmed multiple-significant-trauma (polytrauma) is inpatient/ICU by definition — this DRG group presupposes admission, not observation. Objective triggers: Injury Severity Score (ISS) >15 (major trauma) or Berlin polytrauma definition (>=2 body regions with AIS >=3 PLUS >=1 of: SBP <=90, GCS <=8, base deficit >=6 mmol/L, INR >=1.4, age >=70); physiologic instability (SBP <90, HR >120, shock index >=1.0, GCS <=13, RR <10 or >29, need for transfusion/intubation/emergent OR/angio); ACS field trauma-team activation criteria met; two-or-more-body-region injury requiring operative or ICU management. Isolated minor single-system injury with fully normal physiology may be floor/observation or ED discharge; true polytrauma is never observation status.
risk_stratification: ISS >15 = major trauma / ISS >=25 = severe (drives trauma-center + ICU need); Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) <=8 -> intubate + ICP-monitoring pathway, 9-12 moderate TBI, 13-15 mild; Revised Trauma Score (RTS) and TRISS for probability-of-survival benchmarking; Shock Index (HR/SBP) >=1.0 predicts transfusion and >=1.4 severe -> activate MTP; ABC score (penetrating mechanism, SBP <=90, HR >=120, positive FAST) >=2 -> activate massive transfusion protocol; base deficit >6 mmol/L and lactate >4 mmol/L grade hemorrhagic shock and set resuscitation endpoints; positive FAST + hemodynamic instability -> immediate OR; TASH score for severe-hemorrhage prediction; age >=65 and pre-injury anticoagulation lower thresholds for ICU admission / geriatric-trauma pathway.
initial_workup_labs: Type & screen/crossmatch (activate MTP concurrently if unstable); CBC (baseline Hgb/platelets, serial for occult bleeding); coagulation panel PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen (fibrinogen <1.5 g/L -> cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen concentrate); viscoelastic testing TEG/ROTEM to goal-direct factor/platelet/fibrinogen therapy; VBG/ABG with lactate and base deficit (grade shock, track clearance as resuscitation endpoint); ionized calcium (iCa <1.0-1.1 mmol/L -> replace; hypocalcemia of massive transfusion); CMP/renal function (AKI); CK if crush/rhabdomyolysis; troponin/ECG if blunt cardiac injury suspected; ethanol + urine toxicology; serum/urine beta-hCG in women of childbearing age; serial lactate and normalization of base deficit tracked to guide ongoing resuscitation.
initial_imaging_dx: eFAST in the trauma bay within minutes (free fluid, pericardial effusion, pneumothorax); portable chest and pelvis X-ray during ATLS primary survey; hemodynamically STABLE patient -> whole-body 'pan-scan' CT of head/C-spine/chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast (reduces missed injury; ideally completed <30-60 min from arrival); CT angiography for suspected vascular or blunt cerebrovascular injury (Denver/Memphis criteria); UNSTABLE patient with positive FAST -> straight to OR, do NOT delay for CT; pelvic CT/angiography for arterial blush -> embolization; repeat/delayed head CT for evolving TBI; formal radiology over-read plus structured tertiary survey within 24h to capture injuries missed on initial imaging.
time_critical_interventions: Tranexamic acid within 3h of injury (ideally <1h; 1 g IV over 10 min then 1 g over 8h) per CRASH-2/CRASH-3 — avoid if presenting >3h out; activate massive transfusion protocol immediately on ABC score >=2 or clinical judgment; hemorrhage control to OR/angio within the 'golden hour' — damage-control laparotomy/thoracotomy for uncontrolled bleeding; pelvic binder immediately for suspected unstable pelvic ring; tourniquet/direct pressure for compressible extremity hemorrhage; REBOA or resuscitative thoracotomy in selected noncompressible torso hemorrhage/peri-arrest; open-fracture antibiotics within 1h (<=3h); emergent neurosurgical evacuation of EDH/SDH mass lesion; TBI - prevent SBP <110 mmHg and hypoxia (SpO2 <90); tertiary survey <=24h.
initial_management: Damage-control resuscitation: permissive hypotension (target SBP 80-90 mmHg / palpable radial pulse / MAP ~50-65) until surgical hemostasis — EXCEPT in TBI where SBP >=110 mmHg and CPP 60-70 take priority; minimize crystalloid (<1 L, avoid dilutional coagulopathy); balanced 1:1:1 plasma:platelets:RBC (PROPPR) or low-titer group-O whole blood; TXA as above; fibrinogen concentrate/cryoprecipitate if fibrinogen <1.5 g/L; maintain ionized calcium >1.1 mmol/L; prevent the 'lethal triad' (rewarm, correct acidosis and coagulopathy); once bleeding controlled use restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL (platelets >50, >100 if TBI/ongoing bleed); anticoagulant reversal - 4-factor PCC +/- vitamin K (warfarin), andexanet alfa or PCC (apixaban/rivaroxaban), idarucizumab (dabigatran); source control - laparotomy/packing, preperitoneal pelvic packing + angioembolization, external fixation, vascular shunt/ligation, tube thoracostomy.
day1_milestones: POD0/Day1: complete ATLS primary and secondary survey; achieve definitive hemorrhage control (damage-control surgery with temporary abdominal closure, external fixation, packing, and/or angioembolization as indicated); resuscitate to physiologic endpoints - lactate clearing, base deficit normalizing, urine output >0.5 mL/kg/h, rewarmed, coagulopathy corrected; ICU admission with invasive monitoring; place ICP monitor if GCS <=8 with abnormal head CT (BTF); complete cross-sectional imaging; open-fracture antibiotics plus tetanus prophylaxis; mechanical VTE prophylaxis (sequential compression devices) started; family updated and goals-of-care discussion if catastrophic/non-survivable injury.
day2_milestones: POD1/Day2: planned relook / 'second-look' laparotomy; definitive fascial closure once physiology restored (euthermic, non-acidotic, non-coagulopathic); wean vasopressors and begin ventilator liberation with spontaneous breathing trial/extubation assessment; complete formal tertiary survey (head-to-toe re-examination plus review of all imaging — up to ~10% of injuries are missed on initial survey); initiate pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (LMWH/enoxaparin) once hemostasis confirmed and patient stable (including stable TBI verified on repeat CT); start early enteral nutrition; de-escalate or stop empiric antibiotics; institute delirium-prevention and multimodal-analgesia protocols.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ through discharge readiness: 'early appropriate care' — definitive orthopedic fixation and spine stabilization once resuscitated (lactate <2.5 mmol/L, no coagulopathy, normothermic), converting external to internal fixation; abdominal-wall closure/reconstruction if open abdomen; wean sedation and extubate, prevent/treat delirium; progressive mobilization with PT/OT; SLP swallow evaluation before oral diet if prolonged intubation/TBI; step-down from ICU as instability and organ support resolve; ongoing wound / negative-pressure therapy; actively screen and treat complications (VTE, pneumonia, infection, ACS); rehabilitation and disposition planning; psychological/PTSD and substance-use screening.
consults_multidisciplinary: Trauma / acute-care surgery (primary team); neurosurgery (TBI, spine); orthopedic trauma; interventional radiology (angioembolization); vascular and thoracic/cardiac surgery as injury pattern dictates; anesthesiology / surgical critical care; PT and OT early (POD1-2); speech-language pathology (swallow, cognition); registered dietitian (early enteral nutrition); clinical pharmacy (resuscitation, VTE, antibiotic, reversal-agent dosing); case management + social work (disposition, funding, and hospital violence-intervention referral for penetrating/intentional injury); physiatry/PM&R for rehab planning; acute pain service; psychology/psychiatry (PTSD, substance use, withdrawal); palliative care for catastrophic injury; chaplaincy/family support.
monitoring_level: ICU for hemodynamic instability, ongoing transfusion, mechanical ventilation, vasopressors, ICP monitoring, open abdomen, or high organ-support burden; step-down/telemetry once resuscitated, off pressors, with a stable trajectory; floor when injuries are controlled, hemodynamics and respiration stable, and support minimal. Escalation triggers: recurrent hypotension or rising lactate, falling Hgb / ongoing transfusion requirement, GCS drop >=2, new hypoxia or respiratory failure, abdominal compartment syndrome (bladder pressure >20 mmHg with new organ dysfunction), oliguria/AKI, new arrhythmia, or sepsis — activate rapid-response and consider return to OR/angio.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Early enteral nutrition within 24-48h of resuscitation (trophic then advance as tolerated; hold or run low with open abdomen/ileus); glycemic target 140-180 mg/dL; early progressive mobilization/PT as fracture stability and hemodynamics allow; VTE — mechanical SCDs immediately, pharmacologic LMWH (enoxaparin, preferred over UFH per EAST) started within 24-48h once hemorrhage controlled and stable (including stable TBI on repeat imaging), with anti-Xa-guided dosing; IVC filter ONLY when pharmacologic prophylaxis is contraindicated in a high-risk patient (NOT prophylactic per EAST/WTA); remove central lines and urinary catheter as early as feasible to reduce CLABSI/CAUTI.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable off vasopressors and not transfusion-dependent; all injuries definitively managed or on a defined operative/follow-up plan; adequate respiratory status (extubated, acceptable oxygenation); pain controlled on an oral regimen; tolerating nutrition / adequate enteral intake; no uncontrolled infection (afebrile, source controlled/treated); VTE prophylaxis established with a defined duration; mental status at or near baseline or safe with the planned support; mobilizing at functional baseline or safe for the discharge setting; caregiver/rehabilitation plan in place.
discharge_planning: Disposition frequently to inpatient rehabilitation facility (IRF) or SNF given multi-system injury and deconditioning; home with home-health PT/OT if functional; medication reconciliation with explicit opioid stewardship/taper plan and VTE-prophylaxis duration; scheduled trauma-clinic follow-up in 1-2 weeks plus subspecialty visits (orthopedics, neurosurgery, others) and interval imaging; wound/ostomy/negative-pressure care arrangements; structured patient/caregiver education (injury, warning signs, return precautions, activity and weight-bearing restrictions); TBI cognitive-rehabilitation and post-concussion follow-up; hospital-based violence-intervention referral for intentional/penetrating injury; PCP linkage and warm transitions-of-care handoff.
expected_los: Highly tier-dependent across this collapsed group; blended CMS GMLOS roughly 5-9 days. Approximate by DRG: MCC tiers (955 craniotomy, 957 other-OR-procedure-with-MCC, 963 medical-with-MCC) ~8-11 days; CC tiers (958, 964) ~5-7 days; without CC/MCC (959, 965) ~3-4 days; limb reattachment/hip-femur procedures (956) ~6-8 days. Severe polytrauma (ISS >15) typically 8-14 days total with ~3-6 ICU days.
target_los: Optimized best-practice target ~6-9 days blended (MCC tiers ~8-10d, CC ~4-6d, without CC/MCC ~2-3d) — achieved through early hemorrhage control, protocolized damage-control resuscitation that reduces transfusion volume and organ failure, 'early appropriate care' timing of definitive fixation, early enteral nutrition, timely VTE prophylaxis, ventilator- and ICU-liberation bundles, and early rehabilitation/disposition planning to eliminate avoidable ICU and discharge-delay days.
quality_metrics: ACS TQIP risk-adjusted mortality/morbidity benchmarking and Trauma Quality Programs (TQP) verification; time-to-hemorrhage-control and time-to-OR; massive-transfusion ratio compliance (>=1:1.5, target 1:1:1); TXA-within-3h rate; VTE-prophylaxis initiation timing and rate plus VTE/PE incidence; tertiary-survey completion / missed-injury rate; ICU LOS and ventilator days; unplanned reintubation and unplanned return to OR; CLABSI/CAUTI/VAP and pressure-injury rates; failure-to-rescue; 30-day mortality and 30-day readmission; over-/under-triage rates; open-fracture antibiotic timeliness; adherence to institutional MTP and DCR protocols.
common_complications: Hemorrhagic shock with trauma-induced coagulopathy (mitigate: DCR, 1:1:1 or whole blood, TXA, viscoelastic-guided products, avoid lethal triad); nosocomial/aspiration pneumonia and VAP (vent bundle, HOB elevation, early mobility, SLP); VTE/PE (early LMWH plus SCDs); ARDS (lung-protective ventilation, conservative fluids); abdominal compartment syndrome (open-abdomen management, bladder-pressure monitoring); AKI and rhabdomyolysis (perfusion, avoid nephrotoxins); surgical-site/intra-abdominal infection and sepsis (source control, antibiotic stewardship); delirium and alcohol/substance withdrawal (protocols, CIWA); missed injuries (tertiary survey); fat-embolism syndrome, pressure injury, and multi-organ failure.
readmission_drivers: Surgical-site / deep wound infection and dehiscence; post-discharge VTE/PE; missed or under-treated injuries; inadequate pain control and opioid-related complications; orthopedic hardware failure/nonunion; incisional hernia; nutritional deficits and functional decline/falls; failed outpatient follow-up and fragmented care; mental-health sequelae (PTSD, depression) and substance relapse; recurrent intentional injury (penetrating). Prevention levers: structured trauma-clinic follow-up, wound-care coordination, continued VTE prophylaxis, medication reconciliation, timely rehab placement, opioid stewardship, and hospital-based violence-intervention programs.
guideline_source: American College of Surgeons Committee on Trauma — ATLS Advanced Trauma Life Support 10th edition (2018) and Resources for Optimal Care of the Injured Patient / TQIP Best Practice Guidelines; European guideline on management of major bleeding and coagulopathy following trauma, 6th edition (Rossaint et al., 2023); Brain Trauma Foundation Guidelines for the Management of Severe Traumatic Brain Injury, 4th edition (Carney et al., 2017); Eastern Association for the Surgery of Trauma (EAST) and Western Trauma Association (WTA) practice-management guidelines; AAST/ACS-COT clinical protocol for damage-control resuscitation for the adult trauma patient (2024).

European guideline on management of major bleeding and coagulopathy following trauma, 6th ed. Crit Care 2023;27:80 (PMID 36859355; DOI 10.1186/s13054-023-04327-7); CRASH-2, Shakur/Roberts et al. evidence_grade: Mixed but overall Moderate-to-Strong. STRONG (Class I, multiple RCTs) for the hemostatic-resuscitation core: TXA within 3h (CRASH-2/CRASH-3), balanced ratio/DCR (PROPPR), prehospital plasma (PAMPer), restrictive post-control transfusion; STRONG society-guideline backing (ACS-COT/ATLS, European 6th ed, BTF 4th ed for TBI thresholds ICP >22, CPP 60-70). MODERATE-to-consensus/observational for operative sequencing (damage-control surgery, 'early appropriate care' timing of definitive fixation), exact permissive-hypotension targets, and system-of-care/tertiary-survey elements. The heterogeneous injury mix precludes a single uniform high-grade recommendation.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: This DRG cluster collapses an enormous severity range, and MDC-24 status already denotes >=2 significant injuries. Without CC/MCC (959, 965): lower physiologic burden, often brief or no ICU, LOS ~3-4d, low mortality. With CC (958, 964): moderate organ/physiologic derangement, step-down care, LOS ~5-7d. With MCC (957, 963) and the procedure-defined DRGs (955 craniotomy, 956 limb-reattachment/hip-femur): hemorrhagic shock, severe TBI/coma, respiratory failure/ARDS, sepsis, AKI, and massive transfusion drive ICU + ventilator care, LOS ~8-14+d, and markedly higher mortality, complication, and readmission rates. Higher ISS and the Berlin physiologic parameters (hypotension, GCS <=8, base deficit >=6, coagulopathy, age >=70) map predictably to the MCC tier and drive ICU need, LOS, and cost.
pathway_pearls: 1) Stop the bleeding first: protocolized damage-control resuscitation (permissive hypotension EXCEPT in TBI, 1:1:1 or low-titer whole blood, TXA <3h, minimal crystalloid, prevent hypothermia/acidosis/coagulopathy) plus rapid source control (OR/angio/pelvic binder) outweighs every other early intervention. 2) Damage control THEN 'early appropriate care': never pursue definitive fixation in an unresuscitated patient — do abbreviated surgery, restore physiology (lactate/base deficit/coagulation/temperature), then definitive orthopedic/spine fixation. 3) Tertiary survey within 24h — a formal head-to-toe re-exam catches the ~10% of injuries missed on primary/secondary survey and prevents missed-injury morbidity and readmission. 4) When 'pop the clot' meets 'drain the brain,' TBI wins: with concurrent hemorrhage and brain injury, maintain SBP >=110 mmHg and CPP 60-70 rather than permissive hypotension.
