admission_criteria: Most spontaneous/induced abortion and early pregnancy loss (EPL) is managed outpatient/ED/observation; INPATIENT admission (DRG 770/779) is reserved for: hemodynamic instability or significant hemorrhage (tachycardia >110, SBP <90, orthostasis, falling Hgb, transfusion requirement); septic/infected abortion with SIRS/sepsis (fever >=38C, leukocytosis, purulent/foul discharge, uterine or adnexal tenderness); hemodynamically significant retained products of conception; failed or complicated outpatient medical management with heavy bleeding; second-trimester loss or D&E requiring inpatient care; suspected uterine perforation or bowel/visceral injury; molar pregnancy with complications; or high-risk comorbidity (severe anemia Hgb <7, coagulopathy, cardiac disease). Observation status is appropriate for stable heavy bleeding or uncomplicated procedural recovery.
risk_stratification: No abortion-specific validated score exists; borrow sepsis and hemorrhage tools. qSOFA >=2 of (RR >=22, SBP <=100, GCS <15) or SOFA rise >=2 -> flag sepsis and trigger ICU/critical-care evaluation in septic abortion; SIRS >=2 criteria flags infection; shock index (HR/SBP) >0.9-1.0 flags occult hemorrhagic shock and prompts type & crossmatch/transfusion readiness; hemodynamic thresholds HR >110 or SBP <90 escalate resuscitation; restrictive transfusion trigger Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if symptomatic/cardiac); lactate >=2 mmol/L (repeat if >=4) drives sepsis bundle; serial quantitative beta-hCG trend risk-stratifies pregnancy of unknown location (exclude ectopic) and molar surveillance.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential (anemia, leukocytosis); blood type & Rh(D) plus antibody screen and crossmatch if bleeding (note 2024 ACOG update: Rh testing/RhIg NOT indicated for loss <12 0/7 wks); quantitative beta-hCG (viability/PUL/molar trend); coagulation panel PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen if hemorrhage/DIC/sepsis; CMP for renal/hepatic function; lactate (>=2 repeat, >=4 = tissue hypoperfusion) in suspected sepsis; blood cultures x2 plus endocervical/endometrial cultures and GC/chlamydia NAAT drawn BEFORE antibiotics in septic abortion; urinalysis; confirmatory urine/serum pregnancy test; DIC/hemolysis workup (D-dimer, haptoglobin, smear) if clostridial infection suspected.
initial_imaging_dx: Transvaginal ultrasound is the primary diagnostic at presentation: confirm nonviable intrauterine pregnancy by ACOG/SRU criteria (crown-rump length >=7 mm with no cardiac activity; mean gestational sac diameter >=25 mm with no embryo; no embryo with heartbeat >=11 days after a scan showing sac + yolk sac, or >=2 weeks after a scan showing sac alone), identify retained products, hematometra, or molar (snowstorm) pattern, and assess for free fluid/hemoperitoneum; pelvic/speculum exam for cervical os, products at os, tenderness. CT abdomen/pelvis expedited if suspected uterine perforation, tubo-ovarian/myometrial abscess, or bowel injury; CXR if septic/respiratory compromise; serial beta-hCG for pregnancy of unknown location to exclude ectopic.
time_critical_interventions: Septic abortion is an obstetric sepsis emergency -> Surviving Sepsis Campaign bundle: broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1 hour, blood cultures before antibiotics, serum lactate, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4 mmol/L, vasopressors (norepinephrine) to MAP >=65 mmHg if fluid-refractory, repeat lactate to guide clearance; SOURCE CONTROL is definitive therapy -> prompt uterine evacuation of infected retained products as soon as the patient is resuscitated/stabilized (hours, do not delay for full defervescence). Hemorrhage: two large-bore IVs, activate massive transfusion protocol if massive, tranexamic acid 1 g IV, uterotonics, and urgent surgical evacuation/tamponade. Recognize and treat clostridial or Group A strep toxic shock without delay.
initial_management: Surgical (DRG 770): suction/vacuum aspiration or D&C with prophylactic doxycycline 200 mg PO before procedure (ACOG); uterotonics (misoprostol, oxytocin, methylergonovine) and TXA 1 g IV for bleeding. Medical (DRG 779): mifepristone 200 mg PO then misoprostol 800 mcg vaginal 24 h later (PreFaiR: 83.8% complete expulsion vs 67.1% misoprostol alone, RR 1.25), repeat misoprostol PRN. Septic: broad-spectrum IV antibiotics -- clindamycin 900 mg q8h + gentamicin 5 mg/kg/day +/- ampicillin 2 g q4-6h, OR ampicillin-sulbactam / piperacillin-tazobactam, with anaerobic coverage (metronidazole) -- PLUS prompt evacuation for source control. Restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if cardiac/symptomatic); IV crystalloid resuscitation; NSAID analgesia. Rh(D) immune globulin ONLY if >=12 wks and Rh-negative/unsensitized (300 mcg IM); NOT routine <12 wks per 2024 ACOG update.
day1_milestones: Stabilize hemodynamics and reverse shock; achieve complete uterine evacuation/source control; initiate antibiotics with de-escalation plan (septic); transfuse to target if indicated and confirm hemostasis (bleeding controlled, Hgb stable); pain controlled on oral analgesics; send products of conception to pathology (confirm chorionic villi, exclude ectopic/gestational trophoblastic disease); if molar suspected obtain baseline hCG and CXR; begin contraception counseling and offer immediate postabortion LARC; initiate emotional/bereavement support and chaplaincy as desired.
day2_milestones: Afebrile >=24 h with downtrending WBC (septic); hemodynamically stable with Hgb stable off transfusion; transition IV to oral antibiotics; bleeding minimal (<1 pad/hr); tolerating regular diet and ambulating independently; repeat ultrasound only if concern for retained products or ongoing bleeding; confirm pathology shows villi; reinforce and, if desired, place LARC before discharge; most uncomplicated cases reach discharge readiness.
day3plus_milestones: Reserved for complicated cases (septic shock, uterine perforation/laparotomy, transfusion-dependent hemorrhage, ICU stay): continued source control/abscess drainage, sepsis resolution (off vasopressors, lactate normalized, afebrile, WBC improving), post-operative recovery, and hemoglobin recovery with iron; document complete evacuation clinically/sonographically; for molar pregnancy establish serial hCG surveillance schedule and strict pregnancy-avoidance counseling; ensure clinical resolution and hemodynamic stability before discharge.
consults_multidisciplinary: OB-Gyn / Family Planning as primary service; Maternal-Fetal Medicine or gyn specialist for second-trimester or complex loss; Gynecologic Oncology for molar/gestational trophoblastic disease; General Surgery for suspected bowel injury/perforation; Interventional Radiology for uterine artery embolization in refractory hemorrhage; Critical Care and Infectious Disease for septic shock/antibiotic tailoring; Anesthesia for operative evacuation; Blood Bank for transfusion/MTP; Pharmacy for antimicrobial stewardship and reversal; Social Work plus behavioral health/chaplaincy for pregnancy-loss grief; Care Management for disposition and contraception access.
monitoring_level: Floor/obstetric unit for stable procedural recovery or uncomplicated medical management; telemetry/step-down for significant hemorrhage requiring transfusion or early sepsis; ICU for septic shock (vasopressor need, lactate >=4, qSOFA >=2, respiratory failure/ARDS, DIC) or hemorrhagic shock/massive transfusion. Escalation triggers: persistent hypotension after 30 mL/kg crystalloid, rising or non-clearing lactate, ongoing transfusion requirement, oliguria/AKI, worsening mental status, or signs of clostridial/GAS toxic shock.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Advance to regular diet as tolerated once recovered from anesthesia/procedure; early same-day ambulation. VTE risk is generally low with short stay but pregnancy is prothrombotic -- apply mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs) and add pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin/UFH) for septic, ICU, or prolonged-immobility patients per risk assessment. Remove peripheral IV and any urinary catheter promptly after procedure/recovery to limit infection; glucose control if diabetic or septic; oral iron (and IV iron if severe) for anemia recovery.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable with normal vitals and no orthostasis; bleeding controlled (light, not exceeding one pad/hour); hemoglobin stable and acceptable; afebrile >=24 h with clinical improvement and downtrending WBC in septic cases; complete uterine evacuation confirmed clinically or sonographically; pain controlled on oral medication; tolerating oral intake and oral antibiotics; Rh status addressed (RhIg given only if >=12 wks and Rh-negative); contraception plan established; patient understands warning signs and follow-up.
discharge_planning: Disposition home; medication reconciliation (completing oral antibiotic course for septic cases, iron, analgesics, contraception/LARC); follow-up with OB-Gyn/family planning within 1-2 weeks (sooner if septic/complex); molar pregnancy -> scheduled serial hCG surveillance with strict contraception and pregnancy-avoidance counseling; explicit return precautions (bleeding >2 pads/hr for 2 h, fever >=38C, severe or worsening pelvic pain, foul discharge -> retained products/endometritis); provide bereavement resources and support-group referral; confirm pathology review and, when indicated, trend hCG to nondetectable to exclude persistent trophoblastic disease or missed ectopic.
expected_los: Short-stay obstetric DRGs. Approximate CMS MS-DRG benchmarks (vary by fiscal-year version): DRG 770 (abortion WITH D&C/aspiration/hysterotomy) GMLOS ~1.6-1.9 days; DRG 779 (abortion WITHOUT D&C, captures medical management and many missed/incomplete/septic cases treated without surgery) GMLOS ~2.2-2.5 days. Typical overall inpatient stay 1-3 days; many procedural cases are same-day/observation and become inpatient only when complicated.
target_los: Uncomplicated surgical evacuation or completed medical management: <1-2 days, favoring observation/outpatient and avoiding unnecessary inpatient conversion; septic abortion with timely source control: 2-3 days (discharge once afebrile >=24 h and transitioned to oral antibiotics); complicated (perforation/laparotomy, septic or hemorrhagic shock requiring ICU): individualized 3-5+ days.
quality_metrics: For septic abortion: CMS SEP-1 severe sepsis/septic shock bundle compliance (lactate, blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg fluids, vasopressors, repeat lactate) and timely source control. Rh stewardship: concordance with 2024 ACOG guidance (avoid low-value Rh testing/RhIg for losses <12 wks). Restrictive blood utilization/transfusion appropriateness; reaspiration/completeness-of-evacuation rate; 30-day readmission for retained products or endometritis; immediate postabortion contraception (LARC) uptake; VTE prophylaxis appropriateness; documented bereavement support and patient-reported experience.
common_complications: Retained products of conception -> repeat evacuation (mitigated by confirming complete evacuation; mifepristone pretreatment improves completeness); hemorrhage -> uterotonics, TXA, transfusion, tamponade/uterine artery embolization; endometritis/pelvic infection -> antibiotics plus prompt evacuation; uterine perforation during surgical evacuation -> observation vs laparoscopy/laparotomy; cervical laceration; hematometra; anesthesia complications; progression to septic or hemorrhagic shock with DIC; rarely intrauterine adhesions (Asherman syndrome); molar pregnancy progressing to persistent gestational trophoblastic disease.
readmission_drivers: Top drivers: retained products of conception/incomplete evacuation causing bleeding or infection; endometritis or persistent/undertreated sepsis; delayed or recurrent hemorrhage; missed ectopic or persistent gestational trophoblastic disease when hCG is not followed to resolution; inadequate pain control; failed medical management requiring subsequent procedure. Prevention levers: confirm complete evacuation, use the mifepristone+misoprostol regimen to lower failure, follow hCG to nondetectable when indicated, give clear return precautions and early follow-up, provide immediate contraception to avoid short-interval pregnancy, and treat anemia before discharge.
guideline_source: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 200, Early Pregnancy Loss (2018); ACOG Clinical Practice Update: Rh D Immune Globulin Administration After Abortion or Pregnancy Loss at Less Than 12 Weeks of Gestation (2024); ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 225, Medication Abortion Up to 70 Days of Gestation (2020); ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 135, Second-Trimester Abortion (2013, reaffirmed); Society of Family Planning clinical recommendations; Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 and CMS SEP-1 for septic abortion; Cochrane review, Antibiotics for Treating Septic Abortion (2016).
key_citations: Per PubMed: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 200, Early Pregnancy Loss, Obstet Gynecol 2018;132:e197-e207 (PMID 30157093, doi 10.1097/AOG.0000000000002899); Schreiber CA et al., Mifepristone Pretreatment for the Medical Management of Early Pregnancy Loss (PreFaiR RCT), N Engl J Med 2018;378:2161-2170 (PMID 29874535, doi 10.1056/NEJMoa1715726); ACOG Clinical Practice Update: Rh D Immune Globulin <12 Weeks, Obstet Gynecol 2024;144:e140-e143 (PMID 39255498, doi 10.1097/AOG.0000000000005733); Udoh A et al., Antibiotics for treating septic abortion, Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2016;7:CD011528 (PMID 27364644, doi 10.1002/14651858.CD011528.pub2); Nagendra D et al., Medical management is cost-effective vs uterine aspiration, Am J Obstet Gynecol 2022;227:737.e1 (PMID 35780811, doi 10.1016/j.ajog.2022.06.054).
evidence_grade: Strong for the pathway backbone -- ACOG Level A recommendations for ultrasound diagnostic criteria and for offering expectant/medical/surgical options, reinforced by a high-quality RCT (PreFaiR/NEJM) establishing mifepristone+misoprostol superiority, and by strong sepsis source-control principles (Surviving Sepsis Campaign). Moderate/consensus for the exact septic-abortion antibiotic regimen, which rests on old, small, low-to-moderate-quality Cochrane RCTs plus extrapolation from general sepsis guidelines. LOS benchmarks are administrative CMS estimates (approximate). Overall: Strong for framework, Moderate for septic-specific antimicrobial choice and LOS targets.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: This DRG family is split by PROCEDURE, not by CC/MCC tier: 770 = abortion WITH D&C/aspiration/hysterotomy, 779 = abortion WITHOUT D&C -- neither subdivides into with/without CC/MCC. Real-world acuity is therefore driven by presentation, not the DRG label: uncomplicated evacuation or completed medical management stays on the floor for 1-2 days, whereas sepsis/septic shock, hemorrhage requiring transfusion, DIC, uterine perforation, or acute kidney injury (the CC/MCC-level diagnoses) drive step-down/ICU care, LOS of 3-5+ days, and higher cost. Second-trimester and molar cases skew more complex; septic/hemorrhagic complications are the true severity multipliers even though the collapsed DRG does not encode them.
pathway_pearls: (1) In septic/infected abortion, source control IS the treatment -- run the Surviving Sepsis bundle (antibiotics <1 h, cultures before antibiotics, lactate, 30 mL/kg fluids) AND promptly evacuate the infected retained products; antibiotics alone will not cure it. (2) Use mifepristone 200 mg pretreatment before misoprostol 800 mcg vaginal for medical management -- it raises complete expulsion to ~84% and roughly halves the need for aspiration (PreFaiR). (3) Stop reflexive Rh testing/RhIg for pregnancy loss or abortion <12 weeks (2024 ACOG update) -- it is low-value; reserve RhIg for >=12 weeks in Rh-negative patients. (4) Confirm complete evacuation and follow hCG to resolution to catch retained products, persistent gestational trophoblastic disease, or a missed ectopic (the leading readmission drivers), and provide immediate postabortion contraception plus bereavement support before discharge.

scope_and_volume_notes: Abortion with D&C/aspiration (770) and without (779); includes missed/incomplete/septic abortion.

admission_criteria: Planned/urgent INPATIENT surgical admission (expected >=2 midnights) — distinct from primary TJA which is now often same-day/outpatient. Objective indications for revision surgery: confirmed implant failure — aseptic loosening (radiographic component migration/subsidence, progressive osteolysis), polyethylene/bearing wear, recurrent instability/dislocation, component malposition, periprosthetic fracture (Vancouver B2/B3 hip; Rorabeck/Su type II-III knee), extensor mechanism failure; OR periprosthetic joint infection (PJI) meeting 2018 ICM/MSIS criteria. Urgent/emergent admission: acute PJI with sepsis, unstable periprosthetic fracture, irreducible dislocation, prosthetic loosening with impending fracture. Inpatient (vs outpatient) justified by anticipated extended OR time (>2-3h), significant blood loss/transfusion likelihood, 2-stage complexity, and comorbidity burden. Not for ED discharge/observation — revision is definitive OR-based care.
risk_stratification: ASA physical status (III-IV -> preop optimization + anesthesia/medicine co-management); Charlson Comorbidity Index (higher -> longer LOS, mortality risk); Revised Cardiac Risk Index RCRI (>=2 -> cardiac workup, troponin/BNP, cardiology); modified Frailty Index mFI-5 (>=2 -> prehab, elevated complication/discharge-to-SNF risk); ACS-NSQIP Surgical Risk Calculator (individualized morbidity/mortality/LOS); glycemic: HbA1c >7.5-8% -> defer/optimize (PJI risk); nutrition: albumin <3.5 g/dL, total lymphocyte count <1500/uL, prealbumin/transferrin <200 -> optimize (wound/PJI risk); BMI >40 (higher SSI/complication); Caprini score for VTE risk tier. INFECTION DIAGNOSIS: 2018 ICM/MSIS score — aggregate >=6 = infected (drives 1-stage vs 2-stage decision).
initial_workup_labs: CBC with baseline Hgb (transfusion planning); TYPE & SCREEN/CROSSMATCH (transfusion likely in revision); CMP (renal/hepatic/glucose); coagulation PT/INR/aPTT; HbA1c; nutrition panel — albumin/prealbumin, total lymphocyte count; INFECTION SCREEN before every revision: ESR (>30 mm/h) + CRP (>1 mg/dL) + D-dimer (>860 ng/mL) — any elevation triggers PJI workup; urinalysis + urine culture (treat UTI before implant surgery); MRSA/MSSA nasal PCR screen -> decolonization (mupirocin + chlorhexidine). If PJI suspected: preoperative joint ASPIRATION with synovial WBC (>3000/uL), PMN% (>80%), leukocyte esterase, alpha-defensin, synovial CRP, and culture — HOLD antibiotics until cultures obtained. Metal ion levels (Co/Cr) if metal-on-metal.
initial_imaging_dx: Weight-bearing plain radiographs (AP/lateral + full-length views) FIRST-LINE — assess loosening, osteolysis, component position/migration, periprosthetic fracture; compare serial films for migration. Preoperative joint ASPIRATION/arthrocentesis with synovial analysis is the key diagnostic when PJI suspected (per 2018 ICM). Advanced imaging for equivocal cases: CT (bone-loss quantification/AAOS defect classification, component rotation), MARS-MRI (adverse local tissue reaction/pseudotumor in metal-on-metal), triple-phase bone scan or WBC-labeled leukocyte/SPECT-CT for occult loosening vs infection. Intraoperative: frozen-section histology (>5 PMN/HPF), Gram stain, and >=3-5 deep cultures held 5-14 days (plus explant sonication) to confirm/exclude PJI.
time_critical_interventions: Surgical antibiotic prophylaxis within 60 min before incision (cefazolin; vancomycin within 120 min) — SCIP timing; CRITICAL EXCEPTION: in suspected PJI, HOLD prophylactic antibiotics until intraoperative deep cultures obtained. Intravenous tranexamic acid at/before incision (1 g or 15 mg/kg). Maintain intraoperative normothermia (>=36C) and glucose <180-200 mg/dL. Intraoperative cefazolin redosing q4h or after >1500 mL blood loss. For acute PJI with septic shock: sepsis bundle — broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1h, lactate, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, blood cultures (SEP-1) — with urgent surgical SOURCE CONTROL (debridement/DAIR). This is largely elective surgery, so classic door-to-needle/balloon targets do not apply; the time-critical levers are prophylaxis timing, TXA, and source control.
initial_management: Antibiotic prophylaxis: 1st-gen cephalosporin (cefazolin 2 g, 3 g if >120 kg) +/- vancomycin 15 mg/kg if MRSA-colonized/beta-lactam allergy. Antifibrinolytic: tranexamic acid IV 1 g pre-incision + 1 g at closure (or topical/combined) — reduces transfusion. Anesthesia: neuraxial/SPINAL preferred over general (less blood loss, VTE, delirium — ERAS). Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia: scheduled acetaminophen + NSAID/COX-2 (celecoxib) + periarticular local infiltration analgesia (ropivacaine/bupivacaine cocktail) + peripheral nerve block (adductor-canal for knee) +/- dexamethasone; gabapentinoids selectively. Transfusion: RESTRICTIVE threshold Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if symptomatic/cardiac disease) per AABB. Avoid routine drains and urinary catheters. For PJI: pathogen-directed IV antibiotics after cultures (empiric vancomycin + antipseudomonal beta-lactam e.g. cefepime/pip-tazo) with SOURCE CONTROL — DAIR (acute <3-4 wks, well-fixed implant), 1-stage, or 2-stage exchange with antibiotic-loaded spacer per IDSA.
day1_milestones: POD0 (surgery day): neuraxial anesthesia + IV TXA + periarticular local infiltration delivered intraop; recover in PACU. EARLY MOBILIZATION same day — get patient upright/ambulating within hours of surgery (ERAS accelerates discharge). Start multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia. Resume diet within hours with PONV prophylaxis (minimize fasting; preop carbohydrate loading). Initiate VTE prophylaxis (mechanical IPCs + pharmacologic per protocol). Avoid/remove urinary catheter; voiding trial. Check post-op Hgb; neurovascular limb checks; monitor output. For PJI cases: confirm intraoperative deep cultures sent, then start empiric IV antibiotics; place antibiotic spacer if 2-stage.
day2_milestones: POD1: physical therapy + occupational therapy BID; advance mobility — ambulate with assistive device, transfer training, stair assessment; weight-bearing as tolerated UNLESS surgeon restriction (extended trochanteric osteotomy, allograft/strut, ORIF, constrained construct). Transition IV to ORAL analgesia. Continue VTE prophylaxis. Recheck Hgb/electrolytes/renal function; transfuse only if Hgb <7 (or symptomatic). Wound inspection. Confirm spontaneous voiding; remove any residual lines/catheter. Reinforce discharge plan and arrange DME (walker, raised toilet seat). Many single-stage aseptic revisions are discharge-ready by POD1-2.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ to discharge: continue PT to functional discharge milestones — independent/supervised transfers, ambulation ~100+ ft with device, safe stair negotiation and ADLs. Wound clean/dry/intact without concerning drainage or spreading erythema. Pain controlled on oral regimen. Hemodynamically stable Hgb without ongoing transfusion need. Bowel function returned. For PJI/2-stage: verify spacer stability, finalize ID-directed antibiotic plan, place PICC and arrange OPAT (outpatient parenteral antibiotics ~6 wks), monitor renal function and vancomycin trough/AKI. Discharge when medical + functional milestones met — aseptic typically POD2-3; septic/2-stage longer.
consults_multidisciplinary: Preoperative anesthesia/optimization clinic (prehab, anemia and glycemic optimization) before admission. Physical therapy + occupational therapy from POD0. Case management/discharge planner engaged pre-admission (disposition, DME, home health). Infectious Diseases — MANDATORY for PJI (antibiotic selection/duration, OPAT). Hospitalist/internal medicine co-management (or geriatrics) for frail/multimorbid patients. Clinical pharmacy (antimicrobial stewardship, anticoagulation, renal dosing, vancomycin kinetics). Nutrition (albumin <3.5 / malnutrition). Social work (support, placement). Cardiology if RCRI high; hematology for anemia/complex anticoagulation.
monitoring_level: Most aseptic single-stage revisions: surgical/ortho FLOOR with routine vitals + serial neurovascular limb checks. Telemetry/step-down for significant cardiac history, large-volume blood loss, hemodynamic lability, or frail elderly. ICU reserved for PJI with septic shock, massive transfusion, or major comorbidity decompensation. Escalation triggers: hypotension/tachycardia (hemorrhage), acute hypoxia/dyspnea (PE or fat embolism), sepsis (qSOFA >=2), oliguria/AKI, limb neurovascular compromise, or uncontrolled pain.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: minimize preoperative fasting with carbohydrate loading; resume regular diet POD0 with PONV prophylaxis. Mobility: mobilize DAY OF SURGERY; PT BID; weight-bearing as tolerated unless surgeon-specified restriction (osteotomy/allograft/ORIF/constrained). VTE: mechanical IPCs + risk-stratified pharmacologic prophylaxis — aspirin 81 mg BID for standard-risk, but revision/high-risk favor LMWH enoxaparin 40 mg daily or DOAC (rivaroxaban 10 mg) given CRISTAL showed enoxaparin superior to aspirin (symptomatic VTE 1.82% vs 3.45%); duration 14 days (knee) to 35 days (hip). Glucose control <180 mg/dL (insulin). Early urinary catheter removal POD0-1 (prevent CAUTI/hematogenous seeding); remove IV lines/PICC when no longer needed.
discharge_criteria: Afebrile with stable vital signs; pain controlled on ORAL analgesics; tolerating diet, spontaneously voiding, bowel function returning; wound clean/dry/intact without concerning drainage or spreading erythema; hemodynamically stable Hgb with no active transfusion requirement; PT functional milestones met — safe independent/supervised transfers, ambulation with assistive device, stair negotiation as needed, and independent ADLs; safe discharge environment confirmed. For PJI: functioning OPAT/PICC access with ID follow-up and antibiotic plan in place.
discharge_planning: Disposition: HOME with home health + outpatient PT preferred (better outcomes and lower cost than SNF); SNF/inpatient rehab reserved for frail patients, inadequate support, or complex 2-stage cases. Medication reconciliation: oral analgesic taper plan, VTE prophylaxis prescription with explicit duration (14-35 days), and OPAT antibiotic regimen for PJI. Follow-up: surgeon at ~2 weeks (wound/suture check), ID clinic for PJI with serial ESR/CRP trending, labs as indicated. Patient education: wound care, infection and VTE warning signs, weight-bearing status, and hip dislocation precautions. Transitions of care: warm handoff to home health/OPAT, DME delivery, fall/dislocation precautions, and a follow-up phone touchpoint.
expected_los: Approximately 3-5 days for aseptic single-stage revision (higher acuity than primary TJA, which is now ~1 day/outpatient); 7-12 days for septic/2-stage revision (published mean ~11-12 days for infected revision). CMS MS-DRG geometric-mean-LOS benchmarks (approximate): DRG 468 no CC/MCC ~2.3-2.5 days; DRG 467 with CC ~3.3 days; DRG 466 with MCC ~5.5-6 days. Blended expected LOS ~4 days.
target_los: Under an ideal ERAS pathway: 1-3 days for aseptic single-stage revision (same-day mobilization, TXA, restrictive transfusion, opioid-sparing analgesia, home disposition). 5-7 days for septic first-stage/2-stage revision (irreducible floor of spacer placement + IV antibiotic initiation + OPAT setup). Target ~2 days blended for the aseptic-predominant population.
quality_metrics: Surgical antibiotic prophylaxis timing/selection/discontinuation (<=24h) — SCIP-Inf; appropriate VTE prophylaxis (VTE-1/VTE-6); perioperative normothermia; blood-management/transfusion rate; NHSN deep surgical-site infection / PJI rate; CMS Hospital-level THA/TKA complication (RSCR) and 30-day readmission measures; 90-day readmission and return-to-OR; PSI-90 patient-safety composite; mortality; ERAS protocol-compliance bundle; patient-reported outcomes (HOOS/KOOS, PROMIS) reported to the AJRR registry. Note: revisions are excluded from the primary-TJA CJR bundle but are tracked in the American Joint Replacement Registry.
common_complications: Blood loss/postoperative anemia requiring transfusion (mitigate: TXA, neuraxial anesthesia, restrictive Hgb <7 threshold); surgical-site infection and periprosthetic joint infection (prophylactic antibiotics, MRSA decolonization, glucose <180, wound surveillance); dislocation/instability especially hip (dislocation precautions, dual-mobility/constrained constructs); periprosthetic fracture (meticulous technique, restricted weight-bearing); venous thromboembolism/PE (mechanical + pharmacologic prophylaxis); postoperative delirium in elderly (opioid- and benzodiazepine-sparing, orientation); acute kidney injury (avoid hypovolemia/nephrotoxins, vancomycin level monitoring); wound drainage/dehiscence; nerve/vascular injury.
readmission_drivers: Surgical-site infection / PJI — the leading driver (prevention: MRSA decolonization, perioperative glucose control, antimicrobial stewardship, early wound follow-up and surveillance); persistent wound drainage; dislocation/instability (precautions, appropriate constructs); venous thromboembolism (prophylaxis adherence and adequate duration); uncontrolled pain; and medical decompensation (cardiac, renal, sepsis). Prevention levers: structured discharge education, early (~2-week) wound follow-up, OPAT coordination for PJI, home health, transitions-of-care phone call, and telehealth check-ins.
guideline_source: ERAS Society Consensus — perioperative care in total hip/knee replacement (Wainwright TW, Ljungqvist O, et al.; Acta Orthopaedica 2020) for the perioperative pathway (largely extrapolated to revision). 2018 ICM/MSIS Definition of Periprosthetic Hip and Knee Infection (Parvizi J, et al.; J Arthroplasty 2018) for PJI diagnosis. IDSA Prosthetic Joint Infection Clinical Practice Guideline (Osmon DR, et al.; Clin Infect Dis 2013) for PJI management. AAOS / ASH-2019 / ICM-VTE + CRISTAL trial for thromboprophylaxis; AABB for restrictive transfusion. AAOS Appropriate Use Criteria for surgical indications.
key_citations: Wainwright TW, et al. Consensus statement for perioperative care in total hip and total knee replacement: ERAS Society recommendations. Acta Orthop 2020;91(1):3-19. PMID 31663402; DOI 10.1080/17453674.2019.1683790. Parvizi J, et al. The 2018 Definition of Periprosthetic Hip and Knee Infection: An Evidence-Based and Validated Criteria. J Arthroplasty 2018;33(5):1309-1314.e2. PMID 29551303; DOI 10.1016/j.arth.2018.02.078. Osmon DR, et al. Diagnosis and Management of Prosthetic Joint Infection: IDSA Clinical Practice Guidelines. Clin Infect Dis 2013;56(1):e1-e25. PMID 23223583; DOI 10.1093/cid/cis803. Sidhu VS, et al. Effect of Aspirin vs Enoxaparin on Symptomatic VTE After Hip/Knee Arthroplasty: CRISTAL Randomized Trial. JAMA 2022;328(8):719-727. PMID 35997730; DOI 10.1001/jama.2022.13416.
evidence_grade: Moderate. PJI diagnosis rests on the externally validated 2018 ICM/MSIS criteria (sensitivity 97.7%, specificity 99.5% — strong); thromboprophylaxis and TXA are supported by high-quality RCTs including CRISTAL. However, most ERAS perioperative elements are Class I/strong for PRIMARY arthroplasty and are extrapolated to revision, where dedicated RCT evidence is limited and much guidance is society-consensus/expert opinion (IDSA PJI management, ERAS revision-specific data). Overall: strong validated criteria + strong VTE evidence, but revision-specific pathway strength is Moderate due to extrapolation.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 468 (no CC/MCC): uncomplicated aseptic single-stage revision — floor care, GMLOS ~2.3 days, home disposition, ERAS-eligible for 1-2 day stay. DRG 467 (with CC): a comorbidity or single complication (e.g., post-op anemia with transfusion, UTI, minor arrhythmia) — GMLOS ~3.3 days. DRG 466 (with MCC): PJI with sepsis, acute respiratory or renal failure, major hemorrhage, MI — drives step-down/ICU utilization, IV antibiotics/OPAT, GMLOS ~5.5-6+ days, higher readmission and mortality, and more frequent SNF disposition. Septic/2-stage revisions concentrate in the MCC tier via sepsis/infection MCC codes and are the principal cost and LOS outliers of the collapsed group.
pathway_pearls: (1) In any suspected PJI, HOLD preoperative prophylactic antibiotics until intraoperative deep cultures are obtained — premature antibiotics sabotage the diagnosis and the entire treatment plan. (2) Preoperatively CONFIRM OR EXCLUDE infection using the 2018 ICM/MSIS score (aspiration + ESR/CRP/D-dimer) — it dictates one-stage vs two-stage exchange and shapes the whole pathway. (3) Bundle TXA + neuraxial anesthesia + restrictive transfusion (Hgb <7) to blunt the high blood loss of revision and avoid unnecessary transfusion. (4) Same-day mobilization + multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia + pre-admission discharge planning + (for PJI) early ID/OPAT coordination are the highest-yield LOS and readmission levers.

admission_criteria: Nearly all suspected/confirmed acute bacterial meningitis and encephalitis = inpatient admission (usually ICU); NOT an ED-discharge/observation condition. Admit for: meningism/CSF pleocytosis, positive CSF Gram stain, purulent CSF or CSF WBC >1000, GCS <15/altered sensorium, new seizure, focal neuro deficit, petechial/purpuric rash (meningococcemia), immunocompromise with meningism, suspected encephalitis (fever + altered mentation/behavioral change +/- seizure/focal signs). ICU admission for: GCS <=12 or declining, septic shock/hemodynamic instability, respiratory compromise/airway protection, status epilepticus, signs of raised ICP/cerebral edema, purpura fulminans/DIC. Only exception to admit-all: well-appearing immunocompetent adult with lymphocytic aseptic (enteroviral) meningitis, normal mentation, negative Gram stain, CSF lactate <3.5, low procalcitonin - candidate for short-stay/observation with close follow-up.
risk_stratification: Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): <=8 -> intubate + ICU; drop >=2 points -> escalate. Hasbun CT-before-LP criteria (NEJM 2001): age >=60, immunocompromise, prior CNS disease, seizure <1wk, papilledema, altered LOC, or focal deficit (gaze palsy, visual-field cut, facial palsy, arm/leg drift, aphasia, inability to answer 2 questions/follow 2 commands) -> CT head before LP; none present -> LP without CT. van de Beek/Dutch adverse-outcome predictors (NEJM 2004): advanced age, tachycardia, low admission GCS, positive blood culture, high ESR, thrombocytopenia, low CSF WBC, S. pneumoniae -> flag high mortality (pneumococcal ~30% vs meningococcal ~7%). CSF bacterial-vs-viral thresholds: WBC >1000 neutrophil-predominant, glucose <40 mg/dL or CSF:serum glucose <0.4, protein >200 mg/dL, lactate >=3.5 mmol/L -> treat as bacterial. qSOFA >=2/rising lactate -> sepsis bundle. Bacterial Meningitis Score (Nigrovic, pediatric): 0 predictors (CSF Gram+, CSF ANC>=1000, protein>=80, peripheral ANC>=10k, seizure) = very low bacterial risk.
initial_workup_labs: STAT before antibiotics but WITHOUT delaying them: 2 sets blood cultures; CBC with diff, CMP (paired serum glucose for CSF:serum ratio), PT/INR/aPTT + fibrinogen (DIC/LP safety), lactate, procalcitonin/CRP (elevated favors bacterial). CSF analysis (core): opening pressure, cell count + differential, glucose, protein, Gram stain, bacterial culture, CSF lactate; reserve extra tube. Rapid multiplex CSF PCR (BioFire FilmArray ME panel: HSV-1/2, VZV, enterovirus, S. pneumoniae, N. meningitidis, H. influenzae, L. monocytogenes, E. coli K1, HHV-6, CMV, parechovirus, Cryptococcus). HSV-1/2 + VZV PCR and enterovirus PCR for encephalitis. Cryptococcal antigen (serum + CSF) and HIV test if immunocompromised. TB workup (CSF AFB, mycobacterial culture, GeneXpert MTB/RIF, adenosine deaminase) if subacute/risk factors. Autoimmune-encephalitis antibody panel (anti-NMDA-R, LGI1, GABA-B) if PCR-negative encephalitis. Monitor sodium for SIADH.
initial_imaging_dx: LP is the priority diagnostic - obtain within ~1h of presentation; do NOT delay antibiotics for LP or CT. CT head BEFORE LP ONLY if Hasbun/IDSA criteria met (immunocompromise, prior CNS disease, new seizure <1wk, papilledema, focal deficit, altered LOC, age >=60); otherwise LP without imaging. If CT indicated, sequence: blood cultures -> dexamethasone + empiric antibiotics -> CT -> LP (so antibiotics are never delayed). MRI brain with contrast (preferred over CT for encephalitis) within 24h: temporal-lobe involvement/DWI restriction suggests HSV; also detects infarct, abscess, empyema, hydrocephalus. EEG if encephalitis/altered mentation/seizure - HSV shows temporal PLEDs, rules out nonconvulsive status epilepticus. CXR for concurrent pneumonia/source; CT sinuses/mastoids/temporal bones for parameningeal focus; fundoscopy for papilledema.
time_critical_interventions: Door-to-antibiotic time <=1 hour from arrival for suspected bacterial meningitis (ESCMID 2016) - each hour of delay increases mortality. Adjunctive dexamethasone 10 mg IV given 10-20 min BEFORE or WITH the first antibiotic dose (de Gans/van de Beek NEJM 2002) - must precede or accompany, never after. If CT needed first: blood cultures -> dexamethasone + antibiotics -> CT -> LP; never wait for CT/LP results to start antibiotics. Suspected encephalitis: start empiric IV acyclovir 10 mg/kg q8h within hours (early acyclovir cuts HSV mortality/morbidity - Whitley 1986), do not wait for PCR. Concurrent septic shock: Surviving Sepsis 1-hour bundle - lactate, cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid if hypotensive or lactate >=4, vasopressors for MAP >=65. Meningococcal suspicion: immediate droplet isolation, public-health notification, close-contact chemoprophylaxis. Herniation/raised ICP: head up 30 degrees, hyperosmolar therapy, emergent neurosurgery/EVD.
initial_management: Empiric antibiotics adults <50: vancomycin 15-20 mg/kg IV q8-12h (trough 15-20) + 3rd-gen cephalosporin (ceftriaxone 2 g IV q12h OR cefotaxime 2 g IV q4-6h). ADD ampicillin 2 g IV q4h for Listeria if age >50, immunocompromised, pregnant, or alcoholic. Post-neurosurgical/penetrating trauma/shunt: vancomycin + antipseudomonal beta-lactam (cefepime or meropenem or ceftazidime). Severe beta-lactam allergy: vancomycin + moxifloxacin (+ TMP-SMX for Listeria). Adjunctive dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg (~10 mg) IV q6h x4 days - continue only if S. pneumoniae (or H. influenzae in children) confirmed; discontinue for other organisms. Suspected/HSV encephalitis: acyclovir 10 mg/kg (ideal body weight) IV q8h x14-21d with IV hydration to prevent crystal nephropathy. De-escalate on culture: pen-susceptible pneumococcus -> penicillin G/ceftriaxone; meningococcus -> ceftriaxone/penicillin; Listeria -> ampicillin + gentamicin. Isotonic crystalloid (avoid hypotonic fluids - worsen edema/SIADH); seizures -> benzodiazepine + levetiracetam/fosphenytoin; raised ICP -> mannitol 0.25-1 g/kg or hypertonic saline, EVD for hydrocephalus; source control (drain empyema/abscess, remove infected CSF shunt/hardware per IDSA 2017).
day1_milestones: Antibiotics +/- acyclovir started <=1h with dexamethasone before/with first dose; 2 blood cultures + LP obtained (LP before or shortly after first dose). Airway secured if GCS <=8; disposition set (ICU vs step-down) with monitoring level and baseline neuro exam (GCS, focal deficits) documented for trending. CSF Gram stain + rapid ME-panel PCR reviewed -> narrow/adjust empiric therapy. Droplet precautions and public-health notification + close-contact chemoprophylaxis if meningococcal suspected. Consults placed: Infectious Diseases and Neurology (day 0-1), Critical Care if ICU, Neurosurgery/ENT if parameningeal source or hydrocephalus. Manage raised ICP, seizures, hemodynamics; correct coagulopathy/DIC. MRI + EEG if encephalitis; ensure acyclovir hydration.
day2_milestones: Review 24-48h cultures + susceptibilities -> de-escalate to pathogen-directed therapy; adjust vancomycin per trough/AUC. Confirm or STOP dexamethasone based on organism (continue full 4-day course only if pneumococcal/H. influenzae). Reassess acyclovir: continue if HSV PCR pending/positive; consider stopping if HSV PCR negative and alternative diagnosis established (with clinical judgment/repeat PCR). Neuro reassessment; repeat imaging if deteriorating (hydrocephalus, infarct, subdural empyema). Audiology referral for hearing screen (especially pneumococcal - sensorineural hearing-loss risk). De-escalate monitoring if stabilizing (ICU -> step-down), wean vasopressors. Monitor sodium for SIADH/cerebral salt wasting; begin nutrition and mobilization; confirm VTE prophylaxis.
day3plus_milestones: Continue pathogen-directed IV therapy for organism-specific duration: N. meningitidis 7d; H. influenzae 7d; S. pneumoniae 10-14d; Listeria >=21d; group B strep 14-21d; aerobic gram-negative bacilli 21d; HSV encephalitis 14-21d IV acyclovir (confirm CSF HSV PCR negative before stopping). Repeat LP at 48h if not improving, resistant pneumococcus on dexamethasone, gram-negative/Listeria, or neonatal - document CSF sterilization. Identify/treat complications: hydrocephalus (EVD/shunt), subdural empyema/abscess, refractory seizures, cognitive deficits, hearing loss. Arrange OPAT (PICC + home/SNF infusion) for prolonged courses to shorten inpatient LOS. Neuro-rehab, cognitive and hearing assessment before discharge. Advance toward discharge readiness: afebrile, hemodynamically and neurologically stable/improving, tolerating nutrition, source controlled.
consults_multidisciplinary: Infectious Diseases (early, day 0-1) - empiric/targeted regimen and duration. Neurology (day 0-1) - encephalitis, seizures/EEG, prognosis. Critical Care/intensivist (day 0) if ICU. Neurosurgery - hydrocephalus/EVD, ICP monitoring, abscess/empyema drainage, infected shunt/hardware removal. ENT - parameningeal source (otitis, mastoiditis, sinusitis). Audiology - hearing assessment (especially pneumococcal) before discharge. Ophthalmology if papilledema/visual symptoms. Clinical pharmacy - vancomycin/aminoglycoside dosing, renal-adjusted acyclovir, OPAT. PT/OT - early mobilization and functional/cognitive assessment. Dietitian - early enteral nutrition if intubated/poor intake. Care management + social work - OPAT setup, SNF/rehab placement, follow-up. Infection prevention/public health - reportable meningococcal disease and contact prophylaxis.
monitoring_level: ICU: GCS <=12 or declining, need for airway protection/ventilation, septic shock/vasopressors, status epilepticus, raised ICP/EVD in place, purpura fulminans/DIC, rapidly progressive course. Step-down/telemetry: hemodynamically stable but requiring frequent (q1-2h) neuro checks, resolving shock. Floor: neurologically stable/improving, afebrile trend, no hemodynamic support, tolerating orals. Escalation triggers: GCS drop >=2, new focal deficit, seizure, pupillary asymmetry/fixed pupil, Cushing response (bradycardia + hypertension), hypotension/rising lactate, respiratory failure, worsening rash/DIC -> immediate ICU transfer + neurosurgery for ICP management.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: NPO if depressed LOC/aspiration or intubation risk; advance as mentation clears; early enteral feeding via NG tube (within 24-48h) if prolonged intubation. Mobility: early PT/mobilization once hemodynamically and neurologically stable to prevent deconditioning. VTE prophylaxis: mechanical (SCDs) initially given LP/CNS/coagulopathy concerns; add pharmacologic (LMWH/UFH) once no active bleeding and coagulopathy corrected (~24-48h), important in immobilized septic patients. Glucose target 140-180 mg/dL, avoid hypo/hyperglycemia. Sodium/fluid: isotonic fluids only, monitor for SIADH and cerebral salt wasting, avoid hypotonic fluids (worsen cerebral edema). Lines/catheters: remove central lines/Foley as soon as feasible with daily necessity review; convert to PICC for OPAT.
discharge_criteria: Afebrile >=24-48h and hemodynamically stable off vasopressors. Neurologically stable or improving (GCS and deficits documented and trending better; controlled seizures). Pathogen-directed antibiotic/antiviral course established with a clear plan to complete (inpatient or OPAT). CSF sterilization documented if a repeat LP was indicated. No uncontrolled complications (hydrocephalus managed, empyema/abscess drained). Tolerating oral intake/nutrition with adequate functional status or a rehab plan in place. Hearing and cognitive assessment addressed; follow-up appointments arranged.
discharge_planning: Disposition: home with OPAT (PICC + home infusion) to complete remaining IV course; SNF/LTAC or inpatient neuro-rehab if functional/cognitive deficits or post-encephalitis sequelae. Medication reconciliation: remaining IV antibiotic/antiviral course, anticonvulsants if seizures, dexamethasone completion/taper if applicable. Follow-up: ID clinic 1-2 wks with weekly OPAT labs (CBC, renal function, vancomycin levels); Neurology (encephalitis/seizure/cognition); Audiology (hearing, especially pneumococcal); PCP. Patient education: complete full course, seizure precautions, red-flag return criteria (recurrent fever, headache, neuro change). Vaccinate (pneumococcal, meningococcal, Hib) and evaluate for immune deficiency (complement, asplenia, HIV) if recurrent; confirm meningococcal close-contact chemoprophylaxis (rifampin/ciprofloxacin/ceftriaxone) and public-health reporting. Warm handoff to OPAT team with explicit stop date.
expected_los: Group range approximately 4-10 days by severity tier and pathogen (approximate CMS IPPS GMLOS: DRG 094 bacterial/TB with MCC ~7-8d; 095 with CC ~5-6d; 096 without CC/MCC ~4d; DRG 097 non-bacterial with MCC ~6d; 098 with CC ~4-5d; 099 without CC/MCC ~3d). Note the full IV course (7-21d bacterial; 14-21d HSV encephalitis; months for TB meningitis) often extends beyond the acute inpatient stay and is completed via OPAT; severe pneumococcal/ICU cases run 2-3+ weeks.
target_los: Optimized best-practice target ~4-7 days for the collapsed group: uncomplicated viral/aseptic ~3-4d; uncomplicated bacterial (meningococcal/pneumococcal) ~5-7d with early transition to OPAT to complete the organism-specific IV course rather than remaining inpatient the full 14-21d. Longer for MCC/ICU cases, HSV encephalitis (typically 14-21d), and TB meningitis (extended). Key LOS lever: early OPAT planning + rapid CSF PCR-driven de-escalation.
quality_metrics: Process: door-to-antibiotic time <=1h; dexamethasone administered before/with first antibiotic dose; blood cultures before antibiotics; age/risk-appropriate empiric regimen (Listeria coverage when indicated); appropriate CT-before-LP utilization; empiric acyclovir for suspected encephalitis. SEP-1 sepsis-bundle compliance when septic shock coexists. Antimicrobial stewardship: de-escalation within 48-72h and appropriate total duration. Outcome/balancing: in-hospital and 30-day mortality, 30-day readmission, hearing-screen completion (pneumococcal), meningococcal reporting + contact prophylaxis completion. Note: there is no dedicated CMS core measure specific to meningitis/encephalitis (unlike SEP-1 or stroke GWTG); these are IDSA/ESCMID-derived institutional process measures plus the sepsis overlap.
common_complications: Neurologic: seizures, raised ICP/cerebral edema, hydrocephalus, cerebral infarction from vasculitis, subdural empyema/effusion, cranial-nerve palsies, and sensorineural hearing loss (especially pneumococcal - mitigated by adjunctive dexamethasone + early audiology). Systemic: septic shock, DIC/purpura fulminans (meningococcemia), SIADH/hyponatremia, ARDS, and acyclovir crystal nephropathy/AKI (mitigated by ideal-body-weight dosing + IV hydration). Mitigation levers: antibiotics <=1h + dexamethasone, ICP protocol, seizure control, sodium monitoring with isotonic fluids, neurosurgical source control, and careful renally-dosed acyclovir.
readmission_drivers: Incomplete/interrupted antibiotic course or OPAT line complications -> ensure OPAT follow-up with weekly monitoring labs. Recurrent meningitis from CSF leak, anatomic/skull-base defect, or immune deficiency (asplenia, complement deficiency, HIV) -> workup, repair leak, vaccinate. Persistent seizures, focal deficits, or cognitive/post-encephalitis sequelae -> neuro-rehab and Neurology follow-up with AED management. Untreated parameningeal source (sinusitis/mastoiditis) or recurrent abscess/empyema -> source control before discharge. OPAT line-associated infection and drug toxicity (acyclovir AKI, vancomycin nephrotoxicity) -> lab surveillance.
guideline_source: WHO Guidelines on Meningitis Diagnosis, Treatment and Care (2025) for community-acquired meningitis in children older than 1 month and adults; IDSA 2004 Practice Guidelines for the Management of Bacterial Meningitis (Tunkel et al.); IDSA 2008 Management of Encephalitis (Tunkel et al.); ESCMID 2016 Guideline: Diagnosis and Treatment of Acute Bacterial Meningitis (van de Beek et al.) - most current bacterial-meningitis synthesis; IDSA 2017 Healthcare-Associated Ventriculitis and Meningitis (Tunkel et al.) for device-related/post-neurosurgical CNS infection.

evidence_grade: Strong - IDSA/ESCMID Class A-I society guidelines for empiric antibiotic timing and selection; Level I RCT evidence for adjunctive dexamethasone in pneumococcal meningitis (de Gans/van de Beek NEJM 2002) and for acyclovir in HSV encephalitis (Whitley NEJM 1986). Moderate/consensus for the exact door-to-antibiotic <=1h target, repeat-LP indications, and monitoring thresholds (observational + expert opinion). Caveat: the US IDSA bacterial-meningitis guideline dates to 2004 and encephalitis to 2008 - still authoritative but aging, with no newer US replacement; ESCMID 2016 is the most current bacterial-meningitis synthesis.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 094/097 (with MCC): typically ICU-level - septic shock, coma/GCS<=8, mechanical ventilation, status epilepticus, DIC, cerebral edema/herniation, stroke, AKI; LOS ~7-10+ days and highest mortality (pneumococcal ~30%). MCC drivers include acute respiratory failure, sepsis/septic shock, coma, cerebral edema, hydrocephalus, and status epilepticus. DRG 095/098 (with CC): step-down/floor with complications - controlled seizures, moderate deficits, SIADH/hyponatremia, resolving shock; LOS ~4-6 days. DRG 096/099 (without CC/MCC): uncomplicated (often viral/aseptic or early meningococcal), floor-level, faster recovery; LOS ~3-4 days. Bacterial/TB tier (094-096) is generally sicker with longer courses (Listeria/GNR 21d, TB months) than non-bacterial (097-099, mostly viral, shorter) - but HSV encephalitis is a severe non-bacterial outlier requiring 14-21d IV acyclovir and frequently qualifying as MCC.
pathway_pearls: 1) Antibiotics within 1 hour AND dexamethasone before/with the first dose - never delay antibiotics for CT or LP: draw blood cultures, give dexamethasone + empiric antibiotics, then image/tap. 2) Age >50, immunocompromised, or pregnant -> ADD ampicillin for Listeria (not covered by ceftriaxone + vancomycin). 3) Suspected encephalitis -> start empiric IV acyclovir immediately without waiting for HSV PCR; temporal-lobe signal on MRI is HSV until proven otherwise. 4) Rapid multiplex CSF PCR (BioFire ME panel) accelerates pathogen ID, de-escalation, and the dexamethasone-continuation decision - pair it with early OPAT planning to shorten LOS while completing the organism-specific IV duration.
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Bacterial/TB (094-096) and non-bacterial (097-099) CNS infections. Time-critical; ED/ICU entry.

admission_criteria: Most isolated closed upper-extremity and distal lower-extremity fractures are managed same-day/outpatient or <24h observation after ORIF/IMN/CRPP; reserve INPATIENT admission for: open fracture (any Gustilo grade) needing urgent I&D + IV antibiotics; polytrauma / multiple fractures / elevated ISS; neurovascular compromise or threatened limb; impending or established compartment syndrome requiring monitoring/fasciotomy; fractures needing staged management (spanning ex-fix then delayed ORIF, e.g., tibial plateau/pilon with soft-tissue swelling); intractable pain uncontrolled on oral analgesia; inability to bear weight or self-care with inadequate home support (esp. bilateral upper-extremity or non-weight-bearing elderly); perioperative optimization or anticoagulation reversal for major comorbidity; need for free-flap/soft-tissue coverage. ED discharge/observation appropriate for isolated closed low-energy fracture with intact neurovascular exam, adequate analgesia, and safe disposition
risk_stratification: Gustilo-Anderson open-fracture class (I <1cm clean; II 1-10cm; IIIA extensive soft-tissue/coverage possible; IIIB needs flap; IIIC arterial injury needing repair) -> drives antibiotic spectrum, debridement urgency, plastics/vascular consult; Compartment syndrome: clinical (pain out of proportion, pain on passive stretch) primary, and if obtunded delta pressure (diastolic - compartment) <30 mmHg -> emergent fasciotomy; Mangled Extremity Severity Score (MESS) >=7 -> amputation-vs-salvage discussion; ASA class + Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI) for perioperative cardiac risk; Caprini VTE score (used to risk-stratify in PREVENT CLOT secondary analysis) -> prophylaxis decision, noting isolated distal-limb cast immobilization is low-risk; FRAX 10-yr probability + DXA T-score <=-2.5 for low-energy fragility fractures age >=50 -> bone-health workup/treatment; Clinical Frailty Scale/FRAIL + 4AT delirium screen in elderly -> geriatric co-management
initial_workup_labs: CBC (baseline Hgb for transfusion planning, WBC); BMP (renal function for contrast/antibiotic dosing, electrolytes); coagulation panel PT/INR/aPTT (esp. if anticoagulated -> reversal); type & screen with crossmatch for Gustilo IIIC/polytrauma; CK (rising -> crush/rhabdomyolysis/compartment); lactate in polytrauma/hemodynamic compromise; CRP/ESR baseline for infection tracking; glucose/HbA1c for perioperative glycemic optimization; pregnancy test in women of childbearing age before imaging/OR; in low-energy/fragility fractures add 25-OH vitamin D, calcium/albumin, PTH, TSH for secondary-cause workup; verify tetanus immunization status for open wounds
initial_imaging_dx: Orthogonal plain radiographs (2 views including joint above and below) at presentation; post-reduction films after any closed reduction/splinting; CT with thin-cut reconstructions for intra-articular/complex patterns (tibial plateau, pilon, distal humerus, scaphoid/carpus, calcaneus) for preop planning; CT angiography or ABI (<0.9 triggers arterial imaging) if pulse deficit/expanding hematoma suggests vascular injury (Gustilo IIIC); MRI selectively for occult scaphoid or radiographically-negative fracture and ligamentous injury; standard preop ECG/CXR per age and comorbidity
time_critical_interventions: Open fracture: first IV antibiotic dose ASAP, within 1 hour of presentation (ideally <3h from injury) - strongest modifiable infection-reduction lever; tetanus prophylaxis (Td/Tdap +/- TIG) per CDC by wound and immunization status; emergent bedside gross reduction/realignment + splinting of any deformed fracture or dislocation with neurovascular compromise; arterial injury (IIIC) revascularized within the ~6h warm-ischemia window (vascular surgery emergent); fasciotomy emergently once compartment syndrome diagnosed (delta P <30 mmHg or clinical); formal operative debridement + irrigation of open fractures urgently within 24h (sooner, <12h, for heavily contaminated/type III) - the historical 6-hour rule is not evidence-supported; prompt reduction of fracture-dislocations to relieve skin/neurovascular tension
initial_management: Reduction + immobilization (splint/cast/traction) with documented post-reduction neurovascular check; ERAS multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia: scheduled acetaminophen + NSAID (if no contraindication/healing concern), selective gabapentinoid, short-acting opioid PRN, and peripheral regional anesthesia/nerve blocks (interscalene for shoulder/proximal humerus; supraclavicular/infraclavicular/axillary for arm-forearm-wrist; popliteal-sciatic + adductor-canal/saphenous for tibia-ankle-foot); open-fracture antibiotics (EAST): cefazolin (clindamycin or vancomycin if beta-lactam allergy) for Gustilo I/II, add gram-negative coverage (aminoglycoside e.g. gentamicin, or piperacillin-tazobactam/ceftriaxone) for type III, add high-dose penicillin or metronidazole for soil/fecal/farm (clostridial) contamination, continue 24h after wound closure (up to 72h or 24h post-coverage for type III); irrigate with normal saline at low/very-low pressure (FLOW), avoid soap additives; restrictive transfusion (Hgb <7 g/dL, or <8 with cardiac disease); anticoagulation reversal before urgent surgery (vit K/4F-PCC for warfarin, idarucizumab/andexanet for DOACs); source control = timely operative debridement/fixation
day1_milestones: POD0/operative day: definitive or staged fixation (ORIF/IMN/CRPP/ex-fix) per fracture pattern and soft-tissue status; document post-op compartment and distal neurovascular exam; place single-shot or continuous regional block; start multimodal analgesia; resume oral intake same day; out of bed / mobilize with PT the day of surgery when feasible; establish and document weight-bearing status and immobilization; remove urinary catheter POD0 if placed; start VTE prophylaxis (aspirin 81mg BID or enoxaparin) once hemostasis secured; antiemetic and bowel regimen; glucose control (<180 mg/dL)
day2_milestones: POD1: PT/OT evaluation and progressive mobilization (hand therapy for upper extremity); transition from IV/regional to oral multimodal analgesia; wound/dressing check and drain management; confirm and reinforce weight-bearing and immobilization instructions; tolerating diet; voiding without catheter; continue VTE prophylaxis; for uncomplicated closed fixation meet discharge criteria and discharge POD1 (many isolated cases leave POD0/23h); initiate bone-health workup and osteoporosis-treatment planning for fragility fractures; delirium screening/prevention bundle in elderly
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ (open fractures / staged reconstruction / polytrauma): planned repeat I&D every 24-48h until clean; definitive fixation and soft-tissue coverage (plastics flap for Gustilo IIIB, typically within ~7 days); monitor for infection and compartment syndrome; escalate/de-escalate antibiotics by culture data; progress mobilization and ADLs with PT/OT; confirm fixation stability on radiographs; finalize disposition (home vs SNF/inpatient rehab); complete osteoporosis treatment initiation and Fracture Liaison Service enrollment before discharge
consults_multidisciplinary: Orthopedic trauma / hand-upper-extremity surgery (primary); anesthesia/regional/acute pain service (day 0); PT and OT incl. hand therapy (POD0-1); care management/social work early for disposition, DME, home services; pharmacy for multimodal analgesia, antibiotic stewardship, anticoagulation and opioid stewardship; plastic/reconstructive surgery for Gustilo IIIB coverage; vascular surgery for IIIC; geriatrics or hospitalist co-management for elderly/comorbid medical optimization; endocrinology/bone-health/FLS and dietitian for fragility fractures (protein, vitamin D, calcium); infectious disease for complex/contaminated open fractures
monitoring_level: Floor/med-surg for the large majority of isolated closed fixations; telemetry for cardiac comorbidity/arrhythmia risk; step-down or ICU for polytrauma, Gustilo IIIC after vascular repair, post-fasciotomy compartment syndrome, long-bone fractures at fat-embolism risk, significant blood loss/hemodynamic instability, or major medical comorbidity. Escalation triggers: expanding/tense compartment or pain out of proportion (compartment syndrome), new neurovascular deficit, hypoxia/altered mental status/petechiae (fat embolism syndrome), uncontrolled pain or bleeding, sepsis physiology
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: resume regular diet POD0, optimize protein and vitamin D/calcium especially in fragility fractures. Mobility: early mobilization day of surgery, weight-bearing status per surgeon/fracture, assistive devices and fall precautions. VTE: for operatively-treated extremity fractures give pharmacologic prophylaxis with aspirin 81mg BID (noninferior to enoxaparin 30mg BID, PREVENT CLOT) plus early mobilization/mechanical prophylaxis; do NOT routinely anticoagulate isolated below-knee cast immobilization (POT-CAST); use IPC when pharmacologic prophylaxis contraindicated. Glucose: target <180 mg/dL, avoid hypoglycemia. Lines/catheters: remove Foley POD0, discontinue IV/regional catheters once transitioned to oral analgesia
discharge_criteria: Pain controlled on oral regimen; tolerating diet; afebrile with stable/benign wound and intact distal neurovascular exam; safe mobilization cleared by PT with established weight-bearing status and appropriate assistive device; voiding independently; VTE prophylaxis prescribed and understood; medical comorbidities stable/optimized; confirmed disposition with needed services and DME; for open fractures, source-controlled with a defined antibiotic plan
discharge_planning: Disposition home for most vs SNF/inpatient rehab for frail/elderly or bilateral/non-weight-bearing lower-extremity; medication reconciliation with multimodal analgesia + opioid stewardship (limited quantity, mandatory bowel regimen, naloxone if indicated) and defined VTE prophylaxis duration; splint/cast/sling/brace and DME (walker, crutches, knee scooter); written weight-bearing and wound-care instructions; explicit precautions for infection, compartment syndrome, and DVT/PE; orthopedic/hand clinic follow-up in ~10-14 days for wound/suture check and radiographs; PT/hand-therapy referral; osteoporosis/Fracture Liaison Service follow-up and PCP linkage for fragility fractures; transitions-of-care handoff to PCP
expected_los: Tier-dependent across this collapsed group (approximate CMS MS-DRG GMLOS, FY2024-25): w/o CC/MCC tiers (494 lower-extremity/humerus, 508 major shoulder/elbow, 512 shoulder/elbow/forearm) ~1.3-2.3 days; w CC tiers (507, 511) ~2-3 days; w MCC tiers (492/510) ~4-5.5 days; blended typical ~2-3 days, with many isolated closed fixations same-day or 23-hour observation
target_los: Isolated closed low-energy fixation: same-day discharge or <=23-hour observation (target 0-1 day); uncomplicated inpatient with CC: 1-2 days; open fracture / staged reconstruction / polytrauma: 3-5 days driven by debridement cadence and soft-tissue coverage; optimized blended target ~1-2 days under ERAS with regional anesthesia and same-day mobilization
quality_metrics: Open-fracture antibiotic timing (<1h) and appropriate selection; surgical prophylactic antibiotic within 60 min + timely discontinuation (SCIP-derived); VTE prophylaxis ordered/administered (VTE-1/VTE-2); SSI/deep infection and unplanned return to OR (NHSN/ACS-NSQIP/TQP); 30-day readmission and 30-day mortality; ERAS protocol compliance; osteoporosis evaluation/treatment after fragility fracture (secondary fracture prevention measure); opioid prescribing/stewardship; patient-reported functional outcomes (PROMIS, QuickDASH/DASH for upper extremity, LEFS/FAAM for lower extremity)
common_complications: Surgical-site/deep infection and wound dehiscence (mitigated by <1h antibiotics, timely debridement, low-pressure saline irrigation, glycemic control); compartment syndrome (serial exams, low threshold for fasciotomy); VTE (prophylaxis + early mobilization); nerve injury/neurapraxia and complex regional pain syndrome (early motion, prompt recognition); malunion/nonunion/hardware failure (stable fixation, weight-bearing compliance, smoking cessation); fat embolism syndrome in long-bone fractures (early fixation, respiratory monitoring); joint stiffness/loss of motion (early PT/OT/hand therapy); postoperative delirium in elderly (geriatric co-management, opioid-sparing, orientation/sleep bundle); blood loss/transfusion
readmission_drivers: Wound infection/dehiscence and abscess; uncontrolled pain and opioid-related complications (constipation, oversedation); VTE (DVT/PE); hardware complications, loss of reduction, nonunion; medical decompensation in comorbid/elderly patients; falls and recurrent fragility fracture from untreated osteoporosis. Prevention levers: standardized antibiotic/wound protocols with early follow-up wound checks; multimodal analgesia plus opioid + bowel-regimen counseling; guideline VTE prophylaxis with patient education; clear weight-bearing/immobilization instructions and DME; geriatric co-management and Fracture Liaison Service enrollment; guaranteed 10-14 day clinic follow-up and PCP transition
guideline_source: AAOS Clinical Practice Guidelines (incl. AAOS/ASSH Management of Distal Radius Fractures, 2020); Orthopaedic Trauma Association (OTA) open-fracture/trauma management; EAST Practice Management Guidelines - Prophylactic Antibiotic Use in Open Fractures (2011 update); ACS TQIP Best Practices in the Management of Orthopaedic Trauma (2015); ERAS Society perioperative principles; CDC tetanus prophylaxis; AOA Own the Bone / ASBMR Secondary Fracture Prevention Initiative (2019) and ASH 2019 VTE guidelines for the fragility and VTE components

N Engl J Med 2016;376(6):515-525 (PMID 27959702; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1613303); EAST - Hoff WS et al. Update to PMG for prophylactic antibiotic use in open fractures. J Trauma 2011;70(3):751-4 (PMID 21610369; DOI 10.1097/TA.0b013e31820930e5); AAOS/ASSH Distal Radius CPG 2020 - Kamal RN, Shapiro LM. JAAOS 2022;30(9):e714-e720 (PMID 35383613; DOI 10.5435/JAAOS-D-21-01194); PREVENT CLOT risk-stratified secondary analysis - O'Hara NN et al. J Trauma Acute Care Surg 2024;96(4):573-582 (PMID 38079260; DOI 10.1097/TA.0000000000004226). Citations retrieved via PubMed
evidence_grade: Moderate-to-strong overall. Strong (Level I RCT) for VTE prophylaxis choice (aspirin 81mg BID noninferior to LMWH, PREVENT CLOT), open-fracture irrigation (low/very-low-pressure saline, avoid soap, FLOW), and no benefit of anticoagulation for isolated lower-leg casting (POT-CAST). Moderate/consensus (society guideline, largely Level II-III/expert) for open-fracture antibiotic timing and selection, debridement timing, ERAS elements, and fragility-fracture secondary prevention. Fracture-specific fixation decisions are individualized; a unified inpatient LOS/pathway across this heterogeneous DRG group is partly extrapolated
severity_cc_mcc_notes: CC/MCC assignment sets the tier and expected LOS. w/o CC/MCC (494, 508, 512): typically younger/healthier, isolated closed fixation, floor-level, GMLOS ~1.3-2.3d, often same-day/23h - prime candidates for outpatient migration. w CC (507, 511): comorbidity or minor complication, LOS ~2-3d. w MCC (492/510): major complication or comorbidity (sepsis, acute renal/respiratory failure, fat embolism), polytrauma, or open Gustilo IIIB/IIIC - more likely ICU/step-down, GMLOS ~4-5.5d, higher transfusion/infection/mortality. Open fractures, IIIC vascular repair, and elderly fragility fractures cluster in the higher tiers and lengthen stay; the internal-fixation-device removal DRGs (495-498) are generally short/elective
pathway_pearls: 1) Open fractures: the #1 modifiable lever is IV antibiotics within 1 hour (cefazolin +/- gram-negative/anaerobic coverage by Gustilo grade) + tetanus + timely debridement (<24h - don't chase the debunked 6-hour rule), irrigating with low-pressure saline not soap. 2) VTE: use aspirin 81mg BID for operatively-treated extremity fractures (noninferior to LMWH, cheaper, better satisfaction) and do NOT routinely anticoagulate isolated below-knee cast immobilization. 3) Run it as ERAS - regional nerve blocks + multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia + same-day mobilization convert most isolated closed fixations to same-day/23-hour stays. 4) Never miss compartment syndrome (serial exams; delta P <30 mmHg -> fasciotomy) and always close the osteoporosis loop - start treatment and enroll fragility-fracture patients in a Fracture Liaison Service to prevent the next fracture
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Lower-extremity except hip/femur (494-498), shoulder/upper-arm and other extremity fracture fixation (507-512). Excludes hip/femur (rank 31).
