admission_criteria: Obstructing upper-tract stone WITH infection (T>38C, leukocytosis, pyuria/positive urinalysis, systemic signs) = obstructive pyelonephritis/urosepsis -> EMERGENT admission for decompression; obstruction of a solitary or transplant kidney, bilateral obstruction, or obstruction-related AKI (rising creatinine) or anuria; intractable pain/vomiting or inability to tolerate PO despite ED analgesia/antiemetics; planned complex/staged endourologic surgery (PCNL for >2 cm renal or staghorn calculus, difficult access anatomy); post-procedure complication (hemorrhage, sepsis, ureteral injury). ED discharge/observation is appropriate for an uncomplicated ureteral stone <10 mm (especially <5 mm distal) with controlled pain, no infection, and preserved renal function -> outpatient trial of passage.
risk_stratification: Sepsis/urosepsis: qSOFA >=2 or NEWS2 escalation with serum lactate >2 (>=4 mmol/L = high-risk) drives ICU triage plus emergent source control; SIRS/SOFA per Surviving Sepsis. Spontaneous-passage likelihood by stone size/location (<5 mm ~68-98%, 5-10 mm ~50%, >10 mm unlikely; distal > proximal) drives MET-vs-surgery decision. PCNL outcome/complication prediction: Guy's Stone Score (grade I-IV), S.T.O.N.E. nephrolithometry, and the CROES nomogram estimate stone-free rate and complications and guide staging/counseling. Stone HU density (>1000 HU predicts SWL failure -> favor URS/PCNL) and skin-to-stone distance guide modality. ASA class plus Charlson comorbidity index for perioperative/anesthesia risk.
initial_workup_labs: CBC (leukocytosis, thrombocytopenia -> sepsis severity); CMP/renal panel (creatinine/eGFR for obstructive AKI, K+); urinalysis with microscopy (pyuria, nitrites, hematuria; pH >7 suggests struvite/urease organisms, pH <5.5 uric acid) and URINE CULTURE before ANY instrumentation (targets prophylaxis); blood cultures x2 + serum lactate + CRP/procalcitonin if febrile/septic; coagulation panel and type-and-screen before PCNL (bleeding risk); beta-hCG in women of childbearing age. Positive culture -> culture-directed antibiotics and defer instrumentation; elevated creatinine -> renal dosing/nephrology; coagulopathy -> correct before PCNL. Metabolic stone workup (serum Ca/PTH/uric acid, 24-h urine) deferred to outpatient once stone-free.
initial_imaging_dx: Non-contrast CT (CT-KUB/stone protocol) is the diagnostic gold standard, obtained in the ED within hours for a suspected obstructing stone (sensitivity ~97%); it reports size, location, HU density, skin-to-stone distance, hydronephrosis, and access anatomy. Renal/bladder ultrasound is preferred FIRST-LINE imaging in pregnancy, children, and young patients (STONE trial: initial US = equivalent safety with markedly less radiation). KUB radiograph for radiopaque-stone tracking/follow-up. In obstruction + infection, CT confirms pyonephrosis (hydronephrosis with debris/gas). MAG3/DTPA renography only electively to assess split renal function before nephrectomy of a nonfunctioning kidney.
time_critical_interventions: Obstructed + infected kidney is a urologic emergency: EMERGENT decompression by percutaneous nephrostomy OR retrograde ureteral stent (equivalent efficacy - Pearle RCT), ideally within a few hours of recognition and concurrent with sepsis resuscitation; NEVER attempt primary stone removal (URS/PCNL) in an actively infected obstructed system - drain first, do definitive stone surgery ~1-2 weeks later after infection clears. Sepsis bundle (CMS SEP-1 / Surviving Sepsis 2021): lactate + blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 h of septic-shock recognition, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4, reassess and add vasopressors to MAP >=65. Bilateral obstruction, solitary-kidney obstruction, or anuria -> urgent decompression to preserve renal function.
initial_management: Analgesia: NSAIDs first-line (ketorolac 15-30 mg IV, diclofenac) - superior to opioids for renal colic; opioids (morphine, hydromorphone) as adjunct; hold NSAIDs with AKI/CKD or pre-op bleeding risk. Antiemetics (ondansetron, metoclopramide). Empiric antibiotics for urosepsis: 3rd-gen cephalosporin (ceftriaxone) or piperacillin-tazobactam, carbapenem if ESBL/resistance risk, +/- aminoglycoside per local antibiogram, then de-escalate to culture-directed therapy. Balanced-crystalloid resuscitation. MET: alpha-blocker (tamsulosin 0.4 mg) offered for distal ureteral stones 5-10 mm (modest benefit only; SUSPEND showed no overall effect - reserve for larger distal stones). Source control = decompression, then interval definitive stone treatment. Hold/reverse antiplatelet-anticoagulant before PCNL (high bleeding risk); restrictive transfusion threshold Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 with cardiovascular disease).
day1_milestones: Urgent-decompression pathway (POD0): percutaneous nephrostomy or retrograde stent for source control plus sepsis resuscitation; ICU/step-down if septic shock or lactate >4; empiric antibiotics; confirm drainage (nephrostomy output / stent position on fluoro or KUB); monitor UOP and hemodynamics and watch for post-obstructive diuresis. Planned endourologic surgery (POD0): PCNL or URS with laser lithotripsy; ERAS elements - multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia, PONV prophylaxis, same-day PO intake and ambulation; check post-PCNL Hgb and assess for gross hematuria/bleeding; mechanical VTE prophylaxis (SCDs) intra- and post-op.
day2_milestones: Expect clinical improvement 48-72 h after decompression (Pearle RCT: time-to-afebrile ~2.3 d, WBC normalization ~2 d); transition IV -> PO antibiotics and analgesia once afebrile and tolerating PO; remove Foley (CAUTI prevention); advance diet and ambulate. Post-PCNL: recheck Hgb and creatinine, assess nephrostomy output, and plan tube management (capping/removal vs antegrade nephrostogram). Monitor and replace losses if post-obstructive diuresis develops. Reassess need for pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis once hemostasis is confirmed.
day3plus_milestones: Discharge readiness: afebrile >=24 h, hemodynamically stable off pressors, WBC trending down, pain controlled on oral meds, tolerating diet, stable Hgb and creatinine, secured urinary drainage (functioning or removed stent/nephrostomy) with demonstrated tube-care competency. If decompressed for infection: discharge on culture-directed oral antibiotic to complete the course and SCHEDULE interval definitive stone surgery in ~1-2 weeks. Post-obstructive diuresis resolved. Complex PCNL: confirm stone clearance (KUB/CT) and plan a second-look procedure if residual fragments remain.
consults_multidisciplinary: Urology (primary) day 0; interventional radiology for percutaneous nephrostomy; anesthesia/pre-op clinic for planned surgery; critical care if septic shock. Infectious disease for resistant/ESBL urosepsis, candiduria, or bacteremia; nephrology for obstructive AKI, CKD, solitary kidney, or post-obstructive diuresis. Pharmacy for antibiotic stewardship, renal dosing, and anticoagulation management. Care management/social work early for nephrostomy home-care, DME, and home health; dietitian for outpatient metabolic stone-prevention counseling; PT if deconditioned/elderly.
monitoring_level: ICU: septic shock on vasopressors, lactate >4, respiratory failure, or major PCNL hemorrhage requiring transfusion/angioembolization. Step-down/telemetry: sepsis without shock, significant comorbidity, or early post-PCNL bleeding surveillance. Floor: hemodynamically stable post-URS/stent/nephrostomy. Escalation triggers: rising lactate or new hypotension, oliguria/rising creatinine, falling Hgb (PCNL bleed), or persistent fever despite drainage (inadequate decompression -> re-image and revise drainage).
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet advanced as tolerated with early feeding (most endourology patients eat POD0). Early ambulation POD0. VTE: mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs) for all per AUA/EAU thromboprophylaxis best practice; add pharmacologic LMWH (enoxaparin) or UFH for moderate-to-high risk, balanced against PCNL bleeding risk (often deferred until hemostasis confirmed). Glucose target <180 mg/dL. Remove Foley early; manage stent/nephrostomy per plan; remove central lines when off pressors.
discharge_criteria: Afebrile >=24 h and hemodynamically stable off vasopressors; downtrending WBC; pain controlled on oral analgesics; tolerating oral diet and hydration; stable Hgb and creatinine (no active bleeding, post-obstructive diuresis resolved); adequate urinary drainage established (functioning or removed stent/nephrostomy) with demonstrated tube-care competency; culture-directed oral antibiotic plan and urology follow-up arranged.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home (home health/DME for nephrostomy care). Medication reconciliation: culture-directed oral antibiotic to finish the course, opioid-sparing analgesia, and an alpha-blocker (tamsulosin) to ease indwelling-stent symptoms. Follow-up: urology in 1-2 weeks for interval definitive stone surgery and/or stent removal, using a tracked stent registry/reminder to prevent a 'forgotten stent'; imaging (KUB/CT) to confirm clearance. Education: hydration to >2.5 L urine/day, dietary levers (reduce sodium, moderate animal protein, normal dietary calcium), stent-symptom expectations, and return precautions (fever, uncontrolled pain, anuria). Outpatient 24-h urine metabolic workup once stone-free.
expected_los: Severity-tiered CMS FY2025 IPPS GMLOS (approximate): without CC/MCC (DRG 661/675) ~1.9-2.0 days; with CC (DRG 660/674) ~3.3-3.5 days; with MCC (DRG 659/673, e.g., urosepsis/acute renal failure) ~6-7 days. Blended collapsed-group benchmark ~3-4 days.
target_los: Optimized best-practice: uncomplicated URS / ureteral stent / nephrostomy 0-1 day (many URS as outpatient or 23-h observation); tubeless or mini-PCNL 1-2 days; obstructed-infected stone with prompt decompression 3-4 days (drain -> ~48 h defervescence -> transition to PO antibiotics -> discharge with interval surgery). Group target ~2-3 days.
quality_metrics: CMS SEP-1 severe sepsis/septic-shock bundle compliance (urosepsis); AUA antimicrobial-prophylaxis adherence (correct agent, timing, single-dose, discontinuation at case end) and SSI rate; CAUTI (timely Foley removal); VTE prophylaxis compliance and observed VTE rate; PCNL hemorrhage/transfusion and angioembolization rate (PSI-9/PSI-90); stone-free rate (registry / quality-of-surgery); retained or 'forgotten stent' tracking; 30-day readmission and ED-return rate.
common_complications: Post-instrumentation urosepsis/SIRS after URS or PCNL - mitigated by pre-op culture with targeted antibiotics, draining infected systems first, and low irrigation pressure / access-sheath use. PCNL hemorrhage/transfusion (~2-7%, rare angioembolization) and supracostal-access pleural injury. Stent-related symptoms (dysuria, flank pain, hematuria) - alpha-blocker. Ureteral perforation/avulsion at URS -> stent. Obstructive AKI and post-obstructive diuresis - monitor UOP and replace losses. CAUTI; failed access or residual fragments requiring a second-look procedure.
readmission_drivers: Recurrent or persistent obstruction, stent migration/encrustation/obstruction, retained fragments, recurrent infection/urosepsis, uncontrolled stent or colic pain, and dehydration/AKI; the 'forgotten stent' is a preventable driver. Prevention levers: confirm clearance imaging, schedule and TRACK stent removal/interval surgery, ensure culture-directed antibiotic completion, provide hydration and analgesia counseling, arrange early urology follow-up, and give clear tube-care and return-precaution education.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: AUA Surgical Management of Kidney and Ureteral Stones Guideline (2026, Parts I-III; Pearle/Matlaga). Supporting: EAU Guidelines on Urolithiasis (2025); AUA Best Practice Statement on Urologic Procedures and Antimicrobial Prophylaxis (2019/2020); Surviving Sepsis Campaign (2021) + CMS SEP-1 for urosepsis; AUA/EAU thromboprophylaxis best-practice statements.
key_citations: Per PubMed: AUA Surgical Management of Kidney and Ureteral Stones Guideline (2026) - Part I PMID 41263323 (doi 10.1097/JU.0000000000004842), Part II PMID 41263322 (doi 10.1097/JU.0000000000004843), Part III PMID 41263325 (doi 10.1097/JU.0000000000004844); Pearle MS et al., Optimal method of urgent decompression for obstruction+infection due to ureteral calculi (PCN vs retrograde stent equivalent), J Urol 1998;160(4):1260-4, PMID 9751331; Pickard R et al. (SUSPEND) medical expulsive therapy RCT, Lancet 2015;386(9991):341-9, PMID 25998582 (doi 10.1016/S0140-6736(15)60933-3); Smith-Bindman R et al., Ultrasonography vs CT for suspected nephrolithiasis, N Engl J Med 2014;371(12):1100-10, PMID 25229916 (doi 10.1056/NEJMoa1404446); Lightner DJ et al., AUA Best Practice Statement on Urologic Procedures and Antimicrobial Prophylaxis, J Urol 2020;203(2):351-6, PMID 31441676 (doi 10.1097/JU.0000000000000509).
evidence_grade: Strong - anchored on a current Class I society guideline (AUA 2026, Parts I-III) plus RCT evidence for decompression-before-treatment of infected obstruction (Pearle), initial imaging choice (STONE trial), and medical expulsive therapy (SUSPEND), and Surviving Sepsis/CMS SEP-1 for urosepsis. Perioperative ERAS-endourology elements are extrapolated from general ERAS principles and single-center data (Moderate/consensus). Overall: Strong for the surgical/decompression backbone; Moderate for ERAS-specific perioperative steps.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Without CC/MCC (661/664/675): elective URS/stent/nephrostomy, floor-level, LOS ~1-2 d, low complication rate. With CC (660/674): comorbidity or moderate complication (obstructive AKI, UTI, anemia/transfusion), LOS ~3-4 d. With MCC (659/673): urosepsis/septic shock, acute renal failure, major hemorrhage, or respiratory failure -> ICU, LOS ~6-7+ d and the dominant driver of cost and mortality; severe sepsis is the archetypal MCC. Core pathway leverage = keeping a no-CC case from escalating to an MCC case via pre-op culture, targeted prophylaxis, and drain-first source control. The neoplasm DRGs 656-658 collapsed into this group follow oncologic (partial/radical nephrectomy) pathways and are outside this non-neoplasm scope.
pathway_pearls: 1) Infected + obstructed = DRAIN NOW, treat the stone later - emergent PCN or retrograde stent plus the sepsis bundle; never attempt primary stone extraction in an infected obstructed system (life-saving, highest-yield lever). 2) Obtain a pre-op urine culture and treat with targeted therapy BEFORE any upper-tract instrumentation, then give single-dose targeted prophylaxis - the biggest lever against post-op urosepsis. 3) Track every stent - schedule removal/interval surgery with a registry/reminder to prevent encrustation, obstruction, and readmission ('forgotten stent'). 4) NSAIDs (ketorolac) beat opioids for renal colic; CT-KUB is the diagnostic gold standard but use ultrasound first in pregnancy and young patients; MET (tamsulosin) helps only modestly and only for distal stones >5 mm.

mdc: 23 — Factors Influencing Health Status & Other Contacts
admission_criteria: Heterogeneous MDC-23 bucket (signs/symptoms 947/948; aftercare/convalescence/other-factors 949/950/951) with NO single disease guideline; admit as inpatient only when a defined acute-care need meets the CMS Two-Midnight expectation (>=2 midnights of medically necessary hospital care) vs ED discharge or <2-midnight observation. Inpatient-appropriate: undifferentiated symptom (generalized weakness, syncope, adult failure-to-thrive R62.7, debility R53.81, unintentional weight loss, FUO, delirium/AMS) WITH hemodynamic instability, NEWS2 >=5, hypoxia, actionable abnormal labs, or inability to ambulate/self-care with no safe disposition; or planned inpatient-level aftercare (multiday IV antibiotics, complex wound/ostomy/device or drain management, inpatient chemo Z51.11, post-transplant/post-op complication surveillance). Observation-appropriate: expected <2 midnights, low-risk syncope (Canadian Syncope Risk Score <=0), symptom resolving on a short diagnostic look. ED discharge: reassuring workup, ambulatory-safe, follow-up secured. A 'social admission' without a documented acute medical need is NOT an inpatient indication - engage care management for placement instead.
risk_stratification: Canadian Syncope Risk Score (syncope subset): very-low <=-2 / low -1 to 0 -> discharge; medium 1-3, high 4-5, very-high >=6 -> monitored admission + cardiac workup. NEWS2 >=5 (or any single parameter =3) -> urgent review/rapid-response + sepsis screen. qSOFA >=2 or SIRS >=2 -> sepsis pathway. 4AT >=4 or positive CAM -> delirium bundle. Rockwood Clinical Frailty Scale (1-9): >=5 (mild frailty) triggers comprehensive geriatric assessment; >=7 (severe) flags goals-of-care/palliative. MUST/NRS-2002 positive -> GLIM assessment + dietitian. CDC STEADI / Morse Fall Scale high -> falls precautions + PT. Charlson Comorbidity Index for prognosis. LACE index >=10 (or high HOSPITAL score) -> intensive transitions-of-care bundle to cut 30-day readmission. Padua/Caprini for VTE risk.
initial_workup_labs: Symptom-directed and stewardship-guided (Choosing Wisely - avoid reflexive pan-testing): CBC w/ differential, CMP (electrolytes/renal/hepatic/glucose/calcium), magnesium/phosphate; venous or arterial lactate if NEWS2 high or perfusion concern; TSH; if weakness/FTT add B12/folate and CK; UA with reflex culture; blood cultures x2 ONLY if SIRS/fever; troponin + BNP if cardiac syncope or chest symptoms; venous blood gas if AMS/hypercapnia risk; INR/PT-PTT if bleeding or anticoagulated; CRP/procalcitonin selectively; nutrition labs (albumin/prealbumin as trend, not diagnostic); consider cortisol, HIV, ESR, peripheral smear for true FUO/weight loss. Any positive screen triggers the matching organ-specific pathway and DRG reclassification.
initial_imaging_dx: Targeted, not shotgun: 12-lead ECG within 10 min for syncope/AMS/older adults; CXR for hypoxia, cough, fever, weight loss, or aspiration risk; CT head non-contrast <=1h if focal deficit, new delirium, or fall with head strike/anticoagulation; POCUS or formal TTE if structural cardiac syncope suspected (per 2017 ACC/AHA/HRS); telemetry/24-48h monitoring or outpatient loop recorder for suspected arrhythmic syncope; bladder scan for retention; site-specific imaging for aftercare admissions (wound/soft-tissue US or CT for abscess, contrast study for ostomy/device patency). For aftercare cases, imaging verifies the aftercare target rather than launching a fresh diagnostic hunt.
time_critical_interventions: Few condition-specific time bundles (this is a triage/exclusion group); the time-critical value is rapid identification of a dangerous mimic and prompt reclassification. If sepsis screen positive -> Surviving Sepsis 1-hour bundle (lactate, cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1h, 30 mL/kg crystalloid if hypotensive or lactate >=4). Point-of-care glucose immediately for any AMS (treat <70 mg/dL). ECG <=10 min for syncope; high-risk arrhythmia/ischemia -> cardiology + monitored bed. New focal deficit -> stroke pathway (CT <=25 min). Naloxone for opioid toxidrome. Correct severe K/Na/Ca/glucose derangements and symptomatic bradycardia promptly. Complete first VTE-prophylaxis decision and delirium-prevention screen within Day 1.
initial_management: Cause-directed and supportive: judicious IV isotonic crystalloid for dehydration/orthostasis (avoid over-resuscitation in frail/HFpEF); electrolyte repletion; hold/deprescribe culprit meds (STOPP/START, Beers - orthostatic, sedating, anticholinergic, hypoglycemic agents); non-pharmacologic delirium bundle first-line (reorientation, sleep-wake, early mobility, sensory aids, hydration, bowel/bladder care), reserving low-dose antipsychotic only for dangerous agitation; multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia; treat identified infection per the organ-specific IDSA pathway (no empiric antibiotics without a source); nutrition support per GLIM/ASPEN (oral-first, dietitian-guided, refeeding-syndrome caution). For aftercare admissions deliver the defined service: IV antibiotic completion, wound/ostomy/drain care, scheduled chemo per NCCN with antiemetic prophylaxis, device management. Restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if cardiac) only if the workup uncovers anemia.
day1_milestones: Establish or refute a working diagnosis; confirm admission-status integrity (inpatient vs observation) and document the Two-Midnight rationale; complete NEWS2, 4AT/CAM, Clinical Frailty Scale, falls (STEADI/Morse) and nutrition (MUST/NRS-2002) screens; medication reconciliation with a deprescribing pass; make the VTE-prophylaxis decision; place PT/OT and care-management referrals same day; identify disposition barriers early; set patient-specific discharge criteria; start delirium-prevention and early-mobility bundles; open goals-of-care conversation if CFS >=7.
day2_milestones: Reassess clinical response and diagnostic yield; if a specific diagnosis has emerged, transition to that organ-specific pathway and RECLASSIFY the DRG; de-escalate or stop low-value testing; advance mobility and diet; complete comprehensive geriatric assessment in frail patients; confirm the post-acute pathway (home + services vs SNF/subacute rehab vs home health) and begin authorizations; verify aftercare goals met or on-track; reassess telemetry/monitoring need and discontinue if criteria met; update medication reconciliation.
day3plus_milestones: Confirm discharge-readiness criteria met (stable vitals >=24h, ambulating at or near baseline or with equipment, tolerating intake, cognition at baseline, aftercare objective achieved); finalize placement/home services and durable medical equipment; complete teach-back education; ensure follow-up appointments are booked. If the patient remains inpatient with no acute need, this is a disposition/social barrier - escalate care management and consider observation reclassification or SNF transfer rather than a continued acute stay. Persistent unexplained symptoms -> re-examine for occult malignancy/infection or reconsider goals of care.
consults_multidisciplinary: PT/OT Day 1 (deconditioning, falls, function, equipment); care management/social work Day 1 (disposition, placement, home services, insurance, caregiver/financial barriers); clinical pharmacy (deprescribing, renal dosing, anticoagulation); registered dietitian if nutrition screen positive; geriatrics/CGA if CFS >=5 or a geriatric syndrome; palliative care if CFS >=7, recurrent admissions, or serious illness; specialty consult ONLY when the workup points to an organ system (cardiology for arrhythmic syncope, ID for aftercare antibiotics, surgery/wound-ostomy nursing for post-op aftercare, oncology for chemo admissions); psychiatry/behavioral health as indicated.
monitoring_level: Most are floor/med-surg level. Telemetry for syncope with CSRS >=1, abnormal ECG, arrhythmia risk, or significant electrolyte derangement. Step-down/ICU only if NEWS2 high, hemodynamic instability, respiratory failure, or a dangerous cause emerges. Escalation triggers: NEWS2 >=5 or any parameter =3, new hypotension/hypoxia/tachyarrhythmia, GCS drop or escalating delirium, rising lactate -> rapid-response/MET. De-escalate telemetry once 24-48h event-free and a cardiac cause is excluded to avoid alarm fatigue and unnecessary LOS.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: oral-first, texture/aspiration-appropriate, dietitian-guided oral nutrition supplements if GLIM-positive; watch for refeeding in severe malnutrition (replete phosphate/magnesium/potassium, give thiamine). Mobility: mobilize within 24h, PT/OT-driven, minimize bedrest to prevent deconditioning (a core driver of this group). VTE: risk-assess (Padua/Caprini) and give pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH/heparin) unless bleeding risk, else mechanical. Glucose: target 140-180 mg/dL inpatient, avoid hypoglycemia (deprescribe tight regimens in frail elders). Lines/catheters: place only if needed and remove Foley/central lines as early as possible (CAUTI/CLABSI and delirium prevention); proactive bowel/bladder regimen to prevent retention/constipation-driven delirium.
discharge_criteria: Objective and pre-set: hemodynamically stable and afebrile >=24h with low NEWS2; the acute/dangerous cause excluded or treated; cognition at baseline (delirium resolved or a safe plan in place); ambulating at baseline or safely with equipment/assistance; tolerating oral intake and medications; pain controlled on an oral regimen; aftercare objective achieved or transitionable to outpatient/home-health/SNF; safe discharge destination confirmed with needed services and DME; follow-up arranged and patient/caregiver educated by teach-back.
discharge_planning: Disposition matched to function/frailty: home, home + home-health/skilled nursing/IV therapy, SNF/subacute rehab, or LTAC; medication reconciliation with an explicit deprescribing summary; schedule PCP follow-up <=7 days (<=48-72h if high LACE/HOSPITAL risk) plus any specialty follow-up; apply a structured transitions bundle (Project RED / SHM Project BOOST / INTERACT elements): written after-visit summary, red-flag/return precautions, pending-test tracking, confirmed medication access, secured follow-up; send a timely discharge summary to the receiving clinician/facility; caregiver training for device/wound/ostomy care; document advance-care planning for serious illness.
expected_los: Approximate CMS MS-DRG GMLOS (varies by fiscal-year Table 5): 947 signs/symptoms w MCC ~3.3-3.6d; 948 w/o MCC ~2.5-2.7d; 949 aftercare w CC/MCC ~3.8-4.2d; 950 aftercare w/o CC/MCC ~2.5-2.8d; 951 other factors influencing health status ~2.7-3.0d; blended group ~2.5-4.2d (AMLOS runs ~1-1.5d longer for the MCC tiers).
target_los: 1.5-3 days under the ideal pathway - many cases resolve to observation status or next-day discharge once a dangerous cause is excluded and disposition is pre-planned. The lever is Day-1 status determination plus front-loaded care management/CGA, NOT additional testing; a stay extending past GMLOS in this group usually reflects an unfinished workup or a pure disposition barrier.
quality_metrics: Hospital-Wide All-Cause 30-day Readmission (HWR) and Medicare Spending Per Beneficiary (this bucket is a bounce-back and status-audit hotspot); RAC/QIO short-stay and Two-Midnight status-integrity audits; observation-vs-inpatient appropriateness and potentially-avoidable-admission/ED-conversion rate; SEP-1 if sepsis emerges; delirium screening rate; CAUTI and CLABSI (SIR); patient falls with injury; VTE prophylaxis rate (VTE-1/VTE-2); care-transition measures (CTM-3, HCAHPS care-transitions/discharge domain).
common_complications: Hospital-acquired deconditioning and functional decline; delirium (especially superimposed on dementia); falls with injury; CAUTI/CLABSI and C. difficile from low-value catheters/antibiotics; adverse drug events from polypharmacy; refeeding syndrome; pressure injury; VTE. Mitigation levers: early mobility + PT/OT, delirium-prevention bundle, catheter/line stewardship, antibiotic and diagnostic stewardship, deprescribing, GLIM-guided nutrition with refeeding precautions, VTE prophylaxis, and a pressure-injury bundle.
readmission_drivers: Premature discharge before the diagnosis is clarified; unresolved symptom or occult disease; disposition/social failure (inadequate home support, placement fell through, caregiver strain); polypharmacy/ADEs; poor medication access or health literacy; missed follow-up; frailty and functional decline; recurrent falls; malnutrition. Prevention levers: LACE/HOSPITAL-targeted transitions bundle, <=7-day (high-risk <=72h) follow-up, teach-back, home-health/telehealth, medication reconciliation with access confirmation, caregiver training, and palliative/goals-of-care engagement for high-frailty recurrent utilizers.
guideline_source: NO single disease-specific society guideline exists for this administrative MDC-23 grouping; the pathway is assembled from condition- and process-specific standards: CMS Two-Midnight Rule / admission-status policy (42 CFR 412.3; 2013, clarified in the CY2024 OPPS rule); ABIM Foundation Choosing Wisely (diagnostic stewardship); 2017 ACC/AHA/HRS Syncope Guideline; American Geriatrics Society / NICE CG103 delirium standards (CAM, 4AT); GLIM 2018/2019 malnutrition criteria + ASPEN; Rockwood Clinical Frailty Scale with comprehensive geriatric assessment; CDC STEADI falls initiative; and transitions-of-care programs (Project RED, SHM Project BOOST, INTERACT).
key_citations: Citations verified via PubMed: 2017 ACC/AHA/HRS Syncope Guideline (Shen WK et al., Circulation 2017, PMID 28280231); Canadian Syncope Risk Score (Thiruganasambandamoorthy V et al., CMAJ 2016;188:E289-98, PMID 27378464, DOI 10.1503/cmaj.151469); GLIM malnutrition criteria (Cederholm T et al., Clin Nutr 2019;38:1-9, PMID 30181091, DOI 10.1016/j.clnu.2018.08.002); Clinical Frailty Scale (Rockwood K et al., CMAJ 2005;173:489-95, PMID 16129869, DOI 10.1503/cmaj.050051); 4AT delirium screen (Bellelli G et al., Age Ageing 2014;43:496-502, PMID 24590568, DOI 10.1093/ageing/afu021); LACE readmission index (van Walraven C et al., CMAJ 2010, PMID 20194559). Frameworks (not PMID-indexed): CMS Two-Midnight Rule 42 CFR 412.3; ABIM Choosing Wisely; CDC STEADI; Project RED / SHM Project BOOST.
evidence_grade: Overall LOW / consensus-and-expert-opinion for the grouping as a whole - a heterogeneous administrative bucket with no unifying RCT or disease-specific society guideline. Component tools are stronger: syncope evaluation/management is Class I-IIa (2017 ACC/AHA/HRS); CSRS, 4AT, CFS, GLIM and LACE are validated derivation/validation-cohort instruments (moderate); Two-Midnight, Choosing Wisely and transitions bundles rest on policy and quality-improvement evidence. The pathway's value is operational (correct admission status, stewardship, disposition), not disease-specific.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Severity tier is defined by CC/MCC and drives the split: 947 (signs/symptoms w MCC) and 949 (aftercare w CC/MCC) carry the higher comorbidity burden -> longer LOS (~+1-1.5d), more telemetry/step-down, and higher complication and readmission risk, and the MCC frequently IS the true clinical driver (should prompt reclassification to the organ-specific pathway). 948/950 (without CC/MCC) and 951 (other factors) are lower-acuity, short-stay, disposition- and stewardship-driven, most amenable to observation or same-day resolution. Across all tiers, frailty/functional status (CFS) predicts LOS and disposition more than the DRG label; accurate CC/MCC documentation preserves severity-of-illness and expected-LOS integrity.
pathway_pearls: 1) Status integrity first - most of this group's waste is inpatient-vs-observation misclassification; apply the Two-Midnight expectation and document the acute need on Day 1. 2) These codes are placeholders, not diagnoses - actively pursue and RECLASSIFY to a specific organ-system DRG the moment a cause emerges; a persistent 947/949 stay signals an unfinished workup or a pure disposition problem. 3) Front-load disposition - care management + PT/OT + CGA on Day 1, because the barrier is usually social/functional, not medical; this is the single biggest LOS lever. 4) Stewardship both ways - Choosing Wisely to avoid over-testing, plus delirium/mobility/line/deprescribing bundles to prevent the hospital-acquired harms that turn a 2-day stay into a bounce-back.

admission_criteria: GATEKEEPER FIRST — this subset is defined by EXCLUDING hyperglycemic crisis: confirm NOT DKA (venous pH >=7.3, bicarb >=18 mEq/L, beta-hydroxybutyrate <3.0 mmol/L, anion gap <=12) and NOT HHS (effective serum osm <320 mOsm/kg, glucose <600); crisis reclassifies to the DKA/HHS pathway. Inpatient admission for uncomplicated hyperglycemia only when outpatient/observation management is unsafe: symptomatic marked hyperglycemia (BG typically >300-400 mg/dL) with volume depletion or intractable osmotic symptoms; new-onset/newly insulin-requiring diabetes needing inpatient insulin initiation + survival-skills education not feasible outpatient; hyperglycemia with intercurrent acute illness, inability to tolerate PO, or refractory to outpatient escalation; access/social barriers precluding safe outpatient insulin start. ED discharge/observation preferred for asymptomatic hyperglycemia that corrects with hydration, patient eating, reliable follow-up. Apply CMS 2-midnight benchmark; many cases are observation-appropriate rather than true inpatient.
risk_stratification: ADA hyperglycemic-crisis biochemical criteria (the reclassification gate): pH <7.3, bicarb <18, beta-hydroxybutyrate >=3.0 mmol/L, anion gap >12 => DKA; glucose >600 + effective osm >320 => HHS -> exit to crisis pathway/ICU. Acuity: NEWS2 >=5 urgent review, >=7 critical-care escalation; qSOFA >=2 if infection precipitant suspected. Hypoglycemia risk (drives conservative dosing + CGM): eGFR <30 or CKD stage >=3, age >=65, weight-based insulin >=0.6 units/kg/day, prior hypoglycemia or impaired awareness. A1C-guided discharge-intensity algorithm: A1C <7% reinforce current regimen; 7-9% add/adjust oral agent +/- basal; >9-10% start/intensify insulin. Beta-hydroxybutyrate and effective osm are the two numbers that decide whether the patient stays on this pathway.
initial_workup_labs: POC capillary glucose immediately; BMP (glucose, Na corrected for glucose, K, anion gap, BUN/Cr/eGFR) STAT; venous blood gas or serum bicarb to exclude acidosis; serum/urine ketones or beta-hydroxybutyrate; serum osmolality if glucose markedly elevated; A1C if not measured in prior 3 months (guides discharge regimen); CBC; UA (glucosuria, ketonuria, infection); magnesium/phosphate. Precipitant screen: cultures/CXR if infection suspected, TSH, lipid panel, med review (glucocorticoids). If new-onset or type unclear (young/lean/DKA-prone): C-peptide + GAD-65/islet autoantibodies to distinguish type 1/LADA. TRIGGERS: K <3.3 mmol/L -> hold insulin and replace potassium first; beta-hydroxybutyrate >=3.0 or bicarb <18 -> treat as DKA; K or Mg low -> replete + telemetry.
initial_imaging_dx: Diagnostics are lab-driven; imaging is minimal and precipitant-directed, not routine. 12-lead ECG on admission (screen silent ischemia and K/Mg-related QT/T-wave changes). Point-of-care beta-hydroxybutyrate meter at presentation to exclude ketosis. CXR only if infection/precipitant suspected. No routine advanced imaging. Bedside foot inspection/10 g monofilament exam; refer for outpatient dilated retinal exam and podiatry (not urgent inpatient). Order imaging solely to evaluate a suspected precipitant (e.g., CXR for pneumonia, US/CT if intra-abdominal source).
time_critical_interventions: First hour: POC glucose + BMP/VBG to EXCLUDE DKA/HHS and check potassium. SAFETY never-event guard: if K <3.3 mmol/L, HOLD insulin and replace potassium before dosing (insulin-driven hypokalemia can be fatal). IV isotonic saline (0.9% NaCl) for volume depletion. Correct hyperglycemia gradually (avoid rapid osmotic shifts); target 140-180 mg/dL; initiate scheduled subcutaneous insulin when persistent BG >=180. Nurse-driven hypoglycemia protocol active from admission: BG <70 -> 15-20 g fast carbohydrate PO (or IV D50/IM glucagon if NPO/unable), recheck in 15 min ("rule of 15"), and reassess/reduce insulin. Measure A1C this admission. Not a door-to-intervention emergency, but the K-before-insulin check and crisis-exclusion are strictly time-sensitive.
initial_management: Fluids: 0.9% NaCl for volume depletion, individualized in cardiac/renal disease. INSULIN — subcutaneous basal-bolus (basal + nutritional + correctional) is preferred; SOLE sliding-scale insulin is discouraged (RABBIT-2). Start total daily dose 0.3-0.5 units/kg/day split ~50% basal (glargine/detemir/degludec) + ~50% rapid-acting nutritional (lispro/aspart/glulisine) with a correction scale; reduce to 0.2-0.3 units/kg if age >=70, eGFR <45, or hypoglycemia risk. IV insulin infusion NOT needed for uncomplicated hyperglycemia (reserve for crisis/ICU). Potassium repletion to keep >4.0 (hold insulin if <3.3). Home-regimen handling: HOLD SGLT2 inhibitors inpatient (euglycemic DKA risk); hold metformin if AKI/contrast/hemodynamic instability; generally hold sulfonylureas (hypoglycemia) and hold GLP-1 RA if GI intolerance/NPO; a DPP-4 inhibitor is acceptable for mild hyperglycemia (A1C <7.5%, BG <180). Treat the precipitant (antibiotics, steroid adjustment). Target glucose 140-180 mg/dL (Endocrine Society 100-180).
day1_milestones: Confirm NOT DKA/HHS and document effective osm/anion gap; set glucose target 140-180; start weight/renal-adjusted basal-bolus insulin (stop sole sliding-scale); isotonic fluids and repair volume; result A1C; identify and treat precipitant; begin POC glucose AC+HS (before meals + bedtime) or q4-6h if NPO/tube-fed; initiate CDCES survival-skills education; complete medication reconciliation and hold contraindicated agents (SGLT2i, metformin if unstable); VTE risk assessment; consistent-carbohydrate diet ordered; assess adequacy of home regimen and social/affordability barriers early.
day2_milestones: Titrate insulin off prior-24h data (adjust total daily dose +/-10-20%, roll correction requirements into basal/nutritional); confirm resolution of symptomatic hyperglycemia and euvolemia; discontinue IV fluids and transition fully to subcutaneous regimen; review for any BG <70 events and de-intensify if present; continue survival-skills teaching (glucometer use, insulin injection, hypoglycemia treatment, sick-day rules); begin designing the A1C-guided DISCHARGE regimen; involve endocrinology/CDCES if type 1, insulin pump, steroid-induced, or new insulin start.
day3plus_milestones: Stable glucose 140-180 without recurrent hypoglycemia or severe hyperglycemia on the intended discharge regimen for >=24 h; finalize discharge medications and doses; complete education with teach-back (patient/caregiver demonstrates insulin injection + glucometer + hypoglycemia rescue); secure insulin, syringes/pens, glucometer/strips, glucagon, and sharps container with affordability/prior-auth resolved; schedule follow-up. Most uncomplicated patients (DRG 639) are discharge-ready by day 1-2; CC/MCC comorbidity is what extends stay beyond day 2.
consults_multidisciplinary: Certified Diabetes Care & Education Specialist (CDCES)/diabetes educator EARLY (Day 1) for survival-skills education (ADA/Endocrine Society recommended, reduces readmission); Endocrinology/inpatient glucose-management service for type 1, insulin-pump, steroid-induced, labile, or new insulin starts; clinical pharmacy for insulin titration, medication reconciliation, and affordability/access; Registered Dietitian/Nutrition for MNT and consistent-carbohydrate diet; Case management/Social work for insulin cost, supplies, and follow-up (esp. food insecurity); PT/OT if deconditioned; outpatient referrals to ophthalmology (dilated retinal exam) and podiatry; behavioral health if adherence/psychosocial barriers.
monitoring_level: General medical FLOOR for essentially all uncomplicated hyperglycemia. POC glucose AC+HS if eating; q4-6h if NPO, on continuous enteral feeds, or (rarely) IV insulin. Telemetry only for significant K/Mg derangement or cardiac comorbidity. Step-down/ICU NOT indicated for uncomplicated hyperglycemia. ESCALATION triggers: evolution into DKA/HHS (pH <7.3, anion gap >12, effective osm >320) requiring IV insulin infusion and ICU/step-down; hemodynamic instability; NEWS2 >=7; recurrent severe hypoglycemia. Reassess the insulin regimen at any BG <70.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Consistent-carbohydrate diabetic diet (~45-60 g carb/meal) with RD input; MATCH nutritional (prandial) insulin to actual intake — hold prandial dose if NPO/poor PO to prevent hypoglycemia. Early ambulation; avoid bed rest/deconditioning. VTE prophylaxis by risk (Padua/Caprini) — pharmacologic LMWH/UFH unless contraindicated (hyperglycemia does not itself alter VTE prophylaxis). Glucose control 140-180 mg/dL with nurse-driven hypoglycemia protocol. Remove IV lines/urinary catheters as early as possible (CLABSI/CAUTI prevention); avoid unnecessary indwelling catheters.
discharge_criteria: Acute hyperglycemia trigger resolved and precipitant treated; euvolemic and hemodynamically stable; NOT in DKA/HHS; glucose stable (generally 140-180, no BG <70 and no recurrent BG >250-300) on the intended oral/subcutaneous DISCHARGE regimen for >=24 h; tolerating diet; patient/caregiver demonstrates survival skills via teach-back (glucose monitoring, insulin administration, hypoglycemia treatment, sick-day rules); discharge insulin/supplies obtained and affordable; follow-up arranged.
discharge_planning: Structured discharge (ADA): discharge medication reconciliation with an A1C-guided regimen; provide glucometer/strips/lancets, insulin pens/syringes, glucagon, and sharps container; written individualized regimen, glucose targets, and sick-day plan; follow-up with PCP/endocrinology within 1-2 weeks (within ~1 week or a phone check in a few days for new insulin starts); outpatient DSMES/CDCES referral; FILL prescriptions before discharge and resolve affordability (insulin $35 copay-cap programs, patient-assistance) and prior authorization; transition-of-care communication to PCP; address SDOH (food security, health literacy, transportation).
expected_los: CMS GMLOS by DRG: 639 (no CC/MCC) 2.1 days; 638 (with CC) 2.9 days; 637 (with MCC) 3.9 days (AMLOS 2.4 / 3.7 / 5.3). Typical uncomplicated hyperglycemia stay 2-3 days; stay is largely driven by comorbidity tier, not the glycemic management itself.
target_los: 1-2 days for uncomplicated diabetes (DRG 639) with Day-1 education and early discharge-regimen design; a substantial share are observation-appropriate (<2 midnights) and avoid inpatient status entirely; 2.5-3.5 days when a CC/MCC comorbidity drives the stay. Blended best-practice target ~2 days, below the 639/638 GMLOS.
quality_metrics: CMS/IQR eCQMs: Hospital Harm - Severe Hypoglycemia (BG <54 mg/dL with antihyperglycemic exposure) and Hospital Harm - Severe Hyperglycemia (BG >300 / hyperglycemic days). Inpatient glycemic dashboards: % patient-days in target 70-180 without hypoglycemia, hyperglycemia rate (days with BG >180/>250), hypoglycemia rate (days with BG <70 and <54). Process measures: avoidance of sole sliding-scale insulin, A1C measured this admission, DSMES/CDCES delivered, discharge follow-up scheduled, insulin/supplies secured. Outcome: 30-day all-cause readmission and ED revisit.
common_complications: Iatrogenic HYPOGLYCEMIA (most common; from over-aggressive or non-intake-matched insulin, missed meals/NPO, renal insufficiency) -> mitigate with weight/renal-adjusted dosing, prandial insulin matched to PO intake, nurse-driven hypoglycemia protocol, and CGM in high-risk patients. Insulin-induced HYPOKALEMIA (monitor/replace K, hold insulin if <3.3). Rebound hyperglycemia and glucose variability. Progression to DKA/HHS if under-treated (the escalation event). Volume overload from fluids in cardiac/renal patients. Hospital-acquired infection from unnecessary lines/catheters.
readmission_drivers: Insulin non-adherence or INABILITY TO AFFORD insulin/supplies; inadequate survival-skills education at discharge; no or failed follow-up; recurrent hyperglycemia or progression to DKA; iatrogenic hypoglycemia causing bounce-back; unaddressed precipitant (infection, glucocorticoids, non-adherence); low health literacy and food insecurity. Prevention levers: CDCES education with teach-back, secure affordable insulin BEFORE discharge ($35 cap/patient-assistance), early follow-up (<=7-14 days, phone check for new insulin), transition-of-care call, explicit sick-day rules, and A1C-guided regimen selection.
guideline_source: American Diabetes Association, Standards of Care in Diabetes—2026, Section 16 "Diabetes Care in the Hospital" (with Section 6 glycemic goals/hyperglycemic crises); Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline 2022 (Korytkowski et al.), "Management of Hyperglycemia in Hospitalized Adult Patients in Non-Critical Care Settings." Supported by AACE consensus.
key_citations: ADA. 16. Diabetes Care in the Hospital: Standards of Care in Diabetes—2026. Diabetes Care 2026;49(Suppl 1):S339-S358. Korytkowski MT, et al. Management of Hyperglycemia in Hospitalized Adult Patients in Non-Critical Care Settings: An Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline. J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2022;107(8):2101-2128; PMID 35690958; DOI 10.1210/clinem/dgac278. Umpierrez GE, et al. RABBIT 2 trial (basal-bolus vs sliding-scale, non-ICU T2D). Diabetes Care 2007;30(9):2181-6; PMID 17513708; DOI 10.2337/dc07-0295. Umpierrez GE, et al. RABBIT 2 Surgery. Diabetes Care 2011;34(2):256-61; PMID 21228246; DOI 10.2337/dc10-1407. NICE-SUGAR (Finfer S, et al.) Intensive vs conventional glucose control in the critically ill. N Engl J Med 2009;360(13):1283-97; PMID 19318384; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa0810625. (Citations retrieved/verified via PubMed.)
evidence_grade: Moderate to Strong. Anchored by Class I society guidelines (ADA 2026, Endocrine Society 2022) with strong RCT support for the two load-bearing pillars: basal-bolus over sole sliding-scale (RABBIT-2 medical improved control; RABBIT-2 Surgery reduced a composite complication outcome, OR 3.39) and a 140-180 mg/dL target with avoidance of tight control (NICE-SUGAR: intensive 81-108 increased mortality). However, most Endocrine Society operational recommendations are Conditional with low/very-low certainty, and the necessity/threshold for INPATIENT admission of uncomplicated hyperglycemia is largely expert consensus. Grade the glycemic-target and insulin-strategy elements Strong; grade admission/monitoring/discharge specifics Moderate-to-consensus.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Across the collapsed 637/638/639 group, severity is driven by COMORBIDITIES coded as CC/MCC, not the uncomplicated hyperglycemia itself. 639 (no CC/MCC): shortest stay (GMLOS 2.1 d), floor-level, education-and-transition focus. 638 (CC, e.g., CKD stage 3, moderate infection, controlled comorbidity): GMLOS 2.9 d, more titration/monitoring, higher hypoglycemia risk with renal impairment. 637 (MCC, e.g., sepsis, AKI, acute MI, respiratory failure): GMLOS 3.9 d, may need step-down/ICU and IV insulin and longer stay — but if the MCC is a hyperglycemic crisis (DKA/HHS) the case exits this subset to the rank-13 crisis pathway. Renal MCCs specifically raise hypoglycemia risk and warrant dose reduction + CGM.
pathway_pearls: 1) GATEKEEP FIRST: prove it is NOT DKA/HHS (VBG/bicarb, anion gap, beta-hydroxybutyrate >=3.0, effective osm >320) — that single decision determines the pathway and disposition. 2) Abandon sole sliding-scale; use weight-based basal-bolus 0.3-0.5 units/kg (~50/50), dose DOWN for age/renal disease (RABBIT-2), and check K before the first insulin dose. 3) Target 140-180, NOT tight control (NICE-SUGAR harm); run a nurse-driven hypoglycemia protocol and MATCH prandial insulin to actual PO intake (hold if NPO). 4) Treat the stay as an EDUCATION + TRANSITION event: start CDCES survival-skills teaching Day 1, design the A1C-guided discharge regimen early, and secure affordable insulin/supplies plus <=1-2 week follow-up to prevent bounce-back; hold SGLT2 inhibitors inpatient.

condition: ENT / Airway Procedures (T&A, sinus, upper airway)
admission_criteria: Most T&A and FESS are ambulatory/23-h observation; reserve true INPATIENT admission + overnight monitoring for: age <3 yr (AAO-HNSF KAS 12); severe OSA on PSG (obstructive AHI >=10 events/hr, SpO2 nadir <80%, or both); high-risk comorbidity (Down syndrome, craniofacial anomaly, neuromuscular disease/CP, sickle cell, mucopolysaccharidosis, obesity BMI >=95th %ile, cor pulmonale/pulmonary HTN, failure to thrive, bleeding disorder); intraop/PACU respiratory compromise, desaturation or supplemental-O2/airway-support need; post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage requiring OR control; dehydration/inability to maintain PO; peritonsillar/parapharyngeal/retropharyngeal or deep-neck abscess needing drainage + IV antibiotics; sinusitis with orbital (subperiosteal/orbital abscess) or intracranial complication (epidural/subdural empyema, cavernous sinus thrombosis, meningitis, Pott puffy tumor); posterior epistaxis with packing, instability, coagulopathy or transfusion; airway emergency (epiglottitis/supraglottitis, airway foreign body, angioedema, severe laryngomalacia for supraglottoplasty); >1-h travel from care or inadequate home support
risk_stratification: Obstructive AHI severity (mild 1-4.9, moderate 5-9.9, severe >=10/hr) + SpO2 nadir: AHI>=10 or nadir<80% mandates inpatient monitoring/PICU (KAS 12); adult OSA STOP-BANG >=3 intermediate / >=5 high risk -> continuous oximetry/capnography, opioid caution; ASA physical-status class; difficult-airway assessment (modified Mallampati, Cormack-Lehane) -> difficult-airway cart/plan; Chandler classification of orbital complications I-V (III subperiosteal abscess, IV orbital abscess, V cavernous sinus thrombosis -> urgent surgical drainage + IV antibiotics); post-tonsillectomy bleed timing (primary <24 h vs secondary, typically POD5-10); Centor/McIsaac and Paradise criteria (>=7 infections/yr, 5/yr x2, or 3/yr x3) for recurrent-infection indication; Caprini VTE score in post-pubertal/adult; sickle-cell status; pediatric red flags age <3 yr + listed comorbidities
initial_workup_labs: CBC; type-and-screen (crossmatch if active hemorrhage or high bleeding risk); coagulation studies (PT/INR, aPTT) and von Willebrand/factor panel ONLY if personal/family bleeding history, anticoagulant use, liver disease, or bruising (not routine per guideline); structured bleeding-history questionnaire pre-op; sickle-cell screen in at-risk populations; urine/serum pregnancy test in adolescent females pre-anesthesia; for deep-neck/orbital/intracranial infection: CBC w/ diff, CRP, ESR, blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics, BMP for hydration; rapid GAS/throat culture for pharyngitis; lactate + cultures if septic (Surviving Sepsis); HbA1c/glucose if diabetic; abnormal coags/low platelets trigger hematology and correction before OR
initial_imaging_dx: Polysomnography BEFORE tonsillectomy for obstructive SDB in children <2 yr OR with obesity, Down syndrome, craniofacial anomaly, neuromuscular disorder, sickle cell, or mucopolysaccharidosis (KAS 5), and when exam-symptom severity discordant (KAS 6); contrast-enhanced CT neck for deep-neck infection to distinguish drainable collection (roughly >=2-2.5 cm) from phlegmon (obtain urgently, within hours if airway threat); lateral neck X-ray for retropharyngeal widening / epiglottitis 'thumbprint'; urgent contrast CT orbits/sinuses/brain for suspected orbital or intracranial complication, MRI w/ contrast for intracranial extension/cavernous sinus thrombosis; nasal endoscopy for sinusitis, with sinus CT reserved for complications, unclear/chronic disease, or surgical planning (NOT routine uncomplicated ABRS, AAO-HNSF 2025); flexible laryngoscopy for airway assessment, direct laryngoscopy/bronchoscopy for foreign body/airway lesion; CXR if aspiration/foreign body
time_critical_interventions: Airway emergency (epiglottitis, angioedema, airway foreign body, expanding neck hematoma): secure airway in controlled OR setting with ENT + anesthesia, difficult-airway cart, surgical-airway (tracheostomy/cricothyrotomy) backup, keep child calm/avoid agitation until airway controlled; post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage with active bleed: emergent return to OR for hemostasis, protect airway (aspiration risk), type-and-cross/resuscitate, adjunct tranexamic acid; deep-neck abscess with airway compromise or descending mediastinitis: emergent surgical drainage; orbital subperiosteal/orbital abscess with vision threat (proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, decreasing acuity): IV antibiotics + urgent surgical drainage within hours + emergent ophthalmology; sepsis: Surviving Sepsis 1-h bundle (broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1 h, lactate, blood cultures, 30 mL/kg crystalloid if hypotension or lactate >=4); anaphylaxis/angioedema: IM epinephrine
initial_management: Single intraoperative IV dexamethasone 0.5 mg/kg (max ~10-16 mg) - reduces PONV/pain, improves PO intake (KAS 2, strong); opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia with SCHEDULED acetaminophen + ibuprofen (KAS 13; ibuprofen does NOT meaningfully raise clinically significant bleeding); antiemetic ondansetron; HARD STOP on codeine and any codeine-containing drug <12 yr and avoid tramadol/opioids in OSA (KAS 14, FDA black box); NO routine perioperative antibiotics for tonsillectomy (KAS 10); isotonic IV fluids (LR/balanced or NS) until adequate PO; deep-neck/peritonsillar abscess: IV ampicillin-sulbactam or clindamycin (add vancomycin if MRSA risk/severe) + drainage (needle aspiration or I&D for PTA, surgical drainage for parapharyngeal/retropharyngeal); acute complicated sinusitis: IV ceftriaxone + vancomycin +/- metronidazole (intracranial) + endoscopic drainage; uncomplicated adult ABRS: watchful waiting regardless of severity, amoxicillin +/- clavulanate first-line if treating (AAO-HNSF 2025); epistaxis: topical oxymetazoline, packing, cautery, topical TXA, reverse anticoagulation, SPA ligation/embolization if refractory; airway edema/croup: dexamethasone + nebulized racemic epinephrine +/- heliox; transfusion restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL (higher if active bleed/cardiac); reversal: vit K/4F-PCC (warfarin), protamine (heparin), idarucizumab (dabigatran), andexanet/4F-PCC (Xa inhibitor), DDAVP/platelets (vWD/antiplatelet); continue home CPAP for known OSA
day1_milestones: POD0: PACU recovery with continuous pulse oximetry (mandatory for <3 yr and severe OSA); reapply CPAP for known OSA; start scheduled acetaminophen + ibuprofen, antiemetics PRN, minimize/avoid opioids; encourage clear liquids advancing as tolerated, IV fluids until PO adequate; HOB elevation, cool mist humidification; vigilant monitoring for airway obstruction/desaturation, opioid oversedation, and primary hemorrhage; for deep-neck/sinus infection begin/continue IV antibiotics and confirm source control (drainage) achieved; early mobilization; establish weight-based analgesic and fluid orders
day2_milestones: POD1: continue scheduled multimodal non-opioid analgesia; confirm adequate oral hydration/intake (key discharge gate); wean supplemental O2 and document no respiratory events / stable overnight oximetry off monitoring (esp OSA, <3 yr); for abscess/deep-neck infection reassess exam, downtrending fever/CRP, transition IV-to-PO antibiotics once improving and tolerating PO, remove drain when output low; for orbital/sinus complication reassess vision/proptosis and re-image if not improving in 24-48 h; most uncomplicated monitored T&A/FESS meet discharge criteria and go home
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ (complicated subset only): persistent airway support needs, ongoing IV-antibiotic-requiring infection, post-hemorrhage observation, or slow oral intake/dehydration; continue IV-to-PO antibiotic and analgesic conversion, ensure afebrile with controlled pain and adequate hydration; repeat contrast imaging and consider re-drainage if abscess not responding by 48-72 h; ophthalmology/neurosurgery reassessment for orbital/intracranial cases; finalize discharge with explicit secondary-hemorrhage return precautions (POD5-10 window) and OPAT/home-IV arrangements if prolonged antibiotics needed
consults_multidisciplinary: Otolaryngology (primary surgeon); anesthesiology/pediatric + difficult-airway; sleep medicine/pulmonology for OSA (PSG interpretation, CPAP titration); pediatric hospitalist co-management for infants/comorbidities; pediatric intensivist/PICU for severe OSA, airway procedures, post-op respiratory compromise; infectious disease for complicated deep-neck/orbital/intracranial infection and OPAT; ophthalmology (orbital complication) and neurosurgery (intracranial complication) emergently; hematology for known/suspected bleeding disorder (vWD, hemophilia) for peri-op DDAVP/factor planning; nutrition/dietitian and speech-language/feeding for poor intake, failure to thrive, or airway-swallow concerns; pharmacy for weight-based dosing and opioid stewardship; care management/social work for disposition, home support and follow-up; child life for pediatric coping
monitoring_level: Floor bed with CONTINUOUS pulse oximetry is the standard for admitted overnight T&A; step-down/PICU for severe OSA (AHI >=10 or SpO2 nadir <80%), age <2 yr with severe OSA, significant comorbidity, post-op respiratory compromise, need for CPAP/airway support, airway procedures, or post-hemorrhage with airway risk; add continuous capnography if opioids given to an OSA patient; escalation triggers: recurrent obstructive events or SpO2 <90%, apnea, rising work of breathing, need for airway intervention/reintubation, hemodynamic instability, active bleeding, or altered mental status
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: clear liquids advancing to soft/regular as tolerated with hydration as the key tracked metric; avoid sharp/acidic foods after tonsillectomy; antiemetics to enable intake; Mobility: early same-day ambulation, rapid return to activity; VTE: pediatric patients are low risk -> early mobilization/mechanical measures; pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH) only for post-pubertal/adult patients per Caprini risk, balanced against post-tonsillar bleeding risk (hold in active hemorrhage/immediate post-op); glucose control if diabetic; remove peripheral IV once PO intake adequate, avoid unnecessary Foley/lines
discharge_criteria: Adequate oral hydration and intake with pain controlled on oral analgesics; no respiratory compromise off supplemental O2 with stable oximetry over a defined observation period (overnight for high-risk/severe OSA); no active bleeding and appropriate post-op observation interval elapsed; afebrile or clearly improving infection markers with tolerance of oral antibiotics (infectious cases); stable vitals and baseline mental status; caregiver demonstrates understanding of hydration, pain plan, and bleeding precautions; CPAP arranged/continued for OSA where indicated
discharge_planning: Disposition home with caregiver (majority); medication reconciliation: scheduled acetaminophen + ibuprofen, explicitly NO codeine/tramadol (<12 yr, OSA), antibiotics only if a true infectious indication, resume home meds, continue CPAP; follow-up ENT in ~2-3 weeks (sooner for complicated cases) plus ophthalmology/neurosurgery/ID as needed; education: maintain hydration, expect pain to peak POD3-5, STRICT secondary-hemorrhage warning (any bleeding POD5-10 -> ED immediately), activity limits; transitions of care: written instructions with return precautions, PCP notification, OPAT/home-IV antibiotic setup for prolonged courses
expected_los: Approximate CMS FY GMLOS by tier (typical benchmarks): no-CC/MCC procedure DRGs 134/136/138 and salivary 139 ~1.5-1.8 d; CC/MCC procedure DRGs 133/135/137 ~3.6-4.4 d; medical Other-ENT-diagnoses 156 (no CC/MCC) ~2.1 d, 155 (CC) ~2.7 d, 154 (MCC) ~4.4 d; the common uncomplicated admitted pediatric T&A runs ~1 day (23-h observation) - group blended range roughly 1.5-4.5 d driven by CC/MCC tier (values approximate, confirm against current CMS DRG table)
target_los: 1 day (23-h overnight monitored observation) for uncomplicated pediatric T&A / FESS that meets inpatient/monitoring criteria; 2-3 days for drained peritonsillar/deep-neck abscess with IV-to-PO antibiotic transition; 3-5 days for orbital/intracranial sinus complication or airway procedure after source control; optimize by routing non-high-risk cases to same-day discharge or 23-h observation rather than full inpatient admission
quality_metrics: AAO-HNSF tonsillectomy process measures: intraoperative dexamethasone administration rate, NO perioperative antibiotic rate, NO codeine <12 yr rate, ibuprofen/acetaminophen use, and mandatory annual audit of primary (<24 h) and secondary (>24 h) post-tonsillectomy bleeding rates (KAS 15); return-to-OR hemorrhage rate; 7- and 30-day ED revisit/readmission (bleeding, dehydration); unplanned PICU admission / reintubation / post-op respiratory event rate; same-day-surgery-to-inpatient conversion rate; PONV rate; opioid-prescribing/stewardship; sinusitis antibiotic stewardship (watchful-waiting for uncomplicated ABRS, amoxicillin +/- clavulanate first-line); surgical safety checklist compliance; patient/caregiver experience
common_complications: Post-tonsillectomy hemorrhage (primary <24 h ~0.5-1%, secondary POD5-10 ~2-4%, overall ~3-5%) -> meticulous hemostasis, annual rate tracking, OR-ready protocol, TXA; post-op respiratory compromise/obstruction/desaturation (severe OSA, <3 yr, obesity) -> overnight continuous oximetry, PICU, opioid minimization, CPAP; dehydration/poor oral intake -> hydration focus, IV fluids, antiemetics, analgesia; PONV -> dexamethasone + ondansetron; poorly controlled pain (peaks POD3-5) -> scheduled multimodal regimen; opioid-related respiratory depression (codeine fatalities drove FDA black box) -> opioid avoidance; deep-neck infection sequelae (airway compromise, descending mediastinitis, Lemierre/IJ thrombophlebitis, sepsis); sinus-complication sequelae (vision loss, intracranial extension, meningitis); laryngospasm / negative-pressure pulmonary edema
readmission_drivers: Secondary hemorrhage (POD5-10) is the leading driver of post-T&A ED revisit/readmission; dehydration and inadequate oral intake from uncontrolled pain; PONV; post-op respiratory events; opioid oversedation or conversely undertreated pain; recurrent/persistent or re-accumulated abscess and incomplete source control in the infectious subset; prevention levers: caregiver education on hydration and the exact bleeding-return window, scheduled non-opioid multimodal analgesia, documented PO tolerance before discharge, early ENT follow-up, complete surgical drainage, and clear written return precautions
guideline_source: AAO-HNSF Clinical Practice Guideline: Tonsillectomy in Children (Update), Mitchell et al., Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2019 (primary); AAP Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis and Management of Childhood Obstructive Sleep Apnea Syndrome, Marcus et al., Pediatrics 2012; AAO-HNSF Clinical Practice Guideline: Adult Sinusitis (Update), Payne et al., Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2025 (replaces Rosenfeld 2015); supported by ASA perioperative OSA and difficult-airway guidance

evidence_grade: Strong for core pediatric tonsillectomy/OSA pathway - multiple current Class I society CPGs (AAO-HNSF Tonsillectomy 2019, AAP Childhood OSA 2012, AAO-HNSF Adult Sinusitis 2025) reinforced by a landmark multicenter RCT (CHAT, NEJM 2013); Moderate-to-consensus for the heterogeneous complicated inpatient subset (deep-neck, orbital/intracranial, airway procedures, epistaxis), which rests on observational data and expert consensus with few RCTs; LOS/DRG figures are administrative benchmarks (approximate)
severity_cc_mcc_notes: This collapsed group spans surgical procedure DRGs (133-139) and medical Other-ENT-diagnosis DRGs (154-156), each split by severity. Without-CC/MCC tiers (134/136/138/139) are uncomplicated overnight/23-h stays, ~1.5-1.8 d, floor with oximetry. CC tier (e.g., dehydration, post-op infection, moderate comorbidity) extends LOS to ~2.7-3.6 d. MCC tier (severe OSA with cor pulmonale, sepsis from deep-neck/intracranial infection, aspiration pneumonia, respiratory failure, major hemorrhage requiring transfusion, descending mediastinitis) drives ICU/PICU use and LOS ~4.4-5+ d with higher complication and readmission rates. Note that pediatric severe OSA and airway procedures mandate ICU-level monitoring independent of the coded CC/MCC label
pathway_pearls: 1) Risk-triage who truly needs admission: age <3 yr OR severe OSA (AHI >=10 or SpO2 nadir <80%) require mandatory overnight continuous-oximetry monitoring (AAO-HNSF KAS 12) - route everyone else to 23-h observation/ambulatory, avoiding both over-admission and under-monitoring; 2) Opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia is the safety linchpin: single intraop dexamethasone + scheduled acetaminophen/ibuprofen (ibuprofen does NOT meaningfully increase significant bleeding) with an absolute STOP on codeine/tramadol under 12 and in OSA (FDA black box); 3) Hydration + secondary-hemorrhage education drive the readmission curve - document adequate PO before discharge, teach the POD5-10 bleeding window with immediate-return precautions, and audit your primary/secondary bleed rates annually (KAS 15); 4) For the complicated infectious/airway subset, source control + a difficult-airway plan beat antibiotics alone - contrast CT to find a drainable collection, early ENT drainage of PTA/deep-neck/subperiosteal orbital abscess, and OR-ready airway management for epiglottitis, expanding hematoma, or descending mediastinitis
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Tonsillectomy/adenoidectomy, sinus, and other ENT procedures; substantial pediatric share; mostly the complicated inpatient subset.
