admission_criteria: Bronchiolitis is a CLINICAL diagnosis (AAP 2014) in infants 1-23 mo; admit for objective physiologic failure, not a positive viral test. Inpatient indications: persistent SpO2 <90% on room air despite airway clearance; moderate-severe distress (age-adjusted tachypnea RR >60-70, marked retractions/nasal flaring/grunting); apnea (witnessed or high-risk); inability to maintain hydration / PO intake <50% of baseline or clinical dehydration; need for supplemental O2 or HFNC. High-risk features lowering the admit bar: age <12 wk, ex-premature (esp. <32 wk, postmenstrual age <48 wk), hemodynamically significant CHD, chronic lung disease, immunodeficiency, neuromuscular disease, unreliable follow-up/social risk. ED discharge/observation is appropriate for the well-hydrated infant with SpO2 >=90% RA, mild work of breathing, and reliable caregiver + 24-48 h follow-up. Counsel that illness typically worsens to nadir on day 3-5 of symptoms - factor into disposition.
risk_stratification: No single universally validated score; stratify on physiology plus validated severity scales. Pulse oximetry: supplement O2 if SpO2 persistently <90% RA (AAP threshold); an SpO2 >=90% target is supported as non-inferior to higher targets for discharge fitness. Age-specific tachypnea (RR >60 in infants) and retraction/grunting grade drive severity. Validated scores: Wang Bronchiolitis Severity Score 0-12 (>=8 = severe); modified Tal score 0-12; ReSVinet score 0-20; RDAI/RACS to objectively judge any bronchodilator response. Apnea risk: age <=1 mo or postmenstrual age <48 wk or prematurity -> continuous monitoring and low PICU threshold. Escalation flags: rising Pediatric Early Warning Score (PEWS); HFNC failure defined as >=3 of persistent tachycardia/tachypnea/hypoxemia/clinician-triggered review (PARIS criteria) -> ICU. Feeding <50% baseline or >5% dehydration -> NG/IV hydration.
initial_workup_labs: Routine labs NOT indicated - diagnosis is clinical (AAP 2014, Choosing Wisely). Selective testing only: POC glucose + serum electrolytes if poor feeding/dehydration or on IV fluids (monitor Na for SIADH/hyponatremia); capillary or venous blood gas if severe distress or impending failure (rising pCO2 signals fatigue); RSV/multiplex respiratory PCR ONLY for infection-control cohorting or high-risk/atypical presentation, not to guide therapy; in the febrile infant <60-90 days, evaluate for concurrent serious bacterial infection - obtain UA + urine culture (UTI ~1-3% even when RSV+), with CBC/blood culture/LP per the age-based febrile-infant pathway. CRP/procalcitonin, routine CBC, and viral testing to guide management are NOT recommended.
initial_imaging_dx: Chest radiograph NOT routine (AAP 2014) - it is abnormal in most infants and drives inappropriate antibiotic use. Reserve CXR for: diagnostic uncertainty, severe/ICU-level disease, suspected complication (focal consolidation, pneumothorax, atelectasis not improving), or clinical deterioration; obtain within the hour if acute decompensation. No routine viral-panel or other imaging/procedures. The primary diagnostic assessment is serial bedside evaluation (work of breathing, auscultation, SpO2 trend) repeated before and after airway clearance.
time_critical_interventions: No door-to-drug bundle - the time-critical reframe is rescue of apnea and impending respiratory failure. Immediate: continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring plus PICU activation for apnea or rising pCO2/exhaustion; supplemental O2 within minutes for SpO2 <90%; nasal saline + superficial suction before feeds and PRN to clear obstruction. Early HFNC for hypoxemic infants failing low-flow reduces escalation of care by ~50% (PARIS RCT, NNT ~9) although it does not shorten LOS - start promptly rather than as late rescue. Rapid RSV/viral PCR at admission only to cohort for infection control. Pre-define the escalation ladder: low-flow NC -> HFNC 1-2 L/kg/min -> CPAP/BiPAP -> intubation.
initial_management: Supportive care is the entire evidence-based therapy. Oxygen titrated to SpO2 >=90% (AAP). Airway clearance: nasal saline + superficial (not routine deep) suction. Hydration: if unable to feed, NG feeding or IV ISOTONIC fluids (0.9%-saline-based; avoid hypotonic fluids -> hyponatremia/SIADH); NG equals IV in efficacy. Respiratory support escalated stepwise (NC -> HFNC -> CPAP -> intubation). DO NOT routinely use (AAP/Choosing Wisely, strong recs): albuterol/salbutamol, nebulized epinephrine, systemic OR inhaled corticosteroids, antibiotics (only for proven concurrent bacterial infection e.g. AOM/UTI/bacterial pneumonia), chest physiotherapy, or deep suctioning. Selective adjunct: nebulized 3% hypertonic saline for admitted infants (Cochrane 2023: LOS -0.40 d, low certainty) - reserve for expected LOS >3 d. One-time bronchodilator trial only in an older wheezer with strong asthma risk, continued ONLY if objective RDAI/RACS improvement.
day1_milestones: Confirm clinical diagnosis and severity tier; set respiratory-support level and weaning target (SpO2 >=90%); initiate airway-clearance protocol (saline + superficial suction); establish hydration plan (trial PO; NG/IV isotonic if intake <50% baseline); order intermittent spot-check pulse oximetry if stable and off O2, continuous if on O2/HFNC or apnea-risk; institute contact/droplet precautions and cohorting; weight-based fluid and antipyretic dosing; deliberately withhold bronchodilators/steroids/antibiotics/CXR/viral testing unless specifically indicated; screen for high-risk features (age <12 wk, prematurity, CHD/CLD/immunodeficiency, apnea); begin caregiver education; counsel that the illness typically worsens to nadir on day 3-5.
day2_milestones: Reassess trajectory against the expected day 3-5 nadir; wean O2/HFNC as tolerated while maintaining SpO2 >=90%; advance oral feeds and reduce IV/NG support; de-escalate to intermittent pulse oximetry once stable off supplemental O2; continue airway clearance timed to feeds; monitor for complications (dehydration, hyponatremia - recheck Na if on IV fluids, apnea, worsening distress); reinforce that no antibiotics/steroids/bronchodilators are needed; continue caregiver suctioning teaching and feeding coaching.
day3plus_milestones: Most infants reach nadir (illness day 3-5) then improve - wean off HFNC/supplemental O2; establish sustained full PO intake and discontinue IV; confirm SpO2 >=90% on room air maintained during sleep and feeds for the unit-defined window (commonly 4-12 h); verify mild/improving work of breathing; complete caregiver competency (nasal suctioning, red-flag return precautions); arrange PCP follow-up within 24-48 h; ensure season-appropriate RSV immunoprophylaxis (nirsevimab) is given or scheduled if eligible; discharge when criteria met. Escalate to PICU at any point for HFNC failure, recurrent apnea, or impending respiratory failure.
consults_multidisciplinary: Uncomplicated bronchiolitis needs NO subspecialty consult. Respiratory therapy: HFNC titration and airway-clearance protocol (day 1). PICU/critical care: HFNC failure, recurrent apnea, need for CPAP/BiPAP or intubation. Pulmonology: severe/atypical course, recurrent wheeze, or underlying chronic lung disease. Cardiology: hemodynamically significant CHD. Nutrition/lactation: feeding support and NG plan. Pharmacy: weight-based dosing and fluid stewardship. Care management + social work: home-oxygen setup, transport, follow-up access, and social risk. Complex-care/palliative: technology-dependent or medically complex infants. Infection prevention: cohorting during seasonal surge.
monitoring_level: Floor/ward for most: intermittent spot-check pulse oximetry when stable and off O2 (continuous pulse ox prolongs LOS without safety benefit - McCulloh 2015); continuous cardiorespiratory monitoring if on supplemental O2/HFNC or at apnea risk. Step-down/intermediate: HFNC where unit policy permits it on the floor vs a step-down bed. PICU: HFNC failure (>=3 PARIS criteria), recurrent/severe apnea, need for CPAP/BiPAP or intubation, rising pCO2/impending respiratory failure, or hemodynamic instability. Escalation triggers: rising PEWS, SpO2 persistently <90% despite escalating support, apnea, or exhaustion.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: maintain hydration by PO; use NG feeds or IV ISOTONIC fluids if intake <50% baseline (NG equals IV; hypotonic fluids contraindicated -> hyponatremia). Advance to full PO as work of breathing improves; clear the airway before feeds; assess aspiration risk at high respiratory rates. Mobility: developmentally normal handling/holding by caregiver as tolerated and upright positioning - there is no bed-rest concept in infants. VTE prophylaxis NOT indicated in infants/toddlers (neither pharmacologic nor mechanical prophylaxis is standard in this age group). Glucose control not applicable. Lines/catheters: place an IV only if needed for hydration and remove promptly once PO intake is adequate; minimize invasive devices.
discharge_criteria: Objective readiness: SpO2 >=90% sustained on room air (including during sleep) for the unit-defined observation window (commonly 4-12 h), OR stable on an established home-oxygen program where used; respiratory rate age-appropriate with mild/improving work of breathing; adequate oral hydration maintained without IV/NG (typically >=50-75% of normal intake) with adequate urine output; off HFNC/high-level support; past the expected illness nadir (usually day 4-5) with a clearly improving trajectory; caregiver competent in nasal suctioning and able to recognize return-precaution red flags; reliable follow-up secured.
discharge_planning: Disposition home with caregiver; select programs discharge on low-flow home O2 to shorten LOS (regional practice). Medication reconciliation: typically NO new respiratory medications - reinforce that bronchodilators/steroids/antibiotics are unnecessary. Education: nasal saline + bulb/suction technique, small frequent feeds, red flags (increased work of breathing, poor feeding, apnea/color change, fever, dehydration), and the expected 1-2 week cough course. Follow-up: PCP visit within 24-48 h (earlier for young/high-risk infants). Prevention/transitions: ensure season-appropriate RSV immunoprophylaxis - nirsevimab for eligible infants <8 mo (50 mg if <5 kg, 100 mg if >=5 kg) when not already covered by maternal RSVpreF vaccine; counsel tobacco-smoke avoidance, hand hygiene, and breastfeeding; provide a written pathway summary.
expected_los: Median observed LOS for hospitalized bronchiolitis is ~2 days (typical range 2-3 d). Benchmark GMLOS: MS-DRG 203 (bronchitis & asthma without CC/MCC) ~2.2-2.3 d and MS-DRG 202 (with CC/MCC) ~2.9-3.0 d; APR-DRG 138 (bronchiolitis & RSV pneumonia) severity-of-illness 1-2 ~2 d, rising with severity. PICU/HFNC-failure or apnea cohorts run ~5-7 d.
target_los: Optimized target ~1.5-2.0 days for uncomplicated bronchiolitis under a minimal-intervention pathway (spot-check pulse oximetry, no bronchodilator/steroid/CXR, protocolized O2 weaning to SpO2 >=90%, NG/PO feeding). Early low-flow home-oxygen discharge programs can push median toward ~1-1.5 d. PICU cases individualized.
quality_metrics: De-implementation/overuse measures (AAP Value in Inpatient Pediatrics 'Reducing Overuse in Bronchiolitis'; Choosing Wisely): bronchodilator use rate, systemic steroid use rate, chest radiograph rate, viral testing rate, and continuous pulse-oximetry rate - all lower-is-better. Utilization/outcome: mean LOS, ICU transfer rate, and 7-day and 30-day readmission/ED-revisit rate. Safety: appropriate isotonic-fluid use / hyponatremia rate. Prevention: RSV immunoprophylaxis capture at discharge (nirsevimab/maternal vaccine). Benchmark via PHIS and Solutions for Patient Safety; note there is no CMS SEP-1-equivalent core measure for bronchiolitis.
common_complications: Apnea (esp. age <2 mo / ex-premature) - mitigated with age-based continuous monitoring and a low PICU threshold; progression to respiratory failure needing HFNC/CPAP/intubation - mitigated with early HFNC (PARIS) and stepwise escalation; dehydration - NG/IV isotonic hydration; hyponatremia/SIADH - avoid hypotonic fluids and monitor Na; hospital-acquired secondary bacterial infection/AOM; atelectasis/mucus plugging - airway clearance; aspiration - hold/adjust feeds at high RR; pneumothorax from HFNC/CPAP (<1%, PARIS); healthcare-associated viral transmission - contact/droplet precautions + cohorting; and post-bronchiolitis recurrent wheeze after discharge.
readmission_drivers: Discharge before the illness nadir (RSV worsens through day 3-5) - keep the infant past nadir with an improving trajectory; young age (<3 mo) and prematurity; comorbidity (CHD, chronic lung disease, neuromuscular disease, immunodeficiency); inadequate home feeding/suctioning or caregiver difficulty - require teach-back competency before discharge; home-oxygen misadventures; lack of timely follow-up - book a PCP visit at 24-48 h; and social determinants (transport, housing, tobacco exposure) - engage social work. Ensuring RSV immunoprophylaxis reduces future-season severe disease.
guideline_source: American Academy of Pediatrics (AAP) Clinical Practice Guideline - 'The Diagnosis, Management, and Prevention of Bronchiolitis,' Pediatrics 2014;134(5):e1474-502 (Ralston et al.) - the current authoritative CPG (reaffirmed). Prevention: AAP 2025 policy 'Recommendations for the Prevention of RSV Disease in Infants and Children' plus CDC/ACIP 2023 (MMWR) nirsevimab recommendation. Reinforced by AAP Choosing Wisely and the Value in Inpatient Pediatrics de-implementation work.
key_citations: Per PubMed: AAP CPG bronchiolitis, Pediatrics 2014;134(5):e1474-502, PMID 25349312, DOI 10.1542/peds.2014-2742. PARIS high-flow oxygen RCT, Franklin et al, NEJM 2018;378:1121-1131, PMID 29562151, DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1714855. Hypertonic saline Cochrane review, Zhang et al 2023, PMID 37014057, DOI 10.1002/14651858.CD006458.pub5. Intermittent vs continuous pulse oximetry RCT, McCulloh et al, JAMA Pediatr 2015;169(10):898-904, PMID 26322819, DOI 10.1001/jamapediatrics.2015.1746. Nirsevimab MELODY RCT, Hammitt et al, NEJM 2022;386:837-846, PMID 35235726, DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2110275. Maternal RSVpreF MATISSE RCT, Kampmann et al, NEJM 2023;388:1451-1464, PMID 37018474, DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2216480.
evidence_grade: Overall STRONG for the core do-less strategy - AAP 2014 is a Class I society CPG with strong recommendations (supportive care only; against bronchodilators/steroids/antibiotics/routine imaging), though several underlying recommendations rest on moderate (grade B) evidence. Respiratory-support specifics (early HFNC) are MODERATE (single large RCT, PARIS). Hypertonic saline is LOW certainty (Cochrane). RSV prevention is STRONG (phase-3 RCTs MELODY and MATISSE with ACIP/AAP recommendations). Operational LOS/discharge-window and home-O2 thresholds are largely CONSENSUS/local-pathway (expert opinion).
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The collapsed group spans APR-DRG 138 (bronchiolitis & RSV pneumonia) with severity-of-illness subclasses 1-4 and MS-DRG 202/203 (bronchitis & asthma with/without CC/MCC), with some cases under pertussis/acute-bronchitis DRGs. Low severity (SOI-1/2, no CC/MCC): brief O2/feeding support, floor care, LOS ~2 d. CC/MCC or SOI-3 drivers - apnea, acute respiratory failure, need for HFNC/CPAP, dehydration with hyponatremia, secondary bacterial pneumonia, and prematurity/CHD/CLD comorbidity - shift care to PICU with LOS ~4-7 d. SOI-4/MCC (intubation/mechanical ventilation, ARDS, pneumothorax): PICU with markedly longer LOS and cost. Accurate capture of hypoxemia, apnea, acute respiratory failure, and comorbidities materially changes the DRG/SOI weight.
pathway_pearls: 1) Do LESS: bronchiolitis is clinical - withhold bronchodilators, steroids, antibiotics, and routine CXR/viral testing; de-implementation is the single biggest quality lever. 2) Spot-check, don't tether: intermittent pulse oximetry in stable non-hypoxic infants plus an SpO2 >=90% threshold prevents oximetry-driven LOS creep (McCulloh 2015; AAP). 3) Respect the day 3-5 nadir: don't discharge prematurely on the upswing and don't over-escalate too early - trajectory matters; early HFNC helps hypoxemic infants (PARIS) but will not shorten LOS. 4) Close the prevention loop: use the admission to ensure nirsevimab or maternal-vaccine coverage for the season.

scope_and_volume_notes: Pediatric bronchiolitis/RSV and viral wheezing; highly seasonal and volatile (2022 RSV surge). Primarily infants/toddlers; some coded under whooping cough/acute bronchitis DRGs.

admission_criteria: Inpatient admission (DRG 777) is driven by hemodynamic/surgical need, NOT the diagnosis itself (most stable ectopics are outpatient methotrexate or same-day/23-h laparoscopy). Admit for: ruptured ectopic or hemodynamic instability (SBP <90, HR >110, peritonism, moderate-large hemoperitoneum/free fluid on TVUS or FAST) requiring emergent OR; operative management (laparoscopic salpingectomy/salpingostomy or laparotomy) with post-op recovery need; failed or contraindicated methotrexate; symptomatic anemia or Hgb <7 g/dL requiring transfusion; heterotopic pregnancy; non-tubal ectopic (interstitial/cornual, cervical, cesarean-scar, ovarian, abdominal) needing intervention; intractable pain/vomiting. ED discharge with outpatient management for: stable, unruptured tubal ectopic meeting methotrexate criteria with reliable follow-up, or declining-hCG expectant candidates
risk_stratification: Diagnosis- and decision-threshold driven (no sepsis-style score): hCG DISCRIMINATORY ZONE — single serum hCG >=3,500 mIU/mL (conservative ACOG value; historically 1,500-2,000) with NO intrauterine pregnancy on TVUS => presumptive ectopic/nonviable => act; below zone => serial hCG. SERIAL hCG TREND — viable IUP rises >=35% (historically >=53%) over 48h; sub-35% rise, plateau, or <15% fall => nonviable/ectopic => intervene. METHOTREXATE SUCCESS PREDICTOR — pretreatment hCG <5,000 => ~92-95% single-dose success; hCG >5,000, fetal cardiac activity, or mass >3.5-4 cm => relative contraindication, use 2-dose or surgery. MTX RESPONSE RULE — <15% hCG decline day 4->7 => repeat dose or operate. SHOCK INDEX (HR/SBP) >0.9-1.0 with hemoperitoneum => rupture/instability => emergent surgery + transfusion
initial_workup_labs: Quantitative serum beta-hCG (baseline + serial q48h) — anchors diagnosis and treatment selection; CBC (Hgb/Hct => transfusion if <7 g/dL or symptomatic; WBC); blood type & antibody screen with Rh(D) => anti-D if Rh-negative; type & crossmatch (2 units) if surgical or unstable; CMP for creatinine and LFTs => hepatic/renal dysfunction contraindicates methotrexate; PT/INR/aPTT if bleeding or pre-op; urine/serum hCG to confirm pregnancy; optional serum progesterone (<5 ng/mL supports nonviable, >20 supports viable IUP). Pre-methotrexate baseline mandatory: CBC, LFTs, creatinine, type & Rh. Triggers: cytopenia/transaminitis/renal impairment => MTX contraindicated; falling Hgb => cross-match and prep OR
initial_imaging_dx: Transvaginal ultrasound (TVUS) is first-line — obtain emergently at presentation: assess for IUP (yolk sac/fetal pole), adnexal 'tubal ring'/'blob sign' mass, and cul-de-sac free fluid/hemoperitoneum. Transabdominal US to gauge large hemoperitoneum. Bedside FAST/POCUS in the unstable patient (<10 min) — free fluid => OR without delay. No IUP with hCG above discriminatory zone = presumptive ectopic. MRI reserved for characterizing cesarean-scar/interstitial/cervical ectopic in the stable patient. Diagnostic laparoscopy is both diagnostic and therapeutic when imaging is equivocal but suspicion is high. Never delay definitive surgery for advanced imaging in an unstable patient
time_critical_interventions: Ruptured/unstable ectopic = surgical emergency: activate OR with door-to-incision as fast as feasible (target <1 h for hemorrhagic instability); two large-bore IVs, balanced crystalloid, type-specific or O-negative blood; activate massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 PRBC:FFP:platelets) for class III-IV hemorrhage; definitive surgical hemorrhage control is the priority; consider tranexamic acid. Anti-D immune globulin within 72 h for Rh-negative unsensitized patients. Medical pathway: give methotrexate promptly once a viable IUP is EXCLUDED and no contraindication exists — never before. hCG surveillance timed to day 4 and day 7 post-MTX (expect >=15% fall)
initial_management: SOURCE CONTROL (surgical): laparoscopic salpingectomy preferred when tube is damaged, bleeding is uncontrolled, childbearing complete, or contralateral tube healthy (ESEP: equal fertility, far less persistent trophoblast); salpingostomy (tube-sparing) only if contralateral tube absent/damaged and fertility desired, with mandatory post-op hCG surveillance; laparotomy if unstable or extensive hemoperitoneum. MEDICAL: methotrexate (folate-antagonist antimetabolite) 50 mg/m2 IM — single-dose (hCG days 1/4/7) or 2-dose (50 mg/m2 days 0 & 4); multi/variable-dose = MTX 1 mg/kg IM days 1/3/5/7 with leucovorin 0.1 mg/kg days 2/4/6/8. RESUSCITATION: balanced crystalloid; transfuse PRBC for hemorrhagic shock or Hgb <7 g/dL; MTP for massive hemorrhage. Anti-D immune globulin 50-300 mcg IM if Rh-negative. Analgesia acetaminophen/opioids; AVOID NSAIDs, folate, and alcohol with methotrexate
day1_milestones: POD0 (surgical): laparoscopic salpingectomy/salpingostomy completed with confirmed hemostasis; hemodynamic stabilization; post-op Hgb checked; PONV controlled; same-day ambulation; diet advanced as tolerated (no ileus expected after laparoscopy); transition to oral analgesia; mechanical VTE prophylaxis; anti-D given if Rh-negative; target same-day or 23-h discharge for uncomplicated cases. Day 1 (medical, usually outpatient): methotrexate administered after confirming labs and excluding contraindication/viable IUP; baseline hCG documented; rupture red-flag education; reliable outpatient hCG-surveillance plan secured
day2_milestones: POD1 (if admitted): hemodynamically stable, pain controlled on oral analgesia, tolerating regular diet, ambulating and voiding, Foley removed, hemoglobin stable with no ongoing bleeding; confirm and document surveillance plan (salpingostomy => weekly hCG to <5 mIU/mL; salpingectomy generally needs no hCG follow-up unless concern for retained trophoblast); reinforce contraception/pregnancy-loss counseling; most uncomplicated laparoscopic patients discharged by POD1. Medical/expectant patients: outpatient day-4 hCG scheduled (no inpatient day unless observation)
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ reserved for complicated cases (large hemoperitoneum, transfusion, laparotomy, or ICU stay): milestones = hemodynamic stability and resolution of anemia without further transfusion, return of bowel function, afebrile, clean wound checks, independent ambulation, adequate oral intake/analgesia => discharge. Persistent-trophoblast surveillance after salpingostomy (weekly hCG; prophylactic single-dose MTX if plateau or rise). For medical/expectant patients, confirm a documented hCG downtrend before full transition to outpatient early-pregnancy assessment
consults_multidisciplinary: OB/GYN / gynecologic surgery (primary owner); Emergency Medicine (ED entry, resuscitation); Anesthesia / Perioperative (emergent OR — secondary owner); Reproductive Endocrinology or Maternal-Fetal Medicine (non-tubal/heterotopic/interstitial ectopic, fertility counseling); Interventional Radiology (uterine-artery embolization for cervical or cesarean-scar ectopic); Blood Bank/Transfusion Medicine (crossmatch, MTP); Pharmacy (methotrexate dosing, renal/hepatic and drug-interaction check, teratogen counseling); Social Work / behavioral health (pregnancy-loss support, contraception, follow-up-reliability assessment); Care Management (hCG-surveillance and follow-up coordination). PT/OT rarely needed in this young, ambulatory population
monitoring_level: Floor (with intermittent vitals) for stable post-laparoscopy or medical-management patients — telemetry not routinely required. Continuous monitoring / step-down or ICU for: hemodynamic instability, massive transfusion, ongoing hemorrhage, or post-laparotomy with major blood loss. Escalation triggers: recurrent hypotension (SBP <90), persistent tachycardia, rising shock index, hemoglobin falling despite transfusion, or signs of ongoing intraperitoneal bleeding => return to OR and ICU. Stable unruptured methotrexate candidates need no telemetry and are typically outpatient
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: NPO pre-op then advance to regular as tolerated the same day after laparoscopy (ileus not expected). Mobility: early ambulation POD0. VTE: mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs) perioperatively; pharmacologic prophylaxis generally unnecessary for a short laparoscopic stay in ambulatory patients but consider for laparotomy, prolonged stay, or added risk factors (pregnancy is prothrombotic). Routine glucose control. Lines/catheters: remove Foley POD0-1, convert IV to saline lock once tolerating PO, minimize catheter-days. Methotrexate patients: avoid folic acid/prenatal-vitamin folate, NSAIDs, alcohol, and sun exposure until hCG resolves
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable (normotensive, normal HR) for several hours; hemoglobin stable with no ongoing bleeding or further transfusion need; pain controlled on oral analgesia; tolerating oral intake; ambulating and voiding; anti-D administered if Rh-negative; patient verbalizes rupture warning signs (worsening/one-sided pain, dizziness, syncope, shoulder-tip pain) and return precautions; outpatient serial hCG surveillance arranged when indicated (salpingostomy, medical, or expectant); reliable follow-up and transportation confirmed
discharge_planning: Disposition home. Medication reconciliation (analgesia; reinforce no folate/NSAIDs/alcohol while on methotrexate). Follow-up: OB/GYN in ~1 week; serial serum hCG until <5 mIU/mL (weekly for medical/salpingostomy/expectant; salpingectomy usually none); pre-defined repeat-MTX plan if day 4->7 fall <15%. Education: rupture red-flags, MTX side effects (mucositis, transient day 2-3 abdominal pain, transaminitis), delay conception until hCG resolves and ~3 months post-MTX, contraception counseling, ~10-15% recurrence risk with instruction to seek early US in next pregnancy. Emotional support for pregnancy loss. Safety-net: choose surgery over MTX when follow-up is unreliable; early post-discharge phone contact; warm handoff to early-pregnancy assessment unit
expected_los: Approx 1.7-2.0 days GMLOS for MS-DRG 777 (all-payer ALOS ~1.5-2.3 days); uncomplicated laparoscopic salpingectomy is frequently same-day or 23-h observation, while ruptured/transfused or laparotomy cases run 2-3+ days (exact CMS Table 5 GMLOS not directly verified here — value is the established approximate range)
target_los: ~1 day (<=23-h observation or same-day discharge) for stable laparoscopic salpingectomy on a minimally-invasive/ERAS-style pathway; 0 inpatient days for outpatient methotrexate or expectant candidates; 2-3 days for ruptured/hemorrhagic cases needing transfusion or laparotomy
quality_metrics: No ectopic-specific CMS core measure (no SEP-1 analog). Track: minimally-invasive (laparoscopy) rate vs laparotomy; methotrexate success / rescue-surgery rate (~30% MTX failure benchmark, GEM3); persistent-trophoblast rate after salpingostomy (target <7-8%, ESEP); Rh(D) anti-D administration rate in Rh-negative patients; never-give-MTX-to-viable-IUP safeguard adherence; hemorrhage/transfusion rate and severe maternal morbidity (SMM) hemorrhage indicators; unplanned return to OR; 30-day readmission/reintervention; documented hCG surveillance to resolution; same-day/short-stay rate; ectopic-related maternal mortality (a leading cause of first-trimester maternal death)
common_complications: Hemorrhagic shock from tubal rupture (mitigate: rapid TVUS/hCG diagnosis, emergent surgery, MTP, transfusion); persistent trophoblast after salpingostomy (7% vs <1% for salpingectomy — mitigate: prefer salpingectomy when contralateral tube healthy, weekly hCG, prophylactic single-dose MTX); methotrexate failure needing rescue surgery (~30% — mitigate: select hCG <5,000, no cardiac activity, mass <3.5-4 cm, use 2-dose for higher hCG); MTX toxicity (mucositis, transient transaminitis, cytopenia, rare pneumonitis — mitigate: baseline labs, avoid interacting drugs); surgical/anesthetic complications (bleeding, infection, visceral injury, VTE, conversion to laparotomy); recurrent ectopic
readmission_drivers: Persistent/rising hCG (retained trophoblast) after salpingostomy => reintervention; methotrexate failure or interval tubal rupture after discharge (inadequate hCG decline, lost to follow-up) => emergency surgery; post-op hemorrhage or wound/pelvic infection; inadequate pain control. Prevention levers: strict hCG surveillance to <5 mIU/mL; explicit rupture red-flag education with 24/7 return access; choose surgery over MTX when follow-up cannot be assured; early post-discharge OB phone contact; ensure anti-D and contraception counseling. Recurrent-ectopic risk (~10-15%) drives future presentations — counsel early ultrasound in the next pregnancy
guideline_source: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 193, Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy (2018; reaffirmed 2025); PRIMARY: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 193, 'Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy' (American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists, 2018; reaffirmed, correction 2019). Supporting: ACEP Clinical Policy — Critical Issues in the Initial Evaluation and Management of Patients Presenting to the Emergency Department in Early Pregnancy (2017); RCOG Green-top Guideline No. 21 / NICE NG126 (UK, 2016/2019) for concordant diagnosis and management
key_citations: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 193: Tubal Ectopic Pregnancy. Obstet Gynecol. 2018;131:e91-e103; reaffirmed 2025; ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 193, Obstet Gynecol 2018;131(3):e91-e103 (PMID 29470343). Hajenius PJ, systemic methotrexate vs laparoscopic salpingostomy, Lancet 1997;350:774-9 (PMID 9297998). Hajenius PJ, Interventions for tubal ectopic pregnancy, Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2007;(1):CD000324 (PMID 17253448). Barnhart KT, '2-dose' methotrexate regimen, Fertil Steril 2007;87:250-6 (PMID 17097649). Mol F et al., salpingotomy vs salpingectomy (ESEP), Lancet 2014;383:1483-9 (PMID 24499812). Horne AW et al., gefitinib+methotrexate (GEM3; ~30% need rescue surgery), Lancet 2023;401:655-63 (PMID 36738759). Yang C et al., dose meta-analysis, Reprod Biomed Online 2017;34:383-91 (PMID 28131495). (Citations from PubMed.)
evidence_grade: Strong — ACOG Level A recommendations (good, consistent scientific evidence) for methotrexate medical therapy and for surgical management, backed by multiple RCTs and a Cochrane review; emergent surgery for the ruptured/unstable patient is unquestioned standard of care. Selected elements rest on weaker evidence: the hCG discriminatory-zone threshold, expectant management, and anti-D immune globulin in early pregnancy are Level B/C or consensus/expert opinion
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MS-DRG 777 (Ectopic Pregnancy) and 778 (Threatened Abortion) are SINGLE, non-severity-split DRGs — not partitioned into with-MCC/with-CC/without tiers — so documented CC/MCC does NOT change DRG assignment or base weight (unlike most medical DRGs). Clinical severity instead manifests operationally: ruptured ectopic with hemoperitoneum/hemorrhagic shock drives transfusion, possible ICU, laparotomy, LOS 2-3+ days, and cost outliers, whereas stable unruptured surgical or medical cases are same-day/23-h. Non-tubal ectopics (interstitial/cornual, cervical, cesarean-scar, abdominal — all still coded O00.x => DRG 777) carry much higher hemorrhage risk and may need IR embolization or hysterectomy. Because payment is not severity-adjusted within the DRG, high-acuity hemorrhagic cases are clinical/financial outliers; capture SMM and transfusion documentation for quality (not DRG) purposes. DRG 777 captures both medical and surgical ectopic management (assigned by principal diagnosis O00.-)
pathway_pearls: 1) NEVER give methotrexate without excluding a viable intrauterine pregnancy — combine the conservative hCG discriminatory zone (~3,500 mIU/mL), TVUS, and serial hCG; a single hCG never diagnoses an ectopic. 2) Unstable/ruptured = OR now — do not delay for advanced imaging beyond bedside FAST; activate transfusion/MTP and give anti-D if Rh-negative. 3) Match treatment to follow-up reliability: MTX only if hCG <5,000, no fetal cardiac activity, mass <3.5-4 cm, and dependable surveillance to hCG <5 mIU/mL — otherwise operate. 4) Prefer salpingectomy when the contralateral tube is healthy (ESEP: equal fertility, far less persistent trophoblast); if salpingostomy is chosen, mandate weekly hCG surveillance

admission_criteria: Inpatient for: acute exacerbation of ILD/IPF (AE-IPF/AE-ILD per Collard 2016 = acute worsening/onset of dyspnea typically <1 month WITH new bilateral ground-glass/consolidation on HRCT superimposed on fibrosis, NOT fully explained by cardiac failure/fluid overload); new or increased hypoxemia (resting SpO2 <88-90% on room air, or >=1-2 L/min rise above home O2, or exertional desaturation with distress); acute respiratory failure (low PaO2/FiO2, need for HFNC/NIV/IMV); suspected pneumonia/infection precipitating decompensation; pneumothorax/pneumomediastinum; new cor pulmonale/RV failure or PH crisis. Observation/ED discharge only if mild symptom flare, stable gas exchange on home O2, no new infiltrate, and reliable ILD-clinic follow-up <=1 week. Newly discovered ILD with hypoxemia warrants admission for expedited workup
risk_stratification: GAP index (Gender-Age-Physiology; Ley 2012) stage I/II/III -> 1-yr mortality 6%/16%/39% (uses sex, age, FVC%, DLCO%) - stage III drives palliative/transplant urgency; ILD-GAP extends to non-IPF ILD; AE-IPF itself carries ~50% in-hospital mortality and 3-4 month median survival -> triggers early goals-of-care; oxygenation/ventilation need (HFNC/NIV/IMV) and PaO2/FiO2 predict death; CURB-65/PSI if pneumonia is the trigger; echocardiographic PH/RV dysfunction worsens prognosis; DLCO <35-40% and FVC decline >=10% mark advanced disease; Wells/PESI if PE considered
initial_workup_labs: CBC w/diff, CMP; BNP/NT-proBNP + high-sensitivity troponin (exclude cardiogenic edema/ischemia mimicking AE); ABG (A-a gradient, PaO2/FiO2); procalcitonin + blood/sputum cultures + respiratory viral PCR panel (SARS-CoV-2, influenza, RSV) to exclude infectious trigger; D-dimer to risk-stratify PE; LDH (nonspecific AE marker); lactate if shock; if new/undiagnosed ILD or CTD suspected: ANA, RF, anti-CCP, myositis/antisynthetase panel (anti-Jo-1, anti-MDA5, anti-PL-7/12), ANCA, anti-SSA/SSB, CK, aldolase, ESR/CRP; baseline LFTs before/on antifibrotic; glucose for steroid coverage; KL-6 where available
initial_imaging_dx: HRCT chest is the key diagnostic (obtain within 24h) - new bilateral ground-glass/consolidation superimposed on UIP/fibrotic background defines AE; CT pulmonary angiography if PE suspected; TTE/echocardiogram within 24-48h for RV/PH and to exclude LV dysfunction/diastolic HF; ECG; portable CXR at presentation; BAL only if infection must be excluded AND patient tolerates (defer in severe hypoxemia); AVOID surgical/transbronchial lung biopsy in the acute setting (transbronchial cryobiopsy reserved for stable elective diagnosis)
time_critical_interventions: Titrate supplemental O2 to SpO2 88-92% immediately; escalate early to HFNC (preferred) or NIV for refractory hypoxemia; if infection cannot be excluded, empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics within the first hours per ATS/IDSA CAP pathway (+ antiviral e.g. oseltamivir in influenza season); initiate high-dose corticosteroids promptly once AE diagnosed and mimics addressed. NOTE: there is NO validated time-defined bundle for AE-IPF (unlike sepsis/STEMI); the genuinely time-critical step is early goals-of-care discussion given ~50% in-hospital mortality, because intubation is usually non-beneficial outside transplant candidates
initial_management: Corticosteroids are the mainstay (LOW-quality/expert-opinion evidence): pulse methylprednisolone 500-1000 mg IV daily x3d then prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg/d taper, or prednisone ~1 mg/kg for non-severe; supplemental O2/HFNC/NIV; empiric antibiotics (e.g. ceftriaxone + azithromycin; add antipseudomonal/MRSA cover if HCAP risk) until infection excluded; CONTINUE home antifibrotic (nintedanib 150 mg BID or pirfenidone 801 mg TID) - antifibrotic use associated with LOWER AE mortality (EXAFIP); do NOT start antifibrotic to treat the AE itself. AVOID adding IV cyclophosphamide (EXAFIP: 3-mo mortality 45% vs 31%) and AVOID prednisone+azathioprine+NAC triple therapy (PANTHER: increased death/hospitalization); diurese only if true volume overload; VTE prophylaxis; standard restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL (no ILD-specific threshold)
day1_milestones: Confirm AE via HRCT and exclude HF (BNP/echo), PE (CTPA), infection (cultures/PCR/procalcitonin); start high-dose corticosteroids; titrate O2/initiate HFNC; empiric antibiotics if infection possible; collate baseline PFTs (FVC, DLCO) and prior imaging; calculate GAP stage; pulmonology/ILD consult; establish code status and begin goals-of-care conversation; start VTE prophylaxis and steroid-glucose monitoring
day2_milestones: Reassess oxygenation and steroid response; review culture/PCR/procalcitonin and de-escalate or stop antibiotics; continue home antifibrotic (hold only for ileus/hepatotoxicity); early palliative care consult for dyspnea management and prognosis; screen lung-transplant candidacy (age, comorbidity, BMI); begin PT/early mobility; dietitian if intake poor; confirm no barotrauma/pneumothorax
day3plus_milestones: Define trajectory - improving: convert IV to oral steroid with written taper, arrange home O2 (resting/exertional/nocturnal titration with ambulatory oximetry), pulmonary rehab referral, add PJP prophylaxis if prolonged steroids; not improving: intensify palliative/comfort pathway OR expedite lung-transplant evaluation in candidates; refractory respiratory failure: align care with goals (avoid non-beneficial IMV); reconcile antifibrotic with LFT-monitoring plan; verify discharge criteria met
consults_multidisciplinary: Pulmonology/ILD specialist (day 0-1, essential); palliative care (early, day 1-2, given prognosis); lung-transplant center referral for candidates (early); respiratory therapy (O2/HFNC/NIV); PT/OT and pulmonary rehab; care management/social work for home O2 and disposition; clinical pharmacy (antifibrotic counseling/interactions, steroid taper, PJP prophylaxis); dietitian; rheumatology if CTD-ILD; infectious disease for complex infection; cardiology if significant PH/RV failure
monitoring_level: ICU/step-down for acute respiratory failure needing HFNC/NIV/IMV, PaO2/FiO2 deterioration, hemodynamic instability, or RV failure; telemetry/floor for stable hypoxemia on low-flow nasal cannula; escalation triggers: rising FiO2/O2 requirement, RR >30, SpO2 <88% despite HFNC, hemodynamic instability, altered mentation -> prompt ICU transfer WITH goals-of-care re-check (intubation often non-beneficial except as transplant bridge)
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet as tolerated (aspiration precautions if severe dyspnea/high-flow), dietitian for cachexia/poor intake; early mobilization + inpatient PT transitioning to outpatient pulmonary rehab; pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily or heparin 5000 U q8h) - IPF is prothrombotic; steroid-induced hyperglycemia -> scheduled glucose checks + correctional/basal insulin; add PJP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) when prednisone >=20 mg/d expected >=4 weeks; GI prophylaxis (PPI) during high-dose steroids; remove IV lines/urinary catheters early
discharge_criteria: Stable or improved oxygenation on a home-deliverable O2 regimen with resting + ambulatory SpO2 documented >=88%; afebrile with infection treated/excluded; written steroid taper plan; tolerating oral intake and antifibrotic; ambulating at/near baseline with rehab plan; goals of care and (where relevant) transplant or palliative pathway defined; no unresolved barotrauma or hemodynamic instability
discharge_planning: Home O2 set up with titration (rest/exertion/nocturnal); prednisone taper schedule + PJP and GI prophylaxis; antifibrotic continuation with scheduled LFT monitoring and GI-tolerability counseling; ILD-clinic follow-up within 1-2 weeks with repeat PFTs; pulmonary rehab enrollment; transplant-evaluation or palliative/hospice linkage as indicated; advance care planning documented; vaccinations (influenza, pneumococcal, COVID-19, RSV); smoking cessation; full medication reconciliation; teach-back on a worsening-symptom action plan
expected_los: MS-DRG GMLOS benchmarks (CMS FY2025; DRG 196 relative weight ~1.88): 196 ILD w/MCC ~5.0d (AMLOS ~6.5-7d); 197 w/CC ~3.8-4.0d; 198 w/o CC/MCC ~3.0d; blended ~4-5 days. The AE-IPF subset (usually MCC / acute respiratory failure) commonly runs 7-10 days with high mortality
target_los: ~3-4 days for stable diagnostic/oxygen-titration admissions (198/197 tiers); ~5-7 days realistic for AE-IPF (196/MCC) given acuity - with early goals-of-care and prompt home-O2 setup preventing prolonged non-beneficial stays
quality_metrics: No AE-IPF-specific CMS core measure exists; track: 30-day all-cause readmission and in-hospital/30-day mortality; documented advance care planning/goals-of-care; palliative care engagement in advanced disease; home-O2 assessment with ambulatory oximetry before discharge; antifibrotic use/continuation in eligible patients; avoidance of harmful therapy (triple immunosuppression, cyclophosphamide); vaccination (influenza/pneumococcal/COVID); tobacco cessation counseling; pulmonary rehab referral; timely ILD-clinic follow-up (<=14d); transplant-referral rate in eligible patients
common_complications: Progressive respiratory failure/death (AE-IPF in-hospital mortality ~50%) - mitigate with early goals-of-care, HFNC, avoid non-beneficial IMV; nosocomial/secondary infection - steroid-sparing, antibiotic de-escalation, PJP prophylaxis; VTE/PE (prothrombotic IPF) - prophylaxis; pneumothorax/pneumomediastinum from fibrotic lung/barotrauma - lung-protective settings, low HFNC/NIV pressures; PH/RV failure/cor pulmonale - echo, avoid hypoxemia; steroid toxicity (hyperglycemia, delirium, myopathy) - glucose control and taper; ventilator dependence
readmission_drivers: Recurrent AE; under-treated hypoxemia/inadequate home-O2 titration; secondary infection; antifibrotic intolerance/nonadherence (GI effects, LFT abnormalities); underlying disease progression; missed transplant or palliative pathway; comorbid PH/HF decompensation; steroid complications. Prevention levers: ILD-clinic follow-up <=2 weeks, optimized home O2 with ambulatory oximetry, antifibrotic tolerability management, palliative co-management, pulmonary rehab, vaccination, clear symptom-action plan and medication reconciliation
guideline_source: ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT 2022 Clinical Practice Guideline - Idiopathic Pulmonary Fibrosis (an Update) and Progressive Pulmonary Fibrosis in Adults (Raghu et al., 2022); ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT 2018 IPF Diagnosis Guideline; Collard et al. 2016 International Working Group revised AE-IPF definition. NOTE: no high-quality society guideline governs inpatient AE-IPF treatment - acute management is largely expert consensus
key_citations: Raghu G et al. ATS/ERS/JRS/ALAT IPF+PPF CPG. Am J Respir Crit Care Med 2022;205(9):e18-e47 (PMID 35486072; DOI 10.1164/rccm.202202-0399ST); Collard HR et al. AE-IPF International Working Group Report. AJRCCM 2016;194(3):265-75 (PMID 27299520; DOI 10.1164/rccm.201604-0801CI); King TE et al. ASCEND pirfenidone. NEJM 2014;370:2083-92 (PMID 24836312; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1402582); Richeldi L et al. INPULSIS nintedanib. NEJM 2014;370:2071-82 (PMID 24836310; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1402584); Flaherty KR et al. INBUILD nintedanib in PF-ILD. NEJM 2019;381:1718-27 (PMID 31566307; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1908681); Naccache JM et al. EXAFIP cyclophosphamide (harm). Lancet Respir Med 2022;10:26-34 (PMID 34506761; DOI 10.1016/S2213-2600(21)00354-4); Raghu G et al. PANTHER-IPF triple therapy (harm). NEJM 2012;366:1968-77 (PMID 22607134; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1113354); Ley B et al. GAP index. Ann Intern Med 2012;156:684-91 (PMID 22586007; DOI 10.7326/0003-4819-156-10-201205150-00004). Sourced via PubMed
evidence_grade: Mixed. Chronic antifibrotic therapy = Strong/Moderate (multiple phase-3 RCTs - ASCEND, INPULSIS, INBUILD; conditional-to-strong society recommendations). Acute inpatient AE-IPF management = Weak (consensus/expert opinion; corticosteroids rest on low-quality observational data with no supportive RCT, and RCT evidence actively identifies HARM from cyclophosphamide [EXAFIP] and triple immunosuppression [PANTHER]). Diagnostic/prognostic framework (Collard 2016, GAP) = validated/Moderate. Overall inpatient pathway grade: Moderate-to-Weak - diagnosis robust, acute therapy largely expert-opinion
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 198 (no CC/MCC): milder flares or diagnostic admissions, floor-level, LOS ~3d, low mortality. DRG 197 (CC): moderate hypoxemia +/- infection/comorbidity, floor/telemetry, LOS ~4d. DRG 196 (MCC): the acute-respiratory-failure/AE-IPF tier - HFNC/NIV/ICU, cor pulmonale, LOS ~5-10d, in-hospital mortality up to ~50%, drives palliative/transplant decisions; acute respiratory failure is the pivotal MCC documentation lever. Severity tier escalates ICU need, LOS, and complication burden across the collapsed group
pathway_pearls: (1) AE-IPF is a diagnosis of EXCLUSION - get HRCT early and actively rule out HF (BNP/echo), PE (CTPA), and infection before anchoring. (2) Establish goals of care on day 1 - in-hospital mortality ~50% and mechanical ventilation is usually non-beneficial except as a transplant bridge; involve palliative care early. (3) CONTINUE home antifibrotics (associated with lower AE mortality) but do NOT add cyclophosphamide (EXAFIP harm) or prednisone/azathioprine/NAC (PANTHER harm); high-dose steroids remain expert-opinion only. (4) Decide the fork early - expedited transplant referral for the candidate vs palliative/comfort pathway - and optimize home O2 + pulmonary rehab to prevent readmission

admission_criteria: ADMIT: suspected/confirmed native-joint septic arthritis (hot swollen joint + synovial WBC >50,000/uL and/or positive Gram stain) for urgent drainage + IV antibiotics; any prosthetic-joint infection; polyarticular or systemic sepsis/SIRS. ADMIT organ-threatening autoimmune flare: proliferative/active lupus nephritis (rising creatinine, active urine sediment, nephrotic-range proteinuria), diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, neuropsychiatric SLE (seizure/psychosis/myelitis), severe autoimmune cytopenias (Hgb <8 g/dL hemolytic, platelets <20-30k), myo/pericarditis or tamponade, scleroderma renal crisis, systemic vasculitis with organ involvement, catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome. Also: neutropenic/immunosuppressed fever, inability to ambulate/perform ADLs, uncontrolled pain, need for IV pulse steroids/cyclophosphamide or plasma exchange. ED-discharge/observation instead: isolated mild-moderate arthralgia flare, stable labs, no organ threat, orally controllable pain, reliable outpatient rheumatology follow-up.
risk_stratification: Septic arthritis: synovial WBC >50,000/uL with PMN >90% supports pyogenic infection (likelihood rises with count; >100,000 high-risk), <25,000 lowers but does not exclude (esp. immunosuppressed); Newman/Gachter arthroscopic stage I-IV guides serial-aspiration vs arthroscopic vs open washout. Sepsis overlay: qSOFA >=2, NEWS2 >=5, or lactate >2 mmol/L -> escalate/ICU review and SEP-1 bundle. SLE: SLEDAI-2K >=6 = clinically active, SELENA-SLEDAI Flare Index defines severe flare, BILAG category A = severe single-organ involvement; low C3/C4 + rising anti-dsDNA = serologic activity. Lupus nephritis: ISN/RPS class III/IV(+/-V) on biopsy with UPCR/eGFR set induction intensity. RA flare: DAS28 >5.1 or CDAI >22 = high disease activity.
initial_workup_labs: Septic arthritis: URGENT arthrocentesis BEFORE antibiotics -> synovial WBC + differential, Gram stain, crystals (exclude gout/CPPD), aerobic/anaerobic culture; blood cultures x2 pre-antibiotic; CBC, CRP, ESR, procalcitonin, lactate; gonococcal NAAT (urethral/cervical/pharyngeal) in young sexually active patients; uric acid. Autoimmune flare: CBC with differential, CMP (creatinine), UA with microscopy (dysmorphic RBCs, cellular/RBC casts), spot UPCR, C3/C4 (low = active), anti-dsDNA (rising = active), ESR + CRP (CRP disproportionately high favors infection over lupus flare), LDH/haptoglobin/reticulocyte/direct Coombs if hemolysis, ferritin + triglycerides if MAS/HLH suspected, coagulation studies, antiphospholipid panel if thrombosis. Cultures + CRP + complement + dsDNA together separate infection from flare.
initial_imaging_dx: Septic arthritis: plain radiograph of the affected joint at presentation (baseline; exclude osteomyelitis/contiguous focus); ultrasound-guided aspiration for deep joints (hip/shoulder); MRI if osteomyelitis, spinal, or prosthetic-joint infection suspected; arthrocentesis is the pivotal diagnostic test - perform same-day/immediately, before antibiotics. Autoimmune: CXR (serositis/hemorrhage), TTE if myo/pericarditis, effusion, or tamponade; percutaneous renal biopsy within days for suspected lupus nephritis to establish ISN/RPS class; MRI brain +/- lumbar puncture for neuropsychiatric SLE; CT chest for diffuse alveolar hemorrhage.
time_critical_interventions: Aspirate the joint and draw blood cultures BEFORE empiric antibiotics, then start IV antibiotics within hours (immediately, <=1h, if septic shock - Surviving Sepsis 1-hour bundle: cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, lactate). Urgent joint decompression/drainage (same-day surgical/arthroscopic washout for confirmed large-joint pyogenic arthritis) - delay of days risks irreversible cartilage destruction. Scleroderma renal crisis: start ACE inhibitor (captopril, titrated) STAT. Organ-threatening SLE (diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, severe NPSLE, rapidly progressive nephritis): pulse IV methylprednisolone urgently. Catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome / thrombotic microangiopathy: urgent plasma exchange + anticoagulation.
initial_management: Septic arthritis: after cultures, empiric IV vancomycin (MRSA cover) + 3rd-generation cephalosporin (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime); use antipseudomonal beta-lactam (cefepime or piperacillin-tazobactam) if immunocompromised/IVDU/Gram-negative risk; ceftriaxone if gonococcal; de-escalate to culture-directed therapy; total 2-4 weeks (IV ~1-2 weeks then oral) PLUS joint drainage (serial aspiration vs arthroscopic vs open). Autoimmune flare: glucocorticoids as bridge - IV pulse methylprednisolone 250-1000 mg/day x3 for organ-threatening disease then oral prednisone tapered toward <=5 mg/day; hydroxychloroquine 5 mg/kg/day for all SLE; add immunosuppression - mycophenolate or low-dose IV cyclophosphamide (Euro-Lupus 500 mg q2wk x6) for lupus nephritis/severe disease, with add-on belimumab or a calcineurin inhibitor (voclosporin/tacrolimus) for LN, rituximab for refractory disease, anifrolumab for skin/joint disease. RA flare: bridging glucocorticoid (lowest dose/shortest course) + optimize methotrexate anchor +/- biologic/JAKi. Supportive: analgesia, gastroprotection with steroids, transfuse per restrictive threshold.
day1_milestones: Obtain source (arthrocentesis + blood cultures) before antibiotics; start empiric IV antibiotics and arrange urgent orthopedic drainage for septic arthritis; for flare, initiate glucocorticoid (pulse if organ-threatening) and continue/confirm hydroxychloroquine; same-day rheumatology consult (plus orthopedics/ID or nephrology as indicated); explicitly adjudicate infection vs flare; baseline functional/joint assessment and analgesia; decide VTE prophylaxis; begin glucose monitoring if high-dose steroids started.
day2_milestones: Review Gram stain/preliminary cultures and complement/dsDNA trends - narrow/de-escalate antibiotics; repeat joint aspiration if effusion reaccumulates or reassess post-washout; gauge steroid response (clinical + trending CRP/complement); finalize immunosuppression induction plan; obtain renal biopsy if lupus nephritis; start PT/OT (joint ROM, mobility); monitor and treat steroid effects (hyperglycemia, hypertension); confirm PJP + osteoporosis prophylaxis when on high-dose steroids/cyclophosphamide.
day3plus_milestones: Demonstrate improvement: afebrile, falling CRP/ESR, improving joint exam/organ function, stabilizing renal function; transition IV-to-oral antibiotics once clinically improving and arrange OPAT for any remaining IV course (septic arthritis); finalize outpatient immunosuppression and a written steroid taper; ensure prophylaxis (PJP/TMP-SMX, calcium/vitamin D + bisphosphonate, VTE); reconcile medications and confirm access/prior-authorization for biologics; verify safe disposition and follow-up before discharge.
consults_multidisciplinary: Rheumatology - all autoimmune flares, day 0-1; Orthopedic surgery - septic arthritis, urgent for drainage/washout; Infectious diseases - septic arthritis antibiotic selection/duration and OPAT; Nephrology - lupus nephritis and renal biopsy; PT/OT - joint ROM, mobility, ADLs, early; Pharmacy - immunosuppressant/antibiotic dosing, steroid taper, drug interactions; Care management/social work - OPAT logistics, disposition, medication access/prior-auth; Nutrition - renal/steroid-diabetic diet; Dermatology/Neurology/Pulmonary/Hematology per organ involvement.
monitoring_level: Floor with telemetry for most. ICU/step-down for: septic shock or sepsis with hemodynamic instability, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage or respiratory failure, severe neuropsychiatric SLE, pericardial tamponade, scleroderma renal crisis with malignant hypertension, catastrophic APS/thrombotic microangiopathy, or MAS/HLH. Escalation triggers: qSOFA >=2, NEWS2 >=7, rising lactate, new hypoxia/oxygen requirement, hemodynamic instability, declining urine output/rising creatinine, altered mental status.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet as tolerated; low-sodium/renal diet with active nephritis or steroid-related fluid retention; carbohydrate-controlled for steroid hyperglycemia (target glucose <180 mg/dL). Early mobilization with PT/OT; a septic joint gets brief initial rest then early ROM to preserve function. Pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (LMWH) for immobile/active-SLE/APS patients (hold for severe thrombocytopenia or active hemorrhage). PJP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) with high-dose steroids/cyclophosphamide; calcium/vitamin D + bisphosphonate for steroid osteoporosis. Remove urinary catheters and IV lines as early as feasible to reduce infection risk in immunosuppressed patients.
discharge_criteria: Septic arthritis: afebrile/hemodynamically stable, source controlled (adequately drained), on effective culture-directed antibiotic with OPAT arranged, pain controlled, improving joint function and downtrending inflammatory markers. Autoimmune flare: organ function stabilized/improving, tolerating oral immunosuppression + a defined steroid taper, no untreated active infection, stable/improving labs (rising complement, falling anti-dsDNA, stable creatinine/counts), pain controlled, safe mobility/disposition confirmed.
discharge_planning: Disposition home vs SNF/acute rehab based on function; OPAT enrollment for residual IV antibiotics with ID follow-up and weekly safety labs (CBC, creatinine, drug levels); medication reconciliation including written steroid taper, immunosuppressant/biologic, hydroxychloroquine, PJP prophylaxis, gastroprotection, and bone protection; rheumatology follow-up within 1-2 weeks (nephrology if lupus nephritis, orthopedics for the joint); patient education on infection precautions, steroid sick-day rules, adherence, and hydroxychloroquine annual retinal screening; clear transitions-of-care handoff with teach-back.
expected_los: Approximate CMS GMLOS by tier (benchmarks, approximate): DRG 547 connective-tissue disorder w/o CC/MCC ~2.8 d, 546 w CC ~4.0 d, 545 w MCC ~5.4 d; DRG 550 septic arthritis w/o CC/MCC ~3.6 d, 549 w CC ~4.8 d, 548 w MCC ~6.4 d. Typical 3-6 days across the collapsed group; septic arthritis and MCC tiers run longer.
target_los: Optimized target ~3-4 days for uncomplicated autoimmune flare (early rheumatology, prompt pulse-to-oral steroid transition) and ~4-5 days for native-joint septic arthritis (early drainage + rapid IV-to-oral or OPAT transition); MCC/ICU cases individualized (6-8+ days).
quality_metrics: SEP-1 sepsis bundle compliance when septic arthritis presents with sepsis/septic shock; blood cultures + arthrocentesis before antibiotics; time-to-source-control (joint drainage); time to effective antibiotics; 30-day all-cause readmission; appropriate PJP, osteoporosis, and VTE prophylaxis on chronic/high-dose steroids; steroid-induced hyperglycemia control; hydroxychloroquine retinopathy screening documentation; medication reconciliation at discharge; OPAT safety/complication rate; hospital-onset C. difficile and CLABSI; functional status/patient-reported outcomes. (No autoimmune-specific CMS core measure exists.)
common_complications: Septic arthritis: cartilage/joint destruction, osteomyelitis, bacteremia/sepsis, recurrent effusion requiring repeat drainage. Immunosuppression/steroids: opportunistic and secondary infection (PJP, CMV, reactivation), steroid hyperglycemia, hypertension, psychosis, avascular necrosis, VTE, cyclophosphamide cytopenia/hemorrhagic cystitis; the diagnostic pitfall of treating an occult infection as a flare. Mitigation: culture-guided antibiotics, glucose and BP monitoring, PJP/VTE/bone prophylaxis, careful flare-vs-infection adjudication, and judicious steroid taper.
readmission_drivers: Inadequate source control or recurrent septic arthritis; OPAT-related events (line infection, antibiotic toxicity, subtherapeutic levels); premature or overly rapid steroid taper causing flare recurrence; medication nonadherence or access barriers to expensive biologics; new infection on immunosuppression; inadequate or late follow-up. Prevention levers: structured OPAT program, rheumatology follow-up within 1-2 weeks, prior-authorization/medication-access support, PJP/VTE/bone prophylaxis, and teach-back on adherence and warning signs.
guideline_source: SLE: EULAR 2023 recommendations for management of SLE (Fanouriakis et al, Ann Rheum Dis 2024) + ACR SLE guidance; lupus nephritis KDIGO 2024. RA: ACR 2021 Guideline for the Treatment of Rheumatoid Arthritis (+ EULAR 2022/2023). Septic arthritis: BSR/BHPR-BOA-RCGP-BSAC 2006 hot swollen joint guideline + SANJO 2023 native-joint septic arthritis guideline; IDSA for gonococcal and prosthetic-joint infection. Note: no single US society septic-arthritis guideline exists - that evidence base is thinner and largely consensus.
key_citations: Per PubMed: EULAR SLE 2023 update - Fanouriakis et al, Ann Rheum Dis 2024;83(1):15-29, PMID 37827694 (DOI 10.1136/ard-2023-224762); ACR 2021 RA treatment - Fraenkel et al, Arthritis Rheumatol 2021;73(7):1108-1123, PMID 34101376 (DOI 10.1002/art.41752); Euro-Lupus Nephritis Trial (low- vs high-dose IV cyclophosphamide) - Houssiau et al, Arthritis Rheum 2002;46(8):2121-2131, PMID 12209517 (DOI 10.1002/art.10461); AURORA 1 voclosporin for lupus nephritis - Rovin et al, Lancet 2021;397(10289):2070-2080, PMID 33971155 (DOI 10.1016/S0140-6736(21)00578-X); BSR hot swollen joint guideline - Coakley et al, Rheumatology 2006;45(8):1039-1041; SANJO native-joint septic arthritis guideline, J Bone Jt Infect 2023;8:29-.
evidence_grade: Mixed. Strong (Class I / GRADE-based society guidelines) for autoimmune-flare pharmacotherapy - EULAR 2023 SLE and ACR 2021 RA, supported by RCTs (Euro-Lupus, AURORA/voclosporin, BLISS/belimumab, TULIP/anifrolumab). Weaker for septic arthritis: no high-quality RCTs guide drainage modality or antibiotic duration; recommendations are consensus/expert-opinion (BSR 2006, SANJO 2023). Overall Moderate, with the septic-arthritis limb explicitly extrapolated/consensus-based.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Without CC/MCC (547/550): single hot joint or mild-moderate flare, floor-level, rapid oral/OPAT transition, LOS ~2.8-3.6 d. With CC (546/549): moderate comorbidity - anemia, AKI, diabetes, larger effusions/repeat aspiration, LOS ~4-5 d. With MCC (545/548): sepsis/septic shock, acute renal failure or dialysis-requiring nephritis, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, respiratory failure, severe NPSLE, MAS/HLH, or bacteremia/osteomyelitis with repeat washouts -> ICU/step-down, prolonged IV therapy, LOS 6-8+ d and higher mortality. Common CC/MCC drivers: bacteremia, sepsis, acute renal failure, respiratory failure, thromboembolism.
pathway_pearls: 1) In suspected septic arthritis, aspirate the joint and draw blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics (unless frank sepsis) and get orthopedics for same-day drainage - delayed decompression causes irreversible cartilage loss. 2) Rigorously separate flare from infection: disproportionately high CRP, positive cultures, and normal/high complement favor infection, whereas low C3/C4 with rising anti-dsDNA favors SLE flare - do not reflexively escalate immunosuppression into an occult infection. 3) Give hydroxychloroquine to every SLE patient, minimize maintenance steroids to <=5 mg/day, and start PJP + VTE + bone prophylaxis whenever pulsing high-dose steroids/cyclophosphamide. 4) Early rheumatology plus ID/orthopedics and proactive OPAT + medication-access planning are the biggest LOS and readmission levers; book rheumatology follow-up within 1-2 weeks to prevent taper-related bounce-back.

admission_criteria: New or symptomatic intracranial mass with focal neurologic deficit; seizure (first-time, cluster, or status epilepticus); signs of raised ICP (progressive/positional headache, nausea-vomiting, papilledema, altered mental status or depressed GCS); symptomatic vasogenic edema requiring IV steroids or hyperosmolar therapy; mass effect/midline shift or intratumoral hemorrhage on imaging; obstructive hydrocephalus; or need for expedited tissue diagnosis/staging unsafe as outpatient. ED discharge/observation acceptable for a known stable tumor, a single self-limited seizure back to baseline on therapeutic ASM with reliable follow-up, or a minimally symptomatic incidental lesion. Hard triggers to admit: GCS drop, new focal deficit, herniation signs, intractable symptoms, or hemodynamically/airway-unstable presentation.
risk_stratification: Karnofsky Performance Status (KPS <70 = poor prognosis, tempers aggressive local therapy and favors palliative-leaning plan) or ECOG; RTOG Recursive Partitioning Analysis (RPA class I-III; class III driven by KPS<70); Diagnosis-Specific Graded Prognostic Assessment (DS-GPA 0-4, using age/KPS/extracranial disease/number of mets/molecular subtype) to estimate survival and guide SRS vs WBRT vs supportive care; Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS <=8 -> intubation/ICU); WHO CNS5 2021 integrated grade (IDH mutation, 1p/19q codeletion, MGMT methylation) drives oncologic therapy; VTE risk (Khorana/Caprini) - brain tumor scores high and mandates prophylaxis.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential (baseline lymphopenia flags PJP risk pre-steroid); CMP (sodium for SIADH/cerebral salt wasting, glucose for steroid hyperglycemia, renal function before contrast/mannitol); LFTs, Mg/Phos; coagulation PT/INR/aPTT and platelets (before biopsy and before anticoagulation); type and screen if procedure planned; if germ-cell tumor suspected serum and CSF beta-hCG and AFP; LDH and HIV if primary CNS lymphoma suspected; baseline glucose/HbA1c before high-dose steroids; pregnancy test in women of childbearing potential before imaging/RT.
initial_imaging_dx: MRI brain with and without gadolinium is the gold standard (post-contrast T1, T2/FLAIR, DWI, plus perfusion rCBV and MR spectroscopy when available) - obtain within 24h of admission; non-contrast CT head STAT (<1h) for acute deterioration, suspected herniation or hemorrhage, or when MRI is contraindicated; for suspected metastasis search for primary with contrast CT chest/abdomen/pelvis and/or whole-body FDG-PET/CT plus mammography as indicated; definitive tissue diagnosis via stereotactic/navigated needle biopsy or resection (craniotomy = separate DRG); MRI whole spine plus LP for CSF cytology if leptomeningeal disease suspected (defer LP if mass effect/herniation risk).
time_critical_interventions: Impending herniation/malignant edema: elevate head of bed 30 degrees, hyperosmolar therapy (mannitol 0.25-1 g/kg IV or 3% hypertonic saline bolus), dexamethasone 10 mg IV load, emergent neurosurgery consult, brief hyperventilation only as a temporizing bridge to definitive decompression. Status epilepticus: IV benzodiazepine (lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg, max 4 mg/dose) within 5 min, then non-enzyme-inducing ASM load (levetiracetam 60 mg/kg, or lacosamide/fosphenytoin). Acute obstructive hydrocephalus: emergent CSF diversion (external ventricular drain) by neurosurgery. Symptomatic severe hyponatremia (Na<120 or seizing): 3% saline 100-150 mL bolus, limit correction to <8-10 mEq/L per 24h.
initial_management: Corticosteroid: dexamethasone at minimum effective dose for symptomatic vasogenic edema (4-8 mg/day for mild mass-effect symptoms; 16 mg/day or more, e.g. 10 mg IV load then 4 mg IV/PO q6h, for moderate-severe) per CNS/ASCO/SNO; WITHHOLD steroids if primary CNS lymphoma is suspected and biopsy pending (ghost-tumor effect lowers diagnostic yield). Antiseizure meds ONLY for patients who have seized (levetiracetam 500-1500 mg BID first-line; avoid enzyme-inducing phenytoin/carbamazepine due to chemo/steroid interactions); do NOT give prophylactic ASM to seizure-free patients (SNO/EANO Level A). Hyperosmolar therapy PRN for edema; GI prophylaxis (PPI/H2) with high-dose steroids; PJP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX) if steroids >=4 weeks or lymphopenic; glucose control (insulin, target <180 mg/dL). Source control = definitive neurosurgery/radiation/systemic-therapy planning.
day1_milestones: Confirm diagnosis with MRI and characterize lesion(s); stabilize ICP/edema and start dexamethasone at minimum effective dose; control seizures and load ASM if the patient has seized; dysphagia/aspiration screen and serial neuro checks (q1-4h by acuity); establish tissue-diagnosis plan (biopsy vs resection) and hold steroids if PCNSL suspected; initiate metastatic workup/search for primary; start mechanical VTE prophylaxis; document baseline KPS/ECOG and open goals-of-care discussion; consult neurosurgery and neuro-oncology.
day2_milestones: Assess steroid response (headache, edema symptoms, deficit); complete staging and search-for-primary imaging; obtain tissue diagnosis (biopsy) or schedule resection; add pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis once hemorrhage risk is acceptable; radiation oncology and medical oncology consults for treatment planning; correct sodium and glucose derangements; PT/OT/SLP functional and swallow assessment; begin steroid taper if symptoms controlled; engage palliative care for high symptom burden or poor prognosis.
day3plus_milestones: Finalize integrated WHO CNS5 diagnosis (IDH/1p19q/MGMT or metastatic origin) and multidisciplinary tumor-board plan; initiate or schedule definitive therapy (SRS or WBRT with memantine plus hippocampal avoidance, resection, or systemic/targeted therapy); continue steroid taper to lowest effective dose; confirm seizure control >=24-48h on therapeutic ASM; verify functional status and safe disposition; complete patient education and transitions; discharge once neurologically stable with plan in place.
consults_multidisciplinary: Neurosurgery (biopsy/resection, CSF diversion) and neuro-oncology on day 1; radiation oncology and medical oncology (primary tumor source) for treatment planning; palliative care early for poor prognosis or high symptom burden; PT/OT and speech-language pathology (dysphagia, cognition, function) by day 2; case management/social work for disposition, financial and transport needs; clinical pharmacy for steroid taper, ASM selection and drug-interaction screening; nutrition for dysphagia and steroid hyperglycemia; endocrine if sellar/pituitary involvement; ophthalmology for papilledema/visual fields; neuropsychology for cognitive baseline; multidisciplinary tumor-board presentation.
monitoring_level: Neuro-ICU/ICU for GCS <=8, status epilepticus, impending or active herniation, hyperosmolar therapy, external ventricular drain, large intratumoral hemorrhage, or airway compromise; step-down/telemetry for fluctuating neuro status needing q1-2h neuro checks, active edema management, or hypertonic-saline sodium correction; general floor with q4h neuro checks for stable patients. Escalation triggers: GCS decline >=2 points, new/worsening focal deficit, seizure cluster or status, Cushing response (hypertension with bradycardia), pupillary change, or acute hydrocephalus.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet advanced per swallow/dysphagia screen with aspiration precautions for bulbar/posterior-fossa lesions or depressed consciousness; steroid-induced hyperglycemia managed with scheduled plus correction insulin (target glucose <180 mg/dL); early mobilization with fall AND seizure precautions; VTE prophylaxis - intermittent pneumatic compression on admission plus pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SQ daily or heparin 5000 U SQ q8-12h) once active hemorrhage is excluded and biopsy/surgery timing allows (brain tumor patients are among the highest inpatient VTE risks - do not withhold on unfounded intracranial-hemorrhage fear); remove central lines and urinary catheters as soon as feasible; GI and PJP prophylaxis with prolonged high-dose steroids.
discharge_criteria: Neurologically stable at or near baseline with controlled ICP symptoms; seizures controlled >=24-48h on therapeutic oral ASM (if applicable); tolerating oral intake and oral corticosteroid with a defined taper; pain and nausea controlled; glucose and sodium stable; ambulating safely or with confirmed support/equipment; definitive treatment plan and outpatient follow-up established; safe disposition secured.
discharge_planning: Disposition matched to KPS/function - home with or without services, acute inpatient rehab, skilled nursing, or hospice for poor prognosis; medication reconciliation with an explicit written dexamethasone taper schedule, ASM, PPI, PJP prophylaxis and glucose agents; scheduled follow-up with neuro-oncology, neurosurgery (biopsy/resection results), radiation oncology (RT simulation/planning) and medical oncology within 1-2 weeks; driving-restriction counseling per state seizure law; teach-back on seizure first aid, steroid side effects, and return precautions (worsening headache, new deficit, seizure, fever); tumor-board presentation and closed-loop communication to the outpatient team.
expected_los: DRG 055 (without MCC) CMS GMLOS approximately 2.9-3.0 days (AMLOS ~3.7 days); DRG 054 (with MCC) GMLOS approximately 5.0 days (AMLOS ~6.5-7 days); blended expected LOS ~3-5 days by severity tier.
target_los: Optimized target ~2-3 days for uncomplicated diagnostic/symptom-control admissions (without MCC) and ~4 days for complicated (with MCC) admissions, achieved via rapid MRI plus coordinated tissue diagnosis, early tumor board, prompt steroid/edema control, and proactive disposition planning.
quality_metrics: VTE prophylaxis rate and appropriateness (VTE-1/VTE-2); avoidance of inappropriate prophylactic antiseizure meds in seizure-free patients (Choosing Wisely / SNO-EANO adherence); steroid stewardship (minimum effective dose, taper documented); time-to-tissue-diagnosis and time-to-treatment (RT/systemic) initiation; documented goals-of-care/palliative involvement; glycemic control during steroids; falls and aspiration-pneumonia rates; 30-day readmission; tumor-board presentation rate; patient-reported symptom and functional outcomes.
common_complications: Cerebral edema progression/herniation (mitigated by steroids, hyperosmolar therapy, serial neuro checks, neurosurgery); seizures/status epilepticus (ASM, precautions); VTE/DVT-PE - up to 20-30% in high-grade glioma (mechanical plus pharmacologic prophylaxis); steroid toxicity - hyperglycemia, proximal myopathy, insomnia/psychosis, GI bleed, immunosuppression/PJP, Cushingoid habitus (minimum dose, GI and PJP prophylaxis, glucose control); hyponatremia from SIADH or cerebral salt wasting (fluid/sodium management, hypertonic saline); intratumoral hemorrhage; obstructive hydrocephalus (CSF diversion); delirium, falls, dysphagia/aspiration.
readmission_drivers: Breakthrough/recurrent seizures and ASM nonadherence; rebound cerebral edema from too-rapid steroid taper; VTE; steroid complications (hyperglycemia, infection, GI bleed); symptomatic hyponatremia; disease progression with functional decline/failure to thrive; uncontrolled symptoms or inadequate home support. Prevention levers: individualized steroid taper, ASM adherence and level checks, continued VTE prophylaxis, glucose monitoring, early neuro-onc/RT follow-up, palliative-care engagement, caregiver education and clear written return precautions.
guideline_source: NCCN Central Nervous System Cancers (2024/2025 updates); SNO-EANO Anticonvulsant Prophylaxis in Newly Diagnosed Brain Tumors Practice Guideline Update 2021; ASCO-SNO-ASTRO Treatment for Brain Metastases Guideline 2022; Congress of Neurological Surgeons (CNS) Guidelines on Steroids and Anticonvulsant Prophylaxis in Metastatic Brain Tumors 2019 (ASCO/SNO-endorsed); ASTRO Radiation Therapy for Brain Metastases Guideline 2022; WHO Classification of CNS Tumors 5th edition (CNS5) 2021.
key_citations: Per PubMed: Walbert et al, SNO/EANO Anticonvulsant Prophylaxis in Newly Diagnosed Brain Tumors, Neuro-Oncology 2021;23(11):1835-1844 (PMID 34174071; DOI 10.1093/neuonc/noab152) - Level A against prophylactic AEDs in seizure-free patients; Vogelbaum et al, Treatment for Brain Metastases: ASCO-SNO-ASTRO Guideline, J Clin Oncol 2022;40(5):492-516 (PMID 34932393; DOI 10.1200/JCO.21.02314); Horbinski et al, NCCN Guidelines Insights CNS Cancers v2.2022, JNCCN 2023;21(1):12-20 (PMID 36634606; DOI 10.6004/jnccn.2023.0002); CNS/ASCO/SNO steroid+anticonvulsant endorsement (DOI 10.1200/JCO.18.02085); landmark trial Stupp et al, Radiotherapy plus concomitant/adjuvant temozolomide for glioblastoma, NEJM 2005 (PMID 15758009).
evidence_grade: Moderate-to-Strong - anchored on Class I/Level A society guidance for seizure-prophylaxis avoidance (SNO/EANO 2021) and Class I evidence for brain-metastasis local therapy (ASCO-SNO-ASTRO 2022); steroid dosing and much day-to-day inpatient supportive management (edema, hyponatremia, monitoring cadence, LOS targets) rests on consensus/expert opinion and is extrapolated.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 054 (with MCC) captures patients with MCCs - depressed consciousness/coma, status epilepticus, acute respiratory failure, aspiration pneumonia, sepsis, intracranial hemorrhage - driving neuro-ICU need, EVD/airway management, higher complication rates and GMLOS ~5 days; DRG 055 (without MCC) are typically shorter diagnostic or symptom-control admissions (edema/seizure control, staging) with GMLOS ~3 days. Across this collapsed group, hyponatremia, VTE, and steroid complications frequently act as the CC/MCC that shifts a case into the higher-severity tier.
pathway_pearls: (1) Do NOT start prophylactic antiseizure meds in seizure-free brain-tumor patients (SNO/EANO Level A) - treat only those who have seized, use levetiracetam, and avoid enzyme-inducing AEDs. (2) If primary CNS lymphoma is possible, WITHHOLD corticosteroids until biopsy to preserve diagnostic yield (ghost-tumor effect); otherwise dose dexamethasone at the minimum effective level with a defined taper. (3) Give VTE pharmacoprophylaxis - these are among the highest-VTE-risk inpatients; combine mechanical plus pharmacologic once hemorrhage risk permits rather than withholding. (4) Front-load rapid MRI plus tissue diagnosis, early multidisciplinary tumor board, and proactive disposition/palliative involvement to compress LOS and expedite definitive RT/systemic therapy.

scope_and_volume_notes: Medical CNS-neoplasm admissions (primary/metastatic brain tumors); resection captured under craniotomy (rank 49).
