admission_criteria: Complicated PUD is an emergent INPATIENT admission — observation/ED discharge is NOT appropriate for confirmed perforation or significant obstruction. Perforation: pneumoperitoneum on upright CXR/CT, peritonitis (rebound/rigidity), or contrast extravasation = emergent surgical admission. Gastric outlet obstruction (GOO): intractable vomiting, succussion splash, dehydration, hypochloremic-hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis, or dilated stomach = admit for NG decompression + rehydration + EGD. Sepsis/septic shock (qSOFA>=2, hypotension, lactate>=2) = admit, ICU-level if shock. Highly-selected sealed/contained perforation may be trialed non-operatively but still requires inpatient (step-down/ICU) monitoring. Note: operatively-managed perforation often reclassifies to a surgical DRG; medical DRG 380-382 captures medically/endoscopically managed complicated ulcers.
risk_stratification: Boey score (perforated ulcer; 1 pt each: preop shock SBP<90, major medical comorbidity, symptom duration >24h; 0->~1.5%, 1->~8-15%, 2->~30-45%, 3->~65-100% 30-day mortality) drives ICU triage, urgency of OR, and goals-of-care. PULP score (Peptic Ulcer Perforation, Moller 2012; 0-18 from age>65, active malignancy, cirrhosis, steroids, shock, delayed presentation, Cr>130 umol/L, ASA class; >=7 = high risk ~25%+ mortality). ASA class and hypoalbuminemia are the strongest single mortality predictors (WSES). qSOFA>=2 / SOFA + lactate grade sepsis (Surviving Sepsis). Mannheim Peritonitis Index (>26-29 = high mortality) for peritonitis burden; Caprini/Charlson for VTE/comorbidity.
initial_workup_labs: CBC (leukocytosis; anemia flags concurrent bleed), CMP/electrolytes (hypochloremic-hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis + prerenal azotemia in GOO; Cr for AKI), venous lactate (>2 sepsis, >=4 shock -> bundle), VBG, LFTs, lipase (exclude pancreatitis; mildly elevated in perforation), coags/INR (esp if anticoagulated), type & screen/crossmatch, blood cultures x2 BEFORE antibiotics (SEP-1), CRP/procalcitonin, albumin/prealbumin (prognostic + nutrition), troponin/ECG in elderly for perioperative risk. H. pylori: acute serology unreliable — obtain intraoperative/endoscopic biopsy (rapid urease/histology) or defer to urea breath test/stool antigen. Peritoneal fluid culture (bacteria + fungi) at source control to guide de-escalation.
initial_imaging_dx: Upright chest/abdominal X-ray FIRST for free air under the diaphragm (sensitivity only 30-85%). CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast = imaging of choice (WSES strong rec): detects free air, free fluid, perforation site/size; add water-soluble oral/NG contrast to confirm and localize the leak (up to ~12% of perforations have a normal CT). Target CT within ~1-2h for suspected perforation with peritonitis. GOO: EGD (diagnostic + therapeutic dilation + MANDATORY biopsy to exclude malignancy) +/- CT or upper-GI water-soluble contrast study. Avoid routine acute EGD in free perforation (insufflation risk). EGD within 24h applies to the bleeding-ulcer phenotype (ACG) captured under the GI-hemorrhage DRG.
time_critical_interventions: SOURCE-CONTROL TIMING is the dominant survival lever: each hour of delay to surgery within the first 24h ~ 2.4% lower survival; perforation-to-OR >36h (and even >9h) sharply raises leak and mortality — target definitive source control ASAP after resuscitation. Resuscitate ideally within 1h to MAP>=65 mmHg, urine output >=0.5 mL/kg/h, and lactate clearance. If septic: CMS SEP-1 / Surviving Sepsis 1-hour bundle — measure lactate, draw blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1h, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate>=4, vasopressors for MAP<65. Early NG decompression + IV PPI. GOO: correct alkalosis and volume before any endoscopic/operative intervention.
initial_management: NPO + NG decompression; IV high-dose PPI (pantoprazole 40 mg IV daily-BID; 80 mg bolus + 8 mg/h infusion if concurrent bleeding). Balanced crystalloid resuscitation (LR/Plasma-Lyte); norepinephrine if MAP<65 after fluids. Empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for secondary peritonitis after cultures: piperacillin-tazobactam 3.375-4.5 g IV q6-8h, OR ceftriaxone 2 g + metronidazole 500 mg q8h, OR ertapenem 1 g (carbapenem e.g. meropenem if critically ill/healthcare-associated); add antifungal (fluconazole/echinocandin) only for high-risk or Candida on culture (upper-GI perforation). SOURCE CONTROL: open or laparoscopic omental (Graham/Cellan-Jones) patch for perforation <2 cm; gastric ulcers biopsied/resected to exclude cancer; damage-control laparotomy if septic shock. Reverse coagulopathy (vit K/PCC; hold/reverse anticoagulants). Transfuse RBC restrictively at Hgb<7 g/dL (<8 with CV disease). GOO: rehydrate, replace K/Cl, IV PPI, endoscopic balloon dilation for benign stricture, plus H. pylori eradication.
day1_milestones: Confirm diagnosis on CT; risk-stratify (Boey/PULP/qSOFA). Resuscitate to MAP>=65 / UOP>=0.5 / lactate clearing; NPO, NG tube, IV PPI, analgesia, correct electrolytes/coagulopathy. Cultures then broad-spectrum antibiotics (<=1h if septic). EMERGENT acute-care-surgery consult and source-control decision: OR (omental patch) vs highly-selected non-operative management (Taylor method: sealed perforation, no peritonitis, water-soluble contrast confirms no leak). Foley for UOP, mechanical VTE prophylaxis (SCDs). Assign monitoring level (ICU if shock / Boey 2-3 / PULP>=7). GOO: NG decompression, rehydration, plan EGD + biopsy.
day2_milestones: Post-source-control: continue antibiotics (plan short ~4-day course), IV PPI, fluids; opioid-sparing analgesia, incentive spirometry, EARLY mobilization out of bed. Vigilant leak/sepsis surveillance (fever, WBC/CRP trend, lactate, abdominal exam, drain output). Reassess organ function (renal, respiratory); wean vasopressors. Start pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) once hemostasis is secure. Nutrition assessment; NG stays until function returns. Non-operative cases: serial exams + labs +/- repeat contrast study — escalate to OR for any deterioration. Secure the H. pylori diagnostic plan.
day3plus_milestones: De-escalate/STOP antibiotics ~day 4 after adequate source control (STOP-IT). Remove NG when output low and bowel function returns; advance diet clears->regular as tolerated. Transition IV->oral PPI and oral analgesia; remove Foley/central lines; continue mobilization. If fever/rising WBC or ileus -> CT for abscess/leak -> percutaneous drainage. Finalize 14-day H. pylori regimen, STOP NSAIDs, confirm gastric-ulcer biopsy result. Discharge teaching + schedule surgery/GI follow-up, repeat EGD (gastric ulcers, 8-12 wk) and H. pylori test-of-cure. GOO: perform balloon dilation, confirm tolerating diet; surgery (gastrojejunostomy +/- vagotomy) if refractory to dilation.
consults_multidisciplinary: Acute-care / emergency general surgery — IMMEDIATE (source control). Gastroenterology — EGD/biopsy/dilation for GOO, H. pylori management, repeat-EGD planning. Interventional radiology — percutaneous drainage of collections (and angioembolization if concurrent bleeding). Critical care / anesthesia — perioperative and ICU. Infectious disease — complicated/resistant intra-abdominal infection, antifungal stewardship. Nutrition/dietitian — prolonged NPO, malnutrition, enteral/parenteral plan. Pharmacy — antimicrobial stewardship, PPI, H. pylori regimen, medication reconciliation. Care management + social work — disposition, follow-up, substance/NSAID counseling. PT/OT — deconditioned/elderly, delirium. Palliative care — high Boey/PULP frail elderly, goals of care.
monitoring_level: ICU: septic shock/vasopressors, respiratory failure, lactate>=4, damage-control laparotomy, Boey 2-3 or PULP>=7, hemodynamic instability. Step-down/telemetry: post-source-control but comorbid/elderly, moderate physiologic derangement, or non-operative management under close observation. Floor: hemodynamically stable, source-controlled, non-septic, tolerating (typical DRG 382). Escalation triggers: MAP<65 despite fluids, rising lactate, new/increasing vasopressor need, respiratory distress/SpO2 fall, oliguria/AKI, worsening peritonitis, or signs of leak/abscess.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: NPO with NG decompression initially; advance clears->regular as bowel function returns (typically POD2-3); early enteral nutrition preferred, dietitian if malnourished/hypoalbuminemic, parenteral only if enteral infeasible >5-7 days. Mobility: out of bed POD1, progressive ambulation, incentive spirometry to prevent atelectasis/pneumonia. VTE: mechanical SCDs on admission; add pharmacologic LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg daily) or UFH once hemostasis assured (caution with concurrent bleeding). Glucose target 140-180 mg/dL (avoid tight control). Remove Foley and central lines as early as feasible to prevent CAUTI/CLABSI.
discharge_criteria: Source control confirmed with no ongoing sepsis (afebrile >=24h, normalizing WBC/CRP, off vasopressors); no clinical or imaging evidence of leak or undrained collection. Tolerating oral diet, hydration, and oral medications; adequate pain control on oral analgesics. Return of bowel function (flatus/BM), NG removed. Ambulating at/near baseline with stable vitals. H. pylori eradication and PPI plan in place, NSAIDs stopped; drains managed/planned; follow-up and test-of-cure scheduled.
discharge_planning: Disposition home (SNF/rehab if deconditioned elderly). Med rec: continue PPI >=4-8 wks (longer if indicated); STOP offending NSAIDs/aspirin or add PPI gastroprotection if aspirin required for a CV indication; START/complete 14-day H. pylori eradication (bismuth quadruple therapy: PPI + bismuth + tetracycline + metronidazole — ACG 2024 preferred). Follow-up: surgery 1-2 wks; GI for repeat EGD in 8-12 wks for gastric ulcers (confirm healing / exclude malignancy); PCP. H. pylori TEST-OF-CURE (urea breath test or stool antigen) >=4 wks post-therapy and off PPI 1-2 wks — schedule before discharge. Education: NSAID avoidance, smoking/alcohol cessation, adherence, red-flag symptoms (pain, fever, vomiting, melena). Transitions: 48-72h follow-up call, clear written instructions.
expected_los: MS-DRG group GMLOS/ALOS (medical): DRG 382 (no CC/MCC) GMLOS ~2.2d / ALOS ~2.7d; DRG 381 (CC) GMLOS ~3.1d / ALOS 3.8d (RW 1.08); DRG 380 (MCC) GMLOS ~5.0d / ALOS 6.7d (RW 1.96). Blended benchmark ~3-5 days. Operatively-managed perforation with peritonitis commonly runs 6-9 days (frequently coded to a surgical DRG).
target_los: Non-MCC medically/endoscopically managed or clean laparoscopic omental-patch repair: 2-4 days. CC tier: ~4 days. MCC/septic/ICU/damage-control: 6-8 days. Pathway target: meet or beat GMLOS by tier via prompt source control, STOP-IT short-course antibiotics, early NG removal + diet advancement, and early mobilization.
quality_metrics: CMS SEP-1 (severe sepsis/septic shock bundle) for the septic subset; Surviving Sepsis 1-hour-bundle compliance. Time-to-source-control (door-to-OR). 30-day all-cause readmission and 30-day mortality (risk-adjusted). NSQIP surgical-site infection, organ-space SSI, and anastomotic/repair leak rates. Antimicrobial stewardship: STOP-IT-concordant ~4-day duration + antifungal stewardship. VTE prophylaxis compliance. Documented H. pylori testing + eradication + test-of-cure. Glycemic control, delirium screening, HCAHPS/patient experience.
common_complications: Sepsis/septic shock and diffuse peritonitis; intra-abdominal abscess/organ-space SSI; anastomotic/repair or duodenal-stump leak (re-perforation); ileus/prolonged NPO; AKI; pneumonia/atelectasis; VTE; surgical-site infection; delirium (elderly); pancreatic or enterocutaneous fistula; malnutrition. Mitigation: prompt source control, appropriate + short-course antibiotics, drain surveillance with early CT and percutaneous drainage, IV PPI, early mobilization/incentive spirometry, VTE prophylaxis, glycemic control, delirium prevention, and nutrition support.
readmission_drivers: Recurrent or re-perforated/bleeding ulcer from FAILURE to eradicate H. pylori or continued NSAID/aspirin use (top preventable driver); missed H. pylori testing/eradication or no test-of-cure; post-discharge intra-abdominal abscess/leak; dehydration/poor PO intake/malnutrition; undiagnosed gastric malignancy (unbiopsied gastric ulcer). Prevention levers: guaranteed H. pylori test-and-treat with documented test-of-cure, NSAID cessation/gastroprotection, adequate-duration PPI, mandatory biopsy of all gastric ulcers + repeat EGD at 8-12 wks, nutrition follow-up, and early post-discharge contact.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: WSES (World Society of Emergency Surgery) 2020 — Perforated and bleeding peptic ulcer guidelines (Tarasconi et al., GRADE-based). ACG (American College of Gastroenterology) 2024 — Treatment of H. pylori Infection. Supporting: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 + CMS SEP-1 (septic-peritonitis subset); SIS/IDSA complicated intra-abdominal infection guidance; STOP-IT trial (antibiotic duration). Bleeding phenotype -> ACG 2021 upper-GI-bleed guideline (captured under the GI-hemorrhage DRG).
key_citations: Tarasconi A, et al. Perforated and bleeding peptic ulcer: WSES guidelines. World J Emerg Surg 2020;15:3 (PMID 31921329; DOI 10.1186/s13017-019-0283-9). Chey WD, et al. ACG Clinical Guideline: Treatment of H. pylori Infection. Am J Gastroenterol 2024;119:1730-1753 (PMID 39626064; DOI 10.14309/ajg.0000000000002968); prior 2017 version (PMID 28071659). Sawyer RG, et al. STOP-IT: short-course antimicrobial therapy for intraabdominal infection. N Engl J Med 2015;372:1996-2005 (PMID 25992746; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1411162). Villanueva C, et al. Transfusion strategies for acute upper-GI bleeding. N Engl J Med 2013;368:11-21 (PMID 23281973; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1211801). Landmark risk scores: Boey J, et al. Ann Surg 1987; Moller MH, et al. PULP score 2012 (PMIDs not independently verified here). Citations retrieved via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Moderate-to-Strong. Primary = WSES 2020 (GRADE): rapid resuscitation, source-control timing, and empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics are STRONG; laparoscopic-vs-open approach, non-operative management, and empiric antifungal are WEAK/conditional (expert consensus). H. pylori eradication (ACG 2024) is Strong/Class I. Sepsis bundle (SSC 2021), antibiotic duration (STOP-IT RCT), and restrictive transfusion (Villanueva RCT) are high-quality but extrapolated from adjacent populations. No dedicated US-society RCT-grade guideline exists specifically for perforated PUD — the base is largely observational/surgical-society consensus.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 382 (no CC/MCC): younger/fewer comorbidities, contained perforation or medically/endoscopically managed GOO, quick clean recovery — floor care, LOS ~2-3d, low mortality (Boey 0). DRG 381 (CC): moderate comorbidity or a complication (localized peritonitis, mild AKI, electrolyte derangement, transfusion) — telemetry/step-down, LOS ~4d. DRG 380 (MCC): septic shock, diffuse peritonitis, acute respiratory failure, AKI, need for ICU/vasopressors/damage-control — ICU, LOS ~7-10d, substantial mortality (Boey 2-3, PULP>=7). MCC drivers (sepsis/septic shock, acute respiratory failure, AKI) sharply raise ICU need, complications, and mortality; operative peritonitis frequently reclassifies to a surgical DRG.
pathway_pearls: 1) TIME TO SOURCE CONTROL dominates survival — fast CT, resuscitate to MAP>=65/lactate clearance, run the SEP-1/Surviving Sepsis 1-hour bundle, and get to the OR without delay (each hour of delay in the first 24h ~2.4% survival loss). 2) Never discharge without an H. pylori test-and-treat plan + NSAID cessation + adequate PPI + scheduled test-of-cure — the single highest-yield recurrence/readmission lever. 3) Biopsy EVERY gastric ulcer and re-scope at 8-12 wks — perforation/obstruction can be the first presentation of gastric cancer. 4) After adequate source control, STOP antibiotics at ~4 days (STOP-IT) and remove NG/lines early to cut SSI, C. diff, CAUTI, and LOS.

scope_and_volume_notes: Complicated peptic ulcer (perforation/obstruction). Bleeding ulcers largely captured under GI hemorrhage (rank 22).

admission_criteria: This collapsed DRG group is bimodal; the true inpatient drivers are Guillain-Barre syndrome (GBS) and myasthenic crisis, NOT Bell palsy/isolated neuropathy (usually ED-discharge/outpatient). ADMIT every suspected GBS for monitoring - progression is unpredictable; mandatory inpatient if any bulbar/respiratory involvement, autonomic instability, rapidly progressive weakness, or inability to walk unaided. Myasthenic crisis: admit (ICU) for respiratory distress, bulbar weakness, or falling FVC. Bell palsy and isolated peripheral neuropathy: ED discharge/outpatient - admit ONLY if atypical (bilateral, multiple cranial nerves, suspected GBS/stroke/Lyme), unable to protect the eye, or diagnostic uncertainty. Decision drivers: EGRIS/mEGRIS ventilation risk; any GBS with EGRIS >=4 or bulbar/autonomic features -> ICU/step-down
risk_stratification: EGRIS (Erasmus GBS Respiratory Insufficiency Score, 0-7): onset-to-admission (<=3d=2, 4-7d=1, >7d=0) + facial/bulbar weakness (present=1) + MRC sum score (60-51=0, 50-41=1, 40-31=2, 30-21=3, <=20=4); low 0-2 (~4% ventilation risk), intermediate 3-4 (~24%), high 5-7 (~65%) at 1 week -> drives ICU triage; mEGRIS (onset-to-admission, bulbar palsy, neck-flexion, hip-flexion weakness; AUC 0.84) is the guideline-preferred update. mEGOS (0-12 at day 7: age, preceding diarrhea, MRC sum score) predicts 6-month inability to walk -> rehab/prognosis counseling. Brighton criteria levels 1-4 for diagnostic certainty. Bedside 20/30/40 rule: FVC <20 mL/kg, MIP less negative than -30, MEP <40 cmH2O = impending failure. MGFA class V = myasthenic crisis
initial_workup_labs: GBS: LP/CSF for albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein, WBC <50; may be normal in first week - do not exclude GBS); serum IgA level BEFORE IVIG (IgA deficiency -> anaphylaxis); anti-ganglioside antibodies (anti-GQ1b if Miller Fisher/ophthalmoplegia, anti-GM1 for AMAN); Campylobacter/CMV/EBV/Zika/HIV serologies; CBC, CMP with sodium (SIADH/hyponatremia common), LFTs, ESR/CRP, HbA1c, B12, TSH; pregnancy test pre-IVIG. Myasthenic crisis: AChR-Ab, MuSK-Ab, anti-LRP4; ABG (hypercapnia is late/ominous); infection screen (CBC, cultures, CXR) for precipitant; TFTs; review MG-aggravating drugs. Bell palsy: clinical diagnosis; selective Lyme, HIV, glucose only if atypical/bilateral
initial_imaging_dx: GBS: serial bedside spirometry (FVC and negative inspiratory force/NIF) q4-6h is the single most important test; nerve conduction studies/EMG to classify AIDP vs axonal AMAN/AMSAN (often normal in first 1-2 weeks - repeat at ~2 weeks); MRI spine with gadolinium showing nerve-root enhancement when presentation atypical or to exclude cord compression; continuous telemetry for dysautonomia. Myasthenic crisis: FVC/NIF q2-4h; CT chest for thymoma; repetitive nerve stimulation/single-fiber EMG and ice-pack test if diagnosis uncertain. Bell palsy: NO routine imaging; MRI brain/internal auditory canal with contrast only if atypical, progressive beyond 3 weeks, no recovery by 4 months, recurrent, or additional cranial neuropathies
time_critical_interventions: GBS: start IVIG or plasma exchange as soon as patient cannot walk unaided (greatest benefit within 2 weeks of weakness onset; still indicated up to 4 weeks) - do not wait for a nadir. Airway is the time-critical priority: intubate PROACTIVELY for FVC <15-20 mL/kg, NIF less negative than -30 cmH2O, rapid decline, or bulbar failure/aspiration - do NOT wait for hypoxia or hypercapnia; delayed emergent intubation drives morbidity. Continuous telemetry with atropine/pacing readiness for dysautonomia (labile BP, bradyarrhythmia). Myasthenic crisis: emergent plasma exchange or IVIG within hours of recognition; secure airway early (favor early controlled intubation over failed NIV); treat precipitating infection (antibiotics <=1h if septic shock). No formal national door-to-drug metric exists for these conditions
initial_management: GBS first-line immunotherapy (IVIG and PLEX are equivalent - pick ONE, do not combine): IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day x 5 days (total 2 g/kg) OR plasma exchange ~200-250 mL/kg in 4-5 exchanges (12-15 L) over 1-2 weeks. Corticosteroids are NOT recommended in GBS (ineffective/harmful). Do NOT routinely give a second IVIG course, even in poor-prognosis patients (SID-GBS trial negative). Myasthenic crisis: PLEX (5 exchanges) or IVIG 2 g/kg over 2-5 days; hold pyridostigmine while intubated (secretion burden), resume after; continue/initiate prednisone cautiously (can transiently worsen) plus steroid-sparing agent (azathioprine, mycophenolate); complement inhibitor (eculizumab/ravulizumab) or FcRn antagonist (efgartigimod/rozanolixizumab) for refractory AChR-positive disease; strictly avoid MG-aggravating drugs (aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, magnesium, beta-blockers). Supportive: neuropathic pain with gabapentinoids/TCA/carbamazepine (opioids/NSAIDs limited); treat SIADH; bowel/bladder care
day1_milestones: Establish diagnosis (LP, NCS/EMG, antibody panel sent); calculate EGRIS/mEGRIS and record baseline MRC sum score, FVC and NIF; determine ambulation status and DECIDE on immunotherapy; START IVIG or PLEX the same day if unable to walk unaided; assign correct monitoring level (ICU/step-down if EGRIS >=4, bulbar/autonomic signs, or declining FVC); place on continuous telemetry; initiate VTE prophylaxis; Neurology consult; strict aspiration/swallow precautions with NPO until swallow assessed; baseline pain and autonomic assessment; IgA checked before first IVIG
day2_milestones: Continue immunotherapy course; reassess FVC/NIF trend q4-6h and MRC sum score for ongoing progression; escalate to ICU immediately if respiratory parameters decline (FVC fall >30%, NIF worsening, new bulbar signs); PT/OT evaluation; speech-language pathology swallow evaluation if bulbar involvement; optimize neuropathic pain control; actively monitor for dysautonomia (BP lability, ileus, arrhythmia, urinary retention) and hyponatremia/SIADH; confirm VTE prophylaxis and pressure-injury prevention; bowel regimen started
day3plus_milestones: Complete IVIG (day 5) or full PLEX course; document clinical nadir (GBS usually progresses <=2-4 weeks then plateaus); recalculate mEGOS at day 7 for outcome prognosis and disposition planning; if weakness continues to progress beyond 8 weeks reconsider acute-onset CIDP (A-CIDP, ~5%); watch for treatment-related fluctuation (secondary deterioration 1-2 weeks after therapy -> re-treat with IVIG/PLEX); begin intensive rehabilitation; wean ventilator as strength returns and consider early tracheostomy if ventilation likely to exceed ~2 weeks; secure inpatient-rehab bed; for myasthenic crisis, bridge to maintenance immunosuppression and extubate once FVC recovering
consults_multidisciplinary: Neurology/neuromuscular (primary, day 0); Critical care/ICU (any respiratory or autonomic risk); PT + OT + speech-language pathology early (swallow safety and mobility); Respiratory therapy (serial spirometry, ventilator); Pharmacy (IVIG/PLEX stewardship, MG drug-interaction screen); Pain service if refractory neuropathic pain; Physiatry/rehabilitation medicine for IRF placement; Care management and social work from day 1 for rehab disposition; Registered dietitian/nutrition; Ophthalmology for eye protection in facial weakness/Bell palsy; Psychology/psychiatry for anxiety and ICU delirium; Cardiology if refractory dysautonomia/arrhythmia
monitoring_level: ICU if FVC <20 mL/kg or falling, NIF less negative than -30 cmH2O, bulbar weakness/aspiration, autonomic instability, rapidly progressive weakness, high EGRIS (5-7), or mechanical ventilation. Step-down/telemetry for intermediate EGRIS (3-4) or any bulbar/autonomic sign. Floor only for plateaued, ambulatory, respiratory-stable patients (FVC >20 mL/kg) with q-shift spirometry. Escalation triggers: FVC drop >30% from baseline or <15-20 mL/kg, worsening NIF, new/worsening bulbar or aspiration, dysautonomia (arrhythmia, BP lability, ileus), or rising PaCO2 (a late sign - escalate before it appears)
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: formal swallow assessment before any oral intake; NG/enteral feeding if bulbar dysfunction or intubated; aspiration precautions throughout. Mobility: early PT/OT once hemodynamically stable; range-of-motion and positioning to prevent contractures and pressure injury; splinting for foot/wrist drop. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH) plus mechanical devices for all immobile patients - GBS/crisis carry high VTE risk. Glucose control (tighter on corticosteroids). Bowel/bladder regimen for autonomic ileus and urinary retention. Remove urinary catheters and central lines as early as feasible to reduce CAUTI/CLABSI. Eye lubrication and taping/moisture chamber for lagophthalmos in facial weakness
discharge_criteria: Neurologic deficit stable or improving past the nadir (no progression for 24-48h without escalation); respiratory stability (FVC >20 mL/kg, liberated from ventilator, secretions managed); safe swallow confirmed or stable enteral-feeding plan; autonomic stability (no significant BP lability or arrhythmia); neuropathic pain controlled on an oral regimen; VTE prophylaxis plan in place; mobilization plan established and rehabilitation bed secured; ADLs manageable at discharge setting or accepted to IRF/SNF. Bell palsy: dischargeable once corticosteroids started and eye-protection plan in place (typically never requires admission)
discharge_planning: Disposition most often acute inpatient rehabilitation (IRF) for GBS with residual weakness, or home with outpatient PT for milder cases; medication reconciliation including MG steroid taper and immunosuppressant monitoring; Neurology follow-up within 2-4 weeks with repeat exam and mEGOS-based recovery counseling; explicit education on GBS treatment-related fluctuation (may relapse 1-2 weeks post-therapy -> return precautions) and the typically months-long recovery (~80% walk independently by 6 months), residual fatigue and pain; MG patients get a crisis action plan, a list of aggravating drugs to avoid, and sick-day rules; documented eye-care instructions for Bell palsy; ensure VTE prophylaxis continues during immobility; warm handoff to PCP and rehab team
expected_los: Collapsed DRG group GMLOS approximately 3.0 days (DRG 074, without MCC) to approximately 5.0 days (DRG 073, with MCC), per CMS IPPS Table 5 (AMLOS roughly 3.8-6.6 days). The average is dominated by short Bell palsy/mild-neuropathy stays and masks a bimodal reality: uncomplicated GBS runs ~7-14 acute-care days before rehab, and ventilated GBS or myasthenic crisis frequently runs 3-6+ weeks in ICU. Typical non-ventilated GBS inpatient acute phase ~1-2 weeks
target_los: Optimized targets by phenotype: Bell palsy and isolated peripheral neuropathy = 0 inpatient days (ED discharge/observation with outpatient follow-up); non-ventilated GBS or myasthenic crisis = ~7-10 days to rehab transfer; ventilated crisis benchmarked to early tracheostomy and prompt rehab handoff rather than a fixed number. Collapsed-group operational target ~3-4 days (DRG 074) and ~5 days (DRG 073), achieved by not admitting uncomplicated Bell palsy and by expediting IRF placement once the neurologic nadir is passed
quality_metrics: No condition-specific CMS core measure exists; use process/outcome metrics: time from loss of independent ambulation to immunotherapy start; EGRIS/mEGRIS-appropriate ICU triage rate; rate of unplanned/late/emergent intubation; VTE prophylaxis compliance; hospital-acquired/aspiration pneumonia rate; pressure-injury rate; 30-day readmission (treatment-related fluctuation, aspiration); guideline adherence (avoidance of corticosteroids in GBS; avoidance of routine second IVIG course); Bell palsy: corticosteroid-within-72h rate, documented eye protection, avoidance of unnecessary neuroimaging. IGOS-style registry participation supports benchmarking
common_complications: Respiratory failure/prolonged mechanical ventilation; aspiration pneumonia from bulbar weakness; dysautonomia (arrhythmia, labile BP, ileus, urinary retention); VTE/pulmonary embolism from immobility; hyponatremia/SIADH; severe neuropathic pain; ICU-acquired weakness and deconditioning; pressure injuries and contractures; depression/anxiety and ICU delirium; IVIG-related aseptic meningitis, thrombosis, renal injury, or anaphylaxis (IgA deficiency); PLEX-related catheter infection, hypotension, hypocalcemia, coagulopathy. Mitigation: serial respiratory monitoring with proactive intubation, telemetry, VTE prophylaxis, aspiration precautions, sodium surveillance, early mobilization and skin care
readmission_drivers: GBS treatment-related fluctuation/secondary deterioration (relapse ~5-10% within 8 weeks -> re-dose IVIG or PLEX); aspiration pneumonia; VTE; falls from residual weakness; inadequate rehabilitation access; misclassified A-CIDP (progression continuing beyond 8 weeks); myasthenic relapse from under-immunosuppression, intercurrent infection, or an inadvertently prescribed aggravating drug; corticosteroid complications. Prevention levers: recognize and counsel on treatment-related fluctuation, secure timely rehab, early PT/OT, robust MG maintenance immunotherapy with an aggravating-drug avoidance list, and close early Neurology follow-up
guideline_source: PRIMARY (GBS, the dominant inpatient driver): European Academy of Neurology / Peripheral Nerve Society (EAN/PNS) Guideline on Diagnosis and Treatment of Guillain-Barre Syndrome, 2023 (van Doorn et al). Myasthenia gravis/crisis: Myasthenia Gravis Foundation of America (MGFA) International Consensus Guidance for Management of Myasthenia Gravis - 2020 Update (Narayanaswami et al, Neurology 2021). Bell palsy: American Academy of Neurology (AAN) 2012 evidence-based guideline (Gronseth) plus AAO-HNS 2013 Clinical Practice Guideline (Baugh et al). All current and society-authored
key_citations: EAN/PNS GBS Guideline: Eur J Neurol 2023;30(12):3646-3674, PMID 37814552, DOI 10.1111/ene.16073 (co-published J Peripher Nerv Syst 2023;28(4):535-563, PMID 37814551, DOI 10.1111/jns.12594). MG 2020 Update: Narayanaswami P, et al. Neurology 2021;96(3):114-122, PMID 33144515, DOI 10.1212/WNL.0000000000011124. mEGRIS: Luijten LWG, et al. J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry 2022;94(4):300-308, PMID 36428088, DOI 10.1136/jnnp-2022-329937. Bell palsy: Gronseth GS, Paduga R. Neurology 2012;79(22):2209-2213, PMID 23136264, DOI 10.1212/WNL.0b013e318275978c. Landmark evidence: EGRIS derivation - Walgaard C, et al. Ann Neurol 2010;67:781-787; mEGOS - Walgaard C, et al. Lancet Neurol 2011;10(11):1332; Brighton criteria - Fokke C, et al. Brain 2014;137:33-43. (Citations verified via PubMed; DOIs above)
evidence_grade: Mixed but strong at the core. STRONG - Class I society guideline (GRADE-based EAN/PNS 2023) for IVIG and PLEX equivalence and efficacy (multiple RCTs, Cochrane reviews) and for the recommendation against corticosteroids and against a routine second IVIG course; Bell palsy corticosteroids are AAN Level A (Class I). MODERATE for the prognostic scores (EGRIS, mEGRIS, mEGOS - prospectively derived and internationally validated in IGOS). CONSENSUS/EXPERT OPINION for the bedside respiratory thresholds (20/30/40 rule - weak empirical validation; only FVC independently predicts) and for myasthenic-crisis supportive management. Overall: strong for immunotherapy, moderate-to-consensus for monitoring/supportive elements
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 074 (no CC/MCC): milder GBS, Bell palsy, peripheral neuropathy - short LOS (~3 days), floor-level, ambulatory, home disposition. DRG 073 (with MCC): acute respiratory failure, mechanical ventilation, aspiration pneumonia, sepsis, or autonomic complications drive ICU care, GMLOS ~5 days on paper but real ventilated crises run 3-6+ weeks with high cost and rehab dependence. Severity is set by bulbar/respiratory/autonomic involvement, not the base diagnosis - a coded Bell palsy and a ventilated GBS both fall in this group yet are clinically worlds apart. Operational note: mechanical ventilation >=96 hours or tracheostomy typically regroups the case out to a ventilator/tracheostomy DRG (003-004), so the sickest crises may leave 073/074 entirely
pathway_pearls: 1) Admit and MONITOR every suspected GBS with serial FVC/NIF q4-6h plus EGRIS/mEGRIS, and intubate PROACTIVELY (FVC <15-20 mL/kg, NIF less negative than -30, bulbar failure) before decompensation - late emergent intubation and waiting for hypercapnia cause preventable harm. 2) IVIG and PLEX are equivalent - choose one and start early once the patient cannot walk unaided; do NOT combine them, do NOT give corticosteroids in GBS, and do NOT reflexively give a second IVIG course. 3) Anticipate GBS treatment-related fluctuation (relapse 1-2 weeks after therapy) and re-diagnose as A-CIDP if progression continues beyond 8 weeks. 4) Do not admit uncomplicated Bell palsy - corticosteroids within 72h plus eye protection as an outpatient - and reserve inpatient/ICU resources for genuine neuromuscular respiratory risk
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Guillain-Barre, myasthenic crisis, Bell palsy, peripheral neuropathy; crises -> Critical Care.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 073 — Cranial and Peripheral Nerve Disorders with MCC; 074 — Cranial and Peripheral Nerve Disorders without MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 5/5 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Current pass completed citation-resolvability, coding, taxonomy, schema, and consistency checks. The source workflow contains no completed adversarial high-stakes clinical verdict for this row; clinician revalidation remains required before protocol or order-set use.
data_quality_notes: Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk.

admission_criteria: Inpatient psychiatric admission (vs ED discharge or <24-48h behavioral-health observation) when danger cannot be managed in a less-restrictive setting: imminent risk to self (active suicidal ideation with plan/intent/access, recent attempt, C-SSRS high-risk) or others (homicidal ideation, severe aggression); grave functional decompensation/inability to maintain safety or ADLs; failed outpatient/crisis stabilization; need for 24h supervised stabilization, monitored medication initiation/titration, or diagnostic clarification not achievable outpatient; level-of-care tool (CASII/ECSII) indicating intensive/secure services. ED discharge/observation appropriate when C-SSRS low-risk, feasible safety plan with caregiver supervision + means restriction, and rapid (<=7d) crisis/outpatient follow-up secured. NOTE: a large share of this cluster are boarding admissions to scatter/med-surg beds awaiting a designated psychiatric bed — admit for safe supervised holding when no psych bed is available (rising pediatric BH boarding driver)
risk_stratification: C-SSRS (Columbia): "yes" on ideation items 3-5 or any item 6 (behavior) = high risk -> 1:1 continuous observation + ligature-resistant room + immediate psychiatry; ASQ (NIMH, age >=8): any "yes" = positive -> Brief Suicide Safety Assessment, acute positive -> emergency BSSA + 1:1; PHQ-9/PHQ-A: >=10 moderate, >=20 severe, item 9 >0 triggers suicide assessment; anxiety severity SCARED (>=25) or GAD-7 (>=10 moderate, >=15 severe) to track response; Broset Violence Checklist (BVC 0-6; >=2 = high imminent-aggression risk -> de-escalation/agitation protocol) or DASA; CASII/ECSII level-of-care score determines inpatient vs PHP/IOP/outpatient; HEADS-ED pediatric ED disposition tool (higher score -> admit)
initial_workup_labs: Targeted, history/exam-driven medical clearance (NOT reflexive pan-testing per ACEP/AAP): CBC, CMP (electrolytes/glucose/renal/hepatic), TSH; urine or serum toxicology to exclude substance-induced presentation; urine pregnancy in females of childbearing potential before certain meds/imaging; acetaminophen + salicylate + targeted drug levels if ingestion/overdose suspected. Baseline before antipsychotics/QT-prolonging agents: fasting glucose + lipids, weight/BMI, ECG for QTc, prolactin if symptomatic. Consider B12/folate, CK if restrained/agitated, ethanol level, infectious workup only if indicated. Abnormal tox/metabolic -> treat organic cause and reassess psychiatric disposition
initial_imaging_dx: Not routine — imaging does not aid clearance in a typical behavioral presentation and prolongs boarding. Neuroimaging (CT/MRI brain) ONLY for focal neurologic deficit, new seizures, delirium/altered mental status of unclear cause, head trauma, or first-episode psychosis with atypical features / organic disturbance workup (DRG 884). EEG if seizure or encephalopathy suspected. Baseline 12-lead ECG (QTc) when QT-prolonging psychotropics or overdose involved. Emphasis: medical clearance is clinical, not radiographic
time_critical_interventions: Universal suicide-risk screening at ED triage/admission per Joint Commission NPSG 15.01.01 — positive screen triggers safety precautions within minutes; ligature-resistant room, environmental means removal, and 1:1/continuous observation initiated immediately for high-risk. Acute agitation: verbal de-escalation FIRST (target minutes, reduce stimulation, engage caregiver); pharmacologic only if de-escalation fails, oral preferred, IM reserved for imminent danger; physical restraint time-limited with continuous monitoring per CMS/TJC. Reduce boarding: goal psychiatric evaluation within a few hours of medical clearance ("door-to-psych-eval"); target designated psychiatric bed placement or transfer within ~24h where feasible
initial_management: Core is non-pharmacologic: safety planning, structured therapeutic milieu, CBT/behavioral interventions (exposure-based for anxiety), caregiver involvement. Anxiety disorders: first-line SSRI (sertraline, fluoxetine, escitalopram) + CBT — combination superior (CAMS; AACAP 2020); SNRI (duloxetine, FDA-approved pediatric GAD; venlafaxine) as option; short-term hydroxyzine adjunct; AVOID/limit benzodiazepines in youth. Adjustment disorder: supportive/problem-solving psychotherapy primary, time-limited symptom-targeted meds only. ADHD/behavioral (DRG 886): stimulants (methylphenidate, amphetamine) first-line (AAP 2019); non-stimulants (atomoxetine, guanfacine XR, clonidine XR). Disruptive aggression / autism irritability: psychosocial first, then second-generation antipsychotic (risperidone, aripiprazole — FDA-approved autism irritability). Acute agitation (BETA consensus): etiology-driven — antihistamine (diphenhydramine, hydroxyzine) for young/mild, second-gen antipsychotic (risperidone PO, olanzapine PO/ODT/IM) or lorazepam by cause, haloperidol reserve; watch paradoxical disinhibition (benzos/diphenhydramine in young/developmental), avoid IM olanzapine + parenteral benzodiazepine combo, monitor EPS/QTc/oversedation. Continue home psychotropics; reconcile meds; treat any substance withdrawal
day1_milestones: Complete targeted medical clearance; comprehensive psychiatric evaluation + formal risk assessment (C-SSRS) with collateral from caregivers/school/outpatient providers; confirm diagnosis and level of care (CASII/ECSII); establish safety plan and appropriate observation level in a secured, ligature-resistant environment; initiate evidence-based treatment (start/adjust SSRI or stimulant; begin CBT/behavioral plan and milieu); engage family, obtain guardian consent + minor assent; if boarding, immediately start active bed-search/transfer and deliver therapeutic programming rather than passive holding
day2_milestones: Repeat safety/risk assessment (C-SSRS); monitor medication response and tolerability (SSRI activation/akathisia, sedation, GI, EPS, metabolic); advance psychotherapy (graded exposure for anxiety) and behavioral plan; conduct family/caregiver session and psychoeducation; hold multidisciplinary treatment-team meeting to refine diagnosis and set discharge criteria; address psychosocial drivers (home safety, means restriction, trauma, bullying, school, substance use); begin concrete discharge planning — identify outpatient prescriber + therapist, determine step-down level (PHP/IOP), verify follow-up availability
day3plus_milestones: Demonstrate sustained safety (no active SI/HI, C-SSRS low-risk, agitation controlled without frequent PRN/restraint); medication regimen stabilized and tolerated; symptom improvement (SCARED/GAD-7/PHQ-A trending down) and functional capacity to engage in outpatient care; complete safety plan with family and lethal-means-restriction counseling (firearm/medication lockup); confirm aftercare — outpatient appointment within 7 days (<=72h if high-risk) with warm handoff, PHP/IOP enrollment as indicated, wraparound/community services and school liaison; finish caregiver education; discharge when all criteria met
consults_multidisciplinary: Child & adolescent psychiatry (primary, Day 0-1); psychology/licensed therapists for CBT and behavioral plan (Day 1); social work + care management for psychosocial assessment, disposition, community resources and insurance authorization (Day 1); nursing for milieu/safety/observation; pharmacy for psychotropic dosing, interactions, QTc and metabolic monitoring; pediatric hospitalist for medical clearance and comorbid medical care when boarding on a medical floor; developmental-behavioral pediatrics/neurology for organic or developmental presentations (DRG 884/886, autism); child life, OT/PT (sensory/functional) and nutrition as indicated; family/peer support, school liaison and care coordination for wraparound at discharge
monitoring_level: Most patients managed on a behavioral-health unit or a safe medical/surgical floor at continuous or close observation — ICU is NOT indicated for the psychiatric condition itself. 1:1 continuous observation with ligature-resistant room and environmental safety sweep for high suicide/self-harm or elopement risk. Escalate to medical step-down/ICU ONLY for medical complications: serious overdose/ingestion, severe substance withdrawal, unstable vitals, serotonin syndrome/NMS, restraint-related injury, or refeeding instability. Telemetry/ECG monitoring when QT-prolonging agents or overdose involved; any restraint/seclusion requires time-limited orders plus continuous monitoring per CMS/TJC
nutrition_mobility_vte: Regular age-appropriate diet with intake monitoring (anxiety/behavioral food refusal; screen for ARFID/restrictive-eating overlap, nutrition consult if poor intake); structured therapeutic activity, physical activity and sleep-hygiene programming — patients are ambulatory, not bed-bound. VTE pharmacoprophylaxis generally NOT indicated in ambulatory youth (pediatric VTE risk low); reserve mechanical/pharmacologic prophylaxis for an immobilized/restrained adolescent with additional risk factors. Antipsychotic metabolic monitoring (weight/BMI, glucose, lipids) and bowel regimen for medication-related constipation. Avoid indwelling lines/catheters; remove any promptly
discharge_criteria: No imminent risk to self or others with C-SSRS low-risk; agitation/behavior controlled without frequent PRN medication or restraint; safety maintainable in a less-restrictive setting with a completed safety plan and engaged, educated caregivers; medication regimen stabilized and tolerated with no acute adverse effect needing inpatient monitoring; symptomatic and functional improvement sufficient to participate in outpatient care; aftercare secured (dated appointment at appropriate level of care) and lethal-means restriction addressed
discharge_planning: Disposition: home with outpatient care, PHP/IOP step-down, or transfer to residential/higher psychiatric level as indicated. Medication reconciliation with caregiver teach-back and counseling on SSRI/SNRI activation and the FDA suicidality black-box warning, stimulant and metabolic monitoring. Follow-up scheduled BEFORE discharge — outpatient prescriber + therapist within 7 days (<=72h for high-risk) with warm handoff, plus 988 crisis line and a printed safety plan. Lethal-means-restriction counseling (secure firearms, lock medications). Coordinate with school (504/IEP), PCP and community/wraparound services; transmit a transitions-of-care summary to receiving providers; complete caregiver psychoeducation
expected_los: Acute-care/IPPS scatter-bed CMS GMLOS is approximately 3.5-4.5 days across DRGs 882-887 (lower-acuity 882/887 ~3.4-3.6d; 883/884/886 ~4.2-4.5d). However, realized pediatric acute INPATIENT PSYCHIATRIC ALOS when in a designated bed is typically ~7-10 days (much of it paid under IPF-PPS per-diem, not MS-DRG), and ED/inpatient BOARDING adds a median of ~1-3+ days on top before a bed is secured
target_los: ~3-4 days for the acute stabilization episode (2-3 days for uncomplicated anxiety/adjustment) under an optimized pathway with same-day targeted medical clearance, psychiatric evaluation within hours, early treatment initiation, and boarding minimized to <24h to a designated bed or transfer
quality_metrics: Joint Commission NPSG 15.01.01 (suicide-risk screening/reduction) compliance; Joint Commission/CMS HBIPS core measures — HBIPS-1 admission screening, HBIPS-2/-3 hours of physical restraint/seclusion, HBIPS-5 discharge on multiple antipsychotics with justification, HBIPS-6/-7 continuing-care/aftercare plan transmitted to next provider; HEDIS FUH (7- and 30-day follow-up after hospitalization for mental illness) and FUM (follow-up after ED visit for mental illness); 30-day psychiatric readmission rate; ED behavioral-health boarding time (door-to-disposition, boarding hours) as key operational metric; restraint/seclusion rates and in-stay self-harm/safety-event rate; antipsychotic metabolic monitoring (HEDIS APM/SSD/APP) and suicide-screen completion rates
common_complications: In-stay self-harm/suicide attempt (mitigate: universal screening, 1:1, ligature-resistant environment, means removal); agitation/aggression and restraint/seclusion-related injury (mitigate: de-escalation-first, trained staff, PRN protocol, minimize and monitor restraint); medication adverse effects — SSRI/SNRI activation and rare increased suicidality/black-box (monitor closely), antipsychotic EPS/dystonia/akathisia/sedation/metabolic/QTc (monitor), paradoxical disinhibition from benzodiazepines or diphenhydramine in young/developmental patients; elopement (mitigate: observation, secured unit); boarding-associated deterioration, deconditioning and deprivation of therapeutic care (mitigate: therapeutic programming during boarding); missed organic/medical cause (mitigate: appropriate targeted clearance, especially DRG 884 organic)
readmission_drivers: Absent or delayed outpatient follow-up (largest lever: schedule <=7-day appointment with warm handoff — HEDIS FUH); inadequate lethal-means restriction or unsafe home environment; medication non-adherence or unmanaged adverse effects; premature discharge before true stabilization; unaddressed psychosocial drivers (family conflict, abuse/neglect, bullying, school stress, substance use); lack of PHP/IOP step-down capacity and social-determinant barriers. Prevention: rigorous discharge planning, caregiver engagement/education, completed safety plan, rapid follow-up, community/wraparound linkage, and a written crisis plan (988)
guideline_source: AACAP Clinical Practice Guideline for the Assessment and Treatment of Children and Adolescents With Anxiety Disorders (2020); USPSTF Screening for Anxiety in Children and Adolescents (2022, B recommendation); AAP Clinical Practice Guideline for Diagnosis, Evaluation, and Treatment of ADHD in Children and Adolescents (2019); AAP clinical/technical report The Management of Children and Youth With Pediatric Mental and Behavioral Health Emergencies (2023); AAEP/AACAP pediatric BETA agitation consensus (2019); Joint Commission NPSG 15.01.01 Suicide Risk Reduction; AAP-AACAP-CHA Declaration of a National Emergency in Child and Adolescent Mental Health (2021)

Screening for Anxiety in Children and Adolescents. JAMA 2022;328(14):1438-1444 (PMID 36219403; doi.org/10.1001/jama.2022.16936); Wolraich ML et al. AAP ADHD CPG. Pediatrics 2019;144(4):e20192528; Gerson R et al. Pediatric BETA agitation consensus. West J Emerg Med 2019;20(2):409-418 (PMID 30881565; doi.org/10.5811/westjem.2019.1.41344); AAP clinical report, Pediatric Mental and Behavioral Health Emergencies. Pediatrics 2023;152(3):e2023063256; Bitterman M et al. ED Behavioral Health Observation and Stabilization. Child Adolesc Psychiatr Clin N Am 2026 (PMID 42297547; doi.org/10.1016/j.chc.2026.03.011); Pediatric Mental Health Boarding 2017-2023. Pediatrics 2025;155(3):e2024068283. (Citations retrieved and verified via PubMed)
evidence_grade: Mixed. STRONG (Class I society guideline + RCT) for anxiety treatment — combination CBT+SSRI (AACAP 2020, CAMS RCT, USPSTF-B) — and for ADHD pharmacotherapy (AAP 2019). MODERATE-to-CONSENSUS for the acute inpatient crisis, agitation, and boarding-management pathway (BETA expert consensus, AAP clinical reports; limited pediatric RCT data on acute agitation and disposition). WEAK/consensus for adjustment-disorder pharmacology (psychotherapy-primary). Overall MODERATE: guideline-anchored core with expert-consensus operational scaffolding for the inpatient/boarding elements
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Unlike most medical MDCs, these MDC-19 behavioral DRGs (882 neuroses except depressive, 883 personality/impulse-control, 884 organic disturbances & intellectual disability, 886 behavioral & developmental, 887 other mental disorder) are largely SINGLE-TIER without a CC/MCC split — coded comorbidity shifts payment less than in medical DRGs. Real severity is driven by risk level (active SI/attempt, severe aggression, catatonia), age, developmental/organic complexity, and comorbid substance use rather than CC/MCC capture. DRG 884 (organic/intellectual disability) and complex 886 cases carry longer LOS, more consults, and higher escalation potential. High-acuity cases (attempt, severe agitation, withdrawal, ingestion) drive longer boarding, 1:1 staffing, and possible transfer to a higher psychiatric level or a medical ICU. A serious ingestion, malnutrition, or withdrawal can reclassify the stay to a medical DRG with CC/MCC and materially raise LOS and cost
pathway_pearls: 1) Screen EVERY behavioral presentation for suicide (ASQ/C-SSRS) and act immediately per NPSG 15.01.01 — 1:1 plus ligature-resistant environment is the single highest-yield safety lever. 2) Medical clearance is history/exam-driven, not reflexive pan-labs/imaging — over-testing adds boarding hours without benefit. 3) De-escalate first and medicate by etiology (BETA); avoid physical restraint and benzodiazepine/antihistamine disinhibition in young or developmentally disabled children. 4) Don't just board — deliver evidence-based treatment during the stay: combination CBT + SSRI for anxiety (CAMS/AACAP), start behavioral/stimulant therapy for ADHD. 5) Discharge success is a disposition problem: guarantee a <=7-day follow-up (FUH), lethal-means restriction, caregiver education, and PHP/IOP step-down — these are the top readmission-prevention levers

admission_criteria: Admit/transfer to a verified burn center per ABA referral criteria: partial-thickness burn >10% TBSA; any full-thickness (3rd-degree) burn; burns to face, hands, feet, genitalia, perineum or major joints; electrical injury (incl. high-voltage >=1000V and lightning); chemical burns; inhalation injury; burn with significant preexisting comorbidity; burn with concomitant trauma; pediatric burns at non-pediatric-capable hospitals; poorly controlled pain or special rehab/psychosocial needs. Formal IV resuscitation + ICU/burn-ICU admission for >=20% TBSA adults (>=10-15% in children/elderly). ED discharge/outpatient management reserved for small (<10% TBSA), superficial/superficial-partial, non-critical-area burns with reliable follow-up and adequate oral pain control.
risk_stratification: Quantify %TBSA by Lund-Browder chart (most accurate; Rule of Nines or palmar method [patient palm+digits ~=1%] for field estimate) - drives fluid volume and disposition; Revised Baux score = age + %TBSA + 17(if inhalation injury): ~110-140 approaches LD50 and >140 often non-survivable, informing goals-of-care; Abbreviated Burn Severity Index (ABSI from age/sex/inhalation/full-thickness/%TBSA): 6-7 ~80-90% survival, 8-9 50-70%, 10-11 20-40%, >=12 <10%; carboxyhemoglobin level (>10% = significant CO exposure) and lactate (>10 mmol/L suggests cyanide toxicity); ABA burn-sepsis criteria (temp >39/<36.5C, HR >110, RR >25, platelets <100k after day 3, glucose >200 untreated, enteral-feeding intolerance) trigger infection workup and culture-directed antibiotics.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, CMP (electrolytes, glucose, BUN/creatinine), lactate, ABG with co-oximetry (carboxyhemoglobin, methemoglobin), coagulation panel + fibrinogen, type & screen/crossmatch, creatine kinase and urine myoglobin (electrical burns/rhabdomyolysis), urinalysis, beta-hCG, baseline albumin/prealbumin; carboxyhemoglobin >10% -> 100% O2; lactate >10 mmol/L or unexplained shock -> empiric hydroxocobalamin for cyanide; rising CK/myoglobinuria -> increase fluids and target UOP 1-1.5 mL/kg/hr; 12-lead ECG + troponin for electrical/high-voltage injury.
initial_imaging_dx: Fiberoptic bronchoscopy within 24h to diagnose/grade inhalation injury (Abbreviated Injury Score 0-4) in suspected smoke exposure/closed-space fire/facial burns; CXR (often normal early, baseline for ARDS); 12-lead ECG + continuous cardiac monitoring for electrical injury; CT/FAST per ATLS if concomitant trauma; ophthalmology exam with fluorescein staining for facial/ocular chemical burns; serial bladder (intra-abdominal) pressure monitoring during large-volume resuscitation.
time_critical_interventions: ABLS/ATLS primary survey - airway first: early intubation for stridor, deep facial/oropharyngeal burns or progressive edema BEFORE resuscitation-driven swelling closes the airway; stop the burning process, remove clothing/jewelry, brush off then copiously irrigate chemicals (>=20 min); begin fluid resuscitation timed from the moment of injury (not from ED arrival); escharotomy within hours for circumferential full-thickness burns causing distal limb ischemia or chest-wall restriction impairing ventilation; CO poisoning: 100% O2 (cuts COHb half-life from ~4h to ~1h); cyanide: hydroxocobalamin 5g IV over 15 min (repeat x1); tetanus prophylaxis; active warming to prevent hypothermia/coagulopathy.
initial_management: Isotonic crystalloid (Lactated Ringer's) per ABA 2023: start 2 mL/kg/%TBSA over first 24h (half in first 8h from injury; 4 mL/kg/%TBSA for electrical), titrate rate +/-1/3 each hour to urine output 0.5 mL/kg/hr adults (30-50 mL/hr) / 1 mL/kg/hr children; 5% albumin colloid rescue for large burns or escalating volumes (assess at 8 & 12h; do not start <8h from injury) to reduce fluid creep; multimodal analgesia - IV opioids (fentanyl/hydromorphone/morphine) + acetaminophen +/- ketamine/dexmedetomidine + anxiolytic; topical antimicrobials (silver sulfadiazine, silver-impregnated dressings, mafenide acetate for ears/nose/cartilage) with NO prophylactic systemic antibiotics; stress-ulcer prophylaxis (PPI/H2, Curling ulcer); restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL (intraoperative replacement for excisional blood loss); wound cleansing/debridement and plan for early operative excision.
day1_milestones: POD0: complete/refine primary & secondary survey and secure airway; achieve and hold UOP target while weaning fluids as capillary leak resolves; recalculate %TBSA and adjust plan; escharotomy if compartment/ventilatory compromise; bronchoscopy done and inhalation-injury nebulized regimen started (heparin/N-acetylcysteine/albuterol) if intubated; initiate early enteral nutrition within 24h (start 0.5-1 mL/kg/hr, advance); establish analgesia/sedation and VTE prophylaxis; foley + bladder-pressure monitoring; place burn surgery, critical care, PT/OT and dietitian consults; wound photography and tetanus; vigilance for over-resuscitation/abdominal compartment syndrome.
day2_milestones: POD1: transition from resuscitation to maintenance fluids; confirm resolution of burn shock (lactate cleared, stable UOP, resolving base deficit); first operative excision + grafting of full-thickness wounds (early excision at 24-72h); advance enteral feeds to goal by indirect calorimetry or Curreri formula (protein 1.5-2 g/kg/day adults, up to 2.5-3 in children); daily wound care with topical antimicrobials; PT/OT range-of-motion + anti-contracture splinting; glucose control 140-180 mg/dL; reassess for compartment syndrome, evolving inhalation injury/ARDS, and rhabdomyolysis.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+: serial staged excision and autografting (split-thickness skin graft; temporary allograft/xenograft/dermal substitute coverage for extensive burns until donor sites re-harvestable); hypermetabolic modulation - propranolol (titrate to ~15-20% heart-rate reduction) and oxandrolone 10 mg BID for large burns; daily surveillance for burn-wound infection/invasive sepsis by ABA criteria with culture-directed antibiotics only; continue high-protein nutrition, glucose control, aggressive PT/OT, splinting and early scar management; wean ventilator and extubate; de-escalate ICU -> step-down -> floor; delirium screening, pain/pruritus and psychological support; progress toward wound closure and rehabilitation for discharge readiness.
consults_multidisciplinary: Burn/trauma surgery (primary) and critical-care intensivist at admission; plastic/reconstructive surgery for coverage/reconstruction; PT and OT day 1 (ROM, splinting, contracture prevention, functional rehab); registered dietitian day 1 (nutrition prescription/indirect calorimetry); pharmacy (analgesia, antimicrobial stewardship, VTE dosing); respiratory therapy (inhalation-injury nebulizers, ventilator/airway clearance); social work + case management early (disposition, resources, non-accidental-injury/CPS or elder-abuse screen); psychiatry/psychology (acute stress, PTSD, depression, pain coping); ophthalmology (facial/ocular burns), infectious disease (complex/resistant infection); palliative care/goals-of-care for non-survivable injuries.
monitoring_level: Burn ICU/ICU for >=20% TBSA requiring formal resuscitation, inhalation injury or intubation, high-voltage electrical injury (>=24h arrhythmia telemetry), hemodynamic instability, or significant comorbidity; step-down/telemetry for moderate burns post-resuscitation and electrical injuries needing rhythm monitoring; floor for small stable burns with controlled pain. Escalation triggers: escalating fluid requirement with failing UOP, bladder (intra-abdominal) pressure >20 mmHg or abdominal compartment syndrome physiology, rising peak airway pressures/hypoxemia, new arrhythmia, or burn-sepsis physiology.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Early enteral nutrition <24-48h (gastric, advance to goal); protein 1.5-2 g/kg/day (up to 2.5-3 in children); calories by indirect calorimetry (gold standard) or Curreri [(25xkg)+(40x%TBSA)], avoiding overfeeding; supplement vitamin C, D, zinc, copper, selenium; AVOID high-dose glutamine (mortality signal in critically ill); glucose target 140-180 mg/dL with insulin (avoid tight control/hypoglycemia); early mobilization and PT/OT from day 1 with anti-contracture positioning/splinting; VTE prophylaxis with LMWH (enoxaparin; consider anti-Xa-guided/weight-based dosing in large burns) plus mechanical devices; remove central lines and foley as soon as feasible given high CLABSI/CAUTI risk through burned tissue; stress-ulcer prophylaxis until enteral feeding established.
discharge_criteria: Wounds healed or grafts adherent/stable with an outpatient-manageable wound-care plan (donor sites epithelializing); afebrile with no active/invasive infection, hemodynamically stable off vasopressors and ventilator; pain and pruritus controlled on an oral regimen; meeting nutritional needs by mouth or via enteral access; functional status adequate for disposition (independent ADLs or a defined rehab plan with acceptable range of motion); caregiver trained in wound care and DME/home health arranged.
discharge_planning: Disposition matched to severity - home with home-health wound care for smaller burns, acute inpatient rehabilitation or LTAC for extensive burns/contractures/prolonged ventilation; medication reconciliation (opioid taper + multimodal analgesia, antipruritics [antihistamine/gabapentin], topical wound agents, VTE-prophylaxis duration); schedule burn-clinic follow-up within 1-2 weeks, outpatient PT/OT, and staged reconstructive/scar-management planning (pressure garments, silicone, laser); patient/caregiver education on wound care, infection warning signs, sun protection, scar and itch management, and mental-health screening; warm handoff with transition-of-care summary.
expected_los: Highly TBSA-dependent; validated benchmark of ~1 day of LOS per 1% TBSA burned (National Burn Repository; pediatric median ~0.9 day/%TBSA). Across the collapsed DRG group: non-extensive burns (DRG 935) ~2-4 days; full-thickness/extensive without skin graft (933/934) ~7-14 days; full-thickness with graft or inhalation injury (928/929) ~14-21 days; extensive/full-thickness burns with MV >96h + skin graft (DRG 927) among the longest-stay DRGs at ~30-45 days.
target_los: Optimized target at or below the 1 day per %TBSA benchmark (~0.75-1.0 day/%TBSA) via early excision/grafting, protocolized resuscitation that avoids fluid creep, and early rehabilitation; e.g., small non-extensive burns <=2-3 days, moderate full-thickness grafted burns ~10-14 days, and extensive burns sized to TBSA while beating National Burn Repository risk-adjusted benchmarks.
quality_metrics: ABA National Burn Repository / Burn Care Quality Platform (BCQP) risk-adjusted benchmarking and ABA/ACS burn-center verification; time-to-first-excision, LOS per %TBSA, graft take/reoperation rate; observed-vs-predicted mortality (revised Baux/ABSI risk adjustment); ICU and ventilator days, VAP, CLABSI, CAUTI and VTE rates; burn-wound-infection/sepsis rate; early-enteral-nutrition-within-24-48h rate and glucose control; 30-day unplanned readmission; pain-management and functional/rehabilitation outcomes; perioperative normothermia and VTE-prophylaxis compliance.
common_complications: Over-resuscitation/'fluid creep' -> abdominal, extremity or orbital compartment syndrome (mitigate: 2 mL/kg start, hourly titration, albumin rescue, bladder-pressure monitoring, timely escharotomy); inhalation injury/ARDS/pneumonia (lung-protective ventilation, bronchoscopy, nebulized heparin/NAC, VAP bundle); burn-wound infection/invasive sepsis (topical antimicrobials, early excision, surveillance, no prophylactic systemic antibiotics); acute kidney injury from hypoperfusion or rhabdomyolysis (resuscitation, myoglobin clearance); hypothermia/coagulopathy (active warming); hypermetabolism/muscle wasting (early EN, propranolol, oxandrolone, early excision); Curling stress ulcer/GI bleed (prophylaxis + early feeding); VTE (LMWH); contractures/hypertrophic scarring (OT splinting, ROM, pressure garments); delirium, pain and PTSD/depression.
readmission_drivers: Graft loss/wound breakdown and delayed healing; wound infection/cellulitis; inadequate or unaffordable outpatient wound care with limited caregiver capacity; uncontrolled pain and pruritus; contractures requiring reconstruction; malnutrition; psychosocial instability/substance use. Prevention levers: structured caregiver wound-care training before discharge, home-health/telehealth wound follow-up, burn-clinic visit within 1-2 weeks, aggressive OT/PT and scar management, nutrition and mental-health support, and explicit infection-warning-sign education.
guideline_source: American Burn Association (ABA) - Clinical Practice Guidelines on Burn Shock Resuscitation (2023/2024) and ABA Burn Center Referral Criteria (2023); ABA Advanced Burn Life Support (ABLS); International Society for Burn Injuries (ISBI) Practice Guidelines for Burn Care (2016) and Part 2 (2018); ESPEN endorsed recommendations - Nutritional therapy in major burns (2013).
key_citations: Cartotto R et al. American Burn Association Clinical Practice Guidelines on Burn Shock Resuscitation. J Burn Care Res 2024;45(3):565-589 (PMID 38051821, doi:10.1093/jbcr/irad125) - recommends 2 mL/kg/%TBSA LR start, UOP 30-50 mL/hr, albumin rescue; ISBI Practice Guidelines for Burn Care, Burns 2016;42(5):953-1021 (PMID 27542292) and Part 2, Burns 2018;44(7):1617-1706 (PMID 30343831); Ong YS et al. Meta-analysis of early excision of burns, Burns 2006;32(2):145-150 - mortality RR 0.36 (95% CI 0.20-0.65) in patients without inhalation injury and shorter LOS (PMID 16414197); Jeschke MG/Herndon DN et al. Propranolol in severely burned children, J Trauma 2007;62:676-681 (PMID 17414346); Johnson LS et al. Hospital LOS - does 1% TBSA equal 1 day?, J Burn Care Res 2011;32(1):13-19 (PMID 21131842); Patterson KN et al. LOS per TBSA validation, J Burn Care Res 2022;43(6):1227-1232 (PMID 35986486). Source: PubMed.
evidence_grade: Moderate overall - anchored by a formal GRADE-based Class I society CPG (ABA Burn Shock Resuscitation 2023), though many of its recommendations are weak/conditional; core practices (early excision/grafting, topical antimicrobials, hypermetabolic modulation, nutrition targets) rest on small RCTs, cohort data and expert consensus (ISBI 2016/2018, ABLS). The early-excision mortality benefit and resuscitation targets are moderate-quality, while hypermetabolic-agent and nutrition specifics are lower-quality/extrapolated.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: This collapsed MDC-22 group spans an extreme severity gradient. DRG 935 (non-extensive) - often floor/short stay with minimal ICU need. DRG 934 (full-thickness without graft or inhalation) and 933 (extensive/MV >96h WITHOUT graft, frequently non-survivors, comfort-care or non-operative) - intermediate-to-high acuity. DRG 928/929 (full-thickness WITH graft or inhalation injury, split by CC/MCC) - ICU + OR, where CC/MCC (inhalation injury, ARDS, sepsis, AKI, respiratory failure) markedly raises resource use. DRG 927 (extensive/full-thickness with MV >96h + skin graft) - highest acuity, prolonged ventilation, multiple staged operations, longest LOS/highest cost/mortality. Larger %TBSA and inhalation injury (each adds +17 on the revised Baux) escalate ICU need, ventilator days, number of operations, complication rate, LOS and mortality.
pathway_pearls: (1) Resuscitate from the time of injury with Lactated Ringer's at 2 mL/kg/%TBSA and titrate hourly to UOP 0.5 mL/kg/hr - avoid 'fluid creep' and deploy 5% albumin rescue early in large burns to prevent abdominal/extremity/orbital compartment syndrome. (2) Airway first - anticipate and intubate early for inhalation injury/facial edema before resuscitation swelling closes the airway; bronchoscopy <24h; treat CO with 100% O2 and cyanide with hydroxocobalamin. (3) Early excision and grafting (24-72h) of full-thickness wounds cuts mortality (RR ~0.36 without inhalation injury) and LOS - get to the OR fast and stage coverage. (4) Drive the hypermetabolic bundle - early enteral nutrition <24h, high protein, glucose 140-180, propranolol + oxandrolone, warm room - and use topical (not prophylactic systemic) antimicrobials with ABA-criteria sepsis surveillance.
