admission_criteria: CT- or exam-confirmed peritonitis, hollow-viscus perforation, or intra-abdominal abscess PLUS systemic signs (SIRS >=2, qSOFA >=2: SBP <=100/RR >=22/altered mentation, temp >38 or <36, HR >90, WBC >12k or <4k, lactate >2 mmol/L); need for IV antibiotics + source control (percutaneous drainage or surgery); diffuse peritoneal signs, hemodynamic instability, sepsis/septic shock (ICU), intractable pain/vomiting or failure of PO; immunocompromise/advanced age/major comorbidity lowers threshold. ED-discharge/observation only for well-appearing, hemodynamically stable, localized process with a small (<3-4 cm) phlegmon and NO organ dysfunction, drainable collection, or diffuse peritonitis; septic shock or free perforation is always inpatient.
risk_stratification: APACHE II = IDSA-2024 preferred severity score within 24h of admission (>=10-15 = high-risk -> broad empiric coverage, ICU/aggressive source control); WSES Sepsis Severity Score (range 0-18) acceptable alternative, >5.5 predicts high mortality (WISS study); qSOFA >=2 or SOFA rise >=2 defines sepsis and triggers bundle/escalation; lactate >2 (and >4) flags hypoperfusion/shock; Mannheim Peritonitis Index >26 = high-mortality peritonitis; SIS/WSES risk class (community-acquired low-risk vs high-risk/healthcare-associated: healthcare exposure, prior antibiotics, immunosuppression, delayed source control, known MDRO) drives empiric regimen breadth and Candida coverage.
initial_workup_labs: CBC w/ diff (leukocytosis/bandemia/leukopenia), CMP (renal/hepatic/electrolytes), lactate (repeat q2-4h if >2 for clearance), 2 sets blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics (IDSA-2024: if fever + hypotension/tachypnea/delirium OR MDRO concern), lipase (r/o pancreatitis), INR/coags, type & screen, CRP and procalcitonin to trend, VBG, UA, hCG in women of childbearing age; intra-abdominal/source fluid cultures at time of drainage/surgery (IDSA-2024 moderate certainty) to tailor therapy. Triggers: lactate >2 -> sepsis bundle; rising creatinine -> AKI precautions/contrast caution; culture data -> de-escalation.
initial_imaging_dx: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast = first-line modality (IDSA-2024), obtain promptly (within a few hours; <6h if peritonitis/perforation/sepsis suspected) to localize abscess, perforation, and source; upright CXR/abdominal series for free air if perforation suspected; ultrasound for suspected biliary source or in pregnancy and to guide drainage; MRI if pregnant with inconclusive US. Source-control imaging: IR-guided percutaneous drainage for accessible collections >=3-4 cm; repeat CT at 48-72h ONLY if failing to improve (assess undrained/recurrent collection).
time_critical_interventions: Runs as a sepsis case (SEP-1 / Surviving Sepsis 2021 hour-1 bundle): measure lactate (repeat if >2), blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1h of septic-shock recognition (and ASAP/<=3h for sepsis w/o shock), 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid within 3h for hypotension or lactate >=4, norepinephrine for MAP >=65 if fluid-refractory. SOURCE CONTROL is time-critical: accomplish as soon as feasible and within 24h of diagnosis (SIS 2017/SSC 2021); emergent (<6h) for diffuse peritonitis, free perforation, or uncontrolled septic shock.
initial_management: Risk-tiered empiric antibiotics (SIS/WSES 2017): community-acquired LOW-risk = ceftriaxone + metronidazole, ertapenem, or moxifloxacin (amox/clav alt); HIGH-risk/healthcare-associated = piperacillin-tazobactam 4.5 g q6-8h, cefepime + metronidazole, or a carbapenem (meropenem/imipenem) if ESBL risk; add MRSA (vancomycin/linezolid) and enterococcal coverage and an empiric echinocandin (e.g., micafungin) for Candida risk (upper-GI perforation, healthcare-associated, immunocompromised, recurrent). Balanced crystalloid resuscitation (LR/Plasma-Lyte) 30 mL/kg for hypoperfusion; norepinephrine first-line (MAP >=65), add vasopressin; RESTRICTIVE transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL (SSC 2021). SOURCE CONTROL: percutaneous image-guided drainage first-line for accessible abscess >=3-4 cm; laparotomy/laparoscopy for diffuse peritonitis, perforation, ischemia, or failed/inaccessible drainage (surgical subset -> Perioperative pathway). NPO, NG if obstruction/ileus, analgesia/antiemetics, glucose 140-180, VTE + stress-ulcer prophylaxis.
day1_milestones: Complete hour-1 sepsis bundle; achieve or schedule source control within 24h (IR drain placed or OR booked); CT reviewed and source identified; risk-tier-appropriate empiric antibiotics started with pharmacy/stewardship review; source and blood cultures sent; hemodynamic stabilization with documented lactate clearance and urine output >=0.5 mL/kg/h; assign monitoring level (ICU vs step-down vs floor); initiate VTE prophylaxis, glucose control, and nutrition/mobility orders.
day2_milestones: Reassess response (fever curve, WBC trend, hemodynamics); wean vasopressors; DE-ESCALATE/narrow antibiotics per culture and susceptibility results (stewardship); confirm drain output/position and function; begin diet advancement as ileus resolves and early mobilization; remove unnecessary lines/Foley/NG; transition IV->PO analgesia; repeat imaging only if not improving.
day3plus_milestones: With ADEQUATE source control, set a fixed ~4-day antibiotic course (STOP-IT) dated from the source-control procedure; stop when afebrile, WBC normalizing, and tolerating diet. If not improving by 48-72h: repeat CT for undrained/recurrent collection, arrange repeat drainage or operative re-exploration, and reconsider resistant organisms/Candida rather than blind broadening. Remove drain when output <10-20 mL/day with imaging resolution; step down to oral antibiotics/OPAT; confirm discharge criteria and arrange outpatient drain and follow-up.
consults_multidisciplinary: Interventional Radiology (percutaneous drainage) day 0-1; Acute Care/General Surgery for source-control decision in all peritonitis/perforation cases; Infectious Diseases/Antimicrobial Stewardship for high-risk, MDRO, Candida, or treatment failure; Critical Care for septic shock/organ dysfunction; clinical pharmacy (renal dosing, de-escalation, OPAT); Nutrition (early enteral nutrition, malnutrition screen); Care Management/Social Work (disposition, home drain/OPAT setup); PT/OT for deconditioning; GI/Gyn/Urology per source.
monitoring_level: ICU for septic shock, vasopressor need, lactate >4, respiratory failure, high APACHE II, or post-op instability; step-down/telemetry for resolving shock, high-risk comorbidity, or close drain/hemodynamic watch; floor for hemodynamically stable, source-controlled patients without organ dysfunction. Escalation triggers: rising lactate, MAP <65 or new pressor requirement, worsening organ function, respiratory decompensation, or persistent fever/leukocytosis >72h (signals undrained source).
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: NPO initially if peritonitis/ileus or pending surgery, advance as bowel function returns; early enteral nutrition preferred once tolerating, nutrition consult if malnourished. Mobility: early ambulation from day 1 to reduce ileus, VTE, and deconditioning. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin/LMWH or UFH) unless active bleeding, with mechanical adjunct. Glucose target 140-180 mg/dL (SSC 2021). Lines/catheters: remove central lines, Foley, and NG as soon as unneeded to prevent CLABSI/CAUTI; convert IV->PO antibiotics early.
discharge_criteria: Afebrile >=24h with normalizing WBC; hemodynamically stable off vasopressors; adequate source control confirmed (drain functioning or removed, no undrained collection); tolerating oral intake and oral (or arranged OPAT) antibiotics; pain controlled on oral meds; return of bowel function; no ongoing organ dysfunction.
discharge_planning: Disposition home (often with drain in situ + IR follow-up, or OPAT for IV antibiotics), SNF if deconditioned; medication reconciliation with defined antibiotic stop date (~4 days post-source-control if adequate, PO step-down); follow-up surgery/IR in 1-2 weeks (drain check/imaging), PCP; interval colonoscopy 6-8 weeks after diverticular abscess to exclude malignancy; patient education on drain care and red flags (fever, worsening pain, increased/changed drainage); warm handoff to OPAT/home health with scheduled monitoring labs.
expected_los: CMS FY2024 IPPS GMLOS benchmarks (MS-DRG 371/372/373): DRG 371 (w/ MCC) ~6.5-7 days; DRG 372 (w/ CC) ~4.9-5 days; DRG 373 (w/o CC/MCC) ~3.5 days; blended typical ~5-6 days.
target_los: ~3-4 days for an uncomplicated drainable abscess with source control <=24h + fixed 4-day antibiotics (STOP-IT), early diet and mobilization; ~5-6 days when MCC/septic shock present; target trims roughly 1 day off GMLOS via early drainage, stewardship-driven short-course therapy, and prompt IV->PO/drain transition.
quality_metrics: CMS SEP-1 severe sepsis/septic shock bundle compliance (high relevance); time-to-source-control (<24h) and time-to-antibiotics; antimicrobial stewardship metrics (de-escalation, fixed ~4-day duration per STOP-IT, days-of-therapy); blood cultures before antibiotics; 30-day readmission and 30-day mortality; surgical-site infection rate; C. difficile rate; CLABSI/CAUTI; appropriateness of risk-tiered empiric antibiotic selection.
common_complications: Persistent/recurrent or undrained abscess (mitigate: adequate/repeat drainage, reimage at 48-72h if not improving); progression to septic shock and AKI/multi-organ dysfunction (early bundle + source control); C. difficile colitis (short-course stewardship); enterocutaneous fistula or anastomotic leak (surgical subset); ileus, malnutrition, VTE; healthcare-associated infections (CLABSI/CAUTI) and Candida/MDRO superinfection (line removal, targeted coverage).
readmission_drivers: Inadequate source control / recurrent abscess with residual septations (lever: complete drainage, IR follow-up and reimaging); premature drain removal or premature antibiotic cessation (lever: imaging-confirmed resolution, defined stop dates); underlying disease not addressed such as unresected diverticular disease or missed malignancy (lever: interval colonoscopy, elective resection planning); recurrent sepsis, C. difficile, or dehydration/failure to thrive (levers: OPAT monitoring, clear red-flag education, early 7-14 day follow-up).
guideline_source: Surgical Infection Society (SIS) Revised Guidelines on the Management of Intra-Abdominal Infection, 2017; World Society of Emergency Surgery (WSES) Guidelines for Management of Intra-Abdominal Infections, 2017; IDSA 2024 Clinical Practice Guideline Update on Complicated Intra-Abdominal Infections (risk assessment, diagnostic imaging, microbiological evaluation); Surviving Sepsis Campaign (SCCM/ESICM) 2021 + CMS SEP-1 for the sepsis overlay; foundational IDSA/SIS 2010 (Solomkin).
key_citations: Mazuski JE, et al. The Surgical Infection Society Revised Guidelines on the Management of Intra-Abdominal Infection. Surg Infect. 2017;18(1):1-76. PMID 28085573, doi.org/10.1089/sur.2016.261; Sartelli M, et al. 2017 WSES guidelines for management of intra-abdominal infections. World J Emerg Surg. 2017;12:29. PMID 28702076, doi.org/10.1186/s13017-017-0141-6; IDSA 2024 cIAI Guideline Update. Clin Infect Dis. 2024;79(Suppl 3):S81-S130; Sawyer RG, et al. Trial of Short-Course Antimicrobial Therapy for Intraabdominal Infection (STOP-IT). N Engl J Med. 2015;372(21):1996-2005. PMID 25992746, doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa1411162; Evans L, et al. Surviving Sepsis Campaign: International Guidelines 2021. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143. PMID 34605781; Solomkin JS, et al. IDSA/SIS 2010. Clin Infect Dis. 2010;50(2):133-64. PMID 20034345, doi.org/10.1086/649554. (Citations retrieved via PubMed.)
evidence_grade: Strong overall - anchored by multiple current Class I society guidelines (SIS 2017, WSES 2017, IDSA 2024) plus RCT-level evidence (STOP-IT NEJM RCT for fixed short-course duration; Surviving Sepsis 2021 for the sepsis bundle). Core levers (early source control <=24h, risk-tiered empiric antibiotics, fixed ~4-day course after adequate source control, hour-1 sepsis bundle) are well supported; several IDSA-2024 specifics (APACHE II severity scoring, imaging choices) are GRADE conditional/low-to-moderate certainty.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 373 (no CC/MCC): localized abscess, single percutaneous drain, no organ dysfunction -> floor care, LOS ~3.5 days, ~4-day antibiotics. DRG 372 (CC): comorbidity or moderate complication (e.g., AKI, moderate sepsis) -> possible step-down, LOS ~5 days. DRG 371 (MCC): septic shock, respiratory failure, multi-organ dysfunction, or reoperation -> ICU stay, LOS ~6.5-7+ days, broader/longer antibiotics and higher mortality. Typical MCC drivers: severe sepsis/septic shock, AKI requiring dialysis, acute respiratory failure; severity escalates ICU need, source-control complexity, and empiric antifungal/MDRO coverage.
pathway_pearls: 1) Source control IS the pathway - achieve it within 24h (emergent <6h for diffuse peritonitis/uncontrolled shock); percutaneous drainage is first-line for accessible abscess >=3-4 cm. 2) After ADEQUATE source control, a fixed ~4-day antibiotic course equals longer therapy (STOP-IT) - stewardship + de-escalation cut C. diff and LOS. 3) Treat every case as sepsis: run SEP-1/hour-1 bundle, cultures before antibiotics, and add Candida/MRSA/enterococcus coverage only for high-risk/healthcare-associated patients. 4) If not improving by 48-72h, REIMAGE for an undrained/recurrent collection rather than blindly broadening antibiotics - undrained source is the number-one failure mode.
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Major GI disorders/peritonitis; drainage/surgery subset -> Perioperative. Frequent sepsis source.

admission_criteria: Admit HIV patient with suspected/confirmed AIDS-defining opportunistic infection (OI) when: hypoxemia (SpO2 <92% room air or PaO2 <70 mmHg) or new supplemental-O2 requirement (PCP); suspected CNS OI with altered mental status, focal deficit, seizure, or meningismus (cryptococcal/toxoplasmosis/TB meningitis); hemodynamic instability/sepsis (qSOFA >=2); inability to tolerate PO or maintain hydration (esophageal candidiasis, HIV wasting); new/known CD4 <200 with systemic OI requiring IV therapy; need for invasive diagnostics (BAL, LP with ICP management, brain biopsy). ED discharge/observation only for mild, stable, PO-tolerant OI (e.g., mild PCP with A-a gradient <35, oral thrush) WITH reliable outpatient HIV follow-up.
risk_stratification: CD4 count stages OI risk/prophylaxis: <200 = PCP/AIDS, <100 = toxoplasmosis + cryptococcosis, <50 = CMV + disseminated MAC. PCP severity by ABG A-a gradient: mild <35 mmHg (PaO2 >=70) = oral therapy; moderate-severe A-a >=35 or PaO2 <70 -> adjunctive corticosteroids + IV therapy + admission. Cryptococcal meningitis: CSF opening pressure >25 cm H2O = elevated ICP -> serial therapeutic LP; baseline CrAg titer + CSF quantitative culture prognosticate. Sepsis: qSOFA >=2 / SOFA for ICU triage. LDH and serum (1,3)-beta-D-glucan raise PCP probability. VACS Index for overall mortality risk.
initial_workup_labs: CD4 count/percentage + quantitative HIV-1 RNA viral load (staging); HIV genotype/resistance + HLA-B*5701 (pre-ART); CBC with differential; CMP (renal for TMP-SMX/amphotericin dosing, LFTs); LDH (elevated in PCP); serum (1,3)-beta-D-glucan (sensitive for PCP); ABG with A-a gradient (drives steroid decision); serum cryptococcal antigen (CrAg, if CD4 <100 or CNS symptoms); Toxoplasma gondii IgG; blood cultures x2 including fungal/mycobacterial (AFB); sputum for PCP DFA/PCR + bacterial + AFB; hepatitis B/C serologies, RPR/syphilis; G6PD before dapsone/primaquine; lactate if septic; urine + serum Histoplasma antigen if disseminated fungal suspected.
initial_imaging_dx: CXR on arrival (PCP = diffuse bilateral perihilar/interstitial infiltrates); HRCT chest if CXR equivocal (ground-glass opacities, pneumatoceles); induced sputum then bronchoalveolar lavage (BAL) within 24-48h for PCP immunofluorescence/PCR if sputum negative; CT or MRI brain WITH contrast BEFORE LP for altered mental status/focal deficit/seizure (toxoplasmosis = ring-enhancing lesions, PML, CNS lymphoma); lumbar puncture with opening pressure + CSF CrAg/India ink/fungal culture/cell count for suspected meningitis; dilated fundoscopic exam if CD4 <50 (CMV retinitis); EGD for odynophagia refractory to empiric fluconazole (esophageal candidiasis).
time_critical_interventions: Start empiric TMP-SMX IMMEDIATELY when PCP suspected — do NOT await BAL confirmation (organism persists >=5-7 days on therapy); add adjunctive corticosteroids within 72h if PaO2 <70 mmHg or A-a gradient >=35 mmHg (proven mortality benefit). Cryptococcal meningitis: obtain LP opening pressure and start induction antifungal urgently; if OP >25 cm H2O drain CSF to reduce pressure ~50% (or to <20 cm H2O) and repeat daily until controlled. Concurrent bacterial sepsis: Surviving Sepsis 1-h bundle — cultures, broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1h, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, lactate. CNS mass with edema/mass effect: corticosteroids.
initial_management: PCP: TMP-SMX 15-20 mg/kg/day (TMP component) IV/PO divided q6-8h x21 days; alternatives — primaquine + clindamycin, IV pentamidine, or atovaquone (mild); prednisone 40 mg BID days 1-5, 40 mg daily days 6-10, 20 mg daily days 11-21 if hypoxemic. Cryptococcal meningitis: induction liposomal amphotericin B 3-4 mg/kg/day IV + flucytosine 25 mg/kg PO q6h x2 weeks (US standard) OR single-dose liposomal amphotericin B 10 mg/kg + 14d flucytosine/fluconazole 1200 mg (AMBITION); consolidation fluconazole 800 mg/day x8 weeks; maintenance fluconazole 200 mg/day. Toxoplasmosis: pyrimethamine + sulfadiazine + leucovorin x>=6 weeks. Disseminated MAC: azithromycin/clarithromycin + ethambutol. CMV: IV ganciclovir/PO valganciclovir. Esophageal candidiasis: fluconazole 400 mg/day. TB: RIPE (rifampin/isoniazid/pyrazinamide/ethambutol). Restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL; pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis.
day1_milestones: Confirm/target OI diagnosis (induced sputum/BAL, LP, serologies, blood cultures); start empiric or pathogen-directed therapy without delay; ABG + steroid decision for PCP; draw staging labs (CD4, HIV-1 RNA, genotype, HLA-B*5701); assess for concurrent/second OI; establish oxygenation/hemodynamic support and correct monitoring level; place ID/HIV specialty consult; screen for TB and hepatitis coinfection; do NOT start ART on Day 1.
day2_milestones: Reassess oxygenation and clinical response; refine therapy per micro/BAL/CSF results; manage elevated ICP with repeat therapeutic LP in cryptococcosis; begin ART regimen planning (integrase-inhibitor-based, pending genotype, checking drug-drug interactions with antifungals/rifamycins); confirm OI secondary prophylaxis plan and add primary prophylaxis for other OIs by CD4; pharmacy review of renal dosing and interactions; engage social work/case management on adherence, housing, insurance, Ryan White linkage.
day3plus_milestones: Document clinical response (defervescence, improving oxygenation, stable neuro); transition IV to oral therapy; INITIATE ART within 2 weeks for most OIs once tolerating meds — but DEFER ~4-6 weeks in cryptococcal meningitis and 8 weeks in TB meningitis (early ART increases IRIS/mortality per COAT); monitor for immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS); confirm secondary prophylaxis prescribed; finalize outpatient HIV-clinic linkage; discharge when hypoxemia resolved/at baseline and tolerating full oral regimen.
consults_multidisciplinary: Infectious Disease/HIV specialist (Day 1, essential); Pulmonary/critical care (PCP respiratory failure, BAL); Neurology/Neurosurgery (cryptococcal ICP/hydrocephalus VP shunt, CNS mass); Ophthalmology (CMV retinitis if CD4 <50); Clinical pharmacy (ART selection, antifungal/rifamycin interactions, renal dosing) Day 1-2; Social work/case management + linkage-to-care navigator (Ryan White, housing, insurance, adherence) early; Nutrition (HIV wasting/malnutrition); GI (endoscopy for esophageal disease); Palliative care if advanced/refractory disease.
monitoring_level: ICU/step-down for: PCP with respiratory failure (rising FiO2, high-flow/NIV/mechanical ventilation), septic shock, altered mental status/GCS drop, refractory elevated ICP needing frequent CSF drainage, seizures. Telemetry/floor for hemodynamically stable patients on supplemental O2 with stable neuro status. Escalation triggers: worsening hypoxemia or increasing O2 requirement, declining mental status, new seizure, hemodynamic instability, IRIS with airway/neuro compromise.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Screen for HIV wasting/malnutrition and provide nutrition support (dietitian, oral supplements, treat dysphagia); early mobilization/PT as tolerated; pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin/heparin) unless contraindicated; glucose monitoring/control (corticosteroid- and infection-induced hyperglycemia); daily line/catheter necessity review with prompt removal; aspiration precautions if altered mental status.
discharge_criteria: Afebrile trend and hemodynamic stability; hypoxemia resolved or returned to baseline (off supplemental O2 or at home baseline); improving/controlled neuro status with controlled ICP (cryptococcosis); tolerating full oral OI therapy and able to complete the course; ART initiated OR a clear documented start plan with timing; secondary prophylaxis and other indicated OI prophylaxis prescribed; confirmed outpatient HIV-clinic follow-up and medication access.
discharge_planning: Disposition home with linkage to longitudinal HIV care (Ryan White program) and ID follow-up within 7-14 days; medication reconciliation including ART, OI treatment, secondary + primary prophylaxis, with drug-drug-interaction check; adherence support and case management (pillbox, navigator, substance-use/mental-health referral); patient education on the OI, ART adherence, and IRIS warning signs; ensure insurance/ADAP medication coverage; arrange labs (CD4/viral load) and specialty follow-up (ophthalmology, neurology) as needed; SNF/home health if functional decline.
expected_los: GMLOS by tier: DRG 976 (no CC/MCC) ~3.5-4 days; DRG 975 (CC) ~5 days; DRG 974 (MCC — respiratory failure/sepsis) ~7.5-11 days. Real-world PCP with respiratory failure and cryptococcal meningitis commonly run 7-14 days. Blended benchmark ~5-7 days.
target_los: Optimized target 4-6 days for uncomplicated/CC-tier OI (prompt empiric therapy, early ID consult, rapid IV-to-PO transition, expedited outpatient linkage); 7-10 days for MCC-tier (respiratory failure, cryptococcal meningitis requiring ICP control). Guideline-driven ART deferral in CNS OI occurs outpatient and should NOT extend LOS.
quality_metrics: HRSA HIV care-continuum measures — linkage to care, ART initiation/prescription, and viral suppression; timely/appropriate OI treatment and secondary prophylaxis prescribed at discharge; 30-day all-cause readmission; SEP-1 sepsis-bundle compliance when septic; documented CD4/viral load; smoking-cessation and vaccination status; guideline-concordant ART timing (avoiding premature ART in cryptococcal/TB meningitis).
common_complications: Acute respiratory failure (PCP) — mitigated by early empiric TMP-SMX + timely corticosteroids; immune reconstitution inflammatory syndrome (IRIS) — mitigated by correct OI-specific ART timing and monitoring; drug toxicity — TMP-SMX (rash/SJS, hyperkalemia, cytopenias, AKI), amphotericin (nephrotoxicity, K/Mg wasting, infusion reactions), flucytosine (marrow suppression, level-guided); elevated ICP/hydrocephalus in cryptococcosis — mitigated by serial therapeutic LP; missed second concurrent OI; catheter-related bloodstream infection; steroid-induced hyperglycemia.
readmission_drivers: ART non-adherence and loss to follow-up; relapsed/recurrent or incompletely treated OI; new opportunistic infection from persistent immunosuppression; IRIS; medication toxicity/intolerance; social determinants — unstable housing, substance use, mental illness, insurance/medication-access gaps. Prevention levers: guaranteed HIV-clinic linkage before discharge, case-management/navigation (Ryan White), secondary prophylaxis, adherence support, ADAP coverage, active SDOH intervention.
guideline_source: NIH/CDC/HIVMA-IDSA Guidelines for the Prevention and Treatment of Opportunistic Infections in Adults and Adolescents with HIV (2024, updated 2025); DHHS Guidelines for the Use of Antiretroviral Agents in Adults and Adolescents with HIV (2024); IDSA/HIVMA Primary Care Guidance for Persons with HIV (2020).
key_citations: NIH/CDC/IDSA OI Guidelines (clinicalinfo.hiv.gov, 2024-2025); Zolopa AR et al., ACTG A5164 — early vs deferred ART in acute OI, PLoS One 2009 (PMID 19440326); Jarvis JN et al., AMBITION single-dose liposomal amphotericin B for HIV-associated cryptococcal meningitis, N Engl J Med 2022;386:1109-1120 (PMID 35320642); Boulware DR et al., COAT trial — timing of ART after cryptococcal meningitis, N Engl J Med 2014;370:2487-2498 (PMID 24963568).
evidence_grade: Strong — OI treatment regimens are Class AI/AII society-guideline recommendations (NIH/CDC/IDSA) backed by RCTs (adjunctive steroids in PCP; AMBITION for cryptococcosis); ART-timing rules are Strong from RCTs (ACTG A5164 early ART for most OIs; COAT deferred ART for cryptococcal meningitis). LOS/DRG-tier benchmarks are administrative estimates (Moderate).
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 969/970 = HIV with extensive O.R. procedure (e.g., VP shunt for cryptococcal hydrocephalus, tissue/brain biopsy, port) w/wo MCC — surgical subset, most resource-intensive. Medical tiers: 974 (MCC — acute respiratory failure, sepsis, ICU need) longest LOS (~7.5-11d) and highest mortality; 975 (CC — e.g., moderate anemia, electrolyte disturbance) intermediate (~5d); 976 (no CC/MCC) shortest (~3.5-4d); 977 = HIV with/without other related condition (least acute, no severity split). MCC drivers here are typically OI-related respiratory failure, sepsis, meningitis, or profound cytopenias.
pathway_pearls: 1) Start empiric TMP-SMX the moment PCP is suspected — don't wait for BAL — and give adjunctive steroids within 72h if PaO2 <70 or A-a gradient >=35 (proven mortality benefit). 2) ART timing is OI-specific: within 2 weeks for most OIs, but DEFER ~4-6 weeks in cryptococcal meningitis and 8 weeks in TB meningitis — early ART increases IRIS and mortality (COAT trial). 3) In cryptococcal meningitis, aggressively control elevated ICP with serial therapeutic LPs — it is the leading driver of early death. 4) Guaranteed linkage to longitudinal HIV care + case management before discharge is the highest-yield lever against readmission and mortality.

scope_and_volume_notes: HIV with major related condition (opportunistic infection). ~90% cumulative coverage reached at this rank.

admission_criteria: Admit for IWGDF/IDSA Grade 4 (severe) DFI = local infection PLUS >=2 SIRS criteria (temp >38 or <36C, HR >90, RR >20, WBC >12k or <4k or >10% bands); Grade 3 (moderate: erythema >=2cm or deeper than skin/subcutaneous, e.g. abscess, fasciitis, septic arthritis, osteomyelitis) with critical morbidity esp. PAD/limb ischemia, need for urgent surgical drainage/debridement or IV antibiotics; necrotizing soft-tissue infection, gas gangrene, deep abscess, or compartment syndrome (emergent); metabolic instability (DKA/HHS, glucose uncontrolled, AKI); sepsis/bacteremia; failure of outpatient oral therapy; inability to offload or self-care; need for revascularization. ED discharge/observation appropriate for most Grade 2 (mild) and uncomplicated Grade 3 without ischemia or systemic signs
risk_stratification: IWGDF/IDSA infection severity grade 1-4 (uninfected/mild/moderate/severe) — Grade 4 (>=2 SIRS) drives admission/IV abx/urgent surgery; SVS WIfI (Wound, Ischemia, foot Infection) clinical stage 1-4 — stage 3-4 = high 1-yr amputation risk, triggers vascular+surgical consult; qSOFA >=2 / SOFA and lactate for sepsis-shock bundle; probe-to-bone test (Se 0.87, Sp 0.83) + ESR >=70 mm/h (Se 0.81, Sp 0.80) to raise osteomyelitis probability; ABI <0.9 = PAD, toe pressure <30 mmHg or TcPO2 <25 mmHg = critical limb ischemia -> urgent revascularization; ankle pressure <50 mmHg high-risk; Wagner grade and University of Texas ulcer classification for depth/ischemia/infection
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential; CRP and ESR (ESR >=70 supports osteomyelitis) +/- procalcitonin; CMP with creatinine/eGFR (antibiotic + contrast dosing); glucose and HbA1c (glycemic plan, chronicity); lactate if systemic signs; blood cultures x2 BEFORE antibiotics if Grade 4/bacteremia suspected; DEEP tissue culture by curettage/biopsy after debridement (NOT superficial swab) obtained with/before first antibiotic dose; bone biopsy + culture = criterion standard for osteomyelitis (percutaneous through intact skin, ideally off antibiotics >=2 weeks); albumin/prealbumin and vitamin/micronutrient status for healing; coagulation if operative
initial_imaging_dx: Plain foot radiograph on presentation for all suspected bone infection (cortical erosion/destruction, periosteal reaction, sequestrum, soft-tissue gas, foreign body; insensitive early — repeat in 2-3 wks if suspicion persists); bedside probe-to-bone test; MRI foot with and without contrast (STRONG rec) when osteomyelitis diagnosis remains in doubt after exam/X-ray/labs, ideally within 24-48h; FDG-PET or labeled-WBC SPECT if MRI contraindicated; non-invasive perfusion in ALL patients (Doppler waveforms, ankle + toe pressures, TcPO2), arterial duplex; CTA/MRA if revascularization considered; emergent CT + surgical exploration for suspected necrotizing infection/gas; echocardiogram (TTE/TEE) if S. aureus bacteremia
time_critical_interventions: Severe DFI with sepsis/septic shock: Surviving Sepsis 1-hour bundle — blood cultures then broad-spectrum IV antibiotics <=1h, measure lactate (repeat if >2), 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4, vasopressors for MAP >=65; necrotizing soft-tissue infection, gas gangrene, deep abscess, or compartment syndrome = EMERGENT surgical debridement/decompression (source control is the priority, goal <24h); urgent (same-day) combined surgical + vascular consult for any foot infection with PAD/gangrene; early debridement within 24-48h for moderate/severe DFI (associated with lower major-amputation rate); revascularize critical limb ischemia before or alongside soft-tissue/bone resection
initial_management: Empiric antibiotics by severity then de-escalate at 48-72h on cultures: MILD -> oral gram-positive cover (cephalexin/dicloxacillin/amoxicillin-clavulanate; if MRSA risk doxycycline, TMP-SMX, or clindamycin); MODERATE/SEVERE -> IV beta-lactam/beta-lactamase inhibitor (ampicillin-sulbactam or piperacillin-tazobactam) or carbapenem (ertapenem/meropenem); ADD MRSA cover (vancomycin, linezolid, or daptomycin) if prior MRSA/high local prevalence/severe; ADD anaerobic cover (metronidazole) if ischemia/gangrene/necrosis; Pseudomonas cover (pip-tazo, cefepime, ceftazidime) ONLY if risk (macerated wound, warm climate, prior isolate) — do not empirically target Pseudomonas in temperate settings. Source control = surgical debridement/drainage/minor amputation. Sharp wound debridement + moist dressings (NO topical antibiotics/antiseptics/silver/honey/NPWT as substitute). Offloading (total contact cast or non-removable knee-high walker). Basal-bolus insulin targeting 140-180 mg/dL, avoid <70. IV isotonic fluids for sepsis/DKA; treat DKA/HHS per protocol
day1_milestones: Assign IWGDF/IDSA severity grade + SVS WIfI stage; obtain blood cultures (if systemic) and DEEP tissue/bone culture before or with first antibiotic dose; start empiric IV antibiotics (<=1h if septic) matched to severity; plain foot films + bedside probe-to-bone; non-invasive perfusion assessment (ABI/toe pressures); place urgent surgical + vascular consults if PAD, deep/necrotizing, or abscess; complete sepsis bundle if Grade 4; initiate basal-bolus insulin and glucose protocol; begin strict offloading + limb elevation; VTE prophylaxis; NPO if OR anticipated within 24h
day2_milestones: Review preliminary culture/Gram stain and de-escalate/narrow antibiotics; obtain MRI if osteomyelitis remains uncertain; perform surgical debridement/drainage/minor amputation (target within 24-48h) with intra-op deep tissue and bone-margin cultures; reassess perfusion and finalize revascularization plan if critical limb ischemia; Infectious Diseases consult for definitive regimen + planned duration; trend inflammatory markers, glucose, and renal function (adjust vancomycin/renally-cleared agents); confirm offloading and glycemic targets being met
day3plus_milestones: Reassess wound and re-debride as needed to achieve clean margins; finalize bone culture/histology -> confirm/exclude osteomyelitis and set total antibiotic duration; transition IV to oral antibiotics once clinically stable and source controlled (OVIVA: oral non-inferior for bone/joint infection); complete revascularization if indicated; confirm afebrile >=24h, downtrending CRP/WBC, glucose controlled; establish outpatient plan — OPAT if continued IV, offloading device, wound-care regimen, home health; arrange follow-up and assess discharge readiness (typically day 3-5 for medical, later if surgical/ischemic)
consults_multidisciplinary: Infectious Diseases (empiric-to-definitive antibiotic selection, duration, OPAT) day 1-2; Vascular surgery (perfusion, revascularization) — URGENT/same-day if PAD or ischemia; Podiatric or orthopedic foot-and-ankle surgery (debridement, amputation level, offloading) day 1; Endocrinology/inpatient glycemic team for insulin titration; wound-care/WOCN nursing; Physical + Occupational therapy (offloaded gait training, ADLs, DME); Nutrition (consistent-carbohydrate diet, protein for healing, correct hypoalbuminemia) within 24-48h; Pharmacy (renal dosing, vancomycin kinetics, drug interactions); Care management + Social work early (OPAT logistics, home health, SNF/placement, DME); dedicated multidisciplinary diabetic-foot team reduces amputation and readmission
monitoring_level: Floor/med-surg for most Grade 2-3 DFI; telemetry or step-down for resolving sepsis, significant cardiac comorbidity, or electrolyte/vancomycin management; ICU for septic shock requiring vasopressors, severe DKA/HHS, necrotizing infection perioperatively, hemodynamic or respiratory instability. Escalation triggers: hypotension/MAP <65, rising or non-clearing lactate, worsening SIRS, spreading erythema/crepitus/bullae, new organ dysfunction/AKI, refractory hyperglycemia or DKA, uncontrolled pain suggesting deep progression
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: consistent-carbohydrate diabetic diet with adequate protein (~1.25-1.5 g/kg/day) for wound healing; correct hypoalbuminemia and micronutrient deficits. Glucose: basal-bolus (not sliding-scale-only) insulin targeting 140-180 mg/dL per ADA inpatient standard, avoid hypoglycemia <70. Mobility: early PT but STRICT offloading of the affected foot (total contact cast/removable knee-high walker, wheelchair/crutches), heel protection to prevent new ulcers. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin or heparin) unless contraindicated; reduced mobility raises risk. Lines/catheters: place PICC only if prolonged IV/OPAT needed and remove promptly when oral; avoid/early-remove urinary catheter to prevent CAUTI
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable and afebrile >=24h with systemic infection controlled (improving/normalizing WBC, CRP); source control achieved (debridement/drainage/amputation completed, wound stable and not progressing); established on an effective, tolerated antibiotic regimen — oral or arranged OPAT with defined stop date; perfusion addressed (revascularization done or scheduled); glucose controlled on a sustainable regimen without recurrent hypoglycemia; effective offloading device in place; patient/caregiver able to perform wound care with arranged support; follow-up and home services secured
discharge_planning: Disposition: home with home health/VNA and OPAT, or SNF/wound-care facility per functional and infection status. Medication reconciliation: antibiotic regimen with explicit duration/stop date (soft tissue 1-2 wks, osteomyelitis 3-6 wks), insulin plan, VTE prophylaxis reconciled. Schedule follow-up: ID/OPAT lab monitoring, podiatry/vascular/wound clinic within 1-2 weeks, PCP/endocrinology for glycemic optimization. Patient education: daily foot self-inspection, strict offloading adherence, glycemic self-management, smoking cessation, red-flag return precautions. Transitions of care: warm handoff, DME (offloading boot, wheelchair), wound-care supplies, medication access/prior-auth resolved before discharge
expected_los: Varies by DRG and severity. Medical skin-ulcer DRGs: 594 (no CC/MCC) GMLOS ~3 days, 593 (CC) ~4 days, 592 (MCC) ~5 days. Surgical skin-graft/debridement DRGs: 575 (no CC/MCC) GMLOS ~4 days, 574 (CC) ~6 days, 573 (MCC) ~9-10 days. Typical real-world DFI admission ~5-7 days (approximate CMS IPPS GMLOS benchmarks; exact FY figures vary by version)
target_los: 3-4 days for uncomplicated moderate DFI managed medically with early source control and rapid IV-to-oral transition (OVIVA); 5-7 days when surgical debridement/minor amputation or revascularization is required; osteomyelitis and severe/MCC cases longer, with the goal of completing definitive antibiotics as outpatient OPAT rather than prolonging admission
quality_metrics: SEP-1 sepsis bundle compliance (if severe sepsis/septic shock); antimicrobial stewardship measures (empiric-to-targeted de-escalation, days-of-therapy, appropriate duration); major and minor amputation rate / limb salvage rate; 30-day readmission and 30-day mortality; inpatient glycemic control (percent glucose 70-180 mg/dL, hyperglycemia and hypoglycemia <70/<54 event rates); VTE prophylaxis rate; healthcare-associated infection rates (CLABSI/CAUTI/C. difficile); wound healing rate and time to healing at 12 weeks; documentation of comprehensive foot exam and HbA1c
common_complications: Progression to osteomyelitis or need for amputation; sepsis/bacteremia (esp. S. aureus, including MRSA) with metastatic seeding; need for repeat debridement/reoperation and wound dehiscence; insulin-related hypoglycemia; AKI (sepsis, contrast, vancomycin nephrotoxicity); Clostridioides difficile and selection of MDR organisms from broad-spectrum antibiotics; VTE and deconditioning; new contralateral/heel pressure ulcer. Mitigation: antimicrobial stewardship with early de-escalation and shortest effective duration, protocolized glucose control, prompt source control, universal offloading and heel protection, vancomycin level monitoring, VTE prophylaxis
readmission_drivers: Recurrent or persistent infection and non-healing/new ulcer; inadequate or non-adherent offloading; unaddressed PAD/critical limb ischemia; poor glycemic control; OPAT failure, antibiotic non-adherence, or medication-access gaps; missed specialty follow-up; social/support barriers. Prevention levers: multidisciplinary diabetic-foot clinic follow-up, guaranteed offloading device, revascularization when indicated, glycemic optimization, structured 1-2 week wound/ID/vascular follow-up, patient/caregiver education, home health and reliable OPAT monitoring
guideline_source: PRIMARY: IWGDF/IDSA 2023 Guidelines on the Diagnosis and Treatment of Diabetes-related Foot Infections (Senneville et al., International Working Group on the Diabetic Foot + Infectious Diseases Society of America, 2023). Supporting: IWGDF 2023 guidelines on classification, offloading, peripheral artery disease, and wound healing; ADA Standards of Care in Diabetes 2025-2026 Section 16 (Diabetes Care in the Hospital, inpatient glucose 140-180 mg/dL); SVS/APMA/SVM 2016 management of the diabetic foot; SVS WIfI lower-extremity threatened-limb classification (Mills 2014)
key_citations: Senneville E, et al. IWGDF/IDSA Guidelines on the Diagnosis and Treatment of Diabetes-related Foot Infections (IWGDF/IDSA 2023). Clin Infect Dis 2023 (PMID 37779457, doi 10.1093/cid/ciad527); parallel publication Diabetes Metab Res Rev 2024;40:e3687 (PMID 37779323, doi 10.1002/dmrr.3687). Lazaro-Martinez JL, et al. Antibiotics versus conservative surgery for diabetic foot osteomyelitis: RCT. Diabetes Care 2014;37:789-95 (PMID 24130347, doi 10.2337/dc13-1526). Tone A, et al. Six- vs twelve-week antibiotics for nonsurgical diabetic foot osteomyelitis: RCT. Diabetes Care 2015;38:302-7 (PMID 25414157, doi 10.2337/dc14-1514). Li HK, et al. Oral versus Intravenous Antibiotics for Bone and Joint Infection (OVIVA). N Engl J Med 2019;380:425-436 (PMID 30699315, doi 10.1056/NEJMoa1710926). Mills JL, et al. SVS WIfI classification. J Vasc Surg 2014 (PMID 24126108). Citations verified via PubMed
evidence_grade: Strong for diagnosis/classification framework — current GRADE-based Class I society guideline (IWGDF/IDSA 2023) with strong recommendations for clinical diagnosis, the IWGDF/IDSA severity classification, MRI when osteomyelitis is uncertain, and culture-based therapy. Moderate for therapeutic specifics: many antibiotic-duration and surgery-versus-antibiotic decisions are conditional/low-certainty recommendations, though supported by RCTs (Lazaro-Martinez surgery vs antibiotics, Tone 6 vs 12 weeks, OVIVA oral vs IV). Overall: Strong guideline backbone, Moderate for individual treatment thresholds
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Without CC/MCC (DRG 575/594): mild-to-moderate DFI, often medical or single debridement, short LOS ~3-4 days. With CC (DRG 574/593): moderate DFI plus comorbidity (PAD, CKD, poorly controlled diabetes, cellulitis) requiring IV antibiotics and debridement, LOS ~4-6 days. With MCC (DRG 573/592): severe DFI with sepsis, osteomyelitis, gangrene, DKA/HHS, AKI, or major debridement/amputation — possible ICU, prolonged antibiotics, LOS ~5-10 days and higher amputation risk. Surgical DRGs 573-575 (skin graft/debridement) carry higher relative weight and LOS than medical DRGs 592-594 (skin ulcers) for equivalent severity because of the procedure. Principal MCC drivers: sepsis/septic shock, osteomyelitis, gangrene, AKI, DKA
pathway_pearls: 1) Culture right, then treat: obtain deep tissue and bone-margin cultures before or with the first antibiotic dose and never rely on superficial swabs — source control (adequate debridement) outperforms prolonged antibiotics. 2) Perfusion in every patient: check ABI/toe pressures on all DFI; PAD plus infection is the dominant amputation driver, so trigger urgent combined surgical + vascular consultation. 3) Stewardship and duration discipline: de-escalate at 48-72h, use the shortest effective course (soft tissue 1-2 weeks; osteomyelitis ~6 weeks nonsurgical, ~3 weeks if resected with positive margin, and as little as a few days to <=3 weeks when all infected bone is removed) and switch IV-to-oral early (OVIVA). 4) Bundle the system: multidisciplinary diabetic-foot team plus strict offloading plus glucose 140-180 mg/dL reduces amputations and 30-day readmissions

scope_and_volume_notes: Diabetic foot ulcer/infection subset overlapping skin-ulcer (rank 62) and osteomyelitis (rank 73); shown for pathway granularity. Often precedes amputation.

admission_criteria: Most thyroid/parathyroid surgery is same-day or 23-hour observation; objective indications for true inpatient (>=1 midnight) admission: large substernal/retrosternal goiter with tracheal compression/deviation or requiring sternotomy; thyroid malignancy requiring central and/or lateral neck dissection; bilateral 4-gland parathyroid exploration or reoperative neck with high hungry-bone risk; hypercalcemic crisis (Ca >13-14 mg/dL, AMS) needing IV hydration; symptomatic or anticipated symptomatic hypocalcemia (early post-op intact PTH <10-15 pg/mL); airway compromise, difficult airway, or tracheomalacia risk requiring post-op airway watch; single functioning vocal cord / bilateral RLN concern; uncontrolled thyrotoxicosis/Graves with cardiac risk or significant comorbidity; social barriers (lives alone/far, cannot self-monitor for hematoma). ED-dischargeable / <23h observation: uncomplicated hemithyroidectomy or focused parathyroidectomy with reassuring post-op PTH and no airway concern
risk_stratification: Bethesda System for Reporting Thyroid Cytopathology I-VI (V/VI = high malignancy risk, drives extent of resection); ATA differentiated-thyroid-cancer recurrence risk (low/intermediate/high) + AJCC/TNM 8th-ed staging -> extent of surgery and RAI planning; EARLY POSTOPERATIVE INTACT PTH is the key operational predictor -- PTH <15 pg/mL (some use <10) or decline >65-70% from baseline predicts symptomatic hypocalcemia and triggers prophylactic calcium + calcitriol (ATA 2018); serial corrected/ionized calcium trend -> supplementation vs escalation; IOPTH Miami criterion for parathyroidectomy = >50% drop from highest pre-excision baseline at 10 min post-excision predicts cure and terminates operation, failure to drop drives further exploration; 24-hr urine calcium/calcium-creatinine clearance ratio to exclude FHH before parathyroidectomy; Caprini score for VTE; difficult-airway assessment + substernal extent on CT
initial_workup_labs: Thyroid: TSH, free T4 (+/- free T3); confirm euthyroid before elective surgery in hyperthyroidism; thyroglobulin + anti-Tg antibody baseline for DTC; calcitonin + CEA if medullary carcinoma suspected (RET testing if MEN2/MTC). Calcium/metabolic: corrected or ionized calcium, albumin, magnesium, phosphate; for primary hyperparathyroidism intact PTH, 25-OH vitamin D, 24-hr urine calcium, creatinine/eGFR (AAES 2016). CBC, BMP, coagulation if indicated; type & screen for large goiter/malignancy. POST-OP: early intact PTH (intra-op or 6-24h) + serial corrected/ionized calcium q6-12h -> drives PTH-guided calcium/calcitriol protocol; magnesium (repletion required for refractory hypocalcemia)
initial_imaging_dx: Neck ultrasound of thyroid + central/lateral nodal compartments mandatory pre-op for nodules/cancer, with FNA by ATA sonographic pattern + size thresholds; molecular testing (Afirma GSC, ThyroSeq v3) for Bethesda III/IV indeterminate nodules; flexible laryngoscopy to document baseline vocal-cord mobility pre-op (AAES: for prior neck/chest surgery, voice change, or invasive/posterior tumor) and post-op if voice change; CT or MRI neck +/- chest (non-contrast or iodine-washout plan if RAI anticipated) for substernal goiter, tracheal compression, bulky/invasive disease; parathyroid localization before parathyroidectomy = neck US + 99mTc-sestamibi (SPECT/CT) and/or 4D-CT (nonlocalizing imaging still a surgical candidate per AAES); pre-op ECG for thyrotoxicosis or hypercalcemia (QT)
time_critical_interventions: ANTERIOR/CERVICAL NECK HEMATOMA = AIRWAY EMERGENCY: expanding neck swelling/stridor -> open the wound and evacuate clot AT THE BEDSIDE immediately, do NOT wait for OR; keep a suture-removal/hematoma-evacuation kit at bedside; ~40-45% occur within 6h and the majority within 24h but ~15-25% present after 24h, which calibrates the observation window. Symptomatic hypocalcemia (perioral/acral paresthesia, carpopedal spasm, Chvostek/Trousseau, QT prolongation) -> IV calcium gluconate 1-2 g over 10-20 min then continuous infusion; check/replete magnesium. Hypercalcemic crisis (Ca >14, AMS) -> isotonic saline 200-300 mL/h + calcitonin, definitive Rx is parathyroidectomy. Post-op airway monitoring first 4-6h minimum for bilateral cases. Thyroid storm -> beta-blocker + thionamide + iodine + glucocorticoid
initial_management: PTH-guided/prophylactic calcium + active vitamin D after total/completion thyroidectomy: oral calcium carbonate or citrate 1-3 g elemental Ca/day divided, plus calcitriol 0.25-0.5 mcg BID when early PTH <15 pg/mL (ATA 2018) -> prevents symptomatic hypocalcemia. IV calcium gluconate + magnesium repletion for symptomatic/severe hypocalcemia. Antibiotic prophylaxis NOT routinely indicated (clean case, very low SSI -- AAES recommends against routine antibiotics). Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia: acetaminophen + NSAID (ketorolac/ibuprofen) +/- bilateral superficial cervical plexus block, minimal opioids. PONV prophylaxis (dexamethasone + ondansetron; dexamethasone also reduces PONV/edema). Hyperthyroid optimization: methimazole to euthyroid + beta-blocker, and potassium iodide (SSKI/Lugol) ~10 days pre-op for Graves to reduce gland vascularity. Intra-op RLN monitoring (IONM) and autotransplantation of any devascularized parathyroid. Levothyroxine ~1.6 mcg/kg/day started after total thyroidectomy (adjust/withhold per RAI plan)
day1_milestones: POD0: capsular dissection with RLN identification +/- IONM, parathyroid preservation/autotransplant; IOPTH check (Miami >50% drop) for parathyroidectomy confirms cure intra-op; extubate and assess voice/airway in PACU; draw early postoperative intact PTH (+/- calcium) and START PTH-guided oral calcium/calcitriol; monitor surgical site for hematoma q1-2h during first 6h (routine drains NOT recommended -- AAES); early ambulation same day; advance diet clear -> soft/regular as tolerated; multimodal analgesia + PONV control; mechanical VTE prophylaxis
day2_milestones: POD1: morning corrected/ionized calcium (+/- PTH), titrate calcium/calcitriol to a stable or upward trend; wound check and remove drain if placed and output low; confirm voice (SLP referral if hoarseness/aspiration); confirm no hematoma, tolerating diet, pain controlled on oral meds, ambulating; DISCHARGE POD1 for most uncomplicated cases with hypocalcemia teaching, written action plan, prescriptions, and follow-up scheduled
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ (reserved for CC/MCC and complications): manage hungry bone syndrome after parathyroidectomy for severe pHPT/renal disease (prolonged IV -> oral calcium/calcitriol, document 6-12h stable calcium off IV before discharge); manage chyle leak after lateral neck dissection (low-fat/MCT diet, drain management); airway/tracheomalacia observation or tracheal resection recovery; bilateral RLN injury requiring tracheostomy; resolve hypercalcemic crisis; endocrine follow-up arranged for permanent hypoparathyroidism; discharge when calcium stable on oral regimen and airway/voice safe
consults_multidisciplinary: Endocrine/head-and-neck surgery (primary); Endocrinology (RAI planning for DTC, hyperthyroid optimization, hypoparathyroidism/calcium titration, complex pHPT); Speech-language pathology (voice/swallow if RLN injury or hoarseness); Anesthesia/difficult-airway team (substernal goiter, tracheal compression); Nutrition (calcium/vitamin D counseling, MCT diet for chyle leak); Care management + social work (disposition, teaching for patients living alone); Pharmacy (calcium/calcitriol/levothyroxine reconciliation, anticoagulation timing); Genetics (MEN2/MTC, familial); Cardiology if thyrotoxic AF/cardiomyopathy
monitoring_level: Surgical floor/ward with scheduled neck-and-airway checks is standard for the vast majority; extended PACU/step-down or ICU for airway compromise, tracheomalacia after large-goiter resection, bilateral vocal-cord concern, tracheal resection, hypercalcemic crisis, or severe symptomatic hypocalcemia with QT prolongation (telemetry); telemetry for thyrotoxic AF or marked hypo-/hypercalcemia. Escalation triggers: expanding neck swelling / stridor / respiratory distress (immediate bedside wound opening), refractory hypocalcemia despite IV calcium, new arrhythmia, airway loss
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: advance clear -> soft/regular POD0 (cool/soft for comfort); MCT/low-fat if chyle leak. Mobility: ambulate POD0 (short-stay, inherently low VTE risk). VTE: mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs) standard; pharmacologic LMWH/heparin reserved for high Caprini score, malignancy, or prolonged stay, balanced against neck-bleeding/hematoma risk. Glucose: perioperative target <180 mg/dL (dexamethasone may transiently raise glucose). Lines/catheters: no Foley for short cases; maintain IV access while on IV calcium and remove promptly to reduce infection and enable mobilization
discharge_criteria: Stable or upward-trending corrected/ionized calcium on oral regimen (off IV calcium >=6-12h if it was required) and asymptomatic (no paresthesia/tetany); no expanding hematoma and airway stable; acceptable voice/swallow or documented SLP plan; tolerating oral intake and oral analgesia with controlled pain; ambulating at baseline; patient/caregiver verbalizes hypocalcemia symptoms and action plan plus neck-hematoma red flags; calcium, calcitriol, and levothyroxine (if indicated) prescriptions in hand
discharge_planning: Disposition home for the vast majority. Med rec: calcium carbonate/citrate + calcitriol with a written taper plan; levothyroxine after total thyroidectomy per endocrinology/RAI plan; resume/adjust home meds and set anticoagulation restart timing. Follow-up: surgeon at 1-2 weeks (wound, pathology, voice), endocrinology for calcium titration and cancer staging/RAI, repeat calcium/PTH at 1-2 weeks, and pHPT cure confirmation as eucalcemia at >6 months (AAES). Education: written hypocalcemia action plan (extra calcium PRN paresthesia), hematoma warning signs, wound care, realistic voice expectations. Transitions: PCP notification, closed-loop pathology tracking, RAI scheduling for DTC
expected_los: Largely ambulatory / 23-hour outside these DRGs; for the admitted subset overall ~1-3 days. Approximate CMS geometric-mean LOS benchmarks: DRG 627 (without CC/MCC) ~1.2 days; DRG 626 (with CC) ~2.2-2.5 days; DRG 625 (with MCC) ~5 days
target_los: Uncomplicated total thyroidectomy or parathyroidectomy: <=1 day (POD1 discharge) or 23-hour observation; complex neck dissection or large substernal goiter ~2 days; MCC-driven (hungry bone, airway, crisis) ~3-4 days when actively managed
quality_metrics: Post-thyroidectomy symptomatic hypocalcemia rate and permanent hypoparathyroidism rate (target <1-3%); RLN/vocal-cord paralysis rate (transient <5-10%, permanent <1-2%) with documented pre- and post-op laryngeal exam; neck hematoma / return-to-OR rate (<1-2%); 30-day readmission and ED-revisit rate (hypocalcemia is the leading driver); primary-hyperparathyroidism cure rate (eucalcemia >6 months) and IOPTH utilization; PONV rate, opioid-free discharge, and SSI rate (should be minimal -- antibiotic stewardship, avoid routine prophylaxis); surgeon/hospital volume (high-volume = better outcomes, AAES); PSI-90 component safety events. This is not a SEP-1/core-measure-heavy DRG
common_complications: Hypocalcemia/hypoparathyroidism (transient ~20-30% after total thyroidectomy, permanent 1-3%) -- mitigated by parathyroid preservation/autotransplant and PTH-guided prophylactic calcium + calcitriol; RLN injury / vocal-cord paralysis (transient 3-10%, permanent 1-2%) -- mitigated by IONM, capsular dissection, nerve identification; cervical hematoma with airway compromise (1-2%) -- meticulous hemostasis, intra-op Valsalva check, structured observation, bedside evacuation kit; hungry bone syndrome after parathyroidectomy for severe pHPT -- pre-op vitamin D repletion + aggressive post-op Ca/vitamin D; seroma, SSI (rare), chyle leak (lateral neck dissection), and rebound hypercalcemia from over-supplementation
readmission_drivers: Symptomatic hypocalcemia is the dominant readmission/ED-revisit driver -- prevented by a structured PTH-guided calcium protocol, a written patient action plan, and an early outpatient calcium check; neck hematoma/bleeding -- structured observation window and hemostasis; airway/voice complications and dehydration/poor oral intake; uncontrolled pain; rebound hypercalcemia from supplementation -- addressed with a calcium/calcitriol taper plan and scheduled labs
guideline_source: American Association of Endocrine Surgeons (AAES) 2020 Guidelines for the Definitive Surgical Management of Thyroid Disease in Adults; AAES 2016 Guidelines for Definitive Management of Primary Hyperparathyroidism; American Thyroid Association (ATA) 2015 Management Guidelines for Adult Patients with Thyroid Nodules and Differentiated Thyroid Cancer; ATA 2018 Statement on Postoperative Hypoparathyroidism
key_citations: Patel KN et al. AAES Guidelines for the Definitive Surgical Management of Thyroid Disease in Adults. Ann Surg 2020;271(3):e21-e93 (PMID 32079830; exec summary PMID 32079828; DOI 10.1097/SLA.0000000000003580); Wilhelm SM et al. AAES Guidelines for Definitive Management of Primary Hyperparathyroidism. JAMA Surg 2016;151(10):959-968 (PMID 27532368; DOI 10.1001/jamasurg.2016.2310); Haugen BR et al. 2015 ATA Management Guidelines for Adult Patients with Thyroid Nodules and Differentiated Thyroid Cancer. Thyroid 2016;26(1):1-133 (PMID 26462967; DOI 10.1089/thy.2015.0020); Orloff LA et al. ATA Statement on Postoperative Hypoparathyroidism: Diagnosis, Prevention, and Management in Adults. Thyroid 2018;28(7):830-841 (PMID 29848235; DOI 10.1089/thy.2017.0309); Barczynski M et al. IOPTH improves minimally invasive parathyroidectomy outcomes (Miami criterion). Clin Endocrinol 2007;66(6):878-885 (PMID 17437518). Citations verified via PubMed
evidence_grade: Strong for the core operative and diagnostic framework -- multiple Class I/II recommendations from concordant Class I society guidelines (AAES 2020, AAES 2016, ATA 2015/2018) graded with the ACP system. Moderate-to-consensus for the specific perioperative operational thresholds (PTH <15 pg/mL cutoff, exact calcium/calcitriol dosing, observation-window duration) and for LOS targets, which rest largely on observational data and expert consensus rather than randomized trials
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 627 (no CC/MCC): uncomplicated hemi-/total thyroidectomy or focused parathyroidectomy; largely 23h-1 day on the floor; principal issue is transient hypocalcemia. DRG 626 (CC): moderate severity -- symptomatic hypocalcemia requiring IV calcium, larger goiter, neck dissection, or a controlled comorbidity; LOS ~2 days. DRG 625 (MCC): airway compromise / tracheal resection, thyroid storm, hypercalcemic crisis, severe hungry bone syndrome, bilateral RLN injury requiring tracheostomy, sepsis, or aspiration -> ICU/step-down, LOS ~5 days, with materially higher complication and readmission risk
pathway_pearls: 1) PTH-guided calcium protocol -- draw an early post-op intact PTH; if <15 pg/mL start prophylactic oral calcium + calcitriol; this prevents symptomatic hypocalcemia, enables safe early/same-day discharge, and cuts the #1 readmission driver. 2) Treat EVERY anterior neck hematoma as an airway emergency -- keep a bedside wound-opening/evacuation kit and train staff to open at the bedside; calibrate the observation window to hematoma timing (majority <6-24h, but ~15-25% later). 3) Document vocal-cord function before AND after surgery (laryngoscopy) and use intra-op nerve monitoring to protect against unrecognized bilateral RLN injury. 4) For parathyroidectomy, the IOPTH Miami criterion (>50% drop at 10 min) confirms cure intra-operatively and prevents persistent disease/reoperation; pre-op vitamin D repletion mitigates hungry bone syndrome

admission_criteria: Admit all definite acute cholangitis by Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18): systemic inflammation (fever/chills or abnormal WBC/CRP) + cholestasis (T.bili >=2 mg/dL or ALP/GGT/AST/ALT >1.5x ULN) + imaging (biliary dilation or evidence of etiology-stone/stricture/stent); any TG18 Grade II (moderate) or Grade III (severe) -> admit, Grade III to ICU; Charcot triad (fever+jaundice+RUQ pain) or Reynolds pentad (+hypotension+AMS); choledocholithiasis with obstruction/abnormal LFTs/ductal dilation needing ERCP; sepsis, hemodynamic instability, AKI, coagulopathy, intractable pain/vomiting or inability to tolerate PO. ED discharge/observation appropriate only for isolated biliary colic with normal LFTs, no fever/leukocytosis, no ductal dilation and no CBD stone -> expedited outpatient EUS/MRCP + GI/surgery follow-up.
risk_stratification: TG18 severity grade drives drainage timing/disposition: Grade I (mild) = no organ dysfunction, does not meet II/III -> antibiotics + drainage only if no response by 24h; Grade II (moderate) = >=2 of [WBC >12,000 or <4,000; fever >=39C; age >=75; T.bili >=5 mg/dL; albumin <0.7x LLN] -> early biliary drainage within 48-72h; Grade III (severe) = organ dysfunction in >=1 [CV: hypotension needing dopamine >=5 mcg/kg/min or any norepinephrine; neuro: altered consciousness; resp: PaO2/FiO2 <300; renal: oliguria or Cr >2.0 mg/dL; hepatic: INR >1.5; heme: platelets <100,000] -> ICU + urgent drainage (within 12h if septic shock). Adjuncts: qSOFA >=2 / Sepsis-3 septic-shock criteria (lactate >2 mmol/L + vasopressor-dependent) trigger ICU + SEP-1 bundle; ASGE-2019 high-risk for choledocholithiasis (CBD stone on imaging, OR T.bili >4 mg/dL + dilated CBD, OR clinical ascending cholangitis) -> proceed directly to ERCP.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with diff (leukocytosis/left shift; platelets <100,000 flags Grade III); CMP + LFTs for cholestatic pattern (elevated ALP, GGT, total/direct bilirubin; transaminases may spike early); lipase to exclude concurrent gallstone pancreatitis; serum lactate (perfusion/sepsis); PT/INR + aPTT (hepatic dysfunction + pre-procedure); blood cultures x2 before antibiotics (bacteremia in 20-40%); CRP +/- procalcitonin; type & screen and pre-ERCP labs; BMP for AKI, Mg/Phos. Triggers: T.bili >=5 mg/dL, platelets <100k, Cr >2.0, INR >1.5 escalate to Grade III/ICU; lactate >=2 mmol/L activates sepsis bundle; positive cultures guide antibiotic de-escalation.
initial_imaging_dx: Transabdominal ultrasound first-line on presentation (ductal dilation, gallstones; low sensitivity ~20-50% for CBD stones); contrast-enhanced CT abdomen/pelvis if US nondiagnostic or to evaluate complications (abscess, malignancy, alternative dx); MRCP as noninvasive reference standard (sens/spec >90%) and EUS (best for small/distal CBD stones) for intermediate-probability cases, ideally within 24h; ERCP is therapeutic not diagnostic - reserved for ASGE high-risk/confirmed CBD stones and provides drainage. ERCP timing by TG18 grade: severe within 12h of resuscitation for septic shock; moderate within 48-72h; mild elective/if no response to medical therapy.
time_critical_interventions: Septic shock / TG18 Grade III -> Surviving Sepsis / CMS SEP-1 bundle: measure lactate (repeat if >2 mmol/L), blood cultures before antibiotics, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 1h, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4 mmol/L, norepinephrine first-line for MAP >=65 mmHg, reassess perfusion. Source control is time-critical: urgent endoscopic biliary drainage within 12h of resuscitation for cholangitis with septic shock (ESGE 2019); as soon as possible after stabilization for other Grade III; within 48-72h for Grade II. Antibiotics within 1h of septic-shock recognition; do not delay drainage awaiting full sepsis resolution in refractory shock - decompression is the definitive resuscitation.
initial_management: Empiric IV antibiotics per TG18: community-acquired Grade I-II -> 3rd-gen cephalosporin (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime) or piperacillin-tazobactam or a fluoroquinolone (ciprofloxacin/levofloxacin), add metronidazole if biliary-enteric anastomosis; Grade III or healthcare-associated -> piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime/ceftazidime + metronidazole, or a carbapenem (meropenem/imipenem/ertapenem) + vancomycin for enterococcal coverage; de-escalate by culture, duration 4-7 days after source control (>=2 weeks if Gram-positive Enterococcus/Streptococcus bacteremia). Balanced crystalloid resuscitation + electrolyte correction; NPO; opioid analgesia + antiemetics; pre-ERCP coagulopathy correction (vitamin K, hold/reverse anticoagulants, PCC/FFP if INR elevated with planned sphincterotomy); restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL. Definitive source control = biliary drainage: ERCP with sphincterotomy + stone extraction and/or stent; percutaneous transhepatic biliary drainage (PTBD) if ERCP fails/altered anatomy; EUS-guided or surgical drainage as backup.
day1_milestones: Establish TG18 diagnosis + severity grade and disposition (floor vs ICU); draw blood cultures and start empiric IV antibiotics within 1h if septic; complete fluid resuscitation / sepsis bundle; urgent ERCP within 12h for Grade III/septic shock and expedite ERCP scheduling for Grade II; begin monitoring of organ-dysfunction markers; start VTE prophylaxis and glucose control; NPO with IV fluids; consult GI/ERCP and, for severe disease, critical care. Reassess response to medical therapy - Grade I not improving by 24h proceeds to drainage.
day2_milestones: Ensure biliary drainage achieved (Grade II within 48-72h) if not done Day 1; post-ERCP monitoring for pancreatitis (lipase + pain), bleeding, perforation and persistent cholangitis; de-escalate/narrow antibiotics per culture and sensitivities; confirm down-trending fever, leukocytosis and bilirubin with improving hemodynamics; advance diet after procedure once tolerating; step down from ICU as organ dysfunction resolves; document stone clearance vs need for staged/repeat ERCP or stent exchange.
day3plus_milestones: Confirm clinical resolution: afebrile >=24-48h, normalizing WBC and down-trending bilirubin/LFTs, hemodynamically stable off pressors, tolerating oral diet and oral antibiotics; complete antibiotic course (4-7 days after source control); definitive etiology treatment - same-admission laparoscopic cholecystectomy for gallstone cholangitis/choledocholithiasis (ASGE 2019; ESGE within 2 weeks of ERCP) unless prohibitive operative risk; biliary stenting/oncology referral for malignant obstruction; plan stent removal/exchange and any staged ERCP for retained/difficult stones; verify discharge criteria met.
consults_multidisciplinary: Gastroenterology/advanced endoscopy (ERCP) - immediate on Day 0; general or hepatobiliary surgery for same-admission cholecystectomy planning - early; interventional radiology for PTBD if ERCP infeasible/fails; critical care for Grade III/septic shock; infectious diseases for bacteremia, resistant or healthcare-associated organisms; pharmacy for antimicrobial stewardship, renal dosing and periprocedural anticoagulation; nutrition for NPO management/diet advancement; care management + social work early for disposition and follow-up; PT/OT for mobility in elderly/ICU patients.
monitoring_level: ICU for TG18 Grade III / septic shock (vasopressor need, PaO2/FiO2 <300, altered mentation, Cr >2.0, INR >1.5, platelets <100k); step-down/telemetry for Grade II with borderline hemodynamics or significant comorbidity; general floor with vital-sign + sepsis screening for stable Grade I. Escalation triggers: rising or non-clearing lactate, fluid-refractory hypotension (start norepinephrine, ICU), new organ dysfunction, failure to improve within 24h of antibiotics (expedite drainage), or post-ERCP complication (severe pancreatitis, bleeding, perforation).
nutrition_mobility_vte: NPO before ERCP, then advance to low-fat/regular diet as tolerated after drainage; early mobilization/ambulation to prevent deconditioning; pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (LMWH e.g. enoxaparin, or UFH) once coagulopathy corrected and bleeding/sphincterotomy risk acceptable - mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs) in the interim; glucose control target 140-180 mg/dL; remove central lines/Foley as soon as no longer needed and manage biliary stents/PTBD per endoscopy plan to reduce CLABSI/CAUTI.
discharge_criteria: Afebrile and hemodynamically stable off vasopressors; down-trending/normalizing WBC and serum bilirubin/LFTs; documented successful biliary drainage/source control; tolerating oral intake and transitioned to oral antibiotics or course completed; adequate oral analgesia; no active post-ERCP complication (pancreatitis, bleeding, perforation); and a defined plan for definitive therapy - scheduled same-admission or interval (<=2 weeks) cholecystectomy and/or follow-up ERCP for retained stones/stent exchange.
discharge_planning: Disposition home for most; SNF/rehab for deconditioned or frail elderly. Medication reconciliation: finish or transition to oral antibiotics, resume/bridge anticoagulation, analgesia. Schedule follow-up: surgery for same-admission or interval (<=2 weeks) laparoscopic cholecystectomy, GI for planned stent removal/exchange or repeat ERCP (typically 4-6 weeks), PCP visit and repeat LFTs. Patient education on red-flag return precautions (recurrent fever, jaundice, RUQ pain, dark urine, acholic stools), low-fat diet, and biliary stent care. Transitions of care: written instructions, ERCP/operative reports to the surgeon, and explicit biliary-stent tracking/registry to guarantee retrieval and prevent stent-related complications.
expected_los: Typical 3-5 days blended; CMS GMLOS approx DRG 446 (w/o CC/MCC) ~2.7-3.0 days, DRG 445 (w CC) ~3.7-4.3 days, DRG 444 (w MCC) ~5.3-6.0 days; severe Grade III with ICU/septic shock 7-10+ days.
target_los: 2-3 days for uncomplicated Grade I-II with early ERCP drainage and prompt source control (add ~1 day if same-admission cholecystectomy performed); 5-7 days for Grade III/ICU. Levers: door-to-drainage acceleration, early antibiotic de-escalation, index-admission cholecystectomy to avoid readmission.
quality_metrics: CMS SEP-1 severe sepsis/septic shock bundle compliance; time-to-biliary-drainage within TG18/ESGE window (<12h septic shock, 48-72h moderate); blood cultures before antibiotics and antimicrobial de-escalation/stewardship; post-ERCP pancreatitis rate and overall ERCP adverse-event/cannulation-success rates; same-admission cholecystectomy rate for gallstone disease; 30-day all-cause readmission; in-hospital and 30-day mortality; risk-adjusted LOS; VTE prophylaxis compliance.
common_complications: Post-ERCP pancreatitis (most common, ~3-10%; mitigate with rectal indomethacin/diclofenac, prophylactic pancreatic-duct stent in high-risk, wire-guided cannulation, periprocedural lactated Ringer's); progression to septic shock/multi-organ failure (mitigate with early drainage + sepsis bundle); post-sphincterotomy bleeding and duodenal/ductal perforation; persistent or recurrent cholangitis from incomplete drainage/retained stones (ensure clearance or stent); hepatic abscess; AKI and delirium in the elderly; interval acute cholecystitis if gallbladder left in situ (mitigate with same-admission cholecystectomy).
readmission_drivers: Retained or recurrent CBD stones from incomplete clearance; deferral of cholecystectomy causing recurrent biliary events (recurrent cholangitis, biliary pancreatitis, cholecystitis) - the dominant modifiable driver; biliary stent occlusion/migration or 'forgotten' stents; inadequate source control/persistent obstruction. Prevention levers: verify complete duct clearance, perform same-admission (or <=2-week) laparoscopic cholecystectomy, maintain a stent tracking/registry with scheduled removal, ensure timely GI/surgery follow-up and red-flag patient education.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18), Japanese Society of Hepato-Biliary-Pancreatic Surgery, 2018 - diagnostic criteria, severity grading, initial management/flowchart, and antimicrobial therapy for acute cholangitis. Adjunct: ASGE 2019 and ESGE 2019 guidelines on endoscopic management of choledocholithiasis/CBD stones (ERCP timing, drainage, cholecystectomy). Sepsis overlay for Grade III: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2026 (updates SSC 2021) + CMS SEP-1.
key_citations: Kiriyama S, et al. TG18: diagnostic criteria and severity grading of acute cholangitis. J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci. 2018;25(1):17-30. PMID 29032610; DOI 10.1002/jhbp.512. Miura F, et al. TG18: initial management of acute biliary infection and flowchart for acute cholangitis. J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci. 2018;25(1):31-40. PMID 28941329; DOI 10.1002/jhbp.509. Gomi H, et al. TG18: antimicrobial therapy for acute cholangitis and cholecystitis. J Hepatobiliary Pancreat Sci. 2018;25(1):3-16. PMID 29090866; DOI 10.1002/jhbp.518. Buxbaum JL, et al. ASGE guideline on the role of endoscopy in the evaluation and management of choledocholithiasis. Gastrointest Endosc. 2019;89(6):1075-1105. PMID 30979521; DOI 10.1016/j.gie.2018.10.001. Manes G, et al. Endoscopic management of common bile duct stones: ESGE guideline. Endoscopy. 2019;51(5):472-491. PMID 30943551; DOI 10.1055/a-0862-0346. Prescott HC, et al. Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2026. Crit Care Med. 2026;54(4):725-812. PMID 41869847; DOI 10.1097/CCM.0000000000007075 (updates Evans L, et al. SSC 2021. Crit Care Med. 2021;49(11):e1063-e1143. PMID 34605781). Source: PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong - anchored on established international society guidelines (TG18, ASGE 2019, ESGE 2019) with GRADE strong recommendations; note several drainage-timing thresholds are strong recommendations resting on low-to-moderate quality (largely observational) evidence, and TG18 severity grading is validated but observational. Sepsis-bundle overlay is Class I (SSC 2026 / CMS SEP-1).
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The collapsed group maps closely to TG18 grade: DRG 444 (w MCC) generally = Grade III severe cholangitis with septic shock or acute organ failure (AKI, respiratory failure, coagulopathy, thrombocytopenia, encephalopathy, bacteremia) -> ICU, urgent drainage, LOS ~5-10+ days, highest mortality; DRG 445 (w CC) approximates Grade II moderate or significant comorbidity -> early drainage, step-down/telemetry, LOS ~4 days; DRG 446 (w/o CC/MCC) = Grade I mild -> antibiotics + timely ERCP on the floor, LOS ~2-3 days. Per scope, severe (Grade III) cholangitis routes to critical care; MCC-tier drivers (organ dysfunction, positive blood cultures) both raise the reimbursement weight and mandate faster source control.
pathway_pearls: 1) Match drainage urgency to TG18 grade - within 12h for septic shock and do not wait for shock to fully resolve before decompressing (biliary drainage IS the definitive resuscitation in refractory cholangitic shock). 2) Grade II moderate cholangitis is where early biliary drainage most clearly reduces 30-day mortality (TG18 evidence) - do not let 'moderate' patients drift. 3) Perform same-admission (or <=2-week) laparoscopic cholecystectomy for gallstone disease - the single biggest lever against biliary readmissions. 4) Prevent iatrogenic harm: rectal indomethacin/diclofenac +/- prophylactic pancreatic-duct stent for post-ERCP pancreatitis, and a stent tracking/registry so no biliary stent is forgotten.

scope_and_volume_notes: Medical biliary tract disorders (choledocholithiasis, cholangitis); ERCP-driven. Severe cholangitis -> Critical Care.
