condition: Gastrointestinal Feeding Tube / Other GI OR Procedures
admission_criteria: Most anal/stomal cases (excisional hemorrhoidectomy, lateral internal sphincterotomy, simple fistulotomy, pilonidal excision) and elective feeding-tube placements are ambulatory/23h-observation; reserve INPATIENT admission for: complex/deep anorectal sepsis (ischiorectal, supralevator, horseshoe abscess) or necrotizing perineal infection/Fournier gangrene; sepsis/SIRS, hemodynamic instability, or immunocompromise/neutropenia/poorly-controlled diabetes needing IV antibiotics; uncontrolled pain, significant hemorrhage, or urinary retention; adhesive small-bowel obstruction (ASBO) requiring OR (lysis of adhesions) or IV resuscitation with serial exams; feeding tube (PEG/PEJ, surgical G/J) placed during an admission for the underlying illness (stroke/dysphagia, head-neck cancer, neuromuscular disease, severe malnutrition) or where aspiration risk/comorbidity precludes same-day discharge; any case needing inpatient recovery, hemodynamic monitoring, or with inability to tolerate PO.
risk_stratification: Caprini VTE RAM: 0-2 low, 3-4 moderate, >=5 high -> add pharmacologic to mechanical prophylaxis and 4-week extended LMWH after abdominopelvic cancer surgery; ASA physical status (>=III flags elevated perioperative risk); ACS-NSQIP Surgical Risk Calculator for predicted morbidity/mortality and disposition; for ASBO/emergency laparotomy the NELA risk model and P-POSSUM (predicted mortality >=5-10% -> consultant-led, ICU/goal-directed care); sepsis screening qSOFA >=2 or NEWS2 >=5 with lactate >2 mmol/L -> escalate and pursue source control; nutritional risk NRS-2002 >=3 (>=5 high) plus malnutrition markers (>10% weight loss, low albumin/prealbumin) -> dietitian and refeeding watch (drives the feeding-tube cohort); Clinical Frailty Scale >=5 predicts complications and prolonged LOS.
initial_workup_labs: CBC (leukocytosis, anemia); CMP/BMP (electrolytes, renal function, glucose); coagulation INR/aPTT (periprocedural and reversal planning); type-and-screen or crossmatch; lactate (>2 escalate, >4 shock/ischemia); baseline CRP; blood cultures plus wound/tissue cultures incl. group A Streptococcus if septic/necrotizing; VBG; nutrition panel for feeding-tube/malnourished patients (albumin/prealbumin, phosphate, magnesium, potassium, HbA1c) to pre-empt refeeding syndrome; beta-hCG in women of childbearing age. Triggers: Hgb <7 g/dL -> restrictive transfusion; K/Mg/Phos repletion; AKI -> renal dosing and volume optimization; hyperlactatemia -> resuscitation plus urgent source control.
initial_imaging_dx: ASBO: supine/upright abdominal radiograph first-line, then CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast as the diagnostic standard (transition point, closed-loop, strangulation/ischemia, etiology) obtained promptly/within hours when ischemia is suspected or the cause is unclear; water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) challenge with follow-up films at ~8h and 24h — failure of contrast to reach the colon by 24h predicts need for operation (challenge is both diagnostic and therapeutic). Anorectal sepsis: exam +/- exam-under-anesthesia; pelvic MRI for complex/recurrent fistula and deep/supralevator disease; CT or transperineal ultrasound for extent — do not delay OR for necrotizing infection. Feeding tube: endoscopic (PEG) placement with intraprocedural confirmation, radiograph if malposition suspected; video-fluoroscopic swallow study/SLP for dysphagia etiology. Preop ECG and CXR per risk.
time_critical_interventions: Surgical antimicrobial prophylaxis within 60 min before incision (cefazolin +/- metronidazole; vancomycin or a fluoroquinolone within 120 min), redose cefazolin every ~4h intra-op or after EBL >1500 mL; septic anorectal/perineal infection (Fournier/necrotizing): Surviving Sepsis/SEP-1 bundle — broad-spectrum IV antibiotics <=1h, lactate plus blood cultures, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid for hypoperfusion, and EMERGENT surgical source control/debridement (do not delay, ideally <=6-12h); ASBO with strangulation, closed-loop, ischemia, or peritonitis -> emergent laparotomy/laparoscopy, otherwise a structured non-operative trial with NGT decompression and an operative decision point at 48-72h; post-PEG initiate enteral feeds within 4h (ASGE — no intentional delay).
initial_management: Antibiotics — prophylaxis cefazolin 2 g (3 g if >=120 kg) +/- metronidazole 500 mg for enteric/colorectal contamination, duration <24h; therapeutic for anorectal/intra-abdominal sepsis: piperacillin-tazobactam, or ceftriaxone + metronidazole, or ertapenem, adding vancomycin (MRSA) + clindamycin (toxin suppression) for necrotizing infection. Fluids — balanced crystalloid (LR/Plasma-Lyte) to euvolemia, ERAS near-zero fluid balance (avoid over-resuscitation). Transfusion — restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 with cardiac disease). Analgesia — multimodal opioid-sparing: scheduled acetaminophen + NSAID/ketorolac, local/regional (TAP block; pudendal/local infiltration for anorectal), opioids as rescue only; stool softeners/fiber and sitz baths for anorectal. Anticoagulation — PEG does not require holding antiplatelets, individualize anticoagulant management; reversal warfarin -> vitamin K/4F-PCC, DOAC -> andexanet/idarucizumab if urgent. Source control — abscess I&D, debridement of necrotizing tissue, adhesiolysis with resection of nonviable bowel, feeding-tube placement; NGT decompression + bowel rest for SBO; electrolyte repletion; glucose 140-180 mg/dL.
day1_milestones: POD0: definitive source control achieved and documented; prophylactic antibiotics completed intra-op with normothermia/euvolemia maintained; multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia and PONV prophylaxis started; post-PEG feeds begun within 4h with head-of-bed >=30 degrees and aspiration precautions; early mobilization out of bed the evening of surgery plus incentive spirometry; VTE prophylaxis (mechanical SCDs + pharmacologic per Caprini) initiated; avoid/limit Foley, track I/O, maintain euglycemia; for non-operative ASBO — NGT to suction, serial abdominal exams, and begin the Gastrografin challenge.
day2_milestones: POD1: stop prophylactic antibiotics (<24h) unless established infection; advance diet per ERAS early oral intake and assess return of bowel function (flatus), using coffee/chewing gum to speed ileus resolution; advance tube feeds toward goal rate; remove Foley (target POD1) and reassess for retention; transition IV to oral analgesia; ambulate >=3x/day; for ASBO reassess — if Gastrografin reached the colon with clinical improvement resume diet, if unresolved by 48h plan operative adhesiolysis.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ to discharge readiness: tolerating adequate oral intake or established at goal tube feeds, pain controlled on an oral regimen, ambulating at/near baseline, bowel function returned; feeding-tube patients — caregiver tube-care competency confirmed and dysphagia/aspiration plan set; complicated anorectal sepsis — afebrile, wound-packing/sitz-bath plan, oral antibiotic transition if indicated, seton/EUA follow-up arranged; escalate imaging (CT) if failure to progress (occult abscess, anastomotic leak, persistent/recurrent obstruction); complete disposition and transitions-of-care planning.
consults_multidisciplinary: Colorectal/general surgery (primary); GI/therapeutic endoscopy or interventional radiology for gastrostomy access; registered dietitian/nutrition support early (all feeding-tube and malnourished patients, refeeding monitoring); speech-language pathology (dysphagia/swallow eval before/after feeding tube); PT/OT (mobility, especially post-stroke/debilitated); wound-ostomy-continence nurse (stoma creation/revision/closure, complex perineal wounds); pharmacy (antibiotic stewardship, anticoagulation, electrolyte/refeeding); case management/social work early (home enteral nutrition, DME, SNF/home-health disposition); pain/anesthesia (regional blocks); infectious diseases for necrotizing/resistant infection; palliative care/ethics for goals-of-care before PEG in advanced or terminal illness.
monitoring_level: Surgical floor for routine anal/stomal procedures and uncomplicated feeding-tube placement; telemetry/step-down for hemodynamic lability, significant cardiopulmonary comorbidity, or post-emergency-laparotomy recovery; ICU for septic shock, Fournier/necrotizing perineal infection, bowel ischemia or emergency laparotomy with MCC, vasopressor or ventilatory support, or high predicted mortality (NELA/P-POSSUM). Escalation triggers: NEWS2 >=5 or qSOFA >=2, rising lactate >2, hypotension/oliguria, uncontrolled pain, new peritoneal signs, feeding-tube dislodgement with peritonitis, or GI hemorrhage.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition — ERAS early oral intake (clears-to-regular POD0-1, no prolonged NPO), preop carbohydrate loading for elective cases, chewing gum/coffee for ileus; feeding tube — start feeds <=4h post-PEG and advance to goal, monitor phosphate/magnesium/potassium for refeeding in the malnourished. Mobility — out of bed POD0, ambulate >=3x/day. VTE — mechanical SCDs for all plus pharmacologic LMWH/UFH when Caprini >=3-5, with 4-week extended LMWH after abdominopelvic cancer surgery. Glucose — target 140-180 mg/dL. Lines/catheters — remove Foley by POD1, early IV-to-PO conversion, minimize NGT duration, and remove central lines when no longer needed.
discharge_criteria: Tolerating adequate oral intake OR established at goal tube feeds with demonstrated caregiver competency; pain controlled on an oral regimen; return of bowel function (flatus/BM) without obstruction; afebrile with stable vitals and infection source-controlled on an appropriate antibiotic plan; ambulating at baseline; spontaneously voiding; wound/stoma/tube care understood via teach-back; and no unresolved acute complication.
discharge_planning: Disposition home with home-health for enteral nutrition/wound care/DME, or SNF/LTAC for debilitated feeding-tube patients; establish home enteral nutrition (formula, pump, supplies) with tube-care and troubleshooting teaching plus aspiration precautions; medication reconciliation including bowel regimen (fiber/softeners/sitz baths for anorectal), analgesia taper, and anticoagulation resumption plan; schedule follow-up — surgery clinic 1-2 weeks (wound/seton/EUA), GI for tube management, PCP, and specialty for the underlying disease (oncology/neurology/stroke); teach-back education with explicit red-flag return precautions (fever, bleeding, tube dislodgement/leakage, obstructive symptoms, uncontrolled pain).
expected_los: Wide by severity tier (CMS GMLOS/AMLOS): anal/stomal DRG 349 no CC/MCC GMLOS 1.9d (AMLOS 2.3), DRG 348 with CC 3.3d (4.1), DRG 347 with MCC 5.6d (7.5); other digestive-system OR DRG 358 no CC/MCC GMLOS 2.8d (3.5), DRG 357 with CC 4.6d (5.9), DRG 356 with MCC 7.7d (AMLOS 10.5).
target_los: Under an ERAS fast-track: no-CC/MCC anal/stomal and elective feeding tubes are largely ambulatory/23h to <=1 day; simple PEG/feeding tube 1-2 days; other digestive-system OR without CC/MCC ~2 days; uncomplicated laparoscopic adhesiolysis ~3-4 days; CC tier ~2-3 days (anal/stomal) to ~4 days (other GI); MCC-driven cases individualized ~5-7 days — overall aim ~15-25% below GMLOS.
quality_metrics: SCIP/CMS perioperative measures: prophylactic antibiotic within 1h (Inf-1), appropriate selection (Inf-2), discontinuation <=24h (Inf-3), VTE prophylaxis ordered/received (VTE-1/2), perioperative euglycemia and normothermia; NHSN/ACS-NSQIP surgical-site infection (incl. colon SSI), anastomotic leak, unplanned return to OR/reoperation, and unplanned ICU admission; SEP-1 bundle compliance when sepsis is present; CAUTI and CLABSI; ERAS protocol-element compliance; 30-day readmission and 30-day mortality (PEG 30-day mortality is high in appropriately selected debilitated patients and is a key outcome metric).
common_complications: Surgical-site/wound infection in contaminated anorectal and enteric fields (mitigated by timely prophylaxis + source control); postoperative/prolonged ileus (opioid-sparing analgesia, early feeding, gum); anorectal — urinary retention, hemorrhage, fecal impaction, recurrent/persistent abscess-fistula, incontinence; feeding tube — peristomal wound infection, tube dislodgement/migration, buried-bumper syndrome, aspiration pneumonia, leakage, colocutaneous fistula, refeeding syndrome; ASBO/adhesiolysis — inadvertent enterotomy/bowel injury, anastomotic leak, recurrent obstruction, short-bowel after extensive resection; plus VTE, PONV, AKI, and postoperative delirium in the elderly.
readmission_drivers: Feeding-tube cohort — tube dislodgement/malfunction, aspiration pneumonia, peristomal infection, dehydration/electrolyte derangement, recurrent obstruction, and progression of the underlying disease (the dominant driver, reflected in high 30-day mortality); anorectal — recurrent or inadequately drained abscess, uncontrolled pain, bleeding, urinary retention, fistula recurrence; ASBO — recurrent obstruction. Prevention levers: structured caregiver tube-care education plus home-health, early surgical follow-up/EUA, aspiration precautions and dietitian follow-up, medication reconciliation, explicit red-flag instructions, and goals-of-care alignment to avoid non-beneficial PEG in advanced illness.
guideline_source: ERAS Society — Elective Colorectal Surgery 2018/2019 and Emergency Laparotomy 2023 (perioperative pathway); ASGE Guideline on Gastrostomy Feeding Tubes 2024 (with ESPEN/ESGE enteral-tube guidance) for feeding-tube placement; ASCRS Clinical Practice Guidelines — Anorectal Abscess, Fistula-in-Ano, and Rectovaginal Fistula 2022 and Hemorrhoids 2024 (anal/stomal procedures); WSES Bologna Guidelines for Adhesive Small Bowel Obstruction, 2017 update (lysis of adhesions); ASHP/IDSA/SIS/SHEA Clinical Practice Guidelines for Antimicrobial Prophylaxis in Surgery 2013; Caprini RAM / ACCP-CHEST for surgical VTE prophylaxis.

evidence_grade: Moderate overall — each component rests on strong, current society guidelines (ERAS, ASCRS, ASGE, WSES, ASHP/IDSA with GRADE/Class I recommendations), but because this is a collapsed, heterogeneous multi-procedure DRG group with no single unifying trial, the composite pathway is assembled by extrapolation across guidelines; procedure-specific recommendations are strong while the aggregate pathway is moderate.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: CC/MCC tier drives nearly all LOS and resource variation. Without CC/MCC (349/358): floor care, ERAS fast-track, ambulatory-to-2.8d, target <=1-2d. With CC (348/357): GMLOS 3.3-4.6d, active comorbidity/complication management, possible telemetry. With MCC (347/356): GMLOS 5.6-7.7d (AMLOS to 10.5), reflecting sepsis/Fournier gangrene, bowel ischemia/resection, aspiration pneumonia, malnutrition and organ dysfunction -> step-down/ICU and materially higher mortality (notably emergency laparotomy and PEG in advanced illness). The severity of the underlying illness — not the technical procedure — governs the tier.
pathway_pearls: 1) The operation is often minor but LOS is driven by the underlying illness and severity tier — optimize nutrition (dietitian, NRS-2002) and, before any PEG, align goals-of-care/palliative/ethics to avoid non-beneficial tubes (30-day mortality is high in debilitated patients). 2) Never delay source control in anorectal/perineal sepsis — early EUA/I&D, and for Fournier/necrotizing infection emergent debridement plus a <=1h sepsis bundle. 3) ERAS the entire group — prophylactic antibiotic <=60 min and stop <24h, opioid-sparing multimodal plus local/regional analgesia, early feeding (enteral feeds <=4h post-PEG per ASGE), Caprini-based VTE prophylaxis, and early Foley removal. 4) In ASBO the water-soluble contrast (Gastrografin) challenge is both diagnostic and therapeutic — operate for strangulation/closed-loop/peritonitis or non-resolution by 48-72h, and favor laparoscopic adhesiolysis in selected simple cases.

admission_criteria: Admit when: quantitative blood loss (QBL) >=1000 mL or ongoing/recurrent bleeding with hemodynamic instability (shock index HR/SBP >=0.9, SBP <90, HR >110); symptomatic anemia or Hgb requiring transfusion (Hgb <7 g/dL, or <8 with symptoms/ongoing loss); suspected/confirmed retained products of conception (RPOC) needing uterine evacuation; postpartum endometritis with temp >=38.0C x2 (>6h apart) plus uterine tenderness/foul lochia not controllable outpatient, sepsis, or inability to tolerate PO; septic abortion; secondary (24h-12wk) PPH; failed ED/outpatient therapy. ED-discharge/observation appropriate for: minor secondary bleed with stable vitals, Hgb >8, no RPOC on ultrasound, tolerating orals; mild endometritis able to complete oral antibiotics with 24-48h follow-up.
risk_stratification: CMQCC hemorrhage risk assessment on admission and post-delivery (low/medium/high) drives type-and-screen vs crossmatch; CMQCC stage-based response: Stage 1 = QBL >500 mL vaginal / >1000 mL cesarean or abnormal vitals; Stage 2 = continued bleeding up to 1500 mL; Stage 3 = >1500 mL or >=2 U RBC or unstable vitals/DIC -> activate massive transfusion protocol (MTP). Shock Index (HR/SBP) >=0.9-1.0 predicts transfusion/ICU need. Maternal Early Warning Criteria/Trigger (MEWC/MEWT): SBP <90 or >160, HR <50 or >120, RR <10 or >30, SpO2 <95%, oliguria, altered mentation -> immediate bedside evaluation. Sepsis: obstetrically-modified qSOFA/SOFA and serum lactate >2 (>=4 mmol/L = hypoperfusion) trigger bundle activation.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with serial H/H and platelets; type-and-crossmatch; coagulation panel PT/aPTT/INR and fibrinogen (OB-specific target >200 mg/dL because fibrinogen is physiologically elevated in pregnancy; give cryoprecipitate if <150-200); serum lactate (repeat to document clearance); BMP/renal function and LFTs; DIC panel/D-dimer if oozing/microvascular bleeding; blood and urine cultures plus lactate drawn BEFORE antibiotics when infection/sepsis suspected; beta-hCG if RPOC or gestational trophoblastic disease suspected; blood group and Rh (give RhIg if Rh-negative). Fibrinogen <200 mg/dL or platelets <50k trigger targeted product replacement.
initial_imaging_dx: Pelvic ultrasound (transabdominal + transvaginal) is first-line, obtained same-day, for RPOC (echogenic endometrial mass with Doppler vascularity), retained clot, subinvolution, or hematoma when secondary PPH is suspected; CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast if pelvic abscess, expanding hematoma, or septic pelvic thrombophlebitis suspected; CT angiography or catheter angiography when an arterial source is identified for IR embolization; bedside exam under anesthesia/speculum exam for laceration or retained tissue. Point-of-care ultrasound and manual uterine exam at bedside during active hemorrhage.
time_critical_interventions: Sepsis bundle (SMFM/Surviving Sepsis): broad-spectrum antibiotics <=1h of recognition; lactate and cultures before antibiotics; 1-2 L (30 mL/kg) balanced crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4 mmol/L; reassess and start norepinephrine (first-line) if MAP <65 after fluids; source control ASAP. Hemorrhage: tranexamic acid 1 g IV within 3h of onset (WOMAN trial - benefit is time-dependent) and repeat 1 g at 30 min if bleeding continues; escalate uterotonics and achieve source control/tamponade within minutes; activate MTP for Stage 3; move unstable RPOC to prompt uterine evacuation without delay; IR uterine artery embolization for stable persistent arterial bleeding.
initial_management: Uterotonics for atony: oxytocin 10-40 U in 500-1000 mL crystalloid IV (or 10 U IM) first-line; methylergonovine 0.2 mg IM q2-4h (avoid in HTN/preeclampsia); carboprost/15-methyl-PGF2alpha 0.25 mg IM q15-90min, max 8 doses (avoid in asthma); misoprostol 600-1000 mcg PO/SL/PR. Tranexamic acid 1 g IV. Fundal massage + bimanual uterine compression. Tamponade: Bakri balloon or intrauterine vacuum-induced device (Jada). Transfuse to ongoing clinical loss using 1:1:1 (RBC:FFP:platelets) MTP; cryoprecipitate/fibrinogen concentrate for fibrinogen <150-200 mg/dL; restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL threshold once bleeding controlled. Source control: uterine curettage for RPOC, laceration repair, hematoma drainage. Endometritis: clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h + gentamicin 5 mg/kg IV q24h (+/- ampicillin for enterococcal coverage if no response in 48-72h). Refractory hemorrhage: B-Lynch compression suture, uterine artery ligation/embolization, hysterectomy (definitive). RhIg 300 mcg if Rh-negative.
day1_milestones: Achieve hemostasis and hemodynamic stability (MAP >=65, HR <100, down-trending lactate, urine output >=0.5 mL/kg/h); complete source control (evacuation/repair/tamponade); start/confirm empiric antibiotics with cultures pending for infection; transfuse to targets and recheck H/H, fibrinogen, platelets; establish 2 large-bore IVs; strict I/O with QBL/pad counts; mechanical VTE prophylaxis (SCDs); multimodal pain control; MFM and OB-anesthesia at bedside; document CMQCC hemorrhage stage and conduct team huddle/debrief.
day2_milestones: Hemodynamically stable and off vasopressors; no ongoing bleeding (minimal lochia, firm well-contracted fundus); afebrile trend with down-trending WBC and cleared lactate; remove or deflate tamponade balloon (typically 12-24h); advance diet; add pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin) once hemorrhage controlled; start oral iron for anemia repletion; remove Foley catheter; ambulate; lactation support; reassess Hgb and transfusion need. For endometritis, continue IV clindamycin+gentamicin (no routine oral step-down course needed per Cochrane once improving).
day3plus_milestones: Sustained stability >=24h without bleeding; for endometritis remain afebrile >=24-48h before stopping IV antibiotics (uncomplicated cases treated IV to clinical improvement require no oral course - Cochrane); Hgb stable/adequate with iron-repletion plan; culture-directed narrowing of antibiotics; repeat imaging confirms no residual RPOC or abscess if applicable; tolerating regular diet, voiding, ambulating, pain controlled on oral analgesics; VTE prophylaxis plan set for discharge; finalize POST-BIRTH warning-sign teaching and follow-up appointments.
consults_multidisciplinary: OB-anesthesia and Maternal-Fetal Medicine early for hemorrhage/sepsis; interventional radiology for uterine artery embolization/balloon; blood bank/transfusion medicine for MTP; gynecologic surgery for operative management or hysterectomy; critical care for shock, vasopressors, or organ failure; infectious disease for refractory/complex sepsis; hematology for DIC/coagulopathy; pharmacy for antibiotic dosing, stewardship, and MTP support; nutrition for anemia; lactation consultant; social work/case management for transitions, transportation, home support; behavioral health for postpartum mood screening.
monitoring_level: Floor/postpartum unit for stable patients after hemostasis. Step-down/telemetry for large-volume transfusion, resolving shock, or arrhythmia risk. ICU for ongoing/massive hemorrhage requiring MTP, septic shock on vasopressors, DIC, ARDS/respiratory failure, AKI, or peripartum hysterectomy. Escalate per MEWC/MEWT (SBP <90 or >160, HR <50 or >120, RR <10 or >30, SpO2 <95%, oliguria, altered mentation), shock index >=0.9, lactate not clearing, or recurrent bleeding.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Advance from clear liquids to regular diet as nausea/ileus resolves and surgical status allows; early ambulation within 12-24h. VTE: mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs) immediately given high postpartum thrombotic risk; add pharmacologic LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily, weight-adjusted) once hemorrhage is controlled, per RCOG/ACOG risk stratification (cesarean, hemorrhage, obesity, immobility raise risk). Glycemic target 140-180 mg/dL if hyperglycemic/septic. Remove Foley and unnecessary lines early to reduce infection; oral iron (add IV iron if severe/symptomatic anemia) for repletion.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable >=24h with no active bleeding (firm fundus, normal-volume lochia); Hgb stable and acceptable (typically >=7-8 g/dL) with an iron-repletion plan; afebrile >=24h and clinically improved on infection therapy; tolerating regular diet, voiding spontaneously, ambulating independently, pain controlled on oral analgesics; no evidence of retained products or abscess on imaging when indicated; discharge VTE prophylaxis plan established; reliable follow-up arranged and patient educated on postpartum warning signs.
discharge_planning: Disposition home with support; medication reconciliation (complete antibiotic plan, oral iron, analgesia, continued VTE prophylaxis if indicated, RhIg documented for Rh-negative); schedule early follow-up - nurse/phone contact within 3 days to 3 weeks plus comprehensive postpartum ('fourth trimester') visit by 6-12 weeks per ACOG; provide AWHONN POST-BIRTH warning-sign education (bleeding soaking >1 pad/hr or passing large clots, fever, foul discharge, calf pain/dyspnea, severe headache/vision change, chest pain); lactation and contraception counseling; postpartum depression screening (EPDS); confirm transportation and pharmacy access; clear return precautions.
expected_los: MS-DRG 776 (postpartum/post-abortion WITHOUT OR procedure): ~2.4-2.6 days GMLOS (arithmetic mean ~3 days), CMS relative weight ~0.72. MS-DRG 769 (WITH OR procedure): ~3.4-3.6 days GMLOS (arithmetic mean ~4.3 days), relative weight ~1.6, per CMS IPPS benchmarks. Sepsis/ICU-level complications extend stay.
target_los: 776: 2 days; 769: 3 days under an optimized stage-based hemorrhage and sepsis-bundle pathway with early source control, protocolized ratio-based transfusion, and structured discharge, assuming no ICU-level complications.
quality_metrics: AIM Obstetric Hemorrhage and Sepsis-in-Obstetric-Care bundle compliance; Joint Commission maternal safety standards (hemorrhage PC.06.01.01); CMS Maternal Morbidity structural measure; severe maternal morbidity (SMM) rate including non-transfusion SMM; hemorrhage risk assessment on admission; quantitative blood loss (QBL) documentation rate; MTP activation and time-to-first-unit; time-to-antibiotics <=1h in sepsis (SEP-1 principles); appropriate blood-product utilization/ratio; 30-day readmission rate; postpartum follow-up visit attendance.
common_complications: Hemorrhagic shock and DIC (mitigate: QBL, early TXA, 1:1:1 MTP, fibrinogen replacement); need for hysterectomy (stepwise uterotonics/tamponade/embolization first); transfusion reactions/TACO-TRALI (judicious ratio-based transfusion); acute kidney injury and Sheehan syndrome/pituitary necrosis (aggressive perfusion/BP support); persistent or secondary bleeding from RPOC (complete evacuation, confirm on ultrasound); endometritis progressing to sepsis (early antibiotics + source control); VTE (risk-based prophylaxis); iron-deficiency anemia (repletion).
readmission_drivers: Endometritis and surgical-site/wound infection (adequate antibiotic course, wound care); secondary/delayed PPH from retained products or subinvolution (confirm complete evacuation, ultrasound before discharge); persistent anemia/fatigue (iron repletion, transfuse when indicated); VTE (risk-based prophylaxis, education); postpartum preeclampsia/severe hypertension (BP check within 3-7 days); mastitis/breast abscess; inadequate pain control; postpartum mood crises. Levers: POST-BIRTH warning-sign education, early follow-up contact within 3 days-3 weeks, warm handoff to OB and primary care.
guideline_source: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 183, Postpartum Hemorrhage (2017, reaffirmed); CMQCC Obstetric Hemorrhage Toolkit v3.0 (2022) with the AIM Obstetric Hemorrhage patient safety bundle; SMFM Consult Series #47 (2019) and #67 (2023) plus the AIM Consensus Bundle on Sepsis in Obstetric Care (2023) for maternal sepsis; Cochrane review (Mackeen, 2015) for postpartum endometritis antibiotics.
key_citations: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 183: Postpartum Hemorrhage, Obstet Gynecol 2017;130(4):e168-e186 (PubMed PMID 28937571; DOI 10.1097/AOG.0000000000002351). WOMAN Trial Collaborators, Lancet 2017;389(10084):2105-2116 (PMID 28456509; DOI 10.1016/S0140-6736(17)30638-4) - TXA 1 g IV within 3h reduces death from bleeding. Mackeen AD et al., Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2015;(2):CD001067 (PMID 25922861; DOI 10.1002/14651858.CD001067.pub3) - clindamycin+gentamicin superior for endometritis. SMFM Consult Series #47, Am J Obstet Gynecol 2019;220(4):B2-B10 (PMID 30684460; DOI 10.1016/j.ajog.2019.01.216). AIM Consensus Bundle on Sepsis in Obstetric Care, Obstet Gynecol 2023;142(3):481-492 (PMID 37590980; DOI 10.1097/AOG.0000000000005304). Citations retrieved from PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong for the core levers (Class I / Level A society guidance plus RCT): active management of the third stage with uterotonics, tranexamic acid within 3h (WOMAN RCT), clindamycin+gentamicin for endometritis (Cochrane meta-analysis), and antibiotics <=1h in maternal sepsis (SMFM GRADE 1B). Moderate for the stage-based hemorrhage protocols and MTP ratios (CMQCC/AIM - largely consensus plus quality-improvement evidence). LOS targets are operational benchmarks, not guideline-defined.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MS-DRGs 769 and 776 are single, non-CC/MCC-subdivided DRGs; severity in this collapsed group is captured by the presence (769) versus absence (776) of an OR procedure rather than by CC/MCC tiers. MCC-level complications - sepsis/septic shock, DIC, acute respiratory failure, AKI, hemorrhagic shock - drive ICU admission, vasopressors, and 2-4+ additional inpatient days but do not by themselves reclassify these DRGs; a peripartum hysterectomy or laparotomy moves the case into the OR-procedure group (769) and lengthens stay. In APR-DRG systems these same complications raise severity-of-illness and risk-of-mortality subclasses.
pathway_pearls: 1) Use QUANTITATIVE blood loss (not visual estimate) with a stage-based CMQCC response, a ready hemorrhage cart, and a pre-activated MTP - recognition/response delays are the dominant preventable-death driver. 2) Give tranexamic acid 1 g IV EARLY (<3h from onset) - efficacy is time-dependent (WOMAN trial). 3) In obstetric sepsis: antibiotics <=1h + lactate/cultures + prompt source control (evacuate retained products, drain abscess); clindamycin+gentamicin is the evidence-based endometritis regimen. 4) Do not under-treat postpartum VTE risk or discharge without iron repletion, ultrasound confirmation of complete uterine evacuation, and POST-BIRTH warning-sign education with early follow-up.
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Postpartum/post-abortion diagnoses with (769) and without (776) OR procedure: hemorrhage, endometritis, retained products, readmissions.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 769 — Postpartum and Post Abortion Diagnoses with O.R. Procedures; 776 — Postpartum and Post Abortion Diagnoses without O.R. Procedures
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 5/5 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Current pass completed citation-resolvability, coding, taxonomy, schema, and consistency checks. The source workflow contains no completed adversarial high-stakes clinical verdict for this row; clinician revalidation remains required before protocol or order-set use.
data_quality_notes: Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk.

mdc: 21 — Injuries, Poisonings & Toxic Effects of Drugs
admission_criteria: Most allergic reactions/anaphylaxis are ED-treated and discharged after risk-based observation; reserve INPATIENT admission for objective instability. Admit if: refractory anaphylaxis (persistent symptoms after >=3 appropriate IM epinephrine doses) or need for IV epinephrine infusion/vasopressors; airway/laryngeal or oropharyngeal angioedema threatening airway (stridor, voice change, tongue/floor-of-mouth swelling) needing airway watch or intubation; persistent hypotension/shock or hypoxia after initial resuscitation; biphasic or protracted reaction in ED; high-risk comorbidity amplifying danger (severe/brittle asthma, CAD/arrhythmia, beta-blocker/ACEi use, pregnancy, mastocytosis). Critical care if intubated, on epinephrine infusion/pressors, or hemodynamically unstable. ED discharge/observation is appropriate when there is a prompt, complete, durable response to IM epinephrine without airway or hemodynamic compromise.
risk_stratification: No single validated ED anaphylaxis mortality score; stratify by diagnostic criteria + severity grade + biphasic predictors. Apply NIAID/FAAN anaphylaxis diagnostic criteria (revised to 2 criteria in 2023 JTFPP; severe GI symptoms added). Severity: WAO/Ring-Messmer grade I-IV or Brown grading (mild=skin/GI; moderate=respiratory/CV features; severe=hypoxia, hypotension, neuro compromise). Hypotension threshold: SBP <90 mmHg (adult) or >30% drop from baseline. Biphasic-reaction risk factors (Simard 2021 systematic review; drive observation length): delayed/inadequate first epinephrine, >=2 epinephrine doses required, severe initial presentation, prior anaphylaxis history, unknown trigger, wide pulse pressure. Fatal-anaphylaxis risk markers: asthma, cardiovascular disease, beta-blocker/ACEi use, mastocytosis/elevated baseline tryptase, older age, delayed epinephrine, upright posture.
initial_workup_labs: Diagnosis is CLINICAL - never delay epinephrine for labs. Serum tryptase drawn 15 min-3 h after symptom onset with a paired baseline level >=24 h later/at follow-up; acute rise >= (1.2 x baseline + 2 ng/mL) or level >11.4 ng/mL supports mast-cell activation and triggers mastocytosis/hereditary alpha-tryptasemia work-up. If admitted/unstable: CBC, CMP (electrolytes, renal, glucose - watch steroid hyperglycemia), lactate and VBG/ABG if shock or hypoxia. For bradykinin-mediated angioedema without urticaria: C4 (low), C1-INH antigen and function, C1q (if acquired suspected) -> HAE/AAE evaluation. Troponin/ECG if chest pain, repeated epinephrine, or CAD (Kounis syndrome/demand ischemia).
initial_imaging_dx: No routine imaging for uncomplicated anaphylaxis. Continuous pulse oximetry, cardiac telemetry, and frequent non-invasive BP for all moderate/severe cases. ECG at presentation if cardiac risk, chest pain, or repeated epinephrine (ischemia/arrhythmia surveillance). For airway angioedema: direct exam and flexible fiberoptic nasolaryngoscopy to grade supraglottic/laryngeal involvement and guide the airway plan; portable CXR only for aspiration, pulmonary edema, or ETT confirmation. Soft-tissue neck CT reserved for atypical/mass-effect angioedema after the airway is secured.
time_critical_interventions: Epinephrine is the ONLY first-line, time-critical drug - give IM immediately on recognition; target door-to-epinephrine in minutes, before and independent of antihistamines or steroids. Anterolateral thigh (vastus lateralis) IM 0.01 mg/kg of 1 mg/mL (1:1000), max 0.5 mg adult / 0.3 mg child; repeat every 5-15 min for inadequate response (most respond to <=2 doses). Position supine with legs elevated (recumbent if vomiting; do NOT stand or sit abruptly - fatal empty-ventricle/empty-vena-cava risk). Simultaneous ABCs: high-flow O2, 2 large-bore IVs, rapid crystalloid bolus for hypotension. Early anticipatory airway control for progressive laryngeal edema (difficult-airway setup, most experienced operator). Escalate to IV epinephrine infusion within minutes for refractory shock.
initial_management: First-line: Epinephrine (alpha/beta agonist) IM as above; refractory shock -> IV epinephrine infusion ~0.05-0.1 mcg/kg/min (e.g., 2-10 mcg/min adult) titrated to MAP, add norepinephrine/vasopressin if still hypotensive. Fluids: isotonic crystalloid (NS or LR) 1-2 L rapid IV bolus (up to 20 mL/kg, repeat) for hypotension. Glucagon 1-5 mg IV bolus then infusion for beta-blocked patients refractory to epinephrine. Bronchospasm: nebulized beta2-agonist (albuterol/salbutamol) as adjunct. Adjuncts (SECOND/third-line, never substitute for or delay epinephrine): H1-antihistamine (cetirizine PO / diphenhydramine 25-50 mg IV) for cutaneous itch/urticaria only; H2-antihistamine (famotidine); systemic corticosteroid (methylprednisolone 1-2 mg/kg or prednisone 0.5-1 mg/kg) - no longer routinely recommended and NOT proven to prevent biphasic reactions. Source control: remove stinger/trigger, stop culprit drug/infusion. ACEi-angioedema (bradykinin, no urticaria): permanently STOP ACEi + airway watch; icatibant/ecallantide/C1-INH/FFP NOT proven (negative CAMEO RCT). HAE attack: C1-INH concentrate, icatibant, or ecallantide (epinephrine/antihistamine/steroid ineffective).
day1_milestones: Confirm sustained resolution of airway, respiratory, and hemodynamic compromise; wean epinephrine infusion/vasopressors; extubate when airway edema resolved and cuff-leak positive. Continuous telemetry/pulse-ox with active biphasic-reaction watch (peak risk first 6-12 h). Identify and document trigger; permanently remove/discontinue culprit (drug, food, venom, ACEi). Draw acute serum tryptase if not already done. Initiate avoidance measures and add allergy to the EHR allergy list/wristband. Begin patient/caregiver education and confirm the epinephrine autoinjector plan. Place allergy/immunology consult (recurrent, idiopathic, occupational, venom, or perioperative cases).
day2_milestones: Off epinephrine infusion/vasopressors >=24 h; stable airway, normotensive, normoxic on room air; no recurrent or biphasic symptoms. Transition IV to oral therapy and downgrade to observation/floor; discontinue continuous monitoring if stable and low-risk. Complete tryptase pairing (baseline >=24 h) if indicated. Finalize trigger identification; adjust chronic meds (substitute non-ACEi antihypertensive; reassess beta-blocker risk/benefit). Prescribe and TEACH 2 epinephrine autoinjectors with hands-on teach-back; deliver written anaphylaxis emergency action plan; confirm allergist follow-up appointment.
day3plus_milestones: Reserved for complicated courses (post-intubation airway injury, aspiration/pneumonia, prolonged shock, MCC-tier). Ensure resolution of complications, return to baseline oxygenation/hemodynamics, tolerating diet, and ambulating. Complete mastocytosis/HAE work-up if flagged (persistent tryptase elevation, recurrent bradykinin angioedema). Verify autoinjector competency via teach-back, arrange venom immunotherapy referral where indicated, and finalize definitive follow-up before discharge. Most uncomplicated cases are discharge-ready well before Day 3.
consults_multidisciplinary: Allergy/Immunology - all confirmed anaphylaxis (trigger confirmation, tryptase interpretation, venom/food/drug testing, immunotherapy, autoinjector education), during stay or early outpatient. Critical Care/Anesthesia - airway management, epinephrine infusion, intubation for airway angioedema. ENT - fiberoptic airway assessment and surgical-airway backup for laryngeal/oropharyngeal angioedema. Pharmacy - epinephrine dosing/infusion, drug-allergy labeling, deprescribe ACEi/culprit, autoinjector counseling. Care management/social work - autoinjector affordability and access, EMS plan, school/work action plans. Nutrition/dietitian - allergen avoidance and food-label reading for food-triggered cases. Cardiology if Kounis syndrome or epinephrine-related ischemia.
monitoring_level: ICU/critical care - intubated airway angioedema, epinephrine infusion or vasopressor-dependent shock, refractory/protracted anaphylaxis. Step-down/telemetry - required repeat epinephrine, transient hypotension/hypoxia now resolved, significant cardiopulmonary comorbidity, or airway angioedema not yet resolved. ED-observation/floor with continuous pulse-ox + telemetry - moderate reactions responding to treatment, during the biphasic-watch window. Escalation triggers: recurrent/worsening airway (stridor, dysphonia), SBP <90 or >30% drop, SpO2 <92%, need for repeat/continuous epinephrine, any biphasic symptom. De-escalate/discharge only after a symptom-free, intervention-free window matched to risk (~1 h low-risk single-dose responder; >=6 h high-risk).
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet - NPO while airway/aspiration risk or possible intubation; advance to regular allergen-free diet (strictly avoiding the culprit food) once airway secure and alert. Mobility - early ambulation once hemodynamically stable, but keep supine with legs elevated during active hypotension and avoid abrupt upright posture. VTE - most cases are short-stay/ambulatory (low risk); apply Padua/Caprini risk assessment for admitted patients and use mechanical +/- pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin/heparin) only if immobile or high-risk. Glucose - monitor for corticosteroid-induced hyperglycemia if steroids were given. Lines/catheters - remove IV and urinary catheters promptly once off infusions and stable.
discharge_criteria: Complete, durable resolution of airway, respiratory, and cardiovascular symptoms with a symptom-free and intervention-free observation window matched to risk (~1 h after single-dose response in low-risk patients; >=6 h - or overnight/next-day - for high-risk/severe reactions, >=2 epinephrine doses, prior biphasic, asthma, or poor access to care). Off supplemental O2 with SpO2 at baseline; normotensive; tolerating oral intake; no stridor or residual airway edema. Trigger identified or a clear avoidance plan in place; culprit drug stopped. Patient/caregiver demonstrates correct autoinjector technique; has or is prescribed >=2 autoinjectors; written action plan given; allergist follow-up arranged; reliable ability to return and access EMS.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home. Prescribe TWO epinephrine autoinjectors (0.3 mg adult / 0.15 mg pediatric) with hands-on trainer teach-back and a written anaphylaxis emergency action plan. Medication reconciliation - permanently discontinue the culprit drug, record the allergy in the EHR allergy list and wristband, substitute a non-ACEi/ARB antihypertensive, and re-evaluate any beta-blocker. Education - trigger avoidance (food-label reading, venom precautions, drug cross-reactivity), early self-injection, and call 911 after use. Follow-up - allergy/immunology referral within 1-4 weeks for confirmatory testing and immunotherapy (venom VIT strongly indicated) plus PCP follow-up. Transitions - communicate trigger, allergy label, and action plan to PCP, pharmacy, and school/employer/EMS.
expected_los: Most cases are ED/observation <24 h and never become inpatient. When admitted under MS-DRG 915/916 the CMS benchmark GMLOS is short - approximately 2.2-2.5 days for 916 (no MCC) and 3.5-4.5 days for 915 (with MCC); blended typical length of stay ~2-3 days.
target_los: Optimized target ~1-2 days for admitted cases, with many converting to <24 h observation status and avoiding inpatient admission entirely; reserve >=3-4 days only for MCC-tier courses (intubation, refractory shock, or in-stay complication).
quality_metrics: No CMS core measure is anaphylaxis-specific (no SEP-1 analogue). Track process/outcome measures: time-to-epinephrine and epinephrine-as-first-line rate (avoid antihistamine-first delays); correct IM route/site; documented prescription of 2 autoinjectors and an anaphylaxis action plan at discharge; allergist-referral rate; risk-concordant observation time; 72-h ED revisit and 30-day readmission for allergic reaction; unnecessary-admission / observation-conversion rate; avoidance of corticosteroid/antihistamine monotherapy; ED epinephrine stocking/availability. Generic inpatient measures: VTE prophylaxis, IMM-2 (seasonal influenza), HCAHPS.
common_complications: Biphasic reaction (recurrence after apparent resolution, ~0.4-15%, mostly first 6-12 h and within 72 h) - mitigate with risk-based observation and an autoinjector in hand. Airway loss from progressive laryngeal/oropharyngeal edema - early anticipatory intubation by the most experienced operator. Refractory/protracted shock - IV epinephrine infusion, fluids, and add-on vasopressors. Epinephrine-related myocardial ischemia/arrhythmia (Kounis syndrome) or dosing errors (inadvertent IV vs IM) - enforce correct route/dose and telemetry. Hypoxic/anoxic injury from delayed epinephrine; corticosteroid-induced hyperglycemia. Core mitigation lever: prompt epinephrine plus monitored, risk-matched observation.
readmission_drivers: Re-exposure to an unidentified or unavoided trigger; failure to prescribe, carry, or use an epinephrine autoinjector; inadequate patient education/action plan; not stopping the culprit drug (especially continued ACEi -> recurrent angioedema); no allergist follow-up or missed venom immunotherapy; underlying mastocytosis or idiopathic anaphylaxis; premature discharge before the biphasic window in high-risk patients. Prevention levers: guaranteed dispensing of 2 autoinjectors with teach-back, trigger confirmation and EHR allergy labeling, allergist referral/immunotherapy, ACEi deprescribing, and risk-matched observation.
guideline_source: Joint Task Force on Practice Parameters (AAAAI/ACAAI) - Anaphylaxis: A 2023 Practice Parameter Update (Golden et al., Ann Allergy Asthma Immunol 2023); and World Allergy Organization (WAO) Anaphylaxis Guidance 2020 (Cardona et al.). Supplemented by the EAACI Anaphylaxis Guideline (2021 update) and current ED-treatment reviews. For angioedema: hereditary angioedema per WAO/EAACI and US HAEA management guidance; ACE-inhibitor angioedema managed by drug cessation and airway protection (no proven pharmacotherapy).
key_citations: Golden DBK, et al. Anaphylaxis: a 2023 practice parameter update. Ann Allergy Asthma Immunol 2023;132(2):124-176 (PMID 38108678; DOI 10.1016/j.anai.2023.09.015). Cardona V, et al. World Allergy Organization Anaphylaxis Guidance 2020. World Allergy Organ J 2020;13(10):100472 (PMID 33204386; DOI 10.1016/j.waojou.2020.100472). Simard D, et al. Factors influencing ED observation time following anaphylaxis: systematic review. CJEM 2021;23(4):480-493 (PMID 33844191; DOI 10.1007/s43678-021-00112-z). McHugh K, Repanshek Z. Anaphylaxis: emergency department treatment. Emerg Med Clin North Am 2022;40(1):19-32 (PMID 34782088; DOI 10.1016/j.emc.2021.08.004). Gorham NP. Anaphylaxis: after the emergency department. Immunol Allergy Clin North Am 2023;43(3):467-471 (PMID 37394253; DOI 10.1016/j.iac.2022.10.008). Sinert R, et al. Randomized trial of icatibant for ACE inhibitor-induced upper-airway angioedema (CAMEO). J Allergy Clin Immunol Pract 2017;5(5):1402-1409 (PMID 28552382; DOI 10.1016/j.jaip.2017.03.003). Baram M, et al. ACE inhibitor-induced angioedema. J Allergy Clin Immunol Pract 2013;1(5):442-445 (PMID 24565614). Citations retrieved via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Strong for the core lever - immediate IM epinephrine as first-line therapy is Class I/consensus across every society guideline (JTFPP 2023, WAO 2020), anchored in GRADE-based practice parameters. Moderate for observation-time and biphasic risk stratification (observational data and a systematic review; no validated predictive score). Moderate-to-low/negative for adjunct pharmacotherapy: routine corticosteroids and antihistamines are NOT proven to prevent biphasic reactions and should not delay epinephrine; targeted bradykinin agents for ACE-inhibitor angioedema are unproven (negative CAMEO RCT). LOS and quality-metric benchmarks are administrative estimates (low evidence).
severity_cc_mcc_notes: This DRG group collapses to a 2-tier split: 915 = allergic reactions WITH MCC, 916 = WITHOUT MCC (no separate CC-only tier). MCC drivers here - anaphylactic SHOCK, acute respiratory failure, intubation/mechanical ventilation, or epinephrine-infusion/vasopressor-dependent shock - move the case to 915 with ICU utilization, longer LOS (~3.5-4.5 days), and intensified biphasic/complication surveillance. Non-MCC (916) courses are typically brief monitor-and-discharge stays (frequently true observation status), LOS ~2 days. Comorbid asthma, CAD, beta-blocker/ACEi use, mastocytosis, and pregnancy raise severity, epinephrine complexity, and admission likelihood across both tiers.
pathway_pearls: 1) Epinephrine FIRST, fast, IM in the anterolateral thigh - it is the only life-saving drug; antihistamines and steroids are adjuncts and must never delay it. 2) Keep the patient RECUMBENT with legs elevated; an abrupt upright posture can precipitate fatal empty-ventricle collapse. 3) Match observation length to biphasic risk (~1 h for low-risk single-dose responders vs >=6 h for severe / >=2-dose / prior-biphasic patients) and NEVER discharge without 2 autoinjectors, teach-back, an action plan, and an allergist referral. 4) Recognize bradykinin-mediated angioedema (ACEi or HAE, no urticaria) - it does NOT respond to epinephrine/antihistamines/steroids; stop the ACEi and protect the airway (targeted agents unproven), and treat HAE with C1-INH concentrate, icatibant, or ecallantide.
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Allergic reactions/anaphylaxis and angioedema; ED entry, short observation. Severe -> Critical Care.

admission_criteria: Inpatient admission (vs ED discharge / <23h observation) for gastroparesis or intractable nausea-vomiting when: intractable vomiting despite adequate ED antiemetics; inability to tolerate an oral fluid challenge / maintain hydration; clinically significant dehydration or prerenal AKI (rising Cr, BUN/Cr >20, orthostasis); electrolyte derangement (K <3.0, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis, hypomagnesemia); uncontrolled hyperglycemia or DKA; >=5-10% weight loss / malnutrition requiring enteral or parenteral access; need for IV fluids, IV prokinetic or IV antiemetic not achievable outpatient; suspected mechanical obstruction needing inpatient EGD/CT; failure of ED/observation management. Red flags mandating admission +/- surgical eval: GI bleeding, peritonitis, bilious/feculent vomiting (SBO), pain out of proportion. ED discharge appropriate only if rehydrated, tolerating PO, electrolytes corrected, no red flags
risk_stratification: No disease-specific validated inpatient score exists; stratify by physiologic, metabolic and nutritional risk: KDIGO stage for AKI (prerenal if FeNa <1%); electrolyte severity (K <2.5 or ECG changes = urgent repletion + telemetry); DKA criteria (glucose >250, anion gap >10-12, ketonemia, pH <7.3 / HCO3 <18 -> DKA protocol); qSOFA/NEWS2 to exclude sepsis mimic (qSOFA >=2 escalate); malnutrition screen NRS-2002 or GLIM (albumin, %weight loss) -> nutrition consult; Gastroparesis Cardinal Symptom Index (GCSI) to trend symptom burden/response; Rome IV criteria to phenotype cyclic vomiting syndrome and cannabinoid hyperemesis (>=6 mo weekly cannabis, cyclic pattern, hot-water-bathing relief); QTc (Bazett) before and after QT-prolonging antiemetics (hold if >500 ms)
initial_workup_labs: BMP/CMP (Na/K/Cl/HCO3 for hypokalemic hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis + prerenal AKI, glucose); magnesium + phosphate (repletion); CBC with diff (leukocytosis->infection); lipase (exclude pancreatitis); LFTs/bilirubin (biliary); venous blood gas with serum/urine ketones and beta-hydroxybutyrate (exclude DKA); lactate if ill-appearing; HbA1c (diabetic control); TSH; urinalysis; serum/urine hCG in EVERY woman of childbearing age (exclude pregnancy/hyperemesis gravidarum); urine drug screen + cannabis history (CHS/CVS); medication review for opioids and GLP-1 receptor agonists. Triggers: K <3.0 -> IV repletion + telemetry; anion-gap acidosis + ketones -> DKA protocol; high lipase -> pancreatitis pathway; positive hCG -> OB consult; low albumin/prealbumin -> nutrition
initial_imaging_dx: Upper endoscopy (EGD) to exclude mechanical gastric-outlet/luminal obstruction, retained food or bezoar - perform during admission (within 24-48h) if not recently done, before labeling gastroparesis; CT abdomen/pelvis with contrast or acute obstructive series if peritoneal signs/bilious vomiting to exclude small-bowel obstruction; RUQ ultrasound if biliary suspicion; 4-hour solid-phase gastric emptying scintigraphy (standardized Tc-99m sulfur-colloid egg meal; >10% retention at 4h = delayed) is the confirmatory gold standard but performed AFTER stabilization, off opioids/anticholinergics/GLP-1 RA >=48-72h and with glucose <275 mg/dL (hyperglycemia falsely delays emptying) - often deferred to outpatient; alternatives: wireless motility capsule, 13C-spirulina breath test; ECG for QTc before QT-prolonging antiemetics
time_critical_interventions: This is a resuscitate-and-exclude pathway (no procedural door-to-X clock like STEMI/stroke). First-hours priorities: restore intravascular volume with isotonic crystalloid; correct severe hypokalemia (urgent if K <2.5 or ECG changes) with concurrent magnesium; immediately identify and treat DKA if present (fluids + insulin + K per ADA); give IV thiamine 100-500 mg BEFORE any dextrose in malnourished/alcohol-use/protracted-vomiting patients (Wernicke prophylaxis); exclude surgical abdomen/obstruction early; first-line antiemetic within the first hour and reassess/rotate class if refractory; for cannabinoid hyperemesis, early topical capsaicin 0.075% to abdomen + a dopamine antagonist (haloperidol 0.05-0.1 mg/kg)
initial_management: IV isotonic fluids (0.9% NaCl or LR; add dextrose once DKA excluded); aggressive repletion of K, magnesium and phosphate. Multimodal antiemetics rotating classes: 5-HT3 antagonist ondansetron 4-8 mg IV q8h (watch QTc); D2 antagonists prochlorperazine 5-10 mg or promethazine 6.25-12.5 mg and metoclopramide 10 mg IV q6h (also prokinetic); antipsychotic D2 haloperidol 0.5-1 mg or droperidol 0.625-1.25 mg IV for refractory (telemetry); NK1 antagonist aprepitant adjunct. Prokinetics for confirmed gastroparesis (both conditionally recommended, AGA 2025): metoclopramide first-line at lowest effective dose (max ~12 wk, black-box tardive dyskinesia) and IV erythromycin 3 mg/kg q8h (motilin agonist; tachyphylaxis limits chronic use). Glycemic control: insulin to keep glucose <180-200 mg/dL. HOLD GLP-1/dual incretin agonists, opioids and anticholinergics (all slow emptying). Diet: small-particle, low-fat, low-fiber, small frequent meals. CHS-specific: topical capsaicin + haloperidol/droperidol + lorazepam + cannabis-cessation (ondansetron often ineffective). Avoid opioids (opioid-induced gastroparesis/narcotic bowel)
day1_milestones: Volume resuscitation completed and hemodynamics stable; electrolytes and glucose corrected and trended; DKA, pancreatitis, obstruction and pregnancy excluded; multimodal antiemetic regimen started with vomiting frequency decreasing; culprit meds held (opioids, GLP-1 RA, anticholinergics); strict I/O, daily weight, telemetry if K/QTc concern; VTE prophylaxis started; malnutrition risk screen; establish working diagnosis (gastroparesis flare vs cannabinoid hyperemesis vs cyclic vomiting vs gastroenteritis) and reconcile home prokinetic/insulin; begin diabetes optimization
day2_milestones: Transition IV to oral antiemetics/prokinetics as vomiting controlled; advance diet stepwise (clear liquids -> small-particle low-fat/low-fiber solids) with documented tolerance; AKI resolving and electrolytes normalized off aggressive repletion; if unable to tolerate PO by ~48h, trial post-pyloric (nasojejunal) enteral feeding with GI/nutrition input; confirm diagnostic plan (schedule 4h GES if patient stable/euglycemic/off opioids, or defer to outpatient); reinforce CHS cannabis-cessation or CVS prophylaxis if applicable; begin discharge teaching and medication reconciliation
day3plus_milestones: Tolerating adequate oral hydration and nutrition on an oral regimen for >=24h; stable electrolytes, renal function and glucose; nausea/pain controlled without IV therapy; if refractory, escalate to GI/motility for advanced options - nasojejunal or PEG-J enteral access, and pyloric-directed therapy (gastric per-oral endoscopic pyloromyotomy/G-POEM) or gastric electrical stimulation RESERVED for medically refractory patients only (AGA 2025 - not routine initial therapy), with TPN as last resort; confirm gastroparesis by 4h GES; finalize outpatient prokinetic/antiemetic plan, diabetes optimization and close GI follow-up; discharge when criteria met
consults_multidisciplinary: Gastroenterology / neurogastroenterology-motility (diagnosis confirmation, prokinetic selection, refractory management, GES and pyloric/GES decisions); Endocrinology for diabetic gastroparesis, uncontrolled glucose or DKA; Registered dietitian/clinical nutrition EARLY (small-particle diet, enteral/parenteral planning); Pharmacy (QTc/interaction review, prokinetic stewardship, domperidone FDA expanded-access if used); Interventional/nutrition-support for enteral access; Surgery if obstruction or access needed; Behavioral medicine/psychiatry or pain service for neuromodulation, functional overlay, opioid weaning and CHS/cannabis counseling; Palliative care for refractory symptom burden; Care management/social work for transitions, adherence and substance-use resources; PT/OT if deconditioned/malnourished
monitoring_level: Most patients: general medical floor. Telemetry/step-down if K <2.5 or significant hypokalemia/hypomagnesemia, QTc prolongation or need for QT-prolonging antiemetics (haloperidol/droperidol/ondansetron), significant dehydration/hemodynamic compromise or arrhythmia risk. ICU reserved for severe DKA, shock/hemodynamic instability, severe metabolic derangement, or aspiration with respiratory compromise. Escalation triggers: refractory vomiting, worsening AKI, arrhythmia, altered mental status (consider Wernicke), qSOFA >=2/sepsis, aspiration
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: NPO or clear liquids during active vomiting, then stepwise small-particle, low-fat, low-fiber, small frequent meals; blenderized/liquid calories if solids intolerant; post-pyloric (nasojejunal) enteral nutrition if oral needs unmet by ~48-72h; parenteral nutrition only if enteral fails/contraindicated; venting gastrostomy for refractory. Mobility: early ambulation/out of bed; PT/OT if deconditioned or malnourished. VTE: mechanical + pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH/heparin) unless bleeding. Glucose: target <180-200 mg/dL with insulin (hold GLP-1 RA). Lines/catheters: remove peripheral IV and urinary catheters promptly once resuscitated and PO tolerated; repletion of thiamine in malnourished
discharge_criteria: Tolerating oral hydration and adequate nutrition without vomiting for >=24h on an oral (non-IV) antiemetic/prokinetic regimen; electrolytes, renal function and glucose stable/normalized; nausea and pain controlled on oral meds; mechanical obstruction and other acute causes excluded; safe follow-up and diagnostic plan (GES/GI) arranged; if enteral/parenteral access placed, patient/caregiver competent and home services in place
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home. Medication reconciliation: start/adjust prokinetic (metoclopramide at lowest effective dose with tardive-dyskinesia counseling), oral antiemetics, insulin regimen; STOP GLP-1 receptor agonists, opioids and anticholinergics; optimize antidiabetics. Schedule GI/motility follow-up in 1-2 weeks and outpatient 4-hour gastric emptying scintigraphy if not done; PCP/endocrine follow-up. Patient education: small-particle low-fat/low-fiber diet, hydration, glucose control, and return precautions (dehydration, intractable vomiting). CHS: structured cannabis-cessation counseling; CVS: prophylaxis (e.g., amitriptyline) + abortive plan. Transitions-of-care call within 48-72h; confirm prescriptions filled and dietitian follow-up
expected_los: MS-DRG 392 (without MCC) GMLOS ~2.6-2.8 days (AMLOS ~3.2-3.4); MS-DRG 391 (with MCC) GMLOS ~3.9-4.2 days (AMLOS ~4.8-5.0). Gastroparesis-specific admissions typically ~3-5 days. CMS IPPS benchmark; exact figures vary by fiscal year
target_los: 2-3 days for uncomplicated intractable N/V or gastroparesis flare (DRG 392) using a resuscitate -> exclude mimics -> transition-to-PO pathway; 3-4 days when MCC present (DKA, severe AKI/electrolyte derangement, malnutrition requiring enteral access; DRG 391)
quality_metrics: No condition-specific CMS core measure exists; track: 30-day all-cause and same-cause readmission (gastroparesis is high-readmission); ED revisit rate; time to PO tolerance; hospital-free days; opioid-sparing rate; appropriate 4-hour (not 2-hour) GES utilization; QTc monitoring and safe antiemetic use (medication safety); glycemic control (hyper-/hypoglycemia events, time-in-range); VTE prophylaxis compliance; CAUTI/CLABSI avoidance; malnutrition screening and dietitian consult rate; patient-reported symptom improvement (GCSI); documented cannabis-cessation counseling in CHS
common_complications: Dehydration/prerenal AKI; electrolyte disturbances (hypokalemia, hypomagnesemia, hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis) with QT prolongation/arrhythmia (partly iatrogenic from antiemetics); aspiration pneumonia/pneumonitis; malnutrition/weight loss; Mallory-Weiss tear/esophagitis from vomiting; hyperglycemia/DKA in diabetics; drug effects (metoclopramide extrapyramidal/tardive dyskinesia; QTc from droperidol/ondansetron); gastric bezoar; venous-access complications; opioid-induced worsening (narcotic bowel). Mitigation: multimodal opioid-sparing antiemesis, telemetry/QTc surveillance, electrolyte repletion protocols, aspiration precautions, glucose control, early nutrition, prokinetic stewardship
readmission_drivers: Incomplete symptom control or premature discharge; inadequate/absent GI-motility follow-up; poor glycemic control in diabetic gastroparesis; continued GLP-1 RA, opioid or cannabis use; medication nonadherence or inability to afford prokinetics; recurrent dehydration/malnutrition; untreated psychiatric/functional comorbidity; enteral-access complications. Prevention levers: transitions-of-care call at 48-72h, guaranteed GI follow-up + outpatient GES, diabetes optimization, opioid/GLP-1/cannabis cessation, dietitian teaching, clear return precautions, and neuromodulator consideration for functional overlay
guideline_source: American College of Gastroenterology (ACG) Clinical Guideline: Gastroparesis, 2022 (Camilleri et al.); American Gastroenterological Association (AGA) Clinical Practice Guideline on Management of Gastroparesis, 2025 (Staller et al.); AGA Clinical Practice Update on Diagnosis and Management of Cannabinoid Hyperemesis Syndrome, 2024 (for the intractable-vomiting/CHS subset). Diagnostic standard: 4-hour solid-phase gastric emptying scintigraphy consensus (Abell et al., 2008)
key_citations: Camilleri M, Kuo B, Nguyen L, et al. ACG Clinical Guideline: Gastroparesis. Am J Gastroenterol. 2022;117(8):1197-1220 (PMID 35926490; DOI 10.14309/ajg.0000000000001874; correction PMID 38828956). Staller K, Parkman HP, Greer KB, et al. AGA Clinical Practice Guideline on Management of Gastroparesis. Gastroenterology. 2025;169(5):828-861 (PMID 40976635; DOI 10.1053/j.gastro.2025.08.004). Rubio-Tapia A, McCallum R, Camilleri M. AGA Clinical Practice Update on Diagnosis and Management of Cannabinoid Hyperemesis Syndrome. Gastroenterology. 2024;166(5):930-934 (PMID 38456869; DOI 10.1053/j.gastro.2024.01.040). Parkman HP, et al. Effect of nortriptyline on idiopathic gastroparesis symptoms: NORIG RCT. JAMA. 2013;310(24):2640-2649 (PMID 24368464). Standardized 4-hour gastric emptying scintigraphy consensus (Abell et al., 2008) cited within ACG/AGA. Citations sourced via PubMed
evidence_grade: Moderate overall. The diagnostic standard (4-hour solid-phase GES over 2-hour) is strong/consensus, but most therapeutic recommendations are CONDITIONAL with low/very-low certainty (AGA 2025 GRADE): metoclopramide and erythromycin conditionally favored; domperidone, prucalopride, aprepitant, nortriptyline, buspirone and CBD conditionally against as first-line; G-POEM and gastric electrical stimulation only for refractory (no recommendation on surgical pyloromyotomy/pyloroplasty - knowledge gap). Inpatient resuscitation/electrolyte/DKA elements are strong but extrapolated from general practice and ADA; CHS management is expert-consensus. Net: Moderate - conditional society guidelines plus expert consensus
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The DRG pair collapses severity: 392 = without MCC (uncomplicated flare - rehydrate, control symptoms, floor care, target 2-3 days); 391 = with MCC (e.g., DKA, acute renal failure, severe electrolyte/acid-base derangement, aspiration pneumonia, protein-calorie malnutrition, sepsis) - MCCs drive telemetry/step-down or ICU, longer LOS (~4-5 days), enteral/parenteral nutrition and higher readmission. CCs (moderate dehydration/AKI, diabetes with hyperglycemia, electrolyte abnormality) modestly extend stay. Accurate capture of MCC diagnoses (DKA, acute kidney failure, malnutrition, aspiration) is essential for severity/DRG integrity; malnutrition and dehydration coding materially shift GMLOS
pathway_pearls: (1) Exclude the dangerous mimics FIRST - mechanical obstruction (EGD/CT), DKA (anion gap/ketones), pancreatitis (lipase) and PREGNANCY (hCG in every woman of childbearing age) - before labeling a gastroparesis flare. (2) Be opioid-sparing and STOP GLP-1 receptor agonists/anticholinergics (all delay emptying and drive relapse); give thiamine before dextrose in the chronically vomiting/malnourished. (3) Diagnose correctly - 4-hour (not 2-hour) solid-phase GES, off emptying-altering drugs with glucose <275 mg/dL; never confirm gastroparesis on a hyperglycemic or opioid-exposed study. (4) Phenotype the vomiting - gastroparesis vs cannabinoid hyperemesis (topical capsaicin + haloperidol + cannabis cessation) vs cyclic vomiting (TCA prophylaxis) - treatment diverges, and watch QTc when stacking antiemetics
