condition: Viral Illness / Fever of Unknown Origin
admission_criteria: Admit (inpatient) for objective instability or high-risk host, NOT for a well-appearing viral syndrome. Inpatient indications: sepsis/septic shock (SIRS>=2 with suspected infection, qSOFA>=2, SOFA rise>=2, lactate>=2 mmol/L, SBP<90 or MAP<65); hypoxia SpO2<92% on room air or new O2 requirement; inability to maintain oral hydration or intractable vomiting; dehydration with AKI; altered mentation; hyperpyrexia >41C; febrile neutropenia (ANC<500, or <1000 and falling) unless validated MASCC>=21 low-risk outpatient pathway; immunocompromise (transplant, active chemo, prednisone>=20 mg/d, biologics, asplenia, CD4<200); suspected serious focal infection needing IV therapy/source control (endocarditis, deep abscess, obstructive pyelonephritis); returning traveler with possible malaria/viral hemorrhagic fever; true classic FUO (>=38.3C on >=2 occasions, >=3 weeks, undiagnosed after initial outpatient workup) needing inpatient diagnostics; frailty/extremes of age with instability. ED discharge or Observation (<48h): well-appearing, hemodynamically stable, tolerating PO, reliable follow-up, non-toxic viral illness, or borderline hydration needing brief monitoring. NEWS2>=5 flags admission/escalation.
risk_stratification: qSOFA>=2 (RR>=22, SBP<=100, altered mentation) -> escalate sepsis evaluation (per SSC use alongside other data, not as sole screen); SOFA rise>=2 = organ dysfunction = sepsis (Sepsis-3); SIRS>=2 = CMS SEP-1 severe-sepsis trigger; serum lactate>=2 mmol/L -> repeat in 2-4h, >=4 -> aggressive resuscitation + ICU consideration; NEWS2>=5 moderate / >=7 high -> rapid response/ICU. Febrile neutropenia: MASCC>=21 = low risk (positive predictive value 91%) -> candidate for oral/outpatient therapy, <21 = high risk -> inpatient IV; CISNE score refines clinically-stable FN. Endocarditis suspicion: modified Duke criteria + TTE/TEE. PE as fever source: Wells + PERC. Comorbidity burden (Charlson) and age inform disposition and LOS.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential (leukocytosis/left shift vs leukopenia; ANC for neutropenia; atypical lymphocytes suggest virus; cytopenias -> marrow/heme); CMP (AKI, transaminitis, electrolytes); serum lactate; 2 sets blood cultures from separate sites BEFORE antibiotics; urinalysis + urine culture; CRP and ESR (inflammatory burden); procalcitonin (bacterial vs viral, guides antibiotic start/stop); ferritin (markedly elevated -> adult-onset Still's/HLH); coagulation/DIC panel and VBG if septic. Targeted infectious testing: respiratory viral PCR (influenza A/B, RSV, SARS-CoV-2), 4th-generation HIV Ag/Ab, hepatitis serologies, EBV/CMV, and per exposure thick/thin malaria smears x3, Lyme/rickettsial serologies, TB IGRA, fungal antigens. Triggers: positive blood culture -> ID consult + echo (S. aureus bacteremia -> TTE/TEE); high ferritin/cytopenias -> H-score, soluble IL-2 receptor, triglycerides for HLH; persistent transaminitis -> viral hepatitis/EBV/CMV workup.
initial_imaging_dx: Chest X-ray on arrival (occult pneumonia, TB, adenopathy). If no source after first-line workup: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast within 24-48h (occult abscess, lymphadenopathy, malignancy). Echocardiography: TTE for murmur/bacteremia/suspected endocarditis, TEE if TTE nondiagnostic with high Duke suspicion. For true FUO after negative first-tier workup: 18F-FDG-PET/CT (diagnostic yield/helpful in ~50-60% of patients, EANM 2024) to localize occult infection/inflammation/malignancy and target biopsy; a negative PET helps exclude focal disease and predicts favorable prognosis. Targeted studies: RUQ ultrasound, lower-extremity Doppler (DVT), temporal artery ultrasound/biopsy if giant cell arteritis suspected, LP if meningitis/encephalitis features, and tissue biopsy of PET-avid or accessible lesion (lymph node, bone marrow, liver, temporal artery) for definitive diagnosis.
time_critical_interventions: Septic subset (CMS SEP-1 / SSC hour-1 bundle): measure lactate (repeat if >2 mmol/L), draw blood cultures before antibiotics, give broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1h of septic-shock recognition (target <=3h for sepsis without shock), 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid within 3h for hypotension or lactate>=4, norepinephrine first-line to MAP>=65 if fluid-refractory, then reassess perfusion/lactate. Febrile neutropenia: empiric anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam within 60 minutes (door-to-antibiotic <1h) - an oncologic emergency. Suspected bacterial meningitis: LP + empiric antibiotics + dexamethasone within 1h, do not delay antibiotics for imaging. Suspected HSV encephalitis: immediate empiric IV acyclovir. Returning traveler: same-day malaria smear/RDT and urgent treatment of falciparum. Influenza: start oseltamivir as early as possible (ideally <48h of symptom onset) in hospitalized/severe patients without waiting for test confirmation (IDSA 2018).
initial_management: Antimicrobial STEWARDSHIP is central: uncomplicated viral illness (DRG 865/866) = SUPPORTIVE care (acetaminophen 650-1000 mg q6h PRN, isotonic crystalloid for dehydration, antiemetics, rest) with NO antibiotics; use procalcitonin + culture trajectory to avoid/stop antibiotics. If bacterial infection/sepsis suspected: empiric broad-spectrum by source and local antibiogram - ceftriaxone (community) or piperacillin-tazobactam/cefepime (broad/nosocomial/pseudomonal), add vancomycin for MRSA risk/shock/line infection; de-escalate at 48-72h by cultures. Febrile neutropenia: anti-pseudomonal beta-lactam monotherapy (cefepime, piperacillin-tazobactam, or meropenem); add vancomycin only for instability/catheter/skin-soft-tissue/severe mucositis/MRSA; add antifungal (echinocandin or voriconazole) if fever persists >4-7 days. Antivirals: oseltamivir (influenza), NIH/IDSA-directed antivirals +/- immunomodulation for COVID-19, acyclovir (HSV/VZV). Fluids: balanced crystalloid (lactated Ringer's/Plasma-Lyte preferred over saline). Transfusion: restrictive threshold Hgb<7 g/dL (<8 in cardiac/ACS); prophylactic platelets if <10k. Source control (abscess drainage, infected-line removal, relieve obstruction) as early as feasible. Avoid empiric corticosteroids until an inflammatory/malignant diagnosis is confirmed (reserve for proven giant cell arteritis, adrenal crisis, or specific non-infectious inflammatory disease).
day1_milestones: Day 1: complete the sepsis bundle and reassess perfusion/lactate clearance; confirm blood cultures were drawn pre-antibiotic; drive source identification. Establish a structured differential (infection vs non-infectious inflammatory disease vs malignancy vs miscellaneous/drug fever) and document fever pattern and Tmax. Result first-line labs and CXR; send HIV test; start targeted testing per clues. Right-size antimicrobials (start only if indicated, document stewardship rationale) and start antiviral if influenza/COVID. Give IV fluids and correct electrolytes; antipyretic for comfort; order VTE prophylaxis; set diet/hydration plan. Determine level of care; place ID/specialty consults as indicated; institute isolation precautions if transmissible. Set the diagnostic roadmap and an expected LOS; complete medication reconciliation.
day2_milestones: Day 2: review 24-48h culture and viral PCR data; de-escalate or DISCONTINUE antibiotics if cultures are negative and viral illness is confirmed or procalcitonin is low (stewardship). If a source remains unknown, obtain CT chest/abdomen/pelvis; add echocardiography if bacteremia. Trend CRP/ESR/ferritin/procalcitonin and assess for defervescence. Act on specialty consults (ID, rheumatology, heme/onc); plan 18F-FDG-PET/CT if criteria are met and the case remains FUO. Advance diet, mobilize, reassess IV lines and urinary catheter for removal, and begin discharge-trajectory planning.
day3plus_milestones: Day 3+: if a diagnosis is established -> targeted definitive therapy, transition IV to PO antibiotics once afebrile 24-48h and tolerating PO, arrange OPAT if prolonged IV is needed. If viral/self-limited -> supportive care and confirm improvement (afebrile, hydrated, stable) then discharge. If persistent FUO -> staged tier-2/3 workup (FDG-PET/CT, targeted lymph-node/bone-marrow/temporal-artery/liver biopsy, autoimmune serologies ANA/ANCA/RF); give empiric therapy only when a specific diagnosis is strongly suspected (e.g., giant cell arteritis), avoiding shotgun antibiotics/steroids. Reassess daily for de-escalation and discharge readiness; a stable, defervescing, undiagnosed patient can safely COMPLETE the workup as an outpatient (undiagnosed FUO in a well patient carries a favorable prognosis).
consults_multidisciplinary: Infectious Diseases - early for persistent fever, positive cultures, immunocompromise, atypical/complex cases, and antimicrobial stewardship. Rheumatology - suspected non-infectious inflammatory disease/vasculitis/adult-onset Still's with high inflammatory markers and no infection. Hematology/Oncology - cytopenias, lymphadenopathy, suspected lymphoma/HLH, and neutropenic fever. Pharmacy/antimicrobial stewardship program - empiric selection, de-escalation, and vancomycin/aminoglycoside therapeutic drug monitoring. Critical care/rapid response for deterioration. Surgery/Interventional Radiology for source control (abscess drainage) and image-guided biopsy. Care management/social work for disposition, OPAT setup, follow-up, and cost; PT/OT for deconditioning (especially elderly/prolonged stays); Nutrition for poor intake/weight loss; Palliative care if advanced malignancy is uncovered.
monitoring_level: ICU: septic shock on vasopressors, lactate>=4 refractory to resuscitation, respiratory failure, GCS decline, multi-organ dysfunction, or unstable neutropenic sepsis. Step-down/telemetry: sepsis without shock needing frequent monitoring, borderline hemodynamics, significant comorbidity, or dysrhythmia risk. Floor: hemodynamically stable on room air or stable O2 - most viral illness and FUO diagnostic admissions. Escalation triggers: NEWS2>=7, rising lactate, new hypotension or hypoxia, altered mentation, or any rapid-response criterion.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: advance as tolerated, IV fluids until adequate oral intake; nutrition consult for poor intake/weight loss/prolonged stay. Mobility: early ambulation to prevent deconditioning and VTE; PT for functional decline. VTE prophylaxis: pharmacologic (enoxaparin or unfractionated heparin) unless contraindicated (platelets<50k, active bleeding), otherwise mechanical SCDs; risk-assess with Padua/IMPROVE. Glucose: target 140-180 mg/dL and avoid hypoglycemia (per SSC). Lines/catheters: remove peripheral/central lines and urinary catheters as early as possible to prevent CLABSI/CAUTI; remove promptly if a line-associated infection is suspected.
discharge_criteria: Afebrile or clearly defervescing for >=24h, OR fever explained with definitive therapy underway. Hemodynamically stable >=24h off vasopressors with stable or baseline oxygenation. Tolerating oral intake and oral (or arranged OPAT) medications. Either a diagnosis established with a treatment plan, OR a self-limited viral illness that is improving, OR a stable undiagnosed FUO safe to complete as an outpatient. Cultures reviewed/finalized (or an explicit owner assigned for pending results); antibiotics appropriately de-escalated or discontinued. Safe disposition with follow-up arranged and return precautions understood.
discharge_planning: Disposition: home for most; home with OPAT (PICC + infusion) for those needing prolonged IV therapy; SNF if deconditioned. Medication reconciliation; finish or stop antibiotics per stewardship (avoid unnecessarily prolonged courses); complete the antiviral course. Follow-up: PCP within 7 days; ID clinic for OPAT/complex infection; rheumatology or heme/onc if workup is pending; assign explicit ownership and follow-up for pending cultures, PET, and biopsy results. Patient education: return precautions (recurrent high fever, rigors, confusion, dyspnea, poor intake), medication adherence, and hygiene/isolation for transmissible viruses. Transitions of care: hand off pending results and the undiagnosed-FUO plan to the PCP with documentation of what was excluded.
expected_los: Group typical ~3-4 days (blended). Approximate CMS IPPS GMLOS by severity tier: DRG 866 viral illness without MCC ~2.7-2.9 days; DRG 865 viral illness with MCC ~4.0-4.4 days; DRG 868 other infectious/parasitic diagnoses with CC ~3.6-3.9 days; DRG 867 other infectious/parasitic diagnoses with MCC ~5.3-5.7 days (figures approximate and vary by CMS fiscal-year table).
target_los: Target ~2.5-3.5 days: 2-3 days for uncomplicated viral/self-limited febrile illness with rapid diagnostic turnaround and stewardship-driven early de-escalation; 3-4 days for FUO diagnostic admissions by shifting tier-2/3 workup (PET, biopsy) to the outpatient setting; longer only for MCC/complicated infection requiring source control or prolonged IV therapy.
quality_metrics: CMS SEP-1 severe sepsis/septic shock 3h and 6h bundle compliance (dominant measure for the septic subset) plus the emerging sepsis mortality eCQM. Antimicrobial stewardship: days-of-therapy (DOT), appropriate de-escalation rate, procalcitonin-guided discontinuation, and avoidance of antibiotics in confirmed viral illness (Joint Commission ASP standard, CDC Core Elements). Blood-culture-before-antibiotics rate; neutropenic-fever door-to-antibiotic <60 min. 30-day all-cause readmission and ED revisit rate; LOS index (observed/expected); observation-status appropriateness. Hospital-acquired: CLABSI, CAUTI, C. difficile SIR; influenza vaccination (IMM-2). Diagnostic effectiveness: percent discharged with an established diagnosis and imaging appropriateness (avoidance of low-yield testing).
common_complications: Sepsis progression/septic shock and AKI; hospital-acquired infections (CLABSI, CAUTI, C. difficile) driven by unnecessary antibiotics/catheters; antibiotic adverse effects and resistance from over-treating viral illness; VTE from immobility; delirium (especially elderly); dehydration/electrolyte derangement; drug fever confounding the picture; HLH/DIC in severe viral illness. Mitigation: antimicrobial stewardship with early de-escalation, early line/catheter removal, VTE prophylaxis, a delirium-prevention bundle, and judicious (not shotgun) testing.
readmission_drivers: Premature discharge before defervescence or diagnosis; unresolved undiagnosed FUO; a missed serious diagnosis (endocarditis, occult abscess, malignancy/lymphoma, TB); antibiotic failure/resistance or C. difficile; failure to follow up pending cultures/PET/biopsy; recurrent dehydration; medication nonadherence; and absent PCP/ID follow-up. Prevention levers: ensure defervescence and PO tolerance before discharge, assign explicit ownership of pending results, book PCP/ID follow-up within 7 days, coordinate OPAT, give clear return precautions, and use stewardship to prevent C. difficile.
guideline_source: No single society FUO guideline exists; the pathway integrates several current sources. Sepsis subset: Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 (SCCM/ESICM), updated 2026, plus CMS SEP-1 and Sepsis-3 (Singer 2016) definitions. FUO imaging: EANM 2024 consensus on 18F-FDG-PET/CT in fever/inflammation of unknown origin. Neutropenic fever: IDSA 2010 (Freifeld) with the MASCC risk index. Influenza: IDSA 2018 (Uyeki). COVID-19: NIH/IDSA guidelines. FUO diagnostic framework: Durack & Street 1991 classification (modifying Petersdorf & Beeson 1961) with contemporary staged reviews.

evidence_grade: Moderate overall / mixed. STRONG (Class I, high-certainty society guidelines) for the overlay conditions: sepsis (SSC 2021/2026, CMS SEP-1, Sepsis-3), neutropenic fever (IDSA + MASCC), and influenza (IDSA 2018). CONSENSUS/observational (expert opinion, no RCT-level evidence) for the core FUO diagnostic sequence, including FDG-PET/CT positioning (EANM 2024 consensus) and biopsy staging. Net grade Moderate: apply the strong sepsis/neutropenic/influenza overlays rigorously and treat the FUO diagnostic pathway as consensus-based and individualized.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Non-MCC tiers (866 viral without MCC; 868/869 lower): typically younger/immunocompetent hosts with self-limited viral illness; short LOS 2-3 days, floor care, supportive management - the main hazard is over-testing and over-treatment. CC/MCC tiers (865 viral with MCC; 867 other infectious with MCC): older or immunocompromised hosts, or those with acute organ dysfunction (sepsis, AKI, respiratory failure, encephalopathy) or bacteremia/neutropenic sepsis; these drive ICU/step-down need, longer LOS (4-6+ days), more imaging/consults, and higher complication and readmission risk. The qualifying MCC is often the acute organ dysfunction itself (septic shock, AKI, encephalopathy). Severity shifts the pathway from rapid stewardship-driven diagnostic observation toward resuscitate + source-control + prolonged targeted therapy.
pathway_pearls: 1) Screen and bundle sepsis FIRST - run the septic subset through SEP-1/SSC (cultures before antibiotics, lactate, antibiotics <=1h in shock, 30 mL/kg) before settling into a leisurely FUO workup; that is where mortality lives. 2) Antimicrobial stewardship is the therapeutic core - most 865/866 viral admissions need supportive care, NOT antibiotics; use procalcitonin and culture data to avoid/stop antibiotics and prevent C. difficile and resistance. 3) Do not miss the can't-wait mimics on Day 1 - neutropenic fever (antibiotics <60 min), malaria in a returning traveler (same-day smear), bacterial meningitis/HSV encephalitis, and endocarditis. 4) Stage the FUO workup efficiently - FDG-PET/CT (~50-60% yield) then targeted biopsy; a stable, defervescing, undiagnosed patient can safely finish the workup as an outpatient, shortening LOS without added risk.

admission_criteria: Inpatient (usually ICU) admission for essentially all acute traumatic SCI, non-traumatic cord/cauda equina compression with deficit, and mechanically unstable spinal column injuries. Objective triggers: any new motor/sensory deficit referable to cord or cauda equina; ISNCSCI/ASIA impairment (AIS A-D); MRI cord compression, cord signal change/edema/hemorrhage, or unstable fracture-dislocation; neurogenic shock (SBP<90 with bradycardia); respiratory compromise from high cervical level; need for decompression/stabilization. Emergent admission for malignant epidural cord compression, epidural abscess, or spinal epidural hematoma with deficit. ED discharge/observation only for cleared, stable spine (NEXUS-negative or Canadian C-Spine Rule low-risk) with no deficit and no unstable injury; isolated stable minor spinal disorders (stable radiculopathy, chronic stenosis without progressive deficit) may be outpatient/observation.
risk_stratification: ISNCSCI / ASIA Impairment Scale (AIS A complete to E normal) plus neurologic level of injury = primary prognosticator/disposition driver (AIS A worst); document baseline and reassess at 72h. Cervical clearance: NEXUS (all 5 negative -> no imaging) and Canadian C-Spine Rule. Operative-need scores: SLIC >=5 -> surgery; TLICS >=5 -> surgery, <=3 nonoperative, =4 indeterminate. Non-traumatic metastatic: SINS 13-18 unstable -> surgery (7-12 indeterminate); Tokuhashi/Tomita/modified Bauer for survival. Level thresholds that drive care: injury >=T6 -> neurogenic shock + autonomic dysreflexia risk; >=C5 -> respiratory-failure risk. Baseline MRI (e.g., BASIC score) aids outcome prediction; trauma ISS / ACS-TQIP.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, CMP (electrolytes/renal), coagulation (PT/INR/aPTT, platelets), type & crossmatch, lactate + base deficit (screen occult hemorrhagic shock), ABG (ventilation adequacy in cervical injury), troponin (blunt cardiac), CK, urinalysis, pregnancy test. Critical to distinguish neurogenic shock (hypotension + bradycardia, warm/vasodilated) from hemorrhagic shock (tachycardia) -> lactate/base deficit + FAST/CT must exclude bleeding before attributing hypotension to cord. Serial Hgb. Glucose q-check (avoid hyper/hypoglycemia; relevant if steroids used). Non-traumatic: ESR/CRP + blood cultures if abscess suspected; PSA/SPEP and staging if metastatic.
initial_imaging_dx: CT of the entire spine with reformats = first-line for bony injury, obtained immediately (often within trauma pan-scan). MRI of the affected region without contrast as soon as feasible and BEFORE surgery when possible (AO Spine 2017/2024) to define compression, cord signal/edema/hemorrhage, ligamentous & disc injury and prognosis (target within hours) -- but do NOT delay clearly indicated decompression for MRI. ISNCSCI exam at baseline and at 72h. CT angiography neck for blunt cerebrovascular injury (modified Denver criteria) with foramen transversarium fractures/subluxation. Flexion-extension/upright films only in awake, intact, cooperative patients for ligamentous clearance. Non-traumatic: whole-spine MRI with and without contrast (skip lesions, abscess/tumor).
time_critical_interventions: "Time is spine": surgical decompression within <=24 hours of injury for acute traumatic SCI at all levels and for traumatic central cord syndrome -- AO Spine 2024 "recommended" (STASCIS OR 2.83, and RR 2.76 for >=2-grade AIS improvement). Immediate spinal immobilization/neutral alignment; early closed reduction of cervical fracture-dislocation in awake patients. Begin hemodynamic augmentation immediately: MAP 75-80 up to 90-95 mmHg maintained 3-7 days (AO Spine 2024; legacy AANS/CNS target MAP 85-90 x7d); avoid SBP<90 and hypoxia (secondary injury). Airway: early intubation for high cervical (>=C5) or declining vital capacity -- do not await arrest. Malignant epidural compression: dexamethasone + urgent RT/surgery within hours; epidural abscess with deficit: emergent decompression + antibiotics. If MPSS elected, must start <8h.
initial_management: Neurogenic shock: crystalloid to euvolemia, then vasopressor/inotrope -- norepinephrine (alpha+beta, preferred for combined hypotension+bradycardia) or dopamine; atropine/chronotropic support for symptomatic bradycardia; AVOID phenylephrine (pure alpha) in high injuries (worsens reflex bradycardia); titrate to MAP 85-90 (range 75-95) mmHg. Corticosteroids: routine methylprednisolone NOT recommended (AANS/CNS 2013, Level I against); AO Spine 2017 gives only a weak "suggest as option" for 24h high-dose MPSS if started <8h (30 mg/kg bolus then 5.4 mg/kg/h x23h) -- shared decision, weigh infection/hyperglycemia; do NOT give if >8h, no 48h infusion, contraindicated in penetrating SCI. Restrictive transfusion (Hgb <7 g/dL; higher if active bleed/cardiac). Anticoagulation reversal pre-op (PCC/vit K for warfarin, idarucizumab for dabigatran, andexanet/PCC for Xa inhibitors). Source control: decompression + instrumented fusion/stabilization per SLIC/TLICS. Riluzole NOT routinely recommended (RISCIS). Malignant compression: dexamethasone 10 mg IV then 4-6 mg q6h; abscess: vancomycin + antipseudomonal beta-lactam.
day1_milestones: Definitive ISNCSCI documented; MRI completed; operative decompression/stabilization performed within 24h if indicated. Neuro-ICU admission with arterial line and MAP-goal protocol running; airway secured/ventilation plan set; trend vital capacity/NIF for cervical injuries. Foley for accurate UOP and bladder decompression; NG/OG if ileus; strict aspiration precautions + dysphagia screen. Start mechanical VTE prophylaxis (SCDs) immediately; pressure-injury bundle (specialty mattress, turn q2h). Initiate bowel regimen; glucose control; stress-ulcer prophylaxis. Place PM&R/SCI-service, spine-surgery, and case-management/social-work consults.
day2_milestones: Continue MAP augmentation (through days 3-7); begin weaning vasopressors as tolerated. Initiate pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis -- LMWH preferred (or fixed low-dose UFH) -- within 72h of injury once surgical hemostasis assured (AO Spine 2017). Reassess neuro exam (approaching 72h ISNCSCI). Aggressive pulmonary toilet, incentive spirometry, secretion management; assess extubation vs plan for early tracheostomy in complete cervical injury. Begin/advance enteral nutrition (dietitian-guided); PT/OT at bedside (ROM, positioning, splinting); establish bladder-management plan; control neuropathic pain and spasticity.
day3plus_milestones: Complete MAP protocol at 3-7 days then liberalize; de-escalate from ICU to step-down/floor when off vasopressors, hemodynamically and respiratory stable. Transition indwelling Foley to intermittent catheterization program once UOP reliable; confirm bowel program effective. Early tracheostomy (typically day 5-7) and structured ventilator weaning for ventilator-dependent complete cervical SCI. Formal PM&R evaluation and acute inpatient-rehab (IRF) placement; ongoing DVT surveillance; optimize spasticity (baclofen), neuropathic pain (gabapentinoids), mood; begin caregiver/family training. Reach transfer readiness to acute inpatient rehabilitation.
consults_multidisciplinary: Spine surgery (neurosurgery/orthopedic spine) emergent; trauma surgery for polytrauma. Neurocritical care; PM&R/SCI rehabilitation medicine early (day 1). PT and OT early; SLP for dysphagia/communication in cervical injury; respiratory therapy. Case management + social work early for disposition and psychosocial support; clinical pharmacy (VTE, spasticity, steroid decision, med reconciliation); registered dietitian (hypermetabolic needs). Urology (neurogenic bladder), wound/ostomy nursing (pressure injury), psychology/psychiatry, palliative care for high complete injuries; radiation oncology/oncology for metastatic compression; infectious disease for epidural abscess.
monitoring_level: ICU/neuro-ICU for all acute cervical and high-thoracic SCI, any AIS A-C, neurogenic shock, respiratory compromise, or need for MAP-augmentation protocol -- arterial line, continuous hemodynamic monitoring + telemetry, serial vital capacity/NIF, hourly neuro checks. Step-down/telemetry once off vasopressors, MAP protocol completed, respiratory stable. Floor for stable incomplete/low injuries and stable non-traumatic spinal disorders. Escalation triggers: falling vital capacity (<15 mL/kg or <1 L), rising PaCO2, or hypoxia -> intubation; autonomic dysreflexia crisis; new or ascending neuro deficit -> STAT MRI + surgical re-evaluation; hemodynamic instability/bradyarrhythmia.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: early enteral feeding once ileus resolves, dietitian-guided (SCI is hypermetabolic acutely, hypometabolic later); aspiration precautions + dysphagia screen in cervical injury; stress-ulcer prophylaxis (PPI/H2) given elevated acute GI-bleed risk. Mobility: early mobilization/rehab once medically stable and spine stabilized (AO Spine 2017 rehab CPG); ROM, positioning, splinting from day 1; pressure-injury bundle (turn q2h, specialty surface). VTE: mechanical SCDs immediately + pharmacologic LMWH (preferred) or fixed low-dose UFH started within 72h; continue prophylaxis 8-12 weeks (Consortium for Spinal Cord Medicine); avoid prophylactic IVC filters (reserve for anticoagulation failure/contraindication). Glucose target ~140-180 mg/dL. Early Foley removal -> intermittent catheterization; remove central/arterial lines as ICU needs resolve.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable off vasopressors with MAP protocol completed; respiratory status stable (liberated from ventilator, or stable trach/vent plan with established settings). Spine mechanically stabilized (post-op or defined orthosis/bracing plan); pain and spasticity controlled on oral regimen. Bladder (intermittent catheterization) and bowel programs established and teachable; no untreated infection; adequate nutrition (tolerating diet/tube feeds). Neurologically stable/non-progressing; VTE prophylaxis established; skin intact or managed. Disposition secured (usually acute inpatient rehabilitation) with caregiver training underway.
discharge_planning: Disposition: most moderate-severe SCI transfer to acute inpatient rehabilitation facility (IRF) / SCI Model System center; lesser injuries home with outpatient rehab or SNF. Medication reconciliation: VTE prophylaxis with defined duration, bowel regimen, antispasticity (baclofen), neuropathic pain (gabapentin/pregabalin), bladder meds, skin/DVT instructions. Follow-up scheduled before discharge: spine surgery (wound/imaging), PM&R/SCI clinic, urology. Education: autonomic dysreflexia recognition and emergency management, pressure-injury prevention, bladder/bowel programs, orthosis care; caregiver competency sign-off. Transitions: warm handoff to rehab team, DME (wheelchair, cushion, commode), home accessibility assessment.
expected_los: Collapsed group varies widely by DRG: medical spinal disorders/injuries DRG 053 (no CC/MCC) GMLOS ~2-3d and DRG 052 (with CC/MCC) ~4-5d; spinal-procedure DRGs 030 (no CC/MCC) ~1.5-2d, 029 (CC/neurostimulator) ~3-4d, 028 (with MCC) ~8-10d. The critical-care cohort that defines this pathway -- acute traumatic cervical/complete SCI -- realistically runs ~10-14 days acute-care before IRF transfer (NSCISC ~11d median acute stay).
target_los: Minor/stable spinal disorders 1-3 days. Acute traumatic SCI requiring surgery + neuro-ICU: target ~7-10 days acute-care under the ideal pathway -- decompression <24h, protocolized MAP wean by day 5-7, early tracheostomy when needed, VTE/skin/bladder bundles from day 1, and prompt IRF transfer to avoid acute-care days spent awaiting a rehab bed.
quality_metrics: Process: rate of surgical decompression within 24h (AO Spine "time-is-spine" metric); MRI before surgery; MAP-protocol adherence; VTE prophylaxis initiated within 72h (legacy VTE-1/VTE-2). Outcome/safety: hospital-acquired pressure injury (HAPI, CMS HAC), CAUTI, CLABSI, ventilator-associated pneumonia/events, hospital-acquired VTE (PSI-12), PSI-90 composite; AIS-grade conversion/neurologic recovery; 30-day readmission and mortality. Registry/program: ACS-TQIP trauma registry, SCI Model Systems participation; ERAS-spine compliance for elective spinal procedures. Note: there is no SCI-specific CMS core measure -- quality is tracked via these process/safety/registry proxies.
common_complications: Respiratory failure/pneumonia/atelectasis -- leading cause of morbidity and death in cervical SCI; mitigate with vital-capacity/NIF monitoring, aggressive pulmonary toilet, early intubation/tracheostomy. Neurogenic shock and bradyarrhythmia -- MAP protocol, atropine/pacing readiness. VTE/PE -- early LMWH + SCDs. Autonomic dysreflexia (injuries >=T6) -- trigger avoidance and rapid treatment. Pressure injuries -- turn q2h, specialty surface. UTI from neurogenic bladder -- intermittent catheterization over indwelling. Also ileus/GI bleed (bowel program, stress-ulcer prophylaxis), spasticity, neuropathic pain, hyponatremia (SIADH), and depression.
readmission_drivers: UTI/urosepsis from neurogenic bladder -- top driver; prevent with intermittent-catheterization technique, avoid chronic indwelling catheters, patient education. Pressure injuries/wound infection -- skin surveillance, offloading, caregiver training. Respiratory (pneumonia, mucus plugging, respiratory failure) -- secretion management and pulmonary regimen. Autonomic dysreflexia crises -- patient/caregiver recognition and emergency action plan. VTE, surgical wound complications, uncontrolled spasticity/pain, and depression -- structured SCI-clinic follow-up, adequate prophylaxis duration (8-12 weeks), telehealth check-ins, and reliable rehab linkage.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: AO Spine/Praxis Clinical Practice Guidelines for the Management of Acute Spinal Cord Injury (Global Spine Journal, 2024) -- timing of decompression and hemodynamic management. AO Spine 2017 CPG series (Global Spine Journal) -- methylprednisolone, VTE thromboprophylaxis, baseline MRI, rehabilitation. AANS/CNS Guidelines for the Management of Acute Cervical Spine and Spinal Cord Injuries, 2013 update (Neurosurgery). Consortium for Spinal Cord Medicine CPGs (Prevention of VTE, 3rd ed; Early Acute Management). (Sources verified via PubMed.)
key_citations: Per PubMed-indexed sources: AO Spine/Praxis 2024 CPG -- decompression <24h "recommended", MAP 75-80 to 90-95 mmHg x3-7d "suggested" (Kwon/Fehlings, Global Spine J 2024; PMID 38526930; DOI 10.1177/21925682231189928). STASCIS -- early <24h vs late, 19.8% vs 8.8% >=2-grade AIS improvement, OR 2.83 (Fehlings, PLoS One 2012; PMID 22384132; DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0032037). AO Spine 2017 MPSS CPG -- weak option only if <8h (PMID 29164025; DOI 10.1177/2192568217703085). AO Spine 2017 VTE CPG -- LMWH/low-dose UFH within 72h (PMID 29164026; DOI 10.1177/2192568217702107). AO Spine 2017 timing/central-cord CPG (PMID 29164024; DOI 10.1177/2192568217706367); baseline MRI CPG (PMID 29164028; DOI 10.1177/2192568217703089). AANS/CNS 2013 update (PMID 23839357; DOI 10.1227/01.neu.0000430319.32247.7f). Adjunctive therapies -- no routine riluzole/SCPP (Neal, Global Spine J 2026; PMID 41700822; DOI 10.1177/21925682261423534).
evidence_grade: Moderate overall and mixed. Strongest evidence is Class-I-level AGAINST routine high-dose methylprednisolone (AANS/CNS 2013). Early decompression <=24h is "recommended" (AO Spine 2024) but rests on low-to-moderate-quality observational data (STASCIS, meta-analyses; RR 2.76). Hemodynamic MAP targets are only "suggested" on very-low-quality evidence (no RCT). VTE, MRI, and rehabilitation recommendations are weak/low-quality. Net: guideline-endorsed but few RCTs -- much rests on expert consensus and observational data.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed group spans surgical (028-030) and medical (052-053) DRGs; severity is driven by CC/MCC and by injury completeness/level. MCC drivers common in complete cervical SCI -- acute respiratory failure/ventilator dependence, aspiration pneumonia, sepsis/urosepsis, PE, stage III-IV pressure ulcer -- shift cases to DRG 028 (procedure w/ MCC) or 052 (disorder w/ CC/MCC) with ICU, tracheostomy, and the longest LOS. Higher/complete injuries (AIS A-B, tetraplegia, level >=T6) predict neurogenic shock, respiratory failure, and autonomic dysreflexia -> ICU need, longer LOS, higher complication capture. Without CC/MCC (053, 030), stable incomplete/low injuries or elective/minor spinal procedures need only a short floor stay. Accurate documentation of respiratory failure, tetraplegia, pressure-injury stage, and neurogenic bladder/bowel is essential for correct severity assignment.
pathway_pearls: 1) "Time is spine" -- obtain MRI and decompress within <=24h; the single highest-yield neuro-recovery lever (AO Spine 2024; STASCIS OR 2.83). 2) Protect the cord from secondary injury -- run the MAP 85-90 (range 75-95) mmHg protocol for 3-7 days, avoid hypoxia/hypotension, and treat neurogenic shock with norepinephrine (not phenylephrine) in high injuries. 3) The airway is the killer in cervical SCI -- trend vital capacity/NIF and intubate early / trach by day 5-7 for complete cervical injuries; pneumonia is the leading cause of death. 4) Front-load the SCI bundle: LMWH VTE prophylaxis within 72h + SCDs, q2h turning, intermittent catheterization over indwelling, bowel program, and early PM&R/IRF placement -- these prevent the UTI/pressure-injury/VTE readmissions; skip routine steroids and riluzole.

admission_criteria: Admit (inpatient) if: secondary spontaneous pneumothorax (SSP, i.e., any underlying lung disease/COPD — nearly always admit); any tube thoracostomy/chest drain placed; traumatic pneumothorax/hemopneumothorax on tube drainage; hemodynamic compromise or tension physiology (decompress first); significant hypoxia (SpO2 <92% on room air) or O2 requirement; bilateral pneumothorax; large PSP requiring intervention; persistent air leak; mechanical ventilation. ED-discharge/observation acceptable for: stable, minimally symptomatic PSP managed conservatively with early follow-up (BTS 2023); successful needle aspiration with <2 cm residual and symptom resolution; selected ambulatory-device (Heimlich/Pleural Vent) candidates; stable occult traumatic pneumothorax (CT-only, not on CXR) NOT on positive-pressure ventilation — observe
risk_stratification: BTS 2023 high-risk characteristics (any one drives intervention/admission over conservative care): hemodynamic compromise, significant breathlessness/hypoxia, bilateral pneumothorax, underlying lung disease (=SSP), age >50 with significant smoking history, hemopneumothorax; Size estimation: interpleural distance >=2 cm at hilum on PA CXR (BTS) or >=3 cm apex-to-cupola (ACCP/CHEST) = 'large'; Light index for % volume; Cerfolio air-leak grade (expiratory->continuous) to stratify persistent air leak and surgical referral; eFAST/lung ultrasound (absent lung sliding, 'barcode/stratosphere' sign, lung point) for rapid bedside diagnosis in trauma/unstable; Traumatic polytrauma: ISS and chest-wall injury burden (rib fracture count) for trauma-pathway escalation
initial_workup_labs: CBC (Hgb/Hct — hemopneumothorax detection, transfusion trigger); BMP; coagulation PT/INR/aPTT and platelets BEFORE chest drain (correct if INR>1.5/plt<50k unless emergent); type & screen/crossmatch if hemothorax or trauma; ABG if hypoxic or SSP/COPD (assess PaCO2/retention before high-flow O2); lactate if hemodynamic compromise or major trauma; troponin/ECG to exclude cardiac cause of pleuritic chest pain; beta-hCG in women of childbearing age (limits radiation); blood cultures + pleural fluid studies if febrile/empyema suspected; outpatient alpha-1-antitrypsin in young non-smokers with emphysematous change
initial_imaging_dx: Erect PA chest radiograph = first-line, obtained immediately (diagnosis + size measurement); Point-of-care lung/eFAST ultrasound in trauma bay or unstable patient (<5 min, high sensitivity — absent lung sliding/lung point) and to guide small-bore drain; CT chest = gold standard, reserve for diagnostic uncertainty, complex/loculated or hemopneumothorax, quantifying occult traumatic pneumothorax, evaluating underlying lung disease/bullae vs pneumothorax, and pre-surgical planning; expiratory films NOT routinely recommended; post-procedure CXR after every drain insertion to confirm position + re-expansion; digital air-leak monitoring where available
time_critical_interventions: Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis — IMMEDIATE needle decompression within minutes, BEFORE imaging (14G cannula, 5th ICS anterior axillary line preferred in adults per ATLS 10th ed, or 2nd ICS midclavicular), then definitive chest tube; Massive/large symptomatic pneumothorax: chest drain during the ED encounter; Traumatic tube thoracostomy: antibiotic prophylaxis administered AT time of insertion to reduce empyema (EAST 2022, strongest benefit in penetrating trauma); Massive hemothorax (>1500 mL immediate or >200 mL/hr x2-4 h) -> emergent thoracotomy; ABCDE resuscitation with restrictive/balanced transfusion in hemorrhagic shock
initial_management: Supplemental O2 titrated to SpO2 >=92% (88-92% and cautious in COPD/CO2 retainers) — high-concentration O2 accelerates pleural air resorption in admitted conservative patients; Conservative observation for stable minimally symptomatic PSP (noninferior, fewer serious adverse events — Brown NEJM 2020); Needle aspiration (16-18G) first-line for symptomatic PSP — as effective as chest tube with fewer complications/shorter stay (meta-analysis 2024); Small-bore Seldinger drain 8-14 Fr to underwater seal for failed aspiration/SSP/large symptomatic; large-bore >=28 Fr for hemothorax/trauma/ventilated; Ambulatory one-way-valve device (Rocket Pleural Vent/Heimlich) in selected PSP to cut LOS (RAMPP); NEVER clamp a bubbling drain; low-pressure suction (-10 to -20 cmH2O) only for non-resolving/incomplete re-expansion (avoid high suction — re-expansion pulmonary edema); Multimodal analgesia (acetaminophen + NSAID + opioid PRN; intercostal/paravertebral/epidural block for rib-fracture trauma); Trauma prophylaxis cefazolin at insertion; Transfuse hemothorax at restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL (<7-8 trauma), activate MTP in hemorrhagic shock
day1_milestones: Confirm drain position + lung re-expansion on post-procedure CXR; document air leak (bubbling) and respiratory swing; drain to underwater seal (NOT suction unless incomplete re-expansion); achieve adequate multimodal analgesia and mobilize patient with drain; maintain SpO2 >=92% (88-92% COPD); initiate smoking-cessation counseling + pharmacotherapy (single highest-yield recurrence lever); start VTE risk assessment; for conservative/ambulatory pathway: verify no clinical/radiographic progression and discharge with 2-4 day outpatient review if criteria met
day2_milestones: Repeat CXR to assess re-expansion and air-leak status; if lung fully re-expanded AND no air leak x>=24 h -> remove drain (optional brief clamp trial per local protocol) and obtain post-removal CXR; continue early mobilization, analgesia, incentive spirometry; if persistent air leak -> continue drainage, consider low-pressure suction, begin early thoracic surgery discussion; ensure VTE prophylaxis running once bleeding risk acceptable
day3plus_milestones: Persistent air leak >48-72 h -> thoracic surgery referral for VATS bullectomy + pleurodesis/pleurectomy (definitive recurrence prevention); consider converting stable ongoing air leak to ambulatory Heimlich-valve device to shorten stay; for retained traumatic hemothorax -> early VATS evacuation (day 3-7) to prevent empyema/fibrothorax; non-surgical candidates: autologous blood patch or endobronchial valve for prolonged air leak; remove drain after >=24 h no air leak + full re-expansion, confirm with CXR; complete recurrence-prevention counseling and discharge planning
consults_multidisciplinary: Respiratory/pulmonology (SSP management, conservative/ambulatory pathway supervision); Thoracic surgery (persistent air leak >48-72 h, second ipsilateral or first contralateral/bilateral pneumothorax, hemothorax/retained hemothorax, tension, high-risk occupation — pilots/divers); Trauma surgery for traumatic mechanism; Interventional radiology for image-guided drainage of loculated collections; Anesthesia/acute pain service for regional blocks in rib-fracture trauma; Pharmacy (analgesia stewardship, prophylactic antibiotics, VTE dosing); Smoking-cessation service; Physical therapy/respiratory therapy for mobilization and pulmonary hygiene; Care management/social work for follow-up coordination and home-O2 setup
monitoring_level: Floor with continuous pulse oximetry +/- telemetry for most patients with a chest drain; Step-down/HDU for SSP with respiratory compromise, large air leak on suction, or borderline gas exchange; ICU for post-decompression tension physiology, hemodynamic instability, massive hemothorax, mechanical ventilation, bilateral pneumothorax, polytrauma, or re-expansion pulmonary edema; Escalation triggers: rising O2 requirement/hypoxia, hypotension, progressive subcutaneous emphysema, drain output >1500 mL or >200 mL/hr of blood, increasing work of breathing, or recurrent/tension pneumothorax
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: regular, no restriction (NPO only if surgery planned); Mobility: EARLY ambulation encouraged even with drain in situ (mobile underwater seal or ambulatory valve) plus incentive spirometry and pulmonary hygiene; VTE prophylaxis: mechanical + pharmacologic LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily) once bleeding risk acceptable — hold/caution during active hemothorax, follow trauma VTE protocol; Glucose control if diabetic/on steroids; Line/catheter stewardship: remove urinary catheter early and remove chest drain promptly once criteria met (reduces empyema, pain, and LOS)
discharge_criteria: Full or near-full lung re-expansion on CXR; chest drain removed with NO air leak for >=24 h and stable post-removal radiograph (or ambulatory device functioning appropriately with plan); SpO2 >=92% on room air or at patient baseline (or home O2 arranged); pain controlled on oral analgesia; hemodynamically stable, afebrile, no active bleeding; ambulating and tolerating diet; for conservatively managed PSP: no symptomatic or radiographic progression on serial imaging
discharge_planning: Disposition home in most cases; medication reconciliation with oral analgesia; smoking-cessation pharmacotherapy (NRT/varenicline) and resources; outpatient CXR + respiratory/thoracic clinic follow-up at 2-4 weeks (earlier 2-4 days for conservatively managed or ambulatory-device patients); patient education on recurrence risk (~30% PSP, higher in smokers) and red-flag return symptoms (sudden dyspnea/pleuritic pain); AVOID air travel until radiographic resolution (commonly ~1-2 weeks after confirmed resolution per BTS); scuba diving permanently contraindicated unless definitive bilateral surgical pleurectomy; explicit handoff for ambulatory-device removal and any surgical follow-up
expected_los: GMLOS benchmark by collapsed DRG: ~2.9 days (DRG 201, no CC/MCC), ~3.9 days (DRG 200, w CC), ~5.4 days (DRG 199, w MCC); overall typical inpatient stay 3-5 days
target_los: Uncomplicated PSP via conservative/needle-aspiration/ambulatory pathway: 0-1 day (observation) or same-day ambulatory; small-bore drain uncomplicated PSP: 1-2 days; SSP or traumatic pneumothorax with drain: 3-4 days — overall optimized target 1-2 days for uncomplicated PSP and 3-4 days for SSP/traumatic
quality_metrics: AHRQ PSI-06 iatrogenic pneumothorax rate (procedural safety); tobacco-use screening + cessation intervention documented (TOB measures); 30-day all-cause readmission and pneumothorax recurrence rate; chest-tube complication rate (malposition, empyema, re-insertion); LOS index (observed-to-expected); VTE prophylaxis compliance; antibiotic-prophylaxis compliance and empyema rate for traumatic tube thoracostomy (EAST metric); time-to-drain-removal and ambulatory-management uptake rate; CT imaging stewardship (avoid unnecessary scans/radiation in young patients)
common_complications: Persistent/prolonged air leak >48-72 h (most common) — mitigate with early thoracic surgery referral; re-expansion pulmonary edema — avoid rapid drainage/high suction of large or chronic pneumothorax; chest-tube malposition/dislodgement/blockage — confirm with CXR and secure fixation; surgical/subcutaneous emphysema; empyema and retained hemothorax (esp. traumatic) — prophylactic antibiotics at insertion plus early VATS evacuation of retained blood; procedural bleeding/organ injury; recurrence; tension physiology from drain malfunction — never clamp a bubbling drain
readmission_drivers: Pneumothorax recurrence (highest in first year ~20%, lifetime ~30% PSP) — recurrence prevention with VATS pleurodesis/pleurectomy for indicated cases and smoking cessation; recurrent air leak after premature drain removal — enforce >=24 h no-leak + re-expansion rule before removal; empyema/retained hemothorax after traumatic drainage — antibiotic prophylaxis and adequate evacuation; ambulatory-device failure/complication — structured early follow-up; missed follow-up imaging — schedule 2-4 week CXR; ongoing smoking and underlying lung disease in SSP
guideline_source: PRIMARY: British Thoracic Society (BTS) Guideline for Pleural Disease 2023 (Thorax) — spontaneous pneumothorax (symptom- and risk-characteristic-driven, replacing rigid size cutoffs). Traumatic: Eastern Association for the Surgery of Trauma (EAST) 2022 practice management guideline (antibiotic prophylaxis for tube thoracostomy) + ACS-COT/ATLS 10th ed for resuscitation and tension decompression. Historical US reference: ACCP/CHEST Delphi consensus 2001. Supportive: WSES chest-trauma guidance
key_citations: BTS Guideline for pleural disease — Roberts ME, Rahman NM, Maskell NA, et al. Thorax 2023;78(11):1143-1156 (PMID 37553157; DOI 10.1136/thorax-2023-220304); Conservative vs Interventional Treatment for Spontaneous Pneumothorax (PSP RCT) — Brown SGA et al. N Engl J Med 2020;382(5):405-415 (PMID 31995686; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1910775); RAMPP ambulatory management — Hallifax RJ et al. (protocol BMJ Open Respir Res 2019;6:e000403, PMID 31179004; results Lancet 2020;396:39-49); EAST antibiotic prophylaxis for tube thoracostomy — Freeman JJ et al. Trauma Surg Acute Care Open 2022;7:e000886 (PMID 36312819; DOI 10.1136/tsaco-2022-000886); Needle aspiration vs tube thoracostomy meta-analysis — Mohamed MMG et al. J Thorac Dis 2024;16(6):4011-4015 (PMID 38983182; DOI 10.21037/jtd-24-60); Conservative vs interventional meta-analysis — Liu WL et al. Am J Emerg Med 2020;45:352-357 (PMID 33046307); ACCP Delphi consensus — Baumann MH et al. Chest 2001;119:590-602 (PMID 11171742). Attribution: citations retrieved via PubMed
evidence_grade: Moderate overall — national society guideline (BTS 2023) blending evidence-based and consensus/GRADE low-to-moderate recommendations, supported by one well-conducted RCT (PSP/Brown NEJM 2020, whose primary noninferiority outcome was not robust to conservative missing-data assumptions — hence 'modest') and the RAMPP ambulatory trial, plus a GRADE-conditional trauma guideline (EAST 2022). Immediate needle/tube decompression of tension pneumothorax is near-universal Class I consensus (high certainty of benefit); size-vs-symptom management thresholds are moderate/expert-consensus
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 201 (no CC/MCC): typically young healthy PSP, uncomplicated drain or conservative/ambulatory care — benchmark ~2.9 d, target 1-2 d or same-day ambulatory. DRG 200 (w CC): SSP with COPD/lung disease, moderate comorbidity, or persistent air leak, often older — ~3.9 d. DRG 199 (w MCC): acute respiratory failure/mechanical ventilation, tension physiology, massive hemothorax with transfusion, empyema/sepsis, or polytrauma — ICU-level care, ~5.4+ d, higher complication and surgery rates. CC/MCC drivers = acute respiratory failure, empyema, hemothorax requiring transfusion, need for surgical intervention, sepsis. Traumatic hemopneumothorax with major associated injuries may reassign to chest-trauma/trauma DRGs rather than 199-201
pathway_pearls: 1) Tension pneumothorax is a CLINICAL diagnosis — decompress immediately (needle then tube), never wait for a radiograph. 2) BTS 2023 shifted from rigid size cutoffs to symptom + high-risk-characteristic-driven care: stable minimally symptomatic PSP -> conservative observation, needle aspiration, or ambulatory device (Brown NEJM 2020; RAMPP) to slash LOS; reserve chest drains for symptomatic/high-risk. 3) Drain discipline: never clamp a bubbling drain, avoid high suction (re-expansion pulmonary edema), and remove only after >=24 h no air leak + confirmed re-expansion. 4) Traumatic tube thoracostomy: give antibiotic prophylaxis at insertion (EAST 2022), evacuate retained hemothorax early by VATS, and refer persistent air leak to thoracic surgery by 48-72 h; smoking cessation is the single biggest recurrence-reduction lever (recurrence ~70%->40%)
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Spontaneous/traumatic pneumothorax; chest-tube driven. Traumatic cases may fall under trauma DRGs.
drg_code_titles_v43_1: 199 — Pneumothorax with MCC; 200 — Pneumothorax with CC; 201 — Pneumothorax without CC/MCC
volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 7/7 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Current pass completed citation-resolvability, coding, taxonomy, schema, and consistency checks. The source workflow contains no completed adversarial high-stakes clinical verdict for this row; clinician revalidation remains required before protocol or order-set use.
data_quality_notes: Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk.

admission_criteria: Most skin-malignancy surgery (wide local excision, Mohs, small flap/graft) is AMBULATORY/23-hr — reserve inpatient admission for objective indications: extensive resection needing free/large rotation flap or split-thickness graft with monitored donor site; regional lymph-node dissection (axillary/inguinal/cervical) with drains; head-neck resection needing airway/neurovascular monitoring; perioperative comorbidity requiring inpatient optimization (anticoagulation bridging, brittle diabetes, high cardiac risk). For MAJOR SKIN DISORDERS (DRG 595-596): admit all SJS/TEN and SJS-TEN overlap, DRESS with organ involvement, erythroderma/exfoliative dermatitis, extensive autoimmune bullous disease, generalized pustular psoriasis with systemic toxicity. Objective admit triggers for skin failure: epidermal detachment >10% BSA; positive Nikolsky sign; mucosal (ocular/oral/genital) involvement; fever + fluid/electrolyte derangement; hemodynamic instability; inability to maintain hydration/nutrition. ED-dischargeable: small excisions, low-risk BCC/cSCC, localized stable dermatoses to outpatient derm/surgery
risk_stratification: AJCC 8th-edition TNM (Breslow thickness, ulceration, mitotic rate, nodal status) drives margin, SLNB and adjuvant decisions: <0.8 mm = T1a low-risk; 0.8-1.0 mm OR <0.8 mm with ulceration = T1b (offer SLNB); >1-2 mm T2, >2-4 mm T3, >4 mm T4. cSCC risk by Brigham & Women's (BWH) T-stage (T2b/T3 high-risk) + NCCN high-risk features (>2 mm depth, perineural, poorly differentiated, immunosuppression, ear/lip/temple) → Mohs + consider adjuvant. Caprini VTE score: >=5 = high-risk surgical → pharmacologic prophylaxis. SCAR/SJS-TEN prognosis by SCORTEN (7 variables, 1 pt each: age >=40; malignancy; HR >=120; initial detachment >10% BSA; BUN >28 mg/dL [urea >10 mmol/L]; glucose >252 mg/dL [>14 mmol/L]; bicarbonate <20 mEq/L) — mortality ~3.2% (0-1), 12.1% (2), 35.3% (3), 58.3% (4), >=90% (>=5); score >=2 drives burn-ICU transfer. RegiSCAR for DRESS; ALDEN algorithm for drug causality; ACS-NSQIP/RCRI for surgical-cardiac risk
initial_workup_labs: Surgical: CBC, CMP, coagulation panel (if flap/LND/anticoagulated), type & screen for major resection; LDH (prognostic in stage IV melanoma); tumor BRAF V600 mutation testing (guides targeted therapy); pregnancy test as indicated. Culprit lesion always confirmed by histopathology. SCAR/SJS-TEN: CBC with differential (eosinophilia/atypical lymphocytes in DRESS, lymphopenia), CMP for renal function, electrolytes, glucose and bicarbonate (SCORTEN inputs), LFTs (DRESS hepatitis), CK, blood cultures if febrile/septic, albumin/prealbumin for nutrition; skin biopsy (H&E ± frozen section) plus direct immunofluorescence to confirm and distinguish SSSS/AGEP/autoimmune bullous disease. Erythroderma/bullous: add HIV, consider peripheral smear. Triggers: hypoalbuminemia -> nutrition consult; positive cultures/rising lactate -> targeted antibiotics and sepsis workup
initial_imaging_dx: Skin biopsy is the diagnostic cornerstone — narrow-margin excisional (or full-thickness punch) biopsy for suspicious pigmented lesions, avoiding shave through the base. Melanoma: sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) performed concurrently with wide local excision for T1b and thicker; nodal ultrasound with FNA/core for palpable/suspicious nodes; cross-sectional imaging (CT chest/abdomen/pelvis ± neck or PET-CT, plus brain MRI) reserved for clinical stage III/IV or symptoms — NOT routine for stage I-II. High-risk cSCC/Merkel: CT or MRI for perineural spread or nodal disease; Merkel cell -> SLNB + baseline PET-CT. SCAR/SJS-TEN: bedside diagnosis + confirmatory biopsy (frozen section expedites); slit-lamp ophthalmology exam within 24h; imaging only for organ complications (CXR if respiratory mucosal involvement/hypoxia)
time_critical_interventions: SJS/TEN: IMMEDIATE withdrawal of the culprit drug is the single highest-impact intervention — each day of delay increases mortality; transfer to a burn center/ICU within hours for TEN, SJS-TEN overlap, or SCORTEN >=2; ophthalmology consult <24h to prevent permanent ocular sequelae (symblepharon, blindness); if used, systemic immunomodulation (cyclosporine or etanercept) is most effective started early in the progressive phase (<72h). Necrotizing soft-tissue infection in the differential is a surgical emergency -> debridement within 6-12h. Surgical prophylactic antibiotic (cefazolin) within 60 min before incision when indicated; maintain intraoperative normothermia; coordinate lymphoscintigraphy/tracer timing with excision for SLNB. No thrombolysis/cath-lab analog applies to this group
initial_management: Surgical skin malignancy: wide local excision with margins by Breslow thickness (in situ 0.5-1 cm; <=1 mm 1 cm; >1-2 mm 1-2 cm; >2 mm 2 cm) + SLNB for T1b+; therapeutic lymph-node dissection only for biopsy-proven nodal disease — completion LND after a positive sentinel node is NOT routine (nodal ultrasound surveillance per MSLT-II). cSCC/BCC: Mohs micrographic surgery for high-risk/H-zone/recurrent/immunosuppressed; standard excision 4 mm (low-risk BCC) or 4-6 mm (low-risk cSCC). Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (acetaminophen + NSAID + local/regional anesthetic). Adjuvant (post-resection, oncology-directed): anti-PD-1 (nivolumab or pembrolizumab) for resected stage IIB-IV melanoma; BRAF/MEK inhibitor (dabrafenib+trametinib) for BRAF V600-mutant stage III; radiotherapy ± avelumab for high-risk cSCC/Merkel. SCAR/SJS-TEN: stop culprit drug; burn-type supportive care — fluid/electrolyte repletion titrated to UOP 0.5-1 mL/kg/h (LESS aggressive than thermal burns), thermoregulation, non-adherent dressings preserving detached epidermis as biologic dressing, meticulous mucosal/ocular care, analgesia, early enteral nutrition; systemic therapy cyclosporine 3-5 mg/kg/day OR etanercept 50 mg SC (favored over IVIG/steroids, low-certainty evidence); avoid prophylactic systemic antibiotics. Autoimmune bullous: systemic corticosteroids ± rituximab (pemphigus). Transfusion restrictive: Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if cardiac); rarely needed for skin surgery
day1_milestones: Surgical POD0: complete resection + reconstruction; confirm hemostasis and flap/graft inset; send margins to pathology (frozen if indicated); place drains; start multimodal analgesia and mechanical VTE prophylaxis; same-day ambulation for ambulatory-extended cases; free-flap perfusion checks q1h. Medical/SCAR Day 1: confirm diagnosis and biopsy; STOP culprit drug and flag as allergy; calculate SCORTEN; initiate burn/ICU-level supportive care with IV access and fluid/electrolyte plan; map wounds (%BSA); obtain ophthalmology and urology/gynecology mucosal exams; start systemic immunomodulator if indicated; begin VTE and stress-ulcer prophylaxis; nutrition consult and early enteral feeding
day2_milestones: Surgical POD1: assess flap viability/graft take and drain-output trend; transition IV to oral analgesia; advance diet; mobilize; wound check; review preliminary pathology and margin status; continue VTE prophylaxis; begin discharge teaching (drain and wound care); discharge simple cases. Medical POD1/Day 2: reassess SCORTEN and BSA progression (detachment typically peaks days 2-4); track fluid balance and renal function; continue meticulous wound care and infection surveillance (culture-driven, not prophylactic antibiotics); repeat ophthalmology evaluation; continue immunomodulation; maintain nutrition/hydration and thermoregulation
day3plus_milestones: Surgical POD2+: continue flap/graft monitoring until viability assured; remove drains when output <30-50 mL/24h; ensure pain controlled on oral agents and wound healing on track; finalize pathology and AJCC staging -> refer to medical/radiation oncology for adjuvant discussion and establish nodal-ultrasound surveillance plan; discharge when criteria met. Medical POD2+: re-epithelialization begins ~day 7-14 in SJS/TEN; de-escalate support as skin heals; taper immunomodulator; manage complications (sepsis, AKI, ARDS, ocular, GI hemorrhage); mobilize with PT; step down from burn ICU when detachment halts and hemodynamically stable; continue multidisciplinary complication surveillance
consults_multidisciplinary: Surgical/oncologic: surgical oncology + plastic/reconstructive surgery (resection and reconstruction); medical oncology (adjuvant systemic therapy); radiation oncology (high-risk cSCC/Merkel, desmoplastic or head-neck melanoma); dermatopathology; genetic counseling for familial/multiple-primary melanoma. SCAR/SJS-TEN: burn surgery + intensivist + dermatology co-management; ophthalmology MANDATORY within 24h; urology/gynecology for genital mucosa; wound/ostomy nursing; clinical pharmacy for culprit-drug identification (ALDEN) and dosing. Cross-cutting: PT/OT early; dietitian/nutrition; care management and social work for disposition and home wound care; palliative care for advanced/metastatic disease
monitoring_level: Floor/ambulatory-extended: routine skin excision and low-risk reconstruction. Telemetry/step-down: free-flap reconstruction (q1h flap checks), major lymph-node dissection, or significant comorbidity. ICU/burn unit: TEN and SJS-TEN overlap, SCORTEN >=2, epidermal detachment >10-30% BSA, hemodynamic instability, sepsis, or DRESS with organ failure. Escalation triggers: expanding BSA detachment, hypotension or rising lactate, new AKI, respiratory involvement/hypoxia, sepsis physiology, or flap compromise on serial checks
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: early oral/enteral, high-protein for wound healing; SCAR patients are hypermetabolic — aggressive caloric/protein support with NG feeding if oral mucosa denuded; monitor albumin/prealbumin. Mobility: same-day or POD1 ambulation for surgical patients; PT for deconditioned ICU/SCAR patients. VTE: mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs) universal; add pharmacologic LMWH/UFH for Caprini >=5 surgical and immobile medical patients unless active denudation/bleeding contraindicates (individualize in TEN). Glucose target 140-180 mg/dL. Lines/catheters: remove surgical drains when output low; early Foley removal to limit CAUTI, though Foley is indicated in TEN with >20% BSA or genital involvement; minimize central-line days to reduce CLABSI
discharge_criteria: Surgical: pain controlled on oral analgesics; wound/flap/graft viable without active complication; drains removed OR patient/caregiver competent in drain care; tolerating diet; ambulating at baseline; pathology reviewed with staging and follow-up plan communicated. Medical/SCAR: skin re-epithelializing with no active detachment progression; hemodynamically stable, afebrile, infection controlled; adequate oral intake and hydration; mucosal and ocular status stabilized; SCORTEN-driven physiologic derangements resolved; outpatient dermatology and ophthalmology follow-up arranged
discharge_planning: Disposition: home for the majority; SNF/rehab for deconditioned SJS/TEN survivors; home-health/wound-care services for graft, flap, or drain management. Medication reconciliation: for SCAR, DOCUMENT the culprit drug as a hard-stop allergy with an EHR alert and patient allergy card — re-exposure can be fatal; taper analgesia; finalize adjuvant plan. Follow-up: surgical wound check 1-2 weeks; oncology for adjuvant therapy within 2-4 weeks; dermatology surveillance (melanoma total-body skin exam q3-6 months); ophthalmology for ocular sequelae; lymphedema surveillance after lymph-node dissection. Education: wound/drain care, warning signs (infection, flap compromise, bleeding), strict sun protection and monthly self skin-exam, and (SCAR) lifelong avoidance of the culprit drug class
expected_los: Ambulatory skin-malignancy excision/Mohs is same-day (0 day) or 23-hr observation. For admitted MAJOR SKIN DISORDER DRGs (CMS FY2025 benchmark, approximate): DRG 596 (without MCC) GMLOS ~3.3 days; DRG 595 (with MCC) GMLOS ~5.0 days. SJS/TEN burn-unit stays are re-epithelialization-driven and commonly 2-3+ weeks. Overall admitted-case benchmark ~3-5 days GMLOS for the skin-disorder DRGs
target_los: Ambulatory-extended surgery: 0-1 day (23-hr) for wide local excision ± SLNB or simple reconstruction; 1-2 days for major lymph-node dissection or flap reconstruction. Major skin disorders (non-TEN): target 3-4 days. TEN length is biology-limited (re-epithelialization, not compressible), but immediate culprit-drug withdrawal plus early burn-unit care measurably shortens stay and mortality
quality_metrics: Surgical: perioperative antibiotic timing within 60 min (SCIP), maintained normothermia, VTE prophylaxis (VTE-1/VTE-2), surgical-site-infection rate, margin-negative (R0) resection rate, unplanned return to OR/reoperation, 30-day readmission, PSI-90. Oncology: guideline-concordant SLNB in eligible melanoma, BRAF molecular-testing rate, time-to-adjuvant-therapy, CoC/NCCN concordance. SCAR/derm: time-to-culprit-drug-withdrawal, ophthalmology consult within 24h, SCORTEN-adjusted standardized mortality ratio, burn-center transfer timeliness, medication-allergy documentation rate. General: HCAHPS, inpatient mortality, LOS index, 30-day all-cause readmission
common_complications: Surgical: surgical-site infection, hematoma/seroma, flap or graft necrosis/partial loss, wound dehiscence, lymphedema/lymphocele after LND, sensory nerve injury, VTE — mitigated by prophylactic antibiotics, meticulous hemostasis, serial flap monitoring, drains, early mobilization and VTE prophylaxis. SCAR/SJS-TEN: sepsis (leading cause of death), fluid/electrolyte derangement, AKI, ARDS/respiratory failure, ocular scarring/symblepharon/blindness, GI hemorrhage, hypothermia, malnutrition, and long-term cutaneous/mucosal sequelae — mitigated by burn-unit care, culture-driven (not prophylactic) antibiotics, mandatory ophthalmology, aggressive nutrition and thermoregulation
readmission_drivers: Surgical: SSI/wound complications, hematoma, flap failure, uncontrolled pain, lymphocele — levers: early post-op wound visit, home-health wound care, explicit warning-sign education. SCAR: re-exposure to the culprit drug (preventable and catastrophic), secondary infection, ocular/mucosal complications, dehydration/malnutrition — levers: hard-stop allergy documentation with patient allergy card, structured dermatology/ophthalmology follow-up, home wound care. Oncologic: disease progression and delayed adjuvant initiation — lever: timely medical-oncology linkage and surveillance imaging per stage
guideline_source: PRIMARY: NCCN Clinical Practice Guidelines in Oncology — Melanoma: Cutaneous (2024-2025), Squamous Cell Skin Cancer (2024), Basal Cell Skin Cancer (2024), Merkel Cell Carcinoma (2024-2025). AAD — Guidelines of care for the management of primary cutaneous melanoma (Swetter et al., 2019) and cutaneous SCC/BCC (Alam et al./Kim et al., 2018). ASCO-SSO — Sentinel Lymph Node Biopsy and Management of Regional Lymph Nodes in Melanoma (Wong et al., 2018). ERAS Society principles + ACS NSQIP perioperative standards. For MAJOR SKIN DISORDERS/SCAR: British Association of Dermatologists SJS/TEN management guideline (Creamer et al., 2016) + Cochrane systematic review of systemic interventions (2022)
key_citations: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th ed (melanoma). MSLT-I: Morton DL et al., N Engl J Med 2014, PMID 24521106. MSLT-II: Faries MB et al., N Engl J Med 2017, PMID 28591523. Adjuvant nivolumab (CheckMate 238): Weber J et al., N Engl J Med 2017, PMID 28891423. Adjuvant pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-054): Eggermont AMM et al., N Engl J Med 2018, PMID 29658430. SJS/TEN systemic interventions (Cochrane): Jacobsen A et al., Cochrane Database Syst Rev 2022, PMID 35274741. SCORTEN: Bastuji-Garin S et al., J Invest Dermatol 2000. NCCN Melanoma/NMSC guidelines and AAD primary cutaneous melanoma guideline (J Am Acad Dermatol 2019)
evidence_grade: Mixed by subgroup. STRONG (Class I / NCCN Category 1, multiple RCTs) for melanoma excision margins, SLNB, MSLT-II nodal-surveillance strategy, and adjuvant anti-PD-1/BRAF-MEK therapy. MODERATE-STRONG (NCCN Category 2A, guideline consensus) for cSCC/BCC management and Mohs indications. MODERATE/CONSENSUS for SJS-TEN systemic therapy — cyclosporine and etanercept are favored but rest on small trials and observational data with low certainty (Cochrane 2022); supportive burn-unit care and early culprit-drug withdrawal are consensus standards. Overall: strong for the oncologic-surgical core, moderate/consensus for the severe-dermatosis supportive pathway
severity_cc_mcc_notes: CAUTION — this collapsed group mixes distinct populations and the platform label diverges from official CMS FY2025 titles: 595-596 Major Skin Disorders (with vs without MCC), 597-599 Malignant Breast Disorders, 600-601 Nonmalignant Breast Disorders — i.e., 597-601 are BREAST, not skin, DRGs, and skin-cancer EXCISIONS map to surgical MDC-09 DRGs (579-581), not here. The pathway therefore anchors on the skin-relevant inpatient content (major skin disorders/SCAR) plus admitted skin-malignancy surgery; breast DRGs should be pathwayed separately. Severity tiers: MCC drivers (sepsis, AKI, respiratory failure, major fluid/electrolyte derangement in SJS/TEN and erythroderma) push cases into 595 (w MCC), mandating burn-ICU care and roughly doubling LOS (~3 to 5+ days; TEN weeks). CC-tier drivers (secondary infection, diabetes, malnutrition) modestly lengthen stay. Low-acuity skin excisions are largely ambulatory and not captured as inpatient. Calibrate ICU need, LOS, and mortality by SCORTEN in the SCAR subset
pathway_pearls: (1) Right-size the setting: skin-cancer surgery is overwhelmingly ambulatory/23-hr — admit only for major reconstruction, nodal dissection, or comorbidity, and don't over-admit. (2) In SJS/TEN the two highest-yield levers are IMMEDIATE culprit-drug withdrawal and early burn-center/ICU transfer (SCORTEN >=2) — both independently reduce mortality; ophthalmology within 24h prevents lifelong ocular disability. (3) Follow MSLT-II — after a positive sentinel node, default to nodal ULTRASOUND surveillance rather than routine completion dissection (less lymphedema, no survival loss) and ensure timely medical-oncology linkage for adjuvant anti-PD-1/BRAF-MEK. (4) Prevent the catastrophic bounce-back: hard-stop EHR allergy documentation of the culprit drug (SCAR) plus structured wound/flap follow-up with home health

scope_and_volume_notes: Major skin disorders/malignancy (595-597) and other minor skin procedures (598-601).
