admission_criteria: Predominantly a PLANNED surgical admission for radical (RN) or partial/nephron-sparing (PN) nephrectomy - open, laparoscopic, or robotic - for biopsy-proven or radiographically suspicious RCC (solid enhancing mass or Bosniak 3/4 complex-cystic lesion). Inpatient (vs same-day/23h observation, increasingly used for uncomplicated robotic PN) justified by: open or complex approach, IVC tumor thrombus (level I-IV), high R.E.N.A.L. complexity PN, solitary kidney, significant CKD, major comorbidity, or an anticipated 2-midnight stay. Urgent/emergent admission for: spontaneous/symptomatic tumor hemorrhage (Wunderlich syndrome), obstruction/infection, or paraneoplastic crisis (severe hypercalcemia). Metastatic disease: cytoreductive nephrectomy only after multidisciplinary/systemic-therapy (IMDC-guided) decision.
risk_stratification: R.E.N.A.L. nephrometry score (low 4-6, moderate 7-9, high 10-12) -> PN feasibility, warm-ischemia planning, open vs MIS; clinical T-stage cT1a <=4 cm (prioritize PN), cT1b >4-7 cm, cT2 >7 cm, cT3 venous/perinephric, cT4 -> approach, lymphadenectomy, thrombus team; AUA RN gate = all 3 of high tumor complexity + no CKD/proteinuria + expected post-op eGFR >45; baseline eGFR/CKD stage -> nephron-sparing mandate; ASA class + RCRI (Revised Cardiac Risk Index) for cardiac risk; Caprini score / EAU high-risk tier -> extended VTE prophylaxis; modified Frailty Index (mFI-5) -> prehab/ICU planning; IMDC risk (favorable/intermediate/poor) for metastatic systemic sequencing; post-op Leibovich/SSIGN for recurrence-based surveillance intensity.
initial_workup_labs: CBC (baseline Hgb for transfusion; anemia from hematuria/chronic disease); CMP/BMP with creatinine + eGFR (baseline renal function - drives nephron-sparing decision and post-op AKI detection); LFTs (Stauffer paraneoplastic hepatic dysfunction); calcium + albumin (paraneoplastic hypercalcemia); LDH (prognostic/IMDC); PT/INR-aPTT coagulation; type & screen, crossmatch 2 units for open/complex/IVC-thrombus cases; urinalysis + urine culture (hematuria, exclude infection before instrumentation). Abnormal calcium/LFTs triggers paraneoplastic workup; low eGFR triggers nephrology and mandatory nephron-sparing consideration.
initial_imaging_dx: Multiphase contrast-enhanced CT abdomen/pelvis (renal-mass protocol) - or MRI if contrast contraindicated - for staging, nephrometry, and vascular mapping (obtained pre-admission). CT chest for metastatic staging in >=cT1b or high-grade. MRI abdomen to define IVC tumor-thrombus level I-IV and plan cardiac/vascular involvement. Core-needle renal mass biopsy (NOT FNA) when result changes management (indeterminate mass, suspected lymphoma/metastasis/abscess, pre-ablation); not required if surgery planned regardless. Nuclear renography (MAG3/DMSA) for split function in solitary kidney/bilateral disease. Bone scan/brain MRI only if symptomatic or ALP elevated.
time_critical_interventions: Elective operation - time targets are ERAS/perioperative not door-to-intervention: surgical antibiotic prophylaxis (cefazolin) within 60 min of incision; preoperative carbohydrate drink up to 2h pre-induction, avoid prolonged fasting; maintain intraoperative normothermia and goal-directed euvolemia. During PN keep warm ischemia time <25 min (every minute of ischemia costs nephrons) with early-unclamping/off-clamp technique. VTE prophylaxis: mechanical IPC from induction, pharmacologic LMWH the morning after surgery (EAU). EMERGENT tracks: active tumor hemorrhage -> resuscitation + IR angioembolization or emergent nephrectomy; IVC thrombus -> cardiac/vascular standby +/- bypass.
initial_management: Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (ERAS): scheduled acetaminophen + regional block (TAP/liposomal bupivacaine; thoracic epidural for open flank) + short-course NSAID/ketorolac ONLY if renal function adequate (avoid in solitary/impaired kidney), rescue short-acting opioid; PONV prophylaxis (dexamethasone + ondansetron). Single-dose first-generation cephalosporin (cefazolin) antibiotic prophylaxis. Balanced-crystalloid goal-directed fluids, avoid overload and nephrotoxins. Transfusion at restrictive threshold Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 g/dL if symptomatic CAD). VTE: IPC + enoxaparin per risk. Source control = the resection (PN vs RN). Reversal/hemostasis available: protamine, PCC/vitamin K, tranexamic acid.
day1_milestones: POD0: emergence from anesthesia with hemodynamic stability; confirm urine output >0.5 mL/kg/h (heightened single-kidney monitoring after RN); begin clear liquids/early oral intake the evening of surgery; out of bed to chair/dangle same day (early mobilization); multimodal analgesia + antiemetics; IPC on, first LMWH dose per protocol; avoid routine nasogastric tube; incentive spirometry; check POD0 Hgb and creatinine; for PN watch for hemorrhage/gross hematuria.
day2_milestones: POD1: advance to regular diet as tolerated (ERAS early feeding); stop IV fluids once tolerating PO (convert to heplock); transition to oral analgesia; ambulate 3-4x/day; remove urinary catheter (unless collecting-system/bladder-cuff repair); continue LMWH + IPC; recheck Hgb, creatinine, electrolytes; assess for ileus/nausea; review surgical-drain output and remove if low-volume and non-urinary. Many robotic/laparoscopic RN and PN meet discharge criteria on POD1.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+: confirm return of bowel function (flatus/BM), treat ileus if present; remove drain after checking drain-fluid creatinine when urine leak suspected in PN (drain Cr >> serum Cr = leak); continued ambulation; oral analgesia only; renal function stable/improving; afebrile with no infection; meet discharge criteria (typically POD2-4 for open/complex). Prolonged stay (POD4+) driven by ileus, urine leak, hemorrhage, or MCC-level events. Review final pathology; set risk-based surveillance and refer high-risk (pT2-T4/N1, high-grade clear-cell) for adjuvant pembrolizumab discussion (KEYNOTE-564).
consults_multidisciplinary: Urologic oncology (primary); anesthesiology/pre-op ERAS clinic (POD-planning, cardiac clearance); nephrology if CKD/solitary kidney/AKI; medical oncology for metastatic or high-risk adjuvant (pembrolizumab) decisions; cardiothoracic/vascular surgery for IVC thrombus level III-IV; interventional radiology for pre-op embolization or postop hemorrhage; PT/OT for mobilization and frailty; nutrition for prehab/malnutrition; pharmacy for anticoagulation and opioid stewardship; case management/social work engaged pre-op for discharge planning and home-LMWH teaching; palliative care for advanced disease.
monitoring_level: Most elective PN/RN -> surgical floor with routine vitals; telemetry not routine. Step-down/ICU for: IVC tumor-thrombus resection or bypass, major intraoperative blood loss/transfusion, hemodynamic instability, significant cardiopulmonary comorbidity, or solitary-kidney PN at high AKI risk. Escalation triggers: tachycardia/hypotension with falling serial Hgb (hemorrhage - the most feared early event), oliguria/rising creatinine (AKI), respiratory compromise (pneumothorax after flank/thoracoabdominal approach), or sepsis. Low threshold for CT angiography and IR embolization pathway.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition (ERAS): no prolonged fasting, pre-op carbohydrate loading, clear liquids POD0, rapid advance to regular diet POD1, avoid routine NGT. Mobility: out of bed POD0, ambulate 3-4x/day from POD1. VTE: mechanical IPC on all until ambulating PLUS pharmacologic LMWH (enoxaparin 40 mg daily) for moderate/high risk; EXTENDED 28-day (~4-week) prophylaxis for high-risk cancer-surgery patients (EAU). Glucose <180 mg/dL, avoid hypoglycemia. Lines/catheters: urinary catheter out POD1, IV to heplock once tolerating PO, drains out when low-output and non-urinary (POD1-3). NSAID caution with single/impaired kidney.
discharge_criteria: Tolerating oral diet and controlled on oral analgesia (no IV opioids); ambulating at/near baseline; return of bowel function (flatus, no ileus); afebrile and hemodynamically stable; stable Hgb with no active bleeding; acceptable/stable renal function (creatinine trending appropriately); voiding adequately after catheter removal; no evidence of urine leak; wound intact; extended VTE-prophylaxis plan taught and arranged.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home (home health for LMWH injections if needed). Med reconciliation: extended enoxaparin x~28 days for high-risk; oral opioid-sparing analgesia with scheduled acetaminophen + bowel regimen; adjust antihypertensives for nephron loss; hold nephrotoxins. Follow-up: urology/uro-oncology visit at 2-4 weeks (wound, pathology, surveillance plan); BMP/eGFR at 4-6 weeks to set new renal baseline; nephrology referral if eGFR <45-60. Education: activity/lifting restriction 4-6 weeks, incision care, red flags (fever, bleeding, decreased urine, leg swelling/DVT, chest pain/PE). Risk-based surveillance imaging per AUA tier (LR/IR/HR/VHR). Refer high-risk clear-cell to med onc for adjuvant pembrolizumab (KEYNOTE-564).
expected_los: Clinical: minimally-invasive (robotic/laparoscopic) PN or RN ~1-3 days; open RN ~3-5 days; IVC-thrombus/complex ~5-7+ days. DRG GMLOS benchmarks (CMS IPPS, approximate): DRG 658 neoplasm without CC/MCC ~2-2.5 days; DRG 657 with CC ~3.5-4 days; DRG 656 with MCC ~6.5-7 days. Blended expected LOS ~2-4 days.
target_los: Under ideal ERAS + minimally-invasive pathway: 1-2 days for robotic/laparoscopic PN or RN (uncomplicated robotic PN increasingly 23h/same-day); 2-3 days for open; target discharge by POD1-2 for the no-CC/MCC (DRG 658) case.
quality_metrics: ERAS bundle-compliance %; perioperative/SCIP measures (antibiotic within 60 min, appropriate selection/discontinuation, normothermia, VTE prophylaxis ordered and received - VTE-1/VTE-2, SSI); postoperative opioid consumption (MME); positive surgical margin rate for PN (oncologic quality); warm-ischemia time; renal-function preservation (delta-eGFR); 30-day complication (Clavien-Dindo) and transfusion rates; 30-day readmission; in-hospital/30-day mortality; NSQIP participation; PSI-90; guideline-concordant nephron-sparing utilization for cT1a.
common_complications: Hemorrhage/bleeding requiring transfusion or reintervention (most common serious early event; mitigate: meticulous hemostasis, restrictive transfusion, serial Hgb, IR embolization). AKI/CKD progression (mitigate: nephron-sparing, warm ischemia <25 min, nephrotoxin avoidance, euvolemia). Urine leak/urinoma after PN ~1-4% (mitigate: watertight collecting-system repair, drain-Cr monitoring, prolonged catheter/ureteral stent). Ileus (mitigate: ERAS opioid-sparing, early feeding/mobilization). Pneumothorax/pleural injury (flank/thoracoabdominal; recognize intraop, CXR). Surgical-site infection; VTE/PE (mitigate: prophylaxis); atrial fibrillation; splenic/pancreatic/bowel injury (left-sided).
readmission_drivers: Top drivers: hemorrhage/hematoma and symptomatic anemia; urine leak/urinoma and infection (UTI, abscess, SSI); ileus/GI dysfunction, dehydration, failure to thrive; VTE/PE; uncontrolled pain; AKI/electrolyte derangement. Prevention levers: extended VTE prophylaxis with home-injection teaching; drain-creatinine check before drain removal; structured ERAS discharge (oral analgesia + bowel regimen); nurse-navigator/24-72h phone follow-up plus 2-week visit; explicit red-flag education; nephrology follow-up for renal function.
guideline_source: AUA Renal Mass and Localized Renal Cancer Guideline, 2021 amendment (Parts I & II); EAU Renal Cell Carcinoma Guidelines, 2024/2025 update; EAU Guidelines on Thromboprophylaxis in Urological Surgery, 2022; ERAS Society principles (adapted - no dedicated nephrectomy guideline; renal ERAS/RERAS protocols derived from the ERAS radical-cystectomy guideline); NCCN Kidney Cancer Guidelines; adjuvant immunotherapy per KEYNOTE-564.
key_citations: AUA: Campbell SC et al., J Urol 2021;206:199-208 Part I (PMID 34115547; DOI 10.1097/JU.0000000000001911) and 206:209-218 Part II (PMID 34115531; DOI 10.1097/JU.0000000000001912). EORTC 30904 RCT (partial vs radical): Van Poppel H et al., Eur Urol 2011;59:543-52 oncologic outcomes, 10-yr OS 81.1% RN vs 75.7% NSS, HR 1.50 (PMID 21186077); complications Eur Urol 2007;51:1606-15 (PMID 17140723); renal function Scosyrev E et al., Eur Urol 2014;65:372-7, eGFR<60 in 85.7% RN vs 64.7% NSS (PMID 23850254). EAU RCC 2025 update (PMID 40118739). ERAS-urology GRADE review: Long X et al., J Robot Surg 2025;19:358 (PMID 40622655). RERAS: Roebuck EH et al., Urol Oncol 2022;40:383.e23 (PMID 35752565). Adjuvant pembrolizumab KEYNOTE-564: Choueiri TK et al., N Engl J Med 2021;385:683-694 (PMID 34407342). Per PubMed; citations verified, none fabricated.
evidence_grade: Moderate-to-Strong overall. Surgical oncology backbone (nephron-sparing prioritized for cT1a; RN gating criteria) = Strong, Class I AUA/EAU with RCT support (EORTC 30904); PN for T1a is AUA Moderate/Grade B. Adjuvant pembrolizumab for high-risk = Strong (phase-3 RCT, FDA-approved). ERAS perioperative bundle for nephrectomy = Moderate: individual elements are high-quality (extrapolated from colorectal/cystectomy) but nephrectomy-specific ERAS evidence is largely observational/retrospective and a dedicated ERAS Society nephrectomy guideline does not yet exist. VTE prophylaxis = EAU procedure-specific, low-to-moderate quality.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Group collapses by severity: DRG 658 (neoplasm, no CC/MCC) = uncomplicated minimally-invasive nephrectomy, floor care, LOS ~1-2 d; DRG 657 (with CC) = comorbidity (CKD, controlled DM, anemia) or minor complication (ileus, single transfusion, UTI), LOS ~3-4 d; DRG 656 (with MCC) = major event (hemorrhage needing reoperation, dialysis-requiring AKI, sepsis, PE, respiratory failure) or IVC tumor-thrombus/bypass case -> ICU/step-down, LOS ~6-7+ d. DRGs 686/687 (non-neoplasm with MCC/CC) capture benign/non-cancer kidney-ureter procedures sharing the same OR footprint. Ascending severity shifts monitoring level up, lengthens LOS, and raises readmission and mortality; MCC tier drives cardiac/vascular and ICU consults.
pathway_pearls: 1) Default to nephron-sparing (partial nephrectomy) for cT1a <=4 cm and keep warm ischemia <25 min - preserves eGFR without compromising cancer control (EORTC 30904: equivalent oncologic outcomes, less CKD). 2) Right-size the admission: robotic/laparoscopic approach + full ERAS bundle -> POD1-2 (or 23h) discharge; reserve inpatient/ICU for open, IVC-thrombus, or major comorbidity. 3) Opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia + early feeding + early mobilization is the dominant LOS/opioid lever (RERAS). 4) Own the two gotchas: bleeding (serial Hgb, restrictive transfusion, ready IR embolization) and - for partial - urine leak (check drain creatinine before pulling the drain). 5) Give high-risk cancer-surgery patients extended ~4-week LMWH and teach home injections before discharge.

scope_and_volume_notes: Radical/partial nephrectomy for renal-cell carcinoma; urologic-oncology. Overlaps kidney-procedure DRGs (656-658).

admission_criteria: Admit (inpatient) for objective decompensation/instability from a valvular or adult congenital lesion: acute decompensated HF/pulmonary edema or new NYHA III-IV symptoms; symptomatic severe aortic stenosis (Stage D) with syncope, angina, or HF; acute severe mitral or aortic regurgitation (flail leaflet, endocarditis destruction) with pulmonary edema; hemodynamic instability/low-output or cardiogenic shock; suspected/confirmed infective endocarditis needing IV antibiotics; new rapid AF/flutter with valve disease plus HF or instability; high-grade AV block or symptomatic bradycardia; ACHD with new cyanosis/desaturation, arrhythmia, or HF; troponin-positive demand ischemia or end-organ hypoperfusion. Observation/ED discharge appropriate for stable compensated chronic valve disease without new symptoms, or rate-controlled AF without HF, referable to expedited outpatient TTE + valve clinic.
risk_stratification: Echo severity staging (ACC/AHA VHD Stages A-D): severe AS = Vmax >=4 m/s, mean gradient >=40 mmHg, AVA <=1.0 cm2 (indexed <=0.6 cm2/m2); very severe AS Vmax >=5 / mean >=60 -> expedite intervention; low-flow/low-gradient AS (AVA <=1.0, mean <40, LVEF <50%) -> dobutamine stress echo. Primary severe MR: EROA >=0.40 cm2, RegVol >=60 mL, RF >=50% -> surgery/TEER eval. Severe AR: RF >=50%, VC >0.6 cm; intervene if symptomatic or LVEF <=55% or LVESD >50 mm (>25 mm/m2). Severe MS: MVA <=1.5 cm2 (very severe <=1.0); Wilkins score <=8 favors balloon commissurotomy. Procedural risk: STS-PROM (<4% low, 4-8% intermediate, >8% high) +/- EuroSCORE II + frailty -> TAVR vs SAVR Heart Team decision. AF: CHA2DS2-VASc >=2 men/>=3 women -> anticoagulate (warfarin, not DOAC, for mechanical valve or moderate-severe rheumatic MS). IE: 2023 Duke-ISCVID (Definite/Possible) -> TEE + Endocarditis Team; vegetation >10 mm = high embolic risk. ACHD physiologic stage A-D (2025 guideline) drives disposition.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, CMP (electrolytes/renal for diuretic and contrast planning), magnesium, LFTs (congestion), coags/INR-aPTT (anticoagulation, pre-procedure), type & screen; BNP or NT-proBNP (congestion severity/prognosis); high-sensitivity troponin (ischemia/demand, myocardial involvement in IE); blood cultures 2-3 sets from separate sites BEFORE antibiotics whenever IE suspected (fever, new murmur, emboli) plus ESR/CRP and procalcitonin; TSH (arrhythmia), HbA1c and lipids (pre-procedure optimization); iron studies/ferritin (HF and cyanotic ACHD). In cyanotic ACHD add ABG/SpO2, CBC for secondary erythrocytosis, uric acid. Triggers: rising creatinine -> hold nephrotoxins/limit contrast; hyponatremia -> worse prognosis, fluid restrict; positive cultures -> organism-directed antibiotics + TEE; elevated natriuretic peptide/troponin -> confirm decompensation and admit.
initial_imaging_dx: 12-lead ECG within 10 min of arrival (rhythm, ischemia, AV block, chamber enlargement); portable CXR on admission (pulmonary edema, cardiomegaly, congenital silhouette); comprehensive transthoracic echo (TTE) within 24 h - STAT if unstable, acute severe regurgitation, or suspected endocarditis - to grade lesion severity/stage and LV/RV function; continuous telemetry from admission. Transesophageal echo (TEE) when TTE nondiagnostic, for endocarditis, MR mechanism, or pre-intervention planning; repeat TEE at 3-5-7 days if initial negative but suspicion persists. Pre-intervention: ECG-gated cardiac CTA for TAVR annular sizing/access and coronary angiography before valve surgery/TAVR; cardiac MRI for ACHD anatomy, RV volumes, and regurgitant fraction. For diagnostically uncertain IE: 18F-FDG PET/CT and/or cardiac CT (both now major criteria in 2023 Duke-ISCVID).
time_critical_interventions: No universal minute-level door-to-device metric as in ACS/stroke; the time-critical elements are: acute severe AR/MR with cardiogenic shock or refractory pulmonary edema -> emergent Heart Team/cardiac surgery activation; suspected IE with sepsis/septic shock -> blood cultures then empiric IV antibiotics within 1 h (Surviving Sepsis/SEP-1) and lactate clearance; hemodynamically unstable arrhythmia (AF/flutter/VT) -> ACLS synchronized cardioversion; symptomatic complete heart block -> temporary transvenous/transcutaneous pacing; symptomatic severe AS with decompensation -> urgent (not weeks) inpatient AVR/TAVR evaluation, with balloon aortic valvuloplasty as a bridge in shock or refractory pulmonary edema; acute embolic stroke from valve/IE -> stroke pathway activation.
initial_management: Congestion/decompensated HF: IV loop diuretics (furosemide, bumetanide, torsemide) titrated to euvolemia; in acute severe MR/AR add IV vasodilator/afterload reduction (nitroprusside, nitroglycerin) to improve forward flow. CRITICAL in severe AS: avoid aggressive preload/afterload reduction, nitrates, and over-diuresis (fixed obstruction -> catastrophic hypotension). AF: rate control with beta-blocker (metoprolol) or non-dihydropyridine CCB (diltiazem - avoid in reduced EF/decompensation), digoxin adjunct, amiodarone for rhythm; anticoagulate per CHA2DS2-VASc (warfarin INR 2.5-3.5 for mechanical valves/moderate-severe rheumatic MS; DOACs contraindicated there). Cardiogenic shock: norepinephrine + dobutamine, mechanical support (avoid IABP with significant AR), BAV bridge in AS. IE (after cultures): native valve vancomycin + ceftriaxone; prosthetic valve vancomycin + gentamicin + rifampin +/- cefepime; de-escalate organism-directed per ID/AHA 2015, typically 4-6 weeks IV; source control = valve surgery for HF, uncontrolled infection, or vegetation >10 mm. Transfusion restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 with active ischemia). Reversal for bleeding/urgent surgery: vitamin K + 4-factor PCC (warfarin), idarucizumab (dabigatran), andexanet/PCC (Xa inhibitors).
day1_milestones: Complete TTE and establish valve lesion, severity stage, and LVEF; confirm the primary admission driver; start targeted therapy (diuresis, rate/rhythm control, empiric antibiotics if IE) on continuous telemetry; place Cardiology consult and, for severe lesions, initiate multidisciplinary Heart Valve Team / ACHD-center referral; risk-stratify with STS-PROM and CHA2DS2-VASc; begin daily weights, strict I/O, VTE prophylaxis decision, and admission medication reconciliation; if IE, obtain ID consult, order TEE, and perform embolic/source survey (fundoscopy, skin, neuro).
day2_milestones: Reassess decongestion (net-negative balance, improving dyspnea/oxygenation) and replete electrolytes (K/Mg) with renal function surveillance; transition IV to oral guideline-directed therapy where appropriate; achieve resting rate control (<110 bpm) or restore rhythm and confirm therapeutic anticoagulation/bridging plan; advance pre-intervention workup (TEE, gated cardiac CTA, coronary angiography) and hold Heart Team review of intervention candidacy and timing (TAVR/SAVR/TEER/PMBC vs continued medical therapy); for IE, confirm organism, narrow antibiotics, decide OPAT vs surgery, and monitor for AV block/emboli/HF; early mobilization with PT/OT and frailty assessment if a procedure is planned.
day3plus_milestones: Patient near-euvolemic on a stable oral regimen, hemodynamically stable, rate/rhythm controlled, and therapeutically anticoagulated when indicated; definitive plan finalized - either scheduled inpatient valve intervention/surgery or discharge onto an expedited outpatient valve-clinic pathway with an explicit timeline; for IE, stable on the definitive antibiotic regimen with OPAT secured (PICC + home infusion or SNF) and surgical timing decided if indicated; complete patient/family education, final medication reconciliation, and booked follow-up; verify all objective discharge-readiness criteria are met before departure.
consults_multidisciplinary: Cardiology (valvular/structural/imaging) day 1; multidisciplinary Heart Valve Team (interventional cardiology + cardiac surgery + imaging) for severe lesions; ACHD specialist / regional ACHD center for congenital patients (2025 guideline Class I); cardiac surgery for surgical candidates and acute severe regurgitation; electrophysiology for arrhythmia, high-grade AV block, or CIED; Infectious Diseases + Endocarditis Team for IE; cardiac anesthesia pre-procedure; pharmacy (anticoagulation and antibiotic/OPAT dosing); care management and social work (OPAT logistics, disposition, transportation, insurance); nutrition (HF sodium/fluid, iron repletion in cyanotic ACHD); PT/OT (frailty and mobility); palliative care for inoperable or high-risk symptomatic disease.
monitoring_level: Telemetry/floor: stable valve disease, controlled arrhythmia, uncomplicated IE. Step-down/higher-acuity telemetry: significant arrhythmia, decongestion needing frequent diuretic titration, symptomatic high-grade obstruction. ICU: cardiogenic shock, refractory pulmonary edema, hemodynamic instability, inotrope/vasopressor or mechanical circulatory support need, acute severe AR/MR awaiting surgery, complete heart block or unstable ventricular arrhythmia, post-valvuloplasty complications, and sepsis/septic shock from IE. Escalation triggers: hypotension or rising lactate, worsening hypoxemia, new high-grade AV block, sustained VT, embolic/stroke event, or escalating vasoactive requirements.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: heart-failure sodium restriction (~2 g/day) with fluid restriction if hyponatremic/congested; cardiac/diabetic diet; iron repletion in iron-deficient HF and cyanotic ACHD. Mobility: early progressive mobilization once hemodynamically stable, PT for deconditioning/frailty, avoid prolonged bedrest. VTE prophylaxis: pharmacologic LMWH/heparin unless already therapeutically anticoagulated or high bleeding risk/imminent procedure; mechanical prophylaxis if pharmacologic contraindicated. Glucose target 140-180 mg/dL inpatient. Lines/catheters: remove Foley and central lines early with daily necessity review; if prolonged IV antibiotics required for IE, place PICC for OPAT rather than leaving temporary access.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable and near-euvolemic on a stable oral diuretic/GDMT regimen for >=24 h; oxygen saturation at patient baseline; ventricular rate controlled (<110 bpm) or rhythm restored; a definitive valve-intervention plan established (scheduled procedure or expedited valve-clinic follow-up) and understood by the patient; therapeutic anticoagulation plan in place with stable renal function and electrolytes; for IE - afebrile trend, clearing/negative cultures, a stable definitive antibiotic plan with OPAT secured, and no untreated surgical indication; no unaddressed high-risk feature (uncontrolled arrhythmia, active ischemia, ongoing decompensation).
discharge_planning: Disposition: home with home-health/OPAT for IE, or SNF for IV antibiotics/rehab; ACHD patients linked back to a regional ACHD center. Medication reconciliation including anticoagulation (warfarin with bridging + INR plan or appropriate DOAC), diuretics, rate control, and antibiotics; provide an endocarditis-prophylaxis card to eligible high-risk patients (prosthetic valve, prior IE, specified ACHD). Follow-up: cardiology/valve clinic within 1-2 weeks (sooner for severe lesions awaiting intervention), INR check within 3-5 days if on warfarin, ID follow-up with weekly labs for OPAT, ACHD clinic as applicable. Education: red-flag symptoms (dyspnea, syncope, weight gain, fever), daily weights, dental hygiene and antibiotic prophylaxis, medication adherence. Transitions: 48-72 h follow-up call, teach-back, warm handoff to the valve/ACHD team.
expected_los: CMS FY2025 IPPS benchmarks (approximate): MS-DRG 307 (without MCC) GMLOS ~1.9 days (AMLOS ~2.4); MS-DRG 306 (with MCC) GMLOS ~3.4 days (AMLOS ~4.7); blended typical stay ~2-4 days. Complicated IE requiring prolonged IV antibiotics runs substantially longer unless bridged to OPAT.
target_los: Best-practice optimized target: ~1.5-2 days without MCC (307) with expedited STAT/24-h TTE and early disposition; ~3 days with MCC (306); blended target ~2-3 days. Non-surgical IE stabilized to OPAT in ~5-7 days. Key lever is same-day echo, early Heart Team activation, and a defined intervention plan rather than an open-ended medical stay.
quality_metrics: 30-day all-cause and HF readmission; 30-day and in-hospital mortality; observed-to-expected LOS. Process: time-to-TTE and echo appropriateness; documented Heart Valve Team evaluation for severe VHD; CHA2DS2-VASc-concordant anticoagulation for AF; blood cultures drawn before antibiotics and ID consultation for IE (associated with lower mortality); guideline-concordant antibiotic selection/duration; SEP-1 bundle compliance if septic. Registry participation: STS/ACC TVT Registry (valve interventions), Get With The Guidelines-HF, IMPACT/ACHD registries. Transitions: medication reconciliation, follow-up scheduled within 7-14 days, and documented patient education/teach-back.
common_complications: Acute kidney injury (cardiorenal, over-diuresis, contrast) - weight-based diuretic titration and minimize nephrotoxins/contrast; electrolyte derangement and arrhythmia from diuresis - K/Mg repletion on telemetry; arrhythmia progression (AF with RVR, heart block) - rate/rhythm control and EP; thromboembolism/stroke (AF, endocarditis emboli, prosthetic-valve thrombosis) - anticoagulation and early source control; hemodynamic decompensation/cardiogenic shock in critical AS - avoid vasodilators, cautious diuresis, early escalation; IE-specific perivalvular abscess with AV block, septic emboli, HF from valve destruction, persistent bacteremia - serial ECG, repeat TEE, timely surgery; anticoagulation-related bleeding and contrast reactions.
readmission_drivers: Recurrent decompensated HF from an unaddressed severe valve lesion (delayed definitive intervention) - expedite Heart Team and intervention with close valve-clinic follow-up; incomplete decongestion or un-optimized diuretics at discharge - ensure near-euvolemia, an explicit diuretic plan, and early follow-up labs; anticoagulation gaps (subtherapeutic INR, missed DOAC doses) - structured bridging, INR check in 3-5 days, anticoagulation clinic; recurrent/uncontrolled arrhythmia - rate/rhythm optimization and EP follow-up; IE relapse or OPAT failure (line complications, non-adherence) - ID follow-up, weekly labs, secure infusion access; medication/appointment non-adherence and social barriers - medication reconciliation, teach-back education, care management, transportation support.
guideline_source: PRIMARY: 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Patients With Valvular Heart Disease (Otto & Nishimura; Circulation 2021) and 2025 ACC/AHA/HRS/ISACHD/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Adults With Congenital Heart Disease (Gurvitz & Krieger; Circulation & JACC 2025, replacing the 2018 AHA/ACC ACHD guideline). ADJUNCTS: 2023 Duke-ISCVID Criteria for infective endocarditis and 2015 AHA IE management statement; 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Atrial Fibrillation Guideline; 2022 AHA/ACC/HFSA Heart Failure Guideline for decompensated-HF management.
key_citations: Otto CM, Nishimura RA, et al. 2020 ACC/AHA Guideline for the Management of Patients With Valvular Heart Disease. Circulation. 2021;143(5):e72-e227 (Exec Summary PMID 33332149; DOI 10.1161/CIR.0000000000000923). Gurvitz M, Krieger EV, et al. 2025 ACC/AHA/HRS/ISACHD/SCAI Guideline for the Management of Adults With Congenital Heart Disease. Circulation. 2025;153(8):e115-e251 (PMID 41411375; DOI 10.1161/CIR.0000000000001402) and J Am Coll Cardiol. 2026;87(7):822-976 (PMID 41411480). Fowler VG, et al. 2023 Duke-ISCVID Criteria for Infective Endocarditis. Clin Infect Dis. 2023;77(4):518-526 (PMID 37138445; DOI 10.1093/cid/ciad271). Joglar JA, et al. 2023 ACC/AHA/ACCP/HRS Atrial Fibrillation Guideline. Circulation 2024;149:e1-e156 (PMID 38033089). Landmark intervention-threshold trials (inform disposition, not the medical stay): PARTNER and Evolut Low-Risk TAVR programs - named, PMIDs not asserted to avoid fabrication.
evidence_grade: Strong for lesion-specific management - anchored on Class I recommendations from current ACC/AHA Class I society guidelines (2020 VHD, 2025 ACHD) and validated diagnostic criteria (2023 Duke-ISCVID; CHA2DS2-VASc). Moderate/consensus for the inpatient-logistics layer (stabilization, bridging, LOS targets), which is partly extrapolated because MS-DRG 306/307 bundle heterogeneous medical presentations and definitive therapy is frequently procedural (reassigned to structural/surgical DRGs).
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MS-DRG 307 (no MCC): typically stable symptomatic valve disease or controlled arrhythmia; short ~2-day stay on floor/telemetry with an expedited outpatient valve pathway. MS-DRG 306 (with MCC): MCC drivers include acute HF/pulmonary edema, cardiogenic shock, endocarditis with sepsis/septic emboli/perivalvular abscess, AKI, high-grade AV block, and stroke - producing longer LOS (~3.4+ days), greater ICU/step-down utilization, more complications, and higher readmission/mortality; IE and cardiogenic shock disproportionately populate 306. Note that definitive valve procedures move the case to structural/surgical DRGs (TAVR 266/267; valve surgery 216-221), so this collapsed medical group is fundamentally stabilization plus bridging to intervention.
pathway_pearls: 1) Get the echo early and stage the lesion precisely - severity stage plus symptom status drives disposition, urgency, and Heart Team activation. 2) In critical aortic stenosis, protect preload and afterload: avoid aggressive vasodilators/nitrates and over-diuresis (fixed obstruction -> catastrophic hypotension) - the fix is the valve, not the vasodilator. 3) Don't let the medical admission be a dead end - activate the multidisciplinary Heart Valve Team / ACHD center during the stay and discharge with a definitive intervention plan and timeline, since unaddressed severe valve disease is the dominant readmission lever. 4) Endocarditis: cultures before antibiotics, TEE, ID/Endocarditis Team, and early surgical evaluation for HF, uncontrolled infection, or vegetation >10 mm.

admission_criteria: Planned INPATIENT surgical admission — radical cystectomy is inherently inpatient (not observation/ED-dischargeable): objective indications are muscle-invasive bladder cancer cT2-T4a N0 M0; BCG-unresponsive or very-high-risk non-muscle-invasive disease (recurrent high-grade cT1, CIS, high-volume/variant histology); or palliative cystectomy for intractable hematuria/pain/fistula; typically same-day-of-surgery admit after pre-op optimization/neoadjuvant chemo completed; emergent inpatient admission if candidate presents with obstructive uropathy, urosepsis, or uncontrolled gross hematuria requiring stabilization before staged surgery
risk_stratification: ASA Physical Status III-IV drives ICU/step-down planning; ECOG performance status >=2 flags poor tolerance; Galsky cisplatin-eligibility criteria (GFR >=60 mL/min, ECOG 0-1, no >=grade2 hearing loss/neuropathy, no NYHA >=III heart failure) determines neoadjuvant chemo candidacy; modified Frailty Index mFI-5 >=2 predicts morbidity/mortality and triggers prehab; Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI) for perioperative cardiac risk; Caprini score (cystectomy is high-risk, typically >=5) mandates extended VTE prophylaxis; nutritional risk (albumin <3.0-3.5 g/dL, weight loss >5-10%, BMI <18.5) triggers nutrition prehab; ACS-NSQIP Surgical Risk Calculator for individualized morbidity/mortality counseling
initial_workup_labs: CBC (baseline anemia from hematuria; guides transfusion); CMP incl. eGFR/creatinine (cisplatin eligibility + diversion planning, baseline metabolic acidosis risk); LFTs; PT/INR + aPTT; type & crossmatch (>=2 units PRBC available); albumin/prealbumin (nutrition prehab if low); HbA1c (glycemic optimization); urinalysis + urine culture (treat/clear UTI before surgery). Triggers: low eGFR -> cisplatin-ineligible pathway; positive culture -> targeted pre-op antibiotics; low albumin -> nutrition consult/immunonutrition; anemia -> iron/optimize preoperatively
initial_imaging_dx: Cystoscopy + TURBT with detrusor muscle in specimen (mandatory to establish muscle-invasion) plus bimanual exam under anesthesia; CT urography chest/abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast for local + upper-tract + nodal + metastatic staging; MRI pelvis with VI-RADS if CT equivocal for muscle-invasion/local extent; FDG-PET/CT or bone scan if symptoms, elevated alkaline phosphatase, or high-risk features; pre-op cardiopulmonary work-up (ECG, CXR; echocardiography/CPET if indicated) — all completed during outpatient work-up before admission
time_critical_interventions: Elective operation — key time targets are guideline windows + ERAS bundle: give neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy BEFORE cystectomy for cT2-T4a cisplatin-eligible patients (Class I); perform cystectomy without undue delay — <=12 weeks from diagnosis and within ~8-10 weeks of completing neoadjuvant chemo (longer delays worsen survival); surgical antibiotic prophylaxis within 60 min of incision with intraoperative redosing; maintain normothermia + euvolemia; carbohydrate loading up to 2h pre-op with clear liquids allowed to 2h (no prolonged fasting); perioperative pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis initiated and continued as 28-day extended course
initial_management: Definitive source control = radical cystectomy + bilateral pelvic lymph node dissection + urinary diversion (ileal conduit most common; orthotopic neobladder or continent cutaneous in selected patients). ERAS peri-op: omit mechanical bowel prep; carbohydrate drink; avoid routine NG tube; multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (IV/PO acetaminophen, ketorolac/NSAID, regional/TAP block); alvimopan (peripheral mu-opioid-receptor antagonist) to accelerate GI recovery and cut ileus; PONV prophylaxis. Antibiotic prophylaxis cefazolin + metronidazole (or cefoxitin) covering skin + enteric flora, <=24h. Neoadjuvant systemic therapy (pre-admission): dose-dense MVAC x3-4 or gemcitabine-cisplatin x4, +/- perioperative durvalumab (NIAGARA). Goal-directed euvolemic balanced-crystalloid fluids (avoid overload -> ileus); restrictive transfusion threshold Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if cardiac disease)
day1_milestones: POD0 (surgery day): radical cystectomy + PLND + diversion completed; extubate in OR/PACU; disposition to surgical floor or step-down/ICU by acuity. Launch ERAS: mobilize same day (dangle/out of bed), no routine NG tube, start sips of clear liquids + sham feeding (chewing gum), multimodal analgesia with minimal IV opioids, continue alvimopan, VTE prophylaxis mechanical SCDs + pharmacologic (enoxaparin/UFH), incentive spirometry, maintain euvolemia and normothermia; monitor conduit/stent urine output and pelvic drain output (watch for urine leak/bleeding)
day2_milestones: POD1: out of bed to chair and ambulate >=3-4x; advance diet as tolerated (clears -> light); continue alvimopan and multimodal analgesia, actively wean IV opioids; assess for early ileus; labs CBC + CMP (detect diversion-related metabolic/hyperchloremic acidosis, electrolyte shifts, anemia); begin enterostomal/WOC ostomy-nurse teaching for conduit care; catheter/stent care; continue mechanical + pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis; remove non-essential lines; consider pelvic drain removal if low output and no leak
day3plus_milestones: POD2-POD7: GI recovery is rate-limiting — advance to regular diet once flatus/stool returns; ambulate independently; transition fully to oral analgesia; discontinue IV fluids once tolerating PO; remove pelvic drain; achieve ostomy self-care competency (or neobladder catheter/irrigation teaching); ureteral stents usually left and removed as outpatient (~POD5-14+); vigilance for ileus, infection (UTI/SSI/sepsis), urine leak, AKI, and metabolic acidosis; arrange 28-day extended VTE prophylaxis script + teaching; discharge readiness commonly POD5-8 (ERAS programs POD4-6)
consults_multidisciplinary: Urologic oncology (primary surgeon); medical oncology (neoadjuvant + adjuvant chemo/immunotherapy); enterostomal therapy/WOC ostomy nurse for PRE-OP stoma site marking and post-op teaching (essential ERAS/quality element); anesthesiology/acute pain (multimodal + regional); registered dietitian/nutrition (prehab + post-op); PT/OT (prehabilitation + early mobilization); care management + social work early for disposition, home health, and ostomy-supply arrangement; clinical pharmacy (med rec, VTE, alvimopan); geriatrics/prehab clinic if frail; palliative care if advanced/symptom burden
monitoring_level: Most patients recover on surgical floor or step-down. ICU/step-down indicated for: prolonged operative time, major blood loss/multi-unit transfusion, ASA IV, significant cardiopulmonary comorbidity, hemodynamic instability, or intraoperative complication; telemetry for cardiac history. Escalation triggers: SIRS/sepsis (urinary source), hemodynamic instability, respiratory compromise, ongoing bleeding/falling hemoglobin, oliguria/AKI, severe metabolic acidosis, or uncontrolled pain — driven by NEWS2/MEWS early-warning scoring
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: no prolonged fasting, pre-op carbohydrate loading, early oral intake POD0-1 advanced as GI function returns, avoid routine NGT, alvimopan to speed bowel recovery, nutrition support if prolonged ileus/malnutrition. Mobility: out of bed POD0 and ambulate multiple times daily (ERAS core). VTE: mechanical SCDs + pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin 40 mg SC daily or UFH 5000u q8-12h) started perioperatively and CONTINUED 28 days after discharge (>50% of VTEs occur post-discharge; baseline VTE 3-11%); apixaban 2.5 mg PO BID an oral alternative. Glucose target <180 mg/dL. Lines/catheters: remove central/arterial lines and pelvic drains early; minimize Foley duration; ureteral stents removed later as outpatient
discharge_criteria: Tolerating oral diet without nausea/vomiting; return of bowel function (flatus/stool, no active ileus); adequate pain control on oral analgesics; afebrile and hemodynamically stable with no active infection; stable renal function and acid-base status; demonstrated ostomy self-care competency by patient/caregiver (or understanding of neobladder catheter management); ambulating at/near baseline; confirmed home support + ostomy supplies; 28-day extended VTE prophylaxis plan filled and taught
discharge_planning: Disposition home with home-health nursing for ostomy/wound most common; SNF/rehab if frail or deconditioned. Medication reconciliation: extended 28-day VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin or apixaban) script + teaching, bowel regimen, opioid taper, resume/adjust home meds. Follow-up: urology 1-2 weeks (staple/stent management, wound check), medical oncology for adjuvant therapy decision (adjuvant nivolumab per CheckMate 274 for high-risk ypT3+/N+ especially if no/incomplete neoadjuvant chemo; adjuvant durvalumab per NIAGARA), pathology/staging review. Education: ostomy care, dehydration/AKI warning signs (hydration counseling — top readmission lever for ileal conduit), UTI/pyelonephritis red flags, when to call; teach-back, early (48-72h) phone check-in, ostomy-nurse follow-up
expected_los: Approx CMS IPPS GMLOS by tier: DRG 655 (no CC/MCC) ~4.5 days, DRG 654 (with CC) ~6 days, DRG 653 (with MCC) ~9 days; real-world median LOS historically 8-11 days, contemporary/ERAS series ~5-8 days; blended typical benchmark ~7-9 days
target_los: 5-6 days under full ERAS pathway for uncomplicated ileal conduit (high-volume ERAS programs achieve median 4-5 days); orthotopic neobladder trends ~1 day longer; target balances early discharge against this population's high readmission risk
quality_metrics: ERAS protocol compliance %; surgical antibiotic prophylaxis timing/redose/discontinuation (SCIP-derived); VTE prophylaxis rate + 28-day extended-prophylaxis adherence; neoadjuvant chemotherapy utilization in cT2-T4a cisplatin-eligible patients (known underuse/quality gap); time-to-cystectomy <=12 weeks; pelvic lymph node yield (>=10-16 nodes as PLND quality surrogate); negative soft-tissue surgical margin rate; high-volume center/surgeon (volume-outcome relationship); postoperative ileus, SSI, and CAUTI rates; Clavien-Dindo major complication (>=IIIa) rate; 30- and 90-day readmission; 30- and 90-day mortality
common_complications: Among the highest-morbidity elective operations (30-day complications ~30-64%, 90-day up to ~60%): #1 postoperative ileus/GI dysfunction (mitigate with ERAS, alvimopan, no NGT, early feeding/gum, opioid-sparing analgesia, euvolemia); infectious — UTI/pyelonephritis, surgical-site infection, sepsis (antibiotic prophylaxis, stent/catheter care, source control); VTE (extended 28-day prophylaxis); dehydration/AKI and hyperchloremic metabolic acidosis from diversion (hydration + electrolyte monitoring); wound dehiscence; ureteroenteric anastomotic urine leak; lymphocele; bleeding/transfusion; delirium in elderly; cardiopulmonary events; 90-day mortality ~2-8%
readmission_drivers: 30-day readmission ~25-30% (among the highest of any surgery). Top drivers: dehydration with AKI from high conduit output/poor oral intake (#1); infection (UTI/pyelonephritis, SSI, urosepsis); ileus/GI failure-to-thrive/nausea-vomiting; post-discharge VTE; wound complications; later ureteroenteric stricture/obstruction. Prevention levers: structured hydration counseling, pre-op stoma marking + ostomy teaching + home health, filled 28-day extended VTE prophylaxis, early (48-72h) post-discharge phone/clinic follow-up with low threshold for outpatient evaluation, dedicated cystectomy pathway/nurse navigator and transitions-of-care program
guideline_source: ERAS Society — Guidelines for Perioperative Care after Radical Cystectomy for Bladder Cancer: ERAS Society Recommendations (Cerantola et al., Clin Nutr 2013); AUA/ASCO/ASTRO/SUO — Treatment of Non-Metastatic Muscle-Invasive Bladder Cancer (2017; Amended 2020, 2024); supporting: NCCN Bladder Cancer (2025) and EAU Muscle-Invasive & Metastatic Bladder Cancer (2024)
key_citations: Cerantola Y, et al. ERAS Society radical cystectomy perioperative-care guidelines. Clin Nutr. 2013;32(6):879-887. PMID 24189391. | AUA/ASCO/ASTRO/SUO. Treatment of Non-Metastatic Muscle-Invasive Bladder Cancer Guideline (2017; Amended 2024). J Urol. PMID 38661067. | Grossman HB, et al. (SWOG 8710) Neoadjuvant MVAC + cystectomy vs cystectomy alone. N Engl J Med. 2003;349(9):859-866 (median OS 77 vs 46 mo; pT0 38% vs 15%). PMID 12944571. | Powles T, et al. (NIAGARA) Perioperative Durvalumab with Neoadjuvant Chemotherapy in Operable Bladder Cancer. N Engl J Med. 2024;391(19):1773-1786 (EFS HR ~0.68, OS HR ~0.75). PMID 39282910. | Kamat AM, et al. Bladder cancer (review). Lancet. 2016;388(10061):2796-2810. PMID 27345655. | Supporting landmark trials: RAZOR (Parekh, Lancet 2018 — robot-assisted noninferior to open); CheckMate 274 (Bajorin, NEJM 2021 — adjuvant nivolumab). Citations via PubMed
evidence_grade: Strong overall — oncologic backbone rests on Class I/Level 1 RCT evidence (neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy SWOG 8710; perioperative durvalumab NIAGARA 2024; robotic noninferiority RAZOR) plus ERAS Society and AUA/ASCO/ASTRO/SUO Class I society guidelines; individual ERAS perioperative-bundle elements range Strong-to-Moderate (alvimopan Level 1; several elements extrapolated from colorectal ERAS or expert consensus); extended 28-day VTE prophylaxis Strong
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed group spans DRG 655 (no CC/MCC), 654 (with CC), 653 (with MCC); because comorbidity burden and complication rates are high, most cystectomies fall in 653/654 while uncomplicated younger ERAS patients land in 655. MCC drivers (653): sepsis/urosepsis, acute respiratory failure, AKI requiring intervention, major hemorrhage, MI, severe ileus complications -> ICU need, LOS ~9+ days, higher mortality. CC (654): moderate anemia/transfusion, UTI, electrolyte/acid-base disorders -> LOS ~6 days. Without CC/MCC (655): full ERAS eligibility, LOS ~4.5 days. Severity tier tracks directly with in-stay complications (ileus, infection, VTE, AKI), so the pathway's complication-prevention levers are simultaneously the DRG/LOS-optimization levers
pathway_pearls: 1) Give neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy BEFORE cystectomy for cT2-T4a cisplatin-eligible patients (Level 1 survival benefit, still underutilized) — screen Galsky criteria early and consider perioperative durvalumab (NIAGARA). 2) Deploy the full ERAS bundle + alvimopan + no NGT + early feeding/mobilization to combat postoperative ileus, the #1 driver of prolonged LOS, while planning for an inherently high complication rate. 3) Prescribe 28-day EXTENDED VTE prophylaxis at discharge — do not stop at hospital exit, since >50% of VTEs occur post-discharge. 4) Dehydration/AKI is the #1 readmission driver — pre-op stoma marking + ostomy teaching + hydration counseling + early 48-72h post-discharge follow-up; treat cystectomy as a high-readmission, high-touch transition of care

admission_criteria: Planned inpatient admission for conditioning + hematopoietic cell infusion in eligible patients (confirmed transplant indication: acute leukemia/MDS/lymphoma/myeloma/aplastic anemia). Eligibility gates: KPS/Lansky >=70 (ECOG 0-1); HCT-CI (Sorror) comorbidity assessment; LVEF >=45-50%; DLCO/FEV1 >=50%; CrCl >=50-60 mL/min; bilirubin <2 and transaminases <3x ULN; no uncontrolled infection; confirmed donor/graft + caregiver and psychosocial clearance. Myeloablative allogeneic = always inpatient; select autologous/reduced-intensity may be ambulatory and admitted on complication. Unplanned/urgent admission triggers: febrile neutropenia, sepsis, VOD/SOS, acute GVHD, engraftment syndrome, TA-TMA, uncontrolled mucositis/dehydration
risk_stratification: HCT-CI (Sorror): 0 low, 1-2 intermediate, >=3 high non-relapse-mortality risk; Disease Risk Index (DRI) low/intermediate/high/very-high stratifies relapse risk; EBMT risk score; KPS/ECOG. CMV donor/recipient serostatus (R+ high-risk drives letermovir). Febrile neutropenia: MASCC >=21 = low risk but transplant recipients are high-risk by definition -> admit + IV antibiotics. Sepsis: qSOFA >=2 / SOFA + lactate >2 mmol/L. EASIX score (LDH x creatinine / platelets) for endothelial complications (VOD, TA-TMA). MAGIC/Ann Arbor serum biomarker score (ST2, REG3a) risk-stratifies acute GVHD severity and steroid response
initial_workup_labs: Daily CBC with differential (ANC, platelets to track nadir/engraftment); CMP with LFTs + total bilirubin (VOD marker) + creatinine (AKI/TA-TMA); LDH, haptoglobin, peripheral smear for schistocytes (TA-TMA); coags (PT/INR, fibrinogen); type & screen/crossmatch for irradiated leukoreduced products; blood cultures with any fever; weekly CMV/EBV/adenovirus (and BK if cystitis) PCR surveillance; serum galactomannan + (1,3)-beta-D-glucan for invasive fungal; ferritin; tacrolimus/cyclosporine/sirolimus trough levels; pre-transplant HBV/HCV/HIV/HSV/VZV/toxoplasma serologies; G6PD before dapsone; uric acid/phosphate/potassium for tumor lysis
initial_imaging_dx: Pre-transplant workup: TTE/echo (LVEF), PFTs with DLCO, CT chest/sinus, dental clearance, tunneled central venous catheter (Hickman/Broviac or PICC) placed before conditioning. During stay: abdominal Doppler ultrasound (hepatomegaly, ascites, reversed/reduced portal flow) when VOD/SOS suspected; HRCT chest within 24h for neutropenic pulmonary infiltrates/fungal disease; echo for TA-TMA/pericardial effusion/IPS; upper/lower endoscopy with biopsy to distinguish GI GVHD from CMV/infection; skin biopsy for GVHD; bronchoscopy/BAL for diffuse alveolar hemorrhage or fungal pneumonia
time_critical_interventions: Empiric broad-spectrum IV antibiotics within 60 minutes of FIRST neutropenic fever (T >=38.3C once or >=38.0C x1h with ANC <500) - antipseudomonal beta-lactam (cefepime/piperacillin-tazobactam/meropenem); Surviving Sepsis 1-hour bundle in septic shock (lactate, 2 cultures, antibiotics <=1h, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, vasopressors for MAP >=65); defibrotide 25 mg/kg/day started PROMPTLY once VOD/SOS diagnosed (do not await multi-organ failure); systemic methylprednisolone 1-2 mg/kg/day promptly for grade II-IV acute GVHD; tumor-lysis prophylaxis (hydration + allopurinol/rasburicase) before conditioning; stem-cell infusion after conditioning washout per protocol timing
initial_management: Conditioning: myeloablative (busulfan-cyclophosphamide, busulfan-fludarabine, TBI-based) or reduced-intensity (fludarabine-melphalan, flu-busulfan); autologous myeloma = melphalan 200 mg/m2. GVHD prophylaxis: post-transplant cyclophosphamide (PTCy day +3/+4) + tacrolimus + MMF now preferred (BMT CTN 1703), or calcineurin inhibitor + methotrexate. Prophylaxis bundle: antibacterial fluoroquinolone (levofloxacin) during neutropenia; antifungal mould-active azole (posaconazole/voriconazole) or micafungin; antiviral acyclovir/valacyclovir (HSV/VZV) + letermovir 480 mg/day (240 with cyclosporine) for CMV-seropositive allo through day 100-200; PJP prophylaxis (TMP-SMX; alt atovaquone/dapsone/pentamidine) after engraftment. Transfusion: irradiated leukoreduced; platelets <10-20k prophylactic, RBC restrictive Hgb <7-8 g/dL. Mesna + hyperhydration with cyclophosphamide; ursodiol VOD prophylaxis; G-CSF (filgrastim) post-autologous; mucositis cryotherapy/palifermin; antiemetics
day1_milestones: Transplant-day nomenclature (conditioning = days -7 to -1 pre-infusion). Day 0 = hematopoietic stem-cell infusion: confirm ABO/product/patient, premedicate, infuse graft; monitor infusion reactions - DMSO cryopreservative toxicity in cryopreserved autologous grafts (hypertension, bradycardia, flushing, hemoglobinuria) and volume/histamine reactions; maintain hyperhydration and strict I&O; continue GVHD prophylaxis and antimicrobial prophylaxis; start daily weights + bilirubin trend (VOD watch); baseline mucositis and mental-status assessment; verify antiemetic control
day2_milestones: Days +1 to +5 (peri-nadir): administer post-transplant cyclophosphamide day +3/+4 then start tacrolimus + MMF; anticipate counts falling to nadir; mucositis typically peaks (pain control with opioid PCA, oral care, consider TPN if intake fails); heightened febrile-neutropenia vigilance with antibiotics <=1h protocol; continue daily weights/I&O and bilirubin for early VOD; electrolyte repletion (Mg/K/phosphate with calcineurin inhibitor); reinforce protective isolation and CLABSI bundle; verify prophylaxis (antifungal, letermovir, acyclovir) uninterrupted
day3plus_milestones: Day +6 through engraftment and discharge: monitor for neutrophil engraftment (ANC >=500 x3 days) - typically day +10-14 autologous, +14-21 allogeneic (G-CSF accelerates autologous); platelet recovery; surveillance for CMV/EBV/BK/adenovirus reactivation and invasive fungal disease; vigilance for acute GVHD (skin rash, secretory diarrhea, hyperbilirubinemia) - biopsy + start steroids for grade II-IV; monitor VOD/TA-TMA/engraftment syndrome; taper hydration, advance diet, wean TPN and IV analgesia; transition IV to oral prophylaxis; begin PJP prophylaxis after engraftment; discharge teaching and immunosuppression taper plan
consults_multidisciplinary: BMT/transplant hematology (primary team); infectious disease (febrile neutropenia, resistant/fungal/viral infection); transplant clinical pharmacist (calcineurin-inhibitor TDM, azole-CNI interactions, renal dosing) - daily; dietitian/nutrition (neutropenic diet, TPN for mucositis); PT/OT early (deconditioning from prolonged isolation); social work + psycho-oncology (prolonged stay, caregiver strain); case management/discharge planning from admission; GI/hepatology (VOD, GI GVHD); nephrology (AKI, TA-TMA); dermatology (skin GVHD); dental (pre-transplant clearance); palliative care for symptom burden
monitoring_level: Protective-isolation BMT unit with HEPA-filtered positive-pressure rooms (allogeneic); floor/telemetry-capable with q4h vitals and daily labs. ICU/step-down escalation for: septic shock/vasopressor need, respiratory failure (fungal pneumonia, diffuse alveolar hemorrhage, idiopathic pneumonia syndrome), VOD/SOS with multi-organ failure, TA-TMA with renal failure/neuro changes, grade IV GVHD, tumor lysis with AKI. Escalation triggers: qSOFA >=2, lactate >2 mmol/L, SpO2 <92% or rising O2 requirement, sustained hypotension (MAP <65), altered mental status, oliguria/rising creatinine
nutrition_mobility_vte: Neutropenic/low-microbial diet; nutrition support with TPN when severe mucositis (high-dose melphalan/TBI) prevents intake; daily weights + strict I&O to detect fluid overload/VOD. Early daily ambulation/mobility with PT to counter isolation-related deconditioning. VTE prophylaxis: mechanical (sequential compression devices) preferred; pharmacologic prophylaxis held when platelets <50k and individualized. Glucose control (steroid-induced with GVHD therapy). Line/catheter: tunneled CVC required for infusion/support - strict CLABSI insertion/maintenance bundle, remove/replace for line infection. Mesna + hyperhydration to prevent hemorrhagic cystitis; monitor BK virus
discharge_criteria: Sustained neutrophil engraftment (ANC >=500/uL x3 consecutive days, trending >=0.5-1.0k); platelet recovery/transfusion-independent trend (>=20k); afebrile >=48h and either off IV antibiotics or on stable regimen; adequate oral intake and hydration (off TPN or stable), controlled nausea/diarrhea/mucositis pain; no active uncontrolled GVHD or infection; stable organ function (resolving/absent AKI, bilirubin); therapeutic immunosuppression levels; reliable caregiver present, transport secured, residence within reasonable distance of transplant center for frequent follow-up
discharge_planning: Disposition home with frequent BMT clinic follow-up (2-3x/week initially; Day +30, +60, +100 milestone visits). Medication reconciliation: immunosuppression taper schedule; prophylaxis - acyclovir/valacyclovir ~1 year, PJP prophylaxis 6-12 months/until CD4 recovery, letermovir through day 100 (extend to day 200 if high-risk GVHD), mould-active azole, electrolyte (Mg) repletion. CMV/EBV PCR surveillance schedule; central-line care teaching; neutropenic precautions and infection-avoidance education; GVHD/TA-TMA red-flag symptom education; caregiver training; post-transplant revaccination plan (~6-12 months); warm handoff/transition-of-care to referring oncologist
expected_los: Varies by graft type and DRG. Allogeneic (DRG 014, myeloablative inpatient): CMS GMLOS benchmark ~25-30 days (AMLOS higher). Autologous with CC/MCC (DRG 016): ~15-18 days. Autologous without CC/MCC (DRG 017): ~10-12 days. Overall pre-MDC transplant admission commonly 14-30+ days (approximate CMS IPPS benchmarks; precise GMLOS varies by fiscal-year grouper)
target_los: Autologous under optimized/ambulatory pathway: 10-14 days (or outpatient with daily clinic); reduced-intensity allogeneic: 21-25 days; myeloablative allogeneic: ~26-28 days. Levers to hit target: outpatient/ambulatory transplant models, G-CSF to accelerate engraftment, mucositis mitigation (cryotherapy/palifermin), CLABSI prevention, and prompt complication control to avoid ICU transfer
quality_metrics: CIBMTR center-specific outcomes (risk-adjusted 1-year survival, actual vs predicted) - required reporting; Day +100 non-relapse mortality; 100-day and 1-year overall survival; time to neutrophil and platelet engraftment; CLABSI rate; incidence of grade II-IV acute GVHD; CMV reactivation/disease rate; VOD/SOS incidence; 30-day readmission rate; FACT-JACIE accreditation compliance; SEP-1 sepsis bundle adherence; time-to-antibiotic <60 min in febrile neutropenia
common_complications: Febrile neutropenia/sepsis (mitigate: fluoroquinolone prophylaxis, antibiotics <=1h); mucositis (cryotherapy, palifermin, opioid analgesia, TPN); acute GVHD (PTCy/CNI prophylaxis, prompt steroids); VOD/SOS (ursodiol prophylaxis, avoid hepatotoxins, early defibrotide); CMV/EBV/BK/fungal reactivation (letermovir, azole, PCR surveillance, preemptive therapy); TA-TMA (CNI dose adjustment, complement inhibition eculizumab in severe); engraftment syndrome (corticosteroids); hemorrhagic cystitis (mesna/hydration); diffuse alveolar hemorrhage/IPS; graft failure; tumor lysis
readmission_drivers: Post-discharge neutropenic fever/infection; acute GVHD flare (gut/skin/liver); CMV/viral reactivation; dehydration from diarrhea or poor oral intake; electrolyte derangement (hypomagnesemia/hypokalemia with calcineurin inhibitors); central-line infection; failure to thrive/persistent mucositis. Prevention levers: intensive Day +30/+100 clinic follow-up, telehealth/remote monitoring, aggressive outpatient electrolyte and hydration support, medication-adherence and immunosuppression-level monitoring, verified caregiver readiness, and early GVHD/CMV detection
guideline_source: ASTCT (American Society for Transplantation and Cellular Therapy) practice-guideline series, incl. Updated CMV Guidelines Series #11 (2025); NCCN Guidelines - Hematopoietic Cell Transplantation and Prevention/Treatment of Cancer-Related Infections (2024-2025); IDSA Fever & Neutropenia Guideline (Freifeld 2011) + ASCO/IDSA outpatient FN update (2018); EBMT refined SOS/VOD diagnostic & severity criteria (2023); MAGIC/NIH acute & chronic GVHD consensus criteria; FACT-JACIE accreditation standards
key_citations: Marty FM et al. Letermovir Prophylaxis for CMV in Hematopoietic-Cell Transplantation. N Engl J Med 2017;377(25):2433-2444 (PMID 29211658; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa1706640). Bolanos-Meade J et al. Post-Transplantation Cyclophosphamide-Based GVHD Prophylaxis (BMT CTN 1703). N Engl J Med 2023 (PMID 37342922; DOI 10.1056/NEJMoa2215943). Richardson PG et al. Phase 3 trial of defibrotide for severe VOD and multi-organ failure. Blood 2016;127(13):1656-65 (PMID 26825712; DOI 10.1182/blood-2015-10-676924). Freifeld AG et al. IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline, antimicrobial agents in neutropenic cancer patients: 2010 Update. Clin Infect Dis 2011;52(4):427-31 (PMID 21205990; DOI 10.1093/cid/ciq147). EBMT refined SOS/VOD adult diagnostic & severity criteria 2023 (Bone Marrow Transplant; PMID 37573397; DOI 10.1038/s41409-023-01992-8). Citations retrieved/verified via PubMed
evidence_grade: Strong but heterogeneous. Class I/high-quality RCT evidence for letermovir CMV prophylaxis (NEJM 2017) and PTCy-based GVHD prophylaxis (BMT CTN 1703, NEJM 2023); strong guideline backing for antibiotics <=1h in febrile neutropenia. Moderate for defibrotide in VOD (phase 3 vs historical control) and for EBMT VOD criteria (expert consensus). Much peri-transplant supportive care is registry-/consensus-based. Net: Strong-to-Moderate, current society-guideline supported
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Collapsed pre-MDC group spans sharply different acuity. DRG 014 (allogeneic) is highest-resource: GVHD risk, prolonged protective isolation, most ICU utilization, longest LOS, highest non-relapse mortality. DRG 016 (autologous WITH CC/MCC) - complications such as febrile neutropenia with sepsis, severe mucositis with dehydration, AKI, or VOD define the tier and typically add ~4-6 days and raise cost. DRG 017 (autologous WITHOUT CC/MCC) is shortest and often ambulatory-eligible. MCCs (sepsis, respiratory failure, VOD with MOF, acute renal failure) trigger ICU transfer, extend LOS, and increase mortality; allogeneic is inherently higher-acuity than autologous across all tiers
pathway_pearls: (1) Empiric antibiotics within 60 minutes of the first neutropenic fever is the single highest-yield survival lever - protocolize it. (2) Give letermovir to every CMV-seropositive allogeneic recipient through day 100 (extend to day 200 if high-risk/ongoing GVHD) - it nearly eliminates early clinically significant CMV. (3) Daily weights + bilirubin trending catches VOD/SOS early; start defibrotide before multi-organ failure develops. (4) PTCy + tacrolimus + MMF is now standard-of-care GVHD prophylaxis (superior per BMT CTN 1703) and enables haploidentical donors. (5) Enforce protective isolation + CLABSI bundle and watch azole-calcineurin-inhibitor interactions (tacrolimus level swings drive toxicity/TA-TMA)

scope_and_volume_notes: Autologous/allogeneic hematopoietic stem-cell transplant; prolonged protective-isolation stays. Oncology/BMT unit.

admission_criteria: Inpatient admission for PID per CDC 2021 when any present: surgical emergency (e.g., appendicitis, ectopic) cannot be excluded; tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA); pregnancy; severe illness with nausea/vomiting or oral temperature >38.5C (101F); inability to tolerate/adhere to an outpatient oral regimen; no clinical response after 72h of outpatient oral therapy; also sepsis/hemodynamic instability or immunocompromise (e.g., HIV with low CD4). For menstrual/other disorders (DRG 760/761): admit for hemodynamically significant abnormal uterine bleeding, symptomatic/transfusion-requiring anemia, or intractable pain. ED discharge/observation is appropriate for mild-to-moderate PID meeting outpatient criteria: PEACH RCT showed equivalent reproductive outcomes inpatient vs outpatient (Ness 2002)
risk_stratification: No single validated numeric PID severity score exists; stratify with: CDC clinical diagnostic criteria (minimum: cervical-motion, uterine, or adnexal tenderness; supportive: temp >38.3C, mucopurulent cervical discharge, WBCs on saline microscopy, elevated ESR/CRP, lab-documented GC/CT); sepsis overlay qSOFA >=2 or SOFA rise / lactate >=2 -> escalate care and 1h bundle; mandatory hCG to exclude ectopic. TOA antibiotic-failure predictors (Kinay 2024 meta-analysis of 29 studies/2890 women; TOAST 2025): larger abscess diameter (commonly cited threshold >7 cm, observational), age >40/postmenopausal, IUD, diabetes, fever, high WBC/ESR/CRP -> favor early drainage (about 1/3 of medically managed TOA ultimately need intervention)
initial_workup_labs: Serum/urine hCG (mandatory - exclude ectopic; doxycycline contraindicated in pregnancy); NAAT for N. gonorrhoeae and C. trachomatis (also Trichomonas; Mycoplasma genitalium if refractory); CBC with differential (leukocytosis); CRP and ESR (inflammatory markers; TOA-failure predictors); CMP; lactate + blood cultures if febrile/septic; HIV, syphilis (RPR), hepatitis B/C screening (STI co-testing); saline wet-mount microscopy (WBCs, clue cells/BV) and vaginal pH; urinalysis/urine culture to exclude UTI; Gram stain and culture of any abscess drainage. Positive NAAT/culture triggers pathogen-directed therapy and partner services; rising CRP/WBC despite therapy triggers re-imaging/source control
initial_imaging_dx: Transvaginal pelvic ultrasound (TVUS) - first-line, within hours of presentation; detects TOA, pyosalpinx, thickened/incomplete septa (cogwheel sign), tubal hyperemia, cul-de-sac fluid. CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast when surgical abdomen/appendicitis in differential, TVUS non-diagnostic, or to plan drainage. MRI pelvis - most sensitive for TOA and preferred when avoiding radiation (pregnancy). Diagnostic laparoscopy - reference standard for diagnostic uncertainty or to exclude a surgical emergency. Interval re-imaging at 48-72h if no clinical response
time_critical_interventions: Obtain cultures then start empiric IV antibiotics promptly; in sepsis/septic shock give antibiotics within 1 hour (Surviving Sepsis/SEP-1) with lactate and 30 mL/kg crystalloid for hypotension or lactate >=4. TOA: minimum 24h inpatient observation. Formal clinical-response checkpoint at 72h (CDC) - failure to improve mandates re-imaging plus source control (image-guided drainage) or regimen change and exclusion of alternative diagnosis. Ruptured TOA with peritonitis or septic shock is a surgical emergency -> OR without delay. Image-guided percutaneous/transvaginal drainage generally within 48-72h of non-response
initial_management: CDC 2021 recommended parenteral regimen (first-line): Ceftriaxone 1 g IV q24h PLUS Doxycycline 100 mg PO/IV q12h PLUS Metronidazole 500 mg PO/IV q12h (metronidazole added in 2021 for anaerobic/BV coverage, essential in TOA). Alternatives: Cefotetan 2 g IV q12h + doxycycline; Cefoxitin 2 g IV q6h + doxycycline; Ampicillin-sulbactam 3 g IV q6h + doxycycline; Clindamycin 900 mg IV q8h + Gentamicin (2 mg/kg load then 1.5 mg/kg q8h, or 3-5 mg/kg once daily). Prefer ORAL doxycycline (bioavailability equals IV, avoids infusion phlebitis). Supportive IV crystalloid, antiemetics, analgesia. Source control: image-guided percutaneous/transvaginal drainage or laparoscopy for non-responding TOA. IUD may remain initially with close reassessment - remove if no clinical improvement. Pregnancy: use non-doxycycline regimen and admit
day1_milestones: Confirm PID diagnosis and exclude ectopic/surgical abdomen (hCG, NAAT, CRP/ESR, TVUS); draw cultures then start empiric IV ceftriaxone + doxycycline + metronidazole; initiate sepsis bundle if criteria met; analgesia, antiemetics, IV fluids; set monitoring level and escalation triggers; VTE-risk assessment and prophylaxis; send full STI panel (HIV, RPR, hep B/C); begin partner-notification and Expedited Partner Therapy (EPT) counseling; gynecology consult; if TOA, plan interventional radiology pathway and >=24h observation
day2_milestones: Reassess at 24-48h: fever curve, pain, oral tolerance, WBC/CRP trend. If improving (afebrile, decreasing pain and markers), transition IV -> oral doxycycline 100 mg BID + metronidazole 500 mg BID to complete a 14-day total course. Tailor/de-escalate per NAAT and culture susceptibilities (e.g., confirmed gonorrhea). If TOA not improving or enlarging, arrange image-guided drainage and obtain ID input. Reinforce adherence counseling and partner treatment
day3plus_milestones: 72h formal response checkpoint (CDC): if no improvement, repeat imaging, achieve source control (drainage/surgery), broaden or change antibiotics, and reconsider alternative diagnosis. Continue oral step-down to complete 14 days total (retain metronidazole/clindamycin for anaerobic coverage when TOA present). Confirm drain output is declining and collection is shrinking. Verify hemodynamic stability, afebrile >=24-48h, tolerating orals. Finalize discharge planning with follow-up in 48-72h and at 2-4 weeks
consults_multidisciplinary: Gynecology (primary or co-management; drainage/surgery decisions) - Day 1; Interventional Radiology for image-guided TOA drainage when indicated; Infectious Diseases for severe/refractory/immunocompromised cases and antibiotic tailoring; General Surgery if appendicitis/surgical abdomen not excluded; Obstetrics if pregnant; Pharmacy for antimicrobial dosing/renal adjustment and allergy; Care management/Social work for STI and partner-services counseling, adherence, housing, and substance-use support; Nutrition if prolonged NPO/poor intake
monitoring_level: Most PID managed on general gynecology/medical floor with routine vitals. Step-down/telemetry or ICU for sepsis or septic shock, ruptured TOA, hemodynamic instability, or vasopressor need. Escalation triggers: qSOFA >=2, SBP <90 or MAP <65, lactate >=2-4, worsening peritonitis/rebound, tachypnea/hypoxia, or rising inflammatory markers and persistent fever despite appropriate therapy
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet advanced as tolerated; NPO/clear liquids if drainage or surgery pending or ileus present. Early ambulation. VTE prophylaxis: mechanical plus pharmacologic (LMWH or unfractionated heparin) stratified by Caprini/Padua, held peri-procedurally for bleeding risk. Glycemic control (diabetes is a TOA antibiotic-failure predictor). Line/catheter stewardship: convert IV to oral and remove peripheral IV once afebrile and tolerating orals; remove percutaneous drains when output is minimal; use Foley only if strictly indicated to limit CAUTI
discharge_criteria: Afebrile >=24-48h and hemodynamically stable; clinical improvement with decreasing pelvic pain; tolerating oral intake and an oral antibiotic regimen; downtrending WBC/CRP; for TOA, adequate source control with stable or shrinking collection and low/declining drain output; reliable oral regimen secured and timely follow-up arranged
discharge_planning: Medication reconciliation to complete 14-day total course: oral doxycycline 100 mg BID + metronidazole 500 mg BID (retain anaerobic coverage for TOA). Schedule follow-up within 72h to confirm improvement, re-evaluation at 2-4 weeks, and GC/CT retesting at 3 months (high reinfection rate). Partner notification plus Expedited Partner Therapy; advise abstinence until patient and partners complete therapy and are asymptomatic. Educate on complication warning signs (recurrent fever, worsening pain), barrier contraception and STI risk reduction; ensure HIV/syphilis result follow-up and contraception counseling; arrange drain-care and removal follow-up if applicable
expected_los: Group GMLOS approximately 3-4 days for the CC tier. By DRG: 759 (no CC/MCC) ~2-3 days; 758 (with CC, often TOA) ~3-4 days; 757 (with MCC, sepsis/rupture) ~5-7 days. Menstrual/other disorders 760/761 typically ~2-3 days. Uncomplicated PID often 2-3 days; TOA requiring drainage 4-7 days
target_los: Optimized target: uncomplicated PID 1-2 days (IV-to-oral switch at 24-48h of improvement per CDC, discharge on oral doxycycline+metronidazole); TOA with timely image-guided drainage 3-5 days. Group target ~2 days for non-TOA infections, ~4 days for drained TOA
quality_metrics: No PID-specific CMS core measure; applicable measures: SEP-1 sepsis bundle when sepsis present; guideline-concordant empiric antibiotic selection (ceftriaxone+doxycycline+metronidazole per CDC 2021); GC/CT NAAT testing and HIV testing offered; documented partner services/EPT; GC/CT retest at 3 months; VTE prophylaxis (legacy VTE-1/2); antimicrobial stewardship and IV-to-oral conversion; 30-day all-cause readmission; C. difficile and CAUTI/CLABSI safety metrics
common_complications: TOA formation and rupture -> peritonitis, sepsis, septic shock; Fitz-Hugh-Curtis perihepatitis; antibiotic failure requiring drainage/surgery (about 1/3 of TOA, Kinay/TOAST); C. difficile colitis and IV-catheter phlebitis; long-term tubal-factor infertility, ectopic pregnancy, and chronic pelvic pain from tubal scarring. Mitigation: early anaerobic coverage (metronidazole), 72h response checkpoint with re-imaging, timely image-guided drainage, sepsis bundle, stewardship with early oral conversion, and structured follow-up
readmission_drivers: Poor oral-antibiotic adherence (PEACH-linked data: mean ~70% of doses taken) driving treatment failure/recurrence; untreated sexual partner -> reinfection; inadequately drained or undrained TOA -> recurrent abscess; missed follow-up; ongoing STI risk behavior. Prevention levers: Expedited Partner Therapy, adherence counseling and simplest effective regimen, assured source control before discharge, close 48-72h follow-up, GC/CT retest at 3 months, and clear return-precaution education
guideline_source: PRIMARY: CDC Sexually Transmitted Infections Treatment Guidelines, 2021 (Workowski KA et al; MMWR Recommendations and Reports, 2021) - authoritative for PID diagnosis, hospitalization criteria, and parenteral/oral regimens; aligned with and endorsed by ACOG. TOA drainage thresholds/timing are supported by cohort studies and meta-analysis (no RCT-based guideline)
key_citations: Workowski KA, et al. Sexually Transmitted Infections Treatment Guidelines, 2021. MMWR Recomm Rep 2021;70(4):1-187 (PMID 34292926; DOI 10.15585/mmwr.rr7004a1); Ness RB, et al. Effectiveness of inpatient vs outpatient treatment for PID: PEACH randomized trial. Am J Obstet Gynecol 2002;186(5):929-37 (PMID 12015517; DOI 10.1067/mob.2002.121625); Kinay T, et al. Risk factors for antibiotic therapy failure in tubo-ovarian abscess: systematic review and meta-analysis. J Obstet Gynaecol Res 2024;50(3):298-312 (PMID 38184888; DOI 10.1111/jog.15870); Marshall A, et al. Tubo-ovarian Abscess Study (TOAST): predictors of failed medical management. Int J Gynaecol Obstet 2025;170(2):927-935 (PMID 40162547; DOI 10.1002/ijgo.70100). Citations retrieved via PubMed
evidence_grade: Strong for the core antimicrobial pathway - CDC 2021 is a practice guideline and the treatment-setting question is RCT-supported (PEACH). Moderate/observational for TOA drainage size thresholds and timing (cohort studies and meta-analysis, no RCTs; the >7 cm cutoff is commonly cited but extrapolated). Overall: Strong for antibiotic selection and admission criteria; Moderate for surgical/drainage triggers and LOS targets
severity_cc_mcc_notes: 759 (infection, no CC/MCC): uncomplicated PID; floor care, LOS ~1-3 days, rapid IV-to-oral. 758 (with CC): TOA, moderate systemic illness or anemia; LOS ~3-4 days, may need image-guided drainage. 757 (with MCC): sepsis/septic shock, ruptured TOA, peritonitis, AKI; ICU/step-down, LOS ~5-7+ days, higher operative rate. 760/761 (menstrual and other female reproductive disorders): generally non-infectious (abnormal uterine bleeding, symptomatic leiomyoma, ovarian cyst) - driver is bleeding/anemia/pain, not sepsis; 760 (with CC/MCC, e.g., transfusion-requiring anemia) runs longer; pathway pivots to transfusion thresholds, hemostatic/hormonal management, and gynecology rather than the antibiotic pathway above
pathway_pearls: 1) Add metronidazole to ceftriaxone + doxycycline (2021 CDC change) - anaerobic/BV coverage is essential, especially with TOA. 2) Switch IV-to-oral at 24-48h of improvement; oral doxycycline bioavailability equals IV, so do not prolong admission just to give IV - complete 14 days total. 3) TOA: enforce >=24h observation and a formal 72h response check; escalate early to image-guided drainage for non-response or high-risk features (large abscess, high CRP/WBC, fever, diabetes) and never delay the OR for rupture or septic shock. 4) Treat the partners (Expedited Partner Therapy) and retest GC/CT at 3 months - nonadherence and reinfection are the top readmission drivers

scope_and_volume_notes: Female reproductive infections (757-759) and menstrual/other disorders (760-761); tubo-ovarian abscess may require drainage.
