admission_criteria: Admit (surgical emergency, OR +/- ICU) for any necrotizing soft-tissue infection of perineum/genitalia (Fournier gangrene), confirmed or high-suspicion testicular torsion, ischemic priapism >4h or failed bedside detorsion, penile fracture, or epididymo-orchitis with sepsis/SIRS, scrotal abscess/pyocele, immunocompromise, intractable pain/vomiting, or failed outpatient oral antibiotics; admit post-op GU-procedure complications. ED discharge/observation appropriate for uncomplicated epididymitis tolerating PO, resolved intermittent torsion with normal Doppler US, or minor same-day procedures. Objective triggers: qSOFA >=2, lactate >2 mmol/L, hypotension/pressor need, crepitus or skin necrosis, inability to tolerate PO, or need for operative source control.
risk_stratification: LRINEC (necrotizing fasciitis): >=6 rules IN NSTI (PPV ~92%), >=8 high-risk (>75% probability); score <6 does NOT exclude - explore if clinical suspicion high [CRP>=150 mg/L +4; WBC 15-25 +1/>25 +2; Hgb 11-13.5 +1/<11 +2; Na<135 +2; Cr>1.6 +2; glucose>180 +1]. FGSI/Uludag-UFGSI >9 predicts markedly higher Fournier mortality -> ICU triage + prognosis counseling. TWIST (torsion): 0-2 low (NPV ~100%, no US, observe/discharge), 3-4 intermediate (Doppler US), 5-7 high (PPV ~93%, straight to OR) [testis swelling 2, hard testis 2, absent cremasteric 1, nausea/vomiting 1, high-riding 1]. Sepsis overlay: qSOFA>=2/rising SOFA, lactate>2 -> SEP-1 bundle. FG mortality predictors: age, Charlson index, CHF (OR 2.1), renal failure (OR 3.2), coagulopathy (OR 3.4).
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential (leukocytosis/bandemia, anemia -> LRINEC), CMP/BMP (Na, creatinine, glucose, LFTs), CRP + procalcitonin, serum lactate, VBG, coagulation panel (PT/INR/aPTT, fibrinogen -> DIC), creatine kinase (myonecrosis), HbA1c (diabetes present in most Fournier cases), blood cultures x2 BEFORE antibiotics, type & screen/crossmatch, HIV screen; urinalysis + urine culture; intra-op tissue Gram stain + aerobic/anaerobic wound cultures at debridement; STI NAAT (N. gonorrhoeae/C. trachomatis) for epididymitis; beta-hCG/tumor markers if testicular mass. Triggers: lactate>2 -> sepsis bundle; Na<135/rising Cr/CRP elevation -> LRINEC scoring; glucose/HbA1c -> insulin protocol.
initial_imaging_dx: Testicular torsion: color-Doppler scrotal US (absent/decreased intratesticular flow) within ~60 min for intermediate risk - but do NOT delay operative exploration for imaging when suspicion high (TWIST 5-7 -> OR directly). Fournier/NSTI: clinical diagnosis is primary and imaging must NOT delay debridement; contrast CT (subcutaneous/fascial gas, fascial thickening/fluid tracking) only if diagnosis uncertain and patient stable, obtained <1h; POCUS/plain film may show soft-tissue gas; bedside exploration/'finger test.' Ischemic priapism: cavernosal blood gas (ischemic = PO2<30 mmHg, PCO2>60, pH<7.25) + penile duplex Doppler (absent cavernosal inflow) - diagnostic. Epididymo-orchitis/abscess: scrotal US (hyperemia, abscess, pyocele) to guide drainage.
time_critical_interventions: Fournier/NSTI: operative debridement within <12h of diagnosis (EAST PMG: mortality 14% early vs 26% late), ideally <6h (19% vs 32% mortality, Nawijn meta-analysis); broad-spectrum antibiotics + full sepsis bundle within 1h (lactate, cultures, 30 mL/kg balanced crystalloid, empiric abx per CMS SEP-1/Surviving Sepsis). Testicular torsion: detorsion within 6h of pain onset -> ~90-100% salvage; 6-12h ~50%; >24h ~10% -> emergent OR; attempt manual detorsion as a bridge. Ischemic priapism: treat within 4h; >4h is a corporal compartment-syndrome emergency; detumescence via aspiration + intracavernosal phenylephrine; erectile-dysfunction risk rises sharply beyond 24-48h.
initial_management: Fournier/NSTI: EMERGENT wide surgical debridement to viable tissue (definitive therapy) PLUS empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics per IDSA 2014 - piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem (meropenem) PLUS MRSA coverage (vancomycin or linezolid) PLUS clindamycin for antitoxin/ribosomal toxin suppression; de-escalate on cultures. Balanced crystalloid resuscitation, norepinephrine for septic shock, restrictive transfusion (Hgb <7 g/dL), glycemic control, tetanus prophylaxis; selective fecal (diverting colostomy) or urinary (suprapubic catheter) diversion; IVIG only for streptococcal/clostridial toxic shock (weak). Torsion: OR detorsion + bilateral orchiopexy; orchiectomy if nonviable. Ischemic priapism: corporal aspiration/irrigation + intracavernosal phenylephrine (100-500 mcg q3-5 min, max ~1 mg, cardiac monitoring), distal shunt if refractory, early penile prosthesis for prolonged/refractory. Epididymo-orchitis (CDC 2021): ceftriaxone 500 mg IM x1 + doxycycline 100 mg PO BID x10d (GC/chlamydia); add or substitute levofloxacin 500 mg daily x10d if enteric organisms/insertive anal sex; IV antibiotics + drainage if abscess or sepsis.
day1_milestones: POD0/Day1: Fournier - complete index debridement, ICU admission, sepsis resuscitation, obtain deep tissue cultures, start/refine broad-spectrum + clindamycin, mark tissue demarcation, decide fecal/urinary diversion, and SCHEDULE mandatory second-look within 24h. Torsion - document successful detorsion + bilateral orchiopexy (or orchiectomy) and testicular viability; scrotal support, multimodal analgesia. Priapism - confirm and document detumescence; monitor for recurrence. Epididymo-orchitis - start IV antibiotics, cultures, scrotal elevation, analgesia. All: VTE risk assessment, glycemic protocol, opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia.
day2_milestones: POD1/Day2: Fournier/NSTI - MANDATORY planned second-look re-debridement within 24h (repeat until no residual necrosis); reassess hemodynamics/lactate, de-escalate antibiotics per Gram stain/culture, initiate negative-pressure wound therapy (NPWT) once source controlled, start high-protein nutrition. Elective/torsion - advance diet, transition to oral analgesia, mobilize; discharge if uncomplicated (orchiopexy often 23h observation). Priapism - confirm sustained detumescence, arrange urology follow-up. Epididymo-orchitis - assess clinical response (defervescence, reduced pain/swelling), transition IV->PO antibiotics once afebrile 24h and tolerating PO.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ to discharge: Fournier/NSTI - serial debridements q24-48h until clean granulating wound bed, continue NPWT, wean vasopressors/ventilator, narrow antibiotics (continue until source controlled + clinically improved/afebrile), then reconstructive planning (split-thickness skin grafts, scrotal/perineal flaps) with plastics/urology, aggressive nutrition and rehabilitation. Other arms - confirm discharge readiness, wound checks, remove catheters/drains, complete STI counseling and partner therapy for epididymitis.
consults_multidisciplinary: Urology (lead for GU emergencies) + Acute Care/General/Colorectal Surgery (NSTI debridement, diversion); Infectious Disease (empiric therapy + de-escalation/stewardship, OPAT); Critical Care (septic shock); Plastic/Reconstructive Surgery (staged wound closure, flaps/grafts); Endocrinology (glycemic control/DKA); Wound-ostomy nursing + Nutrition (early, high-protein); PT/OT (deconditioning/rehab); Pharmacy (renal dosing, vasopressors, stewardship); Care management/Social work (disposition, substance use, follow-up); Palliative care for high FGSI/poor prognosis; STI counseling/partner services for epididymitis; consider Hyperbaric medicine as adjunct (selected, weak evidence).
monitoring_level: ICU: septic shock, vasopressor need, high FGSI (>9), post-extensive debridement, respiratory failure, AKI. Step-down/telemetry: borderline hemodynamics, significant comorbidity, high-risk post-op. Floor: stable post-orchiopexy/orchiectomy, uncomplicated epididymitis on IV antibiotics, resolved priapism, minor GU procedures. Escalation triggers: rising lactate, new/increasing pressor requirement, expanding necrosis/crepitus, worsening qSOFA/SIRS, oliguria/AKI, or NEWS/MEWS breach -> ICU + repeat OR.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: early enteral, high-protein feeding for the hypercatabolic NSTI state; dietitian consult; correct hypoalbuminemia. Mobility: early mobilization once hemodynamically stable; PT for ICU-related deconditioning. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin/LMWH or UFH) once surgical hemostasis secure - high risk in septic immobile patients; mechanical (SCDs) when bleeding risk. Glucose: target 140-180 mg/dL with insulin infusion in ICU (avoid tight <110); optimize underlying diabetes. Lines/catheters: daily necessity review; suprapubic/urinary diversion for perineal wounds; remove Foley and central lines as soon as feasible (CAUTI/CLABSI prevention).
discharge_criteria: Source control achieved (no further debridement required); wound stable/granulating on NPWT or definitively closed; afebrile >=24-48h; hemodynamically stable off vasopressors; tolerating diet; pain controlled on oral analgesia; glucose controlled; ambulating at/near baseline; off IV antibiotics OR established OPAT plan; demonstrated home/facility wound-care capability. Elective/torsion arm: pain controlled, voiding, ambulating, wound intact.
discharge_planning: Disposition: home with home-health wound care vs SNF/LTAC for complex wounds or rehab; OPAT coordinated with ID for residual IV antibiotics. Medication reconciliation: antibiotic step-down/OPAT, analgesia taper, glycemic agents. Follow-up scheduled BEFORE discharge: urology and surgery/plastics for staged reconstruction, wound clinic, ID for OPAT, primary care for diabetes. Education: NPWT/wound care, recurrent-infection warning signs, glycemic control, smoking cessation; for epididymitis - STI partner therapy, HIV/syphilis screening, test-of-cure and abstinence counseling; documented return precautions and warm handoff.
expected_los: Strongly bimodal across this collapsed group. Minor/elective GU procedures and uncomplicated inflammation: GMLOS ~2-4 days (e.g., DRG 718 ~2d, 726 BPH ~2d, 728 inflammation ~3d, 725/727 with MCC ~4-5d). Fournier gangrene/NSTI: mean ~13-20 days in US population studies, longer with ICU/MCC. Blended group GMLOS roughly 3-5 days, with FG cases the multi-week outliers.
target_los: Elective/minor procedures and uncomplicated epididymitis: 1-3 days (many orchiopexy/priapism cases 23h observation to 1-2 days). Fournier gangrene/NSTI: optimized target ~10-14 days achieved via debridement <12h, mandatory 24h second-look, early NPWT, regionalized higher-volume care, and early reconstruction - shorter than historical 3-4 week stays.
quality_metrics: CMS SEP-1 severe sepsis/septic shock bundle compliance (septic FG/orchitis); institutional time-to-debridement (<12h) for NSTI; testicular salvage rate and time-to-detorsion; time-to-detumescence for priapism; antibiotic de-escalation/stewardship rate; surgical-site infection and unplanned reoperation; 30-day readmission; 30-day/in-hospital mortality (FG); VTE prophylaxis compliance; glycemic control; perioperative antibiotic timing (SCIP-derived) and ERAS compliance for elective GU surgery; CAUTI/CLABSI rates.
common_complications: Septic shock and multi-organ failure (NSTI) -> early source control + resuscitation bundle; AKI -> avoid nephrotoxins, renal-dose drugs; DIC/coagulopathy; large tissue defects requiring serial debridement and staged reconstruction; wound infection/dehiscence -> NPWT and mandatory re-exploration; testicular loss from delayed detorsion -> protect the 6h salvage window; erectile dysfunction/corporal fibrosis and recurrence (priapism) -> prompt detumescence + follow-up; hospital-acquired CAUTI/CLABSI, VTE, ICU delirium and deconditioning; colostomy/diversion morbidity.
readmission_drivers: Wound complications and recurrent/persistent infection, inadequate index source control, uncontrolled diabetes, poor wound-care follow-through, OPAT/line failure, recurrent ischemic priapism (esp. sickle cell disease), missed salvage requiring return to OR, medication nonadherence, and social determinants (housing instability, substance use). Prevention levers: structured wound-care handoff with home health, ID-supervised OPAT, tight glycemic control, pre-scheduled surgical follow-up for staged reconstruction, STI partner treatment, and care-management engagement.
guideline_source: IDSA 2014 Skin & Soft-Tissue Infections guideline (necrotizing infections); WSES/SIS-E 2018 SSTI consensus; EAST 2018 Practice Management Guideline on NSTI debridement timing; AUA/SMSNA 2021 Acute Ischemic Priapism guideline; AUA Acute Scrotum curriculum + EAU Paediatric Urology (testicular torsion, TWIST); CDC STI Treatment Guidelines 2021 (epididymitis/orchitis); Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 + CMS SEP-1 as the sepsis overlay.
key_citations: Stevens DL et al. IDSA Practice Guidelines for Diagnosis & Management of SSTIs: 2014 Update. Clin Infect Dis 2014;59(2):e10-52 (PMID 24973422). Sartelli M et al. 2018 WSES/SIS-E consensus on SSTIs. World J Emerg Surg 2018;13:58 (PMID 30564282). Gelbard RB et al. Optimal timing of initial debridement for NSTI: EAST PMG. J Trauma Acute Care Surg 2018;85(1):208-214 (PMID 29485428). Nawijn F et al. Time is of the essence when treating NSTIs: meta-analysis. World J Emerg Surg 2020;15:4 (PMID 31921330). Bivalacqua TJ et al. Acute Ischemic Priapism: an AUA/SMSNA Guideline. J Urol 2021;206(5):1114-1121 (PMID 34495686). Sheth KR et al. Validation of the TWIST Score. J Urol 2016;195(6):1870-1876 (PMID 26835833). Sorensen MD, Krieger JN. Fournier's Gangrene: Epidemiology & Outcomes in the General US Population. Urol Int 2016;97(3):249-259 (PMID 27172977). Workowski KA et al. CDC STI Treatment Guidelines, 2021. MMWR Recomm Rep 2021;70(4):1-187. Citations sourced via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Moderate-to-Strong, mixed across the group. Strong (consensus) for early operative source control in NSTI/Fournier and for CDC epididymitis regimens, though the debridement-timing evidence is observational (EAST GRADE, Level IV; no RCTs). Moderate-Strong for AUA/SMSNA priapism management and the 6-hour torsion salvage window. Risk scores (LRINEC, FGSI/UFGSI, TWIST) are validated adjuncts, not stand-alone rule-outs - clinical judgment governs. Heterogeneous collapsed DRG group, so grade varies by condition.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Severity tier dominates this collapsed MDC 12 surgical group. Without CC/MCC (DRG 716/718/724/726/728/730): mostly elective/minor procedures or uncomplicated inflammation - floor care, LOS ~1-3d, low complication rate. With CC/MCC (715/717/722/725/727/729): sepsis, AKI, diabetic complications, extensive necrosis - longer stays, more resource use. MCC (septic shock, ARF, respiratory failure) drives ICU, vasopressors, multiple debridements, LOS ~2-3 weeks; population mortality 7.5% but up to 20-40% in severe/comorbid cohorts. Fournier gangrene almost always maps to a with-MCC DRG once sepsis/organ failure is coded; FGSI >9 and CHF (OR 2.1)/renal failure (OR 3.2)/coagulopathy (OR 3.4) predict ICU need and death.
pathway_pearls: 1) 'Time is fascia' - for Fournier/NSTI get to OR within <12h (ideally <6h); do NOT wait for CT or a low LRINEC to 'rule out' - clinical suspicion trumps the score, and a mandatory planned second-look within 24h with serial debridements is standard. 2) Empiric regimen = broad-spectrum beta-lactam + MRSA coverage + CLINDAMYCIN (antitoxin) within 1h, but antibiotics never substitute for source control; de-escalate on cultures. 3) Testicular torsion is a 6-hour salvage window - high TWIST (5-7) goes straight to OR for bilateral orchiopexy without waiting for ultrasound. 4) Ischemic priapism is a compartment syndrome - aspirate + intracavernosal phenylephrine promptly (>4h threatens erectile function); regionalize Fournier care to higher-volume centers (42-84% lower adjusted mortality).

scope_and_volume_notes: Testes/penis procedures and medical male-reproductive disorders (Fournier gangrene, orchitis).

volume_data_vintage: Primarily HCUP NIS 2021 national estimates; obstetric/newborn estimates cross-checked to CDC 2022 natality. Volumes were not re-estimated during the FY2026 code migration.
citation_audit_status: Completed 2026-07-21: 7/7 cited PMID references resolved in PubMed; CMS code existence and MDC/type metadata validated against v43.1; structured checks passed for risk thresholds, time targets, management detail, LOS, quality metrics, guideline dates, and citations.
clinical_verification_basis: Current pass completed citation-resolvability, coding, taxonomy, schema, and consistency checks. The source workflow contains no completed adversarial high-stakes clinical verdict for this row; clinician revalidation remains required before protocol or order-set use.
data_quality_notes: Condition-level pathways may intentionally share DRGs; this is not a one-row-per-DRG crosswalk.

admission_criteria: All DRGs in this Pre-MDC cluster are ICU-level inpatient by definition — no ED-discharge/observation pathway exists. Heart (001-002): accepted organ offer for orthotopic heart transplant, OR durable LVAD/BiVAD/TAH implant for ACC/AHA Stage D / NYHA IIIB-IV advanced HF with inotrope dependence, cardiac index <2.0 L/min/m2, PCWP >18, or peak VO2 <=14 mL/kg/min (<=12 on beta-blocker); Status 1-2 candidates on VA-ECMO/temporary MCS. Liver (005-006): accepted deceased/living-donor graft for MELD 3.0 >=15 (survival-benefit threshold), HCC exception (within Milan), or Status 1A acute liver failure (King's College criteria); intestinal/multivisceral for irreversible intestinal failure with TPN complications. Lung (007-008): accepted offer under Composite Allocation Score (CAS) for end-stage lung disease. ECMO: refractory cardiogenic shock (VA) or severe ARDS meeting EOLIA criteria (VV). Prolonged mechanical ventilation/tracheostomy (010-011): failure to wean, MV >96h
risk_stratification: MELD 3.0 (liver): >=15 = listing/transplant survival benefit, higher score = allocation priority (components bilirubin, INR, creatinine capped 3.0, sodium, albumin, female-sex adjustment); Status 1A for acute liver failure. INTERMACS profiles 1-7 (MCS): Profile 1 (critical cardiogenic shock/'crash-and-burn') -> temporary MCS/ECMO bridge; Profiles 2-3 (declining or inotrope-dependent) -> durable LVAD; Profiles 4-7 ambulatory. Heart 6-tier allocation (2018): Status 1 = VA-ECMO or non-dischargeable BiVAD, Status 2 = temporary MCS/sustained VT-VF. Lung CAS (0-100 composite: medical urgency + post-transplant survival + biological disadvantages + access). Candidacy/prognosis: peak VO2, Seattle HF Model / Heart Failure Survival Score (heart). VV-ECMO survival: RESP and PRESERVE scores; VA-ECMO: SAVE score. ICU mortality: SOFA, APACHE II. Post-op: Primary Graft Dysfunction grade 0-3 (lung/heart) at T0/24/48/72h. Frailty and Karnofsky/functional status gate candidacy
initial_workup_labs: CBC, CMP, LFTs, ammonia (liver), lactate (arterial + venous for shock/clearance), ABG; coagulation PT/INR/aPTT/fibrinogen +/- TEG/ROTEM; type-and-crossmatch, ABO/Rh; HLA typing + panel-reactive antibody / calculated PRA (cPRA) + physical or virtual crossmatch (positive crossmatch -> desensitization or avoid donor); donor and recipient viral serologies CMV/EBV/HIV/HBV/HCV (CMV D+/R- flags valganciclovir prophylaxis, EBV mismatch flags PTLD risk); BNP/NT-proBNP and troponin (cardiac); baseline tacrolimus level; blood/urine cultures if febrile; DIC panel; pregnancy test. Triggers: rising lactate/falling clearance -> escalate support; coagulopathy -> massive transfusion protocol (esp liver); positive crossmatch -> immunologic risk mitigation
initial_imaging_dx: Heart: TTE/TEE, right-heart catheterization for PVR/PCWP (fixed PVR >3-5 Wood units is a relative HTx contraindication), coronary angiography, CXR. Liver: Doppler ultrasound of hepatic artery/portal vein/hepatic veins at baseline and POD0-1 to exclude hepatic artery thrombosis (repeat if LFTs rise), cross-sectional CT/MR angiography. Lung: chest CT, PFTs, V/Q scan, echo/right-heart cath for pulmonary hypertension, CXR. ECMO: pre-cannulation bedside echo, vascular ultrasound for cannula sizing, daily CXR for cannula/ETT position, cranial CT for VA-ECMO. Post-op: serial CXR, POCUS, graft-specific surveillance imaging
time_critical_interventions: Cold ischemia time is the modifiable clock and drives OR choreography: heart <4h, lung <6-8h, liver ideally <8-12h (<6-8h optimal), pancreas <12-18h; minimizing it reduces primary graft dysfunction. ECMO initiation by EOLIA thresholds: PaO2/FiO2 <50 mmHg for >3h, OR <80 mmHg for >6h, OR arterial pH <7.25 with PaCO2 >=60 mmHg for >6h despite optimized lung-protective ventilation -> VV-ECMO; refractory cardiogenic shock or eCPR within minutes of arrest -> VA-ECMO. Heparin bolus at cannulation (ACT 180-220s). Immediate lung-protective ventilation and iNO/inhaled epoprostenol for severe post-lung PGD. Controlled graft reperfusion to avoid post-reperfusion syndrome (liver). First-hours hyperacute-rejection and bleeding surveillance
initial_management: Induction immunosuppression: IL-2 receptor antagonist basiliximab OR lymphocyte-depleting rabbit anti-thymocyte globulin (thymoglobulin, renal-sparing to delay CNI), plus high-dose IV methylprednisolone intra-operatively. Maintenance triple therapy: calcineurin inhibitor tacrolimus (initial trough target ~8-12 ng/mL) + antimetabolite mycophenolate mofetil + corticosteroid taper; mTOR inhibitor (sirolimus/everolimus) deferred early (impairs wound/bronchial-anastomotic healing). Hemodynamics: norepinephrine/epinephrine/vasopressin and milrinone; iNO/inhaled epoprostenol for RV failure or pulmonary hypertension. MCS/ECMO anticoagulation: unfractionated heparin titrated to anti-Xa 0.3-0.7 or ACT (bivalirudin alternative). Transfusion: restrictive Hgb <7 g/dL when stable (higher in active bleeding/ischemia); goal-directed FFP/platelets/cryoprecipitate by TEG/ROTEM; massive transfusion protocol for liver. Prophylaxis bundle: perioperative antibiotics, PJP prophylaxis TMP-SMX, antifungal (nystatin/voriconazole, esp lung and liver), CMV valganciclovir if D+/R-, stress-ulcer prophylaxis
day1_milestones: POD0: ICU admission with continuous hemodynamic monitoring (arterial line, PA catheter for heart); confirm graft function -- echo (heart), Doppler hepatic-vessel patency to exclude HAT (liver), PaO2/FiO2 and PGD grade at T0 (lung); lung-protective ventilation. Initiate/continue induction immunosuppression and first tacrolimus dose with baseline trough. Achieve lactate clearance, correct coagulopathy, maintain MAP/CVP targets. Monitor mediastinal/chest-tube and abdominal drain output; low threshold for re-exploration if bleeding. Begin weaning vasoactive agents as graft supports circulation
day2_milestones: POD1: extubate as soon as weaning criteria met (early extubation especially reduces lung PGD and ICU days); wean vasopressors/inotropes. Advance tacrolimus toward target trough; trend graft labs (LFTs, EF on echo, oxygenation, +/- donor-derived cell-free DNA). Repeat Doppler graft-patency ultrasound (liver) and echo (heart). Activate PT/OT for early mobilization. Remove non-essential central lines/catheters; begin enteral nutrition. Monitor renal function for CNI nephrotoxicity or hepatorenal recovery
day3plus_milestones: POD2+: transfer ICU -> step-down when extubated, off vasoactives, with stable graft; chest-tube removal, telemetry. Protocolized surveillance: endomyocardial biopsy ~week 1 (heart), surveillance bronchoscopy (lung), serial LFT trend (liver). Optimize immunosuppression troughs, confirm full prophylaxis bundle, begin steroid taper. Rejection and infection surveillance. LVAD-specific: driveline-exit-site care training and bridge to warfarin (target INR 2-3 for HeartMate 3). Rehabilitation, transplant/medication education with caregiver teach-back. Discharge readiness typically POD7-14 (organ-dependent)
consults_multidisciplinary: Pre-transplant multidisciplinary selection committee first. Peri-operative: transplant surgery + organ-specific medicine (cardiology/hepatology/pulmonology), transplant infectious disease, transplant clinical pharmacist (immunosuppression dosing at bedside), transplant nephrology (CNI/AKI), VAD/ECMO coordinator and perfusion, respiratory therapy, dietitian/nutrition, PT/OT and rehab (POD1), social work and transplant psychology (adherence, substance-use history for liver), financial coordinator, and palliative care for goals-of-care in refractory ECMO or non-recovery
monitoring_level: ICU mandatory POD0 through at least POD2 for all: continuous arterial/PA-catheter hemodynamics, ventilator, and ECMO/MCS circuit monitoring. Step-down/telemetry when extubated, off vasoactive infusions, and graft-stable. Floor when independently mobilizing with stable troughs. Escalation triggers: rising lactate, falling graft function (echo/Doppler/PaO2:FiO2), new bleeding or tamponade physiology, arrhythmia, rejection signs, sepsis/new fever. VA-ECMO specific: distal-limb perfusion checks and differential-hypoxia (harlequin/north-south) surveillance
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: early enteral feeding within 24-48h; TPN if gut unusable (mandatory bridge in intestinal transplant); glucose control 140-180 mg/dL (steroid-driven hyperglycemia); high protein for wound/anastomotic healing. Mobility: early PT/OT POD1, progressive ambulation, ambulate-on-ECMO where feasible to preserve function for transplant. VTE prophylaxis: mechanical (SCDs) initially given bleeding risk, add pharmacologic once hemostasis secured (MCS/ECMO patients already systemically anticoagulated). Line/catheter stewardship: early removal of central lines, Foley, and chest tubes to reduce CLABSI/CAUTI/VAP
discharge_criteria: Stable/improving graft function (normalizing LFTs, recovering EF, adequate oxygenation off high support); therapeutic and stable immunosuppression trough levels; afebrile and no active/untreated infection; no active rejection on surveillance; tolerating oral diet and oral analgesia; adequate mobility and rehab progress; healing wounds/anastomoses; patient and caregiver demonstrate competence in immunosuppression, prophylaxis, glucose, and (LVAD) driveline care plus therapeutic INR; reliable outpatient follow-up and 24/7 transplant contact established
discharge_planning: Disposition home vs acute rehab; prolonged-ventilation/tracheostomy patients (010-011) typically to LTACH for vent weaning. Meticulous medication reconciliation of immunosuppression plus full prophylaxis (TMP-SMX, valganciclovir, antifungal) and anticoagulation. Structured early follow-up: transplant clinic within 1-2 weeks, surveillance labs 2-3x/week initially, and a scheduled protocol biopsy/bronchoscopy calendar. Teach-back education, written sick-day and rejection/infection warning plans, 24/7 transplant hotline, pharmacy adherence and financial support, and a warm transitions-of-care handoff to the referring team/PCP
expected_los: Collapsed high-resource group; benchmark GMLOS varies by organ and severity split. Approximate: heart transplant 001 (w/MCC) ~25-30 days vs 002 (w/o MCC) ~13-16; durable LVAD ~15-18; liver transplant 005 (w/MCC or intestinal) ~18-22 vs 006 (w/o MCC) ~8-11; lung transplant ~18-21; tracheostomy/prolonged-vent (010-011) frequently 25-40+. Blended group central tendency ~14-25 days
target_los: Under an ideal ERAS-informed pathway with early extubation, early mobilization, and protocolized immunosuppression/prophylaxis: heart transplant ~12-14 days; uncomplicated LVAD ~12-14; liver transplant ~8-10; lung transplant ~14-16; trach/vent patients shortened via timely LTACH transfer. Represents roughly 15-25% reduction versus benchmark
quality_metrics: SRTR program-specific 1-year patient and graft survival (tied to CMS Conditions of Participation for transplant programs); functioning graft at discharge; primary graft dysfunction/nonfunction rate; 30-day and 1-year mortality; acute-rejection rate; cold ischemia time; CLABSI/CAUTI/SSI/VAP; VAD 2-year survival free of disabling stroke or reoperation (MOMENTUM 3 benchmark); ECMO survival to decannulation and to discharge (ELSO registry); early-extubation and ERAS compliance; 30-day readmission rate
common_complications: Primary graft dysfunction/nonfunction (mitigate: donor optimization, short cold ischemia, lung-protective ventilation, iNO); acute rejection (surveillance biopsy/bronchoscopy, dd-cfDNA, therapeutic troughs); infection -- bacterial, CMV, invasive fungal, PJP (prophylaxis bundles); bleeding/reoperation and tamponade (heart/VAD); hepatic artery thrombosis and biliary leak/stricture (liver, Doppler surveillance); bronchial-anastomotic dehiscence and PGD (lung); RV failure, device thrombosis, GI bleed, and stroke (LVAD/ECMO); AKI/CNI nephrotoxicity; delirium and ICU-acquired weakness; VTE; steroid hyperglycemia. ECMO-specific: limb ischemia, differential hypoxia, hemolysis, circuit thrombosis
readmission_drivers: Infection (leading driver), acute rejection, volume overload/HF, dehydration and AKI, immunosuppression toxicity or non-adherence, biliary complications (liver), GI bleeding and driveline/device infection (LVAD), sub/supra-therapeutic anticoagulation (INR), and pleural/pericardial effusions. Prevention levers: intensive early clinic follow-up with lab surveillance 2-3x/week, medication-adherence support and teach-back, 24/7 access, dietitian involvement, and remote/telehealth monitoring
guideline_source: Heart/MCS: ISHLT Guidelines for Evaluation and Care of Cardiac Transplant Candidates 2024 + ISHLT/HFSA Guidelines for Mechanical Circulatory Support 2023 + ACC/AHA/HFSA HF Guideline 2022. Liver: AASLD/AST Practice Guideline on Adult Liver Transplantation (Candidate Evaluation) 2025 + OPTN/UNOS allocation (MELD 3.0, 2023). Lung: ISHLT Consensus Document for Selection of Lung Transplant Candidates 2021 + OPTN Composite Allocation Score (2023). ECMO: ELSO VV-ECMO and VA-ECMO Guidelines 2021. Immunosuppression: ACCP/AST/ISHLT Consensus on Maintenance Immunosuppression 2022
key_citations: ISHLT Guidelines for the Evaluation and Care of Cardiac Transplant Candidates-2024, J Heart Lung Transplant 2024 (PMID 39115488); AASLD/AST Practice Guideline on Adult Liver Transplantation: Candidate Evaluation, Hepatology 2025 (PMID 41405234); Leard LE et al, Consensus document for selection of lung transplant candidates: ISHLT update, J Heart Lung Transplant 2021;40:1349-1379 (PMID 34419372); ELSO Management of Adult Patients Supported with VV-ECMO, ASAIO J 2021;67:601-610 (PMID 33965970); Combes A et al (EOLIA), ECMO for Severe ARDS, N Engl J Med 2018;378:1965-1975 (PMID 29791822); Mehra MR et al (MOMENTUM 3 final report, HeartMate 3), N Engl J Med 2019;380:1618-1627 (PMID 30883052); ISHLT/HFSA 2023 Guidelines for Mechanical Circulatory Support, J Heart Lung Transplant 2023
evidence_grade: Mixed. Strong (Class I society guidelines) for candidate selection, allocation, and immunosuppression backbone (ISHLT 2024/2021, AASLD/AST 2025, ELSO 2021, ACC/AHA/HFSA); high-quality landmark RCTs anchor key decisions (EOLIA for VV-ECMO in severe ARDS; MOMENTUM 3 for the fully magnetically levitated LVAD). However, the day-by-day perioperative inpatient pathway is largely center-specific protocol plus registry data (SRTR, STS-INTERMACS, ELSO) and expert consensus rather than RCT-driven. Overall: Strong for selection/device/immunosuppression evidence; Moderate-to-consensus for the collapsed peri-operative care pathway
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The group pairs w/MCC and w/o MCC DRGs (001 vs 002 heart; 005 vs 006 liver), and MCC presence -- cardiogenic or septic shock, ARDS, ATN requiring dialysis, prolonged MV, primary graft dysfunction -- shifts patients to longer ICU stays, ECMO/temporary-MCS bridging, and materially higher LOS (heart 001 ~25-30d vs 002 ~13-16d; liver 005 ~18-22d vs 006 ~8-11d), with more reoperation, infection, and worse survival. Intestinal/multivisceral transplant (within 005) and re-transplantation carry the highest resource intensity. Trach/prolonged-vent DRGs (010-011) are LOS-driven by ventilator-weaning failure and LTACH need. Pre-transplant ECMO bridging escalates every downstream cost and complication risk
pathway_pearls: 1) Cold ischemia time is the modifiable clock (heart <4h, lung <6-8h, liver <8-12h) -- tight OR/logistics choreography minimizes primary graft dysfunction and LOS. 2) Get immunosuppression right on day 0: protocolized induction (basiliximab or ATG) + CNI trough targets + the full prophylaxis bundle (PJP/CMV/antifungal), with the transplant pharmacist at the bedside. 3) Early extubation and early mobilization (ERAS) cut PGD, ICU days, and total LOS -- highest yield in lung transplant. 4) Rigorous multidisciplinary selection up front plus relentless post-discharge surveillance (labs 2-3x/week, protocol biopsy/bronchoscopy, 24/7 access, adherence support) is what prevents the rejection- and infection-driven readmissions that dominate this group
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Heart transplant/implant of heart-assist (001-002), liver/intestinal transplant (005-006), lung transplant (007-008), tracheostomy-linked/other (010-011). Highest-resource DRGs.

admission_criteria: Non-circulatory amputation is an inpatient surgical admission by default; admit for: (1) trauma - non-salvageable/mangled extremity, Gustilo-Anderson IIIB/IIIC open fracture with non-reconstructable soft-tissue/vascular loss, warm ischemia >6h, or traumatic subtotal amputation; (2) infection - necrotizing soft-tissue infection/gas gangrene, wet gangrene, or IWGDF/IDSA moderate-severe (grade 3-4) diabetic foot infection/osteomyelitis with systemic sepsis needing source control; (3) tumor - bone/soft-tissue sarcoma not amenable to limb salvage; (4) failed salvage or chronic refractory osteomyelitis. Emergent/urgent for sepsis/nec-fasc/vascular trauma; elective for oncologic/planned revision. ED discharge/observation reserved for isolated minor toe/ray amputation without systemic illness or uncontrolled comorbidity; any amputation at/proximal to transmetatarsal level or with sepsis/trauma/tumor = inpatient
risk_stratification: MESS (Mangled Extremity Severity Score, skeletal-soft-tissue+ischemia+shock+age): historically >=7 predicts amputation (Johansen 1990) BUT LEAP proved poor prospective validity - use as adjunct, low MESS does NOT confirm salvageability, never sole criterion; Gustilo-Anderson open-fracture class I/II/IIIA/IIIB/IIIC - IIIC (arterial injury) highest amputation/infection risk, drives antibiotic breadth; IWGDF/IDSA infection severity grade 1-4 (mild/moderate/severe) + WIfI (Wound-Ischemia-foot Infection) for diabetic foot - severe/sepsis -> urgent OR; RCRI or NSQIP-MICA + ASA class for cardiac risk; Clinical Frailty Scale / 5-item mFI predicts mortality and prosthesis non-use; serum albumin <3.0 g/dL and total lymphocyte count <1500 predict wound-healing failure/reamputation -> nutrition optimization; AJCC/Enneking staging for tumor
initial_workup_labs: CBC, CMP (renal/hepatic), coags (INR/aPTT/fibrinogen), type-and-crossmatch; lactate (>2 triggers sepsis bundle); blood cultures x2 if febrile/septic; CRP + ESR + procalcitonin for infection/osteomyelitis burden; deep tissue and BONE cultures at surgery (not superficial swabs - IWGDF); HbA1c and glucose (diabetic subset); albumin/prealbumin + total lymphocyte count (healing/nutrition; albumin <3.0 -> nutrition consult); CK if crush/rhabdomyolysis (trauma); group-A strep/Clostridium and Gram stain if necrotizing; Hgb <7 g/dL triggers transfusion
initial_imaging_dx: Plain radiographs of involved limb immediately (fracture pattern, subcutaneous gas, osteomyelitis, foreign body); CT/CTA for vascular-injury mapping (trauma IIIC) and osteomyelitis/soft-tissue extent; MRI for osteomyelitis extent and soft-tissue sarcoma with core/incisional biopsy along the planned resection axis; chest CT for tumor staging before oncologic amputation; ABI/toe pressures/TcPO2 and Doppler for amputation-level selection in dysvascular/diabetic limbs; POCUS/CT and LRINEC score as adjuncts for necrotizing infection - imaging must NOT delay OR when nec-fasc/septic; staging imaging completed before elective oncologic amputation
time_critical_interventions: Open-fracture prophylactic antibiotics within 1h of presentation (goal <1h, no later than 3h) - cefazolin for Gustilo I/II, add gram-negative coverage for type III (SIS 2022: total duration <=24h, no extended-spectrum); necrotizing soft-tissue infection = emergent surgical debridement/guillotine amputation for source control (target <6h, sooner if unstable) with broad-spectrum antibiotics + clindamycin (antitoxin) <1h and Surviving Sepsis 1-hour bundle (lactate, cultures, antibiotics, 30 mL/kg crystalloid if MAP<65 or lactate>=4); vascular trauma (IIIC) revascularization-vs-amputation decision with warm-ischemia time <6h; tourniquet/direct pressure for traumatic hemorrhage; tetanus prophylaxis for trauma wounds
initial_management: Source control = amputation at most distal VIABLE level; for infection/sepsis do open guillotine amputation first then delayed definitive closure/revision at 24-72h (never close over active infection). Antibiotics: infected - empiric piperacillin-tazobactam or carbapenem + vancomycin (MRSA) +/- clindamycin (nec-fasc toxin), de-escalate to deep-culture-directed therapy; clean trauma/tumor - perioperative cefazolin <=24h. Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (ERAS): regional (sciatic/femoral perineural catheter or epidural) + acetaminophen + NSAID/celecoxib + gabapentinoid + ketamine adjunct, opioid PCA rescue; perioperative regional analgesia for phantom-limb-pain prevention (evidence mixed). Goal-directed euvolemia with balanced crystalloid (avoid overload). Restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if cardiac disease). Glucose 140-180 mg/dL. LMWH VTE prophylaxis. Reverse anticoagulation for emergent cases (vit-K/4F-PCC for warfarin, idarucizumab for dabigatran, andexanet/PCC for factor-Xa inhibitors)
day1_milestones: POD0 (surgery day): complete amputation at optimal viable level or open guillotine if infected; apply rigid removable dressing for transtibial residual limb (reduces edema, protects, shortens time to prosthetic casting - Churilov 2014); regional catheter + multimodal analgesia running; hemodynamic stabilization and continued/targeted antibiotics; glucose 140-180; start pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis once hemostasis secure; resume oral diet/nutrition same day (ERAS); document residual-limb perfusion and dressing; foley only if truly needed; PT bedside eval (bed mobility/transfers); for open amputation, schedule return to OR for definitive closure
day2_milestones: POD1: out of bed to chair and mobilize with PT (ERAS early mobilization); transition regional/IV analgesia to oral multimodal regimen; residual-limb edema control (rigid dressing/shrinker) and wound inspection; continue or de-escalate antibiotics per deep cultures; remove urinary catheter; OT for ADLs and one-legged transfer training; PM&R/prosthetist counseling and amputee peer-support introduction; nutrition optimization (protein, glycemia); delirium screening in elderly; contralateral-limb offloading/protection in diabetic patients
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ to discharge: progressive mobilization (wheelchair independence, transfers, gait training with assistive device), residual-limb shaping/desensitization, serial wound-healing checks; finalize antibiotic plan - OPAT ~6 weeks if residual osteomyelitis, shorter/none if clean margins (IWGDF); pain controlled on oral regimen; formal prosthetic-candidacy assessment and referral; depression/adjustment and phantom-pain screening; for open cases complete staged definitive closure/revision; for tumor arrange adjuvant chemo/RT and oncology follow-up; finalize disposition (home+home-health vs acute inpatient rehab vs SNF)
consults_multidisciplinary: Primary surgeon - orthopedic/trauma, vascular, or surgical oncology per etiology; Infectious Disease early for nec-fasc/osteomyelitis and OPAT; PT and OT POD0-1; PM&R/physiatry + certified prosthetist early (prosthetic planning drives rehab); Pain/anesthesia for regional analgesia; Endocrinology/diabetes for glycemic and diabetic-foot management; Nutrition/dietitian (albumin <3.0 or diabetic); Care management/social work day 0-1 for disposition, DME, home safety; Psychology/psychiatry for adjustment, depression, phantom pain; amputee peer mentor; Plastic surgery for flap coverage when needed; Palliative care for goals-of-care in frail/oncologic patients
monitoring_level: Surgical floor for uncomplicated planned/elective amputation; telemetry or step-down for significant cardiac risk or major intraoperative blood loss; ICU for septic shock/necrotizing infection with hemodynamic instability, major hemorrhage/coagulopathy, respiratory failure, or ongoing resuscitation. Escalation triggers: sepsis (qSOFA>=2, rising lactate, MAP<65), stump hemorrhage, uncontrolled hyperglycemia/DKA, acute coronary syndrome, new organ dysfunction. De-escalate to floor once source-controlled and hemodynamically stable
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: resume regular/diabetic diet POD0 (ERAS), high-protein, nutrition consult if albumin <3.0; glucose target 140-180 mg/dL with insulin. Mobility: out of bed POD0-1, PT-led progressive mobilization and assistive-device/wheelchair training, position residual limb to prevent flexion contracture (avoid prolonged hip/knee flexion), early prosthetic pathway. VTE: pharmacologic prophylaxis (enoxaparin or UFH) unless contraindicated - amputees are high baseline VTE risk though RCT evidence is limited/low-certainty (Cochrane 2020); mechanical IPC on contralateral limb as adjunct. Lines/catheters: remove foley POD1-2, discontinue central/arterial lines when stable, remove regional catheter by POD2-3
discharge_criteria: Afebrile with infection source-controlled and on a defined definitive antibiotic/OPAT plan; pain controlled on oral regimen; residual limb healing without active bleeding or dehiscence; tolerating diet; glucose controlled; independent in transfers/mobility or safe disposition and support arranged; residual-limb care and infection warning-signs education completed; VTE prophylaxis plan defined; follow-up and prosthetic pathway scheduled; DME (wheelchair, walker) in place
discharge_planning: Disposition: home with home health, or acute inpatient rehabilitation (IRF - preferred for prosthetic candidates/functional recovery), or SNF for higher needs; medication reconciliation (analgesia taper plan, OPAT/antibiotics, diabetes meds, VTE prophylaxis duration); follow-up - surgeon 1-2 weeks for wound/staple removal, ID/OPAT if osteomyelitis, prosthetist/physiatry 2-4 weeks (limb cast once edema settles), oncology for tumor; patient/caregiver education on residual-limb and contralateral-foot care, fall prevention, phantom-limb-pain expectations, infection signs; warm handoff to rehab, DME delivery, home-safety evaluation, and Amputee Coalition/peer-support linkage
expected_los: GMLOS by severity tier (national benchmark, approximate): DRG 476/618 without CC/MCC ~4 days; DRG 475/617 with CC ~6 days; DRG 474/616 with MCC ~9-10 days (AMLOS ~11-12). Blended group GMLOS ~6-7 days. Infected/septic and staged (guillotine then revision) cases run at the longer end
target_los: Under an optimized ERAS/multidisciplinary pathway: ~4-6 days for uncomplicated planned single-stage amputation (no-CC/CC tiers); MCC/septic tier realistically 7-9 days. Target roughly 1-2 day reduction vs GMLOS via multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia, early mobilization, front-loaded prosthetic planning, and avoiding unnecessary staged returns when single-stage closure is safe
quality_metrics: 30-day readmission and reamputation (revision-to-higher-level) rate; 30-day and 1-year mortality; surgical-site/stump infection rate; SEP-1 bundle compliance for septic cases; ERAS protocol compliance; VTE rate (PSI-12); perioperative/open-fracture antibiotic timing-selection-duration (<=24h, SCIP-legacy); time-to-prosthetic-fitting and prosthesis-use rate; discharge-to-community/functional-independence rate; opioid utilization and pain scores; inpatient glycemic control; documented goals-of-care/palliative involvement where appropriate
common_complications: Wound dehiscence/healing failure requiring revision to a higher level (reamputation) - mitigated by correct level selection (perfusion/TcPO2, nutrition, glycemia), rigid dressing, tobacco cessation; surgical-site infection/residual osteomyelitis - deep cultures and directed antibiotics/OPAT; phantom-limb and residual-limb pain - perioperative regional + multimodal analgesia; VTE/PE - prophylaxis; joint flexion contracture - positioning and PT; postoperative delirium in elderly - opioid-sparing regimen and reorientation; falls; hematoma/bleeding; depression/adjustment disorder - screening and psychology; cardiac events - risk stratification and telemetry
readmission_drivers: Stump infection/wound breakdown and reamputation; uncontrolled pain (residual/phantom); VTE/PE; poor glycemic control and contralateral-limb ulceration/gangrene (diabetics face high contralateral-amputation risk); inadequate DME/home support and falls; medication issues (opioids, anticoagulation); missed or delayed prosthetic linkage. Prevention: rigorous residual-limb and contralateral-foot care education, structured ID/OPAT follow-up, diabetes and foot-surveillance programs, early rehab/prosthetic linkage, home-health and scheduled surgical follow-up
guideline_source: ERAS Society and Society for Vascular Surgery 2024 consensus (perioperative care for lower-extremity major limb amputation); VA/DoD 2025 Clinical Practice Guideline for Rehabilitation of Individuals with Lower Limb Amputation; IWGDF/IDSA 2023 (diabetes-related foot infection - infected/diabetic subset); Surgical Infection Society 2022 and EAST/OTA (antibiotics for open extremity fractures - trauma subset); NCCN Bone Cancer and Soft Tissue Sarcoma guidelines (tumor subset)
key_citations: Bosse MJ et al. Analysis of outcomes of reconstruction or amputation after leg-threatening injuries (LEAP). NEJM 2002;347:1924-31, PMID 12477942, doi.org/10.1056/NEJMoa012604; Johansen K et al. Objective criteria accurately predict amputation following lower-extremity trauma (MESS). J Trauma 1990;30:568-72, PMID 2342140, doi.org/10.1097/00005373-199005000-00007; Senneville E et al. IWGDF/IDSA guidelines on diabetes-related foot infections 2023. Diabetes Metab Res Rev 2023;40:e3687, PMID 37779323, doi.org/10.1002/dmrr.3687; Buckman SA et al. SIS 2022 antibiotic use in open extremity fractures. Surg Infect 2022;23:817-28, PMID 36350736, doi.org/10.1089/sur.2022.206; Churilov I et al. Rigid dressings reduce time to prosthetic fitting - meta-analysis. Ann Vasc Surg 2014;28:1801-8, PMID 24911808, doi.org/10.1016/j.avsg.2014.05.002; Herlihy DR et al. Primary VTE prophylaxis in major lower-limb amputation. Cochrane 2020;CD010525, PMID 32692430, doi.org/10.1002/14651858.CD010525.pub3; McGinigle KL, Spangler EL et al. ERAS Society/SVS perioperative framework for lower-extremity major limb amputation, 2024 (all via PubMed)
evidence_grade: Moderate overall, mixed - antibiotic timing/selection and diabetic-foot management rest on Class I society guidelines (SIS 2022, IWGDF/IDSA 2023); rehabilitation on the VA/DoD 2025 CPG (moderate); trauma salvage-vs-amputation on strong prospective cohort (LEAP/NEJM 2002) but decision scores (MESS) have poor prospective predictive validity and must not be used alone; perioperative ERAS-for-amputation is a consensus/expert framework extrapolated from vascular/general ERAS; VTE prophylaxis in amputees is low-certainty (Cochrane found insufficient RCT evidence despite high VTE risk)
severity_cc_mcc_notes: Note the collapsed group spans two MDCs: DRG 474-476 = amputation (any level) for musculoskeletal/connective-tissue disorders (trauma, tumor, osteomyelitis); DRG 616-618 = amputation of LOWER LIMB for endocrine/nutritional/metabolic (predominantly diabetic, non-ischemic coding) - the scope note's 'upper-limb' label is inaccurate. Tiers: without CC/MCC (476/618) - typically planned single-stage distal amputation, floor care, LOS ~4d; with CC (475/617) - diabetes/moderate infection or blood loss, longer antibiotics, LOS ~6d; with MCC (474/616) - sepsis/septic shock, necrotizing infection, major hemorrhage, AKI or respiratory failure, staged guillotine-then-revision, ICU, LOS ~9-10d with higher mortality and reamputation. Diabetic (616-618) cohort carries the highest contralateral-limb and 1-year mortality risk
pathway_pearls: Get the level right ONCE - optimize perfusion (TcPO2/ABI), nutrition (albumin) and glycemia to heal at the most distal viable level and avoid reamputation, the single biggest driver of LOS, cost, readmission and mortality; source control FIRST in infection/sepsis - open guillotine amputation + antibiotics <1h + sepsis bundle, definitive closure later, never close over infection; salvage-vs-amputation is a shared multidisciplinary decision, NOT a MESS cutoff (LEAP showed equivalent 2-year outcomes and poor score validity - involve patient, ortho/vascular/plastics); front-load rehab and prosthetics - early PM&R/prosthetist, rigid removable dressing for transtibial, regional + multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia for phantom-pain prevention, and protect the contralateral limb in diabetics

scope_and_volume_notes: Amputation for musculoskeletal/connective-tissue disorders (474-476) and endocrine/upper-limb (616-618); trauma/infection/tumor etiology.

admission_criteria: Kidney transplant is an inherently inpatient surgical admission (never observation/ED-discharge); 100% inpatient. Two entry paths: (1) accepted deceased-donor organ offer with acceptable final crossmatch -> urgent call-in admission; (2) scheduled elective living-donor transplant. Must clear pre-op gate before OR: negative/acceptable CDC-AHG and flow-cytometry crossmatch (or completed desensitization for planned incompatible/high-DSA case), no active infection/sepsis, ABO-compatible (or ABOi protocol), cardiopulmonary fitness confirmed. Pre-op hemodialysis if K+ >5.5-6.0 mmol/L, volume overload/pulmonary edema, or severe uremia. Absolute holds to proceeding: active infection, acute cardiac/pulmonary decompensation, unacceptable positive crossmatch, positive pregnancy test.
risk_stratification: KDPI (Kidney Donor Profile Index 0-100%; >85% = extended-criteria/high-risk graft, higher DGF/primary-nonfunction risk) drives graft-function expectation and induction intensity; EPTS (Estimated Post-Transplant Survival 0-100%; <=20% = longevity-matched recipient) for candidate-organ matching; cPRA/PRA (calculated Panel-Reactive Antibody 0-100%; highly sensitized >80% and any preformed DSA -> lymphocyte-depleting induction + DSA surveillance); immunologic-risk tier (HIGH = repeat transplant, cPRA>80%, DSA+, Black recipient, young recipient/old donor, DGF-prone -> rATG; LOW -> basiliximab); DGF risk via online DGF risk calculator/Irish nomogram (cold ischemia time, dialysis vintage, KDPI, DCD) -> anticipate post-op dialysis and step case toward DRG 650/651; RCRI/perioperative cardiac risk for MACE.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with diff (baseline WBC/platelets before depleting induction and MMF; monitor daily for leukopenia/thrombocytopenia); BMP/CMP (K+, creatinine, BUN, HCO3 -> drives pre-op dialysis decision); Mg/Phos/Ca; PT/INR + aPTT; type & crossmatch (2 units leukoreduced CMV-safe PRBC); final donor-recipient crossmatch + DSA (Luminex single-antigen); HLA typing + ABO; viral serologies CMV IgG donor/recipient (sets CMV D+/R- vs R+ prophylaxis stratum), EBV IgG (PTLD risk), HIV, HBV/HCV; latent-TB screen; beta-hCG; UA/urine culture. Post-op: q4-6h creatinine + K+ and hourly urine output to detect DGF and hyperkalemia; tacrolimus trough from POD1-2; add BK and CMV PCR surveillance schedule.
initial_imaging_dx: Pre-op 12-lead ECG and CXR. Color-Doppler ultrasound of the transplant kidney within 24h (POD0-1) is the key study: confirms arterial inflow/venous outflow patency, measures resistive index, excludes renal artery/vein thrombosis, perinephric hematoma/collection, hydronephrosis or urine leak. If DGF or perfusion concern: repeat Doppler or MAG3 nuclear renogram to assess perfusion vs ATN vs obstruction. Suspected urine leak -> ultrasound +/- cystogram or fluid creatinine of drain output. Emergent Doppler for any acute anuria/graft-site pain/tenderness (rule out thrombosis -> OR).
time_critical_interventions: Minimize cold ischemia time: target CIT <18-24h for deceased-donor (DGF risk rises steeply beyond 18-24h; hypothermic machine perfusion reduces DGF vs static cold storage); keep vascular anastomosis (second warm ischemia) <30-45 min. First induction dose (basiliximab or rATG) and high-dose methylprednisolone timed to BEFORE graft reperfusion. Intraoperative goal-directed volume optimization to euvolemia/mild hypervolemia before clamp release to promote immediate function. Time-critical graft-threatening emergencies: vascular thrombosis (renal vein/artery) -> emergent Doppler and return to OR within hours; hyperacute rejection at reperfusion. Hyperkalemia with ECG changes -> immediate treatment/dialysis.
initial_management: Induction (KDIGO 1B, all recipients): IL-2 receptor antagonist basiliximab 20 mg IV day 0 + day 4 for LOW immunologic risk; lymphocyte-depleting rabbit antithymocyte globulin (rATG/Thymoglobulin 1.5 mg/kg/day x3-5 days, cumulative ~4.5-6 mg/kg) for HIGH immunologic risk or high DGF risk (alemtuzumab an alternative). Maintenance triple therapy: calcineurin inhibitor tacrolimus (target trough 8-10 ng/mL early) + antimetabolite mycophenolate mofetil 1 g BID (or mycophenolate sodium 720 mg BID) + corticosteroid (intra-op methylprednisolone 250-500 mg then prednisone taper); tacrolimus preferred over cyclosporine (ELITE-Symphony). Fluids: balanced crystalloid, replace urine output mL-for-mL + maintenance, keep euvolemic to protect perfusion. Transfusion restrictive: PRBC for Hgb <7 g/dL, leukoreduced/CMV-safe. Prophylaxis: TMP-SMX (PCP + UTI) 6-12 mo, valganciclovir CMV (risk-based), nystatin/clotrimazole antifungal; cefazolin surgical prophylaxis.
day1_milestones: POD0: transplant completed and graft reperfused; admit to transplant step-down/floor (ICU only if unstable). Hourly urine-output monitoring with replacement; assess immediate graft function (brisk diuresis) vs oliguria (DGF). First basiliximab/rATG dose given peri-op; initiate tacrolimus + MMF + steroid (tacrolimus POD0-1). Foley to gravity; ureteral stent placed intra-op. q4-6h K+/creatinine. Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (ERAS); early oral sips/fluids if tolerating. SCDs on; confirm hemostasis. Verify CMV/PCP prophylaxis ordered.
day2_milestones: POD1: Doppler ultrasound if not done POD0 (confirm perfusion/patency, RI). Draw tacrolimus trough and titrate to 8-10 ng/mL. Advance diet (ERAS early feeding); out of bed and ambulate. Begin weaning IV fluids as oral intake and graft function establish. Continue induction (basiliximab schedule, or daily rATG with CBC to catch leukopenia/thrombocytopenia). Trend urine output/creatinine; if oliguric or creatinine rising -> work up DGF, may initiate hemodialysis (shifts case to DRG 650/651). Transition to oral analgesia. Transplant pharmacist begins immunosuppression teaching.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+: continue tacrolimus titration to target trough; complete rATG course. Remove Foley ~POD3-5 (earlier per ERAS if low leak risk); leave ureteral stent for outpatient removal at 3-6 weeks. Wean IV fluids fully; confirm oral intake maintains graft perfusion/euvolemia. Daily creatinine trend for graft function vs rejection; DGF cases receive intermittent hemodialysis and stay longer (7-10+ days). Complete intensive immunosuppression self-care/adherence teaching; finalize CMV/PCP/antifungal prescriptions and outpatient lab schedule. Reach discharge readiness: stable/improving graft function, therapeutic stable tacrolimus, tolerating diet, ambulating, oral pain control, no active infection.
consults_multidisciplinary: Transplant surgery (primary) + transplant nephrology co-managing immunosuppression from POD0. Transplant clinical pharmacist (essential) for CNI dosing/level titration, drug-interaction screening, and discharge teaching. Transplant coordinator/nurse educator for medication and self-monitoring education. Renal dietitian (post-transplant diet, glucose, K+/phos). Social work/case management early for medication access/financial assistance, transportation, adherence support. PT/OT for mobility as needed. Endocrinology for perioperative hyperglycemia/post-transplant diabetes (PTDM). Infectious disease for complex serostatus (HIV, HCV, prior TB, D+/R- CMV). Interventional radiology on standby for lymphocele/collection drainage.
monitoring_level: Most uncomplicated recipients go to a transplant step-down/floor with q1h urine output and q4-6h labs; routine ICU is NOT required. ICU/telemetry reserved for hemodynamic instability, significant cardiac disease, large fluid shifts, high-risk ECD/DCD grafts, or intraoperative complications. Escalation triggers: hemodynamic instability; hyperkalemia with ECG changes; acute oliguria/anuria with rising K+/creatinine (emergent Doppler + surgical evaluation for thrombosis); falling Hgb/bleeding/hematoma; respiratory compromise; rATG cytokine-release reaction.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Nutrition: early oral intake POD0-1 with diet advancement per ERAS; transplant/renal-appropriate diet; strict glucose control for steroid-induced hyperglycemia (target <180 mg/dL, basal-bolus/sliding-scale insulin) and screen for PTDM. Mobility: early mobilization POD0-1, ambulate POD1. VTE: mechanical SCDs immediately + pharmacologic prophylaxis (LMWH or UFH) once surgical hemostasis confirmed, balancing wound/graft bleeding risk. Lines/catheters: remove Foley POD3-5, ureteral stent at 3-6 weeks outpatient (lowers UTI/leak), central/arterial lines as hemodynamically stable. Maintain euvolemia to protect graft perfusion (avoid hypovolemia and hypervolemia).
discharge_criteria: Stable or improving allograft function (declining/plateauing creatinine) OR established DGF with a defined outpatient dialysis plan; therapeutic and stable tacrolimus trough on a finalized regimen; tolerating oral diet and oral analgesia; afebrile with no active infection; ambulating independently; adequate urine output and euvolemia off IV fluids; wound healing appropriately without leak/collection; patient/caregiver has completed immunosuppression and self-monitoring education and can teach-back the regimen; prophylaxis prescriptions, outpatient labs, and early follow-up all arranged.
discharge_planning: Disposition home (rarely SNF). Meticulous medication reconciliation of complex regimen: tacrolimus + mycophenolate + prednisone taper, plus TMP-SMX, valganciclovir (risk-based), antifungal, PPI, antihypertensives, electrolyte repletion. Teach-back on immunosuppression adherence, key drug interactions (azole antifungals, diltiazem/verapamil, grapefruit raise tacrolimus), infection and rejection warning signs, and home BP/weight/glucose monitoring. Schedule transplant clinic follow-up within 3-7 days with labs 2-3x/week initially (tacrolimus level, creatinine, CBC, plus CMV and BK PCR surveillance). Arrange local lab draw site, pharmacy/medication access and financial coverage, transportation, ureteral stent removal at 3-6 weeks, and provide 24/7 transplant contact line.
expected_los: MS-DRG 652 GMLOS benchmark approximately 5 days (national mean roughly 6-7 days once DGF cases are included). Living-donor recipients typically 3-4 days; uncomplicated deceased-donor 4-7 days; DGF requiring hemodialysis extends to 7-10+ days (and reclassifies to DRG 650/651).
target_los: 3-4 days for uncomplicated living-donor and immediate-function deceased-donor recipients under a full ERAS pathway (ERAS transplant studies consistently show reduced LOS without increased readmission).
quality_metrics: SRTR/OPTN program outcomes: risk-adjusted 1-year patient and graft survival, DGF rate, acute-rejection rate (observed vs expected), cold ischemia time. CMS/registry process-outcome measures: 30-day all-cause readmission (high in transplant, ~18-30%), surgical site infection (SSI), post-op VTE, catheter-associated UTI, perioperative glucose control. ERAS compliance bundle: pre-op carbohydrate/euvolemia, opioid-sparing multimodal analgesia, early feeding, early mobilization, early catheter removal, LOS. Discharge-on-immunosuppression and CMV/PCP prophylaxis ordered rates.
common_complications: Delayed graft function (deceased-donor ~20-40%; mitigate via CIT minimization, machine perfusion, euvolemia, avoiding nephrotoxins); acute rejection (mitigate via risk-based induction + therapeutic CNI trough + adherence); vascular thrombosis of renal artery/vein (graft-threatening; mitigate via early Doppler + prompt re-exploration); urine leak/ureteral obstruction (mitigate via intra-op stent); lymphocele; wound infection/dehiscence; bleeding/perinephric hematoma; hyperkalemia; CMV and BK viremia (mitigate via prophylaxis + PCR surveillance, immunosuppression reduction); post-transplant hyperglycemia/PTDM; induction/MMF/valganciclovir-related leukopenia.
readmission_drivers: Volume depletion/dehydration causing AKI on the graft is the leading driver (prevent via discharge euvolemia teaching, close labs, low threshold for IV-fluid clinic visits); infections - UTI, CMV, BK, wound, PCP (prevent via prophylaxis, timely stent removal, PCR surveillance); rejection or tacrolimus level out of therapeutic range (prevent via pharmacist-led teaching + 2-3x/week monitoring); electrolyte/metabolic derangements (hyperkalemia, hypomagnesemia); surgical complications (lymphocele, urine leak); MMF GI intolerance/dehydration. Core prevention levers: early 3-7 day clinic follow-up, frequent labs, dedicated transplant pharmacist, teach-back adherence education, 24/7 access line.
guideline_source: KDIGO 2009 Clinical Practice Guideline for the Care of Kidney Transplant Recipients (primary post-transplant management - immunosuppression, prophylaxis, monitoring); KDIGO 2020 Clinical Practice Guideline on the Evaluation and Management of Candidates for Kidney Transplantation (candidacy/pre-op, crossmatch, cardiac); ERAS Society-aligned recommendations for renal transplantation (Br J Surg 2022/2023, GRADE-based perioperative bundle); AST Infectious Diseases Community of Practice guidelines (CMV 2019; general antimicrobial prophylaxis); OPTN/UNOS allocation policy.
key_citations: KDIGO 2009 Care of Kidney Transplant Recipients, Am J Transplant 2009;9 Suppl 3:S1-155 (PMID 19845597, doi:10.1111/j.1600-6143.2009.02834.x); KDIGO 2020 Candidate Evaluation, Transplantation 2020;104(4S1):S11-S103 (PMID 32301874, doi:10.1097/TP.0000000000003136); ELITE-Symphony - Ekberg, NEJM 2007;357:2562-75 (PMID 18094377, doi:10.1056/NEJMoa067411); Brennan rATG vs basiliximab, NEJM 2006;355:1967-77 (PMID 17093248, doi:10.1056/NEJMoa060068); Alloway/Brennan rATG pooled analysis, Am J Transplant 2019;19:2252-61 (PMID 30838775, doi:10.1111/ajt.15342); ERAS renal transplantation recommendations - Tan, Br J Surg 2023;110(1):57-59 (PMID 36168725, doi:10.1093/bjs/znac325); AST CMV guideline - Razonable & Humar, Clin Transplant 2019;33:e13512 (PMID 30817026, doi:10.1111/ctr.13512). Citations verified via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Moderate-to-Strong (mixed). Immunosuppression backbone (tacrolimus + mycophenolate + steroid with risk-based induction) and infection prophylaxis are STRONG - KDIGO Class 1/GRADE 1B recommendations supported by multiple RCTs (ELITE-Symphony, Brennan rATG-vs-basiliximab). Perioperative ERAS elements in transplant are MODERATE/consensus - largely extrapolated from other surgical specialties with limited transplant-specific RCTs (GRADE moderate-low). Overall: strong for pharmacologic/immunologic core, moderate/expert-consensus for ERAS perioperative timing and LOS targets.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MS-DRG 652 (Kidney Transplant) is the BASE, non-severity-split DRG - CMS does not tier it into with/without CC/MCC. The severity split lives in a sibling pair: if the recipient requires HEMODIALYSIS during the admission (i.e., DGF, the dominant severity driver), the case reclassifies OUT of 652 into DRG 650 (Kidney Transplant with Hemodialysis WITH MCC) or DRG 651 (with Hemodialysis WITHOUT MCC) - created FY2021 IPPS. So DGF/dialysis need is the pivotal LOS/cost and DRG-migration lever. Other within-group severity escalators (may add MCC and drive 650): high immunologic risk requiring rATG (marrow suppression, longer monitoring), ECD/DCD grafts, perioperative cardiac or infectious complications, and re-operation for vascular/urologic complications. Pancreas-alone and combined kidney-pancreas transplants are separate DRGs, not 650/651/652.
pathway_pearls: 1) Protect the graft with euvolemia and CIT minimization - hourly urine-output-matched fluids, avoid hypovolemia/nephrotoxins, and shorten cold ischemia (machine perfusion) to cut DGF, the biggest LOS and DRG-650/651 driver. 2) Match induction to immunologic risk - basiliximab for low-risk, rATG for high-risk/high-DSA/high-DGF - and dose tacrolimus to an 8-10 ng/mL trough early. 3) Front-load a transplant pharmacist and teach-back education plus locked-in 3-7 day follow-up with 2-3x/week labs to prevent the #1 readmission cause (dehydration/AKI) and catch CMV/BK/rejection early. 4) Never remove the ureteral stent inpatient - leave for 3-6 week outpatient removal to reduce UTI and urine leak.

scope_and_volume_notes: Single DRG 652; deceased/living-donor renal transplant. ~25K/year and rising.
