admission_criteria: Admit (not ED-discharge/observation) for odontogenic deep-space/fascial infection with any airway threat: Ludwig angina (bilateral submandibular + sublingual + submental cellulitis), floor-of-mouth elevation/tongue displacement, trismus with interincisal opening <20-25 mm, dysphagia/odynophagia, drooling/inability to manage secretions, muffled "hot-potato" voice, or stridor; systemic toxicity (T >=38.5C, tachycardia, SIRS/sepsis, qSOFA >=2); CT-confirmed multi-space involvement or drainable abscess requiring OR incision-and-drainage; inability to tolerate PO for hydration/antibiotics or failed outpatient oral antibiotics; immunocompromise or poorly controlled diabetes/DKA; maxillofacial fractures needing operative fixation or with airway/orbital/CSF concern. ED-discharge/obs appropriate for localized vestibular/periapical or single-space abscess, no airway or systemic signs, tolerating PO -> bedside/dental I&D + extraction + oral antibiotics + 24-48h dental follow-up.
risk_stratification: Airway markers drive disposition: interincisal opening <20 mm, floor-of-mouth elevation, tongue protrusion, stridor -> ICU + secured airway. Sepsis: qSOFA >=2 or SIRS with NEWS2 >=5-7 -> escalate, sepsis bundle. Descending necrotizing mediastinitis (DNM) risk (Kimura 2020): age >=55, neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR) >=13, CRP >=30 mg/dL -> mandatory chest CT + thoracic surgery alert. Anatomic burden (Flynn): number of involved spaces predicts LOS and reoperation; >=2-3 spaces or masticator/lateral-pharyngeal/retropharyngeal involvement = severe. Complication predictors (Sakarya 2015): comorbid anemia, Ludwig angina, retropharyngeal involvement. Host: diabetes/HbA1c, immunosuppression worsen outcomes and broaden coverage; ASA class for operative risk.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential (leukocytosis; calculate NLR — >=13 flags DNM risk); CMP (glucose, renal function for antibiotic dosing); CRP (>=30 mg/dL flags mediastinal extension) +/- procalcitonin; lactate (>=2 mmol/L suggests hypoperfusion/sepsis); 2 sets blood cultures BEFORE antibiotics if septic/toxic; HbA1c and finger-stick glucose/ketones in diabetics (rule out DKA); coagulation panel + type-and-screen pre-op; ABG if respiratory compromise; abscess Gram stain with aerobic AND anaerobic cultures obtained at I&D to guide de-escalation (Flynn: ~19% penicillin resistance, 21% penicillin therapeutic failure); HIV/immune workup if recurrent or atypical.
initial_imaging_dx: Contrast-enhanced CT of the neck (IV contrast) is the study of choice — obtain urgently (within hours) to map fascial spaces, distinguish drainable abscess from phlegmon, assess airway caliber and gas/necrosis; EXTEND CT to the chest when any mediastinal sign or Kimura high-risk (age>=55/NLR>=13/CRP>=30) to exclude descending necrotizing mediastinitis. Panoramic radiograph (OPG)/CBCT for odontogenic source and facial fractures; point-of-care ultrasound for superficial fluctuance; flexible fiberoptic nasolaryngoscopy to grade supraglottic/pharyngeal edema and plan intubation. Never delay definitive airway control to obtain imaging in a deteriorating airway.
time_critical_interventions: AIRWAY FIRST — continuous reassessment; secure a definitive airway emergently for stridor/hypoxia/rapid progression via awake flexible fiberoptic nasotracheal intubation (preferred) with a prepared double-setup surgical airway (awake tracheostomy/cricothyrotomy); avoid blind nasotracheal intubation and paralysis. Empiric IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour (immediately after cultures) — do not wait for the OR. Urgent surgical source control: incision-and-drainage of all involved spaces PLUS removal of the odontogenic source (extraction), same-day/within hours for airway-threatening or septic cases. If septic shock, Surviving Sepsis 2021 bundle: lactate, cultures, antibiotics <=1h, 30 mL/kg crystalloid, vasopressors to MAP >=65. Immediate chest CT + cardiothoracic consult if mediastinitis suspected.
initial_management: Empiric IV antibiotics covering oral aerobes + anaerobes: FIRST-LINE ampicillin-sulbactam 3 g IV q6h (or penicillin G + metronidazole 500 mg IV q8h); beta-lactam allergy: clindamycin 600-900 mg IV q8h (note rising resistance and C. difficile risk) or cephalosporin + metronidazole. Immunocompromised/healthcare-associated: escalate to piperacillin-tazobactam, cefepime + metronidazole, or meropenem for gram-negative coverage; add vancomycin or linezolid when MRSA risk (diabetes, recent hospitalization, IVDU); cover Klebsiella in diabetics. IV crystalloid resuscitation while NPO; multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia; antipyretics; insulin for glucose 140-180 mg/dL. Dexamethasone 8-10 mg IV may reduce airway edema (weak, case-level evidence — Tami 2020). SURGERY IS DEFINITIVE: I&D + extraction; send cultures and de-escalate at 48-72h.
day1_milestones: POD0 (admission/operative day): airway secured or an explicit airway plan documented with appropriate monitored/ICU bed; empiric IV antibiotics started <=1h; contrast CT neck (+/- chest) completed; to OR for I&D of ALL involved spaces + extraction of the offending tooth/source with drain placement and intraoperative aerobic/anaerobic cultures; resuscitate (fluids, glycemic control); baseline labs including NLR/CRP/lactate; sepsis bundle if septic; VTE and stress-ulcer prophylaxis initiated; NPO with NG or IV nutrition plan; activate consults (OMFS/ENT, anesthesia/difficult-airway, ID, critical care).
day2_milestones: POD1: reassess airway every shift — plan extubation only when cuff-leak present and edema resolving; trend WBC/CRP/NLR and temperature for treatment response; record drain outputs and inspect wounds; begin culture-directed antibiotic de-escalation as sensitivities return (48-72h); SLP swallow evaluation and advance NPO toward clear liquids/soft as secretions and swallowing improve; early mobilization out of bed; continue glucose control; repeat imaging only if not improving or new findings; assess adequacy of source control and decide on return-to-OR if progression.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ to discharge readiness: transition to oral antibiotics once afebrile >=24-48h, tolerating PO, and WBC/CRP downtrending with resolving swelling/trismus; remove drains when output minimal; extubate/decannulate per daily airway reassessment; continue diet advancement and mobility; complete an individualized total 7-14 day antibiotic course (IV + PO) titrated to source-control adequacy; finalize definitive dental/OMFS plan (remaining extractions, fracture fixation); discharge when objective criteria met.
consults_multidisciplinary: Oral-maxillofacial surgery and/or otolaryngology (ENT) emergently as primary surgical source-control service; anesthesiology/difficult-airway team for the airway plan; infectious diseases and antimicrobial stewardship for agent selection and de-escalation; critical care/ICU for airway/sepsis; cardiothoracic surgery if descending mediastinitis; endocrinology for uncontrolled diabetes/DKA; general dentistry for definitive dental care; speech-language pathology (swallow) before diet advancement; clinical nutrition for prolonged NPO/aspiration risk; pharmacy for renal dosing/stewardship; care management and social work for placement, guaranteed dental follow-up and substance-use resources; PT/OT if deconditioned.
monitoring_level: ICU for a secured or actively threatened airway (post-intubation/tracheostomy), septic shock, mediastinitis, or rapidly progressive multi-space infection; step-down/telemetry for high airway-risk patients under close observation not yet intubated; floor for a drained single/limited-space infection with a stable airway and systemically well patient. Escalation triggers: increasing stridor/work of breathing or hypoxia, rising NLR/CRP, hemodynamic instability, expanding swelling/trismus, or new mediastinal/chest signs -> immediate OR re-exploration and ICU transfer.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: NPO pre-op and while the airway is threatened; SLP swallow eval, then advance liquids -> soft/mechanical-soft as secretions and swallowing allow; NG feeding or short-course parenteral nutrition if NPO is prolonged. VTE prophylaxis: mechanical (SCDs) plus pharmacologic LMWH/heparin per Caprini once hemostasis secure (hold pre-op/active bleeding). Early mobilization from POD1; glucose target 140-180 mg/dL; remove drains, central lines, and urinary catheters as soon as not needed to reduce CLABSI/CAUTI; chlorhexidine oral hygiene.
discharge_criteria: Airway definitively safe (successfully extubated/decannulated or stable and protected); afebrile >=24h; downtrending WBC/CRP with clinical resolution of swelling and improving trismus; adequate source control confirmed (all spaces drained, offending tooth removed, minimal drain output); tolerating oral intake/hydration AND an oral antibiotic regimen; pain controlled on oral analgesia; glucose controlled; and reliable OMFS/dental follow-up arranged.
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home; SNF/rehab if deconditioned or tracheostomy-dependent. Medication reconciliation with the full oral antibiotic course (total 7-14 days) and analgesia; schedule OMFS/ENT wound check and dental follow-up within 1-2 weeks plus completion of remaining dental treatment/extractions; teach-back education on return precautions (airway distress, recurrent swelling, fever, worsening trismus/dysphagia), oral hygiene, glycemic control, and tobacco/alcohol cessation; establish a dental home to prevent recurrence; transitions-of-care summary transmitted to PCP and dentist.
expected_los: DRG-tiered GMLOS (approximate): MS-DRG 159 (no CC/MCC) ~2.3-2.7 days; 158 (with CC) ~3.3-3.7 days; 157 (with MCC) ~4.5-5 days. Prospective severe-odontogenic-infection cohort (Flynn 2006) mean 5.1 +/- 3.0 days; complicated deep-neck infection/mediastinitis 10-14+ days. Blended typical ~3-5 days.
target_los: Uncomplicated single/limited-space drained infection: 2-3 days; with a CC: 3-4 days; airway-secured/ICU or MCC tier: optimized 4-6 days using a daily airway-reassessment/early-extubation protocol and early mediastinitis exclusion to prevent prolongation.
quality_metrics: Time-to-empiric-antibiotic <=1h; decision-to-OR interval for source control; SEP-1 bundle compliance when septic; appropriate empiric-selection and 48-72h de-escalation rate (antimicrobial stewardship); airway adverse-event and unplanned reintubation rate; unplanned ICU transfer; return-to-OR rate; VTE, CLABSI/CAUTI, and C. difficile rates; 30-day readmission and ED revisit; in-hospital mortality; glycemic control; and documented dental source control with follow-up.
common_complications: Airway obstruction/failed airway (leading cause of death) — mitigated by proactive early awake fiberoptic intubation with surgical-airway backup; descending necrotizing mediastinitis (Kimura predictors age>=55/NLR>=13/CRP>=30) — mitigated by chest CT and early thoracic surgery; aspiration pneumonia; septic shock; cervical necrotizing fasciitis; internal jugular septic thrombophlebitis (Lemierre); carotid sheath erosion; inadequate source control requiring reoperation — mitigated by draining ALL spaces + extraction; C. difficile from broad antibiotics — mitigated by stewardship/de-escalation; hyperglycemia/DKA — mitigated by insulin protocols.
readmission_drivers: Inadequate/incomplete source control (retained tooth, undrained space); premature antibiotic discontinuation or nonadherence; recurrent odontogenic infection from no dental home/untreated caries; uncontrolled diabetes; active substance use; missed follow-up; wound breakdown or recurrent abscess. Prevention levers: complete extraction/definitive dental care before or promptly after discharge; full antibiotic course with clear instructions; glycemic optimization; guaranteed dental/OMFS follow-up within 1-2 weeks; teach-back of airway/return precautions.
guideline_source: AAOMS Parameters of Care 2023 (ParCare, 7th Edition) — Clinical Practice Guidelines for Oral and Maxillofacial Surgery (J Oral Maxillofac Surg 2023 supplement), covering management of head-and-neck/odontogenic infections and dentoalveolar/maxillofacial surgery; supplemented by IDSA antimicrobial-therapy principles, the Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2021 (for septic presentations), and the Difficult Airway Society (DAS 2015) airway-management framework. No single RCT-based society guideline exists specifically for Ludwig angina; management rests on society consensus plus high-quality observational evidence.
key_citations: AAOMS Parameters of Care, 7th ed., 2023 (JOMS clinical practice guidelines). Flynn TR et al. Severe odontogenic infections, part 1: prospective report. J Oral Maxillofac Surg 2006;64(7):1093-103 (PMID 16781343). Flynn TR et al. Severe odontogenic infections, part 2: prospective outcomes study. J Oral Maxillofac Surg 2006;64(7):1104-13 (PMID 16781344). Kimura A et al. Clinical predictors of descending necrotizing mediastinitis after deep neck infections. Laryngoscope 2020;130(11):E567-E572 (PMID 31747058). Sakarya EU et al. Clinical features of deep neck infection: analysis of 77 patients. KBB 2015;25(2):102-8 (PMID 25935062). Tami A et al. Ludwig's angina and steroid use: a narrative review. Am J Otolaryngol 2020;41(3):102411 (PMID 32035654). Al Harbi M et al. Anesthetic management of advanced-stage Ludwig's angina (airway). Middle East J Anaesthesiol 2016 (PMID 29939705). Citations retrieved/verified via PubMed.
evidence_grade: Moderate. Anchored by a society consensus clinical-practice guideline (AAOMS ParCare 2023) plus prospective observational cohorts (Flynn 2006) and multiple retrospective series (Sakarya, Kimura); airway-first management and empiric anaerobe-covering antibiotics are strongly and consistently endorsed by consensus but not RCT-proven, and steroid/dexamethasone use rests on case series/expert opinion (evidence explicitly weak). No large randomized trials exist for Ludwig angina — several elements are extrapolated from observational data and expert consensus.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: MS-DRG 159 (no CC/MCC): single/limited-space infection or elective dentoalveolar/maxillofacial procedure, floor care, LOS ~2-3d. MS-DRG 158 (CC): moderate multi-space infection, controlled diabetes, dehydration/AKI, or anemia -> telemetry/step-down, LOS ~3-4d. MS-DRG 157 (MCC): airway compromise requiring intubation/tracheostomy, sepsis/septic shock, descending necrotizing mediastinitis, cervical necrotizing fasciitis, or aspiration pneumonia -> ICU, critical-care/thoracic involvement, higher reoperation, LOS 5-14+ days with markedly higher mortality. Across the collapsed group, severity is driven chiefly by number of spaces involved, airway status, and mediastinal extension.
pathway_pearls: 1) Airway is the priority — reassess continuously and secure it EARLY (awake flexible fiberoptic nasotracheal intubation with a surgical-airway backup) before it becomes emergent; stridor and cyanosis are late, ominous signs. 2) Antibiotics do not replace surgery — definitive care is prompt incision-and-drainage of ALL involved spaces PLUS removal of the odontogenic source (extraction); culture and de-escalate. 3) Screen for descending mediastinitis in high-risk patients (age>=55, NLR>=13, CRP>=30 mg/dL) — get a chest CT and involve thoracic surgery early. 4) Fix the root cause and the diabetes — complete dental source control, glycemic optimization, and a guaranteed dental home/follow-up are the levers that prevent bounce-backs.

admission_criteria: ADMIT (IV therapy/OR): postseptal ('true' orbital) cellulitis = Chandler II-V (proptosis, painful/restricted extraocular movement, diplopia, chemosis, vision change, RAPD); any subperiosteal or orbital abscess on CT; endophthalmitis of any source (post-cataract/post-injection/post-op/bleb/traumatic/endogenous); open-globe or ruptured globe or intraocular foreign body (NPO for repair); orbital compartment syndrome / retrobulbar hemorrhage with IOP >40 mmHg + vision threat; orbital fracture with muscle entrapment/oculocardiac reflex (esp. pediatric 'white-eyed' blowout); plus systemic toxicity/sepsis, immunocompromise, intracranial or cavernous-sinus extension, failed outpatient PO therapy, or inability to tolerate/adhere to PO. ED DISCHARGE or OBSERVATION: mild preseptal cellulitis (Chandler I, immunocompetent, reliable, no proptosis/EOM limitation) on PO antibiotics with 24-48h recheck; simple corneal abrasion; uncomplicated conjunctivitis
risk_stratification: Chandler classification of orbital infection (I preseptal, II orbital cellulitis, III subperiosteal abscess, IV orbital abscess, V cavernous sinus thrombosis): stage >=III triggers contrast CT + surgical consideration, IV-V triggers drainage/ICU. Subperiosteal-abscess surgical-failure predictors: abscess width >4 mm or volume >=0.65 mL, CRP >60 mg/L, WBC >15,600/uL, age >9 yr, non-medial/superior location, ophthalmoplegia, exophthalmos -> favor drainage. Endophthalmitis EVS rule: presenting vision light-perception-only -> immediate pars plana vitrectomy; hand-motion or better -> vitreous tap + intravitreal antibiotics. Open globe: Ocular Trauma Score (OTS; from initial VA, rupture, endophthalmitis, retinal detachment, RAPD) predicts 6-mo VA; Zone of injury (I cornea, II limbus/anterior sclera <=5 mm, III posterior >5 mm) — Zone III worst. Orbital compartment syndrome: IOP >40 mmHg + proptosis + APD -> emergent canthotomy
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential (leukocytosis); CRP and ESR (baseline severity + serial response; CRP >60 mg/L flags surgery); blood cultures x2 before antibiotics (mandatory in endogenous endophthalmitis, sepsis, systemic toxicity); BMP; coagulation panel/type-and-screen and preop labs if OR-bound; wound/discharge and intraoperative cultures; vitreous tap for Gram stain/culture (+/- PCR) in endophthalmitis; glucose/HbA1c (diabetes = risk factor); MRSA nares swab. Endogenous endophthalmitis: blood + urine cultures, transthoracic/transesophageal echocardiogram for endocarditis, serum beta-D-glucan if fungemia suspected, ID-directed source workup
initial_imaging_dx: Contrast CT orbits + paranasal sinuses + brain (thin/coronal cuts) within hours for suspected postseptal cellulitis/abscess or orbital trauma — defines Chandler stage, abscess size, sinus source, intracranial/cavernous extension. Non-contrast thin-cut CT orbit (axial+coronal) for open globe/intraocular foreign body; MRI contraindicated until metallic FB excluded. Slit-lamp Seidel test for occult globe rupture. B-scan ultrasound for vitritis in endophthalmitis (NEVER apply pressure on a suspected open globe). MRI/MRV if cavernous sinus thrombosis or intracranial extension suspected. Formal ophthalmology exam: best-corrected VA, IOP, RAPD, extraocular motility, proptosis (Hertel exophthalmometry), dilated fundus
time_critical_interventions: Endophthalmitis = same-hour emergency: vitreous tap + intravitreal antibiotics without delay; immediate pars plana vitrectomy if presenting vision is light-perception-only (EVS). Orbital compartment syndrome / retrobulbar hemorrhage with IOP >40 mmHg + vision loss: bedside lateral canthotomy + inferior cantholysis within minutes (do NOT wait for imaging). Open globe: rigid eye shield immediately, NPO, IV antibiotics + tetanus + antiemetics on arrival, primary surgical repair within 24h to lower endophthalmitis risk. Orbital cellulitis: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1h of diagnosis; drain abscess within ~24h if worsening or failing medical therapy. Concurrent chemical burn: immediate copious irrigation to neutral pH
initial_management: Orbital cellulitis empiric IV: vancomycin (MRSA) + third-gen cephalosporin (ceftriaxone or cefotaxime) OR ampicillin-sulbactam; ADD metronidazole for intracranial extension/chronic sinus/anaerobic risk; adjunct nasal decongestant/intranasal steroid, consider systemic corticosteroid once antibiotics started. Endophthalmitis intravitreal: vancomycin 1 mg/0.1 mL (Gram-positive) + ceftazidime 2.25 mg/0.1 mL (Gram-negative); amikacin 0.4 mg/0.1 mL if beta-lactam allergy; +/- intravitreal dexamethasone; fortified topical antibiotics. Per EVS, systemic antibiotics add NO benefit in post-cataract endophthalmitis (omit) but ARE used for trauma/endogenous/bleb-associated disease. Endogenous Candida: systemic fluconazole or voriconazole +/- echinocandin + intravitreal amphotericin B or voriconazole; vitrectomy if severe. Open globe: IV vancomycin + ceftazidime (or systemic fluoroquinolone) prophylaxis, tetanus, antiemetics, analgesia, avoid IOP-raising maneuvers, primary closure. Retrobulbar hemorrhage: canthotomy/cantholysis + IOP-lowering (topical, acetazolamide, mannitol)
day1_milestones: POD0/Day1: perform the definitive intervention — vitreous tap-and-inject or vitrectomy (endophthalmitis), primary globe repair (open globe), abscess drainage +/- functional endoscopic sinus surgery (orbital abscess), or lateral canthotomy/cantholysis (orbital compartment syndrome); obtain cultures at or before intervention. Start empiric IV antibiotics. Document baseline VA, IOP, RAPD, extraocular motility, proptosis; establish ophthalmology co-management. Pain and nausea control; rigid shield maintained for open globe; head-of-bed elevation
day2_milestones: POD1/Day2: reassess VA, proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, pain, fever curve, and CRP trend. Orbital cellulitis should show clinical improvement within 24-48h — if worsening or static, repeat contrast CT and escalate to surgical drainage/ENT. Narrow antibiotics to culture sensitivities (de-escalate). Endophthalmitis: assess response; repeat tap-and-inject at 48-72h if worsening. Begin discharge/transition planning
day3plus_milestones: POD2+ to discharge: once afebrile with improving proptosis/EOM/vision and down-trending CRP, transition IV to oral antibiotics (total orbital-cellulitis course ~2-3 weeks; longer with abscess or bony involvement). Open globe: serial monitoring for endophthalmitis, retinal detachment, and sympathetic ophthalmia; schedule staged vitreoretinal surgery if indicated. Confirm discharge readiness and arrange outpatient ophthalmology/retina/oculoplastics/ENT/ID follow-up
consults_multidisciplinary: Ophthalmology mandatory and immediate — vitreoretinal/retina for endophthalmitis and posterior-segment open-globe injury; oculoplastics/orbit for abscess drainage and orbital trauma. ENT/otolaryngology for sinus source control (FESS) in orbital cellulitis. Infectious disease for antibiotic selection and endogenous-endophthalmitis source (endocarditis/candidemia). Neurosurgery for intracranial extension or cavernous sinus thrombosis. Pediatric hospitalist co-management for pediatric orbital cellulitis. Pharmacy for vancomycin dosing/levels and antimicrobial stewardship; care management/social work for follow-up and adherence
monitoring_level: Floor/ward with frequent (q1-4h) neuro-ophthalmic checks (vision, pupil/RAPD, proptosis, extraocular motility) for most. Step-down or ICU for cavernous sinus thrombosis, intracranial extension, sepsis/septic shock, endogenous endophthalmitis with endocarditis/systemic infection, or postoperative airway concern. Escalation triggers: worsening or loss of vision, expanding proptosis, new APD, new cranial neuropathy, altered mental status, or hemodynamic instability
nutrition_mobility_vte: Regular diet once stable; NPO pre-op for open-globe/OR cases. Early mobilization; head-of-bed elevation to reduce orbital edema; strict avoidance of Valsalva/bending/eye pressure in open globe. VTE prophylaxis by risk — mechanical +/- pharmacologic (LMWH) for surgical/immobile patients (most stays are short and low-risk). Glucose control in diabetics (independent risk factor). Early removal of IV lines and urinary catheters
discharge_criteria: Afebrile >=24h with clear clinical improvement (decreasing proptosis, stable/improving vision, improving extraocular motility, controlled pain); tolerating oral antibiotics with down-trending CRP/WBC; stable/controlled IOP; no signs of intracranial or systemic spread; post-op globe stable with shield and IOP controlled; source controlled (abscess drained, sinusitis addressed); reliable follow-up and adherence assured
discharge_planning: Complete culture-directed oral antibiotic course to a total of ~2-3+ weeks (antifungal course of weeks for fungal disease); medication reconciliation. Eye-shield/protection instructions and explicit return precautions (worsening vision, increasing pain/proptosis, fever). Follow-up: ophthalmology within 1-3 days (next-day for post-op or endophthalmitis), plus retina/oculoplastics/ENT/ID as indicated. Patient education on adherence, hand hygiene, no eye rubbing/pressure, protective eyewear. Address the underlying driver — sinusitis management, glycemic control, IV drug use/endocarditis for endogenous cases
expected_los: GMLOS ~3-4 days for the admitted infectious/traumatic subset (orbital cellulitis ~3-5 days; endophthalmitis ~2-4 days; open-globe repair ~1-3 days). Non-CC/MCC medical eye disorders (DRG 123/125) often 1-2 days
target_los: 2-3 days with same-hour intervention, early source control, and prompt IV-to-PO antibiotic switch once afebrile and improving; 1-2 days for uncomplicated post-op globe or non-CC cases
quality_metrics: Door-to-intravitreal-antibiotic time in endophthalmitis; open-globe repair within 24h; time to lateral canthotomy in orbital compartment syndrome; culture obtained before antibiotics; guideline-concordant empiric antibiotic selection and timely IV-to-PO switch (antimicrobial stewardship); documented VA/IOP/RAPD at admission and discharge; unplanned reoperation/return to OR; post-procedure endophthalmitis rate; 30-day readmission; length of stay; hospital-acquired complications (few eye-specific CMS core measures exist — general surgical and readmission measures apply)
common_complications: Permanent vision loss/blindness; progression or relapse of endophthalmitis; retinal detachment. Orbital cellulitis: abscess re-accumulation, cavernous sinus thrombosis, intracranial abscess/meningitis, optic neuropathy, central retinal artery occlusion, sepsis. Open globe: post-traumatic endophthalmitis, sympathetic ophthalmia, phthisis bulbi, enucleation. Systemic: vancomycin/aminoglycoside nephrotoxicity, C. difficile. Mitigation: same-hour intervention, serial vision/pupil/proptosis exams, culture-directed therapy, adequate source control, stewardship
readmission_drivers: Inadequate source control (undrained or re-accumulated abscess, untreated sinusitis); recurrent or progressive infection; premature IV-to-PO switch or antibiotic non-adherence; missed endophthalmitis or need for staged vitreoretinal surgery after open globe; uncontrolled diabetes/immunosuppression. Prevention levers: adequate antibiotic duration, ENT sinus source control, ID co-management, next-day/close ophthalmology follow-up, clear return precautions, and patient education
guideline_source: American Academy of Ophthalmology (AAO) Preferred Practice Patterns — Bacterial Keratitis PPP 2024 and Cataract/Retina PPPs; Endophthalmitis Vitrectomy Study (EVS), Arch Ophthalmol 1995 (Class I RCT for post-cataract endophthalmitis); AAO Clinical Statement — Screening for Endogenous Candida Endophthalmitis 2022; IDSA Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Candidiasis 2016; Chandler classification of orbital infection (1970); Ocular Trauma Score (Kuhn, 2002). Note: much orbital-cellulitis and open-globe management is observational/expert-consensus rather than RCT-based
key_citations: Endophthalmitis Vitrectomy Study Group. Results of the EVS: a randomized trial of immediate vitrectomy and of IV antibiotics for postoperative bacterial endophthalmitis. Arch Ophthalmol 1995;113(12):1479-96 (PMID 7487614). Chandler JR, et al. The pathogenesis of orbital complications in acute sinusitis. Laryngoscope 1970. Kuhn F, et al. The Ocular Trauma Score (OTS). Ophthalmol Clin North Am 2002. AAO Bacterial Keratitis Preferred Practice Pattern. Ophthalmology 2024 (PMID 38349295). Moreddu E, et al. Prognostic factors of pediatric orbital subperiosteal abscess. J Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2025 (DOI 10.1177/19160216251349444). Alsughayer L, et al. Subperiosteal abscess volume as objective surgical indication. Eur Arch Otorhinolaryngol 2024 (DOI 10.1007/s00405-024-08894-5). Pappas PG, et al. IDSA Candidiasis Guideline. Clin Infect Dis 2016. (Citations retrieved/verified via PubMed)
evidence_grade: Moderate overall. Anchored by one landmark Class I RCT (EVS 1995) whose findings are specific to post-cataract endophthalmitis; orbital cellulitis/subperiosteal-abscess thresholds and open-globe/orbital-trauma timing rest on observational cohorts and expert consensus (AAO PPP, society statements). Strong for endophthalmitis tap-and-inject and light-perception-only vitrectomy; Moderate-to-Consensus for surgical-drainage thresholds and prophylactic-antibiotic practice
severity_cc_mcc_notes: The group collapses paired DRGs splitting on CC/MCC (113/114 orbital procedures, 116/117 intraocular procedures, 121/122 acute major eye infection, 124/125 other eye disorders) plus neuro-eye 123. Genuine inpatients almost always carry a CC/MCC — that comorbid burden is usually why they are admitted rather than treated outpatient. MCC drivers (intracranial abscess, cavernous sinus thrombosis, sepsis, endocarditis, meningitis) push to ICU with LOS 7-14+ days and high complication rates. CC (orbital abscess, diabetes, significant comorbidity) lengthens floor stay to ~4-6 days. Without CC/MCC, stays are short (1-2 days), observation-like; DRG 123/125 medical eye disorders are generally the shortest
pathway_pearls: 1) 'Tap and inject NOW' — endophthalmitis is a same-hour emergency (intravitreal vancomycin 1 mg + ceftazidime 2.25 mg); immediate vitrectomy changes outcome only for light-perception-only vision (EVS), and systemic antibiotics add nothing in post-cataract cases. 2) Separate preseptal (Chandler I -> outpatient PO) from postseptal/orbital disease (proptosis, painful/limited EOM, vision change, RAPD -> admit, contrast CT, IV vancomycin + ceftriaxone); drain if abscess >4 mm or no improvement at 24-48h, and get ENT for the sinus source. 3) Open globe = shield, don't poke: rigid shield, NPO, IV antibiotics + tetanus + antiemetics, no IOP-raising maneuvers or B-scan pressure, repair <24h. 4) Orbital compartment syndrome is a bedside diagnosis (IOP >40 + proptosis + APD + pain) -> immediate lateral canthotomy/cantholysis; never wait for imaging

scope_and_volume_notes: Orbital/extraocular/intraocular procedures (113-117) and acute eye disorders (121-125); mostly outpatient, small inpatient subset (trauma, endophthalmitis, orbital cellulitis).

admission_criteria: Elective splenectomy = scheduled OR case; inpatient admission for planned open splenectomy, massive splenomegaly, significant comorbidity, or expected stay >=2 midnights (Medicare 2-midnight rule) — uncomplicated elective laparoscopic cases increasingly outpatient/observation. Urgent/emergent inpatient: hemodynamically significant splenic rupture/hemorrhage; splenic abscess; symptomatic massive splenomegaly; refractory ITP with active or life-threatening bleeding. ITP elective indication: persistent/chronic ITP refractory to first-line, ideally deferred >=12 months from diagnosis (ASH 2019) to allow spontaneous remission. Not appropriate for ED discharge/observation: active bleeding; platelets <20-30x10^9/L with bleeding; hemodynamic instability; sepsis.
risk_stratification: ASA physical status class; Revised Cardiac Risk Index (RCRI) + ACS-NSQIP Surgical Risk Calculator for peri-op cardiac/morbidity risk -> optimize and set monitoring level. Platelet threshold for surgery: target >=50x10^9/L for splenectomy, >=80x10^9/L if neuraxial anesthesia -> pre-op IVIG/steroids/TPO-RA to reach target. Caprini score for surgical VTE risk -> prophylaxis intensity. Portal/splenic vein thrombosis (PVST) risk factors: splenic-vein diameter >=8-10 mm, platelet >650x10^9/L, massive splenomegaly, myeloproliferative neoplasm -> PVST surveillance +/- prophylactic anticoagulation. Frailty screen in elderly; trauma cases graded by AAST splenic injury scale.
initial_workup_labs: CBC with differential, platelet count and peripheral smear; reticulocyte count; type & screen/crossmatch (2 units RBC, group & save); PT/INR, aPTT, fibrinogen; CMP including LFTs and renal function; hemolysis panel (LDH, haptoglobin, indirect bilirubin) with direct antiglobulin/Coombs test if AIHA; iron studies; pregnancy test. ITP is a diagnosis of exclusion — confirm before OR. Abnormal platelets/coags trigger hematology optimization and transfusion planning; baseline platelet count anchors post-op thrombocytosis surveillance.
initial_imaging_dx: Pre-op contrast CT abdomen/pelvis (or US) to size spleen, map splenic-vein anatomy, and identify accessory spleens (present ~15-20%; missed = ITP relapse); baseline splenic/portal-vein Doppler US. Pre-op ECG and CXR per risk; TTE if poor functional capacity or elevated RCRI. Trauma: CT abdomen/pelvis with IV contrast to grade AAST injury. Peripheral smear + flow cytometry when splenomegaly etiology is unclear.
time_critical_interventions: Vaccinate >=14 days BEFORE elective splenectomy — pneumococcal (PCV20 or PCV21; or PCV15 then PPSV23), MenACWY, MenB, and Hib if non-immune — for optimal antibody response; if emergent/unvaccinated give >=14 days AFTER surgery (or before discharge when follow-up is uncertain). Peri-op bundle: prophylactic cefazolin within 60 min of incision, maintain normothermia, mechanical VTE prophylaxis at induction. Splenic rupture/hemorrhagic shock: activate massive transfusion protocol, emergent hemorrhage control, door-to-OR for instability. ITP life-threatening bleed: IVIG 1 g/kg + high-dose corticosteroid + platelet transfusion given concurrently.
initial_management: ITP pre-op platelet optimization: corticosteroids (prednisone 1 mg/kg/day or dexamethasone 40 mg x4 days) +/- IVIG 1 g/kg x1-2 doses; TPO-receptor agonist (romiplostim, eltrombopag) as bridge for refractory disease. Transfusion — restrictive: RBC for Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if cardiac disease); platelets only for active bleeding or to reach pre-op target, transfused at incision due to rapid splenic consumption. Antibiotic prophylaxis: cefazolin (clindamycin/vancomycin if beta-lactam allergy). Multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (acetaminophen + NSAID + TAP/regional block) per ERAS. Source control: laparoscopic splenectomy is gold standard for normal-to-moderate spleens; open/hand-assisted for massive splenomegaly or trauma.
day1_milestones: POD0: laparoscopic splenectomy with confirmed hemostasis and deliberate accessory-spleen search; extubate; PACU to surgical floor (ICU only for hemorrhage/instability/major comorbidity). ERAS: PONV prophylaxis, multimodal analgesia, clear liquids POD0, out of bed same day, incentive spirometry q1h while awake. Mechanical VTE prophylaxis; hold pharmacologic prophylaxis until hemostasis confirmed. Serial vitals + Hgb to detect bleeding; monitor drain amylase (if placed) for pancreatic-tail injury.
day2_milestones: POD1: advance to regular diet as tolerated; stop IV fluids; transition to oral analgesia. Ambulate >=3-4x/day; remove urinary catheter. Start pharmacologic VTE prophylaxis (enoxaparin/LMWH) once hemostasis confirmed. Recheck CBC/platelets (rebound thrombocytosis expected). If emergent/unvaccinated, administer asplenia vaccines. Assess discharge readiness — uncomplicated laparoscopic cases often discharge POD1.
day3plus_milestones: POD2+: continue diet and ambulation, wean to oral analgesics, confirm return of bowel function. Trend platelet count — add low-dose aspirin if platelets >1,000x10^9/L; maintain PVST vigilance (new abdominal pain, fever, rising LFTs -> Doppler US/CT) with prophylactic anticoagulation in high-risk patients. Begin lifelong OPSI antibiotic prophylaxis (penicillin V), issue stand-by emergency antibiotics, finalize vaccination plan, and deliver asplenia education + MedicAlert. Open/MCC cases: manage ileus, respiratory complications, ICU step-down, and longer stay.
consults_multidisciplinary: Hematology (indication confirmation, peri-op platelet management, POD platelet/thrombosis plan); anesthesia/pre-op optimization clinic; pharmacy (vaccine scheduling, VTE and post-op anticoagulation, antibiotic prophylaxis); transfusion medicine; infectious diseases for complex OPSI-risk or established infection; PT/OT (mobility, especially elderly/open cases); nutrition; care management + social work for discharge, vaccine completion and follow-up; primary care linkage for lifelong asplenia care.
monitoring_level: Surgical floor for uncomplicated laparoscopic cases; telemetry if cardiac risk. Step-down/ICU for: massive-splenomegaly/high-blood-loss resections, trauma/hemorrhagic shock, hemodynamic instability, significant comorbidity (MCC tier), or post-op bleeding/sepsis. Escalation triggers: tachycardia, falling serial Hgb, hypotension, worsening/peritonitic abdominal pain, hypoxia, oliguria.
nutrition_mobility_vte: ERAS: clear liquids POD0 advancing to regular diet POD1; mobilize POD0 and >=3-4x/day thereafter; incentive spirometry. VTE: mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs) from induction, add pharmacologic LMWH once hemostasis confirmed — important because post-splenectomy thrombocytosis + PVST risk raise thrombotic burden. Glucose target <180 mg/dL; remove urinary catheter POD1; remove surgical drain per low output/normal amylase. Post-splenectomy thrombocytosis: low-dose aspirin if platelets >1,000x10^9/L.
discharge_criteria: Tolerating oral diet; pain controlled on oral analgesia; ambulating at baseline; afebrile with stable vitals; hemostatic with stable Hgb; no signs of PVST or infection; return of bowel function; asplenia vaccines given or firmly scheduled; OPSI education completed; antibiotic prophylaxis and stand-by emergency-antibiotic prescriptions provided.
discharge_planning: Disposition home for most; SNF/rehab rare (elderly/open/complicated). Medication reconciliation including VTE prophylaxis duration. OPSI bundle: penicillin V (or amoxicillin; macrolide if penicillin-allergic) prophylaxis + stand-by emergency antibiotics, MedicAlert bracelet/asplenia card. Vaccination completion schedule with booster intervals (MenACWY q5y, MenB q2-3y if ongoing risk; PPSV23 sequencing). Follow-up: surgery 1-2 weeks, hematology, PCP for lifelong asplenia care. Education: fever is a medical emergency (seek care, take stand-by antibiotic), animal-bite and travel/malaria counseling, wound care; recheck platelet count as outpatient.
expected_los: GMLOS by CMS severity tier: DRG 801 (no CC/MCC) 2.6 d, DRG 800 (CC) 5.0 d, DRG 799 (MCC) 7.8 d (AMLOS 10.7 d); other blood/immune OR procedures DRG 804 2.0 d / 803 3.9 d / 802 7.5 d. Typical uncomplicated laparoscopic splenectomy 1-3 days; blended ~3-4 days.
target_los: Laparoscopic splenectomy with ERAS: 1-2 days (selected uncomplicated cases same-day or POD1 discharge); open or massive-splenomegaly cases ~3-4 days; MCC/complicated tiers individualized.
quality_metrics: Documented pre-op asplenia vaccination completeness (key measurable gap); SCIP/ERAS process compliance (antibiotic timing, normothermia, VTE prophylaxis); ACS-NSQIP risk-adjusted morbidity/mortality; SSI rate; VTE/PVST rate; reoperation for bleeding; 30-day readmission and 30-day mortality; issuance of OPSI antibiotic prophylaxis + emergency antibiotics + MedicAlert at discharge; patient-reported recovery.
common_complications: Hemorrhage/reoperation (careful hilar dissection, serial Hgb); portal/splenic vein thrombosis (~5-37% by indication/spleen size — highest with massive splenomegaly and myeloproliferative disease; mitigate with surveillance +/- prophylactic anticoagulation); pancreatic-tail injury/fistula (drain amylase); left-lower-lobe atelectasis/pneumonia/pleural effusion (incentive spirometry, mobilization); subphrenic abscess; rebound thrombocytosis and VTE (VTE prophylaxis, aspirin if >1,000x10^9/L); wound infection; ileus; lifelong OPSI (vaccination + antibiotic prophylaxis).
readmission_drivers: Portal/splenic vein thrombosis (presents days-to-weeks post-op with abdominal pain) -> surveillance + prophylactic anticoagulation in high-risk; post-op bleeding; infection (SSI, subphrenic abscess, pneumonia, OPSI); pancreatic leak; uncontrolled pain; VTE; ITP relapse from missed accessory spleen -> intra-op accessory-spleen search. Prevention levers: vaccination + antibiotic prophylaxis + fever action plan + MedicAlert, clear red-flag education, timely surgery/heme follow-up.
guideline_source: No single unified splenectomy-pathway society guideline — assembled from: ASH 2019 ITP guidelines (Neunert et al.) for the dominant elective indication; British Society for Haematology 2024 asplenia infection-prevention guideline (Ladhani et al.); CDC/ACIP 2025 Adult Immunization Schedule (asplenia). Peri-op scaffolding: ERAS Society principles + ACS-NSQIP.
key_citations: ASH 2019 ITP guidelines — Neunert C, et al. Blood Adv 2019;3(23):3829-3866 (PMID 31794604; DOI 10.1182/bloodadvances.2019000966); 2022 review Blood Adv 2024;8(13):3578. BSH asplenia guideline — Ladhani SN, et al. Br J Haematol 2024;204(5):1672-1686 (PMID 38600782; DOI 10.1111/bjh.19361). CDC ACIP Adult Immunization Schedule 2025 (asplenia notes). PVST prophylactic anticoagulation meta-analyses — Zhang N, et al. Biomed Rep 2016;5(4):483-490 (PMID 27699018; DOI 10.3892/br.2016.755); Liao Z, et al. PLoS One 2023;18(8):e0290164 (PMID 37582105; DOI 10.1371/journal.pone.0290164); PVST risk factors — Wu S, et al. Turk J Gastroenterol 2015;26(5):423-8 (PMID 26350689). Source: PubMed.
evidence_grade: Moderate overall (assembled). ITP indication/management = Strong society guideline (ASH 2019, GRADE) but many recommendations conditional/low-certainty (ASH notes scarce RCT data). Asplenia vaccination + OPSI prevention = Strong consensus society guideline (BSH 2024, ACIP). Post-splenectomy PVST prophylactic anticoagulation = Moderate/extrapolated (meta-analyses of mostly observational studies weighted to cirrhotic populations; few RCTs). No unified splenectomy-pathway RCT.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: CMS split: 799/800/801 = splenectomy with MCC/CC/without; 802/803/804 = other blood & blood-forming-organ OR procedures with MCC/CC/without (the task's 799-802 grouping is approximate). No-CC/MCC tier (801/804): elective laparoscopic, healthy, floor care, GMLOS 2.0-2.6 d, minimal complications. CC tier (800/803): comorbidity or minor complication (anemia, effusion, thrombocytosis), GMLOS 3.9-5.0 d. MCC tier (799/802): major complication (hemorrhagic shock, sepsis/OPSI, acute PVST +/- bowel ischemia, pancreatic fistula, respiratory failure) or emergent/trauma/massive-splenomegaly/myeloproliferative disease — ICU likely, GMLOS 7.5-7.8 d (AMLOS ~11 d), highest mortality. Trauma splenectomy skews severity upward.
pathway_pearls: 1) Vaccinate >=14 days BEFORE elective splenectomy (pneumococcal PCV20/21, MenACWY + q5y booster, MenB, Hib if non-immune); emergent cases >=14 days AFTER — the single most-missed quality gap. 2) Deliberately search for accessory spleens intra-op (~15-20%) — a missed accessory spleen causes ITP relapse and readmission. 3) Post-op portal/splenic vein thrombosis surveillance + prophylactic anticoagulation in high-risk (massive splenomegaly, myeloproliferative, platelet >650x10^9/L, splenic vein >=8-10 mm); low threshold for Doppler/CT on new abdominal pain. 4) Discharge with the full lifelong OPSI bundle — penicillin prophylaxis, stand-by emergency antibiotics, MedicAlert, fever action plan — and pair laparoscopic approach + ERAS to hit a 1-2 day LOS.

admission_criteria: Planned/elective inpatient surgical admissions (not ED dispositions) for resection of a functional or neoplastic adrenal or pituitary lesion requiring postoperative endocrine/hemodynamic monitoring. Adrenal indications: biochemically confirmed pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma (elevated plasma free or 24h urine fractionated metanephrines); aldosterone-producing adenoma with AVS-proven lateralization; overt cortisol-secreting adenoma or MACS with attributable comorbidity; suspected adrenocortical carcinoma or nonfunctioning mass >4 cm with malignant features; isolated resectable adrenal metastasis. Pituitary indications: functioning adenoma (Cushing disease, acromegaly, TSH-oma; prolactinoma only if dopamine-agonist resistant/intolerant, apoplexy, or CSF leak) and nonfunctioning macroadenoma with mass effect/chiasm compression/visual-field defect. Urgent/emergent inpatient admission: pituitary apoplexy with progressive visual loss or altered sensorium; pheochromocytoma crisis/catecholamine cardiomyopathy; adrenal (addisonian) crisis. NOT for admission: asymptomatic nonfunctioning adenoma with benign imaging (outpatient surveillance) and simple incidentaloma; observation/ED discharge is inappropriate for these resections
risk_stratification: Adrenal-mass malignancy risk (ESE/ENSAT 2023): unenhanced CT <=10 Hounsfield units = benign adenoma (no surgery); >4 cm AND inhomogeneous or HU >20 -> resect; absolute contrast washout >=60% / relative >=40% = benign lipid-poor adenoma. Pheochromocytoma malignant potential: PASS score >=4 -> aggressive follow-up; SDHB-mutation and paraganglioma raise metastatic risk. Pheo preoperative readiness (modified Roizen criteria): seated BP <130/80 and standing systolic >90, HR 60-70 seated, no ST/T changes x1 week, <=1 PVC/5 min -> cleared for OR. Primary aldosteronism: elevated aldosterone-to-renin ratio (ARR) screen + confirmatory saline/captopril test; AVS lateralization index >4 with cosyntropin (selectivity index >=5) -> unilateral adrenalectomy vs MRA. Cortisol autonomy: 1-mg overnight dexamethasone-suppression cortisol >1.8 ug/dL (>50 nmol/L) = fail; 24h UFC > ULN; ACTH suppressed = adrenal, non-suppressed = pituitary/ectopic. Acromegaly: elevated IGF-1 + OGTT GH nadir >1 ng/mL (>0.4 ultrasensitive). Pituitary anatomy/urgency: Knosp grade 3-4 = cavernous-sinus invasion (subtotal-resection risk); bitemporal hemianopia on Humphrey fields. General: ASA class, RCRI, ACS-NSQIP; TTE for catecholamine/acromegalic cardiomyopathy
initial_workup_labs: Adrenal functional screen BEFORE any surgery/biopsy: plasma free metanephrines or 24h urine fractionated metanephrines/catecholamines (exclude pheochromocytoma); 08:00 aldosterone + plasma renin activity/direct renin (ARR) with potassium repletion; 1-mg overnight dexamethasone-suppression test + ACTH + DHEAS; low ARR/high ARR and hypokalemia trigger MRA + K correction. Pituitary anterior-hormone panel: 08:00 cortisol + ACTH, TSH + free T4, prolactin (serial dilution to avoid hook effect in macroadenoma), IGF-1 + GH, LH/FSH + testosterone (M)/estradiol (F) -> low AM cortisol triggers preop hydrocortisone; central hypothyroidism triggers levothyroxine (after cortisol). Baseline: CBC, CMP (Na/K/glucose/Cr), HbA1c (Cushing/acromegaly), coagulation panel, type & screen, ECG. Postop drivers: serial serum Na, glucose (rebound hypoglycemia post-pheo), potassium (hyperkalemia post-aldosteronoma), AM cortisol POD1
initial_imaging_dx: Dedicated adrenal CT (unenhanced + delayed washout protocol) or chemical-shift MRI to characterize mass before OR. Pheochromocytoma: CT/MRI abdomen/pelvis; functional 68Ga-DOTATATE PET or 123I-MIBG for paraganglioma/metastatic disease and genetic-high-risk. Primary aldosteronism: adrenal CT THEN adrenal venous sampling (AVS) to confirm lateralization before unilateral adrenalectomy (guideline-required in patients >35 y). Pituitary: dedicated sella-protocol MRI with dynamic gadolinium (thin cuts) + formal neuro-ophthalmology Humphrey visual fields preoperatively + ENT/rhinology nasal assessment. Preop cardiac/airway: ECG, CXR, TTE if catecholamine or acromegalic cardiomyopathy suspected, airway exam for acromegalic difficult intubation. Postoperative pituitary MRI generally deferred to ~3 months (early packing artifact) unless a complication is suspected; postop management is lab-driven (Na, cortisol, UOP)
time_critical_interventions: Pheochromocytoma: mandatory preoperative alpha-adrenergic blockade for >=7-14 days before surgery (phenoxybenzamine or doxazosin) to prevent intraoperative hypertensive crisis; NEVER initiate beta-blockade before adequate alpha-blockade (unopposed alpha -> crisis); high-sodium diet (~5000 mg/day) + volume expansion 2-3 days preop to prevent post-resection hypotension. Pituitary apoplexy with deteriorating vision: urgent transsphenoidal decompression (emergent if rapid) + immediate stress-dose hydrocortisone 100 mg IV. Perioperative glucocorticoid at induction (hydrocortisone 100 mg IV) for cortisol-secreting/bilateral adrenalectomy, pituitary with adrenal insufficiency, or apoplexy. Surgical antibiotic prophylaxis within 60 min of incision; VTE mechanical prophylaxis at induction. Postop pituitary DI surveillance: hourly UOP -> if >250 mL/hr x2-3 h with serum Na rising >145 and urine specific gravity <1.005 give desmopressin. Post-pheo resection: anticipate hypotension in first 24-48h (fluids/vasopressors ready) and monitor glucose q1-2h for rebound hypoglycemia
initial_management: Pheo preop: nonselective alpha-blocker phenoxybenzamine 10 mg BID up-titrated OR selective alpha-1 blocker doxazosin/prazosin/terazosin; add beta-blocker (propranolol or metoprolol) ONLY after alpha-blockade for reflex tachycardia; calcium-channel blocker (amlodipine/nicardipine) adjunct; high-sodium diet + fluids. Primary aldosteronism: mineralocorticoid-receptor antagonist spironolactone (or eplerenone) + potassium repletion to normalize K/BP preop, STOP MRA and K supplements postop. Cushing: glycemic control (insulin), antihypertensives, and steroidogenesis inhibitors (ketoconazole, metyrapone, osilodrostat) if severe hypercortisolism preop; aggressive VTE prophylaxis. Perioperative steroid: hydrocortisone stress dose (100 mg IV induction then taper) for cortisol-secreting/bilateral/pituitary cases; after curative Cushing surgery expect adrenal insufficiency -> physiologic replacement (hydrocortisone 15-25 mg/day divided) tapered over months. Pituitary replacement: levothyroxine for central hypothyroidism, desmopressin for DI. Fluids: balanced crystalloid, goal-directed; restrictive transfusion Hgb <7 g/dL (<8 if cardiac); blood loss typically low. Source control: minimally invasive laparoscopic/retroperitoneoscopic adrenalectomy (open for large/invasive ACC and most paragangliomas); endoscopic endonasal transsphenoidal pituitary resection
day1_milestones: Adrenalectomy POD0: laparoscopic/retroperitoneoscopic resection, extubate, PACU then floor or step-down; multimodal opioid-sparing analgesia (acetaminophen + NSAID + TAP/regional block); ERAS early oral intake (clear liquids within hours), remove OG tube; ambulate the evening of surgery; monitor BP (pheo: treat hypotension with fluids/pressors), glucose (rebound hypoglycemia), and potassium (aldosteronoma: watch hyperkalemia); start hydrocortisone if bilateral or cortisol-secreting. Pituitary POD0: endoscopic endonasal resection; neuro checks q1h; strict I/O with hourly UOP for DI; check serum Na on the evening of surgery; stress-dose hydrocortisone; head-of-bed elevated with CSF-leak precautions (no nose-blowing/straining/CPAP); visual-acuity check; aggressive antiemetics; early mobilization
day2_milestones: Adrenalectomy POD1: advance to regular diet, discontinue IV fluids, transition to oral analgesia, remove Foley, ambulate >=3-4x; recheck electrolytes/glucose; for aldosteronoma confirm potassium normal and stop MRA/K supplements; for pheo confirm hemodynamic stability off vasopressors; many uncomplicated laparoscopic patients discharge POD1. Pituitary POD1: 08:00 serum cortisol to assess the HPA axis and guide steroid continuation vs taper (low cortisol -> replace); continue serum Na checks BID and DI surveillance (UOP/specific gravity/Na); watch for CSF rhinorrhea (halo sign, salty postnasal drip -> beta-2-transferrin); visual fields/acuity; advance diet, mobilize, taper stress steroids toward physiologic replacement; neuro-ophthalmology follow-up
day3plus_milestones: Adrenalectomy POD2+: most patients discharge POD1-2 once pain is controlled orally, tolerating diet, voiding, ambulating, hemodynamically stable, and electrolytes stable; arrange endocrine follow-up for biochemical cure (metanephrines, aldosterone/renin, cortisol) and steroid taper. Pituitary POD2-3: confirm DI resolved or controlled on desmopressin with stable Na, no CSF leak, and HPA axis addressed (discharge on hydrocortisone if deficient or after Cushing remission); ERAS target discharge POD2-3; explicitly educate on delayed hyponatremia/SIADH which peaks POD7-8 -> outpatient sodium check ~POD7 plus a fluid-restriction plan; sinonasal care instructions and steroid sick-day rules
consults_multidisciplinary: Endocrinology (preop biochemical diagnosis/optimization and postop hormone management) - essential and early. High-volume endocrine/adrenal surgeon for adrenalectomy; combined neurosurgery + ENT/rhinology team for endoscopic endonasal pituitary surgery. Anesthesiology preop (pheo hemodynamic plan; acromegalic difficult-airway plan). Neuro-ophthalmology (visual fields) for pituitary. Interventional radiology for AVS. Cardiology if catecholamine or acromegalic cardiomyopathy. Genetics/genetic counseling for all pheochromocytoma/paraganglioma (SDHx, MEN2/RET, VHL, NF1) and syndromic pituitary disease. Pharmacy (alpha-blockade titration, steroid taper, insulin, desmopressin), diabetes educator, nutrition, PT/OT, and care management/social work for discharge planning and steroid/emergency-kit education
monitoring_level: ICU or step-down POD0 for: pheochromocytoma (arterial line, hemodynamic lability, ~24h), catecholamine or acromegalic cardiomyopathy, large/complex or open resections, pituitary apoplexy, and any patient needing q1h neuro checks or intensive DI/Na management. Telemetry/step-down: typical pituitary POD0-1 for neurologic and DI surveillance. Floor: uncomplicated laparoscopic adrenalectomy for aldosteronoma or benign incidentaloma. Escalation triggers: refractory hypotension or hypertension, new arrhythmia, UOP >250-300 mL/hr with hypernatremia (DI), Na <130 or rapid Na shift, new/worsening visual deficit, CSF leak or meningitis signs, altered mental status, or adrenal crisis (hypotension plus hypoglycemia)
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: ERAS early oral intake POD0 (adrenalectomy clear liquids within hours, regular diet POD1; pituitary oral intake POD0 as tolerated), minimize prolonged NPO/ileus. Mobility: out of bed and ambulating the evening of surgery, then >=3x/day. VTE: mechanical prophylaxis (sequential compression devices) at induction plus pharmacologic LMWH/unfractionated heparin once hemostasis is secure - especially important in Cushing (hypercoagulable, high VTE risk; consider extended prophylaxis). Glucose: target <180 mg/dL with insulin, tighter control in Cushing/acromegaly. Lines/catheters: remove Foley POD1, arterial line when hemodynamically stable, convert IV to oral early; discontinue nasal packing per surgeon protocol with leak precautions
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamically stable off vasopressors (pheo); electrolytes stable (potassium normalized after aldosteronoma; serum Na stable with DI controlled after pituitary); tolerating an oral diet; pain controlled on oral analgesics; ambulating and independent with ADLs; spontaneously voiding; afebrile with no infection; no active CSF leak; visual status stable or improved; established on appropriate hormone replacement (hydrocortisone +/- desmopressin +/- levothyroxine) with demonstrated understanding of steroid sick-day rules and delayed-hyponatremia warning symptoms
discharge_planning: Disposition usually home. Medication reconciliation: written glucocorticoid taper plus stress-dose/sick-day rules, an emergency injectable hydrocortisone kit, and medical-alert ID for adrenal insufficiency; desmopressin instructions if DI; levothyroxine; STOP MRA/potassium after aldosteronoma; proactively down-titrate antihypertensives and insulin as disease resolves. Follow-up: endocrinology at 1-2 weeks for biochemical cure and medication titration; surgeon at ~2 weeks; neuro-ophthalmology; pituitary MRI at ~3 months; scheduled serum sodium check ~POD7 for SIADH surveillance; genetics referral. Education: adrenal-crisis recognition, CSF-leak precautions, wound/sinonasal care, and explicit return precautions. Transitions: PCP notification with written steroid schedule and confirmed lab appointments
expected_los: Benchmark GMLOS approximately 2 days for uncomplicated cases (canonical MS-DRG 615 Adrenal & Pituitary Procedures without CC/MCC, GMLOS ~1.8-2.2 days) rising to ~4-5 days with CC/MCC (MS-DRG 614). By procedure: laparoscopic adrenalectomy typically 1-2 days; endoscopic transsphenoidal pituitary resection typically 2-4 days. Complex tiers (pheochromocytoma with cardiomyopathy, Cushing, CSF leak, ACC/open resection, MCC) extend to 5-7+ days
target_los: Under an optimized ERAS pathway: laparoscopic adrenalectomy 1 day (selected 23-hour/ambulatory in high-volume centers); endoscopic transsphenoidal pituitary 2-3 days (ERAS PitNET protocols shift discharge to POD2-3, with emerging ambulatory protocols). Overall target ~1-2 days adrenal and ~2-3 days pituitary
quality_metrics: ERAS protocol compliance rate; SSI prevention (appropriate antibiotic selection/timing/redosing); VTE prophylaxis rate (critical in Cushing); 30-day readmission (delayed hyponatremia is the leading pituitary driver); biochemical remission/cure rates (normalized metanephrines, aldosterone-renin, 24h UFC/late-night salivary cortisol, IGF-1); postoperative DI rate (transient vs permanent); CSF-leak rate; new hypopituitarism/endocrine deficit; 30-day mortality; readmission for adrenal crisis; length of stay; transfusion rate; conversion-to-open rate. Registries/benchmarks: ACS-NSQIP, CESQIP (Collaborative Endocrine Surgery Quality Improvement Program), and pituitary/skull-base outcomes registries
common_complications: Adrenalectomy: post-pheochromocytoma hemodynamic instability (hypotension) and rebound hypoglycemia; hyperkalemia after aldosteronoma resection (suppressed contralateral zona glomerulosa); adrenal insufficiency after bilateral or cortisol-secreting resection; hemorrhage, injury to spleen/pancreas/kidney, conversion to open, SSI, ileus, and VTE (Cushing). Pituitary: transient DI (~10-30%; permanent <2-5%), delayed hyponatremia/SIADH (peaks POD7-8, ~5-15%, top readmission cause), CSF rhinorrhea/leak (~2-5%), meningitis, hypopituitarism/adrenal insufficiency, epistaxis, visual deterioration, sinonasal morbidity, and rare carotid injury. Mitigation: preop alpha-blockade + sodium/volume loading; glucose and potassium monitoring; stop MRA/K postop; stress-dose steroids; hourly UOP + serial Na for DI; fluid restriction and Na surveillance for SIADH; meticulous skull-base repair and leak precautions; VTE prophylaxis; concentrate care with high-volume surgeons
readmission_drivers: Pituitary: delayed hyponatremia (SIADH, POD7-8) is the #1 driver -> scheduled outpatient sodium check ~POD7 plus fluid-restriction education; DI-related dehydration/hypernatremia from desmopressin mismanagement -> structured teaching; CSF leak/meningitis; and adrenal crisis from inadequate steroid education. Adrenalectomy: adrenal (addisonian) crisis; symptomatic hyperkalemia; uncontrolled pain/ileus; SSI; VTE. Prevention levers: standardized steroid education with an emergency hydrocortisone kit and medical-alert ID; a hard-wired POD7 sodium-check protocol; desmopressin/fluid teaching; early (1-2 week) endocrine follow-up; and explicit written return precautions
guideline_source: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guidelines: Pheochromocytoma & Paraganglioma 2014 (Lenders); Primary Aldosteronism 2016 (Funder) with 2025 update (Adler/Vaidya); Treatment of Cushing's Syndrome 2015 (Nieman). European Society of Endocrinology/ENSAT Adrenal Incidentaloma 2023 (Fassnacht). Pituitary Society consensus updates: Cushing's Disease 2021 (Fleseriu) and Acromegaly 2021 (Melmed). ERAS Society-aligned ERAS protocol for pituitary neuroendocrine tumors 2023 (Cossu). AAES/ACS and CESQIP for endocrine-surgery quality

evidence_grade: Strong for the diagnostic work-up, preoperative optimization, and postoperative endocrine management - anchored in multiple GRADE-based society Class I/II guidelines (Endocrine Society, ESE/ENSAT, Pituitary Society). Moderate for the surgical LOS/ERAS and enhanced-recovery elements, which are extrapolated from the broader ERAS Society evidence base plus single-center pituitary/adrenal ERAS cohorts rather than large randomized trials. Overall: Strong for endocrine/perioperative principles, Moderate for LOS targets and ERAS specifics
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG-mapping caveat: in the standard CMS grouper the canonical Adrenal & Pituitary Procedures family is a 2-tier split - MS-DRG 614 (with CC/MCC) and 615 (without CC/MCC); the codes 622/623/624 supplied for this row actually title as Skin Grafts & Wound Debridement for Endocrine, Nutritional & Metabolic Disorders (w MCC / w CC / w/o) - verify grouper version and reconcile. Severity behavior for the adrenal/pituitary cohort: no CC/MCC = uncomplicated laparoscopic aldosteronoma/incidentaloma, floor care, LOS ~1-2 days. CC/MCC tier is driven by pheochromocytoma with catecholamine cardiomyopathy/hemodynamic instability, Cushing (VTE, infection, poor wound healing, hyperglycemia), postoperative DI/SIADH or adrenal crisis, CSF leak/meningitis, adrenocortical carcinoma/open resection, and acromegalic airway/cardiac disease -> ICU/step-down utilization and LOS 4-7+ days. Representative MCCs: catecholamine crisis, acute MI, sepsis/meningitis, respiratory failure, severe hyponatremia, adrenal crisis
pathway_pearls: 1) Pheochromocytoma: enforce 7-14 days of preoperative alpha-blockade plus sodium/volume loading, never beta-block before alpha-block, and anticipate post-resection hypotension and rebound hypoglycemia. 2) Always exclude pheochromocytoma biochemically before any adrenal surgery/biopsy, and require AVS-proven lateralization before unilateral adrenalectomy for primary aldosteronism (patients >35 y). 3) Pituitary: own the sodium - hourly UOP with serial Na for DI in-house, and hard-wire a scheduled POD7 outpatient sodium check with fluid-restriction teaching because delayed hyponatremia (SIADH) is the #1 readmission driver. 4) Steroid stewardship: stress-dose then structured taper for cortisol-secreting/bilateral/pituitary cases, and discharge every at-risk patient with an emergency hydrocortisone kit, written sick-day rules, and a medical-alert ID
verification_notes: Independent adversarial verification pass was interrupted by a session usage limit; research is society-guideline-sourced but not yet re-checked.
scope_and_volume_notes: Adrenalectomy and transsphenoidal/pituitary procedures for functional/neoplastic disease.

admission_criteria: Inpatient admission for: PAWSS >=4 (high risk of complicated withdrawal); CIWA-Ar >=15 despite ED benzodiazepines or any CIWA-Ar >=19; history of DTs or withdrawal seizures; active/recurrent withdrawal seizure; DT signs (disorientation, hallucinations, autonomic instability HR >120, SBP >160, T >=38.3C, diaphoresis, tremor); high benzodiazepine requirement in ED (e.g., >diazepam 40-60 mg or lorazepam 8-10 mg over first hours); Wernicke signs (ophthalmoplegia/ataxia/confusion); unstable comorbidity (GI bleed, pancreatitis, hepatic decompensation, arrhythmia, infection/sepsis, DKA); pregnancy; cannot tolerate PO / severe dehydration-electrolyte derangement; substance-induced psychosis or delirium with agitation/danger; active suicidality or unsafe environment. ICU/critical-care admission for DTs, benzodiazepine-resistant withdrawal, seizure with airway risk, need for continuous IV sedation or phenobarbital load, RASS instability, respiratory compromise. ED discharge/observation appropriate if CIWA-Ar <8-10, PAWSS <4, no seizure/DT history, tolerating PO, stable vitals, reliable follow-up.
risk_stratification: PAWSS (Maldonado): threshold >=4 predicts complicated (moderate-severe) AWS -> scheduled prophylaxis, telemetry, q1-2h reassessment. CIWA-Ar (10 items, 0-67): <8-10 minimal/supportive only, 8-15 moderate -> symptom-triggered benzodiazepines, >=15-20 severe/impending DTs -> aggressive benzodiazepines +/- phenobarbital and monitoring escalation; valid only in awake, communicative patients (unreliable in delirium/intubation). RASS (-5 to +4) targets sedation (goal -1 to 0; RASS >=+2 with autonomic surge = undertreated). Opioids: COWS (0-48): 5-12 mild, 13-24 moderate, 25-36 moderately severe, >36 severe; COWS >=8-12 permits buprenorphine induction without precipitated withdrawal. Prior DTs, seizure history, >8 drinks/day, elevated AST and thrombocytopenia raise severe-AWS risk. AUD screen: AUDIT-C >=4 men/>=3 women or positive CAGE.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, CMP (glucose, Na/K, renal, LFTs); magnesium, phosphate, ionized calcium (repletion-triggering; hypomagnesemia lowers seizure threshold); VBG/ABG if altered; serum ethanol level; urine/serum toxicology (benzodiazepines, opioids, stimulants, cannabis) plus acetaminophen/salicylate co-ingestion; lactate; lipase (pancreatitis); creatine kinase (rhabdomyolysis); ammonia if encephalopathic; INR/coags and albumin (hepatic synthetic function); beta-hCG in women; consider TSH and HIV/HBV/HCV. Blood cultures and infectious workup if febrile (do not attribute fever to withdrawal alone). POC glucose immediately, give thiamine before dextrose; repeat electrolytes/glucose q4-6h during active management.
initial_imaging_dx: ECG on arrival (QTc before antipsychotics/ondansetron/methadone; screen ischemia/arrhythmia). Non-contrast CT head for first seizure, focal deficit, head trauma, or delirium not explained by withdrawal (exclude subdural/hemorrhage). CXR if hypoxia/aspiration/infection suspected. EEG if seizures atypical, prolonged post-ictal state, or nonconvulsive status suspected. RUQ ultrasound if hepatic decompensation. Lumbar puncture if meningitis/encephalitis cannot be excluded. Continuous cardiac telemetry for moderate-severe withdrawal.
time_critical_interventions: Give thiamine 500 mg IV BEFORE any glucose in malnourished/altered patients to prevent Wernicke (do not delay correction of hypoglycemia, but give thiamine concurrently/first). Benzodiazepine within minutes for active withdrawal seizure (lorazepam 2-4 mg IV or diazepam 5-10 mg IV, repeat) - first-line abortive and prophylactic; phenytoin is ineffective for withdrawal seizures. Start symptom-triggered benzodiazepine protocol immediately once CIWA-Ar >=8-10. For severe/impending DTs: front-load/rapidly escalate benzodiazepines to symptom control and add IV phenobarbital early (front-load or single 10 mg/kg) - reduces ICU admission (Rosenson RCT 8% vs 25%). Correct hypoglycemia, hypomagnesemia, hypokalemia promptly. Escalate to ICU if uncontrolled within ~1-2h of aggressive dosing.
initial_management: First-line: long-acting benzodiazepines - chlordiazepoxide 50-100 mg PO or diazepam 5-10 mg IV/PO, symptom-triggered by CIWA-Ar (less total dose/duration/oversedation than fixed schedule); use lorazepam or oxazepam (no active metabolites, non-oxidative) in cirrhosis, elderly, or respiratory compromise. Barbiturate: phenobarbital front-loading (~10 mg/kg IV) as alternative or adjunct, especially for benzodiazepine-resistant withdrawal (GABA-agonist + NMDA-antagonist, self-tapering). Refractory DTs: continuous IV benzodiazepine or phenobarbital, adjunct dexmedetomidine (alpha-2 for autonomic control - adjunct only, does not prevent seizures/delirium), propofol if intubated; ketamine adjunct emerging. Adjuncts (never monotherapy): gabapentin/carbamazepine for mild-moderate or taper; alpha-2 agonists + beta-blockers control autonomic signs but mask CIWA and do not prevent seizures/DTs; antipsychotics (haloperidol) only for agitation/hallucinations refractory to benzodiazepines - lower seizure threshold, prolong QT. Vitamins: thiamine (100-200 mg/day prophylaxis; 500 mg IV TID x2-3 days if Wernicke suspected), folate, multivitamin; aggressive Mg/K/Phos repletion; IV crystalloid + dextrose. Opioid withdrawal: buprenorphine induction (COWS >=8-12) or methadone; symptomatic clonidine/loperamide/ondansetron. Stimulant/hallucinogen-induced agitation-psychosis: benzodiazepines +/- short-course antipsychotic, low-stimulation environment.
day1_milestones: Establish substance(s) and diagnosis; calculate PAWSS and baseline CIWA-Ar/COWS; start symptom-triggered protocol plus thiamine and electrolyte repletion; achieve initial symptom control (target RASS -1 to 0, CIWA-Ar trending <8-10); place on appropriate monitoring level; screen and treat concurrent illness (infection, GI bleed, pancreatitis, trauma); consult addiction medicine/consult-liaison psychiatry and social work; assess suicidality and decision-making capacity; start VTE prophylaxis; begin AUD/OUD treatment discussion and naloxone education.
day2_milestones: Demonstrate declining benzodiazepine/phenobarbital requirement and CIWA-Ar consistently <8-10; de-escalate monitoring (ICU -> step-down/floor) after 12-24h without severe symptoms or seizures; transition IV to PO; continue thiamine/vitamins and electrolyte correction; if DTs, remain vigilant for the 48-96h peak; initiate or continue MOUD (buprenorphine/methadone) for opioid use; begin motivational interviewing and relapse-prevention counseling; advance nutrition and mobility; confirm no active infection; start discharge planning and level-of-care determination.
day3plus_milestones: Off benzodiazepines or on minimal taper with CIWA-Ar <8-10 for >=24h and no autonomic instability/seizures/delirium; DTs resolved; tolerating diet, ambulating, cognition at baseline; initiate AUD relapse-prevention pharmacotherapy (naltrexone or acamprosate; disulfiram in selected patients) and continue MOUD; finalize ASAM level-of-care referral (residential/PHP/IOP); complete SBIRT and provide naloxone kit plus education; arrange follow-up and warm handoff; discharge when withdrawal resolved and psychosocial plan is in place.
consults_multidisciplinary: Addiction Medicine / Consult-Liaison Psychiatry (Day 0-1: MOUD and AUD pharmacotherapy, level-of-care determination); Social Work / Case Management (Day 0-1: disposition, insurance, housing, treatment placement); Clinical Pharmacy (protocol dosing, benzodiazepine/phenobarbital stewardship, interactions); Nutrition (thiamine/refeeding, malnutrition); Critical Care (DTs/refractory withdrawal); Neurology (seizures, atypical delirium, EEG); Hepatology/GI (cirrhosis, GI bleed, pancreatitis); PT/OT (deconditioning, falls, cognition); Peer Recovery Specialist; Chaplaincy; Ethics if capacity or AMA concerns.
monitoring_level: ICU/critical care: DTs, benzodiazepine-resistant withdrawal, continuous IV sedation or phenobarbital infusion, withdrawal seizure with airway risk, RASS instability, autonomic storm, sedation-related respiratory depression. Step-down/telemetry: CIWA-Ar 15-20 needing frequent dosing, high hourly benzodiazepine needs, cardiac comorbidity, phenobarbital loading. Floor with q2-4h CIWA-Ar: moderate withdrawal responding to symptom-triggered dosing. Escalation triggers: CIWA-Ar >=15 despite escalating doses, seizure, new disorientation/hallucinations, HR >120 or T >=38.3C or SBP >180, hypoxia or oversedation (RASS <=-3), rising oxygen/vasopressor need.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Diet: NPO if delirious or aspiration risk, otherwise advance as mentation clears; give thiamine before carbohydrate; monitor refeeding (phosphate/K/Mg). VTE prophylaxis: pharmacologic (LMWH/heparin) unless active bleed/coagulopathy, then mechanical. Mobility: fall and seizure precautions, early PT, sitter and restraint minimization per HBIPS. Glucose: treat hypoglycemia, avoid hyperglycemia. Lines/catheters: remove IV and Foley once PO tolerated and stable to reduce CLABSI/CAUTI and support mobility; aspiration precautions.
discharge_criteria: CIWA-Ar <8-10 sustained >=24h off or on minimal taper; no benzodiazepine/phenobarbital escalation and no seizure or delirium for >=24h; stable vitals without autonomic instability; substance-induced psychosis/delirium resolved to cognitive baseline; electrolytes corrected, tolerating PO nutrition/hydration and ambulating; suicidality addressed with safe disposition; MOUD and/or AUD pharmacotherapy initiated with prescriptions; concrete follow-up and treatment placement arranged.
discharge_planning: Disposition per ASAM level of care (outpatient/IOP/PHP/residential); medication reconciliation including relapse-prevention (naltrexone/acamprosate) and MOUD (buprenorphine with adequate bridge script and confirmed intake); naloxone co-prescription plus overdose education for any opioid use; document SBIRT/motivational interviewing; schedule addiction/behavioral-health follow-up within 7 days (warm handoff / peer navigator); arrange primary care and specialty (hepatology) follow-up; educate patient/family on withdrawal recurrence, seizure precautions, and continuing thiamine; connect to recovery supports (mutual-help groups, peer recovery, housing/social services).
expected_los: DRG 897 (no CC/MCC) GMLOS ~2.5-3 days; DRG 896 (with MCC, includes DTs/severe) GMLOS ~4.5-5.5 days; uncomplicated medically supervised detox 3-4 days, complicated withdrawal/DTs 5-8+ days.
target_los: 2-3 days for uncomplicated withdrawal on a symptom-triggered protocol; 4-5 days for DTs/complicated cases with early phenobarbital and prompt ICU-to-floor de-escalation; excess days reduced by symptom-triggered (vs fixed-schedule) dosing and same-day MOUD/placement.
quality_metrics: Joint Commission/CMS SUB measures: SUB-1 (alcohol use screening), SUB-2/2a (alcohol brief intervention), SUB-3/3a (alcohol and other drug use disorder treatment provided or offered at discharge, including medication); TOB tobacco treatment measures; HBIPS if psychiatric unit: HBIPS-2/-3 (physical restraint and seclusion hours), HBIPS-5 (discharge on multiple antipsychotics), HBIPS-1 (admission screening); HEDIS IET (initiation and engagement of SUD treatment). Process: symptom-triggered protocol adherence, thiamine-before-glucose, time-to-first-benzodiazepine for seizures. Outcomes: 30-day all-cause and AMA readmission, ED revisits, in-hospital seizure/DT progression, oversedation/intubation, falls, restraint use, mortality.
common_complications: Delirium tremens (48-96h; mitigate with PAWSS-based prophylaxis, symptom-triggered dosing, early phenobarbital); withdrawal seizures (benzodiazepines abort/prevent, not phenytoin); Wernicke-Korsakoff (parenteral thiamine before glucose); oversedation/respiratory depression/aspiration (symptom-triggered dosing, RASS monitoring, avoid dose stacking); arrhythmia/QT prolongation (limit QT-prolonging agents, replete Mg/K); electrolyte derangement and refeeding syndrome (repletion, cautious carbohydrate); missed concurrent illness - infection/sepsis, GI bleed, pancreatitis, subdural, rhabdomyolysis (broad workup, do not attribute fever to withdrawal); precipitated opioid withdrawal (induce buprenorphine only at COWS >=8-12); AMA departure.
readmission_drivers: Untreated alcohol/opioid use disorder discharged without MOUD or relapse-prevention pharmacotherapy; no warm handoff or follow-up beyond 7 days; AMA discharge before withdrawal resolved; unaddressed housing/social instability and psychiatric comorbidity; incomplete taper or premature benzodiazepine discontinuation; failure to co-prescribe naloxone; recurrent withdrawal (kindling) with escalating severity. Levers: initiate AUD/OUD pharmacotherapy inpatient, peer navigator, 7-day follow-up, naloxone, ASAM-matched placement, treat comorbid depression/PTSD.
guideline_source: American Society of Addiction Medicine (ASAM) Clinical Practice Guideline on Alcohol Withdrawal Management, 2020 (updated 2023); ASAM National Practice Guideline for the Treatment of Opioid Use Disorder, 2020 Focused Update; supported by SAMHSA TIP 45 (Detoxification and Substance Abuse Treatment) and APA substance-use disorder guidance.
key_citations: ASAM 2020 Alcohol Withdrawal Management CPG, J Addict Med 2020;14(3S Suppl 1):1-72, PMID 32511109 (DOI 10.1097/ADM.0000000000000668); PAWSS derivation/validation - Maldonado et al., Alcohol 2014;48:375-90, PMID 24657098, threshold >=4 (DOI 10.1016/j.alcohol.2014.01.004); CIWA-Ar - Sullivan et al., Br J Addict 1989;84:1353-7, PMID 2597811; Rosenson et al. phenobarbital RCT, J Emerg Med 2013;44:592-8, PMID 22999778, ICU 8% vs 25% (DOI 10.1016/j.jemermed.2012.07.056); Lee et al. phenobarbital systematic review/meta-analysis, Acad Emerg Med 2024;31:515-24, PMID 37923363 (DOI 10.1111/acem.14825); ASAM OUD National Practice Guideline 2020 Focused Update, PMID 32511106.
evidence_grade: Strong overall - anchored to current ASAM society guidelines. Strong (Class I / high-quality RCT + ASAM) for benzodiazepines as first-line and symptom-triggered dosing; Strong for parenteral thiamine and for MOUD (buprenorphine/methadone) in opioid withdrawal. Moderate for phenobarbital front-loading and for PAWSS/CIWA-Ar risk tools (validated scales plus one small RCT and cohorts). Adjuncts (dexmedetomidine, ketamine, gabapentin) rest on Moderate-to-expert-consensus evidence.
severity_cc_mcc_notes: DRG 897 (no CC/MCC): uncomplicated withdrawal, floor-level symptom-triggered management, LOS ~2-3 days. DRG 896 (with MCC): DTs, withdrawal seizures/status, aspiration pneumonia, sepsis, hepatic failure, pancreatitis, rhabdomyolysis, or respiratory failure needing ICU with continuous IV sedation or phenobarbital infusion - LOS ~5-8+ days and higher mortality; the MCC frequently defines the critical-care pathway. Wernicke-Korsakoff, metabolic encephalopathy, and severe electrolyte derangement raise acuity. DRG 895 (with rehab therapy) and 894 (left AMA) sit outside this acute-detox pathway; AMA is a key readmission and quality signal.
pathway_pearls: 1) Risk-stratify up front with PAWSS (>=4) and drive dosing with CIWA-Ar - symptom-triggered beats fixed-schedule (less benzodiazepine, shorter LOS, less oversedation), but only in awake, communicable patients. 2) Give parenteral thiamine before glucose, and escalate early to phenobarbital for benzodiazepine-resistant/severe withdrawal to cut ICU admissions. 3) Never attribute fever or new focal delirium to withdrawal alone - hunt for infection, subdural, GI bleed, and pancreatitis. 4) Treat the disorder, not just the episode: start MOUD (COWS >=8-12) and AUD relapse-prevention pharmacotherapy inpatient, co-prescribe naloxone, and secure a 7-day warm handoff to prevent kindling and readmission.
