admission_criteria: Admit when diagnostic uncertainty, symptom burden, treatment intensity or monitoring needs cannot be managed safely in observation or outpatient care; when there is shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation; or when severe comorbidity, frailty, unsafe function, inability to maintain hydration/nutrition, failed outpatient therapy or unreliable follow-up makes discharge unsafe. DRG assignment is retrospective and must never be used as the admission indication.
risk_stratification: At presentation document NEWS2/MEWS or the local deterioration score, frailty, baseline cognition/function, medication and antithrombotic exposure, renal/hepatic function, bleeding and VTE risk, infection risk, pressure-injury risk and goals of care. Escalate for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. Apply a validated disease- or procedure-specific score only when it fits the actual diagnosis; the DRG family itself is not a risk score.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, metabolic panel, blood gas when ventilation is impaired, lactate when hypoperfusion is possible, respiratory pathogen testing and cultures before antibiotics when feasible. Trend only abnormalities that can change treatment, define baseline organ function before contrast or high-risk medication, and avoid standing daily panels once stable.
initial_imaging_dx: chest radiograph first line; CT/CTA, ultrasound, bronchoscopy or pulmonary function assessment when the differential or procedural plan requires it. Reconcile findings with the actual principal diagnosis and procedure; repeat imaging only for deterioration, an unanswered management question or a protocol-defined surveillance need.
time_critical_interventions: This heterogeneous DRG family has no single universal treatment clock; stabilize airway, breathing and circulation immediately and obtain specialty review within 1 hour when organ, limb, airway or hemodynamic deterioration is possible. Complete medication reconciliation, analgesia and prevention of avoidable delay at first assessment. If no emergency trigger is present, document the definitive diagnostic/procedural decision and level of care during the first attending review rather than applying a false universal time target.
initial_management: Use diagnosis-directed therapy with daily medication reconciliation, renal/hepatic dose adjustment, symptom control and explicit stop/reassessment dates for antimicrobials, fluids, oxygen, anticoagulation and other high-risk therapies. Avoid treatment based solely on the DRG family label. Tie each order to the actual pulmonary and respiratory diagnosis, procedure and severity rather than the administrative grouping. Use goal-directed fluids, oxygen and transfusion rather than routine excess; provide multimodal analgesia and antiemetics; prevent delirium, pressure injury and falls; prescribe pharmacologic and/or mechanical VTE prophylaxis after individualized bleeding assessment; and define objective escalation, de-escalation and discharge criteria on admission.
day1_milestones: Within 24 hours: confirm the actual diagnosis and v43.1 grouping inputs, complete specialty and pharmacy reconciliation, close urgent diagnostic gaps, document treatment/procedure and disposition plans, establish pain/nausea control, initiate nutrition and mobility as safe, reconcile lines/tubes/devices, and communicate expected trajectory and contingency triggers to the patient/caregiver.
day2_milestones: By 48 hours: review clinical response and all cultures/pathology/imaging; narrow, stop or convert IV therapies when safe; remove unnecessary urinary/vascular devices; advance diet and activity; confirm procedure-specific restrictions and wound/device care; resolve oxygen, renal, bleeding and delirium risks; and lock the destination, equipment, medication-access and follow-up plan.
day3plus_milestones: For stays beyond day 3, conduct a daily interdisciplinary barrier review covering unresolved physiology, pending procedure/test, medication conversion, nutrition, mobility, cognition, caregiver training, placement and payer/logistics. Reassess goals of care and seek a second specialty or utilization review when trajectory or diagnosis remains unclear.
consults_multidisciplinary: pulmonary/critical care, respiratory therapy, thoracic surgery or trauma surgery when indicated; pharmacy, nutrition, rehabilitation and case management. Clarify one accountable attending/service, the question for every consultation, response time for urgent issues and which team owns discharge medications, pathology/culture follow-up and post-discharge escalation.
monitoring_level: Use standard ward monitoring when stable; telemetry, step-down or ICU according to actual organ risk. Increase observation frequency for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. For procedural patients, use site/wound, neurovascular, airway, bleeding and device checks at locally defined intervals; de-escalate only after a documented period of stability.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Screen nutrition, swallowing, pressure-injury, fall and mobility risk on admission. Resume oral/enteral intake within 24 hours when clinically safe; use protein/calorie support for malnutrition or major wounds. Mobilize at least 3 times daily or follow explicit weight-bearing restrictions. Provide VTE prophylaxis within 24 hours when bleeding risk permits and document any exception plus reassessment date.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamics and oxygenation are stable at the intended destination level; urgent diagnosis and treatment are complete; pain/nausea and intake are manageable on the discharge regimen; mobility, cognition, wound/device care and elimination are safe; no unreviewed critical result remains; medication reconciliation is complete; and the patient/caregiver can state red flags and whom to contact.
discharge_planning: Begin on admission. Record baseline residence/function, caregiver capacity, transportation, language and medication access. Arrange destination, equipment/services, written restrictions and wound/device plan, pending-result owner, and specialty/primary-care follow-up—generally within 7 days for high-risk patients and 7–14 days for stable routine recovery. Complete teach-back and a 48–72-hour transition contact when risk is high.
expected_los: Typical planning range 2–5 days; instability, MCC, diagnostic uncertainty or functional barriers may require 6–10+ days.
target_los: Use a local risk-adjusted target median of 3–4 days; aim for next-day discharge after stability when the diagnosis is secure and treatment, function and follow-up can be completed safely.
quality_metrics: Track risk-adjusted inpatient mortality, observed/expected LOS, ICU transfer after ward admission, 30-day readmission, emergency revisit, major bleeding, acute kidney injury, infection, venous thromboembolism, falls/pressure injury, medication reconciliation, timely specialty decision, unplanned procedure or return to the operating/procedure room, discharge-before-noon only as a balancing metric, and patient-reported understanding. Stratify by exact DRG, MCC/CC, procedure, age, frailty and discharge destination.
common_complications: respiratory failure, pneumothorax or hemothorax, pneumonia, sepsis, ventilator-associated harm, bleeding, venous thromboembolism and deconditioning. Also monitor medication-related harm, delirium, constipation/urinary retention, malnutrition, falls, pressure injury and failed transition; use diagnosis- and procedure-specific surveillance rather than assuming every risk applies to every member of this heterogeneous family.
readmission_drivers: Unresolved or recurrent primary disease, inadequate source/procedure control, medication discrepancy or nonadherence, poor symptom control, wound/device failure, infection, bleeding, renal injury, deconditioning, missed pending results, delayed specialty follow-up, inability to obtain medications/equipment and insufficient caregiver or transportation support.

condition: Intracranial Vascular Procedures with Principal Diagnosis Hemorrhage
admission_criteria: Admit when the operative or invasive intervention and postoperative monitoring cannot be delivered safely in an ambulatory setting; when there is shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation; or when severe comorbidity, frailty, unsafe function, inability to maintain hydration/nutrition, failed outpatient therapy or unreliable follow-up makes discharge unsafe. DRG assignment is retrospective and must never be used as the admission indication.
risk_stratification: At presentation document NEWS2/MEWS or the local deterioration score, frailty, baseline cognition/function, medication and antithrombotic exposure, renal/hepatic function, bleeding and VTE risk, infection risk, pressure-injury risk and goals of care. Escalate for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. Apply a validated disease- or procedure-specific score only when it fits the actual diagnosis; the DRG family itself is not a risk score.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, electrolytes, glucose, renal and hepatic function, magnesium, coagulation studies and toxicology or inflammatory/infectious testing when clinically indicated. Trend only abnormalities that can change treatment, define baseline organ function before contrast or high-risk medication, and avoid standing daily panels once stable.
initial_imaging_dx: focused neurologic examination with documented baseline; CT/CTA, MRI/MRA, EEG, lumbar puncture or vascular studies selected by syndrome and contraindications. Reconcile findings with the actual principal diagnosis and procedure; repeat imaging only for deterioration, an unanswered management question or a protocol-defined surveillance need.
time_critical_interventions: Record last-known-well and perform an immediate neurologic examination for acute deficits; activate stroke, seizure or neurosurgical emergency pathways without waiting for routine ward evaluation when criteria are met. Complete medication reconciliation, analgesia and prevention of avoidable delay at first assessment. If no emergency trigger is present, document the definitive diagnostic/procedural decision and level of care during the first attending review rather than applying a false universal time target.
initial_management: Confirm indication, laterality/site, consent, blood availability, antimicrobial prophylaxis only when indicated, anesthesia plan, medication/antithrombotic management and postoperative destination in a same-day multidisciplinary huddle. Use a procedure-specific checklist and do not substitute anticipated DRG assignment for clinical criteria. Tie each order to the actual neurologic and neurosurgical diagnosis, procedure and severity rather than the administrative grouping. Use goal-directed fluids, oxygen and transfusion rather than routine excess; provide multimodal analgesia and antiemetics; prevent delirium, pressure injury and falls; prescribe pharmacologic and/or mechanical VTE prophylaxis after individualized bleeding assessment; and define objective escalation, de-escalation and discharge criteria on admission.
day1_milestones: Within 24 hours: confirm the actual diagnosis and v43.1 grouping inputs, complete specialty and pharmacy reconciliation, close urgent diagnostic gaps, document treatment/procedure and disposition plans, establish pain/nausea control, initiate nutrition and mobility as safe, reconcile lines/tubes/devices, and communicate expected trajectory and contingency triggers to the patient/caregiver.
day2_milestones: By 48 hours: review clinical response and all cultures/pathology/imaging; narrow, stop or convert IV therapies when safe; remove unnecessary urinary/vascular devices; advance diet and activity; confirm procedure-specific restrictions and wound/device care; resolve oxygen, renal, bleeding and delirium risks; and lock the destination, equipment, medication-access and follow-up plan.
day3plus_milestones: For stays beyond day 3, conduct a daily interdisciplinary barrier review covering unresolved physiology, pending procedure/test, medication conversion, nutrition, mobility, cognition, caregiver training, placement and payer/logistics. Reassess goals of care and seek a second specialty or utilization review when trajectory or diagnosis remains unclear.
consults_multidisciplinary: neurology, neurosurgery or neurointerventional service as appropriate; rehabilitation therapies, pharmacy, case management and speech-language pathology when swallowing or cognition is affected. Clarify one accountable attending/service, the question for every consultation, response time for urgent issues and which team owns discharge medications, pathology/culture follow-up and post-discharge escalation.
monitoring_level: Use standard ward monitoring when stable; telemetry, step-down or ICU according to actual organ risk. Increase observation frequency for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. For procedural patients, use site/wound, neurovascular, airway, bleeding and device checks at locally defined intervals; de-escalate only after a documented period of stability.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Screen nutrition, swallowing, pressure-injury, fall and mobility risk on admission. Resume oral/enteral intake within 24 hours when clinically safe; use protein/calorie support for malnutrition or major wounds. Mobilize at least 3 times daily or follow explicit weight-bearing restrictions. Provide VTE prophylaxis within 24 hours when bleeding risk permits and document any exception plus reassessment date.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamics and oxygenation are stable at the intended destination level; urgent diagnosis and treatment are complete; pain/nausea and intake are manageable on the discharge regimen; mobility, cognition, wound/device care and elimination are safe; no unreviewed critical result remains; medication reconciliation is complete; and the patient/caregiver can state red flags and whom to contact.
discharge_planning: Begin on admission. Record baseline residence/function, caregiver capacity, transportation, language and medication access. Arrange destination, equipment/services, written restrictions and wound/device plan, pending-result owner, and specialty/primary-care follow-up—generally within 7 days for high-risk patients and 7–14 days for stable routine recovery. Complete teach-back and a 48–72-hour transition contact when risk is high.
expected_los: Typical planning range 2–6 days; uncomplicated minimally invasive cases may be 1–3 days, while MCC, open surgery or major complication may require 7–10+ days.
target_los: Use a local risk-adjusted target median of 3–5 days, stratified by procedure and MCC status; pursue discharge by postoperative day 1–3 only when all physiologic, mobility and support criteria are met.
quality_metrics: Track risk-adjusted inpatient mortality, observed/expected LOS, ICU transfer after ward admission, 30-day readmission, emergency revisit, major bleeding, acute kidney injury, infection, venous thromboembolism, falls/pressure injury, medication reconciliation, timely specialty decision, unplanned procedure or return to the operating/procedure room, discharge-before-noon only as a balancing metric, and patient-reported understanding. Stratify by exact DRG, MCC/CC, procedure, age, frailty and discharge destination.
common_complications: neurologic deterioration, seizure, aspiration, delirium, intracranial bleeding, cerebral edema, falls, immobility-related harm and treatment complications. Also monitor medication-related harm, delirium, constipation/urinary retention, malnutrition, falls, pressure injury and failed transition; use diagnosis- and procedure-specific surveillance rather than assuming every risk applies to every member of this heterogeneous family.
readmission_drivers: Unresolved or recurrent primary disease, inadequate source/procedure control, medication discrepancy or nonadherence, poor symptom control, wound/device failure, infection, bleeding, renal injury, deconditioning, missed pending results, delayed specialty follow-up, inability to obtain medications/equipment and insufficient caregiver or transportation support.

admission_criteria: Admit when diagnostic uncertainty, symptom burden, treatment intensity or monitoring needs cannot be managed safely in observation or outpatient care; when there is shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation; or when severe comorbidity, frailty, unsafe function, inability to maintain hydration/nutrition, failed outpatient therapy or unreliable follow-up makes discharge unsafe. DRG assignment is retrospective and must never be used as the admission indication.
risk_stratification: At presentation document NEWS2/MEWS or the local deterioration score, frailty, baseline cognition/function, medication and antithrombotic exposure, renal/hepatic function, bleeding and VTE risk, infection risk, pressure-injury risk and goals of care. Escalate for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. Apply a validated disease- or procedure-specific score only when it fits the actual diagnosis; the DRG family itself is not a risk score.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, electrolytes, glucose, renal and hepatic function, magnesium, coagulation studies and toxicology or inflammatory/infectious testing when clinically indicated. Trend only abnormalities that can change treatment, define baseline organ function before contrast or high-risk medication, and avoid standing daily panels once stable.
initial_imaging_dx: focused neurologic examination with documented baseline; CT/CTA, MRI/MRA, EEG, lumbar puncture or vascular studies selected by syndrome and contraindications. Reconcile findings with the actual principal diagnosis and procedure; repeat imaging only for deterioration, an unanswered management question or a protocol-defined surveillance need.
time_critical_interventions: Record last-known-well and perform an immediate neurologic examination for acute deficits; activate stroke, seizure or neurosurgical emergency pathways without waiting for routine ward evaluation when criteria are met. Complete medication reconciliation, analgesia and prevention of avoidable delay at first assessment. If no emergency trigger is present, document the definitive diagnostic/procedural decision and level of care during the first attending review rather than applying a false universal time target.
initial_management: Use diagnosis-directed therapy with daily medication reconciliation, renal/hepatic dose adjustment, symptom control and explicit stop/reassessment dates for antimicrobials, fluids, oxygen, anticoagulation and other high-risk therapies. Avoid treatment based solely on the DRG family label. Tie each order to the actual neurologic and neurosurgical diagnosis, procedure and severity rather than the administrative grouping. Use goal-directed fluids, oxygen and transfusion rather than routine excess; provide multimodal analgesia and antiemetics; prevent delirium, pressure injury and falls; prescribe pharmacologic and/or mechanical VTE prophylaxis after individualized bleeding assessment; and define objective escalation, de-escalation and discharge criteria on admission.
day1_milestones: Within 24 hours: confirm the actual diagnosis and v43.1 grouping inputs, complete specialty and pharmacy reconciliation, close urgent diagnostic gaps, document treatment/procedure and disposition plans, establish pain/nausea control, initiate nutrition and mobility as safe, reconcile lines/tubes/devices, and communicate expected trajectory and contingency triggers to the patient/caregiver.
day2_milestones: By 48 hours: review clinical response and all cultures/pathology/imaging; narrow, stop or convert IV therapies when safe; remove unnecessary urinary/vascular devices; advance diet and activity; confirm procedure-specific restrictions and wound/device care; resolve oxygen, renal, bleeding and delirium risks; and lock the destination, equipment, medication-access and follow-up plan.
day3plus_milestones: For stays beyond day 3, conduct a daily interdisciplinary barrier review covering unresolved physiology, pending procedure/test, medication conversion, nutrition, mobility, cognition, caregiver training, placement and payer/logistics. Reassess goals of care and seek a second specialty or utilization review when trajectory or diagnosis remains unclear.
consults_multidisciplinary: neurology, neurosurgery or neurointerventional service as appropriate; rehabilitation therapies, pharmacy, case management and speech-language pathology when swallowing or cognition is affected. Clarify one accountable attending/service, the question for every consultation, response time for urgent issues and which team owns discharge medications, pathology/culture follow-up and post-discharge escalation.
monitoring_level: Use standard ward monitoring when stable; telemetry, step-down or ICU according to actual organ risk. Increase observation frequency for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. For procedural patients, use site/wound, neurovascular, airway, bleeding and device checks at locally defined intervals; de-escalate only after a documented period of stability.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Screen nutrition, swallowing, pressure-injury, fall and mobility risk on admission. Resume oral/enteral intake within 24 hours when clinically safe; use protein/calorie support for malnutrition or major wounds. Mobilize at least 3 times daily or follow explicit weight-bearing restrictions. Provide VTE prophylaxis within 24 hours when bleeding risk permits and document any exception plus reassessment date.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamics and oxygenation are stable at the intended destination level; urgent diagnosis and treatment are complete; pain/nausea and intake are manageable on the discharge regimen; mobility, cognition, wound/device care and elimination are safe; no unreviewed critical result remains; medication reconciliation is complete; and the patient/caregiver can state red flags and whom to contact.
discharge_planning: Begin on admission. Record baseline residence/function, caregiver capacity, transportation, language and medication access. Arrange destination, equipment/services, written restrictions and wound/device plan, pending-result owner, and specialty/primary-care follow-up—generally within 7 days for high-risk patients and 7–14 days for stable routine recovery. Complete teach-back and a 48–72-hour transition contact when risk is high.
expected_los: Typical planning range 2–5 days; instability, MCC, diagnostic uncertainty or functional barriers may require 6–10+ days.
target_los: Use a local risk-adjusted target median of 3–4 days; aim for next-day discharge after stability when the diagnosis is secure and treatment, function and follow-up can be completed safely.
quality_metrics: Track risk-adjusted inpatient mortality, observed/expected LOS, ICU transfer after ward admission, 30-day readmission, emergency revisit, major bleeding, acute kidney injury, infection, venous thromboembolism, falls/pressure injury, medication reconciliation, timely specialty decision, unplanned procedure or return to the operating/procedure room, discharge-before-noon only as a balancing metric, and patient-reported understanding. Stratify by exact DRG, MCC/CC, procedure, age, frailty and discharge destination.
common_complications: neurologic deterioration, seizure, aspiration, delirium, intracranial bleeding, cerebral edema, falls, immobility-related harm and treatment complications. Also monitor medication-related harm, delirium, constipation/urinary retention, malnutrition, falls, pressure injury and failed transition; use diagnosis- and procedure-specific surveillance rather than assuming every risk applies to every member of this heterogeneous family.
readmission_drivers: Unresolved or recurrent primary disease, inadequate source/procedure control, medication discrepancy or nonadherence, poor symptom control, wound/device failure, infection, bleeding, renal injury, deconditioning, missed pending results, delayed specialty follow-up, inability to obtain medications/equipment and insufficient caregiver or transportation support.

admission_criteria: Admit when diagnostic uncertainty, symptom burden, treatment intensity or monitoring needs cannot be managed safely in observation or outpatient care; when there is shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation; or when severe comorbidity, frailty, unsafe function, inability to maintain hydration/nutrition, failed outpatient therapy or unreliable follow-up makes discharge unsafe. DRG assignment is retrospective and must never be used as the admission indication.
risk_stratification: At presentation document NEWS2/MEWS or the local deterioration score, frailty, baseline cognition/function, medication and antithrombotic exposure, renal/hepatic function, bleeding and VTE risk, infection risk, pressure-injury risk and goals of care. Escalate for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. Apply a validated disease- or procedure-specific score only when it fits the actual diagnosis; the DRG family itself is not a risk score.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, electrolytes, renal and hepatic panels, lipase when appropriate, coagulation profile, lactate for ischemia or shock, type and screen for bleeding or surgery, and cultures for suspected infection. Trend only abnormalities that can change treatment, define baseline organ function before contrast or high-risk medication, and avoid standing daily panels once stable.
initial_imaging_dx: targeted abdominal radiography or ultrasound; contrast CT, endoscopy, fluoroscopic study or pathology review selected by suspected obstruction, perforation, bleeding, malignancy or postoperative complication. Reconcile findings with the actual principal diagnosis and procedure; repeat imaging only for deterioration, an unanswered management question or a protocol-defined surveillance need.
time_critical_interventions: This heterogeneous DRG family has no single universal treatment clock; stabilize airway, breathing and circulation immediately and obtain specialty review within 1 hour when organ, limb, airway or hemodynamic deterioration is possible. Complete medication reconciliation, analgesia and prevention of avoidable delay at first assessment. If no emergency trigger is present, document the definitive diagnostic/procedural decision and level of care during the first attending review rather than applying a false universal time target.
initial_management: Use diagnosis-directed therapy with daily medication reconciliation, renal/hepatic dose adjustment, symptom control and explicit stop/reassessment dates for antimicrobials, fluids, oxygen, anticoagulation and other high-risk therapies. Avoid treatment based solely on the DRG family label. Tie each order to the actual gastrointestinal and general surgical diagnosis, procedure and severity rather than the administrative grouping. Use goal-directed fluids, oxygen and transfusion rather than routine excess; provide multimodal analgesia and antiemetics; prevent delirium, pressure injury and falls; prescribe pharmacologic and/or mechanical VTE prophylaxis after individualized bleeding assessment; and define objective escalation, de-escalation and discharge criteria on admission.
day1_milestones: Within 24 hours: confirm the actual diagnosis and v43.1 grouping inputs, complete specialty and pharmacy reconciliation, close urgent diagnostic gaps, document treatment/procedure and disposition plans, establish pain/nausea control, initiate nutrition and mobility as safe, reconcile lines/tubes/devices, and communicate expected trajectory and contingency triggers to the patient/caregiver.
day2_milestones: By 48 hours: review clinical response and all cultures/pathology/imaging; narrow, stop or convert IV therapies when safe; remove unnecessary urinary/vascular devices; advance diet and activity; confirm procedure-specific restrictions and wound/device care; resolve oxygen, renal, bleeding and delirium risks; and lock the destination, equipment, medication-access and follow-up plan.
day3plus_milestones: For stays beyond day 3, conduct a daily interdisciplinary barrier review covering unresolved physiology, pending procedure/test, medication conversion, nutrition, mobility, cognition, caregiver training, placement and payer/logistics. Reassess goals of care and seek a second specialty or utilization review when trajectory or diagnosis remains unclear.
consults_multidisciplinary: gastroenterology, general/colorectal surgery and anesthesia as relevant; nutrition, wound/ostomy nursing, pharmacy, rehabilitation and case management. Clarify one accountable attending/service, the question for every consultation, response time for urgent issues and which team owns discharge medications, pathology/culture follow-up and post-discharge escalation.
monitoring_level: Use standard ward monitoring when stable; telemetry, step-down or ICU according to actual organ risk. Increase observation frequency for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. For procedural patients, use site/wound, neurovascular, airway, bleeding and device checks at locally defined intervals; de-escalate only after a documented period of stability.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Screen nutrition, swallowing, pressure-injury, fall and mobility risk on admission. Resume oral/enteral intake within 24 hours when clinically safe; use protein/calorie support for malnutrition or major wounds. Mobilize at least 3 times daily or follow explicit weight-bearing restrictions. Provide VTE prophylaxis within 24 hours when bleeding risk permits and document any exception plus reassessment date.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamics and oxygenation are stable at the intended destination level; urgent diagnosis and treatment are complete; pain/nausea and intake are manageable on the discharge regimen; mobility, cognition, wound/device care and elimination are safe; no unreviewed critical result remains; medication reconciliation is complete; and the patient/caregiver can state red flags and whom to contact.
discharge_planning: Begin on admission. Record baseline residence/function, caregiver capacity, transportation, language and medication access. Arrange destination, equipment/services, written restrictions and wound/device plan, pending-result owner, and specialty/primary-care follow-up—generally within 7 days for high-risk patients and 7–14 days for stable routine recovery. Complete teach-back and a 48–72-hour transition contact when risk is high.
expected_los: Typical planning range 2–5 days; instability, MCC, diagnostic uncertainty or functional barriers may require 6–10+ days.
target_los: Use a local risk-adjusted target median of 3–4 days; aim for next-day discharge after stability when the diagnosis is secure and treatment, function and follow-up can be completed safely.
quality_metrics: Track risk-adjusted inpatient mortality, observed/expected LOS, ICU transfer after ward admission, 30-day readmission, emergency revisit, major bleeding, acute kidney injury, infection, venous thromboembolism, falls/pressure injury, medication reconciliation, timely specialty decision, unplanned procedure or return to the operating/procedure room, discharge-before-noon only as a balancing metric, and patient-reported understanding. Stratify by exact DRG, MCC/CC, procedure, age, frailty and discharge destination.
common_complications: bleeding, anastomotic leak, perforation, ileus or obstruction, intra-abdominal infection, aspiration, malnutrition, venous thromboembolism and acute kidney injury. Also monitor medication-related harm, delirium, constipation/urinary retention, malnutrition, falls, pressure injury and failed transition; use diagnosis- and procedure-specific surveillance rather than assuming every risk applies to every member of this heterogeneous family.
readmission_drivers: Unresolved or recurrent primary disease, inadequate source/procedure control, medication discrepancy or nonadherence, poor symptom control, wound/device failure, infection, bleeding, renal injury, deconditioning, missed pending results, delayed specialty follow-up, inability to obtain medications/equipment and insufficient caregiver or transportation support.

quality_metrics: Track risk-adjusted inpatient mortality, observed/expected LOS, ICU transfer after ward admission, 30-day readmission, emergency revisit, major bleeding, acute kidney injury, infection, venous thromboembolism, falls/pressure injury, medication reconciliation, timely specialty decision, unplanned procedure or return to the operating/procedure room, discharge-before-noon only as a balancing metric, and patient-reported understanding. common_complications: occult hemorrhage, compartment syndrome, infection, organ or neurovascular injury, venous thromboembolism, delirium, pain, pressure injury and loss of function. condition: Upper Limb and Toe Amputation for Circulatory System Disorders
admission_criteria: Admit when the operative or invasive intervention and postoperative monitoring cannot be delivered safely in an ambulatory setting; when there is shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation; or when severe comorbidity, frailty, unsafe function, inability to maintain hydration/nutrition, failed outpatient therapy or unreliable follow-up makes discharge unsafe. DRG assignment is retrospective and must never be used as the admission indication.
risk_stratification: At presentation document NEWS2/MEWS or the local deterioration score, frailty, baseline cognition/function, medication and antithrombotic exposure, renal/hepatic function, bleeding and VTE risk, infection risk, pressure-injury risk and goals of care. Escalate for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. Apply a validated disease- or procedure-specific score only when it fits the actual diagnosis; the DRG family itself is not a risk score.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, electrolytes, creatinine, magnesium, coagulation profile, type and screen; serial troponin, natriuretic peptide, lactate or blood gas when ischemia, failure or shock is possible. Trend only abnormalities that can change treatment, define baseline organ function before contrast or high-risk medication, and avoid standing daily panels once stable.
initial_imaging_dx: 12-lead ECG and telemetry; transthoracic or transesophageal echocardiography, vascular ultrasound, CT angiography, catheterization or device interrogation according to the syndrome. Reconcile findings with the actual principal diagnosis and procedure; repeat imaging only for deterioration, an unanswered management question or a protocol-defined surveillance need.
time_critical_interventions: Obtain ECG within 10 minutes for ischemic symptoms, activate emergency reperfusion or hemodynamic support pathways when indicated, and obtain immediate specialist review for shock, sustained arrhythmia, tamponade or device failure. Complete medication reconciliation, analgesia and prevention of avoidable delay at first assessment. If no emergency trigger is present, document the definitive diagnostic/procedural decision and level of care during the first attending review rather than applying a false universal time target.
initial_management: Confirm indication, laterality/site, consent, blood availability, antimicrobial prophylaxis only when indicated, anesthesia plan, medication/antithrombotic management and postoperative destination in a same-day multidisciplinary huddle. Use a procedure-specific checklist and do not substitute anticipated DRG assignment for clinical criteria. Tie each order to the actual cardiovascular and endovascular diagnosis, procedure and severity rather than the administrative grouping. Use goal-directed fluids, oxygen and transfusion rather than routine excess; provide multimodal analgesia and antiemetics; prevent delirium, pressure injury and falls; prescribe pharmacologic and/or mechanical VTE prophylaxis after individualized bleeding assessment; and define objective escalation, de-escalation and discharge criteria on admission.
day1_milestones: Within 24 hours: confirm the actual diagnosis and v43.1 grouping inputs, complete specialty and pharmacy reconciliation, close urgent diagnostic gaps, document treatment/procedure and disposition plans, establish pain/nausea control, initiate nutrition and mobility as safe, reconcile lines/tubes/devices, and communicate expected trajectory and contingency triggers to the patient/caregiver.
day2_milestones: By 48 hours: review clinical response and all cultures/pathology/imaging; narrow, stop or convert IV therapies when safe; remove unnecessary urinary/vascular devices; advance diet and activity; confirm procedure-specific restrictions and wound/device care; resolve oxygen, renal, bleeding and delirium risks; and lock the destination, equipment, medication-access and follow-up plan.
day3plus_milestones: For stays beyond day 3, conduct a daily interdisciplinary barrier review covering unresolved physiology, pending procedure/test, medication conversion, nutrition, mobility, cognition, caregiver training, placement and payer/logistics. Reassess goals of care and seek a second specialty or utilization review when trajectory or diagnosis remains unclear.
consults_multidisciplinary: cardiology, electrophysiology, interventional cardiology, vascular or cardiac surgery and anesthesia as relevant; pharmacy, rehabilitation, case management and palliative care for advanced disease. Clarify one accountable attending/service, the question for every consultation, response time for urgent issues and which team owns discharge medications, pathology/culture follow-up and post-discharge escalation.
monitoring_level: Use standard ward monitoring when stable; telemetry, step-down or ICU according to actual organ risk. Increase observation frequency for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. For procedural patients, use site/wound, neurovascular, airway, bleeding and device checks at locally defined intervals; de-escalate only after a documented period of stability.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Screen nutrition, swallowing, pressure-injury, fall and mobility risk on admission. Resume oral/enteral intake within 24 hours when clinically safe; use protein/calorie support for malnutrition or major wounds. Mobilize at least 3 times daily or follow explicit weight-bearing restrictions. Provide VTE prophylaxis within 24 hours when bleeding risk permits and document any exception plus reassessment date.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamics and oxygenation are stable at the intended destination level; urgent diagnosis and treatment are complete; pain/nausea and intake are manageable on the discharge regimen; mobility, cognition, wound/device care and elimination are safe; no unreviewed critical result remains; medication reconciliation is complete; and the patient/caregiver can state red flags and whom to contact.
discharge_planning: Begin on admission. Record baseline residence/function, caregiver capacity, transportation, language and medication access. Arrange destination, equipment/services, written restrictions and wound/device plan, pending-result owner, and specialty/primary-care follow-up—generally within 7 days for high-risk patients and 7–14 days for stable routine recovery. Complete teach-back and a 48–72-hour transition contact when risk is high.
expected_los: Typical planning range 2–6 days; uncomplicated minimally invasive cases may be 1–3 days, while MCC, open surgery or major complication may require 7–10+ days.
target_los: Use a local risk-adjusted target median of 3–5 days, stratified by procedure and MCC status; pursue discharge by postoperative day 1–3 only when all physiologic, mobility and support criteria are met.
quality_metrics: Track risk-adjusted inpatient mortality, observed/expected LOS, ICU transfer after ward admission, 30-day readmission, emergency revisit, major bleeding, acute kidney injury, infection, venous thromboembolism, falls/pressure injury, medication reconciliation, timely specialty decision, unplanned procedure or return to the operating/procedure room, discharge-before-noon only as a balancing metric, and patient-reported understanding. Stratify by exact DRG, MCC/CC, procedure, age, frailty and discharge destination.
common_complications: arrhythmia, ischemia, stroke, access-site bleeding, vascular injury, tamponade, acute kidney injury, heart failure, infection and device or procedure failure. Also monitor medication-related harm, delirium, constipation/urinary retention, malnutrition, falls, pressure injury and failed transition; use diagnosis- and procedure-specific surveillance rather than assuming every risk applies to every member of this heterogeneous family.
readmission_drivers: Unresolved or recurrent primary disease, inadequate source/procedure control, medication discrepancy or nonadherence, poor symptom control, wound/device failure, infection, bleeding, renal injury, deconditioning, missed pending results, delayed specialty follow-up, inability to obtain medications/equipment and insufficient caregiver or transportation support.

condition: Other Hepatobiliary OR Pancreas O.R. Procedures
admission_criteria: Admit when the operative or invasive intervention and postoperative monitoring cannot be delivered safely in an ambulatory setting; when there is shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation; or when severe comorbidity, frailty, unsafe function, inability to maintain hydration/nutrition, failed outpatient therapy or unreliable follow-up makes discharge unsafe. DRG assignment is retrospective and must never be used as the admission indication.
risk_stratification: At presentation document NEWS2/MEWS or the local deterioration score, frailty, baseline cognition/function, medication and antithrombotic exposure, renal/hepatic function, bleeding and VTE risk, infection risk, pressure-injury risk and goals of care. Escalate for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. Apply a validated disease- or procedure-specific score only when it fits the actual diagnosis; the DRG family itself is not a risk score.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, comprehensive metabolic and hepatic panels, bilirubin fractions, lipase, INR, lactate when unstable, type and screen and cultures before antibiotics when cholangitis or infected necrosis is possible. Trend only abnormalities that can change treatment, define baseline organ function before contrast or high-risk medication, and avoid standing daily panels once stable.
initial_imaging_dx: right-upper-quadrant ultrasound or protocol CT; MRCP, ERCP, endoscopic ultrasound, angiography or pathology review when it will change intervention. Reconcile findings with the actual principal diagnosis and procedure; repeat imaging only for deterioration, an unanswered management question or a protocol-defined surveillance need.
time_critical_interventions: This heterogeneous DRG family has no single universal treatment clock; stabilize airway, breathing and circulation immediately and obtain specialty review within 1 hour when organ, limb, airway or hemodynamic deterioration is possible. Complete medication reconciliation, analgesia and prevention of avoidable delay at first assessment. If no emergency trigger is present, document the definitive diagnostic/procedural decision and level of care during the first attending review rather than applying a false universal time target.
initial_management: Confirm indication, laterality/site, consent, blood availability, antimicrobial prophylaxis only when indicated, anesthesia plan, medication/antithrombotic management and postoperative destination in a same-day multidisciplinary huddle. Use a procedure-specific checklist and do not substitute anticipated DRG assignment for clinical criteria. Tie each order to the actual hepatobiliary and pancreatic diagnosis, procedure and severity rather than the administrative grouping. Use goal-directed fluids, oxygen and transfusion rather than routine excess; provide multimodal analgesia and antiemetics; prevent delirium, pressure injury and falls; prescribe pharmacologic and/or mechanical VTE prophylaxis after individualized bleeding assessment; and define objective escalation, de-escalation and discharge criteria on admission.
day1_milestones: Within 24 hours: confirm the actual diagnosis and v43.1 grouping inputs, complete specialty and pharmacy reconciliation, close urgent diagnostic gaps, document treatment/procedure and disposition plans, establish pain/nausea control, initiate nutrition and mobility as safe, reconcile lines/tubes/devices, and communicate expected trajectory and contingency triggers to the patient/caregiver.
day2_milestones: By 48 hours: review clinical response and all cultures/pathology/imaging; narrow, stop or convert IV therapies when safe; remove unnecessary urinary/vascular devices; advance diet and activity; confirm procedure-specific restrictions and wound/device care; resolve oxygen, renal, bleeding and delirium risks; and lock the destination, equipment, medication-access and follow-up plan.
day3plus_milestones: For stays beyond day 3, conduct a daily interdisciplinary barrier review covering unresolved physiology, pending procedure/test, medication conversion, nutrition, mobility, cognition, caregiver training, placement and payer/logistics. Reassess goals of care and seek a second specialty or utilization review when trajectory or diagnosis remains unclear.
consults_multidisciplinary: gastroenterology/hepatology, interventional endoscopy, hepatobiliary surgery, interventional radiology and critical care as indicated; nutrition, pharmacy and case management. Clarify one accountable attending/service, the question for every consultation, response time for urgent issues and which team owns discharge medications, pathology/culture follow-up and post-discharge escalation.
monitoring_level: Use standard ward monitoring when stable; telemetry, step-down or ICU according to actual organ risk. Increase observation frequency for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. For procedural patients, use site/wound, neurovascular, airway, bleeding and device checks at locally defined intervals; de-escalate only after a documented period of stability.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Screen nutrition, swallowing, pressure-injury, fall and mobility risk on admission. Resume oral/enteral intake within 24 hours when clinically safe; use protein/calorie support for malnutrition or major wounds. Mobilize at least 3 times daily or follow explicit weight-bearing restrictions. Provide VTE prophylaxis within 24 hours when bleeding risk permits and document any exception plus reassessment date.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamics and oxygenation are stable at the intended destination level; urgent diagnosis and treatment are complete; pain/nausea and intake are manageable on the discharge regimen; mobility, cognition, wound/device care and elimination are safe; no unreviewed critical result remains; medication reconciliation is complete; and the patient/caregiver can state red flags and whom to contact.
discharge_planning: Begin on admission. Record baseline residence/function, caregiver capacity, transportation, language and medication access. Arrange destination, equipment/services, written restrictions and wound/device plan, pending-result owner, and specialty/primary-care follow-up—generally within 7 days for high-risk patients and 7–14 days for stable routine recovery. Complete teach-back and a 48–72-hour transition contact when risk is high.
expected_los: Typical planning range 2–6 days; uncomplicated minimally invasive cases may be 1–3 days, while MCC, open surgery or major complication may require 7–10+ days.
target_los: Use a local risk-adjusted target median of 3–5 days, stratified by procedure and MCC status; pursue discharge by postoperative day 1–3 only when all physiologic, mobility and support criteria are met.
quality_metrics: Track risk-adjusted inpatient mortality, observed/expected LOS, ICU transfer after ward admission, 30-day readmission, emergency revisit, major bleeding, acute kidney injury, infection, venous thromboembolism, falls/pressure injury, medication reconciliation, timely specialty decision, unplanned procedure or return to the operating/procedure room, discharge-before-noon only as a balancing metric, and patient-reported understanding. Stratify by exact DRG, MCC/CC, procedure, age, frailty and discharge destination.
common_complications: biliary sepsis, pancreatitis, bleeding, bile or pancreatic leak, hepatic failure, acute kidney injury, ileus, malnutrition and venous thromboembolism. Also monitor medication-related harm, delirium, constipation/urinary retention, malnutrition, falls, pressure injury and failed transition; use diagnosis- and procedure-specific surveillance rather than assuming every risk applies to every member of this heterogeneous family.
readmission_drivers: Unresolved or recurrent primary disease, inadequate source/procedure control, medication discrepancy or nonadherence, poor symptom control, wound/device failure, infection, bleeding, renal injury, deconditioning, missed pending results, delayed specialty follow-up, inability to obtain medications/equipment and insufficient caregiver or transportation support.
guideline_source: MDC-level clinical safety anchor from audited catalog rank 23 (Cholecystectomy / Biliary Procedures); not asserted as a condition-specific guideline for this heterogeneous tail family. Tokyo Guidelines 2018 (TG18; Japanese Society of Hepato-Biliary-Pancreatic Surgery / international consensus)-diagnosis, severity grading, management flowchart, and antimicrobial therapy for acute cholecystitis and cholangitis. World Society of Emergency Surgery (WSES) 2020 updated guidelines for acute calculous cholecystitis. SAGES/AHPBA/IHPBA/SSAT/EAES multi-society Safe Cholecystectomy practice guideline 2020 (bile duct injury prevention/Critical View of Safety). ASGE 2019 guideline on endoscopy in evaluation and management of choledocholithiasis. Reinforced by ACDC, CHOCOLATE, and PONCHO randomized trials.

If no emergency trigger is present, document the definitive diagnostic/procedural decision and level of care during the first attending review rather than applying a false universal time target.
initial_management: Confirm indication, laterality/site, consent, blood availability, antimicrobial prophylaxis only when indicated, anesthesia plan, medication/antithrombotic management and postoperative destination in a same-day multidisciplinary huddle. Use a procedure-specific checklist and do not substitute anticipated DRG assignment for clinical criteria. Tie each order to the actual high-acuity perioperative and critical care diagnosis, procedure and severity rather than the administrative grouping. Use goal-directed fluids, oxygen and transfusion rather than routine excess; provide multimodal analgesia and antiemetics; prevent delirium, pressure injury and falls; prescribe pharmacologic and/or mechanical VTE prophylaxis after individualized bleeding assessment; and define objective escalation, de-escalation and discharge criteria on admission.
day1_milestones: Within 24 hours: confirm the actual diagnosis and v43.1 grouping inputs, complete specialty and pharmacy reconciliation, close urgent diagnostic gaps, document treatment/procedure and disposition plans, establish pain/nausea control, initiate nutrition and mobility as safe, reconcile lines/tubes/devices, and communicate expected trajectory and contingency triggers to the patient/caregiver.
day2_milestones: By 48 hours: review clinical response and all cultures/pathology/imaging; narrow, stop or convert IV therapies when safe; remove unnecessary urinary/vascular devices; advance diet and activity; confirm procedure-specific restrictions and wound/device care; resolve oxygen, renal, bleeding and delirium risks; and lock the destination, equipment, medication-access and follow-up plan.
day3plus_milestones: For stays beyond day 3, conduct a daily interdisciplinary barrier review covering unresolved physiology, pending procedure/test, medication conversion, nutrition, mobility, cognition, caregiver training, placement and payer/logistics. Reassess goals of care and seek a second specialty or utilization review when trajectory or diagnosis remains unclear.
consults_multidisciplinary: critical care, anesthesia, the operating procedural service, hospital medicine, pharmacy, nutrition, rehabilitation, case management and palliative care when goals or prognosis are uncertain. Clarify one accountable attending/service, the question for every consultation, response time for urgent issues and which team owns discharge medications, pathology/culture follow-up and post-discharge escalation.
monitoring_level: Use standard ward monitoring when stable; telemetry, step-down or ICU according to actual organ risk. Increase observation frequency for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. For procedural patients, use site/wound, neurovascular, airway, bleeding and device checks at locally defined intervals; de-escalate only after a documented period of stability.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Screen nutrition, swallowing, pressure-injury, fall and mobility risk on admission. Resume oral/enteral intake within 24 hours when clinically safe; use protein/calorie support for malnutrition or major wounds. Mobilize at least 3 times daily or follow explicit weight-bearing restrictions. Provide VTE prophylaxis within 24 hours when bleeding risk permits and document any exception plus reassessment date.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamics and oxygenation are stable at the intended destination level; urgent diagnosis and treatment are complete; pain/nausea and intake are manageable on the discharge regimen; mobility, cognition, wound/device care and elimination are safe; no unreviewed critical result remains; medication reconciliation is complete; and the patient/caregiver can state red flags and whom to contact.
discharge_planning: Begin on admission. Record baseline residence/function, caregiver capacity, transportation, language and medication access. Arrange destination, equipment/services, written restrictions and wound/device plan, pending-result owner, and specialty/primary-care follow-up—generally within 7 days for high-risk patients and 7–14 days for stable routine recovery. Complete teach-back and a 48–72-hour transition contact when risk is high.
expected_los: Typical planning range 5–14 days; transplant, prolonged ventilation, major complication or rehabilitation needs may extend beyond 14 days.
target_los: Set a local risk-adjusted median target at or below 10 days only after separating transplant, ventilation and outlier cohorts; never discharge to meet a numeric target.
quality_metrics: Track risk-adjusted inpatient mortality, observed/expected LOS, ICU transfer after ward admission, 30-day readmission, emergency revisit, major bleeding, acute kidney injury, infection, venous thromboembolism, falls/pressure injury, medication reconciliation, timely specialty decision, unplanned procedure or return to the operating/procedure room, discharge-before-noon only as a balancing metric, and patient-reported understanding. Stratify by exact DRG, MCC/CC, procedure, age, frailty and discharge destination.
common_complications: shock, respiratory failure, bleeding, infection, delirium, acute kidney injury, device or procedure failure, venous thromboembolism, pressure injury and severe deconditioning. Also monitor medication-related harm, delirium, constipation/urinary retention, malnutrition, falls, pressure injury and failed transition; use diagnosis- and procedure-specific surveillance rather than assuming every risk applies to every member of this heterogeneous family.
readmission_drivers: Unresolved or recurrent primary disease, inadequate source/procedure control, medication discrepancy or nonadherence, poor symptom control, wound/device failure, infection, bleeding, renal injury, deconditioning, missed pending results, delayed specialty follow-up, inability to obtain medications/equipment and insufficient caregiver or transportation support.

pathway_pearls: Treat the patient’s actual diagnosis and procedure, not the label of a heterogeneous DRG family. condition: Biliary Tract Procedures Except Only Cholecystectomy with OR without C.D.E.
admission_criteria: Admit when the operative or invasive intervention and postoperative monitoring cannot be delivered safely in an ambulatory setting; when there is shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation; or when severe comorbidity, frailty, unsafe function, inability to maintain hydration/nutrition, failed outpatient therapy or unreliable follow-up makes discharge unsafe. DRG assignment is retrospective and must never be used as the admission indication.
risk_stratification: At presentation document NEWS2/MEWS or the local deterioration score, frailty, baseline cognition/function, medication and antithrombotic exposure, renal/hepatic function, bleeding and VTE risk, infection risk, pressure-injury risk and goals of care. Escalate for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. Apply a validated disease- or procedure-specific score only when it fits the actual diagnosis; the DRG family itself is not a risk score.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, comprehensive metabolic and hepatic panels, bilirubin fractions, lipase, INR, lactate when unstable, type and screen and cultures before antibiotics when cholangitis or infected necrosis is possible. Trend only abnormalities that can change treatment, define baseline organ function before contrast or high-risk medication, and avoid standing daily panels once stable.
initial_imaging_dx: right-upper-quadrant ultrasound or protocol CT; MRCP, ERCP, endoscopic ultrasound, angiography or pathology review when it will change intervention. Reconcile findings with the actual principal diagnosis and procedure; repeat imaging only for deterioration, an unanswered management question or a protocol-defined surveillance need.
time_critical_interventions: This heterogeneous DRG family has no single universal treatment clock; stabilize airway, breathing and circulation immediately and obtain specialty review within 1 hour when organ, limb, airway or hemodynamic deterioration is possible. Complete medication reconciliation, analgesia and prevention of avoidable delay at first assessment. If no emergency trigger is present, document the definitive diagnostic/procedural decision and level of care during the first attending review rather than applying a false universal time target.
initial_management: Confirm indication, laterality/site, consent, blood availability, antimicrobial prophylaxis only when indicated, anesthesia plan, medication/antithrombotic management and postoperative destination in a same-day multidisciplinary huddle. Use a procedure-specific checklist and do not substitute anticipated DRG assignment for clinical criteria. Tie each order to the actual hepatobiliary and pancreatic diagnosis, procedure and severity rather than the administrative grouping. Use goal-directed fluids, oxygen and transfusion rather than routine excess; provide multimodal analgesia and antiemetics; prevent delirium, pressure injury and falls; prescribe pharmacologic and/or mechanical VTE prophylaxis after individualized bleeding assessment; and define objective escalation, de-escalation and discharge criteria on admission.
day1_milestones: Within 24 hours: confirm the actual diagnosis and v43.1 grouping inputs, complete specialty and pharmacy reconciliation, close urgent diagnostic gaps, document treatment/procedure and disposition plans, establish pain/nausea control, initiate nutrition and mobility as safe, reconcile lines/tubes/devices, and communicate expected trajectory and contingency triggers to the patient/caregiver.
day2_milestones: By 48 hours: review clinical response and all cultures/pathology/imaging; narrow, stop or convert IV therapies when safe; remove unnecessary urinary/vascular devices; advance diet and activity; confirm procedure-specific restrictions and wound/device care; resolve oxygen, renal, bleeding and delirium risks; and lock the destination, equipment, medication-access and follow-up plan.
day3plus_milestones: For stays beyond day 3, conduct a daily interdisciplinary barrier review covering unresolved physiology, pending procedure/test, medication conversion, nutrition, mobility, cognition, caregiver training, placement and payer/logistics. Reassess goals of care and seek a second specialty or utilization review when trajectory or diagnosis remains unclear.
consults_multidisciplinary: gastroenterology/hepatology, interventional endoscopy, hepatobiliary surgery, interventional radiology and critical care as indicated; nutrition, pharmacy and case management. Clarify one accountable attending/service, the question for every consultation, response time for urgent issues and which team owns discharge medications, pathology/culture follow-up and post-discharge escalation.
monitoring_level: Use standard ward monitoring when stable; telemetry, step-down or ICU according to actual organ risk. Increase observation frequency for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. For procedural patients, use site/wound, neurovascular, airway, bleeding and device checks at locally defined intervals; de-escalate only after a documented period of stability.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Screen nutrition, swallowing, pressure-injury, fall and mobility risk on admission. Resume oral/enteral intake within 24 hours when clinically safe; use protein/calorie support for malnutrition or major wounds. Mobilize at least 3 times daily or follow explicit weight-bearing restrictions. Provide VTE prophylaxis within 24 hours when bleeding risk permits and document any exception plus reassessment date.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamics and oxygenation are stable at the intended destination level; urgent diagnosis and treatment are complete; pain/nausea and intake are manageable on the discharge regimen; mobility, cognition, wound/device care and elimination are safe; no unreviewed critical result remains; medication reconciliation is complete; and the patient/caregiver can state red flags and whom to contact.
discharge_planning: Begin on admission. Record baseline residence/function, caregiver capacity, transportation, language and medication access. Arrange destination, equipment/services, written restrictions and wound/device plan, pending-result owner, and specialty/primary-care follow-up—generally within 7 days for high-risk patients and 7–14 days for stable routine recovery. Complete teach-back and a 48–72-hour transition contact when risk is high.
expected_los: Typical planning range 2–6 days; uncomplicated minimally invasive cases may be 1–3 days, while MCC, open surgery or major complication may require 7–10+ days.
target_los: Use a local risk-adjusted target median of 3–5 days, stratified by procedure and MCC status; pursue discharge by postoperative day 1–3 only when all physiologic, mobility and support criteria are met.
quality_metrics: Track risk-adjusted inpatient mortality, observed/expected LOS, ICU transfer after ward admission, 30-day readmission, emergency revisit, major bleeding, acute kidney injury, infection, venous thromboembolism, falls/pressure injury, medication reconciliation, timely specialty decision, unplanned procedure or return to the operating/procedure room, discharge-before-noon only as a balancing metric, and patient-reported understanding. Stratify by exact DRG, MCC/CC, procedure, age, frailty and discharge destination.
common_complications: biliary sepsis, pancreatitis, bleeding, bile or pancreatic leak, hepatic failure, acute kidney injury, ileus, malnutrition and venous thromboembolism. Also monitor medication-related harm, delirium, constipation/urinary retention, malnutrition, falls, pressure injury and failed transition; use diagnosis- and procedure-specific surveillance rather than assuming every risk applies to every member of this heterogeneous family.
readmission_drivers: Unresolved or recurrent primary disease, inadequate source/procedure control, medication discrepancy or nonadherence, poor symptom control, wound/device failure, infection, bleeding, renal injury, deconditioning, missed pending results, delayed specialty follow-up, inability to obtain medications/equipment and insufficient caregiver or transportation support.

ASGE 2019 guideline on endoscopy in evaluation and management of choledocholithiasis. Reinforced by ACDC, CHOCOLATE, and PONCHO randomized trials.

day3plus_milestones: For stays beyond day 3, conduct a daily interdisciplinary barrier review covering unresolved physiology, pending procedure/test, medication conversion, nutrition, mobility, cognition, caregiver training, placement and payer/logistics. Reassess goals of care and seek a second specialty or utilization review when trajectory or diagnosis remains unclear.
consults_multidisciplinary: cardiology, electrophysiology, interventional cardiology, vascular or cardiac surgery and anesthesia as relevant; pharmacy, rehabilitation, case management and palliative care for advanced disease. Clarify one accountable attending/service, the question for every consultation, response time for urgent issues and which team owns discharge medications, pathology/culture follow-up and post-discharge escalation.
monitoring_level: Use standard ward monitoring when stable; telemetry, step-down or ICU according to actual organ risk. Increase observation frequency for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. For procedural patients, use site/wound, neurovascular, airway, bleeding and device checks at locally defined intervals; de-escalate only after a documented period of stability.
nutrition_mobility_vte: Screen nutrition, swallowing, pressure-injury, fall and mobility risk on admission. Resume oral/enteral intake within 24 hours when clinically safe; use protein/calorie support for malnutrition or major wounds. Mobilize at least 3 times daily or follow explicit weight-bearing restrictions. Provide VTE prophylaxis within 24 hours when bleeding risk permits and document any exception plus reassessment date.
discharge_criteria: Hemodynamics and oxygenation are stable at the intended destination level; urgent diagnosis and treatment are complete; pain/nausea and intake are manageable on the discharge regimen; mobility, cognition, wound/device care and elimination are safe; no unreviewed critical result remains; medication reconciliation is complete; and the patient/caregiver can state red flags and whom to contact.
discharge_planning: Begin on admission. Record baseline residence/function, caregiver capacity, transportation, language and medication access. Arrange destination, equipment/services, written restrictions and wound/device plan, pending-result owner, and specialty/primary-care follow-up—generally within 7 days for high-risk patients and 7–14 days for stable routine recovery. Complete teach-back and a 48–72-hour transition contact when risk is high.
expected_los: Typical planning range 2–6 days; uncomplicated minimally invasive cases may be 1–3 days, while MCC, open surgery or major complication may require 7–10+ days.
target_los: Use a local risk-adjusted target median of 3–5 days, stratified by procedure and MCC status; pursue discharge by postoperative day 1–3 only when all physiologic, mobility and support criteria are met.
quality_metrics: Track risk-adjusted inpatient mortality, observed/expected LOS, ICU transfer after ward admission, 30-day readmission, emergency revisit, major bleeding, acute kidney injury, infection, venous thromboembolism, falls/pressure injury, medication reconciliation, timely specialty decision, unplanned procedure or return to the operating/procedure room, discharge-before-noon only as a balancing metric, and patient-reported understanding. Stratify by exact DRG, MCC/CC, procedure, age, frailty and discharge destination.
common_complications: arrhythmia, ischemia, stroke, access-site bleeding, vascular injury, tamponade, acute kidney injury, heart failure, infection and device or procedure failure. Also monitor medication-related harm, delirium, constipation/urinary retention, malnutrition, falls, pressure injury and failed transition; use diagnosis- and procedure-specific surveillance rather than assuming every risk applies to every member of this heterogeneous family.
readmission_drivers: Unresolved or recurrent primary disease, inadequate source/procedure control, medication discrepancy or nonadherence, poor symptom control, wound/device failure, infection, bleeding, renal injury, deconditioning, missed pending results, delayed specialty follow-up, inability to obtain medications/equipment and insufficient caregiver or transportation support.

pathway_pearls: Treat the patient’s actual diagnosis and procedure, not the label of a heterogeneous DRG family. Put instability, anatomy/site, source or procedural control, function, medication access and pending-result ownership on the daily checklist. Any local order set should branch by the true clinical syndrome and exact procedure.

admission_criteria: Admit when the operative or invasive intervention and postoperative monitoring cannot be delivered safely in an ambulatory setting; when there is shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation; or when severe comorbidity, frailty, unsafe function, inability to maintain hydration/nutrition, failed outpatient therapy or unreliable follow-up makes discharge unsafe. DRG assignment is retrospective and must never be used as the admission indication.
risk_stratification: At presentation document NEWS2/MEWS or the local deterioration score, frailty, baseline cognition/function, medication and antithrombotic exposure, renal/hepatic function, bleeding and VTE risk, infection risk, pressure-injury risk and goals of care. Escalate for shock (SBP <90 mm Hg or MAP <65), rising lactate, escalating oxygen or ventilatory need, active bleeding, new focal neurologic deficit, rapidly worsening mental status, uncontrolled pain, threatened organ/limb function or another need for ICU-level observation. Apply a validated disease- or procedure-specific score only when it fits the actual diagnosis; the DRG family itself is not a risk score.
initial_workup_labs: CBC, electrolytes, creatinine, magnesium, coagulation profile, type and screen; serial troponin, natriuretic peptide, lactate or blood gas when ischemia, failure or shock is possible. Trend only abnormalities that can change treatment, define baseline organ function before contrast or high-risk medication, and avoid standing daily panels once stable.
initial_imaging_dx: 12-lead ECG and telemetry; transthoracic or transesophageal echocardiography, vascular ultrasound, CT angiography, catheterization or device interrogation according to the syndrome. Reconcile findings with the actual principal diagnosis and procedure; repeat imaging only for deterioration, an unanswered management question or a protocol-defined surveillance need.
time_critical_interventions: Obtain ECG within 10 minutes for ischemic symptoms, activate emergency reperfusion or hemodynamic support pathways when indicated, and obtain immediate specialist review for shock, sustained arrhythmia, tamponade or device failure. Complete medication reconciliation, analgesia and prevention of avoidable delay at first assessment. If no emergency trigger is present, document the definitive diagnostic/procedural decision and level of care during the first attending review rather than applying a false universal time target.
initial_management: Confirm indication, laterality/site, consent, blood availability, antimicrobial prophylaxis only when indicated, anesthesia plan, medication/antithrombotic management and postoperative destination in a same-day multidisciplinary huddle. Use a procedure-specific checklist and do not substitute anticipated DRG assignment for clinical criteria. Tie each order to the actual cardiovascular and endovascular diagnosis, procedure and severity rather than the administrative grouping. Use goal-directed fluids, oxygen and transfusion rather than routine excess; provide multimodal analgesia and antiemetics; prevent delirium, pressure injury and falls; prescribe pharmacologic and/or mechanical VTE prophylaxis after individualized bleeding assessment; and define objective escalation, de-escalation and discharge criteria on admission.

clinical_verification_basis: Conservative MDC-level inpatient safety and transition-of-care synthesis anchored to audited catalog rank 23 (Cholecystectomy / Biliary Procedures). data_quality_notes: The original catalog is condition-level and contains intentional DRG overlaps; the new 120 rows use only active v43.1 codes absent from the first 130 rows.
